questions.stem: APAP toxicity causes liver damage via:

choices.feedback: APAP itself is not directly toxic.
APAP does not cause biliary obstruction.

choices.text: NAPQI accumulation when glutathione is depleted
choices.feedback: Correct! CYP2E1 converts APAP → NAPQI (toxic). Normally, glutathione neutralizes NAPQI. In overdose: glutathione depleted → NAPQI accumulates → centrilobular hepatic necrosis (zone 3). NAC works by replenishing glutathione stores and providing alternative substrate for NAPQI conjugation.
APAP toxicity is direct, not autoimmune.

questions.explanation: Acetaminophen hepatotoxicity is caused by NAPQI (N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine), a toxic metabolite produced by CYP2E1. At therapeutic doses, NAPQI is detoxified by conjugation with glutathione. In overdose, glutathione stores are depleted (below ~30% of normal), allowing NAPQI to bind hepatocyte proteins and cause centrilobular necrosis. NAC (N-acetylcysteine) works by replenishing glutathione, providing alternative sulfhydryl groups for NAPQI detoxification, and enhancing sulfate conjugation of acetaminophen. The toxic dose threshold is generally 150 mg/kg in a single ingestion.

NAC (N-acetylcysteine) works by replenishing glutathione, providing alternative sulfhydryl groups for NAPQI detoxification, and enhancing sulfate conjugation of acetaminophen. The toxic dose threshold is generally 150 mg/kg in a single ingestion.

questions.stem: APAP overdose: patient feels fine at 12 hours. Is this reassuring?

choices.text: Yes, if the ingestion was less than 10g
choices.feedback: Even at 7.5g, hepatotoxicity can develop.

choices.text: Yes — symptoms would have appeared by now
choices.feedback: Dangerously false! Stage I (0-24h) is deceptively asymptomatic.

choices.text: No — Stage I is asymptomatic despite ongoing hepatic damage
choices.feedback: Correct! The 'deceptive well period' is a hallmark of APAP toxicity. Patients feel well during Stage I (0-24h) while NAPQI is silently destroying hepatocytes. By Stage II (24-72h), AST/ALT spike and RUQ pain develops. This is why the nomogram and early NAC are critical — don't wait for symptoms.

choices.text: Only reassuring if liver enzymes are checked
choices.feedback: Liver enzymes may be normal in Stage I despite ongoing damage. APAP level and nomogram guide treatment.

questions.explanation: Stage I of acetaminophen toxicity (0-24 hours) is deceptively asymptomatic or causes only mild nonspecific symptoms (nausea, vomiting, malaise). Patients may feel completely well. However, hepatocellular damage is already occurring as NAPQI accumulates and depletes glutathione. Stage II (24-72 hours) shows rising AST/ALT and RUQ pain. Stage III (72-96 hours) is peak hepatotoxicity with potential fulminant liver failure. Stage IV (4-14 days) is recovery or death. The asymptomatic Stage I is clinically treacherous because patients and clinicians may underestimate the ingestion's severity.

The asymptomatic Stage I is clinically treacherous because patients and clinicians may underestimate the ingestion's severity.

questions.stem: NAC is most effective when given within:

choices.feedback: While NAC has benefit even late, efficacy drops significantly after 8h.

Correct! NAC within 8 hours → nearly 100% prevention of hepatotoxicity. Still beneficial after 8h (reduces severity). Even in established liver failure (Stage III), NAC improves outcomes and reduces mortality. Never 'too late' to give NAC if there's evidence of toxicity. Key: don't delay NAC waiting for nomogram results if >8h have passed.
While earlier is always better, the critical window is wider.
NAC works by multiple mechanisms including replenishing glutathione even after NAPQI has formed.

questions.explanation: NAC is most effective when given within 8 hours of acute acetaminophen ingestion — when administered in this window, it virtually eliminates the risk of hepatotoxicity. Efficacy decreases progressively after 8 hours but NAC still provides benefit at any point, even after liver failure has developed (by improving hemodynamics, oxygen delivery, and mitochondrial function). The Rumack-Matthew nomogram plots serum APAP level against time post-ingestion to determine treatment threshold; a level above the treatment line at any time point warrants NAC. Both IV (21-hour protocol) and oral (72-hour protocol) NAC regimens are effective.

Both IV (21-hour protocol) and oral (72-hour protocol) NAC regimens are effective.

questions.stem: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram is NOT valid for:

choices.feedback: Age doesn't affect nomogram validity.

choices.text: Single acute ingestions with known time
choices.feedback: This is exactly what the nomogram IS designed for.
The nomogram applies regardless of reported dose.

choices.text: Chronic/repeated ingestions or unknown time of ingestion
choices.feedback: Correct! The nomogram is only valid for single acute ingestions with known time (plot level at 4-24h post-ingestion). Invalid for: chronic/repeated dosing, unknown time, extended-release APAP, co-ingestion delaying absorption. In these cases → treat empirically with NAC if any concern for significant ingestion.

questions.explanation: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram is valid only for single acute acetaminophen ingestions with a known time of ingestion. It is NOT valid for: chronic/repeated ingestions (staggered dosing over time), unknown time of ingestion, sustained-release formulations, or presentations >24 hours post-ingestion. In these situations, NAC should be started empirically based on clinical judgment, APAP level, and liver function tests. The nomogram starts at 4 hours post-ingestion (earlier levels are unreliable due to ongoing absorption) and plots a treatment line from 150 mcg/mL at 4 hours to 4.7 mcg/mL at 24 hours.
questions.verifiedExplanation: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram is valid only for single acute acetaminophen ingestions with a known time of ingestion. It is NOT valid for: chronic/repeated ingestions (staggered dosing over time), unknown time of ingestion, sustained-release formulations, or presentations >24 hours post-ingestion. In these situations, NAC should be started empirically based on clinical judgment, APAP level, and liver function tests. The nomogram starts at 4 hours post-ingestion (earlier levels are unreliable due to ongoing absorption) and plots a treatment line from 150 mcg/mL at 4 hours to 4.7 mcg/mL at 24 hours.

questions.stem: Which zone of the liver is most affected in APAP toxicity?

choices.feedback: APAP toxicity has a specific centrilobular (zone 3) pattern.
Zone 2 is the least commonly affected in toxicology.
Zone 1 is affected in phosphorus poisoning and eclampsia.

Correct! Zone 3 (centrilobular, around central vein) has the highest concentration of CYP2E1 → most NAPQI production → greatest oxidative damage. Pathology: centrilobular hepatic necrosis. AST/ALT can exceed 10,000 IU/L (very high transaminases are characteristic of APAP toxicity and ischemic hepatitis).

questions.explanation: Zone 3 (centrilobular region) of the hepatic acinus is most affected in acetaminophen toxicity because CYP2E1, the enzyme that converts APAP to the toxic metabolite NAPQI, is concentrated in zone 3 hepatocytes. Zone 3 is also the most vulnerable to ischemic injury due to its position at the end of the sinusoidal blood flow (lowest oxygen tension). This creates a double hit: highest NAPQI production and lowest oxygen supply. Histologically, centrilobular necrosis is the hallmark of APAP toxicity. In contrast, zone 1 (periportal) injury is seen in phosphorus poisoning and eclampsia.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Zone 3 (centrilobular region) of the hepatic acinus is most affected in acetaminophen toxicity because CYP2E1, the enzyme that converts APAP to the toxic metabolite NAPQI, is concentrated in zone 3 hepatocytes. In contrast, zone 1 (periportal) injury is seen in phosphorus poisoning and eclampsia.

questions.stem: King's College Criteria for liver transplant in APAP-induced ALF include:

choices.feedback: Age is a factor in non-APAP King's criteria but not the primary APAP criteria.
High AST alone doesn't meet criteria.
APAP level doesn't directly predict need for transplant.

Correct! King's College Criteria for APAP-ALF: arterial pH <7.30 (refractory acidosis despite resuscitation) is independently sufficient. OR all three of: PT >100s (INR >6.5), Cr >3.4, grade III-IV encephalopathy. Meeting criteria → urgent transplant evaluation.

questions.explanation: King's College Criteria predict the need for liver transplantation in acetaminophen-induced acute liver failure. The criteria are: arterial pH <7.30 after adequate fluid resuscitation (single strongest predictor), OR all three of: INR >6.5 (PT >100 seconds), creatinine >3.4 mg/dL, and grade III-IV hepatic encephalopathy. Meeting these criteria carries a mortality exceeding 90% without transplantation. Other poor prognostic markers include lactate >3.5 mmol/L after early fluid resuscitation, rising lactate despite treatment, and factor V level <10%. Early referral to a transplant center is essential when trajectory suggests progression to ALF.

questions.stem: Chronic alcoholics have increased APAP toxicity risk because:

choices.feedback: The key mechanism is CYP2E1 induction and glutathione depletion.
Alcohol doesn't significantly increase APAP absorption.
NAC metabolism is not significantly affected by alcohol use.

Correct! Chronic alcohol: CYP2E1 upregulation (more NAPQI produced) + chronic malnutrition depletes glutathione → double hit. Toxicity can occur at lower APAP doses (<4g/day). Paradoxically, acute alcohol co-ingestion may be protective (competes for CYP2E1). Chronic use is the risk factor.

questions.explanation: Chronic alcoholics have increased susceptibility to acetaminophen hepatotoxicity through CYP2E1 enzyme induction. Chronic ethanol consumption upregulates CYP2E1, increasing the proportion of APAP metabolized to toxic NAPQI. Additionally, alcoholics often have depleted glutathione stores (from malnutrition and chronic oxidative stress), reducing their capacity to detoxify NAPQI. This means hepatotoxicity can occur at therapeutic or near-therapeutic doses in chronic alcoholics — the so-called 'therapeutic misadventure.' Some guidelines suggest lower treatment thresholds for chronic alcohol users, though the exact risk increase is debated.

questions.stem: Anaphylactoid reaction during IV NAC should be managed by:

choices.text: Giving epinephrine and stopping all treatment
choices.feedback: Epinephrine may be needed for severe reactions, but NAC should be continued.
Charcoal is for GI decontamination, not a substitute for NAC.

choices.text: Slowing the infusion rate and giving antihistamines
choices.feedback: Correct! IV NAC anaphylactoid reactions (flushing, urticaria, bronchospasm) are rate-related (most common during loading dose). Management: pause or slow infusion, give diphenhydramine ± albuterol for bronchospasm, then restart at slower rate. True IgE-mediated anaphylaxis to NAC is extremely rare. Do not withhold NAC for anaphylactoid symptoms.
Do not permanently discontinue — the benefit of NAC outweighs anaphylactoid risk.

questions.explanation: Anaphylactoid reactions during IV NAC administration (flushing, urticaria, bronchospasm, hypotension) occur in approximately 10-20% of patients, most commonly during the initial loading dose. Management is: slow or temporarily stop the infusion, administer antihistamines (diphenhydramine, famotidine), and for bronchospasm add inhaled bronchodilators. Epinephrine is reserved for severe anaphylaxis with hemodynamic instability. The reaction is rate-related (not true IgE-mediated allergy), so the infusion can typically be restarted at a slower rate after symptoms resolve. IV NAC should NOT be permanently discontinued due to an anaphylactoid reaction — the benefit of hepatoprotection outweighs the manageable reaction risk.

questions.stem: What differentiates NSTEMI from unstable angina?

choices.feedback: Both conditions involve CAD with plaque instability.
ST elevation defines STEMI. Both NSTEMI and UA can show ST depression or T-wave changes.
Correct! Troponin elevation = myocardial necrosis = NSTEMI. UA has similar symptoms/EKG but negative troponin (no cell death). High-sensitivity troponin detectable within 1-3 hours.
Both UA and NSTEMI can present with chest pain at rest.

questions.explanation: NSTEMI and unstable angina both represent non-ST-elevation acute coronary syndromes caused by partial coronary artery occlusion. The critical distinction is biomarker evidence of myocardial necrosis: NSTEMI shows elevated cardiac troponin (indicating myocyte death), while unstable angina has normal troponins (ischemia without infarction). Both may present identically with chest pain and ECG changes (ST depression, T-wave inversion), so serial troponin measurements at 0 and 3-6 hours are essential. High-sensitivity troponin assays have improved early detection but also require clinical correlation to avoid false positives from non-ACS causes (PE, myocarditis, renal failure).
references: Basit H, Malik A, Huecker MR. Non-ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK513228/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: Door-to-balloon time goal for STEMI?

choices.feedback: Correct! PCI door-to-balloon ≤90 min (≤120 min if transfer to PCI-capable facility). If PCI not available within 120 min, give fibrinolytics (door-to-needle ≤30 min). Every minute of delay increases mortality.
Far too long. Rapid reperfusion is critical.
30 min is the door-to-needle goal for fibrinolytics.
While benefit exists up to 12h, the target is ≤90 min.

questions.explanation: The AHA/ACC STEMI guidelines recommend a door-to-balloon time of ≤90 minutes for primary PCI at PCI-capable hospitals. This benchmark reflects the time-dependent nature of myocardial salvage: every 30-minute delay in reperfusion increases 1-year mortality. If PCI cannot be performed within 120 minutes of first medical contact, fibrinolytic therapy should be administered within 30 minutes (door-to-needle time). The total ischemic time goal (symptom onset to reperfusion) should be <120 minutes per 2023 ESC guidelines. These time metrics are core quality measures tracked by the AHA Mission: Lifeline program.
references: Singh A, Museedi AS, Grossman SA. Acute Coronary Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459157/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: Inferior STEMI with hypotension, clear lungs, and JVD. What should be AVOIDED?

choices.feedback: IV fluids are the CORRECT treatment — RV infarct is preload-dependent.

Correct! RV infarct = preload-dependent. Nitroglycerin causes venodilation → decreased preload → hemodynamic collapse. Also avoid morphine and diuretics. Treatment: IV fluids to maintain RV preload. Recognize by: inferior STEMI + hypotension + clear lungs + JVD. Confirm with right-sided EKG (ST elevation V4R).
Anticoagulation is still indicated in ACS with RV infarct.
Aspirin should be given to all ACS patients regardless of RV involvement.

questions.explanation: This triad — inferior STEMI, hypotension, clear lungs, and elevated JVP — indicates right ventricular infarction, which complicates approximately 30-50% of inferior STEMIs (the RCA supplies both the inferior LV wall and RV). The RV becomes preload-dependent because it cannot generate adequate forward flow. Nitroglycerin (and other vasodilators/diuretics) reduce preload, which can precipitate catastrophic hemodynamic collapse in RV infarction. Management requires aggressive IV fluid resuscitation to maintain RV preload. Right-sided ECG leads (V4R showing ST elevation) confirm RV involvement.
references: Singh A, Museedi AS, Grossman SA. Acute Coronary Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459157/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: Most IMPORTANT initial medication in ACS?

choices.feedback: P2Y12 inhibitors are important adjuncts but aspirin is the first-line priority.

Correct! Aspirin is the single most important initial therapy. Chewing provides faster buccal absorption than swallowing. Irreversibly inhibits platelet COX-1, blocking thromboxane A2 synthesis and platelet aggregation.
Nitroglycerin relieves ischemic symptoms but doesn't reduce mortality like aspirin.
Morphine is used cautiously for refractory pain only. Evidence suggests it may worsen outcomes.

questions.explanation: Aspirin (non-enteric coated, chewed for rapid buccal absorption) is the single most important initial medication in ACS. It irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) in platelets, blocking thromboxane A2 production and reducing platelet aggregation at the site of plaque rupture. Every minute of delay in aspirin administration increases mortality. It should be given even before the ECG is obtained if ACS is suspected. Contraindications: true aspirin allergy or active GI hemorrhage.
references: Basit H, Malik A, Huecker MR. Non-ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK513228/ [Open Access]

questions.verifiedExplanation: Aspirin (162-325 mg non-enteric coated, chewed for rapid buccal absorption) is the single most important initial medication in ACS. It irreversibly inhibits platelet cyclooxygenase-1, blocking thromboxane A2 production and reducing platelet aggregation at the ruptured plaque site. The ISIS-2 trial demonstrated a 23% relative reduction in vascular mortality with aspirin alone. Chewing ensures rapid bioavailability (peak antiplatelet effect within 15 minutes vs 60+ minutes for swallowed tablets). Aspirin should be given to all ACS patients regardless of planned management strategy, unless true aspirin allergy exists.

questions.stem: New harsh holosystolic murmur with thrill on day 4 post-MI. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Post-MI VSD presents day 3-5 with a new harsh holosystolic murmur at the left sternal border WITH a palpable thrill (key distinguishing feature from MR). Causes acute left-to-right shunt → heart failure. Diagnosis: echocardiogram shows septal defect with color flow. Treatment: emergent surgical repair.
AS causes a systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur, not a new holosystolic murmur post-MI.
Dressler = pericarditis (friction rub, fever) occurring weeks post-MI, not a murmur with thrill.
Papillary muscle rupture causes acute MR (holosystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla) but typically without a palpable thrill.

questions.explanation: A new holosystolic murmur with a palpable thrill occurring 3-5 days after MI is the classic presentation of ventricular septal rupture (VSR). Coagulation necrosis thins and weakens the infarcted septum, which then ruptures under ventricular pressure, creating a left-to-right shunt. The thrill at the left sternal border distinguishes VSR from papillary muscle rupture (PMR), which also presents at 3-5 days post-MI with a holosystolic murmur but at the apex radiating to the axilla, typically without a thrill, and with acute pulmonary edema from severe mitral regurgitation. Diagnosis is confirmed by echocardiography showing the septal defect with left-to-right flow, or by right heart catheterization demonstrating an oxygen saturation step-up in the RV. VSR carries 40-80% mortality without surgical intervention.
references: Mubarik A, Iqbal AM. Ventricular Septal Rupture. [Updated 2022 Sep 19]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK534857/ [Open Access]

layer2.notes: Answer is correct. However, the EXISTING explanation was WRONG — it discussed aortic stenosis and mitral regurgitation instead of VSD. This is a critical error that required correction.

questions.stem: Anterior wall STEMI involves which coronary artery?

choices.feedback: LCx supplies the lateral wall (leads I, aVL, V5-V6).
Left main occlusion is usually rapidly fatal or presents as massive anterior + lateral STEMI.
RCA supplies the inferior wall (leads II, III, aVF) in 85% of patients.

Correct! LAD supplies the anterior wall. Anterior STEMI (leads V1-V4) = LAD occlusion. The LAD is called the 'widow-maker' because anterior MI carries the highest mortality and risk of cardiogenic shock/LV dysfunction.

questions.explanation: The LAD supplies the anterior wall of the left ventricle, the anterior two-thirds of the interventricular septum, and the apex. LAD occlusion causes anterior STEMI with ST elevation in leads V1-V4 (extending to V5-V6 with proximal LAD lesions). Other coronary territory correlations: the right coronary artery (RCA) supplies the inferior wall (leads II, III, aVF), and the left circumflex (LCx) supplies the lateral wall (leads I, aVL, V5-V6).
references: Akbar H, Mountfort S. Acute ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI). [Updated 2024 Oct 6]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532281/ [Open Access]

layer1.supportingText: Electrocardiogram Tracing for a Case of Proximal Left Anterior Descending Occlusion (image reference in context of STEMI discussion)
layer1.note: This is fundamental coronary anatomy universally taught. StatPearls-STEMI references LAD in context of anterior STEMI.

layer2.notes: Coronary anatomy is a fixed anatomical fact. LAD supplies anterior wall.

questions.stem: Fibrinolytics are CONTRAINDICATED in which situation?

choices.feedback: Prior PCI is not a contraindication to fibrinolytics.

Correct! Absolute contraindications to fibrinolytics: aortic dissection (catastrophic hemorrhage), active internal bleeding, prior intracranial hemorrhage, ischemic stroke <3 months, brain tumor, significant head trauma <3 months. Aortic dissection can mimic STEMI (inferior MI from RCA involvement) — always consider before giving lytics.
This is an indication, not a contraindication.
Advanced age increases bleeding risk but is not an absolute contraindication.

questions.explanation: Suspected aortic dissection is an absolute contraindication to fibrinolytic therapy because thrombolysis would dissolve the clot sealing the intimal tear, causing catastrophic hemorrhage into the false lumen and likely fatal rupture. Other absolute contraindications include: active internal bleeding (excluding menses), prior intracranial hemorrhage, ischemic stroke within 3 months, and known intracranial neoplasm. Prior PCI (even years ago) is not a contraindication. Age >75 is a relative consideration but not a contraindication — per StatPearls-STEMI, patients 75 and older should receive half the normal dose rather than being excluded entirely.
references: Akbar H, Mountfort S. Acute ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI). [Updated 2024 Oct 6]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532281/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: Post-ACS, dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) should continue for:

choices.feedback: Too short — premature discontinuation increases stent thrombosis risk.
Lifelong aspirin monotherapy, but DAPT is typically limited to 12 months due to bleeding risk.
Stopping DAPT at discharge dramatically increases recurrent events.

Correct! Standard DAPT (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor) for 12 months post-ACS, regardless of whether PCI/stent was performed. After 12 months, continue aspirin indefinitely. P2Y12 options: ticagrelor (preferred — reversible, superior to clopidogrel per PLATO trial) or clopidogrel. May shorten to 6 months if high bleeding risk.

questions.explanation: Current AHA/ACC guidelines recommend 12 months of dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT: aspirin + a P2Y12 inhibitor) after ACS, regardless of whether the patient undergoes PCI with stenting or is managed medically. This duration balances the reduced risk of recurrent ischemic events against the increased bleeding risk. P2Y12 inhibitor options include ticagrelor (preferred per 2023 ESC, reversible, given twice daily), prasugrel (irreversible, contraindicated in prior stroke/TIA), or clopidogrel (if others are contraindicated or unaffordable). After 12 months, DAPT may be extended or shortened based on individualized ischemic vs. bleeding risk assessment.
references: Singh A, Museedi AS, Grossman SA. Acute Coronary Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459157/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: A 25-year-old has periumbilical pain migrating to RLQ over 12 hours, with anorexia and low-grade fever. CT confirms appendicitis. Treatment?

choices.feedback: CT-confirmed appendicitis warrants surgical intervention, not continued observation.
Correct! Appendectomy (laparoscopic preferred) is standard for acute appendicitis. Classic migration pattern + CT confirmation warrants surgery. Delay >48-72h increases perforation risk.
Colonoscopy has no role in acute appendicitis and risks perforation.
Antibiotics alone may be considered in select uncomplicated cases, but appendectomy remains standard of care.

questions.explanation: Laparoscopic appendectomy is the definitive treatment for confirmed acute appendicitis. It offers lower wound infection rates, less postoperative pain, and faster recovery compared to open appendectomy. While antibiotics-first strategies have been studied, surgical management remains standard of care for uncomplicated appendicitis, particularly in young patients. Observation with serial exams is appropriate only when the diagnosis is uncertain. Colonoscopy is not indicated in acute appendicitis. IV antibiotics alone may be considered in select cases but are not first-line definitive treatment.

questions.stem: Elderly patient with A-fib has severe abdominal pain but a soft, non-tender abdomen with elevated lactate. Most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Classic: pain OUT OF PROPORTION to exam + A-fib (embolic source) + elevated lactate. Abdomen surprisingly benign despite severe pain. CT angiography diagnostic. Vascular emergency with 60-80% mortality.
PUD causes epigastric tenderness. The exam-pain discrepancy is hallmark of mesenteric ischemia.
IBS does not cause elevated lactate or acute severe pain in this context.
Cholecystitis causes RUQ tenderness and Murphy sign. Pain out of proportion to exam suggests mesenteric ischemia.

questions.explanation: Acute mesenteric ischemia classically presents with 'pain out of proportion to exam' — severe abdominal pain with a deceptively benign physical examination (soft, non-tender abdomen). Atrial fibrillation is the most common source of arterial embolism to the superior mesenteric artery. Elevated lactate indicates tissue ischemia/necrosis. CT angiography is the diagnostic study of choice. Mortality is high (60-80%) without early intervention. PUD would have epigastric tenderness. IBS does not cause elevated lactate. Cholecystitis presents with RUQ pain and Murphy sign.

questions.stem: Free air under the diaphragm on upright CXR indicates:

choices.feedback: Appendicitis does not typically cause pneumoperitoneum.
Pneumonia shows infiltrates, not subdiaphragmatic free air.
SBO shows air-fluid levels and dilated loops, not subdiaphragmatic free air.
Correct! Pneumoperitoneum = perforation of a hollow viscus. Surgical emergency requiring laparotomy. Most common cause: perforated peptic ulcer.

questions.explanation: Free air (pneumoperitoneum) on upright chest X-ray is pathognomonic for perforation of a hollow viscus (stomach, duodenum, colon). The most common cause is perforated peptic ulcer. This finding requires emergent surgical consultation. Appendicitis rarely causes free perforation with pneumoperitoneum. Pneumonia causes pulmonary infiltrates, not subdiaphragmatic air. Small bowel obstruction shows air-fluid levels and dilated loops but not free air unless perforation has occurred.

questions.stem: In a reproductive-age female with acute abdominal pain, what test should ALWAYS be obtained first?

choices.feedback: Correct! ALWAYS obtain β-hCG in reproductive-age females with abdominal pain. Ectopic pregnancy can rupture and is a surgical emergency. A positive test changes differential and imaging approach entirely.
CT involves radiation. Must exclude pregnancy first — ectopic is life-threatening and changes the entire approach.
Lipase evaluates pancreatitis but doesn't address the critical need to exclude ectopic pregnancy.
UA helps evaluate UTI/stones but doesn't rule out ectopic pregnancy.

questions.explanation: A β-hCG (pregnancy test) must always be obtained first in any reproductive-age female presenting with acute abdominal pain. Ectopic pregnancy is a life-threatening emergency that can mimic many abdominal conditions and must be excluded before proceeding with other diagnostics or treatments. CT abdomen/pelvis involves radiation exposure harmful in pregnancy. Lipase and urinalysis are appropriate tests but secondary to ruling out pregnancy. This is a fundamental clinical reasoning principle across emergency medicine and surgery.

questions.stem: Most RELIABLE physical exam finding indicating peritonitis?

choices.feedback: Localized tenderness is less specific than involuntary guarding for generalized peritonitis.
Correct! Involuntary guarding = reflex contraction of abdominal muscles from peritoneal inflammation. Cannot be overcome by distraction. Most reliable sign of peritonitis.
Rebound is suggestive but less reliable and can be unreliable.
Hyperactive sounds suggest early obstruction. Peritonitis causes absent/hypoactive sounds.

questions.explanation: Involuntary guarding (rigidity) is the most reliable physical exam finding for peritonitis because it represents a true visceral reflex that cannot be voluntarily produced or suppressed by the patient. It indicates peritoneal irritation causing involuntary abdominal wall muscle contraction. RLQ tenderness is localizing but nonspecific. Rebound tenderness is suggestive but can be unreliable and causes unnecessary patient discomfort — current guidelines recommend using percussion tenderness instead. Hyperactive bowel sounds suggest obstruction, not peritonitis (which typically causes absent/diminished bowel sounds).

questions.stem: A 70-year-old with sudden back pain, hypotension, and pulsatile abdominal mass. Immediate management?

choices.feedback: Ruptured AAA requires surgery, not observation.
In unstable patients with classic presentation, proceed to OR without imaging delay.
Do NOT delay for imaging in an unstable patient with classic ruptured AAA triad.
Correct! Hypotension + pulsatile mass + back pain = ruptured AAA. Unstable → emergent OR. Mortality 80-90% untreated.

questions.explanation: The triad of sudden back/abdominal pain, hypotension, and pulsatile abdominal mass is classic for ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (rAAA), a surgical emergency with >80% mortality without intervention. Emergent surgical repair (open or endovascular) is the only definitive treatment. IV fluids support hemodynamics but permissive hypotension is preferred to avoid disrupting any clot. Diagnostic imaging should not delay surgery in hemodynamically unstable patients with classic presentation. CT angiography is appropriate only if the patient is hemodynamically stable and diagnosis is uncertain.

questions.stem: Rovsing, psoas, and obturator signs are associated with:

choices.feedback: Pancreatitis has Cullen and Grey Turner signs, not these.
Cholecystitis has Murphy sign, not these.
Diverticulitis causes LLQ pain but these signs are specifically appendicitis signs.
Correct! Rovsing = RLQ pain with LLQ palpation. Psoas = pain with hip extension (retrocecal appendix). Obturator = pain with internal rotation of flexed hip (pelvic appendix).

questions.explanation: Rovsing sign (RLQ pain with palpation of LLQ), psoas sign (RLQ pain with right hip extension — retrocecal appendix irritating the psoas muscle), and obturator sign (RLQ pain with internal rotation of flexed right hip — pelvic appendix near obturator internus) are all classically associated with acute appendicitis. These signs help localize the inflamed appendix based on its anatomic position. Pancreatitis presents with epigastric pain radiating to the back. Cholecystitis has Murphy sign. Diverticulitis presents with LLQ pain.

questions.stem: LLQ pain in elderly patients is most commonly caused by:

choices.feedback: Ovarian torsion affects younger women, not typically elderly patients.
True left-sided appendicitis only occurs in situs inversus totalis (extremely rare).

Correct! Diverticulitis is the #1 cause of LLQ pain in elderly. CT shows pericolic fat stranding, wall thickening, ± abscess. Uncomplicated: antibiotics + bowel rest. Complicated: percutaneous drainage or surgery.
Volvulus causes obstruction with distension and coffee-bean sign, not typical acute LLQ pain.

questions.explanation: Diverticulitis is the most common cause of LLQ pain in elderly patients. Left-sided diverticula are most prevalent in Western populations, and inflammation/microperforation produces localized LLQ pain, fever, and leukocytosis. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the diagnostic study of choice. Ovarian torsion is more common in younger women. Left-sided appendicitis is rare (occurs with situs inversus or long appendix). Sigmoid volvulus can cause LLQ pain but presents more commonly with abdominal distension and obstipation.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old presents with bone pain, bruising, and hepatosplenomegaly. CBC shows WBC 45,000 with 80% blasts that are TdT+, CD10+, and PAS+. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! TdT+ (immature lymphoid) + CD10+ (CALLA/common ALL antigen) + PAS+ = B-cell ALL. This is the most common pediatric cancer with peak age 2-5.
CML blast crisis occurs in adults with preceding chronic phase. This is a child with de novo disease.
T-cell ALL would be CD3/CD7+ and often presents with mediastinal mass. CD10+ points to B-cell.
AML blasts would be MPO+, Auer rods, TdT-negative. This pattern is lymphoid.

questions.stem: A patient with newly diagnosed AML presents with DIC and scattered Auer rods forming bundles ('faggot cells'). What translocation is expected, and what treatment must be started immediately?

choices.feedback: inv(16) is M4eo — favorable prognosis but not the APL/DIC picture.
t(9;22) is Philadelphia chromosome — CML or Ph+ ALL, not APL.

Correct! APL (M3) = t(15;17) PML-RARA with DIC and faggot cells (bundles of Auer rods). ATRA must be started IMMEDIATELY upon clinical suspicion — do NOT wait for genetic confirmation. Delays in ATRA increase mortality from DIC-related hemorrhage.
t(8;21) is M2 AML with good prognosis but doesn't typically present with DIC and faggot cells.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the most important prognostic factor that distinguishes favorable from unfavorable childhood ALL?

choices.feedback: Ethnicity affects some outcomes but is not the most important prognostic factor.

Correct! Cytogenetics is the most important prognostic factor. Hyperdiploidy (>50 chromosomes) and t(12;21) = excellent prognosis. Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) and hypodiploidy = poor prognosis. Cytogenetics determines risk stratification and treatment intensity.
Hemoglobin is less prognostic than WBC count and cytogenetics.
Gender has minor prognostic significance (males slightly worse due to testicular relapse), but cytogenetics is far more important.

questions.explanation: Correct: Cytogenetics (hyperdiploidy vs Philadelphia chromosome).

questions.stem: A patient starting chemotherapy for Burkitt lymphoma develops hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hyperuricemia, and acute kidney injury within 24 hours. What preventive agent could have reduced this risk?

choices.feedback: Hydroxyurea reduces WBC in leukostasis but doesn't prevent TLS.

Correct! Rasburicase (recombinant urate oxidase) converts uric acid to allantoin (water-soluble) and is used for TLS prevention/treatment in high-risk patients. It works faster than allopurinol. Contraindicated in G6PD deficiency (produces hydrogen peroxide).
Filgrastim is G-CSF for neutropenia, not TLS prevention.
Dexamethasone treats differentiation syndrome in APL, not tumor lysis.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with BMI 34 and no alcohol history develops acute pancreatitis. Her ALT is 210 U/L. What is the most likely etiology, and what finding supports this?

choices.text: Gallstone pancreatitis — ALT >150 U/L within 48 hours has >85% positive predictive value for biliary etiology
Hypertriglyceridemia — elevated ALT indicates fatty liver with secondary pancreatic inflammation
Autoimmune pancreatitis — obesity and elevated liver enzymes suggest IgG4-related disease
Drug-induced pancreatitis — elevated ALT indicates hepatotoxic medication as the underlying cause

questions.explanation: An ALT greater than 150 U/L within 48 hours of presentation has a positive predictive value exceeding 85% for gallstone pancreatitis. This elevated transaminase reflects hepatocyte injury from transient biliary obstruction by a migrating gallstone. In a patient with no alcohol history and risk factors for cholelithiasis (female, obesity, elevated BMI), gallstone etiology should be strongly suspected. Right upper quadrant ultrasound is the initial imaging study to identify gallstones, dilated common bile duct, or biliary sludge. MRCP or endoscopic ultrasound can further evaluate if suspicion remains high despite negative ultrasound.

This elevated transaminase reflects hepatocyte injury from transient biliary obstruction by a migrating gallstone. In a patient with no alcohol history and risk factors for cholelithiasis (female, obesity, elevated BMI), gallstone etiology should be strongly suspected. Right upper quadrant ultrasound is the initial imaging study to identify gallstones, dilated common bile duct, or biliary sludge. MRCP or endoscopic ultrasound can further evaluate if suspicion remains high despite negative ultrasound.

questions.stem: When ordering pancreatic enzymes for suspected acute pancreatitis, why is serum lipase preferred over amylase as the initial diagnostic marker?

choices.text: Lipase has greater sensitivity and specificity for pancreatic injury and remains elevated longer than amylase
Lipase is exclusively produced by the pancreas whereas amylase is exclusively produced by the salivary glands
Lipase levels directly correlate with disease severity and can guide the need for ICU admission
Amylase is no longer available as a standard laboratory assay at most institutions

questions.explanation: Serum lipase is preferred over amylase for diagnosing acute pancreatitis because it has greater sensitivity and specificity. Lipase remains elevated longer than amylase (up to 14 days vs 3-5 days for amylase), making it more useful in delayed presentations. Amylase can be elevated in many non-pancreatic conditions (salivary gland disease, bowel obstruction, renal failure, macroamylasemia), reducing its specificity. Neither enzyme correlates with disease severity — a patient with mild lipase elevation can have severe necrotizing pancreatitis, and very high levels do not predict worse outcomes. Diagnosis requires lipase or amylase at least 3 times the upper limit of normal per the Revised Atlanta Classification.

Neither enzyme correlates with disease severity — a patient with mild lipase elevation can have severe necrotizing pancreatitis, and very high levels do not predict worse outcomes. Diagnosis requires lipase or amylase at least 3 times the upper limit of normal per the Revised Atlanta Classification.

auditLayers.layer2_accuracyCheck: CORRECT. Lipase preferred, more sensitive/specific, longer elevation.
auditLayers.layer4_crossReference: Consistent with ACG and AGA guidelines

questions.stem: Early enteral nutrition in pancreatitis is preferred over TPN because:

choices.feedback: Nutrition doesn't change the disease course directly but reduces complications.

Correct! Enteral nutrition maintains intestinal mucosal barrier → prevents bacterial translocation → reduces infected necrosis and sepsis. Start oral feeding within 24h as tolerated. If unable to eat: nasojejunal tube feeding. TPN only as last resort (increases infection risk, cholestasis).
While true, the clinical rationale is more important.
Pain is not the distinguishing factor.

questions.explanation: Early enteral nutrition (within 24 hours if tolerated) is preferred over TPN in acute pancreatitis because it maintains gut mucosal barrier integrity, preventing bacterial translocation from the gut lumen into the bloodstream. Gut barrier breakdown is a major contributor to infected pancreatic necrosis and systemic inflammatory response in severe pancreatitis. Enteral feeding also stimulates gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT), preserving immune function. Multiple randomized trials show enteral nutrition reduces infectious complications, organ failure, surgical interventions, and mortality compared to TPN. Oral feeding can be attempted first; nasojejunal tube feeding is used if oral intake is not tolerated.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Early enteral nutrition (within 24 hours if tolerated) is preferred over TPN in acute pancreatitis because it maintains gut mucosal barrier integrity, preventing bacterial translocation from the gut lumen into the bloodstream. Gut barrier breakdown is a major contributor to infected pancreatic necrosis and systemic inflammatory response in severe pancreatitis. Enteral feeding also stimulates gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT), preserving immune function. Multiple randomized trials show enteral nutrition reduces infectious complications, organ failure, surgical interventions, and mortality compared to TPN. Oral feeding can be attempted first; nasojejunal tube feeding is used if oral intake is not tolerated.

auditLayers.layer2_accuracyCheck: CORRECT. Enteral nutrition maintains gut barrier, reduces translocation.
auditLayers.layer4_crossReference: AGA 2018 guidelines, ESPEN guidelines

verifiedReferences.title: Acute Pancreatitis
verifiedReferences.authors: Wang CF, Tariq A, Chandra S

questions.stem: When are antibiotics indicated in acute pancreatitis?

choices.feedback: Leukocytosis is common in pancreatitis from inflammation alone and doesn't mandate antibiotics.

choices.text: Confirmed infected necrosis or concurrent cholangitis
choices.feedback: Correct! Antibiotics only for: infected necrosis (fever + gas on CT at 2-4 weeks → FNA confirms → carbapenems), or cholangitis (gallstone pancreatitis + Charcot triad → ERCP + antibiotics). Prophylactic antibiotics in sterile necrosis increase mortality.
Prophylactic antibiotics are NOT recommended — no benefit and increase resistant organisms/fungal infection.
Lipase level doesn't determine antibiotic need.

questions.explanation: Antibiotics in acute pancreatitis are indicated only for confirmed infected pancreatic necrosis or concurrent cholangitis (ascending biliary infection). Prophylactic antibiotics are NOT recommended — multiple randomized controlled trials and meta-analyses show no mortality benefit and increased risk of fungal superinfection. Infected necrosis is suspected when clinical deterioration occurs (persistent fever, rising inflammatory markers) after the first 7-10 days. CT finding of gas within necrotic collections is highly suggestive. CT-guided fine-needle aspiration can confirm infection when clinical suspicion is equivocal. ERCP with sphincterotomy is indicated when cholangitis accompanies gallstone pancreatitis.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Antibiotics in acute pancreatitis are indicated only for confirmed infected pancreatic necrosis or concurrent cholangitis (ascending biliary infection). ERCP with sphincterotomy is indicated when cholangitis accompanies gallstone pancreatitis.

auditLayers.layer2_accuracyCheck: CORRECT. No prophylactic ABx; only for confirmed infection.
auditLayers.layer4_crossReference: ACG 2024, AGA 2018 guidelines

questions.stem: A resident calculates a Ranson score for a patient admitted with acute alcoholic pancreatitis. Which limitation of this scoring system is most clinically significant?

choices.text: It requires 48 hours of data collection before the full score can be calculated, limiting early triage
It can only be applied to gallstone pancreatitis and is invalid for alcoholic pancreatitis
It does not incorporate any laboratory values and relies solely on clinical examination
It has been fully replaced by CT Severity Index in all current guidelines

questions.explanation: The Ranson criteria is a widely known severity scoring system for acute pancreatitis, but its major limitation is that it requires a full 48 hours of data collection before the complete score can be calculated — 5 criteria at admission and 6 criteria at 48 hours. This delay limits its usefulness for early clinical decision-making. The BISAP score (BUN >25, impaired mental status, SIRS, age >60, pleural effusion) can be calculated at admission and is similarly predictive. The APACHE II score can also be applied early. The Revised Atlanta Classification focuses on organ failure duration (transient vs persistent >48 hours) as the primary determinant of severity.

The Revised Atlanta Classification focuses on organ failure duration (transient vs persistent >48 hours) as the primary determinant of severity.

auditLayers.layer2_accuracyCheck: CORRECT. 48h data collection is the key limitation.
auditLayers.layer4_crossReference: Consistent with ACG guidelines on scoring systems

verifiedReferences.title: Acute Pancreatitis

questions.stem: A patient with severe pancreatitis develops persistent fever on day 10. CT shows pancreatic necrosis with gas bubbles. What does this finding indicate and what is the next step?

choices.text: Infected pancreatic necrosis — requires CT-guided aspiration for culture and targeted antibiotics, with possible necrosectomy
Sterile pancreatic necrosis — gas is from tissue breakdown and requires only continued supportive care
Pancreatic pseudocyst formation — requires urgent surgical drainage regardless of symptoms
Pancreatic abscess — requires immediate open surgical debridement as first-line treatment

questions.explanation: Persistent fever on day 10 of severe pancreatitis with CT showing gas within a necrotic collection is highly suggestive of infected pancreatic necrosis. Gas in the necrotic collection results from gas-producing bacteria (often enteric organisms from gut translocation). This is a critical distinction from sterile necrosis, which is managed conservatively. Infected necrosis requires intervention: the preferred modern approach is a 'step-up' strategy starting with percutaneous or endoscopic drainage, escalating to minimally invasive necrosectomy only if drainage fails. CT-guided fine needle aspiration with Gram stain and culture can confirm infection when clinical presentation is ambiguous. Direct open surgical debridement is now reserved as a last resort.

auditLayers.layer2_accuracyCheck: CORRECT. Gas + fever + day 10 = infected necrosis.
auditLayers.layer4_crossReference: PANTER trial (step-up approach), ACG guidelines

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man is admitted with acute pancreatitis (lipase 950, HR 110, BP 100/65). What is the cornerstone of initial management?

choices.text: Aggressive IV crystalloid resuscitation with goal-directed fluid therapy targeting hemodynamic improvement
Emergent CT abdomen with contrast to grade severity before initiating any treatment
Prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics to prevent infected pancreatic necrosis
Urgent ERCP within 6 hours regardless of suspected etiology

questions.explanation: Aggressive IV crystalloid resuscitation is the cornerstone of initial management in acute pancreatitis. Early goal-directed fluid therapy corrects hypovolemia (from third-spacing, vomiting, and increased vascular permeability), maintains pancreatic microcirculation, and prevents organ failure. Lactated Ringer's solution is preferred over normal saline as it may have anti-inflammatory properties and reduces the risk of hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis. Fluid resuscitation should be initiated immediately and titrated to clinical endpoints (urine output, heart rate, mean arterial pressure). CT imaging is NOT needed emergently — it is most useful 72-96 hours after onset for assessing necrosis. Prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated. ERCP is reserved for concurrent cholangitis.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Aggressive IV crystalloid resuscitation is the cornerstone of initial management in acute pancreatitis. Early goal-directed fluid therapy corrects hypovolemia (from third-spacing, vomiting, and increased vascular permeability), maintains pancreatic microcirculation, and prevents organ failure. Fluid resuscitation should be initiated immediately and titrated to clinical endpoints (urine output, heart rate, mean arterial pressure). CT imaging is NOT needed emergently — it is most useful 72-96 hours after onset for assessing necrosis. Prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated. ERCP is reserved for concurrent cholangitis.

auditLayers.layer2_accuracyCheck: CORRECT. Early aggressive crystalloid is cornerstone.
auditLayers.layer4_crossReference: ACG 2024, AGA guidelines on LR preference

questions.stem: A patient with chronic fatigue, hypotension, and darkening of skin creases has a morning cortisol of 2 mcg/dL and ACTH of 250 pg/mL (elevated). After cosyntropin stimulation, cortisol rises to only 4 mcg/dL. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Cushing would have elevated cortisol, not low cortisol.
Hypothyroidism causes fatigue but not hyperpigmentation or abnormal cortisol/ACTH.

Correct! Low cortisol + HIGH ACTH + failed cosyntropin test + hyperpigmentation = primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison). The adrenals cannot respond to ACTH stimulation. Autoimmune adrenalitis (21-hydroxylase antibodies) is the most common cause.
Secondary AI has LOW ACTH. This patient has elevated ACTH, indicating the pituitary is responding appropriately to low cortisol.

questions.explanation: Correct: Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease).

questions.stem: A 24-hour urine free cortisol is 4x the upper limit of normal. ACTH is suppressed (<5 pg/mL). What is the next best test?

choices.feedback: Correct! Suppressed ACTH = the adrenals are producing cortisol independently (ACTH-independent Cushing). Next step: CT adrenals to look for adenoma or carcinoma.
High-dose DST is used to distinguish pituitary from ectopic ACTH sources. This patient has suppressed ACTH, so the source is adrenal, not pituitary.
MRI pituitary is for ACTH-dependent Cushing (high ACTH). This patient has suppressed ACTH.
IPSS is for distinguishing pituitary from ectopic ACTH when ACTH is elevated. Not needed here.

questions.explanation: Endocrine diagnosis requires systematic evaluation of hormone levels and their regulatory axes. Management follows evidence-based guidelines from the Endocrine Society and ADA.

questions.stem: A patient on chronic prednisone for rheumatoid arthritis is hospitalized for pneumonia. They become hypotensive and unresponsive to fluids. What should you give immediately?

choices.text: Continue oral prednisone at the same dose
choices.feedback: Maintenance oral steroids are inadequate during acute physiologic stress. The suppressed HPA axis cannot mount an appropriate cortisol response.

Correct! This is adrenal crisis triggered by physiologic stress in a patient with suppressed HPA axis. IV hydrocortisone provides both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid activity needed in acute stress.
Topical steroids have no systemic effect and cannot replace the cortisol surge needed during acute illness.

choices.text: Discontinue all steroids to allow HPA axis recovery
choices.feedback: Abrupt discontinuation during acute illness would precipitate life-threatening adrenal crisis.

questions.explanation: Endocrine diagnosis requires systematic evaluation of hormone levels and their regulatory axes. Management follows evidence-based guidelines from the Endocrine Society and ADA.

questions.stem: What clinical feature distinguishes primary from secondary adrenal insufficiency?

choices.feedback: Correct! Hyperpigmentation is present in PRIMARY (Addison) because ACTH is elevated. ACTH is cleaved from POMC, which also produces MSH (melanocyte-stimulating hormone). In secondary AI, ACTH is LOW → no hyperpigmentation.
Fatigue occurs in both primary and secondary AI.
Hyponatremia occurs in both (cortisol loss increases ADH). Hyperkalemia is specific to primary (aldosterone loss).
Hypotension can occur in both (though more severe in primary due to aldosterone loss).

questions.explanation: Adrenal insufficiency: primary (Addison's — autoimmune destruction, elevated ACTH, hyperpigmentation, hyperkalemia) vs secondary (pituitary — low ACTH, no hyperpigmentation, no hyperkalemia). Diagnosis: cosyntropin (ACTH) stimulation test — cortisol should rise >18 mcg/dL. Adrenal crisis: hypotension, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia — treat with IV hydrocortisonebolus + NS. Chronic: hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone (primary only).

questions.stem: The hallmark EKG finding in atrial fibrillation is:

choices.text: Absent P waves with irregularly irregular R-R intervals
choices.feedback: Correct! No organized P waves (chaotic atrial activity creates fibrillatory baseline) + irregularly irregular ventricular response. These two features together are pathognomonic for A-fib.
A-fib has narrow QRS unless concurrent bundle branch block or aberrant conduction.
Sawtooth (flutter waves) = atrial flutter, not A-fib.
Regular narrow complex = SVT (AVNRT, AVRT). A-fib is IRREGULARLY irregular.

questions.explanation: The hallmark EKG finding of atrial fibrillation is absent P waves with irregularly irregular R-R intervals. The absence of organized atrial activity (P waves) reflects the chaotic, disorganized electrical depolarization of the atria. The resulting ventricular response is irregularly irregular because AV nodal conduction of these chaotic impulses varies beat to beat. Wide QRS complexes suggest ventricular tachycardia or bundle branch block, not AF (AF typically has narrow QRS unless pre-existing conduction disease or WPW). A sawtooth pattern is characteristic of atrial FLUTTER, not fibrillation. Regular narrow-complex tachycardia suggests SVT (AVNRT, AVRT), not AF — regularity is the key distinguishing feature.
references: Nesheiwat Z, Goyal A, Jagtap M. Atrial Fibrillation. [Updated 2023 Apr 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK526072/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: On ECG, atrial fibrillation presents with the typical narrow complex irregularly irregular pattern with no distinguishable p-waves

questions.stem: A patient with A-fib, HTN, DM, and age 70. CHA₂DS₂-VASc score and management?

choices.feedback: Calculate: HTN(1) + DM(1) + age 65-74(1) = 3, not 2.
Score is 3. Even at high scores, DOACs are preferred over warfarin unless mechanical valve or mitral stenosis.
Correct! HTN = 1, DM = 1, Age 65-74 = 1. Total = 3. Score ≥2 = anticoagulate. DOACs (apixaban preferred — lowest bleeding in ARISTOTLE trial) are first-line over warfarin for non-valvular A-fib.
Score is 3, not 1. Also, aspirin alone is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in A-fib.

questions.explanation: CHA₂DS₂-VASc scoring: C (CHF) = 1, H (HTN) = 1, A₂ (Age ≥75) = 2, D (DM) = 1, S₂ (prior Stroke/TIA) = 2, V (Vascular disease) = 1, A (Age 65-74) = 1, Sc (Sex category female) = 1. This patient: HTN (1) + DM (1) + Age 70 (1, for age 65-74 category) = Score of 3. With a score ≥2 in males (≥3 in females), oral anticoagulation is recommended. DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) are preferred over warfarin for non-valvular AF due to predictable pharmacokinetics, no INR monitoring, fewer drug-food interactions, and comparable or superior efficacy with lower intracranial hemorrhage risk. Aspirin alone is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in AF at any CHA₂DS₂-VASc score per current guidelines.
references: Nesheiwat Z, Goyal A, Jagtap M. Atrial Fibrillation. [Updated 2023 Apr 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK526072/ [Open Access]

supporting.keyFinding: Aspirin is not recommended in patients with low CHA2DS2-VASc scores

questions.stem: When is warfarin preferred over DOACs in A-fib?

choices.feedback: Some DOACs (apixaban) can be used with dose adjustment even in severe CKD. Warfarin is an option but not exclusively required.
DOACs are safe and preferred even in elderly patients.

choices.text: Mechanical heart valve or moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis
choices.feedback: Correct! Warfarin is required for 'valvular' A-fib: mechanical prosthetic valves and moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis. DOACs are NOT approved for these — the RE-ALIGN trial (dabigatran in mechanical valves) was stopped early due to increased thromboembolism and bleeding.
DOACs are preferred for paroxysmal A-fib with appropriate CHA₂DS₂-VASc score.

questions.explanation: Warfarin is preferred over DOACs specifically in patients with mechanical heart valves or moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis (rheumatic valvular AF). StatPearls explicitly states that DOACs 'are recommended over warfarin, except for patients with moderate to severe MS with a mechanical heart valve in place.' The RE-ALIGN trial demonstrated that dabigatran was inferior to warfarin in mechanical valve patients (increased thromboembolism and bleeding), leading to its contraindication in this population. Mitral stenosis creates LA enlargement and stasis independent of the mechanisms DOACs target. Renal failure with GFR <30 requires dose adjustment or avoidance of certain DOACs, but warfarin can still be used — however, this is not the PRIMARY indication where warfarin is preferred. The definitive indications remain mechanical valves and moderate-severe MS.
references: Nesheiwat Z, Goyal A, Jagtap M. Atrial Fibrillation. [Updated 2023 Apr 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK526072/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: A-fib of unknown duration. Before cardioversion, you must:

choices.text: Anticoagulate 3 weeks OR perform TEE to exclude LA thrombus
choices.feedback: Correct! A-fib ≥48h or unknown: 3 weeks anticoagulation → cardiovert → 4 weeks post-cardioversion anticoagulation. OR: TEE → if no LA appendage thrombus → cardiovert immediately with anticoagulation. Either approach acceptable.

choices.text: Cardioversion is never safe after 48 hours
choices.feedback: Cardioversion is safe after appropriate anticoagulation or TEE clearance.
Unknown duration could mean >48h → thrombus risk. Must anticoagulate or rule out thrombus.

choices.text: Give aspirin for 1 week then cardiovert
choices.feedback: Aspirin is insufficient for stroke prevention around cardioversion.

questions.explanation: Before cardioverting AF of unknown duration (or >48 hours), you must either: (1) anticoagulate therapeutically for at least 3 weeks, then cardiovert, then continue anticoagulation for at least 4 weeks post-cardioversion; OR (2) perform TEE to exclude left atrial thrombus, then cardiovert with concurrent anticoagulation, then continue for at least 4 weeks. StatPearls states: 'If the duration of AF is more than 48 hours or if the time is unknown, start anticoagulation and maintain INR between 2-3 or commence a factor Xa inhibitor for at least three weeks before and at least four weeks after cardioversion.' The 48-hour threshold exists because thrombus formation risk increases with AF duration. Without these precautions, cardioversion carries a 5-7% stroke risk from embolization of pre-formed thrombus or from post-cardioversion atrial stunning. 'Just cardiovert immediately' is dangerous; aspirin alone is insufficient anticoagulation.
references: Nesheiwat Z, Goyal A, Jagtap M. Atrial Fibrillation. [Updated 2023 Apr 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK526072/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: Rate control medications for A-fib include all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: Digoxin is used as adjunct rate control, especially in HF patients.

Correct! Flecainide is a rhythm control drug (Class IC antiarrhythmic) used to restore/maintain sinus rhythm, NOT for rate control. It can paradoxically INCREASE ventricular rate in A-fib by organizing atrial activity into flutter (must co-administer with AV nodal blocker). Rate control agents: BBs, non-DHP CCBs, digoxin.
Beta-blockers are first-line rate control agents.
Non-DHP CCBs are effective rate control agents (avoid in HFrEF).

questions.explanation: Flecainide is a class IC antiarrhythmic used for RHYTHM control (converting AF to sinus rhythm), NOT for rate control. It works by blocking sodium channels and slowing conduction through the atria, which can terminate AF. It does NOT slow the ventricular rate during ongoing AF and can actually be dangerous — flecainide can organize AF into atrial flutter with 1:1 conduction, paradoxically INCREASING the ventricular rate. Rate control agents work by slowing conduction through the AV node to reduce ventricular response during AF: beta-blockers (metoprolol) block sympathetic drive to the AV node; non-dihydropyridine CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) slow AV nodal conduction directly; digoxin increases vagal tone at the AV node. The distinction between rate control (controlling ventricular rate during AF) and rhythm control (converting to/maintaining sinus rhythm) is fundamental to AF management.
references: Nesheiwat Z, Goyal A, Jagtap M. Atrial Fibrillation. [Updated 2023 Apr 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK526072/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Rate or rhythm control should be given to the patient; medications such as beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, amiodarone, dronedarone, and digoxin are available options

questions.stem: Most common cause of atrial fibrillation?

choices.feedback: Thyrotoxicosis is an important and reversible cause but not the most common.
Mitral stenosis is a classic cause but not the most common overall.

Correct! HTN is the #1 cause of A-fib due to left atrial enlargement from chronic pressure overload. Other important causes: valvular disease (especially mitral), HF, thyrotoxicosis (always check TSH in new A-fib), alcohol, PE, post-cardiac surgery, OSA.
'Holiday heart' is a well-known cause but less common than HTN.

questions.explanation: Hypertension is the most common cause of atrial fibrillation, owing to its high prevalence and its direct pathophysiological effects on the atria. Chronic hypertension causes left ventricular hypertrophy, which increases left atrial pressure, leading to left atrial enlargement and fibrosis — the substrate for AF. StatPearls lists hypertension prominently among AF etiologies and notes that 'advanced age, high blood pressure, underlying heart and lung disease' are key risk factors. While thyrotoxicosis, mitral stenosis, and alcohol use are all well-recognized causes of AF, they are far less prevalent in the population than hypertension. Mitral stenosis is a classic association (especially rheumatic heart disease) but relatively uncommon in developed countries. Thyrotoxicosis should always be screened for in new-onset AF but accounts for a small minority of cases. Alcohol use ('holiday heart syndrome') is a recognized trigger but not the most common underlying cause.
references: Nesheiwat Z, Goyal A, Jagtap M. Atrial Fibrillation. [Updated 2023 Apr 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK526072/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: EAST-AFNET 4 trial showed that early rhythm control:

choices.text: Has no benefit over rate control
choices.feedback: That was the AFFIRM trial conclusion. EAST-AFNET 4 challenged this.

choices.text: Should only be used in patients under 50
choices.feedback: The trial included patients of various ages and showed benefit across subgroups.

choices.text: Reduces cardiovascular outcomes when initiated within 1 year of diagnosis
choices.feedback: Correct! EAST-AFNET 4 (2020): early rhythm control (within 1 year of A-fib diagnosis) reduced composite of CV death, stroke, and HF hospitalization by 21%. This shifted practice toward earlier rhythm control, especially with catheter ablation becoming safer and more effective.
Anticoagulation decisions are based on CHA₂DS₂-VASc score regardless of rhythm control strategy.

questions.explanation: The EAST-AFNET 4 trial (Kirchhof et al., NEJM 2020) demonstrated that early rhythm control — initiated within 1 year of AF diagnosis — reduced the composite of cardiovascular death, stroke, and hospitalization for heart failure or ACS compared to usual care (rate control with escalation only if symptomatic). This was a landmark shift because prior trials (AFFIRM, RACE) had shown no survival advantage of rhythm control — but those enrolled patients with longstanding AF and used older antiarrhythmics. EAST-AFNET 4 specifically targeted EARLY intervention. Key caveats: early rhythm control does NOT eliminate the need for anticoagulation — stroke prevention based on CHA₂DS₂-VASc score must continue regardless of rhythm strategy. The benefit was not limited to patients under 50; the trial enrolled patients across a wide age range with recent-onset AF and cardiovascular risk factors.
references: Kirchhof P, Camm AJ, Goette A, et al. Early rhythm-control therapy in patients with atrial fibrillation. N Engl J Med. 2020;383:1305-1316. [Open Access via PMC]

Nesheiwat Z, Goyal A, Jagtap M. Atrial Fibrillation. [Updated 2023 Apr 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK526072/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Evidence shows that rhythm control does not offer a survival advantage over rate control [note: this predates EAST-AFNET 4 which showed early rhythm control IS beneficial]

supporting.keyFinding: Early rhythm control within 1 year of diagnosis reduced composite of CV death, stroke, and hospitalization

questions.stem: Digoxin in A-fib rate control — key limitation?

choices.feedback: Digoxin is available in both oral and IV formulations.

choices.text: It only controls resting heart rate, not exercise rate
choices.feedback: Correct! Digoxin controls resting HR via vagal enhancement but does NOT control exercise HR (sympathetic activation overrides vagal tone). This is why it's used as an adjunct, not first-line. Best role: HFrEF patients with A-fib (inotropic + rate control). Narrow therapeutic window — monitor for toxicity (visual changes, nausea, arrhythmias).

choices.text: It converts A-fib to sinus rhythm
choices.feedback: Digoxin does NOT cardiovert — it's purely for rate control.
Bradycardia is a side effect but not the key limitation.

questions.explanation: Digoxin's key limitation in AF rate control is that it only controls resting heart rate, not exercise heart rate. Digoxin works by increasing vagal tone at the AV node (parasympathetic effect), which slows conduction during rest. However, during exercise, sympathetic activation overwhelms digoxin's vagal effect, allowing the ventricular rate to increase unchecked. This makes digoxin inadequate as monotherapy for rate control in active patients. StatPearls notes digoxin 'is not advised as a first-line agent pertaining to its adverse effects and tolerance.' Other limitations include its narrow therapeutic index (toxicity risk with hypokalemia, renal impairment), and observational data suggesting possible increased mortality. Digoxin is available in both oral and IV forms (so that distractor is wrong). It does NOT convert AF to sinus rhythm — it is strictly a rate control agent. While bradycardia can occur with toxicity, the primary clinical limitation is inadequate exercise rate control.
references: Nesheiwat Z, Goyal A, Jagtap M. Atrial Fibrillation. [Updated 2023 Apr 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK526072/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Digoxin can be considered for rate control but is not advised as a first-line agent pertaining to its adverse effects and tolerance
