questions.stem: FeNa <1% with elevated BUN:Cr ratio (>20:1) indicates:

choices.feedback: ATN has FeNa >2% — tubules can't reabsorb sodium.
CKD is chronic, not acute. BUN:Cr is typically 10-15:1.
Post-renal has variable FeNa and hydronephrosis on US.

Correct! FeNa <1% = kidneys are avidly retaining sodium (tubules intact, just underperfused). BUN:Cr >20:1 because urea is reabsorbed more than creatinine in low-flow states. Treatment: volume resuscitation. If FeNa >2% + muddy brown casts → ATN (tubular damage).

questions.explanation: Fractional excretion of sodium (FeNa) <1% indicates avid sodium reabsorption by functioning tubules, consistent with pre-renal AKI (hypovolemia, heart failure, hepatorenal syndrome). The elevated BUN:Cr ratio (>20:1) reflects increased urea reabsorption in the proximal tubule during low-flow states while creatinine is not reabsorbed. In ATN (intrinsic renal failure), FeNa is typically >2% because damaged tubules cannot reabsorb sodium. Note: FeNa may be <1% in contrast nephropathy, myoglobinuria, and early obstruction despite intrinsic/post-renal etiologies.

questions.stem: Muddy brown granular casts on urinalysis indicate:

choices.feedback: AIN shows WBC casts and urine eosinophils.
GN shows RBC casts.

Correct! Muddy brown granular casts = sloughed tubular epithelial cells from necrosis. Pathognomonic for ATN. Other ATN clues: FeNa >2%, BUN:Cr 10-15:1, urine osmolality ~300 (isosthenuria — tubules can't concentrate).
Pre-renal has bland sediment (hyaline casts at most).

questions.explanation: Muddy brown granular casts are pathognomonic for acute tubular necrosis (ATN). They represent sloughed, necrotic tubular epithelial cells and cellular debris within the tubular lumen. ATN is the most common cause of intrinsic AKI, caused by ischemia (prolonged pre-renal state) or nephrotoxins (aminoglycosides, contrast, myoglobin). AIN shows WBC casts and eosinophiluria. Glomerulonephritis shows RBC casts and dysmorphic red cells. Pre-renal azotemia has a bland sediment (hyaline casts only).

questions.stem: Classic triad of acute interstitial nephritis?

choices.feedback: Correct! AIN: drug-induced hypersensitivity. Classic triad (not always present): fever + maculopapular rash + peripheral eosinophilia. Urine: WBC casts, urine eosinophils (Hansel stain). Common culprits: penicillins, NSAIDs, PPIs, sulfonamides. Treatment: discontinue drug ± steroids.
That's nephritic syndrome (glomerulonephritis).
That's pyelonephritis.
That suggests nephrotic/nephritic syndrome or CHF.

questions.explanation: The classic triad of acute interstitial nephritis (AIN) is fever, maculopapular rash, and peripheral eosinophilia. AIN is most commonly drug-induced (NSAIDs, penicillins, cephalosporins, PPIs, sulfonamides). Urinalysis shows WBC casts, sterile pyuria, and eosinophiluria (detected by Hansel stain). The triad of hematuria, proteinuria, and hypertension describes nephritic syndrome. Flank pain, hematuria, and fever suggest pyelonephritis. Oliguria, edema, and hypertension describe nephrotic/nephritic overlap.

questions.stem: AEIOU indications for emergent dialysis — the 'E' stands for:

choices.feedback: Encephalopathy falls under 'U' (uremia).
Correct! Refractory hyperkalemia (K+ >6.5 with EKG changes not responding to medical therapy) is an emergent dialysis indication. Full AEIOU: Acidosis, Electrolytes, Intoxication, Overload, Uremia.
Embolism is not a dialysis indication.
Edema from fluid overload is 'O' (Overload).

questions.explanation: In the AEIOU mnemonic for emergent dialysis indications, E = Electrolytes (specifically refractory hyperkalemia unresponsive to medical management). The full mnemonic: A = Acidosis (severe metabolic acidosis refractory to bicarbonate), E = Electrolytes (refractory hyperkalemia), I = Ingestions (dialyzable toxins: methanol, ethylene glycol, lithium, salicylates), O = Overload (fluid overload refractory to diuretics), U = Uremia (uremic encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding). Encephalopathy falls under U (uremia), not E.

questions.stem: Contrast-induced nephropathy prevention?

choices.feedback: Steroids prevent contrast allergy reactions, not nephropathy.
Prophylactic dialysis does not prevent CIN.
Diuretics worsen CIN by causing dehydration.

choices.text: IV isotonic saline hydration before and after contrast
choices.feedback: Correct! Pre- and post-hydration with IV normal saline or sodium bicarbonate is the most effective prevention strategy. Also: use lowest contrast volume, iso-osmolar contrast, hold metformin (lactic acidosis risk if AKI develops), avoid concurrent nephrotoxins. NAC (N-acetylcysteine) has uncertain benefit.

questions.explanation: IV isotonic saline (normal saline or isotonic bicarbonate) hydration before and after contrast administration is the primary evidence-based strategy for preventing contrast-induced nephropathy (CIN). Volume expansion maintains renal perfusion and promotes contrast dilution and excretion. Oral prednisone is used for contrast allergy prophylaxis, not nephropathy prevention. Prophylactic dialysis does not prevent CIN and is not recommended. Furosemide can worsen nephrotoxicity by causing volume depletion. N-acetylcysteine was previously used but evidence for benefit is now considered insufficient.

questions.stem: Post-renal AKI in elderly male is most commonly caused by:

choices.feedback: NSAIDs cause intrinsic AKI, not post-renal obstruction.

Correct! BPH causes bladder outlet obstruction → bilateral hydronephrosis → post-renal AKI. #1 cause of post-renal AKI in older males. Diagnosis: renal ultrasound (bilateral hydronephrosis) + elevated post-void residual. Treatment: Foley catheter for immediate decompression.
MCD causes nephrotic syndrome, not post-renal obstruction.
That's a pre-renal (or renovascular) cause.

questions.explanation: Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) is the most common cause of post-renal (obstructive) AKI in elderly males. The enlarged prostate compresses the prostatic urethra, causing bilateral hydronephrosis and obstructive uropathy. Bladder ultrasound showing elevated post-void residual and renal ultrasound showing hydronephrosis confirm the diagnosis. Foley catheter placement is both diagnostic and therapeutic. ATN from NSAIDs is intrinsic (not post-renal). Minimal change disease causes nephrotic syndrome. Renal artery stenosis causes pre-renal AKI.

questions.stem: ATN typically resolves in:

choices.feedback: Too quick — tubular regeneration takes longer.

Correct! ATN is self-limiting. Tubular epithelial cells regenerate over 1-3 weeks. Polyuric phase follows oliguric phase (recovering tubules initially can't concentrate urine). Management is supportive: maintain euvolemia, avoid nephrotoxins, adjust drug doses, dialysis if indicated.
If recovery takes months, consider chronic kidney disease.
Most ATN episodes recover, though some progress to CKD.

questions.explanation: ATN typically resolves in 1-3 weeks as tubular epithelial cells regenerate. During recovery, patients may experience a polyuric phase with significant electrolyte losses requiring monitoring and replacement. Oliguric ATN generally has a longer recovery than non-oliguric ATN. Recovery within 24-48 hours is too rapid — this timeframe is more consistent with pre-renal AKI reversal. Most ATN does resolve (unlike cortical necrosis, which is irreversible). Six to twelve months would suggest chronic kidney disease, not typical ATN recovery.

questions.stem: Which drug combination is particularly nephrotoxic?

choices.feedback: Common and safe cardiovascular combination.

Correct! 'Triple whammy': NSAIDs (constrict afferent arteriole) + ACEi/ARBs (dilate efferent arteriole) + diuretics (volume depletion) → dramatically reduced GFR → AKI. Each drug alone is manageable, but the combination is highly nephrotoxic, especially in elderly or CKD patients. Avoid this combination.
Generally safe combination (metformin held only if AKI develops).
This is a safe combination at appropriate doses.

questions.explanation: NSAIDs + ACE inhibitors/ARBs is a particularly nephrotoxic combination. NSAIDs block prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriole vasodilation while ACEi/ARBs block angiotensin II-mediated efferent arteriole vasoconstriction. Together, they critically reduce glomerular perfusion pressure and GFR. Adding a diuretic creates the 'triple whammy' — the most dangerous combination for AKI. Aspirin + statin, metformin + lisinopril, and acetaminophen + amoxicillin are not recognized nephrotoxic combinations (though metformin requires dose adjustment in renal impairment).

questions.stem: Delirium tremens typically occurs at what time after last drink?

choices.feedback: Withdrawal takes hours to develop after cessation.

Correct! DT peaks at 48-96h (can be later). Timeline: minor withdrawal (6-12h) → hallucinosis (12-24h) → seizures (24-48h) → DT (48-96h). DT features: delirium + autonomic storm + hallucinations. 5-15% mortality untreated. Requires ICU-level care.
By 7-10 days, most withdrawal has resolved.
6-12h = minor withdrawal symptoms (tremor, anxiety, diaphoresis).

questions.explanation: Delirium tremens (DT) typically occurs 48-96 hours after the last drink, though it can present up to 7 days post-cessation. DT represents the most severe form of alcohol withdrawal, characterized by profound autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, diaphoresis), altered sensorium with disorientation, and hallucinations (often visual — classically tactile hallucinations of insects). DT carries mortality of 5-15% even with treatment (historically up to 35% untreated). Risk factors include prior DT episodes, concurrent illness, older age, prolonged/heavy alcohol use, and elevated CIWA scores. The alcohol withdrawal timeline progresses: tremor (6-12h), seizures (12-48h), hallucinations (12-48h), DT (48-96h).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Delirium tremens (DT) typically occurs 48-96 hours after the last drink, though it can present up to 7 days post-cessation. DT represents the most severe form of alcohol withdrawal, characterized by profound autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, diaphoresis), altered sensorium with disorientation, and hallucinations (often visual — classically tactile hallucinations of insects). DT carries mortality of 5-15% even with treatment (historically up to 35% untreated). Risk factors include prior DT episodes, concurrent illness, older age, prolonged/heavy alcohol use, and elevated CIWA scores. The alcohol withdrawal timeline progresses: tremor (6-12h), seizures (12-48h), hallucinations (12-48h), DT (48-96h).

questions.stem: First-line treatment for alcohol withdrawal?

choices.feedback: Correct! BZDs are first-line: cross-tolerance with alcohol at GABA-A receptor. Long-acting (diazepam, chlordiazepoxide) provide smoother withdrawal. Lorazepam preferred in liver failure (no hepatic metabolism). Symptom-triggered (CIWA-based) dosing is preferred over fixed-schedule — lower total dose, shorter treatment.
Antipsychotics lower seizure threshold. Only adjunct for agitation after BZDs.
SSRIs treat depression/anxiety but have no role in acute withdrawal management.
Phenytoin is NOT effective for alcohol withdrawal seizures.

questions.explanation: Benzodiazepines are the first-line treatment for alcohol withdrawal across all severity levels. They work by enhancing GABA-A receptor activity, counteracting the CNS hyperexcitability caused by withdrawal-related GABA downregulation and glutamate/NMDA upregulation. Symptom-triggered dosing using the CIWA-Ar protocol is preferred over fixed-schedule dosing — it results in less total benzodiazepine administered and shorter treatment duration. Long-acting agents (diazepam, chlordiazepoxide) provide smoother control with self-tapering pharmacokinetics. Short-acting agents (lorazepam) are preferred in liver failure. Benzodiazepines prevent progression to seizures and DT when dosed appropriately.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Benzodiazepines are the first-line treatment for alcohol withdrawal across all severity levels. They work by enhancing GABA-A receptor activity, counteracting the CNS hyperexcitability caused by withdrawal-related GABA downregulation and glutamate/NMDA upregulation. Symptom-triggered dosing using the CIWA-Ar protocol is preferred over fixed-schedule dosing — it results in less total benzodiazepine administered and shorter treatment duration. Long-acting agents (diazepam, chlordiazepoxide) provide smoother control with self-tapering pharmacokinetics. Short-acting agents (lorazepam) are preferred in liver failure. Benzodiazepines prevent progression to seizures and DT when dosed appropriately.

questions.stem: Why give thiamine BEFORE glucose?

choices.text: Glucose metabolism consumes thiamine → can precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy
choices.feedback: Correct! Glucose metabolism requires thiamine as a cofactor (pyruvate dehydrogenase, transketolase). IV glucose in a thiamine-depleted patient → accelerates remaining thiamine depletion → acute Wernicke encephalopathy (confusion + ataxia + ophthalmoplegia). Give thiamine IV first. This order is critical.
Glucose doesn't destroy thiamine.

choices.text: Glucose causes hyperkalemia unless thiamine is given
choices.feedback: This interaction doesn't exist.
Thiamine doesn't affect glucose absorption.

questions.explanation: Thiamine must be given BEFORE or concurrent with glucose in alcoholic patients because glucose metabolism through pyruvate dehydrogenase and the Krebs cycle requires thiamine as a cofactor. In thiamine-depleted patients (chronic alcoholics have depleted stores), a glucose load increases metabolic demand for thiamine, potentially exhausting the last remaining reserves and precipitating acute Wernicke encephalopathy (confusion, oculomotor dysfunction, ataxia). Wernicke encephalopathy is a medical emergency — untreated, it progresses to Korsakoff syndrome (irreversible amnestic disorder). IV thiamine should be given empirically to all patients with suspected alcohol use disorder.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Thiamine must be given BEFORE or concurrent with glucose in alcoholic patients because glucose metabolism through pyruvate dehydrogenase and the Krebs cycle requires thiamine as a cofactor. In thiamine-depleted patients (chronic alcoholics have depleted stores), a glucose load increases metabolic demand for thiamine, potentially exhausting the last remaining reserves and precipitating acute Wernicke encephalopathy (confusion, oculomotor dysfunction, ataxia). Wernicke encephalopathy is a medical emergency — untreated, it progresses to Korsakoff syndrome (irreversible amnestic disorder). IV thiamine should be given empirically to all patients with suspected alcohol use disorder.

questions.stem: Alcoholic hallucinosis differs from DT by:

choices.feedback: Both have hallucinations — that's the name.
DT has SEVERE autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia).
Both can have visual hallucinations.

choices.text: Hallucinosis: CLEAR sensorium. DT: IMPAIRED sensorium (delirium)
choices.feedback: Correct! Alcoholic hallucinosis (12-24h): vivid hallucinations but patient is oriented, conversational, and knows the hallucinations aren't real. DT (48-96h): TRUE delirium — disorientation, confusion, agitation, fluctuating consciousness, plus hallucinations. DT is far more dangerous.

questions.explanation: Alcoholic hallucinosis is distinguished from delirium tremens by a CLEAR sensorium. In alcoholic hallucinosis (occurring 12-48 hours after last drink), the patient experiences vivid hallucinations (typically auditory, sometimes visual) but remains oriented, alert, and cognitively intact — they know the hallucinations are not real. In contrast, delirium tremens (48-96 hours) features impaired sensorium with disorientation, confusion, agitation, and the patient cannot distinguish hallucinations from reality. Alcoholic hallucinosis generally resolves within 24-48 hours and carries a much better prognosis than DT. Treatment is benzodiazepines and supportive care.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Alcoholic hallucinosis is distinguished from delirium tremens by a CLEAR sensorium. In alcoholic hallucinosis (occurring 12-48 hours after last drink), the patient experiences vivid hallucinations (typically auditory, sometimes visual) but remains oriented, alert, and cognitively intact — they know the hallucinations are not real. In contrast, delirium tremens (48-96 hours) features impaired sensorium with disorientation, confusion, agitation, and the patient cannot distinguish hallucinations from reality. Alcoholic hallucinosis generally resolves within 24-48 hours and carries a much better prognosis than DT. Treatment is benzodiazepines and supportive care.

questions.stem: Which benzodiazepine is preferred in liver failure?

choices.feedback: Diazepam has active metabolites requiring hepatic metabolism (long-acting → accumulation in liver failure).

Correct! LOT mnemonic: Lorazepam, Oxazepam, Temazepam — no hepatic CYP metabolism (glucuronidation only), no active metabolites. Preferred in liver dysfunction. Diazepam and chlordiazepoxide have active hepatically-metabolized metabolites → risk of accumulation and prolonged sedation.
Midazolam is hepatically metabolized via CYP3A4.
Chlordiazepoxide has multiple active metabolites requiring hepatic metabolism.

questions.explanation: Lorazepam is the preferred benzodiazepine in patients with significant liver disease (cirrhosis, hepatitis) because it undergoes phase II hepatic metabolism only (direct glucuronidation) — it has no active metabolites and its clearance is minimally affected by liver dysfunction. In contrast, diazepam and chlordiazepoxide undergo phase I oxidative metabolism (CYP450-dependent) to produce long-acting active metabolites (desmethyldiazepam, demoxepam) that accumulate in liver failure, causing prolonged sedation and respiratory depression. Oxazepam and temazepam also undergo only glucuronidation and are alternatives, but lorazepam is available IV, making it the most practical choice.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Lorazepam is the preferred benzodiazepine in patients with significant liver disease (cirrhosis, hepatitis) because it undergoes phase II hepatic metabolism only (direct glucuronidation) — it has no active metabolites and its clearance is minimally affected by liver dysfunction. In contrast, diazepam and chlordiazepoxide undergo phase I oxidative metabolism (CYP450-dependent) to produce long-acting active metabolites (desmethyldiazepam, demoxepam) that accumulate in liver failure, causing prolonged sedation and respiratory depression. Oxazepam and temazepam also undergo only glucuronidation and are alternatives, but lorazepam is available IV, making it the most practical choice.

questions.stem: CIWA-Ar score >20 indicates:

choices.text: Severe withdrawal with high DT risk
choices.feedback: Correct! CIWA-Ar >20: severe withdrawal requiring aggressive benzodiazepine treatment. High risk for progression to seizures and DT. ICU monitoring recommended. CIWA-based symptom-triggered dosing: administer BZDs when score ≥10, reassess q1h. This approach results in less total medication use and shorter treatment.
BZDs are always first-line. Antipsychotics lower seizure threshold.
<10 is mild. >20 is severe.

choices.text: Patient is safe for outpatient detox
choices.feedback: >20 is too severe for outpatient management.

questions.explanation: A CIWA-Ar (Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol — revised) score >20 indicates severe alcohol withdrawal with high risk of progression to delirium tremens and seizures. The CIWA-Ar rates 10 domains (nausea/vomiting, tremor, paroxysmal sweats, anxiety, agitation, tactile/auditory/visual disturbances, headache, orientation/clouding) on a scale of 0-67. Scores <10: mild withdrawal, may not require pharmacotherapy. 10-18: moderate withdrawal, treat with benzodiazepines. >20: severe withdrawal, aggressive benzodiazepine dosing required, consider ICU monitoring. Symptom-triggered protocols use CIWA scores assessed every 1-2 hours to guide benzodiazepine administration.
questions.verifiedExplanation: A CIWA-Ar (Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol — revised) score >20 indicates severe alcohol withdrawal with high risk of progression to delirium tremens and seizures. The CIWA-Ar rates 10 domains (nausea/vomiting, tremor, paroxysmal sweats, anxiety, agitation, tactile/auditory/visual disturbances, headache, orientation/clouding) on a scale of 0-67. Scores <10: mild withdrawal, may not require pharmacotherapy. 10-18: moderate withdrawal, treat with benzodiazepines. >20: severe withdrawal, aggressive benzodiazepine dosing required, consider ICU monitoring. Symptom-triggered protocols use CIWA scores assessed every 1-2 hours to guide benzodiazepine administration.

questions.stem: Phenobarbital is useful in alcohol withdrawal because:

choices.feedback: It treats acute withdrawal but doesn't prevent seizures permanently.
Phenobarbital is a potent sedative.

choices.text: It directly activates GABA receptors even when BZD-resistant
choices.feedback: Correct! Phenobarbital is a barbiturate that directly opens GABA-A chloride channels (allosteric agonist) — works even when BZD binding sites are downregulated in severe withdrawal. Increasingly used as first-line or adjunct in severe/refractory withdrawal. Longer half-life provides steady-state coverage. Risk: respiratory depression.

choices.text: It treats the underlying liver disease
choices.feedback: Phenobarbital has no effect on liver disease treatment.

questions.explanation: Phenobarbital is useful in alcohol withdrawal because it directly activates GABA-A receptors at a different binding site than benzodiazepines, providing efficacy even when benzodiazepine tolerance or resistance develops. Benzodiazepines are allosteric modulators that enhance GABA's effect (they require GABA to be present). Barbiturates can open chloride channels independently of GABA at higher doses — this makes phenobarbital effective for benzodiazepine-resistant alcohol withdrawal. It has a long half-life (80-120 hours) providing sustained symptom control. Phenobarbital is increasingly used as primary therapy or adjunctive therapy for severe withdrawal, particularly in ICU settings.

Benzodiazepines are allosteric modulators that enhance GABA's effect (they require GABA to be present). It has a long half-life (80-120 hours) providing sustained symptom control. questions.stem: Kindling effect in alcohol withdrawal refers to:

choices.feedback: Kindling is not related to temperature.

choices.text: The calming effect of the first drink
choices.feedback: That's tolerance/dependence, not kindling.

choices.text: A specific type of seizure unique to alcoholism
choices.feedback: Kindling is a concept, not a seizure type.

choices.text: Each successive withdrawal episode becomes more severe due to neuronal sensitization
choices.feedback: Correct! Repeated withdrawal episodes → progressive neuronal sensitization (NMDA receptor upregulation) → each subsequent withdrawal is more severe with higher seizure risk and DT risk. This is why patients with multiple prior detoxifications are at highest risk. Supports the argument for adequate treatment of each withdrawal episode.

questions.explanation: The kindling effect in alcohol withdrawal refers to the phenomenon where each successive withdrawal episode becomes more severe than the previous one. This occurs through neuroadaptive changes: repeated cycles of alcohol use and withdrawal cause progressive upregulation of excitatory NMDA glutamate receptors and downregulation of inhibitory GABA receptors. Over time, this lowers the seizure threshold and increases the risk and severity of complications (seizures, DT) with each withdrawal episode. A patient who had mild tremor during a first withdrawal may develop seizures or DT during subsequent withdrawals. The kindling concept supports the rationale for aggressive treatment of early withdrawal episodes to prevent neurological progression.

A patient who had mild tremor during a first withdrawal may develop seizures or DT during subsequent withdrawals. The kindling concept supports the rationale for aggressive treatment of early withdrawal episodes to prevent neurological progression.

questions.stem: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Limb-body wall complex is more severe (thoracoabdominal wall defects, limb-body attachment) and may overlap with ABS but is a distinct entity.
Adams-Oliver causes terminal transverse limb defects + aplasia cutis congenita (scalp defects). It follows Mendelian inheritance. ABS is sporadic and has constriction rings.

Correct! ABS occurs when the amnion ruptures, releasing fibrous bands that entangle fetal parts. The bands constrict limbs (rings), amputate digits, or cause facial clefts. KEY features: ASYMMETRIC, do NOT follow developmental patterns or dermatomal distributions, random combination of defects. Not hereditary. The fetus becomes entangled in the sticky mesodermal surface of the chorion.
Thalidomide causes SYMMETRIC bilateral limb reduction (phocomelia/amelia). ABS is characteristically asymmetric.

questions.stem: ABS is classified as a DISRUPTION. What does this mean for recurrence risk?

choices.feedback: While some maternal factors (trauma, amniocentesis) may rarely contribute, recurrence risk is still very low because the event is stochastic.
ABS is not genetic. It's a random event caused by amnion rupture.
ABS has no genetic basis. It's a disruption, not a malformation.

Correct! Disruption = breakdown of a previously normally developing structure by an external destructive process. Since the fetal genome was normal and the event (amnion rupture) was random, recurrence risk is LOW (not genetic). Contrast: Malformation = intrinsically abnormal development (often genetic, may recur). Deformation = abnormal form/position from mechanical forces on normal tissue (oligohydramnios → Potter sequence). Dysplasia = abnormal tissue organization (skeletal dysplasias).

questions.explanation: Key detail: it was an extrinsic random event, not genetic.

questions.stem: What is the surgical approach to a deep circumferential constriction ring?

choices.text: Complete circumferential excision in one stage
choices.feedback: Full circumferential release risks vascular compromise of the distal limb. Staged approach is standard.

Correct! Deep constriction rings are released with Z-plasty in TWO stages: release one half of the circumference first, then the other half 6-12 weeks later. Releasing the full circumference at once risks vascular compromise. Z-plasty lengthens the scar and breaks up the linear constriction. If acute vascular compromise exists, emergent release may be needed. Shallow grooves may be observed if not causing functional problems.
Amputation is a last resort for non-salvageable limbs. Many constriction rings can be successfully released with staged Z-plasty.

choices.text: Observation only — rings always resolve spontaneously
choices.feedback: Deep rings with neurovascular compromise do NOT resolve and require surgical release.

questions.explanation: Key detail: never release the full circumference at once.

questions.stem: A baby is born with BILATERAL symmetric absence of both upper limbs (amelia). No constriction rings are present. What category of defect is this most likely?

choices.text: Amniotic band syndrome — bands can affect both sides equally
choices.feedback: ABS characteristically causes ASYMMETRIC, random defects. Perfect bilateral symmetry argues strongly against ABS.
Symmetric bilateral vascular events affecting identical structures would be extremely unlikely. Symmetry points to intrinsic causes.

Correct! Symmetric bilateral limb defects suggest an intrinsic cause: teratogenic (thalidomide, misoprostol) or genetic (Roberts syndrome, Holt-Oram). ABS causes ASYMMETRIC defects with constriction rings. Key rule: SYMMETRIC suggests intrinsic (malformation/teratogen), ASYMMETRIC without pattern suggests disruption (ABS, vascular accident).
Oligohydramnios causes positional deformities (clubfoot, hip dysplasia) but does not cause limb absence/amputations.

questions.explanation: Key detail: symmetric bilateral defects suggest intrinsic cause.

questions.stem: In Potter sequence, why does bilateral renal agenesis cause pulmonary hypoplasia?

choices.text: The kidneys produce a growth factor needed for lung development
choices.feedback: While some hormonal interactions exist, the primary mechanism is mechanical — lack of amniotic fluid prevents lung expansion.

choices.text: No kidneys → no fetal urine → oligohydramnios → no fluid for fetal breathing → pulmonary hypoplasia
choices.feedback: Correct! Fetal urine is the major source of amniotic fluid in the 2nd and 3rd trimesters. No kidneys → no urine → severe oligohydramnios. Fetal breathing movements of amniotic fluid are essential for lung development — the fluid mechanically stretches the lungs and stimulates growth. Without adequate fluid volume, the lungs cannot expand properly → pulmonary hypoplasia. Additional effects: limb compression → contractures, facial compression → Potter facies (flat nose, recessed chin, low-set ears).

choices.text: Compression of the thorax by the uterus
choices.feedback: While oligohydramnios does cause external compression, the primary mechanism for pulmonary hypoplasia is the lack of fluid for fetal breathing, not chest wall compression.

choices.text: Renal agenesis and pulmonary hypoplasia share a common genetic cause
choices.feedback: In Potter sequence, the lung problem is SECONDARY to the kidney problem (via oligohydramnios). It's a sequence, not a syndrome with independent organ involvement.

questions.stem: What is the underlying embryological defect?

choices.feedback: Vascular accidents cause jejunal/ileal atresia, not anorectal malformations. ARM is caused by abnormal cloacal development.
Neural crest migration failure causes Hirschsprung disease (aganglionosis), not imperforate anus. Different embryological mechanism entirely.
This describes a simple covered anus (low malformation). But the flat perineum suggests a more significant defect — failure of the urorectal septum to divide the cloaca properly.

Correct! The cloaca (common endodermal chamber) is divided by the urorectal septum into the urogenital sinus (anteriorly) and anorectal canal (posteriorly) by week 7. Failure of this septum to descend completely causes anorectal malformations, often with fistulas to the urogenital tract.

questions.stem: What association should be systematically evaluated?

choices.feedback: CHARGE includes choanal atresia, not anorectal. ARM is classically associated with VACTERL.
Down syndrome is associated with duodenal atresia, Hirschsprung, and AVSD — not typically anorectal malformations.
Pierre Robin involves the mandible and airway, not anorectal development.

Correct! ARM is the A in VACTERL. Up to 50-60% of patients with ARM have at least one additional VACTERL anomaly. Must screen: spinal X-ray/US, echocardiogram, renal US, esophageal patency (pass NG tube), limb exam.

questions.stem: In females with high ARM, what is the most common fistula type?

choices.feedback: While rectovaginal fistulas exist, they are actually less common than rectovestibular fistulas. The vestibular fistula (opening in the vestibule between vagina and perineum) is the most common female ARM.
Rectobladder (vesical) fistulas are the highest and rarest type. They carry the worst prognosis for continence.
Rectoperineal fistulas occur in both sexes but represent LOW malformations, not high. They have good prognosis and often need only a simple anoplasty.

Correct! Rectovestibular fistula is the most common ARM in females. The rectal opening is in the vestibule (between the hymen and perineum). In males, rectourethral (prostatic or bulbar) is most common. Persistent cloaca (single perineal opening for rectum, vagina, and urethra) is the most complex female variant.

questions.stem: What is the critical structure the PSARP must preserve for continence?

choices.feedback: While blood supply matters for healing, continence depends on the sphincter complex, not the vascular supply.

Correct! The PSARP (developed by Dr. Alberto Peña) approaches through the posterior sagittal midline, splitting the sphincter complex exactly in the midline to identify and place the rectum within the muscle complex. The puborectalis sling provides the anorectal angle critical for continence. Electrical stimulation guides the midline approach.
The levator ani is important for pelvic floor support, but the puborectalis (part of the levator complex) is the specific muscle that creates the anorectal angle for continence.
The internal sphincter provides resting tone but is often abnormal in ARM. The external sphincter complex (including puborectalis) is more critical for voluntary continence.

questions.explanation: Correct: The sphincter muscle complex (especially puborectalis).

questions.stem: What is the most common long-term functional outcome after ARM repair?

choices.text: Stricture at the repair site requiring dilation
choices.feedback: Strictures can occur early post-op and require dilation, but constipation is the most common LONG-TERM issue.

choices.text: Full voluntary continence in all patients
choices.feedback: Unfortunately, full voluntary continence is achieved in only about 25-30% of high ARM patients. Most require some form of bowel management.
Permanent colostomy is rarely needed. Most patients achieve social continence with bowel management programs, even if they don't have perfect voluntary control.

Correct! Constipation is the #1 long-term problem after ARM repair. The anorectum often has abnormal motility. Bowel management programs (daily enemas or antegrade continence enemas via Malone appendicostomy) allow social continence in >90% of patients. Soiling/incontinence is less common than constipation.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman has 3 early miscarriages and a DVT. Lupus anticoagulant is positive twice, 14 weeks apart. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: DIC is an acute consumptive coagulopathy with bleeding, not chronic thrombosis and pregnancy loss.
Factor V Leiden causes hypercoagulability but does not explain the recurrent pregnancy losses + positive lupus anticoagulant pattern. APS is the unifying diagnosis.

Correct! APS diagnostic criteria met: clinical criterion (recurrent pregnancy losses + DVT) + lab criterion (lupus anticoagulant positive twice ≥12 weeks apart). Treatment: lifelong warfarin for DVT + LMWH + aspirin for future pregnancies.
While APS can be secondary to SLE, the positive lupus anticoagulant with thrombosis and pregnancy morbidity specifically meets APS criteria.

questions.explanation: This patient meets revised Sapporo classification criteria for antiphospholipid syndrome: clinical criteria (≥3 early pregnancy losses AND venous thrombosis) plus laboratory criteria (lupus anticoagulant positive on two occasions ≥12 weeks apart). DIC presents with bleeding, thrombocytopenia, and elevated D-dimer/fibrin split products. Factor V Leiden causes thrombophilia but not recurrent pregnancy loss with positive antiphospholipid antibodies. SLE without APS would lack the specific antiphospholipid antibody positivity pattern.

questions.stem: A patient with APS has a prolonged aPTT. A mixing study does NOT correct the aPTT. This is most consistent with:

choices.feedback: Factor deficiencies CORRECT on mixing study (adding normal plasma provides the missing factor). Failure to correct = inhibitor present.
Vitamin K deficiency affects multiple factors and corrects on mixing study (factors are simply deficient, not inhibited).

Correct! Lupus anticoagulant is a phospholipid-dependent inhibitor. It prolongs aPTT and does NOT correct with a mixing study (unlike factor deficiencies, which correct). Despite prolonging aPTT in vitro, LA causes thrombosis in vivo — the classic APS paradox.
Heparin contamination prolongs aPTT but can be identified by thrombin time and would correct with protamine. The clinical context (APS with thrombosis) points to lupus anticoagulant.

questions.explanation: Lupus anticoagulant (LA) is an antiphospholipid antibody that paradoxically prolongs aPTT in vitro (by interfering with phospholipid-dependent coagulation assays) while causing thrombosis in vivo. A mixing study that fails to correct the aPTT indicates an inhibitor (like LA) rather than a factor deficiency. Factor deficiencies (hemophilia A) would correct on mixing. Vitamin K deficiency affects both PT and aPTT and would correct. Heparin contamination would correct with protamine or thrombin time testing.

questions.stem: Why are DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban) NOT recommended for APS?

choices.text: DOACs cause more bleeding in APS patients
choices.feedback: The concern is inadequate protection from thrombosis, not increased bleeding risk.

choices.text: They are not approved for venous thrombosis
choices.feedback: DOACs are used for VTE treatment generally. The concern is specifically about APS.

choices.text: DOACs interact with lupus anticoagulant testing
choices.feedback: While DOACs can interfere with coagulation testing, this is not the primary reason they're contraindicated in APS.

choices.text: Trials showed higher thrombosis rates compared to warfarin in APS
choices.feedback: Correct! The TRAPS trial (triple-positive APS) and ASTRO-APS trial demonstrated higher rates of arterial thrombosis (particularly stroke) with rivaroxaban vs warfarin. DOACs are therefore NOT recommended for APS, especially triple-positive APS. Warfarin remains the standard of care.

questions.explanation: The TRAPS trial (2018) demonstrated that rivaroxaban was associated with significantly higher thrombotic event rates compared to warfarin in triple-positive APS patients, leading to early trial termination. Current guidelines recommend warfarin (target INR 2-3) as the standard anticoagulant for APS-related thrombosis. DOACs do not cause more bleeding specifically in APS. DOACs are used for VTE in general but not recommended for APS. While DOACs can interfere with some LA assays, that is not the primary reason for avoidance.

questions.stem: What is the recommended treatment during pregnancy for a woman with obstetric APS?

choices.feedback: Untreated obstetric APS has ~80% pregnancy loss rate. Treatment with LMWH + aspirin dramatically improves outcomes.

Correct! LMWH + aspirinis the standard treatment for obstetric APS. Heparin does NOT cross the placenta (safe for fetus). Continue through pregnancy + 6-12 weeks postpartum. This regimen increases live birth rates from ~20% to ~70-80%.
Dual antiplatelet therapy is not the standard for obstetric APS. Anticoagulation (LMWH) is required, not just antiplatelet therapy.
Warfarin is TERATOGENIC (crosses placenta, causes nasal hypoplasia, stippled epiphyses, CNS abnormalities — warfarin embryopathy). Contraindicated in pregnancy.

questions.explanation: LMWH (enoxaparin) + low-dose aspirin (81 mg) is the standard treatment for obstetric APS during pregnancy. LMWH provides anticoagulation without crossing the placenta. Warfarin is contraindicated in the first trimester (causes warfarin embryopathy — nasal hypoplasia, stippled epiphyses) and in the third trimester (fetal hemorrhage risk). Observation alone is insufficient given the high recurrent pregnancy loss rate. Clopidogrel lacks evidence in obstetric APS. This regimen reduces pregnancy loss from ~80% to ~70-80% live birth rate.

questions.stem: Catastrophic APS (CAPS) is defined by thrombosis of how many organs within one week?

choices.feedback: Single-organ thrombosis is typical APS, not catastrophic.
Two organs do not meet CAPS criteria, which requires multi-organ involvement.

Correct! CAPS = rapid (<1 week) thrombosis of ≥3 organ systems, typically small vessel occlusion, with histopathologic confirmation of microthrombi + confirmed aPL antibodies. Mortality ~50%. Treatment requires the 'triple therapy + one' approach: anticoagulation + steroids + plasma exchange + IVIG.
CAPS requires ≥3 organs. Five or more would be CAPS but the threshold is 3.

questions.explanation: Catastrophic APS (CAPS) is defined by thrombosis affecting 3 or more organ systems within approximately one week, with histopathologic confirmation of small vessel occlusion, and positive antiphospholipid antibodies. CAPS is rare (<1% of APS patients) but has high mortality (~30-50%). Treatment includes anticoagulation, high-dose corticosteroids, plasma exchange, and/or IVIG. A single organ or two organs would not meet CAPS criteria. The threshold is specifically 3 or more organs, not 5.

questions.stem: Which antiphospholipid antibody profile carries the HIGHEST thrombotic risk?

choices.feedback: Correct! Triple positivity (all three antibody types positive) carries the highest thrombotic risk — up to 5.3% annual recurrence rate even on anticoagulation. These patients require lifelong warfarin (NOT DOACs), and the threshold for prophylaxis is lower. Triple positivity also has the highest pregnancy morbidity risk.
Isolated IgM anti-cardiolipin has the lowest clinical significance and thrombotic risk among the aPL antibody profiles.
Isolated anti-β2GP1 carries moderate risk but is surpassed by triple positivity.
Isolated LA carries significant thrombotic risk but triple positivity is higher.

questions.explanation: Triple positivity (simultaneous positivity for lupus anticoagulant + anticardiolipin antibodies + anti-β2-glycoprotein I antibodies) carries the highest thrombotic and obstetric risk in APS. These patients have the greatest risk of recurrent thrombosis and are the population in whom DOACs performed worst (TRAPS trial). Isolated single antibody positivity (aCL IgM alone, anti-β2GP1 IgG alone, or isolated LA) confers lower risk. Lupus anticoagulant alone is considered the strongest single predictor, but triple positivity overall carries the highest risk.

questions.stem: Livedo reticularis in a patient with thrombosis and recurrent miscarriages suggests:

choices.feedback: Raynaud causes episodic triphasic color changes in digits (white → blue → red), not the reticular/lace-like skin pattern of livedo reticularis.

Correct! Livedo reticularis (lace-like, purplish, reticular/net-like skin discoloration) is a common dermatologic finding in APS, present in ~25% of patients. Combined with thrombosis and recurrent pregnancy loss, it strongly suggests APS. Also seen in Sneddon syndrome (livedo reticularis + stroke + aPL antibodies).
PAN can cause livedo reticularis but typically presents with systemic vasculitis symptoms (fever, weight loss, renal/mesenteric involvement, HBV association), not recurrent miscarriages.
Cryoglobulinemia can cause purpura and livedo but is associated with hepatitis C, not the thrombosis/pregnancy loss pattern of APS.

questions.explanation: Livedo reticularis (violaceous, net-like mottling of the skin from dermal vessel thrombosis) combined with arterial/venous thrombosis and recurrent miscarriages is highly suggestive of antiphospholipid syndrome. Livedo reticularis is one of the most common cutaneous manifestations of APS. Raynaud phenomenon causes episodic digital color changes (white→blue→red) triggered by cold. Polyarteritis nodosa can cause livedo but is associated with medium-vessel vasculitis and neuropathy. Cryoglobulinemia causes palpable purpura and can cause livedo but lacks the obstetric component.

questions.stem: A patient with APS and mild thrombocytopenia (platelet count 75,000) develops a DVT. Should anticoagulation be withheld?

choices.text: Yes — treat thrombocytopenia first with steroids
choices.feedback: The thrombocytopenia is mild and does not require treatment. The DVT requires immediate anticoagulation.

choices.text: Only if the patient is also on aspirin
choices.feedback: Dual therapy (warfarin + aspirin) decisions are based on thrombotic risk, not platelet count at this level.

choices.text: No — mild thrombocytopenia in APS does not contraindicate anticoagulation
choices.feedback: Correct! Thrombocytopenia in APS is typically mild (50,000-100,000) and is NOT a reason to withhold anticoagulation. The prothrombotic risk far outweighs the bleeding risk at these platelet levels. Anticoagulation should be initiated for the DVT. Only severe thrombocytopenia (<20,000-30,000) with active bleeding warrants holding anticoagulation.
Mild thrombocytopenia in APS is common (30-40% of patients) and is NOT a contraindication to anticoagulation. The platelet count is adequate for anticoagulation.

questions.explanation: Mild thrombocytopenia (typically 50,000-100,000) occurs in approximately 20-30% of APS patients and does not contraindicate anticoagulation. The thrombocytopenia in APS is immune-mediated (anti-platelet antibodies) and is paradoxically associated with thrombosis, not bleeding. Anticoagulation should be initiated for the DVT. Delaying anticoagulation to treat thrombocytopenia first would leave the patient at continued thrombotic risk. Severe thrombocytopenia (<20,000-30,000) might warrant platelet support before full anticoagulation.

questions.stem: A patient has excessive worry about multiple topics (finances, health, relationships) with muscle tension, insomnia, and restlessness for 8 months. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Panic disorder features recurrent discrete panic attacks, not chronic pervasive worry about multiple domains.

Correct! GAD = excessive worry about multiple domains for ≥6 months with ≥3 somatic symptoms (restlessness, muscle tension, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, irritability, sleep disturbance). The chronic, pervasive nature distinguishes GAD from other anxiety disorders.
Social anxiety is specifically about fear of social/performance situations, not broad worry about finances, health, and relationships.
Adjustment disorder requires an identifiable stressor and resolves within 6 months of stressor resolution. This has persisted 8 months with no specific stressor.

questions.explanation: Psychiatric diagnosis follows DSM-5 criteria. Evidence-based treatment combines pharmacotherapy with psychotherapy. Safety assessment (suicidality, homicidality, psychosis) is paramount in psychiatric evaluation.

questions.stem: What is the BEST pharmacologic choice for a patient who gives a public speech quarterly and experiences severe performance anxiety?

choices.feedback: Buspirone requires 2-4 weeks of daily use to be effective. It does not work on an as-needed basis.

Correct! Propranolol (beta-blocker) is ideal for performance-only social anxiety. It blocks peripheral sympathetic symptoms (tremor, tachycardia, sweating) without sedation or dependence. Take it 30-60 minutes before the event. Does NOT treat generalized social anxiety — only performance-specific subtype.
Daily benzodiazepine for quarterly performance anxiety is excessive and carries dependence risk.
Daily SSRI is appropriate for generalized social anxiety disorder, but for isolated performance anxiety occurring quarterly, an as-needed approach is preferred.

questions.explanation: GAD: excessive worry about multiple domains for ≥6 months. Treatment: SSRIs/SNRIs (first-line), buspirone (adjunct, no abuse potential), CBT. Panic disorder: recurrent unexpected panic attacks with persistent concern about future attacks. Treatment: SSRIs first-line, benzodiazepines for acute episodes only (dependency risk). Social anxiety: SSRIs + CBT. PTSD: SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine — first-line) + trauma-focused CBT. Prazosin for PTSD-associated nightmares.

questions.stem: What is the gold standard psychotherapy for OCD?

choices.feedback: DBT is primarily used for borderline personality disorder and emotion dysregulation, not OCD.
Psychodynamic therapy explores unconscious conflicts and is not effective for OCD.

Correct! ERP = systematic exposure to obsession triggers while preventing compulsive response. Gradual habituation reduces anxiety. ERP has the strongest evidence for OCD treatment and is effective in 60-70% of patients. Combined ERP + SSRI is often the most effective approach for moderate-severe OCD.
Supportive therapy provides emotional support but does not address the obsession-compulsion cycle.

questions.explanation: Correct: Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP).

questions.stem: What medication is commonly used to manage PTSD-related nightmares?

choices.feedback: Benzodiazepines are NOT recommended for PTSD — they may worsen outcomes, interfere with trauma processing, and carry dependence risk.

Correct! Prazosin (alpha-1 adrenergic antagonist) is used off-label to reduce PTSD-related nightmares by blocking noradrenergic hyperarousal during sleep. It is not indicated for this indication (only for hypertension), but the 2023 VA/DoD CPG suggests it for PTSD nightmares. Start low and titrate up. Monitor for orthostatic hypotension (first-dose effect).
Melatonin helps with sleep initiation but does not specifically target trauma-related nightmares.
Trazodone is used off-label for insomnia but is not the primary agent used for PTSD nightmares.

questions.explanation: Psychiatric diagnosis follows DSM-5 criteria. Evidence-based treatment combines pharmacotherapy with psychotherapy. Safety assessment (suicidality, homicidality, psychosis) is paramount in psychiatric evaluation.
questions.auditNote: CORRECTED: Stem previously claimed prazosin is indicated for PTSD nightmares. Prazosin is used OFF-LABEL. Stem reworded to 'commonly used to manage.' Feedback updated to note off-label status and 2023 VA/DoD CPG recommendation.
issuesFound: Prazosin is NOT indicated for PTSD nightmares - used off-label only

questions.stem: Benzodiazepines are NOT recommended for chronic treatment of which anxiety conditions?

choices.text: None — they are safe for chronic use in all anxiety disorders
choices.feedback: This is incorrect. Chronic benzo use carries significant risks and is not recommended as first-line maintenance therapy for any anxiety disorder.
Benzos are problematic in most chronic anxiety treatment, not just phobias.

Correct! Chronic benzodiazepine use is discouraged in GAD (use SSRIs/buspirone instead), panic disorder (SSRIs for maintenance, benzos only for acute rescue), and PTSD (may worsen outcomes and prevent trauma processing). Benzos carry tolerance, dependence, withdrawal seizure risk, cognitive impairment, and fall risk in elderly. Short-term or as-needed use is acceptable in select patients.
Benzos are problematic across multiple anxiety disorders, not just social anxiety.

questions.explanation: GAD: excessive worry about multiple domains for ≥6 months. Treatment: SSRIs/SNRIs (first-line), buspirone (adjunct, no abuse potential), CBT. Panic disorder: recurrent unexpected panic attacks with persistent concern about future attacks. Treatment: SSRIs first-line, benzodiazepines for acute episodes only (dependency risk). Social anxiety: SSRIs + CBT. PTSD: SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine — first-line) + trauma-focused CBT. Prazosin for PTSD-associated nightmares.

questions.stem: What distinguishes acute stress disorder from PTSD?

choices.text: PTSD only occurs in combat veterans
choices.feedback: PTSD can result from any traumatic event — sexual assault, accidents, natural disasters, medical trauma, etc. It is not limited to military experience.

choices.text: Duration: ASD = 3 days to 1 month; PTSD = >1 month after trauma
choices.feedback: Correct! Both require exposure to traumatic event with intrusion, avoidance, and arousal symptoms. ASD: 3 days to 1 month post-trauma. PTSD: symptoms persist >1 month. ASD may transition to PTSD if symptoms don't resolve. Early CBT can prevent this transition.
ASD does not require hospitalization by definition. The distinction from PTSD is purely temporal.

choices.text: Acute stress disorder involves less severe symptoms
choices.feedback: Symptom severity can be similar. The distinction is duration, not severity.

questions.explanation: Psychiatric diagnosis follows DSM-5 criteria. Evidence-based treatment combines pharmacotherapy with psychotherapy. Safety assessment (suicidality, homicidality, psychosis) is paramount in psychiatric evaluation.

questions.stem: Buspirone has which advantage over benzodiazepines for GAD?

choices.feedback: Buspirone takes 2-4 weeks for full effect — SLOWER than benzos, which act within minutes.

choices.text: Can be used as-needed for acute anxiety
choices.feedback: Buspirone must be taken daily for 2-4 weeks before therapeutic effect. It does NOT work on an as-needed basis.

choices.text: No dependence, tolerance, or withdrawal risk
choices.feedback: Correct! Buspirone (5-HT1A partial agonist) is non-addictive — no dependence, tolerance, withdrawal, sedation, or cognitive impairment. Ideal for patients with substance use history. Disadvantage: slow onset (2-4 weeks), must take daily (not effective as-needed), less potent anxiolysis than benzos. Does not treat panic attacks.
Buspirone is NOT effective for panic disorder. It works for GAD only.

questions.explanation: GAD: excessive worry about multiple domains for ≥6 months. Treatment: SSRIs/SNRIs (first-line), buspirone (adjunct, no abuse potential), CBT. Panic disorder: recurrent unexpected panic attacks with persistent concern about future attacks. Treatment: SSRIs first-line, benzodiazepines for acute episodes only (dependency risk). Social anxiety: SSRIs + CBT. PTSD: SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine — first-line) + trauma-focused CBT. Prazosin for PTSD-associated nightmares.

questions.stem: Stanford Type A aortic dissection involves the:

choices.feedback: Isolated abdominal aortic dissection is extremely rare.

choices.text: Aortic arch only, sparing the ascending
choices.feedback: Arch involvement with ascending = Type A.

Correct! Type A = any involvement of ascending aorta (regardless of descending extension). Surgical emergency — mortality 1-2% per hour without surgery. Type B = descending only (distal to left subclavian) — medical management unless complicated.
Descending only = Type B.

questions.explanation: Stanford Type A aortic dissection involves the ascending aorta, regardless of the site of the primary intimal tear. It is defined as a dissection proximal to the brachiocephalic artery. Stanford Type B originates distal to the left subclavian artery and involves only the descending aorta. The DeBakey system further subdivides: Type 1 originates in the ascending aorta and extends to the descending aorta, Type 2 is limited to the ascending aorta, and Type 3 begins in the descending aorta. Type A dissections are nearly twice as common as Type B and carry higher mortality without urgent surgical intervention. The most common site for the intimal tear is approximately 2 to 2.5 cm above the aortic root.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Stanford Type A aortic dissection involves the ascending aorta, regardless of the site of the primary intimal tear. It is defined as a dissection proximal to the brachiocephalic artery. Stanford Type B originates distal to the left subclavian artery and involves only the descending aorta. The DeBakey system further subdivides: Type 1 originates in the ascending aorta and extends to the descending aorta, Type 2 is limited to the ascending aorta, and Type 3 begins in the descending aorta. Type A dissections are nearly twice as common as Type B and carry higher mortality without urgent surgical intervention. The most common site for the intimal tear is approximately 2 to 2.5 cm above the aortic root.

auditLayers.layer2_accuracyCheck: Answer CORRECT. Type A = ascending aorta involvement regardless of tear site.
auditLayers.layer4_crossReference: Consistent with IRAD data and ACC/AHA guidelines

verifiedReferences.title: Aortic Dissection
verifiedReferences.authors: Levy D, Sharma S, Farci F, Le JK

questions.stem: First-line antihypertensive for aortic dissection?

choices.feedback: Too slow onset for acute dissection emergency.
Hydralazine causes reflex tachycardia — worsens dissection.
Nitroprusside causes reflex tachycardia → increased shear stress. Must give BB first.

Correct! IV BB first: reduces HR (target <60) and BP (SBP 100-120). Esmolol (titratable, short-acting) or labetalol (alpha + beta). BB BEFORE any vasodilator to prevent reflex tachycardia. Then add nicardipine or nitroprusside if needed.

questions.explanation: IV beta-blockers are the first-line treatment for acute aortic dissection. The goal is to reduce both heart rate and blood pressure — decreasing aortic wall shear stress (dP/dt). Beta-blockers reduce the force and velocity of left ventricular contraction, which is the primary driver of dissection propagation. Target heart rate is less than 60 bpm and systolic blood pressure between 100 and 120 mmHg. Esmolol (short-acting, titratable) and labetalol (combined alpha/beta blockade) are commonly used. Vasodilators like nitroprusside must never be given before adequate beta-blockade because they cause reflex tachycardia that increases shear stress.
questions.verifiedExplanation: IV beta-blockers are the first-line treatment for acute aortic dissection. The goal is to reduce both heart rate and blood pressure — decreasing aortic wall shear stress (dP/dt). Beta-blockers reduce the force and velocity of left ventricular contraction, which is the primary driver of dissection propagation. Target heart rate is less than 60 bpm and systolic blood pressure between 100 and 120 mmHg. Esmolol (short-acting, titratable) and labetalol (combined alpha/beta blockade) are commonly used. Vasodilators like nitroprusside must never be given before adequate beta-blockade because they cause reflex tachycardia that increases shear stress.

auditLayers.layer2_accuracyCheck: Answer CORRECT. IV BB first-line, target HR <60, SBP 100-120.
auditLayers.layer4_crossReference: Consistent with ACC/AHA 2022 aortic disease guidelines

verifiedReferences.title: Aortic Dissection
verifiedReferences.authors: Levy D, Sharma S, Farci F, Le JK

questions.stem: Why must beta-blockers be given BEFORE vasodilators in dissection?

choices.text: Vasodilators are not effective in dissection
choices.feedback: Vasodilators effectively lower BP but have a dangerous side effect when used alone.
This is not a pharmacokinetic interaction.

choices.text: Vasodilators alone cause reflex tachycardia → increased aortic shear stress
choices.feedback: Correct! Vasodilators (nitroprusside, hydralazine) lower BP but trigger baroreceptor-mediated reflex tachycardia → increased dP/dt (rate of aortic pressure rise) → more wall stress → propagates dissection. BB first eliminates reflex tachycardia before adding vasodilators.

choices.text: Vasodilators cause coronary steal in dissection
choices.feedback: Coronary steal is not the primary concern here.

questions.explanation: Vasodilators (nitroprusside, hydralazine, nicardipine without beta-blockade) lower systemic vascular resistance and blood pressure, triggering a baroreceptor-mediated reflex tachycardia. The increased heart rate and left ventricular contractility (dP/dt) dramatically increase shear stress on the aortic wall, which can propagate the dissection flap. Beta-blockers must be given first to blunt this reflex before adding any vasodilator. The concept of 'anti-impulse therapy' targets reducing both the rate of rise of aortic pressure (dP/dt) and the absolute blood pressure. This is why labetalol is particularly useful — it provides both alpha and beta blockade simultaneously.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Vasodilators (nitroprusside, hydralazine, nicardipine without beta-blockade) lower systemic vascular resistance and blood pressure, triggering a baroreceptor-mediated reflex tachycardia. The increased heart rate and left ventricular contractility (dP/dt) dramatically increase shear stress on the aortic wall, which can propagate the dissection flap. Beta-blockers must be given first to blunt this reflex before adding any vasodilator. The concept of 'anti-impulse therapy' targets reducing both the rate of rise of aortic pressure (dP/dt) and the absolute blood pressure. This is why labetalol is particularly useful — it provides both alpha and beta blockade simultaneously.

auditLayers.layer2_accuracyCheck: Answer CORRECT. Reflex tachycardia increases dP/dt and shear stress.

questions.stem: Imaging gold standard for diagnosing aortic dissection?

choices.feedback: CXR shows widened mediastinum in 60-90% but is insufficiently sensitive to confirm or exclude.

Correct! CTA: >95% sensitivity and specificity. Shows intimal flap separating true and false lumens, extent of dissection, branch vessel involvement. Fast and widely available. TEE is the best bedside test for unstable patients who cannot go to CT.
Has no role in aortic dissection diagnosis.
TTE has limited sensitivity for dissection. TEE is much better.

questions.explanation: CT angiography (CTA) is the first-line imaging modality for diagnosing aortic dissection due to its high sensitivity and specificity, widespread availability, and rapid acquisition time. CTA clearly shows the intimal flap separating true and false lumens, aortic dilation, intramural hematoma, contrast leak (indicating rupture), and branch vessel involvement. It effectively differentiates Type A from Type B dissections. Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) is preferred in hemodynamically unstable patients or when CTA is unavailable — it provides real-time assessment of the ascending aorta, aortic regurgitation, and cardiac tamponade. MRI is highly accurate but impractical in acute settings. Chest X-ray may show widened mediastinum (>8 cm) but up to 20% of patients have normal films, so a normal CXR does not exclude dissection.
questions.verifiedExplanation: CT angiography (CTA) is the first-line imaging modality for diagnosing aortic dissection due to its high sensitivity and specificity, widespread availability, and rapid acquisition time. CTA clearly shows the intimal flap separating true and false lumens, aortic dilation, intramural hematoma, contrast leak (indicating rupture), and branch vessel involvement. It effectively differentiates Type A from Type B dissections. Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) is preferred in hemodynamically unstable patients or when CTA is unavailable — it provides real-time assessment of the ascending aorta, aortic regurgitation, and cardiac tamponade. MRI is highly accurate but impractical in acute settings. Chest X-ray may show widened mediastinum (>8 cm) but up to 20% of patients have normal films, so a normal CXR does not exclude dissection.

auditLayers.layer2_accuracyCheck: Answer CORRECT. CTA is first-line with high sensitivity/specificity.
auditLayers.layer4_crossReference: Consistent with ACC/AHA and ESC imaging guidelines

verifiedReferences.title: Aortic Dissection

questions.stem: Aortic dissection can mimic STEMI when it involves:

choices.feedback: Correct! Type A dissection can extend into the right coronary ostium → RCA occlusion → inferior STEMI. This is catastrophic if misdiagnosed as primary ACS and treated with fibrinolytics (causes fatal hemorrhage into the dissection). Always consider dissection in chest pain with aortic regurgitation, pulse deficits, or tearing back pain.
Brachiocephalic involvement causes stroke symptoms, not STEMI.
Descending dissection does not typically involve coronary arteries.
Renal involvement causes renal malperfusion (AKI), not STEMI.

questions.explanation: Type A aortic dissection can extend into the coronary artery ostia (most commonly the right coronary artery due to its anterior position), causing acute coronary occlusion that mimics ST-elevation myocardial infarction. ECG findings consistent with acute MI occur in approximately 8% of aortic dissection cases. This is a critical diagnostic trap — administering thrombolytics or anticoagulation for a presumed STEMI in the setting of unrecognized aortic dissection is catastrophic. The inferior leads are most commonly affected (RCA territory). Clinical clues that suggest dissection rather than primary MI include sudden tearing pain maximal at onset, blood pressure discrepancy between arms, pulse deficits, widened mediastinum on CXR, and new aortic regurgitation murmur.

ECG findings consistent with acute MI occur in approximately 8% of aortic dissection cases. The inferior leads are most commonly affected (RCA territory). questions.stem: A patient with Marfan syndrome presents with sudden tearing chest pain. Which risk factor predisposes them?

choices.text: Cystic medial necrosis from fibrillin-1 deficiency
choices.feedback: Correct! Marfan syndrome = fibrillin-1 (FBN1) gene mutation → defective elastic tissue → cystic medial necrosis → weakened aortic wall → dissection and aneurysm. Other connective tissue disorders: Ehlers-Danlos (type IV — vascular), Loeys-Dietz, Turner syndrome. These patients need lifelong aortic surveillance.
Calcification is degenerative, not the mechanism in Marfan.
Atherosclerosis is less relevant in Marfan — the connective tissue defect is key.
Marfan affects connective tissue structure, not coagulation.

questions.explanation: Marfan syndrome results from mutations in the FBN1 gene encoding fibrillin-1, a glycoprotein essential for elastic fiber formation in the aortic media. Defective fibrillin-1 leads to cystic medial necrosis (degeneration of the elastic and smooth muscle layers of the aortic wall), dramatically weakening the vessel and predisposing to dissection and aneurysm formation. Per IRAD data, Marfan syndrome was present in 50% of aortic dissection patients younger than 40, compared to only 2% of older patients. Other genetic conditions with similar aortic wall pathology include Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (vascular type), Loeys-Dietz syndrome, Turner syndrome, and bicuspid aortic valve. These connective tissue disorders cause structural abnormalities in the elastic fiber meshwork, predisposing to aneurysm and dissection.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Marfan syndrome results from mutations in the FBN1 gene encoding fibrillin-1, a glycoprotein essential for elastic fiber formation in the aortic media. Defective fibrillin-1 leads to cystic medial necrosis (degeneration of the elastic and smooth muscle layers of the aortic wall), dramatically weakening the vessel and predisposing to dissection and aneurysm formation. Per IRAD data, Marfan syndrome was present in 50% of aortic dissection patients younger than 40, compared to only 2% of older patients. Other genetic conditions with similar aortic wall pathology include Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (vascular type), Loeys-Dietz syndrome, Turner syndrome, and bicuspid aortic valve. These connective tissue disorders cause structural abnormalities in the elastic fiber meshwork, predisposing to aneurysm and dissection.

questions.stem: Type B dissection is treated medically UNLESS which complication occurs?

choices.feedback: Correct! Uncomplicated Type B: medical therapy (anti-impulse). Complicated Type B requires endovascular repair (TEVAR): organ malperfusion (renal failure, mesenteric ischemia, limb ischemia), aortic rupture, refractory pain despite optimal medical therapy, rapid aortic expansion.
Mild HTN is expected and managed with anti-impulse therapy.
Persistent pain may indicate complication but isolated pain is managed medically with reassessment.
Age alone does not change the medical management strategy for uncomplicated Type B.

questions.explanation: Uncomplicated Stanford Type B aortic dissection is managed medically with anti-impulse therapy (beta-blockers to reduce heart rate and blood pressure). Surgical or endovascular intervention becomes necessary when complications develop: malperfusion syndromes (renal ischemia causing AKI, mesenteric ischemia causing bowel necrosis, limb ischemia from iliac/femoral obstruction), aortic rupture or contained rupture, rapid aortic expansion, refractory pain despite medical therapy, or refractory hypertension. Thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR) has become the preferred intervention for complicated Type B dissections, covering the primary entry tear to redirect flow into the true lumen and promote false lumen thrombosis.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Uncomplicated Stanford Type B aortic dissection is managed medically with anti-impulse therapy (beta-blockers to reduce heart rate and blood pressure). Surgical or endovascular intervention becomes necessary when complications develop: malperfusion syndromes (renal ischemia causing AKI, mesenteric ischemia causing bowel necrosis, limb ischemia from iliac/femoral obstruction), aortic rupture or contained rupture, rapid aortic expansion, refractory pain despite medical therapy, or refractory hypertension. Thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR) has become the preferred intervention for complicated Type B dissections, covering the primary entry tear to redirect flow into the true lumen and promote false lumen thrombosis.

verifiedReferences.title: Aortic Dissection
verifiedReferences.authors: Levy D, Sharma S, Farci F, Le JK

questions.stem: Chest pain that is maximal at onset with tearing quality suggests dissection over MI because:

choices.feedback: MI pain can be severe but typically crescendos over minutes.

Correct! Dissection: sudden maximal pain from the moment of intimal tear — patients can tell you the exact second. MI: gradual buildup as ischemia progresses. Dissection radiates to the back (interscapular), MI to jaw/arm. This temporal distinction is high-yield for distinguishing the two.

choices.text: MI always presents with shortness of breath first
choices.feedback: MI presents with chest pain/pressure as the primary symptom.
Dissection CAN cause EKG changes (coronary involvement → STEMI, pericardial effusion → low voltage).

questions.explanation: The hallmark distinguishing feature of aortic dissection pain is that it is maximal at onset — patients can often pinpoint the exact moment it began. This reflects the sudden mechanical event of the intimal tear. In contrast, myocardial infarction pain typically crescendos gradually over minutes as ischemia progresses. StatPearls identifies pain intensity at onset as one of the most reliable historical factors in diagnosing acute aortic dissection. The classic description is sudden, severe, 'tearing' or 'ripping' chest or back pain. Anterior chest pain suggests ascending aorta involvement (Type A), while interscapular back pain suggests descending involvement (Type B). The pain may migrate as the dissection propagates distally. Notably, approximately 10% of patients (especially those with Marfan syndrome) present without pain, complicating diagnosis.

questions.stem: ARDS Berlin criteria require all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: This IS required for severity grading.
This IS required — bilateral opacities not explained by other causes.
This IS required.

Correct! ARDS requires PCWP ≤18 (non-cardiogenic). Elevated PCWP (>18) indicates cardiogenic pulmonary edema, not ARDS. Need to exclude heart failure with echo/BNP. Other criteria: within 1 week, bilateral opacities, P/F ratio classification on PEEP ≥5.

questions.explanation: The Berlin criteria for ARDS require: (1) Acute onset within 1 week of a known clinical insult or new/worsening respiratory symptoms; (2) Bilateral opacities on chest imaging (CXR or CT) not fully explained by effusions, atelectasis, or nodules; (3) Respiratory failure NOT fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload (may require echocardiography to exclude); (4) Hypoxemia classified by PaO2/FiO2 ratio on PEEP ≥5 cmH2O — Mild: 200-300, Moderate: 100-200, Severe: ≤100. An elevated PCWP >18 mmHg would suggest cardiogenic pulmonary edema, NOT ARDS. The Berlin criteria replaced the older AECC definition and specifically eliminated the requirement for PCWP measurement, instead requiring that respiratory failure not be 'fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload.' ARDS and heart failure can coexist, but PCWP >18 as a standalone criterion is NOT part of the Berlin definition — it is the OPPOSITE of what Berlin requires.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: ARDS is defined by acute onset, bilateral lung infiltrates on chest radiography or CT scan of a non-cardiac origin, and PaO2/FiO2 ratio

questions.stem: ARDSNet lung-protective ventilation recommends which tidal volume strategy?

choices.text: Low tidal volume based on ideal body weight
choices.feedback: Correct! Low tidal volume ventilation is the cornerstone of ARDS management. The ARDSNet trial demonstrated reduced mortality compared to conventional higher volumes. Tidal volume is calculated from ideal body weight, not actual weight.

choices.text: High tidal volume to maximize oxygenation
choices.feedback: High tidal volumes cause ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI) through overdistension of remaining functional alveoli (baby lung concept). This increases mortality.

choices.text: Tidal volume based on actual body weight
choices.feedback: Using actual body weight overestimates lung size in obese patients, leading to overdistension. Ideal body weight (based on height and sex) correlates with lung size.

choices.text: Variable tidal volumes titrated to plateau pressure only
choices.feedback: While plateau pressure <30 cmH2O is a target, tidal volume based on IBW is the primary ARDSNet recommendation. Both parameters are monitored together.

questions.explanation: The ARDSNet lung-protective ventilation protocol uses low tidal volumes based on ideal body weight (IBW) as its cornerstone. The landmark ARMA trial showed a 22% relative reduction in mortality. IBW is used because lung size correlates with height and sex, not actual weight. Plateau pressure is kept below 30 cmH2O to minimize barotrauma. Permissive hypercapnia may result from low tidal volumes but is generally tolerated if pH remains above 7.25. PEEP tables guide FiO2/PEEP combinations to maintain oxygenation.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: tidal volume from 4 to 8 mL/kg of ideal body weight (IBW), respiratory rate up to 35 bpm, SpO2 88% to 95%, plateau pressure less than 30 cm H2O

questions.verifiedExplanation: The ARDSNet lung-protective ventilation protocol uses a target tidal volume of 6 mL/kg ideal body weight (IBW). StatPearls states the protocol sets 'tidal volume from 4 to 8 mL/kg of ideal body weight' with 6 mL/kg as the target. This strategy reduces ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI) by minimizing volutrauma (overdistension of alveoli) and barotrauma (excessive airway pressures). The landmark ARMA trial (NEJM 2000) demonstrated a 22% relative reduction in mortality with 6 mL/kg IBW vs 12 mL/kg IBW (31% vs 39.8%). Other key ARDSNet parameters: plateau pressure <30 cmH2O, respiratory rate up to 35, SpO2 88-95%, pH goal 7.30-7.45. CRITICAL: tidal volume is calculated using IDEAL body weight (based on height and sex), NOT actual body weight — obese patients receive the same tidal volume as non-obese patients of the same height.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: Prone positioning in ARDS is indicated when P/F ratio is:

choices.feedback: Correct! PROSEVA trial: prone positioning for ≥16h/day in severe ARDS (P/F <150) reduced 28-day mortality from 33% to 16% (~50% reduction). Most impactful non-ventilator intervention. Mechanism: improves V/Q matching, reduces compression atelectasis, improves secretion drainage.
Mild ARDS does not require proning.
Proning is reserved for moderate-severe ARDS, not mild.
P/F >300 doesn't meet ARDS criteria.

questions.explanation: Prone positioning in ARDS is indicated when the P/F ratio is <150 (moderate-to-severe ARDS) despite optimized ventilator settings. The PROSEVA trial (Guérin et al., NEJM 2013) demonstrated that prone positioning for ≥16 hours/day in patients with severe ARDS (P/F <150) reduced 28-day mortality from 32.8% to 16% (NNT = 6). StatPearls notes prone positioning 'has shown benefits in about 50% to 70% of patients' and improves oxygenation by recruiting dependent lung zones, improving diaphragmatic excursion, and increasing functional residual capacity. Prone positioning is NOT indicated for mild ARDS (P/F 200-300) or patients without ARDS (P/F >300). 'Any P/F ratio' is too broad — the evidence supports benefit specifically in moderate-severe disease.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Prone position has shown benefits in about 50% to 70% of patients. The improvement in oxygenation is rapid and allows a reduction in FiO2 and PEEP

questions.stem: #1 cause of ARDS:

choices.feedback: Trauma is an indirect cause but not #1.
PE can contribute but is not a primary cause of ARDS.

Correct! Pneumonia is the #1 direct (pulmonary) cause. Sepsis is the #1 indirect (extrapulmonary) cause. Together they account for the majority of ARDS cases. Other causes: aspiration, pancreatitis, massive transfusion (TRALI), burns.
Heart failure causes cardiogenic pulmonary edema, which must be excluded to diagnose ARDS.

questions.explanation: Pneumonia is the #1 cause of ARDS, accounting for approximately 60% of cases (with or without sepsis). AAFP data confirms 'Most cases of ARDS in adults are associated with pneumonia with or without sepsis (60%) or with nonpulmonary sepsis (16%).' Other causes include aspiration, inhalation injury, pancreatitis, trauma, transfusion (TRALI), drug toxicity, and burns. Trauma is a recognized cause but less common than pneumonia. Pulmonary embolism can cause respiratory failure but is NOT a classic ARDS trigger (it causes V/Q mismatch, not diffuse alveolar damage). Heart failure causes cardiogenic pulmonary edema, which is explicitly EXCLUDED from the ARDS definition — ARDS is by definition non-cardiogenic.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: ARDS is an acute disorder...in the absence of any evidence of cardiogenic pulmonary edema
