questions.stem: Conservative vs liberal fluid strategy in ARDS (FACTT trial):

choices.feedback: Liberal fluids worsen pulmonary edema in ARDS.

Correct! FACTT trial: conservative fluid strategy improved oxygenation and shortened ventilator days without increasing organ failure. ARDS lungs are 'leaky' — extra fluid worsens edema. Target CVP <4 or PCWP <8. Diuretics (furosemide) when hemodynamically stable.
Conservative strategy showed significant benefit.
The trial compared fluid volume strategies, not fluid types.

questions.explanation: The FACTT trial (Fluid and Catheter Treatment Trial, NEJM 2006) demonstrated that a conservative (restrictive) fluid strategy improves ventilator-free days and ICU-free days compared to a liberal fluid strategy in ARDS, without increasing non-pulmonary organ failures. Conservative fluid management aims to keep the patient 'dry' — achieving even to negative fluid balance using diuretics and fluid restriction. The rationale: ARDS involves increased pulmonary capillary permeability, so excess intravascular volume worsens pulmonary edema and impairs gas exchange. Liberal fluids would worsen pulmonary edema. There was no mortality difference at 60 days, but the significant improvement in ventilator-free days (14.6 vs 11.2 days) makes conservative strategy the standard of care. Colloids vs crystalloids was not the focus of this trial.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: careful management of fluids in high-risk patients can be helpful

questions.stem: P/F ratio of 80 on PEEP 10 classifies ARDS as:

choices.feedback: Correct! P/F ≤100 on PEEP ≥5 = severe ARDS (mortality ~45%). P/F ratio = PaO2 ÷ FiO2. Example: PaO2 80 on FiO2 1.0 = P/F 80 = severe. This patient needs: low tidal volume, prone positioning, possible paralysis, consider ECMO if refractory.
Moderate = P/F 100-200.
P/F 80 with bilateral opacities and appropriate timing meets severe ARDS criteria.

questions.explanation: A P/F ratio of 80 on PEEP ≥5 classifies as SEVERE ARDS per the Berlin criteria. The Berlin classification: Mild ARDS: P/F 200-300 on PEEP ≥5 (mortality ~27%); Moderate ARDS: P/F 100-200 on PEEP ≥5 (mortality ~35%); Severe ARDS: P/F ≤100 on PEEP ≥5 (mortality ~45%). A P/F of 80 is well below the ≤100 threshold for severe ARDS. This patient requires lung-protective ventilation (target rate IBW), high PEEP, prone positioning (≥16 hours/day), neuromuscular blockade consideration, and potentially ECMO if refractory. The PEEP requirement of ≥5 cmH2O is specified because some degree of positive pressure is needed to standardize the measurement — this patient on PEEP 10 clearly meets this criterion.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Berlin definition: PaO2/FiO2 categories of ≤100 (severe), 101-200 (moderate), and 201-300 (mild) on PEEP ≥5

questions.verifiedExplanation: A P/F ratio of 80 on PEEP ≥5 classifies as SEVERE ARDS per the Berlin criteria. The Berlin classification: Mild ARDS: P/F 200-300 on PEEP ≥5 (mortality ~27%); Moderate ARDS: P/F 100-200 on PEEP ≥5 (mortality ~35%); Severe ARDS: P/F ≤100 on PEEP ≥5 (mortality ~45%). A P/F of 80 is well below the ≤100 threshold for severe ARDS. This patient requires lung-protective ventilation (6 mL/kg IBW), high PEEP, prone positioning (≥16 hours/day), neuromuscular blockade consideration, and potentially ECMO if refractory. The PEEP requirement of ≥5 cmH2O is specified because some degree of positive pressure is needed to standardize the measurement — this patient on PEEP 10 clearly meets this criterion.

questions.stem: ECMO in ARDS is reserved for:

choices.feedback: ECMO is rescue, not first-line. Start with ARDSNet ventilation + proning.

choices.text: Refractory hypoxemia despite optimal conventional management
choices.feedback: Correct! ECMO (veno-venous for respiratory failure) is a rescue therapy when optimal ventilation, proning, paralysis, and inhaled vasodilators fail. EOLIA trial showed trend toward benefit. Consider when P/F <80 despite ≥6h optimal management. Best outcomes at experienced ECMO centers.
ECMO is never prophylactic.
ECMO is resource-intensive and reserved for refractory cases.

questions.explanation: ECMO (Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation) in ARDS is reserved for refractory hypoxemia despite optimal conventional management — it is a rescue therapy, not first-line. ECMO is considered when patients remain severely hypoxemic (P/F <80 or <60 despite optimal ventilator settings, prone positioning, neuromuscular blockade, and high PEEP). The EOLIA trial (Combes et al., NEJM 2018) suggested a trend toward benefit in severe ARDS refractory to conventional management. VV-ECMO (veno-venous) provides gas exchange support while allowing ultra-lung-protective ventilation, reducing VILI. ECMO requires specialized centers and carries risks including hemorrhage, thrombosis, infection, and hemolysis. It is NOT first-line for severe ARDS (lung-protective ventilation + prone positioning are first), NOT for mild ARDS, and NOT for all moderate ARDS patients.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]
questions.verifiedExplanation: ECMO (Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation) in ARDS is reserved for refractory hypoxemia despite optimal conventional management — it is a rescue therapy, not first-line. ECMO is considered when patients remain severely hypoxemic (P/F <80 or <60 despite optimal ventilator settings, prone positioning, neuromuscular blockade, and high PEEP). The EOLIA trial (Combes et al., NEJM 2018) suggested a trend toward benefit in severe ARDS refractory to conventional management. VV-ECMO (veno-venous) provides gas exchange support while allowing ultra-lung-protective ventilation, reducing VILI. ECMO requires specialized centers and carries risks including hemorrhage, thrombosis, infection, and hemolysis. It is NOT first-line for severe ARDS (lung-protective ventilation + prone positioning are first), NOT for mild ARDS, and NOT for all moderate ARDS patients.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: Permissive hypercapnia in ARDS means accepting:

choices.feedback: Low tidal volumes may lead to high (not low) pCO2.

choices.text: Higher pCO2 levels to keep tidal volumes low (pH >7.25)
choices.feedback: Correct! Low tidal volumes may not fully clear CO2 → pCO2 rises. This is acceptable as long as pH stays >7.25. The mortality benefit of low tidal volumes far outweighs the risk of mild hypercapnia. Exception: contraindicated in elevated ICP (hypercapnia → cerebral vasodilation → raised ICP).
Impossible and not a goal.

choices.text: Normal pCO2 with high tidal volumes
choices.feedback: High tidal volumes cause volutrauma — the entire point is to accept higher pCO2 for lung protection.

questions.explanation: Permissive hypercapnia means accepting higher-than-normal pCO2 levels in order to keep tidal volumes low and prevent ventilator-induced lung injury, provided the pH remains above 7.25 (some protocols accept ≥7.20). When using low tidal volumes (target rate IBW), CO2 clearance may be insufficient, leading to respiratory acidosis. Rather than increasing tidal volume (which would cause volutrauma in stiff ARDS lungs), the strategy 'permits' elevated pCO2 levels — prioritizing lung protection over normal blood gas values. The ARDSNet protocol targets pH 7.30-7.45 but allows respiratory rate increases up to 35 bpm to compensate before accepting hypercapnia. Low pCO2 with high tidal volumes would defeat the purpose of lung protection. 'Zero pCO2' is physiologically impossible. 'Normal pCO2 with high tidal volumes' describes the pre-ARDSNet approach that caused more lung injury and higher mortality.
references: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: tidal volume from 4 to 8 mL/kg of ideal body weight, pH goal 7.30 to 7.45

questions.verifiedExplanation: Permissive hypercapnia means accepting higher-than-normal pCO2 levels in order to keep tidal volumes low and prevent ventilator-induced lung injury, provided the pH remains above 7.25 (some protocols accept ≥7.20). When using low tidal volumes (6 mL/kg IBW), CO2 clearance may be insufficient, leading to respiratory acidosis. Rather than increasing tidal volume (which would cause volutrauma in stiff ARDS lungs), the strategy 'permits' elevated pCO2 levels — prioritizing lung protection over normal blood gas values. The ARDSNet protocol targets pH 7.30-7.45 but allows respiratory rate increases up to 35 bpm to compensate before accepting hypercapnia. Low pCO2 with high tidal volumes would defeat the purpose of lung protection. 'Zero pCO2' is physiologically impossible. 'Normal pCO2 with high tidal volumes' describes the pre-ARDSNet approach that caused more lung injury and higher mortality.
verifiedReferences: Diamond M, Peniston HL, Sanghavi DK, Mahapatra S, Doerr C. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Jan 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436002/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: A 'silent chest' during asthma exacerbation indicates:

choices.text: Severe bronchospasm with minimal air movement (imminent respiratory failure)
choices.feedback: Correct! Silent chest = airways so constricted that insufficient air moves to produce wheezing. This is a pre-arrest sign. Paradox: LESS wheezing = WORSE asthma (not better). Requires aggressive intervention ± intubation.
PTX can cause absent breath sounds but unilaterally; silent chest in asthma is bilateral.
Silent chest is an ominous sign, not improvement.
Not related to the acute finding of silent chest.

questions.explanation: A 'silent chest' during an asthma exacerbation indicates such severe bronchospasm that air movement is insufficient to generate wheezing. This is an ominous sign of imminent respiratory failure requiring immediate intervention (intubation preparation, continuous nebulization, IV magnesium, epinephrine). Paradoxically, worsening obstruction may sound 'quieter' as the patient decompensates. Pneumothorax would present with unilateral absent breath sounds, not diffuse silence. The absence of wheezing in severe asthma indicates worsening, not improvement. This is not a transition to COPD.

questions.stem: Normal or rising PaCO2 in asthma exacerbation suggests:

choices.feedback: In asthma, patients hyperventilate → PaCO2 should be LOW. Normal/rising = failure.
Oxygen is not the cause of rising PaCO2 in asthma.

choices.text: Respiratory muscle fatigue and impending failure
choices.feedback: Correct! During an asthma attack, hyperventilation → LOW PaCO2 is expected. Normal or RISING PaCO2 = respiratory muscles fatiguing, can no longer maintain increased ventilation. This is a sign of impending respiratory arrest — prepare for intubation.
Resolution would show normalizing respiratory rate with improving symptoms, not isolated PaCO2 rise.

questions.explanation: During an asthma exacerbation, patients initially hyperventilate, producing a low PaCO2 (respiratory alkalosis). A normal or rising PaCO2 indicates the patient can no longer maintain the increased work of breathing — respiratory muscle fatigue with impending ventilatory failure. This is a critical warning sign requiring escalation (ICU admission, preparation for intubation). A normal PaCO2 in acute asthma is not appropriate compensation — it signifies the transition from compensated to decompensated respiratory failure. Supplemental oxygen should not be reduced. The exacerbation is worsening.

questions.stem: IV magnesium in acute asthma works by:

choices.feedback: Magnesium is a bronchodilator, not an antibiotic.
That's the role of corticosteroids, not magnesium.
Surfactant is relevant to neonatal RDS, not asthma.

Correct! IV MgSO4 2g over 20 min causes bronchial smooth muscle relaxation. Used for severe exacerbations not responding to initial albuterol/steroids. Most effective in severe attacks (FEV1 <25% predicted). Works within minutes.

questions.explanation: IV magnesium sulfate (2g over 20 minutes) produces bronchial smooth muscle relaxation by inhibiting calcium-mediated smooth muscle contraction and reducing acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction. It is used as adjunctive therapy in severe asthma exacerbations not responding adequately to beta-agonists and ipratropium. Magnesium has no antibacterial activity. While it may have minor anti-inflammatory effects, its primary mechanism in acute asthma is direct smooth muscle relaxation. It does not stimulate surfactant production.

questions.stem: Samter triad consists of:

choices.feedback: These commonly coexist but don't constitute Samter triad.

Correct! Samter triad (aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease). COX-1 inhibition by NSAIDs shifts arachidonic acid to leukotriene pathway → bronchospasm. Treatment: aspirin desensitization, leukotriene receptor antagonists (montelukast). These patients can typically tolerate acetaminophen and COX-2 selective inhibitors.
That pattern can occur with drug reactions but isn't Samter triad.
That's the atopic triad.

questions.explanation: Samter triad (aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease, AERD) consists of asthma, nasal polyps, and hypersensitivity to aspirin/NSAIDs. The mechanism is non-IgE-mediated: COX-1 inhibition shunts arachidonic acid metabolism to the lipoxygenase pathway, increasing cysteinyl leukotrienes while reducing protective PGE2. AERD affects approximately 7% of adult asthmatics. Management includes NSAID avoidance, leukotriene receptor antagonists, and potentially aspirin desensitization. The triad of asthma, sinusitis, GERD is not a recognized clinical entity. Asthma, eczema, food allergy describes the atopic triad/march.

questions.stem: Key spirometry finding distinguishing asthma from COPD?

choices.feedback: Fixed obstruction is COPD. Asthma is reversible.

choices.text: Reduced total lung capacity in asthma
choices.feedback: TLC is normal or increased in asthma (air trapping), not reduced.
DLCO is typically normal in asthma; it's reduced in emphysema.

Correct! Asthma shows significant bronchodilator reversibility. COPD: FEV1/FVC <0.70 post-bronchodilator with minimal reversibility. Note: some COPD patients show partial reversibility (asthma-COPD overlap), and long-standing asthma can develop fixed obstruction.

questions.explanation: The hallmark distinguishing asthma from COPD on spirometry is reversible airflow obstruction — defined as ≥12% AND ≥200 mL improvement in FEV1 after bronchodilator administration. COPD shows fixed (irreversible) obstruction. Both show reduced FEV1/FVC ratio (<0.70). Total lung capacity is often increased (hyperinflation) in both conditions, not reduced in asthma. DLCO is characteristically reduced in emphysema (COPD subtype due to alveolar destruction) but is normal or slightly increased in asthma.

questions.stem: Controller medication backbone for persistent asthma?

choices.feedback: Correct! ICS is the cornerstone of asthma controller therapy from Step 2 onward. Reduces airway inflammation, prevents exacerbations, improves lung function. LABA added at Step 3+ (never LABA alone — increases mortality without ICS per SMART trial). Side effects: oral candidiasis, dysphonia (rinse mouth after use).
Theophylline is rarely used now due to narrow therapeutic window and side effects.
Cromolyn is less effective than ICS and rarely used in adults.
Albuterol is a rescue medication, not a controller.

questions.explanation: Inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) are the cornerstone controller medication for all levels of persistent asthma (mild, moderate, severe). They reduce airway inflammation, decrease bronchial hyperresponsiveness, prevent exacerbations, and improve lung function. All step-up therapy builds upon ICS as the foundation. Oral theophylline is an alternative but has a narrow therapeutic index and significant side effects. Cromolyn sodium is rarely used in current practice. Albuterol is a short-acting rescue medication (reliever), not a controller.

questions.stem: Preferred sedation agent if intubating an asthmatic?

choices.feedback: Morphine causes histamine release → can worsen bronchospasm. Avoid in asthma.
Midazolam provides sedation but lacks the bronchodilatory benefit of ketamine.

Correct! Ketamine has bronchodilatory properties (catecholamine release + direct smooth muscle relaxation) making it the preferred induction agent for intubation in severe asthma. Also provides analgesia and amnesia. Avoid: morphine (histamine release → bronchospasm), succinylcholine (can trigger bronchospasm in some).
Propofol is acceptable but ketamine has additional bronchodilator properties.

questions.explanation: Ketamine is the preferred sedation/induction agent for intubating an asthmatic patient because it has direct bronchodilatory properties (sympathomimetic effects and direct smooth muscle relaxation) while maintaining hemodynamic stability. It also preserves respiratory drive and airway reflexes at sub-anesthetic doses. Morphine can cause histamine release, potentially worsening bronchospasm. Midazolam alone provides inadequate analgesia and no bronchodilation. Propofol is an alternative with mild bronchodilatory effects but can cause significant hypotension.

questions.stem: Biologics for severe asthma target which pathway?

choices.feedback: Antihistamines treat allergic rhinitis/urticaria, not the eosinophilic asthma pathway.
Biologics target immune/inflammatory pathways, not bacteria.
Neutrophilic pathways are more relevant to COPD than asthma.

choices.text: IgE (omalizumab), IL-5 (mepolizumab, benralizumab), IL-4/IL-13 (dupilumab), or TSLP (tezepelumab)
choices.feedback: Correct! Omalizumab: anti-IgE for severe allergic asthma. Mepolizumab/benralizumab: anti-IL-5 for severe eosinophilic asthma. Dupilumab: anti-IL-4Rα (blocks IL-4 and IL-13). Tezepelumab: anti-TSLP. Used for Step 5 severe asthma uncontrolled on high-dose ICS-LABA.

questions.explanation: Biologic therapies for severe asthma target specific inflammatory pathways: omalizumab targets IgE (allergic asthma), mepolizumab/reslizumab target IL-5 and benralizumab targets IL-5 receptor α (eosinophilic asthma), dupilumab targets IL-4Rα blocking both IL-4 and IL-13 (eosinophilic/T2 asthma), and tezepelumab targets TSLP (broad phenotype coverage). Selection is guided by biomarkers: IgE levels, blood eosinophils, and FeNO. Histamine receptors are targeted by antihistamines, not biologics. Bacterial colonization is not the target of asthma biologics. Neutrophil migration is not targeted by current approved asthma biologics.

questions.stem: PALM-COEIN: what does the 'O' stand for?

choices.feedback: O stands for Ovulatory dysfunction.

Correct! PALM-COEIN: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy (structural causes) + Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified (non-structural). Ovulatory dysfunction (anovulation) is one of the most common causes of AUB, especially PCOS.
Ovarian cysts are not part of PALM-COEIN.
Malignancy is represented by M, not O.

questions.explanation: In the PALM-COEIN classification of abnormal uterine bleeding (FIGO 2011), O = Ovulatory dysfunction. The full classification: PALM (structural causes) = Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia; COEIN (non-structural causes) = Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not otherwise classified. Ovulatory dysfunction is the most common cause of AUB in adolescents and perimenopausal women. Obstruction and oncologic emergency are not part of this classification.

questions.stem: First test in a reproductive-age woman with AUB?

choices.feedback: CT is not first-line for AUB evaluation.
Biopsy is indicated if >45 or with risk factors, but pregnancy test comes first.

Correct! ALWAYS rule out pregnancy first in reproductive-age AUB. Ectopic pregnancy, threatened/inevitable abortion, and molar pregnancy can all present with abnormal bleeding. After negative β-hCG: CBC, TSH, coagulation studies (if indicated), pelvic US.
US is important but not first. Rule out pregnancy first.

questions.explanation: A pregnancy test (urine or serum β-hCG) is always the first test in any reproductive-age woman with abnormal uterine bleeding. Pregnancy and its complications (ectopic pregnancy, threatened abortion, gestational trophoblastic disease) must be excluded before further workup or treatment. CT pelvis involves radiation and is not first-line. Endometrial biopsy is indicated for women >45 or with risk factors for endometrial hyperplasia/cancer. Pelvic ultrasound is an important second step but pregnancy must be excluded first.

questions.stem: Most effective MEDICAL treatment for menorrhagia?

choices.feedback: OCPs reduce bleeding ~40% but are not the most effective medical option.

Correct! LNG-IUD reduces menstrual blood loss by >90%, superior to all other medical therapies. Causes endometrial atrophy. Also treats endometrial hyperplasia without atypia. Comparable to hysterectomy for quality of life improvement while preserving fertility. First-line medical therapy for heavy menstrual bleeding.
Tranexamic acid reduces bleeding ~40% — effective but less than LNG-IUD.
Iron treats the resulting anemia but doesn't reduce bleeding.

questions.explanation: The levonorgestrel intrauterine device (LNG-IUD, e.g., Mirena) is the most effective medical treatment for menorrhagia (heavy menstrual bleeding). It reduces menstrual blood loss by >90% through local progestogenic effects on the endometrium (decidualization and atrophy). It is superior to combined OCPs, tranexamic acid, and oral progestins in randomized trials. It also serves as contraception and endometrial protection. Iron supplementation treats resulting anemia but does not reduce bleeding.

questions.stem: #1 cause of anovulatory AUB in reproductive-age women?

choices.feedback: Correct! PCOS is the most common cause of anovulatory bleeding in reproductive-age women. Features: irregular periods, hyperandrogenism (acne, hirsutism), polycystic ovaries on US. Rotterdam criteria: 2 of 3 (oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries). Associated with insulin resistance, obesity, type 2 DM risk.
Endometriosis causes dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain, not typically anovulatory bleeding.
Thyroid dysfunction causes AUB but is not the #1 cause of anovulation.
Cervical cancer causes postcoital or intermenstrual bleeding, not anovulation.

questions.explanation: PCOS is the most common cause of anovulatory AUB in reproductive-age women, affecting 6-12% of this population. Chronic anovulation leads to unopposed estrogen stimulation of the endometrium, resulting in irregular and often heavy bleeding. PCOS diagnosis requires 2 of 3 Rotterdam criteria: oligo/anovulation, clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound. Endometriosis causes dysmenorrhea and chronic pelvic pain. Hypothyroidism can cause AUB but is less common than PCOS. Cervical cancer causes intermenstrual/postcoital bleeding.

questions.stem: Submucosal fibroids cause the MOST bleeding because:

choices.feedback: Size doesn't determine which type bleeds most.

choices.text: They have the highest malignant potential
choices.feedback: Malignant transformation is extremely rare for all fibroid types.
Fibroids are estrogen-responsive but don't produce estrogen.

Correct! Submucosal fibroids project into the uterine cavity, distorting the endometrium and increasing endometrial surface area → heavier menstrual bleeding. Subserosal fibroids (projecting outward) cause bulk symptoms (pressure, urinary frequency) but less bleeding. Intramural (within the wall) cause intermediate symptoms.

questions.explanation: Submucosal fibroids (FIGO type 0-2) cause the most significant bleeding because they protrude into and distort the endometrial cavity, increasing endometrial surface area, disrupting normal endometrial vasculature, and interfering with normal uterine contractility that controls menstrual bleeding. They are not necessarily the largest fibroids — intramural and subserosal fibroids can be much larger. Fibroids (leiomyomas) are benign and have very low malignant potential. Fibroids are estrogen/progesterone-responsive but do not produce estrogen.

questions.stem: Adolescent with heavy menstrual bleeding since menarche. Screen for:

choices.feedback: Ovarian cancer is extremely rare in adolescents.
Endometrial cancer is rare in adolescents.
BRCA screening is not indicated for menorrhagia.

Correct! ~20% of adolescents hospitalized for menorrhagia have an underlying bleeding disorder, most commonly von Willebrand disease (vWD — type 1 most common). Screen: CBC, PT/PTT, vWF antigen, vWF activity (ristocetin cofactor), factor VIII. Heavy periods since menarche = red flag for coagulopathy.

questions.explanation: Von Willebrand disease (vWD) should be screened for in any adolescent presenting with heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) since menarche, as vWD is the most common inherited bleeding disorder (affecting ~1% of the population) and HMB is often its presenting symptom in females. ACOG recommends screening for bleeding disorders in adolescents with HMB. Initial testing includes vWF antigen, vWF activity (ristocetin cofactor), and factor VIII level. Ovarian and endometrial cancers are extremely rare in adolescents. BRCA testing is not indicated for HMB.

questions.stem: Postmenopausal AUB — #1 cause?

choices.feedback: Fibroids shrink after menopause (estrogen-dependent). Rarely cause postmenopausal bleeding.
Cancer is the most concerning cause but not the most common.
PCOS is a reproductive-age diagnosis. Not relevant postmenopausally.

Correct! Atrophy is the #1 cause of postmenopausal bleeding (60-80%). Due to estrogen deficiency. However, endometrial cancer must always be excluded. Workup: TVUS → if endometrial thickness >4mm → endometrial biopsy. ~10% of postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer.

questions.explanation: Endometrial and vaginal atrophy is the most common cause of postmenopausal bleeding (PMB), accounting for approximately 60-80% of cases. Estrogen deficiency after menopause leads to thin, fragile endometrial and vaginal tissue prone to bleeding. However, endometrial cancer must always be excluded because it accounts for ~10% of PMB cases. Any postmenopausal bleeding warrants transvaginal ultrasound (endometrial thickness) and possible endometrial biopsy. Fibroids and PCOS are primarily reproductive-age conditions.

questions.stem: Rapidly growing fibroid in a postmenopausal woman raises concern for:

choices.feedback: Correct! Fibroids are estrogen-dependent and should not grow after menopause. Rapid growth postmenopausally raises concern for leiomyosarcoma (uterine sarcoma). Imaging cannot reliably distinguish benign from malignant. Surgical excision recommended. Overall malignant transformation of fibroids is rare (<1%) but must be considered in this scenario.
Torsion presents acutely with pain, not as gradual growth.
Fibroids should SHRINK after menopause due to estrogen decline. Growth is concerning.
Endometriosis also regresses postmenopausally.

questions.explanation: A rapidly growing uterine mass in a postmenopausal woman raises concern for leiomyosarcoma, a rare but aggressive uterine malignancy. Normal fibroids should not grow after menopause (they are estrogen-dependent and typically shrink). Rapid growth, especially without exogenous estrogen, is a red flag requiring tissue sampling. MRI with gadolinium can help differentiate leiomyosarcoma from benign fibroids. Ovarian torsion presents with acute unilateral pelvic pain. Normal fibroid growth in menopause is unexpected. Endometriosis regresses after menopause.

questions.stem: RUQ pain, fever, jaundice, hypotension. Most likely diagnosis and emergent intervention?

choices.feedback: Pancreatitis causes epigastric pain with elevated lipase, not this pattern.
Hepatitis causes jaundice but not acute RUQ pain + fever + shock.
Cholecystitis typically does NOT cause jaundice or hypotension. Charcot triad + shock = cholangitis.
Correct! Reynolds pentad: pain + fever + jaundice + AMS + hypotension. Emergent ERCP for biliary decompression. Without it, mortality >50%.

questions.explanation: Charcot triad (RUQ pain, fever, jaundice) plus hypotension and altered mental status (Reynolds pentad) indicates ascending cholangitis with sepsis. Emergent biliary decompression via ERCP is required. Ascending cholangitis results from bacterial infection of an obstructed common bile duct (usually choledocholithiasis). IV antibiotics and fluid resuscitation are initiated simultaneously. Without drainage, mortality is very high.

questions.stem: First imaging for suspected gallbladder disease?

choices.feedback: Second-line for cholecystitis if US equivocal.
MRCP evaluates bile ducts, not primary GB test.
Correct! First-line: 95% sensitivity for stones, shows wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, CBD dilation, sonographic Murphy sign. Non-invasive, no radiation.
CT is less sensitive (~80%) for gallstones than US.

questions.explanation: RUQ ultrasound is the first-line imaging for suspected gallbladder disease due to its high sensitivity (~95%) for gallstones, ability to detect wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, and positive sonographic Murphy sign. It is noninvasive, inexpensive, and radiation-free. HIDA scan (cholescintigraphy) is used when ultrasound is equivocal but clinical suspicion for acute cholecystitis remains.

questions.stem: Non-visualization of gallbladder on HIDA scan indicates:

choices.feedback: Correct! Tracer taken up by hepatocytes, excreted into bile. GB non-filling at 4h = obstructed cystic duct = cholecystitis. 97% sensitivity. Gold standard when US equivocal.
While possible, non-visualization primarily indicates cholecystitis in this clinical context.
Normal HIDA fills GB within 1 hour. Non-visualization is abnormal.
CBD obstruction → non-visualization of duodenum, not specifically the GB.

questions.explanation: Non-visualization of the gallbladder on HIDA (hepatobiliary iminodiacetic acid) scan after 4 hours (or 60 minutes with morphine augmentation) indicates cystic duct obstruction, supporting the diagnosis of acute cholecystitis. The radiotracer is taken up by hepatocytes and excreted into bile ducts but cannot enter the gallbladder due to obstruction. HIDA sensitivity for acute cholecystitis is ~95%.

questions.stem: Acalculous cholecystitis typically occurs in:

choices.feedback: Young women get symptomatic gallstones. Acalculous is an ICU disease.
Correct! ICU patients (sepsis, burns, TPN, ventilation, trauma). GB ischemia + stasis without stones. 30-50% mortality. Treatment: percutaneous cholecystostomy.
SCD → pigmented gallstones from hemolysis, not acalculous disease.
That's stone-related. Acalculous = no stones by definition.

questions.explanation: Acalculous cholecystitis (inflammation without gallstones) typically occurs in critically ill ICU patients — post-surgical, trauma, burns, TPN-dependent, or septic patients. The mechanism involves gallbladder stasis, ischemia, and secondary infection. Diagnosis may be challenging as these patients often cannot report symptoms. Ultrasound shows wall thickening and pericholecystic fluid without stones. Treatment is cholecystectomy or percutaneous cholecystostomy if too unstable for surgery.

questions.stem: After ERCP for CBD stones, what additional procedure same hospitalization?

choices.feedback: Increases bleeding/perforation risk without benefit.
Only if stones incompletely removed. The key is preventing recurrence.
Correct! Same-admission cholecystectomy prevents 25-30% recurrence rate within 6 weeks. Reduces readmission and recurrent biliary events.
Not indicated after routine CBD stone removal.

questions.explanation: After ERCP for CBD stones, cholecystectomy should be performed during the same hospitalization (ideally within 72 hours) to prevent recurrent choledocholithiasis. The gallbladder remains the source of stones, and delaying cholecystectomy results in ~10-20% recurrence of biliary events within 6 weeks. Same-admission laparoscopic cholecystectomy after ERCP is safe and reduces readmissions.

questions.stem: Porcelain gallbladder requires:

choices.feedback: Correct! Porcelain gallbladder (wall calcification) carries increased gallbladder carcinoma risk. Cholecystectomy is standard recommendation.
ERCP evaluates bile ducts, not GB wall.
Surveillance is insufficient given the cancer risk.
Ursodiol dissolves cholesterol stones, not wall calcification.

questions.explanation: Porcelain gallbladder (calcification of the gallbladder wall) requires prophylactic cholecystectomy due to historically reported association with gallbladder carcinoma. While recent data suggest the cancer risk may be lower than previously thought, cholecystectomy remains recommended given the low surgical morbidity and the difficulty in excluding malignancy.

questions.stem: Most common complication of ERCP?

choices.feedback: ERCP focuses on bile duct, not GB.
Correct! Occurs in 5-10%. Prevention: rectal indomethacin before/after procedure reduces risk by 50%. Prophylactic pancreatic duct stent also helps.
More commonly the indication for ERCP than a complication.
Perforation occurs in <1% — rare but serious.

questions.explanation: Post-ERCP pancreatitis is the most common complication of ERCP, occurring in 3-15% of procedures. It results from mechanical or hydrostatic injury to the pancreatic duct or ampulla during cannulation and manipulation. Risk factors include young age, female sex, suspected sphincter of Oddi dysfunction, and difficult cannulation. Prophylactic rectal indomethacin and pancreatic duct stenting reduce incidence in high-risk patients.

questions.stem: What distinguishes biliary colic from acute cholecystitis?

choices.feedback: Both can cause RUQ/epigastric pain.

choices.text: Duration >6h, fever, Murphy sign, leukocytosis, and wall thickening on ultrasound
choices.feedback: Correct! Biliary colic: 30 min-6h, self-resolving, no fever, negative Murphy. Cholecystitis: constant >6h, fever, positive Murphy, leukocytosis, US wall thickening. The 6-hour cutoff is key.
Both typically have normal LFTs. Elevation suggests choledocholithiasis.
Both have stones. Stones alone don't distinguish them.

questions.explanation: Biliary colic (uncomplicated gallstone disease) causes episodic postprandial RUQ pain lasting <6 hours that resolves spontaneously, with normal WBC and no fever. Acute cholecystitis features prolonged pain >6 hours, fever, positive Murphy sign (inspiratory arrest on RUQ palpation), leukocytosis, and ultrasound findings of gallbladder wall thickening (>3 mm), pericholecystic fluid, and distension.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old presents with 5 days of decreased sleep (feels 'amazing' after 2 hours), pressured speech, spending $10,000 on a shopping spree, and grandiose beliefs that he is a famous musician. He was hospitalized. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Elevated mood ≥7 days (or hospitalization) with ≥3 DIG FAST criteria (decreased sleep, impulsivity/spending, grandiosity, pressured speech) = manic episode. One manic episode = Bipolar I disorder. Hospitalization alone qualifies for mania duration criterion regardless of actual days.
This presentation shows elevated/expansive mood with grandiosity, not depression.
Bipolar II has hypomania only — no psychotic features, no hospitalization, no marked impairment. This patient's hospitalization and grandiose delusions indicate full mania.
Cyclothymia involves subthreshold mood symptoms for ≥2 years. This is a full manic episode.

questions.explanation: This patient presents with a classic manic episode: markedly decreased need for sleep (feels rested after 2 hours), pressured speech, reckless spending ($10,000 spree), and grandiose delusions. Bipolar I disorder requires at least one manic episode (≥7 days or any duration if hospitalization required). Hospitalization confirms functional impairment meeting Bipolar I criteria. MDD has no manic episodes. Bipolar II involves hypomania (no marked impairment/psychosis) plus major depression. Cyclothymic disorder involves chronic subsyndromal mood fluctuations without full manic or depressive episodes.

questions.stem: Which psychiatric medication is the ONLY one proven to reduce suicide risk?

choices.feedback: Valproate is an effective mood stabilizer but has not been proven to reduce suicide risk.
SSRIs treat depression but have not been specifically proven to reduce suicide rates. They may increase suicidality in young adults during initial weeks.
Clozapine reduces suicide risk in schizophrenia specifically (uniquely indicated for this), but lithium has broader evidence across mood disorders.

Correct! Lithium is the only psychiatric medication with robust evidence for reducing suicide risk in bipolar disorder (and possibly unipolar depression). This anti-suicidal effect is independent of its mood-stabilizing properties and persists even at subtherapeutic levels. This is a major reason to favor lithium in bipolar patients with suicide risk.

questions.explanation: Lithium is the only psychiatric medication with robust evidence for reducing suicide risk in bipolar disorder, demonstrated across multiple randomized trials and meta-analyses. It reduces both suicide attempts and completions by approximately 80%. The mechanism may involve serotonergic effects and reduction of impulsivity/aggression independent of mood stabilization. Valproate has mood-stabilizing properties but no proven anti-suicidal effect. Fluoxetine may reduce suicidality in depression but lacks lithium's evidence base. Clozapine is uniquely indicated for reducing suicidality in schizophrenia, a distinct indication.

questions.stem: A patient on lithium develops tremor, vomiting, ataxia, and confusion. Lithium level is 2.8 mEq/L. What is the management?

choices.feedback: Charcoal does NOT bind lithium (it's a small ion). It is ineffective for lithium toxicity.
While propranolol can treat lithium-induced tremor at therapeutic levels, this patient has severe toxicity requiring lithium discontinuation and aggressive management.
The level is toxic (therapeutic range 0.6-1.2). Increasing the dose would be dangerous.

Correct! Lithium toxicity (level >1.5, severe >2.5). Mild: tremor, GI. Moderate: ataxia, confusion, fasciculations. Severe: seizures, coma, cardiac arrhythmias. Treatment: stop lithium, aggressive IV normal saline (NS — lithium is renally excreted), hemodialysis for severe toxicity (level >2.5 with symptoms, AKI, or altered consciousness).

questions.explanation: Lithium level 2.8 mEq/L represents severe toxicity (>2.5 mEq/L). Immediate management: stop lithium, aggressive IV normal saline hydration, and emergent hemodialysis given the severe level with neurologic symptoms (ataxia, confusion). Activated charcoal does not bind lithium and is ineffective. Treating only the tremor with propranolol while ignoring toxicity is dangerous. Increasing the dose would be lethal. Monitor ECG for cardiac conduction abnormalities. Whole bowel irrigation may help if recent ingestion of extended-release formulation.

questions.stem: Why is valproate absolutely contraindicated in women of childbearing age?

choices.feedback: Lactation suppression is not the primary concern with valproate.
While valproate is associated with polycystic ovary-like changes, this is not the primary contraindication.

choices.text: It is a potent teratogen causing neural tube defects
choices.feedback: Correct! Valproate causes neural tube defects (spina bifida) in 1-2% of exposed pregnancies (10-20x baseline risk), plus cardiac defects and neurodevelopmental impairment. Boxed warning for teratogenicity. Must use reliable contraception if prescribed. Lamotrigine or quetiapine are preferred alternatives in women of reproductive age.
Valproate may affect fertility but the primary contraindication is its severe teratogenicity.

questions.explanation: Valproate is a potent teratogen with a boxed warning against use in women of childbearing potential for conditions other than epilepsy when alternatives exist. It causes neural tube defects (spina bifida, ~1-2% risk — 10x background rate), craniofacial dysmorphism, cardiac malformations, and reduced IQ/neurodevelopmental impairment in exposed children. The risk is dose-dependent. It does not suppress lactation. While PCOS-like features (ovarian cysts, weight gain) can occur, teratogenicity is the primary reason for the absolute contraindication. It does not cause infertility directly.

questions.stem: What distinguishes hypomania from mania?

choices.feedback: Hypomania has a SHORTER minimum duration (≥4 days) than mania (≥7 days).
Both mania and hypomania can involve decreased need for sleep.

choices.text: Hypomania has no marked functional impairment and no psychosis
choices.feedback: Correct! Hypomania = elevated mood ≥4 days with DIG FAST symptoms BUT: 1) No marked impairment in functioning, 2) No psychosis, 3) No hospitalization required. If ANY of these occur, it becomes mania. Hypomania may actually feel productive to the patient, making it harder to diagnose.
Hypomania is a distinct elevated mood state. Depressive symptoms would suggest mixed features.

questions.explanation: Hypomania (Bipolar II) differs from mania (Bipolar I) primarily by the absence of marked functional impairment and absence of psychotic features. Hypomania requires ≥4 consecutive days; mania requires ≥7 days (or any duration if hospitalization needed). Both involve decreased need for sleep, increased energy, and elevated/irritable mood. Hypomania does not last longer — it is shorter. Both involve decreased sleep. Hypomania is characterized by elevated mood and increased productivity, not more depressive symptoms.

questions.stem: Lamotrigine is most effective for which aspect of bipolar disorder?

choices.feedback: Lamotrigine is NOT effective for acute mania. It is not indicated for and should not be used for acute manic episodes.
Valproate and atypical antipsychotics are more effective for rapid cycling than lamotrigine.
Psychotic features require antipsychotics. Lamotrigine does not treat psychosis.

Correct! Lamotrigine's primary role is preventing depressive episodes in bipolar disorder. It has modest acute antidepressant effects and strong evidence for maintenance against depressive relapse. Must be titrated slowly over 6-8 weeks due to risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS), especially with concurrent valproate (which doubles lamotrigine levels).

questions.explanation: Lamotrigine is most effective for preventing bipolar depressive episodes (maintenance therapy). It has strong evidence for delaying time to depressive relapse but minimal efficacy for acute mania or manic episode prevention. It is often first-line for Bipolar II disorder where depression predominates. It does not treat acute mania (lithium, valproate, and atypical antipsychotics are used for that). Lamotrigine requires slow titration to avoid Stevens-Johnson syndrome. It is not specifically effective for rapid cycling mania or psychotic features.

questions.stem: An antidepressant is needed for bipolar depression. What is the critical prescribing rule?

choices.text: MAOIs are first-line for bipolar depression
choices.feedback: MAOIs are rarely used and carry significant dietary and drug interaction risks. They are not first-line for bipolar depression.

choices.text: Tricyclics are safer than SSRIs in bipolar disorder
choices.feedback: TCAs actually have a HIGHER rate of inducing mania than SSRIs. If an antidepressant is used, SSRIs or bupropion are preferred.

choices.text: Use the highest effective SSRI dose for rapid response
choices.feedback: High-dose antidepressants increase mania/switching risk in bipolar disorder.

choices.text: Always combine with a mood stabilizer
choices.feedback: Correct! Antidepressant monotherapy in bipolar disorder is contraindicated — it can trigger mania, rapid cycling, or mixed episodes. If an antidepressant is necessary for bipolar depression, ALWAYS pair it with a mood stabilizer (lithium, valproate) or atypical antipsychotic. Preferred bipolar depression treatments (quetiapine, lurasidone, lamotrigine) often don't require adding an antidepressant.

questions.explanation: When antidepressants are needed for bipolar depression, they must always be prescribed in combination with a mood stabilizer (lithium, valproate) or atypical antipsychotic to prevent antidepressant-induced manic switch. Monotherapy with antidepressants in bipolar disorder carries significant risk of triggering mania or rapid cycling. MAOIs are not first-line for bipolar depression. Tricyclics carry higher manic switch risk than SSRIs. The lowest effective dose should be used, not the highest, and antidepressants should be tapered once remission is achieved.

questions.stem: Ebstein anomaly in a newborn is associated with maternal use of which medication during pregnancy?

choices.feedback: Correct! Lithium exposure in the first trimester is associated with Ebstein anomaly — downward displacement of the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle. The absolute risk is lower than historically believed (~1 in 1000 vs. previously quoted 1 in 20), but fetal echocardiography at 18-20 weeks is recommended if lithium was used in the first trimester.
Lamotrigine has relatively low teratogenicity and is considered one of the safer mood stabilizers in pregnancy.
Carbamazepine is associated with neural tube defects and craniofacial abnormalities, not Ebstein anomaly.
Valproate causes neural tube defects (spina bifida), not cardiac anomalies like Ebstein.

questions.explanation: Ebstein anomaly (apical displacement of the tricuspid valve with atrialization of the right ventricle) is the cardiac malformation classically associated with first-trimester lithium exposure. The absolute risk is low (~0.05-0.1%, much lower than historically reported 1-2%) but represents a ~20-fold increase over baseline. Lamotrigine has the best safety profile of mood stabilizers in pregnancy. Carbamazepine causes neural tube defects and craniofacial dysmorphism. Valproate causes neural tube defects and neurodevelopmental impairment (higher teratogenic risk than lithium).

questions.stem: #1 cause of small bowel obstruction?

choices.feedback: Crohn strictures can cause SBO but account for only ~5%.

Correct! Post-surgical adhesions cause ~60% of SBO. Even a single prior abdominal surgery creates adhesion risk. In patients without prior surgery, hernias are the #1 cause. Always ask about surgical history in a patient with SBO symptoms.
Cancer is the #1 cause of LARGE bowel obstruction.
Intussusception is more common in children (lead point from Meckel diverticulum or lymphoma in adults).

questions.explanation: Adhesions from prior abdominal surgery are the most common cause of small bowel obstruction (SBO), accounting for approximately 60-70% of cases. Adhesive bands form during the healing process after any peritoneal disruption. Other causes include hernias (~15%, second most common — and the most common cause of SBO in patients without prior surgery), malignancy (~10%), Crohn's disease, and intussusception. Management of adhesive SBO depends on whether there are signs of strangulation: uncomplicated partial SBO can be managed with NPO, NGT decompression, IV fluids, and observation. Complete obstruction or signs of strangulation require operative intervention.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Adhesions from prior abdominal surgery are the most common cause of small bowel obstruction (SBO), accounting for approximately 60-70% of cases. Adhesive bands form during the healing process after any peritoneal disruption. Other causes include hernias (~15%, second most common — and the most common cause of SBO in patients without prior surgery), malignancy (~10%), Crohn's disease, and intussusception. Management of adhesive SBO depends on whether there are signs of strangulation: uncomplicated partial SBO can be managed with NPO, NGT decompression, IV fluids, and observation. Complete obstruction or signs of strangulation require operative intervention.

questions.stem: Sigmoid volvulus on X-ray shows:

choices.feedback: Apple-core = annular colorectal carcinoma on barium enema.
Free air suggests perforation, a complication rather than volvulus itself.
This suggests SBO, not sigmoid volvulus.

Correct! Sigmoid volvulus: massively dilated sigmoid loop rising from the pelvis, creating a 'coffee bean' or inverted U shape. The loop often extends up toward the diaphragm. Inner wall creates the 'crease' of the coffee bean. First-line treatment in stable patient: sigmoidoscopic decompression.

questions.explanation: Sigmoid volvulus on abdominal X-ray produces the classic 'coffee bean' or 'omega' sign — a massively dilated loop of sigmoid colon that extends from the pelvis toward the right upper quadrant, with the apex pointing toward the right diaphragm. The walls of the distended loop converge at the point of torsion in the left lower quadrant. Sigmoid volvulus accounts for ~60-75% of all colonic volvulus cases and is more common in elderly, institutionalized, or neuropsychiatric patients with chronic constipation and a redundant sigmoid. Initial treatment for uncomplicated sigmoid volvulus is endoscopic detorsion (flexible sigmoidoscopy), followed by semi-elective sigmoid resection to prevent recurrence (~60% recurrence without surgery).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Sigmoid volvulus on abdominal X-ray produces the classic 'coffee bean' or 'omega' sign — a massively dilated loop of sigmoid colon that extends from the pelvis toward the right upper quadrant, with the apex pointing toward the right diaphragm. The walls of the distended loop converge at the point of torsion in the left lower quadrant. Sigmoid volvulus accounts for ~60-75% of all colonic volvulus cases and is more common in elderly, institutionalized, or neuropsychiatric patients with chronic constipation and a redundant sigmoid. Initial treatment for uncomplicated sigmoid volvulus is endoscopic detorsion (flexible sigmoidoscopy), followed by semi-elective sigmoid resection to prevent recurrence (~60% recurrence without surgery).

questions.stem: Cecal volvulus treatment differs from sigmoid because:

choices.feedback: Correct! Unlike sigmoid volvulus (which can be decompressed via rigid sigmoidoscopy), cecal volvulus is treated surgically (right hemicolectomy or cecopexy). Endoscopic decompression of cecal volvulus has high failure rate and risk of perforation. Delay in surgery increases mortality.
Cecal volvulus occurs in adults with a mobile cecum.
Cecal volvulus does NOT resolve spontaneously and requires intervention.
Antibiotics don't treat mechanical obstruction.

questions.explanation: Cecal volvulus differs from sigmoid volvulus in that it generally cannot be decompressed endoscopically — the colonoscope cannot reach or effectively reduce the cecal torsion. Cecal volvulus typically requires surgical management (right hemicolectomy or cecopexy). In contrast, sigmoid volvulus can usually be detorsed with flexible sigmoidoscopy as an initial temporizing measure. Cecal volvulus is less common than sigmoid volvulus (~25-40% of colonic volvulus) and occurs in younger patients. X-ray shows a 'kidney-bean' shaped distended cecum displaced to the left upper quadrant (the twist moves the cecum from its normal RLQ position).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Cecal volvulus differs from sigmoid volvulus in that it generally cannot be decompressed endoscopically — the colonoscope cannot reach or effectively reduce the cecal torsion. Cecal volvulus typically requires surgical management (right hemicolectomy or cecopexy). In contrast, sigmoid volvulus can usually be detorsed with flexible sigmoidoscopy as an initial temporizing measure. Cecal volvulus is less common than sigmoid volvulus (~25-40% of colonic volvulus) and occurs in younger patients. X-ray shows a 'kidney-bean' shaped distended cecum displaced to the left upper quadrant (the twist moves the cecum from its normal RLQ position).

questions.stem: Ogilvie syndrome treatment?

choices.feedback: Correct! Neostigmine inhibits acetylcholinesterase → increases parasympathetic tone → stimulates colonic motility. ~90% response rate. Monitor with telemetry (risk of bradycardia — have atropine ready). If neostigmine fails: colonoscopic decompression. Surgery for perforation or failed conservative measures.
Antibiotics don't treat pseudo-obstruction.
Ogilvie's is a motility disorder, not a simple constipation problem.
Surgery is reserved for failure of medical therapy or perforation.

questions.explanation: Ogilvie syndrome (acute colonic pseudo-obstruction) is treated with IV neostigmine. Ogilvie syndrome presents as massive colonic dilation without mechanical obstruction, typically in hospitalized patients with severe illness, postoperative state, or electrolyte abnormalities. Neostigmine (an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) restores colonic motility by enhancing parasympathetic activity. It has ~80-90% initial success rate. Administration requires cardiac monitoring due to risk of bradycardia (atropine should be available). Conservative management (NPO, rectal tube, correction of electrolytes, cessation of offending medications like opioids and anticholinergics) is tried first. Cecal diameter >12 cm or failure of conservative/pharmacologic therapy warrants colonoscopic decompression or surgery.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Ogilvie syndrome (acute colonic pseudo-obstruction) is treated with IV neostigmine. Ogilvie syndrome presents as massive colonic dilation without mechanical obstruction, typically in hospitalized patients with severe illness, postoperative state, or electrolyte abnormalities. Neostigmine (an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) restores colonic motility by enhancing parasympathetic activity. It has ~80-90% initial success rate. Administration requires cardiac monitoring due to risk of bradycardia (atropine should be available). Conservative management (NPO, rectal tube, correction of electrolytes, cessation of offending medications like opioids and anticholinergics) is tried first. Cecal diameter >12 cm or failure of conservative/pharmacologic therapy warrants colonoscopic decompression or surgery.

questions.stem: Strangulated bowel obstruction — key clinical finding?

choices.feedback: Pain relief in obstruction can paradoxically indicate perforation (pressure release), not improvement.

choices.text: Continuous (non-colicky) pain with peritoneal signs, fever,
choices.feedback: Correct! Strangulation = compromised mesenteric blood supply → ischemia → necrosis → perforation. Changes from colicky to continuous pain. Peritoneal signs (guarding, rebound), fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis, lactic acidosis. CT: bowel wall thickening, mesenteric haziness, pneumatosis. EMERGENT surgery required.
Colicky pain suggests simple obstruction. Strangulation causes continuous pain.
Painless vomiting suggests gastroparesis, not strangulated obstruction.

questions.explanation: Strangulated bowel obstruction — where the blood supply is compromised — is indicated by continuous (non-colicky) pain with peritoneal signs (guarding, rigidity, rebound tenderness). In simple mechanical obstruction, pain is characteristically colicky/intermittent (corresponding to peristaltic waves against the obstruction). The transition to continuous pain suggests ischemia or perforation. Other signs of strangulation include fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis with left shift, metabolic acidosis (rising lactate), and peritoneal signs. Strangulated obstruction requires emergent surgery — delay leads to bowel necrosis, perforation, and sepsis. CT findings include mesenteric haziness, bowel wall thickening, reduced enhancement, and pneumatosis.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Strangulated bowel obstruction — where the blood supply is compromised — is indicated by continuous (non-colicky) pain with peritoneal signs (guarding, rigidity, rebound tenderness). In simple mechanical obstruction, pain is characteristically colicky/intermittent (corresponding to peristaltic waves against the obstruction). The transition to continuous pain suggests ischemia or perforation. Other signs of strangulation include fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis with left shift, metabolic acidosis (rising lactate), and peritoneal signs. Strangulated obstruction requires emergent surgery — delay leads to bowel necrosis, perforation, and sepsis. CT findings include mesenteric haziness, bowel wall thickening, reduced enhancement, and pneumatosis.

questions.stem: Water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) in SBO is both:

choices.text: Diagnostic for cancer and therapeutic for adhesions
choices.feedback: Gastrografin doesn't diagnose cancer specifically.

choices.text: Therapeutic (osmotic effect aids resolution) AND prognostic (contrast in colon at 24h predicts resolution)
choices.feedback: Correct! Gastrografin has osmotic properties that draw fluid into the bowel lumen → reduces edema → may help resolve partial adhesive SBO. If contrast reaches colon within 24h = likely to resolve without surgery. If not = higher likelihood of needing operative intervention.
Water-soluble contrast is safe. BARIUM is relatively contraindicated (risk of barium peritonitis if perforation).

choices.text: Only diagnostic with no therapeutic benefit
choices.feedback: Gastrografin has both diagnostic and therapeutic properties.

questions.explanation: Water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) administered orally in adhesive SBO serves dual roles: therapeutic — its hyperosmolar nature draws fluid into the bowel lumen, which promotes motility and may hasten resolution of partial obstruction (reduces the need for surgery), and prognostic — if contrast reaches the colon within 24 hours on follow-up X-ray, the obstruction is likely to resolve non-operatively. Failure of contrast to reach the colon predicts the need for surgical intervention. Multiple studies show that Gastrografin reduces hospital length of stay and may reduce the need for surgery in adhesive SBO. It should not be used if strangulation is suspected.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) administered orally in adhesive SBO serves dual roles: therapeutic — its hyperosmolar nature draws fluid into the bowel lumen, which promotes motility and may hasten resolution of partial obstruction (reduces the need for surgery), and prognostic — if contrast reaches the colon within 24 hours on follow-up X-ray, the obstruction is likely to resolve non-operatively. Failure of contrast to reach the colon predicts the need for surgical intervention. Multiple studies show that Gastrografin reduces hospital length of stay and may reduce the need for surgery in adhesive SBO. It should not be used if strangulation is suspected.

questions.stem: Cecal diameter >9 cm on imaging indicates:

choices.feedback: Correct! Rule of 3-6-9: small bowel >3 cm, colon >6 cm, cecum >9 cm = abnormally dilated. Cecum >9 cm (some say >12 cm) = high risk of perforation due to Law of Laplace (wall tension increases with diameter). Requires urgent decompression.
Cecal dilation is an acute finding, not a screening indication.
While volvulus causes dilation, diameter alone doesn't confirm the diagnosis.
Cecal diameter >9 cm is never normal.

questions.explanation: Cecal diameter exceeding 9 cm on imaging indicates significant risk of perforation, particularly in the setting of colonic obstruction or pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie syndrome). The cecum is the most common site of colonic perforation in distal obstruction because of Laplace's law: wall tension is proportional to radius, so the cecum (the widest part of the colon) experiences the greatest wall tension. Some sources use 12 cm as the critical threshold for imminent perforation risk, while others use 9-10 cm. Cecal diameter should be monitored serially with abdominal X-rays. Exceeding the threshold warrants urgent decompression (endoscopic or surgical).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Cecal diameter exceeding 9 cm on imaging indicates significant risk of perforation, particularly in the setting of colonic obstruction or pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie syndrome). The cecum is the most common site of colonic perforation in distal obstruction because of Laplace's law: wall tension is proportional to radius, so the cecum (the widest part of the colon) experiences the greatest wall tension. Some sources use 12 cm as the critical threshold for imminent perforation risk, while others use 9-10 cm. Cecal diameter should be monitored serially with abdominal X-rays. Exceeding the threshold warrants urgent decompression (endoscopic or surgical).

questions.stem: Closed-loop obstruction requires emergent surgery because:

choices.text: It only occurs in the small bowel
choices.feedback: Closed-loop can occur in both small and large bowel.
Antibiotic penetration is not the issue.
Closed-loop can occur from volvulus, hernia, or adhesive bands.

choices.text: Bowel is obstructed at two points → rapid pressure rise → ischemia/perforation
choices.feedback: Correct! Two points of obstruction trap a bowel segment → intraluminal pressure rises rapidly (no decompression possible) → venous then arterial compromise → ischemia → gangrene → perforation. CT shows C-shaped or U-shaped loop with mesenteric whirl sign. This is a surgical emergency.

questions.explanation: Closed-loop obstruction requires emergent surgery because bowel is obstructed at TWO points (creating a sealed segment), causing rapid pressure buildup, vascular compromise, and ischemia. Without an outlet for decompression, intraluminal pressure rises quickly, compressing mesenteric veins first (causing venous congestion and edema) then arteries (causing ischemic necrosis and perforation). This progresses much faster than simple mechanical obstruction. Common causes include adhesive bands creating a closed loop, incarcerated hernias, and sigmoid volvulus. CT shows the 'whirl sign' (twisted mesentery) and a U-shaped or C-shaped dilated loop. Closed-loop obstruction cannot be managed conservatively.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Closed-loop obstruction requires emergent surgery because bowel is obstructed at TWO points (creating a sealed segment), causing rapid pressure buildup, vascular compromise, and ischemia. Without an outlet for decompression, intraluminal pressure rises quickly, compressing mesenteric veins first (causing venous congestion and edema) then arteries (causing ischemic necrosis and perforation). This progresses much faster than simple mechanical obstruction. Common causes include adhesive bands creating a closed loop, incarcerated hernias, and sigmoid volvulus. CT shows the 'whirl sign' (twisted mesentery) and a U-shaped or C-shaped dilated loop. Closed-loop obstruction cannot be managed conservatively.

questions.stem: 22-year-old: firm, rubbery, mobile breast mass. US: well-circumscribed solid. Most likely?

choices.feedback: Phyllodes: rapidly growing, large, older women (40s).
Papilloma presents with bloody discharge, not discrete mobile mass.
Correct! Most common mass in women <35. Firm, rubbery, painless, highly mobile. Observe if <2 cm and stable.
Cancer at 22 uncommon. Mobile, well-circumscribed = benign.

questions.explanation: Fibroadenoma is the most common breast mass in women under 30. Classic features: firm, rubbery, well-circumscribed, mobile ('breast mouse'), painless. Ultrasound shows a solid, well-circumscribed, homogeneous mass. Management in young women with classic features: observation with follow-up or core biopsy if >2 cm or growing. Phyllodes tumors are larger, grow rapidly, and occur in older women (40s). Intraductal papilloma presents with nipple discharge, not a palpable mass typically. Breast cancer in a 22-year-old is rare and typically presents with irregular, fixed masses.

questions.stem: Most common cause of bloody nipple discharge?

choices.feedback: Presents as solid mass, not discharge.
Correct! #1 cause of unilateral bloody discharge from single duct. Benign subareolar tumor. Treatment: duct excision.
Can cause bloody discharge but papilloma is more common.
Causes pain and clear/straw discharge, not bloody.

questions.explanation: Intraductal papilloma is the most common cause of bloody (sanguineous) nipple discharge. These are benign papillary tumors arising within the lactiferous ducts, typically solitary and located near the nipple. They present with spontaneous unilateral bloody or serosanguineous discharge. Diagnosis is confirmed by ductography or duct excision. Fibroadenomas are solid masses that rarely cause discharge. Breast cancer can cause bloody discharge but is less common than papilloma. Fibrocystic changes cause bilateral, non-bloody, often cyclical discharge.

questions.stem: Lumpectomy + radiation equals mastectomy for early-stage breast cancer. Key advantage?

choices.feedback: Systemic therapy based on tumor biology, not surgical approach.
Correct! NSABP B-06, Milan trials: lumpectomy + XRT = equal overall survival to mastectomy for stage I-II. Key advantage: breast conservation.
Cost varies with radiation follow-up requirements.
Local recurrence slightly higher with lumpectomy, but OS equivalent.

questions.explanation: Landmark NSABP B-06 and other randomized trials demonstrated equivalent overall survival between breast-conserving therapy (lumpectomy + whole-breast radiation) and mastectomy for early-stage breast cancer (stages I-II). The key advantage is breast conservation while maintaining equal oncologic outcomes. Systemic therapy decisions are independent of surgical approach. Lumpectomy + radiation is not necessarily lower cost. Local recurrence rates are slightly higher with lumpectomy + radiation (~5-10% at 10 years) but are manageable with salvage mastectomy, and survival remains equivalent.

questions.stem: Complication to monitor with trastuzumab (Herceptin)?

choices.feedback: That's bleomycin.
That's cisplatin.
Correct! HER2 receptors on cardiomyocytes. Reversible cardiomyopathy. Echo every 3 months. Hold if LVEF drops >15% or below 50%.
That's platinum agents and taxanes.

questions.explanation: Trastuzumab (Herceptin), a monoclonal antibody targeting HER2/neu receptor, carries a significant risk of cardiotoxicity (cardiomyopathy, decreased LVEF) in approximately 2-7% of patients (higher when combined with anthracyclines — up to 27%). Cardiac monitoring with echocardiography or MUGA scan is required every 3 months during treatment. Unlike anthracycline cardiotoxicity, trastuzumab-related cardiac dysfunction is usually reversible. Pulmonary fibrosis is associated with bleomycin. Nephrotoxicity is associated with cisplatin. Peripheral neuropathy is associated with taxanes and vinca alkaloids.

questions.stem: ER+/PR+ breast cancer in a premenopausal woman — endocrine therapy?

choices.feedback: Chemotherapy agent, not endocrine therapy.

Correct! Tamoxifen = selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM). First-line endocrine therapy in premenopausal women. Risks: endometrial cancer, VTE. AIs (anastrozole, letrozole) are for postmenopausal patients only.
Trastuzumab targets HER2, not estrogen receptors.
AIs only work in postmenopausal women (block peripheral estrogen conversion). Premenopausal ovaries still produce estrogen.

questions.explanation: Tamoxifen is the standard endocrine therapy for premenopausal women with ER+/PR+ breast cancer. It is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) that competitively blocks estrogen binding at the breast. Duration is 5-10 years. Aromatase inhibitors (letrozole, anastrozole) are for postmenopausal women only — in premenopausal women, they paradoxically stimulate ovarian estrogen production through the hypothalamic-pituitary feedback loop (unless combined with ovarian suppression). Cyclophosphamide is a chemotherapy agent. Trastuzumab targets HER2, not hormone receptors.

questions.stem: Inflammatory breast cancer presents with:

choices.feedback: Bilateral discharge suggests hormonal cause (prolactinoma), not cancer.

choices.text: Erythema, peau d'orange, warmth, NO mass
choices.feedback: Correct! Inflammatory = dermal lymphatic invasion. Skin erythema, edema (peau d'orange = orange peel skin), warmth. Often mistaken for mastitis/cellulitis. NO discrete mass. Worst prognosis of all breast cancers. Biopsy of skin shows tumor emboli in dermal lymphatics.
Inflammatory breast cancer characteristically has NO discrete mass.
Isolated LAD without breast changes is less typical of inflammatory cancer.

questions.explanation: Inflammatory breast cancer (IBC) presents with diffuse erythema, peau d'orange (skin edema with dimpling resembling orange peel from dermal lymphatic obstruction by tumor emboli), warmth, and typically no discrete palpable mass. It is often misdiagnosed as mastitis or cellulitis, delaying diagnosis. IBC is stage IIIB at minimum and requires neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by modified radical mastectomy then radiation. Bilateral nipple discharge suggests a systemic hormonal cause. A well-defined mass is typical of fibroadenoma or carcinoma. Painless axillary lymphadenopathy can accompany various breast cancers.

questions.stem: BRCA1/2 mutation carriers have what lifetime breast cancer risk?

choices.feedback: Far below the actual BRCA carrier risk.
Not all BRCA carriers develop cancer, though risk is very high.
This is the general population risk, not BRCA carriers.

Correct! BRCA1/2 mutations: 60-80% lifetime breast cancer risk, 20-40% ovarian cancer risk. Management: enhanced screening (MRI + mammography starting age 25-30), risk-reducing mastectomy, risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy, chemoprevention (tamoxifen).

questions.explanation: BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutation carriers have an estimated 60-80% lifetime risk of developing breast cancer (compared to ~12% in the general population). BRCA1 mutations also confer ~40-60% ovarian cancer risk; BRCA2 confers ~15-30% ovarian cancer risk. Risk-reduction strategies include enhanced surveillance (annual mammogram + breast MRI starting at age 25-30), chemoprevention (tamoxifen/raloxifene), and prophylactic bilateral mastectomy (reduces risk by ~90-95%) and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. The risk is not 100% — incomplete penetrance exists.

questions.stem: Sentinel lymph node biopsy: if SLN is negative, what next?

choices.feedback: Not standard practice.

Correct! Negative SLN means the cancer has not spread to axillary nodes. No axillary dissection → avoids lymphedema, nerve injury, shoulder dysfunction. Even with 1-2 positive SLN, ACOSOG Z0011 showed no benefit to complete dissection in lumpectomy + radiation patients.
No indication for repeat biopsy if initial SLN is negative.
Negative SLN = no further dissection needed. This is the whole point of SLNBx.

questions.explanation: If the sentinel lymph node (SLN) — the first node draining the tumor — is negative for metastatic disease, no further axillary surgery (complete axillary lymph node dissection, ALND) is needed. SLN biopsy has a >95% negative predictive value and dramatically reduces morbidity compared to routine ALND (lymphedema, seroma, nerve injury). This was established by landmark trials (NSABP B-32, ALMANAC). Contralateral sampling is never indicated. Repeat SLN biopsy is not standard practice. ALND is reserved for positive SLNs (with some exceptions per ACOSOG Z0011 for 1-2 positive SLNs).
questions.citation: Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy. StatPearls. [Open Access]

questions.stem: Painless, dry, white/leathery wound. Burn depth?

choices.feedback: Superficial partial = very painful with blisters.
Superficial burns are painful and red.
Sunburns are red. White/leathery = full thickness.
Correct! Destroys epidermis, dermis, AND nerves → painless, leathery, white. Cannot heal spontaneously — needs excision + graft.

questions.explanation: Full-thickness (third-degree) burns destroy the entire epidermis and dermis, including nerve endings, hair follicles, and sweat glands. The wound appears white, leathery, or waxy and is characteristically PAINLESS because sensory nerve endings are destroyed. This distinguishes it from superficial partial-thickness burns (painful, moist, blistered) and deep partial-thickness burns (decreased sensation, mottled). Full-thickness burns cannot re-epithelialize from dermal appendages (all are destroyed) and require skin grafting for definitive closure. Eschar (the dead tissue) is inelastic and may require escharotomy if circumferential.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Full-thickness (third-degree) burns destroy the entire epidermis and dermis, including nerve endings, hair follicles, and sweat glands. The wound appears white, leathery, or waxy and is characteristically PAINLESS because sensory nerve endings are destroyed. This distinguishes it from superficial partial-thickness burns (painful, moist, blistered) and deep partial-thickness burns (decreased sensation, mottled). Full-thickness burns cannot re-epithelialize from dermal appendages (all are destroyed) and require skin grafting for definitive closure. Eschar (the dead tissue) is inelastic and may require escharotomy if circumferential.

questions.stem: The Parkland formula for burn resuscitation specifies what proportion of calculated fluids should be given in the first 8 hours?

choices.feedback: Too little — would result in inadequate early resuscitation.

Correct! Parkland formula: 4 mL × kg × %TBSA = total 24h crystalloid. Half in the first 8 hours (from time of burn, not arrival), remaining half over the next 16 hours. Monitor urine output to guide titration.
Front-loading this aggressively risks fluid overload and pulmonary edema.

choices.text: The entire amount in the first 8 hours
choices.feedback: Giving all fluids in 8 hours would cause massive fluid overload. The formula distributes fluids over 24 hours.

questions.explanation: The Parkland formula for burn resuscitation calculates the 24-hour IV fluid requirement as 4 mL × body weight (kg) × %TBSA burned (using only second- and third-degree burns). The critical detail is that ONE-HALF (50%) of the calculated volume is given in the first 8 hours from the time of injury (not from hospital arrival), and the remaining half over the next 16 hours. Lactated Ringer's is the preferred crystalloid. The formula is a starting estimate — fluid rate should be titrated to urine output (0.5-1 mL/kg/hr in adults, 1-2 mL/kg/hr in children). Over-resuscitation causes abdominal compartment syndrome, pulmonary edema, and extremity compartment syndromes.
questions.verifiedExplanation: The Parkland formula for burn resuscitation calculates the 24-hour IV fluid requirement as 4 mL × body weight (kg) × %TBSA burned (using only second- and third-degree burns). The critical detail is that ONE-HALF (50%) of the calculated volume is given in the first 8 hours from the time of injury (not from hospital arrival), and the remaining half over the next 16 hours. Lactated Ringer's is the preferred crystalloid. The formula is a starting estimate — fluid rate should be titrated to urine output (0.5-1 mL/kg/hr in adults, 1-2 mL/kg/hr in children). Over-resuscitation causes abdominal compartment syndrome, pulmonary edema, and extremity compartment syndromes.

questions.stem: Circumferential full-thickness burns with absent pulses. Treatment?

choices.feedback: Insufficient for compartment syndrome with absent pulses.
Correct! Rigid eschar cannot expand → compartment syndrome. Escharotomy = incision through eschar (painless in full-thickness burns) to release constriction.
Fasciotomy is for non-burn compartment syndrome. Burns need escharotomy.
Grafting is definitive wound treatment, not acute compartment management.

questions.explanation: Circumferential full-thickness burns with absent distal pulses indicate compartment syndrome from eschar constriction and require emergent escharotomy. The inelastic burned tissue (eschar) cannot expand as underlying tissues swell from fluid resuscitation and inflammation, creating a tourniquet effect that compromises circulation. Escharotomy involves incision through the full thickness of the eschar (which is painless because nerves are destroyed) along the lateral and medial aspects of the affected extremity. For chest burns, escharotomy along the anterior axillary lines and costal margins relieves restriction of chest wall expansion. This is a bedside procedure that does not require the OR.

The inelastic burned tissue (eschar) cannot expand as underlying tissues swell from fluid resuscitation and inflammation, creating a tourniquet effect that compromises circulation. Escharotomy involves incision through the full thickness of the eschar (which is painless because nerves are destroyed) along the lateral and medial aspects of the affected extremity. For chest burns, escharotomy along the anterior axillary lines and costal margins relieves restriction of chest wall expansion. This is a bedside procedure that does not require the OR.

questions.stem: #1 cause of death in burn patients after initial resuscitation?

choices.feedback: Occurs but not the leading cause.
Correct! Loss of skin barrier + immunosuppression. Pseudomonas and S. aureus most common. Prevention: topical antimicrobials, early excision/grafting.
VTE risk elevated but not #1 cause of death.
Acute injury at time of burn, not delayed.

questions.explanation: After the initial resuscitation phase, sepsis and infection are the leading cause of death in burn patients. Burn wound infection is the primary source, with risk proportional to burn size and depth. The loss of the skin barrier, combined with a profoundly immunosuppressed state (impaired cell-mediated immunity, complement consumption, neutrophil dysfunction), creates extreme infection susceptibility. Common organisms include Staphylococcus aureus (early), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (late), and fungi (in prolonged ICU stays). Burn wound cultures, topical antimicrobials, and early excision with grafting are key preventive strategies.
