Burn wound infection is the primary source, with risk proportional to burn size and depth. The loss of the skin barrier, combined with a profoundly immunosuppressed state (impaired cell-mediated immunity, complement consumption, neutrophil dysfunction), creates extreme infection susceptibility. Common organisms include Staphylococcus aureus (early), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (late), and fungi (in prolonged ICU stays). Burn wound cultures, topical antimicrobials, and early excision with grafting are key preventive strategies.

questions.stem: House fire: singed nasal hair, hoarseness, carbonaceous sputum. Priority?

choices.feedback: Helpful for assessment but securing airway comes first.
NC doesn't protect airway from progressive edema.
Correct! Airway edema progresses rapidly — intubatable now may not be later. Most time-sensitive burn intervention.
Imaging not the priority when airway compromise suspected.

questions.explanation: Singed nasal hairs, hoarseness, stridor, carbonaceous sputum, and facial burns in a fire victim are signs of inhalation injury requiring early intubation. Airway edema from thermal and chemical injury progresses rapidly — the upper airway can obstruct within hours as fluid resuscitation causes further swelling. By the time stridor develops, significant edema is already present. Intubation should be performed early and electively before the airway becomes critically narrowed. Inhalation injury significantly increases mortality, fluid requirements, and risk of pneumonia. Direct laryngoscopy/bronchoscopy can assess the degree of airway injury.

By the time stridor develops, significant edema is already present. Intubation should be performed early and electively before the airway becomes critically narrowed. Inhalation injury significantly increases mortality, fluid requirements, and risk of pneumonia. Direct laryngoscopy/bronchoscopy can assess the degree of airway injury.

questions.stem: Curling ulcer occurs in which location?

choices.feedback: Correct! Curling ulcer = duodenal stress ulcer in severe burns. Prophylaxis: PPI/H2 blocker. Compare: Cushing ulcer = gastric, from brain injury.
Curling ulcers specifically affect duodenum.
Cushing ulcer (brain injury) = stomach. Curling = duodenum.
Burn stress ulcers are duodenal.

questions.explanation: Curling ulcer is a stress ulcer occurring in the duodenum, associated with severe burns. The mechanism involves splanchnic hypoperfusion from hypovolemia and massive sympathetic activation, leading to mucosal ischemia and breakdown of the protective duodenal barrier. This is distinct from Cushing ulcer (stress ulcer associated with CNS injury/increased ICP, which characteristically involves the stomach and is associated with gastric acid hypersecretion from vagal stimulation). Stress ulcer prophylaxis with PPIs or H2 blockers is standard in critically ill burn patients to prevent Curling ulcers.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Curling ulcer is a stress ulcer occurring in the duodenum, associated with severe burns. The mechanism involves splanchnic hypoperfusion from hypovolemia and massive sympathetic activation, leading to mucosal ischemia and breakdown of the protective duodenal barrier. This is distinct from Cushing ulcer (stress ulcer associated with CNS injury/increased ICP, which characteristically involves the stomach and is associated with gastric acid hypersecretion from vagal stimulation). Stress ulcer prophylaxis with PPIs or H2 blockers is standard in critically ill burn patients to prevent Curling ulcers.

questions.stem: CO poisoning treatment?

choices.feedback: Correct! CO has 200x affinity for Hb. 100% O2 reduces CO half-life from 4-6h to 60-90 min. Pulse ox unreliable (reads COHb as oxyHb). Check ABG co-oximetry. Hyperbaric for COHb >25%, neuro symptoms, pregnancy.
That treats methemoglobinemia, not CO poisoning.
NAC treats acetaminophen toxicity.
That treats cyanide poisoning.

questions.explanation: Carbon monoxide poisoning in burn patients is treated with 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask. CO binds hemoglobin with 200-250× the affinity of oxygen, forming carboxyhemoglobin (COHb). High-flow 100% O2 reduces COHb half-life from ~4-6 hours on room air to ~60-90 minutes. Pulse oximetry is unreliable in CO poisoning (falsely normal readings). Co-oximetry via ABG is required to measure COHb levels. All fire victims should be presumed to have CO exposure until proven otherwise. Hyperbaric oxygen is considered for severe poisoning (loss of consciousness, COHb >25%, pregnancy, cardiac ischemia).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Carbon monoxide poisoning in burn patients is treated with 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask. CO binds hemoglobin with 200-250× the affinity of oxygen, forming carboxyhemoglobin (COHb). High-flow 100% O2 reduces COHb half-life from ~4-6 hours on room air to ~60-90 minutes. Pulse oximetry is unreliable in CO poisoning (falsely normal readings). Co-oximetry via ABG is required to measure COHb levels. All fire victims should be presumed to have CO exposure until proven otherwise. Hyperbaric oxygen is considered for severe poisoning (loss of consciousness, COHb >25%, pregnancy, cardiac ischemia).

questions.stem: Silver sulfadiazine should be AVOIDED on:

choices.feedback: Correct! Avoided on face due to permanent staining. Use bacitracin instead. Also avoid in: sulfa allergy, pregnancy (kernicterus risk), neonates.
Commonly used on extremities without issue.
Still widely used, just with specific restrictions.
Trunk burns routinely treated with silver sulfadiazine.

questions.explanation: Silver sulfadiazine should be avoided on the face because it can cause permanent skin discoloration (argyria) and is not necessary for facial burns, which heal well with alternative topical agents. Bacitracin or petroleum-based ointments are preferred for facial burns. Silver sulfadiazine also should not be used on patients with sulfa allergy, in neonates (risk of kernicterus from sulfa component), or near the eyes. On other body areas, silver sulfadiazine provides broad-spectrum antimicrobial coverage (gram-positives, gram-negatives, and some fungi) and remains widely used for partial-thickness burns, though mafenide acetate (Sulfamylon) has superior eschar penetration.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Silver sulfadiazine should be avoided on the face because it can cause permanent skin discoloration (argyria) and is not necessary for facial burns, which heal well with alternative topical agents. Bacitracin or petroleum-based ointments are preferred for facial burns. Silver sulfadiazine also should not be used on patients with sulfa allergy, in neonates (risk of kernicterus from sulfa component), or near the eyes. On other body areas, silver sulfadiazine provides broad-spectrum antimicrobial coverage (gram-positives, gram-negatives, and some fungi) and remains widely used for partial-thickness burns, though mafenide acetate (Sulfamylon) has superior eschar penetration.

questions.stem: CO binds hemoglobin with what relative affinity compared to O2?

choices.feedback: If equal, CO wouldn't be dangerous at low concentrations.
The affinity is much higher — 200-250×.
Much higher than that.

Correct! CO's extreme affinity for Hb means even low concentrations displace significant O2. Additionally, CO shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve LEFT → remaining O2 is held more tightly → less O2 released to tissues. Double insult: reduced O2 carrying capacity + impaired O2 unloading.

questions.explanation: Carbon monoxide binds hemoglobin with an affinity approximately 200-250 times greater than oxygen, forming carboxyhemoglobin (COHb). This extreme affinity means even small concentrations of CO in inspired air can rapidly displace oxygen from hemoglobin. Beyond simple competitive displacement, CO binding shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve LEFT, impairing oxygen unloading at the tissues (increased oxygen affinity of remaining unaffected hemoglobin molecules). CO also binds myoglobin and cytochrome oxidase, directly impairing cellular respiration. The combined effect is both reduced oxygen delivery and impaired oxygen utilization.

This extreme affinity means even small concentrations of CO in inspired air can rapidly displace oxygen from hemoglobin. Beyond simple competitive displacement, CO binding shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve LEFT, impairing oxygen unloading at the tissues (increased oxygen affinity of remaining unaffected hemoglobin molecules). CO also binds myoglobin and cytochrome oxidase, directly impairing cellular respiration. The combined effect is both reduced oxygen delivery and impaired oxygen utilization.

questions.stem: Why is pulse oximetry unreliable in CO poisoning?

choices.text: Pulse ox only works on arterial blood
choices.feedback: Pulse ox does measure arterial saturation — it just can't distinguish CO-bound Hb.

choices.text: CO interferes with the infrared signal
choices.feedback: The issue is wavelength overlap, not interference.

choices.text: Standard pulse ox cannot distinguish COHb from OxyHb → reads falsely normal
choices.feedback: Correct! Standard pulse oximeters measure light absorption at 2 wavelengths — COHb absorbs similarly to OxyHb at these wavelengths. SpO2 reads normal even with lethal COHb levels. Diagnosis requires CO-OXIMETRY (ABG with co-oximeter) — measures COHb, OxyHb, MetHb separately using multiple wavelengths.

choices.text: CO destroys the pulse oximeter sensor
choices.feedback: The device functions normally; it just misreads.

questions.explanation: Standard pulse oximetry is unreliable in CO poisoning because conventional two-wavelength pulse oximeters cannot distinguish carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) from oxyhemoglobin (OxyHb) — both absorb light at 660 nm similarly. This results in a falsely normal or only slightly reduced SpO2 reading despite severe hypoxemia. A patient with COHb of 40% may display an SpO2 of 97-99%. CO-oximetry (using multiple wavelengths of light) can accurately measure COHb, OxyHb, MetHb, and deoxyhemoglobin separately. ABG with co-oximetry is the definitive test — the PaO2 will be normal (dissolved oxygen is unaffected) but SaO2 measured by co-oximetry will be low.

This results in a falsely normal or only slightly reduced SpO2 reading despite severe hypoxemia. A patient with COHb of 40% may display an SpO2 of 97-99%. CO-oximetry (using multiple wavelengths of light) can accurately measure COHb, OxyHb, MetHb, and deoxyhemoglobin separately. ABG with co-oximetry is the definitive test — the PaO2 will be normal (dissolved oxygen is unaffected) but SaO2 measured by co-oximetry will be low.

questions.stem: Multiple family members present with headache and nausea in winter. Suspect:

choices.feedback: While possible, the simultaneous presentation + winter + headache pattern is classic for CO.
Lead is chronic, not acute simultaneous presentation.
Food poisoning typically has more GI symptoms and wouldn't affect pets the same way.

Correct! Classic scenario: multiple household members (including pets) ill with headache, nausea, dizziness — especially in winter with heating systems. CO is odorless and colorless. Ask about furnaces, space heaters, generators. Evacuate immediately. Check co-oximetry. 'Headache improving when leaving home' is pathognomonic.

questions.explanation: Multiple family members presenting simultaneously with headache, nausea, and flu-like symptoms (especially during winter months when heating systems are in use) is the classic presentation of carbon monoxide poisoning from an indoor CO source (malfunctioning furnace, generator, space heater, blocked chimney). The 'whole household sick' pattern is the key diagnostic clue — infectious illness rarely affects all household members simultaneously with identical symptom onset. Pets may also be affected. CO detectors should be checked. Diagnosis is confirmed by co-oximetry showing elevated COHb levels. Treatment is immediate removal from the exposure and high-flow 100% O2.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Multiple family members presenting simultaneously with headache, nausea, and flu-like symptoms (especially during winter months when heating systems are in use) is the classic presentation of carbon monoxide poisoning from an indoor CO source (malfunctioning furnace, generator, space heater, blocked chimney). The 'whole household sick' pattern is the key diagnostic clue — infectious illness rarely affects all household members simultaneously with identical symptom onset. Pets may also be affected. CO detectors should be checked. Diagnosis is confirmed by co-oximetry showing elevated COHb levels. Treatment is immediate removal from the exposure and high-flow 100% O2.

questions.stem: COHb half-life on 100% O2 via NRB?

choices.feedback: That would be dangerously long.

Correct! Room air: 4-6h. 100% NRB: 60-90 min. Hyperbaric O2: 20-30 min. High-flow 100% O2 is the immediate first-line treatment — start before co-oximetry results. Continue until COHb <3-5% and symptoms resolve.
4-6 hours is the half-life on room air.
20-30 min is the hyperbaric O2 half-life.

questions.explanation: The half-life of carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) on 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask is approximately 60-90 minutes. This compares to: ~4-6 hours on room air (21% O2), and ~15-30 minutes in a hyperbaric oxygen chamber at 2.5-3 atmospheres. High-flow 100% O2 is the immediate first-line treatment for all CO-poisoned patients — it should be started as soon as CO poisoning is suspected, before COHb levels are available. The non-rebreather mask must be properly fitted with a tight seal and adequate flow rate (15 L/min) to deliver near-100% FiO2.
questions.verifiedExplanation: The half-life of carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) on 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask is approximately 60-90 minutes. This compares to: ~4-6 hours on room air (21% O2), and ~15-30 minutes in a hyperbaric oxygen chamber at 2.5-3 atmospheres. High-flow 100% O2 is the immediate first-line treatment for all CO-poisoned patients — it should be started as soon as CO poisoning is suspected, before COHb levels are available. The non-rebreather mask must be properly fitted with a tight seal and adequate flow rate (15 L/min) to deliver near-100% FiO2.

questions.stem: Indications for hyperbaric oxygen in CO poisoning include:

choices.text: Only if pulse oximetry is low
choices.feedback: Pulse ox is unreliable in CO poisoning — cannot guide treatment decisions.
Mild exposures respond well to 100% NRB alone.

choices.text: LOC, seizures, COHb >25%, cardiac ischemia, pregnancy with significant exposure
choices.feedback: Correct! HBO indications: loss of consciousness/coma, seizures, focal neurologic deficits, COHb >25% (>15% in pregnancy), cardiac ischemia, persistent symptoms despite NRB. HBO reduces COHb half-life to 20-30 min and may reduce delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome risk.
Indications are based on severity, not exposure source.

questions.explanation: Indications for hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) therapy in CO poisoning include: loss of consciousness at any point, seizures, COHb level >25% (some use >20%), cardiac ischemia (troponin elevation, ECG changes), pregnancy (fetal hemoglobin binds CO with even greater affinity), persistent neurological symptoms despite normobaric O2, and severe metabolic acidosis. HBO at 2.5-3 atm reduces COHb half-life to 15-30 minutes, restores cytochrome oxidase function, and may reduce the incidence of delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome. The strongest evidence supports HBO for patients with loss of consciousness. Transfer to an HBO facility should not delay initiation of 100% O2.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Indications for hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) therapy in CO poisoning include: loss of consciousness at any point, seizures, COHb level >25% (some use >20%), cardiac ischemia (troponin elevation, ECG changes), pregnancy (fetal hemoglobin binds CO with even greater affinity), persistent neurological symptoms despite normobaric O2, and severe metabolic acidosis. HBO at 2.5-3 atm reduces COHb half-life to 15-30 minutes, restores cytochrome oxidase function, and may reduce the incidence of delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome. The strongest evidence supports HBO for patients with loss of consciousness. Transfer to an HBO facility should not delay initiation of 100% O2.

questions.stem: Delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome after CO exposure occurs:

choices.feedback: DNS is a delayed phenomenon, not immediate.
Onset is typically within 2-40 days.

Correct! DNS affects 15-40% of significant exposures. Symptoms: memory loss, confusion, personality changes, movement disorders (parkinsonism), depression. May be permanent. Mechanism: delayed demyelination and neuronal apoptosis. HBO therapy may reduce risk. All significant CO exposure patients need cognitive follow-up at 1-2 weeks.
DNS can occur even with moderate exposures that don't cause initial coma.

questions.explanation: Delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome (DNS) occurs 2-40 days after apparent recovery from CO poisoning and affects up to 40% of patients with significant exposure. Symptoms include cognitive impairment (memory, concentration), personality/behavioral changes, parkinsonism, dementia, psychosis, and movement disorders. The mechanism involves CO-mediated lipid peroxidation of myelin in cerebral white matter, delayed immune-mediated demyelination, and apoptosis in vulnerable brain regions (hippocampus, globus pallidus, cerebellum). Hyperbaric oxygen therapy may reduce DNS incidence, though evidence is mixed. MRI may show white matter changes and bilateral globus pallidus lesions.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome (DNS) occurs 2-40 days after apparent recovery from CO poisoning and affects up to 40% of patients with significant exposure. Symptoms include cognitive impairment (memory, concentration), personality/behavioral changes, parkinsonism, dementia, psychosis, and movement disorders. The mechanism involves CO-mediated lipid peroxidation of myelin in cerebral white matter, delayed immune-mediated demyelination, and apoptosis in vulnerable brain regions (hippocampus, globus pallidus, cerebellum). Hyperbaric oxygen therapy may reduce DNS incidence, though evidence is mixed. MRI may show white matter changes and bilateral globus pallidus lesions.

questions.stem: Cherry-red skin in CO poisoning is:

choices.text: The earliest and most reliable sign
choices.feedback: This is a dangerous misconception.

Correct! Cherry-red skin/lips are classically taught but are actually a LATE finding, often postmortem. Most living CO patients appear normal or pale/cyanotic. Do not wait for or rely on cherry-red skin. Diagnose with co-oximetry. Clinical suspicion based on context (winter, multiple affected, headache) is more important.

choices.text: Present in all cases with COHb >10%
choices.feedback: Many patients with significant COHb never develop cherry-red skin.

choices.text: A sign unique to cyanide poisoning
choices.feedback: Cherry-red is classically associated with both CO and cyanide, but is unreliable for both.

questions.explanation: Cherry-red skin coloration in CO poisoning is a classic but misleading teaching point — it is primarily a late or postmortem finding, rarely observed in living patients. The cherry-red color results from high concentrations of carboxyhemoglobin in skin and mucous membranes. In practice, living CO-poisoned patients more commonly appear normal, pale, or cyanotic. Relying on cherry-red coloration to diagnose CO poisoning would miss the vast majority of cases. The diagnosis should be suspected based on clinical context (exposure history, symptom pattern, multiple patients) and confirmed by co-oximetry, not by skin color assessment.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Cherry-red skin coloration in CO poisoning is a classic but misleading teaching point — it is primarily a late or postmortem finding, rarely observed in living patients. The cherry-red color results from high concentrations of carboxyhemoglobin in skin and mucous membranes. In practice, living CO-poisoned patients more commonly appear normal, pale, or cyanotic. Relying on cherry-red coloration to diagnose CO poisoning would miss the vast majority of cases. The diagnosis should be suspected based on clinical context (exposure history, symptom pattern, multiple patients) and confirmed by co-oximetry, not by skin color assessment.

questions.stem: CO poisoning in pregnancy is especially dangerous because:

choices.text: CO only affects the mother, not the fetus
choices.feedback: The fetus is actually MORE vulnerable than the mother.
CO freely crosses the placenta.
100% O2 is safe and essential in pregnant CO patients.

choices.text: Fetal hemoglobin binds CO with even greater affinity + slower clearance
choices.feedback: Correct! Fetal Hb (HbF) has higher CO affinity than adult Hb → fetal COHb levels are 10-15% higher than maternal levels, and clearance is much slower. Fetal tissue hypoxia occurs at maternal levels that may seem only moderate. Lower threshold for HBO in pregnancy. Consider HBO for any pregnant patient with symptoms or COHb >15%.

questions.explanation: CO poisoning in pregnancy is especially dangerous because fetal hemoglobin (HbF) binds CO with 10-15% greater affinity than adult hemoglobin, resulting in higher fetal COHb levels than maternal levels. Additionally, fetal COHb elimination is slower because it depends on maternal-fetal oxygen gradient across the placenta. Fetal tissue oxygen delivery is already marginal under normal conditions — even modest COHb elevations can cause fetal hypoxia, leading to neurological damage, growth restriction, or fetal death. Pregnant patients should receive high-flow 100% O2 for longer durations, and HBO therapy is recommended at lower COHb thresholds than for non-pregnant adults.
questions.verifiedExplanation: CO poisoning in pregnancy is especially dangerous because fetal hemoglobin (HbF) binds CO with 10-15% greater affinity than adult hemoglobin, resulting in higher fetal COHb levels than maternal levels. Additionally, fetal COHb elimination is slower because it depends on maternal-fetal oxygen gradient across the placenta. Fetal tissue oxygen delivery is already marginal under normal conditions — even modest COHb elevations can cause fetal hypoxia, leading to neurological damage, growth restriction, or fetal death. Pregnant patients should receive high-flow 100% O2 for longer durations, and HBO therapy is recommended at lower COHb thresholds than for non-pregnant adults.

questions.stem: What is the critical immediate intervention?

choices.feedback: Correct! Immediate intubation is critical. Bag-mask ventilation is contraindicated because it inflates bowel in the chest, worsening pulmonary compression. Place an OG tube to decompress the stomach.
This is a full-term baby. The problem is mechanical (herniated bowel), not surfactant deficiency. The scaphoid abdomen + absent left breath sounds = CDH.
Bag-mask ventilation worsens CDH by forcing air into herniated bowel in the chest, further compressing the lung. This is a key teaching point.
The findings suggest CDH, not pneumothorax. Needle decompression could injure herniated abdominal organs. The scaphoid abdomen is the key distinguishing feature.

questions.stem: What embryologic structure fails to form in Bochdalek hernia?

choices.feedback: Body wall mesoderm contributes the peripheral muscular rim of the diaphragm, not the area where Bochdalek hernias occur.
The septum transversum forms the central tendon (anterior diaphragm). Bochdalek hernias are POSTEROLATERAL.

Correct! The pleuroperitoneal membranes close the posterolateral canals by week 8. Left closes later than right (explaining left-sided predominance). Failure → Bochdalek hernia. The diaphragm has 4 embryologic components: septum transversum, pleuroperitoneal membranes, body wall mesoderm, and esophageal mesentery.
The esophageal mesentery forms the crura (posterior diaphragm around esophagus), not the posterolateral portion.

questions.stem: Why is pulmonary hypertension so severe in CDH?

choices.feedback: Correct! CDH causes: (1) fewer pulmonary arteries (hypoplasia); (2) thickened arterial walls with excessive smooth muscle (vascular remodeling); (3) increased vascular reactivity. This 'two-hit' of fewer vessels + higher resistance creates severe, often refractory pulmonary hypertension.
While pulmonary vasoconstriction contributes, CDH pulmonary hypertension is primarily structural (fewer, thicker vessels), not just functional.
While the hypoplastic lung has reduced surfactant, the primary problem is the vascular bed.
There is mediastinal shift, but the primary driver of pulmonary hypertension is the abnormal pulmonary vascular bed, not cardiac compression.

questions.stem: When should surgical repair be performed?

choices.feedback: This was the old approach. Current evidence shows delayed repair after physiologic stabilization improves survival.

Correct! Current practice is delayed repair (typically 24-96 hours or more) after pulmonary hypertension is managed. Surgery does not improve the pulmonary hypertension — the vascular bed is structurally abnormal. Operating on a hemodynamically unstable patient worsens outcomes.

choices.text: Only if ECMO is not needed
choices.feedback: Even patients requiring ECMO undergo repair — either on ECMO or shortly after decannulation.
Waiting a full month is too long. Once stable, repair should proceed to allow the lung to expand and develop.

questions.stem: Which prenatal finding is the strongest predictor of CDH outcome?

choices.feedback: Correct! O/E LHR is the best prenatal predictor. Measured on ultrasound: contralateral lung area / head circumference, compared to expected. O/E LHR <25% = severe (survival ~15-20%). O/E LHR >45% = mild (survival >90%). Liver herniation into the chest is also a negative predictor.
Earlier detection may correlate with severity but is not the strongest quantitative predictor.
Bowel volume matters less than the degree of lung hypoplasia, which is directly measured by LHR.

choices.text: Side of the hernia (left vs right)
choices.feedback: Right-sided CDH can be more severe (liver herniation), but the strongest predictor is lung-to-head ratio.

questions.stem: Most important risk factor for C. difficile infection?

choices.feedback: Diet is not a major risk factor for C. diff.

Correct! Antibiotics disrupt normal colonic flora → C. diff overgrowth and toxin production. Highest risk: fluoroquinolones, clindamycin, cephalosporins, carbapenems. Risk persists up to 3 months after antibiotic exposure. Can occur with ANY antibiotic, even a single dose.
Immunosuppression increases risk but antibiotics are the primary driver.
Advanced age increases risk but is not the #1 factor.

questions.explanation: Recent antibiotic use is the single most important risk factor for Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI). Antibiotics disrupt normal colonic microbiota, allowing C. difficile spores to germinate and toxin-producing vegetative forms to proliferate. The highest-risk antibiotics are fluoroquinolones, clindamycin, cephalosporins, and carbapenems, though virtually any antibiotic can cause CDI. Other risk factors include age >65, hospitalization, PPI use, and immunodeficiency, but antibiotic exposure is the dominant risk factor.

questions.stem: C. diff treatment — metronidazole is:

choices.feedback: Correct! 2021 IDSA update: fidaxomicin preferred (lower recurrence rate) or oral vancomycin for initial non-severe and severe CDI. Metronidazole only as adjunct IV in fulminant CDI (when oral vancomycin can't reach colon due to ileus). Metronidazole was inferior to vancomycin in clinical trials.
This is outdated. Metronidazole is no longer first-line per current guidelines.

choices.text: Used only for recurrent C. diff
choices.feedback: Recurrent CDI is managed with fidaxomicin, vancomycin taper, or FMT.
IV metronidazole is only added in fulminant cases as adjunct. It does not achieve adequate colonic levels alone.

questions.explanation: Per IDSA/SHEA 2021 guidelines, metronidazole is no longer recommended as first-line treatment for CDI. Fidaxomicin is now preferred first-line, with oral vancomycin as an alternative. Metronidazole may be used only when fidaxomicin and vancomycin are unavailable. Fidaxomicin has lower recurrence rates than vancomycin (~13% vs ~27%). For fulminant CDI, oral vancomycin (via NG tube and/or rectal retention enema) combined with IV metronidazole is the regimen. The change reflects metronidazole's lower cure rates, higher recurrence, and potential neurotoxicity with prolonged use.

questions.stem: Why must vancomycin be given ORALLY (not IV) for C. diff?

choices.text: IV vancomycin is toxic to the colon
choices.feedback: IV vancomycin isn't directly toxic to the colon.

choices.text: IV vancomycin does not reach adequate colonic concentrations
choices.feedback: Correct! Vancomycin given IV achieves negligible colonic luminal concentrations — it's not excreted into the GI tract. Oral vancomycin (or via NG tube) delivers drug directly to the site of infection. IV vancomycin is used for systemic infections like MRSA bacteremia. IV metronidazole DOES reach the colon (hepatic excretion into bile), which is why it's used as IV adjunct in fulminant CDI.

choices.text: IV vancomycin causes red man syndrome only in GI infections
choices.feedback: Red man syndrome is related to rapid IV infusion, not route specificity for infections.
Cost is not the reason — it's pharmacokinetic necessity.

questions.explanation: Oral vancomycin is required for CDI because IV vancomycin is not secreted into the colonic lumen in therapeutic concentrations. CDI is an intraluminal infection — the toxins act on the colonic mucosa, so the antibiotic must reach adequate concentrations within the colon. Oral vancomycin (125-500 mg QID) achieves very high fecal concentrations with minimal systemic absorption. IV vancomycin is not inherently toxic to the colon. Red man syndrome is related to IV infusion rate, not route-specific to GI infections. Cost is not the primary consideration.

questions.stem: Most effective treatment for recurrent C. diff (≥2 episodes)?

choices.feedback: Prolonged IV metronidazole has neurotoxicity risk and poor colonic levels.
Probiotics have limited evidence for CDI treatment (may have role in prevention).
Extended courses help but recurrence rates remain high.

Correct! FMT restores normal colonic microbiome → competitive exclusion of C. diff. ~90% effective for recurrent CDI after ≥2 recurrences. Delivered via colonoscopy, enema, or oral capsules. Commercially available standardized microbiota products (Rebyota, Vowst) now available. Bezlotoxumab (anti-toxin B mAb) can also reduce recurrence.

questions.explanation: Fecal microbiota transplant (FMT) is the most effective treatment for recurrent CDI (≥2 episodes), with cure rates exceeding 90%. FMT restores normal colonic microbiome diversity, re-establishing colonization resistance against C. difficile. The 2023 commercially available FMT product (Vowst/SER-109) provides standardized oral administration. IV metronidazole alone is inadequate for recurrent CDI. Probiotics alone have insufficient evidence for CDI treatment. Extended/pulsed-tapered vancomycin is a second-line option for first recurrence but inferior to FMT for multiply recurrent CDI.

questions.stem: E. coli O157:H7 — why should antibiotics be AVOIDED?

choices.text: Antibiotics increase toxin release → higher risk of HUS
choices.feedback: Correct! Antibiotics lyse EHEC bacteria → massive Shiga toxin release → increased risk of HUS (hemolytic uremic syndrome: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia + AKI). HUS is the #1 cause of AKI in children. Triad of bloody diarrhea → HUS. Treatment: supportive (fluids, transfusion, dialysis if needed). Avoid anti-motility agents too.

choices.text: Antibiotics are ineffective against E. coli
choices.feedback: Antibiotics can kill EHEC but shouldn't be used for this specific strain.
The concern is specifically increased HUS risk from toxin release.

choices.text: E. coli O157:H7 is a virus
choices.feedback: It's a gram-negative bacterium.

questions.explanation: Antibiotics should be avoided in E. coli O157:H7 (enterohemorrhagic E. coli, EHEC) infections because antibiotic-induced bacterial lysis increases Shiga toxin release, significantly raising the risk of hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, acute kidney injury). HUS is the leading cause of acute renal failure in children. Treatment is supportive: IV fluids, monitoring renal function, and dialysis if needed. Antibiotics are effective against E. coli but cause net harm. E. coli is a bacterium, not a virus.

questions.stem: Hand hygiene for C. diff requires:

choices.feedback: C. diff requires strict contact precautions + hand washing.
Betadine is not the standard recommendation for C. diff hand hygiene.

Correct! C. diff spores are resistant to alcohol, quaternary ammonium, and most disinfectants. Mechanical removal via soap-and-water hand washing is required. Environmental cleaning with bleach-based (hypochlorite) solutions. Contact precautions: gown + gloves for all patient interactions.
Alcohol does NOT kill C. diff spores.

questions.explanation: C. difficile spores are resistant to alcohol-based hand sanitizers, making soap and water hand washing mandatory for effective spore removal through physical friction and rinsing. This is a critical infection control measure — standard alcohol gel protocols are insufficient for C. difficile. Contact precautions (gown and gloves) plus dedicated equipment are also required. Betadine scrub is used for surgical antisepsis, not routine hand hygiene. No special precautions would be a serious infection control failure for this spore-forming organism.

questions.stem: Campylobacter jejuni is important because it triggers:

choices.feedback: HUS is triggered by EHEC (E. coli O157:H7), not Campylobacter.
Liver abscess is associated with E. histolytica (amebic) or Klebsiella.

Correct! Campylobacter is the #1 trigger for GBS. Lipooligosaccharide on C. jejuni mimics gangliosides on peripheral nerves → autoimmune demyelination 1-4 weeks after GI illness. Campylobacter-associated GBS tends to be more severe (axonal variant). Also: reactive arthritis.
Toxic megacolon is associated with UC and C. diff, not Campylobacter.

questions.explanation: Campylobacter jejuni is the most commonly identified infectious trigger of Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS), an acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy causing ascending weakness and areflexia. Molecular mimicry between Campylobacter lipooligosaccharides and gangliosides on peripheral nerves drives the autoimmune attack. GBS typically develops 1-3 weeks after Campylobacter gastroenteritis. HUS is triggered by Shiga toxin-producing E. coli. Liver abscess is associated with Entamoeba histolytica. Toxic megacolon is associated with C. difficile and inflammatory bowel disease.

questions.stem: Which infectious diarrhea should ALWAYS be treated with antibiotics?

choices.feedback: NEVER give antibiotics for EHEC — increases HUS risk.

Correct! Shigella should always be treated: highly contagious (as few as 10-100 organisms), causes invasive bloody diarrhea, can cause seizures in children (Shiga toxin), prevents transmission. Treat with azithromycin or fluoroquinolone. Salmonella: treat only if severe, bacteremia, or immunocompromised (otherwise self-limited).
Rotavirus is viral — supportive care only. Vaccine prevention.
Norovirus is viral — antibiotics are ineffective. Self-limited.

questions.explanation: Shigella should always be treated with antibiotics regardless of severity because it is highly transmissible (very low infectious dose: 10-100 organisms), causes invasive bloody dysentery, and antibiotic treatment shortens symptom duration and reduces transmission. First-line options include fluoroquinolones or azithromycin. E. coli O157:H7 should NOT receive antibiotics (increases HUS risk). Rotavirus and norovirus are viral — antibiotics are ineffective. Other bacterial diarrheas (Salmonella, mild Campylobacter) are usually self-limited and do not always require antibiotics.

questions.stem: Best initial screening test for celiac disease?

choices.feedback: Fecal fat assesses malabsorption but is not specific for celiac.

Correct! tTG-IgA: ~95% sensitive, ~95% specific. Best screening test. MUST check total IgA simultaneously — IgA deficiency (2-3% of celiac patients) causes false-negative tTG-IgA. If IgA deficient: use IgG-based tests (tTG-IgG or deamidated gliadin peptide IgG). Patient must be eating gluten at time of testing.
Anti-EMA is highly specific but less sensitive and more expensive than tTG-IgA.
HLA testing has high NPV (rules out celiac if negative) but poor PPV (30% of general population positive). Not used for initial screening.

questions.explanation: Anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA (tTG-IgA) is the best initial screening test for celiac disease — sensitivity ~95%, specificity ~95%. Total IgA should be measured simultaneously because IgA deficiency (more common in celiac patients) causes false-negative tTG-IgA. If IgA deficient, use deamidated gliadin peptide (DGP) IgG or tTG-IgG instead. The patient must be on a gluten-containing diet at the time of testing.

questions.stem: Gold standard for celiac diagnosis?

choices.feedback: CT is not useful for celiac diagnosis.
Hydrogen breath test diagnoses lactose intolerance or SIBO, not celiac.

Correct! Biopsy shows villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and intraepithelial lymphocytosis (Marsh classification). Must biopsy WHILE patient is on gluten-containing diet. Some guidelines allow diagnosis without biopsy in children with very high tTG-IgA (>10× ULN) + positive EMA + symptoms.
Serology screens but biopsy confirms.

questions.explanation: Upper endoscopy with duodenal biopsy (at least 4-6 biopsies from the second portion of duodenum and 1-2 from the duodenal bulb) is the gold standard for celiac diagnosis. Histology shows villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes (Marsh classification). Biopsy should be performed while the patient is still consuming gluten. Serology-only diagnosis (without biopsy) may be acceptable in pediatric patients with very high tTG-IgA levels (>10× ULN).

questions.stem: Dermatitis herpetiformis is pathognomonic for celiac and shows:

choices.text: Granular IgA deposits at dermal papillae on uninvolved skin biopsy
choices.feedback: Correct! DH: intensely pruritic vesicles on extensor surfaces. Direct immunofluorescence of UNINVOLVED perilesional skin shows granular IgA at dermal papillae. Present even without GI symptoms (but most have villous atrophy on intestinal biopsy). Treatment: dapsone for rapid symptom relief + lifelong GFD.
EN = tender red nodules on shins, associated with IBD/sarcoidosis/infections.
Linear IgA = linear IgA bullous dermatosis (different condition, often drug-induced).
Anti-desmoglein = pemphigus vulgaris.

questions.explanation: Dermatitis herpetiformis (DH) is pathognomonic for celiac disease and shows granular IgA deposits at the tips of dermal papillae on direct immunofluorescence of uninvolved perilesional skin. Clinically, it presents as intensely pruritic, grouped vesicles on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees, buttocks, scalp). All patients with DH have underlying celiac enteropathy, even if asymptomatic. Treatment includes gluten-free diet and dapsone for symptom control.

questions.stem: IgA deficiency in celiac testing causes:

choices.feedback: Correct! IgA deficiency (prevalence 2-3% in celiac, 10-15× general population) → no IgA production → tTG-IgA, EMA-IgA are falsely negative. Always check total IgA. If deficient: use tTG-IgG or deamidated gliadin peptide (DGP) IgG.
IgA deficiency significantly impacts IgA-based serologic tests.
IgA deficiency doesn't affect HLA genotyping.
IgA deficiency causes FALSE NEGATIVE, not elevated.

questions.explanation: Selective IgA deficiency (~1:500 prevalence, 10-15× more common in celiac patients) causes false-negative tTG-IgA results because the test measures IgA-class antibodies. When total IgA is low or absent, IgG-based testing (DGP-IgG or tTG-IgG) must be used instead. This is why total IgA should always be checked alongside tTG-IgA screening.

questions.stem: PAS-positive macrophages on small bowel biopsy. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Celiac shows villous atrophy and intraepithelial lymphocytes, not PAS-positive macrophages.
Crohn has non-caseating granulomas, not PAS-positive macrophages.

Correct! Whipple disease (Tropheryma whipplei): classic triad of arthralgias + diarrhea/malabsorption + weight loss. PAS-positive macrophages on duodenal biopsy are pathognomonic (intracellular bacteria). Also: cardiac, neurologic, ocular involvement. Treatment: IV ceftriaxone → long-term TMP-SMX (1 year).
Tropical sprue has villous atrophy similar to celiac but no PAS-positive macrophages.

questions.explanation: PAS-positive macrophages (foamy macrophages filled with Tropheryma whipplei bacteria) on small bowel biopsy are diagnostic of Whipple disease. This rare systemic infection presents with chronic diarrhea, weight loss, arthralgias, and lymphadenopathy. CNS and cardiac involvement can occur. Treatment is prolonged antibiotics (initial IV ceftriaxone, then TMP-SMX for 1 year). PCR for T. whipplei confirms diagnosis.

questions.stem: Most common presentation of celiac in adults?

choices.feedback: Weight loss can occur but IDA is more common in adults.
DH is pathognomonic but present in only ~15-25% of celiac patients.

Correct! IDA is the #1 presentation in adults (proximal duodenal/jejunal involvement impairs iron absorption). Many adults have no/minimal GI symptoms ('silent celiac'). Other adult presentations: osteoporosis, unexplained transaminitis, infertility, peripheral neuropathy. Screen for celiac in unexplained IDA.
Classic childhood presentation. Adult presentation is more subtle.

questions.explanation: Iron deficiency anemia is the most common presentation of celiac disease in adults, often the sole finding. It results from impaired iron absorption in the proximal duodenum/jejunum, where celiac enteropathy is most severe. Other common adult presentations include osteoporosis, elevated transaminases, infertility, and dermatitis herpetiformis. Classic malabsorptive symptoms (diarrhea, steatorrhea, weight loss) are actually less common in adult celiac disease.

questions.stem: Terminal ileum resection/disease causes deficiency of:

choices.feedback: Iron and folate are absorbed in the duodenum/jejunum (proximal).
Zinc is absorbed in duodenum/jejunum.

Correct! Terminal ileum is the exclusive absorption site for B12 (intrinsic factor-B12 complex) and bile salts. Crohn disease or surgical resection → B12 deficiency (megaloblastic anemia, neurologic deficits) + bile salt malabsorption → diarrhea + fat malabsorption → fat-soluble vitamin deficiency (A, D, E, K).
Vitamin C is absorbed throughout the small bowel and colon.

questions.explanation: Terminal ileum resection or disease (Crohn disease) causes deficiency of vitamin B12 (absorbed exclusively in terminal ileum via intrinsic factor-B12 receptor complex) and bile salts (enterohepatic circulation disrupted, leading to bile salt malabsorption, steatorrhea, and fat-soluble vitamin deficiency). Bile salt malabsorption also causes secretory diarrhea in the colon. Folate is absorbed in the proximal jejunum and is not typically affected by terminal ileum disease.

questions.stem: SIBO (small intestinal bacterial overgrowth) is diagnosed by:

choices.feedback: Stool culture evaluates colonic pathogens, not small bowel overgrowth.
tTG-IgA screens for celiac disease, not SIBO.
Barium studies evaluate anatomy, not bacterial overgrowth.

choices.text: Hydrogen/methane breath test (or jejunal aspirate >10⁵ CFU/mL)
choices.feedback: Correct! Glucose or lactulose breath test: bacteria in small bowel ferment substrate → early rise in hydrogen/methane. Gold standard: jejunal aspirate culture (>10⁵ CFU/mL). Risk factors: anatomic (blind loops, strictures, post-surgical), motility disorders (diabetes, scleroderma), PPI use. Treatment: rifaximin (preferred) or ciprofloxacin + metronidazole.

questions.explanation: SIBO is diagnosed by hydrogen/methane breath testing (glucose or lactulose substrate) or, gold standard, jejunal aspirate culture showing >10⁵ CFU/mL. Breath testing is more practical and widely used. SIBO presents with bloating, diarrhea, malabsorption (especially B12 deficiency with folate excess — bacteria consume B12 and produce folate), and weight loss. Treatment is antibiotics (rifaximin is first-line).

questions.stem: What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Chiari I = cerebellar tonsils herniating ≥5mm below the foramen magnum. Classic presentation: occipital headache worsened by Valsalva maneuvers (cough, strain, laugh). Hand numbness suggests an associated syringomyelia (fluid-filled cavity in the spinal cord).
While posterior fossa tumors cause similar headaches, the MRI shows tonsillar herniation without a mass. The Valsalva-worsening pattern is classic for Chiari I.
Chiari II is nearly always associated with myelomeningocele and is diagnosed in infancy/prenatally. This 12-year-old has no spinal dysraphism.
Dandy-Walker involves cerebellar vermis hypoplasia and a large posterior fossa cyst, not tonsillar herniation.

questions.stem: What is the proposed embryologic basis for the small posterior fossa in Chiari I?

choices.feedback: Hydrocephalus can worsen Chiari I but is not the primary cause. The underlying problem is the small posterior fossa.
The cerebellum is normal-sized in Chiari I. The fossa is too small for it — it's a container problem, not a contents problem.

Correct! The posterior fossa is formed from occipital somites (paraxial mesoderm). If these somites contribute insufficient bone growth, the posterior fossa is too small for the cerebellum → the tonsils herniate through the foramen magnum. This is different from Chiari II, where the small fossa results from CSF diversion through the open spinal defect.
Neural tube closure defects cause spina bifida/anencephaly. Chiari I has a normally closed neural tube — the problem is the bony fossa, not the neural tube.

questions.explanation: Key detail: axial mesoderm.

questions.stem: Why does syringomyelia cause dissociated sensory loss?

choices.feedback: DRG are outside the spinal cord. The syrinx is an intramedullary (within the cord) lesion.
The corticospinal tract is lateral, not central. It's affected later as the syrinx expands, causing weakness — but the initial finding is the central sensory dissociation.
Dorsal columns carry proprioception and vibration, not pain/temperature. Dorsal column damage would cause loss of position sense, not the cape-like pain/temp loss seen here.

choices.text: Disrupts crossing spinothalamic fibers at the anterior commissure
choices.feedback: Correct! The syrinx expands from the central canal, first disrupting the anterior white commissure where spinothalamic (pain/temperature) fibers cross. Light touch is carried by both the dorsal columns AND the anterior spinothalamic tract, so it's relatively preserved. This creates the classic 'dissociated sensory loss' — lost pain/temp with preserved light touch.

questions.stem: What is the primary goal of posterior fossa decompression?

choices.feedback: While some surgeons do cauterize/shrink the tonsils, the primary goal is not tissue removal — it's restoring CSF dynamics.

choices.text: Restore normal CSF flow at the craniocervical junction
choices.feedback: Correct! The surgery enlarges the posterior fossa by removing bone (suboccipital craniectomy + C1 laminectomy) and often opening/patching the dura. This restores CSF flow around the foramen magnum, which reduces the pressure gradient driving syrinx formation. In most cases, the syrinx will decrease or stabilize without direct drainage.
Syrinx shunting is a secondary procedure reserved for cases where the syrinx doesn't improve after PF decompression. Direct drainage addresses the effect, not the cause.
Herniation is typically stable in Chiari I. The surgery addresses the obstructed CSF flow, which is what causes symptoms and syringomyelia.

questions.stem: What causes the brainstem symptoms in Chiari II?

choices.feedback: While hydrocephalus is common in Chiari II, the brainstem symptoms are directly from the herniation and kinking, not just ICP. However, ensuring VP shunt function is part of the workup.

choices.text: Infection from the MMC repair site
choices.feedback: Meningitis would cause fever, bulging fontanelle, and systemic signs. The progressive stridor and apnea pattern here is classic for Chiari II compression.
Syringobulbia can occur in Chiari II but is not the primary cause of acute brainstem symptoms in a neonate. The kinking and compression are the main issue.

choices.text: Brainstem compression and lower cranial nerve traction
choices.feedback: Correct! In Chiari II, the brainstem herniates along with the cerebellar vermis, creating a characteristic 'kink' at the cervicomedullary junction. This compresses the brainstem and stretches cranial nerves IX, X, XI, XII → stridor (vocal cord paralysis via CN X), apnea (respiratory center compression), dysphagia (CN IX, X). This is a neurosurgical emergency.

questions.stem: First-line imaging for suspected acute cholecystitis?

choices.feedback: Only 10-15% of gallstones are radiopaque. X-ray is insensitive.
MRCP evaluates bile ducts (choledocholithiasis), not primary cholecystitis.
CT is less sensitive than US for gallstones and cholecystitis.

Correct! RUQ US is first-line: fast, no radiation, detects gallstones (95% sensitivity), wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy sign. HIDA scan is gold standard if US is equivocal (non-visualization of GB = cystic duct obstruction).

questions.explanation: Right upper quadrant (RUQ) ultrasound is the first-line imaging study for suspected acute cholecystitis. Findings include gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening (>3 mm), pericholecystic fluid, gallbladder distension, and a sonographic Murphy sign (focal tenderness when the ultrasound probe is pressed directly over the gallbladder). Ultrasound has sensitivity of ~80-90% and specificity of ~80% for acute cholecystitis. If ultrasound is equivocal, HIDA scan (cholescintigraphy) is the most specific test — non-visualization of the gallbladder after 4 hours (with CCK or morphine augmentation) indicates cystic duct obstruction, with sensitivity and specificity both >95%.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Right upper quadrant (RUQ) ultrasound is the first-line imaging study for suspected acute cholecystitis. Findings include gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening (>3 mm), pericholecystic fluid, gallbladder distension, and a sonographic Murphy sign (focal tenderness when the ultrasound probe is pressed directly over the gallbladder). Ultrasound has sensitivity of ~80-90% and specificity of ~80% for acute cholecystitis. If ultrasound is equivocal, HIDA scan (cholescintigraphy) is the most specific test — non-visualization of the gallbladder after 4 hours (with CCK or morphine augmentation) indicates cystic duct obstruction, with sensitivity and specificity both >95%.

questions.stem: What distinguishes biliary colic from acute cholecystitis?

choices.feedback: Stone type doesn't differentiate the two clinically.
Both occur in similar age groups.
Both cause RUQ pain. Location alone doesn't differentiate.

Correct! Biliary colic: episodic RUQ pain <6h after fatty meal, self-resolves, no fever/Murphy sign. Cholecystitis: constant pain >6h, fever, positive Murphy sign, leukocytosis. Cholecystitis = inflammation, biliary colic = transient obstruction without inflammation.

questions.explanation: Biliary colic is self-limited RUQ pain lasting <6 hours (typically 30 minutes to a few hours) caused by transient cystic duct obstruction by a gallstone. It resolves when the stone disimpacts. Acute cholecystitis develops when the obstruction persists (>6 hours), leading to gallbladder wall inflammation, edema, and potential ischemia/necrosis. Distinguishing features of cholecystitis vs colic: duration >6 hours, fever, positive Murphy sign on exam, leukocytosis, and persistent/worsening pain. Biliary colic patients are afebrile with normal labs and pain that resolves. Both may show gallstones on ultrasound, but wall thickening and pericholecystic fluid favor cholecystitis.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Biliary colic is self-limited RUQ pain lasting <6 hours (typically 30 minutes to a few hours) caused by transient cystic duct obstruction by a gallstone. It resolves when the stone disimpacts. Acute cholecystitis develops when the obstruction persists (>6 hours), leading to gallbladder wall inflammation, edema, and potential ischemia/necrosis. Distinguishing features of cholecystitis vs colic: duration >6 hours, fever, positive Murphy sign on exam, leukocytosis, and persistent/worsening pain. Biliary colic patients are afebrile with normal labs and pain that resolves. Both may show gallstones on ultrasound, but wall thickening and pericholecystic fluid favor cholecystitis.

questions.stem: Acalculous cholecystitis — classic setting?

choices.feedback: That's classic for symptomatic cholelithiasis, not acalculous.
Post-ERCP pancreatitis is more common than post-ERCP acalculous cholecystitis.
Pregnancy increases cholesterol stone risk, not acalculous cholecystitis.

Correct! Acalculous cholecystitis: GB inflammation without stones in critically ill patients. Mechanism: biliary stasis + GB ischemia from hypoperfusion. Mortality 30-50%. Treatment: percutaneous cholecystostomy (temporizing — many are too sick for OR). HIDA scan best diagnostic test.

questions.explanation: Acalculous cholecystitis occurs in critically ill ICU patients (5-10% of all cholecystitis cases) — typically those with prolonged hospitalization, sepsis, total parenteral nutrition, mechanical ventilation, or major burns/trauma. Without gallstones, the mechanism involves gallbladder stasis and ischemia from splanchnic hypoperfusion. Diagnosis is challenging because ICU patients cannot report symptoms reliably. Ultrasound findings include gallbladder distension, wall thickening, and pericholecystic fluid without stones. Acalculous cholecystitis carries higher mortality than calculous cholecystitis (~30-50% vs ~1-3%) due to the underlying critical illness and delayed diagnosis. Treatment is percutaneous cholecystostomy or cholecystectomy.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Acalculous cholecystitis occurs in critically ill ICU patients (5-10% of all cholecystitis cases) — typically those with prolonged hospitalization, sepsis, total parenteral nutrition, mechanical ventilation, or major burns/trauma. Without gallstones, the mechanism involves gallbladder stasis and ischemia from splanchnic hypoperfusion. Diagnosis is challenging because ICU patients cannot report symptoms reliably. Ultrasound findings include gallbladder distension, wall thickening, and pericholecystic fluid without stones. Acalculous cholecystitis carries higher mortality than calculous cholecystitis (~30-50% vs ~1-3%) due to the underlying critical illness and delayed diagnosis. Treatment is percutaneous cholecystostomy or cholecystectomy.

questions.stem: Charcot triad for cholangitis:

choices.feedback: Correct! Charcot triad = cholangitis (infected bile duct, usually from CBD stone). RUQ pain + fever + jaundice. Present in ~70% of cases. Reynolds pentad adds AMS + hypotension (septic cholangitis → emergent ERCP for biliary drainage). Do not delay decompression.
This describes meningitis.
This describes pulmonary embolism (Virchow triad concepts).
These are nonspecific GI symptoms.

questions.explanation: Charcot's triad for ascending cholangitis consists of: RUQ pain, fever, and jaundice. This represents infection of the biliary system, most commonly from choledocholithiasis (common bile duct stone) causing obstruction and subsequent bacterial overgrowth. Charcot's triad is present in only about 50-70% of cholangitis cases. The most common causative organisms are E. coli, Klebsiella, and Enterococcus. Treatment requires antibiotics plus biliary decompression (ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction is preferred). Labs show elevated bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, GGT, and often transaminases.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Charcot's triad for ascending cholangitis consists of: RUQ pain, fever, and jaundice. This represents infection of the biliary system, most commonly from choledocholithiasis (common bile duct stone) causing obstruction and subsequent bacterial overgrowth. Charcot's triad is present in only about 50-70% of cholangitis cases. The most common causative organisms are E. coli, Klebsiella, and Enterococcus. Treatment requires antibiotics plus biliary decompression (ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction is preferred). Labs show elevated bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, GGT, and often transaminases.

questions.stem: Gallstone ileus — classic imaging triad?

choices.feedback: This describes biliary obstruction with decompensation, not gallstone ileus.
Coffee-bean = sigmoid volvulus.

choices.text: SBO + pneumobilia (air in biliary tree) + ectopic gallstone
choices.feedback: Correct! Rigler triad: SBO (dilated small bowel) + pneumobilia (air in biliary tree from cholecystoenteric fistula) + ectopic gallstone (usually impacted at ileocecal valve — narrowest part of small bowel). Gallstone erodes through GB wall into duodenum → travels distally → impacts. Rare but classic.
This suggests bowel ischemia with perforation.

questions.explanation: Gallstone ileus is a mechanical small bowel obstruction caused by a gallstone that has eroded through the gallbladder wall into the adjacent duodenum (cholecystoduodenal fistula) and traveled distally to obstruct the bowel, most commonly at the ileocecal valve (the narrowest point). The classic Rigler's triad on imaging includes: small bowel obstruction, pneumobilia (air in the biliary tree, from the fistula), and an ectopic gallstone (calcified density in the bowel). This triad is pathognomonic but present in only ~40-50% of cases. Gallstone ileus is rare (~1-4% of SBO cases) but carries high mortality due to the elderly population it typically affects.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Gallstone ileus is a mechanical small bowel obstruction caused by a gallstone that has eroded through the gallbladder wall into the adjacent duodenum (cholecystoduodenal fistula) and traveled distally to obstruct the bowel, most commonly at the ileocecal valve (the narrowest point). The classic Rigler's triad on imaging includes: small bowel obstruction, pneumobilia (air in the biliary tree, from the fistula), and an ectopic gallstone (calcified density in the bowel). This triad is pathognomonic but present in only ~40-50% of cases. Gallstone ileus is rare (~1-4% of SBO cases) but carries high mortality due to the elderly population it typically affects.

questions.stem: Emphysematous cholecystitis — key association?

choices.feedback: Emphysematous cholecystitis is most common in diabetic males.
While immunosuppression increases risk, diabetes is the classic association.
PPIs are not associated with emphysematous cholecystitis.

Correct! Emphysematous cholecystitis: gas-producing organisms (Clostridium, E. coli) infect an ischemic GB wall. Risk: diabetes, male sex, elderly. Imaging: air in GB wall/lumen. 15-20% perforation rate. Treatment: EMERGENT cholecystectomy (high mortality without surgery).

questions.explanation: Emphysematous cholecystitis — gas-forming infection of the gallbladder wall — is classically associated with diabetic men. It is caused by gas-producing organisms (Clostridium spp., E. coli, Klebsiella) and represents a surgical emergency with high perforation and mortality risk (~15-20%). CT is the best imaging modality, showing gas within the gallbladder wall or lumen. Unlike typical cholecystitis, emphysematous cholecystitis requires emergent cholecystectomy (not elective or interval surgery) plus broad-spectrum antibiotics. Acalculous disease is more common in emphysematous cholecystitis than in typical cholecystitis.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Emphysematous cholecystitis — gas-forming infection of the gallbladder wall — is classically associated with diabetic men. It is caused by gas-producing organisms (Clostridium spp., E. coli, Klebsiella) and represents a surgical emergency with high perforation and mortality risk (~15-20%). CT is the best imaging modality, showing gas within the gallbladder wall or lumen. Unlike typical cholecystitis, emphysematous cholecystitis requires emergent cholecystectomy (not elective or interval surgery) plus broad-spectrum antibiotics. Acalculous disease is more common in emphysematous cholecystitis than in typical cholecystitis.

questions.stem: Reynolds pentad adds which TWO features to Charcot triad?

choices.feedback: While present, these are not the defining additions to Reynolds pentad.

Correct! Reynolds pentad = Charcot triad (RUQ pain + fever + jaundice) + AMS + hypotension. Indicates septic cholangitis — biliary sepsis. This is an emergency requiring immediate ERCP for biliary decompression. Mortality is high without drainage.
GI symptoms don't define Reynolds pentad.
These suggest chronic liver disease, not acute cholangitis.

questions.explanation: Reynolds' pentad adds altered mental status and hypotension (septic shock) to Charcot's triad (RUQ pain, fever, jaundice), indicating severe suppurative cholangitis with sepsis. Reynolds' pentad is present in only about 5-10% of cholangitis cases but signifies life-threatening biliary sepsis requiring emergent biliary decompression (ERCP). These patients are hemodynamically unstable and require aggressive resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and urgent source control. Delayed biliary drainage in Reynolds' pentad carries high mortality.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Reynolds' pentad adds altered mental status and hypotension (septic shock) to Charcot's triad (RUQ pain, fever, jaundice), indicating severe suppurative cholangitis with sepsis. Reynolds' pentad is present in only about 5-10% of cholangitis cases but signifies life-threatening biliary sepsis requiring emergent biliary decompression (ERCP). These patients are hemodynamically unstable and require aggressive resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and urgent source control. Delayed biliary drainage in Reynolds' pentad carries high mortality.

questions.stem: Timing of cholecystectomy in acute cholecystitis?

choices.text: Only if antibiotics fail after 2 weeks
choices.feedback: Surgery is first-line, not a fallback after antibiotic failure.

Correct! Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy (within 72h) is preferred over delayed. Benefits: shorter total hospital stay, lower morbidity, lower conversion to open rate, cost-effective. Antibiotics + early surgery is standard of care.
Cholecystectomy is definitive. Antibiotics alone have high recurrence rates.

choices.text: Delay 6-8 weeks for inflammation to resolve
choices.feedback: Studies show early surgery is superior — delayed surgery has higher complications.

questions.explanation: Early cholecystectomy (within 72 hours, ideally within 24 hours of diagnosis) is recommended for acute cholecystitis per current guidelines. Early surgery has equivalent safety to delayed surgery, shorter total hospital stay, fewer readmissions, and lower overall cost. Delaying surgery beyond 72 hours increases technical difficulty due to progressive inflammation and fibrosis. Laparoscopic approach is preferred. For patients who are poor surgical candidates (severe comorbidities), percutaneous cholecystostomy tube provides temporizing drainage. The previous practice of 'cooling off' with antibiotics and interval cholecystectomy at 6-8 weeks is no longer recommended as first-line.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Early cholecystectomy (within 72 hours, ideally within 24 hours of diagnosis) is recommended for acute cholecystitis per current guidelines. Early surgery has equivalent safety to delayed surgery, shorter total hospital stay, fewer readmissions, and lower overall cost. Delaying surgery beyond 72 hours increases technical difficulty due to progressive inflammation and fibrosis. Laparoscopic approach is preferred. For patients who are poor surgical candidates (severe comorbidities), percutaneous cholecystostomy tube provides temporizing drainage. The previous practice of 'cooling off' with antibiotics and interval cholecystectomy at 6-8 weeks is no longer recommended as first-line.

questions.stem: AST:ALT ratio >2:1 suggests:

choices.feedback: Correct! AST:ALT >2:1 is characteristic of alcoholic hepatitis. Alcohol damages mitochondria (where AST is concentrated) + depletes pyridoxal phosphate (cofactor for ALT synthesis). ALT rarely exceeds 300 in alcoholic hepatitis. Elevated GGT supports alcohol etiology.
Viral hepatitis typically has ALT > AST (ratio <1).
MASLD typically has ALT > AST (ratio <1) until cirrhosis develops.
Acetaminophen causes massive transaminase elevation (often >1000) with AST≈ALT.

questions.explanation: AST:ALT ratio >2:1 is characteristic of alcoholic liver disease. Alcohol causes mitochondrial damage, which preferentially releases AST (mitochondrial isoform). Also, alcohol depletes pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (cofactor for ALT synthesis), further reducing ALT. In most other liver diseases, ALT > AST. An AST:ALT ratio >2:1 has ~90% specificity for alcoholic hepatitis.

questions.stem: SAAG ≥1.1 g/dL indicates:

choices.feedback: Malignant ascites has SAAG <1.1 (exudative).

Correct! Serum-Ascites Albumin Gradient ≥1.1 = portal HTN (cirrhosis, heart failure, Budd-Chiari). SAAG <1.1 = non-portal HTN (peritoneal carcinomatosis, TB peritonitis, nephrotic syndrome, pancreatitis). SAAG is superior to the old transudate/exudate classification for ascites.
TB peritonitis has SAAG <1.1 with high protein and lymphocyte predominance.
Nephrotic ascites has SAAG <1.1.

questions.explanation: Serum-ascites albumin gradient (SAAG) ≥1.1 g/dL indicates ascites from portal hypertension (cirrhosis, heart failure, Budd-Chiari). SAAG <1.1 suggests non-portal hypertensive causes (peritoneal carcinomatosis, TB peritonitis, nephrotic syndrome, pancreatitis). SAAG is more accurate than the older exudate/transudate classification for ascites.

questions.stem: Diagnosis of SBP requires:

choices.feedback: Correct! PMN >250 = SBP. Start empiric antibiotics immediately (ceftriaxone/cefotaxime) — don't wait for culture. Also give IV albumin on days 1 and 3 (weight-based dosing day 1, weight-based dosing day 3) to prevent hepatorenal syndrome. Most common organism: E. coli.

choices.text: Blood cultures positive for gram-negative rods
choices.feedback: Blood cultures support but don't define SBP.
CT is not used to diagnose SBP.
Culture is positive in only ~60% of SBP. PMN count is diagnostic.

questions.explanation: SBP is diagnosed when ascitic fluid absolute PMN (polymorphonuclear neutrophil) count >250 cells/mm³, regardless of culture results (culture-negative SBP is common). Treatment with empiric IV cefotaxime (or ceftriaxone) should begin immediately without waiting for culture. IV albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1, 1 g/kg day 3) reduces hepatorenal syndrome risk. SBP prophylaxis with daily norfloxacin or TMP-SMX is indicated in high-risk cirrhotics.

questions.stem: First-line treatment for hepatic encephalopathy?

choices.feedback: Neomycin (oral antibiotic) was historically used but carries ototoxicity/nephrotoxicity risk. Not first-line.

Correct! Lactulose is first-line: osmotic laxative metabolized by colonic bacteria → produces acidic environment → converts NH3 (absorbed) to NH4+ (trapped, excreted). Titrate to 2-3 soft stools/day. Add rifaximin for recurrent episodes (reduces ammonia-producing bacteria). Always identify and treat the precipitant.
IV sodium benzoate/phenylacetate are used in urea cycle defects, not routine HE.
Flumazenil (BZD antagonist) is for BZD overdose, not HE.

questions.explanation: Lactulose is the first-line treatment for hepatic encephalopathy (HE). It is a non-absorbable disaccharide that acts as an osmotic laxative (reducing ammonia transit time) and is metabolized by colonic bacteria to lactic/acetic acid, acidifying the colonic lumen and converting absorbable NH3 to non-absorbable NH4+. Target 2-3 soft stools per day. Rifaximin is added for maintenance/prevention of recurrent HE.

questions.stem: Hepatorenal syndrome is characterized by:

choices.feedback: Correct! HRS = intense renal vasoconstriction from splanchnic vasodilation in portal HTN. Kidneys are structurally normal (would work if transplanted). FENa <1%, bland sediment, no improvement with volume (distinguishes from prerenal). Type 1: rapidly progressive (doubling of Cr to >2.5 in <2 weeks). Definitive treatment: liver transplant.
RAS is a separate entity from HRS.
ATN has FENa >2% and muddy brown casts. HRS has bland sediment.
Hep B can cause membranous nephropathy but this is different from HRS.

questions.explanation: Hepatorenal syndrome (HRS) is characterized by functional renal failure (no structural kidney damage) with FeNa <1%, bland urinalysis, and oliguria in the setting of advanced cirrhosis with portal hypertension. It results from extreme splanchnic vasodilation causing renal vasoconstriction. Type 1 HRS (rapidly progressive, doubling of creatinine within 2 weeks) is most severe. Treatment includes vasoconstrictors (terlipressin, midodrine + octreotide) with albumin. Definitive treatment is liver transplantation.

questions.stem: HCC screening in cirrhotics:

choices.feedback: Colonoscopy screens for colorectal cancer, not HCC.
Biopsy is not a screening tool.
CT is not used for screening due to cost and radiation.

Correct! All cirrhotics need surveillance: abdominal US ± AFP every 6 months. Early detection allows curative treatment (resection, ablation, transplant within Milan criteria). AFP >400 in a cirrhotic strongly suggests HCC. Confirmed with multiphase CT or MRI showing arterial enhancement with portal venous washout.

questions.explanation: HCC screening in cirrhotics involves ultrasound with or without alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) every 6 months. This applies to all patients with cirrhosis regardless of etiology, and HBV-infected patients even without cirrhosis (due to direct oncogenic mechanism). Surveillance detects HCC at earlier stages amenable to curative treatment (ablation, resection, transplantation). CT/MRI is used for diagnostic characterization when screening identifies a lesion.

questions.stem: MELD score components include:

choices.feedback: Blood counts are not MELD components.

Correct! MELD uses bilirubin (liver synthetic function), creatinine (renal function — reflects HRS risk), and INR (coagulation). Predicts 3-month mortality. Used for transplant allocation. MELD-Na adds sodium (hyponatremia worsens prognosis). Higher MELD = higher priority on transplant list.
Transaminases are not part of the MELD score.
That describes the Child-Pugh score, not MELD.

questions.explanation: The MELD (Model for End-Stage Liver Disease) score components are serum bilirubin, creatinine, and INR — all objective lab values. MELD predicts 90-day mortality in cirrhosis and is used to prioritize liver transplant allocation (higher MELD = higher priority). MELD-Na incorporates serum sodium (hyponatremia worsens prognosis). MELD does not include albumin, ascites, or encephalopathy (those are Child-Pugh score components).

questions.stem: Most common primary liver malignancy?

choices.feedback: Correct! HCC is the most common primary liver cancer. Risk factors: cirrhosis (any cause), chronic HBV (can develop HCC even without cirrhosis), aflatoxin B1 (Aspergillus on grains/peanuts). Fibrolamellar variant: young patients WITHOUT cirrhosis, better prognosis.
CCA is the second most common primary liver cancer.
Hemangioma is the most common BENIGN liver tumor.
Adenoma is benign, associated with OCP use.

questions.explanation: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is the most common primary liver malignancy worldwide, accounting for ~75-85% of primary liver cancers. Major risk factors include cirrhosis from any cause, chronic HBV, chronic HCV, alcoholic liver disease, and NAFLD/NASH. HCC presents with weight loss, RUQ pain, elevated AFP, and arterially enhancing liver mass on imaging. Cholangiocarcinoma is the second most common primary liver malignancy.

questions.stem: What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Clubfoot (CTEV) = rigid foot deformity with 4 components remembered by CAVE: Cavus (high arch), Adductus (forefoot turned medially), Varus (heel inverted), Equinus (foot pointed down). The key word is RIGID — it doesn't correct with manipulation. This distinguishes true clubfoot from positional deformity.
Calcaneovalgus is the OPPOSITE of clubfoot — the foot is dorsiflexed (not equinus) and everted (not varus). It's usually positional and benign.
Vertical talus (congenital convex pes valgus) has a rocker-bottom appearance with the sole convex. It's much rarer than clubfoot and is associated with chromosomal abnormalities.
Metatarsus adductus involves only the forefoot (medial deviation) with a normal hindfoot. It's usually flexible and self-correcting. Clubfoot involves the entire foot with hindfoot deformity.

questions.explanation: Correct: Congenital talipes equinovarus (clubfoot).

questions.stem: Which structure controls anterior-posterior patterning of the limb (e.g., thumb vs pinky)?

choices.feedback: AER controls PROXIMAL-DISTAL growth (shoulder → fingers). It produces FGFs to maintain the underlying mesenchyme (progress zone) proliferating.
Somites (specifically myotome) provide the muscle precursors that migrate into the limb bud, but they don't pattern the A-P axis.

Correct! The ZPA in the posterior limb bud mesenchyme produces Sonic Hedgehog (SHH), which creates an anterior-posterior concentration gradient. High SHH = posterior structures (pinky side). Low SHH = anterior structures (thumb side). Duplicating the ZPA → mirror-image polydactyly (extra digits on the radial/preaxial side).
Lateral plate mesoderm contributes to bone and connective tissue of the limb but doesn't control A-P patterning.

questions.explanation: Correct: Zone of Polarizing Activity (ZPA).

questions.stem: Why is equinus corrected LAST in the Ponseti sequence?

choices.feedback: Equinus does not resolve spontaneously. It requires active correction via Achilles tenotomy as the final step of the Ponseti method.

choices.text: Correcting equinus before varus creates a rocker
choices.feedback: Correct! If you try to dorsiflex (correct equinus) while the heel is still in varus, the force goes through the midfoot instead of the ankle → iatrogenic rocker-bottom deformity. The cavus, adductus, and varus must be corrected first so that the calcaneus is properly aligned. Then the Achilles tenotomy can safely correct the equinus.

choices.text: The Achilles tendon needs time to stretch
choices.feedback: The equinus is typically corrected with a percutaneous Achilles tenotomy, not gradual stretching. The tenotomy is done after the other components are corrected.
Equinus is actually the most resistant component. It's corrected last for anatomical reasons, not because it's mild.

questions.explanation: Key detail: bottom foot.

questions.stem: How does the Achilles tendon heal after tenotomy?

choices.text: Permanent tendon discontinuity compensated by other muscles
choices.feedback: The tendon DOES heal. Full continuity is restored. Infants have remarkable tendon regeneration capacity.

choices.text: Scar tissue formation creating a rigid bridge
choices.feedback: The regenerated tissue is functional tendon, not rigid scar. Histologically, it remodels into organized collagen with normal tensile properties.

choices.text: Regeneration at a lengthened position over 3 weeks
choices.feedback: Correct! After tenotomy, the tendon ends separate by ~1cm. The gap fills with granulation tissue that matures into functional tendon over 3 weeks (while in the cast). The tendon regenerates at its new, lengthened position, providing the additional dorsiflexion needed. Ultrasound studies confirm full tendon continuity by 6 weeks.

choices.text: It doesn't heal — that's why bracing is lifelong
choices.feedback: The bracing is to prevent RELAPSE of the clubfoot deformity, not because the tendon failed to heal. The tendon heals fully.

questions.stem: What is the relapse rate if bracing is not followed?

choices.feedback: Correct! Without bracing compliance, relapse rates reach 60-80%. WITH proper bracing, relapse drops to ~10-15%. This is the most critical counseling point for parents — the casts correct the foot, but the brace MAINTAINS the correction during the growth years when the foot would otherwise revert.
Without bracing, relapse is the norm, not the exception. The correction is maintained by the brace during the critical growth period.
Higher than this. Non-compliance is the leading cause of relapse.
Not 100%, but the majority (60-80%) do relapse. Some mild cases may hold correction, but it's unpredictable.

questions.stem: What does the 'sunset sign' indicate?

choices.feedback: Optic nerve compression would cause vision loss, not eye position changes. The sunset sign is from midbrain compression affecting vertical gaze centers.

choices.text: Parinaud syndrome from pressure on the tectal plate
choices.feedback: Correct! The sunset sign (eyes deviated downward, upper sclera visible above the iris) results from pressure on the tectal plate/superior colliculus by the dilated third ventricle or suprapineal recess. This causes paralysis of upward gaze (Parinaud syndrome). It's one of the most recognizable signs of increased intracranial pressure in infants.
CN VI palsy causes failure of abduction (can't look laterally), not downward deviation. CN VI palsy can occur with raised ICP but produces a different finding.
Retinal detachment doesn't cause the sunset sign. The finding is from midbrain compression, not a retinal problem.

questions.stem: What type of hydrocephalus is this?

choices.feedback: Communicating hydrocephalus has ALL ventricles dilated (including 4th) because CSF flows freely through the system but isn't absorbed properly. The normal-sized 4th ventricle here indicates a blockage upstream.
NPH is an adult condition (Hakim triad: gait disturbance, dementia, urinary incontinence). This is an infant with clearly elevated ICP.
External hydrocephalus (benign enlargement of subarachnoid spaces) has normal-sized ventricles with enlarged extra-axial spaces. This infant has massive ventricular dilation.

Correct! The dilated lateral + 3rd ventricles with a NORMAL 4th ventricle = obstruction at the cerebral aqueduct (aqueductal stenosis). This is NON-COMMUNICATING (obstructive) because CSF cannot flow freely through the ventricular system. Aqueductal stenosis is the most common cause of congenital hydrocephalus.

questions.explanation: Key detail: communicating) hydrocephalus.

questions.stem: What is the significance of the L1CAM mutation?

choices.feedback: L1CAM is a cell adhesion molecule gene, not a tumor suppressor. Posterior fossa tumors are a different cause of hydrocephalus.
While aqueductal webs exist, L1CAM mutations cause X-linked recessive hydrocephalus through abnormal neural development, not a structural web.
