Correct! L1CAM mutations cause X-linked hydrocephalus with stenosis of the aqueduct of Sylvius (HSAS). L1CAM is a neural cell adhesion molecule critical for axon guidance and neuronal migration. The aqueduct is narrowed due to abnormal development of the surrounding neural tissue. HSAS also includes spastic paraplegia, adducted thumbs, and aphasia (mnemonic: CRASH — Corpus callosum hypoplasia, Retardation, Adducted thumbs, Spastic paraplegia, Hydrocephalus).
L1CAM is on the X chromosome (Xq28), making it X-linked recessive. Affected males inherit from carrier mothers. It's not mitochondrial.

questions.stem: What is the most common complication of VP shunts?

choices.feedback: Infection is the SECOND most common complication (5-10% per procedure). It typically occurs within 6 months and is most commonly caused by Staph epidermidis. But malfunction is more common overall.

Correct! Shunt malfunction/obstruction is the most common complication, occurring in 30-40% of shunts within the first year. Proximal obstruction (choroid plexus or brain tissue blocking the ventricular catheter) is more common than distal obstruction (peritoneal catheter). Signs: recurrence of hydrocephalus symptoms (bulging fontanelle, sunset sign, irritability, vomiting).
Pseudocysts occur but are less common than obstruction. They may indicate low-grade infection.
Over-drainage causing slit ventricles is a recognized complication but less common than obstruction. Programmable valves help prevent this.

questions.stem: Which patients are the best candidates for ETV?

choices.text: All hydrocephalus patients regardless of type
choices.feedback: ETV is not appropriate for all types. Communicating hydrocephalus and very young infants do better with VP shunts.

Post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus is COMMUNICATING (impaired absorption from blood products), so bypassing the aqueduct with ETV doesn't address the underlying problem. ETV works best for obstructive hydrocephalus.

Correct! ETV works best for OBSTRUCTIVE (non-communicating) hydrocephalus in children >6-12 months. Success rate: ~70-80% in children >1 year with aqueductal stenosis. ETV is LESS effective in infants <6 months (higher failure rate due to immature CSF absorption pathways) and in communicating hydrocephalus (the problem isn't at the aqueduct).
NPH is communicating hydrocephalus. ETV has limited efficacy for communicating types. NPH typically responds to large-volume lumbar puncture and LP shunting.

questions.stem: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Pneumothorax shows absent lung markings in the affected hemithorax. CLE shows hyperinflated lung WITH bronchovascular markings preserved.
CDH shows bowel loops in the chest, typically left-sided. CLE shows hyperinflated LUNG tissue with bronchovascular markings.
CPAM typically shows cystic changes, not diffuse lobar hyperinflation. CLE has overinflated but structurally normal alveoli.

Correct! CLE (also called congenital lobar overinflation) = progressive hyperinflation of a pulmonary lobe. Most common in LUL (43%), then RML (32%), then RUL (21%). Air enters on inspiration but traps on expiration (ball-valve mechanism). Causes: deficient bronchial cartilage (50%), external compression, idiopathic. More common in males.

questions.stem: During which stage of lung development does branching morphogenesis (airway formation) primarily occur?

choices.feedback: The canalicular stage involves vascularization and beginning of respiratory bronchiole/primitive alveoli formation. Conducting airways are already complete.

Correct! The pseudoglandular stage (weeks 5-17) completes all conducting airway branching (through terminal bronchioles). Called 'pseudoglandular' because the histology resembles a gland. By week 17, the airway tree is complete — no new airways form after this. The lung looks like a gland with no gas exchange capability yet.
The embryonic stage involves initial lung bud formation from the foregut, not the detailed branching of airways.
The saccular stage forms terminal sacs that will become alveoli. Airways are already fully formed by this point.

questions.stem: What feature distinguishes bronchopulmonary sequestration from CPAM?

choices.text: Sequestration has a systemic arterial blood supply (from the aorta)
choices.feedback: Correct! Sequestration = non-functioning lung tissue with a SYSTEMIC arterial supply (usually from the thoracic or abdominal aorta), NOT connected to the normal tracheobronchial tree. Intralobar (75%): within visceral pleura, drains via pulmonary veins — presents with recurrent pneumonia. Extralobar (25%): own pleural covering, systemic venous drainage — often found incidentally or with CDH.

choices.text: Sequestration always occurs on the right side
choices.feedback: Both CPAM and sequestration can occur on either side, though sequestrations are more common on the left lower lobe. Laterality alone doesn't distinguish them.

choices.text: CPAM has malignant potential but sequestration does not
choices.feedback: Both can have malignant potential (CPAM → pleuropulmonary blastoma; sequestration → rarely). But the defining feature of sequestration is the systemic blood supply.

choices.text: Sequestration communicates with the airway; CPAM does not
choices.feedback: It's the opposite — sequestration typically does NOT communicate with the tracheobronchial tree (that's what makes it 'sequestered').

questions.stem: At what gestational age is surfactant first produced, and when is it adequate for extrauterine life?

choices.text: First at 20 weeks, adequate at 35-36 weeks
choices.feedback: Correct! Type II pneumocytes first produce surfactant at ~20 weeks (canalicular stage), but adequate levels for extrauterine viability aren't reached until 35-36 weeks. Lecithin/sphingomyelin (L/S) ratio ≥2 and presence of phosphatidylglycerol (PG) indicate lung maturity. Antenatal steroids (betamethasone) accelerate surfactant production if preterm delivery is anticipated.

choices.text: First at 12 weeks, adequate at 24 weeks
choices.feedback: 12 weeks is during the pseudoglandular stage — Type II cells haven't yet differentiated. Surfactant begins at the canalicular stage (~20 weeks).

choices.text: First at 28 weeks, adequate at 36 weeks
choices.feedback: Surfactant production begins much earlier than 28 weeks, though it doesn't reach adequate levels until later.

choices.text: First at 35 weeks, adequate at birth
choices.feedback: Surfactant begins much earlier (20 weeks). The canalicular stage is when Type II cells differentiate.

questions.stem: The trachea and esophagus both develop from which embryological structure?

choices.feedback: Mesoderm forms the muscular wall and cartilage, but the epithelial lining of both structures is endoderm from the foregut.

Correct! Both the trachea and esophagus arise from the foregut. During week 4, the respiratory diverticulum (lung bud) sprouts from the ventral foregut. The tracheoesophageal septum grows caudal-to-cranial to separate the ventral trachea from the dorsal esophagus. Failure of this septum → tracheoesophageal fistula (TEF). The most common type (85%) = esophageal atresia with distal TEF.
Neural crest contributes cartilage and connective tissue to the trachea, but the epithelial lining (the tube itself) is endoderm from the foregut.
The notochord induces the neural plate and floor plate, not the respiratory or GI tract epithelium.

questions.stem: Most effective reversible contraception?

choices.feedback: OCPs have 7% typical-use failure rate. LARCs are superior.
DMPA has 4-6% typical-use failure rate.

Correct! Long-acting reversible contraceptives: subdermal implant (<0.1% failure) and IUDs (copper <1%, LNG-IUD <1%). 'Set and forget' — no adherence issues. ACOG recommends as first-line for all women including nulliparous and adolescents.
Condoms have ~13% typical-use failure. Important for STI prevention but less effective for pregnancy prevention alone.

questions.explanation: Long-acting reversible contraceptives (LARCs) — IUDs (hormonal and copper) and the etonogestrel subdermal implant — are the most effective reversible methods with <1% failure rate, equivalent to sterilization. Their efficacy is user-independent (no daily adherence required). Combined OCPs have a typical-use failure rate of ~7%. DMPA injection has ~4% typical-use failure. Male condoms have ~13% typical-use failure. ACOG recommends LARCs as first-line contraception for most women.

questions.stem: Estrogen-containing contraceptives are CONTRAINDICATED in migraine with aura because:

choices.feedback: The risk is inherent to the combination of aura + estrogen, not a drug interaction.

choices.text: Estrogen causes seizures in migraine patients
choices.feedback: Estrogen does not cause seizures.

Correct! Migraine with aura is an independent stroke risk factor. Adding estrogen (which increases thrombotic risk) creates unacceptable stroke risk. Migraine WITHOUT aura = combined contraceptives are acceptable. Migraine WITH aura = progestin-only methods only. This is one of the most commonly tested contraindications.
The concern is not headache frequency.

questions.explanation: Estrogen-containing contraceptives (combined OCPs, patch, ring) are WHO Category 4 contraindicated in migraine with aura due to a 2-4 fold increased risk of ischemic stroke. Migraine with aura is an independent stroke risk factor, and estrogen further increases thrombotic risk through prothrombotic effects on coagulation factors. Progestin-only methods (POP, DMPA, implant, hormonal IUD) are safe alternatives. Aura medications do not specifically interact with estrogen. Estrogen does not cause seizures. While estrogen can affect headache patterns, stroke risk is the critical contraindication.

questions.stem: Most effective emergency contraception?

choices.feedback: Correct! Copper IUD is >99% effective as emergency contraception when placed within 5 days of unprotected intercourse. Also serves as ongoing contraception for 10-12 years. Ulipristal (Ella) is second-most effective (~98%, up to 5 days). Plan B is least effective of the three options.
Yuzpe method is older and less effective with more side effects.
Mifepristone is used for medical abortion, not standard emergency contraception in the US.
Plan B is ~89% effective and decreases with time and weight.

questions.explanation: The copper IUD is the most effective emergency contraception, with >99% efficacy when placed within 5 days of unprotected intercourse. It prevents fertilization through copper ion toxicity to sperm and also has post-fertilization effects. Additionally, it provides ongoing contraception for up to 10-12 years. The Yuzpe method (high-dose combined OCPs) is older and less effective with more side effects. Mifepristone is not standard EC in the US. Plan B (levonorgestrel 1.5 mg) is widely available but less effective, especially in women >75 kg.

questions.stem: Which contraceptive method also protects against STIs?

choices.feedback: Correct! Condoms are the ONLY contraceptive method that provides STI protection (including HIV). Dual method use (condom + LARC or hormonal method) recommended for both pregnancy and STI prevention. Female condoms (internal condoms) also provide STI protection.
OCPs provide no STI protection.
DMPA prevents pregnancy only.
IUDs prevent pregnancy but not STIs.

questions.explanation: Male and female (internal) condoms are the only contraceptive methods that also provide barrier protection against sexually transmitted infections, including HIV, chlamydia, gonorrhea, and syphilis. Male latex condoms reduce HIV transmission risk by approximately 80% with consistent use. All other methods (OCPs, DMPA, IUDs) provide no STI protection. Dual method use (condoms + hormonal method) is recommended for patients needing both pregnancy prevention and STI protection.

questions.stem: A breastfeeding mother <6 weeks postpartum should AVOID:

choices.feedback: Progestin-only methods are safe and do not affect milk supply.

Correct! Estrogen can decrease breast milk supply, especially in early postpartum. Avoid combined hormonal contraceptives (OCP, patch, ring) until ≥6 weeks postpartum (some guidelines say ≥6 months). Progestin-only methods are safe: mini-pill, implant, LNG-IUD, DMPA.
Barrier methods are always safe.
Copper IUD is safe during breastfeeding (hormone-free).

questions.explanation: Estrogen-containing contraceptives should be avoided in breastfeeding women <6 weeks postpartum because estrogen can suppress lactation by reducing milk production. Additionally, the early postpartum period carries elevated thromboembolism risk, which estrogen further increases. Progestin-only methods (POP, implant, DMPA, hormonal IUD) are safe during breastfeeding and do not significantly affect milk supply. Condoms and copper IUD are also safe. After 6 weeks, combined hormonal methods can be considered if breastfeeding is well established.

questions.stem: Bilateral salpingectomy is increasingly preferred over tubal ligation because:

choices.feedback: Neither procedure is easily reversible. Bilateral salpingectomy is actually less reversible.
Both are permanent sterilization methods.

choices.text: It is less invasive than tubal ligation
choices.feedback: Salpingectomy is slightly more extensive surgically.

Correct! Growing evidence that high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma originates from the fallopian tube fimbriae. Bilateral salpingectomy removes this tissue, potentially reducing ovarian cancer risk by 40-65%. ACOG supports opportunistic salpingectomy when permanent sterilization is desired.

questions.explanation: Bilateral salpingectomy (complete fallopian tube removal) is increasingly preferred over tubal ligation because it significantly reduces the risk of ovarian cancer. Current evidence suggests that many high-grade serous ovarian cancers originate from precursor lesions (serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma, STIC) in the fimbriated end of the fallopian tube. Salpingectomy is not easier to reverse — it is irreversible (unlike tubal ligation which has some reversal potential). It does not preserve more fertility options. The surgical complexity is similar to tubal ligation.

questions.stem: Plan B (levonorgestrel) efficacy decreases with:

choices.feedback: Repeated use doesn't decrease efficacy.
Age is not the primary factor affecting efficacy.

choices.text: Body weight >75 kg and time delay
choices.feedback: Correct! Levonorgestrel efficacy decreases significantly with higher BMI (body weight >75 kg / BMI >25). Also less effective after 72h. For overweight patients or if >72h: ulipristal (Ella) or copper IUD are better options. Copper IUD is the most effective regardless of weight.
Most antibiotics don't interact with Plan B (rifampin is an exception).

questions.explanation: Plan B (levonorgestrel 1.5 mg) efficacy is reduced in women weighing >75 kg (165 lbs), with significantly decreased effectiveness above 80 kg. Efficacy also declines with increasing time from unprotected intercourse — it is most effective within 12 hours and should be taken within 72 hours (some benefit up to 120 hours). For women >75 kg, ulipristal acetate (ella) or copper IUD are preferred alternatives. Previous Plan B use does not reduce its future efficacy. Age over 30 does not affect efficacy. Concurrent antibiotic use does not typically interact with levonorgestrel.

questions.stem: Copper IUD mechanism of contraception:

choices.feedback: Cervical mucus thickening is a progestin effect. Copper IUD is hormone-free.
Copper IUD is hormone-free and does not affect ovulation.
The IUD doesn't physically block the cervix.

choices.text: Copper ions are toxic to sperm, creating an inflammatory intrauterine environment that prevents fertilization
choices.feedback: Correct! Copper ions create a sterile inflammatory reaction toxic to sperm and ova, preventing fertilization. Also alters endometrial lining to prevent implantation. No hormonal effects → no systemic side effects. Can increase menstrual bleeding/cramping. Effective for 10-12 years.

questions.explanation: The copper IUD (Paragard) works through the toxic effects of copper ions on sperm, impairing sperm motility and viability. Copper also creates a sterile inflammatory reaction within the uterus that is spermicidal and prevents implantation. It is hormone-free and effective for up to 10-12 years. The copper IUD does not thicken cervical mucus (that is the mechanism of hormonal IUDs/progestin methods). It does not prevent ovulation. It does not mechanically block the cervical os — the device sits within the uterine cavity.

questions.stem: SpO2 target in COPD exacerbation?

choices.feedback: Excessive O2 in COPD suppresses hypoxic ventilatory drive → CO2 retention → respiratory acidosis.
Appropriate for most patients but NOT for COPD with chronic CO2 retention.
Correct! Target 88-92% — enough to prevent tissue hypoxia without suppressing respiratory drive. Use Venturi mask for precise FiO2 delivery. Higher targets increase mortality in COPD (DECAF study).
Too low — would cause significant tissue hypoxia.

questions.explanation: In COPD exacerbation, the SpO2 target is 88-92% — deliberately lower than the standard 94-98% target for most patients. Excessive oxygen in COPD patients with chronic CO2 retention can worsen hypercapnia through three mechanisms: Haldane effect (oxygen displaces CO2 from hemoglobin), V/Q mismatch redistribution (oxygen reverses hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, increasing dead space), and decreased hypoxic ventilatory drive. Venturi masks provide precise FiO2 delivery (24-50%) and are preferred for controlled oxygen therapy. ABGs should be monitored to ensure PaCO2 is not rising excessively.
questions.verifiedExplanation: In COPD exacerbation, the SpO2 target is 88-92% — deliberately lower than the standard 94-98% target for most patients. Excessive oxygen in COPD patients with chronic CO2 retention can worsen hypercapnia through three mechanisms: Haldane effect (oxygen displaces CO2 from hemoglobin), V/Q mismatch redistribution (oxygen reverses hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, increasing dead space), and decreased hypoxic ventilatory drive. Venturi masks provide precise FiO2 delivery (24-50%) and are preferred for controlled oxygen therapy. ABGs should be monitored to ensure PaCO2 is not rising excessively.

questions.stem: BiPAP (NIV) in COPD exacerbation is indicated when:

choices.text: Any COPD patient presenting to ED
choices.feedback: Not all exacerbations need BiPAP — only those with respiratory acidosis.
SpO2 90% may be adequate for COPD. BiPAP is for respiratory acidosis.
BiPAP is used to PREVENT intubation, not after it fails.

Correct! BiPAP reduces work of breathing, improves ventilation, corrects acidosis. Reduces intubation by 50% and mortality. Contraindications: hemodynamic instability, AMS/inability to protect airway, facial trauma, copious secretions.

questions.explanation: BiPAP (bilevel positive airway pressure) non-invasive ventilation in COPD exacerbation is indicated when pH <7.35 with PaCO2 >45 mmHg (acute or acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis). BiPAP reduces the work of breathing, improves ventilation (reduces PaCO2), prevents intubation, decreases ICU length of stay, and reduces mortality. Contraindications include: hemodynamic instability, inability to protect airway, facial trauma, copious secretions, and lack of patient cooperation. If pH continues to drop or PaCO2 rises despite 1-2 hours of NIV, intubation and mechanical ventilation should not be delayed.
questions.verifiedExplanation: BiPAP (bilevel positive airway pressure) non-invasive ventilation in COPD exacerbation is indicated when pH <7.35 with PaCO2 >45 mmHg (acute or acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis). BiPAP reduces the work of breathing, improves ventilation (reduces PaCO2), prevents intubation, decreases ICU length of stay, and reduces mortality. Contraindications include: hemodynamic instability, inability to protect airway, facial trauma, copious secretions, and lack of patient cooperation. If pH continues to drop or PaCO2 rises despite 1-2 hours of NIV, intubation and mechanical ventilation should not be delayed.

questions.stem: Only TWO interventions reduce mortality in stable COPD:

choices.text: Long-term supplemental O2 and smoking cessation
choices.feedback: Correct! NOTT/MRC trials: O2 for ≥15 h/day improves survival when PaO2 ≤55 (or ≤59 with polycythemia/cor pulmonale/edema). Smoking cessation is the only intervention that slows FEV1 decline. All inhalers are symptom management only.
Theophylline has minimal mortality benefit. Pulmonary rehab improves exercise capacity and quality of life.
Inhalers reduce symptoms/exacerbations but do NOT reduce mortality.
These reduce exacerbation frequency but are not proven to reduce mortality.

questions.explanation: Only two interventions have been proven to reduce mortality in stable COPD: long-term supplemental oxygen therapy (in patients with resting PaO2 ≤55 mmHg or SpO2 ≤88%, based on the NOTT and MRC trials) and smoking cessation. Long-term oxygen must be used ≥15 hours/day to confer mortality benefit. While inhaled bronchodilators, corticosteroids, pulmonary rehabilitation, and lung volume reduction surgery improve symptoms, exercise tolerance, and quality of life, they have not conclusively demonstrated mortality reduction. This is a commonly tested board question emphasizing the primacy of these two interventions.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Only two interventions have been proven to reduce mortality in stable COPD: long-term supplemental oxygen therapy (in patients with resting PaO2 ≤55 mmHg or SpO2 ≤88%, based on the NOTT and MRC trials) and smoking cessation. Long-term oxygen must be used ≥15 hours/day to confer mortality benefit. While inhaled bronchodilators, corticosteroids, pulmonary rehabilitation, and lung volume reduction surgery improve symptoms, exercise tolerance, and quality of life, they have not conclusively demonstrated mortality reduction. This is a commonly tested board question emphasizing the primacy of these two interventions.

questions.stem: #1 cause of COPD?

choices.feedback: Asthma and COPD are distinct diseases, though asthma-COPD overlap exists.
Air pollution contributes but is not the primary cause.

Correct! Cigarette smoking causes 85-90% of COPD. The toxic particles/gases cause chronic inflammation → mucus hypersecretion, airway remodeling, and alveolar destruction. Second most common cause: alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (young, non-smoker, lower lobe panacinar emphysema).
AAT deficiency causes 1-3% of COPD, not the majority.

questions.explanation: Cigarette smoking is the cause of 85-90% of COPD cases, making it the overwhelming primary risk factor. Smoking causes COPD through direct toxic effects on airway epithelium, chronic inflammation (neutrophils, macrophages, CD8+ T cells), protease-antiprotease imbalance (neutrophil elastase overwhelms alpha-1 antitrypsin), and oxidative stress. The pathology involves both chronic bronchitis (mucous gland hyperplasia, airway inflammation) and emphysema (destruction of alveolar walls). Other risk factors include occupational exposures, biomass fuel smoke (important globally), air pollution, and alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Cigarette smoking is the cause of 85-90% of COPD cases, making it the overwhelming primary risk factor. Smoking causes COPD through direct toxic effects on airway epithelium, chronic inflammation (neutrophils, macrophages, CD8+ T cells), protease-antiprotease imbalance (neutrophil elastase overwhelms alpha-1 antitrypsin), and oxidative stress. The pathology involves both chronic bronchitis (mucous gland hyperplasia, airway inflammation) and emphysema (destruction of alveolar walls). Other risk factors include occupational exposures, biomass fuel smoke (important globally), air pollution, and alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency.

questions.stem: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency should be suspected when:

choices.text: Elderly smoker with upper lobe emphysema
choices.feedback: That's typical smoking-related COPD. AAT deficiency has a different pattern.
Chronic cough has many causes; AAT deficiency is specific to early-onset emphysema.

choices.text: Young non-smoker with lower lobe panacinar emphysema
choices.feedback: Correct! AAT deficiency: onset <45 years, minimal/no smoking history, lower lobe predominant, panacinar pattern (vs centrilobular in smoking). Also causes liver disease (cirrhosis). Diagnosis: serum AAT level + phenotype testing. Treatment: IV augmentation therapy.
Upper lobe cavitation suggests TB or Klebsiella, not AAT deficiency.

questions.explanation: Alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT) deficiency should be suspected in a young non-smoker (or light smoker) with lower-lobe predominant panacinar emphysema. Classic AAT deficiency is autosomal codominant (PiZZ genotype), presenting with early-onset emphysema (30s-40s), and often concurrent liver disease (cirrhosis from AAT polymer accumulation in hepatocytes). The panacinar pattern (involving entire acinus, lower lobe predominance) differs from smoking-related centrilobular emphysema (upper lobe predominance). All COPD patients should be screened for AAT deficiency at least once. Treatment includes augmentation therapy (IV pooled human AAT) for patients with severe deficiency and established lung disease.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT) deficiency should be suspected in a young non-smoker (or light smoker) with lower-lobe predominant panacinar emphysema. Classic AAT deficiency is autosomal codominant (PiZZ genotype), presenting with early-onset emphysema (30s-40s), and often concurrent liver disease (cirrhosis from AAT polymer accumulation in hepatocytes). The panacinar pattern (involving entire acinus, lower lobe predominance) differs from smoking-related centrilobular emphysema (upper lobe predominance). All COPD patients should be screened for AAT deficiency at least once. Treatment includes augmentation therapy (IV pooled human AAT) for patients with severe deficiency and established lung disease.

questions.stem: #1 bacterial cause of COPD exacerbation?

choices.feedback: Pseudomonas is important in severe COPD/bronchiectasis but not #1 overall.

Correct! H. influenzae is the most common bacterial pathogen in AECOPD. Others: M. catarrhalis, S. pneumoniae. Note: most exacerbations are triggered by viral URIs (rhinovirus, influenza). Antibiotics indicated for purulent sputum, severe exacerbation, or mechanical ventilation.
S. aureus is more common in HAP/VAP than AECOPD.
Klebsiella is more associated with aspiration/alcoholism.

questions.explanation: Non-typeable Haemophilus influenzae (NTHi) is the most common bacterial cause of COPD exacerbation, followed by Streptococcus pneumoniae and Moraxella catarrhalis. These three organisms account for the majority of bacterial exacerbations. Pseudomonas aeruginosa becomes increasingly important in patients with severe COPD (FEV1 <30% predicted), frequent antibiotic use, or frequent hospitalizations. Not all COPD exacerbations are bacterial — viral infections (rhinovirus most common) and environmental triggers account for a significant proportion. Antibiotics are indicated for exacerbations with increased dyspnea, increased sputum volume, AND increased sputum purulence (Anthonisen criteria).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Non-typeable Haemophilus influenzae (NTHi) is the most common bacterial cause of COPD exacerbation, followed by Streptococcus pneumoniae and Moraxella catarrhalis. These three organisms account for the majority of bacterial exacerbations. Pseudomonas aeruginosa becomes increasingly important in patients with severe COPD (FEV1 <30% predicted), frequent antibiotic use, or frequent hospitalizations. Not all COPD exacerbations are bacterial — viral infections (rhinovirus most common) and environmental triggers account for a significant proportion. Antibiotics are indicated for exacerbations with increased dyspnea, increased sputum volume, AND increased sputum purulence (Anthonisen criteria).

questions.stem: Spirometry finding that defines COPD?

choices.feedback: Significant improvement (≥12% and 200 mL) suggests asthma, not COPD.
That's normal or restrictive pattern. Obstruction shows reduced ratio.
Reduced TLC = restrictive disease. COPD shows increased TLC (air trapping).

Correct! Post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70 = obstruction. 'Post-bronchodilator' distinguishes COPD (fixed obstruction) from asthma (reversible obstruction). GOLD classification uses FEV1 % predicted for severity: GOLD 1 ≥80%, GOLD 2 50-79%, GOLD 3 30-49%, GOLD 4 <30%.

questions.explanation: The spirometric hallmark of COPD is FEV1/FVC ratio <0.70 (or <lower limit of normal) after bronchodilator administration. This post-bronchodilator criterion distinguishes fixed airflow obstruction (COPD) from reversible obstruction (asthma, which normalizes after bronchodilator). GOLD severity staging is based on post-bronchodilator FEV1: GOLD 1 (mild) ≥80% predicted, GOLD 2 (moderate) 50-79%, GOLD 3 (severe) 30-49%, GOLD 4 (very severe) <30%. FVC may also be reduced in COPD (from air trapping), but the ratio remains the diagnostic criterion. Pre-bronchodilator testing alone is insufficient to diagnose COPD.
questions.verifiedExplanation: The spirometric hallmark of COPD is FEV1/FVC ratio <0.70 (or <lower limit of normal) after bronchodilator administration. This post-bronchodilator criterion distinguishes fixed airflow obstruction (COPD) from reversible obstruction (asthma, which normalizes after bronchodilator). GOLD severity staging is based on post-bronchodilator FEV1: GOLD 1 (mild) ≥80% predicted, GOLD 2 (moderate) 50-79%, GOLD 3 (severe) 30-49%, GOLD 4 (very severe) <30%. FVC may also be reduced in COPD (from air trapping), but the ratio remains the diagnostic criterion. Pre-bronchodilator testing alone is insufficient to diagnose COPD.

questions.stem: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! The absent cavum septum pellucidum, parallel ventricles (instead of converging anteriorly where the genu of CC should be), colpocephaly (dilated occipital horns), and elevated 3rd ventricle (riding between the hemispheres) are the classic prenatal signs of ACC.
HPE has FUSED ventricles (single monoventricle) with absent falx. ACC has SEPARATED but parallel ventricles with a present falx. The hemispheres cleave normally in ACC.
SOD includes absent CSP but has NORMAL corpus callosum (usually). The parallel ventricles and elevated 3rd ventricle point to ACC.
Hydrocephalus dilates ALL parts of the ventricles uniformly. Colpocephaly specifically dilates the OCCIPITAL horns while the frontal horns are narrow — this pattern is specific to ACC.

questions.stem: In what order does the corpus callosum develop?

choices.feedback: The CC develops anterior to posterior, not posterior to anterior.
Development is sequential, not simultaneous. This is clinically important for interpreting partial ACC.
Despite being the most anterior part, the rostrum forms LAST by retroflexion. The genu develops first.

Correct! The CC develops from front to back: genu (week 12) → body → splenium (week 18) → rostrum (week 20, forms LAST by bending back). This explains why PARTIAL agenesis typically affects the posterior portions (splenium and rostrum) — if development stops early, the anterior parts may form but the posterior parts don't.

questions.stem: Which syndrome classically includes agenesis of the corpus callosum?

choices.feedback: T21 is associated with AVSD, duodenal atresia, and Brushfield spots, not typically ACC.

Correct! Aicardi syndrome = the classic triad of ACC + infantile spasms (West syndrome) + chorioretinal lacunae (distinctive eye finding). It's X-linked dominant (lethal in males, seen almost exclusively in females and XXY males). Other ACC syndromes: Andermann (ACC + peripheral neuropathy), FG syndrome (ACC + intellectual disability + hypotonia).
Turner is associated with coarctation of the aorta, horseshoe kidney, and cystic hygroma, not ACC.
Marfan involves aortic root dilation, lens subluxation, and skeletal features, not ACC.

questions.stem: What are Probst bundles?

choices.text: Callosal axons that failed to cross and run longitudinally instead
choices.feedback: Correct! In ACC, the axons that would normally cross the midline through the CC instead form thick longitudinal bundles (Probst bundles) that run along the medial walls of the lateral ventricles. They connect ipsilateral frontal to occipital regions instead of crossing to the opposite hemisphere. This is why the lateral ventricles have their characteristic parallel, widely-spaced appearance in ACC.

choices.text: Ectopic gray matter nodules along the ventricles
choices.feedback: Periventricular heterotopia is ectopic gray matter from failed neuronal migration. Probst bundles are WHITE matter (misdirected axonal tracts).

choices.text: Thickened ependymal lining from CSF pressure
choices.feedback: Probst bundles are structural white matter tracts, not reactive changes. They're axons that took the wrong path.

choices.text: Remnants of the fetal glial sling
choices.feedback: The glial sling is a guidance structure that normally degrades. Probst bundles are misdirected callosal axons, not glial remnants.

questions.stem: What cognitive profile is typical of isolated ACC?

choices.feedback: While some individuals with ACC are remarkably compensated, careful neuropsychological testing usually reveals subtle deficits in complex integration tasks.

choices.text: Normal IQ with deficits in complex processing and social cognition
choices.feedback: Correct! Isolated ACC typically shows: normal basic IQ but impaired complex/novel problem-solving, difficulty with social inference and humor comprehension, reduced processing speed, challenges with abstract language. These deficits reflect the CC's role in interhemispheric integration — combining information processed differently by each hemisphere.

choices.text: Isolated motor deficits with preserved cognition
choices.feedback: The CC primarily connects association cortices. Motor deficits are not the primary issue — cognitive integration deficits are more characteristic.
Severe ID occurs in syndromic ACC but is NOT typical of isolated ACC. Many individuals with isolated ACC have normal or near-normal IQ.

questions.stem: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: CDH shows bowel loops (not cysts) in the chest with absent diaphragm. Multicystic appearance with pulmonary arterial supply = CPAM.
CLE shows a hyperinflated (not cystic) lobe, typically diagnosed postnatally when the lobe fails to deflate.

Correct! CPAM (formerly CCAM) Type 1 has large cysts (>2cm, often dominant cyst), accounts for 65% of CPAMs, and has the best prognosis. The pulmonary arterial supply distinguishes it from sequestration (which has systemic/aortic supply). Fetal hydrops from mediastinal shift is the most concerning prenatal complication.
Sequestration receives SYSTEMIC (aortic) supply. This lesion has PULMONARY arterial supply → CPAM. However, 'hybrid lesions' (CPAM + sequestration features) do exist.

questions.stem: What prenatal metric best predicts the risk of fetal hydrops from CPAM?

choices.feedback: While Doppler is useful for monitoring fetal wellbeing, the CVR is the specific metric for CPAM hydrops risk prediction.
AFP is not used for CPAM monitoring. AFP is elevated in open neural tube defects and certain other conditions, not lung lesions.

Correct! CVR = (length × height × width × 0.52) / head circumference. CVR >1.6 indicates high risk (~80%) of developing hydrops. CVR <1.6 has ~<5% hydrops risk. Serial CVR measurements guide management: if CVR rising and approaching 1.6, closer surveillance is needed. If hydrops develops, fetal intervention (steroids for microcystic, thoracoamniotic shunt for macrocystic) may be lifesaving.
The number of cysts is less important than the total volume of the lesion relative to the fetus (CVR). One large cyst can be more dangerous than many small ones.

questions.stem: During which stage of lung development do alveoli begin to form?

choices.feedback: Correct! TRUE alveoli with mature thin walls begin forming at ~36 weeks and continue multiplying until age 8. At birth: ~50 million alveoli. By age 8: ~300-400 million. This is why premature infants have limited gas exchange surface area and why lung development continues postnatally.
The saccular stage forms terminal sacs (primitive air spaces) and type II pneumocytes begin producing surfactant at ~24 weeks, but mature alveoli form later.
The pseudoglandular stage creates the conducting airways (bronchi/bronchioles) but no gas exchange structures. The lung looks like a gland histologically.
The canalicular stage forms respiratory bronchioles and begins vascularization, but TRUE alveoli don't appear until later.

questions.stem: What is a bronchogenic cyst embryologically?

choices.text: Foregut duplication from abnormal ventral budding
choices.feedback: Correct! Bronchogenic cysts are foregut duplication cysts arising from abnormal budding of the tracheobronchial tree during early development (weeks 3-7). The aberrant bud separates and develops into a cyst lined by respiratory epithelium with cartilage, smooth muscle, and sometimes mucous glands in its wall — recapitulating normal airway wall structure.
Vitelline duct remnants produce Meckel diverticulum in the ileum, not mediastinal cysts.
Lymphatic malformations (cystic hygroma) are lined by endothelium, not respiratory epithelium, and don't have cartilage in their walls.
Thymic cysts are in the anterior mediastinum and lined by thymic epithelium. Bronchogenic cysts are middle/posterior mediastinum with respiratory epithelium.

questions.stem: Which congenital lung lesion has a recognized risk of malignancy?

choices.feedback: Sequestrations have minimal malignancy risk. They're resected for recurrent infections, not cancer prevention.

Correct! CPAM (especially Type 1 and Type 4) carries a small risk (~2-4%) of malignancy, specifically pleuropulmonary blastoma (PPB) and bronchioloalveolar carcinoma. Type 4 CPAM and Type I PPB can be histologically identical on prenatal imaging — only pathology distinguishes them. This malignancy risk is the primary argument for elective resection of even asymptomatic CPAMs.

choices.text: None — congenital lung lesions have no malignancy risk
choices.feedback: CPAM does have a recognized malignancy risk, particularly for pleuropulmonary blastoma.

choices.text: Congenital lobar emphysema — risk of lymphoma
choices.feedback: CLE has no significant malignancy risk. It's managed for respiratory symptoms, not cancer prevention.

questions.stem: What is the embryological basis of craniosynostosis?

choices.text: Failure of neural crest migration to calvarium
choices.feedback: Neural crest cells form the frontal and parietal bones, but craniosynostosis is PREMATURE fusion, not failure of formation.

Correct! Craniosynostosis = premature fusion of one or more cranial sutures. Growth is restricted PERPENDICULAR to the fused suture (Virchow's law) and compensates by growing PARALLEL to it. Sagittal synostosis (most common, 40-55%) → scaphocephaly because the skull cannot widen but grows excessively front-to-back.
Positional plagiocephaly (flat head from sleep position) does NOT involve suture fusion — the sutures remain open. This is an important distinction.
Increased ICP can result FROM craniosynostosis (restricted brain growth), but it's not the cause. The suture fuses first.

questions.stem: A baby has a triangular forehead shape (trigonocephaly) with a palpable ridge along the midline forehead. Which suture is prematurely fused?

choices.feedback: Lambdoid synostosis causes posterior plagiocephaly (unilateral occipital flattening). It's the rarest single-suture synostosis.

Correct! Metopic synostosis → trigonocephaly (triangular forehead with midline frontal ridge, hypotelorism/close-set eyes). The metopic suture normally closes by age 2 (the only suture that closes in infancy). Premature closure before age 2 causes trigonocephaly. Metopic synostosis is the second most common single-suture craniosynostosis.
Sagittal fusion causes scaphocephaly (long/narrow head), not triangular forehead.
Unilateral coronal synostosis causes anterior plagiocephaly (asymmetric forehead with ipsilateral flattening and contralateral bossing), not trigonocephaly.

questions.stem: Which finding would suggest a SYNDROMIC craniosynostosis requiring genetic evaluation?

choices.text: Isolated sagittal synostosis in an otherwise normal infant
choices.feedback: Isolated single-suture synostosis is usually non-syndromic and sporadic. Genetic testing is not routinely needed unless there are additional features.

choices.text: Sagittal synostosis with an open anterior fontanelle
choices.feedback: The anterior fontanelle can remain open even with sagittal synostosis — they are different structures. This alone doesn't suggest a syndrome.

choices.text: Family history of large head size
choices.feedback: Familial macrocephaly is a benign condition unrelated to craniosynostosis.

choices.text: Multiple suture synostosis with limb anomalies (syndactyly)
choices.feedback: Correct! Multi-suture synostosis + limb anomalies (especially syndactyly) = syndromic craniosynostosis. Think Apert syndrome (FGFR2 mutation): bilateral coronal synostosis + symmetric syndactyly of hands and feet (mitten hands). Also Crouzon syndrome (FGFR2): multi-suture without limb involvement. Pfeiffer syndrome (FGFR1/2): broad thumbs/great toes.

questions.stem: What is the PRIMARY goal of surgical treatment for craniosynostosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! The primary goal is to allow normal brain growth by removing the restriction. Untreated multi-suture synostosis can cause increased ICP → papilledema, developmental delay, and cognitive impairment. Single-suture synostosis has lower ICP risk but can still cause problems. Cosmetic improvement is an important secondary benefit.
Cosmesis is important but NOT the primary goal. The main concern is preventing increased intracranial pressure from restricted brain growth.
The fontanelle is not closed surgically — it closes naturally. Surgery REMOVES fused bone to allow growth.
Craniosynostosis does not directly cause seizures. ICP elevation can cause developmental issues, but seizure prevention is not the primary surgical indication.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Allow normal brain growth and prevent increased ICP.

questions.stem: What clinical finding distinguishes positional plagiocephaly from lambdoid synostosis?

choices.feedback: Positional plagiocephaly is managed conservatively: tummy time, repositioning, helmet therapy if severe. Surgery is NOT needed.

choices.text: In positional plagiocephaly, the ipsilateral ear shifts ANTERIOR; in lambdoid synostosis, it shifts POSTERIOR
choices.feedback: Correct! Positional plagiocephaly: parallelogram shape — ipsilateral ear shifts ANTERIORLY, ipsilateral forehead bossing. Lambdoid synostosis: trapezoid shape — ipsilateral ear shifts POSTERIORLY and inferiorly, ipsilateral mastoid bulge. Positional is common (1 in 300). Lambdoid synostosis is very rare (<5% of all synostosis).

choices.text: Positional plagiocephaly causes raised intracranial pressure
choices.feedback: Positional plagiocephaly does NOT cause raised ICP because all sutures are open. It's a cosmetic issue that improves with repositioning.

choices.text: CT scan is required — clinical exam cannot distinguish them
choices.feedback: Clinical exam CAN often distinguish them. CT is reserved for equivocal cases to avoid radiation. The ear position is the key clinical finding.

questions.stem: What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Dandy-Walker malformation = the classic triad: (1) Cystic dilation of the fourth ventricle, (2) Complete or partial agenesis of the cerebellar vermis, (3) Enlarged posterior fossa with upward displacement of the tentorium. This is distinct from a mega cisterna magna (normal vermis, normal 4th ventricle) and Dandy-Walker variant (partial vermian hypoplasia, normal posterior fossa size).
Chiari II has a SMALL posterior fossa with herniation downward. Dandy-Walker has an ENLARGED posterior fossa with a cystic 4th ventricle expanding it. Opposite directions.
Mega cisterna magna has an enlarged cisterna magna but NORMAL cerebellar vermis and NORMAL fourth ventricle size. It's a benign normal variant.
Arachnoid cysts are extra-axial and don't communicate with the fourth ventricle. They don't cause vermian hypoplasia.

questions.stem: What is the proposed embryologic mechanism for Dandy-Walker malformation?

choices.text: Failed development of the roof of the fourth ventricle and cerebellar vermis
choices.feedback: Correct! The cerebellar vermis develops from the fusion of the two rhombic lips across the midline. In DWM, this fusion fails → vermian agenesis/hypoplasia. The roof of the fourth ventricle (formed by the anterior medullary velum and choroid plexus) also fails to develop properly → the fourth ventricle expands cystically and balloons the posterior fossa.
DWM is a malformation (abnormal development), not a disruption (vascular destruction). Cerebellar disruptions exist but have different imaging patterns.

choices.text: Obstruction of the foramen of Magendie
choices.feedback: This was the old theory (that DWM was caused by atresia of the foramina of Magendie and Luschka). Modern understanding is that the primary defect is in cerebellar/roof development, not just CSF obstruction.

choices.text: Excessive CSF production by choroid plexus
choices.feedback: CSF overproduction is extremely rare (choroid plexus papilloma). DWM is a structural malformation of the cerebellum and 4th ventricle roof, not a CSF dynamics problem.

questions.stem: Which of the following is most commonly associated with Dandy-Walker malformation?

choices.feedback: Correct! DWM associations: CNS anomalies in 50-70% (agenesis of corpus callosum most common at 25%, heterotopias, occipital encephalocele), cardiac defects in 20-30% (VSD, ASD, TOF), face/palate anomalies. Chromosomal: trisomy 13, 18, triploidy. Syndromes: Walker-Warburg, Joubert (vermian aplasia).
MMC is associated with Chiari II, not Dandy-Walker. DWM and Chiari II are separate entities with different posterior fossa sizes (large vs small).
Polydactyly can occur with trisomy 13 (which can include DWM), but it's not a primary DWM association.
Renal agenesis is associated with Potter sequence and VACTERL, not specifically with DWM.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Cardiac defects and other CNS anomalies.

questions.stem: What percentage of DWM patients develop hydrocephalus requiring treatment?

choices.feedback: Correct! 75-90% of DWM patients develop hydrocephalus requiring treatment. The mechanism is multifactorial: obstruction of CSF flow by the cystic 4th ventricle, stenosis of the aqueduct, and impaired CSF absorption. VP shunt is the standard treatment. Some patients also need posterior fossa cyst-peritoneal shunting if the supratentorial and infratentorial compartments don't communicate well.
Hydrocephalus is one of the most common complications of DWM, occurring in a majority of cases.
Higher than 50%. The large majority of DWM patients need CSF diversion.
Not quite universal — about 10-25% may have stable ventricle sizes. But the majority do need intervention.

questions.stem: What is the strongest predictor of neurodevelopmental outcome in DWM?

choices.text: Size of the posterior fossa cyst
choices.feedback: Cyst size alone doesn't predict outcomes. Some large cysts are associated with reasonable development.

choices.text: Presence and severity of associated anomalies
choices.feedback: Correct! The strongest predictors are: (1) Associated CNS anomalies (corpus callosum agenesis, heterotopias); (2) Chromosomal abnormalities; (3) Degree of vermian hypoplasia; (4) Complications of hydrocephalus/shunting. Isolated DWM without other anomalies can have near-normal intelligence. DWM with multiple anomalies has significantly worse outcomes.
Age at diagnosis doesn't independently predict outcome. What matters is the severity of the malformation and associated anomalies.
The surgical approach matters for managing hydrocephalus but doesn't fundamentally change the neurodevelopmental outcome, which is driven by brain malformation severity.

questions.stem: What does a positive Ortolani test indicate?

choices.feedback: If the hip were irreducible, Ortolani would be NEGATIVE (no clunk, because the head can't reduce). A positive Ortolani means it IS reducible.
Acetabular fracture from delivery is extremely rare. The clunk of Ortolani is from soft tissue and capsular laxity, not fracture.

choices.text: Stable hip with normal variant click
choices.feedback: A 'click' is often benign, but a 'clunk' is pathologic. The distinction matters: clicks are high-pitched, clunks are palpable shifts of the femoral head.

Correct! Ortolani = the 'entry' test. It reduces a dislocated hip back into the socket. The clunk you feel is the femoral head sliding over the posterior acetabular rim INTO the acetabulum. Barlow (the 'exit' test) does the opposite — it dislocates a reducible hip OUT of the socket.

questions.stem: Why is ultrasound preferred over X-ray for DDH in neonates?

choices.feedback: While radiation avoidance is a benefit, the primary reason is that X-ray simply can't see the cartilaginous femoral head in neonates.

choices.text: Better detection of soft tissue inflammation
choices.feedback: DDH is not an inflammatory condition. Ultrasound is used because of the cartilaginous anatomy, not for inflammation assessment.
Cost is not the primary driver. Anatomic visualization of the cartilaginous structures is the key reason.

Correct! The femoral head ossification center doesn't appear on X-ray until 4-6 months of age. Before then, the hip is mostly cartilage, which ultrasound visualizes perfectly. The alpha angle measures the bony acetabular coverage: normal >60°, dysplastic <50°.

questions.stem: What is the primary mechanism by which the Pavlik harness corrects DDH?

choices.text: Maintains the femoral head in the acetabulum to stimulate remodeling
choices.feedback: Correct! The acetabulum needs the femoral head present to develop its normal concave shape. The Pavlik keeps the head centered in the acetabulum, providing the mechanical stimulus for the acetabulum to deepen and the capsule to tighten. This is why early treatment (before 6 months) has the best outcomes — the acetabulum is still actively forming.

choices.text: Forces the head into the socket mechanically
choices.feedback: The Pavlik harness is NOT rigid. Forced abduction can cause avascular necrosis (AVN) of the femoral head. The harness allows GENTLE guided positioning.
Adductor tightness can contribute, but the primary mechanism is acetabular remodeling stimulated by concentric femoral head positioning.

choices.text: Compresses growth plates to redirect growth
choices.feedback: This is not the mechanism. The harness is gentle and doesn't compress growth plates.

questions.stem: What is the most feared complication of DDH treatment?

choices.feedback: Nerve palsy can occur but is rare and usually transient. AVN has permanent consequences.
Growth plate arrest can occur secondary to AVN but is not the primary feared complication itself.
Femoral neck fracture is exceedingly rare with modern techniques. AVN is far more common and clinically significant.

choices.text: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
choices.feedback: Correct! AVN of the femoral head is the most serious complication, occurring in 5-15% of treated DDH cases. The medial femoral circumflex artery is vulnerable during reduction maneuvers and in extreme abduction. AVN causes femoral head deformity, leg length discrepancy, and early osteoarthritis.

questions.stem: What is the next diagnostic step?

choices.feedback: MRI is used for surgical planning (after closed reduction to check position), not for initial diagnosis in a walking child. X-ray is first-line.
After 4-6 months, the ossified femoral head makes ultrasound less useful. X-ray is preferred in the walking child.

Correct! After 6 months, the femoral head ossification center is visible on X-ray. AP pelvis will show: superolateral displacement of the femoral head, shallow acetabulum, disrupted Shenton's line, and increased acetabular index. This presentation — painless limp + leg length discrepancy + limited abduction — is classic for missed/late DDH.
This painless limp with asymmetric folds and limited abduction is classic DDH, not infection or tumor. Start with X-ray.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old post-hip surgery patient is alert in the morning but becomes confused and agitated at night, pulling at IV lines and seeing 'bugs on the wall.' What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: New-onset schizophrenia is extremely rare in an 82-year-old. Schizophrenia typically presents in late teens/twenties and has auditory (not visual) hallucinations.
Brief psychotic disorder is a primary psychiatric diagnosis. In a post-surgical elderly patient, altered mental status should always be attributed to delirium until proven otherwise.

Correct! Acute onset, fluctuating course (lucid AM → confused PM = 'sundowning'), visual hallucinations, and post-surgical setting are classic delirium. Post-operative delirium occurs in 15-50% of elderly surgical patients.
Dementia has insidious onset and stable day-to-day course. This patient's acute, fluctuating course with visual hallucinations is classic delirium.

questions.explanation: This is classic delirium: acute onset (postoperative), fluctuating course (lucid in morning, confused at night — 'sundowning'), visual hallucinations ('bugs on the wall'), and agitation. Key delirium features include acute onset, waxing and waning, inattention, and altered consciousness. Schizophrenia typically presents in young adults (18-35) with auditory hallucinations, not visual. Brief psychotic disorder is a consideration but the postoperative context and fluctuation strongly favor delirium. Dementia is chronic and progressive, not acute, though it is a risk factor for delirium.

questions.stem: Which subtype of delirium is MOST COMMONLY missed?

choices.feedback: Mixed delirium alternates between types and is recognized during hyperactive episodes.
Hyperactive delirium with agitation is easily noticed and diagnosed (though it accounts for only ~25% of cases).

Correct! Hypoactive delirium is the most common subtype (~50%) AND the most missed. Patients are quiet, withdrawn, and lethargic — often misdiagnosed as depression or simply 'tired.' Hypoactive delirium carries WORSE prognosis than hyperactive delirium.
Catatonia is a separate syndrome, not a subtype of delirium (though they can co-occur).

questions.explanation: Hypoactive delirium (quiet, withdrawn, somnolent, decreased psychomotor activity) is the most commonly missed subtype because patients are not disruptive — they are often mistaken for having depression, fatigue, or oversedation. Hypoactive delirium is actually more common than hyperactive delirium and carries a worse prognosis. Screening tools like the CAM and CAM-ICU are essential for detection. Hyperactive delirium (agitation, hallucinations, pulling at lines) is readily identified. Mixed delirium fluctuates between hypoactive and hyperactive. Catatonic is not a standard delirium subtype.

questions.stem: Which medication class is the #1 pharmacologic cause of delirium in elderly patients?

choices.feedback: SSRIs rarely cause delirium. They can cause serotonin syndrome at toxic levels but are not a common delirium trigger.

Correct! Anticholinergic medications are the leading pharmacologic cause of delirium. Examples: diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, TCAs, scopolamine. Acetylcholine is critical for attention and arousal. Anticholinergic burden is cumulative — the more drugs with anticholinergic properties, the higher the risk.
Opioids (especially meperidine) cause delirium, but anticholinergics are the most common pharmacologic cause overall.
Statins are not significantly associated with delirium.

questions.explanation: Anticholinergic medications are the #1 pharmacologic cause of delirium in elderly patients. Common culprits include diphenhydramine (Benadryl), oxybutynin, tricyclic antidepressants, first-generation antihistamines, and antipsychotics with anticholinergic properties. Elderly patients are particularly vulnerable due to age-related decline in cholinergic neurotransmission and reduced hepatic/renal drug clearance. The anticholinergic burden should be minimized in all hospitalized elderly patients. SSRIs have low anticholinergic potential. While opioids can cause delirium, anticholinergics are the most common pharmacologic cause. Statins do not cause delirium.

questions.stem: When is it appropriate to use benzodiazepines for delirium?

choices.feedback: If haloperidol is contraindicated, quetiapine or olanzapine are better alternatives. Benzos should be avoided in non-withdrawal delirium.

choices.text: For hypoactive delirium to prevent agitation
choices.feedback: Hypoactive delirium does not require sedating medications. Treatment focuses on finding and treating the underlying cause.

Correct! Benzodiazepines are specifically indicated for delirium caused by alcohol or benzodiazepine withdrawal (delirium tremens). In withdrawal states, GABAergic tone is dangerously low, and benzos (lorazepam preferred) are life-saving. In all other delirium types, benzos make things worse.
Benzodiazepines WORSEN most forms of delirium by increasing sedation, paradoxical agitation, and respiratory depression.

questions.explanation: Benzodiazepines are appropriate for delirium ONLY when it is caused by alcohol withdrawal or benzodiazepine withdrawal (delirium tremens). In these specific cases, benzodiazepines (lorazepam, diazepam, chlordiazepoxide) are first-line treatment. For all other causes of delirium, benzodiazepines worsen confusion and should be avoided — they are themselves a cause of delirium in elderly patients. Benzodiazepines are not appropriate simply because haloperidol is contraindicated. They should never be used for hypoactive delirium. Low-dose antipsychotics (haloperidol, quetiapine) are used for agitation in non-withdrawal delirium.

questions.stem: The CAM (Confusion Assessment Method) requires which features for delirium diagnosis?

choices.text: Chronic progressive memory loss + executive dysfunction
choices.feedback: This describes dementia, not the CAM criteria for delirium.
These are criteria for major depressive disorder, not delirium.
This describes psychosis criteria, not the CAM for delirium.

choices.text: Acute onset with fluctuating course + inattention + EITHER disorganized thinking OR altered consciousness
choices.feedback: Correct! The CAM requires: 1) Acute onset and fluctuating course AND 2) Inattention AND EITHER 3a) Disorganized thinking OR 3b) Altered level of consciousness. All four features (1+2+3a or 1+2+3b) must be present. The CAM has ~95% sensitivity and ~90% specificity.

questions.explanation: The Confusion Assessment Method (CAM) requires: (1) acute onset with fluctuating course AND (2) inattention, PLUS EITHER (3) disorganized thinking OR (4) altered level of consciousness. Features 1+2 are mandatory; then either 3 or 4 must be present. The CAM has ~95% sensitivity and ~90% specificity for delirium. Chronic progressive memory loss describes dementia. Depressed mood with anhedonia describes major depression. Hallucinations with delusions describes psychotic disorders — though these can overlap with delirium.

questions.stem: A patient with Lewy body dementia develops agitation. Which medication should be AVOIDED?

choices.feedback: Correct! Typical antipsychotics (haloperidol) are CONTRAINDICATED in Lewy body dementia. LBD patients have severe neuroleptic sensitivity — even low doses can cause severe parkinsonism, rigidity, NMS-like reactions, and death. If antipsychotic is necessary, quetiapine or pimavanserin are safer options.
Quetiapine is actually one of the SAFER antipsychotics in Lewy body dementia if medication is absolutely needed, due to lower D2 receptor affinity.
Melatonin is safe and may help with sleep disturbance in delirium/dementia.
Acetaminophen is safe for pain management and may actually reduce delirium by treating undertreated pain.

questions.explanation: Haloperidol (and other typical antipsychotics with strong D2 blockade) should be avoided in Lewy body dementia (LBD) due to severe neuroleptic sensitivity. LBD patients can develop life-threatening reactions including irreversible parkinsonism, rigidity, obtundation, and neuroleptic malignant syndrome-like presentations even at low doses. If an antipsychotic is needed, quetiapine (low D2 affinity) or pimavanserin (selective 5-HT2A inverse agonist) are safer alternatives. Melatonin is safe and may help with sleep disturbances in LBD. Acetaminophen is safe for pain.

questions.stem: What is the MOST IMPORTANT intervention for delirium management?

choices.text: Identify and treat the underlying cause
choices.feedback: Correct! Delirium is a symptom, not a diagnosis. The most important step is finding and treating the cause: infection → antibiotics, medication → discontinue, metabolic → correct, urinary retention → catheterize, pain → treat. Cause-directed treatment resolves delirium faster than any medication.
Restraints should be avoided — they increase agitation, injury risk, and worsen delirium. Use de-escalation and environmental modifications instead.
CBT is for psychiatric disorders (depression, anxiety). Delirium requires medical treatment, not psychotherapy.
Antipsychotics are only for severe agitation endangering the patient. They do not treat the underlying cause and have significant side effects.

questions.explanation: The most important intervention for delirium is identifying and treating the underlying cause. Delirium is a symptom of an acute medical problem — infection (UTI, pneumonia), metabolic derangements (electrolytes, glucose, hypoxia), medication effects, urinary retention, constipation, pain, or organ failure. Treating the cause resolves delirium faster than any pharmacological intervention. Physical restraints increase agitation and are associated with worse outcomes. CBT is not used for acute delirium. Haloperidol may be used for severe agitation but is not first-line management — addressing the cause is.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old develops delirium 48 hours after hospital admission. Which of the following is the LEAST likely cause?

choices.feedback: Urinary retention, catheter discomfort, and UTIs from catheters are common delirium precipitants.

Correct! New-onset schizophrenia at age 75 is extremely rare (typical onset late teens to early 30s). In elderly patients, acute confusion should ALWAYS be attributed to delirium from a medical cause until proven otherwise. Late-onset psychosis can occur but requires exclusion of delirium first.
Diphenhydramine (anticholinergic) is a classic and common cause of iatrogenic delirium in hospitalized elderly patients.
UTIs are one of the most common causes of delirium in elderly hospitalized patients.

questions.explanation: New-onset schizophrenia at age 75 is extremely unlikely — schizophrenia typically has onset between ages 18-35 (males) or 25-35 (females). Very late-onset schizophrenia-like psychosis can occur but is rare and a diagnosis of exclusion. In contrast, Foley catheter discomfort/infection, diphenhydramine (anticholinergic delirium), and UTI are all extremely common causes of hospital-acquired delirium in the elderly. Always consider delirium before attributing new psychiatric symptoms to a primary psychiatric disorder in elderly hospitalized patients.

questions.stem: A patient meets 6 of 9 SIG E CAPS criteria for 3 weeks but does NOT have depressed mood or anhedonia. Can MDD be diagnosed?

choices.text: Yes — 6 criteria exceeds the threshold of 5
choices.feedback: Meeting ≥5 criteria is necessary but not sufficient. At least ONE must be depressed mood or anhedonia.

choices.text: No — either depressed mood or anhedonia must be present
choices.feedback: Correct! DSM-5 requires ≥5 of 9 criteria for ≥2 weeks AND at least one must be depressed mood or anhedonia (loss of interest/pleasure). Without either gateway symptom, MDD cannot be diagnosed regardless of how many other criteria are met.

choices.text: Only if psychomotor retardation is prominent
choices.feedback: Psychomotor retardation is one criterion but cannot substitute for the required gateway symptoms.

choices.text: Yes — if a psychiatrist confirms the diagnosis
choices.feedback: Diagnostic criteria are standardized. Even expert clinical judgment requires the gateway symptoms.

questions.explanation: MDD diagnosis by DSM-5 requires ≥5 of 9 SIG E CAPS criteria for ≥2 weeks, with the mandatory requirement that at least one of the five must be either depressed mood or anhedonia (loss of interest/pleasure). These two 'gateway' symptoms are essential — without either, MDD cannot be diagnosed regardless of how many other criteria are met. This prevents over-diagnosis of somatic symptom clusters as depression. The criteria are: Sleep changes, Interest loss (anhedonia), Guilt/worthlessness, Energy loss, Concentration difficulty, Appetite/weight changes, Psychomotor changes, Suicidal ideation.

questions.stem: Which SSRI is preferred for a pregnant patient with depression who plans to breastfeed?

choices.feedback: Citalopram has more QTc prolongation concerns and less breastfeeding safety data than sertraline.
Paroxetine is AVOIDED in pregnancy (associated with cardiac malformations — category D) and has the worst discontinuation syndrome.

Correct! Sertraline is the best-studied SSRI in pregnancy and breastfeeding, with the lowest transfer into breast milk. It also has the fewest drug interactions. Fluoxetine is an alternative but has a long half-life and more drug interactions.
Fluvoxamine has significant drug interactions and less safety data in pregnancy/lactation compared to sertraline.

questions.explanation: Sertraline is the preferred SSRI during pregnancy and breastfeeding due to extensive safety data, very low placental transfer, and the lowest breast milk concentrations among SSRIs. It does not increase major malformation risk. Paroxetine should be avoided in pregnancy due to increased risk of cardiac malformations (particularly ventricular septal defects) and carries a pregnancy warning (Category D). Citalopram and fluvoxamine have less safety data in pregnancy than sertraline. All SSRIs carry small risk of neonatal adaptation syndrome and persistent pulmonary hypertension, but benefits typically outweigh risks for moderate-severe depression.

questions.stem: What is the MOST EFFECTIVE treatment for depression overall?

choices.feedback: Correct! ECT is the most effective treatment for depression, with response rates of 80-90%. Indications: treatment-resistant depression, psychotic depression, severe suicidality, catatonia, and situations requiring rapid response. Main side effect is transient retrograde amnesia.
SSRIs are first-line pharmacotherapy but are not the most effective treatment. Response rates are ~50-60%.
Ketamine has rapid onset (hours) which is valuable, but ECT has higher overall remission rates for treatment-resistant depression.
CBT is effective and comparable to medication for mild-moderate depression but not the most effective treatment available.

questions.explanation: Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) is the most effective treatment for depression overall, with response rates of 70-90% (compared to ~50-60% for medications). It is the gold standard for treatment-resistant depression, depression with psychotic features, severe suicidality requiring rapid response, and catatonic depression. It works faster than medications (often within 1-2 weeks). SSRIs are the most commonly used first-line treatment but not the most effective. Ketamine/esketamine has rapid onset but evidence for sustained response is still developing. CBT is effective but slower-acting than ECT.

questions.stem: A patient on fluoxetine is prescribed tramadol for pain. 24 hours later they develop agitation, tremor, hyperreflexia, clonus, diaphoresis, and fever. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Anticholinergic toxicity causes dry skin, urinary retention, mydriasis, decreased bowel sounds — the opposite of the diaphoresis and hyperreflexia seen here.

Correct! Fluoxetine (SSRI) + tramadol (serotonin reuptake inhibitor + weak opioid) = serotonin syndrome. Classic triad: mental status changes + autonomic instability (fever, diaphoresis, tachycardia) + neuromuscular hyperactivity (clonus, hyperreflexia, tremor). Treatment: stop both drugs, supportive care, cyproheptadine.
NMS presents with lead-pipe rigidity, hyperthermia, and elevated CK — typically from antipsychotics, not serotonergic drugs. Clonus and hyperreflexia point to serotonin syndrome.
Malignant hyperthermia occurs with volatile anesthetics + succinylcholine, not serotonergic medications.

questions.explanation: This is serotonin syndrome from the combination of fluoxetine (SSRI) and tramadol (synthetic opioid with serotonin reuptake inhibition). The classic triad is: (1) neuromuscular excitability (tremor, hyperreflexia, clonus, myoclonus), (2) autonomic dysfunction (diaphoresis, tachycardia, hyperthermia), and (3) altered mental status (agitation, confusion). Treatment includes stopping offending agents, benzodiazepines for agitation, and cyproheptadine (serotonin antagonist) for moderate-severe cases. Anticholinergic toxicity causes dry, hot skin (not diaphoresis). NMS has lead-pipe rigidity and develops over days. Malignant hyperthermia occurs after anesthetic exposure.

questions.stem: Bupropion is CONTRAINDICATED in which conditions?

choices.feedback: Bupropion is actually weight-neutral or promotes slight weight loss — it is not contraindicated in metabolic conditions.
Bupropion is not absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy (category C). The specific contraindications are seizures and eating disorders.
Bupropion does not have specific cardiac or renal contraindications (unlike venlafaxine, which requires dose adjustment).

Correct! Bupropion lowers the seizure threshold dose-dependently. Contraindicated in: seizure disorders, bulimia/anorexia (electrolyte abnormalities further lower threshold), concurrent MAOI use, and abrupt alcohol/benzo withdrawal. It is unique among antidepressants for having no sexual dysfunction and no weight gain.

questions.explanation: Bupropion is contraindicated in seizure disorders (it lowers seizure threshold in a dose-dependent manner) and eating disorders, particularly bulimia nervosa and anorexia nervosa (electrolyte abnormalities from purging further increase seizure risk). Bupropion's mechanism (norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibition) does not involve serotonin, giving it a distinct side effect profile: no sexual dysfunction, no weight gain. It is actually used for smoking cessation and can aid weight loss. It is not specifically contraindicated in diabetes, pregnancy (Category C), heart failure, or renal disease.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old with depression and insomnia also has poor appetite and weight loss. Which antidepressant would address all three issues?

choices.feedback: Bupropion is activating (can worsen insomnia) and does not increase appetite.

Correct! Mirtazapine's side effect profile (sedation + increased appetite + weight gain) becomes therapeutic here. It treats depression, promotes sleep (H1 antagonism), and stimulates appetite. Ideal for elderly depressed patients with insomnia and poor oral intake. Low doses are more sedating than higher doses (paradoxically).
Fluoxetine can suppress appetite and cause insomnia — it would worsen two of this patient's problems.
Venlafaxine can cause insomnia and GI side effects, not ideal for this patient's symptom profile.

questions.explanation: Mirtazapine addresses all three symptoms: it is strongly sedating (H1 receptor antagonism — improves insomnia), stimulates appetite/causes weight gain (5-HT2C and H1 antagonism), and treats depression (noradrenergic and specific serotonergic antidepressant, NaSSA). It is particularly useful in elderly depressed patients with insomnia, anorexia, and weight loss. Bupropion is activating (worsens insomnia) and may suppress appetite. Fluoxetine is activating and can cause anorexia. Venlafaxine is somewhat activating and does not specifically address appetite or insomnia.

questions.stem: What distinguishes atypical depression from typical MDD?

choices.text: Mood reactivity with hypersomnia, hyperphagia, leaden paralysis, and interpersonal rejection sensitivity
choices.feedback: Correct! Atypical depression features mood REACTIVITY (mood improves with positive events) plus ≥2 of: hypersomnia, hyperphagia/weight gain, leaden paralysis (heavy feeling in limbs), and interpersonal rejection sensitivity. This is the opposite of melancholic depression (mood non-reactive, worse in AM, insomnia, anorexia).
Psychotic features define 'depression with psychotic features,' a different specifier from atypical features.
Depressed mood is still present in atypical depression, but it is reactive (can temporarily improve with good news).
Suicidal ideation can occur in both typical and atypical depression.

questions.explanation: Atypical depression is distinguished from typical MDD by mood reactivity (mood brightens in response to positive events) plus ≥2 of: hypersomnia (excessive sleeping, opposite of insomnia in typical MDD), hyperphagia with weight gain (opposite of decreased appetite), leaden paralysis (heavy, leaden feeling in arms/legs), and interpersonal rejection sensitivity. MAOIs (phenelzine) are classically most effective for atypical depression, though SSRIs are used first-line in practice. Psychotic features define psychotic depression, a different specifier. Depressed mood is present. Suicidal ideation can occur in any depression subtype.

questions.stem: Before starting an antidepressant, what condition must ALWAYS be screened for?

choices.feedback: Personality disorders may affect treatment planning but are not a mandatory screen before antidepressant initiation.

Correct! ALWAYS screen for history of mania/hypomania before starting an antidepressant. Antidepressants in undiagnosed bipolar disorder can trigger mania, rapid cycling, or mixed episodes. Use the MDQ (Mood Disorder Questionnaire). If bipolar is suspected, use mood stabilizers, not antidepressant monotherapy.
While important to assess, substance use does not contraindicate antidepressants the way undiagnosed bipolar disorder does.
TSH should be checked as part of depression workup, but it's not the critical screening before antidepressant initiation.

questions.explanation: Bipolar disorder must always be screened for before initiating antidepressant therapy because antidepressant monotherapy in bipolar disorder can trigger manic episodes, rapid cycling, or mixed states. Key screening questions include history of manic/hypomanic episodes, family history of bipolar disorder, and prior antidepressant-induced mood elevation. The Mood Disorder Questionnaire (MDQ) is a useful screening tool. While hypothyroidism should also be checked (it can cause depression-like symptoms), it is a laboratory test, not the critical psychiatric screening. Personality disorders and substance use are relevant but not the mandatory pre-prescribing screen.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old with Type 2 DM presents with glucose 1,100 mg/dL, osmolality 380, pH 7.38, and no ketones. What is the most important initial treatment?

choices.feedback: Insulin is important but fluids come first in HHS. The extreme hyperosmolality and massive fluid deficit (8-12 L) make volume resuscitation the priority.

Correct! HHS treatment starts with aggressive IV fluids. The extreme dehydration and hyperosmolality are the immediate threat. 1-1.5 L NS in the first hour, then adjust based on corrected sodium. Insulin can be started after initial fluid resuscitation.
Potassium should be checked and replaced as needed, but the primary intervention is fluid resuscitation.
pH is 7.38 (normal) — bicarbonate is not indicated.

questions.explanation: HHS (hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state) presents with extreme hyperglycemia (often >600 mg/dL, sometimes >1000), profound dehydration (average fluid deficit 8-10 L), and hyperosmolality (>320 mOsm/kg) but minimal or no ketoacidosis. The cornerstone of HHS treatment is aggressive IV fluid resuscitation — isotonic saline initially (1-1.5 L/hr for the first hour), then adjusted based on corrected sodium and hemodynamics. Insulin is secondary to fluids in HHS and should be given at lower doses than in DKA. HHS has higher mortality than DKA (~10-20% vs ~1-5%) primarily due to the older patient population and comorbidities.
questions.verifiedExplanation: HHS (hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state) presents with extreme hyperglycemia (often >600 mg/dL, sometimes >1000), profound dehydration (average fluid deficit 8-10 L), and hyperosmolality (>320 mOsm/kg) but minimal or no ketoacidosis. The cornerstone of HHS treatment is aggressive IV fluid resuscitation — isotonic saline initially (1-1.5 L/hr for the first hour), then adjusted based on corrected sodium and hemodynamics. Insulin is secondary to fluids in HHS and should be given at lower doses than in DKA. HHS has higher mortality than DKA (~10-20% vs ~1-5%) primarily due to the older patient population and comorbidities.

questions.stem: During DKA treatment, a patient's serum potassium is 3.1 mEq/L. What should you do?

choices.text: Continue current management and recheck K in 4 hours
choices.feedback: Waiting 4 hours with K of 3.1 and no replacement risks cardiac arrhythmia.

choices.text: Hold insulin, aggressively replace potassium first
choices.feedback: Correct! K <3.3 = HOLD insulin until K is ≥3.3. Aggressively replace K (20-40 mEq/hr in severe hypokalemia). All DKA patients are total-body potassium depleted. Insulin + rehydration will drive K even lower.

choices.text: Give sodium bicarbonate to shift K out of cells
choices.feedback: Bicarbonate would worsen hypokalemia by shifting K intracellularly.

choices.text: Start insulin immediately and add potassium to the fluids
choices.feedback: Starting insulin with K <3.3 is dangerous — insulin drives K further into cells, risking fatal arrhythmia.

questions.explanation: In DKA, if serum potassium is <3.3 mEq/L, insulin must be HELD and potassium aggressively replaced first. Insulin drives potassium intracellularly, and administering insulin to a hypokalemic patient can cause life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias (including fatal cardiac arrest). The protocol is: K+ <3.3 → hold insulin, replace K+ aggressively. K+ 3.3-5.3 → give insulin with concurrent K+ replacement (20-30 mEq/L in each liter of IV fluid). K+ >5.3 → start insulin, recheck K+ every 2 hours. Although patients with DKA often present with normal or elevated serum K+ (from acidosis-driven extracellular shift), total body potassium is invariably depleted.
questions.verifiedExplanation: In DKA, if serum potassium is <3.3 mEq/L, insulin must be HELD and potassium aggressively replaced first.
