Insulin drives potassium intracellularly, and administering insulin to a hypokalemic patient can cause life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias (including fatal cardiac arrest). The protocol is: K+ <3.3 → hold insulin, replace K+ aggressively. K+ 3.3-5.3 → give insulin with concurrent K+ replacement (20-30 mEq/L in each liter of IV fluid). K+ >5.3 → start insulin, recheck K+ every 2 hours. Although patients with DKA often present with normal or elevated serum K+ (from acidosis-driven extracellular shift), total body potassium is invariably depleted.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old being treated for DKA develops headache, vomiting, and bradycardia 6 hours into treatment. What is the most likely complication?

choices.feedback: Hypokalemia would cause cardiac arrhythmias (tachycardia, not bradycardia) and muscle weakness.
PE is not a typical complication of pediatric DKA treatment.
Hypoglycemia would cause tachycardia, diaphoresis, and altered mental status but not bradycardia.

Correct! Cerebral edema: the most feared complication of pediatric DKA treatment. Headache + vomiting + bradycardia (Cushing triad) 4-12 hours after starting treatment = cerebral edema until proven otherwise. Immediate treatment: mannitol or 3% saline, slow IV fluids, elevate head of bed.

questions.explanation: Cerebral edema is the most feared complication of DKA treatment in children, occurring in approximately 0.5-1% of pediatric DKA episodes but carrying mortality of 20-25%. It typically presents 4-12 hours after initiation of treatment with headache, vomiting, altered consciousness, and Cushing's triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations). Risk factors include young age, new-onset diabetes, severe acidosis, high BUN, failure of sodium to rise during treatment, and excessive fluid administration. Treatment is IV mannitol or hypertonic saline. Prevention involves gradual correction of hyperglycemia and osmolality — avoid overly aggressive fluid resuscitation.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Cerebral edema is the most feared complication of DKA treatment in children, occurring in approximately 0.5-1% of pediatric DKA episodes but carrying mortality of 20-25%. It typically presents 4-12 hours after initiation of treatment with headache, vomiting, altered consciousness, and Cushing's triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations). Risk factors include young age, new-onset diabetes, severe acidosis, high BUN, failure of sodium to rise during treatment, and excessive fluid administration. Treatment is IV mannitol or hypertonic saline. Prevention involves gradual correction of hyperglycemia and osmolality — avoid overly aggressive fluid resuscitation.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism by which DKA causes an elevated anion gap metabolic acidosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! In DKA, absolute insulin deficiency → unrestrained lipolysis → free fatty acids → hepatic ketogenesis → β-hydroxybutyrate (main ketoacid) and acetoacetate. These unmeasured anions increase the anion gap. β-hydroxybutyrate:acetoacetate ratio is 3:1 in DKA.
Hyperchloremia from saline causes a non-anion gap acidosis (which can occur during DKA treatment, not as the presenting acidosis).
Lactic acidosis can contribute but is not the primary mechanism. The primary anion gap elevation is from ketoacids.
RTA causes a non-anion gap acidosis, not an elevated anion gap.

questions.explanation: DKA causes an elevated anion gap metabolic acidosis through accumulation of ketone bodies — primarily beta-hydroxybutyrate and acetoacetate. In insulin deficiency, unrestrained lipolysis releases free fatty acids, which undergo hepatic beta-oxidation to produce ketone bodies. These are unmeasured anions that increase the anion gap (AG = Na - Cl - HCO3; normal 12 ± 4). Beta-hydroxybutyrate predominates (~3:1 ratio over acetoacetate) and is the more reliable marker of ketosis severity. Standard urine dipstick detects only acetoacetate (via nitroprusside reaction), which can underestimate the severity of ketosis — serum beta-hydroxybutyrate is preferred for monitoring.
questions.verifiedExplanation: DKA causes an elevated anion gap metabolic acidosis through accumulation of ketone bodies — primarily beta-hydroxybutyrate and acetoacetate. In insulin deficiency, unrestrained lipolysis releases free fatty acids, which undergo hepatic beta-oxidation to produce ketone bodies. These are unmeasured anions that increase the anion gap (AG = Na - Cl - HCO3; normal 12 ± 4). Beta-hydroxybutyrate predominates (~3:1 ratio over acetoacetate) and is the more reliable marker of ketosis severity. Standard urine dipstick detects only acetoacetate (via nitroprusside reaction), which can underestimate the severity of ketosis — serum beta-hydroxybutyrate is preferred for monitoring.

questions.stem: What X-ray finding is expected?

choices.feedback: Multiple air-fluid levels suggest distal small bowel obstruction (jejunal/ileal atresia), not duodenal atresia.
Ground glass is a pulmonary finding (RDS/HMD), not abdominal. The vomiting here is a GI problem.

Correct! The double bubble sign shows air in the stomach (first bubble) and proximal duodenum (second bubble) with NO distal bowel gas if the atresia is complete. This is pathognomonic for duodenal atresia.
Free air indicates perforation, not atresia. Duodenal atresia doesn't typically perforate.

questions.stem: Why is duodenal atresia embryologically different from jejunal/ileal atresia?

choices.text: Duodenal = failed recanalization; jejunal/ileal = vascular accident
choices.feedback: Correct! Duodenal atresia = INTRINSIC (failure of lumen recanalization during solid cord stage). Jejunal/ileal atresia = EXTRINSIC (mesenteric vascular accident causing ischemic necrosis of the bowel segment in utero). This is why jejunal/ileal atresia can have 'apple peel' deformity and is NOT associated with Down syndrome.
Both are endoderm-lined. The difference is in the mechanism of atresia, not germ layer.
The duodenum forms at the foregut-midgut junction earlier, not later. The key difference is the mechanism (recanalization vs vascular).
Neural crest cell migration failure causes Hirschsprung disease (aganglionosis), not duodenal atresia.

questions.stem: What causes annular pancreas?

choices.text: Failure of ventral pancreatic bud rotation
choices.feedback: Correct! Normally, the ventral pancreatic bud rotates posteriorly around the duodenum to fuse with the dorsal bud. If the ventral bud fails to rotate properly (or bifid ventral bud wraps around both sides), it encircles the duodenum → extrinsic compression → obstruction. Annular pancreas is a common co-finding with duodenal atresia in T21.
The pancreas doesn't fuse with the duodenal wall. It wraps around the outside due to failed rotation of the ventral bud.

choices.text: Ectopic pancreatic tissue in the duodenal wall
choices.feedback: Heterotopic pancreas (pancreatic rest) is a different entity — usually found in the stomach or Meckel's and is typically asymptomatic.
It's not overgrowth — it's abnormal positioning of normal pancreatic tissue around the duodenum due to rotational failure.

questions.stem: Why is the annular pancreas not divided?

choices.text: Dividing it risks pancreatic duct injury and fistula
choices.feedback: Correct! The pancreatic duct often courses through the annular ring. Dividing the ring risks cutting the duct → pancreatic fistula, pancreatitis, or duct stricture. Instead, the obstruction is bypassed with a duodenoduodenostomy (connecting proximal to distal duodenum around the obstruction).

choices.text: The pancreas will atrophy after bypass
choices.feedback: The annular pancreas remains viable after bypass. It simply no longer causes obstruction.
Annular pancreas does not regress. But dividing it is not the answer either.

choices.text: The annular tissue provides structural support
choices.feedback: The annular tissue provides no structural benefit — it's the cause of obstruction. But bypass is safer than division.

questions.stem: Which type can present with the 'windsock' deformity?

choices.feedback: Only Type 1 with its intraluminal web can create the windsock deformity.
Type 3 has complete separation — no connection at all. Windsock requires an intraluminal membrane.

Correct! In Type 1, an intraluminal membrane/web can be stretched distally by peristalsis, creating a 'windsock' deformity that may cause partial obstruction. Some Type 1 webs have a central perforation → duodenal stenosis instead of complete atresia. This variant may present later in childhood with intermittent vomiting.
Type 2 has two blind-ending pouches connected by a fibrous cord. There's no intraluminal structure to create a windsock.

questions.stem: Most common location for ectopic pregnancy?

choices.feedback: Abdominal pregnancy is extremely rare.

Correct! Ampulla (~70%), then isthmus (~12%), fimbria (~11%), interstitial/cornual (~2%). Ampullary ectopics are most common because fertilization normally occurs here — the embryo may implant before reaching the uterus if tubal transport is impaired.
Cervical ectopics are rare (<1%) but dangerous (massive hemorrhage).
Ovarian ectopics are rare (<3%).

questions.explanation: The ampulla (distal third) of the fallopian tube is the most common location for ectopic pregnancy, accounting for approximately 70% of cases. The isthmus accounts for ~12%, fimbrial ~11%, and interstitial (cornual) ~2-3%. Ovarian, cervical, and abdominal (peritoneal) ectopics are rare. The ampulla is the most common site because it is where fertilization normally occurs, and tubal damage from prior infection, surgery, or endometriosis can impede the embryo's transit to the uterus.

questions.stem: β-hCG above discriminatory zone + empty uterus on TVUS means:

choices.feedback: Above discriminatory zone, a normal IUP should be visible.
Miscarriage shows intrauterine contents (POC), not empty uterus with high β-hCG.

Correct! If β-hCG >1500-3500 mIU/mL and TVUS shows no IUP, presume ectopic. Further evaluation: adnexal mass? Free fluid? If β-hCG below discriminatory zone with empty uterus = pregnancy of unknown location → serial β-hCG q48h to determine trajectory.
Molar pregnancy shows intrauterine 'snowstorm' pattern, not empty uterus.

questions.explanation: When β-hCG is above the discriminatory zone (typically 1500-2000 mIU/mL for TVUS) and no intrauterine pregnancy is visualized, ectopic pregnancy must be presumed until proven otherwise. This is a critical clinical decision point. An intrauterine pregnancy should be visible by TVUS at these levels. Very early normal pregnancy is unlikely above the discriminatory zone (by definition). Inevitable miscarriage would typically show intrauterine products. Molar pregnancy usually shows a 'snowstorm' pattern on ultrasound with disproportionately high β-hCG.

questions.stem: Methotrexate for ectopic is appropriate when:

choices.text: Stable, unruptured, β-hCG <5000, no fetal cardiac activity
choices.feedback: Correct! MTX criteria: hemodynamically stable, unruptured, β-hCG <5000 (best results), no fetal cardiac activity on US, ectopic mass <3.5 cm, reliable patient for follow-up. Folic acid antagonist → inhibits trophoblastic proliferation. Monitor β-hCG to zero. Contraindicated: hepatic/renal disease, immunodeficiency, breastfeeding.

choices.text: Any ectopic regardless of size or stability
choices.feedback: MTX has specific criteria and contraindications.
Instability = ruptured ectopic → emergent surgery, not MTX.

choices.text: Only if β-hCG is declining spontaneously
choices.feedback: Declining β-hCG may qualify for expectant management instead of MTX.

questions.explanation: Methotrexate (single-dose IM protocol) is appropriate for hemodynamically stable patients with an unruptured ectopic, β-hCG ideally <5000 mIU/mL (higher levels associated with treatment failure), no fetal cardiac activity on TVUS, and tubal mass <3.5-4 cm. Methotrexate inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, blocking rapidly dividing trophoblastic cells. It is not appropriate for any ectopic regardless of parameters. Hemodynamically unstable patients require emergent surgical intervention (salpingectomy or salpingostomy). Spontaneously declining β-hCG may be managed expectantly without methotrexate.

questions.stem: Normal intrauterine pregnancy β-hCG doubling time?

choices.feedback: Correct! Normal early IUP: β-hCG doubles approximately every 48h. Abnormal rise (<35% increase in 48h) suggests ectopic or nonviable pregnancy. Declining β-hCG suggests miscarriage. Used for pregnancy of unknown location (PUL) to determine viability and location.

choices.text: β-hCG doesn't change in normal pregnancy
choices.feedback: β-hCG rises steadily in early pregnancy, peaking at 8-10 weeks.
Weekly is too slow for normal doubling.
24h is too fast for normal β-hCG doubling.

questions.explanation: In a viable early intrauterine pregnancy, β-hCG approximately doubles every 48 hours (minimum 35% rise in 48 hours is considered normal). A slower-than-expected rise suggests ectopic pregnancy or nonviable intrauterine pregnancy, while a decline suggests pregnancy failure. Serial β-hCG monitoring is essential when the initial ultrasound is inconclusive. β-hCG levels plateau and begin declining around 10-12 weeks as the placenta takes over hormone production. Doubling does not occur every 24 hours (too fast) or every week (too slow).

questions.stem: #1 risk factor for ectopic pregnancy?

choices.feedback: OCPs are protective against pregnancy and thus ectopic.
Smoking impairs tubal motility but is not the highest risk factor.
Correct! Prior ectopic = 10-25% recurrence risk (strongest risk factor). Other major risks: PID/STI (Chlamydia causes tubal scarring), prior tubal surgery, IVF, smoking, DES exposure, endometriosis.
Age mildly increases risk but is not the #1 factor.

questions.explanation: Prior ectopic pregnancy is the strongest single risk factor for subsequent ectopic, with a recurrence rate of approximately 10-25%. Other major risk factors include prior tubal surgery, PID/salpingitis history, tubal pathology, IVF, smoking, and DES exposure. The common mechanism is tubal damage impairing embryo transport. OCP use actually decreases overall pregnancy risk (including ectopic). Smoking is a significant risk factor but not the #1. Advanced maternal age is a minor risk factor compared to prior ectopic.

questions.stem: Rh-negative patient with ectopic pregnancy should receive:

choices.feedback: Folate prevents neural tube defects but isn't specifically indicated for ectopic.
Transfusion only if hemodynamically unstable from hemorrhage.
Rh status is always relevant in pregnancy-related bleeding.

choices.text: RhoGAM (anti-D immunoglobulin) to prevent Rh sensitization
choices.feedback: Correct! ALL Rh-negative women with any pregnancy event (ectopic, miscarriage, abortion, trauma, delivery) should receive RhoGAM within 72h to prevent Rh isoimmunization. Standard dose: IM. Prevents formation of anti-D antibodies that could cause hemolytic disease in future pregnancies.

questions.explanation: All Rh-negative women with ectopic pregnancy (or any pregnancy event involving potential fetomaternal hemorrhage) should receive RhoGAM (anti-D immunoglobulin, 50 mcg for first trimester, 300 mcg after 12 weeks) to prevent Rh alloimmunization. Without RhoGAM, an Rh-negative mother exposed to Rh-positive fetal blood will develop anti-D antibodies, which could cause hemolytic disease of the fetus/newborn (HDFN) in subsequent pregnancies. This applies to all ectopic pregnancies regardless of management approach (medical, surgical, or expectant). Folate is given with methotrexate recovery, not for Rh prophylaxis.

questions.stem: After methotrexate for ectopic, β-hCG should decline by ≥15% between:

choices.feedback: Too early — β-hCG may initially rise after MTX.
Day 4 to 7 comparison is the standard monitoring window.
Monitoring starts earlier than this.

Correct! β-hCG often RISES initially (days 1-4) as trophoblast lyses. Measure on days 4 and 7. If ≥15% decline from day 4 to 7 → successful response → weekly monitoring until zero. If <15% decline → repeat MTX dose or surgery. Treatment failure rate: ~10-15%.

questions.explanation: After methotrexate treatment for ectopic pregnancy, β-hCG should decline by ≥15% between days 4 and 7 post-injection, indicating successful treatment. β-hCG often rises initially between days 1-4 (expected due to continued trophoblast activity before methotrexate effect), so early measurement may be falsely reassuring or alarming. If β-hCG does not decline ≥15% by day 7, a second dose of methotrexate or surgical intervention is needed. Weekly β-hCG monitoring continues until undetectable. Days 1-2 is too early. Days 14-21 would delay recognition of treatment failure.

questions.stem: IUD and ectopic pregnancy — the relationship is:

choices.text: Only hormonal IUDs affect ectopic risk
choices.feedback: Both copper and hormonal IUDs have similar ectopic pregnancy considerations.
IUDs reduce overall pregnancy risk but don't eliminate ectopic risk entirely.
IUDs don't cause ectopics — they prevent intrauterine pregnancy.

choices.text: If pregnancy occurs WITH IUD in place, higher proportion are ectopic
choices.feedback: Correct! IUDs are highly effective at preventing intrauterine pregnancy but less effective at preventing ectopic. If a patient gets pregnant with an IUD, there's a higher proportion of ectopics (not higher absolute risk vs no contraception). Most IUD pregnancies are still intrauterine, but always evaluate for ectopic.

questions.explanation: IUDs are highly effective contraceptives that dramatically reduce overall pregnancy risk (including ectopic). However, IF pregnancy occurs with an IUD in place, a higher proportion of those pregnancies are ectopic compared to pregnancies without an IUD. This is because IUDs are more effective at preventing intrauterine implantation than tubal implantation. IUDs do not cause ectopic pregnancies — they prevent pregnancies overall. This distinction is important for patient counseling. Both hormonal and copper IUDs have this pattern, not only hormonal IUDs.

questions.stem: The 300 method for calculating heart rate uses:

choices.feedback: The 300 method is an independent EKG-based calculation.
The 300 method divides, not multiplies.
PR interval is used for assessing heart block, not heart rate.

choices.text: 300 ÷ number of large boxes between R waves
choices.feedback: Correct! Count large boxes between two consecutive R waves → 300 ÷ boxes = HR. Quick markers: 1 box = 300, 2 = 150, 3 = 100, 4 = 75, 5 = 60, 6 = 50. Only accurate for regular rhythms. For irregular rhythms: count R waves in a 6-second strip × 10.

questions.explanation: The 300 method calculates heart rate by dividing 300 by the number of large boxes (each = 0.2 seconds) between two consecutive R waves. For example, 2 large boxes = 150 bpm, 3 = 100, 4 = 75, 5 = 60, 6 = 50. This works for regular rhythms. For irregular rhythms, count R waves in 6 seconds (30 large boxes) and multiply by 10. The method works because 300 large boxes = 60 seconds, so 300 ÷ R-R interval in large boxes = beats per minute.

questions.stem: Diffuse ST elevation with PR depression and no reciprocal changes suggests:

choices.feedback: Hyperkalemia shows peaked T waves, widened QRS, and eventually sine wave — not diffuse ST elevation.
Brugada shows coved ST elevation in V1-V3 only, not diffuse.
STEMI has territorial ST elevation (corresponding to a coronary artery) WITH reciprocal ST depression in other leads.

Correct! Pericarditis: diffuse concave-up (smiley face) ST elevation in multiple non-contiguous leads, PR depression (most specific finding, best in II), NO reciprocal changes. STEMI: convex-up ST elevation in contiguous leads with reciprocal depression. This distinction is high-yield.

questions.explanation: Diffuse ST elevation with PR depression (PR segment depression in most leads, PR elevation in aVR) in the absence of reciprocal ST depression is the classic ECG pattern of acute pericarditis. STEMI shows ST elevation in a coronary territory distribution with reciprocal ST depression in contiguous leads. Hyperkalemia shows peaked T waves, widened QRS, and eventually sine wave pattern. Brugada syndrome shows coved ST elevation in V1-V3 with right bundle branch block pattern. The four stages of pericarditis ECG changes should be recognized.

questions.stem: WPW (Wolff-Parkinson-White) is characterized by:

choices.feedback: WPW has a SHORT PR interval (the accessory pathway bypasses the AV node delay).
Sawtooth = atrial flutter.

choices.text: Short PR + delta wave + wide QRS
choices.feedback: Correct! WPW: accessory pathway (Bundle of Kent) bypasses the AV node → early ventricular depolarization. Short PR (<120 ms), delta wave (slurred QRS upstroke), wide QRS. Danger: if A-fib develops, rapid conduction through accessory pathway → VF. Treatment of A-fib in WPW: procainamide or cardioversion. AVOID AV nodal blockers (digoxin, verapamil, adenosine).

choices.text: Peaked T waves + absent P waves
choices.feedback: Peaked T waves = hyperkalemia. Absent P waves = A-fib.

questions.explanation: Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome is characterized by a short PR interval (<120 ms, reflecting early ventricular activation via accessory pathway bypassing the AV node), delta wave (slurred upstroke of the QRS from pre-excitation), and wide QRS complex (>100 ms from fusion of normal and accessory pathway conduction). WPW carries risk of re-entrant tachycardia and, critically, rapid ventricular response in atrial fibrillation via the accessory pathway. Prolonged PR suggests AV block. Sawtooth pattern describes atrial flutter. Peaked T waves suggest hyperkalemia.

questions.stem: New LBBB with chest pain should be treated as:

choices.feedback: LBBB is a conduction abnormality, not an arrhythmia.
PE has different EKG findings (S1Q3T3, right heart strain).

Correct! New LBBB in the setting of ischemic symptoms is considered a STEMI equivalent because LBBB obscures ST-segment analysis. These patients should receive emergent reperfusion therapy (PCI). Old/chronic LBBB does not warrant emergent PCI.
New LBBB + chest pain is potentially a STEMI equivalent and should not be observed.

questions.explanation: New left bundle branch block (LBBB) in the setting of chest pain is treated as a STEMI equivalent requiring emergent percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI). New LBBB can mask ST-segment changes of acute MI, so its new appearance with ischemic symptoms warrants emergent catheterization. Sgarbossa criteria can help identify STEMI in the presence of LBBB but should not delay treatment when clinical suspicion is high. This is not atrial fibrillation, pulmonary embolism, or a benign finding — new LBBB with chest pain is an emergency.

questions.stem: Torsades de pointes treatment:

choices.feedback: Correct! IV magnesium sulfate 2g IV is first-line for torsades de pointes. It stabilizes cardiac membrane and suppresses early afterdepolarizations. For refractory cases: overdrive pacing (increases HR → shortens QT). Also correct underlying cause (stop QT-prolonging drugs, replace K+/Mg²+). AVOID amiodarone, procainamide, sotalol — all prolong QT.
Amiodarone PROLONGS QT and can WORSEN torsades. Absolutely contraindicated.
If unstable/pulseless, defibrillate. But first-line pharmacotherapy is magnesium. Synchronized cardioversion is for stable re-entrant SVTs.
Adenosine is for SVT, not ventricular arrhythmias.

questions.explanation: IV magnesium sulfate (1-2g IV over 5-20 minutes) is the first-line treatment for Torsades de Pointes (TdP), a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia occurring in the setting of prolonged QT interval. Magnesium stabilizes cardiac membrane and shortens the QT interval. For unstable TdP, immediate defibrillation is needed. Isoproterenol or temporary overdrive pacing can be used for refractory cases by increasing heart rate and shortening QT. Amiodarone should be AVOIDED — it prolongs QT and can worsen TdP. Adenosine is used for SVT, not ventricular tachycardia.

questions.stem: First EKG change in hyperkalemia?

choices.feedback: Delta waves indicate WPW, not hyperkalemia.
ST elevation suggests ischemia or pericarditis, not hyperkalemia.
Wide QRS occurs later in the progression. The earliest change is different.

Correct! Hyperkalemia EKG progression: peaked T waves (earliest, K+ ~5.5-6.5) → PR prolongation → P wave flattening/loss → QRS widening → sine wave → VF/asystole. First intervention: IV calcium gluconate (cardioprotective — stabilizes membrane). Does NOT lower K+; add insulin/glucose and other shifting agents.

questions.explanation: Peaked, narrow, tall T waves are the earliest ECG change in hyperkalemia (K+ ~5.5-6.5 mEq/L). As potassium rises further: PR prolongation and P wave flattening (6.5-7.5), QRS widening (7.0-8.0), sine wave pattern (>8.0), and eventually ventricular fibrillation/asystole. Delta waves are seen in WPW syndrome. ST elevation occurs in MI or pericarditis. QRS widening occurs with more severe hyperkalemia but is not the first change.

questions.stem: MaRRoW/WiLLiaM mnemonics help identify:

choices.feedback: Correct! MaRRoW = RBBB: M-shaped (rsR') in right precordial leads (V1-V2), W-shaped (wide S) in lateral leads (I, V6). WiLLiaM = LBBB: W-shaped in V1, M-shaped (broad notched R) in lateral leads (I, aVL, V5-V6). QRS must be >120 ms for either diagnosis.
Heart blocks are classified by PR interval and P-QRS relationship.
Flutter has sawtooth pattern; A-fib has absent P waves with irregular R-R.
ST patterns relate to ischemia/infarction, not bundle branch blocks.

questions.explanation: MaRRoW and WiLLiaM are mnemonics for distinguishing right and left bundle branch blocks in leads V1 and V6. MaRRoW = in RBBB, V1 shows M-shaped rsR' pattern (two peaks) and V6 shows W-shaped broad S wave. WiLLiaM = in LBBB, V1 shows W-shaped QS/rS pattern (deep S wave) and V6 shows M-shaped broad R wave with notched peak. These mnemonics do not identify heart block types, atrial flutter/fibrillation, or ST elevation patterns.

questions.stem: A-fib with WPW should NOT be treated with which drug?

choices.feedback: Cardioversion is safe and appropriate if the patient is hemodynamically unstable.
Procainamide is actually one of the preferred treatments — it slows accessory pathway conduction.

Correct! In A-fib with WPW, blocking the AV node forces ALL conduction through the accessory pathway → extremely rapid ventricular response → VF → cardiac arrest. NEVER give digoxin, verapamil, diltiazem, or adenosine. Safe options: procainamide (slows accessory pathway), cardioversion (if unstable).
Amiodarone can be considered cautiously but AV nodal blockers are the critical contraindication.

questions.explanation: In atrial fibrillation with WPW, AV nodal blocking agents (adenosine, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, digoxin) are strictly contraindicated. Blocking the AV node removes the 'braking' effect on ventricular rate, forcing all conduction down the unregulated accessory pathway, which can precipitate ventricular fibrillation and cardiac arrest. Electrical cardioversion is the treatment of choice for unstable patients. Procainamide is safe (slows accessory pathway conduction). IV amiodarone is also considered acceptable by some guidelines, though procainamide is preferred.

questions.stem: Classic presentation of GBS?

choices.text: Ascending symmetric weakness with areflexia following a recent infection
choices.feedback: Correct! Classic GBS: 1-4 weeks post-infection → ascending symmetric weakness (legs → arms → respiratory → cranial nerves) + areflexia/hyporeflexia (LMN). Campylobacter jejuni is the #1 infectious trigger.

choices.text: Descending paralysis starting with cranial nerves
choices.feedback: Descending = botulism or Miller Fisher variant, not classic GBS.

choices.text: Fluctuating weakness worse at end of day
choices.feedback: Fatigable weakness = myasthenia gravis, not GBS.

choices.text: Unilateral facial droop with forehead sparing
choices.feedback: Upper motor neuron facial palsy (stroke). GBS can cause bilateral facial weakness.

questions.explanation: GBS classically presents with ascending symmetric weakness beginning in the distal lower extremities and progressing proximally (legs→trunk→arms→face/respiratory muscles), with areflexia/hyporeflexia, typically 1-4 weeks after a preceding infection (upper respiratory or gastrointestinal). Sensory symptoms may occur but motor weakness predominates. Autonomic dysfunction (tachycardia, BP fluctuations, urinary retention) occurs in ~70%. The weakness is symmetric, distinguishing it from focal neurologic conditions.

questions.stem: CSF findings in GBS?

choices.feedback: Low glucose + high WBC = bacterial meningitis.
Elevated WBC suggests infection (meningitis), not GBS.

Correct! Classic CSF finding in GBS. Elevated protein from inflammation-related breakdown of blood-nerve barrier. Normal cell count distinguishes from infectious processes. May be normal in the first week — repeat LP if suspicion remains high.
While possible early, elevated protein with normal cells is the classic finding.

questions.explanation: Albuminocytologic dissociation — elevated CSF protein with normal cell count — is the hallmark CSF finding in GBS. Protein elevation reflects blood-nerve barrier disruption and demyelination. Cell count remains normal because GBS is not an infectious or inflammatory meningeal process. Note that CSF may be normal in the first week; the classic finding appears after 1-2 weeks. Elevated WBC in CSF should prompt consideration of alternative diagnoses (HIV polyradiculopathy, Lyme disease, CMV).

questions.stem: When should you intubate a GBS patient?

choices.text: Only when the patient becomes unresponsive
choices.feedback: Waiting for unresponsiveness risks respiratory arrest.

choices.text: FVC <20 mL/kg or NIF < -30 cmH2O (20/30 rule)
choices.feedback: Correct! Monitor pulmonary function serially. Intubate when FVC <target rate or NIF (negative inspiratory force) weaker than -30 cmH2O. Also intubate for: rapid decline in FVC (>30% drop), bulbar dysfunction with aspiration risk, inability to cough effectively. 30% of GBS patients need ventilation.

choices.text: Based on arterial blood gas showing CO2 retention
choices.feedback: CO2 retention is a very late finding. FVC/NIF monitoring catches decline earlier.
SpO2 drops LATE in neuromuscular respiratory failure — waiting for desaturation is too late.

questions.explanation: The 20/30 rule for intubation in GBS: intubate when forced vital capacity (FVC) drops below 20 mL/kg or negative inspiratory force (NIF) is weaker than -30 cmH2O. These thresholds indicate respiratory muscle weakness insufficient to maintain adequate ventilation. Approximately 30% of GBS patients require mechanical ventilation. Serial bedside pulmonary function monitoring (FVC, NIF) every 4-6 hours is essential. Do not rely solely on oxygen saturation — it may remain normal until respiratory arrest is imminent.

questions.stem: Which treatment is NOT effective in GBS?

choices.feedback: IVIG is first-line treatment for GBS.
Supportive care is essential for GBS management.
Plasmapheresis is equally effective as IVIG for GBS.

Correct! HIGH-YIELD: Steroids are NOT effective in GBS (unlike most other autoimmune conditions). Multiple trials show no benefit. IVIG + steroids = no additional benefit. Do NOT give steroids for GBS. This distinguishes GBS from CIDP (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), where steroids ARE effective.

questions.explanation: Corticosteroids are NOT effective in GBS and may even worsen outcomes — multiple randomized trials have shown no benefit. This distinguishes GBS from CIDP (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), which does respond to steroids. First-line GBS treatments are IVIG (0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days) or plasmapheresis (5 exchanges over 1-2 weeks), both equally effective. Treatment should begin within 2 weeks of symptom onset for maximum benefit.

questions.stem: Miller Fisher variant of GBS presents with:

choices.feedback: Systemic inflammatory signs suggest other autoimmune conditions, not GBS.

Correct! Miller Fisher triad: ophthalmoplegia (eye movement paralysis) + ataxia + areflexia. Associated with anti-GQ1b antibodies. Descending pattern (starts cranially). Generally better prognosis than classic AIDP. Treat with IVIG or plasmapheresis.
CNS symptoms suggest encephalitis, not GBS variants.
That's classic GBS. Miller Fisher is different.

questions.explanation: Miller Fisher syndrome (MFS) is a GBS variant characterized by the triad of ophthalmoplegia (eye movement paralysis), ataxia (cerebellar-type gait unsteadiness), and areflexia. It is strongly associated with anti-GQ1b antibodies (~85% positive). MFS accounts for ~5% of GBS cases and generally has an excellent prognosis. Unlike typical GBS, limb weakness is usually absent or mild. Anti-GQ1b antibodies target gangliosides concentrated in cranial nerves III, IV, VI and cerebellar neurons.

questions.stem: #1 infectious trigger for GBS?

choices.feedback: Influenza can trigger GBS but is not the #1 cause.

Correct! C. jejuni gastroenteritis precedes ~30% of GBS cases. The lipopolysaccharides on C. jejuni resemble gangliosides on peripheral nerves → molecular mimicry → autoimmune attack. C. jejuni-associated GBS tends to have worse prognosis (often axonal variant — AMAN).
E. coli is a common GI pathogen but not a major GBS trigger.
S. aureus is not typically associated with GBS.

questions.explanation: Campylobacter jejuni gastroenteritis is the most common infectious trigger for GBS, preceding approximately 25-40% of cases. The mechanism involves molecular mimicry between Campylobacter lipooligosaccharides and gangliosides (GM1, GD1a, GQ1b) on peripheral nerve myelin/axons, triggering autoimmune cross-reactivity. Other triggers include CMV, EBV, Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Zika virus, and influenza. GBS can also occur post-vaccination, though the absolute risk is extremely low.

questions.stem: Why should IVIG and plasmapheresis NOT be combined?

choices.text: Plasmapheresis removes the IVIG antibodies that were just infused
choices.feedback: Correct! Plasmapheresis filters out plasma proteins, including the therapeutic immunoglobulins from IVIG. Combining them is counterproductive. Choose one or the other. If one fails, can switch to the alternative. IVIG is generally preferred due to easier administration.
While IVIG can rarely cause renal issues, this is not the reason to avoid combining.

choices.text: They cause a dangerous drug interaction
choices.feedback: No pharmacologic interaction — the issue is more practical.

choices.text: They have identical mechanisms so combining is redundant
choices.feedback: They have different mechanisms (IVIG = immunomodulation, PLEX = antibody removal), but combining is counterproductive because PLEX removes IVIG.

questions.explanation: IVIG and plasmapheresis should not be combined because plasmapheresis removes the immunoglobulin antibodies from circulation — including the IVIG that was just infused, negating its therapeutic benefit. Either treatment alone is equally effective. If IVIG is given first and the patient fails to improve, switching to plasmapheresis is reasonable. The reverse sequence is also acceptable. Combining them does not provide additive benefit and wastes the IVIG.

questions.stem: GBS vs botulism — key distinguishing feature?

choices.feedback: GBS ascends; botulism DESCENDS. This is the key distinction.

choices.text: GBS: ascending weakness. Botulism: descending weakness starting with cranial nerves
choices.feedback: Correct! GBS = ascending (legs → arms → respiratory). Botulism = descending (cranial nerves → arms → legs). Botulism also has: dilated/fixed pupils, autonomic dysfunction, associated with home-canned foods (C. botulinum toxin blocks ACh release). Both cause flaccid paralysis and areflexia.

choices.text: GBS has UMN signs; botulism has LMN signs
choices.feedback: Both are LMN patterns with flaccid weakness and areflexia.
Both GBS and botulism can have autonomic dysfunction.

questions.explanation: The key distinguishing feature between GBS and botulism is the direction of weakness: GBS produces ascending weakness (legs→arms→face/respiratory), while botulism produces descending weakness (cranial nerves first: diplopia, dysphagia, dysarthria→limbs→respiratory). Both can cause respiratory failure. Botulism also features dilated/fixed pupils and autonomic dysfunction from cholinergic blockade. Botulism is caused by Clostridium botulinum toxin blocking acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction.

questions.stem: Duodenal ulcer pain pattern?

choices.feedback: Spicy foods may exacerbate but the pattern is meal-timing dependent.
That's gastric ulcer — food stimulates acid directly on the ulcer.

choices.text: Improves with eating, worsens 2-3 hours postprandially and at night
choices.feedback: Correct! Food buffers acid → temporary relief. When stomach empties, acid floods duodenum → pain returns. Night pain (empty stomach) is classic. Gastric ulcer: pain worsens with eating (acid + food directly irritate).
PUD pain characteristically relates to meals/fasting.

questions.explanation: Duodenal ulcer pain characteristically improves with eating (food buffers gastric acid) and worsens 2-3 hours postprandially when the stomach empties and acid secretion remains elevated, and also at night. This differs from gastric ulcer pain, which often worsens with eating. Duodenal ulcers are associated with H. pylori (~90%) and are almost never malignant.

questions.stem: Gastric ulcers should ALWAYS be biopsied because:

choices.text: H. pylori can only be detected in gastric ulcers
choices.feedback: H. pylori can be detected from any gastric/duodenal biopsy.

choices.text: Gastric ulcers are always due to cancer
choices.feedback: Most are from H. pylori or NSAIDs, but cancer must be excluded.

Correct! Gastric ulcers can harbor adenocarcinoma. Always biopsy at EGD. Duodenal ulcers are virtually never malignant. Follow-up EGD in 8-12 weeks to document healing of gastric ulcers. Non-healing gastric ulcer = repeat biopsy (cancer concern).
Most are benign, but malignancy must be excluded.

questions.explanation: Gastric ulcers must always be biopsied because they can harbor gastric adenocarcinoma. Unlike duodenal ulcers, which are virtually never malignant, 3-5% of gastric ulcers are malignant. Multiple biopsies (6-8) from the ulcer margin are recommended. Follow-up endoscopy at 8-12 weeks is necessary to document complete healing — persistent ulcers require repeat biopsy.

questions.stem: Barrett esophagus is characterized by:

choices.feedback: Correct! Barrett = intestinal metaplasia of distal esophagus. Normal squamous epithelium replaced by columnar with goblet cells. Premalignant condition → risk of esophageal adenocarcinoma. Risk factors: chronic GERD, male, white, obesity, smoking. Requires surveillance EGD with systematic biopsy.
Eosinophils suggest eosinophilic esophagitis, not Barrett.
Hyperplasia is not the defining feature of Barrett.
Granulomas suggest Crohn disease, not Barrett.

questions.explanation: Barrett esophagus is characterized by intestinal metaplasia — replacement of normal squamous esophageal epithelium with specialized columnar epithelium containing goblet cells. It is a premalignant condition resulting from chronic GERD with risk of progression to esophageal adenocarcinoma. Surveillance endoscopy with 4-quadrant biopsies every 1-3 years depending on dysplasia status is recommended.

questions.stem: Test-of-cure for H. pylori should use:

choices.text: Urea breath test or stool antigen (≥4 weeks after treatment, ≥2 weeks off PPI)
choices.feedback: Correct! Both detect active infection. Must wait ≥4 weeks after antibiotics and ≥2 weeks off PPI (PPI suppresses H. pylori → false negative). Serology (IgG) remains positive for months-years after eradication — useless for test-of-cure.
Serology stays positive after successful treatment — cannot confirm cure.
Non-invasive tests suffice for test-of-cure unless EGD is needed for other reasons (e.g., gastric ulcer follow-up).
H. pylori is not detected by blood culture.

questions.explanation: H. pylori test-of-cure should use urea breath test (UBT) or stool antigen testing ≥4 weeks after completing eradication therapy and ≥2 weeks after stopping PPIs (PPIs reduce bacterial load causing false-negative results). Serology (IgG antibodies) should NOT be used for test-of-cure because antibodies persist for months after successful eradication.

questions.stem: Posterior duodenal ulcer that erodes posteriorly causes:

choices.feedback: Free air = anterior perforation (into peritoneal cavity).

Correct! Posterior duodenal ulcers erode into the GDA → massive upper GI hemorrhage. Anterior duodenal ulcers perforate into the peritoneal cavity → pneumoperitoneum (free air on upright CXR). This anatomic distinction is high-yield.
Posterior ulcers affect the GDA, not the portal vein.
While posterior ulcers are near the pancreas, the classic complication is GDA hemorrhage.

questions.explanation: A posterior duodenal ulcer that erodes posteriorly can hemorrhage from the gastroduodenal artery (GDA), which runs behind the first portion of the duodenum. This can cause massive, life-threatening upper GI bleeding. Anterior duodenal ulcer perforation causes peritonitis (free air). The GDA is a branch of the common hepatic artery and gives rise to the superior pancreaticoduodenal artery.

questions.stem: Long-term PPI risks include all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: PPIs can cause hypomagnesemia, sometimes severe.
PPIs increase C. diff risk by reducing gastric acid barrier.
PPIs impair acid-dependent B12 absorption.

Correct! PPIs are NOT associated with significant hepatotoxicity. Known long-term risks: C. diff, hypomagnesemia, B12 deficiency, calcium malabsorption (fractures), fundic gland polyps, CKD (rare), community-acquired pneumonia. Despite these risks, PPIs remain safe when indicated.

questions.explanation: The answer choices (not shown here) include hepatotoxicity requiring liver transplant as the exception. Long-term PPI risks include C. difficile infection, community-acquired pneumonia, hypomagnesemia, calcium malabsorption with increased fracture risk, B12 deficiency, and possible increased risk of CKD and dementia (observational data). PPIs do not cause hepatotoxicity requiring transplant.

questions.stem: Misoprostol for NSAID ulcer prevention is CONTRAINDICATED in:

choices.feedback: HTN is not a contraindication for misoprostol.
No significant interaction with antibiotics.
Age alone is not a contraindication.

Correct! Misoprostol is a prostaglandin E1 analog that promotes mucosal defense. It causes uterine contractions → abortion. Absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy or women of childbearing age without contraception. Used intentionally for medical abortion (with mifepristone). Alternative for NSAID gastroprotection: PPI co-prescription.

questions.explanation: Misoprostol (PGE1 analog used for NSAID-induced ulcer prophylaxis) is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy because it causes uterine contractions and can induce abortion. It is also used intentionally as an abortifacient in combination with mifepristone. Women of childbearing age prescribed misoprostol must have documented negative pregnancy test and reliable contraception.

questions.stem: H. pylori first-line eradication regimen?

choices.feedback: PPI alone suppresses but does not eradicate H. pylori.
Single antibiotic cannot eradicate H. pylori (resistance develops).
This is not a standard H. pylori regimen.

Correct! Triple therapy for 14 days (not 7 — longer duration improves eradication rates). Use if local clarithromycin resistance <15%. If resistance >15%, penicillin allergy, or prior macrolide exposure: bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI + bismuth + metronidazole + tetracycline × 14 days). Always confirm eradication after treatment.

questions.explanation: First-line H. pylori eradication is triple therapy: PPI (twice daily) + clarithromycin 500 mg BID + amoxicillin 1000 mg BID × 14 days. In areas with >15% clarithromycin resistance, bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI + bismuth subsalicylate + metronidazole + tetracycline × 14 days) is preferred. Concomitant therapy (PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin + metronidazole) is also an option.

questions.stem: Melena indicates bleeding from:

choices.feedback: Melena (black tarry stool) = upper GI. Blood is digested during transit.

Correct! Melena = oxidized hemoglobin from upper GI bleed. Only ~100-200 mL blood needed. As little as the duodenum can produce melena. Right-sided colonic bleeds can occasionally cause melena if transit is slow.
Hemorrhoids cause bright red blood per rectum, not melena.
Rectal bleeding is hematochezia, not melena.

questions.explanation: Melena (black, tarry, foul-smelling stool) indicates upper GI bleeding — typically proximal to the ligament of Treitz. The black color results from bacterial degradation and oxidation of hemoglobin to hematin as blood passes through the GI tract, requiring transit time of at least 14 hours and a minimum of ~50-100 mL of blood. Melena can occasionally result from small bowel or right-sided colonic bleeding if transit is slow enough. In contrast, hematochezia (bright red blood per rectum) usually indicates lower GI bleeding but can occur with massive upper GI hemorrhage (>1 L, with rapid transit). Hematemesis (bloody or coffee-ground vomiting) definitively localizes bleeding above the ligament of Treitz.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Melena (black, tarry, foul-smelling stool) indicates upper GI bleeding — typically proximal to the ligament of Treitz. The black color results from bacterial degradation and oxidation of hemoglobin to hematin as blood passes through the GI tract, requiring transit time of at least 14 hours and a minimum of ~50-100 mL of blood. Melena can occasionally result from small bowel or right-sided colonic bleeding if transit is slow enough. In contrast, hematochezia (bright red blood per rectum) usually indicates lower GI bleeding but can occur with massive upper GI hemorrhage (>1 L, with rapid transit). Hematemesis (bloody or coffee-ground vomiting) definitively localizes bleeding above the ligament of Treitz.

questions.stem: Restrictive transfusion threshold in GI bleeding?

choices.feedback: Only consider this threshold for patients with active CAD.

Correct! Transfuse pRBCs when Hb <7 (TRIGGER trial). Restrictive strategy improves survival, especially in variceal bleeding (excessive transfusion increases portal pressure → more bleeding). Exception: active massive hemorrhage with hemodynamic instability — transfuse to maintain perfusion.
Objective Hb threshold is used, not symptoms alone.
Liberal threshold — TRIGGER trial showed worse outcomes, especially in variceal bleeding.

questions.explanation: The restrictive transfusion threshold in GI bleeding is hemoglobin <7 g/dL for most patients. This is based on the TRIGGER trial and other evidence showing that restrictive transfusion (Hb <7) is non-inferior or superior to liberal transfusion (Hb <9) in terms of mortality and rebleeding, particularly in variceal bleeding. Over-transfusion in variceal bleeding can worsen portal hypertension and increase rebleeding risk. Exceptions where a higher threshold (~8-9 g/dL) may be appropriate include patients with acute coronary syndrome, symptomatic anemia, or massive hemorrhage with hemodynamic instability requiring resuscitation.
questions.verifiedExplanation: The restrictive transfusion threshold in GI bleeding is hemoglobin <7 g/dL for most patients. This is based on the TRIGGER trial and other evidence showing that restrictive transfusion (Hb <7) is non-inferior or superior to liberal transfusion (Hb <9) in terms of mortality and rebleeding, particularly in variceal bleeding. Over-transfusion in variceal bleeding can worsen portal hypertension and increase rebleeding risk. Exceptions where a higher threshold (~8-9 g/dL) may be appropriate include patients with acute coronary syndrome, symptomatic anemia, or massive hemorrhage with hemodynamic instability requiring resuscitation.

questions.stem: #1 cause of upper GI bleeding?

choices.feedback: Correct! PUD accounts for ~40% of upper GIB. H. pylori and NSAIDs are the two main causes. Endoscopic therapy + high-dose PPI + H. pylori eradication (if positive). Duodenal ulcers bleed from the gastroduodenal artery; gastric ulcers from the left gastric artery.
MW tears (mucosal tear at GE junction from forceful vomiting) account for ~5-10%.
Malignancy is an uncommon cause of acute upper GIB.
Varices are #1 in cirrhotic patients but not overall.

questions.explanation: Peptic ulcer disease (PUD) is the most common cause of upper GI bleeding, accounting for approximately 40-50% of cases. The two major risk factors are Helicobacter pylori infection and NSAID use. Other causes of upper GI bleeding include esophageal/gastric varices (~10-20%), Mallory-Weiss tears (~5-10%), erosive gastritis/esophagitis, Dieulafoy lesions, and malignancy. Urgent EGD (within 24 hours of presentation) is both diagnostic and therapeutic. High-risk stigmata on endoscopy (active bleeding, visible vessel, adherent clot) require endoscopic intervention (thermal coagulation, hemoclips, injection therapy).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Peptic ulcer disease (PUD) is the most common cause of upper GI bleeding, accounting for approximately 40-50% of cases. The two major risk factors are Helicobacter pylori infection and NSAID use. Other causes of upper GI bleeding include esophageal/gastric varices (~10-20%), Mallory-Weiss tears (~5-10%), erosive gastritis/esophagitis, Dieulafoy lesions, and malignancy. Urgent EGD (within 24 hours of presentation) is both diagnostic and therapeutic. High-risk stigmata on endoscopy (active bleeding, visible vessel, adherent clot) require endoscopic intervention (thermal coagulation, hemoclips, injection therapy).

questions.stem: In variceal bleeding, which medication reduces mortality?

choices.feedback: TXA has not shown clear benefit in GI bleeding (HALT-IT trial).
PPIs are for ulcer bleeding, not effective for varices.
Calcium is given during massive transfusion but doesn't specifically reduce variceal mortality.

Correct! Prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone7 days) reduce bacterial infections, rebleeding, AND mortality in variceal hemorrhage. Always part of the variceal bleed bundle: octreotide + antibiotics + EBL. This is one of the most important evidence-based interventions.

questions.explanation: In variceal bleeding, IV ceftriaxone (or norfloxacin) reduces mortality. Prophylactic antibiotics in cirrhotic patients with GI bleeding reduce bacterial infections (spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, UTI, pneumonia) and independently reduce mortality and rebleeding rates. Ceftriaxone 1g IV daily for 7 days is the preferred regimen. Other key management components include: IV octreotide (splanchnic vasoconstriction to reduce portal pressure), urgent EGD with band ligation (within 12 hours), IV proton pump inhibitor, and transfusion with restrictive threshold (Hb <7, to avoid worsening portal hypertension). TIPS is considered for refractory or recurrent variceal bleeding.
questions.verifiedExplanation: In variceal bleeding, IV ceftriaxone (or norfloxacin) reduces mortality. Prophylactic antibiotics in cirrhotic patients with GI bleeding reduce bacterial infections (spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, UTI, pneumonia) and independently reduce mortality and rebleeding rates. Ceftriaxone 1g IV daily for 7 days is the preferred regimen. Other key management components include: IV octreotide (splanchnic vasoconstriction to reduce portal pressure), urgent EGD with band ligation (within 12 hours), IV proton pump inhibitor, and transfusion with restrictive threshold (Hb <7, to avoid worsening portal hypertension). TIPS is considered for refractory or recurrent variceal bleeding.

questions.stem: #1 cause of lower GI bleeding in adults over 60?

choices.feedback: Ischemic colitis causes bloody diarrhea with pain, usually self-limited.
Hemorrhoids are the most common cause of minor rectal bleeding but not significant lower GIB.
Cancer causes chronic/occult bleeding more than acute hemorrhage.

Correct! Diverticulosis is the #1 cause of significant lower GIB in older adults. Painless, large-volume hematochezia. 80% self-limited. Left-sided diverticula are more common, but RIGHT-sided diverticula bleed more often. Angiodysplasia is #2.

questions.explanation: Diverticulosis is the most common cause of lower GI bleeding in adults over 60 years. Diverticular hemorrhage results from rupture of the vasa recta (arteriolar branches) at the dome or neck of a diverticulum. Bleeding is typically painless, acute, and can be massive but is self-limited in approximately 70-80% of cases. Diverticula are most common in the left colon (sigmoid), but bleeding diverticula are more frequently right-sided. Other common causes of lower GI bleeding include angiodysplasia (most common cause in elderly after diverticulosis), hemorrhoids (most common cause overall if including minor bleeding), ischemic colitis, and colorectal neoplasia.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Diverticulosis is the most common cause of lower GI bleeding in adults over 60 years. Diverticular hemorrhage results from rupture of the vasa recta (arteriolar branches) at the dome or neck of a diverticulum. Bleeding is typically painless, acute, and can be massive but is self-limited in approximately 70-80% of cases. Diverticula are most common in the left colon (sigmoid), but bleeding diverticula are more frequently right-sided. Other common causes of lower GI bleeding include angiodysplasia (most common cause in elderly after diverticulosis), hemorrhoids (most common cause overall if including minor bleeding), ischemic colitis, and colorectal neoplasia.

questions.stem: Heyde syndrome is the association between:

choices.feedback: That's portal hypertension, not Heyde syndrome.

choices.text: Peptic ulcer disease and H. pylori
choices.feedback: That's a direct causative relationship, not Heyde syndrome.
Gallstone pancreatitis is a separate entity.

choices.text: Aortic stenosis and GI bleeding from angiodysplasia
choices.feedback: Correct! Heyde syndrome: aortic stenosis → acquired von Willebrand syndrome (high shear forces destroy vWF multimers) → bleeding from angiodysplasia (AVM). Resolves with aortic valve replacement. Classic boards association.

questions.explanation: Heyde syndrome is the association between aortic stenosis (AS) and GI bleeding from angiodysplasia. The mechanism involves acquired von Willebrand syndrome type 2A: high shear stress across the stenotic aortic valve causes proteolytic cleavage of large von Willebrand factor (vWF) multimers, which are essential for hemostasis particularly in the low-shear venous environment of angiodysplastic vessels. This creates a specific bleeding diathesis that targets angiodysplasia. Resolution of bleeding occurs after aortic valve replacement, which eliminates the high-shear environment and allows vWF multimers to recover. Heyde syndrome is an important diagnostic consideration in elderly patients with AS and recurrent GI bleeding.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Heyde syndrome is the association between aortic stenosis (AS) and GI bleeding from angiodysplasia. The mechanism involves acquired von Willebrand syndrome type 2A: high shear stress across the stenotic aortic valve causes proteolytic cleavage of large von Willebrand factor (vWF) multimers, which are essential for hemostasis particularly in the low-shear venous environment of angiodysplastic vessels. This creates a specific bleeding diathesis that targets angiodysplasia. Resolution of bleeding occurs after aortic valve replacement, which eliminates the high-shear environment and allows vWF multimers to recover. Heyde syndrome is an important diagnostic consideration in elderly patients with AS and recurrent GI bleeding.

questions.stem: Massive hematochezia with hemodynamic instability — first step?

choices.feedback: Barium enema is obsolete for acute GI bleeding evaluation.
Hemodynamic instability requires urgent intervention, not observation.

choices.text: Resuscitate + consider upper GI source (EGD first)
choices.feedback: Correct! Massive upper GIB can present as hematochezia (rapid transit). With hemodynamic instability, always consider upper source first — EGD before colonoscopy. Simultaneously resuscitate with 2 large-bore IVs, crystalloid, type & crossmatch, transfuse if Hb <7.
Colonoscopy requires prep and hemodynamic stability.

questions.explanation: Massive hematochezia (bright red blood per rectum) with hemodynamic instability requires immediate resuscitation (large-bore IV access, volume resuscitation, blood products) and consideration of an upper GI source. Up to 10-15% of apparent lower GI bleeding is actually from a brisk upper GI hemorrhage — bright red blood can result from massive upper GI bleeding with rapid transit. Therefore, EGD should be performed first (or nasogastric lavage to assess for upper source) before assuming a lower GI etiology. Once upper GI source is excluded, CT angiography or tagged RBC scan can localize the bleeding site, followed by colonoscopy or angiographic intervention.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Massive hematochezia (bright red blood per rectum) with hemodynamic instability requires immediate resuscitation (large-bore IV access, volume resuscitation, blood products) and consideration of an upper GI source. Up to 10-15% of apparent lower GI bleeding is actually from a brisk upper GI hemorrhage — bright red blood can result from massive upper GI bleeding with rapid transit. Therefore, EGD should be performed first (or nasogastric lavage to assess for upper source) before assuming a lower GI etiology. Once upper GI source is excluded, CT angiography or tagged RBC scan can localize the bleeding site, followed by colonoscopy or angiographic intervention.

questions.stem: Forrest Ia ulcer classification means:

choices.feedback: Clean base = Forrest III (very low rebleed risk ~5%).
Adherent clot = Forrest IIb.
Visible vessel = Forrest IIa.

Correct! Forrest classification: Ia = spurting (highest risk ~90% rebleed without treatment), Ib = oozing, IIa = visible vessel (50% rebleed), IIb = adherent clot, IIc = flat spot, III = clean base (5% rebleed). Ia/Ib/IIa require endoscopic therapy. IIb controversial. IIc/III = medical management only.

questions.explanation: Forrest classification categorizes peptic ulcer bleeding stigmata by rebleeding risk: Ia = active spurting arterial hemorrhage (highest risk, ~90% rebleeding rate without treatment), Ib = active oozing, IIa = visible vessel (non-bleeding), IIb = adherent clot, IIc = flat pigmented spot, III = clean-base ulcer (lowest risk, ~5%). Forrest Ia (spurting) and Ib (oozing) represent active hemorrhage requiring immediate endoscopic intervention. IIa (visible vessel) also has high rebleeding risk (~50%) and requires treatment. IIb (adherent clot) is controversial — some guidelines recommend clot removal with treatment of the underlying lesion. IIc and III can generally be managed medically.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Forrest classification categorizes peptic ulcer bleeding stigmata by rebleeding risk: Ia = active spurting arterial hemorrhage (highest risk, ~90% rebleeding rate without treatment), Ib = active oozing, IIa = visible vessel (non-bleeding), IIb = adherent clot, IIc = flat pigmented spot, III = clean-base ulcer (lowest risk, ~5%). Forrest Ia (spurting) and Ib (oozing) represent active hemorrhage requiring immediate endoscopic intervention. IIa (visible vessel) also has high rebleeding risk (~50%) and requires treatment. IIb (adherent clot) is controversial — some guidelines recommend clot removal with treatment of the underlying lesion. IIc and III can generally be managed medically.

questions.stem: Joint aspiration of an acutely swollen 1st MTP shows needle-shaped, negatively birefringent crystals. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Hydroxyapatite crystals are not birefringent under polarized microscopy and cause calcific tendinitis, not classic crystal arthropathy.

Correct! Needle-shaped, strongly negatively birefringent crystals = monosodium urate (MSU) = gout. The 1st MTP (podagra) is the classic location for acute gouty arthritis. Arthrocentesis with polarized microscopy is the gold standard for diagnosis.
Septic arthritis is diagnosed by positive Gram stain/culture. Crystals are absent in purely infectious arthritis (though both can coexist).
Pseudogout shows rhomboid-shaped, weakly positively birefringent CPPD crystals, not needle-shaped negatively birefringent crystals.

questions.explanation: Needle-shaped, negatively birefringent crystals (yellow when parallel to the compensator axis) under polarized light microscopy of joint aspirate are diagnostic of gout (monosodium urate crystals). Pseudogout (CPPD) shows rhomboid, weakly positively birefringent crystals (blue when parallel). Joint aspiration is the gold standard for crystal arthropathy diagnosis and also rules out septic arthritis.

questions.stem: A patient with gout and CKD stage 4 has an acute flare. NSAIDs are contraindicated. What is the best treatment?

choices.feedback: Probenecid is contraindicated in CKD (requires adequate GFR for uricosuric effect) and is not an acute flare treatment.
Allopurinol is for chronic urate-lowering, NOT acute flare treatment. Starting ULT during a flare can worsen or prolong it.

Correct! Corticosteroids (oral prednisone burst or intra-articular injection) are the best option when NSAIDs and colchicine are contraindicated due to CKD. Intra-articular injection is preferred for monoarticular flares to minimize systemic effects.
High-dose colchicine has significant toxicity in CKD (accumulates → diarrhea, bone marrow suppression). Low-dose may be considered with dose adjustment, but steroids are safer.

questions.explanation: In a gout patient with CKD stage 4 where NSAIDs are contraindicated (nephrotoxic), corticosteroids (oral prednisone, intra-articular injection, or IV methylprednisolone) are the best treatment for acute flare. Colchicine is an alternative but requires dose adjustment in renal impairment. IL-1 inhibitors (anakinra) are reserved for refractory cases. NSAIDs and colchicine at full doses are both problematic with advanced CKD.

questions.stem: Which genetic marker should be screened before starting allopurinol in Southeast Asian and Black patients?

choices.feedback: CYP2D6 testing is relevant for drug metabolism (codeine, tamoxifen) but not allopurinol hypersensitivity.
HLA-B*5701 is screened before abacavir (HIV medication) for hypersensitivity, not allopurinol.
HLA-B27 is associated with ankylosing spondylitis and spondyloarthropathies, not allopurinol hypersensitivity.

Correct! HLA-B*5801 screening is recommended before allopurinol in Southeast Asian, Korean, and Black patients due to high carrier rates. Positive HLA-B*5801 → risk of severe allopurinol hypersensitivity syndrome (DRESS, SJS/TEN) with 25% mortality. Use febuxostat instead if positive.

questions.explanation: HLA-B*5801 should be screened before starting allopurinol in Southeast Asian and Black patients due to increased risk of severe cutaneous adverse reactions (DRESS, SJS/TEN). ACR 2020 guidelines conditionally recommend testing in all patients before starting allopurinol. If positive, allopurinol is contraindicated and febuxostat is an alternative xanthine oxidase inhibitor.

questions.stem: What radiographic finding is characteristic of CPPD (pseudogout)?

choices.feedback: Pencil-in-cup is seen in psoriatic arthritis (arthritis mutilans), not pseudogout.
This radiographic pattern is characteristic of chronic tophaceous gout, not pseudogout.

Correct! Chondrocalcinosis = linear calcification within articular cartilage (menisci, triangular fibrocartilage of wrist) and is the hallmark radiographic finding of CPPD. Most commonly seen in the knee and wrist. Triggers for acute pseudogout include surgery, illness, and metabolic derangements.
Bamboo spine is characteristic of ankylosing spondylitis (syndesmophytes bridging vertebral bodies).

questions.explanation: Chondrocalcinosis (calcification of cartilage, especially fibrocartilage of the knee menisci, triangular fibrocartilage of the wrist, and symphysis pubis) on X-ray is characteristic of CPPD (calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease/pseudogout). This radiographic finding is distinct from gout, which may show periarticular erosions with overhanging edges (Martel sign) and tophi in chronic disease.

questions.stem: Why should urate-lowering therapy NOT be initiated during an acute gout flare?

choices.feedback: The diagnosis should already be confirmed by crystal analysis before considering ULT.
While allopurinol can cause hepatotoxicity, this is not the reason for avoiding initiation during flares.

choices.text: ULT requires fasting uric acid levels
choices.feedback: While fasting can affect levels, the primary concern is clinical — destabilizing crystal deposits during active inflammation.

choices.text: Rapid uric acid changes can prolong or trigger flares
choices.feedback: Correct! Rapidly lowering uric acid during an acute attack causes crystal shedding from tophi into joints, prolonging or worsening the current flare and potentially triggering new attacks. Wait 2-4 weeks after flare resolution. When starting ULT, co-prescribe prophylactic colchicine for 3-6 months.

questions.explanation: Urate-lowering therapy (ULT — allopurinol, febuxostat) should not be initiated during an acute gout flare because rapid changes in serum uric acid (either increase or decrease) can trigger crystal dissolution/formation and paradoxically prolong or precipitate additional flares. ULT should be started after the acute flare has resolved (typically 2 weeks), with anti-inflammatory prophylaxis (colchicine or low-dose NSAID) for the first 3-6 months. However, if a patient is already on ULT during a flare, it should be continued.

questions.stem: Which condition is associated with pseudogout in younger patients and should be screened for?

choices.feedback: Marfan syndrome causes connective tissue abnormalities but is not associated with CPPD deposition.
Celiac disease is not directly linked to pseudogout.
Turner syndrome is associated with horseshoe kidney and coarctation, not crystal arthropathy.

Correct! CPPD/pseudogout in patients under 55 should prompt evaluation for underlying metabolic conditions: hemochromatosis (iron overload), hyperparathyroidism (hypercalcemia), hypomagnesemia, hypothyroidism, and Wilson disease. The 2nd-3rd MCP hooklike osteophytes are classic for hemochromatosis arthropathy.

questions.explanation: Hemochromatosis should be screened for in younger patients (particularly <60 years) presenting with pseudogout/CPPD. Iron overload promotes calcium pyrophosphate crystal formation in cartilage. Other metabolic conditions associated with early-onset CPPD include hyperparathyroidism, hypomagnesemia, and hypothyroidism. Screening includes transferrin saturation and ferritin.

questions.stem: A patient with recurrent gout attacks has a serum uric acid of 5.8 mg/dL during an acute flare. Does this rule out gout?

choices.text: No — uric acid can be normal during acute flares
choices.feedback: Correct! Serum uric acid is UNRELIABLE during acute attacks — it can be normal in up to 40% of acute flares due to cytokine-mediated uricosuria (IL-6 increases renal uric acid excretion). The gold standard is synovial fluid crystal analysis, not serum uric acid. Recheck uric acid 2-4 weeks after the flare resolves.

choices.text: Yes — any level below 6.8 mg/dL excludes gout
choices.feedback: The solubility threshold of 6.8 mg/dL is for chronic supersaturation. During acute attacks, levels fluctuate and can be falsely normal.

choices.text: Yes — uric acid must be elevated for gout diagnosis
choices.feedback: This is a common misconception. Serum uric acid can be misleadingly normal or even low during acute attacks.

choices.text: Only if the uric acid was also normal before the attack
choices.feedback: The timing of uric acid measurement matters — during flares, levels are unreliable regardless of prior values.

questions.explanation: Serum uric acid can be normal or even low during an acute gout flare due to the inflammatory cytokine surge causing uricosuric effects (increased renal excretion of uric acid). Therefore, a normal uric acid during a flare does not exclude gout. Uric acid levels should be rechecked 2-4 weeks after flare resolution for accurate assessment. Joint aspiration showing MSU crystals remains the gold standard regardless of serum uric acid.

questions.stem: Colchicine works by which mechanism?

choices.feedback: COX inhibition is the mechanism of NSAIDs (ibuprofen, indomethacin, naproxen), not colchicine.
IL-1β receptor blockade is the mechanism of anakinra, used for refractory gout. Colchicine acts upstream by disrupting microtubules.

Correct! Colchicine binds tubulin and disrupts microtubule polymerization → inhibits neutrophil chemotaxis, adhesion, and NLRP3 inflammasome activation. This prevents the inflammatory cascade triggered by MSU crystals. Most effective when started within 36 hours of flare onset.
Xanthine oxidase inhibition is the mechanism of allopurinol and febuxostat (urate-lowering therapy), not colchicine.

questions.explanation: Colchicine works by binding to tubulin and disrupting microtubule assembly in neutrophils, inhibiting neutrophil migration, chemotaxis, and phagocytosis at the site of crystal deposition. It also inhibits the NLRP3 inflammasome, reducing IL-1β production. Colchicine is most effective when given within 12-24 hours of flare onset. Side effects include GI toxicity (diarrhea, nausea, vomiting) and bone marrow suppression at high doses.

questions.stem: Classic CSF finding in GBS?

choices.feedback: Xanthochromia indicates subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Oligoclonal bands are characteristic of multiple sclerosis.

Correct! Elevated protein from nerve root inflammation + normal WBC count. This dissociation is characteristic of GBS. May be normal in the first week — repeat LP if initial is normal and clinical suspicion remains high.
That's bacterial meningitis. GBS has normal cell count.

questions.explanation: Albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated CSF protein with normal white cell count) is the characteristic CSF finding in GBS. It reflects inflammatory demyelination of nerve roots within the subarachnoid space, increasing protein content without meningeal cellular inflammation. The finding may be absent early (<1 week) and is most reliable after the first week of illness. Normal CSF early in the course does not exclude GBS.

questions.stem: Most common preceding infection in GBS?

choices.feedback: GAS → rheumatic fever/glomerulonephritis, not GBS.

Correct! C. jejuni gastroenteritis precedes ~30% of GBS cases. Molecular mimicry between bacterial lipooligosaccharides and gangliosides on peripheral nerves triggers autoimmune attack. Anti-GM1 antibodies associated with axonal variants and worse prognosis.
E. coli is associated with UTI/meningitis, not GBS.
Pneumococcus causes pneumonia/meningitis, not typically GBS.

questions.explanation: Campylobacter jejuni is the most commonly identified preceding infection in GBS, occurring in approximately 25-40% of cases. Molecular mimicry between bacterial lipooligosaccharides and peripheral nerve gangliosides triggers the autoimmune response. GBS typically manifests 1-3 weeks after the inciting infection.

questions.stem: When to intubate a GBS patient?

choices.feedback: Far too late — proactive monitoring and early intubation are essential.
Intubation is based on objective respiratory parameters, not patient preference.
Hypoxia is a LATE sign — intubate based on respiratory mechanics, not oxygenation.

Correct! The 20/30 rule: FVC <target rate or negative inspiratory force (NIF) weaker than -30 cmH2O. Monitor FVC serially q4-6h. Rapid decline (>30% over 24h) also warrants intubation. 30% of GBS patients will need mechanical ventilation.

questions.explanation: Intubation in GBS is guided by the 20/30 rule: FVC <20 mL/kg or NIF weaker than -30 cmH2O. Serial monitoring every 4-6 hours is critical as respiratory failure can progress rapidly. Approximately 30% of GBS patients require mechanical ventilation.

questions.stem: Treatment NOT effective in GBS?

choices.feedback: Supportive care including respiratory monitoring is essential.

Correct! Unlike most autoimmune conditions, corticosteroids are NOT effective in GBS and may worsen outcomes. This distinguishes GBS from CIDP (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), where steroids ARE effective. Treatment options: IVIG OR plasmapheresis (not both together).
IVIG is first-line treatment for GBS.
Plasmapheresis is equally effective as IVIG for GBS.

questions.explanation: Corticosteroids have no proven benefit in GBS and may delay recovery. Effective treatments are IVIG and plasmapheresis, which are equally effective. This distinguishes GBS from CIDP, where steroids are first-line.

questions.stem: GBS weakness pattern is:

choices.feedback: Unilateral weakness suggests stroke, not GBS.

choices.text: Descending from cranial nerves to legs
choices.feedback: Descending pattern is more typical of myasthenia gravis or botulism.

Correct! Classic ascending paralysis: starts in feet/legs → thighs → trunk → arms → cranial nerves → respiratory muscles. Bilateral and relatively symmetric. Areflexia is the hallmark physical exam finding. Peak severity reached within 2-4 weeks.
Fatigable weakness is characteristic of myasthenia gravis, not GBS.

questions.explanation: GBS weakness follows an ascending pattern, starting distally in the lower extremities and progressing proximally to the upper extremities, trunk, and cranial nerves. This reflects the peripheral nerve demyelination pattern affecting the longest nerves first. The ascending pattern helps distinguish GBS from botulism (descending) and myasthenia gravis (fluctuating, fatigable, ocular-predominant).

questions.stem: Why should IVIG and plasmapheresis NOT be combined?

choices.text: Plasmapheresis removes the infused IVIG from circulation
choices.feedback: Correct! PLEX removes plasma proteins including the therapeutic IVIG antibodies, negating its benefit. Choose one treatment. Both are equally effective as monotherapy. IVIG is often preferred due to ease of administration (no need for large-bore vascular access/apheresis machine).
The clinical rationale, not coverage, is why they shouldn't be combined.
Not the primary reason for avoiding combination.
There's no pharmacologic interaction per se.

questions.explanation: Plasmapheresis physically removes circulating immunoglobulins, including any IVIG that has been administered. Combining the two treatments is counterproductive. Either treatment alone is effective; they should be used sequentially only if the first-line choice fails.

questions.stem: Key feature distinguishing GBS from myasthenia gravis?

choices.feedback: Cholinesterase inhibitors (pyridostigmine) treat MG, not GBS.
Only myasthenic crisis requires ventilation; many MG patients are well-controlled.
GBS has areflexia/hyporeflexia; MG has normal reflexes.

Correct! GBS: ascending, areflexic, can have sensory symptoms, CSF protein elevated, preceding infection. MG: descending (starts bulbar — ptosis, diplopia, dysarthria → limbs), fatigable weakness (worsens with repetitive use), normal reflexes, no sensory involvement, anti-AChR/MuSK antibodies.

questions.explanation: Areflexia is the key feature distinguishing GBS from myasthenia gravis (MG). GBS affects peripheral nerve myelin/axons causing absent reflexes, while MG is a neuromuscular junction disorder with preserved reflexes. MG also features fatigable weakness (worsens with repetition, improves with rest), ptosis, and diplopia. GBS weakness is constant and does not fluctuate with activity. Nerve conduction studies show demyelination in GBS vs decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation in MG.

questions.stem: Prognosis of GBS — what percentage walk independently at 1 year?

choices.feedback: Correct! ~80% of GBS patients walk independently at 1 year. Most recovery occurs in the first 6-12 months. Poor prognostic factors: older age, rapid progression, need for ventilation, preceding C. jejuni infection (axonal variant), low compound muscle action potential amplitudes on EMG.
Recovery rate is higher than 50%.
GBS has a much better prognosis than this.
While most recover well, 5-10% have significant residual disability and 3-5% die.

questions.explanation: Approximately 80% of GBS patients walk independently at 1 year. GBS has generally favorable prognosis: recovery begins 2-4 weeks after symptom nadir. However, ~5% mortality persists (from respiratory failure, autonomic dysfunction, pulmonary embolism), ~10-20% have significant residual disability, and up to 5% relapse. Poor prognostic factors include older age, rapid onset, preceding Campylobacter infection, need for mechanical ventilation, and axonal variant (AMAN).

questions.stem: Most common gynecologic cancer in the US?

choices.feedback: Vulvar cancer is rare.
Cervical cancer incidence has declined significantly due to screening and HPV vaccination.

Correct! Endometrial cancer is #1 in incidence. Ovarian is #1 in mortality. Key risk factor: unopposed estrogen (obesity, PCOS, nulliparity, tamoxifen). Presentation: postmenopausal bleeding → always perform endometrial biopsy.
Ovarian is the deadliest but not the most common.

questions.explanation: Endometrial cancer is the most common gynecologic malignancy in the US, with approximately 65,000 new cases annually. Risk factors include obesity, chronic anovulation (PCOS), nulliparity, unopposed estrogen exposure, tamoxifen use, and Lynch syndrome. The most common presenting symptom is postmenopausal bleeding. Most cases are diagnosed at an early stage (type 1, endometrioid histology) with favorable prognosis (5-year survival >90% for stage I).

questions.stem: #1 cause of gynecologic cancer death?

choices.feedback: Endometrial cancer generally has good prognosis due to early detection.
Uterine sarcomas are aggressive but rare.

Correct! Ovarian cancer is the #1 GYN cancer killer because it presents late (75% stage III-IV at diagnosis). No effective screening test. Vague symptoms (bloating, satiety, pelvic pain) lead to delayed diagnosis. 5-year survival for advanced disease: ~30%.
Cervical cancer deaths declining due to Pap screening and HPV vaccination.

questions.explanation: Ovarian cancer is the leading cause of gynecologic cancer death in the US despite not being the most common gynecologic cancer. This is because most ovarian cancers present at advanced stages (III-IV) due to the absence of reliable screening methods and vague early symptoms. The 5-year survival for stage III/IV is ~30%. CA-125 is used for monitoring but is not an effective screening tool in the general population.

questions.stem: Postmenopausal bleeding warrants what evaluation?

choices.text: Reassurance — spotting is normal after menopause
choices.feedback: ANY postmenopausal bleeding is abnormal and requires evaluation.
