Correct! All postmenopausal bleeding needs evaluation to rule out endometrial cancer. Transvaginal US first: endometrial thickness ≤4mm has high negative predictive value. If >4mm or symptomatic → endometrial biopsy (office pipelle). ~10% of postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer.
Imaging alone is insufficient — tissue sampling is needed.
Pap screens for cervical cancer, not endometrial.

questions.explanation: Any postmenopausal bleeding (PMB) requires endometrial biopsy to exclude endometrial cancer, which accounts for ~10% of PMB cases (atrophy is the most common cause at 60-80%). Office endometrial biopsy (Pipelle) is the first-line tissue sampling method. Transvaginal ultrasound measuring endometrial thickness (<4 mm is reassuring) may be used as initial assessment, but biopsy is definitive. Pap smear does not adequately sample the endometrium.

questions.stem: HPV types most associated with cervical cancer?

choices.feedback: These are low-risk HPV types.
HPV 6 and 11 cause genital warts (low-risk), not cancer.
HPV 1 and 2 cause common skin warts.

Correct! HPV 16 and 18 are high-risk types responsible for ~70% of cervical cancers. HPV 16 alone accounts for ~50%. The 9-valent HPV vaccine (Gardasil 9) covers HPV 6, 11, 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, 58 — preventing ~90% of cervical cancers.

questions.explanation: HPV types 16 and 18 are responsible for approximately 70% of cervical cancers. HPV 16 is the single most oncogenic type. Both are high-risk types that integrate into the host genome, producing E6 and E7 oncoproteins that inactivate p53 and Rb tumor suppressors, respectively. The HPV vaccine (Gardasil 9) covers HPV 16, 18, plus five additional high-risk types (31, 33, 45, 52, 58) and two low-risk types (6, 11 — genital warts).

questions.stem: BRCA1/2 mutation carriers should be offered:

choices.feedback: Correct! BRCA1/2 carriers have 15-45% lifetime ovarian cancer risk. RRSO after completing childbearing reduces ovarian cancer risk by ~80% and breast cancer risk by ~50% (premenopausal oophorectomy). Also: enhanced breast screening (MRI + mammography), consider PARP inhibitors if cancer develops.
BRCA mutations don't increase cervical cancer risk.
Mammograms are important but ovarian cancer risk also needs addressing.
Chemoprevention is not standard for BRCA carriers.

questions.explanation: BRCA1/2 mutation carriers should be offered risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO), ideally by age 35-40 for BRCA1 and by age 40-45 for BRCA2. RRSO reduces ovarian cancer risk by ~80-90% and breast cancer risk by ~50% (when performed premenopausally due to estrogen reduction). Current evidence supports complete salpingectomy over tubal ligation, as many high-grade serous ovarian cancers originate from fallopian tube fimbriae. Genetic counseling and shared decision-making are essential.

questions.stem: CIN 2-3 on colposcopy-directed biopsy. Treatment?

choices.feedback: CIN 1 can be observed. CIN 2-3 requires treatment due to progression risk.
Hysterectomy is for invasive cancer, not CIN.
Topical agents are not standard for CIN treatment.

Correct! CIN 2-3 (high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion) has significant progression risk to invasive cancer. Excision: LEEP (loop electrosurgical excision) preferred, or cold knife cone. Evaluates margins. Follow-up with co-testing at 12 and 24 months. CIN 1: observe (60% regress spontaneously).

questions.explanation: CIN 2-3 (high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia/HSIL) on colposcopy-directed biopsy is treated with excisional procedures: LEEP (loop electrosurgical excision procedure) or cold knife cone biopsy. These remove the transformation zone containing the dysplastic cells and provide tissue for margin assessment. LEEP is most commonly used due to its office-based convenience. Cold knife cone is preferred when invasive cancer cannot be excluded or for adenocarcinoma in situ (AIS). CIN 1 may be observed, but CIN 2-3 requires treatment due to significant progression risk to invasive cancer.

questions.stem: Lynch syndrome increases lifetime risk of which GYN cancer to 40-60%?

choices.feedback: Lynch doesn't significantly increase cervical cancer risk.
Vulvar cancer is not a Lynch-associated cancer.
Lynch increases ovarian risk to ~10-12%, not 40-60%.

Correct! Lynch syndrome (HNPCC): mismatch repair mutations (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) increase endometrial cancer risk to 40-60% (higher than colon cancer risk in women!). Screen: annual endometrial biopsy starting age 30-35. Prophylactic hysterectomy + BSO after childbearing.

questions.explanation: Lynch syndrome (hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer, HNPCC) increases lifetime endometrial cancer risk to approximately 40-60%, making it the most common extracolonic cancer in Lynch syndrome. Lynch syndrome results from germline mutations in mismatch repair genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2). Women with Lynch syndrome should be offered annual endometrial sampling starting at age 30-35 and consider risk-reducing hysterectomy with BSO after completing childbearing. Lynch also increases risks of ovarian, gastric, urinary tract, and other cancers.

questions.stem: OCPs reduce ovarian cancer risk by:

choices.feedback: The protective effect is much larger.
Benefit occurs regardless of starting age, though longer use = greater protection.

Correct! Combined OCPs reduce ovarian cancer risk by 40-50% with ≥5 years of use. Protection lasts 15-20 years after stopping. Mechanism: suppression of ovulation reduces repetitive ovarian epithelial injury. Also reduces endometrial cancer risk by 50%. One of the most significant non-contraceptive benefits of OCPs.

choices.text: The effect is only seen in BRCA carriers
choices.feedback: Protection applies to all women, not just BRCA carriers.

questions.explanation: Combined oral contraceptive pills reduce ovarian cancer risk by approximately 40-50% with ≥5 years of use, with protection persisting for 15-20 years after discontinuation. The mechanism involves suppression of ovulation (reducing 'incessant ovulation' theory of ovarian carcinogenesis) and possibly direct effects on ovarian epithelium. This protective effect applies to the general population and BRCA mutation carriers. Longer duration of use provides greater risk reduction.

questions.stem: Most common type of headache?

choices.feedback: Correct! Tension-type headache is the most common primary headache disorder. Bilateral, band-like, pressing/tightening quality, mild-moderate. No nausea/vomiting, no aura. Distinguished from migraine by the absence of unilateral pulsating quality, nausea, and photo/phonophobia.
Cluster headaches are relatively rare but extremely severe.
Migraines are common but tension-type is the most prevalent.
Medication overuse headache is common but secondary, not primary.

questions.explanation: Tension-type headache (TTH) is the most common primary headache, affecting approximately 40% of the population. It presents as bilateral, pressing/tightening (non-pulsating), mild-to-moderate pain without nausea/vomiting or significant photo/phonophobia. It is not aggravated by routine physical activity (unlike migraine). TTH is more common than migraine, cluster headache, or other primary headache types.

questions.stem: First-line ACUTE treatment for cluster headache?

choices.feedback: Correct! High-flow O2 (target rate × 15-20 min) is the first-line abortive. SC sumatriptan (faster than oral) is the second key treatment. Prevention: verapamil (#1 preventive). Cluster = the only primary headache where O2 is a primary treatment.
Opioids are NOT effective for cluster headache and risk dependency.
Oral triptans are too slow for cluster attacks (peak 15-180 min).
Acetaminophen is ineffective for the severe pain of cluster headache.

questions.explanation: High-flow 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask (12-15 L/min for 15-20 minutes) is the first-line acute treatment for cluster headache, providing relief in ~70-80% of attacks within 15 minutes. Subcutaneous sumatriptan (6 mg) is an equally effective alternative. Cluster headache is the most severe primary headache (often called 'suicide headache') with unilateral periorbital pain and autonomic features (lacrimation, rhinorrhea, ptosis, miosis). Verapamil is first-line prophylaxis.

questions.stem: New headache in a patient >50 with jaw claudication and elevated ESR. Immediate step?

choices.text: Temporal artery biopsy first, then treat based on results
choices.feedback: Delaying treatment risks permanent vision loss.
GCA requires immediate steroids, not NSAIDs.

Correct! Giant cell arteritis (temporal arteritis): risk of irreversible vision loss from ophthalmic artery involvement. Start prednisoneMEDIATELY. Do NOT wait for temporal artery biopsy (gold standard — remains positive for 1-2 weeks after starting steroids). Biopsy may show skip lesions (sample ≥2 cm).
MRI doesn't diagnose GCA. The immediate concern is preventing vision loss.

questions.explanation: New headache in a patient >50 with jaw claudication and elevated ESR strongly suggests giant cell arteritis (GCA/temporal arteritis), requiring immediate high-dose corticosteroids (prednisone 60-80 mg/day or IV methylprednisolone if vision threatened) to prevent irreversible vision loss from anterior ischemic optic neuropathy. Treatment should NOT wait for temporal artery biopsy (which remains positive for 1-2 weeks after starting steroids). ESR >50 mm/hr and CRP elevation support diagnosis. Visual symptoms are the most feared complication.

questions.stem: Cushing triad indicates:

choices.feedback: Cushing disease/syndrome is adrenal. Cushing TRIAD is a separate neurologic entity.

Correct! Cushing triad = last-ditch effort by brainstem to maintain cerebral perfusion against rising ICP: hypertension (to force blood past elevated ICP), reflexive bradycardia (baroreceptor response), irregular respirations (brainstem compression). This is a pre-terminal sign — emergent intervention required.
Not related to Cushing triad.
Not related to Cushing triad.

questions.explanation: Cushing triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations) indicates critically elevated intracranial pressure with imminent brainstem herniation. It represents a late and ominous sign — the brainstem response to ischemia from herniation. Hypertension is a reflex attempt to maintain cerebral perfusion pressure, bradycardia results from vagal nerve compression, and irregular respirations reflect medullary dysfunction. This requires immediate intervention (osmotic therapy, hyperventilation, neurosurgical decompression).

questions.stem: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri) typical patient?

choices.feedback: IIH predominantly affects young obese women.

choices.text: Thin elderly woman with temporal headache
choices.feedback: That profile suggests temporal arteritis/GCA.

choices.text: Obese young woman with headache and papilledema
choices.feedback: Correct! Classic demographics: obese women of childbearing age. Headache, papilledema (bilateral), pulsatile tinnitus, transient visual obscurations. Normal MRI. LP: opening pressure >25 cm H₂O with normal CSF. Associated drugs: tetracyclines, vitamin A/retinoids, growth hormone. Treatment: weight loss, acetazolamide.
Exercise headache is a different entity.

questions.explanation: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH/pseudotumor cerebri) typically affects obese women of childbearing age (20-40). Classic presentation: headache (often positional), papilledema on fundoscopy, visual changes (transient visual obscurations, enlarged blind spots), and pulsatile tinnitus. Diagnosis requires elevated LP opening pressure (>25 cmH2O) with normal CSF composition and normal neuroimaging. Treatment: weight loss, acetazolamide (reduces CSF production), serial LPs for acute relief, and optic nerve sheath fenestration or CSF shunting for refractory cases.

questions.stem: CN VI palsy with increased ICP is called a 'false localizing sign' because:

choices.text: CN VI is never affected by ICP
choices.feedback: CN VI is actually commonly affected, which is why it's a false localizer.

choices.text: CN VI is too small to be affected by mass lesions
choices.feedback: Size is not the issue; it's the vulnerability from the long course.

choices.text: The long intracranial course makes CN VI vulnerable to diffuse ICP elevation, not a focal lesion
choices.feedback: Correct! CN VI (abducens) has the longest intracranial course of any cranial nerve. Diffusely elevated ICP stretches it over the petrous ridge → lateral rectus palsy → inability to abduct the eye. This occurs with generalized ICP elevation, not necessarily a lesion at that specific location — hence 'false localizing.'

choices.text: The palsy is always bilateral, indicating diffuse disease
choices.feedback: Can be unilateral or bilateral. The key is that it doesn't indicate the lesion location.

questions.explanation: CN VI (abducens) palsy is a classic false localizing sign in increased ICP. The abducens nerve has the longest intracranial course of any cranial nerve, running along the clivus and through Dorello canal, making it vulnerable to stretching and compression from diffuse ICP elevation anywhere in the cranium — not just from a focal lesion near the sixth nerve nucleus. This means CN VI palsy does not reliably localize the lesion causing the increased ICP.

questions.stem: Triptans are contraindicated in:

choices.feedback: Age alone is not a contraindication — cardiovascular risk assessment determines eligibility.

choices.text: Patients who have already taken NSAIDs
choices.feedback: Triptans can be safely combined with or used after NSAIDs.

choices.text: Patients with coronary artery disease or uncontrolled HTN
choices.feedback: Correct! Triptans are 5-HT1B/1D agonists → vasoconstriction. Contraindicated in: CAD, prior MI/stroke, uncontrolled HTN, basilar/hemiplegic migraine, peripheral vascular disease. Also avoid within 24h of ergotamine. Risk of serotonin syndrome if combined with SSRIs (low but real).
Triptans are simply ineffective for tension headache, not necessarily contraindicated.

questions.explanation: Triptans (5-HT1B/1D receptor agonists) cause vasoconstriction and are contraindicated in coronary artery disease, cerebrovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, peripheral vascular disease, and hemiplegic/basilar migraine (risk of stroke). They should not be used within 24 hours of ergotamine derivatives. Triptans are effective for acute migraine and cluster headache but their vasoconstrictive properties make them dangerous in patients with vascular disease.

questions.stem: Hyperventilation reduces ICP by:

choices.text: Increasing oxygen delivery to the brain
choices.feedback: The mechanism is CO2-mediated vasoconstriction, not oxygen delivery.
While hyperventilation affects metabolism, the ICP mechanism is vasoconstriction.
Hyperventilation does not directly affect CSF dynamics.

Correct! Reducing PCO2 from ~40 to 30-35 mmHg causes cerebral arteriolar vasoconstriction → decreased cerebral blood volume → rapid ICP reduction. TEMPORARY effect only (hours) — brain compensates. Used as a bridge to definitive treatment (mannitol, surgery). Target PCO2 30-35 mmHg. Excessive hyperventilation (<25) risks cerebral ischemia.

questions.explanation: Hyperventilation reduces ICP by lowering arterial PCO2, which causes cerebral vasoconstriction, reducing cerebral blood volume and thus intracranial pressure. Target PCO2 is 30-35 mmHg. This is a temporizing measure only (effective for minutes to hours) as the brain adapts by adjusting CSF bicarbonate. Excessive or prolonged hyperventilation (PCO2 <25 mmHg) can cause dangerous cerebral ischemia. It is used as a bridge to definitive treatment (surgery, osmotic therapy).

questions.stem: Most common type of primary headache?

choices.feedback: Correct! Tension-type is the most common primary headache (~70% of primary headaches). Bilateral, band-like pressure, mild-moderate severity. No aura, typically no N/V or photophobia. Associated with pericranial muscle tenderness and stress.
TN is relatively uncommon and classified differently.
Cluster is the least common of the three major primary headaches.
Migraine is the second most common. Tension-type is more prevalent.

questions.explanation: Tension-type headache is the most prevalent primary headache disorder, affecting ~40% of the population. It presents with bilateral, non-pulsating, band-like pressure of mild-to-moderate intensity without nausea or significant sensory sensitivity. First-line treatment is simple analgesics (NSAIDs, acetaminophen). Preventive therapy with amitriptyline is considered for chronic TTH (≥15 days/month).

questions.stem: Behavioral difference between migraine and cluster headache?

choices.feedback: Correct! Migraine → photophobia, worsened by movement, patients lie in dark quiet room. Cluster → severe pain causes restlessness — patients pace, rock, bang head. This behavioral difference is a high-yield board pearl for distinguishing the two.

choices.text: Cluster patients prefer standing; migraine patients prefer sitting
choices.feedback: It's more about agitation (cluster) vs stillness (migraine).
The behavioral distinction is one of the most reliable clinical clues.

choices.text: Both patients lie still in dark rooms
choices.feedback: Only migraine patients prefer stillness. Cluster patients are agitated.

questions.explanation: A key behavioral distinction: migraine patients prefer to lie still in a dark, quiet room (movement worsens pain), while cluster headache patients are characteristically restless and agitated, often pacing, rocking, or even banging their head against a wall during attacks. This behavioral difference is diagnostically useful. Both involve severe pain, but cluster headache produces more extreme agitation due to the excruciating unilateral periorbital pain and autonomic activation.

questions.stem: First-line acute treatment for cluster headache?

choices.feedback: Simple analgesics are ineffective for the severe pain of cluster headache.

Correct! 100% O2 at target rate via non-rebreather × 15-20 min is first-line. ~70% response rate within 15 min. Also effective: subcutaneous sumatriptan (fastest triptan route). Prophylaxis: verapamil (first-line). Prednisone for cluster period bridge.
Opioids are ineffective for cluster headache and should be avoided.
Oral triptans are too slow for cluster headache (peaks in 15-180 min). SC sumatriptan or high-flow O2 needed.

questions.explanation: High-flow 100% oxygen (12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask for 15-20 minutes) is first-line acute treatment for cluster headache, providing rapid relief in 70-80% of attacks. Subcutaneous sumatriptan is equally effective. Inhaled oxygen works through uncertain mechanisms, possibly including cerebral vasoconstriction and suppression of trigeminovascular activation.

questions.stem: New headache in a 65-year-old with scalp tenderness and jaw claudication. Immediate concern?

choices.feedback: Correct! GCA: age >50, new headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, visual symptoms (amaurosis fugax, vision loss). Elevated ESR (often >50) and CRP. START HIGH-DOSE STEROIDS IMMEDIATELY — do NOT wait for biopsy (prevents irreversible blindness from ophthalmic artery vasculitis). Temporal artery biopsy confirms diagnosis.
New headache at 65 with these features is not typical migraine.
Tension headache doesn't cause jaw claudication or scalp tenderness in this pattern.
MOH presents differently — progressive daily headache from analgesic overuse.

questions.explanation: A new headache in a 65-year-old with scalp tenderness and jaw claudication is temporal arteritis (giant cell arteritis) until proven otherwise. This is a medical emergency — untreated GCA can cause permanent bilateral blindness from anterior ischemic optic neuropathy. Immediate high-dose corticosteroids should be started before awaiting temporal artery biopsy results. ESR and CRP should be obtained. GCA affects patients >50 almost exclusively.

questions.stem: Triptans are CONTRAINDICATED in:

choices.text: Coronary artery disease, uncontrolled HTN, hemiplegic migraine
choices.feedback: Correct! Triptans are 5-HT1B/1D agonists that cause vasoconstriction. Contraindicated in: CAD, uncontrolled HTN, prior stroke/TIA, hemiplegic or basilar migraine, peripheral vascular disease, concurrent ergotamines or MAOIs. Risk: coronary vasospasm, MI, stroke.
Photophobia is a migraine symptom — triptans are the treatment for migraine.
Triptans are category C — can be used with caution. They're not absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy.
Some triptans are approved for adolescents. Age alone is not a contraindication.

questions.explanation: Triptans are contraindicated in CAD, uncontrolled hypertension, and hemiplegic migraine due to their vasoconstrictive mechanism (5-HT1B/1D agonism). In hemiplegic migraine, vasoconstriction risks cerebral ischemia during the aura phase. Alternative treatments for these patients include gepants (CGRP receptor antagonists) and lasmiditan (5-HT1F agonist without vasoconstrictive activity).

questions.stem: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri) classic patient?

choices.text: Child with fever and neck stiffness
choices.feedback: This suggests meningitis, not IIH.

choices.text: Obese young woman with headache, papilledema, and elevated LP opening pressure
choices.feedback: Correct! Classic: young obese woman (often on tetracyclines, vitamin A, or OCP) with headache, papilledema, visual changes (enlarged blind spot), pulsatile tinnitus. LP: opening pressure >25 cmH2O with normal CSF composition. MRI: normal (or empty sella, flattened posterior sclera). Treatment: weight loss, acetazolamide, serial LP, optic nerve sheath fenestration if vision threatened.
This pattern suggests normal pressure hydrocephalus or dementia.
IIH predominantly affects young obese women.

questions.explanation: IIH (pseudotumor cerebri) classically affects obese young women with headache, papilledema, and elevated LP opening pressure (>25 cmH2O) with normal CSF and neuroimaging. Treatment includes weight loss, acetazolamide, and surgical options for refractory cases. Vision monitoring is essential as untreated IIH can cause permanent visual field loss.

questions.stem: First-line treatment for trigeminal neuralgia?

choices.feedback: Amitriptyline is used for tension headache and migraine prophylaxis.

Correct! Carbamazepine (sodium channel blocker) is first-line for trigeminal neuralgia. Voltage-dependent sodium channel stabilization reduces neuronal hyperexcitability. Oxcarbazepine is an alternative with fewer side effects. For refractory: microvascular decompression (surgical — addresses vascular compression of CN V). MRI with CISS/FIESTA sequence to evaluate for compression.
Triptans treat migraine and cluster headache, not TN.
Verapamil is used for cluster headache prophylaxis.

questions.explanation: Carbamazepine is the first-line treatment for trigeminal neuralgia (TN), with ~70% initial response rate. TN presents with sudden, severe, electric shock-like facial pain in the V2/V3 distribution triggered by touch, chewing, or wind. Oxcarbazepine is an alternative with fewer drug interactions. If medications fail, options include microvascular decompression (for vascular compression of CN V), gamma knife radiosurgery, or percutaneous ablative procedures.

questions.stem: SNOOP red flags in headache — the 'O' for Onset refers to:

choices.text: Sudden onset thunderclap headache (SAH until proven otherwise)
choices.feedback: Correct! Thunderclap headache = maximal intensity within seconds. SAH must be excluded: CT head (95-98% sensitive within 6h) → LP if CT negative (xanthochromia). Other causes of thunderclap: cerebral venous thrombosis, pituitary apoplexy, cervical artery dissection, RCVS. Never dismiss a thunderclap headache as benign.
Exertional headache can be concerning but 'O' specifically refers to sudden/thunderclap onset.
While concerning, the SNOOP 'O' refers to sudden onset.
New headache after age 50 (the second 'O' in SNOOP) is the age concern.

questions.explanation: In the SNOOP mnemonic for headache red flags, O for Onset refers to sudden-onset thunderclap headache — reaching maximum intensity within seconds to minutes. This must be treated as subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) until proven otherwise, requiring emergent non-contrast CT head (sensitivity ~98% within 6 hours) followed by lumbar puncture if CT is negative (looking for xanthochromia). SNOOP: Systemic symptoms/Secondary risk factors, Neurologic signs, Onset sudden, Older age (>50 new onset), Prior headache pattern change.

questions.stem: A patient has EF 30%, dilated LV, and S3 gallop. Classification?

choices.feedback: Cor pulmonale = right heart failure from pulmonary disease, not primary LV dilation.
Restrictive = stiff ventricle with preserved size but impaired filling (diastolic).
HFpEF has EF ≥50% with stiff/non-compliant ventricle. EF 30% is reduced.
Correct! EF ≤40% = HFrEF. Dilated LV with impaired contraction. S3 gallop from rapid ventricular filling (volume overload). This patient needs GDMT: ARNI + BB + MRA + SGLT2i.

questions.explanation: An EF of 30% meets the definition of HFrEF (LVEF ≤40%) per the 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA guidelines. The dilated LV and S3 gallop are hallmarks of systolic dysfunction: the ventricle enlarges and weakens, leading to increased end-diastolic volume and rapid early filling that produces the S3 sound. This contrasts with HFpEF (LVEF ≥50%), where the ventricle is stiff rather than dilated and produces an S4 gallop from atrial contraction against a noncompliant ventricle. Restrictive cardiomyopathy shows normal or near-normal chamber size with severely impaired diastolic filling. Cor pulmonale is right-sided heart failure from pulmonary disease, not left ventricular systolic dysfunction. The current classification also recognizes HFmrEF (LVEF 41-49%) and HF with improved EF (previously ≤40%, now >40%).
references: Shams P, Malik A, Chhabra L. Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure). [Updated 2025 Feb 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430873/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: Which diuretic REDUCES MORTALITY in HFrEF?

choices.feedback: Correct! Spironolactone (MRA) reduces mortality by ~30% in HFrEF (RALES trial). It's an aldosterone antagonist with anti-fibrotic and anti-remodeling effects beyond diuresis. Monitor for hyperkalemia. Eplerenone is an alternative with fewer anti-androgenic side effects.
Loop diuretics relieve congestion symptoms but have NO mortality benefit.
Metolazone augments loop diuretic effect but does not reduce mortality.
HCTZ is a thiazide diuretic used for HTN; no mortality benefit in HF.

questions.explanation: Spironolactone (an MRA/mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist) is the only option listed that reduces mortality in HFrEF. The RALES trial demonstrated a 30% mortality reduction with spironolactone in NYHA class III-IV HFrEF. MRAs are now one of the 'four pillars' of guideline-directed medical therapy for HFrEF (along with beta-blockers, ARNI/ACEi/ARB, and SGLT2 inhibitors). In contrast, loop diuretics (furosemide) relieve congestion and improve symptoms but have never shown mortality benefit — they are essential for volume management but are not disease-modifying. Metolazone and hydrochlorothiazide are thiazide diuretics used as adjuncts when patients are resistant to loop diuretics, also without mortality benefit. MRA prescribing requires monitoring: eGFR >30, potassium <5.0.
references: Golla MSG, Hajouli S, Ludhwani D. Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction. [Updated 2024 May 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553115/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: Only three beta-blockers are proven to reduce mortality in HFrEF. They are:

choices.feedback: Correct! ONLY these 3 BBs are evidence-based for HFrEF mortality reduction: carvedilol (COPERNICUS), metoprolol succinate (MERIT-HF), bisoprolol (CIBIS-II). Metoprolol tartrate is NOT the same — only the succinate (extended-release) formulation is proven.
Metoprolol tartrate (immediate-release) is NOT proven. Sotalol is primarily antiarrhythmic.
HF mortality benefit is specific to these 3 agents only.
None of these three have been proven in HFrEF mortality trials.

questions.explanation: Only three beta-blockers have RCT evidence for mortality reduction in HFrEF: carvedilol (COPERNICUS, CAPRICORN trials), metoprolol succinate (MERIT-HF trial), and bisoprolol (CIBIS-II trial). This is not a class effect — other beta-blockers have not shown mortality benefit and some may be harmful. Critical distinction: metoprolol succinate (extended-release) has the evidence, NOT metoprolol tartrate (immediate-release), which was used in the COMET trial and was inferior to carvedilol. Sotalol has antiarrhythmic properties but no HF mortality benefit. Atenolol, propranolol, and nadolol lack HFrEF outcome data. Beta-blockers should be started at low doses and titrated to target doses as tolerated, and should not be initiated during acute decompensation.
references: Golla MSG, Hajouli S, Ludhwani D. Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction. [Updated 2024 May 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553115/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: SGLT2 inhibitors in HFrEF benefit which patients?

choices.feedback: Correct! DAPA-HF (dapagliflozin) and EMPEROR-Reduced (empagliflozin) showed mortality and HF hospitalization reduction in HFrEF patients WITH and WITHOUT diabetes. SGLT2i are now a standard pillar of HF therapy. Mechanism includes natriuresis, improved cardiac energetics, and reduced fibrosis.
CKD patients benefit from SGLT2i, but the HF benefit extends to patients with normal kidney function.
Benefit seen across NYHA classes II-IV.
A common misconception. SGLT2i benefit HFrEF patients regardless of diabetes status.

questions.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) carry a Class 1A recommendation for HFrEF regardless of diabetes status. The DAPA-HF trial (dapagliflozin) and EMPEROR-Reduced trial (empagliflozin) both demonstrated reduced cardiovascular death and HF hospitalization in patients with and without diabetes. SGLT2 inhibitors are now considered one of the four pillars of HFrEF GDMT (alongside beta-blockers, ARNI/ACEi/ARB, and MRAs). They have a Class 2A recommendation for HFmrEF and HFpEF as well. Their mechanism in HF extends beyond glucose lowering — they promote natriuresis, reduce preload, improve cardiac energetics, and decrease myocardial fibrosis. Contraindications include symptomatic hypotension, SBP <95 mmHg, and eGFR <30.
references: Golla MSG, Hajouli S, Ludhwani D. Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction. [Updated 2024 May 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553115/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: S4 gallop is caused by:

choices.text: Atrial contraction against a stiff ventricle
choices.feedback: Correct! S4 = late diastolic sound from forceful atrial contraction pushing blood into a non-compliant ventricle. Causes: HTN (LVH), HCM, aortic stenosis, acute MI. S4 disappears in A-fib (no organized atrial kick). S4 can be normal in elderly.
That describes S3. S3 = passive rapid filling in early diastole.
MVP causes a mid-systolic click, not S4.
VSD causes a holosystolic murmur, not a gallop.

questions.explanation: The S4 gallop (atrial gallop) is a low-frequency sound occurring in late diastole, just before S1, produced by forceful atrial contraction against a stiff, noncompliant ventricle. It is heard best with the bell of the stethoscope at the apex. S4 is characteristic of diastolic dysfunction (HFpEF) — conditions like hypertensive heart disease, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, and aortic stenosis that cause ventricular hypertrophy and decreased compliance. This contrasts with the S3 gallop (ventricular gallop), which occurs in early diastole from rapid filling of a volume-overloaded, dilated ventricle — characteristic of systolic dysfunction (HFrEF). S4 is absent in atrial fibrillation (no organized atrial contraction). MVP produces a mid-systolic click, not a gallop. VSD produces a holosystolic murmur.
references: Shams P, Malik A, Chhabra L. Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure). [Updated 2025 Feb 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430873/ [Open Access]

S3 is the ventricular gallop — occurs in early diastole from rapid filling of a dilated ventricle (volume overload/systolic dysfunction). The StatPearls articles describe the underlying pathophysiology (ventricular stiffness in HFpEF) that produces the S4 sound.

questions.stem: BNP may be FALSELY LOW in which condition?

choices.feedback: BNP naturally increases with age (falsely high).
A-fib causes falsely elevated BNP from atrial stretch.

Correct! BNP is falsely LOW in obesity — adipose tissue expresses neprilysin which degrades BNP. Use lower diagnostic threshold in obese patients. NT-proBNP may be more reliable. BNP falsely HIGH in: renal failure, A-fib, PE, older age, sepsis.
Renal failure causes falsely HIGH BNP (reduced clearance).

questions.explanation: Obesity causes falsely low BNP levels because adipose tissue expresses neprilysin, the enzyme that degrades natriuretic peptides (BNP and ANP). This is clinically important: obese patients with genuine heart failure may have BNP levels below typical diagnostic thresholds, leading to missed or delayed diagnosis. Lower BNP cutoffs should be used in obese patients. The distractors all INCREASE BNP: advanced age raises baseline BNP (higher thresholds needed in elderly), atrial fibrillation elevates BNP through atrial stretch and neurohormonal activation, and renal failure elevates BNP due to decreased renal clearance (especially NT-proBNP, which is exclusively renally cleared). Understanding what raises vs. lowers BNP is essential for accurate clinical interpretation.
references: Shams P, Malik A, Chhabra L. Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure). [Updated 2025 Feb 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430873/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: Acute decompensated HF with pulmonary edema. First-line treatment?

choices.feedback: Do NOT start beta-blockers during acute decompensation — may worsen hemodynamics.

Correct! Acute pulmonary edema: sit upright, O2/BiPAP (NIV preferred over intubation), IV furosemide (diuresis), IV nitroglycerin (preload/afterload reduction). BiPAP reduces work of breathing and preload. If cardiogenic shock (hypotension): inotropes (dobutamine) ± mechanical support.
Fluid bolus worsens pulmonary edema! These patients are volume overloaded.
Cardioversion is for arrhythmia-driven instability, not primary pulmonary edema.

questions.explanation: IV furosemide (a loop diuretic) is the first-line treatment for acute decompensated HF with pulmonary edema. It acts within minutes when given IV, producing rapid diuresis and preload reduction. Initial IV dosing should be at least equivalent to the patient's home oral dose; diuretic-naive patients typically receive 20-IV. Additional therapies include IV nitroglycerin (afterload and preload reduction) and non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (BiPAP/CPAP) for respiratory support. IV metoprolol would be harmful in acute decompensation — beta-blockers are negative inotropes that should not be initiated during active decompensation (they are continued only if the patient was previously on a stable dose). IV normal saline would worsen pulmonary edema. Immediate cardioversion is only indicated for hemodynamically unstable tachyarrhythmias.
references: Golla MSG, Hajouli S, Ludhwani D. Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction. [Updated 2024 May 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553115/ [Open Access]

IV metoprolol would be harmful in acute decompensation (negative inotrope). IV NS would worsen volume overload. Cardioversion is only indicated for hemodynamically unstable arrhythmia, not routine ADHF.

questions.stem: In HFrEF, sacubitril-valsartan (ARNI) is preferred over ACEi because:

choices.text: It replaces the need for beta-blockers
choices.feedback: ARNI does NOT replace BBs. All four GDMT pillars should be used together.

choices.text: PARADIGM-HF showed superior mortality and HF hospitalization reduction
choices.feedback: Correct! PARADIGM-HF: sacubitril-valsartan reduced CV death and HF hospitalization by 20% vs enalapril. Sacubitril inhibits neprilysin (increases natriuretic peptides → vasodilation, natriuresis). Key safety: 36-hour washout when switching FROM ACEi TO ARNI (risk of angioedema). Never combine ARNI + ACEi.

choices.text: It has fewer side effects than all other HF drugs
choices.feedback: ARNIs can cause hypotension and have the angioedema risk, especially without washout from ACEi.

choices.text: It is cheaper and more widely available
choices.feedback: ARNIs are actually more expensive than ACEi.

questions.explanation: The PARADIGM-HF trial demonstrated that sacubitril-valsartan (ARNI) reduced cardiovascular death and HF hospitalization by 20% compared to enalapril (ACEi) in HFrEF patients. Sacubitril inhibits neprilysin, the enzyme that degrades beneficial natriuretic peptides (BNP, ANP) and bradykinin, while valsartan blocks the RAAS. This dual mechanism provides superior neurohormonal modulation. ARNI does NOT replace beta-blockers — all four pillars of GDMT (ARNI, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i) should be used together. ARNI has a unique safety concern: it must never be combined with ACEi (36-hour washout required) due to severe angioedema risk. ARNI is more expensive than generic ACEi, not cheaper. The PARAGON-HF trial also showed benefit in HFpEF, particularly in women.
references: Golla MSG, Hajouli S, Ludhwani D. Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction. [Updated 2024 May 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553115/ [Open Access]

Shams P, Malik A, Chhabra L. Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure). [Updated 2025 Feb 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430873/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: sacubitril/valsartan demonstrated reduced cardiovascular and all-cause mortality in HFrEF compared to enalapril in the PARADIGM-HF trial

supporting.keyFinding: Neprilysin breaks down BNP, ANP, and bradykinin; neprilysin inhibition (sacubitril) must always be combined with ARB, not ACEi, due to angioedema risk

layer1.supportingText: Angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor (ARNI) sacubitril/valsartan demonstrated reduced cardiovascular and all-cause mortality in HFrEF compared to enalapril in the Prospective Comparison of ARNI with ACE-I to Determine Impact on Global Mortality and Morbidity in Heart Failure (PARADIGM-HF) trial.
layer1.additionalSupport: statpearls-hf: 'Neprilysin is an enzyme that breaks down several hormones, including BNP, ANP, and bradykinin; it targets several novel therapeutics.'
layer1.notes: PARADIGM-HF is explicitly named in StatPearls. The trial showed 20% reduction in CV death/HF hospitalization vs enalapril.

layer2.notes: ARNI is Class I recommendation for HFrEF per 2022 guidelines, preferred over ACEi/ARB.

primary.supportingQuote: sacubitril/valsartan demonstrated reduced cardiovascular and all-cause mortality in HFrEF compared to enalapril in the PARADIGM-HF trial

supporting.keyFinding: Neprilysin breaks down BNP, ANP, and bradykinin; neprilysin inhibition (sacubitril) must always be combined with ARB, not ACEi, due to angioedema risk

questions.verifiedExplanation: The PARADIGM-HF trial demonstrated that sacubitril-valsartan (ARNI) reduced cardiovascular death and HF hospitalization by 20% compared to enalapril (ACEi) in HFrEF patients. Sacubitril inhibits neprilysin, the enzyme that degrades beneficial natriuretic peptides (BNP, ANP) and bradykinin, while valsartan blocks the RAAS. This dual mechanism provides superior neurohormonal modulation. ARNI does NOT replace beta-blockers — all four pillars of GDMT (ARNI, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i) should be used together. ARNI has a unique safety concern: it must never be combined with ACEi (36-hour washout required) due to severe angioedema risk. ARNI is more expensive than generic ACEi, not cheaper. The PARAGON-HF trial also showed benefit in HFpEF, particularly in women.
verifiedReferences: Golla MSG, Hajouli S, Ludhwani D. Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction. [Updated 2024 May 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553115/ [Open Access]

Shams P, Malik A, Chhabra L. Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure). [Updated 2025 Feb 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430873/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: Hepatitis B serology: HBsAg(-), Anti-HBs(+), Anti-HBc(-). Interpretation?

choices.feedback: Chronic = HBsAg+ and anti-HBc IgG+.
Window: HBsAg cleared, anti-HBs not yet positive, IgM anti-HBc positive.

Correct! Anti-HBs ALONE positive (no anti-HBc) = immune from vaccination. The vaccine produces only anti-HBs. Natural infection produces both anti-HBs AND anti-HBc. This serologic pattern is the single most tested HBV question.
Acute would have HBsAg+ and IgM anti-HBc+.

questions.explanation: HBsAg(-), Anti-HBs(+), Anti-HBc(-) = vaccination. The patient has surface antibody (immunity) without core antibody (no prior infection). Natural infection immunity shows HBsAg(-), Anti-HBs(+), Anti-HBc(+). Chronic infection: HBsAg(+), Anti-HBs(-), Anti-HBc(+). Window period: HBsAg(-), Anti-HBs(-), Anti-HBc IgM(+).

questions.stem: Which hepatitis virus is most likely to become chronic?

choices.feedback: Hep A NEVER becomes chronic.

Correct! HCV → chronic in ~80% of infections. Most patients are asymptomatic during acute phase. Chronic HCV → cirrhosis (20-30% over 20-30 years) → HCC. NOW curable with DAAs (>95% SVR in 8-12 weeks). Screen all adults 18-79 at least once (USPSTF).
Hep D requires Hep B — cannot exist independently.
Hep E rarely becomes chronic (only in immunosuppressed).

questions.explanation: Hepatitis C is most likely to become chronic — approximately 55-85% of acute HCV infections become chronic, compared to <5% of adult HBV infections and 0% for HAV and HEV (in immunocompetent). HCV chronicity is due to the virus's high mutation rate and ability to evade immune clearance. Chronic HCV leads to cirrhosis in ~20-30% over 20-30 years.

questions.stem: Hepatitis E is particularly dangerous in:

choices.feedback: Vertical transmission of HEV is rare compared to HBV.
HEV is usually mild in children.

Correct! HEV in pregnancy, especially 3rd trimester: fulminant hepatic failure in up to 20%. Fecal-oral transmission via contaminated water. Endemic in South/Southeast Asia, Africa, Mexico. Generally self-limited in non-pregnant adults. No vaccine available in the US.
Elderly may have complications but pregnancy is the key risk factor.

questions.explanation: Hepatitis E is particularly dangerous in pregnant women, with mortality rates up to 20-25% (especially in the third trimester), compared to <1% in the general population. It causes fulminant hepatic failure in pregnancy. Hepatitis E is an RNA virus transmitted fecal-orally, endemic in developing countries. In immunocompetent non-pregnant individuals, it is typically self-limited.

questions.stem: Hepatitis C is now curable with:

choices.feedback: Transplant is for end-stage liver disease. DAAs can cure the infection and prevent progression.
Old regimen — poorly tolerated, ~50% cure rate. Replaced by DAAs.
Tenofovir treats Hepatitis B, not C.

Correct! DAAs target HCV proteins directly: NS5B polymerase inhibitors (sofosbuvir), NS5A inhibitors (velpatasvir, ledipasvir), NS3 protease inhibitors (glecaprevir). Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir = pan-genotypic, 12 weeks, >95% SVR. Revolution in hepatitis treatment.

questions.explanation: Hepatitis C is now curable with direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) such as sofosbuvir/velpatasvir (Epclusa) or glecaprevir/pibrentasvir (Mavyret), achieving sustained virologic response (SVR, considered cure) rates >95% across all genotypes with 8-12 weeks of oral therapy. This has revolutionized HCV treatment, replacing interferon-based regimens that had lower efficacy and significant side effects.

questions.stem: HBV can cause HCC even without cirrhosis because:

choices.text: All hepatitis viruses cause cancer equally
choices.feedback: HCC risk varies by virus. HCV typically requires cirrhosis first.

choices.text: HBV DNA integrates into host genome → direct oncogenesis
choices.feedback: Correct! HBV is a DNA virus that integrates into hepatocyte genome → insertional mutagenesis, chromosomal instability, direct activation of oncogenes (including HBx protein). This means HBV carriers need HCC screening even WITHOUT cirrhosis (especially if Asian male >40, Asian female >50, African >20, family history of HCC).

choices.text: HBV always causes rapid progression to cancer
choices.feedback: HBV-related HCC develops over years-decades.

choices.text: Only in patients co-infected with HDV
choices.feedback: HBV alone has oncogenic potential via DNA integration.

questions.explanation: HBV can cause hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) even without underlying cirrhosis because HBV DNA integrates into the host hepatocyte genome, causing insertional mutagenesis, genomic instability, and direct oncogenesis via the HBx protein (which activates oncogenes and inhibits tumor suppressors). This is why HBV-infected patients require HCC screening regardless of cirrhosis status. HCV, in contrast, typically requires cirrhosis before HCC develops.

questions.stem: Infant born to HBsAg-positive mother. Immediate management?

choices.feedback: Delay increases chronic infection risk from ~90% to nearly certain.
Too late. HBIG + vaccine must be given within 12h.
C-section does not prevent HBV transmission (unlike HCV, where it may help).

choices.text: HBIG + HBV vaccine within 12 hours of birth
choices.feedback: Correct! Both HBIG (passive immunity) + first dose of HBV vaccine (active immunity) within 12h of birth. Reduces vertical transmission from ~90% to <5%. Complete the 3-dose vaccine series. Check HBsAg and anti-HBs at 9-12 months to confirm protection. Mother should receive antiviral if high viral load in 3rd trimester.

questions.explanation: Infants born to HBsAg-positive mothers must receive hepatitis B immunoglobulin (HBIG) AND the first dose of HBV vaccine within 12 hours of birth to prevent vertical (perinatal) transmission. This combination provides >95% protection. Subsequent vaccine doses complete the series. Post-vaccination serology (HBsAg and anti-HBs) should be checked at 9-12 months to confirm protection.

questions.stem: Window period in HBV serology means:

choices.feedback: Chronic: HBsAg+ persisting >6 months with IgG anti-HBc+.
That doesn't occur — HBsAg and anti-HBs are mutually exclusive (mostly).
Vaccinated = anti-HBs alone positive.

choices.text: HBsAg cleared but anti-HBs not yet detectable; anti-HBc IgM is the sole marker
choices.feedback: Correct! Window period: HBsAg has been cleared by immune system, but anti-HBs hasn't risen to detectable levels yet. The only positive marker is IgM anti-HBc. If you don't test for anti-HBc, you'll MISS the diagnosis. Window period typically lasts weeks. Resolves when anti-HBs appears.

questions.explanation: The window period in HBV serology is when HBsAg has been cleared (negative) but anti-HBs has not yet appeared. During this interval, anti-HBc IgM is the only detectable marker of recent/acute HBV infection. This window can last weeks to months. Without checking anti-HBc, the infection would be missed. Anti-HBc total (IgM + IgG) remains positive lifelong after any HBV exposure.

questions.stem: Hepatitis D requires which co-factor?

choices.feedback: HAV and HDV have no relationship.
HCV and HDV are independent. HDV specifically needs HBV.
Immunosuppression isn't required for HDV, though it may worsen outcomes.

choices.text: Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) — HDV is a defective virus needing HBV envelope
choices.feedback: Correct! HDV is a defective RNA virus that requires HBsAg to form its viral envelope. Can only infect HBV-positive patients. Two patterns: coinfection (both acute simultaneously — usually self-limited) vs superinfection (HDV on chronic HBV — worse prognosis, accelerated fibrosis). HBV vaccination prevents HDV.

questions.explanation: Hepatitis D (delta virus) is a defective RNA virus that requires HBsAg (hepatitis B surface antigen) for assembly and replication — it cannot cause infection without active HBV co-infection. HDV uses the HBV envelope to enter hepatocytes. Co-infection (HBV+HDV simultaneously) usually self-resolves. Superinfection (HDV on chronic HBV) causes accelerated progression to cirrhosis and has the worst prognosis of all hepatitis viral combinations.

questions.stem: Most COMMON type of hernia?

choices.feedback: Second most common inguinal; older males through Hesselbach triangle.
Less common but highest strangulation risk.
Correct! Most common in ALL ages and BOTH sexes. Through deep ring, lateral to epigastric vessels. May descend into scrotum.
Common in infants but less common overall.

questions.explanation: Indirect inguinal hernia is the most common type of hernia overall, accounting for ~75% of abdominal wall hernias. It occurs when abdominal contents protrude through the deep (internal) inguinal ring, lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels, following the path of the spermatic cord through the inguinal canal. It is more common in males due to the patent processus vaginalis. Indirect hernias are the most common type in all age groups and both sexes.

questions.stem: Inferior epigastric vessels distinguish:

choices.feedback: Inguinal ligament separates those.
Distinguished by surgical history.
Rare types, different anatomy.
Correct! Indirect = LATERAL. Direct = MEDIAL. MDs are Direct = Medial = Direct.

questions.explanation: The inferior epigastric vessels are the key anatomic landmark distinguishing indirect from direct inguinal hernias. Indirect hernias emerge lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels (through the deep inguinal ring). Direct hernias emerge medial to the inferior epigastric vessels (through a weakness in the posterior wall of the inguinal canal within Hesselbach triangle). This distinction is most relevant during laparoscopic repair and is included in the Nyhus classification system.

questions.stem: Which hernia has HIGHEST strangulation risk?

choices.feedback: Correct! ~40% incarceration rate — narrow, rigid canal. ALL femoral hernias should be repaired regardless of symptoms.
LOWEST incarceration rate — wide area of floor weakness.
Wide inguinal ring usually allows reduction.
Lower strangulation rate than femoral.

questions.explanation: Femoral hernias have the highest strangulation risk (~40%) of all hernias due to the rigid, narrow femoral canal bounded by the inguinal ligament (anterior), pectineal ligament (posterior), lacunar ligament (medial), and femoral vein (lateral). Once bowel enters this unyielding space, it is easily incarcerated and strangulated. Femoral hernias are more common in women and always require surgical repair — watchful waiting is contraindicated.

questions.stem: Tender, non-reducible groin mass with fever and vomiting, erythematous skin. Management?

choices.feedback: NEVER reduce suspected strangulated hernia — may push necrotic bowel intraperitoneally.
Observation risks bowel necrosis and death.
Clinically obvious strangulation should not be delayed for imaging.
Correct! Strangulated hernia = surgical emergency. OR for exploration, bowel assessment, ± resection and repair.

questions.explanation: A tender, non-reducible groin mass with fever, vomiting, and overlying erythema indicates a strangulated hernia — a surgical emergency requiring immediate operative exploration. Strangulation means the blood supply to the herniated bowel is compromised, leading to ischemia, necrosis, perforation, and sepsis if not treated emergently. Attempts at bedside reduction of a strangulated hernia are contraindicated (risk of reducing dead bowel into the abdomen). CT may confirm diagnosis but should not delay surgery.

questions.stem: Infant umbilical hernias close spontaneously by age:

choices.feedback: By age 5 the surgical decision should already be made.
Too early for spontaneous closure.
Most still present at 1 year.
Correct! Most close by age 4-5 (especially if <1.5 cm). Surgery if: persists past 5, defect >1.5-2 cm, symptomatic, or incarcerated.

questions.explanation: Umbilical hernias in infants close spontaneously in the vast majority of cases by age 4-5 years. Surgical repair is recommended only if the hernia persists beyond age 5, is symptomatic, incarcerated, or enlarging, or if the defect is >2 cm (which is less likely to close spontaneously). Umbilical hernias are more common in premature infants and African American infants. Unlike inguinal hernias in infants, observation is the standard initial approach.

questions.stem: Structure at greatest risk during inguinal hernia repair?

choices.feedback: Correct! Runs through inguinal canal. Injury → chronic inguinodynia (most common long-term complication). Also at risk: vas deferens, testicular vessels.
Deep retroperitoneal, not in inguinal field.
Intra-abdominal vessel, not in inguinal field.
Below inguinal ligament, not in operative field.

questions.explanation: The ilioinguinal nerve is the structure at greatest risk of injury during open inguinal hernia repair, particularly during dissection of the external oblique aponeurosis and spermatic cord. Injury causes inguinal/scrotal numbness and chronic groin pain (inguinodynia). Other nerves at risk include the iliohypogastric nerve and the genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve. Chronic pain from nerve injury is the most common long-term complication of inguinal herniorrhaphy, occurring in up to 10-15% of cases.

questions.stem: Hesselbach triangle boundaries?

choices.text: Epigastric vessels (lateral), rectus sheath (medial), inguinal ligament (inferior)
choices.feedback: Correct! Direct hernias protrude through this triangle. Understanding boundaries distinguishes direct from indirect intraoperatively.

choices.text: Transversus abdominis, conjoint tendon, iliopubic tract
choices.feedback: Canal structures but not Hesselbach triangle borders.

choices.text: Inguinal ligament, external oblique, internal oblique
choices.feedback: Those form inguinal canal walls.

choices.text: Femoral vein, Cooper ligament, lacunar ligament
choices.feedback: Those border the femoral canal.

questions.explanation: Hesselbach triangle is bounded by the inferior epigastric vessels (laterally), the lateral border of the rectus sheath/rectus abdominis muscle (medially), and the inguinal ligament (inferiorly). Direct inguinal hernias protrude through a weakness in the transversalis fascia within this triangle, medial to the inferior epigastric vessels. Understanding these boundaries is essential for distinguishing direct from indirect inguinal hernias both clinically and surgically.

questions.stem: Why is watchful waiting NOT appropriate for femoral hernias?

choices.feedback: Correct! Rigid, narrow femoral canal cannot accommodate swelling → rapid incarceration/strangulation. All femoral hernias should be repaired electively.
Some can be reduced, but high recurrence/strangulation risk mandates repair.
Not always, but the narrow canal is the issue.
Recurrence isn't the reason; strangulation risk is.

questions.explanation: Watchful waiting is inappropriate for femoral hernias because their rigid, narrow canal creates approximately a 40% strangulation rate — the highest of any hernia type. The unyielding boundaries (inguinal ligament, pectineal ligament, lacunar ligament, femoral vein) make incarceration and rapid progression to strangulation likely. All femoral hernias require elective surgical repair at diagnosis, regardless of symptoms. This contrasts with small, asymptomatic inguinal hernias in males, where watchful waiting may be reasonable.

questions.stem: A patient with CD4 count of 80 presents with headache, fever, and neck stiffness. LP shows elevated opening pressure (35 cm H2O), low glucose, and positive India ink prep. What is the diagnosis and initial treatment?

choices.feedback: Bacterial meningitis would show elevated WBC (PMNs) and gram stain positivity, not India ink positivity.

Correct! India ink positive = Cryptococcus neoformans. Treatment: induction with amphotericin B + flucytosine (2 weeks), then consolidation with fluconazole (8 weeks), then maintenance fluconazole. Elevated opening pressure needs therapeutic LPs (serial large-volume taps) — NOT ventriculostomy as first-line.
Toxoplasmosis presents with ring-enhancing brain lesions, not meningitis with India ink positivity.
TB meningitis has similar CSF but India ink would be negative. AFB stain/culture would be needed.

questions.explanation: In an AIDS patient (CD4 <100), headache, fever, neck stiffness, elevated CSF opening pressure (>20 cmH2O), and India ink positivity are pathognomonic for cryptococcal meningitis (Cryptococcus neoformans). Elevated opening pressure is a hallmark and correlates with morbidity — serial therapeutic LPs or VP shunt may be needed. Treatment is induction with amphotericin B + flucytosine × 2 weeks, then fluconazole consolidation/maintenance. CSF cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) is highly sensitive/specific. India ink shows encapsulated budding yeast but is less sensitive than CrAg.

questions.stem: Before starting abacavir, which test must be performed?

choices.feedback: Correct! HLA-B*5701 testing is MANDATORY before abacavir. Positive HLA-B*5701 → hypersensitivity reaction (fever, rash, GI symptoms, potentially fatal rechallenge). If positive, NEVER give abacavir.
Troponin is not relevant to abacavir prescribing.
G6PD testing is needed before dapsone or rasburicase, not abacavir.
CYP2D6 is relevant for some pain medications and antidepressants, not abacavir.

questions.explanation: HLA-B*5701 testing is mandatory before starting abacavir (an NRTI). Patients carrying HLA-B*5701 have a high risk (~50%) of developing abacavir hypersensitivity reaction (fever, rash, GI symptoms, potentially fatal if rechallenge occurs). If HLA-B*5701 positive, abacavir is absolutely contraindicated. This is one of the most established pharmacogenomic tests in clinical practice. HLA-B*5801 is for allopurinol. HLA-B*1502 is for carbamazepine in Southeast Asians.

questions.stem: A newly diagnosed HIV+ patient starts ART. Four weeks later, a previously known small pulmonary TB lesion dramatically worsens with high fevers and new lymphadenopathy. What is happening?

choices.feedback: PJP would show bilateral ground-glass opacities, not worsening of a known TB lesion.
ART failure would show rising viral load and falling CD4, not worsening of a known OI.
Drug resistance is possible but the timing (4 weeks after ART initiation) is classic for IRIS.

Correct! IRIS: paradoxical worsening of a known infection after ART initiation due to immune reconstitution. TB-IRIS is common. Continue ART + TB treatment + consider steroids if severe. Most common within 4-8 weeks of starting ART, especially if CD4 was very low.

questions.explanation: Paradoxical worsening of a known TB lesion after starting ART in an immunosuppressed HIV patient is classic immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS). As the immune system recovers with rising CD4 counts, it mounts an exaggerated inflammatory response against pre-existing (often subclinical) infections. IRIS typically occurs 2-8 weeks after ART initiation, especially when baseline CD4 is <100. Management includes continuing ART and TB treatment, with corticosteroids for severe cases. IRIS does not indicate ART failure or new infection.

questions.stem: What is the maximum time window for starting post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) after a high-risk HIV exposure?

choices.feedback: Correct! PEP should be started within 72 hours of exposure. The sooner the better — efficacy decreases with time. 3-drug regimen for 28 days. Beyond 72 hours, PEP is generally not recommended.
14 days is far outside the PEP window.
24 hours is ideal but the window extends further.
7 days is too late for PEP to be effective.

questions.explanation: Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) for HIV must be initiated within 72 hours of a high-risk exposure (needle stick, sexual assault, unprotected sex with known HIV+ partner). Earlier initiation improves efficacy — ideally within 2 hours. PEP consists of a 28-day course of three-drug ART (typically tenofovir/emtricitabine + dolutegravir or raltegravir). Beyond 72 hours, PEP is not recommended as efficacy drops significantly. Baseline HIV testing should be performed before starting PEP, with follow-up at 4-6 weeks and 3 months.
questions.citation: HIV Disease. StatPearls. [Open Access] | CDC PEP Guidelines. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: ACC has absent corpus callosum but SEPARATE hemispheres and ventricles (often with colpocephaly). The hemispheres cleave normally.
Hydranencephaly has cerebral hemispheres replaced by CSF-filled sacs, but the falx is PRESENT and the thalami are SEPARATE. It's a destructive lesion, not a cleavage failure.
Hydrocephalus has DILATED but SEPARATE ventricles with preserved midline structures. The fused single ventricle here indicates failure of cleavage.

Correct! Alobar holoprosencephaly = complete failure of the prosencephalon to cleave into two hemispheres. Findings: single monoventricle, fused thalami, absent falx, absent interhemispheric fissure. The facial features reflect the adage: 'The face predicts the brain.'

questions.stem: Which signaling pathway is critical for forebrain cleavage?

choices.feedback: Correct! SHH from the prechordal plate and notochord patterns the ventral forebrain. It induces the midline cleavage that separates hemispheres. SHH mutations are the most common genetic cause of HPE. Cholesterol is required for SHH processing — this is why teratogens that affect cholesterol (cyclopamine, alcohol) cause HPE.
Wnt signaling patterns the dorsal brain but is not the primary driver of ventral midline cleavage.
FGFs contribute to brain development but SHH is the critical pathway for ventral forebrain patterning and midline cleavage.
Notch is important for neuronal differentiation but not specifically for forebrain cleavage.

questions.explanation: Correct: Sonic Hedgehog (SHH).

questions.stem: Which facial anomaly is most specific to severe holoprosencephaly?

choices.feedback: Macroglossia is associated with Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome and Down syndrome, not HPE.

Correct! Cyclopia (single fused eye) with a proboscis (tube-like nasal structure above the eye) is pathognomonic for alobar HPE. The facial midline fails to develop, just as the brain midline fails. Less severe facial features in the HPE spectrum: hypotelorism, single central incisor, flat nose, median cleft lip.
Micrognathia is associated with Pierre Robin sequence and Treacher Collins, not specifically HPE.
Cleft lip/palate occurs in HPE but is not specific — it occurs in many other conditions. Midline clefting is more suggestive than lateral clefting.

questions.stem: Which HPE subtype has the best prognosis?

choices.feedback: MIH (syntelencephaly) has a variable prognosis but is not the classic answer for 'best prognosis.' Lobar HPE is the standard answer.
Semilobar has intermediate prognosis — better than alobar but still significant developmental disability.
Alobar HPE has the worst prognosis regardless of intervention. VP shunting does not address the fundamental brain malformation.

Correct! Lobar HPE has the best prognosis within the spectrum. The hemispheres are nearly completely separated. Some individuals with lobar HPE have mild-moderate intellectual disability and can survive to adulthood. Some may have only a single central incisor as the only external clue (microform HPE).

questions.stem: Why does this child have temperature instability?

choices.feedback: If this is the first presentation with seizures, medications haven't been started yet. Temperature instability preceded seizure treatment.
While seizures can affect temperature, the persistent instability suggests a hypothalamic cause.

Correct! The hypothalamus develops from the diencephalon, which is the midline structure affected by HPE. Even in lobar HPE, hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction is common: temperature dysregulation, diabetes insipidus, growth hormone deficiency, cortisol deficiency, thyroid dysfunction. All HPE patients need endocrine screening.
HPE does not typically cause immunodeficiency. The temperature instability pattern (both hypo- and hyperthermia) suggests hypothalamic, not infectious, origin.

questions.stem: FIRST intervention in hyperkalemia with EKG changes?

choices.feedback: Correct! Calcium gluconate stabilizes the cardiac membrane within 1-3 minutes by raising the threshold potential (antagonizes K+ effect on cardiac conduction). Does NOT lower serum K+. Repeat in 5 min if EKG unchanged. Then proceed to K+-lowering measures.
Kayexalate removes K+ but takes hours — too slow for acute emergency.
Diuretics help excrete K+ but don't protect the heart immediately.
Insulin shifts K+ into cells but doesn't immediately stabilize the heart.

questions.explanation: The FIRST intervention in hyperkalemia with EKG changes is IV calcium gluconate. Calcium does NOT lower potassium — it stabilizes the cardiac membrane by restoring the transmembrane voltage gradient, protecting against lethal arrhythmias. Onset is 1-3 minutes, duration 30-60 minutes. This buys time for potassium-lowering therapies to take effect. StatPearls states: 'Calcium gluconate is generally preferred as the initial agent in patients with evidence of cardiac toxicity.' The treatment sequence is: (1) IV calcium gluconate — membrane stabilization (immediate); (2) IV insulin + glucose — shifts K+ intracellularly (onset 20-30 min); (3) Albuterol nebulization — additional intracellular shift; (4) Kayexalate/patiromer/SZC — potassium elimination (slow onset); (5) Hemodialysis — definitive removal if refractory. Kayexalate, furosemide, and insulin all lower K+ but do NOT protect the heart immediately.
references: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

Chakraborty A, Patel P, Can AS. Calcium Gluconate. [Updated 2024 Feb 28]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK557463/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Calcium gluconate is generally preferred as the initial agent in patients with evidence of cardiac toxicity

supporting.keyFinding: Calcium should promptly be administered to any patient with hyperkalemia and EKG changes... does not resolve the issue of hyperkalemia

questions.verifiedExplanation: The FIRST intervention in hyperkalemia with EKG changes is IV calcium gluconate. Calcium does NOT lower potassium — it stabilizes the cardiac membrane by restoring the transmembrane voltage gradient, protecting against lethal arrhythmias. Onset is 1-3 minutes, duration 30-60 minutes. This buys time for potassium-lowering therapies to take effect. StatPearls states: 'Calcium gluconate is generally preferred as the initial agent in patients with evidence of cardiac toxicity.' The treatment sequence is: (1) IV calcium gluconate — membrane stabilization (immediate); (2) IV insulin + glucose — shifts K+ intracellularly (onset 20-30 min); (3) Albuterol nebulization — additional intracellular shift; (4) Kayexalate/patiromer/SZC — potassium elimination (slow onset); (5) Hemodialysis — definitive removal if refractory. Kayexalate, furosemide, and insulin all lower K+ but do NOT protect the heart immediately.

Chakraborty A, Patel P, Can AS. Calcium Gluconate. [Updated 2024 Feb 28]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK557463/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: Earliest EKG change in hyperkalemia?

choices.feedback: ST elevation is associated with ischemia/pericarditis, not hyperkalemia.
Wide QRS occurs later (K+ 7.0-8.0).

Correct! Peaked, tent-shaped T waves are the earliest and most sensitive EKG finding (K+ ~5.5-6.5). Progression: peaked T → PR prolongation → P wave loss → QRS widening → sine wave → VF/asystole. Any EKG change = treat immediately.
Prolonged QT is more associated with HYPOkalemia (U waves).

questions.explanation: The earliest EKG change in hyperkalemia is peaked, narrow T waves (especially in precordial leads V2-V4). This occurs because elevated extracellular K+ accelerates phase 3 repolarization of the action potential. The classic progression of hyperkalemic EKG changes with rising K+ is: (1) Peaked T waves (5.5-6.5 mEq/L); (2) Prolonged PR interval (6.5-7.0); (3) Loss of P waves (7.0-7.5); (4) Widened QRS (7.5-8.0); (5) Sine wave pattern (>8.0); (6) Asystole/VF. ST elevation is associated with MI or pericarditis, not hyperkalemia. Widened QRS is a LATE finding of hyperkalemia, not early. Prolonged QT is associated with hypokalemia and hypocalcemia, not hyperkalemia (hyperkalemia actually shortens the QT interval initially).

questions.stem: Insulin lowers K+ by:

choices.feedback: K+ is not chelated by any medication.
Insulin doesn't increase renal K+ excretion. That's diuretics.

choices.text: Driving K+ into cells via Na/K
choices.feedback: Correct! Insulin stimulates the Na+/K+-ATPase pump → K+ moves from ECF into cells. Give regular insulin IV with dextrose (to prevent hypoglycemia). Most RELIABLE shifting agent. Onset 15-30 min, lasts 4-6h. Monitor glucose q1h.

choices.text: Binding K+ in the GI tract
choices.feedback: That's the mechanism of Kayexalate/patiromer.

questions.explanation: Insulin lowers serum potassium by driving K+ into cells via stimulation of the Na+/K+-ATPase pump on cell membranes. Insulin activates this pump, which moves 2 K+ ions into the cell for every 3 Na+ ions pumped out, shifting potassium from the extracellular to intracellular compartment. A common regimen is regular insulin IV along with dextrose to prevent hypoglycemia, with onset in 20-30 minutes and peak effect at 30-60 minutes. Insulin does NOT chelate potassium (chelation involves binding ions in the blood), does NOT increase renal excretion (that's the mechanism of loop diuretics), and does NOT bind K+ in the GI tract (that's the mechanism of kayexalate/patiromer/SZC). Glucose monitoring is essential post-administration, as hypoglycemia is a common and dangerous complication.
references: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Insulin, with or without glucose in hyperglycemic patients, shifts potassium into cells, lowering serum potassium levels effectively... by increasing Na-K-ATPase activity

questions.stem: #1 cause of hyperkalemia?

choices.feedback: Correct! The kidneys are responsible for 90% of K+ excretion. Impaired renal function is the #1 cause of hyperkalemia. Other common causes: ACEi/ARBs + spironolactone (especially in CKD patients), acidosis, cell lysis (rhabdomyolysis, TLS).
Dietary excess alone rarely causes hyperkalemia with normal kidney function.
Alkalosis causes HYPOkalemia (K+ shifts into cells).
Insulin LOWERS K+, not raises it.

questions.explanation: Renal failure is the #1 cause of hyperkalemia. The kidneys are responsible for excreting approximately 90% of daily potassium load. When GFR declines below target rate, potassium excretion becomes impaired, leading to accumulation. StatPearls states: 'Acute and chronic kidney disease are common causes of hyperkalemia. Elevations in serum potassium typically do not occur until the glomerular filtration rate declines below target rate.' Excessive dietary K+ intake rarely causes hyperkalemia alone in patients with normal renal function. Alkalosis actually causes HYPOkalemia (not hyperkalemia) by shifting K+ INTO cells. Insulin therapy causes hypokalemia, not hyperkalemia — insulin deficiency (as in DKA) causes hyperkalemia.
references: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Acute and chronic kidney disease are common causes of hyperkalemia. Elevations in serum potassium typically do not occur until the glomerular filtration rate declines below 30 mL/min

questions.stem: Sine wave pattern on EKG with K+ >8 indicates:

choices.feedback: Sine wave in context of known hyperkalemia is real and deadly.

choices.text: Typical finding — no action needed if asymptomatic
choices.feedback: Even 'asymptomatic' patients with sine wave are in immediate danger.

Correct! Sine wave = K+ >8.0, fusion of widened QRS with peaked T waves. VF or asystole imminent. IV calcium gluconate STAT + insulin/glucose + emergent hemodialysis. Defibrillation may be needed if VF develops.
Sine wave = pre-arrest rhythm. Immediate action needed.

questions.explanation: A sine wave pattern on EKG with K+ >8 mEq/L indicates imminent cardiac arrest and requires emergent treatment. The sine wave represents the final EKG stage before asystole or ventricular fibrillation — the widened QRS merges with the T wave, creating a sinusoidal pattern that indicates loss of effective cardiac conduction. This is a true medical emergency requiring immediate IV calcium gluconate (cardiac membrane stabilization), followed by aggressive potassium-lowering therapies and likely emergent hemodialysis. This is NEVER artifact, NEVER a 'typical finding,' and NEVER appropriate for observation alone. Treatment should not be delayed for repeat testing or confirmatory labs.
references: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: merging of the QRS complex with the T wave producing a sine wave pattern, and asystole

questions.stem: Which medications commonly CAUSE hyperkalemia?

choices.feedback: CCBs don't significantly affect potassium levels.
Diabetes medications don't typically cause hyperkalemia directly.
These cause HYPOkalemia from renal K+ wasting.

Correct! ACEi/ARBs reduce aldosterone → decreased K+ excretion. K+-sparing diuretics (spironolactone, eplerenone, amiloride, triamterene) directly block K+ secretion. The combination is particularly dangerous in CKD. Also: NSAIDs, beta-blockers, calcineurin inhibitors, heparin, TMP-SMX (blocks ENaC).

questions.explanation: ACE inhibitors/ARBs combined with potassium-sparing diuretics (spironolactone, eplerenone, amiloride, triamterene) are a well-known combination that commonly causes hyperkalemia. ACEi/ARBs reduce aldosterone secretion, decreasing renal K+ excretion. K+-sparing diuretics independently block potassium secretion in the collecting duct. Together, the additive effect on K+ retention creates significant hyperkalemia risk. StatPearls lists 'medications that disrupt potassium homeostasis, such as potassium-sparing diuretics and renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibitors' as key causes. Calcium channel blockers do not significantly affect potassium. Metformin and glipizide do not cause hyperkalemia. Loop diuretics and thiazides cause HYPOkalemia (increased renal K+ excretion), not hyperkalemia.
references: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: Pseudohyperkalemia should be suspected when:

choices.text: K+ is mildly elevated with symptoms
choices.feedback: Symptomatic hyperkalemia is usually real.

choices.text: K+ is elevated but EKG is normal and result is unexpected
choices.feedback: Correct! Pseudohyperkalemia: falsely elevated K+ from hemolyzed sample (most common), prolonged tourniquet, excessive fist clenching, extreme leukocytosis (>100K), thrombocytosis (>500K). Always repeat the test if unexpected elevation with normal EKG and no clinical cause.
K+ >8 with cardiac arrest is real hyperkalemia, not pseudo.

choices.text: Any hyperkalemia in a dialysis patient
choices.feedback: Dialysis patients frequently have real hyperkalemia.

questions.explanation: Pseudohyperkalemia should be suspected when K+ is elevated but the EKG is normal and the result is clinically unexpected. Pseudohyperkalemia is an in vitro artifact caused by K+ release from cells during or after blood draw — NOT a true elevation of in vivo serum potassium. Common causes include hemolysis during phlebotomy (most common), prolonged tourniquet application, fist clenching during draw, extreme thrombocytosis (>500,000/μL), or leukocytosis (>100,000/μL). The key clinical clue: the patient has no EKG changes and no symptoms consistent with hyperkalemia. Management: redraw the specimen carefully (no tourniquet, no fist clenching, gentle handling) and recheck. Pseudohyperkalemia does NOT require treatment. However, K+ >8 with cardiac arrest is real hyperkalemia requiring emergent treatment, not pseudohyperkalemia.
references: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: A systematic approach to diagnosing hyperkalemia allows clinicians to distinguish true potassium elevation from pseudohyperkalemia
