questions.stem: For every 0.1 decrease in pH, K+ rises approximately:

choices.feedback: Correct! Acidosis causes transcellular K+ shift: H+ enters cells to be buffered → K+ exits to maintain electroneutrality. This means a patient in DKA with pH 7.0 may have artificially elevated K+ that will DROP dramatically once acidosis is corrected with insulin. Monitor K+ closely during correction.
This overstates the relationship.
While approximate, the 0.6 mEq/L per 0.1 pH rule is clinically useful.
The relationship is more pronounced than this.

questions.explanation: For every 0.1 decrease in blood pH, serum potassium rises approximately 0.6 mEq/L. This occurs because acidosis (excess H+ ions) drives hydrogen ions INTO cells via the H+/K+ exchanger, and potassium moves OUT of cells to maintain electroneutrality. This is particularly relevant in metabolic acidosis (e.g., DKA, lactic acidosis, renal tubular acidosis). The clinical implication: a patient in DKA may have a 'normal' or elevated serum K+ despite massive total body potassium depletion — as acidosis is corrected with insulin and fluids, K+ will shift back into cells and the patient may develop life-threatening hypokalemia. This is why potassium replacement is started early in DKA management even when serum K+ appears normal. Note: this relationship is most predictable with mineral acidoses; organic acidoses (lactic, ketoacidosis) may show a less predictable relationship.
references: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: What DEFINES a hypertensive emergency vs urgency?

choices.feedback: Treatment resistance doesn't define emergency vs urgency.

Correct! End-organ damage (encephalopathy, ACS, HF, dissection, AKI, eclampsia, retinopathy) is what makes it an EMERGENCY. Without end-organ damage, even very high BP is urgency (treated with oral meds). The BP number alone is insufficient.
The absolute number doesn't define it — a patient at 190/115 WITH stroke is an emergency.
Duration is not the distinguishing factor.

questions.explanation: The defining distinction between hypertensive emergency and urgency is the presence of acute end-organ damage, NOT a specific BP threshold. Hypertensive emergency = severely elevated BP with evidence of acute target-organ injury (encephalopathy, stroke, MI, pulmonary edema, aortic dissection, AKI, eclampsia). Hypertensive urgency = severely elevated BP without end-organ damage. The BP number alone does not determine classification — patients with chronic uncontrolled HTN may tolerate very high pressures without acute injury, while previously normotensive patients (e.g., eclampsia) may develop emergencies at lower BP levels. As StatPearls emphasizes: 'treat the patient, not the number.' Emergency requires IV antihypertensives and ICU admission; urgency requires oral medications and outpatient follow-up.
references: Alley WD, Schick MA. Hypertensive Emergency. [Updated 2023 Jul 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470371/ [Open Access]

supporting.keyFinding: Urgencies involve severe BP elevation without evidence of acute target-organ damage; emergencies are associated with life-threatening organ injury

questions.stem: How fast should BP be lowered in most hypertensive emergencies?

choices.feedback: Too rapid — risks watershed infarcts from cerebral hypoperfusion.

choices.text: Do not lower BP at all for 24 hours
choices.feedback: Ongoing end-organ damage requires prompt (but controlled) BP reduction.

choices.text: Reduce MAP by ≤25% in first hour, then 160/100 over 2-6 hours
choices.feedback: Correct! Gradual reduction prevents hypoperfusion injury. The brain has adapted autoregulatory curves to higher pressures. Exceptions: aortic dissection (aggressive — SBP <120 ASAP), eclampsia (<160 within 30-60 min), ischemic stroke pre-tPA (<185/110).
50% reduction risks ischemic complications from hypoperfusion.

questions.explanation: In most hypertensive emergencies, BP should be reduced gradually: MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour, then to approximately 160/100 mmHg over 2-6 hours, with normalization over 24-48 hours. This stepwise approach prevents iatrogenic hypoperfusion — patients with chronic HTN have rightward-shifted cerebral autoregulation curves, so rapid normalization can cause watershed infarction. The key exception is aortic dissection, which requires more aggressive lowering (SBP <120 within minutes). IV agents must be rapidly titratable — oral agents like clonidine and sublingual nifedipine have no role in acute management because their effects are unpredictable and not easily reversed.
references: Potter T, Agarwal A, Schaefer TJ. Hypertensive Encephalopathy. [Updated 2024 Feb 2]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK554499/ [Open Access]

supporting.keyFinding: lower the mean arterial pressure by 20% to 25% within the first 1 to 2 hours

questions.stem: In hypertensive emergency with aortic dissection, the BP target is:

choices.feedback: That's the general rule. Dissection requires MORE aggressive reduction.

choices.text: SBP <120 mmHg and HR <60 bpm within minutes
choices.feedback: Correct! Dissection is the exception to gradual reduction. Immediate goal: SBP 100-120, HR <60 with IV BB (esmolol/labetalol) to minimize aortic wall shear stress. Add vasodilator only after HR controlled.

choices.text: No specific target — just start oral medications
choices.feedback: Dissection requires IV medications and aggressive targets.
Far too slow for dissection — mortality increases 1-2% per hour.

questions.explanation: Aortic dissection is the critical exception to the gradual 25% MAP reduction rule. The target is SBP <120 mmHg AND HR <60 bpm within 20 minutes using IV beta-blockers (esmolol or labetalol) as first-line. Beta-blockers reduce both BP and the rate of aortic pressure rise (dP/dt), decreasing shear stress on the intimal flap. If beta-blockers alone are insufficient, add IV vasodilators (nicardipine, nitroprusside) AFTER adequate heart rate control — vasodilators alone cause reflex tachycardia, which worsens dissection propagation. The standard 25% MAP reduction would be dangerously inadequate because continued hypertension and tachycardia drive ongoing dissection extension, aortic rupture, and death.
references: Potter T, Agarwal A, Schaefer TJ. Hypertensive Encephalopathy. [Updated 2024 Feb 2]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK554499/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: PRES (Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome) shows what on MRI?

choices.feedback: PRES primarily affects posterior (parieto-occipital) regions.

Correct! PRES = vasogenic edema from loss of cerebral autoregulation in the posterior circulation (watershed zone). MRI: bilateral parieto-occipital T2/FLAIR hyperintensities. Usually reversible with BP control. Causes: hypertensive emergency, eclampsia, immunosuppressants (tacrolimus, cyclosporine).
Midline shift suggests mass effect (tumor, hemorrhage), not PRES.
Gray matter necrosis suggests ischemic injury, not PRES.

questions.explanation: PRES classically shows bilateral parieto-occipital vasogenic edema on MRI, appearing as hyperintense signals on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences. The posterior predominance is explained by the sparser sympathetic innervation of the posterior cerebral circulation compared to the anterior (carotid) circulation, making it more vulnerable to autoregulatory breakthrough during acute hypertension. Key MRI features: vasogenic edema (increased ADC, distinguishing from cytotoxic edema/infarction). PRES spares the calcarine cortex, differentiating it from PCA stroke. Atypical regions (frontal lobes, cerebellum, brainstem, basal ganglia) occur in up to 30% of cases. PRES is usually reversible with prompt BP control and treatment of the underlying cause (hypertension, eclampsia, immunosuppressants), but delayed treatment can lead to irreversible infarction.
references: Zelaya JE, Al-Khoury L. Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Oct 11]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK554492/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: classically diagnosed based on MRI imaging most commonly seen in the parieto-occipital lobes...vasogenic edema hyperintense on T2 with FLAIR sequences

questions.stem: Which IV antihypertensive is preferred in hypertensive emergency with AKI?

choices.feedback: Nitroprusside is renally cleared and risks cyanide toxicity. Not ideal with AKI.
Hydralazine has unpredictable response and reflex tachycardia — not preferred.
Esmolol is effective for HR/BP but doesn't specifically improve renal perfusion.

Correct! Fenoldopam is a dopamine-1 receptor agonist that lowers BP while improving renal blood flow and GFR. It's the preferred agent when hypertensive emergency is accompanied by acute kidney injury. No cyanide toxicity risk (unlike nitroprusside).

questions.explanation: Fenoldopam is a selective dopamine-1 (D1) receptor agonist that simultaneously lowers BP and increases renal blood flow, GFR, and natriuresis — making it the preferred choice for hypertensive emergency complicated by AKI. Its renal-protective mechanism (D1-mediated renal arteriolar vasodilation) is unique among IV antihypertensives. Nitroprusside should be avoided in renal impairment because thiocyanate (its metabolite) accumulates with reduced renal clearance, causing neurotoxicity. Hydralazine is a direct arteriolar vasodilator but has unpredictable onset and duration, is poorly titratable, and causes reflex tachycardia. Esmolol is an ultra-short-acting beta-blocker used for dissection and perioperative HTN but may reduce renal perfusion and lacks renal-protective properties.
references: Alley WD, Schick MA. Hypertensive Emergency. [Updated 2023 Jul 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470371/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: A pregnant patient at 36 weeks with BP 180/110, headache, and proteinuria. Diagnosis and first-line antihypertensive?

choices.feedback: ACE inhibitors are CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy (teratogenic). This is also an emergency, not urgency.

choices.text: Severe preeclampsia — IV labetalol or hydralazine
choices.feedback: Correct! Severe preeclampsia: BP ≥160/110 + proteinuria + end-organ signs (headache, visual changes). First-line: IV labetalol or IV hydralazine (both safe in pregnancy). Also give IV magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis. Definitive treatment: delivery. ACEi/ARBs absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy.
Eclampsia = preeclampsia + seizures. Treatment is IV magnesium, not diazepam. Also, this patient hasn't seized yet.
This is acute severe preeclampsia requiring emergency management, not routine chronic HTN.

questions.explanation: This presentation — BP ≥160/110, headache, and proteinuria at 36 weeks — defines severe preeclampsia. First-line antihypertensives are IV labetalol or IV hydralazine, both safe in pregnancy. Magnesium sulfate should be started concurrently for seizure prophylaxis (it is NOT an antihypertensive). Critical distractor analysis: ACE inhibitors (lisinopril) are absolutely CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy — they cause fetal renal agenesis, oligohydramnios, and neonatal death. This is NOT eclampsia, which requires seizures (headache alone is insufficient). For eclamptic seizures, magnesium sulfate is first-line, NOT diazepam. Simply continuing home medications ignores the new-onset proteinuria and symptoms indicating preeclampsia. Definitive treatment is delivery, and at ≥37 weeks (or 36 weeks with severe features), delivery should be expedited after maternal stabilization.
references: Alley WD, Schick MA. Hypertensive Emergency. [Updated 2023 Jul 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470371/ [Open Access]

supporting.keyFinding: Blood pressure control in pregnancy; seizures warrant immediate delivery

questions.verifiedExplanation: This presentation — BP ≥160/110, headache, and proteinuria at 36 weeks — defines severe preeclampsia. First-line antihypertensives are IV labetalol or IV hydralazine, both safe in pregnancy. Magnesium sulfate should be started concurrently for seizure prophylaxis (it is NOT an antihypertensive). This is NOT eclampsia, which requires seizures (headache alone is insufficient). For eclamptic seizures, magnesium sulfate is first-line, NOT diazepam. questions.stem: Nitroprusside risk with prolonged use?

choices.feedback: Correct! Nitroprusside → cyanide → metabolized to thiocyanate. Prolonged use (>48-72h) or high doses → cyanide accumulation → lactic acidosis, AMS, seizures. Treatment: hydroxocobalamin (cyanide antidote) or sodium thiosulfate. Monitor thiocyanate levels if used >48h.
Nitroprusside doesn't cause permanent renal damage directly.
Nitroprusside is metabolized to cyanide, not a direct hepatotoxin.
Bronchospasm is associated with non-selective beta-blockers, not nitroprusside.

questions.explanation: Sodium nitroprusside contains five cyanide (CN) groups per molecule. During metabolism, cyanide is released and normally converted to thiocyanate by hepatic rhodanese, then excreted renally. With prolonged infusion (>48-72 hours) or high doses (>weight-based dosing), cyanide production exceeds detoxification capacity, causing cyanide toxicity — which inhibits cytochrome c oxidase (complex IV), blocking mitochondrial aerobic metabolism and causing lactic acidosis. Clinical signs include tachyphylaxis (rising BP despite increasing dose), altered mental status, seizures, and cardiovascular collapse. Thiocyanate toxicity (distinct from cyanide) can additionally occur in renal failure. Treatment: sodium thiosulfate + hydroxocobalamin. Due to these risks, nitroprusside has been largely replaced by safer agents (nicardipine, clevidipine).
references: Alley WD, Schick MA. Hypertensive Emergency. [Updated 2023 Jul 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470371/ [Open Access]
questions.verifiedExplanation: Sodium nitroprusside contains five cyanide (CN) groups per molecule. During metabolism, cyanide is released and normally converted to thiocyanate by hepatic rhodanese, then excreted renally. With prolonged infusion (>48-72 hours) or high doses (>2 mcg/kg/min), cyanide production exceeds detoxification capacity, causing cyanide toxicity — which inhibits cytochrome c oxidase (complex IV), blocking mitochondrial aerobic metabolism and causing lactic acidosis. Clinical signs include tachyphylaxis (rising BP despite increasing dose), altered mental status, seizures, and cardiovascular collapse. Thiocyanate toxicity (distinct from cyanide) can additionally occur in renal failure. Treatment: sodium thiosulfate + hydroxocobalamin. Due to these risks, nitroprusside has been largely replaced by safer agents (nicardipine, clevidipine).
verifiedReferences: Alley WD, Schick MA. Hypertensive Emergency. [Updated 2023 Jul 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470371/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: Sublingual nifedipine should be AVOIDED in hypertensive emergencies because:

choices.text: It causes unpredictable precipitous BP drops → stroke/MI
choices.feedback: Correct! Sublingual/bite-and-swallow nifedipine causes rapid, uncontrolled BP drops that can precipitate cerebral ischemia, MI, or death. Banned from use in hypertensive emergencies. Use IV titratable agents instead for controlled, predictable BP reduction.

choices.text: It is ineffective at lowering BP
choices.feedback: Nifedipine is actually very effective but too unpredictable.

choices.text: It only works for pulmonary hypertension
choices.feedback: Nifedipine works systemically but is too dangerous in this context.
Nifedipine is oral/sublingual. The issue is unpredictable absorption and BP drop.

questions.explanation: Sublingual nifedipine (a short-acting dihydropyridine CCB) is explicitly contraindicated in hypertensive emergencies because it causes rapid, unpredictable, and uncontrollable BP drops that have been associated with stroke, MI, and death. Unlike IV agents, sublingual nifedipine cannot be titrated — once administered, the precipitous vasodilation cannot be reversed. The rapid BP drop can fall below the cerebral autoregulatory range (rightward-shifted in chronically hypertensive patients), causing watershed infarcts. This led to a regulatory advisory against the practice. StatPearls states that 'oral medications, such as clonidine and nifedipine, play no role in the immediate management of a hypertensive emergency.' The key principle: agents for HTN emergency must be both rapidly acting AND easily titratable, which requires IV administration with continuous monitoring.
references: Alley WD, Schick MA. Hypertensive Emergency. [Updated 2023 Jul 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470371/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: oral medications, such as clonidine and nifedipine, play no role in the immediate management of a hypertensive emergency

questions.verifiedExplanation: Sublingual nifedipine (a short-acting dihydropyridine CCB) is explicitly contraindicated in hypertensive emergencies because it causes rapid, unpredictable, and uncontrollable BP drops that have been associated with stroke, MI, and death. Unlike IV agents, sublingual nifedipine cannot be titrated — once administered, the precipitous vasodilation cannot be reversed. The rapid BP drop can fall below the cerebral autoregulatory range (rightward-shifted in chronically hypertensive patients), causing watershed infarcts. This led to a regulatory advisory against the practice. StatPearls states that 'oral medications, such as clonidine and nifedipine, play no role in the immediate management of a hypertensive emergency.' The key principle: agents for HTN emergency must be both rapidly acting AND easily titratable, which requires IV administration with continuous monitoring.

questions.stem: Most common cause of euvolemic hyponatremia?

choices.feedback: Adrenal insufficiency can cause euvolemic hyponatremia but is less common than SIADH.
Psychogenic polydipsia causes euvolemic hyponatremia but is less common than SIADH.

Correct! SIADH is the #1 cause of euvolemic hyponatremia. Criteria: hypo-osmolar serum + concentrated urine (Uosm >100) + UNa >20 + euvolemic + normal thyroid/adrenal. Must exclude hypothyroidism and adrenal insufficiency first. Common causes: CNS/pulmonary disease, drugs, SCLC (ectopic ADH).
CHF causes hypervolemic hyponatremia (edema, JVD).

questions.explanation: SIADH (syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone) is the most common cause of euvolemic hyponatremia. Excess ADH causes water retention and dilutional hyponatremia despite normal volume status. Common causes include CNS disorders (stroke, meningitis, tumors), pulmonary disease (pneumonia, SCLC), medications (SSRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide), and post-surgical states. Diagnostic criteria: hyponatremia, concentrated urine (>100 mOsm/kg), elevated urine sodium (>40 mEq/L), and clinical euvolemia.

questions.stem: Maximum safe rate of Na+ correction in chronic hyponatremia?

choices.feedback: Correct! Maximum 8-10 mEq/L in 24h for chronic hyponatremia. Brain has adapted by losing intracellular osmoles. Rapid correction → water exits brain cells → pontine demyelination (ODS/CPM). If overcorrected: administer D5W or desmopressin (DDAVP) to re-lower Na+.
This rate risks osmotic demyelination syndrome.

choices.text: No limit — correct as fast as possible
choices.feedback: Rapid correction of chronic hyponatremia causes ODS.
Too slow — even chronic hyponatremia needs some correction.

questions.explanation: Maximum safe rate of sodium correction in chronic hyponatremia is 8-10 mEq/L per 24 hours (some guidelines recommend ≤8 mEq/L). Overly rapid correction risks osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS/central pontine myelinolysis). The risk is highest in chronic hyponatremia (>48 hours duration) because brain cells have already adapted by extruding organic osmolytes. If overcorrected, re-lower sodium with D5W ± DDAVP.

questions.stem: Osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS) presents with:

choices.feedback: ODS is a devastating irreversible complication, not a minor symptom.

Correct! ODS/CPM: destruction of myelin in central pons from overly rapid Na+ correction. Develops 2-6 days after overcorrection. Locked-in syndrome: conscious but unable to move or speak (only eye movements preserved). Largely irreversible. Prevention: strict adherence to correction rate limits.
Seizures are a symptom of severe hyponatremia, not ODS.
ODS affects the central pons, not peripheral nerves.

questions.explanation: Osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS, formerly central pontine myelinolysis) presents with quadriplegia (corticospinal tract damage), dysarthria, dysphagia (corticobulbar tract damage), and locked-in syndrome in severe cases. Symptoms typically appear 2-6 days after overly rapid sodium correction. MRI shows characteristic pontine demyelination (may take 2 weeks to appear). Extrapontine myelinolysis can also occur. ODS is largely irreversible, making prevention through careful sodium correction critical.

questions.stem: Severe symptomatic hyponatremia (seizures) requires:

choices.feedback: Fluid restriction is too slow for acute neurologic emergency.
NS may not raise Na+ fast enough for acute neurologic emergency.
DDAVP worsens hyponatremia (ADH analog). Used to PREVENT overcorrection, not treat acute hyponatremia.

choices.text: 3% hypertonic saline 100-150 mL bolus to raise Na 4-6 mEq/L in first few hours
choices.feedback: Correct! Hypertonic saline for acute severe symptoms (seizures, coma, herniation). Hypertonic saline bolus, may repeat as needed. Goal: raise Na+ by 4-6 mEq/L in first hours to stop neurologic symptoms. Then slow correction to stay within 8-10 mEq/L per 24h total.

questions.explanation: Severe symptomatic hyponatremia (seizures, obtundation) requires emergent treatment with 3% hypertonic saline, typically a 100-150 mL bolus over 10-20 minutes, which can be repeated 2-3 times as needed to raise sodium by 4-6 mEq/L in the first few hours. This acute correction is safe and necessary to reduce cerebral edema and prevent herniation. After initial stabilization, switch to slower correction within the 8-10 mEq/24h limit.

questions.stem: Corrected sodium formula for hyperglycemia:

choices.text: Measured Na+ + 1.6 mEq/L for every 100 mg/dL glucose above 100
choices.feedback: Correct! Hyperglycemia causes pseudohyponatremia (dilutional). Glucose draws water osmotically → dilutes Na+. Corrected Na+ = measured Na+ + [1.6 × (glucose - 100)/100]. Example: glucose 500, Na+ 128 → corrected Na+ = 128 + [1.6 × 4] = 134.4 (nearly normal). Always correct before diagnosing true hyponatremia in DKA.
This is not the correction formula.
The formula uses a specific ratio, not simple subtraction.

choices.text: No correction needed — just treat the glucose
choices.feedback: The correction is essential for clinical decision-making.

questions.explanation: The corrected sodium formula for hyperglycemia: add 1.6 mEq/L to the measured sodium for every 100 mg/dL increase in glucose above 100 mg/dL. Some sources use a correction factor of 2.4 mEq/L for glucose >400 mg/dL. This accounts for the osmotic shift of water from intracellular to extracellular space caused by hyperglycemia, which dilutes measured sodium (pseudohyponatremia of sorts). As glucose is corrected, sodium rises.

questions.stem: SIADH treatment includes all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: This combination forces renal free water excretion — effective in SIADH.

Correct! NS can WORSEN SIADH hyponatremia. In SIADH, kidneys retain free water. NS provides Na+ that kidneys excrete, but the water is retained → dilution worsens. Use: fluid restriction, salt tabs + loop diuretics (force free water excretion), tolvaptan (V2 receptor antagonist) for refractory cases.
Tolvaptan blocks ADH receptor → aquaresis. Used for refractory SIADH (with close Na+ monitoring).
Fluid restriction is first-line for SIADH.

questions.explanation: Normal saline infusion should NOT be used to treat SIADH — in SIADH, the urine osmolality often exceeds the osmolality of normal saline (308 mOsm/L), so the kidneys excrete the sodium but retain the water, paradoxically worsening hyponatremia. SIADH treatments include fluid restriction (first-line), salt tablets, loop diuretics (to excrete free water), and vasopressin receptor antagonists (tolvaptan) for refractory cases.

questions.stem: Thiazide diuretics cause hyponatremia more than loop diuretics because:

choices.feedback: Loops are actually more potent diuretics.
Thiazides may mildly increase ADH but the primary mechanism is diluting segment blockade.
Loops can cause either hypo- or hypernatremia but less often hyponatremia than thiazides.

choices.text: Thiazides impair diluting ability without impairing concentrating ability
choices.feedback: Correct! Thiazides block NaCl reabsorption in the distal convoluted tubule (diluting segment) → can't dilute urine → retain free water. Loops block in the thick ascending limb → impair BOTH concentrating AND diluting → net effect is free water loss (less hyponatremia risk). This is why thiazides are the #1 drug cause of hyponatremia.

questions.explanation: Thiazide diuretics cause hyponatremia more frequently than loop diuretics because they impair the diluting ability of the distal convoluted tubule (where free water is normally generated) without impairing the concentrating ability of the medullary collecting duct. Loop diuretics impair both diluting and concentrating segments (thick ascending limb), washing out the medullary concentration gradient and actually increasing free water excretion.

questions.stem: Hypovolemic hyponatremia with UNa <20 suggests:

choices.feedback: Nephrotic syndrome causes hypervolemic hyponatremia.

Correct! UNa <20 in hypovolemic hyponatremia = kidneys are avidly retaining Na+ in response to volume depletion from extra-renal losses (GI, third-spacing, burns). UNa >20 in hypovolemic hyponatremia = renal losses (diuretics, adrenal insufficiency, cerebral salt wasting). Treatment: IV NS to restore volume.
Renal losses have UNa >20.
SIADH is euvolemic with UNa >20.

questions.explanation: Hypovolemic hyponatremia with UNa <20 mEq/L suggests extra-renal sodium and fluid losses (GI losses — diarrhea, vomiting; third-spacing — burns, pancreatitis; insensible losses). The kidneys are appropriately retaining sodium in response to volume depletion. UNa >20 mEq/L in hypovolemic hyponatremia suggests renal losses (diuretics, mineralocorticoid deficiency, salt-wasting nephropathy, cerebral salt wasting). Treatment is IV normal saline to restore volume.

questions.stem: Terminal ileum involvement with skip lesions and non-caseating granulomas. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Celiac affects duodenum/jejunum with villous atrophy, not granulomas.
Ischemic colitis affects watershed areas, no granulomas.
UC is continuous from rectum, mucosal only, no granulomas.

Correct! Crohn hallmarks: skip lesions (discontinuous), transmural inflammation, non-caseating granulomas, terminal ileum predilection. Can affect any part of GI tract (mouth to anus). Complications: fistulas, strictures, abscesses.

questions.explanation: Terminal ileum involvement, skip lesions (discontinuous inflammation with normal segments between), transmural inflammation, and non-caseating granulomas are pathognomonic for Crohn disease. Crohn can affect any part of the GI tract (mouth to anus) but most commonly involves the terminal ileum and colon. Complications include strictures, fistulas, and abscesses (transmural nature). UC is limited to the colon/rectum with continuous mucosal inflammation.

questions.stem: Which IBD is CURABLE with surgery?

choices.feedback: Correct! UC is confined to the colon. Total proctocolectomy with ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA) is curative. Crohn can affect any part of the GI tract and recurs after surgery (endoscopic recurrence at neo-terminal ileum in ~70% by 1 year).
UC is curable with total proctocolectomy.
Only UC is surgically curable.
Crohn recurs at anastomosis — surgery only for complications.

questions.explanation: Ulcerative colitis is the only IBD curable by surgery (total proctocolectomy removes the entire disease-bearing organ — colon and rectum). Restorative proctocolectomy with ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA/J-pouch) maintains continence. Crohn disease cannot be cured surgically because it can recur anywhere in the GI tract, and surgery is reserved for complications (strictures, fistulas, abscesses).

questions.stem: Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) is most associated with:

choices.feedback: Celiac is associated with dermatitis herpetiformis, not PSC.
PSC has some Crohn association but is predominantly linked to UC.
IBS has no hepatobiliary complications.

Correct! ~70% of PSC patients have UC. PSC = progressive fibrosis and stricturing of intrahepatic/extrahepatic bile ducts. ERCP: beading pattern. Increased risk of cholangiocarcinoma. UC patients with PSC have higher CRC risk → annual colonoscopy from PSC diagnosis.

questions.explanation: Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) is most strongly associated with ulcerative colitis — approximately 70-80% of PSC patients have UC, though only ~5% of UC patients develop PSC. PSC is characterized by progressive inflammation and fibrosis of intra- and extrahepatic bile ducts, leading to cholestasis, cirrhosis, and increased risk of cholangiocarcinoma. PSC can progress independently of UC colectomy.

questions.stem: Smoking's effect on IBD?

choices.feedback: Only protective in UC. Worsens Crohn.

choices.text: Worsens Crohn, paradoxically protective in UC
choices.feedback: Correct! One of the most counter-intuitive IBD facts. Smoking increases Crohn flares, recurrence after surgery, and need for immunosuppression. Nicotine may be protective in UC (mechanism unclear — possibly via anti-inflammatory effects on colonic mucosa). Former smokers have highest UC risk. Nicotine patches studied as UC adjunct.
It's the opposite: worsens Crohn, protective in UC.
Smoking significantly affects both diseases, in opposite directions.

questions.explanation: Smoking worsens Crohn disease (increases flares, recurrence after surgery, and complications) but is paradoxically protective in ulcerative colitis (nicotine may suppress mucosal inflammation). Former smokers have the highest risk of developing UC. Smoking cessation is strongly recommended for Crohn patients. This opposing effect is a classic board-tested distinction between the two IBDs.

questions.stem: Toxic megacolon — which interventions should be AVOIDED?

choices.feedback: These are part of the treatment for toxic megacolon.

Correct! Colonoscopy risks perforation. Opioids and anticholinergics decrease colonic motility → worsen dilation. Treatment: NPO, NG suction, IV fluids, IV steroids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, serial abdominal X-rays. Surgery (colectomy) if no improvement in 48-72h or signs of perforation.
Aggressive IV hydration is essential in toxic megacolon management.
NG decompression is part of the treatment.

questions.explanation: In toxic megacolon (acute colonic dilation with systemic toxicity), colonoscopy should be avoided (risk of perforation), and opioids and anticholinergics must be avoided (they further impair colonic motility and worsen dilation). Management includes bowel rest, NG decompression, IV fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, IV corticosteroids, and serial abdominal imaging. Failure to improve within 48-72 hours warrants surgical consultation for colectomy.

questions.stem: CRC surveillance in UC should begin:

choices.text: 8-10 years after diagnosis, then every 1-2 years
choices.feedback: Correct! CRC risk increases significantly after 8-10 years of UC. Surveillance colonoscopy with chromoendoscopy and random biopsies every 1-2 years. Risk factors for earlier/more frequent screening: pancolitis, PSC, family history of CRC, prior dysplasia. PSC patients: annual colonoscopy from PSC diagnosis.
CRC risk increases over time; surveillance starts later.
UC-related CRC can develop at any age based on disease duration, not patient age.
CRC surveillance is scheduled regardless of symptoms.

questions.explanation: CRC surveillance in UC should begin 8-10 years after diagnosis of extensive colitis (pancolitis or left-sided colitis), then every 1-2 years with chromoendoscopy or random 4-quadrant biopsies every 10 cm. Left-sided colitis carries lower CRC risk than pancolitis. Concomitant PSC increases CRC risk further, requiring annual surveillance from diagnosis. Proctitis alone has minimal increased CRC risk.

questions.stem: Fecal calprotectin is useful because:

choices.text: It distinguishes IBD from irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) noninvasively
choices.feedback: Correct! Fecal calprotectin is a neutrophil-derived protein elevated in intestinal inflammation. High sensitivity for IBD vs IBS. Calprotectin <50 μg/g essentially rules out active IBD. Also useful for monitoring disease activity and treatment response without colonoscopy.
FIT (fecal immunochemical test) screens for CRC, not calprotectin.
Calprotectin doesn't distinguish between the two types of IBD.
Iron studies assess absorption, not calprotectin.

questions.explanation: Fecal calprotectin (a neutrophil-derived protein) is a noninvasive biomarker that distinguishes IBD (elevated, typically >250 μg/g) from irritable bowel syndrome (IBS, normal levels). It correlates with intestinal inflammation severity and is useful for monitoring disease activity, predicting relapse, and assessing treatment response in known IBD. It reduces unnecessary colonoscopies in patients with GI symptoms.

questions.stem: Anti-TNF therapy is first-line biologic for moderate-severe IBD. Example?

choices.feedback: Rituximab is anti-CD20 (used in RA, MS, lymphoma), not for IBD.
MTX is an immunomodulator used as steroid-sparing agent in Crohn, not a biologic.

Correct! Anti-TNF agents: infliximab (IV infusion) and adalimumab (SC injection) are first-line biologics for moderate-severe Crohn and UC. Check for latent TB before starting (risk of reactivation). Other biologics: vedolizumab (gut-selective anti-integrin), ustekinumab (anti-IL-12/23), tofacitinib (JAK inhibitor, UC).
Omalizumab is anti-IgE for asthma/allergies, not IBD.

questions.explanation: Anti-TNF therapy is the first-line biologic for moderate-to-severe IBD unresponsive to conventional therapy. Infliximab (IV, chimeric) and adalimumab (SC, fully human) are the most widely used. They reduce mucosal inflammation, induce/maintain remission, promote mucosal healing, and reduce hospitalizations/surgeries. Monitor for tuberculosis reactivation (screen with IGRA before initiation), hepatitis B reactivation, and increased infection risk.

questions.stem: Biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdensity on CT that does NOT cross suture lines:

choices.feedback: SAH fills sulci and cisterns, not a discrete biconvex collection.

Correct! Epidural: between skull and dura. Biconvex/lenticular shape because blood is confined by dural attachments at suture lines. Usually arterial (middle meningeal artery from temporal bone fracture). Classic: lucid interval → rapid deterioration → uncal herniation.
IVH is within the ventricular system, not extra-axial.
Subdural is crescent-shaped and crosses suture lines.

questions.explanation: A biconvex (lens-shaped/lenticular) hyperdensity on CT that does NOT cross suture lines is characteristic of epidural hematoma. The dura is tightly adherent to the skull at suture lines, limiting the hematoma's spread. Most epidural hematomas are arterial, caused by rupture of the middle meningeal artery from temporal bone fracture. The biconvex shape results from blood dissecting the dura away from the inner skull table. Contrast with subdural hematoma, which is crescent-shaped and crosses suture lines.

questions.stem: 'Lucid interval' followed by rapid deterioration is classic for:

choices.feedback: Correct! Epidural: trauma → brief LOC → lucid interval (minutes to hours) → rapid deterioration from arterial bleeding expanding hematoma → uncal herniation (ipsilateral dilated pupil, contralateral hemiparesis). Surgical emergency requiring craniotomy. The lucid interval can be deceptive — always image after head trauma with LOC.
SDH typically has gradual progressive symptoms (especially chronic), not the classic lucid interval pattern.
Concussion resolves without deterioration.
SAH has progressive headache, not a lucid interval pattern.

questions.explanation: The 'lucid interval' — initial loss of consciousness followed by transient neurologic improvement then rapid deterioration — is the classic presentation of epidural hematoma. It occurs because the initial concussion causes LOC, followed by brief improvement as the patient recovers from the concussion, then rapid decline as the expanding arterial hematoma causes uncal herniation (ipsilateral pupil dilation, contralateral hemiparesis). The lucid interval occurs in ~20-50% of epidural hematomas and is an emergency requiring immediate surgical evacuation.

questions.stem: Crescent-shaped hyperdensity crossing suture lines on CT:

choices.feedback: SAH fills cisterns and sulci, not a crescent-shaped collection.

Correct! Subdural: between dura and arachnoid. Crescent-shaped (conforms to brain surface), crosses suture lines (dura not attached at sutures). Acute = hyperdense (white). Caused by bridging vein rupture. Surgery if >10 mm thickness or >5 mm midline shift.
Contusion is intraparenchymal, not extra-axial crescent.
Epidural is biconvex and does NOT cross sutures.

questions.explanation: A crescent-shaped hyperdensity that crosses suture lines on CT is characteristic of acute subdural hematoma (SDH). Subdural hematomas occur in the potential space between the dura and arachnoid, usually from torn bridging veins. Because the subdural space is not limited by sutural dural attachments, the hematoma can spread across suture lines, forming a concave-shaped collection conforming to the brain surface. Acute SDH is hyperdense on CT; subacute becomes isodense; chronic becomes hypodense.

questions.stem: Most common location for hypertensive ICH:

choices.feedback: Correct! Hypertensive ICH: basal ganglia/putamen (#1, ~35-50%), thalamus (~15%), pons (~5-10%), cerebellum (~5-10%). These are deep brain structures supplied by small penetrating arteries (lenticulostriate) that are damaged by chronic hypertension. Lobar hemorrhage in elderly → think cerebral amyloid angiopathy.
Occipital hemorrhage is less common from hypertension.
Frontal lobe ICH is less common from HTN.
Corpus callosum hemorrhage is rare and suggests vascular malformation.

questions.explanation: The basal ganglia (specifically the putamen) is the most common location for hypertensive intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH), accounting for approximately 50-60% of cases. This is due to the vulnerability of small perforating lenticulostriate arteries (branches of the MCA) to chronic hypertension-induced lipohyalinosis and Charcot-Bouchard microaneurysm formation. Other hypertensive ICH locations include thalamus (~15%), pons (~10%), and cerebellum (~10%). Lobar hemorrhages in elderly patients suggest cerebral amyloid angiopathy.

questions.stem: Reversal agent for dabigatran-associated hemorrhage:

choices.feedback: Protamine reverses heparin, not DOACs.
TXA is antifibrinolytic but does not specifically reverse dabigatran.
Vitamin K reverses warfarin (factor synthesis), not dabigatran.

Correct! Idarucizumab is a monoclonal antibody fragment that specifically binds dabigatran. Complete reversal within minutes. For factor Xa inhibitors (rivaroxaban, apixaban): andexanet alfa. For warfarin: 4-factor PCC + IV vitamin K. For heparin: protamine sulfate.

questions.explanation: Idarucizumab (Praxbind) is the specific reversal agent for dabigatran (Pradaxa), a direct thrombin inhibitor. Idarucizumab is a monoclonal antibody fragment that binds dabigatran with 350x greater affinity than thrombin, providing complete reversal within minutes. For factor Xa inhibitor-associated hemorrhage (rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban), andexanet alfa (Andexxa) is the specific reversal agent. 4-factor PCC is used when specific reversal agents are unavailable.

questions.stem: Chronic subdural hematoma appears on CT as:

choices.feedback: Correct! CT density changes over time: acute (<3d) = hyperdense (white), subacute (3-21d) = isodense (can be missed!), chronic (>21d) = hypodense (dark). Chronic SDH: elderly patients, often bilateral, may not recall trauma. Symptoms: progressive confusion, personality changes, headache, gait instability. Treatment: burr hole drainage if symptomatic.
Isodense = subacute (3-21 days), not chronic.
Enhancement pattern suggests tumor, not chronic SDH.
Hyperdense = acute (<3 days) blood.

questions.explanation: Chronic subdural hematoma (>3 weeks old) appears hypodense (dark) on non-contrast CT because the blood products have broken down over time — hemoglobin degrades, protein content decreases, and the collection becomes more serous. Acute SDH is hyperdense (bright white), subacute SDH (~1-3 weeks) is isodense (similar to brain parenchyma, and therefore easily missed), and chronic SDH is hypodense (dark). Mixed-density SDH suggests acute-on-chronic bleeding.

questions.stem: Cerebellar hemorrhage requires surgical evacuation when:

choices.feedback: Anticoagulant status affects urgency of reversal but not surgical indication.
Medical management alone for large cerebellar hemorrhage risks brainstem herniation.
Small asymptomatic cerebellar hemorrhages can be managed conservatively.

Correct! Posterior fossa is a confined space — cerebellar hemorrhage >3 cm or with brainstem compression/hydrocephalus requires emergent surgical evacuation (suboccipital craniectomy). Unlike supratentorial ICH (STICH trials: no surgical benefit), cerebellar ICH has clear surgical indications.

questions.explanation: Cerebellar hemorrhage requires emergent surgical evacuation (posterior fossa decompression) when the hematoma is >3 cm in diameter or when there is neurological deterioration, hydrocephalus (from fourth ventricle compression), or brainstem compression. Posterior fossa hemorrhages are particularly dangerous because the limited space allows rapid brainstem compression and herniation. Smaller hematomas without mass effect can be managed conservatively with close monitoring in an ICU with neurosurgical backup.

questions.stem: ICH BP target per INTERACT2:

choices.feedback: Too aggressive — risks cerebral hypoperfusion.

Correct! INTERACT2 trial: intensive SBP lowering to <140 within 1 hour showed trend toward improved functional outcomes in ICH. Safe and well-tolerated. IV nicardipine or labetalol for rapid control. Note: avoid SBP <110 (risks hypoperfusion).
Uncontrolled hypertension promotes hematoma expansion.
180 is the post-tPA target for ischemic stroke, not ICH.

questions.explanation: The INTERACT2 trial demonstrated that intensive blood pressure lowering to SBP <140 mmHg within 1 hour of acute ICH presentation is safe and associated with improved functional outcomes compared to a target of <180 mmHg. Current AHA/ASA guidelines recommend this target for patients presenting with SBP 150-220 mmHg. Rapid IV antihypertensives (nicardipine, clevidipine, labetalol) are used. Overly aggressive lowering may reduce cerebral perfusion, so careful monitoring is essential.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with proximal muscle weakness, violaceous eyelid discoloration, and papules over her knuckles. CK is 5,000 U/L. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Heliotrope rash (violaceous eyelid discoloration) + Gottron papules (papules over knuckles/MCPs) + proximal weakness + elevated CK = classic dermatomyositis. Must screen for underlying malignancy (15-30% association).
IBM affects elderly men, causes distal (finger flexor) + proximal weakness asymmetrically, and does not have skin findings.
Fibromyalgia causes diffuse pain and fatigue but has NORMAL CK and no muscle weakness on exam.
PM has proximal weakness and elevated CK but does NOT have skin findings. The heliotrope rash and Gottron papules are diagnostic of DM.

questions.explanation: This presentation — proximal muscle weakness with violaceous eyelid discoloration (heliotrope rash), likely with Gottron papules over the knuckles — is classic for dermatomyositis. DM is an inflammatory myopathy with characteristic skin findings: heliotrope rash (violaceous periorbital edema), Gottron papules (erythematous papules over MCP/PIP joints), V-sign (chest), and shawl sign (upper back). Elevated CK, aldolase, and anti-Mi-2 antibodies support diagnosis. EMG shows myopathic changes. MRI shows muscle edema. Biopsy shows perifascicular atrophy (pathognomonic).

questions.stem: Which antibody is associated with antisynthetase syndrome?

choices.feedback: Anti-centromere is associated with limited scleroderma (CREST), not antisynthetase syndrome.
Anti-Mi-2 is associated with classic DM with prominent skin findings and GOOD prognosis (good steroid response, low cancer risk).

Correct! Anti-Jo-1 (anti-histidyl-tRNA synthetase) is the classic antisynthetase antibody. The syndrome includes: myositis + interstitial lung disease + mechanic's hands (cracked, fissured fingers) + non-erosive arthritis + Raynaud phenomenon + fever. ILD is often the most life-threatening component.
Anti-Scl-70 is specific for diffuse systemic sclerosis, not myositis.

questions.explanation: Anti-Jo-1 (anti-histidyl-tRNA synthetase) is the antibody associated with antisynthetase syndrome, which features inflammatory myopathy, interstitial lung disease, mechanic's hands (cracked, fissured skin on lateral fingers), arthritis, Raynaud phenomenon, and fever. Anti-Jo-1 is the most common antisynthetase antibody (~20-30% of myositis patients). Other antisynthetase antibodies include anti-PL-7, anti-PL-12, anti-EJ, and anti-OJ. Antisynthetase syndrome carries significant morbidity from progressive ILD.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with slowly progressive weakness of grip strength and knee extension. CK is mildly elevated. Muscle biopsy shows rimmed vacuoles. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Statin myopathy causes elevated CK and weakness but biopsy shows necrosis without rimmed vacuoles or inflammation.

Correct! IBM: elderly men, insidious onset, asymmetric weakness of finger flexors (grip) and quadriceps (knee extensors), mildly elevated CK, rimmed vacuoles + inclusion bodies on biopsy. RESISTANT to immunosuppressive therapy — physical therapy is the mainstay.
PM shows endomysial CD8+ T cell infiltration on biopsy, not rimmed vacuoles. PM also responds to immunosuppression.
DM has characteristic skin findings and perifascicular atrophy on biopsy, not rimmed vacuoles.

questions.explanation: Inclusion body myositis (IBM) is the most common acquired myopathy in patients >50 years old. It uniquely presents with asymmetric weakness affecting both proximal AND distal muscles, particularly finger flexors (grip weakness) and knee extensors (quadriceps). Onset is insidious over months to years. CK is mildly elevated or normal. Biopsy shows rimmed vacuoles with endomysial inflammation and intracellular amyloid deposits. IBM is characteristically resistant to immunosuppressive therapy, distinguishing it from polymyositis and dermatomyositis.

questions.stem: What is the most appropriate next step in a 50-year-old woman newly diagnosed with dermatomyositis?

choices.feedback: While physical therapy is important, it is not the most critical immediate step. Cancer screening is essential given the strong DM-cancer association.

Correct! Dermatomyositis has a 15-30% malignancy association, highest in the first 1-3 years. Age-appropriate cancer screening PLUS CT chest/abdomen/pelvis is recommended. Most common: ovarian, lung, GI, breast, and lymphoma. Anti-TIF1γ and anti-NXP2 antibodies increase cancer suspicion further.
Steroids (not MTX alone) are first-line. More importantly, cancer screening should occur at diagnosis.
HLA typing is not part of the standard DM workup or cancer screening.

questions.explanation: Dermatomyositis carries a significant risk of occult malignancy (10-25%), particularly in adults. The most common associated cancers are ovarian, lung, breast, colon, and pancreatic. Cancer screening with CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, age-appropriate cancer screening (mammography, colonoscopy), and gynecologic evaluation should be performed at diagnosis and repeated during follow-up. The cancer risk is highest within the first 3 years of DM diagnosis. Cancer risk is highest with anti-TIF1γ antibodies.

questions.stem: A patient with DM on chronic prednisone develops worsening proximal weakness. CK is normal. What is the most likely cause?

choices.feedback: In a disease flare, CK would be elevated. Normal CK with worsening weakness on steroids suggests steroid myopathy.

Correct! Normal CK + worsening weakness on chronic steroids = steroid myopathy. The treatment is to TAPER steroids (counterintuitive). In contrast, disease flare shows elevated CK → increase immunosuppression. EMG and clinical context help differentiate.
DM does not transform into IBM. They are distinct entities.
While hypothyroidism causes myopathy with elevated CK, this scenario points to steroid myopathy given the normal CK and steroid use.

questions.explanation: Worsening proximal weakness with a NORMAL CK in a patient on chronic corticosteroids indicates steroid-induced myopathy rather than DM flare. In active DM, CK would be elevated. Steroid myopathy causes painless proximal weakness (especially hip flexors and quadriceps) from type II muscle fiber atrophy, with normal CK because there is no muscle inflammation or necrosis. Treatment is steroid dose reduction. This clinical distinction (weakness + normal CK on steroids = steroid myopathy; weakness + elevated CK = disease flare) is a classic exam scenario.

questions.stem: Gottron papules are pathognomonic for which condition?

choices.feedback: Correct! Gottron papules (erythematous-violaceous papules/plaques over MCPs, PIPs, elbows, knees — extensor surfaces) are PATHOGNOMONIC for dermatomyositis. Note: Gottron papules are over the knuckles, while SLE rash spares the knuckles and involves the skin between joints.
PsA has psoriatic plaques, nail pitting, and dactylitis — not Gottron papules.
RA causes rheumatoid nodules on extensor surfaces but not the violaceous papular rash of Gottron papules.
SLE has a malar rash (spares nasolabial folds) but not Gottron papules.

questions.explanation: Gottron papules — violaceous or erythematous, flat-topped papules or plaques overlying the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints — are pathognomonic for dermatomyositis. They may also appear over elbows, knees, and malleoli. Gottron papules are one of the characteristic cutaneous findings used in the Bohan and Peter diagnostic criteria for DM, alongside the heliotrope rash. Unlike lupus rash (which spares the knuckles), Gottron papules specifically involve the joint prominences.

questions.stem: Which myositis antibody carries the highest cancer risk?

choices.feedback: Correct! Anti-TIF1γ (anti-p155/140) is the antibody most strongly associated with cancer in adult DM. Up to 50-75% of anti-TIF1γ-positive DM patients have an underlying malignancy. Anti-NXP2 is also cancer-associated. These antibodies should prompt aggressive malignancy workup.
Anti-Jo-1 is associated with antisynthetase syndrome and ILD, not particularly increased cancer risk.
Anti-Mi-2 is actually associated with classic DM and GOOD prognosis — lower cancer risk and good treatment response.
Anti-SRP is associated with necrotizing autoimmune myopathy (severe, refractory) but not specifically cancer.

questions.explanation: Anti-TIF1γ (anti-p155/140) antibody carries the highest cancer risk among myositis-specific antibodies, with malignancy identified in approximately 40-80% of anti-TIF1γ-positive DM patients. TIF1γ (transcriptional intermediary factor 1 gamma) is involved in TGF-β signaling and tumor suppression. Patients with anti-TIF1γ positivity require intensive and repeated cancer screening. Other cancer-associated antibodies include anti-NXP2, though the association is less strong than anti-TIF1γ.

questions.stem: Which inflammatory myopathy does NOT respond to immunosuppressive therapy?

choices.feedback: PM responds to steroids and immunosuppressive agents like azathioprine and methotrexate.
Antisynthetase syndrome responds to immunosuppression, though ILD management may require aggressive multi-agent therapy.
DM generally responds well to steroids, IVIG, and steroid-sparing agents.

Correct! IBM is characteristically resistant to all forms of immunosuppressive therapy, including steroids, MTX, AZA, and IVIG. Physical therapy and assistive devices are the mainstay of management. Dysphagia management is also important. IBM is increasingly recognized as having both inflammatory and degenerative components.

questions.explanation: Inclusion body myositis (IBM) is the inflammatory myopathy that characteristically does NOT respond to immunosuppressive therapy (corticosteroids, methotrexate, azathioprine, IVIG). This treatment resistance is a defining clinical feature and helps distinguish IBM from polymyositis and dermatomyositis, both of which typically respond to immunosuppression. The pathogenesis of IBM involves both inflammatory and degenerative components (protein aggregation, mitochondrial dysfunction), which may explain immunotherapy resistance. Management is supportive: physical therapy, fall prevention, swallowing evaluation.

questions.stem: What X-ray finding distinguishes jejunal atresia from duodenal atresia?

choices.feedback: Double bubble is specific to DUODENAL atresia. Jejunal atresia shows multiple dilated loops because the obstruction is further downstream.
Pneumatosis is the hallmark of necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC), not intestinal atresia.

choices.text: Multiple dilated loops with air-fluid levels
choices.feedback: Correct! Jejunal/ileal atresia shows multiple dilated bowel loops proximal to the obstruction with air-fluid levels and absent distal gas. The more distal the atresia, the more loops are dilated. This differs from the characteristic double bubble of duodenal atresia.
Bird-beak sign suggests volvulus or intussusception on contrast studies, not atresia.

questions.stem: Why does this distinction matter clinically?

choices.text: It means surgery can be delayed
choices.feedback: Vascular accidents cause permanent atresia — they do not heal. Surgery is required and should not be delayed.

choices.text: It explains why jejunal atresia is NOT associated with chromosomal anomalies
choices.feedback: Correct! Duodenal atresia is intrinsic (recanalization failure) and associated with Down syndrome (30%). Jejunal/ileal atresia is extrinsic (vascular accident) — a random event NOT associated with chromosomal anomalies. This means karyotype and extensive association screening are less critical for jejunal/ileal atresia compared to duodenal atresia.

choices.text: It predicts the patient will have short bowel syndrome
choices.feedback: Some patients develop short bowel syndrome (especially Type 3b/apple peel), but the distinction matters more for associated anomaly screening, not predicting bowel length.

choices.text: It determines which antibiotics to give
choices.feedback: Intestinal atresia is not infectious. The vascular accident happens in utero and is sterile.

questions.stem: What defines the apple peel (Christmas tree) deformity?

choices.text: Two blind ends connected by a fibrous cord
choices.feedback: That describes Type 3a (mesenteric gap) — no apple peel deformity.

choices.text: Multiple atresias along the bowel like a string of sausages
choices.feedback: Multiple atresias is Type 4, not Type 3b. Type 4 resembles a 'string of sausages' with multiple atretic segments.

choices.text: A web-like membrane across the bowel lumen
choices.feedback: An intraluminal membrane/web is Type 1 atresia, similar to duodenal Type 1. Apple peel is far more complex.

choices.text: Proximal jejunal atresia with absent SMA; distal bowel spirals around a marginal vessel
choices.feedback: Correct! Type 3b (apple peel/Christmas tree): The proximal jejunum ends blindly near the ligament of Treitz. The superior mesenteric artery is absent beyond this point. The remaining distal small bowel spirals around a single marginal artery from the ileocolic or right colic vessels, resembling an apple peel. These patients are at highest risk for short bowel syndrome.

questions.stem: Why is the dilated proximal bowel resected rather than used for the anastomosis?

choices.text: It has abnormal peristalsis and poor motility
choices.feedback: Correct! The proximal dilated segment has hypertrophied but dysfunctional muscle. It acts as a functional obstruction even after anastomosis if not resected. The dilated-to-normal size mismatch also makes anastomosis difficult. Tapering (reducing the caliber) is an alternative to resection when bowel length must be preserved.

choices.text: It contains meconium that cannot be cleared
choices.feedback: While meconium may be present, it can be irrigated. The real reason is the dysfunctional motility of the dilated segment.

choices.text: The blood supply is always compromised
choices.feedback: The proximal segment typically has adequate blood supply — it's dilated because it received intestinal contents but couldn't pass them distally. Motility, not blood supply, is the issue.

choices.text: It is at risk for malignant transformation
choices.feedback: Neonatal bowel does not develop malignancy from dilation. The issue is purely functional — poor motility.

questions.stem: What determines whether this baby will develop short bowel syndrome?

choices.feedback: Gestational age affects overall prognosis but doesn't determine short bowel syndrome — that depends on remaining bowel length and ICV status.

choices.text: Whether the atresia was Type 1 or Type 3
choices.feedback: While Type 3b has the highest short bowel risk, the actual remaining length and ICV preservation are what matter for the individual patient.
Formula type matters for feeding tolerance but doesn't determine whether short bowel syndrome develops. Anatomy is the primary determinant.

choices.text: Presence of the ileocecal valve and remaining bowel length
choices.feedback: Correct! Short bowel syndrome risk depends on: remaining bowel length (<75 cm without ICV or <40 cm with ICV in neonates is high-risk), presence of ileocecal valve (prevents bacterial reflux, improves transit time), and intestinal adaptation capacity. This baby at 80 cm WITH ICV has a favorable prognosis. The microcolon is simply a disused colon — it will grow to normal caliber with enteral feeding.

questions.stem: Active phase arrest of labor is defined as no cervical change for:

choices.feedback: 1h without progress in active labor is not arrest.
12h exceeds the criteria — intervention would occur much sooner.
2h arrest criteria are for the 2nd stage, not active phase.

Correct! Active phase (6-10 cm): arrest = no cervical change for ≥6h with adequate contractions OR ≥4h with adequate contractions + ruptured membranes + oxytocin. This defines failed induction/arrest of dilation → indication for cesarean delivery.

questions.explanation: Stages of labor: First (onset to full dilation) — latent phase (0-6 cm, variable duration) and active phase (6-10 cm, ~1 cm/hr). Second: full dilation to delivery. Third: delivery to placental expulsion (normally <30 min). Fourth: first 1-2 hours postpartum. Active management of third stage (oxytocin after delivery, controlled cord traction) reduces PPH risk. Indications for cesarean: failed induction, non-reassuring fetal heart tracing, malpresentation, placenta previa.

questions.stem: Late decelerations indicate:

choices.feedback: Head compression = early decelerations (mirror contractions). Benign.
Cord compression = variable decelerations (abrupt, variable shape/timing).
Fetal sleep causes decreased variability, not late decelerations.

Correct! Late decels: gradual onset AFTER contraction peak, gradual return. Reflect decreased placental perfusion during contractions. Concerning for fetal hypoxia. Management: left lateral positioning, IV fluids, O2, stop oxytocin. If persistent with absent variability (category III) → emergent delivery.

questions.explanation: OB/GYN management requires systematic assessment of gestational age, maternal hemodynamic status, and fetal well-being. Evidence-based guidelines from ACOG guide management decisions for obstetric emergencies and gynecologic conditions.

questions.stem: #1 cause of postpartum hemorrhage?

choices.feedback: Correct! Uterine atony accounts for ~80% of PPH. The uterus fails to contract after delivery → exposed spiral arteries bleed massively. Treatment ladder: bimanual uterine massage + oxytocin IV → methylergonovine IM (contraindicated in HTN) → carboprost IM (contraindicated in asthma) → misoprostol PR → tamponade (Bakri balloon) → surgery (B-Lynch suture, uterine artery ligation, hysterectomy).
Lacerations are the #2 cause (trauma).
DIC/coagulopathy is the #4 cause (4 T's: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin).
Retained placenta is the #3 cause. Manual extraction or curettage needed.

questions.explanation: Postpartum hemorrhage (≥1,000 mL blood loss or hemodynamic instability): most common cause is uterine atony (70%). Management: bimanual uterine massage, oxytocin infusion (first-line), methylergonovine (contraindicated in hypertension), carboprost/hemabate (contraindicated in asthma), misoprostol (rectal). If medical management fails: intrauterine balloon tamponade, uterine compression sutures (B-Lynch), uterine artery embolization, or hysterectomy. Tranexamic acid should be given within 3 hours.

questions.stem: Shoulder dystocia — FIRST maneuver?

choices.feedback: C-section after head delivery is extremely difficult. Vaginal maneuvers first.
NEVER apply fundal pressure — worsens impaction and increases injury risk.

Correct! McRoberts: sharp flexion of maternal thighs toward abdomen → widens pelvic outlet, flattens sacral promontory. Resolves ~40% of shoulder dystocias. Simultaneously apply suprapubic pressure (NOT fundal). If McRoberts fails → Rubin maneuver, Wood's corkscrew, posterior arm delivery.
Zavanelli is a last resort (cephalic replacement → emergency C-section). Not first-line.

questions.explanation: Correct: McRoberts maneuver (hyperflexion of maternal thighs).

questions.stem: First-line maneuver for shoulder dystocia results in Erb palsy affecting:

choices.feedback: Klumpke affects C8-T1: hand/wrist weakness ('claw hand'). Less common.

Correct! Erb-Duchenne palsy: upper brachial plexus (C5-C6) injury → loss of shoulder abduction/external rotation, elbow flexion, forearm supination. Arm hangs limply at side in 'waiter's tip' position. Most common brachial plexus injury from shoulder dystocia. ~90% resolve spontaneously.
Lumbar plexus injury doesn't occur in shoulder dystocia.
C3-C5 = phrenic nerve → diaphragm paralysis. Would cause respiratory distress.

questions.explanation: OB/GYN management requires systematic assessment of gestational age, maternal hemodynamic status, and fetal well-being. Evidence-based guidelines from ACOG guide management decisions for obstetric emergencies and gynecologic conditions.

questions.stem: Bishop score is used to assess:

choices.feedback: Gestational age is determined by LMP and ultrasound dating.
Biophysical profile and NST assess fetal well-being.
Preeclampsia risk is assessed by clinical factors, not Bishop score.

Correct! Bishop score (0-13): cervical dilation, effacement, station, consistency, position. Score ≥6 = favorable for induction (high success rate). <6 = unfavorable → cervical ripening needed first (misoprostol, dinoprostone, or mechanical Foley bulb).

questions.explanation: OB/GYN management requires systematic assessment of gestational age, maternal hemodynamic status, and fetal well-being. Evidence-based guidelines from ACOG guide management decisions for obstetric emergencies and gynecologic conditions.

questions.stem: Category III FHR tracing requires:

choices.feedback: Amniotomy alone won't resolve category III tracings.

Correct! Category III: absent variability + recurrent late or variable decels, OR bradycardia, OR sinusoidal pattern. Indicates potential fetal acidemia. Immediate intrauterine resuscitation (positioning, fluids, O2, stop oxytocin). If no improvement → emergent cesarean delivery. Category I = normal, Category II = indeterminate.
Category III is immediately concerning — not safe to observe.
Terbutaline (tocolytic) may briefly help by relaxing the uterus but category III requires preparation for delivery.

questions.explanation: OB/GYN management requires systematic assessment of gestational age, maternal hemodynamic status, and fetal well-being. Evidence-based guidelines from ACOG guide management decisions for obstetric emergencies and gynecologic conditions.

questions.stem: Sinusoidal FHR pattern is most associated with:

choices.feedback: Chorioamnionitis causes fetal tachycardia, not sinusoidal pattern.
Sleep causes decreased variability but not a sinusoidal pattern.
Sinusoidal pattern is never normal.

Correct! Sinusoidal pattern: smooth, undulating, sine wave-like FHR pattern without variability. Most ominous when caused by severe fetal anemia (Rh disease, fetomaternal hemorrhage, ruptured vasa previa). Category III tracing → emergent delivery. Can also be seen with narcotics (pseudosinusoidal — less concerning).

questions.explanation: Correct: Severe fetal anemia (Rh isoimmunization, massive fetomaternal hemorrhage).

questions.stem: What is the diagnosis, and what is the embryological basis?

choices.text: Laryngomalacia — immature laryngeal cartilage from 4th/6th arch
choices.feedback: Correct! Laryngomalacia is the most common cause of stridor in newborns (60-70%). The laryngeal cartilages (derived from pharyngeal arches 4 and 6) are immature/flaccid. The supraglottic structures (omega-shaped epiglottis, aryepiglottic folds, arytenoids) collapse inward during inspiration. Most cases (>90%) resolve spontaneously by age 18-24 months as cartilage matures. Severe cases: supraglottoplasty.

choices.text: Vocal cord paralysis — recurrent laryngeal nerve injury
choices.feedback: Vocal cord paralysis causes a weak cry and stridor but laryngoscopy would show immobile cords, not omega epiglottis with arytenoid prolapse.
Subglottic stenosis causes biphasic stridor (inspiratory AND expiratory), not purely inspiratory. It's also more commonly acquired (from intubation) than congenital.
Tracheomalacia causes expiratory stridor/wheezing, not inspiratory stridor. The lesion is below the larynx.

questions.stem: Which nerve is injured, and why is the LEFT side more commonly affected?

choices.text: Left recurrent laryngeal nerve — longer course looping under the aortic arch
choices.feedback: Correct! The left recurrent laryngeal nerve loops under the aortic arch (4th arch derivative on the left), giving it a longer intrathoracic course than the right (which loops under the subclavian). This makes the left RLN vulnerable to: birth trauma, thoracic surgery, mediastinal masses, PDA ligation, and aortic arch anomalies. The RLN innervates all intrinsic laryngeal muscles EXCEPT the cricothyroid (which is innervated by the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve).

choices.text: Vagus nerve trunk — complete vagal injury
choices.feedback: Complete vagal injury would cause both cord paralysis and pharyngeal dysfunction. Isolated cord paralysis points to the recurrent laryngeal branch specifically.

choices.text: Superior laryngeal nerve — damage above the larynx
choices.feedback: SLN injury causes voice fatigue and pitch changes (cricothyroid dysfunction), not vocal cord paralysis.

choices.text: Right recurrent laryngeal nerve — compressed by the right subclavian
choices.feedback: The right RLN has a shorter course (loops under the right subclavian) and is actually LESS commonly affected than the left.

questions.stem: Why is the subglottis the most common site for acquired stenosis after intubation?

choices.text: The subglottis has the poorest blood supply
choices.feedback: Blood supply is adequate. The issue is the mechanical rigidity of the complete cricoid ring that prevents expansion.

choices.text: The cricoid is the only complete cartilage ring — it cannot expand
choices.feedback: Correct! The cricoid cartilage is the ONLY complete ring in the airway (all tracheal rings are C-shaped with a posterior membranous portion). Because it cannot expand, any mucosal edema or scarring in the subglottis causes significant obstruction. This is also why the subglottis is the narrowest point of the pediatric airway. Endotracheal tube pressure against the non-expandable cricoid ring → pressure necrosis → scarring → stenosis.

choices.text: The vocal cords protect the upper airway but not the subglottis
choices.feedback: The ET tube passes through both the glottis and subglottis. The issue is the non-distensible nature of the cricoid, not differential exposure.

choices.text: The subglottic mucosa is squamous epithelium
choices.feedback: The subglottis is actually lined by pseudostratified columnar (respiratory) epithelium. The epithelial type isn't the primary issue — it's the rigid cartilaginous ring.

questions.stem: What is the normal shape of tracheal cartilage rings, and why does it matter?

choices.text: O-shaped (complete circles) — to maintain airway patency
choices.feedback: O-shaped complete rings are ABNORMAL. Normal tracheal rings are C-shaped with a posterior membranous portion.
Tracheal cartilage is specifically C-shaped, not flat. The shape is critical for function.
Tracheal cartilage consists of discrete C-shaped rings, not a continuous spiral.

choices.text: C-shaped with a posterior membranous portion — allows esophageal bolus passage
choices.feedback: Correct! Normal tracheal rings are C-shaped (horseshoe) with a posterior membranous wall (trachealis muscle). This allows: 1) The esophagus to expand during swallowing (shared posterior wall). 2) Dynamic narrowing during cough (increasing velocity to clear secretions). Complete rings (O-shaped) are rigid, cannot expand, and cause fixed stenosis. Associated with PA sling (50-65%) and cardiac anomalies. Treatment: slide tracheoplasty (divide trachea, slide segments to widen).

questions.explanation: Best answer: C-shaped with a posterior membranous portion — allows esophageal bolus passage and cough.

questions.stem: Which is the ONLY intrinsic laryngeal muscle NOT innervated by the recurrent laryngeal nerve?

choices.feedback: The lateral cricoarytenoid is innervated by the RLN. It adducts the cords (closes for phonation/protection).
The thyroarytenoid is innervated by the RLN. It relaxes and shortens the vocal cords.

Correct! The cricothyroid muscle is innervated by the EXTERNAL branch of the SUPERIOR laryngeal nerve (from arch 4), NOT the RLN (arch 6). It tenses the vocal cords (increases pitch). All OTHER intrinsic laryngeal muscles are RLN (arch 6). The INTERNAL branch of the SLN is sensory above the cords. Memory aid: The cricothyroid is the odd one out — 4th arch muscle with 4th arch nerve.
The PCA is the only abductor of the cords and IS innervated by the RLN. Loss of PCA = inability to open cords = airway compromise.

questions.stem: AST:ALT ratio >2:1 suggests which etiology?

choices.feedback: Correct! AST:ALT >2:1 is classic for alcoholic hepatitis. Alcohol depletes pyridoxine (B6), which is required for ALT synthesis → ALT is disproportionately low. AST rarely exceeds 300 in alcoholic hepatitis. If transaminases >1000, think viral hepatitis, ischemia, or toxin.
MASLD typically has mildly elevated ALT > AST.
Hep C typically has ALT > AST.
AIH has very high transaminases but no specific AST:ALT pattern.

questions.explanation: AST:ALT ratio >2:1 suggests alcoholic liver disease. Alcohol preferentially elevates AST through mitochondrial damage and pyridoxal 5'-phosphate depletion (reducing ALT synthesis). Values rarely exceed 300 U/L in alcoholic hepatitis. Markedly elevated transaminases (>1000 U/L) suggest viral hepatitis, drug-induced liver injury, or ischemic hepatitis rather than alcohol.

questions.stem: SAAG ≥1.1 indicates:

choices.feedback: Correct! SAAG ≥1.1 = portal hypertension with 97% accuracy. Cirrhosis is the most common cause. Also: right heart failure, Budd-Chiari, portal vein thrombosis. SAAG <1.1: peritoneal carcinomatosis, TB peritonitis, nephrotic syndrome, pancreatitis.
TB peritonitis: SAAG <1.1, high protein, lymphocytic predominance.
Nephrotic ascites has SAAG <1.1.
Malignancy typically has SAAG <1.1 (except hepatic metastases causing portal HTN).

questions.explanation: SAAG ≥1.1 g/dL indicates portal hypertension as the cause of ascites. Calculate by subtracting ascitic fluid albumin from serum albumin. Portal hypertensive causes: cirrhosis (most common), cardiac ascites (constrictive pericarditis, right heart failure), Budd-Chiari syndrome. SAAG <1.1: peritoneal carcinomatosis, tuberculous peritonitis, nephrotic syndrome, pancreatic ascites.

questions.stem: SBP is diagnosed when ascitic fluid PMN count is:

choices.feedback: Small numbers of WBCs are normal in ascitic fluid.
Waiting for culture delays treatment and increases mortality.

Correct! PMN >250/mm³ = SBP. Start empiric ceftriaxone immediately — cultures are positive in only ~40% of cases. IV albumin (weight-based dosing day 1 + weight-based dosing day 3) reduces hepatorenal syndrome and mortality. After recovery: lifelong SBP prophylaxis with daily fluoroquinolone or TMP-SMX.
>1000 is likely but the diagnostic threshold is 250.

questions.explanation: SBP is diagnosed at ascitic fluid PMN >250/mm³ and requires empiric antibiotic treatment immediately, without waiting for culture results. Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli, Klebsiella) are the most common causative organisms. A single organism on culture with PMN >250 confirms the diagnosis. Polymicrobial infection or very high PMN suggests secondary bacterial peritonitis (bowel perforation) requiring CT imaging.

questions.stem: Hepatic encephalopathy first-line treatment?

choices.text: Lactulose (goal 2-3 soft stools/day) and rifaximin for maintenance
choices.feedback: Correct! Lactulose: osmotic laxative that acidifies colonic lumen → converts NH3 to NH4+ (non-absorbable) → fecal excretion. Titrate to 2-3 soft BMs/day. Rifaximin: non-absorbable antibiotic reducing ammonia-producing gut bacteria. ALSO: identify and treat precipitant (infection, GI bleed, constipation, hypoK, sedatives).
Transplant is definitive for cirrhosis but HE episodes are manageable medically.
Systemic antibiotics are not first-line for HE.
Outdated practice! Protein restriction worsens sarcopenia and outcomes. Normal/high protein diet is now recommended.

questions.explanation: Lactulose targets 2-3 soft stools/day to reduce ammonia absorption. For acute HE, lactulose can be given orally or by retention enema. Rifaximin (non-absorbable antibiotic) is added for secondary prophylaxis after the first episode of overt HE, reducing recurrence by ~50%. Dietary protein restriction is no longer recommended. Addressing precipitating factors (infection, GI bleeding, constipation, medications, electrolyte abnormalities) is essential.

questions.stem: Wilson disease classic findings?

choices.feedback: These are general signs of chronic liver disease, not specific to Wilson.

choices.text: Kayser-Fleischer rings, neuropsych symptoms, low ceruloplasmin
choices.feedback: Correct! Wilson disease: autosomal recessive copper overload (ATP7B mutation). Kayser-Fleischer rings (copper in Descemet membrane — slit lamp exam), neuropsychiatric symptoms (tremor, dysarthria, personality change), hepatic disease (hepatitis → cirrhosis), hemolytic anemia (Coombs-negative). Low ceruloplasmin, elevated 24h urine copper. Treatment: D-penicillamine, trientine, zinc.
That's hemochromatosis (iron overload).

choices.text: Positive ANA and anti-smooth muscle antibodies
choices.feedback: That's autoimmune hepatitis.

questions.explanation: Wilson disease classic findings include Kayser-Fleischer rings (copper deposits in Descemet membrane of the cornea on slit-lamp exam), neuropsychiatric symptoms (tremor, dysarthria, behavioral changes), and low serum ceruloplasmin. Wilson disease is an autosomal recessive defect in ATP7B gene causing hepatic copper accumulation. Diagnosis confirmed by 24-hour urine copper (elevated), liver biopsy copper quantification. Treatment: penicillamine or trientine (copper chelators) and zinc (blocks intestinal absorption).

questions.stem: Hemochromatosis screening test?

choices.feedback: Ceruloplasmin screens for Wilson disease (copper), not hemochromatosis (iron).
These are autoimmune hepatitis markers.
Biopsy is confirmatory but not the screening test.

Correct! Transferrin saturation >45% is the best screening test. Elevated ferritin supports the diagnosis. Confirm with HFE gene testing (C282Y homozygote). Liver biopsy/MRI for iron quantification. 'Bronze diabetes': skin hyperpigmentation + diabetes + cirrhosis. Treatment: therapeutic phlebotomy (target ferritin 50-100).

questions.explanation: Hemochromatosis screening uses transferrin saturation (>45% is abnormal) and serum ferritin (elevated). Transferrin saturation is the most sensitive initial screening test. If both are elevated, HFE gene testing for C282Y and H63D mutations confirms hereditary hemochromatosis. Classic findings: liver disease, diabetes mellitus ('bronze diabetes'), skin hyperpigmentation, cardiomyopathy, hypogonadism, arthropathy. Treatment: serial phlebotomy targeting ferritin <50 ng/mL.

questions.stem: HCC screening in cirrhotic patients?

choices.feedback: CT has radiation exposure and is not the recommended screening modality.
AFP alone has poor sensitivity (~60%). Combined with US improves detection.

choices.text: No screening needed until symptoms develop
choices.feedback: HCC is often asymptomatic until advanced. Screening catches treatable disease.

Correct! All patients with cirrhosis (any cause) should be screened with liver ultrasound + alpha-fetoprotein every 6 months. Also screen: chronic HBV carriers (even without cirrhosis — HBV can cause HCC without cirrhosis). If suspicious lesion: multiphase CT or MRI for characterization (arterial enhancement + venous washout = diagnostic for HCC).
