questions.explanation: HCC screening in cirrhotic patients uses ultrasound plus AFP every 6 months. This is identical to the recommendation for all cirrhotics and extends to chronic HBV carriers without cirrhosis. Screening improves detection of early-stage tumors amenable to curative therapies. AFP alone has insufficient sensitivity (~60%) and should not be used as the sole screening test.

questions.stem: Hepatorenal syndrome type 1 treatment?

choices.feedback: ACEi reduce renal perfusion pressure — absolutely contraindicated in HRS.
Diuretics are contraindicated in HRS — further reduce renal perfusion.

choices.text: Midodrine + octreotide + albumin (or norepinephrine infusion); terlipressin where available
choices.feedback: Correct! HRS type 1: rapidly progressive (Cr doubles in 2 weeks). Splanchnic vasodilation → reduced effective circulating volume → renal vasoconstriction. Treatment: vasoconstrictors (midodrine + octreotide, or norepinephrine) + IV albumin (volume expansion). Definitive: liver transplant. Terlipressin (vasopressin analog) is also effective.
Dialysis is a bridge but doesn't treat the underlying pathophysiology.

questions.explanation: HRS type 1 treatment involves vasoconstrictors (terlipressin preferred where available; otherwise midodrine + octreotide) combined with IV albumin to improve effective arterial blood volume and renal perfusion. Norepinephrine infusion in ICU is an alternative. These are bridge therapies — liver transplantation is the only definitive cure. Dialysis may be used as a bridge in transplant candidates. TIPS may be considered in select patients.

questions.stem: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: IBD is rare at age 2 and typically presents with chronic diarrhea, abdominal pain, and weight loss — not isolated painless bleeding.
Juvenile polyps cause painless bleeding but typically produce small amounts of bright red blood on the stool surface, not significant enough to drop hemoglobin.

choices.text: Meckel diverticulum with ectopic gastric mucosa
choices.feedback: Correct! The classic presentation of Meckel diverticulum: painless rectal bleeding in a young child, often with significant blood loss. Ectopic gastric mucosa in the diverticulum produces acid → ulceration of adjacent ileal mucosa → bleeding. This is the most common cause of significant painless GI bleeding in children under 5.
Intussusception causes 'currant jelly' stools (blood + mucus) WITH colicky abdominal pain and vomiting. This child is pain-free between episodes.

questions.stem: Where is a Meckel diverticulum located?

choices.feedback: The vitelline duct remnant is in the ILEUM, not jejunum, and on the ANTIMESENTERIC border.
The duodenum is at the foregut-midgut junction. The vitelline duct connects to the MIDGUT, specifically the ileum.

choices.text: Antimesenteric border of the ileum, ~2 feet from the ileocecal valve
choices.feedback: Correct! Meckel diverticulum is found on the ANTIMESENTERIC border of the ileum, approximately 2 feet (60cm) from the ileocecal valve in adults (shorter in children). It's antimesenteric because the vitelline duct connected to the ventral (antimesenteric) side of the midgut. Rule of 2s: 2% of population, 2 feet from ICV, 2 inches long, 2 types of ectopic tissue (gastric and pancreatic).
The diverticulum is 2 feet PROXIMAL to the ileocecal valve, not at the cecum. The appendix is a completely different structure.

questions.stem: What does the Meckel scan detect?

choices.feedback: Obstruction is diagnosed on X-ray or CT. The Meckel scan is nuclear medicine, designed to find ectopic gastric mucosa.

Correct! Tc-99m pertechnetate has affinity for gastric parietal cells (and thyroid follicular cells). A positive Meckel scan shows ectopic gastric mucosa in the diverticulum. The ectopic gastric tissue secretes acid, which ulcerates the adjacent normal ileal mucosa, causing bleeding. Note: the scan detects the GASTRIC tissue, not the diverticulum itself — a Meckel without ectopic gastric mucosa may be scan-negative.
This isn't an inflammation scan. It specifically detects gastric mucosal tissue. Inflammation would be better seen on CT or tagged WBC scan.
Tc-99m pertechnetate doesn't detect bleeding. A tagged RBC scan detects active bleeding sites. The Meckel scan detects ectopic gastric tissue.

questions.stem: When should an incidentally discovered Meckel diverticulum be removed?

choices.text: Never — incidental Meckels should always be left alone
choices.feedback: Not always left alone. In children, the lifetime risk of complications is higher, and certain features warrant removal.

choices.text: Selectively — based on age, size, and palpable abnormalities
choices.feedback: Correct! Current consensus: Remove incidental Meckels if (1) patient is young (higher lifetime risk of complications), (2) the diverticulum has palpable thickening (suggesting ectopic tissue), (3) there's a fibrous band to the umbilicus (obstruction risk), (4) the diverticulum is long or has a narrow base (higher complication risk). Lifetime risk of complications from an incidental Meckel is ~4-6%.

choices.text: Only if the patient has Down syndrome
choices.feedback: Meckel diverticulum is not specifically associated with Down syndrome. The decision is based on anatomic features and patient age.

choices.text: Always — all Meckels should be removed
choices.feedback: Routine removal of incidental Meckels is controversial. Most (95%) remain asymptomatic throughout life. Prophylactic resection adds surgical risk.

questions.stem: Which two types of ectopic tissue are found in Meckel diverticulum?

choices.feedback: Colonic and esophageal ectopic tissue are not typical in Meckel diverticulum.
Hepatic and renal ectopic tissue in a Meckel would be extremely unusual. The common types relate to the foregut derivatives.

Correct! The two most common types of ectopic tissue: GASTRIC (most common, ~50% of symptomatic Meckels — causes bleeding) and PANCREATIC (~5% — usually incidental). Why these two? Because the vitelline duct connects to the foregut-midgut junction, where gastric and pancreatic tissue are neighbors. These tissues get incorporated into the persistent duct remnant.
Thyroid and adrenal tissue are not found in Meckel diverticulum. These arise from different embryologic precursors.

questions.stem: Most common cause of bacterial meningitis in adults?

choices.feedback: Correct! S. pneumoniae is the #1 cause of bacterial meningitis in adults overall. Highest mortality of all bacterial causes (~20-30%). N. meningitidis is #1 in adolescents and associated with petechial rash + waterhouse-friderichsen (adrenal hemorrhage). S. pneumoniae also #1 in elderly.
N. meningitidis is #1 in adolescents/young adults but not overall adults.
Listeria is important in neonates, elderly, immunocompromised, and pregnant but not #1 overall.
Dramatically reduced since Hib vaccination.

questions.explanation: Streptococcus pneumoniae is the most common cause of bacterial meningitis in adults (>18 years), accounting for approximately 50% of cases. Neisseria meningitidis is second most common in young adults and adolescents. In neonates, Group B Streptococcus, E. coli, and Listeria monocytogenes predominate. In adults >50 years and immunocompromised patients, Listeria must be covered (hence the addition of ampicillin to empiric regimens). The classic triad of bacterial meningitis (headache, fever, neck stiffness) is present in only about 44% of patients, but almost all have at least 2 of 4 features (headache, fever, stiff neck, altered mental status).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Streptococcus pneumoniae is the most common cause of bacterial meningitis in adults (>18 years), accounting for approximately 50% of cases. Neisseria meningitidis is second most common in young adults and adolescents. In neonates, Group B Streptococcus, E. coli, and Listeria monocytogenes predominate. In adults >50 years and immunocompromised patients, Listeria must be covered (hence the addition of ampicillin to empiric regimens). The classic triad of bacterial meningitis (headache, fever, neck stiffness) is present in only about 44% of patients, but almost all have at least 2 of 4 features (headache, fever, stiff neck, altered mental status).

questions.stem: When should you get CT BEFORE LP?

choices.feedback: Temperature doesn't determine need for pre-LP imaging.

choices.text: Only if the patient is over 65
choices.feedback: Age alone doesn't mandate CT before LP.

choices.text: Immunocompromised, focal deficits, papilledema, altered consciousness, new seizure, CNS disease history
choices.feedback: Correct! CT before LP to rule out mass effect/risk of herniation. In these high-risk scenarios, draw blood cultures → give empiric antibiotics + dexamethasone FIRST → CT → LP when safe. NEVER delay antibiotics to get imaging or LP.

choices.text: Always — CT is required before every LP
choices.feedback: LP can be done without CT in most cases of suspected meningitis.

questions.explanation: CT before LP is indicated when there is risk of cerebral herniation from lumbar puncture. Indications include: immunocompromised state, history of CNS disease (mass, stroke, focal infection), new-onset seizure, papilledema, focal neurological deficits, and severely altered consciousness (GCS <10). Importantly, CT should NOT delay antibiotic administration — if CT is needed, draw blood cultures and start empiric antibiotics immediately BEFORE imaging, then perform LP after CT clears the risk of herniation. In the absence of these risk factors, LP can be performed without preceding CT.
questions.verifiedExplanation: CT before LP is indicated when there is risk of cerebral herniation from lumbar puncture. Indications include: immunocompromised state, history of CNS disease (mass, stroke, focal infection), new-onset seizure, papilledema, focal neurological deficits, and severely altered consciousness (GCS <10). Importantly, CT should NOT delay antibiotic administration — if CT is needed, draw blood cultures and start empiric antibiotics immediately BEFORE imaging, then perform LP after CT clears the risk of herniation. In the absence of these risk factors, LP can be performed without preceding CT.

questions.stem: CSF glucose is LOW in which type of meningitis?

choices.text: Neither — glucose is always normal in meningitis
choices.feedback: Glucose is characteristically low in bacterial, TB, and fungal meningitis.

choices.text: Viral meningitis has the lowest glucose
choices.feedback: Viral meningitis has normal glucose. Bacterial has low glucose.
Viral meningitis has NORMAL CSF glucose.

Correct! Bacterial meningitis: low glucose (<40 mg/dL, CSF/serum ratio <0.4) because bacteria consume glucose + impaired glucose transport across inflamed meninges. Also low in TB and fungal meningitis. Viral meningitis has normal glucose. Low glucose + high PMN WBC + high protein = bacterial.

questions.explanation: CSF glucose is characteristically LOW in bacterial meningitis (typically <40 mg/dL or CSF-to-serum glucose ratio <0.4). Bacteria and neutrophils consume glucose in the CSF. The full CSF profile in bacterial meningitis includes: elevated opening pressure, high WBC count (>1000, neutrophil-predominant), elevated protein (>250 mg/dL), and low glucose. Viral meningitis shows lymphocytic pleocytosis with normal glucose and mildly elevated protein. Tuberculous and fungal meningitis also have low glucose but with lymphocytic predominance and very high protein. Partially treated bacterial meningitis can mimic viral profiles.
questions.verifiedExplanation: CSF glucose is characteristically LOW in bacterial meningitis (typically <40 mg/dL or CSF-to-serum glucose ratio <0.4). Bacteria and neutrophils consume glucose in the CSF. The full CSF profile in bacterial meningitis includes: elevated opening pressure, high WBC count (>1000, neutrophil-predominant), elevated protein (>250 mg/dL), and low glucose. Viral meningitis shows lymphocytic pleocytosis with normal glucose and mildly elevated protein. Tuberculous and fungal meningitis also have low glucose but with lymphocytic predominance and very high protein. Partially treated bacterial meningitis can mimic viral profiles.

questions.stem: HSV encephalitis has predilection for which brain region?

choices.feedback: Occipital involvement suggests PRES or PCA stroke, not HSV.
Cerebellar involvement suggests different infectious or autoimmune etiologies.

Correct! HSV-1 enters CNS via olfactory or trigeminal nerve → preferentially affects temporal and frontal lobes (limbic system). Presents: fever + altered mental status + behavioral changes + seizures + aphasia. MRI: T2 hyperintensity in medial temporal lobes (often asymmetric). CSF PCR for HSV is diagnostic gold standard.
Basal ganglia involvement is more typical of hypertensive hemorrhage or Wilson disease.

questions.explanation: Herpes simplex encephalitis (HSE) has a strong predilection for the temporal lobes (especially medial temporal and orbitofrontal cortex). This tropism is thought to relate to the virus traveling along the olfactory or trigeminal nerve pathways to these regions. MRI shows T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the temporal lobes, often asymmetric and sometimes with hemorrhagic changes. HSE presents with fever, altered mental status, seizures, and behavioral changes (reflecting temporal lobe involvement). HSV-1 is the most common cause of sporadic fatal encephalitis. Diagnosis is confirmed by CSF HSV PCR. Treatment with IV acyclovir must begin empirically whenever HSE is suspected — delay increases mortality.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Herpes simplex encephalitis (HSE) has a strong predilection for the temporal lobes (especially medial temporal and orbitofrontal cortex). This tropism is thought to relate to the virus traveling along the olfactory or trigeminal nerve pathways to these regions. MRI shows T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the temporal lobes, often asymmetric and sometimes with hemorrhagic changes. HSE presents with fever, altered mental status, seizures, and behavioral changes (reflecting temporal lobe involvement). HSV-1 is the most common cause of sporadic fatal encephalitis. Diagnosis is confirmed by CSF HSV PCR. Treatment with IV acyclovir must begin empirically whenever HSE is suspected — delay increases mortality.

questions.stem: Empiric treatment for suspected bacterial meningitis in a 65-year-old?

choices.feedback: This covers pneumococcus but misses Listeria, which is important in patients >50.
Insufficient coverage for the range of organisms in elderly meningitis.

Correct! Age >50, immunocompromised, alcoholic, or pregnant → add ampicillin to cover Listeria monocytogenes. Listeria is intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins. Vancomycin covers resistant pneumococcus. Also give dexamethasone BEFORE or WITH first antibiotic dose.
This covers anaerobes and gram-negatives but misses the key meningitis organisms.

questions.explanation: Empiric treatment for suspected bacterial meningitis in adults ≥50 years requires ceftriaxone (covers S. pneumoniae and N. meningitidis) plus vancomycin (covers penicillin-resistant pneumococci) plus ampicillin (covers Listeria monocytogenes). Listeria coverage with ampicillin is essential in patients >50, immunocompromised patients, pregnant women, and neonates because Listeria is intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins. For patients 18-49 without immunocompromise, the standard empiric regimen is ceftriaxone plus vancomycin (no ampicillin). Dexamethasone should be given before or with the first antibiotic dose.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Empiric treatment for suspected bacterial meningitis in adults ≥50 years requires ceftriaxone (covers S. pneumoniae and N. meningitidis) plus vancomycin (covers penicillin-resistant pneumococci) plus ampicillin (covers Listeria monocytogenes). Listeria coverage with ampicillin is essential in patients >50, immunocompromised patients, pregnant women, and neonates because Listeria is intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins. For patients 18-49 without immunocompromise, the standard empiric regimen is ceftriaxone plus vancomycin (no ampicillin). Dexamethasone should be given before or with the first antibiotic dose.

questions.stem: Dexamethasone in bacterial meningitis should be given:

choices.feedback: Dexamethasone is specifically for bacterial meningitis, not viral.
Waiting defeats the purpose — dexamethasone must be given early.

choices.text: BEFORE or WITH the first antibiotic dose
choices.feedback: Correct! Dexamethasone4 days. Must be given before or simultaneously with first antibiotic — antibiotics cause bacterial lysis → massive inflammatory response → cerebral edema. Dexamethasone blunts this. Primary benefit: reduces hearing loss in pneumococcal meningitis. No benefit if given after antibiotics started.

choices.text: Only if CSF glucose is normal
choices.feedback: Dexamethasone is given empirically, not based on CSF glucose.

questions.explanation: Dexamethasone in bacterial meningitis must be given BEFORE or WITH the first dose of antibiotics — not after. The mechanism is suppression of the inflammatory cascade triggered by bacterial lysis (which releases cell wall components that drive inflammation). If given after antibiotics have already lysed bacteria and initiated the inflammatory response, dexamethasone provides no benefit. Evidence is strongest for S. pneumoniae meningitis, where dexamethasone reduces mortality and hearing loss. The typical regimen is dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV every 6 hours for 4 days. If the organism turns out not to be S. pneumoniae, some guidelines suggest discontinuing dexamethasone.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Dexamethasone in bacterial meningitis must be given BEFORE or WITH the first dose of antibiotics — not after. The mechanism is suppression of the inflammatory cascade triggered by bacterial lysis (which releases cell wall components that drive inflammation). If given after antibiotics have already lysed bacteria and initiated the inflammatory response, dexamethasone provides no benefit. Evidence is strongest for S. pneumoniae meningitis, where dexamethasone reduces mortality and hearing loss. The typical regimen is dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV every 6 hours for 4 days. If the organism turns out not to be S. pneumoniae, some guidelines suggest discontinuing dexamethasone.

questions.stem: Close contacts of N. meningitidis meningitis should receive:

choices.feedback: IV antibiotics are for treatment, not prophylaxis of contacts.

Correct! Chemoprophylaxis for close contacts (household, kissing contacts, exposure to secretions): rifampin2 days, OR ciprofloxacin1 dose, OR ceftriaxone1 dose (preferred in pregnancy). Give within 24h of diagnosis. Also vaccinate if serogroup covered by available vaccine.
Close contacts have significantly elevated risk and need prophylaxis.
Steroids are not used for contact prophylaxis.

questions.explanation: Close contacts of a patient with Neisseria meningitidis meningitis should receive chemoprophylaxis to prevent secondary cases. Options include rifampin (600 mg PO q12h for 2 days), ciprofloxacin (single 500 mg PO dose — often preferred for convenience), or ceftriaxone (single 250 mg IM dose — preferred in pregnancy). Prophylaxis should be given to household contacts, childcare/nursery contacts, anyone with direct exposure to respiratory secretions (kissing, mouth-to-mouth resuscitation), and healthcare workers who performed intubation or suctioning without a mask. Casual contacts (same workplace, school classroom) generally do NOT require prophylaxis.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Close contacts of a patient with Neisseria meningitidis meningitis should receive chemoprophylaxis to prevent secondary cases. Options include rifampin (600 mg PO q12h for 2 days), ciprofloxacin (single 500 mg PO dose — often preferred for convenience), or ceftriaxone (single 250 mg IM dose — preferred in pregnancy). Prophylaxis should be given to household contacts, childcare/nursery contacts, anyone with direct exposure to respiratory secretions (kissing, mouth-to-mouth resuscitation), and healthcare workers who performed intubation or suctioning without a mask. Casual contacts (same workplace, school classroom) generally do NOT require prophylaxis.

questions.stem: A patient with fever, altered mental status, and seizures has MRI showing T2 hyperintensity in bilateral temporal lobes. First action?

choices.feedback: NEVER wait for PCR — HSV encephalitis mortality is ~70% untreated. Start acyclovir immediately.
Bacterial meningitis antibiotics don't cover HSV. The temporal lobe MRI is classic for HSV encephalitis.

Correct! HSV encephalitis is the most likely diagnosis (fever + AMS + seizures + temporal lobe MRI findings). Start IV acyclovir14-21 days IMMEDIATELY. Early treatment reduces mortality from ~70% to <20%. Send CSF for HSV PCR but do NOT delay treatment.

choices.text: Observe and repeat MRI in 48 hours
choices.feedback: Observation allows disease progression and increases mortality.

questions.explanation: Fever, altered mental status, seizures, and MRI showing temporal lobe involvement is the classic presentation of HSV encephalitis. IV acyclovir must be started empirically and immediately — this is a time-critical diagnosis where delay in treatment dramatically increases mortality (from ~70% untreated to ~20% with treatment, though many survivors have significant neurological sequelae). LP with CSF HSV PCR confirms the diagnosis, but treatment should never be delayed pending results. CSF in HSE typically shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, normal glucose, and sometimes RBCs (from hemorrhagic necrosis of temporal lobes).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Fever, altered mental status, seizures, and MRI showing temporal lobe involvement is the classic presentation of HSV encephalitis. IV acyclovir must be started empirically and immediately — this is a time-critical diagnosis where delay in treatment dramatically increases mortality (from ~70% untreated to ~20% with treatment, though many survivors have significant neurological sequelae). LP with CSF HSV PCR confirms the diagnosis, but treatment should never be delayed pending results. CSF in HSE typically shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, normal glucose, and sometimes RBCs (from hemorrhagic necrosis of temporal lobes).

questions.stem: Most common initial presentation of MS?

choices.feedback: Seizures are uncommon in MS — they occur in <5% of patients.

Correct! Unilateral optic neuritis is the most common first presentation: painful vision loss (pain with eye movement), afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil — swinging flashlight test shows paradoxical dilation of affected eye). ~50% of optic neuritis patients develop MS within 15 years.
MS affects CNS myelin (oligodendrocytes), NOT peripheral nerves. Peripheral neuropathy suggests GBS, diabetes, etc.
Transverse myelitis is a common MS presentation but not the most common initial one.

questions.stem: MRI hallmark of MS?

choices.feedback: Ring enhancement suggests abscess, toxoplasmosis, or glioblastoma — not typical MS.
Basal ganglia calcification suggests Fahr disease or hypoparathyroidism.
Cortical ribbon sign suggests Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (prion disease).

Correct! Multiple ovoid lesions perpendicular to the ventricles (Dawson fingers), best seen on T2/FLAIR sequences. McDonald criteria require dissemination in space (≥2 CNS areas) AND time (new lesions on follow-up MRI or simultaneous enhancing + non-enhancing lesions). Spinal cord lesions also common.

questions.explanation: Correct: Periventricular white matter plaques (Dawson fingers).

questions.stem: Uhthoff phenomenon refers to:

choices.feedback: Steroid response is expected in acute relapses but not the definition of Uhthoff.

Correct! Heat (exercise, hot baths, fever) worsens conduction in demyelinated axons by further slowing already impaired saltatory conduction. Symptoms improve when the body cools. This is pathognomonic for MS and can be used as a diagnostic clue. Not a true relapse — no new inflammation.
Progressive vision loss in MS is a sign of disease progression, not Uhthoff phenomenon.
MS symptoms don't specifically follow a diurnal pattern.

questions.stem: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) in a young patient suggests:

choices.feedback: MG causes fatigable ptosis and diplopia but not INO specifically.
PD affects basal ganglia causing movement disorder, not specific eye movement patterns.
Migraine can cause visual symptoms but not INO.

Correct! Bilateral INO in a young patient is highly suggestive of MS (MLF lesion). INO: impaired ADDuction of ipsilateral eye + nystagmus of ABDucting contralateral eye. Unilateral INO in an older patient suggests brainstem stroke. Convergence is preserved in INO (distinguishes from CN III palsy).

questions.explanation: GI diagnosis and management relies on systematic assessment of symptom characteristics (location, timing, associated features), appropriate laboratory and imaging workup, and evidence-based treatment algorithms. Endoscopy is both diagnostic and therapeutic for many GI conditions.

questions.stem: CSF findings in MS?

choices.feedback: High WBC suggests bacterial meningitis, not MS.

choices.text: Elevated protein with normal cell count
choices.feedback: That's albuminocytologic dissociation of GBS. MS may have mildly elevated protein but the key finding is different.
Xanthochromia indicates SAH, not MS.

choices.text: Oligoclonal bands and elevated IgG index
choices.feedback: Correct! Oligoclonal bands (>90% of MS patients) represent intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis — bands in CSF NOT present in serum. Elevated IgG index confirms intrathecal antibody production. Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis may be present. CSF is supportive but not required if MRI meets McDonald criteria.

questions.stem: Risk of natalizumab in MS treatment?

choices.feedback: Hepatotoxicity is associated with some DMTs but PML is the key natalizumab concern.
Optic neuritis is a symptom of MS itself, not a natalizumab side effect.
Osteoporosis is associated with chronic steroid use, not natalizumab.

Correct! Natalizumab (anti-α4 integrin) prevents lymphocyte CNS entry but suppresses CNS immune surveillance → JC virus reactivation → PML. Risk factors: JCV antibody positive + treatment >2 years + prior immunosuppression. Monitor JCV antibody status. PML is often fatal or causes severe disability. Risk stratification guides treatment decisions.

questions.stem: Treatment of acute MS relapse?

choices.feedback: DMTs prevent future relapses but don't treat acute ones.
Low-dose oral steroids are insufficient for acute relapse management.
IVIG has limited evidence for acute MS relapses compared to steroids.

choices.text: IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy for 3-5 days
choices.feedback: Correct! High-dose IV steroids shorten relapse duration and speed recovery but do NOT change long-term disability outcomes. Oral taper may follow. For steroid-refractory relapses: plasmapheresis. Not all relapses require treatment — minor sensory symptoms may be observed.

questions.stem: Ocrelizumab is unique among MS DMTs because:

choices.text: It is approved for primary progressive MS (PPMS)
choices.feedback: Correct! Ocrelizumab (anti-CD20 — depletes B cells) is the FIRST and currently only DMT approved for PPMS (ORATORIO trial). Also effective in RRMS (OPERA trials). Infused every 6 months. Screening required: hepatitis B (risk of reactivation), tuberculosis. Most DMTs only work for relapsing forms.
No current therapy cures MS. Ocrelizumab slows progression but requires ongoing treatment.
Side effects include infusion reactions, increased infection risk, and hepatitis B reactivation.

choices.text: It is the only injectable DMT
choices.feedback: Multiple injectable DMTs exist (IFN-beta, glatiramer). Ocrelizumab is actually IV infusion.

questions.stem: Most common initial presentation of MS?

choices.feedback: Transverse myelitis can occur but is not the most common initial presentation.
Seizures are uncommon in MS (cortical gray matter is relatively spared).
Intention tremor can occur in MS (cerebellar) but is not the most common initial symptom.

Correct! Optic neuritis is the most common initial MS presentation — unilateral vision loss, pain with eye movement, afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil). ~50% of optic neuritis patients develop MS within 15 years. MRI brain at presentation determines risk.

questions.explanation: Correct: Optic neuritis (unilateral vision loss with pain on eye movement).

questions.stem: Bilateral INO in a young adult is highly suggestive of:

choices.feedback: Tumors can cause INO but bilateral INO in a young adult strongly suggests MS.
MG causes fatigable ptosis and diplopia but not INO specifically.
GBS is a peripheral nerve disease; INO is a CNS brainstem finding.

Correct! Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) from MLF lesion: impaired adduction ipsilateral + abducting nystagmus contralateral. Bilateral INO in a young adult = MS until proven otherwise. In elderly: most commonly caused by brainstem stroke.

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Multiple sclerosis) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: MS symptoms worsening with heat exposure is called:

choices.feedback: Lhermitte = electric shock sensation with neck flexion (posterior column).

Correct! Uhthoff phenomenon: heat slows conduction velocity in demyelinated nerves → transient worsening of existing MS symptoms. Classic: vision worsens after hot bath, exercise, or fever. Pseudoexacerbation (not a true relapse). Symptoms resolve with cooling.
Romberg tests proprioception (dorsal column) — positive when balance worsens with eyes closed.
Babinski (upgoing plantar response) indicates UMN lesion — can be present in MS but is not specific to heat.

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Uhthoff phenomenon) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: CSF finding most specific for MS?

choices.feedback: Xanthochromia indicates prior subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Low CSF glucose = bacterial meningitis or TB meningitis, not MS.

choices.text: Oligoclonal bands (IgG) present in CSF but not serum
choices.feedback: Correct! Oligoclonal bands (OCBs) present in CSF but absent in serum indicate intrathecal IgG synthesis — present in >95% of MS patients. Not 100% specific (also seen in neurosyphilis, Lyme, SSPE), but in the right clinical context, strongly supports MS diagnosis. Elevated IgG index is also supportive.
That's albuminocytologic dissociation — classic for GBS.

questions.explanation: Bacterial meningitis presents with the classic triad of fever, nuchal rigidity, and altered mental status (though all three are present in <50% of cases). CSF findings: elevated WBC with neutrophilic predominance, elevated protein, and low glucose (<40 mg/dL or CSF:serum ratio <0.4). Empiric treatment: ceftriaxone + vancomycin (± ampicillin for age >50 or immunocompromised to cover Listeria). Dexamethasone given before or with the first antibiotic dose reduces mortality in pneumococcal meningitis. Do not delay antibiotics for imaging if clinical suspicion is high.

questions.stem: Acute MS relapse treatment?

choices.text: IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy for 3-5 days
choices.feedback: Correct! High-dose IV steroids shorten relapse duration and speed recovery. Does NOT change long-term disease progression or prevent future relapses. For steroid-refractory relapses: plasmapheresis (PLEX). Long-term disease modification requires DMTs, not steroids.
Natalizumab is a DMT for relapse prevention, not acute relapse treatment.
IFN-β prevents future relapses (DMT) but does not treat acute attacks.
Low-dose oral steroids are less effective than IV pulse therapy for MS relapses.

questions.explanation: Multiple sclerosis diagnosis requires dissemination in space (≥2 CNS regions affected) and time (≥2 episodes separated in time). McDonald criteria (2017 revision) allow MRI to substitute for clinical episodes. CSF oligoclonal bands support the diagnosis. Acute relapses: high-dose IV methylprednisolone. Disease-modifying therapy: interferons, glatiramer acetate, natalizumab, ocrelizumab, fingolimod. MRI shows periventricular white matter lesions (Dawson's fingers). Optic neuritis is a common presenting symptom.

questions.stem: Which DMT carries risk of PML from JC virus reactivation?

choices.feedback: IFN-β does not carry significant PML risk.
DMF has rare PML reports but it's primarily associated with natalizumab.
Glatiramer has a very favorable safety profile with no PML risk.

Correct! Natalizumab (α4-integrin antibody) prevents lymphocyte CNS entry — highly effective but carries PML risk (JC virus reactivation). Risk factors: JCV antibody positive, treatment >2 years, prior immunosuppression. Screen JCV antibody before starting. Consider switching after 2 years if JCV+.

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Natalizumab) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: Ocrelizumab is significant because it is:

choices.feedback: Ocrelizumab is effective in both sexes.
No DMT cures MS. They reduce relapse frequency and slow disability progression.
First oral MS drug was fingolimod. Ocrelizumab is IV infusion.

choices.text: The first DMT approved for primary progressive MS (PPMS)
choices.feedback: Correct! Ocrelizumab (anti-CD20, B-cell depleting) is the FIRST and currently only DMT with proven benefit in PPMS (ORATORIO trial). Also highly effective in RRMS. PPMS has historically been treatment-resistant, making this a landmark approval.

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (The first DMT approved for primary progressive MS (PPMS)) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: MS lesion on MRI that enhances with gadolinium indicates:

choices.feedback: Normal white matter does not enhance with gadolinium.
Chronic plaques show T2 hyperintensity but do NOT enhance with gadolinium.

Correct! Gadolinium enhancement = breakdown of blood-brain barrier = active inflammation. Enhancing lesions represent new or actively inflamed plaques (within ~6 weeks). Non-enhancing T2 lesions = older/chronic plaques. Simultaneous enhancing + non-enhancing lesions on single MRI can satisfy dissemination in time criterion.
While tumors enhance, ring enhancement and clinical context distinguish them from MS plaques.

questions.explanation: Correct: Active inflammation (recent or ongoing demyelination).

questions.stem: Hallmark feature that distinguishes MG from other causes of weakness?

choices.text: Weakness is constant throughout the day
choices.feedback: Constant weakness suggests other myopathies. MG fluctuates.

choices.text: Fatigable weakness — worsens with use, improves with rest
choices.feedback: Correct! Fatigability is the hallmark of MG. Antibodies block AChR → fewer functional receptors → repeated stimulation depletes available ACh → progressive weakness. Rest allows ACh to rebuild. Classic: ptosis worsens with sustained upgaze, voice fades during conversation, difficulty chewing at end of meal.
Improvement with use = Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (pre-synaptic, facilitating).
Elevated CK with proximal weakness = inflammatory myopathy (polymyositis/dermatomyositis).

questions.explanation: Myasthenia gravis is caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors (AChR-Ab in 85%) or muscle-specific kinase (MuSK in 5-8%). Fatigable weakness worsening with repetitive use is the hallmark. Diagnosis: AChR antibodies, decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation, edrophonium (Tensilon) test. Treatment: pyridostigmine (symptomatic), immunosuppression (corticosteroids, azathioprine), thymectomy for thymoma or generalized MG. Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure) requires ICU, IVIG or plasmapheresis, and avoiding aminoglycosides/beta-blockers.

questions.stem: Most SENSITIVE diagnostic test for MG?

choices.feedback: Most specific but only ~85% sensitive for generalized MG and 50% for ocular MG.
Historically used but less sensitive and rarely performed now due to cardiac risks.
CT evaluates for thymoma but doesn't diagnose MG.

Correct! Single-fiber EMG detects increased 'jitter' (variability in neuromuscular transmission time) — most sensitive test (>95%). Used when clinical suspicion is high but antibodies and RNS are negative. Repetitive nerve stimulation (decremental response) is more commonly used but less sensitive.

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Single-fiber EMG) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: ALL MG patients should get which imaging study?

choices.feedback: MG is not associated with abdominal pathology.

Correct! 10% of MG patients have thymoma, 65% have thymic hyperplasia. ALL patients need CT chest at diagnosis. Thymoma requires surgical resection regardless. Even without thymoma, thymectomy is beneficial in generalized AChR+ MG (MGTX trial — improved outcomes over 3 years).
MRI spine evaluates for spinal cord pathology, not NMJ disease.
MG is a peripheral NMJ disorder, not a CNS disease.

questions.explanation: Correct: CT chest (to evaluate for thymoma).

questions.stem: Lambert-Eaton vs MG — key distinction?

choices.feedback: Correct! LEMS = pre-synaptic (VGCC antibodies block calcium-mediated ACh release). Repeated stimulation → calcium accumulates → MORE ACh released → strength improves (facilitating response). Associated with small cell lung cancer (paraneoplastic). RNS shows INCREMENTAL response. MG = post-synaptic, decremental response, associated with thymoma.
LEMS treatment includes 3,4-DAP (amifampridine) to increase ACh release, plus treating underlying cancer.
LEMS typically causes proximal limb weakness, autonomic dysfunction (dry mouth), and hyporeflexia.

choices.text: LEMS has autoantibodies against AChR receptors
choices.feedback: LEMS has anti-VGCC antibodies (pre-synaptic), not anti-AChR.

questions.explanation: Correct: LEMS: weakness IMPROVES with use (pre-synaptic VGCC antibodies, associated with SCLC).

questions.stem: Drugs that can worsen MG include all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: Aminoglycosides worsen MG — they impair neuromuscular transmission.
Magnesium impairs neuromuscular transmission and can precipitate myasthenic crisis.

Correct! Pyridostigmine is the primary TREATMENT for MG (AChE inhibitor → increases ACh at NMJ). Drugs that worsen MG: aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, magnesium, D-penicillamine, botulinum toxin. Always check the drug list before prescribing to MG patients.
Fluoroquinolones can worsen MG and carry a boxed warning.

questions.explanation: Myasthenia gravis is caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors (AChR-Ab in 85%) or muscle-specific kinase (MuSK in 5-8%). Fatigable weakness worsening with repetitive use is the hallmark. Diagnosis: AChR antibodies, decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation, edrophonium (Tensilon) test. Treatment: pyridostigmine (symptomatic), immunosuppression (corticosteroids, azathioprine), thymectomy for thymoma or generalized MG. Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure) requires ICU, IVIG or plasmapheresis, and avoiding aminoglycosides/beta-blockers.

questions.stem: #1 trigger for myasthenic crisis?

choices.feedback: Heat can worsen MG but is not the primary crisis trigger.
Stress can worsen MG but is not the #1 trigger.

Correct! Infection (especially respiratory) is the #1 trigger for myasthenic crisis. Others: surgery, medication changes (stopping immunosuppression, starting MG-worsening drugs), pregnancy, emotional stress. Management: ICU, monitor FVC/NIF, IVIG or plasmapheresis, treat infection, adjust MG medications.
Exercise worsens MG symptoms but rarely triggers full crisis.

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Infection) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: Repetitive nerve stimulation in MG shows:

choices.feedback: Normal RNS occurs in ~50% of ocular MG; doesn't rule out disease if clinical suspicion high.
Fibrillations suggest denervation (ALS, radiculopathy), not NMJ disease.
Incremental response = Lambert-Eaton (facilitating, pre-synaptic).

Correct! RNS at 2-3 Hz → progressive decrease in compound muscle action potential amplitude (>10% decrement). Reflects post-synaptic AChR depletion with repeated stimulation. This is the most commonly used electrodiagnostic test for MG. Compare: LEMS shows incremental response with rapid repetitive stimulation.

questions.explanation: Correct: Decremental response (>10% amplitude decrease).

questions.stem: Cholinergic crisis from excess pyridostigmine presents with:

choices.feedback: Cholinergic excess causes bradycardia and hypotension, not the reverse.
Fever + rigidity suggests NMS or serotonin syndrome, not cholinergic crisis.

choices.text: DUMBBELSS (diarrhea, urination, miosis, bronchospasm, bradycardia, emesis, lacrimation, salivation, sweating) + weakness
choices.feedback: Correct! Excess AChE inhibitor → too much ACh at muscarinic AND nicotinic receptors. Muscarinic: DUMBBELSS. Nicotinic: fasciculations + weakness (depolarization block). Distinguishing from myasthenic crisis is critical — both cause weakness, but cholinergic crisis has cholinergic signs. Treatment: stop pyridostigmine, atropine for muscarinic effects.
Those are anticholinergic effects — the OPPOSITE of cholinergic excess.

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (DUMBBELSS (diarrhea, urination, miosis, bronchospasm, bradycardia, emesis, lacrimation, salivation, sweating) + weakness) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: Hallmark clinical feature of myasthenia gravis?

choices.feedback: That's GBS. MG has a different pattern.

choices.text: Fixed weakness worse in the morning
choices.feedback: MG is typically worse later in the day with sustained activity.
Resting tremor is characteristic of Parkinson disease.

choices.text: Fatigable weakness that worsens with use
choices.feedback: Correct! Fatigable weakness is pathognomonic for MG. Ptosis worsens through the day or with sustained upward gaze. Improvement after rest distinguishes MG from other neuromuscular conditions. Ice pack test: ptosis improves with cold application (reduces AChE activity at NMJ).

questions.explanation: Best answer: Fatigable weakness that worsens with use and improves with rest.

questions.stem: Most common antibody in myasthenia gravis?

choices.feedback: Anti-Jo1 is associated with antisynthetase syndrome/inflammatory myopathy.

Correct! Anti-AChR antibodies in 85% of generalized MG. They block, cross-link, and destroy postsynaptic receptors via complement activation. Most specific diagnostic test. If negative, check anti-MuSK. All MG patients need CT chest to screen for thymoma.
Anti-dsDNA is specific for SLE.
Anti-MuSK is found in 5-8%, typically with more severe bulbar symptoms.

questions.explanation: Correct: Anti-AChR (acetylcholine receptor).

questions.stem: #1 trigger for myasthenic crisis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Infection (especially respiratory) is the most common precipitant of myasthenic crisis. The inflammatory response and physiologic stress exacerbate NMJ dysfunction. Always workup and treat infection in crisis. Other triggers: surgery, medication changes, certain drugs (aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones).
Exercise exacerbates fatigable weakness but rarely triggers full crisis without another factor.
Temperature can affect NMJ function but is not the primary trigger.
Stress can worsen MG but is not the #1 trigger.

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Infection) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: Medications that can WORSEN myasthenia gravis include:

choices.feedback: Standard analgesics do not affect NMJ transmission.
These are treatments for MG, not exacerbating medications.

Correct! These drugs worsen NMJ transmission. Aminoglycosides/fluoroquinolones: impair presynaptic ACh release. Beta-blockers: worsen fatigue. Magnesium: blocks NMJ calcium channels. Also: succinylcholine (prolonged paralysis), D-penicillamine, phenytoin, telithromycin.
These medications are safe in MG patients.

questions.explanation: Myasthenia gravis is caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors (AChR-Ab in 85%) or muscle-specific kinase (MuSK in 5-8%). Fatigable weakness worsening with repetitive use is the hallmark. Diagnosis: AChR antibodies, decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation, edrophonium (Tensilon) test. Treatment: pyridostigmine (symptomatic), immunosuppression (corticosteroids, azathioprine), thymectomy for thymoma or generalized MG. Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure) requires ICU, IVIG or plasmapheresis, and avoiding aminoglycosides/beta-blockers.

questions.stem: DUMBBELSS symptoms indicate:

choices.feedback: Myasthenic crisis = weakness from insufficient treatment. DUMBBELSS = excessive cholinergic stimulation.
Serotonin syndrome: hyperthermia, rigidity, clonus, agitation.

Correct! Cholinergic crisis from excessive pyridostigmine: Diarrhea, Urination, Miosis, Bradycardia, Bronchospasm/Bronchorrhea, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation, Sweating. Treatment: hold AChE inhibitors, atropine for muscarinic symptoms, supportive care.
Organophosphates cause similar cholinergic symptoms but from external toxin, not overtreatment.

questions.explanation: Correct: Cholinergic crisis (overtreatment with AChE inhibitors).

questions.stem: Repetitive nerve stimulation in MG shows:

choices.feedback: Conduction block is seen in GBS/CIDP demyelinating conditions.
Incrementing response is seen in Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome.
Fibrillation potentials suggest denervation (ALS, radiculopathy), not MG.

Correct! Low-frequency (2-3 Hz) repetitive nerve stimulation: progressive decrease in compound muscle action potential amplitude >10% = decremental response. This reflects progressive depletion of available AChR at the NMJ. Lambert-Eaton shows INCREMENTAL response at HIGH frequency (post-tetanic facilitation).

questions.explanation: Myasthenia gravis is caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors (AChR-Ab in 85%) or muscle-specific kinase (MuSK in 5-8%). Fatigable weakness worsening with repetitive use is the hallmark. Diagnosis: AChR antibodies, decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation, edrophonium (Tensilon) test. Treatment: pyridostigmine (symptomatic), immunosuppression (corticosteroids, azathioprine), thymectomy for thymoma or generalized MG. Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure) requires ICU, IVIG or plasmapheresis, and avoiding aminoglycosides/beta-blockers.

questions.stem: Thymectomy in MG is recommended for:

choices.feedback: Thymectomy benefits even non-thymoma MG patients.

choices.text: All thymoma patients AND non-thymoma generalized MG
choices.feedback: Correct! MGTX trial: thymectomy + prednisone was superior to prednisone alone in non-thymoma generalized AChR+ MG (ages 18-65). All thymoma patients should have thymectomy regardless. CT chest screening is mandatory for all MG patients.

choices.text: Only patients who fail all other treatments
choices.feedback: Thymectomy is recommended early, not as a last resort.
Seronegative patients may benefit but the strongest evidence is for AChR+ MG.

questions.explanation: Correct: All thymoma patients AND non-thymoma generalized MG (ages 18-65).

questions.stem: In myasthenic crisis, which treatments provide rapid improvement?

choices.feedback: Azathioprine takes 3-6 months for full effect — too slow for crisis.
Steroids take weeks to work and can initially worsen weakness.
Excessive pyridostigmine can cause cholinergic crisis. Typically held or reduced during crisis.

Correct! Both provide rapid improvement (days). IVIG: weight-based dosing × 5 days. Plasmapheresis: removes circulating antibodies. Choose one (don't combine — PLEX removes IVIG). Also: intubate if FVC <target rate, treat trigger (infection), hold/reduce pyridostigmine (may worsen secretions during crisis).

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (IVIG or plasmapheresis) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: What is the most likely explanation for elevated maternal serum AFP?

choices.feedback: GDM doesn't significantly affect AFP levels. Elevated AFP should prompt evaluation for structural defects.
Down syndrome causes LOW AFP. The triple screen pattern for T21 is: low AFP, low unconjugated estriol, high hCG.
Correct! Elevated MSAFP (>2.5 MoM) is the classic screening marker for open NTDs. AFP leaks from exposed fetal tissue into amniotic fluid and then maternal serum.
Twins can elevate AFP, but ultrasound would have already identified twins. This needs NTD evaluation.

questions.stem: What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Spina bifida occulta has intact skin coverage and would NOT elevate AFP (it's closed). It's usually an incidental finding.
Meningocele contains only meninges and CSF, no neural elements. It has better prognosis. This sac contains neural tissue.
Anencephaly is failure of the CRANIAL neuropore — absent calvarium and cerebral hemispheres. This defect is lumbosacral (caudal neuropore failure).

Correct! Myelomeningocele = meninges + spinal cord/nerve roots protruding through the defect. The lemon and banana signs indicate associated Chiari II malformation (hindbrain herniation). This is the most common clinically significant NTD.

questions.stem: Which factor most likely contributed to this NTD?

choices.feedback: Late folic acid is a contributing factor, but the neural tube closes by day 28 (~week 6). The valproic acid exposure during this critical window is the primary risk factor.
Age 28 is not a risk factor for NTDs. NTD risk is not age-dependent (unlike trisomies).

Correct! Valproic acid is one of the strongest risk factors for NTDs (1-2% risk vs 0.1% baseline). It interferes with folate metabolism and histone deacetylase. Women on valproic acid need high-dose folic acid starting before conception.
Multiparity itself doesn't increase NTD risk. Prior NTD-affected pregnancy does increase recurrence risk (2-5%).

questions.stem: What did the MOMS trial demonstrate about fetal surgery for myelomeningocele?

choices.feedback: Fetal surgery improves outcomes but does not restore full function. Most children still have some motor and bladder dysfunction.

choices.text: Reduced need for VP shunt at 12 months
choices.feedback: Correct! The MOMS trial (2011) showed prenatal repair reduced VP shunt placement from 82% to 40% at 12 months, and improved motor function at 30 months. However, it increased risk of preterm delivery and uterine dehiscence.
Fetal repair can improve hindbrain herniation but doesn't eliminate it completely.
The MOMS trial showed clear benefits for prenatal repair in specific measures.

questions.stem: What is the most urgent concern in the first 24 hours?

choices.feedback: Correct! The open myelomeningocele must be closed within 24-72 hours to prevent meningitis and further neural damage. Until surgery, the sac is kept moist with sterile saline-soaked gauze and the baby positioned prone.
Orthopedic issues are important but not urgent. Preventing CNS infection from the open defect takes priority.
MRI is important for long-term planning but should not delay surgical closure.
Bladder management is crucial but the immediate priority is closing the defect to prevent infection.

questions.stem: What does the functional motor level predict for ambulation?

choices.feedback: L4 motor level typically allows community ambulation but usually needs ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) and may need crutches.
The neurological deficit is permanent. The absent ankle function means normal gait is not achievable.

Correct! L4 motor level (intact quadriceps) is a favorable prognostic indicator. These children typically achieve community ambulation with AFOs. Higher levels (L1-L2) are usually wheelchair-dependent; lower levels (L5-S1) may walk with minimal support.
Wheelchair dependence is typical for thoracic and high lumbar (L1-L2) levels. L4 has preserved quadriceps function, enabling walking.

questions.stem: What embryological process has failed in lissencephaly?

choices.feedback: Neural tube closure defects (NTDs) cause spina bifida and anencephaly — different from migration disorders.
Excessive neurons would cause megalencephaly, not a smooth cortex. Lissencephaly is about WHERE neurons end up, not how many.
Proliferation occurs in the ventricular zone and is normal in lissencephaly. The neurons are produced but fail to reach their destination.

choices.text: Neuronal migration from the ventricular zone to the cortical plate
choices.feedback: Correct! Lissencephaly results from defective neuronal migration. Normally, neurons born in the ventricular/subventricular zone migrate along radial glial fibers to form the 6-layered cortex (inside-out pattern — later neurons pass earlier ones). In lissencephaly, migration fails → smooth cortex with only 4 disorganized layers instead of 6. Caused by LIS1 (17p13.3) or DCX (X-linked) mutations.

questions.stem: A female infant has MRI showing bands of gray matter lining the ventricles underneath an apparently normal cortex. What is this finding called?

choices.feedback: PVL is hypoxic-ischemic white matter injury, not a migration disorder.
Schizencephaly involves full-thickness clefts lined by gray matter extending from ventricle to surface — not bands.
Polymicrogyria involves abnormal cortical folding with many small, fused gyri. The finding described is heterotopic neurons lining the ventricles — a different pattern.

Correct! Band heterotopia (subcortical band heterotopia / double cortex) = a band of neurons that stopped migrating partway, creating a layer of gray matter between the ventricular surface and the cortex. Classically seen in females with DCX mutations (X-linked). Males with DCX get full lissencephaly; females (one normal X) get band heterotopia. Presentation: seizures, variable intellectual disability.

questions.explanation: Correct: Band heterotopia (double cortex syndrome).

questions.stem: What is the key embryological difference between polymicrogyria and lissencephaly?

choices.text: PMG affects migration; lissencephaly affects proliferation
choices.feedback: Both can involve migration, but PMG is primarily a post-migrational cortical organization defect.

choices.text: PMG is always infectious; lissencephaly is always genetic
choices.feedback: PMG can be genetic (SRPX2, GPR56) or acquired (CMV, ischemia). Lissencephaly is primarily genetic but the distinction is the developmental stage affected.

choices.text: PMG is a late cortical organization defect; lissencephaly is early migration failure
choices.feedback: Correct! Lissencephaly = early migration failure (weeks 12-16) → neurons don't reach the cortex. Polymicrogyria = late organization/lamination defect (weeks 20-24) → neurons reach the cortex but organize abnormally, forming many small fused gyri with disordered lamination. PMG can also result from in utero vascular injury or infection (CMV). Bilateral perisylvian PMG → pseudobulbar palsy (drooling, dysphagia, dysarthria).

choices.text: They are the same process with different severity
choices.feedback: They are distinct processes occurring at different developmental timepoints with different genetic causes.

questions.stem: What distinguishes open-lip from closed-lip schizencephaly?

choices.text: Open-lip involves both hemispheres; closed-lip is unilateral
choices.feedback: Both open and closed-lip can be unilateral or bilateral. The distinction is whether the cleft walls are separated (open) or in contact (closed).

choices.text: Open-lip has white matter lining; closed-lip has gray matter
choices.feedback: Both types are lined by gray matter (polymicrogyric cortex). A CSF-filled cavity lined by WHITE matter/gliosis is porencephaly (a destructive lesion), not schizencephaly.

choices.text: Open-lip is caused by infection; closed-lip is genetic
choices.feedback: Both types share similar etiologies (EMX2 mutations, in utero ischemia). The morphology, not the etiology, determines the type.

choices.text: Open-lip: cleft walls separated, CSF-filled. Closed-lip: walls apposed.
choices.feedback: Correct! Open-lip schizencephaly: the walls of the cleft are separated with CSF flowing between ventricle and subarachnoid space. Worse prognosis — motor deficits, seizures, often severe. Closed-lip: the gray matter-lined walls are in contact (fused lips). Better prognosis — may present later with seizures or mild hemiparesis. Both are lined by polymicrogyric gray matter (key distinction from porencephaly, which is lined by white matter/gliosis).

questions.stem: What is the most common cause of medically refractory focal epilepsy in children that benefits from surgical resection?

choices.feedback: Correct! Focal cortical dysplasia (FCD) is the #1 cause of refractory focal epilepsy in children and the most common finding in pediatric epilepsy surgery specimens. FCD is a localized organization defect (disordered lamination, dysmorphic neurons, ± balloon cells). Unlike diffuse PMG, FCD can be surgically resected if the focus is well-defined. Seizure-free rates after FCD resection: 50-80%.
Tumor-associated epilepsy is important but less common than FCD as the substrate for refractory focal pediatric epilepsy.
Tubers can cause epilepsy but are less common than FCD overall as a cause of refractory pediatric epilepsy.
Mesial temporal sclerosis is the most common cause in adults. In children, focal cortical dysplasia is more common.

questions.stem: The tetrad of NMS (FARM) includes:

choices.feedback: These are nonspecific systemic symptoms, not NMS.
Ataxia, rash, and myoclonus are not core features of NMS.

choices.text: Fever, Anemia, Renal failure, Mucosal bleeding
choices.feedback: This describes TTP/HUS-like features, not NMS.

Correct! FARM: Fever (often >40°C), Autonomic instability (tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis), Rigidity (generalized lead-pipe — distinguishes from serotonin syndrome), Mental status changes (confusion → obtundation → coma). CK is markedly elevated.

questions.explanation: Key detail: pipe), Mental status changes.

questions.stem: What MOST reliably distinguishes NMS from serotonin syndrome?

choices.feedback: Both NMS and serotonin syndrome cause hyperthermia.
Both cause altered mental status.

Correct! NMS: generalized lead-pipe rigidity with NORMAL or decreased reflexes. Serotonin syndrome: clonus (especially lower extremity), hyperreflexia, and tremor. Clonus is the single most distinguishing feature of serotonin syndrome. Also: NMS develops over days-weeks; serotonin syndrome within hours.
Both cause diaphoresis as part of autonomic dysfunction.

questions.explanation: Correct: Lead-pipe rigidity (NMS) vs clonus/hyperreflexia (serotonin syndrome).

questions.stem: Treatment of NMS includes:

choices.feedback: The antipsychotic is the CAUSE — it must be stopped immediately.
Cyproheptadine is the treatment for serotonin syndrome, not NMS.

Correct! Dantrolene (muscle relaxant — reduces rigidity/heat) + bromocriptine (dopamine agonist — reverses dopamine blockade). Also: stop offending agent, aggressive cooling, IV fluids, ICU monitoring. Amantadine can substitute for bromocriptine. Benzodiazepines for agitation.
Naloxone reverses opioids, not NMS.

questions.explanation: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) is a life-threatening reaction to dopamine antagonists (antipsychotics). Tetrad: hyperthermia, lead-pipe rigidity, altered mental status, and autonomic instability. Distinguishing from serotonin syndrome: NMS has lead-pipe rigidity and bradyreflexia vs serotonin syndrome with clonus, hyperreflexia, and myoclonus. Treatment: discontinue offending agent, dantrolene (muscle relaxant), bromocriptine (dopamine agonist), aggressive cooling, supportive care. CK is markedly elevated due to rhabdomyolysis.

questions.stem: NMS can be triggered by withdrawal of which medication?

choices.feedback: Opioid withdrawal causes GI symptoms and autonomic changes but not NMS.

Correct! Abrupt withdrawal of dopaminergic agents (levodopa, dopamine agonists) in Parkinson patients can trigger NMS-like syndrome. Same pathophysiology: sudden central dopamine depletion. Never abruptly stop Parkinson medications. Treat the same as NMS + resume dopaminergic therapy.
SSRI withdrawal causes discontinuation syndrome, not NMS.
Benzodiazepine withdrawal causes seizures and autonomic instability but not NMS.

questions.explanation: Correct: Levodopa (in Parkinson disease).

questions.stem: Masseter spasm during induction of anesthesia suggests:

choices.feedback: Cholinergic crisis has DUMBBELSS symptoms, not masseter spasm during anesthesia.
Serotonin syndrome is caused by serotonergic drugs, not anesthetics.
NMS develops over days, not acutely during anesthesia.

Correct! Masseter spasm (trismus) during anesthesia, especially with succinylcholine, is an early warning of malignant hyperthermia. MH: genetic (RYR1 mutation, autosomal dominant), triggered by volatile anesthetics + succinylcholine. Treatment: stop triggers, dantrolene IV, active cooling.

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Malignant hyperthermia) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: CK elevation in NMS is typically:

choices.text: Elevated only if patient has seizures
choices.feedback: CK is elevated from sustained muscle rigidity, not seizures (NMS rarely causes seizures).
Normal CK essentially rules out NMS.

Correct! Massive CK elevation from generalized muscle rigidity → rhabdomyolysis. CK >1000 is common; levels >100,000 occur in severe cases. Rhabdomyolysis → myoglobinuria → AKI (leading cause of death in NMS). Aggressive IV hydration is essential for renal protection.
Mild elevation is more typical of statin myopathy. NMS causes severe elevation.

questions.explanation: Correct: Markedly elevated (often >1000, can reach >100,000).

questions.stem: After NMS resolves, can the patient ever take antipsychotics again?

choices.feedback: Re-challenge is possible with careful precautions.

choices.text: Yes — after 2+ weeks, using a low
choices.feedback: Correct! Re-challenge is reasonable after full NMS resolution (minimum 2 weeks, ideally longer). Use a different antipsychotic (low-potency typical or atypical, different class from trigger), start at low dose with very slow titration, close monitoring. Recurrence risk: ~10-30%. Shared decision-making with patient.
Route of administration doesn't change NMS risk — it's the dopamine blockade mechanism.

choices.text: Only if CK returns to normal within 24 hours
choices.feedback: CK normalization takes days-weeks. Re-challenge timing is based on clinical resolution, not just CK.

questions.explanation: Key detail: potency or atypical agent with slow titration.

questions.stem: Dantrolene works by:

choices.feedback: Bromocriptine is the dopamine agonist. Dantrolene works peripherally on muscle.
AChE inhibition is the mechanism of pyridostigmine (treats MG) and nerve agents.

choices.text: Blocking calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum in skeletal muscle
choices.feedback: Correct! Dantrolene inhibits ryanodine receptor (RYR1) → reduces intracellular calcium release → decreases muscle contraction → reduces rigidity and heat generation. Used in both NMS and malignant hyperthermia. Side effects: hepatotoxicity, muscle weakness. Only acts on skeletal muscle — does not affect cardiac or smooth muscle.
That's the mechanism of benzodiazepines and barbiturates.

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Blocking calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum in skeletal muscle) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: Which diagnosis applies to each patient?

choices.feedback: Reversed! The membrane-covered central defect = omphalocele. The uncovered paraumbilical defect = gastroschisis.
Umbilical hernia is a postnatal condition with intact skin coverage. Omphalocele is a congenital defect with a membranous sac, present at birth.

choices.text: Both are omphaloceles of different sizes
choices.feedback: These are fundamentally different conditions with different embryology, associations, and outcomes.

Correct! Omphalocele: midline defect AT the umbilicus, covered by a membrane (peritoneum + amnion), often contains liver. Gastroschisis: defect to the RIGHT of the umbilicus, NO covering membrane, bowel floating freely in amniotic fluid. Note the demographics: omphalocele is associated with advanced maternal age, gastroschisis with young mothers.

questions.stem: What is the embryologic mechanism of omphalocele?

choices.text: Involution of the right umbilical vein
choices.feedback: This is the proposed mechanism for GASTROSCHISIS, not omphalocele. Gastroschisis may result from right umbilical vein involution leading to ischemic weakness in the body wall.
This is not the mechanism. The abdominal wall lateral folds (cephalic, caudal, lateral) fail to completely close, leaving the midline defect.
Neural crest cells don't contribute to the anterior abdominal wall. They're relevant for heart, face, and enteric nervous system.

choices.text: Failure of midgut return to the abdomen
choices.feedback: Correct! During weeks 6-10, the midgut herniates into the umbilical cord (physiologic herniation) for growth and rotation. Omphalocele occurs when the gut FAILS TO RETURN to the abdominal cavity by week 10-12. The contents remain in the umbilical sac, covered by the membrane.

questions.stem: What percentage of omphaloceles have associated chromosomal anomalies?

choices.feedback: Much higher than 5%. This is the rate for gastroschisis.

Correct! 30-50% of omphaloceles have chromosomal anomalies (T13, T18, T21 most common). Additional associations: Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome (omphalocele + macroglossia + macrosomia + hypoglycemia), pentalogy of Cantrell (midline defects), cardiac defects in 30-50%. This is why genetic workup is essential for omphalocele.
While the rate is high, it's not that high. About half of omphaloceles are isolated.
The rate is higher. Combined chromosomal + syndromic associations approach 50%.

questions.stem: What is the immediate priority for exposed gastroschisis bowel?

choices.feedback: Gauze is not ideal — a plastic bag/silo is preferred because it allows visualization, reduces evaporative losses more effectively, and doesn't adhere to the bowel.

choices.text: Wrap bowel in sterile bag, prevent heat/fluid loss
choices.feedback: Correct! The exposed bowel loses massive fluid and heat. Priorities: (1) Place bowel in sterile plastic bag (silo/spring-loaded or improvised); (2) Position baby on right side to prevent mesenteric kinking; (3) IV fluids at 2-3× maintenance (insensible losses); (4) NG tube for decompression; (5) Broad-spectrum antibiotics.
Immediate closure is rarely possible — the abdominal cavity is often too small for the edematous bowel. Staged reduction is usually needed.
The exposed, matted bowel is non-functional. The baby needs TPN until bowel function returns after repair (often weeks).

questions.stem: What is the most common long-term GI complication of gastroschisis?

choices.feedback: While the bowel may not rotate normally, clinically significant volvulus after gastroschisis repair is uncommon.
Adhesive bowel obstruction can occur but is less common than chronic dysmotility.
Incisional hernia can occur but is not the most common long-term complication.

Correct! The bowel's serosal damage from amniotic fluid exposure causes long-term dysmotility in many gastroschisis patients. Feeding intolerance, GERD, and need for prolonged TPN are common. About 10-15% develop intestinal atresia or short bowel syndrome requiring long-term nutritional support.

questions.stem: Classic opioid overdose triad?

choices.feedback: These can occur but aren't the classic triad.
That suggests serotonin syndrome or sympathomimetic.

Correct! The triad is pathognomonic. Respiratory depression is the lethal mechanism — opioids suppress the brainstem respiratory drive (mu receptors). Pinpoint pupils (miosis) is characteristic but not universal (exceptions: meperidine, severe hypoxia, co-ingestions).
That's a sympathomimetic toxidrome.

questions.explanation: The classic opioid overdose triad is: CNS depression (altered consciousness, ranging from drowsiness to coma), respiratory depression (decreased rate and depth, the primary cause of death), and miosis (pinpoint pupils from stimulation of the Edinger-Westphal nucleus). Respiratory depression is the mechanism of death — opioids suppress medullary respiratory centers, reducing both the ventilatory drive and response to hypercapnia/hypoxia. Miosis is present in most opioid overdoses but can be absent with co-ingestion of sympathomimetics, with meperidine (which has anticholinergic properties), or in the setting of severe hypoxia/anoxic brain injury (mydriasis).
questions.verifiedExplanation: The classic opioid overdose triad is: CNS depression (altered consciousness, ranging from drowsiness to coma), respiratory depression (decreased rate and depth, the primary cause of death), and miosis (pinpoint pupils from stimulation of the Edinger-Westphal nucleus). Respiratory depression is the mechanism of death — opioids suppress medullary respiratory centers, reducing both the ventilatory drive and response to hypercapnia/hypoxia. Miosis is present in most opioid overdoses but can be absent with co-ingestion of sympathomimetics, with meperidine (which has anticholinergic properties), or in the setting of severe hypoxia/anoxic brain injury (mydriasis).

questions.stem: Naloxone should be titrated to:

choices.feedback: Full reversal in dependent patients precipitates withdrawal and is unnecessary.
Pupil size is not the target of naloxone titration.

Correct! Goal: reverse life-threatening respiratory depression without precipitating full withdrawal. Start low, titrate up. Opioid-dependent patients: use smaller initial doses. Complete reversal causes acute withdrawal (non-lethal but causes agitation, vomiting, aspiration risk).
Naloxone shouldn't be titrated to pain endpoints in overdose.

questions.explanation: Naloxone should be titrated to adequate respiratory effort — NOT to full consciousness. The goal is to restore spontaneous ventilation (respiratory rate >12, adequate tidal volume, SpO2 >92%) while avoiding precipitating acute opioid withdrawal. Full reversal in opioid-dependent patients triggers severe withdrawal (agitation, vomiting with aspiration risk, hypertension, tachycardia, and rarely pulmonary edema). Start with low doses (0.04-0.4 mg IV) in suspected opioid-dependent patients, escalating as needed. Higher initial doses (2 mg) are appropriate when dependency is unlikely or when respiratory arrest is imminent.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Naloxone should be titrated to adequate respiratory effort — NOT to full consciousness. The goal is to restore spontaneous ventilation (respiratory rate >12, adequate tidal volume, SpO2 >92%) while avoiding precipitating acute opioid withdrawal. Full reversal in opioid-dependent patients triggers severe withdrawal (agitation, vomiting with aspiration risk, hypertension, tachycardia, and rarely pulmonary edema). Start with low doses (0.04-0.4 mg IV) in suspected opioid-dependent patients, escalating as needed. Higher initial doses (2 mg) are appropriate when dependency is unlikely or when respiratory arrest is imminent.

questions.stem: Why is re-sedation a major concern after naloxone?

choices.feedback: Naloxone is a pure antagonist with no sedative effects.
Tolerance to naloxone doesn't develop with single doses.
Re-sedation can occur with any opioid, especially long-acting agents.

Correct! Most opioids last longer than naloxone → as naloxone wears off, opioid effects return → re-sedation with respiratory depression. Especially dangerous with: methadone (half-life 24-36h), extended-release formulations, fentanyl patches. Observe ≥4h (short-acting) to ≥24h (long-acting). Consider naloxone infusion.

questions.explanation: Re-sedation after naloxone is a major concern because naloxone has a shorter duration of action (30-90 minutes) than most opioids. Long-acting opioids such as methadone (half-life 15-60 hours), extended-release formulations, and fentanyl patches can continue to release drug long after naloxone wears off. Patients who respond to naloxone must be monitored for at minimum 4-6 hours (longer for long-acting opioids). Repeated naloxone boluses or a continuous naloxone infusion (two-thirds of the effective bolus dose per hour) may be needed. Patients should never be discharged immediately after naloxone administration.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Re-sedation after naloxone is a major concern because naloxone has a shorter duration of action (30-90 minutes) than most opioids. Long-acting opioids such as methadone (half-life 15-60 hours), extended-release formulations, and fentanyl patches can continue to release drug long after naloxone wears off. Patients who respond to naloxone must be monitored for at minimum 4-6 hours (longer for long-acting opioids). Repeated naloxone boluses or a continuous naloxone infusion (two-thirds of the effective bolus dose per hour) may be needed. Patients should never be discharged immediately after naloxone administration.

questions.stem: Methadone overdose has unique risks including:

choices.feedback: Correct! Methadone uniquely prolongs QTc (blocks hERG K+ channels) → risk of torsades de pointes. Also: very long half-life (24-36h) means toxicity outlasts naloxone → need extended observation (≥24h) and possible naloxone infusion. Unlike most opioids, methadone requires cardiac monitoring.
Blindness is associated with methanol poisoning.
Hepatotoxicity is more associated with acetaminophen.

choices.text: Seizures as the primary lethal mechanism
choices.feedback: Respiratory depression remains the primary lethal mechanism. Meperidine is the opioid most associated with seizures.

questions.explanation: Methadone carries unique risks beyond standard opioid toxicity, most importantly QTc prolongation with risk of torsades de pointes. Methadone blocks the hERG (human ether-à-go-go-related gene) potassium channel, prolonging cardiac repolarization. This risk is dose-dependent but can occur at therapeutic doses, especially with concurrent QTc-prolonging medications. Other unique methadone risks include extremely long and variable half-life (15-60 hours, necessitating prolonged monitoring after overdose), accumulation during dose titration, and drug interactions with CYP3A4 and CYP2B6 inhibitors. Baseline and follow-up ECGs are recommended during methadone maintenance.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Methadone carries unique risks beyond standard opioid toxicity, most importantly QTc prolongation with risk of torsades de pointes. Methadone blocks the hERG (human ether-à-go-go-related gene) potassium channel, prolonging cardiac repolarization. This risk is dose-dependent but can occur at therapeutic doses, especially with concurrent QTc-prolonging medications. Other unique methadone risks include extremely long and variable half-life (15-60 hours, necessitating prolonged monitoring after overdose), accumulation during dose titration, and drug interactions with CYP3A4 and CYP2B6 inhibitors. Baseline and follow-up ECGs are recommended during methadone maintenance.

questions.stem: If a patient does not respond to adequate doses of IV naloxone, you should:

choices.feedback: Correct! No response to IV naloxone → strongly reconsider diagnosis. Consider: co-ingestions (benzodiazepines, GHB, barbiturates), non-opioid causes of CNS depression (stroke, hypoglycemia, head trauma, sepsis), polysubstance use. Exception: massive fentanyl exposure may require higher doses.

choices.text: Give progressively higher doses of naloxone
choices.feedback: If adequate naloxone doesn't work, opioids are unlikely the sole cause.
Charcoal is unlikely to help at this point and is dangerous in an obtunded patient without airway protection.
Flumazenil should be used cautiously and only if benzodiazepine overdose is suspected.

questions.explanation: If a patient does not respond to adequate doses of IV naloxone (typically after 10 mg cumulative dose), the diagnosis of opioid toxicity should be reconsidered. Alternative diagnoses include: other sedative overdose (benzodiazepines, barbiturates, GHB — not reversed by naloxone), mixed overdose with non-opioid predominating, stroke, hypoglycemia, post-ictal state, sepsis, hypothermia, or traumatic brain injury. Newer synthetic opioids (carfentanil, isotonitazene) may rarely require very high naloxone doses, but failure to respond to 10 mg should always prompt a broad differential reassessment while continuing supportive care.
questions.verifiedExplanation: If a patient does not respond to adequate doses of IV naloxone (typically after 10 mg cumulative dose), the diagnosis of opioid toxicity should be reconsidered.
