Light's criteria have nearly 100% sensitivity for identifying exudates but can misclassify 25-30% of transudates as exudates in heart failure patients on diuretics. In such cases, serum-to-pleural fluid albumin gradient >1.2 g/dL or protein gradient >3.1 g/dL suggests a true transudate. Understanding Light's criteria is essential for determining whether further workup (infection, malignancy, TB) is needed for an exudative effusion versus treating the underlying cause of a transudative effusion.

citation.supportingQuote: An exudative effusion is diagnosed if one or more of the following criteria are met: Pleural fluid protein/serum protein ratio of more than 0.5; Pleural fluid LDH/serum LDH ratio of more than 0.6; Pleural fluid LDH is more than two-thirds of the upper limit of the normal serum LDH value

questions.stem: Most common cause of pleural effusion overall?

choices.feedback: Malignancy is a common exudative cause but not #1 overall.
Parapneumonic effusion is the most common exudative cause but not #1 overall.

Correct! CHF is the most common cause of transudative effusion and the most common cause of pleural effusion overall. Typically bilateral (right > left). Responds to diuretics. Thoracentesis NOT needed if bilateral + known CHF + responding to treatment.
TB effusion is common worldwide but not #1 overall.

questions.explanation: Congestive heart failure (CHF) is the most common cause of pleural effusion overall and the most common cause of transudative effusion. CHF produces transudative effusions through increased hydrostatic pressure in the pulmonary capillaries, which drives fluid across the visceral pleura into the pleural space. Effusions from CHF are typically bilateral and respond to diuretic therapy. When bilateral effusions are present in a patient with known CHF, thoracentesis may be deferred in favor of a trial of diuresis. Malignancy is the most common cause of exudative effusions. Other common transudative causes include liver cirrhosis and nephrotic syndrome. For exudative effusions, pneumonia, malignancy, pulmonary embolism, and TB are the leading causes.

citation.specificSection: Etiology - Common Causes of Transudative Effusion
citation.supportingQuote: Common causes of transudative pleural effusion include conditions that alter the hydrostatic or oncotic pressures in the pleural space, such as left heart failure

questions.stem: Milky pleural fluid with TG >110 mg/dL indicates:

choices.feedback: Empyema is purulent, not milky. Differentiated by TG levels.
Malignant effusions are typically bloody or serosanguinous, not milky.

Correct! Chylothorax = thoracic duct disruption → chyle leak into pleural space. Milky/opalescent fluid, TG >110 mg/dL, chylomicrons present. Causes: trauma/surgery (#1), malignancy (lymphoma), congenital. Treatment: NPO + TPN (reduces chyle production), octreotide, surgical ligation if refractory.
Hemothorax is bloody, not milky.

questions.explanation: Milky pleural fluid with triglycerides >110 mg/dL is diagnostic of chylothorax, which results from disruption of the thoracic duct or its tributaries, allowing chyle (lymphatic fluid rich in triglycerides) to leak into the pleural space. Common causes include trauma (especially thoracic surgery), lymphoma, and other malignancies that obstruct lymphatic drainage. Chylothorax is initially managed conservatively with dietary modification (medium-chain triglycerides or total parenteral nutrition to reduce chyle flow), though some cases require surgical intervention. This is distinguished from pseudochylothorax (cholesterol effusion), which has high cholesterol but normal triglycerides and occurs in chronic effusions. Empyema appears purulent, hemothorax appears bloody (hematocrit >50% of serum), and malignant effusions vary in appearance.

citation.supportingQuote: Pleural fluid triglycerides greater than 110 mg/dL signify a chylothorax. The pleural fluid in such conditions typically has a milky-white appearance.

questions.stem: Parapneumonic effusion requires chest tube drainage when:

choices.feedback: Simple parapneumonic effusions resolve with antibiotics alone.

choices.text: pH <7.2, glucose <60, positive Gram stain, or pus (empyema)
choices.feedback: Correct! These indicate complicated parapneumonic effusion or empyema — will NOT resolve with antibiotics alone. Chest tube drainage is essential. Loculated effusions may need fibrinolytics (tPA + DNase) instilled through the chest tube, or VATS decortication for organized empyema.

choices.text: Only when the patient has fever
choices.feedback: Fever alone doesn't determine drainage need — fluid characteristics do.
Elevated protein defines exudate but doesn't specifically indicate drainage need.

questions.explanation: A parapneumonic effusion requires chest tube drainage (complicated parapneumonic effusion or empyema) when the fluid demonstrates pH <7.2, glucose <60 mg/dL, positive Gram stain, or frank pus. These findings indicate that the effusion has become complicated and will not resolve with antibiotics alone. A pH <7.2 in the setting of pneumonia almost always requires chest tube thoracostomy insertion. The progression from simple parapneumonic effusion to complicated effusion to empyema represents increasingly severe pleural space infection. Small-bore drains (10-14 gauge) are as effective as larger drains and may be preferable due to ease of placement. Intrapleural fibrinolytics and DNase can enhance drainage in loculated collections. Thoracoscopic decortication may be necessary when these measures fail.

citation.supportingQuote: Chest tube drainage with antibiotic treatment is warranted in complex parapneumonic effusions or empyema

questions.stem: What is the clinical concern with removing too much fluid during therapeutic thoracentesis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Removing too much fluid too rapidly can cause re-expansion pulmonary edema as the collapsed lung re-inflates. The procedure should be stopped if the patient develops cough, chest tightness, or dyspnea.
Pneumothorax is a risk of the needle insertion itself, not of the volume removed. It relates to technique, not drainage amount.

choices.text: Risk of hypovolemic shock from fluid loss
choices.feedback: Pleural fluid is an extravascular collection — removing it does not significantly deplete intravascular volume.

choices.text: Risk of cardiac tamponade from mediastinal shift
choices.feedback: Mediastinal shift can occur with large effusions, but drainage actually corrects this. It is not worsened by thoracentesis.

questions.explanation: Re-expansion pulmonary edema is the main volume-related complication of therapeutic thoracentesis. It occurs when a chronically collapsed lung rapidly re-expands, causing increased capillary permeability. The procedure should be stopped if the patient develops cough, chest tightness, or desaturation. Clinical practice limits drainage volume per session, and slow drainage with monitoring is recommended.

citation.supportingQuote: limiting fluid extraction to no more than 1500 mL per session is important to avoid the risk of reexpansion pulmonary edema

questions.stem: Bilateral pleural effusions in a CHF patient responding to diuretics. Next step?

choices.feedback: Thoracentesis is unnecessary if the clinical picture is clear and patient is improving.
No infection concern in typical bilateral CHF effusions.

Correct! Bilateral effusions in known CHF responding to diuresis = clinical diagnosis. Thoracentesis NOT indicated. Tap if: unilateral, febrile, not responding to diuretics, atypical presentation, concern for alternative diagnosis. Unnecessary thoracentesis exposes patient to procedural risk.
Imaging may be helpful but thoracentesis decision is the key question.

questions.explanation: In a CHF patient with bilateral pleural effusions responding to diuretics, the appropriate next step is to continue diuretic therapy. Thoracentesis is not necessary when the clinical picture is consistent with heart failure and the patient is improving with medical management. Bilateral effusions in heart failure are transudative and result from increased hydrostatic pressure. A thoracentesis in the setting of heart failure is recommended only if diuretics fail or the patient remains significantly symptomatic despite adequate diuresis. Unnecessary thoracentesis exposes the patient to procedural risks including pneumothorax and infection without diagnostic benefit. If the effusion is unilateral, asymmetric, or fails to respond to diuretics, thoracentesis should be performed to evaluate for alternative etiologies such as infection, malignancy, or pulmonary embolism.

citation.supportingQuote: A thoracentesis in the setting of heart failure is recommended only if diuretics fail or the patient is significantly symptomatic

questions.stem: Pleural fluid cytology is most useful for diagnosing:

choices.feedback: Correct! Cytology detects malignant cells in pleural fluid. Sensitivity ~60% on first thoracentesis (increases to ~75% with repeat). Most common causes: lung cancer (#1), breast cancer, lymphoma. If cytology negative but malignancy suspected: pleural biopsy (CT-guided or thoracoscopic).
Chylothorax is diagnosed by milky appearance and elevated triglycerides.
Parapneumonic is diagnosed by pH, glucose, Gram stain, and culture.
CHF effusions are diagnosed clinically and by Light criteria (transudate).

questions.explanation: Pleural fluid cytology is most useful for diagnosing malignant pleural effusion. The sensitivity of cytology for detecting malignant cells after the first thoracentesis is approximately 60%, but the diagnostic yield increases to up to 90% with three separate samples collected on different days. Lung cancer (especially adenocarcinoma) is the most common cause of malignant pleural effusion, followed by breast cancer and lymphoma. If malignancy is highly suspected despite two negative cytologies, medical thoracoscopy with pleural biopsy is recommended. Malignant effusions are almost always exudative, typically lymphocyte-predominant, and carry a poor prognosis. Cytology is not the primary diagnostic tool for chylothorax (triglyceride levels), parapneumonic effusion (pH, glucose, Gram stain), or CHF-related effusion (clinical context and Light's criteria).

citation.supportingQuote: Cytological testing is necessary in suspected cases of malignant pleural effusion. The sensitivity of pleural fluid cytology for diagnosing malignant effusions after the first thoracentesis is approximately 60%.

questions.stem: Costophrenic angle blunting on CXR requires approximately how much fluid?

choices.feedback: 50 mL is typically undetectable on standard upright CXR.
1 liter would cause significant opacification beyond just blunting.
2 liters would cause near-complete hemithorax opacification.

Correct! Upright CXR: >200 mL needed for costophrenic blunting. Lateral decubitus: can detect as little as 50-100 mL (fluid layers dependently). US is most sensitive for detecting small effusions and guiding thoracentesis.

questions.explanation: Costophrenic angle blunting on upright posteroanterior (PA) chest X-ray requires approximately 200 mL or more of pleural fluid. This is an important clinical threshold because smaller effusions may not be detected on standard PA radiography. The meniscus sign on upright CXR indicates significant pleural fluid, typically exceeding 200 mL. For detecting smaller amounts, a lateral decubitus view can identify as little as 50 mL of fluid. Ultrasound is even more sensitive than radiography for detecting and characterizing pleural effusions and is recommended for guiding thoracentesis to improve success rates and reduce the risk of pneumothorax. CT scanning provides the most detailed visualization of pleural surfaces and can help differentiate between benign and malignant causes of effusion.

citation.supportingQuote: the meniscus sign indicates the presence of a significant amount of pleural fluid, typically exceeding 200 mL and blunting the costophrenic angle

questions.stem: Most common pathogen in community-acquired pneumonia?

choices.feedback: S. aureus is more common in post-influenza pneumonia and HAP/VAP (MRSA).

Correct! S. pneumoniae (pneumococcus) is the #1 cause of CAP in all age groups. Presents with acute onset, productive cough (rusty sputum), consolidation on CXR. Urine antigen test available. Vaccination: PCV20 or PCV15 + PPSV23.
Mycoplasma is common in young adults but not the overall #1.
Pseudomonas is nosocomial — HAP/VAP and CF/bronchiectasis patients.

questions.explanation: Streptococcus pneumoniae is the most common cause of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) across all age groups and severity levels. It classically presents with abrupt onset of fever, productive cough with rusty sputum, and lobar consolidation on chest X-ray. Gram stain shows gram-positive lancet-shaped diplococci. Urinary antigen testing has ~70% sensitivity. Other common CAP pathogens include Haemophilus influenzae, Mycoplasma pneumoniae (atypical, common in young adults), Legionella, and Chlamydia pneumoniae. Empiric outpatient CAP treatment covers both typical and atypical organisms (amoxicillin + macrolide, or respiratory fluoroquinolone).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Streptococcus pneumoniae is the most common cause of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) across all age groups and severity levels. It classically presents with abrupt onset of fever, productive cough with rusty sputum, and lobar consolidation on chest X-ray. Gram stain shows gram-positive lancet-shaped diplococci. Urinary antigen testing has ~70% sensitivity. Other common CAP pathogens include Haemophilus influenzae, Mycoplasma pneumoniae (atypical, common in young adults), Legionella, and Chlamydia pneumoniae. Empiric outpatient CAP treatment covers both typical and atypical organisms (amoxicillin + macrolide, or respiratory fluoroquinolone).

questions.stem: Aspiration pneumonia most commonly affects which lobe?

choices.feedback: Correct! The right main bronchus is wider, shorter, and more vertical than the left, making the right lower lobe the most common site for aspiration. If supine: posterior segment of right upper lobe or superior segment of right lower lobe.
Less common than right lower lobe aspiration.
LUL aspiration is uncommon due to the angle of the left main bronchus.
Can be affected but RLL is most common.

questions.explanation: Aspiration pneumonia most commonly affects the right lower lobe due to the anatomy of the bronchial tree. The right main bronchus is wider, shorter, and more vertically oriented than the left, making it the path of least resistance for aspirated material. In the supine position, the posterior segment of the right upper lobe and superior segment of the right lower lobe are most commonly affected. In the upright or semi-recumbent position, the basal segments of the right lower lobe are affected. Risk factors include altered consciousness (intoxication, anesthesia, stroke), dysphagia, GERD, and poor dentition.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Aspiration pneumonia most commonly affects the right lower lobe due to the anatomy of the bronchial tree. The right main bronchus is wider, shorter, and more vertically oriented than the left, making it the path of least resistance for aspirated material. In the supine position, the posterior segment of the right upper lobe and superior segment of the right lower lobe are most commonly affected. In the upright or semi-recumbent position, the basal segments of the right lower lobe are affected. Risk factors include altered consciousness (intoxication, anesthesia, stroke), dysphagia, GERD, and poor dentition.

questions.stem: CURB-65 score of 3 in a pneumonia patient suggests:

choices.feedback: Score 3 is too high for discharge.
Correct! CURB-65 ≥3: consider ICU. Score 0-1: outpatient. Score 2: short inpatient stay or closely supervised outpatient. Each point = 1 of: Confusion, Uremia (BUN>20), RR≥30, low BP, age ≥65.
Score 3-5 indicates severe disease requiring hospitalization.

choices.text: No antibiotics needed, viral etiology likely
choices.feedback: CURB-65 assesses severity, not etiology. Score 3 needs aggressive treatment.

questions.explanation: CURB-65 is a severity assessment tool for CAP: Confusion, Urea >7 mmol/L (or BUN >19 mg/dL), Respiratory rate ≥30, Blood pressure (SBP <90 or DBP ≤60), age ≥65. Each criterion = 1 point. Score 0-1: outpatient treatment. Score 2: consider short hospitalization. Score 3-5: hospitalize, with scores 4-5 warranting ICU consideration. A score of 3 indicates significant mortality risk (approximately 17%) and should prompt at minimum hospital-level care and possibly ICU evaluation. CRB-65 (without urea) can be used in the outpatient setting where labs are unavailable.
questions.verifiedExplanation: CURB-65 is a severity assessment tool for CAP: Confusion, Urea >7 mmol/L (or BUN >19 mg/dL), Respiratory rate ≥30, Blood pressure (SBP <90 or DBP ≤60), age ≥65. Each criterion = 1 point. Score 0-1: outpatient treatment. Score 2: consider short hospitalization. Score 3-5: hospitalize, with scores 4-5 warranting ICU consideration. A score of 3 indicates significant mortality risk (approximately 17%) and should prompt at minimum hospital-level care and possibly ICU evaluation. CRB-65 (without urea) can be used in the outpatient setting where labs are unavailable.

questions.stem: PCP pneumonia prophylaxis is indicated when CD4 count is:

choices.feedback: <350 is the threshold for starting ART (though current guidelines recommend ART for all HIV+).
<50 is the threshold for MAC prophylaxis (azithromycin), not PCP.
Below 500: some OIs become risks, but PCP prophylaxis threshold is lower.

Correct! PCP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX when CD4 <200 (or CD4% <14%, or history of oropharyngeal candidiasis). TMP-SMX also provides toxoplasma prophylaxis. Alternatives: dapsone, atovaquone, aerosolized pentamidine.

questions.explanation: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP) prophylaxis is indicated when CD4 count falls below 200 cells/μL (or CD4 percentage <14%). First-line prophylaxis is trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX), which also provides prophylaxis against Toxoplasma gondii. Alternatives include dapsone, atovaquone, or aerosolized pentamidine. PCP classically presents with subacute progressive dyspnea, dry cough, and diffuse bilateral ground-glass opacities on imaging. Elevated LDH and beta-D-glucan support the diagnosis. Prophylaxis can be discontinued once CD4 count rises above 200 for ≥3 months on antiretroviral therapy.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP) prophylaxis is indicated when CD4 count falls below 200 cells/μL (or CD4 percentage <14%). First-line prophylaxis is trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX), which also provides prophylaxis against Toxoplasma gondii. Alternatives include dapsone, atovaquone, or aerosolized pentamidine. PCP classically presents with subacute progressive dyspnea, dry cough, and diffuse bilateral ground-glass opacities on imaging. Elevated LDH and beta-D-glucan support the diagnosis. Prophylaxis can be discontinued once CD4 count rises above 200 for ≥3 months on antiretroviral therapy.

questions.stem: CAP in alcoholic patient: upper lobe cavitation, currant jelly sputum. Pathogen?

choices.feedback: Mycoplasma causes atypical walking pneumonia in young adults.
Legionella: contaminated water exposure, GI symptoms, hyponatremia.

Correct! Klebsiella: classic in alcoholics and diabetics. Upper lobe cavitary pneumonia with thick, bloody 'currant jelly' sputum. Gram-negative rod with large mucoid capsule. Bulging fissure sign on CXR (heavy inflammatory exudate expands the lobe).
Pneumococcus rarely causes cavitation or currant jelly sputum.

questions.explanation: Klebsiella pneumoniae classically affects alcoholic and debilitated patients, presenting with upper lobe cavitary pneumonia and characteristic currant-jelly sputum (thick, bloody, mucoid). The organism is a gram-negative rod with a prominent polysaccharide capsule that gives colonies a mucoid appearance. Klebsiella pneumonia is notable for tissue necrosis, abscess formation, and the 'bulging fissure sign' on chest X-ray (caused by heavy exudate expanding the involved lobe). Treatment requires coverage with third-generation cephalosporins, carbapenems, or fluoroquinolones. Community-acquired Klebsiella is increasingly affected by antibiotic resistance, including extended-spectrum beta-lactamase (ESBL) producing strains.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Klebsiella pneumoniae classically affects alcoholic and debilitated patients, presenting with upper lobe cavitary pneumonia and characteristic currant-jelly sputum (thick, bloody, mucoid). The organism is a gram-negative rod with a prominent polysaccharide capsule that gives colonies a mucoid appearance. Klebsiella pneumonia is notable for tissue necrosis, abscess formation, and the 'bulging fissure sign' on chest X-ray (caused by heavy exudate expanding the involved lobe). Treatment requires coverage with third-generation cephalosporins, carbapenems, or fluoroquinolones. Community-acquired Klebsiella is increasingly affected by antibiotic resistance, including extended-spectrum beta-lactamase (ESBL) producing strains.

questions.stem: Legionella pneumonia clue on labs?

choices.feedback: Not a feature of Legionella.
Eosinophilia suggests parasitic or allergic disease.

Correct! Legionella associations: contaminated water (cooling towers, hot tubs), elderly/immunocompromised, GI symptoms (diarrhea, nausea), high fever, hyponatremia (SIADH). Urine antigen test detects serogroup 1 only. Treatment: fluoroquinolone or macrolide.
Not associated with Legionella.

questions.explanation: Legionella pneumophila causes Legionnaires' disease, an atypical pneumonia with distinctive extrapulmonary features. Hyponatremia is the classic lab clue — SIADH-mediated dilutional hyponatremia occurs in up to 50% of cases. Other Legionella clues include: GI symptoms (diarrhea, nausea), high fevers (>40°C), relative bradycardia, elevated transaminases, and failure to respond to beta-lactam antibiotics. Legionella is contracted from contaminated water sources (cooling towers, hot tubs, hospital water systems) — it is NOT transmitted person-to-person. Diagnosis is by urinary antigen (serogroup 1 only) or culture on BCYE agar. Treatment: fluoroquinolone or macrolide.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Legionella pneumophila causes Legionnaires' disease, an atypical pneumonia with distinctive extrapulmonary features. Hyponatremia is the classic lab clue — SIADH-mediated dilutional hyponatremia occurs in up to 50% of cases. Other Legionella clues include: GI symptoms (diarrhea, nausea), high fevers (>40°C), relative bradycardia, elevated transaminases, and failure to respond to beta-lactam antibiotics. Legionella is contracted from contaminated water sources (cooling towers, hot tubs, hospital water systems) — it is NOT transmitted person-to-person. Diagnosis is by urinary antigen (serogroup 1 only) or culture on BCYE agar. Treatment: fluoroquinolone or macrolide.

questions.stem: HAP/VAP empiric antibiotics must cover:

choices.feedback: HAP/VAP pathogens are nosocomial — pneumococcus is less relevant.

Correct! HAP/VAP: anti-pseudomonal coverage (pip-tazo, cefepime, meropenem, or aztreonam) + MRSA coverage (vancomycin or linezolid). De-escalate based on cultures. Risk factors for MDR: prior IV antibiotics within 90 days, hospitalization ≥5 days, high MDR prevalence.
Anaerobic coverage is more important for aspiration pneumonia.
Atypicals are community pathogens, not typical HAP/VAP organisms.

questions.explanation: Hospital-acquired pneumonia (HAP, onset ≥48 hours after admission) and ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) require empiric antibiotics that cover both Pseudomonas aeruginosa and MRSA, as these are the most dangerous and common resistant organisms in the healthcare setting. A typical empiric regimen includes an antipseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or meropenem) plus vancomycin or linezolid (for MRSA coverage). Double coverage of Pseudomonas (adding an aminoglycoside or fluoroquinolone) is considered for patients with risk factors for multidrug-resistant organisms or in units with high resistance rates.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Hospital-acquired pneumonia (HAP, onset ≥48 hours after admission) and ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) require empiric antibiotics that cover both Pseudomonas aeruginosa and MRSA, as these are the most dangerous and common resistant organisms in the healthcare setting. A typical empiric regimen includes an antipseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or meropenem) plus vancomycin or linezolid (for MRSA coverage). Double coverage of Pseudomonas (adding an aminoglycoside or fluoroquinolone) is considered for patients with risk factors for multidrug-resistant organisms or in units with high resistance rates.

questions.stem: PCP treatment includes steroids when:

choices.feedback: Steroids improve outcomes in moderate-severe PCP.

Correct! Adjunctive prednisone reduces mortality in moderate-severe PCP. Start within 72h of antibiotics. Taper over 21 days. Treatment: high-dose TMP-SMX × 21 days. CXR: bilateral diffuse ground-glass opacities. Elevated LDH supports diagnosis. Beta-glucan often elevated.
Steroids only when moderate-severe hypoxemia is present.
Steroids are based on oxygenation thresholds, not intubation status.

questions.explanation: Adjunctive corticosteroids in PCP treatment are indicated when PaO2 <70 mmHg or A-a gradient >35 mmHg on room air. Prednisone (40 mg BID for 5 days, then taper over 21 days) is started within 72 hours of antimicrobial therapy and significantly reduces mortality and respiratory failure in moderate-to-severe PCP. The mechanism is reduction of the inflammatory response to dying organisms, which paradoxically worsens gas exchange early in treatment. First-line PCP treatment is TMP-SMX for 21 days. Alternatives for TMP-SMX allergy include IV pentamidine, primaquine plus clindamycin, or atovaquone (mild disease only).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Adjunctive corticosteroids in PCP treatment are indicated when PaO2 <70 mmHg or A-a gradient >35 mmHg on room air. Prednisone (40 mg BID for 5 days, then taper over 21 days) is started within 72 hours of antimicrobial therapy and significantly reduces mortality and respiratory failure in moderate-to-severe PCP. The mechanism is reduction of the inflammatory response to dying organisms, which paradoxically worsens gas exchange early in treatment. First-line PCP treatment is TMP-SMX for 21 days. Alternatives for TMP-SMX allergy include IV pentamidine, primaquine plus clindamycin, or atovaquone (mild disease only).

questions.stem: What is the embryologic basis for postaxial polydactyly?

choices.text: Excessive SHH signaling from the ZPA
choices.feedback: Correct! Postaxial (ulnar/fibular side) polydactyly involves the posterior (SHH-producing) side of the limb. Excessive or expanded SHH signaling can cause duplication of posterior (pinky-side) structures. Isolated postaxial polydactyly Type B is the most common congenital hand anomaly in Black populations (~1/300) and is inherited as autosomal dominant with variable penetrance.
Failure of apoptosis causes SYNDACTYLY (fused digits), not polydactyly. Polydactyly involves extra digit formation, not failed separation.
AER duplication would cause limb duplication more broadly, not just an extra digit. The AER controls proximal-distal growth, not digit number.
Neural crest cells contribute to the face and heart, not limb digit formation. Limb mesenchyme comes from lateral plate mesoderm.

questions.stem: What molecular signal triggers interdigital apoptosis?

choices.feedback: Notch is important in many developmental contexts but is not the primary driver of interdigital apoptosis.

Correct! BMPs (especially BMP2, BMP4, BMP7) in the interdigital mesenchyme trigger apoptosis. This is why BMP signaling inhibition → failed apoptosis → syndactyly. Retinoic acid also promotes interdigital apoptosis. FGFs from the AER INHIBIT apoptosis (keeping digits alive while the interdigital tissue dies).
SHH patterns the anterior-posterior axis (which digit is which) but doesn't directly trigger interdigital apoptosis.
Wnt7a is involved in dorsal-ventral patterning (extensor vs flexor) but not interdigital apoptosis.

questions.stem: What distinguishes simple from complex syndactyly?

choices.feedback: Both forms can be familial or sporadic. The classification is anatomical (skin vs bone involvement).
Laterality doesn't define simple vs complex. The classification is based on whether bone is involved.

choices.text: Simple = skin/soft tissue only; Complex = bone fusion
choices.feedback: Correct! SIMPLE syndactyly = skin and soft tissue connection only, no bony fusion. COMPLEX syndactyly = shared or fused bones, often with abnormal nails. Additionally: COMPLETE syndactyly = fusion extends to fingertip; INCOMPLETE = fusion doesn't reach the tip. Complex syndactyly is more likely to be syndromic (Apert, Poland).
The number of digits involved doesn't determine simple vs complex. Even two-digit syndactyly can be complex if bones are fused.

questions.stem: Which syndrome should be considered with radial-side limb anomalies + cardiac defect?

choices.feedback: Correct! Holt-Oram syndrome = radial ray defects (thumb anomalies, absent radius, preaxial polydactyly) + congenital heart defects (ASD most common, VSD, conduction defects). Caused by TBX5 mutations (autosomal dominant). TBX5 is a transcription factor critical for both upper limb and cardiac development.
Turner has short 4th metacarpals and coarctation of aorta, not preaxial polydactyly.
T21 has a single transverse palmar crease and AVSD, but not preaxial polydactyly or radial ray defects.
Marfan has arachnodactyly (long thin fingers), not polydactyly, and mitral valve prolapse/aortic root dilation.

questions.stem: Why does thumb duplication require more complex surgery than postaxial nubbin removal?

choices.text: The radial artery is always involved
choices.feedback: While vascular anatomy is considered, the primary reason is functional importance of the thumb, not vascular complexity.

choices.text: The growth plate is always shared
choices.feedback: The growth plates may be separate or shared depending on the type of duplication. The complexity is driven by functional reconstruction needs.

choices.text: Preaxial digits have more nerve fibers
choices.feedback: While sensation matters, the primary reason for complex reconstruction is the functional importance of the thumb for hand use.

choices.text: The thumb is critical for grip; reconstruction must optimize function
choices.feedback: Correct! Thumb function (opposition, pinch grip) is critical for hand function. Simple amputation of one thumb often leaves a suboptimal result. The Bilhaut-Cloquet procedure combines elements from both duplicated thumbs (bone, nail, soft tissue) to create one well-aligned, stable, functional thumb. Postaxial nubbin removal is simple because the extra digit has no functional importance.

questions.stem: #1 cause of postpartum hemorrhage?

choices.feedback: Correct! Uterine atony (failure to contract after delivery) accounts for 70-80% of PPH. Boggy, soft uterus on exam. Risk factors: uterine overdistension, prolonged labor, chorioamnionitis, MgSO4 use. First treatment: bimanual uterine massage + oxytocin.
Retained tissue accounts for ~10%.
Coagulopathy accounts for ~1% of PPH.
Lacerations account for ~20% — trauma is #2.

questions.explanation: Postpartum hemorrhage (≥1,000 mL blood loss or hemodynamic instability): most common cause is uterine atony (70%). Management: bimanual uterine massage, oxytocin infusion (first-line), methylergonovine (contraindicated in hypertension), carboprost/hemabate (contraindicated in asthma), misoprostol (rectal). If medical management fails: intrauterine balloon tamponade, uterine compression sutures (B-Lynch), uterine artery embolization, or hysterectomy. Tranexamic acid should be given within 3 hours.

questions.stem: Carboprost (hemabate) is CONTRAINDICATED in:

choices.feedback: Correct! Carboprost (15-methyl PGF2α) causes bronchospasm — contraindicated in asthma. Other side effects: diarrhea, fever, vomiting. Methylergonovine (ergot alkaloid) is contraindicated in hypertension (vasoconstriction). Know the contraindication pairs: carboprost = asthma, methylergonovine = HTN.
Methylergonovine is contraindicated in HTN. Carboprost is safe in HTN.
Accreta requires surgical management, but carboprost isn't specifically contraindicated.
Diabetes is not a contraindication to carboprost.

questions.explanation: OB/GYN management requires systematic assessment of gestational age, maternal hemodynamic status, and fetal well-being. Evidence-based guidelines from ACOG guide management decisions for obstetric emergencies and gynecologic conditions.

questions.stem: TXA for PPH should be given within:

choices.feedback: TXA is for acute PPH management, not delayed use.
Benefit diminishes significantly after 3 hours.

Correct! WOMAN trial: TXA given within 3 hours reduces death from PPH. 1g IV over 10 min, repeat at 30 min if still bleeding. No benefit if given >3h after onset. TXA is an antifibrinolytic (inhibits plasminogen activation → stabilizes clots). Now a standard part of PPH management protocols.

choices.text: Only after all other uterotonics fail
choices.feedback: TXA should be given early alongside uterotonics, not as last resort.

questions.explanation: Postpartum hemorrhage (≥1,000 mL blood loss or hemodynamic instability): most common cause is uterine atony (70%). Management: bimanual uterine massage, oxytocin infusion (first-line), methylergonovine (contraindicated in hypertension), carboprost/hemabate (contraindicated in asthma), misoprostol (rectal). If medical management fails: intrauterine balloon tamponade, uterine compression sutures (B-Lynch), uterine artery embolization, or hysterectomy. Tranexamic acid should be given within 3 hours.

questions.stem: Placenta accreta spectrum — highest risk with:

choices.feedback: GDM is not a risk factor for placenta accreta.
First pregnancy has very low accreta risk.
Multiparity alone without prior uterine surgery has minimal accreta risk.

Correct! The combination of prior CS (scarred lower segment) + placenta previa (placenta over the scar) creates the highest risk. Risk increases with number of prior CS: 1 prior CS + previa = 3%, 2 prior = 11%, 3 prior = 40%, 4+ prior = 61%. Planned cesarean hysterectomy with multidisciplinary team is standard of care.

questions.explanation: Placental abruption: premature separation of placenta from uterine wall. Presents with painful vaginal bleeding, uterine rigidity/tenderness, and fetal distress. Risk factors: hypertension, cocaine, trauma, prior abruption. Placenta previa: painless vaginal bleeding in third trimester from placenta covering the internal cervical os. Diagnosed by ultrasound. Never perform digital cervical exam with suspected previa. Management: bed rest, steroids for fetal lung maturity if preterm, cesarean delivery.

questions.stem: Bakri balloon is used for:

choices.feedback: IUDs are for contraception, not Bakri balloons.

choices.text: Intrauterine tamponade in PPH unresponsive to uterotonics
choices.feedback: Correct! Bakri balloon: inserted into uterus, inflated with saline → direct pressure on uterine wall → controls bleeding. Used when uterotonics + massage fail. Effective in >80% of cases. Bridge to surgery if needed. Remove after 12-24h. Alternative: Ebb balloon, condom catheter.
Bakri is a tamponade device, not a dilator.
Different balloons (Foley/Cook) are used for cervical ripening.

questions.explanation: OB/GYN management requires systematic assessment of gestational age, maternal hemodynamic status, and fetal well-being. Evidence-based guidelines from ACOG guide management decisions for obstetric emergencies and gynecologic conditions.

questions.stem: Placenta percreta invades:

choices.feedback: Decidual attachment is normal.
Into myometrium = increta.
Endometrial attachment without invasion = normal placentation.

choices.text: Through the myometrium to the serosa (and possibly into bladder)
choices.feedback: Correct! Percreta = most severe: placenta invades completely through the myometrium to the serosal surface. Can invade into adjacent organs (bladder most common). Accreta = superficial attachment. Increta = into myometrium. Percreta = through myometrium. Mnemonic: A/I/P = Attach/Into/Permeate.

questions.explanation: Placental abruption: premature separation of placenta from uterine wall. Presents with painful vaginal bleeding, uterine rigidity/tenderness, and fetal distress. Risk factors: hypertension, cocaine, trauma, prior abruption. Placenta previa: painless vaginal bleeding in third trimester from placenta covering the internal cervical os. Diagnosed by ultrasound. Never perform digital cervical exam with suspected previa. Management: bed rest, steroids for fetal lung maturity if preterm, cesarean delivery.

questions.stem: B-Lynch suture is a:

choices.feedback: Episiotomy repair uses standard suture techniques.
Cesarean closure uses standard layered closure.
B-Lynch is a surgical procedure, not a device.

Correct! B-Lynch is a surgical compression suture that mechanically compresses the uterus to control hemorrhage. Used when conservative measures (uterotonics, tamponade) fail but before hysterectomy. Uterine-sparing procedure. Other surgical options: uterine artery ligation, internal iliac artery ligation.

questions.explanation: OB/GYN management requires systematic assessment of gestational age, maternal hemodynamic status, and fetal well-being. Evidence-based guidelines from ACOG guide management decisions for obstetric emergencies and gynecologic conditions.

questions.stem: First uterotonic for PPH?

choices.feedback: Correct! Oxytocin is first-line uterotonic for both PPH treatment and prevention (IV infusion). Acts on oxytocin receptors → myometrial contraction. If oxytocin fails → methylergonovine (avoid in HTN), carboprost (avoid in asthma), misoprostol (rectally — universal availability). Step up as needed.
Carboprost is second/third-line after oxytocin.
Methylergonovine is second-line after oxytocin.
Misoprostol is used when oxytocin is unavailable or as adjunct.

questions.explanation: Postpartum hemorrhage (≥1,000 mL blood loss or hemodynamic instability): most common cause is uterine atony (70%). Management: bimanual uterine massage, oxytocin infusion (first-line), methylergonovine (contraindicated in hypertension), carboprost/hemabate (contraindicated in asthma), misoprostol (rectal). If medical management fails: intrauterine balloon tamponade, uterine compression sutures (B-Lynch), uterine artery embolization, or hysterectomy. Tranexamic acid should be given within 3 hours.

questions.stem: Preeclampsia is defined as hypertension after 20 weeks PLUS:

choices.feedback: Edema is common in pregnancy and no longer part of the diagnostic criteria.

Correct! Proteinuria is classic but NOT required if end-organ damage present (thrombocytopenia <100K, elevated Cr >1.1, elevated LFTs, pulmonary edema, cerebral/visual symptoms). The 2013 ACOG task force updated criteria to not require proteinuria.
GDM and preeclampsia are separate conditions.
Seizures = eclampsia, a progression beyond preeclampsia.

questions.explanation: Preeclampsia: new-onset hypertension (≥140/90) + proteinuria (≥300 mg/24h) or end-organ damage after 20 weeks gestation. Severe features: SBP ≥160 or DBP ≥110, platelets <100K, elevated creatinine/LFTs, pulmonary edema, visual/cerebral symptoms. Management: delivery is definitive treatment. Magnesium sulfate prevents eclamptic seizures. HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) is a severe variant requiring delivery regardless of gestational age.

questions.stem: First-line treatment for eclamptic seizures?

choices.feedback: Lorazepam is first-line for status epilepticus but NOT for eclampsia.
Benzodiazepines are NOT first-line for eclamptic seizures.
Phenytoin is inferior to MgSO4 for eclampsia.

Correct! MgSO4 is first-line for both treatment and prophylaxis of eclamptic seizures (MAGPIE trial). Superior to diazepam (Eclampsia Trial Collaborative) and phenytoin. Loading 4-6g IV → maintenance 1-2g/hr. Also has tocolytic and neuroprotective effects on fetus.

questions.explanation: OB/GYN management requires systematic assessment of gestational age, maternal hemodynamic status, and fetal well-being. Evidence-based guidelines from ACOG guide management decisions for obstetric emergencies and gynecologic conditions.

questions.stem: HELLP syndrome is characterized by:

choices.text: Heart failure, Eclampsia, Liver failure, Low Potassium
choices.feedback: Incorrect expansion of the HELLP acronym.

choices.text: Hematemesis, Edema, Lung disease, Low Pressure
choices.feedback: Not related to HELLP.
That's not what HELLP stands for.

choices.text: Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
choices.feedback: Correct! HELLP: microangiopathic hemolysis (schistocytes, elevated LDH, low haptoglobin), elevated AST/ALT, thrombocytopenia. Can occur without severe HTN. RUQ pain from hepatic capsule distension. Risk of hepatic rupture, DIC, placental abruption. Definitive treatment: delivery regardless of gestational age.

questions.explanation: Preeclampsia: new-onset hypertension (≥140/90) + proteinuria (≥300 mg/24h) or end-organ damage after 20 weeks gestation. Severe features: SBP ≥160 or DBP ≥110, platelets <100K, elevated creatinine/LFTs, pulmonary edema, visual/cerebral symptoms. Management: delivery is definitive treatment. Magnesium sulfate prevents eclamptic seizures. HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) is a severe variant requiring delivery regardless of gestational age.

questions.stem: Antidote for magnesium toxicity?

choices.feedback: Naloxone reverses opioids.

Correct! Calcium gluconate antagonizes magnesium at the cardiac membrane. Give IV calcium gluconate. Signs of Mg toxicity (in order): loss of DTRs (first) → respiratory depression → cardiac arrest. Monitor: reflexes, RR >12, UOP >target rate.
Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines, not magnesium.
Bicarb is for TCA overdose and salicylate poisoning.

questions.explanation: OB/GYN management requires systematic assessment of gestational age, maternal hemodynamic status, and fetal well-being. Evidence-based guidelines from ACOG guide management decisions for obstetric emergencies and gynecologic conditions.

questions.stem: Low-dose aspirin for preeclampsia prevention should start at:

choices.feedback: Aspirin is for PREVENTION, not treatment of established preeclampsia.
Too late — placental development occurs in first/second trimesters.

Correct! ACOG recommends low-dose aspirin starting at 12-16 weeks (before 16 weeks for optimal benefit) in patients with high-risk factors. Reduces preeclampsia risk by ~17%. High-risk: prior preeclampsia, multifetal pregnancy, chronic HTN, DM, renal disease, autoimmune disease.
Evidence supports starting at 12-16 weeks, not earlier.

questions.explanation: Preeclampsia: new-onset hypertension (≥140/90) + proteinuria (≥300 mg/24h) or end-organ damage after 20 weeks gestation. Severe features: SBP ≥160 or DBP ≥110, platelets <100K, elevated creatinine/LFTs, pulmonary edema, visual/cerebral symptoms. Management: delivery is definitive treatment. Magnesium sulfate prevents eclamptic seizures. HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) is a severe variant requiring delivery regardless of gestational age.

questions.stem: Definitive treatment for preeclampsia?

choices.feedback: Antihypertensives manage BP but don't treat the underlying disease.
Bed rest is no longer recommended as treatment.

choices.text: Delivery of the fetus and placenta
choices.feedback: Correct! Preeclampsia is a placental disease — removing the placenta is curative. Timing depends on severity and gestational age. Mild <37 wks: expectant management, deliver at 37 wks. Severe ≥34 wks: deliver after stabilization. Severe <34 wks: corticosteroids for fetal lungs → deliver at 34 wks or sooner if worsening.
MgSO4 prevents seizures but doesn't cure preeclampsia.

questions.explanation: Preeclampsia: new-onset hypertension (≥140/90) + proteinuria (≥300 mg/24h) or end-organ damage after 20 weeks gestation. Severe features: SBP ≥160 or DBP ≥110, platelets <100K, elevated creatinine/LFTs, pulmonary edema, visual/cerebral symptoms. Management: delivery is definitive treatment. Magnesium sulfate prevents eclamptic seizures. HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) is a severe variant requiring delivery regardless of gestational age.

questions.stem: BP goal when treating severe preeclampsia?

choices.text: No treatment needed — hypertension is expected in pregnancy
choices.feedback: Severe HTN (≥160/110) requires treatment to prevent maternal stroke and organ damage.
Too aggressive — risks fetal compromise.

Correct! Target <160/110 to prevent maternal stroke while maintaining adequate uteroplacental blood flow. Agents: IV labetalol (first-line), IV hydralazine, PO nifedipine (immediate-release). Do NOT use ACEi/ARBs (teratogenic), nitroprusside (fetal cyanide), or atenolol (IUGR).
Aggressive normalization can compromise uteroplacental perfusion.

questions.explanation: Key detail: maintain placental perfusion).

questions.stem: Preeclampsia occurring BEFORE 20 weeks suggests:

choices.feedback: Preeclampsia by definition occurs after 20 weeks.

Correct! Classic preeclampsia occurs after 20 weeks. If preeclampsia-like features before 20 weeks, suspect molar pregnancy (complete hydatidiform mole). Also: very elevated β-hCG ('snowstorm' on US, uterus larger than dates, no fetal parts). Treatment: suction evacuation + monitor β-hCG for GTN.
GDM doesn't present with hypertension/proteinuria before 20 weeks.
Twins increase preeclampsia risk but still present after 20 weeks.

questions.explanation: Correct: Hydatidiform mole (molar pregnancy).

questions.stem: Post-op fever on day 1 is MOST commonly caused by:

choices.feedback: Correct! Atelectasis is #1 cause of fever in first 24-48h post-op. Collapsed alveoli → cytokine release → fever. Treatment: incentive spirometry, ambulation.
DVT/PE presents POD 5-7 or later.
SSI occurs POD 5-7, not day 1.
C. diff develops POD 7+ after antibiotic exposure.

questions.explanation: Surgical decision-making requires systematic assessment using evidence-based algorithms. Key principles include appropriate preoperative risk stratification, understanding surgical anatomy and pathophysiology, and recognizing conditions requiring emergent vs elective intervention.

questions.stem: A patient on chronic daily prednisone needs surgery. What perioperative steroid management is needed?

choices.feedback: Correct! Chronic steroids suppress the HPA axis. Stress-dose hydrocortisone at induction provides the cortisol surge the body needs but cannot produce. Taper back to maintenance doses postoperatively.
Home dose may be insufficient for surgical stress demand.
Abrupt stop risks adrenal crisis from HPA suppression.
Inappropriately high dose. Standard stress dosing uses hydrocortisone.

questions.explanation: Perioperative management: Hold warfarin 5 days preoperatively (INR normalizes). Bridge with LMWH for high-risk patients (mechanical valve, recent VTE). Continue aspirin for most procedures. Beta-blockers: continue if already taking, do not start de novo preoperatively (POISE trial). VTE prophylaxis: early ambulation + mechanical (SCDs) ± pharmacologic (enoxaparin or heparin) based on risk stratification. Perioperative glucose target 140-180 mg/dL.

questions.stem: Prophylactic antibiotics should be given:

choices.feedback: Correct! Within 60 min (120 for vancomycin). Ensures adequate tissue levels during procedure. Redose q4h for long cases. Discontinue within 24h post-op.
Too early — not at therapeutic levels at incision time.
That's treatment, not prophylaxis.
Post-incision antibiotics miss the contamination prevention window.

questions.explanation: Surgical decision-making requires systematic assessment using evidence-based algorithms. Key principles include appropriate preoperative risk stratification, understanding surgical anatomy and pathophysiology, and recognizing conditions requiring emergent vs elective intervention.

questions.stem: Warfarin should be held how many days before elective surgery?

choices.feedback: Correct! Hold 5 days for INR <1.5. Bridge with LMWH/UFH for high-risk patients (mechanical valve, recent VTE). Check INR day before surgery.
Excessively long, increases VTE risk without benefit.
Half-life is 36-42h. One day insufficient to normalize INR.
Appropriate for some DOACs, not warfarin.

questions.explanation: Surgical decision-making requires systematic assessment using evidence-based algorithms. Key principles include appropriate preoperative risk stratification, understanding surgical anatomy and pathophysiology, and recognizing conditions requiring emergent vs elective intervention.

questions.stem: POD 6: fever, tachycardia, distension after colonic anastomosis. CT shows contrast extravasation. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Ileus causes distension but not fever/tachycardia/contrast leak.
Correct! POD 5-7 with fever + tachycardia + peritonitis + contrast extravasation = anastomotic leak. Surgical emergency → OR for washout ± diversion. Mortality 6-22%.
C. diff causes watery diarrhea, not contrast extravasation on CT.
Dehiscence is superficial/external, not intra-abdominal.

questions.explanation: Tension pneumothorax: air trapped in pleural space with each breath, causing mediastinal shift, compressed contralateral lung, and decreased venous return. Presentation: hypotension, tracheal deviation away from affected side, absent breath sounds, JVD. Treatment: immediate needle decompression (2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line) followed by tube thoracostomy. This is a CLINICAL diagnosis — do not delay treatment for imaging. Simple PTX: chest tube if large (>2cm at apex) or symptomatic.

questions.stem: Transfusion threshold for Hb in most stable post-op patients?

choices.feedback: Correct! Restrictive strategy (Hb <7) recommended for stable patients (TRICC, TRACS trials). Exceptions: active ACS, symptomatic anemia (threshold ~8-9).
Dangerously low to wait; standard is 7.
Liberal strategy — shown no better than restrictive in most patients.
Inappropriate — increases transfusion complications without benefit.

questions.explanation: Surgical decision-making requires systematic assessment using evidence-based algorithms. Key principles include appropriate preoperative risk stratification, understanding surgical anatomy and pathophysiology, and recognizing conditions requiring emergent vs elective intervention.

questions.stem: NPO guideline for clear liquids before elective surgery?

choices.feedback: Correct! Current ASA: clear liquids up to 2h before surgery. Light meal: 6h. Full meal: 8h. Liberal policy improves comfort without aspiration risk increase.
That's for a light meal, not clear liquids.
Outdated for clear liquids.
Unnecessarily restrictive.

questions.explanation: Surgical decision-making requires systematic assessment using evidence-based algorithms. Key principles include appropriate preoperative risk stratification, understanding surgical anatomy and pathophysiology, and recognizing conditions requiring emergent vs elective intervention.

questions.stem: A patient on an MAOI eats aged cheese and develops severe headache, hypertension (BP 220/130), and diaphoresis. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: NMS is caused by antipsychotics and features rigidity + fever, not specifically triggered by dietary tyramine.
Serotonin syndrome involves neuromuscular hyperactivity (clonus, hyperreflexia) and is triggered by serotonergic drugs, not tyramine-containing foods.

Correct! MAOIs inhibit intestinal and hepatic MAO → inability to metabolize tyramine from aged foods → massive norepinephrine release → hypertensive crisis. Aged cheese, cured meats, red wine, fermented foods, and tap/draft beer are the main culprits. Treatment: phentolamine (alpha-blocker) or nitroprusside for acute hypertension.
While the presentation resembles pheo, the context (MAOI + aged cheese) makes tyramine crisis the clear diagnosis.

questions.explanation: Psychiatric diagnosis follows DSM-5 criteria. Evidence-based treatment combines pharmacotherapy with psychotherapy. Safety assessment (suicidality, homicidality, psychosis) is paramount in psychiatric evaluation.

questions.stem: What is the treatment for TCA overdose with QRS widening?

choices.feedback: Correct! Sodium bicarbonate is the specific antidote for TCA cardiotoxicity. Mechanism: 1) Sodium loading overcomes sodium channel blockade → narrows QRS. 2) Alkalinization reduces free drug binding to sodium channels. Target blood pH 7.45-7.55. Also: IV benzos for seizures, intralipid for refractory cases. QRS >100 ms = seizure risk; >160 ms = arrhythmia risk.
Charcoal may help if given early (within 1-2 hours) but does not address the cardiotoxicity.
Magnesium treats torsades de pointes but sodium bicarbonate is the specific treatment for TCA-induced QRS widening.
Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepine overdose, not TCA overdose. It is contraindicated in TCA ingestion (may precipitate seizures).

questions.explanation: Psychiatric diagnosis follows DSM-5 criteria. Evidence-based treatment combines pharmacotherapy with psychotherapy. Safety assessment (suicidality, homicidality, psychosis) is paramount in psychiatric evaluation.

questions.stem: Which antidepressant causes priapism?

choices.feedback: Correct! Trazodone (5-HT2A antagonist/reuptake inhibitor) is associated with priapism — a prolonged, painful erection that constitutes a urologic emergency. Risk is ~1 in 10,000 but patients should be counseled. Trazodone is primarily used off-label for insomnia at low doses and for depression at higher doses.
Mirtazapine causes sedation and weight gain but is not associated with priapism.
SSRIs cause sexual dysfunction (decreased libido, anorgasmia) but not priapism.
Bupropion is notable for having no sexual side effects (and no weight gain), making it attractive for patients with SSRI-induced sexual dysfunction.

questions.explanation: Psychiatric diagnosis follows DSM-5 criteria. Evidence-based treatment combines pharmacotherapy with psychotherapy. Safety assessment (suicidality, homicidality, psychosis) is paramount in psychiatric evaluation.

questions.stem: Acute dystonia from an antipsychotic should be treated with:

choices.feedback: Valbenazine (VMAT2 inhibitor) is for tardive dyskinesia (a late EPS), not acute dystonia.

Correct! Acute dystonia (torticollis, oculogyric crisis, jaw dystonia, potentially life-threatening laryngospasm) is treated with anticholinergic agents: benztropine (Cogentin) or diphenhydramine (Benadryl) IM/IV. Response is rapid (minutes). Most common in young males started on high-potency typical antipsychotics.
Propranolol treats akathisia, not acute dystonia.
Dantrolene is for NMS and malignant hyperthermia, not simple acute dystonia.

questions.explanation: Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech/behavior), negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, asociality), and cognitive symptoms. Diagnosis requires ≥6 months of symptoms with ≥1 month of active phase. Treatment: second-generation antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine) preferred first-line due to lower EPS risk. Clozapine reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (requires REMS monitoring for agranulocytosis — weekly CBC initially).

questions.stem: What is the MAOI washout period required BEFORE starting an SSRI?

choices.feedback: 24 hours is insufficient for MAO regeneration.
6 months is excessively long. The standard MAOI washout is 2 weeks.

Correct! A 2-week washout is required after stopping an MAOI before starting any serotonergic drug (SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, tramadol). This allows new MAO enzyme to be synthesized. Combining MAOIs with serotonergic drugs without adequate washout risks fatal serotonin syndrome. Special case: fluoxetine to MAOI requires 5 weeks due to its long half-life.
48 hours is far too short. MAO regeneration takes approximately 2 weeks.

questions.explanation: Major depressive disorder requires ≥5 symptoms for ≥2 weeks (SIG E CAPS: Sleep disturbance, Interest loss, Guilt, Energy loss, Concentration difficulty, Appetite change, Psychomotor changes, Suicidal ideation) with depressed mood or anhedonia required. First-line treatment: SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram). Sertraline is preferred in pregnancy and breastfeeding due to lowest placental transfer and breast milk levels. Full therapeutic effect takes 4-6 weeks. Add CBT for combination therapy. Switch or augment (lithium, aripiprazole, bupropion) if inadequate response after adequate trial.

questions.stem: Tardive dyskinesia is treated with which class of medications?

choices.feedback: Anticholinergics treat acute EPS (dystonia, parkinsonism) but can WORSEN tardive dyskinesia.

Correct! VMAT2 (vesicular monoamine transporter 2) inhibitors are used to treat tardive dyskinesia. They reduce dopamine release in the striatum. Valbenazine and deutetrabenazine are first-line. TD develops after months-years of antipsychotic use and may be irreversible. Also important: switch to a lower-risk antipsychotic (quetiapine, clozapine).
SSRIs do not treat movement disorders like tardive dyskinesia.
Dopamine agonists would be counterproductive — TD is related to dopamine receptor supersensitivity from chronic D2 blockade.

questions.explanation: Correct: VMAT2 inhibitors (valbenazine, deutetrabenazine).

questions.stem: Which SSRI has the WORST discontinuation syndrome?

choices.feedback: Correct! Paroxetine has the shortest half-life among SSRIs (~21 hours) AND is the most anticholinergic, leading to the worst discontinuation syndrome (FINISH: flu-like, insomnia, nausea, imbalance, sensory disturbances/brain zaps, hyperarousal). Venlafaxine (SNRI) is similarly problematic. Always taper gradually.
Fluoxetine has the LONGEST half-life (4-6 days including active metabolite norfluoxetine), giving it the BEST discontinuation profile — self-tapering effect.
Sertraline has an intermediate half-life and moderate discontinuation risk — less than paroxetine.
Escitalopram has a moderate half-life and relatively smooth discontinuation compared to paroxetine.

questions.explanation: Major depressive disorder requires ≥5 symptoms for ≥2 weeks (SIG E CAPS: Sleep disturbance, Interest loss, Guilt, Energy loss, Concentration difficulty, Appetite change, Psychomotor changes, Suicidal ideation) with depressed mood or anhedonia required. First-line treatment: SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram). Sertraline is preferred in pregnancy and breastfeeding due to lowest placental transfer and breast milk levels. Full therapeutic effect takes 4-6 weeks. Add CBT for combination therapy. Switch or augment (lithium, aripiprazole, bupropion) if inadequate response after adequate trial.

questions.stem: Serotonin syndrome vs NMS — how do you distinguish them clinically?

choices.feedback: SS can occur with any combination of serotonergic drugs (SSRIs + tramadol, SSRIs + triptans, SSRIs + linezolid), not just MAOIs.

choices.text: NMS only occurs with typical antipsychotics
choices.feedback: NMS can occur with both typical AND atypical antipsychotics, though typical high-potency agents carry higher risk.

choices.text: SS has clonus/hyperreflexia; NMS has lead
choices.feedback: Correct! Key distinctions: Serotonin syndrome: rapid onset (hours), CLONUS (especially lower extremity), hyperreflexia, tremor, mydriasis, diarrhea. Caused by serotonergic drugs. NMS: slower onset (days-weeks), LEAD-PIPE RIGIDITY, bradyreflexia, massively elevated CK (>1000), caused by antipsychotics. SS treatment: cyproheptadine. NMS treatment: dantrolene + bromocriptine.
While they share features (fever, altered mental status), key differences in muscle findings and causative agents distinguish them.

questions.explanation: Psychiatric diagnosis follows DSM-5 criteria. Evidence-based treatment combines pharmacotherapy with psychotherapy. Safety assessment (suicidality, homicidality, psychosis) is paramount in psychiatric evaluation.

questions.stem: What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: CPAM communicates with the bronchial tree and receives pulmonary (not systemic) arterial supply. The key finding here is the anomalous aortic supply.
Bronchogenic cysts are fluid-filled and don't typically cause recurrent pneumonia with a solid mass and systemic arterial supply.

Correct! Intralobar sequestration (ILS) = non-functioning lung tissue within the visceral pleura that receives systemic arterial supply (usually from the aorta) and has NO bronchial connection. Recurrent infections in the same lobe are the classic presentation because secretions pool with no drainage.
Extralobar sequestration has its OWN pleural covering (separate from the lung) and typically presents in infancy, not with recurrent pneumonia.

questions.stem: Why does sequestered lung maintain systemic arterial supply instead of developing pulmonary arterial supply?

choices.text: It separates before pulmonary arteries develop
choices.feedback: Correct! The accessory lung bud separates from the foregut early, before the pulmonary vascular plexus incorporates it. It retains its primitive connection to the dorsal aorta (which supplies all embryonic tissues initially). The normal lung acquires pulmonary arterial supply as the pulmonary vasculature develops — the sequestration misses this transition.

choices.text: Inflammation redirects blood supply after birth
choices.feedback: The systemic supply is congenital, not acquired. It's present from embryologic development.
The ductus arteriosus is unrelated to sequestration blood supply. The supply comes directly from the aorta via embryonic segmental arteries.
While molecular signals play a role, the primary explanation is anatomical — the tissue buds off before pulmonary vasculature develops.

questions.explanation: Best answer: It separates before pulmonary arteries develop and retains primitive aortic supply.

questions.stem: Which type is more commonly associated with other congenital anomalies?

choices.feedback: Intralobar sequestration is usually ISOLATED with few associated anomalies (only ~15%). It's the extralobar type that has more associations.
The rates are quite different — extralobar has 3-4× higher association rate than intralobar.

Correct! Extralobar sequestration has associated anomalies in 50-65% of cases: congenital diaphragmatic hernia (most common — the sequestration may be below the diaphragm), cardiac defects, foregut duplications, and vertebral anomalies. Intralobar is usually isolated. Mnemonic: 'Extra-lobar has EXTRA problems.'
Extralobar sequestrations have a very high rate of associated congenital anomalies.

questions.stem: Why is identifying the systemic arterial supply the most critical surgical step?

choices.text: The artery must be preserved for collateral circulation
choices.feedback: The artery supplies only the sequestered tissue, which is being removed. It serves no purpose for the remaining normal lung.

choices.text: Massive hemorrhage if the aortic branch retracts unligated
choices.feedback: Correct! The aberrant artery comes directly from the aorta (systemic pressure). If cut without ligation, it retracts into the mediastinum/retroperitoneum where hemostasis is extremely difficult → potentially fatal hemorrhage. Preoperative CTA to map the feeding vessel is essential. Some sequestrations have multiple feeding arteries.
CLE causes hyperinflation of a lobe (not cystic), and is typically diagnosed postnatally when the lobe fails to deflate properly.

Correct! CPAM (formerly CCAM) = hamartomatous overgrowth of terminal bronchioles forming cysts. Key differences from sequestration: communicates with bronchial tree, receives PULMONARY artery supply, and represents abnormal branching morphogenesis rather than an accessory lung bud.

questions.auditNote: CHOICES CORRUPTED: Choices C and D contain content from a different question (lung lesion identification). Only A and B are relevant to the stem about identifying systemic arterial supply during surgery. Also has two correct answers marked (B and D). Needs choice C and D rewritten to match stem.

questions.stem: Most important treatment for rhabdomyolysis?

choices.feedback: Dialysis is reserved for complications (refractory hyperkalemia, severe AKI).
Stopping the offending agent is important but IV fluids are the priority.

Correct! IV NS target rate initially, targeting UOP target rate. Fluids dilute myoglobin, maintain renal perfusion, and prevent tubular precipitation. This is the single most important intervention to prevent AKI. Start immediately upon diagnosis.
Calcium is generally AVOIDED in rhabdomyolysis (deposits in damaged muscle).

questions.explanation: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation is the cornerstone treatment for rhabdomyolysis. Early and vigorous hydration prevents acute kidney injury (AKI) by diluting nephrotoxic myoglobin in the renal tubules, maintaining renal perfusion, and promoting urinary flow to clear myoglobin before it can precipitate and cause tubular obstruction. Normal isotonic saline is the standard initial fluid. The target urine output is target rate (approximately target rate/hr) to ensure adequate myoglobin clearance. IV fluids should be continued until myoglobinuria resolves completely and CPK levels are trending downward. Dialysis is reserved for cases of refractory AKI, severe hyperkalemia, or volume overload unresponsive to conservative measures. Simply discontinuing the offending agent (e.g., statins) without aggressive hydration is insufficient to prevent renal complications.

citation.supportingQuote: Patients with crush syndrome should have a Foley catheter placed; intravenous fluids should be given to maintain urine output at 200 to 300 mL/h

questions.stem: Urinalysis shows blood on dipstick but NO red blood cells on microscopy. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Myoglobin cross-reacts with the heme-detecting dipstick reagent (peroxidase activity), giving a false-positive for 'blood.' Microscopy confirms no RBCs. Cola/tea-colored urine + dipstick positive for blood + no RBCs = myoglobinuria. Check CK to confirm rhabdomyolysis.
UTI would show WBCs and bacteria, not isolated positive blood dipstick.
True hematuria would show RBCs on microscopy.
Positive blood on dipstick without RBCs is abnormal and suggests myoglobinuria or hemoglobinuria.

questions.explanation: A urine dipstick positive for 'blood' with no red blood cells (RBCs) on microscopic urinalysis indicates myoglobinuria, a hallmark of rhabdomyolysis. The dipstick reagent detects the heme moiety using a peroxidase reaction, but it cannot distinguish between hemoglobin and myoglobin — both trigger a positive result. Microscopic analysis then differentiates: if RBCs are present, the positive dipstick reflects true hematuria or hemoglobinuria from intravascular hemolysis; if RBCs are absent, the positive dipstick is due to myoglobin from skeletal muscle breakdown. An important additional distinguishing feature is that the serum appears normal (clear) in myoglobinuria but visibly discolored (pink/red) in hemoglobinuria. Myoglobinuria may present as dark reddish-brown urine in approximately 50% of rhabdomyolysis cases.

citation.supportingQuote: the peroxidase agent used on a urine dipstick to detect blood will also react with myoglobin. Microscopic urinalysis differentiates myoglobinuria from hemoglobinuria, as myoglobinuria is associated with an absence of red blood cells

questions.stem: Why should calcium NOT be supplemented in rhabdomyolysis despite low levels?

choices.feedback: Hypocalcemia is common and expected.
The concern is deposition in damaged muscle, not myoglobin clearance.

choices.text: Exogenous calcium deposits in damaged muscle and worsens injury
choices.feedback: Correct! Hypocalcemia is EXPECTED in rhabdomyolysis (calcium deposits in damaged muscle). Giving IV calcium increases heterotopic calcification in injured tissue. Only supplement if: symptomatic hypocalcemia (tetany, seizures, Chvostek/Trousseau signs) or hyperkalemia with EKG changes requiring calcium for cardiac membrane stabilization. Calcium often rebounds HIGH during recovery phase.
Calcium doesn't directly worsen renal function in this context.

questions.explanation: In rhabdomyolysis, hypocalcemia is common in the early phase because calcium deposits into damaged muscle tissue (dystrophic calcification). Supplementing calcium exogenously worsens this process — the exogenous calcium migrates into injured muscle cells and exacerbates tissue damage. Therefore, calcium should NOT be supplemented in rhabdomyolysis unless the patient has symptomatic hypocalcemia (tetany, seizures, cardiac arrhythmias) or significant EKG changes. Importantly, during the recovery phase, previously deposited calcium is released from healing muscle tissue back into the circulation, often causing rebound hypercalcemia. This biphasic calcium pattern (early hypocalcemia → late hypercalcemia) is characteristic of rhabdomyolysis and is another reason to avoid early calcium supplementation.

citation.supportingQuote: Hyperkalemia and hypocalcemia may require emergent treatment. In the recovery phase, patients may develop hypercalcemia as calcium precipitated in damaged tissue returns to the circulation.

questions.stem: Statin-induced rhabdomyolysis risk is increased when combined with:

choices.feedback: Correct! Statin + fibrate (especially gemfibrozil) significantly increases rhabdomyolysis risk. Gemfibrozil inhibits statin glucuronidation → elevated statin levels → myotoxicity. Fenofibrate is safer with statins. Other rhabdomyolysis-risk combinations: statin + CYP3A4 inhibitors (azole antifungals, macrolides, grapefruit juice), statin + cyclosporine.
Statin + aspirin is standard cardiovascular combination.
Statin + metformin is common and safe.
ACEi + statins is a common and safe combination.

questions.explanation: Statin-induced rhabdomyolysis risk increases significantly when statins are combined with fibrates (particularly gemfibrozil). Both drug classes independently cause muscle toxicity, and their combination has a synergistic effect on myotoxicity. Gemfibrozil inhibits the glucuronidation of statins, increasing statin plasma levels and thereby amplifying muscle damage. Other medications that increase statin-associated rhabdomyolysis risk include cyclosporine, cytochrome P450 inhibitors (macrolide antibiotics, azole antifungals), and corticosteroids. Statins are the most common pharmaceuticals to cause rhabdomyolysis, with up to 10% of patients experiencing some degree of muscle symptoms. The 2002 ACC/NHLBI/AHA advisory defined statin-associated rhabdomyolysis as muscle symptoms with CPK >11 times the upper limit of normal with renal injury consistent with pigment nephropathy.

citation.supportingQuote: Concomitant use of other medications like gemfibrozil, cyclosporine, cytochrome P450 inhibitors, and corticosteroids poses an increased risk for statin-induced rhabdomyolysis

questions.stem: CK level typically peaks at what time after muscle injury?

choices.feedback: CK should be declining by this point if injury has stopped.
CK begins rising within hours but doesn't peak that quickly.
CK takes hours to begin rising and days to peak.

Correct! CK peaks at 24-72 hours post-injury, then gradually declines over 3-5 days. If CK is not trending down → ongoing muscle damage (compartment syndrome, continued crush, recurrent seizures). CK half-life is ~36 hours. AKI risk correlates with peak CK level (highest risk >15,000-20,000).

questions.explanation: Creatine kinase (CK/CPK) levels typically peak at 24-72 hours (1-5 days) after muscle injury. CK begins rising within 2-12 hours after the initial insult, peaks at 1-5 days, and then declines with a half-life of approximately 36 hours. The peak timing depends on the nature and duration of the muscle injury. CPK is the most sensitive laboratory marker for muscle injury, though its elevation does not directly correlate with the severity of muscle damage or predict renal injury. A CK level of 5 times the upper limit of normal (approximately 1000 U/L) is commonly used as the diagnostic threshold for rhabdomyolysis. If CK fails to trend downward as expected, ongoing muscle injury or development of compartment syndrome should be suspected. Serial CK monitoring guides the duration of IV fluid resuscitation.

citation.supportingQuote: serum CPK levels begin to rise within 2 to 12 hours after the injury and peak within 1 to 5 days

questions.stem: Why is the target urine output in rhabdomyolysis higher than standard resuscitation targets?

choices.text: To flush myoglobin through the renal tubules before it can precipitate and cause obstruction
choices.feedback: Correct! Myoglobin precipitates in acidic, concentrated urine and causes direct tubular injury and obstruction. High-volume diuresis dilutes myoglobin and maintains tubular flow, preventing cast formation and acute kidney injury.

choices.text: To compensate for ongoing fluid losses from damaged muscle
choices.feedback: While third-spacing into damaged muscle does occur, the primary reason for aggressive UOP targets is renal protection, not volume replacement.

choices.text: To prevent hyperkalemia by increasing renal potassium excretion
choices.feedback: Potassium excretion does increase with higher UOP, but this is a secondary benefit. The primary goal is myoglobin clearance.

choices.text: To maintain blood pressure and prevent rhabdomyolysis-induced shock
choices.feedback: Hemodynamic support is important but is not the reason for supranormal UOP targets. Standard resuscitation targets would suffice for blood pressure maintenance.

questions.explanation: Rhabdomyolysis requires aggressive IV fluid resuscitation targeting supranormal urine output to protect the kidneys. Myoglobin released from damaged muscle is freely filtered by the glomerulus but precipitates in acidic, concentrated urine within the renal tubules. This causes direct tubular toxicity, cast formation, and obstruction leading to acute kidney injury. High-volume diuresis dilutes myoglobin, maintains tubular flow, and reduces the contact time between myoglobin and tubular epithelium.

citation.supportingQuote: intravenous fluids should be given to maintain urine output at 200 to 300 mL/h

questions.stem: Compartment syndrome as both a CAUSE and COMPLICATION of rhabdomyolysis:

choices.text: Compartment syndrome only causes rhabdo, never the reverse
choices.feedback: Both directions of causation occur.

choices.text: Rhabdomyolysis prevents compartment syndrome by softening muscle
choices.feedback: Muscle swelling from rhabdomyolysis actually INCREASES compartment syndrome risk.

choices.text: This is incorrect — they are unrelated
choices.feedback: They are closely interconnected.

choices.text: Compartment syndrome causes muscle ischemia → rhabdo. Rhabdo causes muscle swelling → compartment syndrome
choices.feedback: Correct! Bidirectional relationship: 1) Compartment syndrome → muscle ischemia → necrosis → rhabdomyolysis. 2) Rhabdomyolysis → massive muscle edema (especially with aggressive IV fluids) → increased compartment pressure → compartment syndrome. Monitor compartment pressures. Fasciotomy if pressure >30 mmHg or within 30 mmHg of diastolic pressure.

questions.explanation: Compartment syndrome has a bidirectional relationship with rhabdomyolysis — it serves as both a CAUSE and a COMPLICATION. As a cause: compartment syndrome increases intracompartmental pressure above 30 mmHg, which compresses blood vessels and causes muscle ischemia. The resulting muscle necrosis releases intracellular contents (myoglobin, CK, potassium) into the circulation, producing rhabdomyolysis. As a complication: rhabdomyolysis causes massive muscle swelling from cellular edema and fluid sequestration into damaged tissue, which raises intracompartmental pressure and can trigger new or worsening compartment syndrome. This creates a vicious cycle. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation can paradoxically worsen compartment syndrome by increasing tissue edema. When intracompartmental pressure exceeds 30 mmHg, emergent fasciotomy is required. Serial compartment pressure monitoring and physical examinations are essential in rhabdomyolysis patients.

citation.supportingQuote: rhabdomyolysis can range from mild elevation in creatinine phosphokinase to medical emergencies like compartment syndrome, intravascular fluid depletion, disseminated intravascular coagulation

questions.stem: Electrical injury and crush injury both cause rhabdomyolysis. What unique risk does electrical injury add?

choices.feedback: Correct! Electrical current passes through the body causing deep tissue injury (external appearance underestimates internal damage). Unique risks: cardiac arrhythmias (VF most dangerous), spinal cord injury, internal organ damage. Requires: continuous cardiac monitoring ×24h, serial CK levels, aggressive IV fluids, and monitoring for myoglobinuria. Both low and high voltage can cause rhabdomyolysis.
Electrical injury goes far deeper than skin.
Electrical injury causes thermal damage, not hypothermia.
Electrical injury causes deep tissue destruction along the current path.

questions.explanation: While both electrical injury and crush injury cause rhabdomyolysis through direct muscle damage, electrical injury carries the additional unique risk of cardiac arrhythmias requiring continuous cardiac monitoring. High-voltage electrical current passes through the body and can directly damage the cardiac conduction system, causing arrhythmias ranging from sinus tachycardia to ventricular fibrillation and cardiac arrest. Electrical injury also causes direct injury to the sarcoplasmic membrane with massive calcium influx, producing severe rhabdomyolysis that may be disproportionate to visible external injuries. The extent of internal muscle damage from electrical injury is often much greater than surface burns suggest, as current travels along paths of least resistance (nerves, blood vessels, muscle). All patients with significant electrical injury require continuous ECG monitoring, serial cardiac enzymes (CK-MB, troponin), and aggressive fluid resuscitation for the anticipated rhabdomyolysis.

citation.supportingQuote: High voltage electric shock and lightning strikes, which also cause direct injury to the sarcoplasmic membrane, with massive calcium influx, along with severe rhabdomyolysis

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with 4 months of symmetric joint pain and swelling in her MCPs, PIPs, and wrists. Morning stiffness lasts 2 hours. DIP joints are spared. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Gout is typically monoarticular (1st MTP initially) with acute flares, not chronic symmetric polyarthritis.
PsA more commonly involves DIPs and can be asymmetric. Look for nail pitting, dactylitis, and skin psoriasis.
OA affects DIPs, 1st CMC, and weight-bearing joints. Morning stiffness is <30 minutes. Symmetric small joint involvement with prolonged stiffness points to RA.
