Correct! Symmetric inflammatory polyarthritis of MCPs, PIPs, and wrists with >1 hour morning stiffness is classic RA. DIP sparing distinguishes it from OA and psoriatic arthritis.

questions.explanation: Rheumatologic diagnosis requires integration of clinical findings, serologic markers, and imaging. Pattern recognition of joint involvement, extra-articular manifestations, and autoantibody profiles guides differential diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.

questions.stem: Which test is MOST SPECIFIC for rheumatoid arthritis?

choices.feedback: ESR is a non-specific inflammatory marker elevated in many conditions.
RF is ~70-80% sensitive but only ~50% specific. It is positive in many conditions: Sjögren, HCV, endocarditis, sarcoidosis, and even healthy elderly.
ANA is associated with SLE and other connective tissue diseases, not specific for RA.
Correct! Anti-CCP has 95-98% specificity for RA, making it the most specific serologic test. It can be positive years before clinical onset and predicts erosive, aggressive disease.

questions.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis: symmetric inflammatory polyarthritis of small joints (MCP, PIP — spares DIP). Morning stiffness >1 hour. Labs: RF (sensitive but not specific), anti-CCP (most specific). Extra-articular: rheumatoid nodules, interstitial lung disease, Felty syndrome (RA + splenomegaly + neutropenia). Treatment: early DMARD initiation (methotrexate first-line). Biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL-6 inhibitors, B-cell depletion) for inadequate response. Screen for TB before starting biologics.

questions.stem: What is the FIRST-LINE DMARD for rheumatoid arthritis?

choices.feedback: HCQ is a mild DMARD used for early/mild RA or in combination. Not the first-line monotherapy for moderate-severe RA.
TNF inhibitors are second-line, used when MTX is inadequate. They are not first-line monotherapy.
Steroids are bridge therapy while DMARDs take effect, not a disease-modifying strategy.

Correct! Methotrexate is the anchor DMARD for RA. Start within 3 months of diagnosis. Given weekly with folic acid supplementation. Monitor LFTs and CBC. It serves as the foundation for combination and biologic strategies.

questions.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis: symmetric inflammatory polyarthritis of small joints (MCP, PIP — spares DIP). Morning stiffness >1 hour. Labs: RF (sensitive but not specific), anti-CCP (most specific). Extra-articular: rheumatoid nodules, interstitial lung disease, Felty syndrome (RA + splenomegaly + neutropenia). Treatment: early DMARD initiation (methotrexate first-line). Biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL-6 inhibitors, B-cell depletion) for inadequate response. Screen for TB before starting biologics.

questions.stem: A patient on methotrexate for RA develops pancytopenia. What is the RESCUE agent?

choices.feedback: Daily folic acid prevents MTX side effects but is insufficient to rescue acute toxicity with pancytopenia.
B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia but is not related to MTX toxicity rescue.
G-CSF stimulates neutrophil production but does not address the underlying MTX toxicity mechanism.

Correct! Leucovorin bypasses the dihydrofolate reductase block caused by MTX, providing reduced folate directly. It is the rescue agent for MTX toxicity (pancytopenia, mucositis). Folic acid supplementation is preventive, not rescue.

questions.explanation: Correct: Leucovorin (folinic acid).

questions.stem: What must be screened for BEFORE starting a TNF inhibitor for RA?

choices.feedback: While important in general care, HIV screening is not specifically mandated before TNF inhibitors. TB and hepatitis B are the critical screens.
Malaria screening is not part of pre-biologic workup.

Correct! Latent TB MUST be screened for (PPD or IGRA) before starting TNF inhibitors. TNF-α is critical for granuloma maintenance. TNF inhibition → TB reactivation with disseminated or extrapulmonary disease. If LTBI positive → treat with INH before starting the biologic. Also screen for hepatitis B.
HCV screening is important but TNF inhibitors do not significantly worsen HCV. TB is the critical mandatory screen.

questions.explanation: Rheumatologic diagnosis requires integration of clinical findings, serologic markers, and imaging. Pattern recognition of joint involvement, extra-articular manifestations, and autoantibody profiles guides differential diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.

questions.stem: Felty syndrome consists of which triad?

choices.feedback: This combination is not Felty syndrome.
Correct! Felty syndrome = RA + splenomegaly + neutropenia. Occurs in longstanding seropositive RA. The neutropenia increases infection risk. Treatment: MTX (first-line DMARD) or rituximab.
This combination does not constitute Felty syndrome.
Felty involves splenomegaly (not hepatomegaly) and neutropenia (not thrombocytopenia).

questions.explanation: Rheumatologic diagnosis requires integration of clinical findings, serologic markers, and imaging. Pattern recognition of joint involvement, extra-articular manifestations, and autoantibody profiles guides differential diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.

questions.stem: Which radiographic finding is characteristic of RA but NOT osteoarthritis?

choices.feedback: Osteophytes (bone spurs) are characteristic of OA, not RA.
Subchondral cysts (geodes) are more characteristic of OA, though they can occur in RA.

Correct! Marginal (periarticular) erosions with periarticular osteopenia are hallmarks of RA. OA shows osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis, and subchondral cysts. Joint space narrowing occurs in both but is uniform in RA vs. asymmetric in OA.
Subchondral sclerosis is an OA finding from altered stress distribution in degenerating joints.

questions.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis: symmetric inflammatory polyarthritis of small joints (MCP, PIP — spares DIP). Morning stiffness >1 hour. Labs: RF (sensitive but not specific), anti-CCP (most specific). Extra-articular: rheumatoid nodules, interstitial lung disease, Felty syndrome (RA + splenomegaly + neutropenia). Treatment: early DMARD initiation (methotrexate first-line). Biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL-6 inhibitors, B-cell depletion) for inadequate response. Screen for TB before starting biologics.

questions.stem: A patient with longstanding RA develops upper extremity weakness and difficulty walking. What cervical spine complication must be evaluated?

choices.feedback: While disc herniation can cause myelopathy, the specific RA concern is atlantoaxial instability.

Correct! RA causes erosion of the transverse ligament and odontoid process → C1-C2 instability → potential spinal cord compression. Flexion-extension X-rays show atlantodental interval >3 mm. Critical to assess before intubation/surgery. Can cause myelopathy, quadriplegia, or sudden death.
Bamboo spine with ankylosis is characteristic of AS, not RA.
While osteoporosis is common in RA (steroids), the myelopathic symptoms specifically suggest C1-C2 instability.

questions.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis: symmetric inflammatory polyarthritis of small joints (MCP, PIP — spares DIP). Morning stiffness >1 hour. Labs: RF (sensitive but not specific), anti-CCP (most specific). Extra-articular: rheumatoid nodules, interstitial lung disease, Felty syndrome (RA + splenomegaly + neutropenia). Treatment: early DMARD initiation (methotrexate first-line). Biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL-6 inhibitors, B-cell depletion) for inadequate response. Screen for TB before starting biologics.

questions.stem: Earliest symptom of salicylate toxicity?

choices.feedback: Seizures are a LATE and severe finding.
Renal involvement is a late complication.
Hyperthermia occurs later from uncoupled oxidative phosphorylation.

Correct! Tinnitus (ringing in the ears) is the earliest and most reliable symptom of salicylate toxicity. Occurs at relatively low levels (~30 mg/dL). Followed by nausea, vomiting, hyperventilation. Late: confusion, seizures, coma. Tinnitus in a patient with unexplained acid-base disturbance = check salicylate level.

questions.explanation: Tinnitus (ringing in the ears) is typically the earliest symptom of salicylate toxicity, occurring at serum levels of 20-40 mg/dL. It results from direct ototoxic effects on cochlear hair cells (increased sensitivity and spontaneous discharge). Other early symptoms include nausea, vomiting, and diaphoresis. As toxicity progresses: tachypnea (respiratory alkalosis from direct stimulation of the medullary respiratory center), altered mental status, hyperthermia, seizures, and eventually cardiovascular collapse. Tinnitus in the setting of aspirin use is an important clinical clue that levels may be supratherapeutic.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Tinnitus (ringing in the ears) is typically the earliest symptom of salicylate toxicity, occurring at serum levels of 20-40 mg/dL. It results from direct ototoxic effects on cochlear hair cells (increased sensitivity and spontaneous discharge). Other early symptoms include nausea, vomiting, and diaphoresis. As toxicity progresses: tachypnea (respiratory alkalosis from direct stimulation of the medullary respiratory center), altered mental status, hyperthermia, seizures, and eventually cardiovascular collapse. Tinnitus in the setting of aspirin use is an important clinical clue that levels may be supratherapeutic.

questions.stem: Classic acid-base disturbance in salicylate poisoning?

choices.feedback: Correct! Pathognomonic pattern: early respiratory alkalosis (direct medullary stimulation → hyperventilation) + late HAGMA (lactic acid, ketoacids from uncoupled oxidative phosphorylation). The mixed pattern on ABG should always prompt checking a salicylate level. Children may present with predominant HAGMA.
Significant salicylate poisoning always disrupts acid-base balance.
Metabolic alkalosis is not a feature of salicylate toxicity.
Salicylates cause hyperventilation (respiratory alkalosis), not hypoventilation.

questions.explanation: Salicylate poisoning produces a characteristic mixed acid-base disturbance: primary respiratory alkalosis (from direct stimulation of the medullary respiratory center causing hyperventilation) combined with primary metabolic acidosis (from uncoupling of oxidative phosphorylation, inhibition of Krebs cycle enzymes, and accumulation of organic acids). In adults, respiratory alkalosis typically predominates early. In children, metabolic acidosis predominates earlier due to less respiratory reserve. The arterial pH can be alkalemic, normal, or acidemic depending on the stage — a normal pH with a low bicarbonate and low PaCO2 is a red flag for impending decompensation.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Salicylate poisoning produces a characteristic mixed acid-base disturbance: primary respiratory alkalosis (from direct stimulation of the medullary respiratory center causing hyperventilation) combined with primary metabolic acidosis (from uncoupling of oxidative phosphorylation, inhibition of Krebs cycle enzymes, and accumulation of organic acids). In adults, respiratory alkalosis typically predominates early. In children, metabolic acidosis predominates earlier due to less respiratory reserve. The arterial pH can be alkalemic, normal, or acidemic depending on the stage — a normal pH with a low bicarbonate and low PaCO2 is a red flag for impending decompensation.

questions.stem: Why is sodium bicarbonate the key treatment?

choices.text: Alkalinization traps ionized salicylate in blood
choices.feedback: Correct! Ion trapping: alkaline pH → salicylate ionized → cannot cross BBB (reduces CNS toxicity) and cannot be reabsorbed in renal tubules (enhances urinary excretion). Goal pH: serum 7.45-7.55, urine 7.5-8.0. Must correct hypokalemia first — kidneys cannot alkalinize urine when K is low.
The respiratory alkalosis is actually protective — don't suppress it.

choices.text: It neutralizes aspirin in the stomach
choices.feedback: Bicarb works systemically, not as a GI antacid.
Bicarb doesn't bind salicylate — it changes pH to trap the ionized form.

questions.explanation: Sodium bicarbonate is the cornerstone of salicylate poisoning treatment because urinary alkalinization (target urine pH 7.5-8.0) traps ionized salicylate in the renal tubules, dramatically enhancing urinary excretion. Salicylic acid (weak acid, pKa ~3.0) exists in ionized form at alkaline pH — ionized molecules cannot cross cell membranes, so they are 'trapped' in the alkaline urine and excreted. Simultaneously, serum alkalinization reduces CNS penetration of salicylate (the same ion-trapping principle at the blood-brain barrier). Even a small decrease in blood pH dramatically increases CNS salicylate concentration — maintaining serum alkalemia is critical.

Salicylic acid (weak acid, pKa ~3.0) exists in ionized form at alkaline pH — ionized molecules cannot cross cell membranes, so they are 'trapped' in the alkaline urine and excreted. Simultaneously, serum alkalinization reduces CNS penetration of salicylate (the same ion-trapping principle at the blood-brain barrier). Even a small decrease in blood pH dramatically increases CNS salicylate concentration — maintaining serum alkalemia is critical.

questions.stem: Why is intubation DANGEROUS in salicylate poisoning?

choices.feedback: Laryngospasm is not a feature of salicylate toxicity.

choices.text: Loss of hyperventilation → CO2 rises → acidemia → increased brain salicylate → cardiac arrest
choices.feedback: Correct! The patient's hyperventilation is a critical compensatory mechanism maintaining alkaline pH. Intubation + sedation → loss of this compensation → rapid CO2 accumulation → acute acidemia → un-ionized salicylate floods CNS → seizures, cerebral edema, cardiac arrest. Many salicylate deaths occur peri-intubation. If must intubate: pre-bolus bicarb, match high minute ventilation, emergency HD.
While salicylate can cause pulmonary edema, that's not the main intubation risk.
Drug interaction is not the concern.

questions.explanation: Intubation in salicylate poisoning is extremely dangerous because it removes the patient's compensatory hyperventilation. Salicylate-poisoned patients hyperventilate to maintain respiratory alkalosis, which keeps salicylate ionized and prevents CNS penetration. If intubated, the ventilator typically cannot match the patient's pre-intubation minute ventilation (which may be 2-3× normal). CO2 rises, pH drops, and the resulting acidemia shifts un-ionized salicylate across the blood-brain barrier into the CNS — causing rapid clinical deterioration, seizures, and death. If intubation is absolutely necessary, it must be performed with the highest possible respiratory rate and tidal volume to maintain alkalemia.

Salicylate-poisoned patients hyperventilate to maintain respiratory alkalosis, which keeps salicylate ionized and prevents CNS penetration. If intubated, the ventilator typically cannot match the patient's pre-intubation minute ventilation (which may be 2-3× normal). CO2 rises, pH drops, and the resulting acidemia shifts un-ionized salicylate across the blood-brain barrier into the CNS — causing rapid clinical deterioration, seizures, and death. If intubation is absolutely necessary, it must be performed with the highest possible respiratory rate and tidal volume to maintain alkalemia.

questions.stem: Hypokalemia must be corrected in salicylate poisoning because:

choices.text: Kidneys cannot alkalinize urine when potassium is low
choices.feedback: Correct! In hypokalemia, kidneys reabsorb K+ and excrete H+ to compensate → urine becomes acidic → bicarb therapy fails (can't trap salicylate in urine). Must replete K aggressively for alkalinization to work. Target K >4.0. This is a frequently tested concept.
While true, this isn't the primary concern in this context.

choices.text: Potassium is needed for activated charcoal to work
choices.feedback: Charcoal mechanism is unrelated to potassium.
Potassium doesn't affect salicylate absorption.

questions.explanation: Hypokalemia must be corrected in salicylate poisoning because the kidneys cannot effectively alkalinize the urine when potassium is depleted. In hypokalemia, the renal tubules reabsorb potassium in exchange for hydrogen ions — the kidneys excrete H+ to conserve K+, producing paradoxically acidic urine despite sodium bicarbonate infusion. This defeats the therapeutic goal of urinary alkalinization. Aggressive potassium replacement (targeting K+ >4.0 mEq/L) is essential to allow bicarbonate therapy to achieve adequate urinary pH. Volume resuscitation with potassium-containing fluids should be initiated early.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Hypokalemia must be corrected in salicylate poisoning because the kidneys cannot effectively alkalinize the urine when potassium is depleted. In hypokalemia, the renal tubules reabsorb potassium in exchange for hydrogen ions — the kidneys excrete H+ to conserve K+, producing paradoxically acidic urine despite sodium bicarbonate infusion. This defeats the therapeutic goal of urinary alkalinization. Aggressive potassium replacement (targeting K+ >4.0 mEq/L) is essential to allow bicarbonate therapy to achieve adequate urinary pH. Volume resuscitation with potassium-containing fluids should be initiated early.

questions.stem: Indications for hemodialysis in salicylate poisoning:

choices.feedback: HD is indicated for severe toxicity regardless of acute or chronic ingestion.

choices.text: Only after 24 hours of bicarb therapy
choices.feedback: Don't wait — HD should be initiated promptly when indicated.

choices.text: Level >100, renal failure, AMS, pulmonary edema, acidemia (pH <7.2), clinical deterioration
choices.feedback: Correct! HD directly removes salicylate from blood AND corrects acid-base disturbances. Definitive treatment for severe poisoning. Don't delay HD for bicarb to 'work' — if HD criteria met, initiate immediately. Also indicated: failure to respond to aggressive bicarb therapy, need for intubation (relative indication).
30 mg/dL is mild toxicity — bicarb is sufficient.

questions.explanation: Indications for hemodialysis in salicylate poisoning include: serum salicylate level >100 mg/dL, altered mental status, renal failure (inability to renally eliminate salicylate), pulmonary edema (ARDS from salicylate-induced capillary leak), severe acidemia (pH <7.2 despite bicarbonate therapy), and clinical deterioration despite aggressive medical management. Hemodialysis rapidly removes salicylate and corrects acid-base disturbances. It is the definitive treatment for severe poisoning. Early nephrology consultation is recommended when levels approach 80-90 mg/dL or when there is any evidence of end-organ toxicity.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Indications for hemodialysis in salicylate poisoning include: serum salicylate level >100 mg/dL, altered mental status, renal failure (inability to renally eliminate salicylate), pulmonary edema (ARDS from salicylate-induced capillary leak), severe acidemia (pH <7.2 despite bicarbonate therapy), and clinical deterioration despite aggressive medical management. Hemodialysis rapidly removes salicylate and corrects acid-base disturbances. It is the definitive treatment for severe poisoning. Early nephrology consultation is recommended when levels approach 80-90 mg/dL or when there is any evidence of end-organ toxicity.

questions.stem: Multi-dose activated charcoal is uniquely useful in salicylate poisoning because:

choices.feedback: MDAC use is based on clinical pharmacology, not regulation.

choices.text: Charcoal prevents vomiting in salicylate poisoning
choices.feedback: Charcoal doesn't prevent vomiting — salicylates commonly cause nausea.

choices.text: Salicylates are not absorbed by charcoal on first dose
choices.feedback: Charcoal does absorb salicylates on first dose.

Correct! Salicylates (especially enteric-coated or large ingestions) form concretions/bezoars in the stomach → continued absorption over hours. Multi-dose charcoal (MDAC) can adsorb salicylate still in the GI tract AND may enhance elimination through 'gut dialysis' (salicylate excreted into the GI lumen is re-adsorbed by charcoal). Whole bowel irrigation also considered for large ingestions.

questions.explanation: Multi-dose activated charcoal (MDAC) is uniquely useful in salicylate poisoning because salicylates can form bezoars/concretions in the stomach (particularly enteric-coated formulations), leading to prolonged and erratic absorption. MDAC (50 g every 4 hours) can bind residual salicylate in the GI tract. Additionally, salicylates undergo enterohepatic recirculation — MDAC can interrupt this cycle by binding salicylate secreted into the bile. Salicylate absorption can continue for many hours post-ingestion with large or enteric-coated doses, so serial salicylate levels are essential (do not rely on a single level to exclude significant ingestion).

Additionally, salicylates undergo enterohepatic recirculation — MDAC can interrupt this cycle by binding salicylate secreted into the bile. Salicylate absorption can continue for many hours post-ingestion with large or enteric-coated doses, so serial salicylate levels are essential (do not rely on a single level to exclude significant ingestion).

questions.stem: Chronic salicylate toxicity compared to acute:

choices.feedback: Chronic toxicity still causes acid-base disturbance, often HAGMA predominant.

choices.text: Only occurs with topical salicylate products
choices.feedback: Chronic toxicity occurs with oral aspirin and other salicylate formulations.
Chronic toxicity can be more dangerous.

Correct! Chronic toxicity is often more severe because: (1) salicylate accumulates gradually → more time for CNS penetration, (2) patients are often elderly on therapeutic doses → diagnosis delayed (misattributed to other conditions), (3) presents with AMS/confusion without classic acute findings, (4) higher mortality than acute. Always check salicylate levels in elderly patients with unexplained altered mental status + acid-base disturbance.

questions.explanation: Chronic salicylate toxicity is often more dangerous than acute overdose because: it is frequently unrecognized (presenting as altered mental status, tachypnea, or metabolic derangements in elderly patients), diagnosis is delayed (not suspected in non-overdose settings), and total body salicylate burden is higher due to saturable elimination kinetics (at steady-state toxic levels, small dose increases cause disproportionate level elevations). Chronic toxicity occurs at lower serum levels than acute toxicity because tissue distribution has reached equilibrium — CNS levels are proportionally higher. Mortality from chronic salicylate toxicity (up to 25%) exceeds that from acute intentional overdose.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Chronic salicylate toxicity is often more dangerous than acute overdose because: it is frequently unrecognized (presenting as altered mental status, tachypnea, or metabolic derangements in elderly patients), diagnosis is delayed (not suspected in non-overdose settings), and total body salicylate burden is higher due to saturable elimination kinetics (at steady-state toxic levels, small dose increases cause disproportionate level elevations). Chronic toxicity occurs at lower serum levels than acute toxicity because tissue distribution has reached equilibrium — CNS levels are proportionally higher. Mortality from chronic salicylate toxicity (up to 25%) exceeds that from acute intentional overdose.

questions.stem: Which type of hallucination is MOST characteristic of schizophrenia?

choices.feedback: Visual hallucinations are more suggestive of organic/medical causes (delirium, Lewy body dementia, substance intoxication). Always think medical etiology first with visual hallucinations.
Olfactory hallucinations suggest temporal lobe epilepsy or brain tumor (uncinate fits), not typical schizophrenia.
Tactile hallucinations (formication — bugs crawling on skin) are most associated with substance use (cocaine, methamphetamine, alcohol withdrawal) and delirium.

Correct! Auditory hallucinations (especially voices commenting on behavior or two voices conversing) are the most common and characteristic hallucinations in schizophrenia. Present in ~70% of patients. Command auditory hallucinations require safety assessment.

questions.explanation: Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech/behavior), negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, asociality), and cognitive symptoms. Diagnosis requires ≥6 months of symptoms with ≥1 month of active phase. Treatment: second-generation antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine) preferred first-line due to lower EPS risk. Clozapine reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (requires REMS monitoring for agranulocytosis — weekly CBC initially).

questions.stem: What is the ONLY antipsychotic approved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia?

choices.feedback: Haloperidol is a first-generation antipsychotic with no special efficacy in treatment resistance.

Correct! Clozapine is the most effective antipsychotic and the only one approved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (defined as failure of ≥2 adequate antipsychotic trials). Also approved for reducing suicide risk in schizophrenia. Requires mandatory ANC monitoring due to risk of agranulocytosis (~1%). Other risks: metabolic syndrome, seizures, myocarditis.
Olanzapine is effective but not approved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia and has significant metabolic side effects.
Aripiprazole is a D2 partial agonist with good tolerability but is not specifically indicated for treatment-resistant schizophrenia.

questions.explanation: Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech/behavior), negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, asociality), and cognitive symptoms. Diagnosis requires ≥6 months of symptoms with ≥1 month of active phase. Treatment: second-generation antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine) preferred first-line due to lower EPS risk. Clozapine reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (requires REMS monitoring for agranulocytosis — weekly CBC initially).

questions.stem: A patient on haloperidol develops high fever, lead-pipe rigidity, altered consciousness, and CK of 15,000 U/L. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Malignant hyperthermia occurs with volatile anesthetics + succinylcholine, not antipsychotics. Though treatment with dantrolene is similar.
Serotonin syndrome causes clonus, hyperreflexia, and myoclonus — NOT lead-pipe rigidity. It results from serotonergic drugs, not antipsychotics.

Correct! NMS tetrad: Fever + Rigidity (lead-pipe) + Autonomic instability + Mental status changes, with massively elevated CK. Caused by dopamine D2 blockade from antipsychotics (higher risk with high-potency typical agents like haloperidol). Treatment: STOP the antipsychotic, dantrolene + bromocriptine + supportive ICU care.

Catatonia can cause rigidity and altered mental status but typically without fever to this degree or massively elevated CK. NMS and catatonia can overlap but the context (antipsychotic use + CK elevation) points to NMS.

questions.explanation: Psychiatric diagnosis follows DSM-5 criteria. Evidence-based treatment combines pharmacotherapy with psychotherapy. Safety assessment (suicidality, homicidality, psychosis) is paramount in psychiatric evaluation.

questions.stem: The negative symptoms of schizophrenia (5 A's) include all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: Alogia (poverty of speech) is a core negative symptom.

Correct! Auditory hallucinations are a POSITIVE symptom, not negative. The 5 A's of negative symptoms: Affective flattening (flat affect), Alogia (poverty of speech), Avolition (lack of motivation), Anhedonia (inability to feel pleasure), Asociality (social withdrawal). Negative symptoms are harder to treat and carry worse prognosis than positive symptoms.
Anhedonia is a core negative symptom.
Avolition is a core negative symptom.

questions.explanation: Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech/behavior), negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, asociality), and cognitive symptoms. Diagnosis requires ≥6 months of symptoms with ≥1 month of active phase. Treatment: second-generation antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine) preferred first-line due to lower EPS risk. Clozapine reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (requires REMS monitoring for agranulocytosis — weekly CBC initially).

questions.stem: A 23-year-old woman presents with acute psychosis. Which medical condition must be specifically excluded, especially if a teratoma is found?

choices.feedback: Hashimoto encephalopathy (steroid-responsive encephalopathy) can cause psychiatric symptoms but is not associated with teratomas.
SLE can cause neuropsychiatric symptoms but is not specifically associated with teratomas.

Correct! Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis classically affects young women, often associated with ovarian teratoma. Presents with psychiatric symptoms (psychosis, agitation), seizures, movement disorders, autonomic instability, and decreased consciousness. ALWAYS check for ovarian teratoma (pelvic ultrasound) and anti-NMDA antibodies in young women with new-onset psychosis. Treatment: tumor removal + immunotherapy.
Pheochromocytoma causes hypertensive crises and anxiety, not psychosis with teratoma.

questions.explanation: Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech/behavior), negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, asociality), and cognitive symptoms. Diagnosis requires ≥6 months of symptoms with ≥1 month of active phase. Treatment: second-generation antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine) preferred first-line due to lower EPS risk. Clozapine reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (requires REMS monitoring for agranulocytosis — weekly CBC initially).

questions.stem: Which atypical antipsychotic has the WORST metabolic side effect profile?

choices.feedback: Correct! Olanzapine causes the most weight gain and metabolic syndrome (diabetes, dyslipidemia) among atypical antipsychotics. Patients can gain 10+ kg in the first year. Requires baseline and periodic monitoring of weight, BMI, fasting glucose, lipid panel, and waist circumference. Despite this, it is highly effective.
Lurasidone has relatively favorable metabolic effects compared to other atypicals.
Ziprasidone is weight-neutral but carries QTc prolongation risk.
Aripiprazole (D2 partial agonist) has the LEAST metabolic effects among atypicals.

questions.explanation: Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech/behavior), negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, asociality), and cognitive symptoms. Diagnosis requires ≥6 months of symptoms with ≥1 month of active phase. Treatment: second-generation antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine) preferred first-line due to lower EPS risk. Clozapine reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (requires REMS monitoring for agranulocytosis — weekly CBC initially).

questions.stem: What is the minimum total duration of illness required to diagnose schizophrenia?

choices.feedback: Correct! Schizophrenia requires ≥6 months total disturbance, including at least 1 month of active-phase symptoms (≥2 of: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, negative symptoms). The remaining time may include prodromal or residual symptoms. If 1-6 months: schizophreniform. If <1 month: brief psychotic disorder.
1 month of active symptoms is required, but the TOTAL illness duration must be longer.
2 weeks is relevant for MDD criteria, not schizophrenia.
One year is not required. The 6-month threshold distinguishes schizophrenia from schizophreniform disorder.

questions.explanation: Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech/behavior), negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, asociality), and cognitive symptoms. Diagnosis requires ≥6 months of symptoms with ≥1 month of active phase. Treatment: second-generation antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine) preferred first-line due to lower EPS risk. Clozapine reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (requires REMS monitoring for agranulocytosis — weekly CBC initially).

questions.stem: Which antipsychotic requires mandatory blood monitoring due to risk of agranulocytosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Clozapine requires mandatory ANC monitoring through the Clozapine REMS program: weekly for 6 months, then biweekly for 6 months, then monthly thereafter. Agranulocytosis risk is ~1%. ANC <1000 → hold clozapine. ANC <500 → discontinue permanently. Despite this burden, clozapine's superior efficacy makes it essential for treatment-resistant schizophrenia.
Quetiapine does not require mandatory blood monitoring for agranulocytosis.
Haloperidol requires monitoring for EPS and QTc but not mandatory ANC monitoring.
Risperidone does not carry significant agranulocytosis risk and does not require mandatory blood monitoring.

questions.explanation: Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech/behavior), negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, asociality), and cognitive symptoms. Diagnosis requires ≥6 months of symptoms with ≥1 month of active phase. Treatment: second-generation antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine) preferred first-line due to lower EPS risk. Clozapine reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (requires REMS monitoring for agranulocytosis — weekly CBC initially).

questions.stem: A patient with limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis has the antibody anti-centromere. What is the leading cause of death in this subtype?

choices.feedback: While chronic GERD increases Barrett esophagus risk, esophageal cancer is not the leading cause of death in lcSSc.
Renal crisis primarily occurs in diffuse SSc, especially with anti-RNA polymerase III antibodies.

Correct! PAH is the leading cause of death in limited cutaneous SSc (formerly CREST). Anti-centromere antibody is the associated marker. Screen annually with echocardiography and PFTs (isolated decreased DLCO without restriction suggests PAH). Treatment: endothelin receptor antagonists, PDE5 inhibitors, prostacyclins.
ILD is the leading cause of death in DIFFUSE cutaneous SSc, not limited.

questions.explanation: Systemic sclerosis: limited (CREST — Calcinosis, Raynaud's, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasia; anti-centromere antibody) vs diffuse (rapid skin thickening, early organ involvement; anti-Scl-70/topoisomerase I). Scleroderma renal crisis: acute hypertensive emergency with renal failure — treat with ACE inhibitors (NOT beta-blockers or calcium channel blockers). Pulmonary hypertension screening with echocardiography.

questions.stem: A patient with diffuse SSc develops acute severe hypertension, schistocytes on peripheral smear, and rising creatinine. What is the FIRST treatment?

choices.feedback: While SRC can resemble TTP (MAHA + renal failure), plasmapheresis is not first-line. ACE inhibitors are the specific treatment.
While blood pressure control is important, ACE inhibitors (not beta-blockers) are specifically indicated for scleroderma renal crisis. They address the underlying renin-mediated pathophysiology.

Correct! Scleroderma renal crisis is a medical emergency treated with ACE inhibitors (captopril). This is one of the few situations where ACE inhibitors are specifically life-saving. They block the renin-angiotensin pathway driving the crisis. Do NOT stop even if creatinine initially rises. Avoid high-dose steroids (can precipitate SRC).
High-dose steroids are a RISK FACTOR for scleroderma renal crisis and should be avoided, not used as treatment.

questions.explanation: Correct: ACE inhibitor (captopril).

questions.stem: Which antibody is most specific for diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis?

choices.feedback: Anti-Jo-1 is associated with antisynthetase syndrome/polymyositis, not scleroderma.
Anti-centromere is associated with LIMITED cutaneous SSc (CREST syndrome), not diffuse.
Anti-dsDNA is specific for SLE, not scleroderma.

Correct! Anti-Scl-70 is highly specific for diffuse cutaneous SSc and is associated with interstitial lung disease (ILD). Anti-centromere = limited SSc. Anti-RNA polymerase III = diffuse SSc with renal crisis risk.

questions.explanation: Correct: Anti-Scl-70 (anti-topoisomerase I).

questions.stem: CREST syndrome includes all of the following EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: Raynaud is the R in CREST and is present in >95% of SSc patients.
Calcinosis (calcium deposits in skin/subcutaneous tissue) is the C in CREST.
Telangiectasias (on face, hands, mucous membranes) is the T in CREST.

Correct! CREST = Calcinosis, Raynaud, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasias. Renal crisis is associated with DIFFUSE SSc, not limited/CREST. This is a common boards trap — renal crisis occurs in dcSSc with anti-RNA polymerase III.

questions.stem: What is the most common GI manifestation of systemic sclerosis?

choices.feedback: SIBO occurs due to intestinal dysmotility but is not the most common GI manifestation overall.

Correct! Esophageal dysmotility is the #1 visceral involvement in SSc (80-90%). Fibrosis of smooth muscle in the lower 2/3 of the esophagus → decreased LES pressure → severe GERD, dysphagia, and Barrett esophagus risk. Barium swallow shows dilated distal esophagus with loss of peristalsis.
Wide-mouth diverticula and pseudo-obstruction can occur but are less common than esophageal disease.
Gastric antral vascular ectasia (GAVE/watermelon stomach) occurs in SSc but is less common than esophageal involvement.

questions.explanation: Systemic sclerosis: limited (CREST — Calcinosis, Raynaud's, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasia; anti-centromere antibody) vs diffuse (rapid skin thickening, early organ involvement; anti-Scl-70/topoisomerase I). Scleroderma renal crisis: acute hypertensive emergency with renal failure — treat with ACE inhibitors (NOT beta-blockers or calcium channel blockers). Pulmonary hypertension screening with echocardiography.

questions.stem: Why should high-dose corticosteroids be avoided in diffuse systemic sclerosis?

choices.text: They can precipitate scleroderma renal crisis
choices.feedback: Correct! High-dose corticosteroids are an independent risk factor for precipitating scleroderma renal crisis. If steroids are absolutely necessary, use the lowest effective dose and monitor blood pressure and renal function closely.
Drug interactions are not the primary concern. The risk of precipitating renal crisis is the specific reason.

choices.text: They cause avascular necrosis in SSc patients
choices.feedback: While AVN is a steroid side effect generally, this is not the primary reason to avoid steroids specifically in dcSSc.
Steroids may transiently improve skin thickening. The critical concern is renal, not dermatologic.

questions.explanation: Rheumatologic diagnosis requires integration of clinical findings, serologic markers, and imaging. Pattern recognition of joint involvement, extra-articular manifestations, and autoantibody profiles guides differential diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.

questions.stem: Raynaud phenomenon is distinguished from Raynaud disease by:

choices.feedback: Primary Raynaud has NEGATIVE ANA and normal nailfold capillaroscopy. Positive ANA suggests secondary/underlying connective tissue disease.
Both primary and secondary Raynaud predominantly affect the fingers (and sometimes toes). No foot-only distinction exists.

choices.text: The presence of an underlying autoimmune or vascular condition
choices.feedback: Correct! Raynaud phenomenon = secondary to an underlying condition (SSc, SLE, MCTD, cryoglobulinemia, thoracic outlet syndrome). Raynaud disease = primary/idiopathic (young women, symmetric, no tissue damage). Red flags for secondary: asymmetric attacks, digital ulcers/gangrene, abnormal nailfold capillaroscopy, positive ANA.

choices.text: The color changes are different (white vs blue)
choices.feedback: Both primary Raynaud disease and secondary Raynaud phenomenon show the classic triphasic color change (white → blue → red).

questions.explanation: Rheumatologic diagnosis requires integration of clinical findings, serologic markers, and imaging. Pattern recognition of joint involvement, extra-articular manifestations, and autoantibody profiles guides differential diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.

questions.stem: Annual screening for PAH in limited SSc should include:

choices.feedback: Correct! Annual echocardiography (estimates right ventricular systolic pressure) + PFTs with DLCO are recommended for PAH screening in SSc. An isolated decrease in DLCO (without proportional FVC decrease) is an early indicator of PAH. If RVSP >40 mmHg or declining DLCO, right heart catheterization is needed for definitive diagnosis.
BNP/NT-proBNP can support PAH diagnosis but is not sufficient as a sole screening tool. Echocardiography and PFTs are the standard screening combination.
Chest X-ray is insensitive for early PAH. It may show right heart enlargement only in advanced disease.
CTPA is used for acute PE evaluation, not routine PAH screening.

questions.explanation: Rheumatologic diagnosis requires integration of clinical findings, serologic markers, and imaging. Pattern recognition of joint involvement, extra-articular manifestations, and autoantibody profiles guides differential diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old with HbSS presents with acute severe anemia (Hb 3.8, baseline 7.5) and a massively enlarged spleen. Reticulocyte count is elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Acute splenic sequestration: rapidly enlarging spleen + severe anemia with elevated reticulocytes (still producing RBCs, but they're trapped). Emergency transfusion needed.
ACS presents with pulmonary infiltrate and respiratory symptoms, not isolated splenomegaly and anemia.
Aplastic crisis has a LOW reticulocyte count (parvovirus B19 stops RBC production). This patient has elevated reticulocytes.
Hemolytic crises don't typically cause this degree of acute Hb drop with massive splenomegaly.

questions.explanation: Anemia classification by MCV: microcytic (iron deficiency, thalassemia, chronic disease, sideroblastic), normocytic (acute blood loss, chronic disease, renal failure, hemolysis), macrocytic (B12/folate deficiency, MDS, liver disease, hypothyroidism). Iron deficiency shows low ferritin, low iron, high TIBC, low transferrin saturation. B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils and can cause subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (posterior columns + lateral corticospinal tracts).

questions.stem: What is the most common cause of osteomyelitis in sickle cell disease?

choices.feedback: Pseudomonas is associated with osteomyelitis from puncture wounds through sneakers, not SCD.
S. pneumoniae causes sepsis/meningitis in SCD but is not the leading cause of osteomyelitis.
S. aureus is the most common cause of osteomyelitis in the GENERAL population, but in SCD, Salmonella is more common due to functional asplenia and gut translocation.
Correct! Salmonella is the most common cause of osteomyelitis in SCD — a classic boards question. The ischemic gut mucosa allows Salmonella translocation, and functional asplenia impairs clearance.

questions.explanation: Sickle cell disease results from a point mutation in the beta-globin gene (Glu→Val at position 6). Acute complications include vaso-occlusive crisis (pain management, hydration, avoid triggers), acute chest syndrome (fever + new pulmonary infiltrate — treat with antibiotics, exchange transfusion if severe), splenic sequestration (emergent transfusion), and stroke. Hydroxyurea increases fetal hemoglobin (HbF) and is the mainstay of disease-modifying therapy, reducing pain crises, ACS, and transfusion needs.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old with SCD presents with sudden severe anemia and reticulocyte count of 0.1%. What infection is the most likely cause?

choices.feedback: S. pneumoniae causes sepsis, not aplastic crisis. Reticulocytes would be elevated in sepsis-related hemolysis.

Correct! Parvovirus B19 causes aplastic crisis by infecting erythroid precursors in the bone marrow → transient red cell aplasia → severe anemia with absent/very low reticulocytes. Self-limited but may need transfusion.
Salmonella causes osteomyelitis in SCD, not aplastic crisis.
Mycoplasma causes pneumonia/ACS, not reticulocyte suppression.

questions.explanation: Anemia classification by MCV: microcytic (iron deficiency, thalassemia, chronic disease, sideroblastic), normocytic (acute blood loss, chronic disease, renal failure, hemolysis), macrocytic (B12/folate deficiency, MDS, liver disease, hypothyroidism). Iron deficiency shows low ferritin, low iron, high TIBC, low transferrin saturation. B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils and can cause subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (posterior columns + lateral corticospinal tracts).

questions.stem: At what age should TCD screening begin for stroke prevention in SCD, and what velocity indicates high risk?

choices.feedback: Age 1 is too early for reliable TCD, and the threshold is 200, not 170.
Screening should begin earlier, and 150 cm/s is considered conditional, not high-risk.
Age 10 would miss many strokes — 11% of SCD patients have stroke by age 20, often in childhood.
Correct! TCD screening starts at age 2 and continues annually through age 16. Velocity >200 cm/s = high risk → chronic transfusion therapy (target HbS <30%) reduces stroke risk by 92%.

questions.explanation: DIC is a consumptive coagulopathy with simultaneous thrombosis and bleeding. Lab findings: elevated PT/PTT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer, low platelets, schistocytes on smear. Most common triggers: sepsis, trauma, malignancy (especially acute promyelocytic leukemia), obstetric complications (amniotic fluid embolism, placental abruption). Treatment: address the underlying cause, supportive transfusion (platelets if <10K or bleeding, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <100, FFP for active bleeding with prolonged PT/PTT).

questions.stem: What is the most common skeletal dysplasia, and what type of bone formation is affected?

choices.text: Thanatophoric dysplasia — most common lethal dysplasia
choices.feedback: Thanatophoric dysplasia is also FGFR3 but is LETHAL (more severe mutation). Achondroplasia is the most common NON-LETHAL skeletal dysplasia.
OI affects collagen, not cartilage-to-bone conversion. OI presents with fractures, blue sclerae, and normal-length but fragile bones.
The opposite! In achondroplasia, intramembranous ossification is NORMAL (that's why the skull is large). Endochondral ossification at growth plates is defective.

Correct! Achondroplasia is the most common skeletal dysplasia (1 in 15,000-40,000). It's caused by a gain-of-function FGFR3 mutation (G380R) that INHIBITS chondrocyte proliferation at the growth plate → impaired endochondral ossification. Long bones (formed by endochondral) are short, while the skull vault (formed by intramembranous) is large. Autosomal dominant, but 80% are de novo mutations.

questions.stem: In achondroplasia, which growth plate zone is primarily affected by the FGFR3 mutation?

choices.feedback: The hypertrophic zone is involved but the primary defect is in the proliferative zone where FGFR3 inhibits cell division.

Correct! FGFR3 gain-of-function primarily inhibits the PROLIFERATIVE zone — where chondrocytes divide rapidly in organized columns (stacked coin appearance). Reduced proliferation → shorter columns → shorter bones. The hypertrophic zone (where chondrocytes enlarge and signal for vascularization/ossification) is also affected but proliferation inhibition is the primary mechanism.
The reserve zone stores cartilage progenitors and is not the primary target of FGFR3's inhibitory effect.
Ossification itself is normal in achondroplasia. The problem is upstream — not enough cartilage is being produced to replace.

questions.stem: What is the fundamental difference between OI and achondroplasia at the molecular level?

choices.text: OI is autosomal recessive; achondroplasia is X-linked
choices.feedback: OI is mostly autosomal DOMINANT (most types). Achondroplasia is also autosomal dominant. Neither is X-linked.

choices.text: Both are caused by FGFR3 mutations of different severity
choices.feedback: OI is caused by COL1A1/COL1A2 mutations (collagen genes), NOT FGFR3. They are completely different molecular pathways.

choices.text: OI = type I collagen defect (bone matrix); Achondroplasia = FGFR3 (growth plate signaling)
choices.feedback: Correct! OI: defective type I collagen (COL1A1/COL1A2 mutations) → bones form at normal length but are structurally fragile → fractures, blue sclerae (thin sclera shows choroid), hearing loss. Achondroplasia: FGFR3 gain-of-function → inhibited growth plate proliferation → bones are SHORT but structurally STRONG (normal collagen, don't fracture easily). Completely different mechanisms.

choices.text: Both affect endochondral ossification at different stages
choices.feedback: OI affects collagen throughout the body (including bone matrix), not specifically endochondral ossification. Achondroplasia specifically targets the growth plate.

questions.stem: Which signaling center controls anterior-posterior patterning (thumb vs pinky side) of the limb?

choices.feedback: Dorsal ectoderm secretes WNT7a which controls dorsal-ventral patterning (nail side vs palm side), not AP patterning.
The AER controls proximal-distal outgrowth (shoulder → hand direction) via FGF signaling, not AP patterning.

Correct! The ZPA is a cluster of mesenchymal cells in the POSTERIOR limb bud that secretes Sonic hedgehog (SHH). SHH creates a concentration gradient that specifies digit identity: highest concentration (posterior/ulnar) → digit 5 (pinky), lowest (anterior/radial) → digit 1 (thumb). Postaxial polydactyly = extra posterior digits from SHH pathway abnormalities. Preaxial polydactyly (extra thumb) = different mechanism.
The progress zone contains proliferating undifferentiated mesenchyme. It determines proximal-distal identity based on time spent in the zone, not AP patterning.

questions.explanation: Correct: Zone of polarizing activity (ZPA).

questions.stem: Why is thanatophoric dysplasia lethal while achondroplasia is compatible with a near-normal lifespan?

choices.text: Thanatophoric is autosomal recessive with complete loss of FGFR3
choices.feedback: Thanatophoric is also autosomal DOMINANT (de novo). It's a GAIN-of-function like achondroplasia, but more severe — not loss of function.
While brain abnormalities can occur (temporal lobe dysplasia), death is from pulmonary hypoplasia/respiratory failure, not CNS causes.

choices.text: Thanatophoric affects both endochondral and intramembranous ossification
choices.feedback: Both achondroplasia and thanatophoric primarily affect endochondral. The severity difference is in degree, not mechanism type.

choices.text: Thanatophoric has a more severe FGFR3 mutation causing extreme thoracic narrowing → pulmonary hypoplasia
choices.feedback: Correct! Thanatophoric dysplasia has a different, more activating FGFR3 mutation (K650E or R248C). The ribs are extremely short → severely narrow chest → pulmonary hypoplasia and respiratory failure. Infants typically die within hours to days from respiratory insufficiency. The name means 'death-bearing' in Greek. Features: telephone receiver femurs, cloverleaf skull (kleeblattschädel in type II), severe platyspondyly.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with SLE presents with proteinuria, hematuria, and rising anti-dsDNA titers. Kidney biopsy shows diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis with wire loop lesions. What is the lupus nephritis class?

choices.feedback: Class II shows only mesangial deposits without wire loop lesions or endocapillary proliferation.
Correct! Class IV is the most common and severe form. Wire loop lesions (subendothelial immune complex deposits) are pathognomonic. Treat with pulse steroids + mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide.
Class III affects <50% of glomeruli. Diffuse involvement with wire loops indicates Class IV.
Class V presents with nephrotic syndrome (heavy proteinuria) and subepithelial deposits, not wire loops.

questions.explanation: SLE is a systemic autoimmune disease with multiorgan involvement. Diagnosis: SLICC criteria (≥4 of 11 criteria or biopsy-proven lupus nephritis + ANA or anti-dsDNA). Key antibodies: ANA (sensitive, not specific), anti-dsDNA (correlates with disease activity and nephritis), anti-Smith (most specific), anti-Ro/La (neonatal lupus, congenital heart block). Treatment: hydroxychloroquine for all SLE patients (reduces flares and mortality), corticosteroids and immunosuppressants for organ-threatening disease.

questions.stem: Which autoantibody is MOST SPECIFIC for SLE?

choices.feedback: Anti-dsDNA is highly specific for SLE and correlates with nephritis/activity, but anti-Smith is MORE specific.
Anti-Ro is associated with neonatal lupus and Sjögren syndrome, not specific to SLE.
ANA is 95-99% sensitive but very non-specific — positive in many autoimmune diseases, infections, and even healthy individuals.
Correct! Anti-Smith is the most specific antibody for SLE (~99% specificity) though it has low sensitivity (25-30%). Anti-dsDNA is second most specific and more clinically useful for monitoring.

questions.explanation: SLE is a systemic autoimmune disease with multiorgan involvement. Diagnosis: SLICC criteria (≥4 of 11 criteria or biopsy-proven lupus nephritis + ANA or anti-dsDNA). Key antibodies: ANA (sensitive, not specific), anti-dsDNA (correlates with disease activity and nephritis), anti-Smith (most specific), anti-Ro/La (neonatal lupus, congenital heart block). Treatment: hydroxychloroquine for all SLE patients (reduces flares and mortality), corticosteroids and immunosuppressants for organ-threatening disease.

questions.stem: Which drug is recommended for ALL SLE patients regardless of disease severity?

choices.feedback: Methotrexate is used as a steroid-sparing agent for moderate disease, not universal therapy.
Mycophenolate is used for lupus nephritis induction/maintenance, not universal therapy.
Steroids are used for flares but not recommended long-term for all patients due to side effects.

Correct! HCQ is the cornerstone of SLE treatment. It reduces flares by 50%, decreases thrombotic risk, improves survival, and has a favorable safety profile. Only drug proven to improve SLE mortality. Requires annual ophthalmologic screening.

questions.explanation: SLE is a systemic autoimmune disease with multiorgan involvement. Diagnosis: SLICC criteria (≥4 of 11 criteria or biopsy-proven lupus nephritis + ANA or anti-dsDNA). Key antibodies: ANA (sensitive, not specific), anti-dsDNA (correlates with disease activity and nephritis), anti-Smith (most specific), anti-Ro/La (neonatal lupus, congenital heart block). Treatment: hydroxychloroquine for all SLE patients (reduces flares and mortality), corticosteroids and immunosuppressants for organ-threatening disease.

questions.stem: A patient develops arthralgias, pleuritis, and positive ANA with anti-histone antibodies after starting a new medication. Anti-dsDNA is negative. Which medication is MOST LIKELY responsible?

choices.feedback: ARBs are not associated with drug-induced lupus.
Calcium channel blockers do not cause drug-induced lupus.
ACE inhibitors are not associated with drug-induced lupus.

Correct! Hydralazine is a classic cause of drug-induced lupus. Anti-histone antibodies are positive in >95% of drug-induced lupus. Other culprits: procainamide (highest incidence), isoniazid, minocycline. Key distinction: NO nephritis or CNS involvement.

questions.explanation: Rheumatologic diagnosis requires integration of clinical findings, serologic markers, and imaging. Pattern recognition of joint involvement, extra-articular manifestations, and autoantibody profiles guides differential diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.

questions.stem: During an SLE flare, which complement pattern would you expect?

choices.feedback: This pattern suggests alternative pathway activation (e.g., C3 nephritic factor, MPGN type II), not SLE which activates the classical pathway.

Correct! SLE flares consume complement via the classical pathway (immune complex activation). Low C3 + Low C4 is characteristic. Rising dsDNA + falling complement = impending flare. This distinguishes SLE from other causes of hypocomplementemia.
Complement is consumed (not elevated) during active SLE flares due to immune complex activation.
Isolated low C4 with normal C3 suggests hereditary C4 deficiency or cryoglobulinemia, not typical SLE flare.

questions.explanation: SLE is a systemic autoimmune disease with multiorgan involvement. Diagnosis: SLICC criteria (≥4 of 11 criteria or biopsy-proven lupus nephritis + ANA or anti-dsDNA). Key antibodies: ANA (sensitive, not specific), anti-dsDNA (correlates with disease activity and nephritis), anti-Smith (most specific), anti-Ro/La (neonatal lupus, congenital heart block). Treatment: hydroxychloroquine for all SLE patients (reduces flares and mortality), corticosteroids and immunosuppressants for organ-threatening disease.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with SLE and anti-Ro antibodies is pregnant. What is the major fetal risk?

choices.feedback: Renal agenesis is not associated with maternal anti-Ro antibodies.
NTDs are associated with folate deficiency and certain medications (valproate, carbamazepine), not anti-Ro antibodies.
Cleft palate is associated with genetic factors and certain teratogens, not autoantibodies.

Correct! Anti-Ro (SSA) antibodies cross the placenta and can cause neonatal lupus, including congenital heart block (irreversible, may need pacemaker) and transient rash. Risk is ~2% per pregnancy with anti-Ro. Screen with serial fetal echocardiography starting at 16 weeks.

questions.explanation: SLE is a systemic autoimmune disease with multiorgan involvement. Diagnosis: SLICC criteria (≥4 of 11 criteria or biopsy-proven lupus nephritis + ANA or anti-dsDNA). Key antibodies: ANA (sensitive, not specific), anti-dsDNA (correlates with disease activity and nephritis), anti-Smith (most specific), anti-Ro/La (neonatal lupus, congenital heart block). Treatment: hydroxychloroquine for all SLE patients (reduces flares and mortality), corticosteroids and immunosuppressants for organ-threatening disease.

questions.stem: Libman-Sacks endocarditis in SLE is characterized by:

choices.text: Calcific deposits on the aortic valve
choices.feedback: This describes degenerative/calcific aortic stenosis, not lupus-related valvulopathy.

choices.text: Large oscillating vegetations with positive blood cultures
choices.feedback: This describes infective endocarditis, not Libman-Sacks.

choices.text: Vegetations on the atrial side of the mitral valve only
choices.feedback: Unlike rheumatic heart disease (which affects the atrial side), Libman-Sacks affects BOTH sides of valves.

choices.text: Sterile vegetations on both sides of valve leaflets
choices.feedback: Correct! Libman-Sacks endocarditis produces sterile (non-infectious) verrucous vegetations on BOTH sides of valve leaflets, most commonly the mitral valve. This distinguishes it from rheumatic (atrial side) and infective endocarditis. Can cause valvular regurgitation and embolic events.

questions.explanation: SLE is a systemic autoimmune disease with multiorgan involvement. Diagnosis: SLICC criteria (≥4 of 11 criteria or biopsy-proven lupus nephritis + ANA or anti-dsDNA). Key antibodies: ANA (sensitive, not specific), anti-dsDNA (correlates with disease activity and nephritis), anti-Smith (most specific), anti-Ro/La (neonatal lupus, congenital heart block). Treatment: hydroxychloroquine for all SLE patients (reduces flares and mortality), corticosteroids and immunosuppressants for organ-threatening disease.

questions.stem: Which lab finding helps distinguish SLE flare from infection in a patient with active lupus?

choices.feedback: Correct! In SLE, CRP is typically NORMAL during flares (unless serositis is present). Elevated CRP in an SLE patient suggests infection rather than flare. ESR rises in both scenarios. This CRP paradox is a classic boards pearl for distinguishing flare from infection.
ANA remains positive regardless of disease activity and does not distinguish flare from infection.
CBC abnormalities (cytopenias) can occur in both active SLE and sepsis, making it unreliable as a sole distinguishing factor.
ESR rises in both SLE flares AND infection — not a reliable distinguishing marker.

questions.explanation: SLE is a systemic autoimmune disease with multiorgan involvement. Diagnosis: SLICC criteria (≥4 of 11 criteria or biopsy-proven lupus nephritis + ANA or anti-dsDNA). Key antibodies: ANA (sensitive, not specific), anti-dsDNA (correlates with disease activity and nephritis), anti-Smith (most specific), anti-Ro/La (neonatal lupus, congenital heart block). Treatment: hydroxychloroquine for all SLE patients (reduces flares and mortality), corticosteroids and immunosuppressants for organ-threatening disease.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents with chronic low back pain that is worse in the morning, improves with exercise, and has lasted 6 months. HLA-B27 is positive. MRI shows bilateral sacroiliitis. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Classic presentation: young male, insidious inflammatory back pain (>3 months, worse at rest, better with exercise, morning stiffness), HLA-B27 positive, bilateral sacroiliitis on MRI. MRI is gold standard for early AS before X-ray changes develop.
RA does not typically involve the sacroiliac joints or spine (except C1-C2). RA affects peripheral joints symmetrically and is RF/CCP positive.
Disc herniation causes acute radicular pain, not insidious bilateral sacroiliitis.
Mechanical pain is worse with activity and better with rest — opposite of inflammatory back pain. This patient's pattern is classic inflammatory.

questions.explanation: Rheumatologic diagnosis requires integration of clinical findings, serologic markers, and imaging. Pattern recognition of joint involvement, extra-articular manifestations, and autoantibody profiles guides differential diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.

questions.stem: Which radiographic finding is characteristic of advanced ankylosing spondylitis?

choices.feedback: Periarticular erosions are characteristic of RA, not AS.
Chondrocalcinosis indicates CPPD/pseudogout, not AS.

Correct! Bamboo spine = syndesmophytes (bony bridges) connecting vertebral bodies → complete spinal fusion. This is pathognomonic for advanced AS. Also see 'shiny corners' (Romanus lesions) and squaring of vertebral bodies in earlier stages. The fused spine is fracture-prone (chalk stick fractures with minimal trauma).
Pencil-in-cup is characteristic of psoriatic arthritis (arthritis mutilans), not AS.

questions.explanation: Seronegative spondyloarthropathies: ankylosing spondylitis (sacroiliitis, bamboo spine, HLA-B27+), psoriatic arthritis (dactylitis, nail pitting, DIP involvement), reactive arthritis (post-infectious — GU or GI; conjunctivitis, urethritis, arthritis triad), enteropathic arthritis (IBD-associated). Treatment: NSAIDs first-line, TNF inhibitors for refractory disease, physical therapy for axial disease. HLA-B27 is associated but not diagnostic.

questions.stem: Reactive arthritis classically follows infection with which organisms?

choices.feedback: These organisms cause UTIs and sepsis but are not classic triggers for reactive arthritis.
Borrelia causes Lyme arthritis (different mechanism). Treponema causes syphilitic arthritis.
Staph/Strep cause septic arthritis and rheumatic fever, not reactive arthritis.

Correct! Reactive arthritis follows GI infections (Shigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter, Yersinia) or GU infections (Chlamydia trachomatis). Classic triad: arthritis + urethritis + conjunctivitis. Can't see, can't pee, can't climb a tree. Typically 1-4 weeks post-infection.

questions.explanation: Rheumatologic diagnosis requires integration of clinical findings, serologic markers, and imaging. Pattern recognition of joint involvement, extra-articular manifestations, and autoantibody profiles guides differential diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.

questions.stem: Which type of psoriatic arthritis pattern involves the DIP joints and is associated with nail changes?

choices.feedback: Arthritis mutilans is the most severe (and rarest) PsA pattern with severe bone resorption (pencil-in-cup, opera glass hand).
The spondylitis pattern involves axial skeleton (sacroiliitis, spondylitis) and may not prominently affect DIPs.
This pattern resembles RA and primarily involves MCPs and PIPs, not DIPs.

Correct! DIP-predominant PsA specifically targets distal interphalangeal joints and strongly correlates with nail psoriasis (pitting, onycholysis, oil drop spots). This is pathognomonic for PsA and distinguishes it from RA (which spares DIPs). About 5-10% of PsA patients.

questions.explanation: Rheumatologic diagnosis requires integration of clinical findings, serologic markers, and imaging. Pattern recognition of joint involvement, extra-articular manifestations, and autoantibody profiles guides differential diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.

questions.stem: Why are traditional DMARDs (methotrexate, sulfasalazine) ineffective for axial spondyloarthritis?

choices.text: The axial inflammation pathway is TNF/IL
choices.feedback: Correct! Axial SpA inflammation is driven primarily by TNF-α, IL-17, and IL-23 pathways at entheseal sites. Traditional DMARDs target different immune mechanisms effective in synovial inflammation (RA) but not enthesitis-driven axial disease. Only biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL-17 inhibitors) effectively treat axial symptoms.

choices.text: The blood-brain barrier prevents DMARDs from reaching the spine
choices.feedback: The spine is not protected by the blood-brain barrier. DMARDs reach spinal tissues but simply don't target the relevant inflammatory pathways.

choices.text: DMARDs only work in seropositive (RF+) diseases
choices.feedback: DMARDs work in seronegative conditions like peripheral PsA and reactive arthritis. The issue is specifically axial inflammation.

choices.text: They cause immunosuppression that worsens spinal infections
choices.feedback: Infection risk is a consideration but is not why DMARDs fail in axial SpA.

questions.explanation: Key detail: 17 driven, not responsive to conventional DMARDs.

questions.stem: What is the most common extra-articular manifestation of ankylosing spondylitis?

choices.feedback: Apical fibrosis is a rare late manifestation of AS, much less common than uveitis.
Aortic regurgitation occurs in AS but is less common (1-10%) than uveitis.
IgA nephropathy can occur secondarily in AS but is uncommon compared to uveitis.

Correct! Acute anterior uveitis is the most common extra-articular manifestation (25-40% of AS patients). Presents with acute unilateral eye pain, redness, photophobia, and blurred vision. Can precede or follow spinal symptoms. Treat with topical steroids + cycloplegics. Strongly associated with HLA-B27.

questions.explanation: Seronegative spondyloarthropathies: ankylosing spondylitis (sacroiliitis, bamboo spine, HLA-B27+), psoriatic arthritis (dactylitis, nail pitting, DIP involvement), reactive arthritis (post-infectious — GU or GI; conjunctivitis, urethritis, arthritis triad), enteropathic arthritis (IBD-associated). Treatment: NSAIDs first-line, TNF inhibitors for refractory disease, physical therapy for axial disease. HLA-B27 is associated but not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A positive Schober test in suspected AS indicates:

choices.feedback: A positive Schober test indicates DECREASED (not increased) lumbar flexion.
The Schober test does not assess nerve root function. Straight leg raise assesses nerve root compression.
Schober test measures lumbar flexion, not SI joint stability. SI joint provocative tests are different (FABER, compression, distraction).

Correct! Schober test: mark 10 cm above L5. Patient bends forward maximally. Normal = ≥5 cm expansion. Positive (abnormal) = <5 cm expansion, indicating restricted lumbar flexion from sacroiliitis or spinal ankylosis. Simple bedside test for AS screening.

questions.explanation: Rheumatologic diagnosis requires integration of clinical findings, serologic markers, and imaging. Pattern recognition of joint involvement, extra-articular manifestations, and autoantibody profiles guides differential diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.

questions.stem: Dactylitis ('sausage digit') is characteristic of which group of arthropathies?

choices.feedback: Correct! Dactylitis (diffuse swelling of an entire digit — sausage digit) is highly characteristic of SpA, particularly psoriatic arthritis and reactive arthritis. It results from inflammation of the flexor tendon sheath + synovitis + soft tissue edema. It distinguishes SpA from RA, which causes fusiform swelling of individual joints.
Gout can cause podagra and tophi but classic sausage digit dactylitis is more specific to SpA.
RA causes symmetric joint swelling but not the diffuse digit swelling seen in dactylitis.
OA causes Heberden (DIP) and Bouchard (PIP) nodes — bony enlargement, not diffuse digit swelling.

questions.explanation: Rheumatologic diagnosis requires integration of clinical findings, serologic markers, and imaging. Pattern recognition of joint involvement, extra-articular manifestations, and autoantibody profiles guides differential diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.

questions.stem: Status epilepticus is defined as continuous seizure activity lasting:

choices.feedback: This was the old definition. Current operational definition is 5 minutes to encourage earlier intervention.
Most seizures self-terminate by 1-2 minutes. SE threshold is longer.
Most normal seizures last 30-120 seconds.

Correct! ≥5 minutes of continuous seizure activity OR ≥2 seizures without returning to baseline between them. The old definition was 30 minutes, but the 5-minute operational definition allows earlier treatment since seizures rarely self-terminate after 5 minutes and prolonged activity causes neuronal damage.

questions.explanation: Status epilepticus is a neurologic emergency defined as continuous seizure activity >5 minutes or recurrent seizures without return to baseline. First-line: IV benzodiazepines (lorazepamor midazolam). Second-line: IV fosphenytoin, valproate, or levetiracetam. Third-line (refractory SE): IV anesthetics (propofol, midazolam infusion, or pentobarbital) with continuous EEG monitoring. Seizure classification (ILAE 2017): focal (aware or impaired awareness), generalized (tonic-clonic, absence, myoclonic), or unknown onset.

questions.stem: First-line treatment for status epilepticus?

choices.feedback: Correct! Benzodiazepines are first-line. IV lorazepam(preferred IV — longer brain duration than diazepam) or IM midazolam(if no IV access — RAMPART trial showed non-inferior outcomes and faster administration). Can repeat lorazepam × 1 if seizure continues.
Phenytoin/fosphenytoin is second-line, given after benzodiazepines fail.
Oral medications are too slow to absorb during active SE.
Valproate is a second-line option after benzodiazepines.

questions.explanation: Status epilepticus is a neurologic emergency defined as continuous seizure activity >5 minutes or recurrent seizures without return to baseline. First-line: IV benzodiazepines (lorazepamor midazolam). Second-line: IV fosphenytoin, valproate, or levetiracetam. Third-line (refractory SE): IV anesthetics (propofol, midazolam infusion, or pentobarbital) with continuous EEG monitoring. Seizure classification (ILAE 2017): focal (aware or impaired awareness), generalized (tonic-clonic, absence, myoclonic), or unknown onset.

questions.stem: Most common cause of SE in adults?

choices.feedback: Alcohol withdrawal (not intoxication) causes seizures. Not the most common overall.
Thyroid disorders rarely cause SE.
Tumors cause seizures but are not the #1 cause of SE.

Correct! Non-compliance with or subtherapeutic levels of anti-epileptic drugs is the most common cause of SE in known epilepsy patients. Always check AED levels. Other common causes: CNS infection, stroke, metabolic derangements (hypoglycemia, hyponatremia), alcohol withdrawal, drug toxicity.

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (AED non-compliance/subtherapeutic levels) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: RAMPART trial demonstrated that IM midazolam is:

choices.feedback: RAMPART included adults. IM midazolam is effective across age groups.

choices.text: Non-inferior to IV lorazepam and faster to administer
choices.feedback: Correct! RAMPART (2012): IM midazolam was non-inferior (and actually showed a numerical advantage) compared to IV lorazepam for pre-hospital SE. Key advantage: no IV access needed → faster administration in the field. Changed practice for EMS protocols.
IM midazolam works for generalized convulsive SE, the most dangerous type.
RAMPART showed IM midazolam was at least as effective.

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Non-inferior to IV lorazepam and faster to administer) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: Fosphenytoin is preferred over phenytoin because:

choices.feedback: Fosphenytoin is given IV or IM.

choices.text: It does not require cardiac monitoring
choices.feedback: Both require cardiac monitoring during infusion.

choices.text: It is more effective at stopping seizures
choices.feedback: Efficacy is equivalent. The advantage is in the safety profile.

choices.text: It causes less hypotension and avoids Purple Glove Syndrome
choices.feedback: Correct! Fosphenytoin (prodrug of phenytoin) advantages: can be infused faster, can be given IM, does not cause Purple Glove Syndrome (tissue necrosis from phenytoin extravasation — alkaline pH damages tissue). Both can cause hypotension and cardiac arrhythmias — monitor on telemetry during infusion.

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (It causes less hypotension and avoids Purple Glove Syndrome) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: Non-convulsive SE should be suspected in:

choices.feedback: NCSE by definition involves altered consciousness.

choices.text: Patients with unexplained altered mental status who don't wake up post-ictally
choices.feedback: Correct! NCSE: altered consciousness WITHOUT motor manifestations. Suspect when: post-ictal patient doesn't recover, unexplained AMS in ICU, subtle signs (eye deviation, nystagmus, eyelid fluttering). Requires continuous EEG to diagnose. Commonly missed — has significant morbidity if untreated.
Syncope has rapid recovery and different EEG findings from NCSE.

choices.text: Patients with headache and normal EEG
choices.feedback: Normal EEG rules out NCSE. Headache alone is not suggestive.

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Patients with unexplained altered mental status who don't wake up post-ictally) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: ESETT trial showed that for second-line SE treatment:

choices.feedback: All three work in about half of benzodiazepine-refractory cases.

choices.text: Fosphenytoin, levetiracetam, and valproate are equally effective (~47%)
choices.feedback: Correct! ESETT (2019): all three second-line agents were equally effective at ~47% seizure cessation rate. This validated levetiracetam and valproate as equivalent alternatives to the traditional fosphenytoin. Choice based on: drug interactions, hepatic/renal function, patient factors. Levetiracetam gaining favor due to fewer interactions.

choices.text: Phenytoin is clearly superior to all alternatives
choices.feedback: ESETT showed no significant difference among the three agents.
All three agents were about 47% effective — meaning >50% of patients needed third-line treatment.

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Fosphenytoin, levetiracetam, and valproate are equally effective (~47%)) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: Refractory SE requires:

choices.feedback: Refractory SE will NOT self-terminate and causes progressive neuronal damage.
Surgery is for refractory epilepsy, not acute SE management.
Oral medications are insufficient. Refractory SE needs IV anesthetics.

choices.text: Continuous IV anesthetics (midazolam, propofol, or pentobarbital) with intubation + EEG monitoring
choices.feedback: Correct! Refractory SE (fails 2 appropriately dosed medications) requires: intubation for airway protection, continuous IV anesthetic infusion, continuous EEG monitoring targeting burst suppression. Options: midazolam (fewest hemodynamic effects), propofol (fast onset — watch for propofol infusion syndrome with prolonged use), pentobarbital (most potent but most hemodynamic compromise).

questions.explanation: Best answer: Continuous IV anesthetics (midazolam, propofol, or pentobarbital) with intubation + EEG monitoring.

questions.stem: Most common type of stroke?

choices.feedback: SAH accounts for ~5%.
ICH accounts for ~10% of strokes.
Correct! Ischemic strokes account for ~85% of all strokes. Caused by thrombotic or embolic arterial occlusion. MCA territory is most commonly affected.
Rare cause of stroke.

questions.explanation: Ischemic stroke accounts for approximately 87% of all strokes and is caused by thrombotic or embolic occlusion of a cerebral artery. Hemorrhagic stroke (intracerebral hemorrhage and subarachnoid hemorrhage) accounts for the remaining 13%. The middle cerebral artery (MCA) is the most commonly affected vessel. Ischemic stroke subtypes are classified by the TOAST system: large-artery atherosclerosis, cardioembolism (often from atrial fibrillation), small-vessel occlusion (lacunar), stroke of other determined etiology, and stroke of undetermined etiology. Venous sinus thrombosis is a rare cause of stroke, accounting for less than 1% of cases.
