The Valsalva maneuver decreases preload, reducing LV volume and worsening the dynamic LVOT obstruction — making the murmur louder. Conversely, squatting increases preload and afterload, reducing obstruction and softening the murmur. HCM is the most common cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes. Family history is critical because HCM follows autosomal dominant inheritance (sarcomere protein gene mutations, most commonly beta-myosin heavy chain and myosin-binding protein C).

questions.stem: In vasovagal syncope, first-line management includes:

choices.feedback: ICD is for ventricular arrhythmias/sudden death risk, not vasovagal syncope.
Aspirin has no role in vasovagal syncope management.

choices.text: Education, trigger avoidance, hydration, counterpressure maneuvers
choices.feedback: Correct! First-line is conservative: educate about triggers and prodrome, increased fluid + salt intake, counterpressure maneuvers (leg crossing, isometric hand grip) when prodrome occurs. These reduce recurrence significantly. Midodrine (alpha-agonist) for refractory cases. Pacemaker only for documented cardioinhibitory type.
Pacemaker is reserved for cardioinhibitory vasovagal with documented prolonged asystole, not first-line.

questions.explanation: First-line management of vasovagal syncope is conservative: patient education about triggers and prodromal recognition, trigger avoidance (prolonged standing, heat, dehydration), adequate hydration and salt intake, and physical counterpressure maneuvers (leg crossing with isometric contraction, hand grip, arm tensing) performed during prodromal symptoms. These maneuvers increase venous return and can abort an impending episode. Pharmacotherapy (midodrine, fludrocortisone) is reserved for refractory cases. Pacemaker implantation is considered only for recurrent vasovagal syncope with documented prolonged asystole (cardioinhibitory type).
questions.verifiedExplanation: First-line management of vasovagal syncope is conservative: patient education about triggers and prodromal recognition, trigger avoidance (prolonged standing, heat, dehydration), adequate hydration and salt intake, and physical counterpressure maneuvers (leg crossing with isometric contraction, hand grip, arm tensing) performed during prodromal symptoms. These maneuvers increase venous return and can abort an impending episode. Pharmacotherapy (midodrine, fludrocortisone) is reserved for refractory cases. Pacemaker implantation is considered only for recurrent vasovagal syncope with documented prolonged asystole (cardioinhibitory type).

questions.stem: What is the most critical immediate step?

choices.feedback: CT is not first-line. A simple OG tube + chest/abdomen X-ray will confirm the diagnosis.
Correct! An OG tube that coils in the upper pouch (stops at 10-12cm) is the classic diagnostic finding. This confirms esophageal atresia.
Never reattempt feeding when TEF is suspected — aspiration risk is extreme.
The presentation is mechanical, not infectious. The triad of polyhydramnios + drooling + cyanosis with feeding points to EA/TEF.

questions.stem: Gas in the stomach with esophageal atresia indicates which TEF type?

choices.text: Type A — Pure EA, no fistula
choices.feedback: Type A has NO gas in the stomach or intestines because there's no connection to the airway.

choices.text: Type C — EA with distal TEF
choices.feedback: Correct! Type C is the most common (85%). The distal esophagus connects to the trachea, allowing air into the stomach. Mnemonic: 'C is the most Common.'
H-type has no atresia, so there would be no OG tube coiling. These patients present later with recurrent pneumonia.

choices.text: Type D — EA with proximal + distal TEF
choices.feedback: Type D is very rare (~1%). While it could show gas, Type C is far more likely.

questions.stem: Which VACTERL component does esophageal atresia represent?

choices.feedback: A is for anorectal malformations (hindgut). EA is foregut.
V is for vertebral anomalies. EA falls under the 'TE' component.
The VSD found on echo is the C component. EA itself is the TE component.
Correct! VACTERL: Vertebral, Anorectal, Cardiac, Tracheo-Esophageal, Renal, Limb. Three or more features = VACTERL association.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of continuous Replogle suction?

choices.feedback: The tube doesn't reach the stomach. All medications and nutrition must be IV.
Gap measurement is done radiographically, not by suction. Replogle suction is therapeutic, not diagnostic.
Correct! Continuous low-pressure suction removes pooled saliva from the upper esophageal pouch, preventing overflow aspiration into the trachea.
The Replogle tube is in the UPPER pouch (proximal esophagus), not the stomach. It can't reach the stomach in EA.

questions.stem: What is the most common early postoperative complication?

choices.feedback: RLN injury can occur but is less common than leak. It causes hoarseness and feeding difficulties.
Correct! Anastomotic leak occurs in 10-20% of repairs, usually within the first week. An esophagram at day 5-7 checks for leak before starting oral feeds.
Stricture is the most common LATE complication (30-40%), not early. Presents with dysphagia weeks to months later.
Recurrent fistula is a LATE complication (weeks to months), not early. It occurs in ~5-10%.

questions.stem: What two complications should be evaluated?

choices.feedback: Achalasia is extremely rare in infants. Laryngomalacia would have presented earlier with stridor.

Correct! Dysphagia with solids → stricture (confirmed by esophagram, treated with balloon dilation). Cyanotic spells during feeds → tracheomalacia (confirmed by bronchoscopy), where the trachea collapses during swallowing/coughing.
Recurrent TEF causes recurrent pneumonia, not simple dysphagia. EoE is possible in EA patients but less likely at 6 months.
Pyloric stenosis presents at 3-6 weeks with projectile vomiting, not at 6 months with dysphagia.

questions.stem: Tension PTX is a:

choices.feedback: Correct! Absent breath sounds + tracheal deviation + JVD + hypotension = immediate needle decompression. Do NOT delay for CXR. Every second of delay worsens obstructive shock. CXR can confirm SIMPLE PTX but tension requires immediate intervention.
Classic findings make it a positive clinical diagnosis, not exclusion.

choices.text: Radiographic diagnosis — always get CXR first
choices.feedback: Tension PTX is clinical — waiting for CXR risks cardiac arrest.
CT is unnecessary and dangerous to obtain during hemodynamic compromise.

questions.explanation: Key detail: treat immediately without imaging.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis — treatment must not be delayed for radiographic confirmation. The diagnosis is based on clinical findings: hypotension, tachycardia, absent breath sounds on the affected side, tracheal deviation away from the affected side, jugular venous distension, and hemodynamic instability. In a decompensating patient with these signs, immediate needle decompression followed by tube thoracostomy is life-saving. Obtaining a chest X-ray before treatment can lead to fatal delays.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: clinical diagnosis requiring immediate treatment without imaging confirmation

questions.stem: Needle decompression site for tension PTX?

choices.feedback: Subxiphoid approach is for pericardiocentesis, not PTX.
That's the chest tube insertion site, not needle decompression.

Correct! 14-16G needle at 2nd ICS MCL on affected side. Insert OVER the superior border of the 3rd rib (avoids intercostal NVB along inferior border of 2nd rib). Rush of air confirms tension PTX. Always follow with definitive chest tube at 4th-5th ICS MAL.
Too posterior for needle decompression.

questions.explanation: Pulmonary diagnosis relies on systematic interpretation of PFTs, imaging, and arterial blood gases. Evidence-based management follows society guidelines (GINA for asthma, GOLD for COPD, ATS/IDSA for pneumonia).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Needle decompression for tension pneumothorax is performed at the 2nd intercostal space (ICS) at the midclavicular line on the affected side. A large-bore needle (14-16 gauge) is inserted just above the third rib to avoid the neurovascular bundle running along the inferior border of each rib. An alternative site is the 4th or 5th ICS at the anterior axillary line, which may have a thinner chest wall in some patients. Needle decompression is a temporizing measure — definitive treatment requires tube thoracostomy (chest tube).

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space midclavicular line

questions.stem: Primary spontaneous PTX typical patient?

choices.feedback: That's iatrogenic (traumatic) PTX.
Neonatal PTX exists but primary spontaneous PTX refers to adolescents/young adults.

choices.text: Tall, thin young male, often a smoker
choices.feedback: Correct! Primary spontaneous PTX: rupture of subpleural apical blebs. Tall, thin males (15-35 years). Smoking increases risk 20x. Presents with sudden pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. Recurrence ~30% within 1 year (higher with continued smoking).
That's secondary spontaneous PTX from underlying lung disease.

questions.explanation: Pulmonary diagnosis relies on systematic interpretation of PFTs, imaging, and arterial blood gases. Evidence-based management follows society guidelines (GINA for asthma, GOLD for COPD, ATS/IDSA for pneumonia).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax typically occurs in tall, thin young males (often smokers). The classic patient is a male between 15-30 years old with a tall, slender habitus. It results from rupture of apical subpleural blebs that form preferentially in tall individuals due to greater negative intrapleural pressure at the lung apex. Smoking increases the risk approximately 20-fold. There is no underlying lung disease by definition. Recurrence rate is approximately 30% after the first episode.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: young tall thin males, often smokers, with apical bleb rupture

questions.stem: Tracheal deviation in tension PTX is AWAY from the affected side because:

choices.feedback: JVD is from impaired venous return, not the cause of tracheal deviation.

choices.text: The collapsed lung pulls the trachea toward it
choices.feedback: In simple atelectasis, the trachea deviates TOWARD the collapsed side. Tension is opposite.

choices.text: The heart shifts to decompress the affected lung
choices.feedback: The mediastinal shift is from pressure, not a compensatory mechanism.

choices.text: Increasing pressure pushes the mediastinum to the contralateral side
choices.feedback: Correct! Tension = progressive air accumulation under pressure → pushes mediastinum (including trachea) AWAY from the affected side. This distinguishes tension from simple PTX (midline trachea) and atelectasis (trachea deviates TOWARD the lesion due to volume loss).

questions.explanation: Pulmonary diagnosis relies on systematic interpretation of PFTs, imaging, and arterial blood gases. Evidence-based management follows society guidelines (GINA for asthma, GOLD for COPD, ATS/IDSA for pneumonia).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Tracheal deviation in tension pneumothorax occurs away from the affected side because progressive air accumulation in the pleural space generates increasing positive pressure that pushes the mediastinum (including the trachea) to the contralateral side. This mediastinal shift also compresses the contralateral lung and kinks the great vessels (SVC/IVC), impairing venous return and causing obstructive shock. Tracheal deviation is typically a late finding and indicates severe hemodynamic compromise.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: increasing pressure pushes the mediastinum to the contralateral side

questions.stem: Supplemental O2 accelerates PTX reabsorption by approximately:

choices.feedback: Overestimates the acceleration.
The acceleration factor is higher.

Correct! Supplemental O2 replaces nitrogen in the pleural space with oxygen, which is absorbed much more rapidly. Increases reabsorption rate ~4x (from ~1.25%/day to ~5%/day). Useful for small, stable pneumothoraces managed with observation.

choices.text: It doesn't — O2 has no effect on reabsorption
choices.feedback: O2 significantly accelerates reabsorption.

questions.explanation: Pulmonary diagnosis relies on systematic interpretation of PFTs, imaging, and arterial blood gases. Evidence-based management follows society guidelines (GINA for asthma, GOLD for COPD, ATS/IDSA for pneumonia).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Supplemental high-flow oxygen accelerates pneumothorax reabsorption by approximately 4 times the baseline rate. Under normal conditions, pneumothorax reabsorbs at about 1.25-2.2% of the hemithorax volume per day. High-flow O2 works by reducing the partial pressure of nitrogen in the blood, creating a concentration gradient that facilitates absorption of nitrogen from the pleural space. This nitrogen washout effect is the primary mechanism. Small primary spontaneous pneumothoraces can be managed with supplemental O2 and observation alone.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: supplemental oxygen increases reabsorption rate approximately fourfold

questions.stem: Iatrogenic PTX most commonly results from:

choices.feedback: Intubation itself doesn't cause PTX (though positive pressure ventilation can).

choices.text: Central venous catheter placement (subclavian/IJ) or thoracentesis
choices.feedback: Correct! Subclavian vein catheterization is the most common cause of iatrogenic PTX (needle can puncture lung apex). Thoracentesis, pleural biopsy, and mechanical ventilation (barotrauma) are other common causes. Always obtain post-procedure CXR.
LP does not involve the thoracic cavity.
Bronchoscopy rarely causes PTX.

questions.explanation: Pulmonary diagnosis relies on systematic interpretation of PFTs, imaging, and arterial blood gases. Evidence-based management follows society guidelines (GINA for asthma, GOLD for COPD, ATS/IDSA for pneumonia).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Iatrogenic pneumothorax most commonly results from central venous catheter placement (particularly subclavian and internal jugular approaches) and thoracentesis. Other causes include mechanical ventilation (barotrauma), transthoracic needle biopsy, bronchoscopy, and nerve blocks. Subclavian vein catheterization carries the highest risk of pneumothorax among central line sites. Post-procedure chest X-ray is standard practice after subclavian or IJ line placement. Ultrasound guidance has significantly reduced the rate of iatrogenic pneumothorax during central line placement.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: central venous catheter placement and thoracentesis as common iatrogenic causes

questions.stem: Recurrent pneumothorax prevention?

choices.text: VATS with pleurodesis or bleb resection
choices.feedback: Correct! Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS): resect blebs + mechanical/chemical pleurodesis (creates adhesions between visceral and parietal pleura preventing recurrence). Indicated after 2nd ipsilateral PTX, bilateral PTX, or persistent air leak >5-7 days. Reduces recurrence to <5%.
Chest tubes are temporary drainage devices.
Spirometry monitors lung function but doesn't prevent PTX.
Antibiotics don't prevent PTX recurrence.

questions.explanation: Pulmonary diagnosis relies on systematic interpretation of PFTs, imaging, and arterial blood gases. Evidence-based management follows society guidelines (GINA for asthma, GOLD for COPD, ATS/IDSA for pneumonia).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Recurrent pneumothorax prevention involves VATS (video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery) with either mechanical pleurodesis or chemical pleurodesis, combined with bleb resection/stapling if blebs are identified. Pleurodesis creates adhesions between the visceral and parietal pleura, obliterating the pleural space and preventing re-accumulation of air. Recurrence risk after a first primary spontaneous pneumothorax is approximately 30%, rising to over 50% after a second episode. VATS is generally recommended after the second ipsilateral episode or first episode with risk factors (bilateral, tension, hemothorax, or high-risk occupation).

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: VATS with pleurodesis for recurrence prevention

questions.stem: Tension PTX causes obstructive shock by:

choices.feedback: While vagal responses can occur, the primary mechanism is mechanical obstruction.
Hemorrhage is not the primary mechanism in tension PTX.

choices.text: Compressing the vena cava/heart → impaired venous return → decreased cardiac output
choices.feedback: Correct! Progressive intrapleural pressure → mediastinal shift → compresses SVC/IVC and right heart → decreased venous return → decreased preload → decreased cardiac output → obstructive shock. Mechanism is similar to cardiac tamponade (both = impaired cardiac filling). Treatment: relieve the pressure.
Tension PTX typically affects one hemithorax.

questions.explanation: Pulmonary diagnosis relies on systematic interpretation of PFTs, imaging, and arterial blood gases. Evidence-based management follows society guidelines (GINA for asthma, GOLD for COPD, ATS/IDSA for pneumonia).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Tension pneumothorax causes obstructive shock by compressing the vena cava and heart, which impairs venous return and decreases cardiac output. As air accumulates under pressure in the pleural space, the mediastinum shifts, kinking the SVC/IVC and compressing the heart chambers. This reduces preload to the right heart, causing a precipitous drop in cardiac output. The mechanism is analogous to cardiac tamponade (both cause obstructive shock via impaired venous return) but the treatment is different — needle decompression for tension pneumothorax versus pericardiocentesis for tamponade.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: compression of vena cava and heart causing impaired venous return and decreased cardiac output

questions.stem: Methanol toxicity primarily damages which organ?

choices.feedback: Cardiac toxicity is not a primary feature of methanol poisoning.

Correct! Formic acid (methanol metabolite) is selectively toxic to retinal ganglion cells and optic nerve. Symptoms: blurred vision, 'snowstorm' visual field, central scotoma, blindness. Optic disc hyperemia on fundoscopy. Visual complaints in a patient with HAGMA = methanol until proven otherwise.
Hepatotoxicity is more characteristic of acetaminophen.
Kidney damage is characteristic of ethylene glycol (calcium oxalate crystals).

questions.explanation: Methanol toxicity primarily damages the eyes through its toxic metabolite formic acid (formate). Formic acid inhibits mitochondrial cytochrome oxidase in the retina and optic nerve, causing retinal edema, optic disc hyperemia, and ultimately optic nerve atrophy and blindness. Visual symptoms include blurred vision, central scotomata, decreased visual acuity, and 'snowfield vision.' The putamen/basal ganglia are also selectively vulnerable, potentially causing parkinsonian features. There is a latent period of 12-24 hours between ingestion and symptom onset (while alcohol dehydrogenase converts methanol to formaldehyde, then formaldehyde dehydrogenase converts it to formic acid).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Methanol toxicity primarily damages the eyes through its toxic metabolite formic acid (formate). Formic acid inhibits mitochondrial cytochrome oxidase in the retina and optic nerve, causing retinal edema, optic disc hyperemia, and ultimately optic nerve atrophy and blindness. Visual symptoms include blurred vision, central scotomata, decreased visual acuity, and 'snowfield vision.' The putamen/basal ganglia are also selectively vulnerable, potentially causing parkinsonian features. There is a latent period of 12-24 hours between ingestion and symptom onset (while alcohol dehydrogenase converts methanol to formaldehyde, then formaldehyde dehydrogenase converts it to formic acid).

questions.stem: Calcium oxalate crystals in urine suggest poisoning with:

choices.feedback: Isopropyl produces acetone, not oxalate.

Correct! Ethylene glycol → glycolic acid → oxalic acid → combines with calcium → calcium oxalate crystals deposited in renal tubules (AKI) and found in urine (envelope-shaped/monohydrate or needle-shaped/dihydrate). Also: Wood lamp fluorescence of urine (antifreeze contains fluorescein).
Methanol produces formic acid, not oxalate.
Ethanol doesn't produce oxalate crystals.

questions.explanation: Calcium oxalate crystals (envelope-shaped under polarized light) in the urine are pathognomonic for ethylene glycol poisoning. Ethylene glycol is metabolized by alcohol dehydrogenase to glycoaldehyde → glycolic acid → glyoxylic acid → oxalic acid. Oxalic acid combines with calcium to form calcium oxalate crystals that deposit in renal tubules, causing acute kidney injury. Other EG poisoning features include initial CNS depression (appearing 'drunk'), followed by cardiopulmonary compromise (12-24 hours), then renal failure (24-72 hours). The initial metabolite glycolic acid is primarily responsible for the anion gap acidosis.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Calcium oxalate crystals (envelope-shaped under polarized light) in the urine are pathognomonic for ethylene glycol poisoning. Ethylene glycol is metabolized by alcohol dehydrogenase to glycoaldehyde → glycolic acid → glyoxylic acid → oxalic acid. Oxalic acid combines with calcium to form calcium oxalate crystals that deposit in renal tubules, causing acute kidney injury. Other EG poisoning features include initial CNS depression (appearing 'drunk'), followed by cardiopulmonary compromise (12-24 hours), then renal failure (24-72 hours). The initial metabolite glycolic acid is primarily responsible for the anion gap acidosis.

questions.stem: First-line antidote for methanol/ethylene glycol?

choices.feedback: NAC is for acetaminophen, not toxic alcohols.
Alcohols are rapidly absorbed; charcoal is ineffective.
Ethanol works but is harder to dose and has more side effects. It's the alternative, not first-line.

Correct! Fomepizole inhibits alcohol dehydrogenase → blocks formation of toxic metabolites. Give early (before metabolites accumulate). Loading weight-based dosing IV, then weight-based dosing q12h. Preferred over ethanol: predictable pharmacokinetics, fewer side effects, no need for level monitoring.

questions.explanation: Fomepizole (4-methylpyrazole) is the first-line antidote for methanol and ethylene glycol poisoning. It competitively inhibits alcohol dehydrogenase, blocking the conversion of the parent alcohol to its toxic metabolites (formic acid from methanol; glycolic/oxalic acid from ethylene glycol). Fomepizole is preferred over ethanol because it has predictable pharmacokinetics, does not cause CNS depression, does not require ICU-level monitoring for titration, and has fewer side effects. IV ethanol is an acceptable alternative when fomepizole is unavailable. Fomepizole dosing must be adjusted during hemodialysis as it is dialyzable.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Fomepizole (4-methylpyrazole) is the first-line antidote for methanol and ethylene glycol poisoning. It competitively inhibits alcohol dehydrogenase, blocking the conversion of the parent alcohol to its toxic metabolites (formic acid from methanol; glycolic/oxalic acid from ethylene glycol). Fomepizole is preferred over ethanol because it has predictable pharmacokinetics, does not cause CNS depression, does not require ICU-level monitoring for titration, and has fewer side effects. IV ethanol is an acceptable alternative when fomepizole is unavailable. Fomepizole dosing must be adjusted during hemodialysis as it is dialyzable.

questions.stem: Isopropyl alcohol is distinguished from methanol/EG by:

choices.feedback: Isopropyl is generally less dangerous than methanol/EG.

choices.text: Osmolar gap WITHOUT anion gap acidosis
choices.feedback: Correct! Isopropyl alcohol → acetone (a ketone, not an acid). Creates osmolar gap and positive ketones but NO metabolic acidosis. Methanol/EG both cause HAGMA. Ketones without acidosis = isopropyl. Treatment is supportive (no fomepizole needed).
Oxalate crystals are from ethylene glycol.
Blindness is from methanol (formic acid retinal toxicity).

questions.explanation: Isopropyl alcohol (isopropanol/rubbing alcohol) is distinguished from methanol and ethylene glycol by producing an osmolar gap WITHOUT an anion gap metabolic acidosis. Isopropanol is metabolized to acetone (a ketone, not an acid), so it causes ketonemia and ketonuria without acidosis. The urine ketone dipstick will be positive (nitroprusside reacts with acetone), and the patient's breath may smell fruity. In contrast, methanol and ethylene glycol produce severe anion gap metabolic acidosis through their toxic acid metabolites. Isopropanol is the least toxic of the three common toxic alcohols and rarely requires hemodialysis.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Isopropyl alcohol (isopropanol/rubbing alcohol) is distinguished from methanol and ethylene glycol by producing an osmolar gap WITHOUT an anion gap metabolic acidosis. Isopropanol is metabolized to acetone (a ketone, not an acid), so it causes ketonemia and ketonuria without acidosis. The urine ketone dipstick will be positive (nitroprusside reacts with acetone), and the patient's breath may smell fruity. In contrast, methanol and ethylene glycol produce severe anion gap metabolic acidosis through their toxic acid metabolites. Isopropanol is the least toxic of the three common toxic alcohols and rarely requires hemodialysis.

questions.stem: Indications for hemodialysis in toxic alcohol ingestion include:

choices.feedback: HD is indicated based on severity criteria regardless of fomepizole availability.
Detectable levels alone don't mandate dialysis.

choices.text: Severe acidosis (pH <7.25), renal failure, visual changes, level >50 mg/dL
choices.feedback: Correct! HD removes both parent alcohol and toxic metabolites. Indicated for: pH <7.25 (severe acidosis), end-organ damage (visual changes in methanol, AKI in EG), alcohol level >50 mg/dL, deterioration despite fomepizole. Fomepizole alone may suffice if caught early with no significant acidosis.
Osmolar gap alone without acidosis or end-organ damage doesn't mandate HD.

questions.explanation: Indications for hemodialysis in toxic alcohol poisoning include: severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.25), renal failure, visual disturbance in methanol poisoning, significant end-organ damage, serum methanol or ethylene glycol level >50 mg/dL, and deterioration despite appropriate antidote therapy. Hemodialysis removes both the parent alcohol and its toxic metabolites, corrects the acidosis, and corrects electrolyte abnormalities. It is the definitive treatment for severe poisoning. During dialysis, fomepizole dosing must be increased (it is dialyzable) or ethanol infusion rate increased to maintain therapeutic levels.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Indications for hemodialysis in toxic alcohol poisoning include: severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.25), renal failure, visual disturbance in methanol poisoning, significant end-organ damage, serum methanol or ethylene glycol level >50 mg/dL, and deterioration despite appropriate antidote therapy. Hemodialysis removes both the parent alcohol and its toxic metabolites, corrects the acidosis, and corrects electrolyte abnormalities. It is the definitive treatment for severe poisoning. During dialysis, fomepizole dosing must be increased (it is dialyzable) or ethanol infusion rate increased to maintain therapeutic levels.

questions.stem: Early toxic alcohol ingestion shows high osmolar gap. As metabolism continues:

choices.text: Osmolar gap increases while anion gap decreases
choices.feedback: It's the opposite — osmolar gap decreases as the parent is metabolized.
The gaps evolve as parent compound is metabolized.

choices.text: Osmolar gap decreases while anion gap increases
choices.feedback: Correct! Early: parent compound (osmotically active) → high osmolar gap, minimal acidosis. As ADH metabolizes parent → toxic acid metabolites: osmolar gap falls (parent cleared) while anion gap rises (acid accumulates). Late presentation may have normal osmolar gap but severe HAGMA — don't be falsely reassured by normal osmolar gap.
They move in opposite directions.

questions.explanation: The evolution of toxic alcohol poisoning shows a characteristic reciprocal relationship between the osmolar gap and anion gap. Early after ingestion, the parent alcohol (methanol or ethylene glycol) is present in high concentrations, causing a high osmolar gap. As alcohol dehydrogenase converts the parent alcohol to its toxic acid metabolites, the osmolar gap decreases while the anion gap increases. Late presentations may show a normal osmolar gap with a severely elevated anion gap — the parent alcohol has been fully metabolized. This 'osmolar gap to anion gap transition' is diagnostically important: a normal osmolar gap does NOT exclude toxic alcohol poisoning.
questions.verifiedExplanation: The evolution of toxic alcohol poisoning shows a characteristic reciprocal relationship between the osmolar gap and anion gap. Early after ingestion, the parent alcohol (methanol or ethylene glycol) is present in high concentrations, causing a high osmolar gap. As alcohol dehydrogenase converts the parent alcohol to its toxic acid metabolites, the osmolar gap decreases while the anion gap increases. Late presentations may show a normal osmolar gap with a severely elevated anion gap — the parent alcohol has been fully metabolized. This 'osmolar gap to anion gap transition' is diagnostically important: a normal osmolar gap does NOT exclude toxic alcohol poisoning.

questions.stem: Folate is given adjunctively in methanol poisoning because:

choices.feedback: Folate doesn't reverse established retinal damage.

choices.text: It enhances formate metabolism to CO2 and water
choices.feedback: Correct! Folate (leucovorin or folic acid) is a cofactor for the enzyme that converts formic acid → CO2 + H2O. Enhances elimination of the toxic metabolite. Give IV q4-6h. Analogous: thiamine + pyridoxine in EG poisoning shunt metabolism away from oxalate.
Fomepizole blocks ADH. Folate enhances downstream metabolism.
Renal protection is relevant to EG, not methanol.

questions.explanation: Folate (leucovorin or folinic acid) is given adjunctively in methanol poisoning because it serves as a cofactor for the enzyme that converts formic acid (formate) to carbon dioxide and water via the tetrahydrofolate pathway. By enhancing formate metabolism, folate helps reduce the accumulation of the toxic metabolite responsible for optic nerve damage and metabolic acidosis. This is analogous to how pyridoxine and thiamine are given adjunctively in ethylene glycol poisoning to promote metabolism of glyoxylic acid to nontoxic products (glycine via pyridoxine, alpha-hydroxy-beta-ketoadipate via thiamine).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Folate (leucovorin or folinic acid) is given adjunctively in methanol poisoning because it serves as a cofactor for the enzyme that converts formic acid (formate) to carbon dioxide and water via the tetrahydrofolate pathway. By enhancing formate metabolism, folate helps reduce the accumulation of the toxic metabolite responsible for optic nerve damage and metabolic acidosis. This is analogous to how pyridoxine and thiamine are given adjunctively in ethylene glycol poisoning to promote metabolism of glyoxylic acid to nontoxic products (glycine via pyridoxine, alpha-hydroxy-beta-ketoadipate via thiamine).

questions.stem: Sweet-tasting antifreeze ingestion causing AKI in a child. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Methanol (windshield fluid) is not typically sweet-tasting.
Isopropyl (rubbing alcohol) doesn't cause AKI and has a bitter taste.
APAP causes hepatotoxicity, not AKI from crystal deposition.

Correct! Ethylene glycol (antifreeze) has a notoriously sweet taste → accidental ingestion in children and pets. Metabolized to glycolic/oxalic acid → calcium oxalate crystals → renal tubular obstruction → AKI. Treatment: fomepizole + HD if severe. Prevention: bitter additives now added to some antifreeze products.

questions.explanation: Sweet-tasting antifreeze ingestion causing acute kidney injury in a child is the classic presentation of ethylene glycol poisoning. Ethylene glycol (the main component of automotive antifreeze) has a characteristically sweet taste, making accidental pediatric ingestion common. The metabolic pathway produces glycolic acid (causing anion gap metabolic acidosis) and oxalic acid (which precipitates as calcium oxalate crystals in renal tubules, causing AKI). Other clues include fluorescence of the urine under Wood's lamp (some antifreeze contains fluorescein), hypocalcemia (from calcium binding to oxalate), and cranial nerve palsies. Treatment: fomepizole and possible hemodialysis.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Sweet-tasting antifreeze ingestion causing acute kidney injury in a child is the classic presentation of ethylene glycol poisoning. Ethylene glycol (the main component of automotive antifreeze) has a characteristically sweet taste, making accidental pediatric ingestion common. The metabolic pathway produces glycolic acid (causing anion gap metabolic acidosis) and oxalic acid (which precipitates as calcium oxalate crystals in renal tubules, causing AKI). Other clues include fluorescence of the urine under Wood's lamp (some antifreeze contains fluorescein), hypocalcemia (from calcium binding to oxalate), and cranial nerve palsies. Treatment: fomepizole and possible hemodialysis.

questions.stem: Sympathomimetic and anticholinergic toxidromes are distinguished by:

choices.feedback: Both can cause agitation and altered mental status.
Both cause tachycardia.
Both have mydriasis (dilated pupils).

Correct! Both: tachycardia, hypertension, mydriasis, agitation. Key difference: sympathomimetic = DIAPHORETIC (wet/sweating). Anticholinergic = DRY ('dry as a bone'). This is the single most reliable distinguishing feature and extremely high-yield.

questions.explanation: Correct: Skin moisture (diaphoretic vs dry).

questions.stem: Pinpoint pupils + respiratory depression + CNS depression =

choices.feedback: Sympathomimetic has dilated pupils, tachycardia, hypertension.
Anticholinergic has DILATED pupils and dry skin.
Serotonin syndrome has clonus, hyperreflexia, hyperthermia, mydriasis.

Correct! Classic opioid triad: miosis (pinpoint pupils) + respiratory depression + CNS depression. Treat with naloxone (0.4-IV, titrate to respiratory effort). Distinguish from sedative-hypnotic (normal/mildly small pupils, no miosis).

questions.explanation: Toxicology management follows the systematic approach: stabilize ABCs, identify the toxidrome, decontaminate when appropriate, administer specific antidote if available, and provide supportive care. Always contact Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) for guidance.

questions.stem: DUMBBELSS mnemonic describes which toxidrome?

choices.feedback: Sympathomimetic causes the opposite of most DUMBBELSS features.

Correct! DUMBBELSS: Diarrhea, Urination, Miosis, Bronchospasm, Bradycardia, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation, Sweating. Caused by: organophosphates, carbamates, nerve agents, excess pyridostigmine. Treatment: atropine (muscarinic antagonist) + pralidoxime (reactivates acetylcholinesterase).
Anticholinergic is the OPPOSITE (dry, not DUMBBELSS).
Opioid toxidrome: miosis + CNS/respiratory depression. No DUMBBELSS.

questions.explanation: Correct: Cholinergic (muscarinic excess).

questions.stem: Antidote for TCA overdose?

choices.feedback: Atropine treats cholinergic toxicity, not anticholinergic/TCA.
Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines. Contraindicated in TCA overdose (seizure risk).

Correct! TCA overdose: wide QRS (>100ms), seizures, hypotension, anticholinergic effects. Sodium bicarb: alkalinizes blood → increases protein binding of TCA, overcomes sodium channel blockade → narrows QRS. Give bolus 1-2 mEq/kg IV for QRS >100ms. Target pH 7.50-7.55.
NAC is the antidote for acetaminophen.

questions.explanation: TCA overdose causes a dangerous triad: (1) cardiac toxicity (QRS widening → sodium channel blockade, QT prolongation → ventricular arrhythmias), (2) anticholinergic syndrome (mydriasis, dry skin, urinary retention, tachycardia, hyperthermia), (3) CNS toxicity (seizures, altered mental status). Sodium bicarbonate is the antidote for QRS >100ms — it provides sodium to overcome channel blockade and alkalinization to reduce free drug. Target serum pH 7.45-7.55.

questions.stem: Antidote for β-blocker overdose?

choices.feedback: Fab fragments are for digoxin toxicity.

Correct! Glucagon bypasses the beta-receptor → activates adenylyl cyclase directly → increases cAMP → positive inotropy and chronotropy. Dose: 3-IV bolus, then infusion. For beta-blocker overdose causing severe bradycardia and hypotension refractory to atropine. Also: high-dose insulin for refractory cases.
Calcium is first-line for CCB overdose, not beta-blockers.
Naloxone reverses opioids, not beta-blockers.

questions.explanation: Toxicology management follows the systematic approach: stabilize ABCs, identify the toxidrome, decontaminate when appropriate, administer specific antidote if available, and provide supportive care. Always contact Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) for guidance.

questions.stem: Fomepizole is the antidote for:

choices.feedback: Isopropyl alcohol is metabolized to acetone (not as toxic). Supportive care usually sufficient.

Correct! Fomepizole inhibits alcohol dehydrogenase, preventing metabolism of methanol/ethylene glycol into toxic metabolites (formic acid from methanol → blindness; oxalic acid from ethylene glycol → renal failure). Give early — before toxic metabolites accumulate. Also: hemodialysis for severe cases.
Ethanol intoxication is treated supportively, not with fomepizole.
NAC is for acetaminophen.

questions.explanation: Toxicology management follows the systematic approach: stabilize ABCs, identify the toxidrome, decontaminate when appropriate, administer specific antidote if available, and provide supportive care. Always contact Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) for guidance.

questions.stem: Activated charcoal is most effective when given:

choices.feedback: Inducing emesis is no longer recommended.

Correct! Activated charcoal adsorbs most drugs in the GI tract but must be given early (within 1-2h, ideally <1h). Contraindicated: altered mental status without protected airway (aspiration risk), caustic/acid ingestion, hydrocarbons. Does NOT adsorb: metals (iron, lithium, lead), alcohols, acids/alkalis. Single dose for most ingestions.
After 2 hours, most ingested material has left the stomach.
Efficacy drops dramatically after 1-2 hours.

questions.explanation: Toxicology management follows the systematic approach: stabilize ABCs, identify the toxidrome, decontaminate when appropriate, administer specific antidote if available, and provide supportive care. Always contact Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) for guidance.

questions.stem: Digoxin toxicity is treated with:

choices.feedback: High-dose insulin is for CCB/BB overdose, not digoxin.
Bicarb is for TCA and salicylate overdose, not digoxin.
Calcium is generally AVOIDED in digoxin toxicity (may worsen cardiac toxicity — 'stone heart' phenomenon, though evidence is debated).

Correct! Digoxin-specific Fab fragments bind free digoxin → rapid reversal. Indications: life-threatening arrhythmias, hyperkalemia (K >5), hemodynamic instability. Also correct: treat hyperkalemia (but avoid calcium), atropine for bradycardia, do NOT cardiovert (may precipitate VF). Classic findings: 'scooped' ST segments, any arrhythmia (especially bidirectional VT, accelerated junctional rhythm with AV block).

questions.explanation: Toxicology management follows the systematic approach: stabilize ABCs, identify the toxidrome, decontaminate when appropriate, administer specific antidote if available, and provide supportive care. Always contact Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) for guidance.

questions.stem: Minutes into a transfusion, a patient develops fever, flank pain, dark urine, and hypotension. What is the diagnosis and first action?

choices.text: Febrile non-hemolytic reaction — give acetaminophen and continue
choices.feedback: Febrile non-hemolytic reactions don't cause flank pain, dark urine, or hemodynamic instability.
Allergic reactions cause urticaria/pruritus, not hemolysis.

Correct! Acute hemolytic reaction: ABO incompatibility → intravascular hemolysis → hemoglobinuria (dark urine), flank pain, fever, hypotension, DIC. STOP transfusion, IV NS, send blood for DAT/Coombs, free Hb, haptoglobin. Most caused by clerical error (wrong patient).
TRALI presents with respiratory distress, not flank pain and dark urine.

questions.explanation: Key detail: STOP the transfusion immediately.

questions.stem: A patient develops respiratory distress and bilateral pulmonary infiltrates 4 hours after a platelet transfusion. BNP is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: PE typically shows unilateral infiltrate or wedge-shaped infarct, not bilateral infiltrates.
TACO would have elevated BNP, JVD, and respond to diuretics. This patient has normal BNP.

Correct! TRALI: respiratory distress + bilateral infiltrates + NORMAL BNP + within 6 hours of transfusion = non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema from donor anti-HLA antibodies. Treatment: supportive (O2, ventilation). Do NOT give diuretics. Leading cause of transfusion-related death.
Pneumonia onset is not this acute or temporally related to transfusion.

questions.explanation: Key detail: related acute lung injury).

questions.stem: An IgA-deficient patient has an anaphylactic reaction during a transfusion. What product modification should be used for future transfusions?

choices.feedback: Leukoreduced products prevent febrile non-hemolytic reactions and CMV transmission, not anaphylaxis.
CMV-negative products are for preventing CMV transmission, not for IgA deficiency.

Correct! Washed products have plasma proteins (including IgA) removed. IgA-deficient patients form anti-IgA antibodies → anaphylaxis when exposed to donor IgA in plasma. Washing removes the offending protein.
Irradiated products prevent GVHD by destroying donor lymphocytes, not relevant to IgA deficiency.

questions.explanation: Transfusion reactions: Acute hemolytic (ABO incompatibility) — fever, flank pain, hemoglobinuria, DIC within minutes; caused by preformed IgM antibodies. Febrile non-hemolytic — fever/chills without hemolysis; from recipient antibodies to donor WBC cytokines; prevented by leukoreduction. Allergic — urticaria/anaphylaxis from plasma proteins; treat with antihistamines. TRALI — noncardiogenic pulmonary edema within 6 hours; caused by donor antibodies activating recipient neutrophils. TACO — volume overload with pulmonary edema; elevated BNP distinguishes from TRALI.

questions.stem: During massive transfusion, what electrolyte abnormality is caused by the citrate preservative in blood products?

choices.feedback: Hyperkalemia can occur from potassium leaking from stored RBCs, but that's not caused by citrate.

Correct! Citrate in blood products chelates calcium → hypocalcemia. Signs: perioral tingling, muscle cramps, prolonged QT, cardiac dysfunction. Monitor ionized calcium during massive transfusion and replace with IV calcium gluconate.
Sodium changes are not typically caused by citrate.
Hypomagnesemia can occur but is not the primary citrate-related electrolyte issue.

questions.explanation: Transfusion reactions: Acute hemolytic (ABO incompatibility) — fever, flank pain, hemoglobinuria, DIC within minutes; caused by preformed IgM antibodies. Febrile non-hemolytic — fever/chills without hemolysis; from recipient antibodies to donor WBC cytokines; prevented by leukoreduction. Allergic — urticaria/anaphylaxis from plasma proteins; treat with antihistamines. TRALI — noncardiogenic pulmonary edema within 6 hours; caused by donor antibodies activating recipient neutrophils. TACO — volume overload with pulmonary edema; elevated BNP distinguishes from TRALI.

questions.stem: Absent left breath sounds, tracheal deviation right, JVD, hypotension. Immediate intervention?

choices.feedback: CT inappropriate in unstable patient with tension PTX.
That treats tamponade (Beck triad). Tracheal deviation + absent sounds = tension PTX.
Tension PTX is a clinical diagnosis — do NOT delay for imaging.
Correct! Tension PTX: absent sounds + deviation AWAY + JVD + hypotension. Needle decompression 2nd ICS MCL → chest tube. Delay = cardiac arrest.

questions.explanation: Absent left breath sounds with tracheal deviation to the right, JVD, and hypotension in a trauma patient is tension pneumothorax — a clinical diagnosis requiring immediate needle decompression of the LEFT chest. Do NOT wait for imaging. Needle decompression is performed at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line (or 4th-5th ICS, anterior axillary line) on the affected side, followed by tube thoracostomy. Tension pneumothorax causes obstructive shock by compressing mediastinal structures, kinking great vessels, and preventing venous return. It is one of the immediately reversible causes of traumatic arrest (along with tamponade, massive hemorrhage, and auto-PEEP).

It is one of the immediately reversible causes of traumatic arrest (along with tamponade, massive hemorrhage, and auto-PEEP).

questions.stem: Most commonly injured organ in blunt abdominal trauma?

choices.feedback: Pancreatic injuries relatively uncommon in blunt trauma.
Renal injuries less common than splenic.
Correct! Spleen is #1 in blunt trauma. Kehr sign = left shoulder pain. Stable + low-grade → observe. Unstable → splenectomy + post-splenectomy vaccines.
Liver is #1 in PENETRATING trauma, #2 in blunt.

questions.explanation: The spleen is the most commonly injured organ in blunt abdominal trauma. Its subcapsular location under the left lower ribs, fixed ligamentous attachments, and friable parenchyma make it vulnerable to deceleration and direct impact forces. Splenic injury is graded I-V (AAST scale): Grade I-II (subcapsular hematoma, minor laceration) are typically managed non-operatively with close monitoring. Grade III-IV may be managed with angioembolization. Grade V (shattered spleen, hilar vascular injury) usually requires splenectomy. Non-operative management succeeds in >80% of hemodynamically stable patients. The liver is the second most commonly injured organ.
questions.verifiedExplanation: The spleen is the most commonly injured organ in blunt abdominal trauma. Its subcapsular location under the left lower ribs, fixed ligamentous attachments, and friable parenchyma make it vulnerable to deceleration and direct impact forces. Grade III-IV may be managed with angioembolization. Grade V (shattered spleen, hilar vascular injury) usually requires splenectomy. Non-operative management succeeds in >80% of hemodynamically stable patients. The liver is the second most commonly injured organ.

questions.stem: Most sensitive FAST window for intra-abdominal free fluid?

choices.feedback: Evaluates pericardium, not peritoneum.
Correct! Most dependent area in supine patient — fluid accumulates here first. Positive FAST + unstable = OR.
Can detect pelvic fluid but less sensitive than Morrison pouch.
Splenorenal space — less sensitive than Morrison pouch.

questions.explanation: The RUQ window (Morrison's pouch / hepatorenal recess) is the most sensitive FAST window for detecting intra-abdominal free fluid because it is the most dependent area in the supine patient. Fluid from any abdominal source gravitates here first. FAST (Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma) includes 4 windows: RUQ (Morrison's pouch), LUQ (splenorenal recess), suprapubic (pelvic/pouch of Douglas), and subxiphoid (pericardial). FAST is used in the initial trauma assessment to rapidly identify free fluid requiring intervention — it has high specificity but limited sensitivity for solid organ injury without significant free fluid.
questions.verifiedExplanation: The RUQ window (Morrison's pouch / hepatorenal recess) is the most sensitive FAST window for detecting intra-abdominal free fluid because it is the most dependent area in the supine patient. Fluid from any abdominal source gravitates here first. FAST (Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma) includes 4 windows: RUQ (Morrison's pouch), LUQ (splenorenal recess), suprapubic (pelvic/pouch of Douglas), and subxiphoid (pericardial). FAST is used in the initial trauma assessment to rapidly identify free fluid requiring intervention — it has high specificity but limited sensitivity for solid organ injury without significant free fluid.

questions.stem: MTP ratio for pRBCs:FFP:Platelets?

choices.feedback: Correct! Balanced resuscitation (PROPPR trial). Also: TXA within 3h, calcium replacement, warm products.
Excess FFP increases volume overload without benefit.
Causes dilutional coagulopathy.
Leads to severe dilutional coagulopathy.

questions.explanation: Massive transfusion protocol (MTP) uses a balanced ratio of blood products — pRBCs:FFP:Platelets in a 1:1:1 ratio. This is based on the PROPPR trial, which showed that a balanced resuscitation strategy mimicking whole blood composition results in better hemostasis, less crystalloid use, and fewer deaths from hemorrhage at 24 hours. The 1:1:1 ratio addresses the 'acute coagulopathy of trauma' by replacing clotting factors and platelets concurrently with red cells. Tranexamic acid (TXA) should be given within 3 hours of injury. Calcium replacement is important because citrate in stored blood products chelates calcium, causing hypocalcemia.

This is based on the PROPPR trial, which showed that a balanced resuscitation strategy mimicking whole blood composition results in better hemostasis, less crystalloid use, and fewer deaths from hemorrhage at 24 hours. The 1:1:1 ratio addresses the 'acute coagulopathy of trauma' by replacing clotting factors and platelets concurrently with red cells. Tranexamic acid (TXA) should be given within 3 hours of injury. Calcium replacement is important because citrate in stored blood products chelates calcium, causing hypocalcemia.

questions.stem: The lethal triad in trauma:

choices.feedback: These are shock signs, not the lethal triad.
Specific thoracic injuries, not the systemic lethal triad.
Later complications, not the acute lethal triad.
Correct! Each worsens the others in a vicious cycle. Damage control surgery specifically targets breaking this cycle.

questions.explanation: The lethal triad (also called the 'trauma triad of death') consists of hypothermia, acidosis, and coagulopathy — three mutually reinforcing conditions that create a vicious cycle of deterioration. Hypothermia impairs clotting factor enzymatic activity and platelet function. Acidosis reduces clotting factor activity and worsens vasodilation. Coagulopathy leads to ongoing hemorrhage, causing more hypothermia (from blood loss and cold fluid resuscitation) and acidosis (from hypoperfusion). Breaking this cycle requires damage control surgery (abbreviated operations to stop bleeding and contamination), aggressive rewarming, balanced blood product transfusion, and early recognition.

Coagulopathy leads to ongoing hemorrhage, causing more hypothermia (from blood loss and cold fluid resuscitation) and acidosis (from hypoperfusion). Breaking this cycle requires damage control surgery (abbreviated operations to stop bleeding and contamination), aggressive rewarming, balanced blood product transfusion, and early recognition.

questions.stem: Post-splenectomy vaccines include:

choices.feedback: Routine vaccines, not specifically post-splenectomy.
Live vaccines, not related to asplenic risk.
Critical vaccines target encapsulated organisms.
Correct! Asplenic patients vulnerable to encapsulated organisms (SHiN: Strep pneumo, H. flu, Neisseria). Give 2 weeks post-op if possible.

questions.explanation: Post-splenectomy patients are at lifelong risk of overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI), particularly from encapsulated organisms. Required vaccinations include pneumococcal (PCV13 and PPSV23), meningococcal (MenACWY and MenB), and Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib). These should be given at least 2 weeks after elective splenectomy or 2 weeks after emergent splenectomy (once the patient is stable). Annual influenza vaccination is also recommended. Some guidelines recommend prophylactic antibiotics (penicillin or amoxicillin) for 1-2 years post-splenectomy or lifelong in children. Patients should carry medical alert identification and seek immediate care for febrile illness.

questions.stem: What chest tube output findings indicate the need for emergent thoracotomy in trauma?

choices.text: Large initial output or persistently high ongoing output
choices.feedback: Correct! A large initial volume of blood upon chest tube insertion OR persistently high ongoing output suggests a major vascular injury (intercostal, internal mammary, or great vessel) requiring surgical control.

choices.text: Any amount of blood in the chest tube
choices.feedback: Small hemothoraces are common in trauma and are managed with the chest tube alone. Surgery is reserved for large or ongoing hemorrhage.
Air leak suggests lung parenchymal injury or bronchopleural fistula, not necessarily a surgical emergency unless massive and continuous.
Decreasing output is reassuring — it suggests the bleeding is slowing and the chest tube is effectively draining. This is the expected course for most traumatic hemothoraces.

questions.explanation: Emergent thoracotomy for hemothorax is indicated based on chest tube output: large initial output (>1500 mL immediately upon chest tube insertion) or persistently high ongoing output (>200 mL/hour for 2-4 consecutive hours). These thresholds suggest major vascular injury (intercostal artery, internal mammary artery, great vessel, or hilar injury) that requires surgical repair. Massive hemothorax (>1500 mL) also causes hemodynamic instability from blood loss and respiratory compromise from lung compression. Autotransfusion of chest tube output can be performed as a bridge. Retained hemothorax that does not drain adequately risks empyema and fibrothorax.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Emergent thoracotomy for hemothorax is indicated based on chest tube output: large initial output (>1500 mL immediately upon chest tube insertion) or persistently high ongoing output (>200 mL/hour for 2-4 consecutive hours). These thresholds suggest major vascular injury (intercostal artery, internal mammary artery, great vessel, or hilar injury) that requires surgical repair. Massive hemothorax (>1500 mL) also causes hemodynamic instability from blood loss and respiratory compromise from lung compression. Autotransfusion of chest tube output can be performed as a bridge. Retained hemothorax that does not drain adequately risks empyema and fibrothorax.

questions.stem: Permissive hypotension targets what SBP?

choices.feedback: Correct! Permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90) during active hemorrhage prevents clot disruption and dilutional coagulopathy. Exception: traumatic brain injury (need cerebral perfusion).
Too low — risks organ damage.
Normal SBP — goal AFTER hemorrhage control.
Would require aggressive fluids that worsen coagulopathy.

questions.explanation: Permissive hypotension in trauma targets SBP 80-90 mmHg (MAP ~50-60) in actively hemorrhaging patients before definitive bleeding control. The rationale is that aggressive fluid resuscitation to normal blood pressure before hemorrhage is controlled: (1) disrupts nascent clot formation by increasing perfusion pressure, (2) dilutes clotting factors and platelets, (3) causes hypothermia from cold fluid administration, and (4) worsens the lethal triad. Permissive hypotension aims to maintain minimum organ perfusion without exacerbating bleeding. Exceptions: traumatic brain injury requires higher MAP targets (>80 mmHg) to maintain cerebral perfusion pressure.
