Permissive hypotension aims to maintain minimum organ perfusion without exacerbating bleeding. Exceptions: traumatic brain injury requires higher MAP targets (>80 mmHg) to maintain cerebral perfusion pressure.

questions.stem: A healthcare worker from India (BCG-vaccinated) has a PPD of 12 mm. Which test should be performed next to avoid a BCG-related false positive?

choices.feedback: Sputum AFB is for active TB. Need to first determine if this is LTBI vs false positive.

choices.text: Interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) — QuantiFERON or T-SPOT
choices.feedback: Correct! IGRA (QuantiFERON or T-SPOT) is preferred in BCG-vaccinated individuals because it uses TB-specific antigens (ESAT-6, CFP-10) not present in BCG → no false positives from vaccination.
Repeating PPD would boost the reaction and not resolve the BCG question.
CT is not the next step — first confirm if the PPD is a true positive with IGRA.

questions.explanation: In a BCG-vaccinated individual, PPD (tuberculin skin test) may produce a false-positive result because BCG cross-reacts with PPD antigens. IGRA tests (QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus or T-SPOT.TB) use Mycobacterium tuberculosis-specific antigens (ESAT-6, CFP-10) not present in BCG strains or most nontuberculous mycobacteria. Therefore, IGRAs have higher specificity than PPD in BCG-vaccinated populations. A positive IGRA in this healthcare worker would indicate true latent TB infection requiring treatment, regardless of BCG status.

questions.stem: A patient on RIPE therapy develops tingling and numbness in the feet. Which drug is the likely cause, and what should be supplemented?

choices.feedback: Correct! Isoniazid causes peripheral neuropathy by depleting pyridoxine (vitamin B6). All patients on INH should receive B6 supplementation, especially pregnant women, alcoholics, diabetics, and malnourished patients.
Rifampin causes hepatotoxicity and drug interactions but not peripheral neuropathy.
Pyrazinamide causes hyperuricemia/gout, not neuropathy.
Ethambutol causes optic neuritis (visual changes), not peripheral neuropathy.

questions.explanation: Isoniazid causes peripheral neuropathy (tingling, numbness in extremities) by depleting pyridoxine (vitamin B6) through inhibition of pyridoxine phosphokinase. Treatment and prevention: supplement pyridoxine 25-50 mg daily in all patients receiving isoniazid, especially those at higher risk (diabetes, HIV, malnutrition, alcohol use, pregnancy). Other isoniazid toxicities include hepatotoxicity (monitor LFTs), drug-induced lupus, and seizures in overdose (treat with IV pyridoxine). Rifampin causes hepatotoxicity and orange discoloration of body fluids. Ethambutol causes optic neuritis. Pyrazinamide causes hyperuricemia.

questions.stem: Which antitubercular drug causes optic neuritis and requires monthly visual acuity testing?

choices.feedback: Isoniazid causes peripheral neuropathy and hepatotoxicity, not optic neuritis.
Pyrazinamide causes hyperuricemia and hepatotoxicity, not visual changes.

Correct! Ethambutol causes dose-dependent optic neuritis → red-green color blindness, decreased visual acuity. Test baseline visual acuity and monitor monthly. Typically reversible if caught early and drug is stopped.
Rifampin causes hepatotoxicity and drug interactions, not optic changes.

questions.explanation: Ethambutol causes dose-dependent optic neuritis (retrobulbar neuritis) presenting as decreased visual acuity, loss of red-green color discrimination, and central scotomas. Monthly visual acuity and color vision testing are recommended during ethambutol therapy. Patients should be counseled to report any visual changes immediately. The mechanism involves disruption of mitochondrial function in retinal ganglion cells. Optic neuritis is generally reversible upon discontinuation if detected early, but can be irreversible with prolonged use. Isoniazid causes peripheral neuropathy. Rifampin and pyrazinamide cause hepatotoxicity.

questions.stem: Active pulmonary TB is treated with RIPE for the initial 2 months, then what continuation phase?

choices.feedback: Correct! Standard active TB: RIPE × 2 months (intensive phase), then Rifampin + Isoniazid (RI) × 4 months (continuation phase) = 6 months total. Pyrazinamide and ethambutol are stopped after the initial phase.
Rifampin alone would risk resistance development.
Isoniazid alone for 9 months is a LTBI regimen, not active TB treatment.

choices.text: All four drugs for 4 more months
choices.feedback: All four drugs for 6 months total is unnecessary and increases toxicity risk.

questions.explanation: Standard active pulmonary TB treatment follows a two-phase regimen: (1) Intensive phase: RIPE (Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol) daily for 2 months. Ethambutol is included until drug susceptibility results confirm sensitivity. (2) Continuation phase: Rifampin + Isoniazid for 4 additional months (total 6 months of treatment). Directly observed therapy (DOT) is recommended throughout to ensure adherence. Longer treatment (9-12 months) may be needed for extrapulmonary TB (meningitis, bone/joint), cavitary disease with positive 2-month cultures, or if pyrazinamide cannot be used.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old sexually active woman presents with dysuria and frequency. UA shows pyuria and positive nitrites. What is the most appropriate empiric treatment?

choices.feedback: Ceftriaxone IM is for pyelonephritis or complicated UTI, not simple cystitis.
Amoxicillin has high resistance rates against E. coli and is not recommended empirically for UTI.
Fluoroquinolones should be reserved for complicated UTI/pyelonephritis. Not first-line for uncomplicated cystitis.

Correct! Nitrofurantoin 5 days is first-line for uncomplicated cystitis. It concentrates in the urine, has good activity against common uropathogens, and preserves fluoroquinolones for more serious infections.

questions.explanation: Infectious disease management follows the principles of identifying the pathogen, selecting appropriate empiric therapy, then narrowing based on culture and sensitivity. Antibiotic stewardship optimizes outcomes while minimizing resistance.

questions.stem: A patient with recurrent UTIs has a CT showing a large stone filling the renal pelvis (staghorn calculus). Urine pH is 8.0. What organism is most likely responsible?

choices.feedback: Correct! Proteus mirabilis produces urease → splits urea to ammonia → alkaline urine (pH >7.5) → struvite (magnesium ammonium phosphate) staghorn calculi. Classic boards association.
Enterococcus causes UTI but doesn't produce urease or struvite stones.
S. saprophyticus causes cystitis in young women but doesn't produce urease or staghorn stones.
E. coli is the most common UTI cause but typically produces acidic urine and calcium oxalate stones, not staghorn calculi.

questions.explanation: Infectious disease management follows the principles of identifying the pathogen, selecting appropriate empiric therapy, then narrowing based on culture and sensitivity. Antibiotic stewardship optimizes outcomes while minimizing resistance.

questions.stem: A pregnant woman at her first prenatal visit has a urine culture growing 100,000 CFU/mL E. coli but no symptoms. What should you do?

choices.feedback: Delaying treatment risks progression to pyelonephritis, which can cause preterm labor.

choices.text: No treatment needed — asymptomatic bacteriuria is benign
choices.feedback: Asymptomatic bacteriuria IS benign in most populations, but pregnancy is the exception — 40% progress to pyelonephritis if untreated.

Correct! Treat ALL bacteriuria in pregnancy (even asymptomatic). Untreated ASB → 40% risk of pyelonephritis → preterm labor, low birth weight. Use pregnancy-safe antibiotics (nitrofurantoin, cephalexin, amoxicillin-clavulanate). Avoid fluoroquinolones and TMP-SMX (especially 1st trimester).
Cranberry supplements are not adequate treatment for bacteriuria in pregnancy.

questions.explanation: Infectious disease management follows the principles of identifying the pathogen, selecting appropriate empiric therapy, then narrowing based on culture and sensitivity. Antibiotic stewardship optimizes outcomes while minimizing resistance.

questions.stem: Which UTI pathogen is characteristically novobiocin-resistant and most common in sexually active young women after E. coli?

choices.feedback: Correct! S. saprophyticus = 2nd most common cause of UTI in young sexually active women. Novobiocin-resistant (vs S. epidermidis which is novobiocin-sensitive). Coagulase-negative.
Proteus is associated with staghorn calculi and alkaline urine, not as the second most common in young women.
Klebsiella is common in diabetics and catheterized patients, not specifically young sexually active women.
Pseudomonas is associated with complicated/catheter-related UTIs, not uncomplicated UTI in young women.

questions.explanation: Infectious disease management follows the principles of identifying the pathogen, selecting appropriate empiric therapy, then narrowing based on culture and sensitivity. Antibiotic stewardship optimizes outcomes while minimizing resistance.

questions.stem: Crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at RUSB radiating to carotids with delayed carotid upstroke. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: MR is holosystolic at apex radiating to axilla.
MVP = mid-systolic click ± late systolic murmur at apex.
AR is early diastolic decrescendo at LUSB.

Correct! Classic AS: crescendo-decrescendo (diamond-shaped) systolic murmur, best at RUSB, radiating to carotids. Pulsus parvus et tardus (weak, delayed carotid upstroke). Murmur decreases with Valsalva/standing (decreased preload).

questions.explanation: Valvular heart disease assessment combines auscultation with echocardiography. Aortic stenosis: crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at right upper sternal border radiating to carotids; severe when valve area <1.0 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg. Mitral regurgitation: holosystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla. Intervention thresholds are based on symptom onset and echocardiographic severity per 2020 ACC/AHA guidelines.

questions.stem: Which murmurs get LOUDER with Valsalva?

choices.feedback: Both decrease with Valsalva (decreased preload → less flow across valve).

Correct! Valsalva decreases preload → smaller LV cavity → MORE obstruction in HCM (louder) and EARLIER click in MVP (more prolapse). Everything else gets softer. This is the most tested dynamic maneuver concept on boards.
Both decrease with Valsalva.
Only HCM and MVP increase; others decrease.

questions.explanation: Valvular heart disease assessment combines auscultation with echocardiography. Aortic stenosis: crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at right upper sternal border radiating to carotids; severe when valve area <1.0 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg. Mitral regurgitation: holosystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla. Intervention thresholds are based on symptom onset and echocardiographic severity per 2020 ACC/AHA guidelines.

questions.stem: Mitral stenosis is almost always caused by:

choices.feedback: Correct! Rheumatic heart disease (Group A Strep → rheumatic fever → autoimmune valve damage) is the cause of MS in ~99% of cases worldwide. Leads to valve thickening, commissural fusion, fish-mouth orifice. Complications: LA enlargement → A-fib → LA thrombus → stroke.
Endocarditis typically causes regurgitation (valve destruction), not stenosis.
Bicuspid valve causes aortic stenosis, not mitral stenosis.
Degenerative calcification causes aortic stenosis, not MS.

questions.explanation: Valvular heart disease assessment combines auscultation with echocardiography. Aortic stenosis: crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at right upper sternal border radiating to carotids; severe when valve area <1.0 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg. Mitral regurgitation: holosystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla. Intervention thresholds are based on symptom onset and echocardiographic severity per 2020 ACC/AHA guidelines.

questions.stem: Classic symptom triad of aortic stenosis (SAD)?

choices.feedback: Correct! SAD: Syncope (exertional — fixed output can't meet demand), Angina (increased O2 demand from LVH), Dyspnea (HF as late manifestation). Once any symptom develops, prognosis is poor without valve replacement. Dyspnea/HF onset = <1 year survival untreated.
These can be consequences but are not the classic symptom triad.
These suggest right heart failure, not AS.
Close but the 'D' = dyspnea on exertion, and the classic triad uses syncope rather than dizziness.

questions.explanation: Valvular heart disease assessment combines auscultation with echocardiography. Aortic stenosis: crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at right upper sternal border radiating to carotids; severe when valve area <1.0 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg. Mitral regurgitation: holosystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla. Intervention thresholds are based on symptom onset and echocardiographic severity per 2020 ACC/AHA guidelines.

questions.stem: Right-sided murmurs increase with inspiration because:

choices.text: The diaphragm pushes the heart closer to the chest wall
choices.feedback: This is not the mechanism for right-sided murmur augmentation.

choices.text: Inspiration increases systemic venous return to the right heart
choices.feedback: Close — this is the mechanism, but the answer should capture the full concept.
Inspiration does not significantly compress the left atrium.

choices.text: Negative intrathoracic pressure increases venous return → more flow across right-sided valves
choices.feedback: Correct! Inspiration creates negative intrathoracic pressure → increases venous return to RA/RV → more flow across tricuspid/pulmonic valves → louder murmur. This is Carvallo sign (tricuspid regurgitation louder with inspiration). Left-sided murmurs typically decrease with inspiration.

questions.explanation: Valvular heart disease assessment combines auscultation with echocardiography. Aortic stenosis: crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at right upper sternal border radiating to carotids; severe when valve area <1.0 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg. Mitral regurgitation: holosystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla. Intervention thresholds are based on symptom onset and echocardiographic severity per 2020 ACC/AHA guidelines.

questions.stem: When is endocarditis prophylaxis indicated before dental procedures?

choices.text: Any patient undergoing any surgical procedure
choices.feedback: Prophylaxis is only for specific high-risk cardiac conditions AND only before specific dental procedures.

choices.text: All patients with mitral valve prolapse
choices.feedback: MVP alone is NOT an indication for prophylaxis (changed in 2007 AHA guidelines).
Most murmurs do NOT require prophylaxis. Only specific high-risk conditions.

choices.text: Prosthetic valve, prior endocarditis, certain congenital defects
choices.feedback: Correct! High-risk only: prosthetic valve, prior IE, unrepaired cyanotic CHD, repaired CHD with residual defects, cardiac transplant with valvulopathy. Give amoxicillin1h before dental procedures involving gingival manipulation. NOT required before GI/GU procedures.

questions.explanation: Infective endocarditis diagnosis uses Modified Duke Criteria: 2 major, 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor criteria = definite IE. Major criteria: (1) positive blood cultures with typical organisms (viridans streptococci, Staph aureus, HACEK) and (2) echocardiographic evidence of vegetation. Empiric treatment for native valve: nafcillin/oxacillin + gentamicin (or vancomycin if MRSA suspected). Prosthetic valve: vancomycin + gentamicin + rifampin.

questions.stem: Wide pulse pressure, bounding pulses, and early diastolic murmur at LUSB. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! AR: wide pulse pressure (elevated systolic from increased stroke volume, low diastolic from regurgitant flow back into LV). Named signs: bounding 'water hammer' pulses (Corrigan), head bobbing (de Musset), nail bed pulsations (Quincke). Blowing early diastolic decrescendo murmur at LUSB.
MS has a low-pitched diastolic rumble at apex with opening snap.
AS has a narrow pulse pressure and delayed upstroke, not wide pulse pressure.
PDA causes a continuous 'machinery' murmur, not an isolated diastolic murmur.

questions.explanation: Valvular heart disease assessment combines auscultation with echocardiography. Aortic stenosis: crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at right upper sternal border radiating to carotids; severe when valve area <1.0 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg. Mitral regurgitation: holosystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla. Intervention thresholds are based on symptom onset and echocardiographic severity per 2020 ACC/AHA guidelines.

questions.stem: For severe symptomatic aortic stenosis, TAVR is preferred over SAVR in which patients?

choices.text: Only patients with concurrent coronary disease
choices.feedback: Concurrent CABG need may favor SAVR, but this is not the defining criterion for TAVR.
SAVR remains standard for young, low-risk patients.
TAVR is a primary treatment option, not just for refusals.

choices.text: Patients at high/prohibitive surgical risk or age ≥65
choices.feedback: Correct! TAVR (transcatheter aortic valve replacement) is preferred for high/prohibitive surgical risk patients and increasingly for those ≥65 years (PARTNER 3, Evolut Low Risk trials showed non-inferiority to SAVR). For patients <65 or with bicuspid valves, SAVR is still often preferred due to long-term durability data. Shared decision-making is key.

questions.explanation: Valvular heart disease assessment combines auscultation with echocardiography. Aortic stenosis: crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at right upper sternal border radiating to carotids; severe when valve area <1.0 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg. Mitral regurgitation: holosystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla. Intervention thresholds are based on symptom onset and echocardiographic severity per 2020 ACC/AHA guidelines.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with new-onset severe headache, jaw claudication, and sudden vision loss in the right eye. ESR is 95 mm/hr. What should you do FIRST?

choices.text: Order a temporal artery biopsy before treatment
choices.feedback: NEVER delay treatment for biopsy in suspected GCA. Vision loss can become bilateral and permanent. Steroids do not significantly alter biopsy results within 2 weeks.
This is not a stroke protocol. GCA requires high-dose steroids, not antiplatelet therapy alone.

Correct! GCA with vision loss is an emergency. Start prednisone(or IV methylprednisolone if acute vision loss) IMMEDIATELY. Then arrange biopsy within 2 weeks. Delay = risk of bilateral blindness from anterior ischemic optic neuropathy.

choices.text: Obtain CT angiography of the head
choices.feedback: While imaging can support GCA diagnosis, the immediate priority is starting steroids to prevent further vision loss.

questions.explanation: Rheumatologic diagnosis requires integration of clinical findings, serologic markers, and imaging. Pattern recognition of joint involvement, extra-articular manifestations, and autoantibody profiles guides differential diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.

questions.stem: Which ANCA pattern and target antigen is associated with GPA (Wegener granulomatosis)?

choices.feedback: p-ANCA/MPO is associated with MPA and EGPA (Churg-Strauss), not GPA.
c-ANCA targets PR3, not MPO. Anti-MPO produces a perinuclear (p-ANCA) pattern.
PR3 produces a cytoplasmic (c-ANCA) pattern, not perinuclear.

Correct! GPA is associated with c-ANCA (cytoplasmic pattern) targeting proteinase 3 (PR3). This is >90% specific for GPA. The classic triad is upper airway (sinusitis, saddle nose) + lungs (cavitary lesions) + kidneys (crescentic GN).

questions.explanation: ANCA-associated vasculitides: GPA (granulomatosis with polyangiitis) — c-ANCA/PR3, upper/lower respiratory tract + kidneys (pulmonary-renal syndrome). MPA (microscopic polyangiitis) — p-ANCA/MPO, kidneys + lungs without granulomas. EGPA (eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis) — p-ANCA, asthma + eosinophilia + vasculitis. Treatment: induction with pulse corticosteroids + cyclophosphamide or rituximab, maintenance with azathioprine or rituximab.

questions.stem: Which vasculitis spares the lungs and is associated with hepatitis B?

choices.feedback: Correct! PAN is a medium-vessel vasculitis that classically spares the lungs (no pulmonary involvement) and has a strong hepatitis B association (~30% of cases). Diagnosis: mesenteric/renal angiography showing microaneurysms. ANCA-negative.
MPA involves the lungs (pulmonary-renal syndrome) and is ANCA-positive (p-ANCA/MPO).
IgA vasculitis is immune complex-mediated and primarily affects children. Not associated with HBV.
GPA specifically involves the lungs (cavitary nodules) and is not associated with HBV.

questions.explanation: Correct: Polyarteritis nodosa (PAN).

questions.stem: A 4-year-old presents with fever for 6 days, bilateral conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, cervical lymphadenopathy, and desquamating rash. What is the most serious complication to screen for?

choices.feedback: Pulmonary fibrosis is not a complication of Kawasaki disease.
While aseptic meningitis can occur in Kawasaki disease, it is not the major life-threatening complication.
Renal involvement is uncommon in Kawasaki disease, unlike IgA vasculitis or ANCA-associated vasculitis.

Correct! Kawasaki disease causes coronary artery aneurysms in 25% of untreated cases (reduced to <5% with IVIG). Echocardiography is essential at diagnosis, 2 weeks, and 6-8 weeks. Giant aneurysms (>8 mm) carry risk of MI and sudden death.

questions.explanation: Rheumatologic diagnosis requires integration of clinical findings, serologic markers, and imaging. Pattern recognition of joint involvement, extra-articular manifestations, and autoantibody profiles guides differential diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.

questions.stem: A child presents with palpable purpura on the buttocks and legs, colicky abdominal pain, arthritis, and hematuria. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Classic tetrad: palpable purpura (non-thrombocytopenic, buttocks/legs), arthritis/arthralgias, abdominal pain (intussusception risk), and renal involvement (IgA nephropathy). Most common vasculitis in children. Usually self-limited. Monitor urine for persistent nephropathy.
Kawasaki presents with high fever, conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, and coronary involvement — not purpura and nephritis.

TTP causes microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia + neurological symptoms + renal dysfunction. Purpura in TTP is due to thrombocytopenia, unlike the immune-mediated purpura in IgA vasculitis.
Meningococcemia causes purpura fulminans with DIC and septic shock, a much more acutely ill presentation.

questions.explanation: Correct: IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein purpura).

questions.stem: Which vasculitis is characterized by asthma, eosinophilia, and cardiac involvement?

choices.feedback: MPA causes pulmonary-renal syndrome without asthma or eosinophilia.

Correct! EGPA triad: late-onset asthma + peripheral eosinophilia (often >10%) + vasculitis. Cardiac involvement (myocarditis, cardiomyopathy) is the leading cause of death. p-ANCA/MPO positive in ~40%. Treat with steroids ± mepolizumab (anti-IL-5).
Takayasu affects large vessels (aorta and branches) in young women. No association with asthma or eosinophilia.
GPA involves sinuses, lungs, and kidneys but is NOT associated with asthma or eosinophilia.

questions.explanation: Correct: Eosinophilic GPA (Churg-Strauss/EGPA).

questions.stem: Takayasu arteritis most commonly affects which patient population?

choices.feedback: GCA (not Takayasu) affects patients over 50. Takayasu affects younger patients.
Kawasaki disease (not Takayasu) primarily affects children under 5.
Takayasu has a strong female predominance in young adults.

Correct! Takayasu arteritis predominantly affects women under 40 (female:male ratio 9:1). It involves the aorta and its major branches → 'pulseless disease' with limb claudication, blood pressure discrepancies between arms (>10 mmHg), and vascular bruits. Diagnosis: MRA or CTA showing vessel wall thickening and stenosis.

questions.explanation: Rheumatologic diagnosis requires integration of clinical findings, serologic markers, and imaging. Pattern recognition of joint involvement, extra-articular manifestations, and autoantibody profiles guides differential diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.

questions.stem: In ANCA-associated vasculitis, what type of glomerulonephritis is seen on kidney biopsy?

choices.feedback: Correct! ANCA-associated vasculitis causes pauci-immune crescentic (rapidly progressive) GN — meaning few or NO immune deposits on immunofluorescence. The crescents represent epithelial cell proliferation in Bowman capsule. This distinguishes it from anti-GBM disease (linear IgG) and lupus nephritis (full house pattern).
IgA nephropathy shows mesangial IgA deposits and is associated with IgA vasculitis, not ANCA-associated vasculitis.
MCD causes nephrotic syndrome with normal light microscopy and foot process effacement on EM. Not associated with vasculitis.
Membranous GN shows subepithelial deposits on IF and is associated with SLE Class V, PLA2R antibodies, or malignancy.

questions.explanation: ANCA-associated vasculitides: GPA (granulomatosis with polyangiitis) — c-ANCA/PR3, upper/lower respiratory tract + kidneys (pulmonary-renal syndrome). MPA (microscopic polyangiitis) — p-ANCA/MPO, kidneys + lungs without granulomas. EGPA (eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis) — p-ANCA, asthma + eosinophilia + vasculitis. Treatment: induction with pulse corticosteroids + cyclophosphamide or rituximab, maintenance with azathioprine or rituximab.

questions.stem: PE primarily kills by:

choices.feedback: Hemorrhage is rare; the primary issue is obstruction.

Correct! PE → increased pulmonary vascular resistance → acute RV pressure overload → RV dilation → interventricular septum shifts left → decreased LV filling → cardiogenic shock. This is why massive PE requires emergent clot removal/lysis, not just oxygen.
The left ventricle fails secondarily from decreased filling, not primary infarction.
V/Q mismatch occurs but the lethal mechanism is hemodynamic.

questions.explanation: PE primarily kills through acute right ventricular (RV) failure. Massive PE causes sudden increase in pulmonary vascular resistance, which the thin-walled RV cannot overcome. The RV dilates, its wall tension increases, coronary perfusion to the RV decreases (the RV is perfused primarily in diastole), and the interventricular septum bows into the LV, impairing left ventricular filling (interventricular dependence). This cascade results in decreased cardiac output, hypotension, and ultimately obstructive shock and cardiovascular collapse. The amount of clot burden and the patient's baseline cardiopulmonary reserve determine whether this cascade is survivable.

This cascade results in decreased cardiac output, hypotension, and ultimately obstructive shock and cardiovascular collapse. The amount of clot burden and the patient's baseline cardiopulmonary reserve determine whether this cascade is survivable.

verifiedReferences.title: Pulmonary Embolism
verifiedReferences.authors: Vyas V, Goyal A

questions.stem: Low clinical probability (Wells <2). Next step?

choices.feedback: Correct! Low Wells → D-dimer. Negative D-dimer effectively excludes PE (high sensitivity ~95%). Positive D-dimer → CTPA. D-dimer is NOT useful in high-probability patients (too many false positives; go directly to CTPA).
Low probability should first undergo D-dimer to avoid unnecessary CT radiation/contrast.
Empiric treatment for low-probability PE without confirmatory testing is inappropriate.
V/Q is an alternative to CTPA, not a screening test for low-probability PE.

questions.explanation: In a patient with low clinical probability for PE (Wells score <2), D-dimer is the appropriate next step. A negative D-dimer in low-probability patients has a negative predictive value exceeding 99%, effectively ruling out PE without imaging. If D-dimer is positive, proceed to CTPA. In high clinical probability (Wells >6), skip D-dimer and go directly to CTPA — D-dimer lacks specificity in this population and a negative result does not safely exclude PE. The PERC rule can be applied before D-dimer in very low-risk patients to avoid unnecessary testing entirely.

verifiedReferences.title: Pulmonary Embolism
verifiedReferences.authors: Vyas V, Goyal A

questions.stem: Massive PE with SBP <90. Treatment?

choices.feedback: Warfarin takes days to work — inappropriate for acute massive PE.

Correct! Massive PE (hemodynamic instability): systemic tPA is first-line. Reduces mortality despite bleeding risk. If thrombolytics contraindicated: catheter-directed therapy or surgical embolectomy. Also: IV fluids (cautious — excess may worsen RV dilation), vasopressors (norepinephrine), avoid intubation if possible (positive pressure worsens RV).
Heparin alone is insufficient for massive PE with hemodynamic compromise.
IVC filters prevent future embolization but don't treat current massive PE.

questions.explanation: Massive PE with systolic BP <90 mmHg requires systemic thrombolysis (alteplase) as the primary treatment. This is the one situation where the survival benefit of thrombolysis clearly outweighs the bleeding risk. Thrombolytics work by activating plasminogen to plasmin, which lyses fibrin clots. If thrombolysis is contraindicated or fails, surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed therapy are alternatives. Concurrent IV unfractionated heparin is given for anticoagulation. Massive PE carries mortality exceeding 50% without aggressive intervention. Submassive PE (RV dysfunction without hypotension) may benefit from thrombolysis in select cases, but this remains controversial.

This is the one situation where the survival benefit of thrombolysis clearly outweighs the bleeding risk. Thrombolytics work by activating plasminogen to plasmin, which lyses fibrin clots. If thrombolysis is contraindicated or fails, surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed therapy are alternatives. Concurrent IV unfractionated heparin is given for anticoagulation. Massive PE carries mortality exceeding 50% without aggressive intervention. Submassive PE (RV dysfunction without hypotension) may benefit from thrombolysis in select cases, but this remains controversial.

verifiedReferences.authors: Vyas V, Goyal A

questions.stem: Factor V Leiden is:

choices.feedback: FVL affects the coagulation cascade, not platelet function.
Factor V Leiden is inherited, not acquired.

Correct! Factor V Leiden mutation: resistance to activated protein C → increased VTE risk. Heterozygous: 5-7x risk. Homozygous: 25-50x risk. Most common in Caucasians (5% carrier rate). Testing: activated protein C resistance assay, confirmed by genetic testing.
FVL primarily increases VENOUS thromboembolism risk.

questions.explanation: Factor V Leiden is the most common inherited thrombophilia, found in approximately 5% of Caucasians. The mutation (R506Q) makes Factor V resistant to cleavage by activated protein C, resulting in a hypercoagulable state. Heterozygous carriers have 3-8× increased VTE risk; homozygous carriers have up to 80× increased risk. The second most common inherited thrombophilia is prothrombin gene mutation (G20210A). Testing for inherited thrombophilia is generally not recommended during acute VTE episodes or while on anticoagulation, as results may be unreliable and rarely change acute management.

questions.stem: DOAC advantage over warfarin for PE treatment?

choices.text: They can be used in pregnancy
choices.feedback: DOACs are contraindicated in pregnancy. Use LMWH.
DOACs are typically more expensive than warfarin.

choices.text: No bridging needed, no INR monitoring, fewer drug interactions
choices.feedback: Correct! Apixaban/rivaroxaban: can start immediately without heparin bridging. No routine monitoring needed (vs INR for warfarin). Fewer food/drug interactions. Exceptions: use heparin → warfarin for antiphospholipid syndrome (DOACs inferior — TRAPS trial), severe CKD, mechanical valves, pregnancy (LMWH only).
DOACs still carry bleeding risk, just easier to manage than warfarin.

questions.explanation: DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban, dabigatran) offer several advantages over warfarin for PE treatment: no need for heparin bridging (rivaroxaban and apixaban start with a loading dose), no routine INR monitoring required, fewer drug-drug and drug-food interactions, predictable pharmacokinetics, and lower rates of intracranial hemorrhage. The EINSTEIN-PE, AMPLIFY, Hokusai-VTE, and RE-COVER trials established non-inferiority or superiority of DOACs. Limitations include lack of reliable monitoring for compliance, cost, and specific contraindications (severe renal impairment for some agents, mechanical heart valves, antiphospholipid syndrome).

verifiedReferences.title: Pulmonary Embolism
verifiedReferences.authors: Vyas V, Goyal A

questions.stem: Duration of anticoagulation for UNPROVOKED PE?

choices.feedback: Far too short for any PE.

Correct! Unprovoked (idiopathic) PE: recommended indefinite anticoagulation (reassess annually). Recurrence rate without anticoagulation ~10%/year. Provoked PE (surgery, immobility, OCP): 3 months. Cancer-associated: anticoagulate while cancer is active (LMWH or DOAC).
Duration depends on provoked vs unprovoked status.
Symptom resolution doesn't determine anticoagulation duration.

questions.explanation: Unprovoked PE (no identifiable transient risk factor) requires extended anticoagulation — at minimum 3 months, with strong consideration for indefinite/lifelong treatment. The recurrence risk after stopping anticoagulation for unprovoked VTE is approximately 10% at 1 year and 30% at 5 years. In contrast, provoked VTE (surgery, immobilization, OCP use) has a much lower recurrence risk and typically requires only 3 months of anticoagulation. The decision for indefinite therapy balances recurrence risk against bleeding risk (assessed by tools like HAS-BLED) and patient preference. Male sex, elevated D-dimer after stopping anticoagulation, and residual DVT favor extended therapy.

Male sex, elevated D-dimer after stopping anticoagulation, and residual DVT favor extended therapy.

questions.stem: EKG finding most commonly associated with PE?

choices.feedback: A-fib can occur with PE but is not the most common finding.
Heart block is not typically associated with PE.

Correct! Sinus tachycardia is the most common EKG finding in PE. Classic findings (less common): S1Q3T3 (S wave in I, Q wave and inverted T in III), right heart strain pattern, right axis deviation, RBBB. A normal EKG does NOT exclude PE.
That's anterior STEMI, not PE.

questions.explanation: Sinus tachycardia is the most common ECG finding in acute PE, present in approximately 40-50% of cases. While S1Q3T3 is the 'classic' textbook pattern, it is much less common (~20%). Other ECG findings include right axis deviation, right bundle branch block, T-wave inversions in V1-V4 (RV strain pattern), P-pulmonale, and new atrial fibrillation. Importantly, a normal ECG does not exclude PE. ECG changes in PE reflect acute right heart strain from increased pulmonary vascular resistance. The ECG helps exclude other causes of chest pain (STEMI, pericarditis) but is not diagnostic for PE.

While S1Q3T3 is the 'classic' textbook pattern, it is much less common (~20%). questions.stem: ABG pattern in acute PE?

choices.text: Respiratory alkalosis with elevated A-a gradient
choices.feedback: Correct! Hyperventilation from dyspnea → low PaCO2 → respiratory alkalosis. Elevated A-a gradient (normal <15 in young adults) reflects V/Q mismatch. However, up to 20% of PEs have normal A-a gradient — does NOT exclude PE.
Most PEs show ABG abnormalities, though a normal ABG can occur.
PE causes respiratory alkalosis, not metabolic acidosis (unless shock develops).
Respiratory acidosis suggests hypoventilation (COPD, neuromuscular), not PE.

questions.explanation: The classic ABG pattern in acute PE is respiratory alkalosis (low PaCO2 from hyperventilation) with an elevated alveolar-arterial (A-a) oxygen gradient. The A-a gradient is elevated because PE creates dead-space ventilation — ventilated but unperfused lung segments. Tachypnea-driven hyperventilation lowers PaCO2 (respiratory alkalosis). Hypoxemia may be present but is not universal — up to 20% of PE patients have a normal PaO2. A normal ABG does NOT exclude PE. The A-a gradient is the most reliable ABG finding, as it reflects the V/Q mismatch caused by the clot.

The A-a gradient is elevated because PE creates dead-space ventilation — ventilated but unperfused lung segments. Tachypnea-driven hyperventilation lowers PaCO2 (respiratory alkalosis). Hypoxemia may be present but is not universal — up to 20% of PE patients have a normal PaO2. A normal ABG does NOT exclude PE. The A-a gradient is the most reliable ABG finding, as it reflects the V/Q mismatch caused by the clot.

questions.stem: What is the MOST IMPORTANT cell in wound healing?

choices.feedback: Platelets initiate hemostasis but are not the key coordinator of wound healing.
Fibroblasts produce collagen (proliferative phase) but macrophages are more critical as overall coordinators.

Correct! Macrophages are the MOST IMPORTANT cell in wound healing. They coordinate the transition from inflammation to proliferation, release growth factors (PDGF, TGF-β, VEGF, FGF), phagocytose debris, and orchestrate all subsequent phases. Wound healing can proceed without neutrophils but NOT without macrophages.
Neutrophils arrive first (24-48h) and clear debris/bacteria, but are not the most important coordinator.

questions.explanation: Wound healing phases: (1) Hemostasis (seconds-minutes: platelet plug, coagulation cascade), (2) Inflammation (hours-days: neutrophils first, then macrophages — key orchestrators), (3) Proliferation (days-weeks: fibroblasts, collagen deposition, angiogenesis, granulation tissue), (4) Remodeling (weeks-years: type III collagen replaced by type I, maximum wound strength ~80% of original). Factors impairing healing: diabetes, malnutrition, infection, smoking, corticosteroids, radiation, peripheral vascular disease.

questions.stem: Maximum tensile strength a healed wound reaches?

choices.feedback: Healed wounds NEVER reach full original strength.
Wounds do not reach 95%. 80% is the maximum.
50% is reached by about 6 weeks but continues to improve to ~80%.

Correct! Maximum tensile strength = 80% of unwounded tissue. Type III collagen (immature) is replaced by Type I (mature), and cross-linking increases strength over months. This is why surgical sites and healed wounds remain weaker than native tissue permanently.

questions.explanation: Wound healing phases: (1) Hemostasis (seconds-minutes: platelet plug, coagulation cascade), (2) Inflammation (hours-days: neutrophils first, then macrophages — key orchestrators), (3) Proliferation (days-weeks: fibroblasts, collagen deposition, angiogenesis, granulation tissue), (4) Remodeling (weeks-years: type III collagen replaced by type I, maximum wound strength ~80% of original). Factors impairing healing: diabetes, malnutrition, infection, smoking, corticosteroids, radiation, peripheral vascular disease.

questions.stem: Vitamin C deficiency impairs wound healing by affecting:

choices.feedback: Correct! Vitamin C is an essential cofactor for prolyl and lysyl hydroxylase, enzymes that hydroxylate proline and lysine residues in collagen. Without hydroxylation, collagen cannot form stable triple helices. Deficiency = scurvy: wound breakdown, bleeding gums, poor healing.
DNA synthesis is primarily zinc-dependent, not vitamin C-dependent.
Epithelialization is more dependent on vitamin A than vitamin C.
Platelet function is not directly vitamin C-dependent.

questions.explanation: Correct: Collagen synthesis (proline/lysine hydroxylation).

questions.stem: Which vitamin REVERSES steroid-impaired wound healing?

choices.feedback: Vitamin C is critical for collagen synthesis but does not specifically reverse steroid effects.
Vitamin K is essential for clotting factors but not for reversing steroid wound impairment.

Correct! Vitamin A counteracts steroid-induced wound healing impairment by increasing macrophage activity, inflammation, and collagen synthesis. HIGH-YIELD pearl. Important: Vitamin A does NOT reverse impairment from diabetes, radiation, or other causes — only steroids.
Vitamin D is important for bone metabolism but does not reverse steroid wound impairment.

questions.explanation: Wound healing phases: (1) Hemostasis (seconds-minutes: platelet plug, coagulation cascade), (2) Inflammation (hours-days: neutrophils first, then macrophages — key orchestrators), (3) Proliferation (days-weeks: fibroblasts, collagen deposition, angiogenesis, granulation tissue), (4) Remodeling (weeks-years: type III collagen replaced by type I, maximum wound strength ~80% of original). Factors impairing healing: diabetes, malnutrition, infection, smoking, corticosteroids, radiation, peripheral vascular disease.

questions.stem: What distinguishes a keloid from a hypertrophic scar?

choices.text: Hypertrophic scars occur only on the face
choices.feedback: Hypertrophic scars can occur anywhere, not limited to the face.

choices.text: Keloids extend BEYOND wound borders; hypertrophic scars stay WITHIN
choices.feedback: Correct! Keloid = extends beyond original wound margins, does not regress, recurs after excision. Hypertrophic = raised but contained within wound borders, often improves over time. Keloids more common in dark-skinned individuals, earlobes, sternum, deltoid.

choices.text: Keloids are painful; hypertrophic scars are not
choices.feedback: Both can be painful or pruritic.
Both may require treatment depending on symptoms and cosmetic concerns.

questions.explanation: Surgical decision-making requires systematic assessment using evidence-based algorithms. Key principles include appropriate preoperative risk stratification, understanding surgical anatomy and pathophysiology, and recognizing conditions requiring emergent vs elective intervention.

questions.stem: Wound left open to heal by granulation and contraction is:

choices.feedback: Correct! Secondary intention: wound left open, heals from bottom up with granulation tissue formation and contraction by myofibroblasts. Examples: abscess cavity after I&D, pressure ulcers, burns. Results in larger scar.
Tertiary (delayed primary) = wound left open initially then closed 3-5 days later after contamination risk passes.
There is no quaternary intention in wound healing classification.
Primary = edges approximated and closed (surgical incision with sutures).

questions.explanation: Wound healing phases: (1) Hemostasis (seconds-minutes: platelet plug, coagulation cascade), (2) Inflammation (hours-days: neutrophils first, then macrophages — key orchestrators), (3) Proliferation (days-weeks: fibroblasts, collagen deposition, angiogenesis, granulation tissue), (4) Remodeling (weeks-years: type III collagen replaced by type I, maximum wound strength ~80% of original). Factors impairing healing: diabetes, malnutrition, infection, smoking, corticosteroids, radiation, peripheral vascular disease.

questions.stem: Pre-albumin is preferred over albumin for assessing nutritional status because:

choices.feedback: Correct! Pre-albumin (transthyretin) half-life = 2-3 days vs albumin half-life = 20 days. Pre-albumin reflects recent nutritional changes, making it a better marker for acute nutritional status and response to nutritional support. Albumin reflects chronic status and is affected by many non-nutritional factors (inflammation, liver disease).
Pre-albumin does not measure collagen synthesis. It is a hepatic protein.
While malnutrition increases infection risk, pre-albumin specifically reflects acute nutritional status.
Albumin is actually more widely available. Pre-albumin's advantage is different.

questions.explanation: Key detail: life (2 days vs 20 days), reflecting acute nutritional changes.

questions.stem: The #1 modifiable risk factor for impaired wound healing?

choices.feedback: Age is a risk factor but is not modifiable.
Malnutrition is modifiable and important, but smoking has the most evidence as a modifiable risk factor.
Diabetes significantly impairs healing but is a chronic condition that is managed, not eliminated.

Correct! Smoking is the #1 modifiable risk factor. Nicotine causes vasoconstriction → tissue hypoxia, carbon monoxide reduces oxygen-carrying capacity, and cigarette chemicals impair inflammatory cell function. Smoking cessation at least 4-8 weeks before elective surgery significantly improves wound healing outcomes.

questions.explanation: Wound healing phases: (1) Hemostasis (seconds-minutes: platelet plug, coagulation cascade), (2) Inflammation (hours-days: neutrophils first, then macrophages — key orchestrators), (3) Proliferation (days-weeks: fibroblasts, collagen deposition, angiogenesis, granulation tissue), (4) Remodeling (weeks-years: type III collagen replaced by type I, maximum wound strength ~80% of original). Factors impairing healing: diabetes, malnutrition, infection, smoking, corticosteroids, radiation, peripheral vascular disease.

questions.stem: Which virulence factor of S. aureus causes scalded skin syndrome?

choices.feedback: PVL destroys WBCs and is associated with necrotizing pneumonia and severe skin infections.
TSST-1 causes toxic shock syndrome via superantigen activity.
Correct! Exfoliative toxins A and B cleave desmoglein-1 in the granular layer of the epidermis, causing superficial skin sloughing (SSSS). Targets neonates and young children.
Protein A binds IgG Fc region, preventing opsonization.

questions.stem: MRSA resistance is mediated by:

choices.feedback: Beta-lactamase confers penicillin resistance but not methicillin resistance.
Porin mutations are a gram-negative resistance mechanism (e.g., Pseudomonas).

choices.text: Altered PBP2a encoded by mecA gene
choices.feedback: Correct! The mecA gene encodes PBP2a (penicillin-binding protein 2a), which has low affinity for all beta-lactams. This is carried on the SCCmec cassette. Treatment: vancomycin, daptomycin, linezolid, or TMP-SMX depending on site.
Efflux pumps contribute to tetracycline resistance, not methicillin resistance.

questions.stem: S. aureus endocarditis most commonly affects which valve in IV drug users?

choices.feedback: Correct! IVDU-associated S. aureus endocarditis classically affects the tricuspid valve (right-sided). Presents with septic pulmonary emboli — multiple peripheral nodular infiltrates on CXR. Left-sided endocarditis is more common in non-IVDU patients.
Pulmonary valve endocarditis is rare in all populations.
Mitral valve endocarditis is more common with viridans streptococci.
Aortic valve is the second most common overall but not the classic IVDU association.

questions.stem: Coagulase test differentiates S. aureus from:

choices.feedback: Streptococci are differentiated by catalase test (catalase-negative).
Enterococci are differentiated from streptococci by growth in 6.5% NaCl and bile esculin.

Correct! Coagulase converts fibrinogen to fibrin, forming a clot. S. aureus is coagulase-positive; S. epidermidis and S. saprophyticus are coagulase-negative. This is the key differentiating test within Staphylococcus genus.
Pseudomonas is a gram-negative rod, already morphologically distinct.

questions.stem: Which S. aureus toxin acts as a superantigen?

choices.feedback: Correct! TSST-1 (toxic shock syndrome toxin-1) is a superantigen that cross-links MHC II on APCs with TCR Vβ region on T cells, bypassing normal antigen processing. Causes massive nonspecific T-cell activation and cytokine storm — fever, hypotension, diffuse rash, desquamation.
Coagulase is an enzyme that clots plasma, not a superantigen.
Exfoliative toxin targets desmoglein-1 in skin.
Alpha-toxin is a pore-forming hemolysin.

questions.stem: The most important virulence factor of S. pneumoniae is:

choices.feedback: M protein is a virulence factor of Group A Strep (S. pyogenes).

Correct! The polysaccharide capsule prevents complement-mediated opsonization and phagocytosis. It is the basis for the pneumococcal vaccines (PCV13, PPSV23). Quellung reaction (capsular swelling) is the diagnostic test. Asplenic patients are especially vulnerable because the spleen filters encapsulated organisms.
Pneumolysin is a pore-forming toxin that contributes to pathogenesis but the capsule is the key virulence factor.
IgA protease aids mucosal colonization but is not the primary virulence factor.

questions.stem: S. pneumoniae is differentiated from other alpha-hemolytic streptococci by:

choices.feedback: MacConkey agar selects for gram-negative organisms.
All streptococci are catalase-negative.
Bacitracin sensitivity differentiates Group A Strep from other beta-hemolytic streptococci.

Correct! S. pneumoniae is optochin-sensitive (zone of inhibition around optochin disk) and bile-soluble (lysed by bile salts/deoxycholate). Viridans streptococci are optochin-resistant and bile-insoluble. Both show alpha hemolysis (green) on blood agar.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old asplenic patient presents with fulminant sepsis. Blood cultures show lancet-shaped gram-positive diplococci. The organism's ability to evade this patient's immune system is primarily due to:

choices.feedback: Intracellular survival is characteristic of Mycobacterium, Listeria, and Salmonella.
Antigenic variation is seen in Neisseria gonorrhoeae (pilin) and Borrelia (VlsE).

Correct! The polysaccharide capsule resists complement deposition. The spleen is critical for clearing encapsulated bacteria because splenic macrophages filter opsonized organisms from blood. Asplenic patients need vaccination against S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis, and H. influenzae type b.
Biofilm formation is characteristic of S. epidermidis on hardware.

questions.stem: The most common cause of bacterial meningitis in adults is:

choices.feedback: N. meningitidis is most common in adolescents and young adults living in close quarters.
Listeria causes meningitis in neonates, elderly, and immunocompromised — add ampicillin for these populations.
GBS is the most common cause of neonatal meningitis.

Correct! S. pneumoniae is the most common cause of bacterial meningitis in adults (>18 years). Empiric treatment: vancomycin + ceftriaxone + dexamethasone (steroids before or with first antibiotic dose reduce mortality and hearing loss). N. meningitidis is second.

questions.stem: Which pneumococcal infection is NOT typically caused by S. pneumoniae?

choices.feedback: S. pneumoniae is the most common cause of acute otitis media.
S. pneumoniae is the most common cause of community-acquired pneumonia with classic rust-colored sputum.

Correct! S. pneumoniae does not commonly cause UTIs. The classic triad of pneumococcal infections: pneumonia (most common CAP), meningitis (most common in adults), and otitis media (most common in children). UTIs are caused by gram-negatives (E. coli) or enterococci.
S. pneumoniae is a common cause of acute bacterial sinusitis.

questions.stem: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis is associated with which antibody finding?

choices.feedback: ANCA is associated with vasculitis (GPA, MPA, EGPA).

Correct! Elevated ASO titers confirm recent GAS infection. PSGN occurs 1-3 weeks after pharyngitis or 3-6 weeks after skin infection. Features: nephritic syndrome with hematuria, RBC casts, hypertension, low C3 complement. Unlike rheumatic fever, PSGN can follow either pharyngeal or skin GAS infection.
Anti-phospholipid antibodies are seen in antiphospholipid syndrome.
Anti-dsDNA is seen in lupus nephritis.

questions.stem: The Jones criteria are used to diagnose:

choices.feedback: Correct! Jones criteria require evidence of prior GAS pharyngitis PLUS 2 major criteria OR 1 major + 2 minor. Major: Joint (migratory polyarthritis), Obvious (carditis), Nodules (subcutaneous), Erythema marginatum, Sydenham chorea. Minor: fever, arthralgia, elevated ESR/CRP, prolonged PR interval. Rheumatic fever only follows pharyngitis, NOT skin infection.
Scarlet fever is diagnosed clinically by sandpaper rash + pharyngitis + strawberry tongue.
Necrotizing fasciitis is a surgical emergency diagnosed by LRINEC score and clinical findings.
PSGN is diagnosed clinically with nephritic findings + evidence of recent strep infection.

questions.stem: GAS is differentiated from other beta-hemolytic streptococci by:

choices.feedback: Bile esculin positivity identifies Enterococcus and Group D Streptococcus.

Correct! GAS (S. pyogenes) is bacitracin-sensitive (zone of inhibition around bacitracin disk). GBS (S. agalactiae) is bacitracin-resistant. Alternative: Lancefield grouping — GAS has Group A antigen detected by latex agglutination. Rapid strep tests detect the Group A carbohydrate antigen.
Optochin differentiates S. pneumoniae from viridans streptococci (both alpha-hemolytic).
Coagulase differentiates S. aureus from coagulase-negative staphylococci.

questions.stem: The M protein of S. pyogenes contributes to virulence by:

choices.feedback: Exfoliative toxin is produced by S. aureus.

choices.text: Inhibiting phagocytosis by binding complement regulators
choices.feedback: Correct! M protein extends from the cell surface and binds factor H and fibrinogen, inhibiting complement activation and opsonophagocytosis. It is the major virulence factor and basis for >200 serotypes. Molecular mimicry between M protein and cardiac myosin drives autoimmune cross-reactivity in rheumatic fever.

choices.text: Producing catalase to resist oxidative killing
choices.feedback: Streptococci are catalase-negative. Catalase is a staphylococcal feature.
Biofilm on valves is seen with S. epidermidis on prosthetic valves.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old with a sore throat tests positive on rapid strep. Penicillin is prescribed primarily to prevent:

choices.feedback: Preventing suppurative complications is a secondary benefit, not the primary indication.
Antibiotics do NOT prevent PSGN (already triggered by the time of diagnosis).

Correct! The primary reason to treat GAS pharyngitis is to prevent acute rheumatic fever. Antibiotics within 9 days of symptom onset prevent ARF. Secondary benefits: faster symptom resolution, reduced transmission. Note: antibiotics do NOT prevent PSGN. Penicillin remains first-line (no GAS resistance to penicillin).
Scarlet fever develops early in disease; treatment shortens course but prevention isn't the primary goal.

questions.stem: GBS screening during pregnancy occurs at:

choices.feedback: First visit screens for rubella, syphilis, HIV, and blood type, not GBS.

Correct! Universal vaginal-rectal GBS screening at 35-37 weeks. If positive, give intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (IAP) — penicillin G IV during labor. Also give IAP if: GBS bacteriuria in current pregnancy, prior GBS-positive infant, unknown status with risk factors (preterm <37 weeks, ROM >18 hours, fever).
Screening should occur before labor to allow planning; however, rapid intrapartum testing is available.
The anatomy scan occurs at 20 weeks, not GBS screening.

questions.stem: The most common cause of neonatal meningitis in the first week of life is:

choices.feedback: S. pneumoniae causes meningitis in adults, not typically neonates.
E. coli K1 is the second most common cause of early-onset neonatal meningitis.

Correct! GBS is the most common cause of early-onset neonatal sepsis and meningitis (first 7 days of life). Acquired during passage through colonized birth canal. Late-onset (7-90 days) can also be GBS. Empiric neonatal meningitis treatment: ampicillin + gentamicin (or cefotaxime).
Listeria is a less common but important cause — covered by ampicillin in empiric regimen.

questions.stem: GBS is identified in the lab by:

choices.feedback: Bile esculin identifies Enterococcus and Group D Streptococcus (S. bovis/gallolyticus).
Bacitracin sensitivity identifies Group A Strep.
Optochin identifies S. pneumoniae.

Correct! GBS produces CAMP factor that enhances the hemolysis zone of S. aureus beta-hemolysin (arrowhead-shaped zone). Also positive for hippurate hydrolysis. Lancefield Group B antigen. Beta-hemolytic on blood agar.

questions.explanation: Best answer: CAMP test positivity and hippurate hydrolysis.

questions.stem: Which is TRUE about GBS intrapartum prophylaxis?

choices.text: A negative screen at 35 weeks means no prophylaxis is ever needed
choices.feedback: If GBS bacteriuria occurred during pregnancy or prior infant had GBS disease, prophylaxis is still needed.

choices.text: IV penicillin G during labor is the recommended regimen
choices.feedback: Correct! Penicillin G 5 million units IV loading then 2.5-3 million units q4h until delivery. Adequate prophylaxis = at least 4 hours before delivery. Penicillin-allergic: cefazolin if low-risk allergy, clindamycin (if susceptible) or vancomycin if high-risk allergy.

choices.text: Oral amoxicillin is given at 36 weeks
choices.feedback: Prophylaxis is IV (not oral) and given during labor (not before).

choices.text: Prophylaxis is only needed if the mother is febrile
choices.feedback: All GBS-positive mothers need IAP regardless of symptoms.

questions.stem: An adult patient with recurrent GBS bacteremia should be evaluated for:

choices.feedback: Sickle cell predisposes to encapsulated organisms (S. pneumoniae, Salmonella).
IgA deficiency predisposes to sinopulmonary and GI mucosal infections.

Correct! In non-pregnant adults, GBS infections (UTI, skin/soft tissue, bacteremia, endocarditis) are associated with diabetes mellitus, liver cirrhosis, malignancy, and immunosuppression. GBS is an increasingly recognized pathogen in elderly diabetic patients.
CF predisposes to Pseudomonas and S. aureus respiratory infections.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Diabetes mellitus and liver disease.

questions.stem: Enterococci are intrinsically resistant to:

choices.feedback: VRE is acquired resistance; most enterococci remain vancomycin-susceptible.
Linezolid is one of the treatments for VRE.
Ampicillin is actually a first-line treatment for susceptible enterococcal infections.

Correct! Enterococci have intrinsic resistance to all cephalosporins, as well as low-level aminoglycosides, clindamycin, and TMP-SMX (in vivo). This is clinically critical: a patient with enterococcal bacteremia treated with cephalosporins will not improve. Ampicillin or vancomycin are first-line.

questions.stem: The bile esculin test identifies:

choices.feedback: Correct! Bile esculin agar: organism grows in 40% bile AND hydrolyzes esculin (turns agar black). Positive for Enterococcus and S. gallolyticus (formerly S. bovis). Enterococcus is further differentiated by growth in 6.5% NaCl (positive) vs. S. gallolyticus (negative). S. gallolyticus bacteremia warrants colonoscopy (associated with colon cancer).
Viridans streptococci are bile esculin-negative.
Listeria is identified by tumbling motility and growth at 4°C.
S. aureus is identified by coagulase and catalase tests.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Enterococcus and Group D Streptococcus.

questions.stem: VRE resistance is mediated by:

choices.feedback: Porin mutations are a gram-negative resistance mechanism.
Beta-lactamase confers penicillin resistance, not vancomycin resistance.

Correct! vanA and vanB genes encode enzymes that modify the peptidoglycan precursor from D-Ala-D-Ala to D-Ala-D-Lac, reducing vancomycin binding 1000-fold. vanA: high-level resistance to vancomycin AND teicoplanin. vanB: resistance to vancomycin only. Treatment: linezolid or daptomycin.
PBP2a alteration is the MRSA resistance mechanism.

questions.explanation: Key detail: Ala-D-Ala in peptidoglycan.

questions.stem: Enterococcal endocarditis treatment requires synergistic bactericidal therapy because:

choices.text: Enterococci are intrinsically tolerant to killing by cell wall agents alone
choices.feedback: Correct! Enterococci are tolerant (inhibited but not killed by ampicillin/vancomycin alone). Bactericidal synergy requires combination: ampicillin + gentamicin (or ampicillin + ceftriaxone for E. faecalis). Must check for high-level aminoglycoside resistance (HLAR) — if present, synergy is lost.

choices.text: The biofilm on valves blocks all single antibiotics
choices.feedback: While biofilm contributes, the primary issue is intrinsic tolerance to bactericidal killing.

choices.text: Enterococci rapidly develop resistance during monotherapy
choices.feedback: Combination therapy is for synergistic killing, not resistance prevention.
Enterococci are facultative anaerobes.

questions.stem: Which is the most common cause of hospital-acquired UTIs?

choices.feedback: Pseudomonas is a common cause of hospital-acquired pneumonia and catheter infections, but not the most common UTI pathogen.
Klebsiella is a common UTI pathogen but not the most common overall.

Correct! E. coli remains the most common cause of both community and hospital-acquired UTIs. However, Enterococcus is the second most common cause of hospital-acquired UTIs (especially catheter-associated). Key: if treating a hospital-acquired UTI with cephalosporins and patient is not improving, suspect Enterococcus (intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins).
Enterococcus is the second most common hospital-acquired UTI pathogen, not the first.

questions.stem: EHEC (E. coli O157:H7) causes hemolytic uremic syndrome via:

choices.feedback: Correct! Shiga-like toxin (Stx) inhibits 60S ribosomal subunit → kills renal glomerular endothelial cells → microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia + acute renal failure (HUS triad). Do NOT give antibiotics (may increase toxin release). Supportive care only.

choices.text: Type III secretion system injecting effector proteins
choices.feedback: Type III secretion (EPEC) causes attaching/effacing lesions but not HUS.
Heat-labile toxin is produced by ETEC, causing traveler's diarrhea.
K1 capsule is associated with neonatal meningitis E. coli strains.

questions.stem: The most common cause of community-acquired UTI is:

choices.feedback: Klebsiella is a common UTI pathogen but second to E. coli.
Proteus causes UTIs (especially staghorn calculi) but is less common than E. coli.

Correct! E. coli causes ~80% of uncomplicated community-acquired UTIs. Virulence factors: P fimbriae (bind uroepithelium), K capsule (resists phagocytosis). First-line treatment: nitrofurantoin or TMP-SMX (if local resistance <20%).
Enterococcus is more common in hospital-acquired/catheter-associated UTIs.

questions.stem: ETEC is the most common cause of:

choices.feedback: Correct! ETEC produces heat-labile toxin (LT, activates adenylate cyclase → cAMP → watery diarrhea, similar to cholera toxin) and heat-stable toxin (ST, activates guanylate cyclase → cGMP). Watery, non-bloody diarrhea. Self-limited; rifaximin or azithromycin if needed.
Hemorrhagic colitis (bloody diarrhea) is caused by EHEC O157:H7.
Invasive dysentery is caused by EIEC (enteroinvasive E. coli) and Shigella.
Neonatal meningitis is caused by K1 capsule strains.

questions.stem: E. coli is differentiated from other gram-negative rods by:

choices.feedback: H2S production is characteristic of Salmonella and Proteus.
This cold-motility pattern is characteristic of Listeria and Yersinia.
E. coli is oxidase-negative. Pseudomonas is oxidase-positive.

choices.text: Growth on MacConkey agar with pink lactose-fermenting colonies
choices.feedback: Correct! E. coli ferments lactose → produces acid → turns MacConkey agar pink. Also grows on EMB agar with characteristic green metallic sheen. Lactose fermenters on MacConkey: E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterobacter, Citrobacter, Serratia.

questions.stem: Extended-spectrum beta-lactamase (ESBL) producing E. coli is treated with:

choices.feedback: ESBLs hydrolyze third-generation cephalosporins — this is the defining feature.
ESBLs are not reliably inhibited by standard beta-lactamase inhibitors.
ESBL-producing organisms often have co-resistance to fluoroquinolones.

Correct! Carbapenems (meropenem, ertapenem, imipenem) are the treatment of choice for serious ESBL infections. ESBLs hydrolyze penicillins, cephalosporins (including 3rd/4th gen), and aztreonam. Piperacillin-tazobactam may work for UTIs but carbapenems preferred for serious infections.

questions.stem: Klebsiella pneumoniae pneumonia classically presents with:

choices.feedback: Bronchorrhea suggests bronchoalveolar carcinoma.

choices.text: Dry cough with diffuse bilateral infiltrates
choices.feedback: This pattern suggests atypical pneumonia (Mycoplasma, PCP).

choices.text: Currant jelly sputum with upper lobe cavitation in alcoholics
choices.feedback: Correct! Klebsiella pneumonia classically affects alcoholics, diabetics, and debilitated patients. Thick, bloody (currant jelly) sputum. CXR: upper lobe cavitary pneumonia with bulging fissure sign (heavy, mucoid consolidation). Aspiration predisposes to upper lobe involvement.

choices.text: Rusty sputum in a young healthy patient
choices.feedback: Rusty sputum is classic for S. pneumoniae.

questions.stem: The large mucoid capsule of Klebsiella contributes to:

choices.feedback: Klebsiella causes respiratory and urinary infections, not invasive colitis.
The capsule actually impedes antibiotic and immune cell access.
Klebsiella is not a primary cause of diarrhea.

choices.text: Resistance to phagocytosis and characteristic string test
choices.feedback: Correct! The thick polysaccharide capsule gives Klebsiella its mucoid colonies on agar and resistance to phagocytosis. Hypervirulent K. pneumoniae (hvKP) has a hypermucoviscous phenotype detected by the string test (>5mm string when colony is pulled). hvKP causes liver abscesses, endophthalmitis, and meningitis.

questions.stem: Klebsiella is a common cause of all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: Hypervirulent Klebsiella is a major cause of pyogenic liver abscess, especially in Asia.

Correct! Klebsiella rarely causes endocarditis. Common Klebsiella infections: pneumonia (aspiration, HAP), UTI, hepatobiliary infections, pyogenic liver abscess (hypervirulent strains), wound infections, and bacteremia. Endocarditis is predominantly caused by Staphylococci, Streptococci, and Enterococci (HACEK group for culture-negative).
Klebsiella is the second most common gram-negative cause of UTI.
Klebsiella is a leading cause of HAP/VAP.

questions.stem: Carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella (CRE/KPC) should be treated with:

choices.feedback: Correct! Novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations are first-line for KPC-producing CRE: ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, imipenem-relebactam. Alternatives: cefiderocol, tigecycline-based combinations. Colistin is a last-resort agent with significant nephrotoxicity.
Vancomycin has no gram-negative activity.
KPC enzymes hydrolyze piperacillin-tazobactam.
CRE is resistant to carbapenems by definition.

questions.stem: On MacConkey agar, Klebsiella produces:

choices.feedback: Non-lactose fermenters (Salmonella, Shigella, Proteus) are clear/colorless.

Correct! Klebsiella ferments lactose (pink on MacConkey) and forms large, mucoid colonies due to its prominent polysaccharide capsule. The mucoid appearance distinguishes it from E. coli (pink but not mucoid). On EMB agar, Klebsiella forms pink-purple mucoid colonies (vs. E. coli green metallic sheen).

choices.text: Swarming colonies covering the entire plate
choices.feedback: Swarming motility is characteristic of Proteus species.

choices.text: Green pigmented colonies with grape-like odor
choices.feedback: Green pigment (pyocyanin) and grape-like odor are Pseudomonas features.

questions.stem: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is identified by all of the following EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: Pseudomonas is oxidase-positive, distinguishing it from Enterobacteriaceae.

Correct! Pseudomonas is a non-lactose fermenter (colorless/clear colonies on MacConkey). This distinguishes it from E. coli and Klebsiella. Pseudomonas is an obligate aerobe, oxidase-positive, produces pyocyanin (blue-green) and pyoverdin (yellow-green), and has a grape-like odor.
Pyocyanin gives Pseudomonas its characteristic blue-green color.
The distinctive grape-like odor is a classic feature.

questions.stem: Empiric anti-pseudomonal therapy for suspected Pseudomonas bacteremia includes:

choices.feedback: Correct! Anti-pseudomonal agents: piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, ceftazidime, meropenem, aztreonam, ciprofloxacin, aminoglycosides. Double coverage (two classes) is recommended for serious infections until susceptibilities return. Pseudomonas has intrinsic and acquired resistance mechanisms.
Neither has gram-negative aerobic activity.
Ampicillin-sulbactam has no Pseudomonas activity.
Ceftriaxone has no Pseudomonas activity. Azithromycin has no gram-negative activity.

questions.stem: Ecthyma gangrenosum is pathognomonic for:

choices.feedback: MRSA causes septic emboli but not ecthyma gangrenosum.
Meningococcemia causes petechial and purpuric lesions.

Correct! Ecthyma gangrenosum: round, painless, necrotic skin lesions (black eschar with erythematous rim) in neutropenic patients with Pseudomonas bacteremia. Due to perivascular bacterial invasion causing vascular necrosis. Almost pathognomonic for Pseudomonas sepsis.
DGI causes petechial or pustular skin lesions, not necrotic eschars.

questions.stem: Hot tub folliculitis is caused by:

choices.feedback: Dermatophytes cause tinea infections, not folliculitis.
Candida causes satellite lesion rash in skin folds, not folliculitis.
S. aureus causes typical folliculitis but not the hot tub variety.

Correct! Pseudomonas thrives in warm, moist environments (hot tubs, swimming pools). Causes self-limited pruritic papulopustular rash on trunk (areas covered by bathing suit). Usually resolves without treatment. Also causes malignant otitis externa (diabetic/elderly), swimmer's ear, and wound infections after burns.

questions.stem: Malignant (necrotizing) otitis externa most commonly occurs in:

choices.feedback: Ear piercing infections are usually S. aureus or Pseudomonas but not malignant otitis.
NICU Pseudomonas infections are typically respiratory or bloodstream.
Children get simple swimmer's ear, not malignant otitis externa.

Correct! Malignant otitis externa: Pseudomonas invades from ear canal into temporal bone and skull base. Almost exclusively in elderly diabetics. Presents: severe otalgia, granulation tissue in ear canal, cranial nerve palsies (facial nerve CN VII most common). Can be fatal. Treat with prolonged IV anti-pseudomonal therapy.

questions.stem: Salmonella typhi spreads to the bloodstream via:

choices.text: Uptake by M cells and survival within macrophages
choices.feedback: Correct! Salmonella is a facultative intracellular organism. Ingested → crosses intestinal epithelium via M cells → phagocytosed by macrophages → survives within phagosomes → disseminates via reticuloendothelial system to liver, spleen, bone marrow, gallbladder. This intracellular survival is why bacteremia and systemic disease occur.
Shigella invades colonic mucosa but does not typically cause bacteremia.

choices.text: Direct hematogenous seeding from GI tract
choices.feedback: The bacteria must first be internalized by macrophages.
Salmonella's systemic spread is through intracellular survival, not toxin-mediated.

questions.stem: Shigella dysentery differs from Salmonella gastroenteritis because Shigella:

choices.text: Has a very low infectious dose and invades colonic mucosa
choices.feedback: Correct! Shigella has an extremely low infectious dose (~10-100 organisms vs. 10^5-10^6 for Salmonella). Shigella invades colonic epithelium using Type III secretion system → bloody diarrhea (dysentery) with mucus, tenesmus, fecal WBCs. Does NOT typically cause bacteremia (unlike Salmonella). S. dysenteriae produces Shiga toxin → can cause HUS.
Shigella is exclusively human-to-human (no animal reservoir).
Salmonella produces H2S; Shigella does not.

choices.text: Is transmitted by contaminated poultry and eggs
choices.feedback: Poultry/eggs = Salmonella. Shigella is fecal-oral (person-to-person, flies, water).

questions.stem: A patient with sickle cell disease presenting with osteomyelitis — the most likely pathogen is:

choices.feedback: Pseudomonas osteomyelitis is associated with puncture wounds through shoes.

Correct! Sickle cell patients have functional asplenia and vascular infarction of bone — Salmonella is the most common cause of osteomyelitis in sickle cell disease. S. aureus is still the most common overall osteomyelitis pathogen, even in SCD, but Salmonella's association with SCD is a classic board teaching point.
GBS causes neonatal bone infections but not the classic SCD association.
S. aureus is the most common overall cause of osteomyelitis, but in sickle cell, Salmonella is classically associated.

questions.stem: Antibiotic treatment is recommended for Shigella but NOT uncomplicated Salmonella gastroenteritis because:

choices.text: Shigella is always resistant to standard antibiotics
choices.feedback: Shigella is treatable with fluoroquinolones or azithromycin.
Salmonella is a bacterial pathogen.

choices.text: Shigella only responds to IV antibiotics
choices.feedback: Oral fluoroquinolones or azithromycin are effective for Shigella.

Correct! Salmonella gastroenteritis: self-limited, antibiotics prolong fecal shedding and carrier state (do NOT treat unless bacteremia, extremes of age, immunocompromised, or prosthetic hardware). Shigella: antibiotics shorten illness and reduce transmission (treat all cases). Typhoid fever (S. typhi) always requires antibiotics.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Antibiotics shorten Shigella illness and reduce transmission; antibiotics prolong Salmonella carrier state.

questions.stem: Rose spots on the abdomen are associated with:

choices.feedback: Shigella causes dysentery without systemic skin findings.
EHEC causes hemorrhagic colitis and HUS, not rose spots.
Cholera causes profuse rice-water diarrhea without skin findings.

Correct! Rose spots: faint, salmon-colored, blanching maculopapular lesions on trunk during the second week of typhoid fever. S. typhi causes a systemic febrile illness (not just gastroenteritis): stepwise fever, relative bradycardia, hepatosplenomegaly, rose spots. Diagnosed by blood culture (first week) or stool culture (second week).

questions.explanation: Correct: Salmonella typhi (typhoid fever).

questions.stem: H. pylori produces urease to:

choices.feedback: Adhesion involves BabA and other adhesins, not urease.
PPIs inhibit proton pumps; H. pylori urease works by generating ammonia.
CagA and VacA are the cytotoxin virulence factors, separate from urease.

choices.text: Neutralize gastric acid by generating ammonia
choices.feedback: Correct! Urease converts urea → ammonia + CO2. Ammonia neutralizes local gastric acid, creating a protective alkaline microenvironment. This is exploited diagnostically: urea breath test (13C/14C-labeled urea → labeled CO2 if urease present), rapid urease test on biopsy (CLO test). H. pylori is the only common organism that can survive in the stomach.

questions.stem: H. pylori is associated with all of the following EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: H. pylori is the cause of gastric MALT lymphoma; eradication can cure early-stage disease.
H. pylori is classified as a Group 1 carcinogen for gastric cancer.
H. pylori causes >90% of duodenal ulcers.

Correct! H. pylori is actually inversely associated with esophageal adenocarcinoma (possibly protective via reduced acid production). H. pylori IS associated with: gastric/duodenal ulcers, gastric adenocarcinoma (intestinal type), gastric MALT lymphoma. GERD and Barrett's esophagus → esophageal adenocarcinoma (different pathway).

questions.stem: Campylobacter jejuni is the most common bacterial cause of diarrhea in the US. It is also associated with:

choices.feedback: HUS is associated with EHEC (Shiga toxin), not Campylobacter.

Correct! Campylobacter infection is the most common antecedent to GBS (ascending paralysis from molecular mimicry between C. jejuni lipooligosaccharide and gangliosides on peripheral nerves). Also associated with reactive arthritis. Transmitted by undercooked poultry, unpasteurized milk.
Pyogenic liver abscess is associated with E. histolytica and Klebsiella.
Toxic megacolon is associated with C. difficile and inflammatory bowel disease.

questions.stem: The recommended first-line triple therapy for H. pylori eradication is:

choices.feedback: PPI alone heals ulcers but does not eradicate H. pylori.

Correct! Standard triple therapy: PPI (bid) + clarithromycin+ amoxicillin. If clarithromycin resistance is >15% locally, use bismuth quadruple therapy instead: PPI + bismuth + metronidazole + tetracycline for 14 days. Confirm eradication with urea breath test ≥4 weeks after completing therapy.

choices.text: H2 blocker + bismuth + erythromycin for 10 days
choices.feedback: H2 blockers are less effective than PPIs; erythromycin is not part of standard H. pylori regimens.
This combination can work but is not the standard first-line in North America.

questions.stem: Campylobacter jejuni grows best at:

choices.feedback: Campylobacter is microaerophilic, not strictly anaerobic.
Cold enrichment is used for Listeria and Yersinia, not Campylobacter.

Correct! Campylobacter grows optimally at 42°C (body temperature of poultry, its primary reservoir) in microaerophilic conditions (5% O2, 10% CO2). Comma-shaped or S-shaped gram-negative rods with polar flagella (darting motility). Grows on Campy-BAP selective media or charcoal-based media.
Campylobacter requires specific microaerophilic conditions.

questions.stem: N. meningitidis transmission is prevented primarily by targeting:

choices.feedback: Neisseria is not transmitted by blood exposure.

Correct! N. meningitidis colonizes the nasopharynx and spreads via respiratory droplets. Close contacts (household, dormitory) need chemoprophylaxis: rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone. Vaccination (MenACWY for teens; MenB for outbreaks) targets polysaccharide capsule. Serogroup B vaccine exists (protein-based since B capsule mimics neural tissue).
Neisseria is transmitted by respiratory droplets, not fecal-oral.
No arthropod vector is involved in Neisseria transmission.

questions.stem: N. gonorrhoeae evades the immune system primarily through:

choices.text: Antigenic variation of pilin and Opa proteins
choices.feedback: Correct! N. gonorrhoeae undergoes rapid antigenic variation of surface pili (attachment) and Opa proteins (invasion). This prevents effective immune memory and reinfection can occur. Also produces IgA protease (degrades mucosal IgA) and acquires host sialyl groups to mimic host cells.
N. meningitidis has a capsule; N. gonorrhoeae does NOT have a polysaccharide capsule.
Biofilm contributes but antigenic variation is the primary evasion strategy.
While gonococci survive within neutrophils briefly, antigenic variation is the primary evasion mechanism.

questions.stem: Both N. meningitidis and N. gonorrhoeae share which feature?

choices.feedback: Only N. meningitidis has a polysaccharide capsule.

Correct! Both are kidney-shaped gram-negative diplococci, oxidase-positive, and grow on chocolate agar (need CO2).
