Key difference: N. meningitidis ferments maltose AND glucose; N. gonorrhoeae ferments glucose only. Both grow on Thayer-Martin agar (selective: VCN inhibitors — vancomycin, colistin, nystatin).
Ceftriaxone is still effective against both species.

choices.text: Ability to cause septic arthritis only
choices.feedback: Both can cause arthritis but they cause many other infections.

questions.stem: The Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome is a complication of:

choices.feedback: Correct! Waterhouse-Friderichsen: bilateral adrenal hemorrhagic necrosis during fulminant meningococcal sepsis. DIC → petechial/purpuric rash → hemorrhagic infarction of adrenals → acute adrenal insufficiency → refractory shock. High mortality. Associated with asplenia and terminal complement (C5-C9) deficiencies.
DGI causes different manifestations (arthritis-dermatitis syndrome).
While E. coli sepsis can cause DIC, Waterhouse-Friderichsen is classically meningococcal.
Pneumococcal meningitis does not typically cause adrenal hemorrhage.

questions.stem: H. influenzae requires which growth factors?

choices.feedback: While CO2 enhances growth, the specific requirement is factors X and V.
Cysteine requirement is characteristic of Legionella and Francisella.

Correct! H. influenzae needs both factor X (hemin/hematin) and factor V (NAD+/NADP+) for growth. Grows on chocolate agar (heated blood releases these factors) but NOT on blood agar (intact RBCs sequester factors). Satellite phenomenon: grows near S. aureus colonies on blood agar (S. aureus lyses RBCs, releasing factors X and V).
These are staphylococcal enzymes, not growth factors.

questions.stem: Since widespread Hib vaccination, the most common type of H. influenzae infection in adults is:

choices.feedback: Hib meningitis has become rare due to vaccination.
Epiglottitis has become rare since Hib vaccine; when it occurs in adults, S. aureus is more common.
Neonatal meningitis is caused by GBS and E. coli, not Haemophilus.

choices.text: Non-typeable H. influenzae causing exacerbations of COPD, sinusitis, and otitis media
choices.feedback: Correct! Hib vaccine dramatically reduced type b (encapsulated) invasive disease (meningitis, epiglottitis, septic arthritis). Non-typeable (unencapsulated) strains now dominate, causing: acute exacerbations of COPD, sinusitis, otitis media, conjunctivitis, and pneumonia. These are mucosal, not invasive, infections.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Non-typeable H. influenzae causing exacerbations of COPD, sinusitis, and otitis media.

questions.stem: H. influenzae meningitis in an unvaccinated child is treated with:

choices.feedback: Correct! Third-generation cephalosporins (ceftriaxone/cefotaxime) are first-line because 30-40% of H. influenzae produce beta-lactamase. Dexamethasone before or with first antibiotic dose reduces hearing loss and neurological sequelae (best evidence for Hib meningitis). Close contacts <4 years unvaccinated need rifampin prophylaxis.
TMP-SMX is not appropriate for meningitis treatment.
Vancomycin has no gram-negative activity; metronidazole is for anaerobes.
Many H. influenzae strains produce beta-lactamase, making penicillin unreliable.

questions.stem: The Hib vaccine targets which component?

choices.text: Type b polyribosylribitol phosphate (PRP) capsule conjugated to protein
choices.feedback: Correct! PRP conjugate vaccine: the PRP polysaccharide capsule is conjugated to a protein carrier (tetanus toxoid, diphtheria, or meningococcal OMP). Conjugation converts T-cell-independent to T-cell-dependent response, enabling effective immunity in infants <2 years. Given at 2, 4, 6, and 12-15 months.
IgA protease is a virulence factor but not a vaccine target.
Pili are not the vaccine target.
LPS is a general gram-negative component, not the vaccine target.

questions.stem: H. ducreyi causes:

choices.feedback: Whooping cough is caused by Bordetella pertussis.

choices.text: Chancroid — painful genital ulcer with suppurative inguinal lymphadenopathy
choices.feedback: Correct! Chancroid: painful, soft genital ulcer (vs. syphilis: painless, hard chancre). Suppurative inguinal lymph nodes (buboes). School of Fish appearance on Gram stain. Treat: azithromycinsingle dose or ceftriaxone. Key DDx: syphilis (painless), HSV (vesicular), LGV (painless ulcer → painful nodes).
Neonatal conjunctivitis is caused by N. gonorrhoeae, Chlamydia, or chemical.
Brazilian purpuric fever is associated with H. influenzae biogroup aegyptius.

questions.stem: The paroxysmal stage of pertussis is characterized by:

choices.feedback: Rusty sputum suggests pneumococcal pneumonia.
Pertussis typically has minimal or no fever.
Barking cough suggests croup (parainfluenza virus).

choices.text: Severe coughing spells followed by inspiratory whoop
choices.feedback: Correct! Pertussis (whooping cough) has 3 stages: Catarrhal (1-2 weeks, URI symptoms, most contagious), Paroxysmal (2-8 weeks, severe cough paroxysms → inspiratory whoop, post-tussive emesis, lymphocytosis), Convalescent (weeks-months, gradual resolution). In infants: apnea may replace the whoop. Treat with azithromycin.

questions.stem: Pertussis toxin acts by:

choices.text: ADP-ribosylating Gi protein, disabling inhibition of adenylate cyclase
choices.feedback: Correct! Pertussis toxin ADP-ribosylates the inhibitory Gi protein → adenylate cyclase is constitutively active → increased cAMP. Effects: lymphocytosis (blocks chemokine receptor signaling, lymphocytes can't leave blood), islet cell activation (insulin secretion → hypoglycemia in infants), enhanced histamine sensitivity.

choices.text: Inhibiting protein synthesis at the ribosome
choices.feedback: Ribosome inhibition is the mechanism of Shiga toxin and diphtheria toxin.
Pore-forming toxins are produced by S. aureus (alpha-toxin) and Streptococcus (streptolysin).
Adenylate cyclase toxin (ACT) is a separate B. pertussis toxin that does this.

questions.stem: Post-exposure prophylaxis for pertussis contacts uses:

choices.feedback: Ciprofloxacin is used for meningococcal prophylaxis.
Beta-lactams are NOT effective for pertussis treatment or prophylaxis.
Rifampin is used for meningococcal and Hib prophylaxis, not pertussis.

Correct! PEP for close contacts regardless of vaccination status: azithromycin (5 days), clarithromycin (7 days), or TMP-SMX (14 days if macrolide contraindicated). Also give Tdap booster if >2 years since last dose. PEP is most effective within 21 days of exposure.

questions.stem: A striking lab finding in pertussis is:

choices.feedback: This pattern suggests TTP/HUS or DIC.

Correct! Pertussis toxin prevents lymphocyte extravasation from blood (blocks chemokine receptor signaling on Gi protein). Results in dramatic lymphocytosis — WBC can exceed 100,000 in severe infant pertussis (leukemoid reaction). Extreme leukocytosis in infants is a poor prognostic sign (pulmonary hypertension risk).
Pertussis causes lymphocytosis, not leukopenia.
Eosinophilia suggests parasitic infection or allergic conditions.

questions.explanation: Correct: Profound lymphocytosis (WBC 20,000-100,000 with >60% lymphocytes).

questions.stem: The DTaP vaccine given to children differs from Tdap given to adolescents/adults because:

choices.text: DTaP has higher diphtheria and pertussis antigen content; Tdap has reduced doses
choices.feedback: Correct! Capital letters = full dose; lowercase = reduced dose. DTaP (children <7): full-dose diphtheria, tetanus, acellular pertussis. Tdap (>7 years): reduced diphtheria and pertussis antigens (better tolerated in older individuals). DTaP: 5-dose series (2,4,6,15-18 months, 4-6 years). Tdap: one dose at 11-12 years, then every pregnancy (27-36 weeks).

choices.text: DTaP is given IM while Tdap is given subcutaneously
choices.feedback: Both are given intramuscularly.
Tdap covers all three: tetanus, diphtheria, and pertussis.
Neither contains live organisms; both use acellular pertussis components.

questions.stem: Listeria monocytogenes is unique among foodborne pathogens because it:

choices.feedback: Correct! Listeria is a cold-growth organism (psychrophilic) — multiplies in refrigerated foods (deli meats, unpasteurized cheese, hot dogs). Also: facultative intracellular pathogen, tumbling motility at 25°C (flagella), beta-hemolytic on blood agar, gram-positive rod. Actin rocket tails propel cell-to-cell spread.
Listeria does not form spores; it's killed by pasteurization and cooking.
Listeria's pathogenesis is through direct invasion, not toxin production.
Listeria is a facultative anaerobe.

questions.explanation: Correct: Can grow at refrigerator temperatures (4°C).

questions.stem: Listeria meningitis requires ampicillin because:

choices.text: Cephalosporins have no activity against Listeria
choices.feedback: Correct! Listeria is intrinsically resistant to all cephalosporins. Empiric meningitis regimens (vancomycin + ceftriaxone) do NOT cover Listeria. Add ampicillin for patients at risk: age >50, neonates, pregnant women, immunocompromised. Ampicillin + gentamicin is the standard Listeria treatment.
Listeria does not form biofilm in meningitis.
Listeria's pathogenesis is through intracellular invasion, not toxin.
Listeria is susceptible to several antibiotics, but the empiric regimen matters.

questions.stem: Listeria spreads from cell to cell using:

choices.feedback: Flagella provide motility in the environment but not intracellular spread.

choices.text: Actin polymerization (rocket tails) to propel through cytoplasm
choices.feedback: Correct! Listeriolysin O (LLO) lyses phagosome → bacteria escape into cytoplasm → ActA protein nucleates host actin polymerization → actin rocket tails propel bacteria through cytoplasm → push into neighboring cell membrane → form double-membrane vacuole → LLO lyses vacuole → cycle repeats. This cell-to-cell spread avoids antibody exposure.
Listeria is gram-positive and does not have endotoxin.
Type III secretion is a gram-negative mechanism (Salmonella, Shigella).

questions.stem: Which patient population is NOT at increased risk for Listeria infection?

choices.feedback: Pregnant women have 10x risk; Listeria crosses placenta → granulomatosis infantiseptica.
Cell-mediated immunity suppression greatly increases Listeria risk.

choices.text: Healthy young adults with no comorbidities
choices.feedback: Correct! Listeria primarily affects immunocompromised individuals: pregnant women (neonatal sepsis/meningitis), elderly (>65), organ transplant recipients, HIV/AIDS, chronic steroid use, and neonates. Healthy adults typically get only mild gastroenteritis if exposed.
Neonatal listeriosis (early-onset from transplacental, late-onset from birth canal) is a serious infection.

questions.stem: Granulomatosis infantiseptica refers to:

choices.feedback: CMV causes different congenital findings (hearing loss, petechiae, microcephaly).

choices.text: Disseminated neonatal Listeria infection with widespread granulomas
choices.feedback: Correct! Granulomatosis infantiseptica: severe form of neonatal listeriosis from transplacental (hematogenous) transmission. Disseminated abscesses and granulomas in liver, spleen, adrenals, lungs, brain. High mortality. Presents at birth with respiratory distress, hepatosplenomegaly, skin lesions. Maternal infection may present as mild flu-like illness.
GBS causes neonatal sepsis but not granulomatosis infantiseptica.
Neonatal herpes causes vesicular skin lesions and encephalitis, not granulomas.

questions.stem: C. difficile infection is most commonly precipitated by:

choices.feedback: C. diff is acquired through fecal-oral transmission of spores in healthcare settings.

choices.text: Antibiotic disruption of normal gut flora
choices.feedback: Correct! Antibiotics (especially clindamycin, fluoroquinolones, broad-spectrum cephalosporins, carbapenems) disrupt normal colonic flora → C. difficile spores germinate and overgrow → toxin A (enterotoxin) and toxin B (cytotoxin) damage colonic mucosa → pseudomembranous colitis. First-line treatment: oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin.
C. difficile is not sexually transmitted.
C. difficile has no vector transmission.

questions.stem: C. botulinum toxin acts by:

choices.text: Blocking acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction
choices.feedback: Correct! Botulinum toxin cleaves SNARE proteins → blocks ACh vesicle fusion at presynaptic NMJ → flaccid descending paralysis. Starts with cranial nerves (diplopia, dysphagia, dysarthria) → descends. Infant botulism: floppy baby (honey is the classic source). Adult: canned foods. Wound botulism: injection drug use.

choices.text: Activating adenylate cyclase via Gi protein modification
choices.feedback: This is the mechanism of pertussis toxin.

choices.text: Blocking glycine and GABA release (spastic paralysis)
choices.feedback: This is the mechanism of tetanus toxin (tetanospasmin).
C. perfringens alpha-toxin forms pores (phospholipase C/lecithinase).

questions.explanation: Correct: Blocking acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction (flaccid paralysis).

questions.stem: C. tetani toxin (tetanospasmin) causes spastic paralysis by:

choices.feedback: The toxin acts on inhibitory neurons, not directly on muscle.

choices.text: Blocking inhibitory neurotransmitter (glycine/GABA) release in the spinal cord
choices.feedback: Correct! Tetanospasmin travels retrograde up motor neurons to the spinal cord → blocks release of glycine and GABA from Renshaw cells (inhibitory interneurons) → unopposed motor neuron firing → spastic paralysis, opisthotonus, risus sardonicus, lockjaw (trismus). Treatment: tetanus immune globulin + metronidazole + wound debridement.

choices.text: Blocking ACh release at the NMJ
choices.feedback: Blocking ACh release causes flaccid paralysis (botulinum toxin mechanism).

choices.text: Destroying myelin sheath of motor neurons
choices.feedback: Demyelination is seen in GBS and MS, not tetanus.

questions.stem: C. perfringens gas gangrene is treated with:

choices.text: Surgical debridement plus IV penicillin and clindamycin
choices.feedback: Correct! Gas gangrene (myonecrosis): surgical emergency requiring aggressive debridement of necrotic tissue + high-dose IV penicillin G + clindamycin (suppresses toxin production). Alpha-toxin (lecithinase/phospholipase C) causes tissue destruction and gas production. Crepitus on exam, rapid progression. Hyperbaric oxygen is adjunctive.
Gas gangrene requires aggressive surgical debridement plus IV antibiotics.
Surgery is essential — antibiotics alone are insufficient for gas gangrene.
While broad-spectrum coverage may be needed, penicillin + clindamycin is the targeted C. perfringens regimen.

questions.stem: All Clostridium species share which feature?

choices.feedback: Clostridium species are catalase-negative.
MacConkey agar selects for gram-negative organisms; Clostridium won't grow on it.

Correct! All Clostridium: obligate anaerobes, form endospores (survive harsh conditions — heat, disinfectants, antibiotics), gram-positive rods. Key species: C. difficile (pseudomembranous colitis), C. perfringens (gas gangrene, food poisoning), C. tetani (tetanus), C. botulinum (botulism). Spores are the reason C. diff persists in hospital environments.
Clostridium species are gram-positive rods (bacilli), not cocci.

questions.stem: Mycoplasma pneumoniae lacks a cell wall, which means:

choices.feedback: Mycoplasma does not form spores.

choices.text: It is classified as a fungus
choices.feedback: Mycoplasma is a bacterium, just without a cell wall.

Correct! No cell wall → no peptidoglycan target → beta-lactams and vancomycin are useless. Treat with macrolides (azithromycin), doxycycline, or fluoroquinolones. Mycoplasma is the smallest free-living organism, grows on Eaton agar (fried egg colonies), and has cholesterol in its membrane (unique among bacteria).

choices.text: It is visible on Gram stain
choices.feedback: Without a cell wall, Mycoplasma does not retain Gram stain.

questions.stem: Walking pneumonia in a college student is most commonly caused by:

choices.feedback: Legionella causes severe pneumonia with GI symptoms and hyponatremia, typically in older/immunocompromised patients.
Klebsiella causes necrotizing/cavitary pneumonia in alcoholics, not walking pneumonia.
S. pneumoniae causes typical (lobar) pneumonia, not atypical walking pneumonia.

Correct! Mycoplasma is the most common cause of atypical (walking) pneumonia in young adults (5-40 years). Gradual onset, dry cough, low-grade fever, headache, malaise. CXR often looks worse than patient appears. May cause bullous myringitis (ear), erythema multiforme, cold agglutinins (IgM anti-I), hemolytic anemia.

questions.stem: Cold agglutinins (IgM anti-I) are associated with:

choices.feedback: Influenza does not typically cause cold agglutinins.
S. pneumoniae does not typically cause cold agglutinins.

Correct! Cold agglutinins (IgM antibodies that agglutinate RBCs at cold temperatures) develop in ~50% of Mycoplasma infections. Can cause autoimmune hemolytic anemia (cold AIHA). Also associated with EBV and lymphoma. Diagnosed by positive direct Coombs (anti-C3) and cold agglutinin titer.
C. pneumoniae causes atypical pneumonia but is not associated with cold agglutinins.

questions.stem: Mycoplasma pneumoniae is diagnosed most accurately by:

choices.feedback: That test is for Legionella, not Mycoplasma.

choices.text: Sputum Gram stain showing intracellular diplococci
choices.feedback: Mycoplasma has no cell wall and does not Gram stain.

Correct! PCR (nasopharyngeal swab or sputum) is the most sensitive and specific diagnostic test. Serology (IgM) takes 1-2 weeks and can be falsely negative early. Culture on Eaton agar takes 2-3 weeks (too slow for clinical use). Cold agglutinins are suggestive but not specific. In practice, Mycoplasma pneumonia is often diagnosed clinically.
Mycoplasma does not grow on standard blood culture media.

questions.stem: Which extrapulmonary complication of Mycoplasma is correctly paired?

choices.text: Bullous myringitis — painful hemorrhagic ear blisters
choices.feedback: Correct! Mycoplasma causes: bullous myringitis (hemorrhagic blisters on tympanic membrane), erythema multiforme/Stevens-Johnson syndrome, autoimmune hemolytic anemia (cold agglutinins), encephalitis, transverse myelitis, Guillain-Barré syndrome, cardiac (myocarditis, pericarditis), arthritis.
Rose spots are associated with typhoid fever (Salmonella typhi).
Painless chancre is syphilis (T. pallidum).

choices.text: Ascending paralysis — always means GBS from Campylobacter
choices.feedback: Mycoplasma can also cause GBS, though Campylobacter is the most common trigger.

questions.stem: Chlamydia species are obligate intracellular organisms because they:

choices.text: Require specific tissue types for invasion
choices.feedback: The ATP dependency, not tissue specificity, makes them obligate intracellular.

Correct! Chlamydia is an energy parasite — it cannot produce ATP and must steal it from host cells. Unique biphasic lifecycle: elementary body (EB, infectious, extracellular, metabolically inert) → attaches to host → enters → converts to reticulate body (RB, intracellular, metabolically active, replicating) → condenses back to EB → released.

choices.text: Lack cell walls entirely like Mycoplasma
choices.feedback: Chlamydia has a cell wall (but lacks peptidoglycan muramic acid).

choices.text: Are too large to survive extracellularly
choices.feedback: Elementary bodies survive extracellularly; the issue is ATP production.

questions.stem: C. trachomatis serotypes D-K cause:

choices.feedback: C. pneumoniae (not trachomatis) causes atypical pneumonia.
LGV is caused by serotypes L1, L2, L3.

choices.text: Urethritis, cervicitis, PID, and neonatal conjunctivitis/pneumonia
choices.feedback: Correct! D-K: most common bacterial STI in the US. Often asymptomatic. Complications: PID → ectopic pregnancy, infertility. Neonatal: conjunctivitis (5-14 days after birth), staccato pneumonia (afebrile, eosinophilia). Serotypes L1-L3: lymphogranuloma venereum (LGV). Treat: azithromycinsingle dose or doxycycline 7 days.
Trachoma is caused by serotypes A-C.

questions.stem: Reactive arthritis (formerly Reiter syndrome) can be triggered by which Chlamydia species?

choices.feedback: C. pneumoniae causes respiratory infections, not typically reactive arthritis.

Correct! Reactive arthritis triad: arthritis, urethritis, conjunctivitis (can't see, can't pee, can't climb a tree). Triggered by C. trachomatis (GU route) or enteric pathogens (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia). HLA-B27 associated. Asymmetric oligoarthritis of large joints, typically lower extremity.
C. psittaci causes psittacosis (atypical pneumonia from bird exposure).
C. trachomatis is the primary Chlamydia trigger for reactive arthritis.

questions.stem: Chlamydial neonatal pneumonia presents with:

choices.feedback: This describes bronchiolitis.

choices.text: High fever, purulent sputum, lobar consolidation
choices.feedback: This describes typical bacterial pneumonia, not chlamydial.
This describes croup (parainfluenza virus).

choices.text: Afebrile, staccato cough, bilateral infiltrates, and eosinophilia
choices.feedback: Correct! Chlamydial neonatal pneumonia (2-19 weeks of age): characteristically afebrile, staccato cough (short bursts), tachypnea, bilateral interstitial infiltrates on CXR, elevated eosinophils in CBC. Often preceded by chlamydial conjunctivitis. Treat with oral erythromycin or azithromycin.

questions.stem: Screening for Chlamydia is recommended annually for:

choices.feedback: Most chlamydia infections are asymptomatic, making screening essential.

choices.text: Men who have sex with men only
choices.feedback: MSM should be screened, but the primary screening target is young women.
Chlamydia screening targets sexually active young women.

Correct! CDC recommends annual screening: all sexually active women <25, older women with new/multiple partners, pregnant women. NAAT (nucleic acid amplification test) on urine or cervical/vaginal swab is the diagnostic gold standard. Screen because most infections are asymptomatic but can cause PID, ectopic pregnancy, and infertility.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Sexually active women under 25 and older women with risk factors.

questions.stem: Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever rash characteristically:

choices.feedback: Dermatomal distribution is characteristic of varicella-zoster reactivation.

choices.text: Starts on the trunk and spares palms/soles
choices.feedback: This pattern describes many viral exanthems, not RMSF.

choices.text: Starts on wrists and ankles and spreads centripetally, including palms and soles
choices.feedback: Correct! RMSF (R. rickettsii): tick-borne, rash starts on extremities (wrists/ankles) at day 3-5, spreads to trunk (centripetal). Involves palms and soles. Starts as macular → becomes petechial. Triad: fever, headache, rash. Despite the name, most common in southeastern US. Treat empirically with doxycycline (don't wait for confirmation).

choices.text: Is a painless, non-blanching purpura only on the legs
choices.feedback: This could describe Henoch-Schonlein purpura.

questions.stem: Rickettsial organisms are obligate intracellular pathogens that target:

choices.feedback: Babesia and malaria target RBCs, not Rickettsia.
Neurons are not the primary target of Rickettsia.
While liver involvement occurs, the primary target is vascular endothelium.

Correct! Rickettsia invades and proliferates within vascular endothelial cells → endothelial damage → vasculitis → increased vascular permeability → edema, petechiae, end-organ damage. This explains RMSF manifestations: rash (dermal vasculitis), encephalitis, pulmonary edema, renal failure, DIC. Confirmed by skin biopsy with immunofluorescence.

questions.stem: The treatment of choice for all rickettsial diseases is:

choices.feedback: Beta-lactams are ineffective against Rickettsia.
Cephalosporins are ineffective against obligate intracellular organisms.

Correct! Doxycycline is first-line for ALL rickettsial infections (RMSF, ehrlichiosis, anaplasmosis, typhus, scrub typhus). Start empirically when suspected — do not delay for confirmation. Doxycycline is recommended even in children (risk of RMSF mortality > risk of tooth staining at treatment doses). Chloramphenicol is an alternative in pregnancy.
Macrolides are less effective than doxycycline for Rickettsia.

questions.stem: Ehrlichiosis differs from RMSF in that ehrlichiosis:

choices.feedback: Ehrlichiosis often LACKS a rash (present in only ~30% of cases).

Correct! Ehrlichiosis (Ehrlichia chaffeensis, Lone Star tick): fever, headache, myalgia + leukopenia + thrombocytopenia + elevated AST/ALT. Rash in only ~30%. Morulae (mulberry-like inclusions) seen in monocytes on peripheral smear. Anaplasmosis is similar but morulae in neutrophils (granulocytes). Both treated with doxycycline.
Ehrlichiosis is tick-borne (Lone Star tick, Amblyomma americanum).

choices.text: Causes painless eschar at the bite site
choices.feedback: Eschar at bite site is characteristic of scrub typhus (Orientia tsutsugamushi).

questions.explanation: Best answer: Presents with leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and elevated transaminases without prominent rash.

questions.stem: The Weil-Felix test for Rickettsia detects:

choices.feedback: PCR is a separate molecular test, not the Weil-Felix reaction.
The Weil-Felix is not specific — it's a cross-reactive agglutination test.

choices.text: Cross-reactive antibodies that agglutinate Proteus OX strains
choices.feedback: Correct! The Weil-Felix test detects antibodies that cross-react with Proteus vulgaris OX-19, OX-2, and OX-K strains (sharing antigens with Rickettsia). Historically important but now largely replaced by indirect immunofluorescence assay (IFA), which is the gold standard for serologic diagnosis. PCR is also available.
Cold agglutinins are associated with Mycoplasma, not Rickettsia.

questions.stem: The primary stage of syphilis presents as:

choices.text: Painless chancre at the site of inoculation
choices.feedback: Correct! Primary syphilis: single, painless, firm, clean-based ulcer (chancre) at inoculation site (3-90 days after exposure). Heals spontaneously in 3-6 weeks even without treatment. Non-tender inguinal lymphadenopathy. Dark-field microscopy of chancre fluid shows spirochetes. Highly infectious stage.

choices.text: Diffuse maculopapular rash on palms and soles
choices.feedback: Rash on palms/soles is secondary syphilis.

choices.text: Argyll Robertson pupils and tabes dorsalis
choices.feedback: These are features of tertiary neurosyphilis.

choices.text: Gummatous lesions of skin and bone
choices.feedback: Gummas occur in tertiary (late) syphilis.

questions.stem: The screening algorithm for syphilis uses:

choices.feedback: T. pallidum cannot be cultured in vitro.

choices.text: Dark-field microscopy as the initial screen
choices.feedback: Dark-field is used for primary chancre diagnosis, not screening.

Correct! Traditional algorithm: screen with RPR or VDRL (non-treponemal, quantitative, titers follow disease activity) → confirm positive with FTA-ABS or TP-PA (treponemal, remains positive for life). Reverse algorithm (increasingly used): screen with treponemal EIA/CIA → confirm with RPR → discordant results resolved with TP-PA.

choices.text: Treponemal PCR as the sole test
choices.feedback: PCR is not the standard screening approach for syphilis.

questions.explanation: Correct: Non-treponemal test (RPR/VDRL) confirmed by treponemal test (FTA-ABS/TP-PA).

questions.stem: Secondary syphilis is characterized by all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: Mucous patches (painless, highly infectious oral lesions) occur in secondary syphilis.

choices.text: Condylomata lata (moist, flat, gray lesions in intertriginous areas)
choices.feedback: Condylomata lata are characteristic of secondary syphilis (vs. condylomata acuminata from HPV).

Correct! Argyll Robertson pupils (accommodate but do not react to light — prostitute's pupil) are a sign of tertiary neurosyphilis, not secondary. Secondary syphilis features: diffuse rash (palms/soles), condylomata lata, mucous patches, diffuse lymphadenopathy, constitutional symptoms. Most infectious stage after primary.

choices.text: Diffuse maculopapular rash involving palms and soles
choices.feedback: This is the hallmark of secondary syphilis.

questions.stem: The Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction after syphilis treatment is caused by:

choices.feedback: This is not an allergic/anaphylactic reaction.

choices.text: Release of cytokines from spirochete lysis
choices.feedback: Correct! Within 2-8 hours of first penicillin dose, rapid spirochete killing releases lipoproteins and endotoxin-like molecules → cytokine storm (TNF-α, IL-6) → fever, rigors, headache, myalgia, hypotension. Self-limited (12-24 hours). More common in early syphilis (higher spirochete burden). Managed with NSAIDs and fluids. Do not stop treatment.
JHR is not a superinfection.
Immune complex deposition is a different mechanism (like serum sickness).

questions.stem: The hallmark of early localized Lyme disease is:

choices.text: Vesicular rash in a dermatomal pattern
choices.feedback: This describes herpes zoster.

choices.text: Painless eschar at the tick bite site
choices.feedback: Eschar is characteristic of scrub typhus, not Lyme.

choices.text: Erythema migrans — expanding annular lesion with central clearing
choices.feedback: Correct! Erythema migrans (EM): pathognomonic target/bull's-eye lesion at tick bite site, expanding over days-weeks (usually >5 cm). Appears 3-30 days after Ixodes tick bite. EM alone is sufficient for clinical diagnosis — no serologic confirmation needed. B. burgdorferi transmitted by Ixodes scapularis (deer tick). Early localized: EM ± flu-like symptoms.

choices.text: Petechial rash starting on wrists and ankles
choices.feedback: This describes RMSF.

questions.stem: Lyme disease is transmitted by:

choices.feedback: Lone Star tick transmits Ehrlichia and causes STARI (Southern tick-associated rash illness), not Lyme.
Mosquitoes transmit malaria, dengue, Zika, etc., not Lyme.
Dog tick transmits RMSF and tularemia, not Lyme.

Correct! Ixodes ticks: I. scapularis (eastern US), I. pacificus (western US). The tick must be attached for >36-48 hours to transmit B. burgdorferi (spirochete lives in tick midgut and must migrate to salivary glands). Ixodes ticks also transmit Babesia (babesiosis), Anaplasma (anaplasmosis), and Powassan virus — coinfections possible.

questions.explanation: Correct: Ixodes scapularis (black-legged/deer tick).

questions.stem: The two-tier serologic testing for Lyme disease consists of:

choices.feedback: That is the syphilis testing algorithm.

choices.text: Monospot followed by heterophile antibody confirmation
choices.feedback: That is the EBV/mononucleosis testing approach.

choices.text: PCR of blood followed by culture
choices.feedback: Blood PCR has low sensitivity for Lyme; culture is very difficult.

choices.text: ELISA/EIA followed by Western blot (or second EIA) if positive
choices.feedback: Correct! Two-tier testing: 1) ELISA or EIA (sensitive screening) → if positive or equivocal → 2) Western blot (specific confirmation) or a second EIA using different antigen. IgM Western blot positive if ≥2 of 3 bands; IgG positive if ≥5 of 10 bands. Important: do not skip to Western blot (poor specificity if used alone). Serology may be negative in early EM.

questions.stem: Late Lyme disease can cause:

choices.feedback: Correct! Late Lyme (months-years): Lyme arthritis (intermittent monoarthritis of large joints, classically the knee — can become chronic), chronic encephalopathy, peripheral neuropathy. Early disseminated (weeks-months): multiple EM lesions, facial nerve palsy (Bell's palsy — bilateral in Lyme), AV block, meningitis. Treat late Lyme arthritis with oral doxycycline 28 days.
Lyme does not cause pulmonary fibrosis.
Aortic aneurysm (aortitis) is associated with tertiary syphilis, not Lyme.
Lyme does not cause adrenal insufficiency.

questions.explanation: Correct: Chronic arthritis (especially large joint monoarthritis of the knee).

questions.stem: First-line treatment for early Lyme disease (without CNS involvement) is:

choices.feedback: Azithromycin is second-line and less effective than doxycycline for Lyme.

choices.text: Single dose of benzathine penicillin G
choices.feedback: Single-dose penicillin is for syphilis, not Lyme.

Correct! Doxycyclinefor 10-21 days is first-line for early Lyme (EM, early disseminated without meningitis/high-degree AV block). Alternatives: amoxicillin or cefuroxime. Doxycycline also treats potential Anaplasma co-infection (same Ixodes tick). IV ceftriaxone for: Lyme meningitis, high-degree AV block, severe cardiac involvement.
IV ceftriaxone is reserved for neuroborreliosis, cardiac Lyme with high-degree AV block, or treatment failure.

questions.stem: Antigenic shift in influenza produces pandemics because:

choices.text: Reassortment of genome segments between different influenza strains creates a novel HA/NA combination
choices.feedback: Correct! Antigenic shift: reassortment of segmented RNA genome between human and animal (avian/swine) influenza strains → novel HA and/or NA proteins that the population has no immunity against → pandemic. Only influenza A (has animal reservoirs). Antigenic drift: point mutations in HA/NA → seasonal epidemics (both A and B).
Influenza does not acquire bacterial genes.

choices.text: Minor point mutations gradually change surface proteins
choices.feedback: That describes antigenic drift (epidemic), not shift (pandemic).
Drug resistance is separate from antigenic shift.

questions.stem: Oseltamivir (Tamiflu) works by:

choices.text: Inhibiting neuraminidase, preventing release of new virions from infected cells
choices.feedback: Correct! Neuraminidase cleaves sialic acid residues → releases budding virions from host cell surface. Oseltamivir and zanamivir block this step. Must be started within 48 hours of symptom onset. Indicated for: high-risk patients, severe illness, hospitalized patients. Reduces symptom duration by ~1-2 days.

choices.text: Blocking M2 ion channel preventing viral uncoating
choices.feedback: M2 blockers (amantadine/rimantadine) have this mechanism but are no longer recommended due to resistance.
Baloxavir marboxil inhibits cap-dependent endonuclease, not oseltamivir.

choices.text: Preventing viral attachment to sialic acid receptors
choices.feedback: No current antiviral blocks influenza attachment.

questions.stem: The most common complication of influenza is:

choices.feedback: Influenza does not typically cause liver failure.
Myocarditis can occur but is not the most common complication.
Influenza does not cause chronic infection.

choices.text: Secondary bacterial pneumonia (especially S. aureus and S. pneumoniae)
choices.feedback: Correct! Influenza damages respiratory epithelium → increased susceptibility to secondary bacterial pneumonia (biphasic illness: improving then worsening). S. pneumoniae is most common; S. aureus (including MRSA) causes necrotizing pneumonia with high mortality. Other complications: otitis media, Reye syndrome (aspirin + influenza in children), myositis.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Secondary bacterial pneumonia (especially S. aureus and S. pneumoniae).

questions.stem: Reye syndrome is associated with:

choices.feedback: This is not the Reye syndrome association.

choices.text: Ibuprofen use in children with RSV
choices.feedback: Ibuprofen is not associated with Reye syndrome.

choices.text: Aspirin use in children with influenza or varicella
choices.feedback: Correct! Reye syndrome: acute encephalopathy + fatty liver degeneration in children given aspirin during viral illness (especially influenza B and varicella). Presents with vomiting, altered consciousness, hepatomegaly, elevated ammonia and transaminases. Since aspirin avoidance in children, Reye syndrome has become extremely rare.
Acetaminophen is safe during influenza; aspirin is the problem.

questions.stem: Annual influenza vaccination is recommended for:

choices.text: All persons aged 6 months and older
choices.feedback: Correct! CDC recommends annual influenza vaccination for everyone ≥6 months. Trivalent or quadrivalent (2 influenza A + 1-2 influenza B strains). High-dose or adjuvanted formulations for ≥65. Live attenuated (LAIV/FluMist): healthy non-pregnant 2-49 years only. Egg allergy: no longer a contraindication (can receive any age-appropriate vaccine).
Universal vaccination is recommended, not just immunocompromised.
Universal vaccination is now recommended.
Children are a priority group but vaccination is universal.

questions.stem: RSV is the most common cause of:

choices.feedback: RSV can affect elderly but is most notably associated with infant bronchiolitis.
Croup is most commonly caused by parainfluenza virus.

Correct! RSV causes ~70% of bronchiolitis cases in infants <1 year. Peak incidence: 2-6 months. Presents: rhinorrhea → cough → wheezing/crackles → tachypnea → respiratory distress. Diagnosis: nasal swab rapid antigen or PCR. Treatment: supportive (O2, hydration, suctioning). Palivizumab (monoclonal antibody) or nirsevimab for prophylaxis in high-risk infants.
Otitis media is most commonly bacterial (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae).

questions.stem: RSV prophylaxis with nirsevimab or palivizumab is indicated for:

choices.feedback: RSV vaccines exist for adults ≥60, but prophylactic monoclonal antibodies target infants.
Prophylaxis targets high-risk infants, not all children.

choices.text: Any child with recurrent ear infections
choices.feedback: RSV prophylaxis is not indicated for otitis media.

choices.text: Premature infants, infants with chronic lung disease or hemodynamically significant CHD
choices.feedback: Correct! Nirsevimab (long-acting monoclonal, single dose): recommended for all infants entering their first RSV season and children <20 months at increased risk entering second season. Palivizumab (monthly injections): preterm <29 weeks, CLD, hemodynamically significant CHD. New maternal RSV vaccine (Abrysvo) also available for pregnant women at 32-36 weeks gestation.

questions.stem: The treatment of RSV bronchiolitis in infants is primarily:

choices.feedback: RSV is viral; antibiotics are not indicated.
Ribavirin is rarely used; reserved for severe immunocompromised cases.
Unlike asthma, bronchiolitis does not respond to steroids or bronchodilators.

Correct! RSV bronchiolitis treatment is supportive: supplemental O2 for SpO2 <90%, IV/NG fluids for dehydration, nasal suctioning for secretions, high-flow nasal cannula for respiratory distress. NOT recommended: bronchodilators (no proven benefit), corticosteroids (no benefit in bronchiolitis), antibiotics (viral illness). Hospitalization criteria: apnea, severe respiratory distress, poor feeding.

questions.explanation: Key detail: oxygen, hydration, and nasal suctioning.

questions.stem: RSV testing is most commonly performed by:

choices.feedback: Gram stain would not detect a virus.
Serology is not used for acute RSV diagnosis.

choices.text: Nasal swab with rapid antigen detection or RT-PCR
choices.feedback: Correct! Nasal swab or nasopharyngeal aspirate → rapid antigen test (quick, ~15 min, moderate sensitivity) or RT-PCR (gold standard, highly sensitive/specific). PCR-based multiplex respiratory panels can detect RSV along with influenza, parainfluenza, rhinovirus, etc. Testing guides isolation precautions and may reduce unnecessary antibiotic use.
RSV is a respiratory virus and does not grow in blood cultures.

questions.stem: RSV transmission is primarily through:

choices.feedback: RSV has no arthropod vector.
RSV is not truly airborne like measles.

Correct! RSV spreads via large respiratory droplets (close contact) and fomites (virus survives on surfaces for hours). Contact precautions + droplet precautions in healthcare settings. Hand hygiene is the most important prevention measure. RSV is highly contagious — nearly all children infected by age 2. Nosocomial outbreaks common in NICUs and pediatric wards.
RSV is a respiratory pathogen, not fecal-oral.

questions.stem: The most common cause of nosocomial fungal bloodstream infection is:

choices.feedback: Cryptococcus primarily causes meningitis in immunocompromised, not bloodstream infections.
Aspergillus causes invasive pulmonary disease but is less common in bloodstream infections.

Correct! Candida is the 4th most common cause of nosocomial bloodstream infections overall. Risk factors: broad-spectrum antibiotics, central lines, TPN, ICU stay, immunosuppression. C. albicans most common, but C. glabrata and C. auris (highly resistant) are increasing. Blood cultures are only ~50% sensitive; beta-D-glucan is an adjunctive marker.
Mucor causes rhinocerebral/pulmonary disease but not typical candidemia.

questions.stem: Candida species form germ tubes when incubated in serum at 37°C. This identifies:

choices.feedback: C. glabrata does not form germ tubes (it also doesn't form true hyphae).

Correct! Germ tube test: serum incubation at 37°C for 2-3 hours → C. albicans forms germ tubes (short hyphal extensions). This rapid test differentiates C. albicans from other Candida species. C. albicans also forms chlamydospores on cornmeal agar. Confirmation: CHROMagar (species-specific colony colors) or MALDI-TOF.
C. auris does not form germ tubes and is notoriously difficult to identify.
C. krusei is germ tube-negative.

questions.stem: Echinocandins (caspofungin, micafungin) are first-line for candidemia because they:

choices.text: Are effective against Mucor and Aspergillus equally
choices.feedback: Echinocandins are not effective against Mucor.
Echinocandins are IV-only (no oral formulation).

choices.text: Inhibit beta-1,3-D-glucan synthase in the fungal cell wall with broad Candida coverage
choices.feedback: Correct! Echinocandins target beta-1,3-D-glucan (fungal cell wall component not present in human cells) → fungicidal against Candida. Cover C. albicans, C. glabrata (often azole-resistant), C. tropicalis. Do NOT cover C. neoformans or Mucor. C. auris may be echinocandin-resistant. Fluconazole is appropriate for step-down after blood cultures clear.
Echinocandins have poor CNS penetration.

questions.stem: Oral thrush in an otherwise healthy adult should prompt evaluation for:

choices.feedback: Correct! Oral candidiasis in a healthy adult (not on antibiotics, inhaled steroids, or dentures) should raise suspicion for HIV or other immunodeficiency. Oral thrush is common in: infants (immature immune system), denture wearers, inhaled corticosteroid users (rinse mouth after use), antibiotics, diabetes, and immunocompromised (HIV, chemotherapy).
Celiac causes GI symptoms, not oral candidiasis.
Iron deficiency causes angular cheilitis, not thrush specifically.
HCV does not typically cause oral thrush.

questions.stem: C. auris is a significant public health concern because:

choices.text: It is transmitted by food contamination
choices.feedback: C. auris is healthcare-associated, not foodborne.

choices.text: It causes a unique hemorrhagic syndrome
choices.feedback: C. auris causes typical candidemia and wound infections.
C. auris primarily affects immunocompromised/hospitalized patients.

Correct! C. auris: often resistant to fluconazole (~90%), sometimes to echinocandins (~30%) and amphotericin B (~10%). Difficult to identify (misidentified as other species by standard methods — need MALDI-TOF or specific PCR). Persists on surfaces for weeks. Causes healthcare-associated outbreaks. CDC urgent threat category.

questions.explanation: Best answer: It is frequently multidrug-resistant and persists on surfaces, causing nosocomial outbreaks.

questions.stem: Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis most commonly occurs in:

choices.feedback: Healthy adults may get allergic reactions but not invasive disease.

Correct! Invasive aspergillosis requires significant immunosuppression: prolonged neutropenia (AML chemotherapy, stem cell transplant), high-dose corticosteroids, organ transplant recipients on immunosuppression. Aspergillus invades blood vessels → hemorrhagic infarction, cavitation. Classic CT finding: halo sign (ground glass surrounding nodule) early; air crescent sign late.
While neonates can get Aspergillus, the primary risk group is severely neutropenic patients.
DKA is the classic risk factor for Mucor (rhinocerebral mucormycosis), not Aspergillus.

questions.stem: ABPA (allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis) occurs in patients with:

choices.feedback: Post-transplant patients get invasive aspergillosis, not ABPA.

Correct! ABPA: type I (IgE) + type III (immune complex) hypersensitivity to Aspergillus antigens colonizing airways. Diagnostic criteria: asthma/CF + elevated total IgE + Aspergillus-specific IgE + elevated eosinophils + central bronchiectasis + fleeting pulmonary infiltrates. Treatment: systemic corticosteroids ± itraconazole. Not an infection — it's a hypersensitivity reaction.
Liver disease is not a risk factor for ABPA.
ABPA is an allergic/hypersensitivity condition, not an opportunistic infection.

questions.explanation: Key detail: a hypersensitivity reaction to Aspergillus.

questions.stem: First-line treatment for invasive aspergillosis is:

choices.feedback: Correct! Voriconazole is first-line for invasive aspergillosis (superior to amphotericin B in landmark trial). Alternatives: isavuconazole, liposomal amphotericin B. Echinocandins (caspofungin) are salvage therapy or combination. Monitor voriconazole trough levels (therapeutic drug monitoring). Common side effects: visual disturbances, hepatotoxicity, photosensitivity.
Metronidazole is an antibacterial with no antifungal activity.
Echinocandins are salvage/adjunctive therapy for aspergillosis, not first-line.
Fluconazole has NO activity against Aspergillus.

questions.stem: Galactomannan antigen testing is used to diagnose:

choices.feedback: Correct! Galactomannan: polysaccharide component of Aspergillus cell wall released during invasive growth. Detected in serum or BAL fluid by ELISA. Useful for early diagnosis and monitoring treatment response in neutropenic patients. BAL galactomannan is more sensitive than serum. False positives: piperacillin-tazobactam (historical), some foods, other fungi. Beta-D-glucan is broader (detects most fungi except Mucor/Crypto).
Beta-D-glucan is the marker for Candida; galactomannan is Aspergillus-specific.
PCP is diagnosed by organism identification on staining or beta-D-glucan.
Cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) testing is used for cryptococcosis.

questions.stem: Aspergilloma (fungus ball) is:

choices.text: A disseminated fungal infection in neutropenic patients
choices.feedback: That describes invasive aspergillosis.
That describes ABPA, not aspergilloma.

choices.text: An invasive form of aspergillosis requiring urgent surgery
choices.feedback: Aspergilloma is non-invasive and often monitored conservatively.

choices.text: A colonization of a pre-existing lung cavity by Aspergillus without tissue invasion
choices.feedback: Correct! Aspergilloma: saprophytic colonization of a pre-existing cavity (TB, sarcoidosis, emphysema). The fungus ball (matted hyphae) forms within the cavity but does not invade tissue. CXR: mobile intracavitary mass with air crescent sign (Monod sign). Usually asymptomatic; can cause hemoptysis (sometimes massive). Treatment: observation unless hemoptysis → surgical resection or embolization.

questions.stem: Acid-fast staining of M. tuberculosis is due to:

choices.feedback: Thick peptidoglycan is a gram-positive feature.

choices.text: High mycolic acid content in the cell wall
choices.feedback: Correct! Mycolic acids (long-chain fatty acids) make the cell wall waxy and hydrophobic → resists Gram stain, resists decolorization with acid-alcohol (acid-fast). Ziehl-Neelsen stain: red (acid-fast) bacilli against blue background. Auramine-rhodamine fluorescent stain is more sensitive for screening. Also resists drying, disinfectants, and many antibiotics.
M. tuberculosis does not form spores.
TB's acid-fastness comes from mycolic acids, not a capsule.

questions.stem: Latent TB infection is diagnosed by:

choices.text: Positive TST or IGRA in an asymptomatic patient with normal CXR
choices.feedback: Correct! Latent TB: positive tuberculin skin test (TST/PPD) or interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA/QuantiFERON), PLUS normal CXR and no symptoms. Patient is infected but not infectious. IGRA advantages: single visit, not affected by BCG vaccination, more specific. Treatment: isoniazid 9 months, or rifampin 4 months, or isoniazid+rifapentine 12 weekly doses.
Sputum AFB is positive in active TB, not latent TB.
Calcified granulomas suggest prior exposure but are not diagnostic of latent TB infection.

choices.text: Positive blood culture for M. tuberculosis
choices.feedback: Blood cultures are rarely positive even in active disseminated TB.

questions.stem: The standard initial treatment for active drug-susceptible TB is:

choices.feedback: Doxycycline has no role in TB treatment.
This regimen is for MAC (M. avium complex), not TB.
This is only 2 drugs; the intensive phase requires 4.

choices.text: RIPE therapy: rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol for 2 months, then rifampin and isoniazid for 4 months
choices.feedback: Correct! RIPE x 2 months (intensive phase) then RI x 4 months (continuation phase) = 6 months total. Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) given with INH to prevent peripheral neuropathy. Monitor: hepatotoxicity (INH, RIF, PZA), visual acuity (ethambutol), hyperuricemia (PZA), red-orange body fluids (RIF).

questions.stem: Miliary TB refers to:

choices.feedback: Correct! Miliary (disseminated) TB: hematogenous spread → tiny (1-2mm, millet seed-sized) granulomas in lungs, liver, spleen, bone marrow, meninges. CXR: diffuse bilateral reticulonodular pattern. More common in immunocompromised (HIV, children, elderly). Can present with pancytopenia, hepatitis, meningitis. May have negative TST (anergy).

choices.text: Extrapulmonary TB affecting only the spine
choices.feedback: Spinal TB (Pott disease) is a specific form of extrapulmonary TB, not miliary.

choices.text: TB limited to the lung apex
choices.feedback: Apical cavitary disease is reactivation TB, not miliary.
Drug resistance is defined by susceptibility testing, not dissemination pattern.

questions.explanation: Key detail: sized granulomas throughout the body.

questions.stem: TB immunity primarily depends on:

choices.feedback: While NK cells contribute, the primary defense is T cell-macrophage activation.

choices.text: Cell-mediated immunity via CD4+ T cells and macrophages
choices.feedback: Correct! CD4+ Th1 cells activate macrophages via IFN-γ → activated macrophages form granulomas to wall off M. tuberculosis. This is why HIV (destroys CD4+ cells) and TNF-α inhibitors (disrupt granuloma maintenance) dramatically increase TB risk. The TST/PPD tests detect this cell-mediated immune response (type IV hypersensitivity).
Complement plays a minimal role in TB defense.
Antibodies play a minimal role in TB immunity.

questions.stem: HIV binds to CD4+ T cells using:

choices.feedback: This is the SARS-CoV-2 mechanism.
This is a rotavirus mechanism.
HA and NA are influenza viral proteins.

choices.text: gp120 binding CD4, then gp41 mediating fusion via CCR5 or CXCR4 coreceptors
choices.feedback: Correct! HIV entry: gp120 binds CD4 receptor → conformational change → gp120 binds coreceptor (CCR5 on macrophages/early infection, CXCR4 on T cells/late infection) → gp41 mediates membrane fusion → viral RNA enters cell. CCR5-delta32 mutation: homozygous individuals resistant to HIV infection. Maraviroc blocks CCR5.

questions.stem: The window period in HIV testing refers to:

choices.text: Time between seroconversion and AIDS diagnosis
choices.feedback: The latency period (years without treatment) is a different concept.

choices.text: Time between infection and when the test becomes positive
choices.feedback: Correct! 4th-generation Ag/Ab combo tests (detect both p24 antigen and HIV-1/2 antibodies) have the shortest window: ~2-4 weeks. Antibody-only tests: 3-12 weeks. RNA PCR (viral load): detectable within 10-14 days. CDC algorithm: 4th-gen screen → if positive → HIV-1/2 differentiation assay → if indeterminate → RNA PCR. Window period is why recent exposure may test negative.

choices.text: Time between infection and first symptoms
choices.feedback: The window period is about detectability, not symptoms.
ARS typically lasts 2-4 weeks but is a clinical phenomenon, not the window period.

questions.stem: Opportunistic infections in HIV occur predominantly when CD4 count falls below:

choices.feedback: At CD4 <500, oral hairy leukoplakia and Kaposi sarcoma may appear, but most OIs occur at lower counts.
CD4 1000 is within normal range.

Correct! CD4 <200: PCP (Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia), esophageal candidiasis, Kaposi sarcoma — these are AIDS-defining conditions. CD4 <100: toxoplasmosis, cryptococcal meningitis. CD4 <50: MAC (M. avium complex), CMV retinitis. PCP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) starts at CD4 <200. ART should be initiated regardless of CD4 count.
At CD4 <50, the most severe OIs occur (MAC, CMV), but many OIs start at higher counts.

questions.explanation: Correct: 200 cells/µL (AIDS-defining).

questions.stem: The primary mechanism of HIV drug resistance is:

choices.feedback: HIV does not form biofilm.
HIV does not have a polysaccharide capsule.

choices.text: High error rate of reverse transcriptase leading to rapid mutation
choices.feedback: Correct! HIV reverse transcriptase lacks proofreading (3'→5' exonuclease) activity → ~1 mutation per replication cycle → rapid generation of resistant variants. This is why combination ART (≥3 drugs from ≥2 classes) is essential — single drugs rapidly select for resistance. Genotypic resistance testing guides regimen selection.
Plasmid transfer is a bacterial resistance mechanism.

questions.stem: Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) for HIV should be initiated within:

choices.text: 72 hours of exposure, ideally within 2 hours
choices.feedback: Correct! PEP: start within 72 hours (ideally ≤2 hours). Regimen: tenofovir/emtricitabine + raltegravir (or dolutegravir) for 28 days. Indicated after occupational (needlestick) or non-occupational (sexual assault, condom failure) exposure to HIV-positive source. Test source and exposed person. Follow up at 4-6 weeks and 3 months.

choices.text: Only after confirmed HIV-positive source test returns
choices.feedback: Do not delay PEP waiting for source testing results.
PEP efficacy decreases significantly if started after 72 hours.
PEP is not effective if started this late.

questions.stem: Hepatitis B serology showing HBsAg(−), anti-HBs(+), anti-HBc(−) indicates:

choices.feedback: Window period has anti-HBc IgM positive.

Correct! Vaccination produces anti-HBs only (the vaccine contains only HBsAg). Prior infection produces both anti-HBs and anti-HBc. Key patterns: Acute = HBsAg+, IgM anti-HBc+. Chronic = HBsAg+ >6 months, IgG anti-HBc+. Recovered = HBsAg−, anti-HBs+, anti-HBc+. Window period = HBsAg−, anti-HBs−, IgM anti-HBc+.
Acute infection would show HBsAg(+) and anti-HBc IgM(+).
Chronic carrier has HBsAg(+), not negative.

questions.stem: Hepatitis C is unique among hepatitis viruses because:

choices.text: It is transmitted only by the fecal-oral route
choices.feedback: Fecal-oral transmission is HAV and HEV. HCV is parenteral.

choices.text: It is the only DNA virus
choices.feedback: HCV is an RNA virus. HBV is the only DNA hepatitis virus.

choices.text: It has the highest rate of chronic infection (~75-85%)
choices.feedback: Correct! HCV: RNA flavivirus, ~75-85% develop chronic infection (highest chronicity rate). Leading cause of liver transplantation. Now curable with DAAs (sofosbuvir-based regimens) achieving >95% SVR in 8-12 weeks. No vaccine available. Transmitted parenterally (IVDU, blood products before 1992). Screen: anti-HCV antibody → if positive → HCV RNA PCR.
Chronic HCV is a major cause of cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma.

questions.explanation: Best answer: It has the highest rate of chronic infection (~75-85%) and is curable with direct-acting antivirals.

questions.stem: Hepatitis D (delta virus) requires which virus for replication?

choices.feedback: HDV is dependent on HBV specifically.
HDV requires HBV, not HAV.

Correct! HDV is a defective (incomplete) RNA virus that requires HBsAg coat from HBV to replicate and infect. Coinfection (HDV + HBV simultaneously): usually self-limited. Superinfection (HDV in chronic HBV carrier): severe, high risk of fulminant hepatitis and rapid cirrhosis. HBV vaccination prevents HDV infection.
HDV requires HBV surface antigen specifically.

questions.stem: Which hepatitis virus is a DNA virus?

choices.feedback: HAV is a picornavirus (RNA).
HEV is an RNA hepevirus.
HCV is an RNA flavivirus.

Correct! HBV is the only DNA hepatitis virus (partially double-stranded DNA, hepadnavirus family). Uses reverse transcriptase in its replication cycle. All others are RNA viruses: HAV (picornavirus), HCV (flavivirus), HDV (delta agent), HEV (hepevirus). HBV integrates into host genome → risk of hepatocellular carcinoma even without cirrhosis.

questions.stem: Hepatitis E is especially dangerous in:

choices.feedback: HEV is not specifically more dangerous in elderly men.
HEV is not a major neonatal pathogen.
IVDU is a risk factor for HBV and HCV, not HEV.

Correct! HEV has ~20% mortality in pregnant women (especially third trimester) — the highest of any hepatitis virus in pregnancy. Fecal-oral transmission (contaminated water in developing countries). Usually self-limited in others. No chronic infection in immunocompetent (can become chronic in transplant recipients). No vaccine available in the US.

questions.explanation: Correct: Pregnant women (high mortality in third trimester).

questions.stem: All herpes viruses share the ability to:

choices.feedback: Different herpesviruses have different transmission routes (contact, sexual, saliva, blood).
Hemorrhagic fevers are caused by filoviruses, not herpesviruses.

choices.text: Establish lifelong latency with potential reactivation
choices.feedback: Correct! All 8 human herpesviruses establish latency: HSV-1 (trigeminal ganglia), HSV-2 (sacral ganglia), VZV (dorsal root ganglia), EBV (B cells), CMV (mononuclear cells), HHV-6 (T cells), HHV-7 (T cells), HHV-8/KSHV (B cells → Kaposi sarcoma). Reactivation occurs with immunosuppression, stress, or other triggers.
Herpesviruses maintain episomal (circular) DNA during latency; they don't integrate like HIV.

questions.stem: EBV (HHV-4) is the causative agent of infectious mononucleosis and is also associated with:

choices.feedback: Kaposi sarcoma is caused by HHV-8 (KSHV).
Cervical carcinoma is caused by HPV (especially types 16 and 18).

choices.text: Burkitt lymphoma, nasopharyngeal carcinoma, and post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder
choices.feedback: Correct! EBV-associated malignancies: Burkitt lymphoma (jaw mass in African children, c-myc translocation t(8;14)), nasopharyngeal carcinoma (Southern Chinese population), Hodgkin lymphoma (mixed cellularity subtype), post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder (PTLD), oral hairy leukoplakia (HIV). EBV infects B cells via CD21 receptor.
HCC is associated with HBV and HCV.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Burkitt lymphoma, nasopharyngeal carcinoma, and post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder.

questions.stem: Congenital CMV is the most common congenital infection and can cause:

choices.feedback: This triad (+ deafness) is congenital rubella syndrome.

choices.text: Sensorineural hearing loss, periventricular calcifications, and hepatosplenomegaly
choices.feedback: Correct! Congenital CMV: most common congenital infection (0.5-1% of births). Symptomatic (~10%): sensorineural hearing loss (most common long-term sequela), periventricular calcifications (vs. toxoplasma = diffuse), microcephaly, hepatosplenomegaly, petechiae/blueberry muffin rash, chorioretinitis. 90% asymptomatic at birth but 10-15% develop late-onset hearing loss.
Hydrops can occur but is not the primary CMV presentation.
Limb defects are associated with thalidomide and amniotic band syndrome.

questions.stem: Neonatal herpes is most commonly caused by:

choices.feedback: HHV-6 (roseola) does not cause neonatal herpes.
Varicella can cause neonatal infection but neonatal herpes specifically refers to HSV.

choices.text: HSV-2 acquired during passage through an infected birth canal
choices.feedback: Correct! Neonatal herpes: highest risk when mother has primary genital HSV at delivery (~30-50% transmission). Recurrent maternal HSV has lower risk (~2-5%). Three forms: SEM (skin, eye, mouth — best prognosis), CNS (encephalitis), disseminated (worst prognosis, hepatitis, DIC). Treat with IV acyclovir. C-section recommended if active lesions at labor.
While HSV-1 genital infections are increasing, the classic cause is HSV-2.

questions.stem: Shingles (herpes zoster) reactivation can be prevented by:

choices.feedback: Correct! Shingrix (recombinant, adjuvanted): 2 doses, >90% efficacy, recommended for all adults ≥50 and immunocompromised adults ≥19. Replaced Zostavax (live attenuated, no longer available). VZV reactivates from dorsal root ganglia → dermatomal vesicular rash. Complications: postherpetic neuralgia, herpes zoster ophthalmicus (V1), Ramsay Hunt (CN VII + ear).
Flu vaccine does not prevent zoster.
Stress avoidance may help but vaccination is the proven preventive measure.
Daily antivirals are for HSV suppression, not routine zoster prevention.

questions.stem: A patient with AIDS (CD4 < 100) presents with headache, fever, and altered mental status. India ink prep of CSF shows encapsulated yeast. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?

choices.feedback: Fluconazole is used for maintenance therapy AFTER induction. Initial treatment of cryptococcal meningitis requires amphotericin B + flucytosine for at least 2 weeks.

Correct! Cryptococcal meningitis induction: amphotericin B deoxycholate + flucytosine × 2 weeks, then consolidation with fluconazole8 weeks, then maintenance fluconazole. Opening pressure management with serial LPs is critical — elevated ICP is the main cause of early mortality.
Echinocandins (caspofungin) have NO activity against Cryptococcus. The polysaccharide capsule makes it resistant to echinocandins.
Voriconazole is primarily used for Aspergillus. Cryptococcus requires amphotericin B-based induction.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Amphotericin B + flucytosine.

questions.stem: What is the primary virulence factor of Cryptococcus neoformans?

choices.feedback: Endotoxin (LPS) is a virulence factor of gram-negative bacteria, not fungi.
Cryptococcus is NOT dimorphic — it is a monomorphic yeast (always yeast form at both 25°C and 37°C). Dimorphism is seen in Histoplasma, Blastomyces, Coccidioides.
Ergot alkaloids are produced by Claviceps species, not Cryptococcus.

Correct! The polysaccharide capsule (glucuronoxylomannan) is the major virulence factor. It inhibits phagocytosis, interferes with antigen presentation, and suppresses T-cell responses. Detected by latex agglutination or lateral flow assay for capsular antigen in serum/CSF. India ink shows the capsule as a clear halo.

questions.stem: What is the most common environmental source of Cryptococcus neoformans?

choices.feedback: Bat caves (and bird droppings in endemic areas like Ohio/Mississippi River valleys) are associated with Histoplasma capsulatum.
Contaminated water is associated with Legionella, not Cryptococcus.
Rose thorns and plant matter are associated with Sporothrix schenckii (sporotrichosis).

Correct! C. neoformans is found in soil enriched with pigeon (bird) droppings. Inhalation of desiccated yeast cells or spores → primary pulmonary infection → hematogenous spread to CNS in immunocompromised. C. gattii is associated with eucalyptus trees and can infect immunocompetent hosts.

questions.stem: Which diagnostic test has the highest sensitivity for cryptococcal meningitis?

choices.feedback: Culture is the gold standard for confirmation but takes 3-7 days. CrAg provides rapid point-of-care diagnosis.

Correct! CrAg lateral flow assay has >99% sensitivity and >99% specificity in both serum and CSF. It detects capsular polysaccharide antigen. Serum CrAg can be positive 2-3 weeks before symptom onset, making it useful for screening in high-risk patients (CD4 < 100).
Gram stain is poorly sensitive for Cryptococcus. The encapsulated yeast is better visualized with India ink, and CrAg testing is far more sensitive than either stain.
India ink sensitivity is only ~50-75% and even lower in non-HIV patients. It visualizes the capsule but misses many cases.

questions.stem: A patient with cryptococcal meningitis has persistent headaches despite antifungal therapy. CSF opening pressure is 35 cm H2O. What is the most appropriate next step?

choices.feedback: Elevated ICP in cryptococcal meningitis is due to impaired CSF reabsorption by capsular polysaccharide, not treatment failure. Dose escalation won't address the pressure.

choices.text: Therapeutic lumbar puncture to reduce pressure
choices.feedback: Correct! Elevated ICP is the major cause of morbidity/mortality in cryptococcal meningitis. Serial therapeutic LPs (removing CSF) to maintain opening pressure < 20 cm H2O. If repeated LPs needed, consider lumbar drain or VP shunt. Goal: reduce pressure by 50% or to < 20 cm H2O.
The issue is elevated ICP from impaired CSF reabsorption, not antifungal resistance. Pressure management is the priority.

Corticosteroids are NOT recommended in cryptococcal meningitis — they worsen outcomes. Unlike bacterial meningitis, the inflammation is not the primary problem; the capsular antigen causing CSF obstruction is.

questions.stem: A patient from the Ohio River Valley presents with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and pulmonary infiltrates. What organism should be highest on the differential?

choices.feedback: Blastomyces is also endemic in the Ohio/Mississippi valleys but typically presents with pulmonary AND skin lesions (verrucous/papulopustular). Bilateral hilar LAD is more classic for Histoplasma.
Coccidioides is endemic in the Southwest US (Arizona, California, New Mexico), not the Ohio River Valley.
TB can cause similar findings but is less likely as the primary consideration in an Ohio River Valley resident without specific risk factors. Histoplasma is endemic in this region.

Correct! Histoplasma is endemic in the Ohio and Mississippi River valleys. Found in soil contaminated with bat/bird droppings. Inhalation → primary pulmonary infection that can mimic sarcoidosis or TB with bilateral hilar LAD. Dimorphic: mold at 25°C → yeast at 37°C (in macrophages). Diagnose with urine/serum Histoplasma antigen, culture, or tissue biopsy showing yeast within macrophages.

questions.stem: Histoplasma capsulatum is a dimorphic fungus. What is its form at body temperature (37°C)?

choices.feedback: Broad-based budding yeast is Blastomyces dermatitidis. Histoplasma has narrow-based budding and is much smaller (2-4 μm vs 8-15 μm).

Correct! At 37°C: small (2-4 μm) oval yeast found WITHIN macrophages. This intracellular location is key — Histoplasma survives inside macrophage phagolysosomes. At 25°C: mold with tuberculate macroconidia (diagnostic on culture). Mnemonic: 'Histo Hides in macropHages.'
Mold form occurs at 25°C (environmental temperature). At 37°C, Histoplasma converts to yeast form.
Spherules with endospores are the tissue form of Coccidioides immitis, not Histoplasma.

questions.stem: What is the most sensitive rapid diagnostic test for disseminated histoplasmosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Urine Histoplasma antigen (EIA) has ~90% sensitivity in disseminated disease and ~75% in acute pulmonary. Can also test serum antigen. Cross-reactivity with Blastomyces and Coccidioides occurs. Serial levels useful for monitoring treatment response. Culture remains gold standard but takes 2-4 weeks.
Blood culture can detect Histoplasma but requires specialized lysis-centrifugation technique and takes 2-4 weeks. Antigen testing provides much faster results.
Antibody tests have poor sensitivity in immunocompromised patients (who are most likely to have disseminated disease) because they may not mount an adequate antibody response.
KOH prep is insensitive for Histoplasma because the yeast are very small (2-4 μm) and intracellular. Tissue biopsy with GMS stain is better for direct visualization.

questions.stem: A construction worker in Tennessee develops acute fever, cough, and chest pain after demolishing an old barn with bird droppings. CXR shows diffuse reticulonodular infiltrates. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: While CAP is common, the exposure history (demolishing old barn with bird droppings in Tennessee — Ohio/Mississippi River valley endemic area) makes acute pulmonary histoplasmosis the top diagnosis.

Correct! Classic exposure: disruption of bird/bat droppings in endemic area (Ohio/Mississippi River valleys). Heavy inoculum → acute pulmonary histoplasmosis: fever, cough, chest pain, diffuse infiltrates. Usually self-limited in immunocompetent. Treat if symptoms > 4 weeks, severe disease, or immunocompromised. Mild: itraconazole. Severe: amphotericin B → itraconazole step-down.
Hypersensitivity pneumonitis from organic antigen exposure is possible but would not explain the fever pattern typical of acute histoplasmosis in an endemic area.
Sarcoidosis can mimic histoplasmosis radiographically, but the acute exposure history with a clear point source strongly suggests histoplasmosis over sarcoidosis.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the most common extrapulmonary manifestation of disseminated histoplasmosis in AIDS patients?

choices.feedback: Bone involvement can occur but is relatively uncommon compared to reticuloendothelial involvement.
Histoplasma endocarditis is rare and usually occurs on prosthetic valves, not as the most common manifestation.

Correct! Disseminated histoplasmosis in AIDS (CD4 < 150) most commonly involves the reticuloendothelial system: hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia (from bone marrow infiltration), and elevated LDH/ferritin. Also see oral mucosal ulcers, skin lesions, and adrenal involvement. Diagnosed by blood cultures (lysis-centrifugation), urine/serum antigen, and bone marrow biopsy showing intracellular yeast.
CNS involvement occurs in 5-20% of disseminated cases but is not the most common extrapulmonary manifestation.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man from Wisconsin presents with chronic cough, verrucous skin lesions, and a CXR showing a mass-like pulmonary infiltrate. Biopsy shows broad-based budding yeast. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Blastomyces presents with pulmonary + skin involvement (verrucous or papulopustular lesions). Biopsy: large (8-15 μm) broad-based budding yeast with thick refractile cell wall — pathognomonic. Endemic in Great Lakes, Ohio/Mississippi River valleys. Dimorphic: mold at 25°C, yeast at 37°C. Treatment: mild-moderate → itraconazole; severe → amphotericin B.
Coccidioides tissue form shows spherules with endospores, not broad-based budding yeast. It is also endemic in the Southwest, not Wisconsin.
Sporothrix causes lymphocutaneous lesions ascending along lymphatic drainage (gardener's rose thorn). It does not typically cause pulmonary mass lesions.
Histoplasma shows SMALL (2-4 μm) yeast WITHIN macrophages with narrow-based budding. The broad-based budding yeast described here is Blastomyces.

questions.stem: What is the characteristic microscopic finding of Blastomyces dermatitidis in tissue?

choices.feedback: Correct! Large (8-15 μm), round yeast with a thick double-contoured cell wall and BROAD-based budding (wide attachment between mother and daughter cells). This is pathognomonic. Compare: Histoplasma = small, intracellular, narrow-based; Cryptococcus = encapsulated, narrow-based; Coccidioides = spherules.
Pseudohyphae and germ tubes are seen with Candida albicans.
Spherules with endospores are Coccidioides immitis.
Small intracellular yeast within macrophages is Histoplasma capsulatum. Blastomyces is extracellular and much larger.

questions.stem: What is the treatment of choice for mild-to-moderate pulmonary blastomycosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Mild-moderate blastomycosis: itraconazole for 6-12 months . Severe/disseminated/CNS: amphotericin B lipid formulation → itraconazole step-down. Monitor itraconazole levels (therapeutic drug monitoring). All forms of blastomycosis require treatment (unlike histoplasmosis where mild cases may be observed).
TMP-SMX is an antibacterial/antiprotozoal, not used for fungal infections. It is the treatment for Pneumocystis (PCP).
Echinocandins have limited activity against dimorphic fungi including Blastomyces. They are primarily used for Candida and Aspergillus.
Fluconazole has poor activity against Blastomyces. Itraconazole is the preferred azole for blastomycosis.

questions.stem: Which feature helps distinguish blastomycosis from histoplasmosis clinically?

choices.feedback: Both Histoplasma and Blastomyces produce positive urine antigen tests with significant cross-reactivity, so this cannot distinguish them.

choices.text: Concurrent skin lesions with pulmonary disease
choices.feedback: Correct! Blastomycosis classically presents with BOTH pulmonary and skin lesions (verrucous, crusted, or papulopustular). Histoplasma rarely involves the skin in immunocompetent patients. Blastomyces can also involve bone (osteomyelitis) and genitourinary tract (prostatitis, epididymitis). Skin involvement occurs in 40-80% of extrapulmonary cases.
Fluconazole has poor activity against Blastomyces but good activity against many other fungi. Treatment response is not a reliable distinguishing feature.
Both Blastomyces and Histoplasma are endemic in the Ohio/Mississippi River valleys. Geography alone cannot distinguish them.

questions.stem: A patient with blastomycosis develops back pain and a vertebral lytic lesion. Which complication has occurred?

choices.feedback: Correct! Bone involvement occurs in 25-50% of disseminated blastomycosis, with vertebrae, ribs, and long bones most commonly affected. Presents as lytic lesions mimicking malignancy or TB. Treatment: severe → amphotericin B; mild → itraconazole × 12 months minimum. Always image the spine in disseminated blastomycosis.
While spinal TB is in the differential for vertebral lytic lesions, this patient has known blastomycosis. Blastomyces osteomyelitis is the most likely explanation.
While steroid-related fractures are possible, lytic lesions in the setting of blastomycosis indicate direct fungal bone involvement.
Lytic vertebral lesions in a patient with known blastomycosis should be attributed to fungal osteomyelitis rather than assuming a new malignancy.

questions.stem: A patient who recently moved from Arizona presents with fever, cough, erythema nodosum, and arthralgias. CXR shows hilar lymphadenopathy. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Sarcoidosis can cause erythema nodosum and hilar LAD, but the Arizona travel history makes Coccidioides the top consideration. This triad is classic for Valley Fever.

Correct! The triad of erythema nodosum + arthralgias + hilar LAD in a patient from the Southwest is classic for primary coccidioidomycosis (Valley Fever). Actually a good prognostic sign — indicates robust immune response. Endemic in Arizona, Central California (San Joaquin Valley), New Mexico, West Texas. Caused by inhalation of arthroconidia from desert soil. 60% asymptomatic; 40% flu-like illness.
CAP would not explain erythema nodosum and arthralgias. These extra-pulmonary findings with geographic exposure point to Coccidioides.
Acute HIV can cause fever and rash but not the classic triad of erythema nodosum + arthralgias + hilar LAD seen with Valley Fever.

questions.explanation: Correct: Coccidioidomycosis (Valley Fever).

questions.stem: What is the tissue form of Coccidioides immitis?

choices.feedback: Narrow-based budding intracellular yeast describes Histoplasma capsulatum.
Pseudohyphae are seen with Candida species, not Coccidioides.
Broad-based budding yeast is Blastomyces dermatitidis. Coccidioides has a unique tissue form.

Correct! In tissue (37°C): large spherules (20-70 μm) packed with endospores (2-5 μm). Spherule ruptures → releases endospores → each can develop into new spherule. In soil (25°C): septate hyphae with arthroconidia (barrel-shaped). Arthroconidia are extremely infectious — lab biosafety level 3 required.

questions.stem: Which patient with primary coccidioidomycosis requires antifungal treatment?

choices.text: All patients with positive CXR findings
choices.feedback: Most immunocompetent patients with primary pulmonary coccidioidomycosis self-resolve without treatment within weeks to months.

choices.text: Immunocompromised patient or Filipino/African American descent with severe symptoms
choices.feedback: Correct! Treatment indicated for: immunocompromised (HIV, transplant, TNF-α inhibitors), high-risk ethnicity (Filipino, African American — higher risk of dissemination), pregnant (3rd trimester), severe/prolonged symptoms (>2 months), high complement fixation titers (≥1:16), or disseminated disease. Mild: fluconazole or itraconazole. Severe/meningitis: fluconazole (lifelong for meningitis). Amphotericin B for life-threatening disease.
Erythema nodosum in coccidioidomycosis actually indicates a good immune response and favorable prognosis. It does NOT by itself require treatment.
Asymptomatic infection requires no treatment — most cases are self-limited in immunocompetent patients.

questions.stem: What is the most feared complication of disseminated coccidioidomycosis?

choices.feedback: Hepatic involvement can occur but is not the most feared complication. Meningitis carries the highest morbidity/mortality.
Erythema nodosum is actually a favorable prognostic sign indicating good cell-mediated immunity, not a feared complication.
Pulmonary cavities occur in ~5% of primary infections but are usually asymptomatic and often resolve spontaneously. They are not the most feared complication.

Correct! Meningitis occurs in ~30-50% of disseminated cases and is FATAL if untreated. Requires LIFELONG fluconazole. CSF findings: lymphocytic pleocytosis, low glucose, elevated protein, positive complement fixation antibodies in CSF. Hydrocephalus is a common complication requiring VP shunt. Intrathecal amphotericin B for refractory cases.

questions.stem: Which serologic test indicates high fungal burden and risk of dissemination in coccidioidomycosis?

choices.feedback: IgE is not used in coccidioidomycosis monitoring. It is relevant in allergic conditions and Aspergillus (ABPA).
A positive skin test indicates prior exposure and cell-mediated immunity. It is actually a GOOD prognostic sign. A negative skin test in active disease suggests anergy and higher dissemination risk.
IgM appears early (1-3 weeks) and indicates acute infection. It does NOT correlate with disease severity or dissemination risk.

Correct! IgG complement fixation (CF) titers correlate with disease severity. Titer ≥ 1:16 suggests high fungal burden and increased risk of dissemination. Rising titers indicate worsening; falling titers suggest improvement. Serial CF titers are used to monitor treatment response. CF titers in CSF confirm meningitis.
