questions.stem: A diabetic patient in DKA presents with black necrotic tissue on the hard palate, facial pain, and proptosis. CT shows sinus opacification with bone erosion. What is the most likely pathogen?

choices.feedback: Candida causes mucosal/systemic infections but not rhinocerebral disease with tissue necrosis.
S. aureus can cause sinus infections but does not cause the angioinvasive black necrotic tissue pattern seen in mucormycosis.
Aspergillus can cause invasive sinusitis but is more common in neutropenic patients. The DKA + rhinocerebral presentation is classic for Mucor/Rhizopus.

Correct! Rhinocerebral mucormycosis: DKA is the #1 risk factor (also neutropenia, iron overload/deferoxamine, transplant). Rhizopus species are most common. Angioinvasive → thrombosis → tissue necrosis (black eschar). Spreads from sinuses through cribriform plate to brain. Emergent treatment: surgical debridement + IV amphotericin B lipid formulation. Mortality 50-70% even with treatment.

questions.stem: What is the microscopic appearance of Mucor/Rhizopus on tissue biopsy?

choices.feedback: Septate hyphae with 45° dichotomous branching is Aspergillus. Mucor has a distinctly different appearance.
Budding yeast with pseudohyphae describes Candida species, not Mucor.
Encapsulated yeast describes Cryptococcus neoformans, not Mucor.

choices.text: Broad, ribbon-like, pauciseptate hyphae with 90° branching
choices.feedback: Correct! Mucorales show wide (6-16 μm), ribbon-like, irregularly branching hyphae with FEW or NO septa (pauciseptate) and 90° (right-angle) branching. Compare Aspergillus: narrow (3-6 μm), regularly septate, 45° branching. The wide, empty-looking hyphae with right-angle branching are pathognomonic for mucormycosis.

questions.stem: What is the most critical intervention for rhinocerebral mucormycosis?

choices.feedback: Fluconazole has NO activity against Mucorales. Surgical debridement (not just drainage) plus amphotericin B is required.
Mucorales are RESISTANT to voriconazole (unlike Aspergillus). This is a critical distinction — voriconazole use in breakthrough mucormycosis is actually a risk factor.
Echinocandins have minimal activity against Mucorales as monotherapy. However, combination echinocandin + amphotericin B may have synergy in some cases.

Correct! BOTH surgery AND amphotericin B are essential. Surgery: wide debridement of all necrotic tissue (may require orbital exenteration, maxillectomy). Medical: amphotericin B lipid formulation (high doses, 5-weight-based dosing). Also: correct underlying condition (reverse DKA, reduce immunosuppression). Isavuconazole or posaconazole for step-down or salvage therapy. Voriconazole has NO activity.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Aggressive surgical debridement + amphotericin B.

questions.stem: Which antifungal class is Mucor/Rhizopus notably RESISTANT to?

choices.feedback: Flucytosine is not typically used for mucormycosis but its resistance is not the key clinical teaching point. Voriconazole resistance is critical knowledge.
Amphotericin B is the FIRST-LINE treatment for mucormycosis. Mucorales are susceptible to polyenes.
While echinocandins are not first-line for mucormycosis, they are not the class most associated with resistance concerns. Voriconazole resistance is the most clinically important distinction.

Correct! Mucorales are intrinsically RESISTANT to voriconazole. This is clinically critical because voriconazole prophylaxis in neutropenic patients may select for breakthrough mucormycosis. Active azoles: posaconazole and isavuconazole (approved for mucormycosis). Fluconazole and itraconazole also have no activity. Treatment: amphotericin B lipid formulation first-line.

questions.stem: Why is diabetic ketoacidosis the strongest risk factor for rhinocerebral mucormycosis?

choices.text: Ketone bodies directly feed fungal metabolism
choices.feedback: Ketone bodies do not directly promote Mucorales growth. The iron availability mechanism is the key pathophysiologic link.
While dehydration occurs in DKA, reduced mucosal blood flow is not the primary mechanism enabling mucormycosis.
While hyperglycemia impairs neutrophil function, this is not the primary mechanism. The acidosis itself plays a more specific role.

choices.text: Acidosis impairs transferrin iron binding, providing free iron for fungal growth
choices.feedback: Correct! Acidosis disrupts transferrin's ability to bind iron → elevated free (unbound) serum iron → Mucorales have a specific iron uptake system (rhizoferrin siderophore) that thrives on free iron. Additionally, acidosis impairs chemotaxis and oxidative burst of phagocytes. This explains why deferoxamine (iron chelator) paradoxically INCREASES mucormycosis risk — Mucorales use deferoxamine-bound iron as a siderophore.

questions.stem: An HIV patient with CD4 count of 50 presents with progressive dyspnea, dry cough, and bilateral ground-glass opacities on CT. LDH is elevated. What is the most likely pathogen?

choices.feedback: Correct! PCP presents with subacute progressive dyspnea, dry cough, fever, bilateral ground-glass opacities, and elevated LDH in HIV patients with CD4 < 200. Classically diffuse bilateral infiltrates that spare the apices early. Diagnose with induced sputum or BAL for direct fluorescent antibody (DFA), GMS stain, or PCR. Beta-D-glucan elevated. Treatment: TMP-SMX (21 days); add prednisone if PaO2 < 70 or A-a gradient > 35.
CMV pneumonitis is rare even in AIDS and typically occurs at very low CD4 counts. PCP is far more common with this presentation.
TB in HIV can have atypical presentations but typically shows upper lobe infiltrates, cavitation, or miliary pattern — not the bilateral ground-glass pattern of PCP.
S. pneumoniae causes bacterial pneumonia with lobar consolidation, not bilateral ground-glass opacities. Also, LDH elevation is more characteristic of PCP.

questions.stem: When should adjunctive corticosteroids be added to TMP-SMX for PCP treatment?

choices.feedback: Steroids are NOT indicated for all PCP. They are reserved for moderate-to-severe hypoxemia.

choices.text: Only if patient is not already on ART
choices.feedback: ART status does not determine steroid use. The decision is based on degree of hypoxemia.
Steroids should be started BEFORE intubation becomes necessary. The trigger is PaO2 < 70 or A-a gradient > 35, not mechanical ventilation.
Correct! Adjunctive prednisone taper over 21 days. START within 72 hours of PCP treatment. Mechanism: reduces inflammatory lung injury during organism killing.

questions.explanation: Key detail: a gradient > 35 on room air.

questions.stem: At what CD4 count threshold should PCP prophylaxis be initiated in HIV patients?

choices.feedback: CD4 < 100 is the threshold for MAC prophylaxis (azithromycin) and when Toxoplasma/Crypto risk increases. PCP prophylaxis starts at < 200.
CD4 < 500 does not require PCP prophylaxis. Other OI prophylaxis thresholds: < 100 for MAC and toxoplasma (if seropositive).

Correct! Start PCP prophylaxis when CD4 < 200, CD4% < 14%, or presence of oropharyngeal candidiasis (indicates significant immunosuppression). First-line: TMP-SMX DS daily or 3×/week. Alternatives: dapsone, atovaquone, or aerosolized pentamidine. Discontinue when CD4 > 200 for ≥ 3 months on ART. TMP-SMX also provides cross-protection against Toxoplasma.
CD4 < 50 is the threshold for CMV retinitis risk. PCP prophylaxis should have been started much earlier at CD4 < 200.

questions.stem: Pneumocystis jirovecii is classified as which type of organism?

choices.feedback: Pneumocystis is a fungus, not a bacterium. It responds to TMP-SMX because of its unique folate synthesis pathway, similar to certain bacteria.

Correct! Despite being treated with TMP-SMX (an antibacterial), Pneumocystis is genetically classified as a FUNGUS based on rRNA sequencing. However, it lacks ergosterol in its cell membrane → amphotericin B and azoles are INEFFECTIVE. Contains beta-D-glucan in cell wall (elevated serum beta-D-glucan is a useful diagnostic marker). Cannot be cultured in vitro.
PCP was historically classified as a protozoan but rRNA analysis reclassified it as a fungus.
Mycobacteria are acid-fast bacteria. Pneumocystis is an atypical fungus.

questions.stem: Which laboratory marker is characteristically elevated in PCP and helps differentiate it from other pneumonias?

choices.feedback: Correct! LDH is characteristically elevated in PCP (often > 500 IU/L), reflecting alveolar cell damage. Sensitivity ~90% but low specificity. Also elevated: serum beta-D-glucan (from Pneumocystis cell wall glucan). The combination of elevated LDH + beta-D-glucan + bilateral GGOs + CD4 < 200 is highly suggestive. Definitive diagnosis requires organism identification on induced sputum or BAL (DFA, GMS stain, PCR).
Procalcitonin is elevated in bacterial infections. It is typically low or normal in PCP, helping distinguish PCP from bacterial pneumonia.
Galactomannan is specific for Aspergillus, not Pneumocystis. Beta-D-glucan is the fungal marker elevated in PCP.
CRP is nonspecific and elevated in many inflammatory conditions. LDH is more characteristic of PCP specifically.

questions.stem: A traveler returning from sub-Saharan Africa presents with cyclic fevers, rigors, and splenomegaly. Peripheral smear shows ring forms inside red blood cells. Which species causes the most severe disease?

choices.feedback: P. vivax causes benign tertian malaria with 48-hour fever cycles. It has a dormant liver stage (hypnozoite) but rarely causes severe disease.
P. malariae causes quartan malaria (72-hour cycles) and the mildest disease. It can cause nephrotic syndrome in chronic infections.
P. ovale is similar to P. vivax — benign tertian malaria with hypnozoites. It is less common and causes mild disease.

Correct! P. falciparum causes the most severe and fatal malaria. Unique features: can infect RBCs of ALL ages (high parasitemia), RBCs express PfEMP1 surface protein causing sequestration in capillaries → cerebral malaria, severe anemia, ARDS, hypoglycemia, renal failure, lactic acidosis. No dormant liver stage. Diagnosed by ring forms; banana-shaped gametocytes are pathognomonic. Treatment: severe → IV artesunate; uncomplicated → artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT).

questions.stem: What is the treatment for severe Plasmodium falciparum malaria?

choices.feedback: Atovaquone-proguanil (Malarone) is used for uncomplicated malaria and prophylaxis, not severe disease requiring IV therapy.
P. falciparum is widely chloroquine-RESISTANT in most endemic areas. Chloroquine is only used for P. vivax, P. ovale, P. malariae, and P. falciparum in limited chloroquine-sensitive areas.

Correct! Severe falciparum malaria: IV artesunate is first-line (superior to IV quinine in reducing mortality — SEAQUAMAT and AQUAMAT trials). Follow with full course of oral ACT once patient can take PO. Monitor for delayed hemolysis post-artesunate. Exchange transfusion considered if parasitemia > 10%. Uncomplicated: oral ACT (artemether-lumefantrine or atovaquone-proguanil).
Primaquine targets hypnozoites (dormant liver stages) of P. vivax and P. ovale. It is not effective against acute blood-stage P. falciparum. Must check G6PD before use.

questions.stem: Why must G6PD status be checked before administering primaquine?

choices.text: Primaquine causes hepatotoxicity in G6PD-deficient patients
choices.feedback: Hepatotoxicity is not the primary concern. The risk is hemolytic anemia.
G6PD is a human enzyme, not a parasite factor. The concern is drug toxicity to the patient, not parasite resistance.

choices.text: Primaquine causes oxidative hemolysis in G6PD-deficient patients
choices.feedback: Correct! Primaquine generates reactive oxygen species that damage RBCs. G6PD-deficient patients cannot regenerate glutathione → oxidative damage to hemoglobin → Heinz bodies → hemolytic anemia. Same mechanism as dapsone, nitrofurantoin, sulfonamides. Always test G6PD before prescribing primaquine or tafenoquine (for P. vivax/ovale hypnozoite eradication). Alternative for G6PD-deficient: tafenoquine is contraindicated; close monitoring with weekly primaquine regimen.

choices.text: Primaquine is ineffective in G6PD-deficient patients
choices.feedback: Primaquine still works in G6PD deficiency — the problem is it causes dangerous hemolytic anemia, not reduced efficacy.

questions.stem: Which Plasmodium species have a dormant liver stage (hypnozoite) that can cause relapse months to years later?

choices.feedback: Only P. vivax and P. ovale form hypnozoites. P. falciparum and P. malariae do not.

Correct! Only P. vivax and P. ovale form hypnozoites — dormant stages in hepatocytes that can reactivate months to years after initial infection. Requires primaquine or tafenoquine (anti-hypnozoite therapy) to achieve radical cure. Without hypnozoite treatment, patients will relapse. P. falciparum: no hypnozoites (relapses = reinfection or recrudescence from incomplete treatment).
P. falciparum does NOT form hypnozoites. It is the most lethal species but does not cause relapses from dormant liver stages.
P. falciparum has NO hypnozoite stage. P. malariae can persist as low-level blood-stage parasitemia (recrudescence) but does not form true hypnozoites.

questions.stem: What finding on peripheral blood smear is pathognomonic for Plasmodium falciparum?

choices.feedback: Band-form trophozoites are characteristic of P. malariae, not P. falciparum.

Correct! Crescent/banana-shaped gametocytes are pathognomonic for P. falciparum. Other unique features: multiple ring forms per RBC (high parasitemia), normal RBC size (unlike P. vivax which infects reticulocytes → enlarged RBCs), and ring forms may show applique/accolé forms (attached to RBC membrane). Trophozoites and schizonts rarely seen on smear (sequestered in capillaries).
Schuffner dots (stippling) are seen in P. vivax and P. ovale infected RBCs, not P. falciparum.
Enlarged infected RBCs are seen with P. vivax (which preferentially infects young reticulocytes). P. falciparum infects RBCs of all ages.

questions.stem: An HIV patient with CD4 of 50 presents with headache, confusion, and focal neurologic deficits. MRI shows multiple ring-enhancing lesions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: PML (JC virus) causes non-enhancing white matter lesions WITHOUT mass effect. Ring enhancement and mass effect favor toxoplasmosis.

CNS lymphoma is in the differential but typically shows a SINGLE ring-enhancing lesion (usually periventricular). Multiple lesions favor toxoplasmosis. Empiric toxo treatment trial helps differentiate.

Correct! In AIDS with CD4 < 100: multiple ring-enhancing lesions with surrounding edema = toxoplasma encephalitis until proven otherwise. Most common CNS mass lesion in AIDS. Usually reactivation of latent infection (Toxoplasma IgG positive). Treatment: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + leucovorin × 6 weeks. Empiric trial — if no improvement in 2 weeks, biopsy to rule out lymphoma. Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX (same as PCP prophylaxis) when CD4 < 100 and Toxo IgG+.
Cryptococcal meningitis causes meningeal inflammation, not ring-enhancing parenchymal lesions. Diagnosed by CrAg in CSF.

questions.stem: What is the definitive host for Toxoplasma gondii?

choices.feedback: Pigs are intermediate hosts (like humans) that harbor tissue cysts. Eating undercooked pork is a transmission route, but pigs are not the definitive host.
Dogs are not part of the Toxoplasma life cycle. Cats are the only definitive host.
Humans are intermediate hosts. The sexual cycle of Toxoplasma only occurs in felines.

Correct! Cats are the definitive host — sexual reproduction occurs in feline intestinal epithelium → oocysts shed in cat feces. Humans acquire infection by: ingesting oocysts from cat feces (contaminated soil/water), eating undercooked meat containing tissue cysts (bradyzoites), or congenital transmission (tachyzoites cross placenta). Pregnant women should avoid cat litter boxes.

questions.stem: What are the classic findings of congenital toxoplasmosis?

choices.feedback: Cataracts, sensorineural deafness, and PDA describe congenital rubella syndrome, not toxoplasmosis.
Hydrops fetalis with hepatosplenomegaly and anemia suggests parvovirus B19 or Rh incompatibility, not toxoplasmosis.
Limb hypoplasia and skin scarring in a dermatomal pattern describe congenital varicella syndrome, not toxoplasmosis.

Correct! Classic triad of congenital toxoplasmosis: chorioretinitis (most common finding), hydrocephalus, and diffuse INTRACRANIAL calcifications. Compare with congenital CMV: periventricular calcifications. Most infected neonates are asymptomatic at birth but develop chorioretinitis later. Highest risk: maternal PRIMARY infection during pregnancy (especially 1st trimester for severity, 3rd trimester for transmission rate). Treatment: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + leucovorin × 12 months.

questions.stem: What prophylactic medication prevents both PCP and Toxoplasma encephalitis in HIV patients?

choices.feedback: Correct! TMP-SMX (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) at double-strength daily prevents BOTH PCP (CD4 < 200) and Toxoplasma encephalitis (CD4 < 100, Toxo IgG+). This dual coverage makes TMP-SMX the preferred prophylactic agent. Alternatives for sulfa-allergic: dapsone + pyrimethamine + leucovorin, or atovaquone.
Fluconazole is used for fungal prophylaxis (Cryptococcus), not for PCP or Toxoplasma prevention.
Dapsone alone covers PCP but NOT Toxoplasma. For dual prophylaxis, dapsone must be combined with pyrimethamine + leucovorin.
Azithromycin is used for MAC prophylaxis (CD4 < 50), not PCP/Toxo prophylaxis.

questions.stem: How do you differentiate Toxoplasma encephalitis from primary CNS lymphoma on imaging in an AIDS patient?

choices.text: Toxo has surrounding calcification; lymphoma does not
choices.feedback: Calcification is not a reliable distinguishing feature in the acute setting. Number and location of lesions are more helpful.

choices.text: Only toxo is positive on Toxoplasma IgG serology
choices.feedback: Toxo IgG positivity shows prior exposure (present in ~30% of the population) and supports the diagnosis but does not definitively distinguish the two. A negative Toxo IgG makes toxo encephalitis very unlikely.
Both toxoplasmosis and lymphoma can show ring-enhancing lesions. The pattern of lesions helps differentiate.

Correct! Toxo: typically MULTIPLE ring-enhancing lesions at the gray-white matter junction, especially in basal ganglia. Lymphoma: usually SINGLE lesion, often periventricular, with homogeneous or ring enhancement. Thallium SPECT or PET-CT: lymphoma shows increased uptake; toxo does not. Empiric approach: treat for toxo × 2 weeks → if no improvement on imaging, biopsy for lymphoma. EBV PCR in CSF supports lymphoma diagnosis.

questions.explanation: Key detail: white junction; Lymphoma: single periventricular lesion.

questions.stem: A hiker returns from a camping trip and develops foul-smelling, greasy diarrhea, bloating, and flatulence without blood or fever. What is the most likely pathogen?

choices.feedback: Cryptosporidium causes profuse watery diarrhea, especially in immunocompromised. In immunocompetent, it is self-limited. The greasy/fatty stool pattern favors Giardia.
E. histolytica causes bloody diarrhea (dysentery) with flask-shaped ulcers. This non-bloody, malabsorptive picture is more consistent with Giardia.
Campylobacter causes inflammatory bloody diarrhea with fever, not the fatty malabsorptive picture described here.

Correct! Giardia: most common intestinal parasite in the US. Transmitted by contaminated water (cysts survive chlorination). Attaches to duodenal/jejunal mucosa → malabsorption of fats → steatorrhea (foul-smelling, greasy, floating stools), bloating, flatulence, NO blood or fever. Diagnosed by stool O&P (trophozoites/cysts), stool antigen test, or string test (Entero-Test). Treatment: metronidazole or tinidazole.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism by which Giardia causes diarrhea?

choices.feedback: Giardia does NOT produce toxins. This is the mechanism for cholera (cholera toxin) and ETEC (heat-labile/heat-stable toxins).
Mucosal invasion with bloody dysentery is Entamoeba histolytica or Shigella. Giardia does NOT invade tissue.

choices.text: Mucosal attachment causing malabsorption without invasion
choices.feedback: Correct! Giardia trophozoites attach to the duodenal/jejunal brush border via a ventral adhesive disc → physical coverage of absorptive surface + shortening of microvilli + increased intestinal permeability → malabsorption of fats (steatorrhea) and nutrients (lactose intolerance common). NO tissue invasion, NO blood in stool, NO significant inflammation. IgA deficiency is a risk factor for chronic giardiasis.
Giardia is extracellular — it adheres to the mucosal surface but does not enter cells. Intracellular replication is seen with Cryptosporidium.

questions.stem: What is the first-line treatment for Giardia lamblia infection?

choices.feedback: Albendazole has moderate anti-Giardia activity but is not first-line. It is primarily used for helminths (nematodes). Used as alternative or in combination for refractory giardiasis.
TMP-SMX is antibacterial. It has no activity against Giardia. It treats Cyclospora and Isospora but not Giardia.

Correct! First-line: tinidazole (single dose, ~90% cure rate, fewer side effects) or metronidazole (5-7 day course, ~85% cure rate). Alternatives: nitazoxanide (good for children, available as suspension), paromomycin (aminoglycoside, safe in pregnancy — not absorbed systemically). For refractory cases: combination metronidazole + quinacrine or albendazole.
Fluoroquinolones are antibacterial and have no activity against Giardia (a protozoan parasite).

questions.stem: Which immunodeficiency predisposes most strongly to chronic/recurrent Giardia infections?

choices.feedback: While HIV increases susceptibility to many OIs, IgA deficiency is more specifically linked to chronic giardiasis.

Correct! IgA is the primary mucosal antibody and the most important immune defense against Giardia. IgA deficiency (most common primary immunodeficiency, ~1:500) → inability to clear Giardia from intestinal mucosa → chronic/recurrent infections. Also at risk: X-linked agammaglobulinemia (Bruton), CVID, and other humoral immunodeficiencies. Cell-mediated immunity plays a secondary role.
CGD affects neutrophil oxidative burst and predisposes to catalase-positive bacterial/fungal infections (S. aureus, Aspergillus), not Giardia.
Terminal complement deficiencies predispose to Neisseria infections, not Giardia.

questions.stem: What diagnostic test has the highest sensitivity for Giardia?

choices.feedback: Giardia cannot be routinely cultured in clinical labs. Diagnosis relies on microscopy, antigen detection, or PCR.
A single O&P exam has only ~50-70% sensitivity due to intermittent cyst shedding. Three specimens collected on different days increase sensitivity to ~90%.

Correct! Stool antigen tests (EIA or DFA) have 95-100% sensitivity and >95% specificity — superior to microscopy. Detect Giardia-specific antigen (GSA-65) regardless of cyst shedding pattern. Faster and more reliable than O&P. PCR-based multiplex panels are also excellent but more expensive. If antigen negative but clinical suspicion remains: duodenal aspirate or string test (Entero-Test).
Serology is not useful for diagnosing active Giardia infection. Many people have antibodies from prior exposure without active infection.

questions.explanation: Correct: Stool antigen immunoassay (EIA/DFA).

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action shared by all beta-lactam antibiotics?

choices.feedback: Folic acid synthesis inhibition is the mechanism of sulfonamides (DHPS) and trimethoprim (DHFR).
DNA gyrase inhibition is the mechanism of fluoroquinolones, not beta-lactams.

Correct! All beta-lactams (penicillins, cephalosporins, carbapenems, monobactams) bind PBPs → block transpeptidation step of peptidoglycan cross-linking → weakened cell wall → osmotic lysis. They are bactericidal and time-dependent (efficacy depends on time above MIC, not peak concentration). Key resistance mechanisms: beta-lactamases (hydrolysis), altered PBPs (MRSA: mecA gene → PBP2a), porin mutations (gram-negatives).
30S ribosomal inhibition is the mechanism of aminoglycosides and tetracyclines, not beta-lactams.

questions.explanation: Key detail: binding proteins (PBPs) that cross-link peptidoglycan.

questions.stem: A patient with a documented penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis) needs treatment for a serious gram-negative infection. Which beta-lactam has the LOWEST cross-reactivity risk?

choices.feedback: Amoxicillin is a penicillin — contraindicated with a history of penicillin anaphylaxis.
First-gen cephalosporins share similar side chains with penicillins. While overall cross-reactivity is low (~2%), cefazolin is not the SAFEST option in severe penicillin allergy.
Carbapenems have very low but non-zero cross-reactivity with penicillins (~1%). Aztreonam has even lower risk with essentially zero cross-reactivity.

Correct! Aztreonam (a monobactam) has a unique ring structure with essentially NO cross-reactivity with penicillins or cephalosporins (except ceftazidime — shared R-group side chain). Safe in penicillin-anaphylaxis patients. Limitation: only covers gram-NEGATIVES (including Pseudomonas). No gram-positive or anaerobic coverage. Carbapenems also have very low cross-reactivity (~1%) with penicillins and can be used with caution.

questions.stem: Why are beta-lactam antibiotics considered time-dependent killers?

choices.text: They require prolonged courses of therapy to be effective
choices.feedback: Course length is not what time-dependent killing means. It refers to pharmacodynamic relationship between drug concentration and bacterial killing.

choices.text: Bacterial killing correlates with duration of drug concentration above MIC, not peak level
choices.feedback: Correct! Beta-lactam efficacy depends on fT>MIC (fraction of time concentration exceeds MIC). Goal: 40-70% of the dosing interval above MIC. This is why extended/continuous infusions improve outcomes for serious infections. Compare concentration-dependent killers (aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones): efficacy depends on Cmax/MIC ratio, so higher single doses are preferred.

choices.text: They only work during the growth phase of bacteria
choices.feedback: While beta-lactams are most effective against actively dividing bacteria, this is not what time-dependent killing refers to.

choices.text: They accumulate in tissues over time
choices.feedback: Tissue accumulation is a pharmacokinetic property, not the definition of time-dependent killing. Time-dependent refers to the PD parameter fT>MIC.

questions.stem: MRSA is resistant to methicillin (and all beta-lactams except ceftaroline) by which mechanism?

choices.feedback: While S. aureus does produce beta-lactamases (penicillinase), MRSA resistance is due to an altered target, not enzymatic destruction.

choices.text: Acquisition of mecA gene encoding altered PBP2a
choices.feedback: Correct! MRSA carries the mecA gene (on SCCmec mobile genetic element) → produces PBP2a, which has very low affinity for all beta-lactams → cross-links peptidoglycan normally despite beta-lactam presence. Ceftaroline (5th-gen cephalosporin) is the ONLY beta-lactam with activity against PBP2a. Treatment options: vancomycin, daptomycin, linezolid, TMP-SMX, ceftaroline.
Porin mutations cause resistance in gram-negative bacteria. S. aureus is gram-positive and does not have an outer membrane with porins.
Efflux pumps are a resistance mechanism for tetracyclines and some other antibiotics, not the primary MRSA mechanism.

questions.stem: What is the role of clavulanate in amoxicillin-clavulanate (Augmentin)?

choices.feedback: Clavulanate has minimal direct antibacterial activity. Its role is solely as a beta-lactamase inhibitor to protect the partner antibiotic.

choices.text: Increases renal tubular reabsorption of amoxicillin
choices.feedback: That describes probenecid's mechanism (blocks tubular secretion of penicillins), not clavulanate.

choices.text: Broadens gram-negative spectrum by altered PBP binding
choices.feedback: Clavulanate does not directly kill bacteria or bind PBPs. It protects the beta-lactam antibiotic.

Correct! Clavulanate is a beta-lactamase inhibitor — structurally resembles a beta-lactam but has minimal antibacterial activity. Binds irreversibly to beta-lactamases (suicide substrate) → protects amoxicillin from enzymatic destruction. Extends coverage to beta-lactamase producers: H. influenzae, Moraxella, E. coli, Klebsiella, anaerobes. Other BLIs: sulbactam (+ ampicillin), tazobactam (+ piperacillin), avibactam (+ ceftazidime — covers some KPC-producing CRE).

questions.stem: What is the primary mechanism of action of fluoroquinolones?

choices.text: Inhibition of DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV
choices.feedback: Correct! Fluoroquinolones inhibit bacterial DNA gyrase (primary target in gram-negatives) and topoisomerase IV (primary target in gram-positives) → prevent DNA supercoiling and separation → inhibit DNA replication/transcription → bactericidal. Concentration-dependent killing (Cmax/MIC ratio). Key FQs: ciprofloxacin (best Pseudomonas), levofloxacin (respiratory FQ), moxifloxacin (respiratory + anaerobes, no Pseudomonas).
Membrane disruption is the mechanism of daptomycin and polymyxins, not fluoroquinolones.
RNA polymerase inhibition is the mechanism of rifampin, not fluoroquinolones.
Cell wall synthesis inhibition is the mechanism of beta-lactams and vancomycin.

questions.stem: Which fluoroquinolone has the BEST antipseudomonal activity?

choices.feedback: Correct! Ciprofloxacin has the strongest antipseudomonal activity among FQs. Often used for Pseudomonas UTIs, osteomyelitis (excellent oral bioavailability), and as step-down therapy. Levofloxacin also covers Pseudomonas but less potently. Moxifloxacin does NOT cover Pseudomonas. Cipro limitations: poor Streptococcus pneumoniae coverage → not ideal for CAP monotherapy.
Levofloxacin has some Pseudomonas activity but less potent than ciprofloxacin. It is preferred for respiratory infections due to better pneumococcal coverage.
Gemifloxacin is a respiratory FQ similar to moxifloxacin with no significant Pseudomonas activity.
Moxifloxacin is a respiratory fluoroquinolone with excellent gram-positive and anaerobic coverage but POOR Pseudomonas activity. Never use moxifloxacin for Pseudomonas.

questions.stem: Which serious adverse effect carries a boxed warning for fluoroquinolones?

choices.text: Tendon rupture, peripheral neuropathy, and CNS effects
choices.feedback: Correct! Boxed warnings include: tendinitis/tendon rupture (especially Achilles — risk increased with age > 60, steroids, renal failure, organ transplant), peripheral neuropathy (may be irreversible), CNS effects (seizures, psychosis, confusion), and aortic dissection/aneurysm risk. Also: QTc prolongation (especially moxifloxacin), C. difficile, hypoglycemia. Avoid in children (cartilage damage) except for specific indications.
Bone marrow suppression is associated with chloramphenicol and linezolid, not fluoroquinolones.
While FQ-related hepatotoxicity can occur, it is not the primary black box warning concern.
Ototoxicity is the hallmark adverse effect of aminoglycosides, not fluoroquinolones.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Tendon rupture, peripheral neuropathy, and CNS effects.

questions.stem: Why should fluoroquinolones NOT be used as first-line monotherapy for community-acquired pneumonia from Streptococcus pneumoniae?

choices.feedback: FQs have excellent oral bioavailability (near 100% for some), which is actually an advantage. This is not a limitation.

choices.text: Drug interactions with common cardiac medications
choices.feedback: While FQs have some drug interactions (QTc prolongation), this is not the primary reason they are not first-line for CAP.

choices.text: Overuse promotes resistance and masks tuberculosis
choices.feedback: Correct! FQs are active against both S. pneumoniae AND M. tuberculosis. Using FQs for suspected CAP in a patient who actually has TB → partial treatment → symptom improvement → delayed TB diagnosis → ongoing transmission. ATS/IDSA guidelines reserve respiratory FQs for CAP with comorbidities or when beta-lactam allergy exists. Also, FQ overuse drives pneumococcal resistance. Use beta-lactam + macrolide as first-line for CAP.

choices.text: They have no activity against S. pneumoniae
choices.feedback: Respiratory FQs (levofloxacin, moxifloxacin) DO cover S. pneumoniae. The concern is different.

questions.stem: A patient on ciprofloxacin takes an antacid containing aluminum/magnesium. What interaction occurs?

choices.text: Chelation reduces ciprofloxacin absorption, causing therapeutic failure
choices.feedback: Correct! Divalent/trivalent cations (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺, Fe²⁺/³⁺, Zn²⁺) chelate FQs in the GI tract → form insoluble complexes → dramatically reduced oral absorption → subtherapeutic levels → treatment failure. Avoid: antacids, calcium supplements, iron, dairy (for cipro), sucralfate, multivitamins within 2 hours before or 6 hours after FQ dose.
The interaction does not increase toxicity — it decreases efficacy.
Ciprofloxacin does not affect antacid metabolism. The interaction is one-directional: the cations reduce FQ absorption.
pH changes do not inactivate FQs. The mechanism is chelation (direct chemical binding), not pH-dependent inactivation.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of macrolide antibiotics (azithromycin, erythromycin, clarithromycin)?

choices.feedback: DNA gyrase inhibition is the mechanism of fluoroquinolones, not macrolides.

choices.text: Inhibition of cell wall synthesis via D-ala-D-ala binding
choices.feedback: D-ala-D-ala binding is the mechanism of vancomycin (glycopeptide), not macrolides.
30S inhibition is the mechanism of aminoglycosides and tetracyclines. Macrolides target the 50S subunit.

choices.text: Binding to 50S ribosomal subunit, blocking translocation
choices.feedback: Correct! Macrolides bind the 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit → block translocation step of protein synthesis → bacteriostatic (bactericidal at high concentrations against some organisms). Cover: gram-positives, atypicals (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella), some gram-negatives (H. influenzae, Moraxella). Also: anti-inflammatory/immunomodulatory properties (used in CF, COPD, bronchiectasis).

questions.stem: Why is azithromycin preferred over erythromycin for most clinical indications?

choices.feedback: Azithromycin's spectrum is similar to erythromycin. The advantages are pharmacokinetic.
No macrolide covers MRSA. MRSA often carries erm genes (methylation of 23S rRNA) conferring macrolide resistance.

Correct! Azithromycin advantages: very long half-life (~68 hours) → once-daily dosing and short courses (Z-pack: 5 days); excellent tissue/intracellular penetration (concentrates in macrophages → direct delivery to infection sites); significantly fewer GI side effects than erythromycin (which stimulates motilin receptors → nausea, vomiting, diarrhea). Erythromycin is rarely used now except for gastroparesis (motilin agonist effect) and neonatal infections.
Azithromycin has FEWER drug interactions than erythromycin/clarithromycin (which are CYP3A4 inhibitors), but it still has some interactions and can prolong QTc.

questions.explanation: Key detail: life, and fewer GI side effects.

questions.stem: Which macrolide is the strongest CYP3A4 inhibitor and has the most drug interactions?

choices.feedback: Correct! Erythromycin is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor → increases levels of: statins (rhabdomyolysis risk), warfarin (bleeding), carbamazepine (toxicity), theophylline (seizures), cyclosporine, and many others. Clarithromycin is also a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor. Azithromycin has minimal CYP inhibition → preferred when drug interactions are a concern.
Azithromycin has minimal CYP3A4 inhibition — this is one of its advantages over other macrolides.
Clarithromycin is a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor but erythromycin is classically considered the strongest and most problematic for drug interactions among the macrolides.
Fidaxomicin is a macrolide-class antibiotic used specifically for C. difficile. It acts locally in the gut with minimal systemic absorption and minimal CYP interactions.

questions.stem: Which cardiac adverse effect is associated with macrolides, especially azithromycin?

choices.feedback: Myocarditis is not a recognized macrolide adverse effect. QTc prolongation is the primary cardiac concern.

choices.text: QTc prolongation with risk of Torsades de Pointes
choices.feedback: Correct! All macrolides can prolong the QTc interval → risk of Torsades de Pointes (polymorphic ventricular tachycardia). Warning for azithromycin especially in patients with: baseline QTc prolongation, electrolyte abnormalities (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia), concurrent QTc-prolonging drugs, or cardiac disease. Erythromycin IV has the highest risk. Always check ECG and electrolytes in high-risk patients.
Macrolides do not cause structural valve disease. QTc prolongation and arrhythmia risk are the concerns.
Macrolides do not typically cause bradycardia. The cardiac risk is arrhythmia-related.

questions.stem: For which non-infectious condition is azithromycin used as chronic therapy?

choices.feedback: Correct! Azithromycin3×/week reduces COPD exacerbation frequency by ~27% and improves quality of life (MACRO trial). Also used in bronchiectasis, cystic fibrosis, and diffuse panbronchiolitis. Mechanism: anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory properties (reduces neutrophilic inflammation, inhibits biofilm formation, modulates macrophage function). Monitor for hearing loss and QTc prolongation with chronic use.
Azithromycin is not used for RA. Disease-modifying agents (methotrexate, biologics) are the mainstay.
While macrolides have been studied in chronic sinusitis, this is not an established indication. The strongest evidence is for COPD/bronchiectasis.
Azithromycin is not a standard treatment for IBD. Aminosalicylates, steroids, and biologics are used.

questions.explanation: Best answer: COPD exacerbation prevention and bronchiectasis.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of aminoglycosides (gentamicin, tobramycin, amikacin)?

choices.feedback: RNA polymerase inhibition is the mechanism of rifampin, not aminoglycosides.

choices.text: Irreversible binding to 30S ribosomal subunit causing misread of mRNA
choices.feedback: Correct! Aminoglycosides bind irreversibly to the 16S rRNA of the 30S ribosomal subunit → misreading of mRNA codons → incorporation of wrong amino acids → nonfunctional proteins + membrane damage → bactericidal. Concentration-dependent killing (Cmax/MIC ratio) with post-antibiotic effect (PAE). Extended-interval (once-daily) dosing exploits both properties. Require oxygen for uptake → NO activity against anaerobes.
Folate synthesis inhibition is the mechanism of TMP-SMX, not aminoglycosides.
50S inhibition is the mechanism of macrolides, chloramphenicol, and linezolid. Aminoglycosides target the 30S subunit.

questions.stem: What are the two major dose-limiting toxicities of aminoglycosides?

choices.feedback: Tendon rupture is associated with fluoroquinolones. These are not aminoglycoside toxicities.

Correct! Nephrotoxicity: accumulation in proximal tubular cells → acute tubular necrosis (usually reversible). Risk factors: prolonged use, trough levels, concurrent nephrotoxins (vancomycin, amphotericin B). Ototoxicity: damage to cochlear hair cells (hearing loss — often irreversible) and vestibular damage (vertigo). Monitor: serum levels (peaks and troughs), renal function, audiometry for prolonged courses.
QTc prolongation is associated with fluoroquinolones and macrolides. Neuromuscular blockade (not seizures) can occur with aminoglycosides.
Aminoglycosides are not significantly hepatotoxic or myelosuppressive. Their toxicities are organ-specific.

questions.stem: Why do aminoglycosides have no activity against anaerobic bacteria?

choices.feedback: This would be an acquired resistance mechanism. Anaerobe resistance to aminoglycosides is intrinsic, due to uptake requirements.
All bacteria have ribosomes. Anaerobes lack the oxygen-dependent transport required for aminoglycoside uptake.

Correct! Aminoglycosides require oxygen-dependent electron transport for active uptake across the bacterial cell membrane. Anaerobes lack this oxidative metabolism → cannot transport aminoglycosides intracellularly → intrinsic resistance. This also explains reduced activity in abscesses (low O2, low pH). For the same reason, aminoglycosides have reduced activity in low-pH environments.

choices.text: Anaerobes have thicker cell walls that block penetration
choices.feedback: Cell wall thickness is not the barrier. The issue is energy-dependent transport requiring aerobic metabolism.

questions.explanation: Key detail: dependent active transport.

questions.stem: Why is once-daily (extended-interval) dosing preferred for aminoglycosides?

choices.feedback: Aminoglycosides have essentially zero oral bioavailability — they must be given IV/IM (or topical/inhaled). Dosing interval does not affect this.

choices.text: Aminoglycosides have a very long half-life
choices.feedback: Aminoglycosides actually have relatively short half-lives (2-3 hours). The rationale for once-daily dosing is pharmacodynamic, not pharmacokinetic.

Correct! Aminoglycosides exhibit concentration-dependent killing (higher peak = more bacterial killing) and a prolonged post-antibiotic effect (PAE — continued bacterial suppression after levels drop below MIC). Once-daily dosing: higher Cmax/MIC ratio → better killing; longer drug-free interval → reduced proximal tubular accumulation → less nephrotoxicity. Monitor with a trough level drawn before the next dose (goal: undetectable).

choices.text: It reduces the total daily dose required
choices.feedback: The total daily dose is approximately the same; it's given as one large dose instead of divided doses.

questions.explanation: Key detail: dependent killing while reducing trough-related toxicity.

questions.stem: A patient on gentamicin develops high-frequency hearing loss. What is the mechanism?

choices.feedback: Aminoglycoside ototoxicity affects the hair cells of the cochlea, not the auditory nerve.
Aminoglycosides do not cause tympanic membrane damage. The toxicity is to cochlear hair cells.

choices.text: Destruction of outer hair cells in the organ of Corti, starting at the base
choices.feedback: Correct! Aminoglycosides accumulate in inner ear perilymph → generate reactive oxygen species → destroy outer hair cells of the cochlea. Damage starts at the BASE of the cochlea (which detects high frequencies) → progressive high-frequency hearing loss that may advance to lower frequencies. Often IRREVERSIBLE. A mitochondrial mutation (MT-RNR1 A1555G) causes profound susceptibility — even a single dose can cause deafness in carriers.
Aminoglycoside ototoxicity is sensorineural (inner ear), not conductive (middle ear/Eustachian tube).

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of vancomycin?

choices.feedback: 50S ribosomal inhibition is the mechanism of macrolides and oxazolidinones (linezolid), not vancomycin.

choices.text: Disruption of cell membrane via lipopeptide insertion
choices.feedback: Cell membrane disruption is the mechanism of daptomycin, not vancomycin.
PBP binding is the mechanism of beta-lactams. Vancomycin acts at an earlier step.

choices.text: Binding to D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of peptidoglycan precursors, blocking cross-linking
choices.feedback: Correct! Vancomycin (a glycopeptide) binds to the D-alanyl-D-alanine (D-Ala-D-Ala) terminus of the NAM-pentapeptide → sterically blocks both transglycosylation and transpeptidation → no peptidoglycan cross-linking → cell wall weakened → bactericidal. Too large to cross gram-negative outer membrane → only covers gram-POSITIVES. Time-dependent killing with AUC/MIC as the key PD parameter.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of vancomycin-resistant enterococcus (VRE)?

choices.feedback: VRE resistance is not due to enzymatic destruction. The target is modified.

choices.text: Modification of D-Ala-D-Ala to D-Ala-D-Lac, reducing vancomycin binding
choices.feedback: Correct! VRE (vanA/vanB genes) produces modified peptidoglycan precursors: D-Ala-D-Ala → D-Ala-D-Lactate (vanA) or D-Ala-D-Serine (vanB). Vancomycin binding affinity reduced 1000-fold → resistance. vanA: high-level resistance to both vancomycin AND teicoplanin. vanB: resistant to vancomycin only. Treatment options for VRE: linezolid, daptomycin, tigecycline.
While enterococci can form biofilms, VRE resistance is genetically encoded target modification, not biofilm-mediated.
Efflux pumps are not the primary VRE resistance mechanism. Target modification is the key mechanism.

questions.stem: What adverse reaction occurs with rapid IV vancomycin infusion?

choices.feedback: Red man syndrome is NOT IgE-mediated and is not true anaphylaxis. It is a direct histamine release.

choices.text: Red man syndrome from direct mast cell histamine release
choices.feedback: Correct! Rapid vancomycin infusion → direct (non-IgE-mediated) mast cell degranulation → histamine release → flushing, erythema, pruritus of face/neck/upper body, and hypotension. NOT a true allergy. Management: slow infusion rate (≥1 hour for 1g), pretreat with diphenhydramine. Does NOT preclude future vancomycin use. True vancomycin allergy (rare) presents as anaphylaxis or severe rash.
Serotonin syndrome is associated with serotonergic drugs (SSRIs, linezolid), not vancomycin.
SJS is a delayed hypersensitivity reaction associated with certain drugs (sulfonamides, anticonvulsants) but not typically caused by rapid vancomycin infusion.

questions.stem: When is ORAL vancomycin indicated?

choices.feedback: Oral vancomycin has no role in UTI — it is not absorbed and never reaches the urinary tract. Also, VRE is by definition resistant to vancomycin.
Oral vancomycin is not used for surgical prophylaxis. IV vancomycin (for MRSA-colonized patients) or IV cefazolin are used.
Oral vancomycin is NOT absorbed systemically — it stays in the GI tract. MRSA bacteremia requires IV vancomycin.

Correct! Oral vancomycin is first-line for C. difficile infection (with or without fidaxomicin). It remains in the GI lumen → high colonic concentrations → kills C. difficile locally. NOT absorbed systemically → cannot treat systemic infections orally. Standard dose: PO QID × 10 days. Severe/fulminant: higher dose + consider IV metronidazole and/or rectal vancomycin if ileus.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of tetracyclines (doxycycline, minocycline)?

choices.feedback: Cell wall synthesis inhibition is the mechanism of beta-lactams and vancomycin, not tetracyclines.
Membrane disruption is the mechanism of daptomycin and polymyxins, not tetracyclines.

choices.text: Reversible binding to 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking aminoacyl-tRNA binding
choices.feedback: Correct! Tetracyclines bind reversibly to the 30S ribosomal subunit → block binding of aminoacyl-tRNA to the A site of the mRNA-ribosome complex → inhibit protein synthesis → bacteriostatic. Broad-spectrum: gram-positives, gram-negatives, atypicals (Rickettsia, Chlamydia, Mycoplasma), spirochetes, some protozoa. Doxycycline is the most commonly used: BID dosing, does NOT require renal dose adjustment.
DNA gyrase inhibition is the mechanism of fluoroquinolones, not tetracyclines.

questions.stem: Why are tetracyclines contraindicated in children under 8 and during pregnancy?

choices.text: They deposit in developing teeth and bones, causing permanent discoloration
choices.feedback: Correct! Tetracyclines chelate calcium → deposit in developing teeth (permanent yellow-brown discoloration, enamel hypoplasia) and bones (temporary growth retardation). Teeth are most vulnerable during calcification (in utero through age 8). Pregnancy category D. Exception: doxycycline binds calcium less avidly and the AAP considers short courses (≤21 days) acceptable in children for specific indications (Rocky Mountain spotted fever, Lyme disease) when benefits outweigh risks.

choices.text: They cause renal failure in developing kidneys
choices.feedback: Renal issues are not the primary concern. The toxicity affects calcified tissues.

choices.text: They suppress bone marrow in pediatric patients
choices.feedback: Bone marrow suppression is not a tetracycline adverse effect. Chloramphenicol and linezolid cause this.

choices.text: They cause kernicterus by displacing bilirubin
choices.feedback: Bilirubin displacement and kernicterus risk is associated with sulfonamides in neonates, not tetracyclines.

questions.stem: Which tetracycline is the drug of choice for Rocky Mountain spotted fever?

choices.feedback: While older tetracyclines work, doxycycline is specifically recommended due to better PK and fewer side effects.
Minocycline is primarily used for acne and some MRSA infections. Doxycycline is preferred for rickettsial diseases.
Chloramphenicol was historically used as an alternative but doxycycline is now universally preferred due to better safety profile.

Correct! Doxycycline is the drug of choice for ALL rickettsial infections (RMSF, ehrlichiosis, anaplasmosis) — even in children and pregnant women when the diagnosis is suspected. RMSF mortality is high without early empiric doxycycline. Do NOT wait for confirmatory testing. Also first-line for: Lyme disease (doxycycline), Chlamydia, MRSA skin infections (community-acquired), malaria prophylaxis, Q fever.

questions.stem: What common drug interaction reduces tetracycline absorption?

choices.feedback: While rifampin can reduce doxycycline levels via enzyme induction, the classic and most common interaction is chelation with divalent cations.
PPIs affect absorption of some drugs but are not the classic tetracycline interaction. The issue is chelation.
ACE inhibitors do not significantly interact with tetracyclines.

Correct! Same mechanism as fluoroquinolone chelation: divalent/trivalent cations (Ca²⁺, Fe²⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺) form insoluble chelation complexes with tetracyclines → dramatically reduced absorption. Avoid: dairy products, antacids, calcium/iron supplements, sucralfate within 2-3 hours of dose. Doxycycline is least affected by food (take with food to reduce GI upset — but avoid dairy/supplements at the time of dosing).

questions.explanation: Correct: Divalent cations (calcium, iron, magnesium, antacids).

questions.stem: Which adverse effect is unique to minocycline compared to other tetracyclines?

choices.text: Vestibular toxicity (vertigo, dizziness) and blue-gray skin discoloration
choices.feedback: Correct! Minocycline-specific: vestibular toxicity (dose-dependent vertigo, nausea, ataxia — more common in women) and blue-gray skin/mucous membrane discoloration (with prolonged use, can involve face, shins, sclera — may be permanent). Also can cause drug-induced lupus and autoimmune hepatitis. These unique toxicities make doxycycline generally preferred unless minocycline's specific advantages (e.g., acne, some MRSA) are needed.
Hepatic steatosis (especially microvesicular fatty change) is associated with IV tetracycline at high doses, not specifically minocycline.
All antibiotics can predispose to C. difficile. This is not unique to minocycline.
Photosensitivity occurs with all tetracyclines (especially doxycycline). It is not unique to minocycline.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Vestibular toxicity (vertigo, dizziness) and blue-gray skin discoloration.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of TMP-SMX (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole)?

choices.feedback: DNA cross-linking is not the mechanism of TMP-SMX. It inhibits folate metabolism.

Correct! TMP-SMX achieves synergistic bactericidal activity through sequential folate pathway blockade: Sulfamethoxazole (SMX) = PABA analog → inhibits dihydropteroate synthase (DHPS) → blocks dihydrofolic acid formation. Trimethoprim (TMP) = inhibits dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) → blocks conversion of dihydrofolic acid → tetrahydrofolic acid. Double blockade → no THF → no nucleotide synthesis → bactericidal.

choices.text: Inhibition of DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV
choices.feedback: DNA gyrase/topoisomerase IV inhibition is the mechanism of fluoroquinolones, not TMP-SMX.

choices.text: Inhibition of cell wall synthesis at two sequential steps
choices.feedback: TMP-SMX inhibits folate synthesis, not cell wall synthesis.

questions.explanation: Key detail: sulfamethoxazole inhibits DHPS and trimethoprim inhibits DHFR.

questions.stem: TMP-SMX is first-line prophylaxis AND treatment for which opportunistic infection in HIV?

choices.feedback: MAC prophylaxis uses azithromycin (CD4 < 50). TMP-SMX does not cover mycobacteria.
Cryptococcal meningitis is treated with amphotericin B + flucytosine, then fluconazole maintenance. TMP-SMX has no role.

Correct! TMP-SMX is first-line for both: PCP prophylaxis (CD4 < 200): TMP-SMX DS daily or 3×/week. PCP treatment: TMP-SMX (15-weight-based dosing TMP component) IV or PO × 21 days. Also provides cross-protection against Toxoplasma (CD4 < 100) and some protection against Isospora and Nocardia. Alternatives for sulfa-allergic: dapsone, atovaquone, pentamidine.
CMV retinitis is treated with ganciclovir/valganciclovir, not TMP-SMX.

questions.explanation: Correct: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP).

questions.stem: Which serious adverse effect of TMP-SMX can mimic renal impairment without actual kidney damage?

choices.feedback: Correct! Trimethoprim blocks renal tubular secretion of creatinine → elevated serum creatinine WITHOUT true GFR reduction (similar to cimetidine). Also blocks ENaC in collecting duct (similar to amiloride) → potassium retention → hyperkalemia. This is a pharmacologic effect, not nephrotoxicity. Clinical impact: can cause dangerous hyperkalemia especially in patients on ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or spironolactone. Always check K+ with TMP-SMX.
Crystalluria can occur with sulfonamides (especially in acidic urine) but this represents actual renal damage, not a benign mimic.
While AIN can occur with TMP-SMX, the question asks about mimicking renal impairment WITHOUT actual damage. The creatinine elevation from blocked tubular secretion is the benign mimic.
Rhabdomyolysis is not typically associated with TMP-SMX. It occurs with statins, trauma, and certain drugs of abuse.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Trimethoprim-induced hyperkalemia and elevated creatinine from blocked tubular secretion.

questions.stem: Why is TMP-SMX effective against community-acquired MRSA?

choices.text: Sulfonamides penetrate the altered cell wall more effectively
choices.feedback: MRSA does not have an altered cell wall per se — it has altered PBPs. TMP-SMX works because its mechanism is completely unrelated to cell wall synthesis.

choices.text: Its folate synthesis inhibition is independent of the cell wall resistance mechanism
choices.feedback: Correct! MRSA resistance (mecA → PBP2a) only affects beta-lactam binding. TMP-SMX inhibits folate synthesis — a completely separate metabolic pathway. Community-acquired MRSA (CA-MRSA, USA300 strain) is typically susceptible to TMP-SMX, clindamycin, and doxycycline. Hospital-acquired MRSA may be resistant to more agents. TMP-SMX is commonly used for CA-MRSA skin/soft tissue infections (abscess + TMP-SMX after I&D).

choices.text: The combination overcomes the efflux pumps in MRSA
choices.feedback: TMP-SMX activity against MRSA is not related to efflux pump evasion. It targets a different pathway entirely.

choices.text: It disrupts the mecA gene product PBP2a
choices.feedback: TMP-SMX does not target PBPs. It works through folate pathway inhibition, which is independent of cell wall mechanisms.

questions.stem: A patient on TMP-SMX develops a maculopapular rash and fever. What is the most important adverse reaction to rule out?

choices.feedback: Serum sickness-like reactions are rare with TMP-SMX. SJS/TEN is the more important reaction to exclude urgently.
Drug-induced lupus is more commonly associated with hydralazine, procainamide, isoniazid, and minocycline. It is not the primary concern with a sulfonamide rash.
Contact dermatitis is not caused by systemic medications. Sulfonamide hypersensitivity reactions are the concern.

Correct! Sulfonamides are one of the most common drug classes causing SJS/TEN — potentially life-threatening mucocutaneous reactions. Red flags: mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital), skin pain out of proportion to appearance, sheet-like epidermal detachment (Nikolsky sign), systemic symptoms (fever >38.5°C). SJS: <10% BSA detachment; TEN: >30%. Immediate drug discontinuation essential. Also watch for: drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS), hemolytic anemia in G6PD deficiency.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of metronidazole?

choices.feedback: 30S ribosomal inhibition is aminoglycosides/tetracyclines. Metronidazole works through DNA damage.

choices.text: Forms toxic free radicals that damage DNA after reduction by anaerobic electron transport proteins
choices.feedback: Correct! Metronidazole is a prodrug activated by reduction in anaerobic environments. The nitro group is reduced by ferredoxin → toxic free radical intermediates → DNA strand breakage → bactericidal. Only works in anaerobic/microaerophilic organisms (requires low-redox electron transport). Covers: anaerobes (Bacteroides, Clostridium), protozoa (Giardia, Entamoeba, Trichomonas), H. pylori (in combination). No aerobic gram-positive or gram-negative activity.
Folate synthesis inhibition is TMP-SMX. Metronidazole damages DNA via free radical generation.
RNA polymerase inhibition is rifampin. Metronidazole's mechanism involves DNA-damaging free radicals.

questions.stem: What is the classic drug-alcohol interaction with metronidazole?

choices.feedback: Serotonin syndrome occurs with serotonergic drug combinations (SSRIs + MAOIs, linezolid). Not related to metronidazole-alcohol interaction.
While metronidazole can cause neurotoxicity (peripheral neuropathy, seizures) with prolonged use, the alcohol interaction specifically causes a disulfiram-like reaction.

choices.text: Disulfiram-like reaction with nausea, vomiting, flushing, and tachycardia
choices.feedback: Correct! Metronidazole inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase → acetaldehyde accumulation with alcohol → flushing, nausea, vomiting, headache, tachycardia (disulfiram-like reaction). Patients must avoid ALL alcohol (including topical products, mouthwash, cooking wine) during therapy and 48-72 hours after completion. Other drugs with disulfiram-like reactions: disulfiram (Antabuse), certain cephalosporins (cefotetan, cefoperazone — those with methylthiotetrazole side chain).
The reaction is more severe than simple sedation.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Disulfiram-like reaction with nausea, vomiting, flushing, and tachycardia.

questions.stem: Which adverse effect limits prolonged use of metronidazole?

choices.feedback: Ototoxicity is the signature toxicity of aminoglycosides, not metronidazole.

Correct! Cumulative dose-dependent peripheral neuropathy: sensory > motor, starts distally (stocking-glove pattern). Can be irreversible if not caught early. Mechanism: accumulation of neurotoxic metabolites. Also: CNS toxicity (cerebellar dysfunction, seizures, encephalopathy — seen on MRI as bilateral dentate nuclei signal changes). Limit courses to < 4 weeks when possible. Monitor for paresthesias.
Aplastic anemia is associated with chloramphenicol, not metronidazole.
Metronidazole is not significantly nephrotoxic. The dose-limiting toxicity is neurological.

questions.explanation: Correct: Peripheral neuropathy (dose-dependent, potentially irreversible).

questions.stem: Metronidazole is first-line treatment for which protozoal infections?

choices.feedback: Correct! Metronidazole covers anaerobic protozoa: Giardia lamblia (intestinal), Entamoeba histolytica (amebic dysentery/liver abscess), Trichomonas vaginalis (STI). Also: C. difficile (alternative to oral vancomycin), Bacteroides/anaerobic infections (intra-abdominal, pelvic, brain abscess). Tinidazole (related nitroimidazole) is equally effective with potentially fewer side effects and single-dose convenience.
Cryptosporidium is treated with nitazoxanide (or just supportive care in immunocompetent). Cyclospora is treated with TMP-SMX. Metronidazole does not cover these.
Leishmaniasis requires amphotericin B or miltefosine. Trypanosomiasis requires suramin, melarsoprol, or nifurtimox. These are not metronidazole indications.
Malaria is treated with artemisinins/chloroquine. Toxo is treated with pyrimethamine-sulfadiazine. Metronidazole covers different protozoa.

questions.stem: A patient has a brain abscess suspected to be from mixed flora including anaerobes. Which antibiotic combination covers the likely organisms?

choices.feedback: Correct! Brain abscesses commonly involve mixed flora: streptococci (Strep milleri group), gram-negatives (from sinus/ear source), and anaerobes (Bacteroides, Fusobacterium, Prevotella). Ceftriaxone covers strep and gram-negatives; metronidazole provides excellent anaerobic coverage AND crosses the blood-brain barrier well. Add vancomycin if MRSA risk. Metronidazole achieves bactericidal CSF concentrations — one of the few antibiotics with reliable CNS penetration.
Pip-tazo has broad coverage including anaerobes but poor CNS penetration. Not ideal for brain abscess.
This combination provides poor gram-negative coverage and redundant gram-positive coverage. Not appropriate for brain abscess.
This combination covers gram-positives and gram-negatives but has NO anaerobic coverage (ciprofloxacin lacks anaerobic activity).

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of clindamycin?

choices.feedback: Membrane disruption is daptomycin's mechanism. Clindamycin inhibits protein synthesis via 50S ribosomal binding.
Clindamycin does not target DNA. It inhibits protein synthesis.
Folate metabolism inhibition is TMP-SMX. Clindamycin works at the ribosomal level.

choices.text: Binding to 50S ribosomal subunit, blocking peptide bond formation
choices.feedback: Correct! Clindamycin (a lincosamide) binds to the 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit → blocks translocation and peptidyl transferase → inhibits protein synthesis → bacteriostatic. Covers: gram-positives (including CA-MRSA), anaerobes above the diaphragm (not Bacteroides fragilis group), and some protozoa (Toxoplasma, Babesia in combination). Anti-toxin effect: suppresses toxin production in necrotizing fasciitis (GAS) and toxic shock syndrome.

questions.stem: Why is clindamycin added to penicillin in treating necrotizing fasciitis caused by Group A Streptococcus?

choices.text: It has better tissue penetration into necrotic areas
choices.feedback: While clindamycin does have good tissue penetration, the primary reason for addition is toxin suppression and growth-phase independence.

Correct! Two reasons: (1) Eagle effect: In high-inoculum GAS infections, bacteria are in stationary phase → reduced PBP expression → beta-lactams are less effective (require actively dividing bacteria). Clindamycin works regardless of growth phase. (2) Anti-toxin: Clindamycin suppresses production of streptococcal pyrogenic exotoxins and M protein → reduces superantigen-mediated tissue destruction and shock. Same principle applies in staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome.
GAS is fully susceptible to penicillin. The reason for adding clindamycin is not spectrum expansion.
Adding clindamycin does not primarily serve to prevent resistance. Its role is specifically to suppress toxin production.

questions.explanation: Best answer: It suppresses toxin production (Eagle effect) and works regardless of bacterial growth phase.

questions.stem: What serious adverse effect is most strongly associated with clindamycin?

choices.feedback: Ototoxicity is aminoglycosides. Clindamycin's signature toxicity is gastrointestinal.

Correct! Clindamycin was historically the antibiotic MOST associated with C. difficile colitis. Disrupts normal colonic flora → C. difficile overgrowth → toxin production → pseudomembranous colitis (yellow-white plaques on colonoscopy). Now, fluoroquinolones and cephalosporins are equally common causes. Symptoms: watery diarrhea, fever, abdominal pain, leukocytosis. Treatment: oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin. Metronidazole for mild cases.
Aplastic anemia is associated with chloramphenicol, not clindamycin.
SJS is most commonly associated with sulfonamides, anticonvulsants, and allopurinol. While clindamycin can rarely cause skin reactions, C. difficile is its signature adverse effect.

questions.stem: What is the D-test used for when prescribing clindamycin?

choices.text: Detecting inducible clindamycin resistance in erythromycin-resistant staphylococci
choices.feedback: Correct! The D-zone test: erythromycin and clindamycin discs placed adjacent on culture plate. If erythromycin-resistant but clindamycin-susceptible: flattening of the clindamycin zone near the erythromycin disc (D-shaped zone) = positive D-test = inducible erm-mediated resistance. This means clindamycin may fail in vivo despite appearing susceptible. Mechanism: erm genes (methylase) → 23S rRNA methylation → cross-resistance to macrolides, lincosamides, and streptogramins (MLSB resistance). A positive D-test → avoid clindamycin.
The D-test is a microbiologic susceptibility test, not an allergy test.

choices.text: Determining if the infection is monomicrobial
choices.feedback: The D-test is specifically about detecting inducible clindamycin resistance, not identifying organisms.
Clindamycin does not require therapeutic drug monitoring. The D-test is about susceptibility testing.

questions.stem: For which protozoan infection is clindamycin used in combination therapy?

choices.feedback: Cryptosporidium is treated with nitazoxanide or immune reconstitution (in HIV). Clindamycin has no role.
Giardia is treated with metronidazole or tinidazole, not clindamycin.
While clindamycin has some antimalarial activity and is used in combinations for pregnant women, babesiosis is the classic pairing.

Correct! Babesiosis: clindamycin + quinine (standard) or atovaquone + azithromycin (milder cases, better tolerated). Babesia infects RBCs → hemolytic anemia, fevers, dark urine. Transmitted by Ixodes tick (same as Lyme). Also: clindamycin + pyrimethamine is an alternative regimen for toxoplasmosis in sulfa-allergic patients.

questions.explanation: Correct: Babesia microti (babesiosis).

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of azole antifungals (fluconazole, voriconazole, itraconazole)?

choices.feedback: Correct! Azoles inhibit the fungal CYP51 enzyme (lanosterol 14-α-demethylase) → blocks conversion of lanosterol to ergosterol → depleted ergosterol + accumulation of toxic methylated sterols → disrupted membrane integrity → fungistatic (some cidal activity at high concentrations). Key clinical differences: fluconazole (Candida, Crypto — NOT Aspergillus), voriconazole (Aspergillus first-line), itraconazole (dimorphic fungi), posaconazole/isavuconazole (broadest spectrum including Mucor for isavuconazole).

choices.text: Inhibition of beta-glucan synthase in the cell wall
choices.feedback: Beta-glucan synthase inhibition is the mechanism of echinocandins (caspofungin, micafungin, anidulafungin), not azoles.

choices.text: Binding to ergosterol and creating membrane pores
choices.feedback: Direct ergosterol binding and pore formation is the mechanism of amphotericin B (polyene), not azoles.
Squalene epoxidase inhibition is the mechanism of terbinafine (used for dermatophytes/onychomycosis), not azoles.

questions.explanation: Key detail: alpha-demethylase (CYP51), blocking ergosterol synthesis.

questions.stem: What is the major mechanism of amphotericin B toxicity?

choices.text: Competitive inhibition of human dihydrofolate reductase
choices.feedback: This describes methotrexate toxicity, not amphotericin B.
Inner ear toxicity is aminoglycosides. Amphotericin B primarily causes renal toxicity.
CYP450 inhibition is an azole issue. Amphotericin B's toxicity mechanism is different.

choices.text: Binding to cholesterol in human cell membranes, especially renal tubular cells
choices.feedback: Correct! Amphotericin B preferentially binds fungal ergosterol but also cross-reacts with human cholesterol → renal tubular cell damage → nephrotoxicity (dose-limiting): decreased GFR, renal tubular acidosis (type 1 distal RTA), potassium/magnesium wasting. Also: infusion reactions (fever, rigors — from cytokine release), anemia. Lipid formulations (liposomal AmB) dramatically reduce nephrotoxicity by preferentially delivering drug to fungal membranes. Always monitor K+, Mg2+, and creatinine.

questions.stem: Which antifungal class has NO activity against Cryptococcus neoformans?

choices.feedback: Correct! Echinocandins inhibit beta-1,3-glucan synthase in the cell wall — but Cryptococcus has a thick polysaccharide CAPSULE and minimal beta-glucan in its wall → intrinsic resistance. Also ineffective against: Mucorales (for different reasons). Echinocandins cover: Candida (first-line for candidemia) and Aspergillus (alternative). They are the safest antifungal class (few drug interactions, low toxicity).
Fluconazole is the cornerstone of Cryptococcus maintenance therapy. Azoles are active.
Flucytosine is used WITH amphotericin B for cryptococcal meningitis induction. It is active against Cryptococcus (as combination therapy).
Amphotericin B (a polyene) is the foundation of cryptococcal meningitis induction therapy. Polyenes are highly active against Cryptococcus.

questions.stem: Why is voriconazole first-line for invasive aspergillosis rather than amphotericin B?

choices.text: Voriconazole covers both Aspergillus and Mucor
choices.feedback: CRITICAL: Voriconazole has NO activity against Mucorales. This is a high-yield testing point. Voriconazole prophylaxis can actually select for breakthrough mucormycosis.

choices.text: Voriconazole showed superior survival in a randomized trial
choices.feedback: Correct! The landmark Herbrecht et al. (NEJM 2002) trial showed voriconazole vs. amphotericin B for invasive aspergillosis: better survival (71% vs 58%) and fewer adverse effects. Voriconazole is now standard first-line. Key caveat: voriconazole has NO activity against Mucorales — if mucormycosis is in the differential, use amphotericin B or isavuconazole instead. Voriconazole also causes visual disturbances, hepatotoxicity, photosensitivity (skin cancer risk with prolonged use), and phototoxicity.

choices.text: Amphotericin B cannot penetrate lung tissue
choices.feedback: Amphotericin B does penetrate lung tissue. The reason for preferring voriconazole is superior clinical outcomes demonstrated in a randomized trial.
Cost is not the primary reason. Clinical trial data drove the recommendation change.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of echinocandins?

choices.feedback: Ergosterol synthesis inhibition is the azole mechanism. Echinocandins target the cell wall.

choices.text: Binding directly to ergosterol in the membrane
choices.feedback: Direct ergosterol binding is amphotericin B (polyene). Echinocandins target the cell wall glucan synthase enzyme.

choices.text: Inhibition of beta-1,3-D-glucan synthase, disrupting cell wall integrity
choices.feedback: Correct! Echinocandins (caspofungin, micafungin, anidulafungin) inhibit beta-1,3-D-glucan synthase → depleted beta-glucan in fungal cell wall → osmotic instability → fungicidal against Candida, fungistatic against Aspergillus. Advantages: very safe (few drug interactions, hepatic metabolism varies by agent), IV only. First-line for candidemia/invasive candidiasis. NOT active against: Cryptococcus, Mucorales, dimorphic fungi, Pneumocystis.
No antifungal class targets DNA polymerase. This is more of an antiviral mechanism (acyclovir).

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of acyclovir?

choices.feedback: Protease inhibition is used in HIV (e.g., darunavir) and HCV therapy, not for herpes viruses.

choices.text: Direct inhibition of viral RNA polymerase
choices.feedback: RNA polymerase inhibition describes some other antivirals (e.g., remdesivir for RNA viruses). Acyclovir targets DNA synthesis.

choices.text: Guanosine analog activated by viral thymidine kinase, then inhibits viral DNA polymerase
choices.feedback: Correct! Acyclovir is a prodrug requiring 3 phosphorylation steps: first by viral thymidine kinase (TK) → monophosphate → then by cellular kinases → triphosphate. Acyclovir triphosphate: (1) incorporated into growing viral DNA chain → chain termination (lacks 3'-OH), (2) directly inhibits viral DNA polymerase. Selective toxicity: only activated in infected cells (viral TK). Covers: HSV-1, HSV-2, VZV. Valacyclovir = oral prodrug with better bioavailability. Resistance: TK mutations (common in immunocompromised).
Neuraminidase inhibition is the mechanism of oseltamivir (Tamiflu) for influenza, not acyclovir.

questions.stem: Which antiviral is first-line for influenza treatment?

choices.feedback: Remdesivir (RNA-dependent RNA polymerase inhibitor) was developed for Ebola and used for SARS-CoV-2, not influenza.

Correct! Oseltamivir is a neuraminidase inhibitor → blocks viral release from infected cells → limits spread. Must start within 48 hours of symptom onset for maximum benefit. Also: baloxavir (cap-dependent endonuclease inhibitor — single-dose oral), zanamivir (inhaled neuraminidase inhibitor). Amantadine/rimantadine (M2 ion channel blockers) are no longer recommended due to widespread resistance. Oseltamivir prophylaxis can be used for close contacts during outbreaks.
Acyclovir treats herpes viruses (HSV, VZV), not influenza.
Ribavirin has been used for RSV and hepatitis C (in older regimens) but is not first-line for influenza.

questions.explanation: Correct: Oseltamivir (Tamiflu).

questions.stem: What class of antiretroviral drugs carries the risk of lactic acidosis and hepatic steatosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! NRTIs (especially older agents: stavudine, didanosine, zidovudine) inhibit mitochondrial DNA polymerase gamma → mitochondrial toxicity → lactic acidosis, hepatic steatosis, lipodystrophy, peripheral neuropathy, pancreatitis. Newer NRTIs (tenofovir, emtricitabine, abacavir) have much less mitochondrial toxicity. Abacavir: must test HLA-B*5701 before starting (fatal hypersensitivity if positive). Tenofovir: renal toxicity (Fanconi syndrome with TDF; TAF is safer).
Maraviroc (CCR5 antagonist) has a distinct toxicity profile but lactic acidosis is not a class effect.
Protease inhibitors cause metabolic syndrome (dyslipidemia, insulin resistance, lipodystrophy) but not typically lactic acidosis.
INSTIs (dolutegravir, bictegravir, raltegravir) are very well tolerated with minimal metabolic toxicity. They are the preferred backbone of modern ART.

questions.explanation: Correct: Nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs).

questions.stem: Acyclovir-resistant HSV in an immunocompromised patient is most often due to which mechanism?

choices.feedback: Correct! Most acyclovir resistance is due to thymidine kinase (TK) mutations → TK-negative or TK-altered mutants → cannot activate acyclovir to its monophosphate. Primarily seen in immunocompromised (HIV, transplant) with prolonged acyclovir exposure. Treatment of acyclovir-resistant HSV: foscarnet (directly inhibits DNA polymerase — does NOT require TK activation) or cidofovir. TK-negative mutants retain sensitivity to foscarnet.
Cellular kinases are host enzymes and are not altered by viral resistance. The first phosphorylation step (by viral TK) is where resistance occurs.
DNA polymerase mutations can cause resistance but are less common than the primary mechanism.
Viruses do not have efflux pumps. Efflux is a bacterial resistance mechanism.

questions.explanation: Correct: Loss of viral thymidine kinase (TK-negative mutants).

questions.stem: Before starting abacavir, which genetic test is mandatory?

choices.feedback: G6PD testing is required before primaquine/dapsone, not abacavir.
While HBV screening is important before ART (risk of flare with NRTI discontinuation), HLA-B*5701 is the specific test required before abacavir.
CYP2D6 testing is used for codeine, tamoxifen, and some psychiatric medications. Abacavir requires a different pharmacogenomic test.

Correct! HLA-B*5701 allele carriers have a 50% risk of severe, potentially fatal hypersensitivity reaction to abacavir (fever, rash, GI symptoms, respiratory distress — worsens with rechallenge). Testing is MANDATORY before initiation. If positive → absolute contraindication. If negative → <1% risk. This is one of the best-validated pharmacogenomic tests in clinical practice. Never rechallenge a patient who had a suspected hypersensitivity reaction regardless of HLA status.

questions.stem: What is the standard first-line regimen for active pulmonary tuberculosis?

choices.feedback: This is not a standard TB regimen. The four-drug RIPE combination is required for the intensive phase.
FQs and aminoglycosides are second-line agents for drug-resistant TB, not first-line therapy.
Isoniazid monotherapy is for latent TB only. Active TB requires multi-drug therapy to prevent resistance.

choices.text: RIPE: Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol for 2 months, then Rifampin + Isoniazid for 4 months
choices.feedback: Correct! Standard regimen: Intensive phase: RIPE × 2 months (all four drugs). Continuation phase: RI (rifampin + isoniazid) × 4 months. Total: 6 months minimum. Each drug's role: Isoniazid (most potent bactericidal against active organisms), Rifampin (sterilizing — kills semi-dormant persisters), Pyrazinamide (kills intracellular organisms in acidic environment of macrophages), Ethambutol (prevents resistance emergence while awaiting susceptibilities).

questions.stem: Isoniazid requires supplementation with which vitamin to prevent peripheral neuropathy?

choices.feedback: Correct! Isoniazid inhibits pyridoxine (B6) metabolism → depletion of active B6 (pyridoxal phosphate) → peripheral neuropathy (dose-dependent, symmetric, sensory > motor). Supplementation: pyridoxine 25- for all patients, especially high-risk (diabetes, HIV, alcoholism, malnutrition, pregnancy, renal failure). Other INH toxicities: hepatotoxicity (monitor LFTs — can be fatal), drug-induced lupus (slow acetylators at higher risk), sideroblastic anemia.
Vitamin C supplementation is not related to isoniazid therapy. Pyridoxine (B6) is the specific requirement.
B12 supplementation is for pernicious anemia/B12 deficiency. Isoniazid depletes a different B vitamin.
Vitamin D is not specifically depleted by isoniazid. B6 depletion causing neuropathy is the key INH concern.

questions.stem: Which anti-TB drug causes the most significant drug interactions through CYP450 induction?

choices.feedback: Correct! Rifampin is one of the most potent CYP3A4 inducers known → dramatically increases metabolism of: warfarin (subtherapeutic INR), oral contraceptives (breakthrough pregnancy), protease inhibitors/NNRTIs (HIV treatment failure), cyclosporine (transplant rejection), methadone (withdrawal), and many more. Always review drug interactions before starting rifampin. Rifabutin is a less potent inducer → preferred with HIV protease inhibitors.
