Isoniazid is actually a CYP450 INHIBITOR (inhibits CYP2E1), not an inducer. It can increase levels of phenytoin and some other drugs.
Pyrazinamide causes hepatotoxicity and hyperuricemia but has minimal CYP450 interaction.
Ethambutol causes optic neuritis (red-green color blindness), not drug interactions. It has minimal CYP450 effects.

questions.stem: What is the dose-limiting toxicity of ethambutol?

choices.feedback: Ethambutol is primarily cleared renally and requires dose adjustment in renal impairment, but its signature toxicity is optic, not renal.

choices.text: Optic neuritis with red-green color blindness
choices.feedback: Correct! Ethambutol → retrobulbar optic neuritis → decreased visual acuity and loss of red-green color discrimination. Dose-dependent and usually reversible if caught early. Baseline visual acuity and color vision testing required before starting. Monthly monitoring during therapy. Patients must report any visual changes immediately. Contraindicated in young children who cannot report visual symptoms or cooperate with eye exams.
Peripheral neuropathy is isoniazid's toxicity (B6 depletion). Ethambutol has a different signature toxicity.
Ototoxicity is aminoglycosides (and streptomycin, a second-line anti-TB aminoglycoside), not ethambutol.

questions.stem: A patient on RIPE therapy develops jaundice and elevated transaminases. Which drug(s) are most likely responsible?

choices.text: Isoniazid, Rifampin, and/or Pyrazinamide — all three are hepatotoxic
choices.feedback: Correct! Three of the four RIPE drugs are hepatotoxic: Isoniazid (metabolite hepatotoxicity, risk increased by CYP2E1 induction — alcohol, slow acetylators), Pyrazinamide (most hepatotoxic, dose-dependent), Rifampin (cholestatic pattern). Management: stop all hepatotoxins, reintroduce one at a time (usually rifampin first, then INH, then PZA) while monitoring LFTs. Ethambutol + a fluoroquinolone can bridge therapy during hepatotoxicity workup.
While rifampin can cause hepatotoxicity, isoniazid and pyrazinamide are equally or more hepatotoxic. All three must be considered.
Ethambutol causes optic neuritis, not hepatotoxicity. It is the one RIPE drug that can be continued when hepatotoxicity occurs.
Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) does not cause hepatotoxicity. It prevents isoniazid-induced neuropathy.

questions.stem: What is the drug of choice for intestinal nematode infections (roundworms, hookworms, pinworms)?

choices.feedback: Ivermectin covers Strongyloides, Onchocerca, and some other nematodes but benzimidazoles are the broader first-line for most intestinal nematodes.
Metronidazole covers anaerobic protozoa (Giardia, Entamoeba, Trichomonas) and anaerobic bacteria, NOT helminths.

Correct! Benzimidazoles (albendazole, mebendazole) are broad-spectrum anthelmintics. Mechanism: inhibit beta-tubulin polymerization → disrupt microtubule formation → impaired glucose uptake and cell division → worm death. Cover: Ascaris (roundworm), Ancylostoma/Necator (hookworm), Enterobius (pinworm), Trichuris (whipworm), and others. Albendazole is preferred (better systemic absorption). Also used for echinococcosis (hydatid cysts) and neurocysticercosis (with corticosteroids).
Praziquantel is the drug of choice for trematodes (flukes) and cestodes (tapeworms), not nematodes (roundworms).

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of ivermectin?

choices.feedback: Folate metabolism inhibition is TMP-SMX, not ivermectin.
Beta-tubulin inhibition is the mechanism of benzimidazoles (albendazole/mebendazole), not ivermectin.

choices.text: Disruption of parasitic cell membrane via ergosterol binding
choices.feedback: Ergosterol binding is the mechanism of amphotericin B (antifungal), not an antiparasitic mechanism.

choices.text: Binding to glutamate-gated chloride channels causing parasite paralysis
choices.feedback: Correct! Ivermectin (an avermectin) binds to glutamate-gated chloride channels (specific to invertebrates) → increased Cl⁻ permeability → hyperpolarization → flaccid paralysis of parasite pharyngeal and body wall muscles → death. These channels do not exist in mammals → selective toxicity. Key indications: Strongyloides (drug of choice), Onchocerciasis (river blindness), scabies, lice. Avoids BBB in humans (P-glycoprotein efflux) → safe in most patients.

questions.stem: What is the drug of choice for treating tapeworm (cestode) and fluke (trematode) infections?

choices.feedback: Ivermectin covers certain nematodes (Strongyloides, Onchocerca) and ectoparasites but NOT cestodes or trematodes.
Albendazole primarily covers nematodes. While it has some cestode activity (especially for neurocysticercosis in combination), praziquantel is the broader drug of choice for cestodes and trematodes.

Correct! Praziquantel: mechanism → increases cell membrane permeability to Ca²⁺ → muscle contraction/paralysis + tegument damage → worm death + immune exposure. Covers: all Schistosoma species (trematode), Clonorchis/Opisthorchis (liver flukes), Taenia (tapeworm), Diphyllobothrium (fish tapeworm), cysticercosis (in combination with albendazole + steroids). Notable exception: Fasciola hepatica (sheep liver fluke) — requires triclabendazole instead.
Metronidazole covers protozoa (Giardia, Entamoeba, Trichomonas) but NOT helminths (worms).

questions.stem: Which antiparasitic is the drug of choice for Strongyloides stercoralis, including hyperinfection syndrome?

choices.feedback: Correct! Ivermectin is first-line for Strongyloides in all settings including hyperinfection syndrome (which occurs with immunosuppression, especially corticosteroids). Hyperinfection: massive larval burden → disseminated infection → polymicrobial sepsis (larvae carry gut flora). Screen ALL patients from endemic areas for Strongyloides before starting immunosuppression (especially corticosteroids). Ivermectin dosing: weight-based dosing, continue until symptoms resolve and larvae clear (may require prolonged courses in hyperinfection).
DEC is used for lymphatic filariasis (Wuchereria bancrofti) and Loa loa, not Strongyloides.
Praziquantel covers tapeworms and flukes, NOT nematodes like Strongyloides.
Albendazole is an alternative but ivermectin is superior for Strongyloides, especially in hyperinfection.

questions.stem: Why is Strongyloides screening essential before initiating corticosteroid therapy?

choices.text: Immunosuppression triggers hyperinfection through unchecked autoinfection cycle
choices.feedback: Correct! Strongyloides has a unique autoinfection cycle: larvae mature within the gut → penetrate intestinal mucosa → reinfect the host without leaving the body. In immunocompetent: controlled by eosinophils and Th2 immunity. With corticosteroids: suppressed eosinophils and immune control → exponential larval replication → hyperinfection (massive GI involvement) → dissemination (larvae in lungs, liver, CNS, carrying enteric bacteria → gram-negative sepsis). Mortality 60-85% in hyperinfection. Prevention: screen with Strongyloides serology + stool before immunosuppression.
Strongyloides does not form cysts. The mechanism involves immune suppression enabling autoinfection.
Corticosteroids may directly stimulate larval development via ecdysteroid-like effects, but the primary mechanism is loss of immune control over the autoinfection cycle.

choices.text: Corticosteroids interfere with antiparasitic drug metabolism
choices.feedback: The danger is not drug interaction — it is that untreated Strongyloides becomes lethal under immunosuppression.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of linezolid?

choices.text: Binding to 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit, preventing 70S initiation complex formation
choices.feedback: Correct! Linezolid (an oxazolidinone) binds to the 23S rRNA on the 50S subunit → prevents formation of the 70S initiation complex (blocks the very first step of translation) → bacteriostatic. This unique binding site means NO cross-resistance with other protein synthesis inhibitors. Covers: MRSA, VRE (both E. faecium and E. faecalis), VISA/VRSA, drug-resistant pneumococci, and drug-resistant TB (second-line). 100% oral bioavailability → easy IV-to-PO switch.
Cell wall synthesis inhibition is beta-lactams/vancomycin. Linezolid targets protein synthesis.

choices.text: Disruption of cell membrane by lipopeptide insertion
choices.feedback: Membrane disruption is daptomycin's mechanism. Linezolid inhibits protein synthesis.
RNA polymerase inhibition is rifampin. Linezolid targets the 50S ribosomal subunit.

questions.stem: What serious adverse effects limit prolonged linezolid use beyond 2 weeks?

choices.text: Thrombocytopenia, serotonin syndrome, and lactic acidosis from mitochondrial toxicity
choices.feedback: Correct! Linezolid toxicities: Thrombocytopenia (reversible, monitor CBC weekly — most common limiting factor), peripheral/optic neuropathy (irreversible if prolonged), lactic acidosis (mitochondrial toxicity — inhibits mitochondrial protein synthesis), serotonin syndrome (linezolid is a weak reversible MAO inhibitor → avoid SSRIs, SNRIs, meperidine, serotonergic drugs). Limit use to < 2 weeks when possible. Tedizolid (newer oxazolidinone): less thrombocytopenia, once-daily.
These are aminoglycoside toxicities. Linezolid has different dose-limiting effects.
Red man syndrome is vancomycin. Crystalluria is sulfonamides. Neither is associated with linezolid.
QTc prolongation is macrolides/FQs. Hepatotoxicity is not linezolid's primary concern. Thrombocytopenia and serotonin syndrome are the key issues.

questions.stem: Why is daptomycin ineffective for pneumonia?

choices.text: It cannot penetrate the alveolar epithelium
choices.feedback: The issue is not tissue penetration per se — it's inactivation by surfactant.

choices.text: Pulmonary surfactant binds and inactivates daptomycin
choices.feedback: Correct! Daptomycin (a lipopeptide) inserts into bacterial cell membranes → depolarization → cell death. However, pulmonary surfactant (a phospholipid) sequesters daptomycin → cannot interact with bacterial membranes → ineffective in the lung. This is a unique pharmacologic limitation. For MRSA pneumonia: use vancomycin or linezolid instead. Daptomycin is excellent for MRSA bacteremia, endocarditis (right-sided), and skin/soft tissue infections.

choices.text: It requires anaerobic conditions to be activated
choices.feedback: Daptomycin does not require anaerobic activation. That describes metronidazole. Daptomycin is inactivated by surfactant.
S. aureus (including MRSA) is susceptible to daptomycin in vitro. The problem is the lung environment, not pathogen resistance.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of daptomycin?

choices.feedback: D-Ala-D-Ala binding is vancomycin's mechanism. Daptomycin disrupts the cell membrane through lipopeptide insertion.

choices.text: Calcium-dependent insertion into bacterial cell membrane causing rapid depolarization
choices.feedback: Correct! Daptomycin (cyclic lipopeptide) inserts its lipophilic tail into the gram-positive bacterial cell membrane in a calcium-dependent manner → forms ion channels → rapid membrane depolarization → loss of membrane potential → efflux of potassium → cessation of DNA, RNA, and protein synthesis → bactericidal. Unique features: concentration-dependent killing, rapid bactericidal activity (faster kill than vancomycin for MRSA bacteremia), does NOT cause cell lysis (does NOT release cell wall fragments → may reduce inflammatory response).

choices.text: Inhibition of protein synthesis via 50S ribosomal binding
choices.feedback: 50S ribosomal binding is linezolid's mechanism. Daptomycin has a unique membrane-based mechanism.

choices.text: Inhibition of teichoic acid synthesis in the cell wall
choices.feedback: Teichoic acid inhibition is not an established antibiotic mechanism. Daptomycin targets the cell membrane, not the cell wall.

questions.stem: For MRSA bacteremia, which agent is preferred when vancomycin MIC is 2 mcg/mL (susceptible but at the upper breakpoint)?

choices.feedback: Higher vancomycin doses at MIC = 2 increase nephrotoxicity without reliably improving outcomes. Guidelines recommend switching.
Ceftaroline (anti-MRSA cephalosporin) is sometimes used as combination or salvage therapy for persistent MRSA bacteremia but daptomycin is the standard first switch from vancomycin.
Linezolid is bacteriostatic and generally not preferred for bacteremia where bactericidal activity is desired. Daptomycin is the standard alternative.

Correct! When vancomycin MIC ≥ 2 (MIC creep), clinical outcomes with vancomycin worsen significantly. IDSA guidelines recommend switching to daptomycin for MRSA bacteremia when: vancomycin MIC ≥ 2, persistent bacteremia on vancomycin (>3-5 days), or treatment failure. Daptomycin dose: weight-based dosing (uncomplicated bacteremia), 8-weight-based dosing (endocarditis or complicated bacteremia). Monitor CPK (daptomycin can cause myopathy). Never combine daptomycin with statins.

questions.stem: What is the first-line antibiotic for uncomplicated cystitis in a non-pregnant woman?

choices.feedback: Fluoroquinolones are NOT first-line for uncomplicated UTI — reserved for complicated UTIs/pyelonephritis due to resistance concerns and adverse effects.
Amoxicillin has high E. coli resistance rates (>30%) and is not recommended as empiric therapy for uncomplicated UTI.
First-gen cephalosporins are second-line alternatives. Nitrofurantoin or TMP-SMX are preferred first-line agents.

Correct! AUA/IDSA guidelines: first-line for uncomplicated cystitis: nitrofurantoin (Macrobid) × 5 days, TMP-SMX × 3 days (if local resistance < 20%), or fosfomycin single dose. Nitrofurantoin advantages: low resistance rates (concentrated in urine, minimal systemic exposure → less selection pressure), bactericidal at urinary concentrations. Limitations: does NOT achieve therapeutic levels in kidney/blood → only for lower UTI (cystitis), not pyelonephritis. Avoid if CrCl < 30.

questions.stem: Why is nitrofurantoin contraindicated in pyelonephritis?

choices.text: E. coli strains causing pyelonephritis are inherently resistant
choices.feedback: Nitrofurantoin susceptibility is organism-dependent, not anatomical. The issue is that it doesn't reach kidney tissue at therapeutic levels.

choices.text: It is nephrotoxic at therapeutic doses
choices.feedback: Nitrofurantoin is not nephrotoxic. The issue is pharmacokinetic, not toxic.

choices.text: It causes ureteral spasm worsening obstruction
choices.feedback: Nitrofurantoin does not cause ureteral spasm. Its limitation for pyelonephritis is purely pharmacokinetic.

choices.text: It achieves therapeutic concentrations only in urine, not in renal parenchyma or blood
choices.feedback: Correct! Nitrofurantoin is rapidly concentrated in urine by renal tubular secretion but achieves negligible serum/tissue levels. For pyelonephritis (kidney parenchymal infection): need antibiotics that achieve therapeutic TISSUE concentrations (fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX, beta-lactams). Nitrofurantoin only works for lower tract (bladder) infections where its high urinary concentration is sufficient.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of nitrofurantoin?

choices.feedback: PABA inhibition is the mechanism of sulfonamides, not nitrofurantoin.
DHFR inhibition is trimethoprim's mechanism. Nitrofurantoin works through reactive intermediate generation.
Nitrofurantoin has a multi-target mechanism that does not specifically target cell wall synthesis.

choices.text: Multiple mechanisms: reduced by bacterial nitroreductases to reactive intermediates that damage DNA, RNA, and proteins
choices.feedback: Correct! Nitrofurantoin is reduced by bacterial flavoproteins (nitroreductases) → reactive intermediates → damage to DNA (strand breakage), ribosomal proteins, and other macromolecules → bactericidal. This multi-target mechanism explains its persistently low resistance rates (hard to develop resistance to multiple simultaneous targets). Active against most uropathogens: E. coli, Enterococcus, S. saprophyticus. Intrinsically resistant: Proteus, Pseudomonas, Serratia (lack nitroreductases).

questions.explanation: Best answer: Multiple mechanisms: reduced by bacterial nitroreductases to reactive intermediates that damage DNA, RNA, and proteins.

questions.stem: What adverse effect is associated with long-term nitrofurantoin prophylaxis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Long-term nitrofurantoin → two patterns of pulmonary toxicity: Acute (hypersensitivity — fever, eosinophilia, infiltrates — reversible upon discontinuation) and Chronic (insidious pulmonary fibrosis with prolonged use — may be irreversible). Monitor for cough, dyspnea in patients on prophylactic nitrofurantoin. Also: peripheral neuropathy (especially with renal impairment), hepatotoxicity (rare). Contraindicated if CrCl < 30 (drug accumulates, efficacy decreases, toxicity increases).
Tendon rupture is a fluoroquinolone adverse effect, not nitrofurantoin.
Nitrofurantoin has minimal impact on gut flora (concentrated in urine, not in GI tract) and is one of the antibiotics LEAST associated with C. difficile.
Photosensitivity is associated with tetracyclines, fluoroquinolones, and sulfonamides, not nitrofurantoin.

questions.stem: A postoperative patient develops unilateral leg swelling, warmth, and calf tenderness. What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?

choices.feedback: MRI venography is reserved for suspected iliac/pelvic DVT or when ultrasound is inconclusive. Compression ultrasound is first-line.
D-dimer is a rule-OUT test for low-probability patients. In a postoperative patient, D-dimer is almost always elevated and nondiagnostic.

choices.text: Compression ultrasonography of the lower extremity
choices.feedback: Correct! Duplex compression ultrasound is the first-line diagnostic test for DVT: sensitivity >95% for proximal DVT, specificity >95%. Lack of vein compressibility is diagnostic. D-dimer is only useful for RULING OUT DVT in LOW-PROBABILITY patients (Wells score ≤1). Post-surgical patients have elevated D-dimer regardless → go directly to ultrasound.
CTA chest is for suspected PE, not DVT diagnosis. Start with leg ultrasound for suspected DVT.

questions.explanation: Compression ultrasonography (CUS) is the first-line diagnostic test for suspected DVT. It has sensitivity exceeding 95% and specificity exceeding 97% for proximal DVT (popliteal vein and above). The test is non-invasive, readily available, and does not require contrast. A positive finding is non-compressibility of the vein. For distal DVT (calf veins), sensitivity is lower (~70%), so serial ultrasound at 1 week may be performed if initial CUS is negative but clinical suspicion persists. D-dimer combined with clinical probability (Wells score) can be used to determine whether imaging is needed at all.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Compression ultrasonography (CUS) is the first-line diagnostic test for suspected DVT. It has sensitivity exceeding 95% and specificity exceeding 97% for proximal DVT (popliteal vein and above). The test is non-invasive, readily available, and does not require contrast. A positive finding is non-compressibility of the vein. For distal DVT (calf veins), sensitivity is lower (~70%), so serial ultrasound at 1 week may be performed if initial CUS is negative but clinical suspicion persists. D-dimer combined with clinical probability (Wells score) can be used to determine whether imaging is needed at all.

questions.stem: Which Wells score component carries the highest point value for DVT probability?

choices.feedback: Active cancer scores 1 point. Another criterion carries more weight.

choices.text: Alternative diagnosis as likely or more likely than DVT (−2 points)
choices.feedback: Correct! The most impactful Wells DVT criterion is 'alternative diagnosis as likely or greater than DVT' at −2 points. This single criterion can shift a patient from moderate to low probability. Other criteria (1 pt each): active cancer, paralysis/immobilization, bedridden >3 days/surgery <12 weeks, entire leg swelling, calf swelling >3cm, pitting edema, collateral veins, previously documented DVT. Low ≤0, Moderate 1-2, High ≥3.
This criterion scores 1 point.

choices.text: Calf swelling >3 cm compared to the other leg
choices.feedback: Calf asymmetry scores 1 point. The −2 point alternative diagnosis criterion has the greatest impact.

questions.explanation: The Wells DVT score includes several clinical criteria, and the 'alternative diagnosis as likely or more likely than DVT' component carries −2 points — the highest magnitude of any single item. This negative weighting reflects the principle that when another diagnosis explains the symptoms equally well, DVT becomes less likely. Other Wells DVT criteria include active cancer (+1), paralysis/recent cast (+1), recently bedridden >3 days or major surgery within 12 weeks (+1), localized tenderness along deep venous system (+1), entire leg swollen (+1), calf swelling >3 cm compared to asymptomatic leg (+1), pitting edema (+1), and collateral superficial veins (+1).
questions.verifiedExplanation: The Wells DVT score includes several clinical criteria, and the 'alternative diagnosis as likely or more likely than DVT' component carries −2 points — the highest magnitude of any single item. This negative weighting reflects the principle that when another diagnosis explains the symptoms equally well, DVT becomes less likely. Other Wells DVT criteria include active cancer (+1), paralysis/recent cast (+1), recently bedridden >3 days or major surgery within 12 weeks (+1), localized tenderness along deep venous system (+1), entire leg swollen (+1), calf swelling >3 cm compared to asymptomatic leg (+1), pitting edema (+1), and collateral superficial veins (+1).

questions.stem: What is the recommended duration of anticoagulation for a first unprovoked DVT?

choices.feedback: 3 months is appropriate for PROVOKED VTE (e.g., post-surgical). Unprovoked DVT carries higher recurrence risk.

choices.text: At least 3 months, then reassess for extended/indefinite therapy
choices.feedback: Correct! Unprovoked DVT: minimum 3 months of anticoagulation, then shared decision about extended (indefinite) therapy. Recurrence risk without continued anticoagulation: ~10% at 1 year, ~30% at 5 years. Factors favoring extended therapy: male sex, elevated D-dimer after stopping, proximal DVT, persistent risk factors. DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban) preferred over warfarin for most patients. Provoked VTE (surgery, trauma): 3 months then stop.
6 weeks is too short for any DVT. Minimum is 3 months even for provoked events.
Not all DVTs require lifelong anticoagulation. The decision depends on provoked vs. unprovoked and individual risk-benefit assessment.

questions.explanation: First unprovoked DVT requires at least 3 months of anticoagulation, after which the decision to continue extended/indefinite therapy is made by reassessing the risk-benefit balance. Unprovoked VTE has a recurrence rate of approximately 10% at 1 year and 30% at 5 years after stopping anticoagulation. Factors favoring extended therapy include male sex, D-dimer positive after stopping treatment, persistent residual vein thrombosis, underlying thrombophilia, and patient preference. Provoked DVT (clear transient risk factor like surgery or immobilization) typically needs only 3 months of treatment due to much lower recurrence risk.
questions.verifiedExplanation: First unprovoked DVT requires at least 3 months of anticoagulation, after which the decision to continue extended/indefinite therapy is made by reassessing the risk-benefit balance. Unprovoked VTE has a recurrence rate of approximately 10% at 1 year and 30% at 5 years after stopping anticoagulation. Factors favoring extended therapy include male sex, D-dimer positive after stopping treatment, persistent residual vein thrombosis, underlying thrombophilia, and patient preference. Provoked DVT (clear transient risk factor like surgery or immobilization) typically needs only 3 months of treatment due to much lower recurrence risk.

questions.stem: Which anticoagulant is preferred for VTE treatment in patients with active cancer?

choices.feedback: Warfarin is inferior to LMWH/DOACs for cancer-associated VTE — harder to manage with drug interactions and variable INRs.
Aspirin is antiplatelet, not anticoagulant. It has no role in VTE treatment.

Correct! Cancer-associated VTE: LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin) was historically standard (CLOT trial). Now DOACs (edoxaban — HOKUSAI-VTE Cancer; rivaroxaban — SELECT-D; apixaban — Caravaggio) are options. Caution with DOACs in GI/GU cancers (increased mucosal bleeding). LMWH preferred if: GI cancer, drug interactions with cancer therapy, thrombocytopenia, or poor oral intake. Duration: as long as cancer is active.
UFH is used for acute bridging but not for extended VTE treatment. LMWH or DOACs are used for ongoing therapy.

questions.explanation: LMWH has traditionally been the preferred anticoagulant for cancer-associated VTE because of superior efficacy compared to warfarin in this population (CLOT trial). However, DOACs (edoxaban per Hokusai-VTE Cancer trial, rivaroxaban per SELECT-D trial) are now acceptable alternatives for cancer patients without high GI bleeding risk. Apixaban (CARAVAGGIO trial) showed non-inferior efficacy with lower GI bleeding compared to other DOACs in cancer-associated VTE. Warfarin is generally avoided in cancer patients due to drug interactions with chemotherapy, unpredictable INR, and inferior efficacy. Choice depends on cancer type, bleeding risk, and drug interactions.

verifiedReferences.title: Deep Venous Thrombosis
verifiedReferences.authors: Waheed SM, Kudaravalli P, Hotwagner DT

questions.stem: A patient with suspected DVT has a low Wells score and negative D-dimer. What is the next step?

choices.text: DVT is effectively ruled out; no further workup needed
choices.feedback: Correct! Low pretest probability (Wells ≤0) + negative high-sensitivity D-dimer: negative predictive value >99%. DVT can be safely excluded without imaging. This is the validated diagnostic pathway — avoids unnecessary ultrasounds. If D-dimer positive (even in low-probability patients): proceed to compression ultrasound. If Wells moderate-high: skip D-dimer, go directly to ultrasound.
Low probability + negative D-dimer safely rules out DVT. No anticoagulation needed.
In the validated pathway, low Wells + negative D-dimer is sufficient to exclude DVT. Ultrasound is not needed.
Serial D-dimer testing is not part of the standard DVT diagnostic algorithm. The combination of low Wells + negative D-dimer is definitive.

questions.explanation: A low Wells score combined with a negative D-dimer effectively rules out DVT with a negative predictive value exceeding 99%. No further workup is needed — this combination safely excludes DVT. This evidence-based approach prevents unnecessary imaging, reduces healthcare costs, and avoids incidental findings. However, if clinical suspicion changes (worsening symptoms, new risk factors), the patient should be reassessed. For patients with high Wells scores, D-dimer should not be used to exclude DVT — proceed directly to compression ultrasonography.

verifiedReferences.title: Deep Venous Thrombosis
verifiedReferences.authors: Waheed SM, Kudaravalli P, Hotwagner DT

questions.stem: A patient with acute PE is hemodynamically unstable (systolic BP < 90) despite fluids. What is the next step?

choices.feedback: IVC filters prevent future embolism but do not treat the acute hemodynamic crisis from existing PE.
Coronary stenting is for ACS, not PE. Catheter-directed therapy for PE involves thrombus aspiration or local thrombolytics, not stenting.
Heparin prevents clot propagation but does not address the acute hemodynamic compromise from massive PE.

Correct! Massive PE (hemodynamic instability): systemic thrombolysis with alteplase is indicated (reduces mortality from ~25% to ~10%). Can give even if sub-massive CTA — clinical hemodynamic instability is the indication. If contraindicated to thrombolytics: catheter-directed therapy or surgical embolectomy. Time is critical. Also: IV fluids cautiously (RV failure — overload worsens RV dilation), vasopressors (norepinephrine preferred), avoid intubation if possible (positive pressure worsens RV).

questions.explanation: Hemodynamically unstable PE (systolic BP <90 mmHg despite fluid resuscitation) requires systemic thrombolysis as the primary intervention. This is the clearest indication for thrombolysis in VTE — the mortality benefit significantly outweighs bleeding risk. If thrombolysis is contraindicated (recent surgery, active hemorrhage, intracranial pathology), catheter-directed therapy or surgical embolectomy are alternatives. Concurrent IV unfractionated heparin provides anticoagulation. Without aggressive clot-directed therapy, massive PE has mortality exceeding 50%.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Hemodynamically unstable PE (systolic BP <90 mmHg despite fluid resuscitation) requires systemic thrombolysis as the primary intervention. This is the clearest indication for thrombolysis in VTE — the mortality benefit significantly outweighs bleeding risk. If thrombolysis is contraindicated (recent surgery, active hemorrhage, intracranial pathology), catheter-directed therapy or surgical embolectomy are alternatives. Concurrent IV unfractionated heparin provides anticoagulation. Without aggressive clot-directed therapy, massive PE has mortality exceeding 50%.

verifiedReferences.title: Pulmonary Embolism
verifiedReferences.authors: Vyas V, Goyal A

questions.stem: What CTPA finding distinguishes submassive PE from massive PE?

choices.feedback: Saddle embolus (straddling the pulmonary artery bifurcation) sounds dramatic but does not inherently define massive PE. Hemodynamic status is the classification criterion.

choices.text: Submassive: RV dysfunction without hypotension; Massive: sustained hypotension (SBP < 90)
choices.feedback: Correct! Classification: Massive PE: sustained systolic BP < 90, or requiring vasopressors, or cardiac arrest → thrombolytics indicated. Submassive PE: hemodynamically stable BUT with RV dysfunction (RV/LV ratio > 0.9 on CTPA, elevated troponin/BNP, RV hypokinesis on echo) → consider advanced therapies case-by-case. Low-risk PE: stable, no RV dysfunction → anticoagulation alone, consider early discharge.

choices.text: Clot burden in the pulmonary arteries
choices.feedback: Clot location/burden does not define the massive vs. submassive distinction. Hemodynamic status does.
Laterality of clot does not define PE severity classification. The hemodynamic impact (blood pressure, RV function) determines it.

questions.explanation: PE severity classification is based on hemodynamic status and right ventricular (RV) function: Massive PE = sustained hypotension (SBP <90 for ≥15 min), need for vasopressors, or cardiac arrest. Submassive PE = hemodynamically stable BUT with evidence of RV dysfunction (RV dilation on CTPA or echo, elevated troponin or BNP) or myocardial necrosis. Low-risk PE = hemodynamically stable without RV dysfunction. The critical distinguishing feature on CTPA is RV-to-LV diameter ratio: a ratio >0.9 indicates RV dysfunction. Submassive PE is the 'gray zone' where escalation of therapy (thrombolysis, catheter-directed therapy) may be considered.
questions.verifiedExplanation: PE severity classification is based on hemodynamic status and right ventricular (RV) function: Massive PE = sustained hypotension (SBP <90 for ≥15 min), need for vasopressors, or cardiac arrest. Submassive PE = hemodynamically stable BUT with evidence of RV dysfunction (RV dilation on CTPA or echo, elevated troponin or BNP) or myocardial necrosis. Low-risk PE = hemodynamically stable without RV dysfunction. The critical distinguishing feature on CTPA is RV-to-LV diameter ratio: a ratio >0.9 indicates RV dysfunction. Submassive PE is the 'gray zone' where escalation of therapy (thrombolysis, catheter-directed therapy) may be considered.

verifiedReferences.title: Pulmonary Embolism
verifiedReferences.authors: Vyas V, Goyal A

questions.stem: Which scoring system is used to determine pretest probability for pulmonary embolism?

choices.text: Wells PE score or revised Geneva score
choices.feedback: Correct! Wells PE criteria: clinical signs of DVT (3 pts), PE most likely diagnosis (3 pts), heart rate >100 (1.5), immobilization/surgery (1.5), previous DVT/PE (1.5), hemoptysis (1), active cancer (1). Score ≤4: PE unlikely → D-dimer to rule out. Score >4: PE likely → CTPA. Alternative: PERC rule for very low-risk patients (all 8 criteria must be negative to skip D-dimer). Revised Geneva score is an alternative validated system.
qSOFA is for sepsis screening, not PE probability assessment.
HEART score is for acute coronary syndrome risk stratification, not PE.
CURB-65 is for pneumonia severity assessment, not PE probability.

questions.explanation: The Wells PE score and the revised Geneva score are the two validated clinical prediction rules for determining pre-test probability of PE. The Wells PE score includes: clinical signs/symptoms of DVT (+3), PE is the most likely diagnosis (+3), heart rate >100 (+1.5), immobilization or surgery within 4 weeks (+1.5), prior DVT/PE (+1.5), hemoptysis (+1), malignancy (+1). Scores are stratified as: low (0-1), moderate (2-6), high (≥7) — or simplified: PE unlikely (<5) vs PE likely (≥5). The revised Geneva score uses objective criteria only (no subjective 'most likely diagnosis' component).
questions.verifiedExplanation: The Wells PE score and the revised Geneva score are the two validated clinical prediction rules for determining pre-test probability of PE. The Wells PE score includes: clinical signs/symptoms of DVT (+3), PE is the most likely diagnosis (+3), heart rate >100 (+1.5), immobilization or surgery within 4 weeks (+1.5), prior DVT/PE (+1.5), hemoptysis (+1), malignancy (+1). Scores are stratified as: low (0-1), moderate (2-6), high (≥7) — or simplified: PE unlikely (<5) vs PE likely (≥5). The revised Geneva score uses objective criteria only (no subjective 'most likely diagnosis' component).

verifiedReferences.title: Pulmonary Embolism
verifiedReferences.authors: Vyas V, Goyal A

questions.stem: What ECG finding is classically associated with acute right heart strain from PE?

choices.feedback: ST elevation in V1-V4 suggests anterior STEMI, not PE. PE causes right heart strain patterns.

choices.text: Atrial fibrillation with slow ventricular response
choices.feedback: New-onset AF can occur with PE, but slow ventricular response is not characteristic. Tachycardia is more typical.
AV block is not associated with PE. Right heart strain patterns (S1Q3T3, RBBB, right axis) are the classic findings.

choices.text: S1Q3T3 pattern (S wave in I, Q wave and inverted T in III)
choices.feedback: Correct! The S1Q3T3 pattern is the classic but INSENSITIVE (seen in <20% of PE) ECG finding. More common findings: sinus tachycardia (most common), right axis deviation, RBBB (new), T-wave inversions in V1-V4 (anterior), P pulmonale. Normal ECG does NOT rule out PE. ECG is most useful for excluding other diagnoses (STEMI). CTPA is the definitive imaging study.

questions.explanation: The S1Q3T3 pattern (deep S wave in lead I, Q wave in lead III, inverted T wave in lead III) is the classic ECG finding associated with acute right heart strain from PE. It represents the acute RV dilation and strain that shifts the cardiac axis rightward. However, this pattern is neither sensitive (present in only ~20% of PE cases) nor specific (can occur in other causes of RV strain). The most common ECG finding is sinus tachycardia. Other right heart strain patterns include new RBBB, T-wave inversions in V1-V4, right axis deviation, and P-pulmonale. A normal ECG does not exclude PE.

It represents the acute RV dilation and strain that shifts the cardiac axis rightward. However, this pattern is neither sensitive (present in only ~20% of PE cases) nor specific (can occur in other causes of RV strain). The most common ECG finding is sinus tachycardia. Other right heart strain patterns include new RBBB, T-wave inversions in V1-V4, right axis deviation, and P-pulmonale. A normal ECG does not exclude PE.

verifiedReferences.title: Pulmonary Embolism
verifiedReferences.authors: Vyas V, Goyal A

questions.stem: A patient with acute PE has a contraindication to anticoagulation due to recent hemorrhagic stroke. What is the appropriate intervention?

choices.feedback: A patient with acute PE cannot simply be observed without any intervention when anticoagulation is contraindicated. An IVC filter provides protection against recurrent embolism.
Aspirin has no role in VTE management and would still pose bleeding risk in a hemorrhagic stroke patient.
Compression stockings help with post-thrombotic syndrome but do not prevent PE. An IVC filter is needed when anticoagulation is contraindicated.

Correct! Absolute contraindication to anticoagulation → retrievable IVC filter to prevent PE from lower extremity DVT. Important: IVC filters do NOT treat existing PE, they prevent NEW embolism. Retrievable filters should be removed once anticoagulation can be safely initiated (within weeks-months). Complications: filter migration, IVC thrombosis, filter fracture. Anticoagulation should be started as soon as the contraindication resolves.

questions.explanation: IVC filter placement is indicated when a patient with acute VTE has an absolute contraindication to anticoagulation (such as recent CNS hemorrhage or active major bleeding). The filter mechanically traps emboli from the lower extremities before they reach the pulmonary vasculature. Retrievable filters are preferred and should be removed as soon as anticoagulation can be safely initiated. IVC filters do NOT treat existing PE or DVT — they only prevent new emboli. Complications include filter migration, IVC thrombosis, filter fracture, and recurrent DVT (paradoxically increased risk due to the foreign body in the vascular system).

The filter mechanically traps emboli from the lower extremities before they reach the pulmonary vasculature. Retrievable filters are preferred and should be removed as soon as anticoagulation can be safely initiated. IVC filters do NOT treat existing PE or DVT — they only prevent new emboli. Complications include filter migration, IVC thrombosis, filter fracture, and recurrent DVT (paradoxically increased risk due to the foreign body in the vascular system).

questions.stem: What is the most common mechanism of hypercalcemia in malignancy?

choices.feedback: True ectopic PTH secretion by tumors is extremely rare. PTHrP (parathyroid hormone-RELATED peptide) is the common tumor product.
Bone metastases cause ~20% of malignant hypercalcemia. The most common mechanism is humoral.

choices.text: PTHrP (parathyroid hormone-related peptide) secretion by the tumor
choices.feedback: Correct! Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy (HHM) via PTHrP accounts for ~80% of cases. PTHrP mimics PTH → increases renal calcium reabsorption, increases bone resorption, increases 1,25-vitamin D (indirectly). Common tumors: squamous cell cancers (lung, head/neck, esophageal), renal cell, bladder, breast. PTHrP is elevated, PTH is suppressed (distinguishes from primary hyperparathyroidism). Other mechanisms: osteolytic metastases (20%), 1,25-vitamin D overproduction (lymphomas — <1%).

choices.text: Vitamin D excess from tumor metabolism
choices.feedback: 1,25-vitamin D overproduction occurs in lymphomas (granulomatous 1-alpha-hydroxylase activity) but accounts for <1% of malignant hypercalcemia.

questions.explanation: Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy (HHM) mediated by PTHrP accounts for approximately 80% of cancer-related hypercalcemia. PTHrP is structurally homologous to PTH at the N-terminus and binds the same PTH/PTHrP receptor (type 1), causing increased bone resorption, increased renal calcium reabsorption, and phosphaturia. Squamous cell carcinomas (lung, head and neck, esophagus, cervix) are the most common producers. Unlike ectopic PTH (extremely rare), PTHrP is not detected by standard PTH assays — PTH will be appropriately suppressed. Direct osteolytic bone metastases account for ~20% of cases (breast cancer, multiple myeloma). Tumor-produced 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D is seen in lymphomas (Hodgkin and non-Hodgkin).

references.supportingQuote: Hypercalcemia of malignancy is the most common cause of hypercalcemia in hospitalized patients

questions.stem: A patient with lung cancer has calcium of 14.2 mg/dL. After aggressive IV saline hydration, what is the next pharmacologic step?

choices.feedback: Calcium channel blockers affect vascular smooth muscle calcium channels, not serum calcium levels.
Loop diuretics were historically used for calciuresis but are NOT first-line and can worsen dehydration. Only use if volume overloaded after adequate hydration.

Correct! Treatment ladder for malignant hypercalcemia: (1) IV NS hydration (1-2 L bolus then target rate — restore intravascular volume, promote calciuresis), (2) Bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid IV preferred — onset 24-48h, peak 4-7 days, duration weeks), (3) Calcitonin (rapid onset 4-6h but tachyphylaxis in 48h — use as bridge while waiting for bisphosphonate effect), (4) Denosumab if bisphosphonate-refractory. Severe symptomatic: add calcitonin immediately with bisphosphonate.
Corticosteroids help in lymphoma/myeloma-related hypercalcemia (reduce 1,25-vitamin D or tumor burden) but not in PTHrP-mediated HHM from solid tumors.

questions.explanation: After aggressive IV saline hydration (first-line to restore intravascular volume and increase renal calcium excretion), the next pharmacologic step for severe hypercalcemia of malignancy is IV zoledronic acid (a bisphosphonate). Bisphosphonates inhibit osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, which is the primary mechanism of calcium elevation regardless of whether the cause is PTHrP, osteolysis, or vitamin D. Zoledronic acid (4 mg IV over 15 minutes) is preferred over pamidronate for superior efficacy. Onset is 2-4 days with peak effect at 4-7 days. Calcitonin (rapid onset within hours, short duration) is used as bridge therapy while awaiting bisphosphonate effect. Furosemide was historically used for calciuresis but is no longer recommended routinely. Oral calcium channel blockers and prednisone are not appropriate initial treatments.

references.supportingQuote: Hypercalcemia of malignancy is the most common cause of hypercalcemia in hospitalized patients

questions.stem: A patient with sickle cell disease presents with acute chest pain, fever, dyspnea, and a new pulmonary infiltrate. What is the diagnosis and initial management?

choices.feedback: While this overlaps with CAP, acute chest syndrome in SCD requires a broader approach than antibiotics alone.
Pneumothorax would show absent lung markings without infiltrate on CXR. The new infiltrate points to ACS.
PE is in the differential but the combination of fever + new infiltrate + SCD history makes ACS the primary diagnosis. Evaluate for PE if not improving.

choices.text: Acute chest syndrome — antibiotics, supplemental O2, pain control, exchange transfusion if severe
choices.feedback: Correct! ACS is the #1 cause of death in adult SCD. Defined by: new infiltrate on CXR + one of: chest pain, fever, respiratory symptoms. Triggers: infection, fat embolism from bone marrow necrosis, in situ sickling. Management: broad-spectrum antibiotics (ceftriaxone + azithromycin — cover atypicals + encapsulated organisms), supplemental O2 (target SpO2 >95%), aggressive pain control (avoid undertreatment — but monitor for hypoventilation), incentive spirometry, simple transfusion (Hgb < 10) or exchange transfusion (severe/worsening).

questions.explanation: Acute chest syndrome (ACS) is a leading cause of death in sickle cell disease and is defined by a new pulmonary infiltrate plus at least one of: chest pain, fever, respiratory symptoms. Pathophysiology involves vaso-occlusion in the pulmonary vasculature, fat embolism from bone marrow infarction, and/or infection. Management includes: broad-spectrum antibiotics (covering atypical organisms including Mycoplasma and Chlamydia), supplemental oxygen to prevent further sickling, adequate pain control (avoiding over-sedation), simple transfusion to improve hemoglobin (target ~10 g/dL), and exchange transfusion for severe cases (HbS target <30%). It is not simple CAP because it involves sickling pathophysiology. Pneumothorax would show absent breath sounds and hyperresonance. PE would not typically cause a new infiltrate.

references.supportingQuote: Sickle cell disease results from a point mutation in the beta-globin gene causing hemoglobin polymerization

questions.stem: What is the primary pharmacologic disease-modifying treatment for sickle cell disease?

choices.feedback: Correct! Hydroxyurea is the most important disease-modifying drug in SCD. Mechanism: increases fetal hemoglobin (HbF) production → HbF inhibits HbS polymerization → fewer sickling events. Benefits: reduces vaso-occlusive crises by ~50%, reduces ACS, reduces transfusion needs, reduces mortality. Indicated for all adults and children ≥9 months with SCD (regardless of severity per NHLBI 2014 guidelines). Monitor CBC for myelosuppression. Newer agents: L-glutamine, crizanlizumab (anti-P-selectin), voxelotor (HbS polymerization inhibitor — withdrawn from US market Sept 2024; no clinical benefit despite improving hemoglobin).
Folic acid supports erythropoiesis but does not modify disease pathophysiology or prevent crises.
Iron supplementation is contraindicated in SCD unless documented iron deficiency. Most SCD patients have iron overload from chronic transfusions.
Aspirin does not prevent vaso-occlusive crises. The pathophysiology involves HbS polymerization and vascular adhesion, not platelet aggregation primarily.

questions.explanation: Hydroxyurea is the primary disease-modifying pharmacotherapy for sickle cell disease. Its main mechanism is increasing fetal hemoglobin (HbF) production, which inhibits HbS polymerization and sickling. HbF cannot participate in the deoxygenation-induced polymerization that causes the sickle shape. Additional benefits include reduced white blood cell count (decreasing vaso-occlusion), increased nitric oxide (vasodilation), improved red cell hydration, and increased mean corpuscular volume. Clinical benefits: reduced frequency of vaso-occlusive crises, decreased ACS episodes, reduced transfusion requirements, and improved survival. Folic acid supplementation supports erythropoiesis but does not modify disease course. Iron supplementation is typically contraindicated (chronic hemolysis causes iron overload). Aspirin does not prevent vaso-occlusive crises.

references.supportingQuote: Sickle cell disease results from a point mutation in the beta-globin gene causing hemoglobin polymerization

questions.stem: Which vaccines are particularly important for patients with sickle cell disease and why?

choices.feedback: While respiratory vaccines are important, the priority vaccination need in SCD is protection against encapsulated organisms due to functional asplenia.
While hepatitis vaccines are important for transfused patients, the critical vaccines relate to functional asplenia.

choices.text: Pneumococcal, meningococcal, and Haemophilus influenzae type b — due to functional asplenia
choices.feedback: Correct! SCD causes functional asplenia from repeated splenic infarction (autosplenectomy by age 5 in HbSS). Encapsulated organisms (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, Haemophilus influenzae) require splenic opsonization → markedly increased susceptibility. Vaccinations: PCV13 + PPSV23, meningococcal ACWY + MenB, Hib. Also: daily penicillin prophylaxis until at least age 5 (reduces pneumococcal sepsis by 84%). Annual influenza vaccine.
These are part of routine immunization but are not the critical vaccines specific to SCD's functional asplenia.

questions.explanation: Patients with sickle cell disease develop functional asplenia (autosplenectomy) by early childhood due to repeated splenic infarction. This leaves them highly susceptible to encapsulated organisms: Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, and Haemophilus influenzae type b. Therefore, pneumococcal (PCV13 + PPSV23), meningococcal (MenACWY + MenB), and Hib vaccines are critical. Patients also receive prophylactic penicillin from diagnosis through at least age 5. Additionally, annual influenza and age-appropriate COVID vaccines are recommended. The other options are incomplete — while all routine vaccines are important, the encapsulated organism vaccines are specifically essential because of functional asplenia.

references.supportingQuote: Sickle cell disease results from a point mutation in the beta-globin gene causing hemoglobin polymerization

questions.stem: A child presents 2 weeks after a viral illness with petechiae and platelet count of 15,000. The rest of the CBC is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: DIC shows prolonged PT/PTT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer, and schistocytes. Isolated thrombocytopenia with normal coagulation studies is ITP.

Correct! ITP: isolated thrombocytopenia with normal WBC and hemoglobin, no schistocytes, often follows a viral illness in children (1-4 weeks post-infection). Mechanism: anti-platelet antibodies (usually anti-GPIIb/IIIa) → splenic destruction. Childhood ITP is usually self-limited (resolves in 80% within 6 months). Treatment only if significant bleeding or platelets < 20-30K: first-line IVIG or corticosteroids. Anti-D immunoglobulin if Rh-positive. Splenectomy reserved for chronic refractory ITP.
TTP presents with MAHA (schistocytes, elevated LDH, low haptoglobin) + thrombocytopenia + neurologic changes + renal dysfunction + fever. Normal CBC otherwise points away from TTP.
Aplastic anemia causes pancytopenia (all cell lines decreased), not isolated thrombocytopenia with normal WBC and hemoglobin.

questions.explanation: The classic presentation of childhood immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) is an otherwise well child with isolated thrombocytopenia 1-3 weeks after a viral illness. Key features: petechiae/purpura, normal WBC and hemoglobin, no hepatosplenomegaly, no schistocytes. The mechanism is autoantibody-mediated platelet destruction (anti-GPIIb/IIIa or anti-GPIb/IX antibodies). Most pediatric ITP is self-limited (~80% resolve within 6-12 months). DIC would show prolonged PT/PTT, elevated D-dimer, and fibrinogen consumption. TTP has schistocytes and multisystem involvement. Aplastic anemia shows pancytopenia (low WBC and hemoglobin in addition to low platelets).

references.supportingQuote: Thrombocytopenia has multiple etiologies including decreased production, increased destruction, and sequestration

questions.stem: What is the key laboratory finding that distinguishes TTP from ITP?

choices.feedback: PT/PTT are normal in both TTP and ITP. Prolonged PT/PTT suggest DIC instead.
Both can cause severe thrombocytopenia. The platelet count alone doesn't distinguish them.

Correct! TTP has MAHA: schistocytes on peripheral smear, elevated LDH, low haptoglobin, elevated indirect bilirubin, negative DAT (non-immune hemolysis). ITP: isolated thrombocytopenia WITHOUT hemolysis. TTP pathophysiology: ADAMTS13 deficiency → uncleaved ultra-large vWF multimers → platelet aggregation in microvasculature → consumptive thrombocytopenia + RBC fragmentation. Send ADAMTS13 activity (severely deficient <10% in TTP). Treatment: emergent plasma exchange (TPE). NEVER give platelets in TTP (fuels microvascular thrombosis).

choices.text: Bone marrow biopsy showing megakaryocyte absence
choices.feedback: Bone marrow in both TTP and ITP shows INCREASED megakaryocytes (compensatory production). Biopsy is not the distinguishing feature.

questions.explanation: The key laboratory distinction between TTP and ITP is microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA): schistocytes on peripheral smear, elevated LDH, elevated indirect bilirubin, and low haptoglobin. ITP has isolated thrombocytopenia without hemolysis. Both conditions have normal coagulation studies (PT/PTT), which distinguishes them from DIC. The degree of thrombocytopenia overlaps significantly and cannot differentiate them. Bone marrow biopsy in both shows increased megakaryocytes (increased production attempting to compensate for peripheral destruction). The PLASMIC score helps estimate likelihood of TTP by incorporating hemolysis markers, platelet count, MCV, creatinine, and INR.

references.supportingQuote: Thrombocytopenia has multiple etiologies including decreased production, increased destruction, and sequestration

questions.stem: What is the treatment of choice for TTP?

choices.feedback: NEVER give platelets in TTP — platelets fuel microvascular thrombosis and can worsen organ damage. This is a critical teaching point.
IVIG is the treatment for ITP, not TTP. TTP requires plasma exchange to remove pathogenic antibodies and replenish ADAMTS13.

Correct! TPE is the cornerstone: removes pathogenic anti-ADAMTS13 antibodies AND ultra-large vWF multimers, while replacing functional ADAMTS13. Start empirically if ADAMTS13 pending — do NOT wait for results. Mortality without TPE: >90%; with TPE: ~10-20%. Adjuncts: corticosteroids, rituximab (refractory/relapsing), caplacizumab (anti-vWF nanobody — prevents vWF-platelet interaction). Monitor platelet count and LDH for treatment response.
Heparin is not standard TTP therapy. The thrombotic microangiopathy in TTP is driven by vWF-platelet interaction, not the coagulation cascade.

questions.explanation: Emergent therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) is the treatment of choice for TTP and should be initiated as soon as the diagnosis is suspected. TPE simultaneously removes the ADAMTS13 autoantibodies and ultra-large VWF multimers from circulation while replenishing functional ADAMTS13 via the replacement plasma. Without TPE, TTP has >90% mortality; with TPE plus corticosteroids, survival exceeds 80%. Caplacizumab (anti-VWF nanobody) and rituximab are adjunctive therapies. Platelet transfusions are contraindicated because they provide substrate for ongoing microvascular thrombosis, potentially worsening organ damage. IVIG has a limited role. Heparin is not indicated as this is a thrombotic microangiopathy, not DIC.

references.supportingQuote: TTP results from congenital or acquired decrease/absence of the von Willebrand factor-cleaving protease ADAMTS13

questions.stem: What is the pentad of TTP?

choices.text: MAHA, thrombocytopenia, neurologic symptoms, renal dysfunction, fever
choices.feedback: Correct! Classic pentad (full pentad seen in <5% of cases — do NOT wait for all 5): (1) Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (schistocytes), (2) Thrombocytopenia, (3) Neurologic symptoms (confusion, headache, seizures, focal deficits), (4) Renal dysfunction (usually mild), (5) Fever. In practice: MAHA + thrombocytopenia alone should trigger urgent ADAMTS13 testing and empiric TPE. The PLASMIC score helps estimate probability of severe ADAMTS13 deficiency.
This describes lupus, not TTP.

choices.text: Anemia, jaundice, splenomegaly, gallstones, leg ulcers
choices.feedback: This describes chronic hemolytic anemias (hereditary spherocytosis, SCD), not TTP.

choices.text: Thrombocytopenia, eczema, recurrent infections, small platelets, immunodeficiency
choices.feedback: This describes Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome, not TTP.

questions.explanation: The classic pentad of TTP is: (1) microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA) with schistocytes, elevated LDH, indirect hyperbilirubinemia, low haptoglobin; (2) thrombocytopenia from platelet consumption; (3) neurologic symptoms (confusion, headache, seizures, focal deficits); (4) renal dysfunction (elevated creatinine, proteinuria); and (5) fever. The full pentad is present in fewer than 10% of patients at diagnosis. Current practice initiates treatment based on MAHA plus thrombocytopenia alone (the "dyad") without waiting for the full pentad, as delay increases mortality. The other answer choices describe SLE, hereditary spherocytosis, and Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome respectively.

references.supportingQuote: TTP results from congenital or acquired decrease/absence of the von Willebrand factor-cleaving protease ADAMTS13

questions.stem: A patient with newly diagnosed acute leukemia (WBC 200,000) develops hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia, and elevated uric acid after starting chemotherapy. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! TLS: massive cell death → release of intracellular contents: potassium ↑, phosphorus ↑ (binds calcium → Ca²⁺ ↓), uric acid ↑ (from nucleic acid breakdown → xanthine oxidase → uric acid), LDH ↑. Risks: high tumor burden (WBC >100K), aggressive chemo-sensitive cancers (ALL, Burkitt lymphoma, DLBCL). Complications: cardiac arrhythmias (hyperK), renal failure (uric acid/calcium phosphate crystal deposition), seizures (hypocalcemia).

choices.text: Acute renal failure from nephrotoxic chemotherapy
choices.feedback: While chemo can cause nephrotoxicity, this specific metabolic pattern is diagnostic of a different complication.
Sepsis would not cause this specific metabolic pattern of hyperK/hyperPhos/hypoCa/hyperuricemia. TLS has a pathognomonic metabolic signature.
Adrenal crisis causes hypotension, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia but not the complete TLS metabolic pattern.

questions.explanation: Tumor lysis syndrome (TLS) occurs when rapid destruction of tumor cells releases massive amounts of intracellular contents into the bloodstream: potassium (hyperkalemia), phosphate (hyperphosphatemia with secondary hypocalcemia), nucleic acids metabolized to uric acid (hyperuricemia), and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH). The classic laboratory tetrad is hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia, and hyperuricemia. Acute kidney injury results from uric acid and calcium phosphate crystal deposition in renal tubules. TLS is most common in rapidly proliferating, highly chemo-sensitive malignancies: Burkitt lymphoma, acute lymphoblastic leukemia (especially with high WBC), and acute myeloid leukemia. Nephrotoxic chemotherapy does not cause the specific electrolyte pattern. Sepsis has different electrolyte derangements. Adrenal crisis causes hyponatremia and hyperkalemia but not hyperphosphatemia.

references.supportingQuote: Tumor lysis syndrome results from massive tumor cell lysis releasing intracellular contents

questions.stem: What is the first-line prophylactic agent for tumor lysis syndrome?

choices.feedback: Calcium supplementation is generally avoided in TLS prevention because hyperphosphatemia + calcium → calcium phosphate crystal deposition in tissues and kidneys.
While maintaining UOP is critical, the primary approach is aggressive IV hydration. Diuretics are only used if volume overloaded. Allopurinol/rasburicase addresses the uric acid burden.
Urine alkalinization with bicarbonate was historically used but is now NOT recommended — it promotes calcium phosphate precipitation in the kidneys.

Correct! Prevention: aggressive IV hydration (target rate target UOP >target rate/hr) + allopurinol (xanthine oxidase inhibitor — prevents NEW uric acid formation). High-risk patients (Burkitt, ALL with WBC >100K): rasburicase (recombinant urate oxidase — converts EXISTING uric acid to allantoin, which is highly soluble). Rasburicase works within hours vs. days for allopurinol. Contraindicated: rasburicase in G6PD deficiency (produces H₂O₂ → hemolysis). Monitor electrolytes q6-8h during treatment.

questions.explanation: Allopurinol is the standard first-line prophylactic agent for tumor lysis syndrome. It is a xanthine oxidase inhibitor that prevents the conversion of hypoxanthine and xanthine to uric acid, reducing the risk of uric acid nephropathy. Rasburicase (recombinant urate oxidase) is preferred for high-risk patients because it directly converts existing uric acid to allantoin (a highly water-soluble, easily excreted metabolite), providing both prophylaxis and treatment. Rasburicase is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency because it generates hydrogen peroxide during uric acid metabolism. Calcium gluconate treats symptomatic hypocalcemia but is not prophylactic for TLS. Furosemide maintains urine output but does not prevent TLS. Sodium bicarbonate was previously used for urine alkalinization but is no longer recommended as it may worsen calcium phosphate precipitation.

references.supportingQuote: Tumor lysis syndrome results from massive tumor cell lysis releasing intracellular contents

questions.stem: A patient on fluoxetine (SSRI) is prescribed tramadol for pain. What dangerous drug interaction can occur?

choices.feedback: Neither SSRIs nor tramadol cause aplastic anemia.
While SSRIs can prolong QTc, the more dangerous acute interaction with tramadol is serotonergic.

Correct! Fluoxetine (SSRI) + tramadol (weak serotonin reuptake inhibitor + opioid) → serotonin excess → serotonin syndrome: triad of mental status changes (agitation, confusion), autonomic instability (hyperthermia, tachycardia, diaphoresis, mydriasis), neuromuscular hyperactivity (clonus, hyperreflexia, tremor). Treatment: stop offending agents, cyproheptadine (serotonin antagonist), supportive care, benzodiazepines. Other dangerous combinations: SSRIs + MAOIs, SSRIs + linezolid, SSRIs + St. John's wort.
Hypertensive crisis from tyramine is associated with MAOIs, not SSRIs.

questions.explanation: Major depressive disorder requires ≥5 symptoms for ≥2 weeks (SIG E CAPS: Sleep disturbance, Interest loss, Guilt, Energy loss, Concentration difficulty, Appetite change, Psychomotor changes, Suicidal ideation) with depressed mood or anhedonia required. First-line treatment: SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram). Sertraline is preferred in pregnancy and breastfeeding due to lowest placental transfer and breast milk levels. Full therapeutic effect takes 4-6 weeks. Add CBT for combination therapy. Switch or augment (lithium, aripiprazole, bupropion) if inadequate response after adequate trial.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of SSRIs?

choices.feedback: Alpha-2 antagonism is the mechanism of mirtazapine, not SSRIs.

choices.text: Blockade of dopamine reuptake in the mesolimbic pathway
choices.feedback: Dopamine reuptake inhibition is the mechanism of bupropion and stimulants, not SSRIs.

choices.text: Selective inhibition of serotonin reuptake transporter (SERT) in the synaptic cleft
choices.feedback: Correct! SSRIs (fluoxetine, sertraline, paroxetine, citalopram, escitalopram, fluvoxamine) block SERT → increased serotonin in the synaptic cleft. First-line for MDD, GAD, panic disorder, OCD, PTSD, social anxiety. Therapeutic effect takes 4-6 weeks (neuroplasticity changes, receptor downregulation). Side effects: GI (nausea), sexual dysfunction, weight gain, emotional blunting, increased bleeding risk (decreased platelet serotonin), discontinuation syndrome (short half-life agents like paroxetine).
MAO inhibition is the mechanism of MAOIs (phenelzine, tranylcypromine, selegiline). SSRIs work through reuptake inhibition.

questions.explanation: Major depressive disorder requires ≥5 symptoms for ≥2 weeks (SIG E CAPS: Sleep disturbance, Interest loss, Guilt, Energy loss, Concentration difficulty, Appetite change, Psychomotor changes, Suicidal ideation) with depressed mood or anhedonia required. First-line treatment: SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram). Sertraline is preferred in pregnancy and breastfeeding due to lowest placental transfer and breast milk levels. Full therapeutic effect takes 4-6 weeks. Add CBT for combination therapy. Switch or augment (lithium, aripiprazole, bupropion) if inadequate response after adequate trial.

questions.stem: Which antidepressant is preferred for a patient with MDD who is trying to quit smoking?

choices.feedback: Sertraline is a good first-line antidepressant but has no specific smoking cessation benefit.
Mirtazapine causes sedation, increased appetite, and weight gain. It has no smoking cessation benefit.
Paroxetine is an SSRI with the most anticholinergic effects and weight gain, and no smoking cessation benefit.

Correct! Bupropion (NDRI — norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibitor) treats both depression and aids smoking cessation (marketed as Zyban for this indication). Also: no sexual dysfunction (unlike SSRIs), weight-neutral or weight-loss effect. Contraindications: seizure disorder, eating disorders (lowers seizure threshold), concurrent MAOI use. Not effective for anxiety disorders or OCD.

questions.explanation: Major depressive disorder requires ≥5 symptoms for ≥2 weeks (SIG E CAPS: Sleep disturbance, Interest loss, Guilt, Energy loss, Concentration difficulty, Appetite change, Psychomotor changes, Suicidal ideation) with depressed mood or anhedonia required. First-line treatment: SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram). Sertraline is preferred in pregnancy and breastfeeding due to lowest placental transfer and breast milk levels. Full therapeutic effect takes 4-6 weeks. Add CBT for combination therapy. Switch or augment (lithium, aripiprazole, bupropion) if inadequate response after adequate trial.

questions.stem: A patient on haloperidol develops high fever, muscle rigidity, elevated CK, and altered mental status. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Malignant hyperthermia is triggered by inhaled anesthetics (sevoflurane) or succinylcholine, not antipsychotics. It does share the dantrolene treatment.

Correct! NMS: idiosyncratic reaction to dopamine antagonists (typical > atypical antipsychotics, also metoclopramide). Classic tetrad: Fever (often >40°C), lead-pipe Rigidity, Altered mental status, Autonomic instability (tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis). Labs: markedly elevated CK (rhabdomyolysis), leukocytosis, metabolic acidosis. Treatment: stop antipsychotic, aggressive cooling, IV fluids, dantrolene (muscle relaxant) ± bromocriptine (dopamine agonist). Mortality ~10% if untreated.
Serotonin syndrome features CLONUS and hyperreflexia (neuromuscular excitation). NMS features RIGIDITY and elevated CK. Different mechanisms.
Heat stroke would not explain the lead-pipe rigidity and extremely elevated CK in the context of antipsychotic use.

questions.explanation: Psychiatric diagnosis follows DSM-5 criteria. Evidence-based treatment combines pharmacotherapy with psychotherapy. Safety assessment (suicidality, homicidality, psychosis) is paramount in psychiatric evaluation.

questions.stem: What is the most concerning metabolic side effect of second-generation (atypical) antipsychotics like olanzapine and clozapine?

choices.feedback: Hepatotoxicity is not the primary metabolic concern. The metabolic syndrome cluster is more important.
QTc prolongation occurs (especially ziprasidone, IV haloperidol) but metabolic syndrome is the more prevalent and clinically impactful adverse effect.

EPS (dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia) are more common with TYPICAL antipsychotics. Atypicals were developed specifically to reduce EPS, though metabolic effects emerged as the trade-off.

Correct! Atypical antipsychotics (especially olanzapine, clozapine) cause significant metabolic effects: weight gain (olanzapine averages 4-5 kg in 10 weeks), insulin resistance → type 2 diabetes, dyslipidemia (elevated triglycerides and LDL). Monitoring required: fasting glucose, lipid panel, weight, waist circumference, BP at baseline, 12 weeks, then annually. Clozapine additionally requires weekly/biweekly CBC monitoring for agranulocytosis (1-2% risk). Lowest metabolic risk: aripiprazole, ziprasidone.

questions.explanation: Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech/behavior), negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, asociality), and cognitive symptoms. Diagnosis requires ≥6 months of symptoms with ≥1 month of active phase. Treatment: second-generation antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine) preferred first-line due to lower EPS risk. Clozapine reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (requires REMS monitoring for agranulocytosis — weekly CBC initially).

questions.stem: Which atypical antipsychotic requires mandatory blood monitoring and is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia?

choices.feedback: Quetiapine does not require blood monitoring and is widely used for schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and insomnia. It is not specifically reserved for treatment resistance.
Risperidone does not require blood monitoring. It is a first-line atypical antipsychotic.
Aripiprazole (partial dopamine agonist) has the lowest metabolic burden and no blood monitoring requirement.

Correct! Clozapine is the ONLY antipsychotic with proven efficacy in treatment-resistant schizophrenia (~30% of patients who fail 2+ adequate antipsychotic trials). Risk: agranulocytosis (1-2%) → mandatory ANC monitoring through REMS program (weekly × 6 months → biweekly × 6 months → monthly). Also: metabolic syndrome (worst among atypicals), myocarditis (1st month), seizures (dose-dependent), sialorrhea, sedation. Despite risks, clozapine also uniquely reduces suicidality in schizophrenia.

questions.explanation: Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech/behavior), negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, asociality), and cognitive symptoms. Diagnosis requires ≥6 months of symptoms with ≥1 month of active phase. Treatment: second-generation antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine) preferred first-line due to lower EPS risk. Clozapine reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (requires REMS monitoring for agranulocytosis — weekly CBC initially).

questions.stem: A patient with schizophrenia refuses a recommended surgery. Which element is NOT required to demonstrate decision-making capacity?

choices.text: Ability to reason about treatment options
choices.feedback: Rational reasoning IS required — the patient must be able to weigh pros/cons and explain their rationale.

choices.text: Understanding of the proposed treatment and alternatives
choices.feedback: Understanding IS required. The patient must comprehend the diagnosis, proposed treatment, alternatives, and consequences of refusing.

Correct! Capacity requires four elements: (1) Understanding the relevant information, (2) Appreciation of how it applies to their situation, (3) Reasoning — ability to weigh risks/benefits rationally, (4) Communicating a consistent choice. A patient with capacity can make decisions physicians disagree with, including refusing life-saving treatment. Mental illness or cognitive impairment does NOT automatically negate capacity — it must be assessed for each specific decision. Capacity is decision-specific, not global.

choices.text: Ability to communicate a consistent choice
choices.feedback: Communicating a choice IS one of the four required elements of capacity.

questions.explanation: Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech/behavior), negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, asociality), and cognitive symptoms. Diagnosis requires ≥6 months of symptoms with ≥1 month of active phase. Treatment: second-generation antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine) preferred first-line due to lower EPS risk. Clozapine reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (requires REMS monitoring for agranulocytosis — weekly CBC initially).

questions.stem: Which component of the mental status examination assesses a patient's awareness of their illness?

choices.feedback: Orientation (person, place, time, situation) assesses cognitive awareness of surroundings, not illness awareness.

Correct! Insight: patient's awareness and understanding of their illness. Levels: good (acknowledges illness and need for treatment), partial (acknowledges some symptoms but minimizes), poor/absent (denies illness — common in psychosis, mania, anosognosia). Related concept: Judgment — ability to make sound decisions and anticipate consequences. Both are assessed through interview questions. Full MSE components: Appearance, Behavior, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought process, Thought content, Perceptions, Cognition, Insight, Judgment.
Thought process describes HOW a patient thinks (linear, tangential, circumstantial, loose associations), not their awareness of illness.
Affect describes the observed emotional expression (flat, blunted, labile, congruent), not illness awareness.

questions.explanation: Psychiatric diagnosis follows DSM-5 criteria. Evidence-based treatment combines pharmacotherapy with psychotherapy. Safety assessment (suicidality, homicidality, psychosis) is paramount in psychiatric evaluation.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old presents with 8 months of auditory hallucinations, persecutory delusions, and social withdrawal. He has not used substances. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Schizophrenia requires ≥2 of the following for ≥6 months (with at least 1 month of active symptoms): delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized/catatonic behavior, negative symptoms (avolition, alogia, anhedonia, flat affect). Onset typically late teens to early 20s (men) or late 20s to early 30s (women). This patient has auditory hallucinations + delusions + negative symptoms (social withdrawal) for 8 months. Schizophreniform disorder: same symptoms for 1-6 months.
Brief psychotic disorder lasts <1 month. This patient has had symptoms for 8 months.
Delusional disorder involves non-bizarre delusions WITHOUT other psychotic features (no hallucinations, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms). This patient has multiple psychotic symptoms.
Schizoaffective requires concurrent mood episodes (major depressive or manic) with psychotic symptoms. No mood episode is described here.

questions.explanation: Alcohol withdrawal timeline: 6-12h: tremor, anxiety, insomnia. 12-24h: visual/tactile/auditory hallucinations (alcoholic hallucinosis). 24-48h: seizures (generalized tonic-clonic). 48-96h: delirium tremens (confusion, autonomic instability, hallucinations — 5% mortality untreated). Treatment: benzodiazepines (chlordiazepoxide or lorazepam) using CIWA protocol. Thiamine BEFORE glucose (to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy). Long-term: naltrexone (reduces craving) or acamprosate (stabilizes GABA/glutamate).

questions.stem: A patient has elevated calcium, elevated PTH, and low phosphorus. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Primary hyperPTH: autonomous PTH secretion from parathyroid adenoma (85%), hyperplasia (15%), or rarely carcinoma → elevated calcium, elevated PTH (inappropriately normal/elevated), low phosphorus (PTH promotes renal phosphate wasting), elevated urine calcium. Diagnosis: elevated calcium + non-suppressed PTH. Localization: sestamibi scan, neck ultrasound. Surgery (parathyroidectomy) is definitive treatment. Indications: symptomatic, Ca >1 mg/dL above normal, age <50, reduced BMD, renal complications.
Malignancy causes hypercalcemia via PTHrP (PTH itself is SUPPRESSED) or bone metastases. Elevated PTH points to a parathyroid source.
Vitamin D excess causes hypercalcemia but PTH is SUPPRESSED, not elevated.
Sarcoidosis causes hypercalcemia via 1,25-vitamin D overproduction by granulomas. PTH is SUPPRESSED (appropriate response), not elevated.

questions.explanation: Endocrine diagnosis requires systematic evaluation of hormone levels and their regulatory axes. Management follows evidence-based guidelines from the Endocrine Society and ADA.

questions.stem: An asymptomatic patient is found to have calcium of 11.5 with elevated PTH. What ECG finding may be present?

choices.feedback: Correct! Hypercalcemia → shortened QT interval (decreased phase 2 plateau duration). Severe hypercalcemia can also cause: Osborn waves (J waves), widened QRS, bradycardia, heart block, and cardiac arrest. Classic mnemonic for hypercalcemia symptoms: 'Stones (renal calculi), Bones (osteitis fibrosa cystica), Groans (abdominal pain, constipation, pancreatitis), Thrones (polyuria from nephrogenic DI), Psychiatric overtones (confusion, depression, psychosis).'
Prolonged QTc is seen in HYPOcalcemia (and hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia). Hypercalcemia shortens the QTc.
ST elevation suggests acute MI or pericarditis, not hypercalcemia. Hypercalcemia primarily affects the QT interval.
Peaked T waves are the earliest ECG sign of hyperKALEMIA, not hypercalcemia.

questions.explanation: Endocrine diagnosis requires systematic evaluation of hormone levels and their regulatory axes. Management follows evidence-based guidelines from the Endocrine Society and ADA.

questions.stem: What is the most common cause of hypocalcemia?

choices.feedback: Loop diuretics promote calcium excretion but rarely cause significant hypocalcemia as the sole cause. They are more commonly associated with hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia.

Correct! Vitamin D deficiency is the most common cause of hypocalcemia worldwide. Without adequate vitamin D → decreased intestinal calcium absorption → low serum calcium → compensatory PTH elevation (secondary hyperparathyroidism). Other causes: hypoparathyroidism (surgical, autoimmune), hypomagnesemia (impairs PTH secretion AND causes PTH resistance), CKD (decreased 1,25-vitamin D production), acute pancreatitis (calcium saponification). Symptoms: Chvostek sign, Trousseau sign, perioral tingling, tetany, seizures, prolonged QTc.
Hypoparathyroidism (usually post-surgical) is an important cause but not the most common.
Medullary thyroid cancer secretes calcitonin but rarely causes clinically significant hypocalcemia. Calcitonin's calcium-lowering effect is weak in humans.

questions.explanation: Endocrine diagnosis requires systematic evaluation of hormone levels and their regulatory axes. Management follows evidence-based guidelines from the Endocrine Society and ADA.

questions.stem: A non-diabetic patient presents with Whipple triad: symptoms of hypoglycemia, documented low blood glucose, and symptom resolution with glucose administration. Labs show low glucose with elevated insulin and C-peptide. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Reactive (postprandial) hypoglycemia occurs 2-4 hours after meals and does not show persistently elevated fasting insulin with Whipple triad.

Correct! Insulinoma: pancreatic beta-cell tumor causing endogenous hyperinsulinemia. Pattern: low glucose + HIGH insulin + HIGH C-peptide + HIGH proinsulin + negative sulfonylurea screen. If C-peptide low → exogenous insulin. If sulfonylurea screen positive → surreptitious sulfonylurea use. Localization: CT/MRI, endoscopic ultrasound. Treatment: surgical resection. Most insulinomas are benign, solitary, and <2 cm. Associated with MEN1 (multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1).
Exogenous insulin causes LOW C-peptide (injected insulin suppresses endogenous production). This patient has HIGH C-peptide.
Adrenal insufficiency can cause hypoglycemia (cortisol is a counter-regulatory hormone) but would NOT cause elevated insulin and C-peptide.

questions.explanation: Endocrine diagnosis requires systematic evaluation of hormone levels and their regulatory axes. Management follows evidence-based guidelines from the Endocrine Society and ADA.

questions.stem: What is the most common cause of hypoglycemia in hospitalized patients?

choices.feedback: Alcohol inhibits gluconeogenesis and can cause hypoglycemia (especially in fasting/malnourished patients) but is not the most common cause in hospitalized patients.
Insulinoma is rare. The most common cause in hospitals is iatrogenic.
