Sepsis can cause hypoglycemia (liver dysfunction, increased glucose utilization) but iatrogenic causes from diabetes medications are far more common.

choices.text: Insulin or sulfonylurea therapy in diabetic patients
choices.feedback: Correct! Iatrogenic hypoglycemia from diabetes medications is by far the most common cause. Risk factors: insulin dose errors, reduced oral intake without dose adjustment, renal impairment (sulfonylureas and insulin clearance), hepatic dysfunction. Sulfonylureas (especially glyburide — long-acting, renally cleared) cause prolonged hypoglycemia requiring dextrose infusion and sometimes octreotide. Metformin does NOT cause hypoglycemia (does not stimulate insulin secretion).

questions.explanation: Endocrine diagnosis requires systematic evaluation of hormone levels and their regulatory axes. Management follows evidence-based guidelines from the Endocrine Society and ADA.

questions.stem: How does the C-peptide level help distinguish endogenous from exogenous hyperinsulinemia?

choices.feedback: High C-peptide indicates increased endogenous insulin secretion, which can occur in insulinoma, obesity/insulin resistance, or sulfonylurea use — not exclusively diabetes.

choices.text: C-peptide is always elevated in hypoglycemia
choices.feedback: C-peptide levels depend on the source of insulin. They are not always elevated.

choices.text: C-peptide is a marker of insulin resistance, not secretion
choices.feedback: C-peptide is a direct marker of endogenous insulin SECRETION, not resistance. It is released 1:1 with insulin from beta cells.

Correct! Proinsulin is cleaved in beta cells → insulin + C-peptide released in equimolar amounts. Exogenous insulin preparations do NOT contain C-peptide. Therefore: high insulin + high C-peptide = endogenous source (insulinoma, sulfonylurea). High insulin + low C-peptide = exogenous insulin injection (factitious hypoglycemia). Also check: proinsulin (elevated in insulinoma), sulfonylurea screen, and insulin antibodies.

questions.explanation: Key detail: peptide; exogenous insulin does not.

questions.stem: A patient presents with galactorrhea, amenorrhea, and a 1.5 cm pituitary mass. Prolactin is 250 ng/mL. What is the first-line treatment?

choices.feedback: Radiation is a last resort for aggressive/refractory pituitary tumors. Prolactinomas respond excellently to dopamine agonists.
Surgery is reserved for prolactinomas refractory to medical therapy or causing acute visual compromise. Medical therapy is first-line.
Estrogen replacement addresses the hypogonadal symptoms but does not treat the underlying prolactinoma. Dopamine agonists normalize prolactin and restore gonadal function.

Correct! Prolactinomas (most common pituitary adenoma) are unique among pituitary tumors: medical therapy is first-line. Dopamine agonists (cabergoline preferred — more effective, fewer side effects, twice-weekly dosing; bromocriptine for pregnancy planning): reduce prolactin levels AND shrink tumor mass. Even macroprolatinomas (≥1 cm) respond to medical therapy in >90% of cases. Monitor: prolactin levels and MRI for tumor size. Surgery only if: medication intolerance, resistance, or acute visual field compromise.

questions.explanation: Correct: Dopamine agonist (cabergoline or bromocriptine).

questions.stem: A patient with a pituitary macroadenoma reports progressive bitemporal hemianopia. What structure is being compressed?

choices.feedback: Unilateral optic nerve compression causes monocular vision loss, not bitemporal hemianopia.
LGN lesions cause contralateral homonymous hemianopia, not bitemporal hemianopia.
Occipital lesions cause contralateral homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing, not bitemporal hemianopia.

Correct! The optic chiasm sits directly above the pituitary fossa. Pituitary macroadenomas growing superiorly compress the chiasm → selectively damage crossing nasal fibers (which carry temporal visual field information from both eyes) → bitemporal hemianopia (loss of peripheral vision bilaterally). This is a surgical emergency if progressive. All pituitary macroadenomas require formal visual field testing (Goldmann or Humphrey perimetry). Other compression effects: cavernous sinus invasion → cranial nerve palsies (CN III, IV, V1/V2, VI).

questions.explanation: Endocrine diagnosis requires systematic evaluation of hormone levels and their regulatory axes. Management follows evidence-based guidelines from the Endocrine Society and ADA.

questions.stem: What is the appropriate workup before pituitary surgery for a non-functioning macroadenoma?

choices.feedback: A complete pituitary hormone panel is needed to assess for hypopituitarism and rule out functioning adenoma.
MRI with gadolinium is the imaging modality of choice for pituitary adenomas. CT is inferior for soft tissue detail in the sella.

choices.text: Complete pituitary hormone panel, visual field testing,
choices.feedback: Correct! Pre-operative workup: (1) Full anterior pituitary panel: prolactin, IGF-1 (growth hormone), ACTH/cortisol (8 AM), TSH/free T4, LH/FSH, testosterone/estradiol. Rule out prolactinoma (which would be treated medically, not surgically). (2) Visual field testing (quantify any chiasmal compression). (3) MRI with gadolinium (tumor size, cavernous sinus invasion, relationship to optic chiasm). (4) Assess posterior pituitary: check for diabetes insipidus (serum/urine osmolality). If cortisol deficiency found → replace BEFORE surgery.

choices.text: PET scan to evaluate for metastatic disease
choices.feedback: Pituitary adenomas are benign neoplasms. PET scanning is not indicated. MRI is the standard imaging.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Complete pituitary hormone panel, visual field testing, and MRI.

questions.stem: A patient has elevated free T4, suppressed TSH, and diffuse increased uptake on radioactive iodine scan. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Exogenous thyroid hormone ingestion causes SUPPRESSED RAI uptake (exogenous hormone suppresses TSH → no thyroid stimulation → no iodine uptake).
Toxic MNG shows patchy/irregular uptake on RAI scan, not diffuse increased uptake.
Subacute (de Quervain) thyroiditis causes LOW/absent RAI uptake (thyroid cells damaged, not actively trapping iodine). Diffuse high uptake = Graves.

Correct! Graves disease: autoimmune — thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins (TSI) bind and activate the TSH receptor → diffuse thyroid stimulation → diffuse increased RAI uptake (distinguishing feature). Labs: elevated free T4, suppressed TSH, positive TSI/TRAb antibodies, sometimes elevated TPO antibodies. Clinical: thyrotoxicosis + Graves ophthalmopathy (proptosis, lid retraction) + pretibial myxedema + thyroid acropachy. Treatment: antithyroid drugs (methimazole preferred; PTU for 1st trimester pregnancy), RAI ablation, or thyroidectomy.

questions.explanation: Endocrine diagnosis requires systematic evaluation of hormone levels and their regulatory axes. Management follows evidence-based guidelines from the Endocrine Society and ADA.

questions.stem: What is the most feared adverse effect of methimazole and PTU?

choices.feedback: Correct! Agranulocytosis (ANC <500): occurs in 0.2-0.5% of patients, usually within first 3 months. Life-threatening — can present with fever, sore throat, oral ulcers → rapidly fatal sepsis if not recognized. All patients must be counseled: if fever/sore throat develops → stop drug immediately → get urgent CBC. Not dose-dependent for methimazole (idiosyncratic). Also: PTU-specific → ANCA-positive vasculitis, severe hepatocellular necrosis (boxed warning). Methimazole preferred in nearly all situations except 1st trimester pregnancy.
Thyroid storm is a complication of uncontrolled hyperthyroidism, not an adverse effect of antithyroid drugs.
Antithyroid drugs cause temporary hypothyroidism (dose-adjustable), not permanent. RAI ablation and surgery cause permanent hypothyroidism.

Hepatotoxicity patterns differ: PTU causes hepatocellular necrosis (can be fatal); methimazole causes cholestatic injury (usually milder). But hepatotoxicity is not the most common feared side effect.

questions.explanation: Endocrine diagnosis requires systematic evaluation of hormone levels and their regulatory axes. Management follows evidence-based guidelines from the Endocrine Society and ADA.

questions.stem: A patient with hypothyroidism has a TSH of 85 and free T4 of 0.2. What is the most likely etiology?

choices.feedback: Sick euthyroid (non-thyroidal illness) typically shows low T3, low/normal T4, and variable TSH. TSH of 85 is not consistent with this.
Acute iodine excess can transiently suppress thyroid function, but it would not cause TSH of 85. This degree of elevation indicates chronic primary thyroid failure.
Pituitary dysfunction would cause central hypothyroidism with LOW or inappropriately normal TSH, not an extremely elevated TSH.

Correct! Hashimoto (chronic lymphocytic thyroiditis): most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient areas. Autoimmune: anti-TPO antibodies (~95%), anti-thyroglobulin antibodies. Pathology: lymphocytic infiltration → gradual thyroid destruction → primary hypothyroidism (elevated TSH, low free T4). Very high TSH (>50) indicates severe primary thyroid failure. Treatment: levothyroxine replacement. Dose: ~weight-based dosing. Monitor TSH in 6-8 weeks, adjust until euthyroid (TSH 0.5-2.5).

questions.explanation: Hypothyroidism: fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, bradycardia. TSH elevated, free T4 low. Hashimoto's (anti-TPO antibodies) most common cause. Treatment: levothyroxine. Hyperthyroidism: weight loss, heat intolerance, tachycardia, tremor. TSH suppressed, elevated free T4/T3. Graves' disease (TSI antibodies, diffuse goiter, exophthalmos) most common cause. Treatment: methimazole (preferred), PTU (first trimester pregnancy), radioactive iodine, or surgery. Thyroid storm: fever, tachycardia, AMS — treat with PTU, beta-blocker, corticosteroids, iodine (after PTU).

questions.stem: A patient presents with altered mental status. Using the mnemonic AEIOU-TIPS, which of the following is NOT a common cause?

choices.feedback: U = Uremia is included in AEIOU-TIPS and is a common metabolic cause of AMS.

Correct! AEIOU-TIPS: A=Alcohol/Acidosis, E=Endocrine/Electrolytes/Encephalopathy, I=Infection (meningitis, UTI, sepsis), O=Overdose/Oxygen (hypoxia), U=Uremia. T=Trauma/Temperature, I=Insulin (hypo/hyperglycemia), P=Psychiatric/Poisoning, S=Stroke/Seizure/Syncope. Asthma is a respiratory condition, not typically categorized in AMS differential. The systematic approach ensures no reversible cause is missed.
T = Trauma (subdural hematoma, epidural, TBI) is a key cause of AMS.
I = Infection is a critical cause of AMS, especially UTI in elderly, meningitis, sepsis.

questions.stem: An elderly patient with baseline dementia is found to be acutely more confused. UA shows pyuria. What is the most important next step?

choices.text: Perform a systematic evaluation for all reversible causes before attributing AMS to UTI alone
choices.feedback: Correct! UTI is overdiagnosed as the cause of AMS in elderly. Asymptomatic bacteriuria is present in 25-50% of nursing home residents. Always evaluate: medication changes (anticholinergics, opioids, benzodiazepines), metabolic (glucose, Na, Ca, TSH), infectious (blood cultures, CXR, UA), neurologic (stroke, seizure), cardiac (MI can present as confusion in elderly). Treat UTI if present, but don't stop the workup there.

choices.text: Restrain the patient and administer haloperidol
choices.feedback: Chemical/physical restraints should be avoided as first-line. Identifying and treating the underlying cause is the priority.
CT head may be indicated but should be part of a systematic evaluation, not the sole next step. The question asks about the approach to AMS.

choices.text: Start broad-spectrum antibiotics for UTI immediately
choices.feedback: Asymptomatic bacteriuria is common in elderly — pyuria alone may not explain AMS. A broader workup is needed.

questions.stem: What bedside test should be performed on ALL patients presenting with altered mental status?

choices.feedback: Correct! Fingerstick glucose is the single most important immediate bedside test for AMS. Hypoglycemia is rapidly reversible and can present identically to stroke, intoxication, or psychosis. Delays in recognition → irreversible brain injury. If glucose <60: give D50W IV immediately. Other immediate bedside assessments: vital signs (fever, hypotension, hypoxia), pupil exam, GCS/mental status, fingerstick glucose, pulse oximetry.
LP is indicated if meningitis/encephalitis is suspected but is not performed on ALL AMS patients as an initial step.
CT head is important when indicated (focal deficits, trauma, anticoagulation) but is not a universal bedside test.
Tox screen is useful but results take time and do not change immediate management as quickly as glucose correction.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with crushing substernal chest pain radiating to the left arm, diaphoresis, and nausea. ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. What is the diagnosis and affected coronary artery?

choices.feedback: Correct! ST elevation in II, III, aVF = inferior wall STEMI. The RCA supplies the inferior wall in ~85% of patients (right-dominant circulation). Always check right-sided leads (V4R) for RV involvement. RV infarction: avoid nitroglycerin and diuretics (preload-dependent). Treatment: emergent PCI (door-to-balloon <90 min) or fibrinolytics if PCI unavailable within 120 min. Aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor + anticoagulation.
Posterior STEMI shows ST depression in V1-V3 (reciprocal changes) and ST elevation in posterior leads V7-V9. The leads described are inferior.
Lateral STEMI shows ST elevation in I, aVL, V5, V6 and involves the left circumflex artery. Inferior leads point to RCA.
Anterior STEMI affects leads V1-V4 and involves the LAD. Inferior leads (II, III, aVF) indicate a different territory.

questions.explanation: Key detail: Right Coronary Artery (RCA).

questions.stem: Which chest pain feature is MOST concerning for aortic dissection rather than ACS?

choices.text: Sudden-onset, severe, tearing/ripping pain radiating to the back with blood pressure differential between arms
choices.feedback: Correct! Aortic dissection red flags: sudden onset of maximal-intensity pain (vs. crescendo pattern in ACS), tearing/ripping quality, radiation to back (Type A: ascending → anterior chest; Type B: descending → back), BP differential >20 mmHg between arms, new aortic regurgitation murmur, pulse deficits, widened mediastinum on CXR. CT angiography is diagnostic. CRITICAL: thrombolytics (given for STEMI) are LETHAL in aortic dissection — always consider dissection before administering.
Troponin elevation suggests myocardial injury (ACS). Dissection can occasionally cause troponin elevation if it occludes a coronary ostium, but this is not a distinguishing feature.
Exertional pain is more suggestive of angina/ACS than dissection.
Nitroglycerin relief suggests angina. Dissection pain is not relieved by nitroglycerin.

questions.stem: A patient presents with pleuritic chest pain that improves with sitting forward. ECG shows diffuse ST elevation with PR depression. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Takotsubo can mimic STEMI with ST elevation and troponin rise but typically shows apical ballooning on echo, not the PR depression pattern of pericarditis.
STEMI shows ST elevation in a specific coronary territory with reciprocal depression. Diffuse ST elevation is not ACS.
PE pain is pleuritic but ECG shows sinus tachycardia, right heart strain (S1Q3T3, RBBB), not diffuse ST elevation with PR depression.

Correct! Pericarditis classic findings: sharp/pleuritic chest pain worse supine, better sitting forward; diffuse ST elevation (concave up) with PR depression in all leads except aVR (PR elevation in aVR); friction rub on auscultation. Causes: viral (most common), uremia, post-MI (Dressler syndrome), autoimmune, malignancy. Treatment: NSAIDs + colchicine (reduces recurrence by 50%). Avoid anticoagulation if effusion present (hemorrhagic conversion risk). Echocardiogram to evaluate for effusion/tamponade.

questions.stem: A patient has fever > 38.3°C for more than 3 weeks with no diagnosis after 1 week of inpatient workup. What are the three classic categories of FUO?

choices.feedback: This is a temporal classification of infections, not the FUO diagnostic framework.

choices.text: Nosocomial, drug fever, and factitious fever
choices.feedback: These are causes of fever in hospitalized patients but represent specific subtypes, not the three classic FUO categories.

Correct! Classic FUO categories: Infections (~30%): TB, endocarditis, abscess, osteomyelitis, CMV/EBV. Malignancies (~20%): lymphoma (most common), leukemia, renal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma. Autoimmune/inflammatory (~20%): adult-onset Still disease, GCA/PMR, SLE, vasculitis, sarcoidosis. Undiagnosed (~30%): many resolve spontaneously. Workup: repeat cultures, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, echocardiogram, ESR/CRP/ferritin, peripheral smear, LDH, consider PET-CT if standard workup negative.
FUO categories are broader than just different infection types.

questions.stem: Which malignancy is the most common cause of FUO?

choices.feedback: Correct! Lymphoma (especially non-Hodgkin lymphoma) is the most common malignancy causing FUO. Classic B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss) may precede lymphadenopathy. CT or PET-CT often reveals occult lymphadenopathy or extranodal disease. Other malignancies in FUO: renal cell carcinoma (paraneoplastic fever), hepatocellular carcinoma, atrial myxoma (mimics endocarditis). Markedly elevated LDH, ferritin, or ESR should raise suspicion for malignancy.
Melanoma is typically diagnosed by skin examination. It is not a classic cause of prolonged FUO.
Lung cancer causes fever but is usually identified on chest imaging early in FUO workup.
Colon cancer can cause low-grade fever but is not the most common malignancy causing FUO.

questions.stem: Drug fever should be suspected in FUO when which pattern is observed?

choices.text: High fever with rigors occurring only during drug infusion
choices.feedback: Infusion-related reactions occur during administration. Drug fever is sustained and present even between doses.

choices.text: Fever only during the night with normal daytime temperatures
choices.feedback: This pattern is not specific to drug fever. Nocturnal fevers can occur with many infections and malignancies.

choices.text: Relatively well-appearing patient with fever, eosinophilia, and temporal correlation with drug initiation
choices.feedback: Correct! Drug fever clues: patient looks better than expected for degree of fever, relative bradycardia (pulse-temperature dissociation), eosinophilia (~25% of cases), rash (variable), temporal correlation with drug start (usually 7-10 days after initiation but can be delayed). Common culprits: antibiotics (beta-lactams, sulfonamides), anticonvulsants (phenytoin, carbamazepine), allopurinol. Diagnosis of exclusion — resolves within 48-72h of stopping offending drug.

choices.text: Fever resolving immediately after stopping the suspected drug
choices.feedback: Drug fever may take 48-72 hours to resolve after drug discontinuation, not immediate resolution.

questions.stem: A patient in the ED has BP 70/40, HR 130, warm extremities, and bounding pulses. CVP is low after fluid resuscitation. What type of shock is this?

choices.feedback: Obstructive shock (PE, tamponade, tension pneumothorax) presents with elevated CVP/JVD. This patient has low CVP.
Cardiogenic shock presents with cool extremities, elevated CVP/JVP, and pulmonary edema — not warm skin with bounding pulses.
Hypovolemic shock presents with cool, clammy skin, thready pulses, and narrow pulse pressure — not warm extremities and bounding pulses.

Correct! Warm shock (early septic/distributive): peripheral vasodilation → warm extremities, bounding pulses, wide pulse pressure, tachycardia, hypotension despite fluids. Low SVR, high cardiac output initially. Causes: sepsis (most common), anaphylaxis, neurogenic, adrenal crisis. Treatment: IV fluids (target rate crystalloid), if still hypotensive → vasopressors (norepinephrine first-line), source control, broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour. Late/cold septic shock: vasoconstriction, cool extremities, narrow pulse pressure → worse prognosis.

questions.explanation: Warm extremities with bounding pulses, tachycardia, and hypotension is the hallmark of distributive (septic) shock in its early/warm phase. In septic shock, inflammatory mediators cause systemic vasodilation (low SVR), which initially triggers compensatory increased cardiac output (warm, bounding pulses). This distinguishes it from hypovolemic shock (cold, clammy extremities, collapsed veins), cardiogenic shock (JVD, pulmonary edema, cold extremities), and obstructive shock (JVD, muffled heart sounds in tamponade). Late/cold septic shock occurs when cardiac output falls due to myocardial depression, and the patient becomes cool and mottled.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Warm extremities with bounding pulses, tachycardia, and hypotension is the hallmark of distributive (septic) shock in its early/warm phase. In septic shock, inflammatory mediators cause systemic vasodilation (low SVR), which initially triggers compensatory increased cardiac output (warm, bounding pulses). This distinguishes it from hypovolemic shock (cold, clammy extremities, collapsed veins), cardiogenic shock (JVD, pulmonary edema, cold extremities), and obstructive shock (JVD, muffled heart sounds in tamponade). Late/cold septic shock occurs when cardiac output falls due to myocardial depression, and the patient becomes cool and mottled.

questions.stem: What is the first-line vasopressor for septic shock after adequate fluid resuscitation?

choices.feedback: Dobutamine is an inotrope (beta-1 agonist) used for cardiogenic shock or as an add-on in septic shock with myocardial depression. It causes vasodilation and can worsen hypotension.
Phenylephrine is a pure alpha-1 agonist that increases SVR but can decrease cardiac output. Not recommended as first-line in septic shock.
Dopamine is no longer first-line — associated with more arrhythmias and higher mortality compared to norepinephrine in septic shock.

Correct! Surviving Sepsis Campaign: norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor for septic shock. Mechanism: potent alpha-1 agonist (vasoconstriction) + mild beta-1 (increased cardiac output). Target MAP ≥ 65 mmHg. If norepinephrine insufficient: add vasopressin (0.03 U/min — V1 receptor mediated vasoconstriction, catecholamine-sparing). If still refractory: add epinephrine. Consider hydrocortisoneif vasopressor requirements escalating. Phenylephrine: pure alpha-1, avoid in sepsis (reduces cardiac output).

questions.explanation: Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor for septic shock after adequate fluid resuscitation (typically 30 mL/kg crystalloid within the first 3 hours). Norepinephrine acts primarily on alpha-1 receptors (vasoconstriction, increases SVR) with some beta-1 activity (modest inotropic effect). It effectively counteracts the pathological vasodilation of sepsis. Vasopressin is the recommended second-line agent, added when norepinephrine alone is insufficient. Epinephrine is a third-line option. Dopamine is no longer recommended as first-line due to increased arrhythmia risk. Phenylephrine (pure alpha) is reserved for specific situations.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor for septic shock after adequate fluid resuscitation (typically 30 mL/kg crystalloid within the first 3 hours). Norepinephrine acts primarily on alpha-1 receptors (vasoconstriction, increases SVR) with some beta-1 activity (modest inotropic effect). It effectively counteracts the pathological vasodilation of sepsis. Vasopressin is the recommended second-line agent, added when norepinephrine alone is insufficient. Epinephrine is a third-line option. Dopamine is no longer recommended as first-line due to increased arrhythmia risk. Phenylephrine (pure alpha) is reserved for specific situations.

questions.stem: Beck's triad (hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds) is associated with which cause of shock?

choices.feedback: Tension pneumothorax causes hypotension and JVD but with absent breath sounds and tracheal deviation, not muffled heart sounds.
Massive PE causes hypotension and JVD but heart sounds are typically normal or show a loud P2, not muffled.

Correct! Beck's triad: hypotension (decreased cardiac output from impaired filling), JVD (elevated right heart pressures from pericardial compression), muffled/distant heart sounds (fluid between heart and chest wall). Also: pulsus paradoxus (>10 mmHg SBP drop with inspiration), electrical alternans on ECG. Diagnosis: bedside echocardiogram (emergent — diastolic collapse of RV). Treatment: emergent pericardiocentesis. Causes: trauma, malignancy, uremia, post-MI rupture, aortic dissection.
Aortic dissection presents with tearing chest/back pain and BP differential. It can cause tamponade if blood enters the pericardium, but Beck's triad directly describes tamponade.

questions.explanation: Beck's triad — hypotension, jugular venous distension (JVD), and muffled heart sounds — is the classic presentation of cardiac tamponade. Tamponade occurs when fluid accumulation in the pericardial space (blood, effusion, pus) compresses the heart, impairing diastolic filling. JVD occurs because venous return cannot enter the compressed right heart. Muffled heart sounds result from fluid insulating the heart. Pulsus paradoxus (>10 mmHg drop in systolic BP during inspiration) is another hallmark finding. Emergency treatment is pericardiocentesis. ECG may show electrical alternans (beat-to-beat variation in QRS amplitude) and low voltage.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Beck's triad — hypotension, jugular venous distension (JVD), and muffled heart sounds — is the classic presentation of cardiac tamponade. Tamponade occurs when fluid accumulation in the pericardial space (blood, effusion, pus) compresses the heart, impairing diastolic filling. JVD occurs because venous return cannot enter the compressed right heart. Muffled heart sounds result from fluid insulating the heart. Pulsus paradoxus (>10 mmHg drop in systolic BP during inspiration) is another hallmark finding. Emergency treatment is pericardiocentesis. ECG may show electrical alternans (beat-to-beat variation in QRS amplitude) and low voltage.

questions.stem: A patient has pH 7.28, pCO2 24, HCO3 11, Na 140, Cl 104. What is the acid-base disturbance and anion gap?

choices.feedback: Calculate the anion gap: Na - (Cl + HCO3) = 140 - (104 + 11) = 25. This is elevated (normal 12 ± 2).
The primary disturbance is metabolic (low HCO3 with low pH). The low pCO2 is compensatory hyperventilation, not a primary respiratory alkalosis.

choices.text: Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
choices.feedback: Correct! AG = 140 - (104 + 11) = 25 (elevated, normal ~12). Primary AGMA. Check Winter's formula for appropriate respiratory compensation: expected pCO2 = 1.5(HCO3) + 8 ± 2 = 1.5(11) + 8 = 24.5 ± 2. Actual pCO2 = 24 → appropriate compensation. AGMA differential (MUDPILES): Methanol, Uremia, DKA, Propylene glycol, Isoniazid/Iron, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates. Also calculate delta-delta: ΔAG/ΔHCO3 = 13/13 = 1.0 → pure AGMA without concurrent non-AG acidosis or metabolic alkalosis.
The pCO2 is LOW (24), indicating respiratory compensation (hyperventilation), not respiratory acidosis. Respiratory acidosis would show elevated pCO2.

questions.explanation: Nephrology assessment requires systematic evaluation of renal function, electrolytes, acid-base status, and urinalysis. KDIGO guidelines provide evidence-based staging and management recommendations.

questions.stem: What is the clinical significance of the delta-delta (ΔAG/ΔHCO3) ratio?

choices.text: It determines if respiratory compensation is adequate
choices.feedback: Winter's formula assesses respiratory compensation. The delta-delta identifies concurrent metabolic disorders.

choices.text: It determines the etiology of the anion gap
choices.feedback: The delta-delta identifies concurrent metabolic disorders but does not determine the cause of the anion gap itself (which requires clinical context and labs like lactate, ketones, toxicology).

Correct! Delta-delta: ΔAG = (measured AG − 12); ΔHCO3 = (24 − measured HCO3). Ratio: ΔAG/ΔHCO3 = 1-2: pure AGMA. <1: concurrent non-AG metabolic acidosis (the HCO3 dropped MORE than the AG rose). >2: concurrent metabolic alkalosis (the HCO3 didn't drop as much as expected → something is keeping it up). Example: DKA + vomiting → AGMA + metabolic alkalosis → delta-delta > 2. This is a critical step in acid-base analysis that prevents missing mixed disorders.

choices.text: It predicts the severity of lactic acidosis
choices.feedback: The delta-delta does not specifically predict lactate levels. It identifies mixed metabolic acid-base disorders.

questions.explanation: Key detail: base disorders hidden by the anion gap.

questions.stem: A patient with COPD has pH 7.35, pCO2 60, HCO3 32. What is this acid-base pattern?

choices.feedback: The primary disorder is respiratory (elevated pCO2 in COPD patient). The elevated HCO3 is compensatory, not a primary metabolic alkalosis.

choices.text: Mixed respiratory acidosis and metabolic alkalosis
choices.feedback: The HCO3 is at the expected level for chronic respiratory acidosis. There's no evidence of a superimposed metabolic alkalosis (which would push pH above expected).
Acute respiratory acidosis would show pH < 7.35 with less HCO3 compensation (3-4 mEq/L per 10 mmHg pCO2 rise acutely vs 3.5 mEq/L per 10 mmHg chronically).

choices.text: Chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
choices.feedback: Correct! pCO2 elevated (60) = respiratory acidosis. pH near-normal (7.35) = significant compensation. Chronic compensation: HCO3 rises 3.5 mEq/L per 10 mmHg pCO2 increase. Expected: 24 + 3.5 × (60-40)/10 = 24 + 7 = 31. Actual HCO3 = 32 → appropriate chronic compensation. This takes 3-5 days of renal adaptation (increased H+ secretion, HCO3 reabsorption). Acute compensation: only 1 mEq/L HCO3 per 10 mmHg pCO2 → would expect HCO3 ~26, pH ~7.22.

questions.explanation: Nephrology assessment requires systematic evaluation of renal function, electrolytes, acid-base status, and urinalysis. KDIGO guidelines provide evidence-based staging and management recommendations.

questions.stem: A patient is pulseless with the monitor showing a chaotic, irregular waveform with no identifiable QRS complexes. What is the rhythm and initial treatment?

choices.feedback: Asystole is a flat line with no electrical activity. This chaotic irregular waveform is VF, which requires defibrillation.
VT shows wide regular complexes. Chaotic irregular waveform without QRS complexes is VF. Also, pulseless rhythms get defibrillation, not synchronized cardioversion.

Correct! VF: chaotic, irregular electrical activity with no organized QRS complexes = cardiac arrest. Treatment: immediate defibrillation (120-200J biphasic), CPR, epinephrineq3-5 min, amiodarone IV bolus (then 150 mg). Defibrillation is the ONLY definitive treatment for VF — every minute delay reduces survival by 7-10%. Shockable rhythms: VF and pulseless VT. Non-shockable: PEA and asystole (CPR + epinephrine, no defibrillation).

choices.text: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response — rate control with diltiazem
choices.feedback: The patient is PULSELESS — this is cardiac arrest, not a rate control situation. AFib with RVR has identifiable narrow complexes despite irregular rhythm.

questions.explanation: The correct answer (Ventricular fibrillation — immediate defibrillation) reflects current evidence-based cardiology guidelines. Key cardiovascular principles include systematic assessment of hemodynamic status, risk stratification using validated scores, and guideline-directed pharmacotherapy based on condition severity and patient characteristics.

questions.stem: During a cardiac arrest, the monitor shows a flat line. Before calling asystole, what must you verify?

choices.feedback: Defibrillation is NOT indicated for asystole. You must first verify the rhythm is truly asystole.
Transcutaneous pacing is for symptomatic bradycardia, not asystole in cardiac arrest. Evidence does not support pacing for asystole.

Correct! Before diagnosing asystole: (1) Check that leads are connected and properly placed, (2) Increase ECG gain (fine VF can appear as a flat line at low gain), (3) Confirm in a second lead (rotate 90°). Fine VF is a shockable rhythm that can mimic asystole. If truly asystole: CPR + epinephrine q3-5 min, identify and treat reversible causes (H's and T's: Hypovolemia, Hypoxia, Hydrogen ion, Hypo/hyperkalemia, Hypothermia, Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade, Toxins, Thrombosis pulmonary/coronary). NO defibrillation for asystole.
Atropine is no longer in the ACLS asystole/PEA algorithm (removed in 2010 guidelines). Epinephrine is the drug used.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Check leads, gain, and confirm in a second lead to rule out fine VF.

questions.stem: A patient has a heart rate of 35 with BP 80/50 and altered mental status. Atropine has been ineffective after multiple doses. What is the next step?

choices.feedback: Correct! Symptomatic bradycardia algorithm: (1) Atropineq3-5 min (max 3 mg). If atropine fails: (2a) Transcutaneous pacing (place pads, increase mA until capture, sedate if conscious), OR (2b) Dopamine infusion (5-weight-based dosing), OR (2c) Epinephrine infusion. Prepare for transvenous pacing. Causes: AV block (Mobitz II, 3rd degree), inferior MI, beta-blocker/CCB overdose, hyperkalemia. High-degree AV blocks often don't respond to atropine.
Defibrillation is for VF/pulseless VT, not bradycardia. This would potentially harm the patient.
Adenosine treats SVT by blocking the AV node — it would WORSEN bradycardia and is contraindicated.
Amiodarone treats tachyarrhythmias (VT/VF), not bradycardia.

questions.explanation: The correct answer (Transcutaneous pacing or dopamine/epinephrine infusion) reflects current evidence-based cardiology guidelines. Key cardiovascular principles include systematic assessment of hemodynamic status, risk stratification using validated scores, and guideline-directed pharmacotherapy based on condition severity and patient characteristics.

questions.stem: A patient is having continuous generalized tonic-clonic seizures for 15 minutes despite IV lorazepam. What is the diagnosis and next treatment step?

choices.text: Status epilepticus — IV fosphenytoin or levetiracetam as second-line
choices.feedback: Correct! Status epilepticus: continuous seizure >5 minutes or ≥2 seizures without return to baseline. Life-threatening emergency. Treatment ladder: (1) Benzodiazepines first-line (lorazepam, may repeat once; or midazolam IM if no IV). (2) If BZD fails: fosphenytoinPE/kg IV, OR levetiracetam, OR valproate. (3) Refractory: continuous infusion midazolam, propofol, or pentobarbital + continuous EEG monitoring + ICU. Also: ABCs, glucose check, thiamine if alcoholism suspected.

choices.text: Complex partial seizure — increase oral antiepileptic
choices.feedback: 15 minutes of continuous generalized tonic-clonic seizures is status epilepticus requiring emergent IV treatment, not outpatient medication adjustment.
Febrile seizures are brief (<5 min) and self-limited. Continuous seizures for 15 minutes is status epilepticus.

choices.text: Psychogenic non-epileptic event — no pharmacologic treatment needed
choices.feedback: PNEE is a consideration but 15-minute continuous generalized seizures must be treated as status epilepticus until proven otherwise. Safety first.

questions.explanation: Status epilepticus is defined as continuous seizure activity lasting >5 minutes or ≥2 seizures without return to baseline consciousness. It is a neurological emergency with mortality of 10-20%. First-line treatment is IV benzodiazepines (lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg or midazolam IM if no IV access). If seizures persist after adequate benzodiazepine dosing, second-line agents include fosphenytoin (preferred over phenytoin for better safety profile), levetiracetam, or valproic acid. If seizures continue (refractory status epilepticus), continuous IV infusion of propofol, midazolam, or pentobarbital with continuous EEG monitoring is required.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Status epilepticus is defined as continuous seizure activity lasting >5 minutes or ≥2 seizures without return to baseline consciousness. It is a neurological emergency with mortality of 10-20%. First-line treatment is IV benzodiazepines (lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg or midazolam IM if no IV access). If seizures persist after adequate benzodiazepine dosing, second-line agents include fosphenytoin (preferred over phenytoin for better safety profile), levetiracetam, or valproic acid. If seizures continue (refractory status epilepticus), continuous IV infusion of propofol, midazolam, or pentobarbital with continuous EEG monitoring is required.

questions.stem: Which antiepileptic drug is the most common cause of teratogenic neural tube defects?

choices.feedback: Carbamazepine has some teratogenicity risk (~3-5%) including neural tube defects, but the risk is significantly lower than valproate.

Correct! Valproate has the highest teratogenicity risk among AEDs: 10-15% major malformation rate (vs 2-3% general population). Neural tube defects (spina bifida) are the hallmark — 1-2% risk. Also: cardiac defects, cleft palate, cognitive/behavioral effects (decreased IQ in exposed children). Contraindicated in pregnancy (category X). All women on valproate must use effective contraception. High-dose folic acid partially reduces but does NOT eliminate neural tube defect risk. Safer alternatives: levetiracetam, lamotrigine.
Gabapentin has limited data but no established major teratogenic risk comparable to valproate.
Levetiracetam has the best safety profile in pregnancy among AEDs. It is preferred in women of childbearing age.

questions.explanation: Valproic acid is the most teratogenic commonly used antiepileptic drug. It carries a significant risk of neural tube defects (spina bifida, ~1-2% risk), as well as cardiac malformations, craniofacial abnormalities, and neurodevelopmental delays (lower IQ, increased autism risk). It is classified as pregnancy category X by many guidelines. Women of childbearing potential on valproate should receive high-dose folic acid supplementation and counseling about contraception. If possible, valproate should be switched to a safer alternative (levetiracetam, lamotrigine) before conception. Carbamazepine and phenytoin also carry teratogenic risks but generally less than valproate.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Valproic acid is the most teratogenic commonly used antiepileptic drug. It carries a significant risk of neural tube defects (spina bifida, ~1-2% risk), as well as cardiac malformations, craniofacial abnormalities, and neurodevelopmental delays (lower IQ, increased autism risk). It is classified as pregnancy category X by many guidelines. Women of childbearing potential on valproate should receive high-dose folic acid supplementation and counseling about contraception. If possible, valproate should be switched to a safer alternative (levetiracetam, lamotrigine) before conception. Carbamazepine and phenytoin also carry teratogenic risks but generally less than valproate.

questions.stem: What first-line medication is recommended for absence seizures?

choices.feedback: Phenobarbital is not effective for absence seizures. It is used for neonatal seizures and as a last-line agent for other seizure types.
Phenytoin is effective for focal and generalized tonic-clonic seizures but is INEFFECTIVE for absence seizures and may worsen them.
Topiramate has broad-spectrum activity but ethosuximide remains first-line specifically for absence seizures.

Correct! Ethosuximide: first-line for PURE absence seizures (childhood absence epilepsy). Mechanism: blocks T-type calcium channels in thalamic neurons → inhibits the thalamocortical oscillation circuit that generates 3 Hz spike-and-wave absence seizures. If mixed seizure types (absence + generalized tonic-clonic): valproate or lamotrigine (ethosuximide only covers absence). Avoid: carbamazepine, phenytoin, gabapentin, tiagabine → can WORSEN absence seizures.

questions.explanation: Ethosuximide is the first-line treatment for absence seizures (childhood absence epilepsy). It works by blocking T-type calcium channels in thalamic relay neurons, which are responsible for generating the 3 Hz spike-and-wave discharges characteristic of absence seizures. Absence seizures present as brief (5-30 seconds) episodes of staring with behavioral arrest, often with eyelid fluttering or subtle automatisms, and abrupt return to baseline. EEG shows the pathognomonic 3 Hz generalized spike-and-wave pattern. Valproic acid is an alternative (especially if there are concurrent generalized tonic-clonic seizures), but ethosuximide is preferred for pure absence epilepsy due to fewer side effects.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Ethosuximide is the first-line treatment for absence seizures (childhood absence epilepsy). It works by blocking T-type calcium channels in thalamic relay neurons, which are responsible for generating the 3 Hz spike-and-wave discharges characteristic of absence seizures. Absence seizures present as brief (5-30 seconds) episodes of staring with behavioral arrest, often with eyelid fluttering or subtle automatisms, and abrupt return to baseline. EEG shows the pathognomonic 3 Hz generalized spike-and-wave pattern. Valproic acid is an alternative (especially if there are concurrent generalized tonic-clonic seizures), but ethosuximide is preferred for pure absence epilepsy due to fewer side effects.

questions.stem: A patient with severe TBI has a GCS of 7 and ICP monitor showing 28 mmHg (normal < 20). What is the initial management?

choices.feedback: Corticosteroids are NOT recommended for TBI-associated cerebral edema (CRASH trial showed increased mortality). Steroids help with vasogenic edema from tumors, not TBI.
LP is CONTRAINDICATED with elevated ICP and mass effect — risk of transtentorial herniation. Ventricular drainage (EVD) is used instead.
Surgery is reserved for refractory ICP elevation after medical management fails. Start with medical interventions.

choices.text: Elevate head of bed to 30°, IV mannitol or hypertonic saline, hyperventilation as temporizing measure
choices.feedback: Correct! Elevated ICP management ladder: (1) Head of bed 30° (optimize venous drainage), midline head position, loosen cervical collar. (2) Sedation/analgesia (propofol, fentanyl — reduce metabolic demand). (3) Osmotic therapy: mannitol 20% OR hypertonic saline 23.4% (30 mL via central line). (4) Brief hyperventilation (target pCO2 30-35 mmHg — temporizing only, cerebral vasoconstriction). (5) CSF drainage via EVD. (6) Refractory: pentobarbital coma, decompressive craniectomy. Target: ICP < 22 mmHg, CPP 60-70 mmHg (CPP = MAP − ICP).

questions.explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Elevate head of bed to 30°, IV mannitol or hypertonic saline, hyperventilation as temporizing measure) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

questions.stem: A patient on allopurinol develops painful oral erosions, widespread targetoid skin lesions with epidermal detachment involving 15% BSA. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! SJS: 1-10% BSA epidermal detachment + mucosal involvement (≥2 mucosal sites). SJS/TEN overlap: 10-30% BSA. TEN: >30% BSA. This patient at 15% is SJS/TEN overlap. Nikolsky sign positive (epidermal shearing with lateral pressure). Common drug triggers: allopurinol, sulfonamides, anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine), NSAIDs (piroxicam). Treatment: stop offending drug immediately, burn unit/ICU care, wound care, ophthalmology consult (ocular scarring), IV fluids. Role of IVIG, cyclosporine — debated. Mortality: SJS ~5%, TEN ~30%.
Bullous pemphigoid causes tense blisters (subepidermal) primarily in elderly. Nikolsky sign is negative. Not related to drug exposure in this manner.
EM involves <10% BSA with typical target lesions but minimal mucosal involvement and no epidermal detachment.
Pemphigus vulgaris causes flaccid blisters from intraepidermal acantholysis with + Nikolsky sign but is autoimmune (anti-desmoglein antibodies), not drug-induced, and develops gradually.

questions.stem: Which HLA allele testing should be performed before prescribing carbamazepine to prevent SJS/TEN?

choices.feedback: HLA-DQ2 and DQ8 are associated with celiac disease, not drug-induced SJS/TEN.

choices.text: HLA-B*1502 in patients of Southeast Asian descent
choices.feedback: Correct! HLA-B*1502 allele: strongly associated with carbamazepine-induced SJS/TEN in Southeast Asian populations (Han Chinese, Thai, Malaysian, Filipino, Indian). Boxed warning: test before starting carbamazepine in at-risk populations. If positive → do not prescribe. Prevalence: 5-15% in Southeast Asians, very rare in Europeans. Also: HLA-A*3101 associated with carbamazepine hypersensitivity in Europeans/Japanese. HLA-B*5801 → allopurinol hypersensitivity (SJS/DRESS) in Korean, Thai, African American populations.
HLA-DR4 is associated with rheumatoid arthritis susceptibility, not drug hypersensitivity.
HLA-B*5701 testing is for abacavir hypersensitivity, not carbamazepine.

questions.stem: A patient develops a rapidly expanding area of painful erythema with a well-defined border on the face. They have fever and leukocytosis. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Erysipelas: superficial cellulitis (upper dermis + lymphatics) caused primarily by Group A Streptococcus. Classic: sharply demarcated, raised, erythematous, painful plaque with peau d'orange texture. Commonly involves face (butterfly distribution) or lower extremities. Systemic symptoms: fever, chills, leukocytosis. Differs from deeper cellulitis (poorly defined borders, no raised edge). Treatment: penicillin or amoxicillin (covers GAS). If deeper cellulitis suspected: add MRSA coverage if indicated.
Contact dermatitis is pruritic, not painful, and does not cause fever or leukocytosis.
Zoster follows a dermatomal distribution with vesicular lesions, not a uniformly erythematous plaque with raised borders.
Angioedema causes painless subcutaneous swelling without erythema and without fever/leukocytosis.

questions.stem: A patient's PFTs show FEV1/FVC ratio of 55%, reduced FEV1, and increased TLC. What pattern is this?

choices.feedback: Mixed pattern shows reduced FEV1/FVC AND reduced TLC. This patient has increased TLC, consistent with pure obstruction with hyperinflation.
FEV1/FVC of 55% is clearly abnormal (normal ≥70%). This is not a normal pattern.
Restrictive disease shows REDUCED TLC with normal or increased FEV1/FVC ratio.

Correct! Obstructive pattern: reduced FEV1/FVC ratio (<70% or below LLN) = hallmark. Reduced FEV1 (airflow limitation). Increased TLC = air trapping/hyperinflation (seen in emphysema). Increased RV (residual volume). Common causes: COPD, asthma, bronchiectasis. Severity grading by FEV1 (% predicted): mild >80%, moderate 50-80%, severe 30-50%, very severe <30%. Reversibility testing: >12% and >200 mL FEV1 improvement post-bronchodilator suggests asthma.

questions.explanation: Pulmonary diagnosis relies on systematic interpretation of PFTs, imaging, and arterial blood gases. Evidence-based management follows society guidelines (GINA for asthma, GOLD for COPD, ATS/IDSA for pneumonia).

questions.stem: A patient's PFTs show normal FEV1/FVC ratio with reduced FEV1, FVC, and TLC. What pattern is this?

choices.feedback: Upper airway obstruction shows characteristic flow-volume loop patterns (flattened inspiratory and/or expiratory limbs) rather than a low TLC.
Poor effort can reduce FVC but TLC would typically be normal with plethysmography. A truly reduced TLC indicates restriction.
Obstruction requires a REDUCED FEV1/FVC ratio. This patient's ratio is normal.

Correct! Restrictive pattern: normal or increased FEV1/FVC ratio (both FEV1 and FVC decrease proportionally → ratio preserved), reduced TLC (the defining criterion). Causes: Parenchymal (pulmonary fibrosis, sarcoidosis, asbestosis), Extraparenchymal (neuromuscular disease, chest wall deformity, obesity, pleural disease). DLCO helps differentiate: reduced in parenchymal disease (thickened alveolar membrane), normal in extraparenchymal (normal gas exchange surface). TLC measurement requires body plethysmography or helium dilution.

questions.explanation: Pulmonary diagnosis relies on systematic interpretation of PFTs, imaging, and arterial blood gases. Evidence-based management follows society guidelines (GINA for asthma, GOLD for COPD, ATS/IDSA for pneumonia).

questions.stem: What does a reduced DLCO (diffusion capacity) indicate?

choices.feedback: Airway obstruction is measured by FEV1/FVC ratio and flow rates. DLCO measures gas exchange at the alveolar-capillary membrane.
Upper airway obstruction affects large airway flow patterns but does not reduce DLCO.
Respiratory muscle weakness affects lung volumes (reduced TLC, FVC) but DLCO corrected for volume is typically normal — the alveolar-capillary membrane itself is intact.

choices.text: Impaired gas exchange across the alveolar-capillary membrane
choices.feedback: Correct! DLCO measures CO transfer from alveoli to hemoglobin. Reduced in: emphysema (destroyed alveolar surface area), pulmonary fibrosis (thickened alveolar membrane), pulmonary vascular disease (reduced capillary blood volume), anemia (fewer Hgb molecules to bind CO). Increased in: polycythemia, pulmonary hemorrhage (intra-alveolar blood), left-to-right shunts, asthma (may be normal or increased). DLCO helps distinguish emphysema (reduced) from chronic bronchitis (normal) in COPD.

questions.explanation: Pulmonary diagnosis relies on systematic interpretation of PFTs, imaging, and arterial blood gases. Evidence-based management follows society guidelines (GINA for asthma, GOLD for COPD, ATS/IDSA for pneumonia).

questions.stem: A newborn screening test is positive for elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone. What condition is being screened for?

choices.feedback: PKU screening measures phenylalanine levels, not 17-hydroxyprogesterone.
Congenital hypothyroidism screening measures TSH and/or T4, not 17-hydroxyprogesterone.

Correct! Elevated 17-OHP on newborn screen suggests 21-hydroxylase deficiency (95% of CAH). 21-hydroxylase deficiency → cannot convert 17-OHP to 11-deoxycortisol → cortisol deficiency + aldosterone deficiency + androgen excess (shunted precursors). Presentation: salt-wasting crisis (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, shock in first 2 weeks of life), ambiguous genitalia in females (virilization from excess androgens). Treatment: hydrocortisone replacement + fludrocortisone (salt-wasting form). Early detection prevents life-threatening adrenal crisis.
Galactosemia screening measures GALT enzyme activity or galactose levels, not 17-hydroxyprogesterone.

questions.stem: Which newborn screening condition requires immediate dietary restriction to prevent intellectual disability?

choices.feedback: CF requires enzyme replacement, airway clearance, and nutritional support but not specific dietary amino acid restriction.
SCD is managed with penicillin prophylaxis and monitoring, not dietary restriction.
Congenital hypothyroidism requires levothyroxine replacement, not dietary restriction.

Correct! PKU (phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency): cannot convert phenylalanine → tyrosine → accumulation of phenylalanine → neurotoxicity → intellectual disability if untreated. Requires immediate lifelong phenylalanine-restricted diet (special medical formulas). If started in first weeks of life → normal cognitive development. Delayed treatment → irreversible brain damage. Also detected: maple syrup urine disease (leucine restriction), galactosemia (galactose-free diet — stop breastfeeding/regular formula immediately).

questions.stem: A 2-year-old presents with barking cough, inspiratory stridor, and hoarseness after a URI. What is the diagnosis and initial treatment?

choices.feedback: Epiglottitis presents with drooling, tripod positioning, toxic appearance, and NO barking cough. Croup has barking cough and stridor.

Correct! Croup: parainfluenza virus (75%) → subglottic inflammation → barking/seal-like cough, inspiratory stridor, hoarseness. Age: 6 months to 3 years. Steeple sign on AP neck X-ray (subglottic narrowing). Treatment: mild (barking cough, no stridor at rest) → single dose dexamethasone/IM. Moderate-severe (stridor at rest, retractions) → nebulized racemic epinephrine + dexamethasone. Observe 2-4 hours post-epinephrine for rebound. Humidified air: no proven benefit.
Foreign body causes sudden-onset stridor/cough without URI prodrome. This patient had antecedent URI symptoms.
Bacterial tracheitis presents with high fever, toxic appearance, and mucopurulent secretions after a viral prodrome. This presentation is classic croup.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis) — dexamethasone and nebulized epinephrine if severe.

questions.stem: A child presents with high fever, drooling, tripod positioning, and muffled voice. They appear toxic. What is the diagnosis and what should you AVOID doing?

choices.feedback: Retropharyngeal abscess causes neck stiffness and may present similarly but tripod positioning with drooling in a toxic child is most concerning for epiglottitis.
This is not croup (no barking cough). The toxic appearance and drooling suggest a different diagnosis.

choices.text: Epiglottitis — avoid examining the throat or causing agitation
choices.feedback: Correct! Epiglottitis: H. influenzae type b (now rare with Hib vaccine), S. pyogenes, S. aureus → supraglottic inflammation. Classic: 4 D's — Drooling, Dysphagia, Dysphonia (muffled/hot potato voice), Distress. Thumb sign on lateral neck X-ray (swollen epiglottis). CRITICAL: do NOT examine the throat, do NOT lay the child down, do NOT cause agitation → any of these can trigger complete airway obstruction. Management: keep child calm, prepare for emergent airway in OR (controlled intubation by ENT/anesthesia), IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone).

choices.text: Peritonsillar abscess — incision and drainage at bedside
choices.feedback: Peritonsillar abscess causes unilateral tonsillar swelling and uvular deviation in an older child/adult. The tripod positioning and drooling pattern is classic for epiglottitis.

questions.stem: A diabetic patient presents with a deep plantar ulcer with exposed bone, purulent drainage, and surrounding erythema extending 4 cm. What classification and empiric antibiotic approach is most appropriate?

choices.feedback: Amputation is reserved for limb-threatening or life-threatening infections that fail medical/surgical management. Initial treatment is antibiotics + surgical debridement.

Correct! IDSA DFI classification: Mild (superficial, <2cm cellulitis), Moderate (deeper structures, >2cm, no SIRS), Severe (SIRS/sepsis, limb-threatening). This is moderate-severe with exposed bone (probe-to-bone test positive → osteomyelitis probability ~90%). Empiric: vancomycin (MRSA) + piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem (gram-negatives + anaerobes). Obtain deep wound culture (NOT superficial swab), MRI for osteomyelitis, vascular assessment.
Ciprofloxacin alone has no MRSA or anaerobic coverage. DFIs are typically polymicrobial and require broader coverage.
Exposed bone and purulent drainage with >2 cm erythema constitutes moderate-to-severe DFI, not mild.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Moderate-severe DFI — broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering MRSA, gram-negatives, and anaerobes.

questions.stem: What does a positive probe-to-bone test indicate in a diabetic foot ulcer?

choices.text: The patient needs emergent vascular surgery
choices.feedback: Probe-to-bone tests for osteomyelitis, not vascular insufficiency. Vascular assessment uses ABI, TcPO2, or angiography.

Correct! The probe-to-bone test: inserting a sterile metal probe through the ulcer — if it contacts hard, gritty bone, osteomyelitis is highly likely (PPV ~89% in high-risk DFI). Confirm with MRI (best imaging for DFI osteomyelitis — 90% sensitivity/specificity). Bone biopsy with culture is the gold standard for pathogen identification. Treatment: prolonged antibiotics (4-6 weeks) ± surgical debridement of infected bone.

choices.text: Wound culture results will be unreliable
choices.feedback: Probe-to-bone positive means osteomyelitis is likely. Deep tissue/bone cultures are actually especially important in this setting.

choices.text: The ulcer has extended to the subcutaneous fat
choices.feedback: Probe-to-bone goes deeper than subcutaneous fat — it tests for bone involvement.

questions.explanation: Correct: High likelihood of underlying osteomyelitis (~90% positive predictive value).

questions.stem: Which organisms are most commonly found in chronic, previously treated diabetic foot infections?

choices.feedback: Fungal infections are uncommon in DFIs unless severely immunocompromised. Polymicrobial bacterial infections are the norm.
TB osteomyelitis is rare in diabetic feet. Polymicrobial bacterial infections are overwhelmingly more common.

Correct! Chronic/previously treated DFIs are typically polymicrobial: gram-positives (MRSA, Enterococcus), gram-negatives (Pseudomonas, E. coli, Proteus, Klebsiella), and anaerobes (Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus). Acute, mild, treatment-naïve DFIs often have simpler microbiology (Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus). Always obtain DEEP tissue cultures (not surface swabs) to guide targeted therapy.
GAS is more common in acute infections. Chronic DFIs are polymicrobial.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Polymicrobial: MRSA, gram-negatives (Pseudomonas, Enterobacteriaceae), and anaerobes.

questions.stem: How long should antibiotics be administered for diabetic foot osteomyelitis when the infected bone is NOT surgically resected?

choices.feedback: Correct! DFI osteomyelitis without surgical resection: 4-6 weeks of culture-directed antibiotics. If infected bone is surgically resected with clean margins: 2-5 days of post-operative antibiotics may suffice (treat as soft tissue infection). Monitoring: clinical improvement, normalization of ESR/CRP. MRI may show persistent marrow signal changes even after successful treatment, so clinical response is key.
6-12 months exceeds standard guidelines. 4-6 weeks is the recommended duration for non-resected osteomyelitis.
Wound healing may take months and is not the endpoint for antibiotic duration. 4-6 weeks is standard for osteomyelitis.
7-10 days is appropriate for soft tissue DFI only, not osteomyelitis.

questions.explanation: Osteomyelitis: S. aureus is the most common cause in all age groups. Hematogenous spread most common in children (metaphysis of long bones). Diagnosis: MRI (most sensitive imaging), blood cultures, bone biopsy for definitive culture. Treatment: 4-6 weeks of IV antibiotics targeted to culture (empiric: nafcillin/oxacillin or vancomycin if MRSA risk). Surgical debridement for chronic or complicated cases.

questions.stem: What vascular assessment is most important in managing diabetic foot infections?

choices.feedback: Correct! Peripheral arterial disease (PAD) coexists in 50%+ of DFI patients and critically impairs healing and antibiotic delivery. ABI < 0.9 suggests PAD; ABI > 1.3 may be falsely elevated (medial arterial calcification — common in diabetes). Toe pressures (TBI) and transcutaneous oxygen (TcPO2 > 40 mmHg needed for healing) are more reliable in diabetics. Revascularization may be necessary before infection can clear.
Aortic imaging is not the first step. Peripheral vascular assessment of the affected extremity is the priority.
Cardiac output assessment is not the first-line vascular assessment for DFI. Peripheral arterial disease assessment is key.

choices.text: Venous duplex of the lower extremity
choices.feedback: Venous disease (DVT, insufficiency) is a separate issue. DFI vascular assessment focuses on arterial perfusion to the foot.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Ankle-brachial index (ABI) and assessment of pedal pulses to evaluate peripheral arterial disease.

questions.stem: At what CD4 count should antiretroviral therapy (ART) be initiated in a newly diagnosed HIV patient?

choices.feedback: Waiting for symptomatic AIDS to start ART dramatically increases morbidity and mortality. Treat immediately.

choices.text: Immediately upon diagnosis, regardless of CD4 count
choices.feedback: Correct! Current guidelines (DHHS, WHO): start ART immediately upon HIV diagnosis regardless of CD4 count. Rapid ART initiation (ideally within 7 days, same-day if possible) improves outcomes, reduces transmission, and achieves viral suppression faster. Exceptions: evaluate for OIs first in very low CD4 to avoid immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS), especially TB and cryptococcal meningitis — initiate OI treatment first, then start ART within 2-8 weeks.
This older threshold led to worse outcomes. The evidence now clearly supports immediate ART initiation (START trial, TEMPRANO trial).
This was an older guideline threshold. Current recommendations are more aggressive.

questions.explanation: Current guidelines (DHHS, WHO, IAS-USA) recommend initiating ART immediately upon HIV diagnosis regardless of CD4 count. The START trial (2015) and TEMPRANO trial demonstrated that early ART reduces morbidity and mortality even in patients with high CD4 counts (>500). Early ART also reduces HIV transmission (Treatment as Prevention — the HPTN 052 trial showed 96% reduction in transmission). Same-day ART initiation (rapid start) is now recommended in many guidelines to improve linkage to care and outcomes.

questions.stem: What is the preferred initial ART regimen for most treatment-naïve HIV patients?

choices.text: Two NRTIs + an integrase strand transfer inhibitor (INSTI)
choices.feedback: Correct! Current preferred regimens (DHHS 2024): bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir alafenamide (Biktarvy) — most commonly prescribed single-tablet regimen. Alternative: dolutegravir + emtricitabine/TAF. INSTIs: high barrier to resistance (bictegravir, dolutegravir), excellent tolerability, rapid viral suppression. Two-drug options: dolutegravir/lamivudine (Dovato) for appropriate patients.
Triple-NRTI regimens are inferior to INSTI-based regimens and are no longer recommended as first-line.
This older regimen has more side effects. INSTI-based regimens are now preferred.
PI-based regimens are alternative but not preferred first-line due to more drug interactions and metabolic side effects compared to INSTIs.

questions.explanation: The preferred initial ART regimen for most treatment-naïve HIV patients is two NRTIs (backbone, typically tenofovir + emtricitabine [TAF/FTC or TDF/FTC]) plus an integrase strand transfer inhibitor (INSTI — dolutegravir or bictegravir). INSTIs are preferred over PIs and NNRTIs due to higher potency, better tolerability, fewer drug interactions, and higher barrier to resistance (dolutegravir/bictegravir). Biktarvy (bictegravir/TAF/FTC) is a commonly prescribed single-tablet regimen. Dolutegravir-based regimens are preferred by WHO for global use.
questions.citation: HIV Disease. StatPearls. [Open Access] | DHHS ART Guidelines. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A patient with HIV and CD4 of 80 starts ART. Two weeks later, they develop fever, worsening lymphadenopathy, and new pulmonary infiltrates despite an improving viral load. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Drug toxicity would not explain worsening lymphadenopathy with improving viral load. The improving VL confirms ART is working.
While HIV-associated lymphoma is a concern, the temporal relationship (2 weeks after ART initiation with improving VL) strongly suggests IRIS.

Correct! IRIS occurs when recovering immune system mounts an exaggerated inflammatory response to previously occult or treated opportunistic infections. Risk factors: low baseline CD4 (<50), high viral load, rapid immune recovery, active OI at ART initiation. Two types: paradoxical (worsening of known OI despite treatment) and unmasking (new OI symptoms as immune system recovers). Management: continue ART + treat underlying OI + NSAIDs/steroids for severe inflammation.

choices.text: New opportunistic infection from continued immunosuppression
choices.feedback: The improving viral load indicates ART is effective. New OI from worsening immunosuppression is unlikely. This clinical picture is classic for IRIS.

questions.explanation: Fever, worsening lymphadenopathy, and new pulmonary infiltrates 2 weeks after starting ART in a patient with CD4 of 80 is IRIS. The reconstituting immune system is mounting an inflammatory response against opportunistic pathogens (TB, MAC, CMV, Cryptococcus, or PML-causing JC virus). Paradoxical IRIS involves worsening of a known treated infection; unmasking IRIS reveals a previously undiagnosed infection. Risk factors: low baseline CD4, high viral load, early ART initiation relative to OI treatment. Management: continue ART and OI treatment; NSAIDs or corticosteroids for severe inflammation.

questions.stem: Which opportunistic infection prophylaxis thresholds must be memorized for HIV patients?

choices.feedback: Different OIs have different CD4 thresholds. There is no single threshold for all prophylaxis.

choices.text: PCP at CD4 < 200, Toxo at CD4 < 100 (if IgG+), MAC at CD4 < 50
choices.feedback: Correct! Key OI prophylaxis thresholds: CD4 < 200: PCP → TMP-SMX. CD4 < 100: Toxoplasma (if Toxo IgG+) → TMP-SMX (same drug covers both!). CD4 < 50: MAC → azithromycin. Discontinue prophylaxis when CD4 > threshold for ≥ 3 months on ART. Other considerations: Cryptococcus screening (CrAg) at CD4 < 100 in endemic areas. TB screening at all CD4 counts.

choices.text: PCP at CD4 < 500, MAC at CD4 < 200, Toxo at CD4 < 100
choices.feedback: These thresholds are incorrect. PCP prophylaxis starts at CD4 < 200, not 500.

choices.text: PCP at CD4 < 100, MAC at CD4 < 200, Toxo at CD4 < 500
choices.feedback: These thresholds are reversed. PCP is < 200, MAC is < 50, Toxo is < 100.

questions.explanation: Critical OI prophylaxis thresholds in HIV: (1) Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP) prophylaxis with TMP-SMX when CD4 <200 cells/μL (or CD4 <14%, or history of oral candidiasis); (2) Toxoplasma gondii prophylaxis with TMP-SMX when CD4 <100 and positive Toxoplasma IgG; (3) Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) prophylaxis with azithromycin when CD4 <50 (after ruling out active MAC). Prophylaxis can be discontinued when CD4 recovers above these thresholds for ≥3-6 months on ART. These thresholds reflect the immune surveillance required to prevent each specific pathogen.

questions.stem: What is the goal of ART in terms of viral load?

choices.feedback: VL < 1,000 is below the WHO threshold for treatment failure but above the target. Full suppression to < 50 is the standard-of-care goal.
This is an inadequate response. The goal is much lower.

choices.text: Achieve and maintain undetectable viral load
choices.feedback: Correct! ART goal: viral suppression to < 50 copies/mL (undetectable). Most patients achieve this within 8-24 weeks of ART initiation. U=U (Undetectable = Untransmittable): patients with sustained undetectable VL have effectively zero risk of sexual transmission (PARTNER 1 & 2 studies, HPTN 052). Monitor VL every 3-4 months initially, then every 6 months once stable. Persistent viremia > 200 copies/mL = virologic failure → assess adherence, resistance testing.

choices.text: Eliminate HIV from the body entirely
choices.feedback: ART suppresses viral replication but does NOT eliminate HIV. Latent viral reservoirs persist in resting CD4 cells. ART is lifelong therapy. Cure research is ongoing but not yet achieved clinically.

questions.explanation: The goal of ART is to achieve and maintain an undetectable viral load (<50 copies/mL by current assays), which indicates virologic suppression. Undetectable viral load has three critical implications: (1) halts immune decline and allows CD4 recovery, (2) prevents development of drug resistance, and (3) eliminates sexual transmission of HIV (U=U: Undetectable = Untransmittable, supported by PARTNER and HPTN 052 studies). Viral load is the primary marker for ART response, monitored 4-8 weeks after initiation and periodically thereafter.

questions.stem: What is the gold standard test for diagnosing active pulmonary tuberculosis?

choices.feedback: IGRA (like QuantiFERON) detects immune sensitization to TB antigens but, like TST, cannot distinguish active from latent TB.
TST detects latent TB exposure/immunity but cannot distinguish active from latent TB. A positive TST does NOT diagnose active disease.

Correct! Active TB diagnosis requires: (1) Sputum AFB smear × 3 (morning specimens, 8+ hours apart) — provides rapid results but only 50-80% sensitive. (2) Mycobacterial culture (Löwenstein-Jensen or liquid BACTEC) — gold standard, 80-85% sensitive, provides drug susceptibility testing. Takes 2-6 weeks. (3) GeneXpert MTB/RIF (nucleic acid amplification) — rapid (<2 hours), detects both TB AND rifampin resistance. 98% specific, ~90% sensitive for smear-positive cases.
CXR is suggestive (upper lobe cavitary lesions) but not diagnostic. Many conditions mimic TB radiographically. Microbiologic confirmation is required.

questions.explanation: The gold standard for diagnosing active pulmonary TB is sputum mycobacterial CULTURE (not AFB smear). Culture on solid media (Löwenstein-Jensen) or liquid media (BACTEC MGIT 960) has much higher sensitivity (80-85%) than AFB smear (50-80%) and provides drug susceptibility testing. AFB smear is the initial rapid test (results in hours) but has limited sensitivity, especially in HIV-coinfected and paucibacillary disease. GeneXpert MTB/RIF provides rapid molecular diagnosis with simultaneous rifampin resistance detection. Culture remains definitive but takes 2-8 weeks.
questions.auditNote: CORRECTED: Answer changed from AFB smear to mycobacterial culture (culture is the true gold standard)

questions.stem: What is the treatment for latent tuberculosis infection (LTBI)?

choices.feedback: Ethambutol monotherapy is not used for LTBI. It is only part of the combination RIPE regimen for active TB.
RIPE is the regimen for ACTIVE TB. LTBI requires shorter, simpler regimens.

choices.text: No treatment needed — just monitor with serial CXRs
choices.feedback: Untreated LTBI carries a 5-10% lifetime risk of reactivation (much higher in immunocompromised). Treatment significantly reduces this risk.

choices.text: Isoniazid × 9 months, or rifampin × 4 months, or isoniazid + rifapentine weekly × 12 doses (3HP regimen)
choices.feedback: Correct! LTBI regimens: Preferred: 3HP (isoniazid + rifapentine weekly × 12 doses — highest completion rate, DOT possible), 4R (rifampin daily × 4 months — shorter, better tolerated). Alternative: 9H (isoniazid daily × 9 months — oldest regimen, lowest completion rate). All supplemented with pyridoxine for INH-containing regimens. Always rule out active TB before starting LTBI treatment (CXR, symptoms).

questions.explanation: LTBI treatment options include: (1) Isoniazid daily × 9 months (traditional standard), (2) Rifampin daily × 4 months (shorter, no pyrazinamide hepatotoxicity risk), (3) Isoniazid + rifapentine (3HP regimen) weekly × 12 doses/3 months (directly observed, excellent completion rates), and (4) Isoniazid daily × 6 months (acceptable alternative). The 3HP and 4R regimens are increasingly preferred due to shorter duration and better completion rates. Active TB must be excluded before starting LTBI treatment (chest X-ray, symptom screen). Pyridoxine supplementation accompanies isoniazid-containing regimens.

questions.stem: Which TB diagnostic test can simultaneously detect Mycobacterium tuberculosis AND rifampin resistance within 2 hours?

choices.feedback: Correct! GeneXpert MTB/RIF: automated nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT). Detects TB-specific DNA AND mutations in rpoB gene (encodes rifampin target — RNA polymerase). Rifampin resistance is a surrogate marker for MDR-TB (>95% of rifampin-resistant TB is also INH-resistant). Sensitivity: ~98% for smear-positive, ~68% for smear-negative pulmonary TB. WHO recommended as initial diagnostic test in TB-endemic settings. Results in <2 hours.
QuantiFERON is an IGRA for detecting TB exposure/latent infection. It cannot diagnose active TB or detect drug resistance.
Culture provides definitive susceptibility results but requires 2-6 weeks. GeneXpert provides rapid rifampin resistance detection in <2 hours.
AFB smear detects acid-fast organisms but cannot identify species or resistance patterns.

questions.explanation: GeneXpert MTB/RIF (Xpert) is a nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) that simultaneously detects Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex DNA and rifampin resistance mutations (rpoB gene) from sputum within approximately 2 hours. It has sensitivity of ~88% for smear-positive and ~67% for smear-negative pulmonary TB, with >95% specificity. WHO recommends GeneXpert as the initial diagnostic test for TB in settings with high HIV or MDR-TB prevalence. Rifampin resistance serves as a surrogate marker for MDR-TB since isolated rifampin resistance is rare.

questions.stem: What defines multidrug-resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB)?

choices.text: Resistance to at least isoniazid AND rifampin
choices.feedback: Correct! MDR-TB: resistant to at least isoniazid + rifampin (the two most potent first-line drugs). Treatment: individualized regimen based on susceptibility testing, typically includes a fluoroquinolone (moxifloxacin/levofloxacin), an injectable (amikacin), plus 2-3 additional agents (linezolid, bedaquiline, clofazimine, cycloserine) × 18-20 months minimum. XDR-TB: MDR + resistance to any fluoroquinolone + at least one injectable → extremely difficult to treat. Bedaquiline and linezolid have improved XDR-TB outcomes.

choices.text: Resistance to any single first-line anti-TB drug
choices.feedback: Single-drug resistance does not constitute MDR-TB. The definition requires resistance to specific drugs.

choices.text: Failure to respond to 2 weeks of standard therapy
choices.feedback: Treatment response time is not the definition. MDR-TB is defined by laboratory drug susceptibility testing.

choices.text: Resistance to all four RIPE drugs
choices.feedback: Resistance to all RIPE drugs is beyond MDR-TB. MDR requires only isoniazid + rifampin resistance.

questions.explanation: Multidrug-resistant TB (MDR-TB) is defined by resistance to at least both isoniazid AND rifampin, the two most potent first-line antitubercular drugs. MDR-TB requires prolonged treatment (18-20 months) with second-line agents (fluoroquinolones, injectable aminoglycosides, linezolid, bedaquiline, cycloserine). Extensively drug-resistant TB (XDR-TB) is MDR-TB with additional resistance to any fluoroquinolone and at least one injectable agent. Treatment success rates for MDR-TB (~55-80%) are significantly lower than drug-susceptible TB (~85-95%).

questions.stem: In which clinical scenario is TB most likely to present with atypical CXR findings (non-cavitary, lower lobe infiltrates, adenopathy)?

choices.feedback: Healthcare workers who develop active TB typically present with standard reactivation patterns unless immunocompromised.

Correct! HIV + TB: low CD4 → impaired granuloma formation → atypical CXR: lower lobe infiltrates, miliary pattern, isolated adenopathy, pleural effusions, or even NORMAL CXR in 10-20% of cases. Classic upper lobe cavitary disease requires robust cell-mediated immunity. Lower CD4 → less typical presentation → higher index of suspicion needed. Always test for TB in HIV patients with any pulmonary symptoms. Sputum AFB may also be smear-negative more frequently.

choices.text: Immunocompetent young adult with primary TB
choices.feedback: Primary TB in immunocompetent adults typically shows middle/lower lobe infiltrates with hilar adenopathy, but this is actually the EXPECTED primary TB presentation. Atypical presentations are more notable in a different population.
Reactivation TB in elderly immunocompetent patients typically shows classic upper lobe cavitary disease, not atypical presentations.

questions.explanation: HIV-coinfected patients with advanced immunosuppression (CD4 <200) are most likely to present with atypical TB findings on CXR: non-cavitary infiltrates, lower lobe disease, mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy, pleural effusions, miliary pattern, or even normal CXR. Classic upper lobe cavitary disease requires intact cell-mediated immunity to form granulomas and cavities — severely immunosuppressed patients cannot mount this response. This makes TB diagnosis more challenging in HIV patients, requiring higher clinical suspicion and more liberal use of molecular diagnostics (GeneXpert) and cultures.
questions.citation: Tuberculosis. StatPearls. [Open Access] | HIV Disease. StatPearls. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What is the most common causative organism of non-purulent cellulitis?

choices.feedback: S. aureus is more commonly associated with PURULENT skin infections (abscesses, furuncles). Non-purulent cellulitis has a different predominant pathogen.
Pseudomonas cellulitis is uncommon except in specific scenarios: puncture wounds through shoes (plantar), hot tub folliculitis, or immunocompromised.

Correct! Non-purulent cellulitis (no abscess/drainage): predominantly beta-hemolytic streptococci (Group A > B, C, G). Treatment: beta-lactam antibiotics (cephalexin, dicloxacillin, ceftriaxone). MRSA coverage NOT needed unless purulence present. Purulent cellulitis/abscess: S. aureus (often MRSA in community-acquired) → I&D + TMP-SMX, doxycycline, or clindamycin. This purulent vs non-purulent distinction drives antibiotic selection.
E. coli cellulitis is rare. Streptococci are the predominant non-purulent cellulitis pathogens.

questions.explanation: Correct: Beta-hemolytic streptococci (Group A Strep).

questions.stem: What clinical features should prompt concern for necrotizing fasciitis rather than simple cellulitis?

choices.text: Pain out of proportion to exam, crepitus, rapid spread, systemic toxicity, and skin necrosis
choices.feedback: Correct! Red flags for necrotizing fasciitis: pain DISPROPORTIONATE to visible skin changes (most important early sign), rapid progression despite antibiotics, crepitus (gas in tissues — especially Clostridium), skin necrosis/bullae/ecchymosis, systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, hypotension). LRINEC score ≥ 6 suggests necrotizing fasciitis. Emergency surgical exploration is both diagnostic AND therapeutic — do NOT delay for imaging. Treatment: emergent wide surgical debridement + broad-spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin + pip-tazo + clindamycin).
Necrotizing fasciitis progresses rapidly (hours to days), not slowly. Slow progression suggests other diagnoses.
