Well-demarcated borders with mild tenderness is more typical of erysipelas or simple cellulitis. Necrotizing fasciitis has different features.
Pruritus and scaling suggest tinea (fungal infection) or eczema, not necrotizing fasciitis.

questions.stem: What is the antibiotic regimen for suspected necrotizing fasciitis?

choices.feedback: Metronidazole covers anaerobes only. Necrotizing fasciitis requires broad coverage for gram-positives, gram-negatives, and anaerobes, plus surgery.
While this covers gram-positives and gram-negatives, it lacks clindamycin's crucial anti-toxin effect and has no anaerobic coverage from aztreonam.

Correct! Triple antibiotic coverage: Vancomycin (MRSA), piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem (gram-negatives + anaerobes), Clindamycin (anti-toxin effect — suppresses toxin production by GAS and S. aureus). SURGERY is the definitive treatment — antibiotics alone are insufficient. Repeat debridement (planned second-look) at 24-48 hours is standard. Mortality: 20-40% even with treatment; higher with delayed surgery.
Necrotizing fasciitis is a surgical emergency requiring IV antibiotics. Oral cephalexin is inadequate.

questions.explanation: Key detail: tazobactam + clindamycin, PLUS emergent surgical debridement.

questions.stem: A patient with recurrent lower extremity cellulitis has chronic lymphedema. What is the most effective strategy to reduce recurrences?

choices.feedback: Topical mupirocin is used for nasal MRSA decolonization and impetigo, not for prevention of recurrent cellulitis.

Correct! Recurrent cellulitis (≥3-4 episodes/year): prophylactic antibiotics (penicillin VK 250- or erythromycin if allergic) reduce recurrence by ~50%. PLUS addressing predisposing factors: lymphedema management (compression stockings, elevation, pneumatic pumps), tinea pedis treatment (portal of entry), skin hydration/care, weight management. Interdigital tinea pedis is a common unrecognized entry point — always examine between toes.
Skin excision is not standard for recurrent cellulitis. Prophylactic antibiotics and lymphedema management are the evidence-based approaches.
IV vancomycin prophylaxis is excessive, impractical, and promotes resistance. Oral penicillin prophylaxis is effective and practical.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Prophylactic oral penicillin and lymphedema management (compression, elevation, skin care).

questions.stem: What is the key clinical distinction between cellulitis and erysipelas?

choices.text: Erysipelas has sharply demarcated, raised borders and involves the superficial dermis/lymphatics
choices.feedback: Correct! Erysipelas: sharply demarcated, raised (indurated), bright red borders, involves upper dermis and superficial lymphatics. Almost exclusively streptococcal. Classically on the face or lower extremities. Cellulitis: deeper (deep dermis/subcutaneous), borders are INDISTINCT and flat, may involve strep or staph. Both treated with beta-lactams; erysipelas may respond to penicillin alone. The sharp border of erysipelas reflects lymphatic involvement creating a visible boundary.

choices.text: Cellulitis always has pus; erysipelas never does
choices.feedback: Purulence depends on pathogen, not the type of infection. Non-purulent cellulitis and erysipelas both typically lack pus.

choices.text: Erysipelas is caused by viruses; cellulitis by bacteria
choices.feedback: Both are bacterial skin infections. Erysipelas is caused by beta-hemolytic streptococci affecting the upper dermis/lymphatics.

choices.text: They are the same condition with different names
choices.feedback: They are distinct entities with different depth of involvement, border characteristics, and slightly different microbiology.

questions.stem: A patient presents with sudden painless vision loss in one eye. Fundoscopy shows a pale retina with a cherry-red spot at the macula. What is the diagnosis and how quickly must treatment be initiated?

choices.feedback: Retinal detachment presents with flashes, floaters, and a curtain-like visual field loss. Pale retina with cherry-red spot is vascular.

choices.text: Acute angle closure glaucoma — start pilocarpine
choices.feedback: Acute angle closure presents with painful red eye, halos, mid-dilated fixed pupil, and elevated IOP. Not painless vision loss with pale retina.
Vitreous hemorrhage causes red haze/loss of red reflex but not a pale retina with cherry-red spot.

Correct! CRAO: embolic occlusion of the central retinal artery → retinal ischemia. Pale/edematous retina + cherry-red spot (fovea appears red because it's thin enough to see underlying choroid). Irreversible damage occurs within 90-120 minutes (same as stroke). Emergency treatment: ocular massage, anterior chamber paracentesis to lower IOP, hypercarbia (rebreathe CO2), intra-arterial thrombolytics (if within window). Work up as stroke equivalent: carotid imaging, echocardiography, cardiac monitoring.

questions.explanation: Key detail: treat within 90-120 minutes.

questions.stem: A patient with acute angle-closure glaucoma presents with a painful red eye, mid-dilated fixed pupil, and IOP of 55 mmHg. What is the definitive treatment?

choices.feedback: Enucleation is removal of the eye — never indicated for acute angle closure. LPI is the definitive treatment.
This is not an infection. Acute angle closure requires urgent IOP-lowering treatment.
Antibiotics are not part of acute angle closure management. This is a mechanical/fluid dynamics problem requiring IOP reduction and LPI.

choices.text: Laser peripheral iridotomy after IOP reduction with topical and systemic agents
choices.feedback: Correct! Acute angle closure: Emergency IOP reduction: topical timolol + pilocarpine + alpha-agonist + topical steroid, acetazolamide IV/PO, IV mannitol if refractory. Definitive: laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI) — creates a hole in the peripheral iris to restore aqueous flow from posterior to anterior chamber. Treat BOTH eyes (contralateral prophylactic LPI). Medications alone are temporizing — LPI is curative. Normal IOP: 10-21 mmHg; acute closure: 40-80+ mmHg.

questions.stem: A welder presents with severe eye pain, tearing, and photophobia 6-12 hours after welding without eye protection. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Endophthalmitis is an intraocular infection typically after surgery or penetrating trauma, not UV exposure.
Chemical burns cause immediate symptoms, not delayed onset. The 6-12 hour delay after UV exposure is characteristic of a different diagnosis.

choices.text: UV keratitis (photokeratitis) — corneal epithelial damage from ultraviolet exposure
choices.feedback: Correct! UV keratitis: UV radiation damages corneal epithelium → symptoms peak 6-12 hours after exposure. Also seen with snow blindness, tanning beds without goggles, prolonged sun exposure near water/snow. Bilateral! Fluorescein staining shows punctate epithelial erosions. Treatment: topical antibiotics (prevent secondary infection), cycloplegics (reduce ciliary spasm pain), oral analgesics. Self-resolves in 24-48 hours. Prevention: proper UV-filtering eye protection.
Open globe injury would present with visible wound, abnormal pupil shape, decreased vision, and would be apparent immediately — not delayed 6-12 hours.

questions.stem: In a chemical eye injury, what is the FIRST step in management before any other intervention?

choices.text: Apply topical anesthetic and examine with slit lamp
choices.feedback: While topical anesthetic may facilitate irrigation, irrigation must begin immediately before formal examination.
Visual acuity assessment can wait. Immediate irrigation is the priority to limit ongoing tissue damage.

choices.text: Immediate copious irrigation with saline or water for at least 30 minutes
choices.feedback: Correct! IRRIGATE FIRST — before exam, before checking acuity, before anything else. Goal: normalize pH (check with litmus paper, target 7.0-7.4). Alkali burns are WORSE than acid (alkali penetrates deeper — liquefactive necrosis vs acid's coagulative necrosis). Continue irrigation until pH normalizes (may need liters). After irrigation: comprehensive eye exam, slit lamp, IOP check. Severe burns: ophthalmology emergency.
Identifying the chemical is helpful but should NOT delay irrigation. Begin irrigation immediately, identify the agent concurrently.

questions.stem: A patient presents with sudden flashes of light, a shower of floaters, and a curtain-like visual field defect progressing from the periphery. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: CRVO presents with sudden painless vision loss, dilated tortuous veins, and widespread retinal hemorrhages (blood and thunder fundus) — not flashes, floaters, and curtain-like defect.

Correct! Classic triad: sudden photopsias (flashes), shower of floaters (from vitreous hemorrhage/pigment release), and progressive visual field loss (curtain/shadow — corresponds to area of detached retina). Risk factors: myopia, prior cataract surgery, trauma, family history, lattice degeneration. Emergency: refer to retinal specialist immediately. Treatment: pneumatic retinopexy, scleral buckle, or pars plana vitrectomy depending on characteristics. Macula-on vs macula-off status affects visual prognosis.
PVD causes flashes and floaters but typically NOT a progressive curtain-like field defect. PVD can lead to retinal detachment though.
Migraine aura typically involves bilateral scintillating scotoma (zigzag lines) that resolves in 20-30 minutes. Progressive curtain-like field loss is retinal detachment until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: What is the classic clinical triad of serotonin syndrome?

choices.text: Neuromuscular excitation (clonus, hyperreflexia), autonomic instability, and altered mental status
choices.feedback: Correct! Serotonin syndrome triad: (1) Neuromuscular hyperactivity: clonus (especially lower extremity), hyperreflexia, tremor, rigidity (2) Autonomic dysfunction: hyperthermia, diaphoresis, tachycardia, hypertension, diarrhea (3) Altered mental status: agitation, confusion, delirium. Hunter criteria used for diagnosis. KEY differentiator from NMS: rapid onset (hours) and CLONUS (NMS has lead-pipe rigidity WITHOUT clonus). Caused by serotonergic drug combinations or overdose.

choices.text: Dry mouth, urinary retention, and mydriasis
choices.feedback: These describe anticholinergic toxidrome, not serotonin syndrome (though some overlap in mydriasis exists).
These are opposite findings. Serotonin syndrome features excitatory symptoms: tachycardia, hyperthermia, agitation.
This describes drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS), not serotonin syndrome.

questions.explanation: Serotonin syndrome is caused by excess serotonergic activity, often from drug combinations (SSRI + MAOI, SSRI + tramadol, SSRI + linezolid). Clinical triad: mental status changes (agitation, confusion), autonomic instability (hyperthermia, diaphoresis, tachycardia), and neuromuscular excitation (clonus, hyperreflexia, myoclonus). Clonus (especially ocular clonus) is the most reliable distinguishing feature from NMS. Treatment: discontinue offending agents, benzodiazepines, cyproheptadine (serotonin antagonist), aggressive cooling.

questions.stem: Which drug combination is most commonly implicated in serotonin syndrome?

choices.feedback: Neither amoxicillin nor metformin affects serotonin. This combination does not cause serotonin syndrome.

Correct! Most common precipitants: adding a serotonergic agent to an existing SSRI/SNRI. High-risk combinations: SSRI/SNRI + MAOIs (most dangerous — contraindicated, requires 2-week washout), SSRI + tramadol (very common emergency), SSRI + linezolid (linezolid = reversible MAOI), SSRI + dextromethorphan, SSRI + triptans. Also: meperidine, fentanyl, lithium, St. John's wort. Risk is pharmacodynamic (multiple serotonergic mechanisms) not just dose-dependent.
These OTC analgesics do not affect serotonin pathways and cannot cause serotonin syndrome.
These cardiovascular drugs do not affect serotonin pathways.

questions.explanation: Correct: SSRI + tramadol (or SSRI + MAOI, or SSRI + linezolid).

questions.stem: What is the antidote/key management step for serotonin syndrome?

choices.feedback: Flumazenil is the antidote for benzodiazepine overdose, not serotonin syndrome.

choices.text: Cyproheptadine (serotonin antagonist) + discontinue offending agents + supportive care
choices.feedback: Correct! Management: (1) STOP all serotonergic drugs immediately, (2) Cyproheptadine (first-generation antihistamine with 5-HT2A antagonism): 12 mg initial dose, then PRN. (3) Supportive: IV fluids, cooling for hyperthermia, benzodiazepines for agitation/myoclonus, avoid paralysis without addressing temperature. Most cases resolve within 24-72 hours after stopping offending drugs. Severe: ICU monitoring, intubation if needed. Key distinction from NMS: SS is rapid onset/resolution; NMS develops over days and resolves over 1-2 weeks.
Dantrolene is the treatment for neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) and malignant hyperthermia, NOT serotonin syndrome.
Bromocriptine (dopamine agonist) is used for neuroleptic malignant syndrome, not serotonin syndrome.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Cyproheptadine (serotonin antagonist) + discontinue offending agents + supportive care.

questions.stem: How do you clinically distinguish serotonin syndrome from neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS)?

choices.text: NMS has clonus; serotonin syndrome has rigidity
choices.feedback: This is reversed. CLONUS is the hallmark of serotonin syndrome, while RIGIDITY is more prominent in NMS.

choices.text: Serotonin syndrome: rapid onset, clonus, hyperreflexia; NMS: gradual onset, lead-pipe rigidity, bradyreflexia
choices.feedback: Correct! Key differentiators: Serotonin syndrome: rapid onset (hours), CLONUS (lower extremity > upper), HYPERreflexia, tremor, diarrhea, drug ADDITION as trigger. NMS: gradual onset (days-weeks), lead-pipe RIGIDITY, HYPOreflexia or normal reflexes, elevated CK (often >1000), drug INITIATION or DOSE INCREASE of dopamine antagonist (antipsychotics) as trigger. Both: hyperthermia, altered mental status, autonomic instability. Treatment: SS → cyproheptadine; NMS → dantrolene + bromocriptine.
Both conditions cause hyperthermia. Temperature alone cannot distinguish them.

choices.text: They are the same condition with different names
choices.feedback: They are distinct syndromes with different causes, clinical features, timecourses, and treatments. Accurate differentiation is critical.

questions.explanation: Serotonin syndrome is caused by excess serotonergic activity, often from drug combinations (SSRI + MAOI, SSRI + tramadol, SSRI + linezolid). Clinical triad: mental status changes (agitation, confusion), autonomic instability (hyperthermia, diaphoresis, tachycardia), and neuromuscular excitation (clonus, hyperreflexia, myoclonus). Clonus (especially ocular clonus) is the most reliable distinguishing feature from NMS. Treatment: discontinue offending agents, benzodiazepines, cyproheptadine (serotonin antagonist), aggressive cooling.

questions.stem: Why is linezolid a risk factor for serotonin syndrome when combined with SSRIs?

choices.feedback: Linezolid does not directly activate serotonin receptors. It inhibits MAO → prevents serotonin degradation → serotonin accumulation.

choices.text: Linezolid is a reversible MAO inhibitor, preventing serotonin breakdown
choices.feedback: Correct! Linezolid (oxazolidinone antibiotic) has weak reversible MAO-inhibiting activity → prevents enzymatic breakdown of serotonin. When combined with SSRIs/SNRIs: serotonin accumulation → serotonin syndrome. Clinical significance: always check for serotonergic medications before starting linezolid. If possible, hold SSRIs/SNRIs during linezolid course (typically ≤2 weeks). If SS develops: stop both agents, give cyproheptadine. Same risk exists with IV methylene blue (also a MAO inhibitor).
Linezolid does not directly inhibit serotonin reuptake. Its serotonergic mechanism is different.

choices.text: Linezolid increases SSRI absorption from the gut
choices.feedback: Linezolid does not affect SSRI pharmacokinetics. The interaction is pharmacodynamic — MAO inhibition increases serotonin levels.

questions.explanation: Serotonin syndrome is caused by excess serotonergic activity, often from drug combinations (SSRI + MAOI, SSRI + tramadol, SSRI + linezolid). Clinical triad: mental status changes (agitation, confusion), autonomic instability (hyperthermia, diaphoresis, tachycardia), and neuromuscular excitation (clonus, hyperreflexia, myoclonus). Clonus (especially ocular clonus) is the most reliable distinguishing feature from NMS. Treatment: discontinue offending agents, benzodiazepines, cyproheptadine (serotonin antagonist), aggressive cooling.

questions.stem: What criteria differentiate acute bacterial sinusitis from viral upper respiratory infection?

choices.feedback: Imaging is NOT recommended for uncomplicated acute sinusitis — CT abnormalities are present in >80% of viral URIs. Clinical criteria are used for diagnosis.

choices.text: Symptoms lasting >10 days without improvement, OR severe onset (fever ≥39°C + purulent drainage for 3-4 days), OR double-worsening
choices.feedback: Correct! IDSA criteria for bacterial sinusitis: (1) Persistent symptoms ≥ 10 days without improvement (most common), (2) Severe onset: fever ≥ 39°C + purulent nasal discharge for ≥ 3-4 consecutive days, (3) Double-worsening: initial improvement followed by new fever, headache, or increased discharge after 5-6 days. Viral URIs typically improve within 7-10 days. Imaging NOT recommended for uncomplicated acute sinusitis.

choices.text: Any facial pain lasting more than 24 hours
choices.feedback: Facial pain alone is nonspecific and present in viral URIs, tension headaches, and other conditions. Duration and pattern of symptoms are more important.
Nasal congestion is present in both viral URI and bacterial sinusitis. More specific criteria are needed.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Symptoms lasting >10 days without improvement, OR severe onset (fever ≥39°C + purulent drainage for 3-4 days), OR double-worsening.

questions.stem: What is the first-line antibiotic for acute bacterial sinusitis?

choices.feedback: TMP-SMX is no longer recommended due to high resistance rates among sinusitis pathogens (up to 40% of S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae).
Ciprofloxacin has poor pneumococcal coverage. Respiratory FQs (levofloxacin, moxifloxacin) are alternatives but not first-line.

Correct! IDSA guidelines: first-line = amoxicillin-clavulanate (standard or high-dose: 2g BID for high resistance areas). Covers the big three pathogens: S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae (beta-lactamase producer → clavulanate needed), M. catarrhalis. Duration: 5-7 days for adults (vs older 10-14 day recommendation). Alternatives for penicillin allergy: doxycycline or respiratory fluoroquinolone. Amoxicillin alone is second choice (doesn't cover beta-lactamase producers).
Macrolides are NOT recommended for sinusitis due to high S. pneumoniae resistance rates (30%+).

questions.explanation: References: Sinusitis. StatPearls. NBK470383. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What complication of sinusitis requires emergent evaluation and treatment?

choices.feedback: Temporary anosmia during sinusitis is common and typically resolves. It does not constitute an emergency.

choices.text: Orbital cellulitis from ethmoid sinusitis (Chandler classification)
choices.feedback: Correct! Sinusitis complications requiring emergent evaluation: Orbital (most common): preseptal/orbital cellulitis, subperiosteal abscess, orbital abscess, cavernous sinus thrombosis — from ethmoid sinusitis (paper-thin lamina papyracea). Red flags: proptosis, restricted extraocular movements, vision changes, fever. Intracranial: meningitis, epidural/subdural abscess, brain abscess — from frontal sinusitis. All require CT with contrast, IV antibiotics, and possible surgical drainage.
Post-nasal drip is a common symptom of sinusitis but is not a complication requiring emergent intervention.
Mild headache is a common sinusitis symptom, not a complication requiring emergent care.

questions.explanation: References: Sinusitis. StatPearls. NBK470383. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What adjunctive therapy is recommended for all acute sinusitis (viral and bacterial)?

choices.feedback: Systemic steroids may be considered for severe symptoms or nasal polyps but are NOT recommended for all sinusitis patients.
Long-term prophylactic antibiotics are not recommended for acute sinusitis. They may be considered only for recurrent acute bacterial sinusitis after specialist evaluation.
Oral decongestants may provide short-term relief but prolonged use is not recommended.

choices.text: Intranasal saline irrigation and intranasal corticosteroids
choices.feedback: Correct! Recommended adjuncts for all sinusitis: Saline irrigation (neti pot, squeeze bottle): mechanical clearance of mucus and pathogens, reduces inflammation, improves mucociliary clearance. Intranasal corticosteroids (fluticasone, mometasone): reduce mucosal edema, improve sinus drainage, reduce symptoms. Both are safe and well-tolerated. Additional: analgesics for pain, topical decongestants (≤3 days to avoid rebound). NOT recommended: oral decongestants long-term, antihistamines (unless allergic component), mucolytics (limited evidence).

questions.explanation: References: Sinusitis. StatPearls. NBK470383. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A patient has had 4 episodes of acute bacterial sinusitis in the past year. What workup is indicated?

choices.text: CT sinuses, nasal endoscopy, consider allergy testing and immunoglobulin levels
choices.feedback: Correct! Recurrent acute bacterial sinusitis (RABRS: ≥4 episodes/year with symptom-free intervals) workup: CT sinuses (non-contrast — look for anatomic abnormalities, polyps, mucosal thickening), nasal endoscopy (polyps, septal deviation, middle meatus disease), allergy evaluation (skin testing/IgE), immunodeficiency screen (IgG subclasses, IgA, vaccine response titers — especially if recurrent respiratory infections), consider ciliary dysfunction testing, GERD evaluation. Functional endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS) if anatomic obstruction identified.

choices.text: MRI brain to rule out tumor
choices.feedback: MRI brain is not the first-line workup for recurrent sinusitis. CT sinuses with nasal endoscopy is the appropriate imaging.

choices.text: No workup needed — continue treating each episode
choices.feedback: Recurrent sinusitis (≥4 episodes/year) warrants investigation for underlying causes.
Long-term empiric antibiotics without identifying the underlying cause is inappropriate and promotes resistance.

questions.explanation: References: Sinusitis. StatPearls. NBK470383. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A 3-year-old presents with a painless, smooth abdominal mass that does not cross the midline. The parents noticed it during bath time. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Polycystic kidneys are bilateral and typically present with hypertension and renal dysfunction, not a unilateral smooth mass.
Hepatoblastoma is a liver tumor presenting in the right upper quadrant with elevated AFP. A flank mass in a 3-year-old is more consistent with Wilms tumor.
Neuroblastoma: rough, irregular mass that often CROSSES the midline, elevated catecholamines (VMA, HVA), median age 2 years. A smooth mass NOT crossing midline favors Wilms tumor.

Correct! Wilms tumor: most common renal tumor of childhood. Peak age 3-4 years. Presents as painless, smooth, firm abdominal/flank mass that does NOT cross the midline (unlike neuroblastoma). May have hypertension (renin production), hematuria, fever. Associations: WAGR syndrome (Wilms, Aniridia, GU anomalies, mental Retardation — WT1 deletion), Beckwith-Wiedemann (macrosomia, omphalocele, macroglossia — WT2), Denys-Drash (nephropathy, male pseudohermaphroditism). Treatment: surgery + chemo ± radiation (based on stage).

questions.explanation: Correct: Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma).

questions.stem: What clinical feature most reliably distinguishes Wilms tumor from neuroblastoma on initial presentation?

choices.feedback: Wilms tumor is usually unilateral. Bilateral Wilms occurs in ~5-7% of cases, typically associated with genetic syndromes.
Both can cause hypertension — Wilms through renin secretion, neuroblastoma through catecholamine production. This is not a reliable distinguishing feature.
Both can present in young children, though neuroblastoma peaks slightly younger. Age alone is not the most reliable distinguisher.

choices.text: Wilms: smooth mass that does NOT cross midline; Neuroblastoma: irregular mass that CROSSES midline
choices.feedback: Correct! Key differentiators: Wilms tumor: smooth, well-circumscribed, does NOT cross midline (renal origin pushes rather than infiltrates). Neuroblastoma: irregular, firm, CROSSES midline (arises from neural crest/adrenal, infiltrative growth). Additional distinguishers: Neuroblastoma → elevated urine catecholamines (VMA/HVA), may present with opsoclonus-myoclonus, periorbital ecchymoses (raccoon eyes from orbital metastases), Hutchinson syndrome (limping from bone mets). Wilms → hypertension, hematuria.

questions.stem: What critical instruction must be given regarding physical examination of a child with a suspected Wilms tumor?

choices.text: Perform rectal exam to assess for pelvic extension
choices.feedback: Rectal exam is not a standard part of Wilms tumor evaluation. The key instruction is avoiding aggressive abdominal palpation.

choices.text: Avoid aggressive palpation — risk of tumor rupture and upstaging
choices.feedback: Correct! CRITICAL: gentle examination only! Wilms tumors have a pseudocapsule that can rupture with aggressive palpation or biopsy → tumor spillage into the peritoneum → upstages from stage II to stage III → requires more intensive treatment (radiation added). All providers examining the child must be warned. Pre-operative biopsy is generally AVOIDED for this reason (unlike neuroblastoma where biopsy is standard). Diagnosis: imaging (ultrasound → CT/MRI) → surgical excision (radical nephrectomy) → pathologic staging.

choices.text: Perform deep palpation to determine mobility
choices.feedback: Deep palpation is specifically CONTRAINDICATED in suspected Wilms tumor.

choices.text: Apply pressure to assess for tenderness
choices.feedback: Provocative pressure testing is dangerous in suspected Wilms tumor due to rupture risk.

questions.stem: What congenital syndrome associated with Wilms tumor includes aniridia (absent iris)?

choices.feedback: Turner syndrome (45,X) is associated with coarctation of aorta, short stature, and gonadal dysgenesis — not Wilms tumor or aniridia.
Beckwith-Wiedemann features macrosomia, macroglossia, omphalocele, and hemihyperplasia — NOT aniridia. Also associated with Wilms tumor but through a different genetic mechanism.

Correct! WAGR: Wilms tumor, Aniridia, Genitourinary anomalies, mental Retardation (intellectual disability). Caused by deletion of chromosome 11p13 (includes both WT1 tumor suppressor and PAX6 genes). PAX6 deletion → aniridia. WT1 deletion → Wilms tumor (~50% lifetime risk). Children with sporadic aniridia should be screened for 11p13 deletion and monitored with abdominal ultrasounds every 3 months until age 7-8. Other Wilms associations: Denys-Drash (WT1 point mutation), Beckwith-Wiedemann (11p15 — WT2).
Down syndrome (trisomy 21) is associated with ALL (leukemia), cardiac defects, and duodenal atresia — not Wilms tumor.

questions.stem: What is the overall prognosis for favorable-histology Wilms tumor with current multimodal treatment?

choices.feedback: Correct! Wilms tumor is a pediatric oncology success story: favorable histology (most cases) has >90% 5-year survival across stages, with localized disease approaching 99%. Treatment: radical nephrectomy → chemotherapy (vincristine, actinomycin-D ± doxorubicin based on stage) ± radiation (stage III-IV). Unfavorable histology (anaplastic — especially diffuse anaplasia): worse prognosis, requires more intensive chemotherapy. Long-term survivors need monitoring for: contralateral kidney function, cardiotoxicity from doxorubicin, secondary malignancies, and musculoskeletal effects of radiation.
While prognosis is excellent, cure rate is not 100%, and surgery is a critical component of treatment.
This dramatically underestimates the prognosis. Wilms tumor is one of the great success stories of pediatric oncology.
Surgery alone is not standard treatment. Multimodal therapy (surgery + chemo ± radiation) achieves >90% survival for favorable histology.

questions.stem: A child develops urticaria, lip swelling, and wheezing within 10 minutes of eating peanuts. What type of hypersensitivity reaction is this?

choices.feedback: Type II involves IgG/IgM against cell surface antigens (e.g., transfusion reactions, autoimmune hemolysis). Food allergy is IgE-mediated.
Type IV is T-cell mediated (contact dermatitis, TB skin test). IgE-mediated food allergy is Type I (immediate).

Correct! IgE-mediated food allergy: prior sensitization produces allergen-specific IgE → binds mast cell FcεRI receptors. Re-exposure → cross-linking → mast cell degranulation → histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes, prostaglandins → rapid onset (<2 hours): urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm, GI symptoms, anaphylaxis. Top 9 allergens (FASTER Act): milk, egg, peanut, tree nuts, wheat, soy, fish, shellfish, sesame.
Type III involves antigen-antibody complex deposition (serum sickness, lupus nephritis). Food allergy is Type I.

questions.explanation: Urticaria, lip swelling, and wheezing within minutes of food ingestion is classic Type I IgE-mediated immediate hypersensitivity. The mechanism: food allergen cross-links allergen-specific IgE on mast cell/basophil surfaces, causing degranulation and release of histamine, leukotrienes, and prostaglandins. The rapid onset (minutes), multisystem involvement (skin, respiratory, GI), and characteristic symptoms distinguish IgE-mediated food allergy from non-IgE reactions (FPIES, EoE) which have delayed onset and different clinical features.

questions.stem: What is the gold standard for confirming a food allergy diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Specific IgE quantifies sensitization but cannot confirm clinical allergy — many sensitized individuals are tolerant. Positive predictive values vary by allergen and cutoff.

Correct! DBPCFC is the gold standard: patient ingests increasing doses of allergen vs placebo under medical supervision. Open challenges acceptable in practice when DBPCFC not feasible. Diagnostic approach: history → skin prick test (SPT) and/or specific IgE → oral food challenge if needed. Component-resolved diagnostics (CRD) can improve specificity (e.g., Ara h 2 for peanut → higher specificity for clinical reactivity than whole peanut IgE).
SPT has good sensitivity (~90%) but modest specificity (~50%). A positive SPT means sensitization, not necessarily clinical allergy.
Elimination diet can suggest but not confirm food allergy. It doesn't differentiate IgE-mediated allergy from other food intolerances.

questions.explanation: The double-blind, placebo-controlled oral food challenge (DBPCFC) is the gold standard for confirming food allergy. Both the patient and clinician are blinded to whether the active food or placebo is administered, eliminating bias. The patient ingests increasing doses at timed intervals with monitoring for objective signs. DBPCFC is primarily used in research settings. In clinical practice, open or single-blind oral food challenges are more commonly used but are still considered highly reliable when performed by experienced allergists in equipped facilities.

questions.stem: Current guidelines recommend introducing peanut-containing foods to high-risk infants at what age?

choices.text: At 12 months, coinciding with introduction of cow's milk
choices.feedback: Waiting until 12 months misses the optimal tolerance-induction window. Early introduction at 4-6 months is recommended for high-risk infants.

choices.text: Only after confirmed negative allergy testing at age 2
choices.feedback: Universal allergy testing before introduction is not recommended. Testing is only for high-risk infants before early introduction.
Delayed introduction actually INCREASES allergy risk. The LEAP trial changed this paradigm.

choices.text: Around 4-6 months of age, after evaluation for existing egg allergy/eczema
choices.feedback: Correct! LEAP trial: early peanut introduction (4-6 months) in high-risk infants reduced peanut allergy by 81%. NIAID guidelines: High risk (severe eczema/egg allergy): evaluate with SPT/IgE at 4-6 months → if negative, introduce peanut. Moderate risk (mild-moderate eczema): introduce around 6 months. Low risk: introduce freely with other complementary foods. This revolutionized food allergy prevention.

questions.explanation: Based on the LEAP trial (Learning Early About Peanut Allergy), current guidelines recommend introducing peanut-containing foods to high-risk infants around 4-6 months of age, after evaluation for existing egg allergy and/or moderate-to-severe eczema. High-risk infants (those with severe eczema and/or egg allergy) should undergo peanut-specific IgE testing or SPT before introduction, with supervised introduction if sensitized. Early introduction reduces peanut allergy risk by up to 80% compared to avoidance. This reversed previous recommendations to delay allergenic food introduction.
questions.citation: Food Allergy. StatPearls. [Open Access] | LEAP Trial. NEJM. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What is the first-line treatment for food-induced anaphylaxis?

choices.feedback: Corticosteroids may prevent biphasic reactions but have NO role as first-line anaphylaxis treatment — they take hours to work.
Albuterol can be adjunctive for bronchospasm but does NOT address hypotension, angioedema, or the systemic anaphylactic cascade. Epinephrine first.
Antihistamines treat urticaria/pruritus but do NOT reverse anaphylaxis. They cannot treat bronchospasm, hypotension, or laryngeal edema.

choices.text: Intramuscular epinephrine in the anterolateral thigh
choices.feedback: Correct! Epinephrine IM (weight-based dosing, max 0.3 mg child / 0.5 mg adult) in the anterolateral thigh — FIRST-LINE for ALL anaphylaxis. Mechanism: α1 → vasoconstriction (reverses hypotension, reduces mucosal edema); β1 → increased cardiac output; β2 → bronchodilation, mast cell stabilization. Repeat q5-15 min if needed. NEVER delay epinephrine for antihistamines, steroids, or observation. Position: supine with legs elevated (unless respiratory distress).

questions.explanation: Intramuscular epinephrine in the anterolateral thigh is the first-line treatment for food-induced anaphylaxis. Epinephrine should be administered immediately upon recognition of anaphylaxis — delay is the primary risk factor for fatal outcomes. The dose is 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.3 mg for children, 0.5 mg for adults) of 1:1000 concentration, repeated every 5-15 minutes as needed. Antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjunctive but never first-line. All patients with food allergy at risk for anaphylaxis should carry two epinephrine auto-injectors.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What is oral immunotherapy (OIT) for food allergy?

choices.text: Gradual administration of increasing allergen doses to raise the reaction threshold
choices.feedback: Correct! OIT: supervised escalation of allergenic food protein (starting with micrograms → grams) over months. Palforzia is available for (peanut OIT) for ages 4-17. Goal: desensitization (raise reaction threshold to protect against accidental exposures — not cure). Requires daily maintenance dosing indefinitely in most cases. Risks: allergic reactions during dosing (~10-20% have significant reactions), eosinophilic esophagitis (2-5%). Emerging: sublingual (SLIT) and epicutaneous (EPIT) immunotherapy.

choices.text: Avoiding the allergen to allow natural tolerance development
choices.feedback: Strict avoidance is the traditional approach but does not actively induce tolerance. OIT is an active treatment.

choices.text: Using probiotics to modify the immune response to foods
choices.feedback: While the microbiome may influence food allergy, probiotics are not an established food allergy treatment.
Subcutaneous injection of food allergens is too dangerous due to high anaphylaxis risk. OIT uses oral/sublingual routes for safer desensitization.

questions.explanation: Oral immunotherapy (OIT) for food allergy involves gradual administration of increasing doses of the allergenic food (typically peanut, milk, or egg) under medical supervision to raise the patient's reaction threshold (desensitization). The goal is not cure but increased tolerance to accidental exposures. Palforzia (peanut OIT) involves dose escalation over months, then daily maintenance. OIT reduces the risk of severe reactions to accidental exposures but requires ongoing daily dosing to maintain desensitization. Risks include allergic reactions during treatment.

questions.stem: What distinguishes Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) from toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN)?

choices.text: Body surface area of epidermal detachment
choices.feedback: Correct! SJS and TEN are a spectrum of the same disease: SJS: <10% BSA epidermal detachment. SJS-TEN overlap: 10-30% BSA. TEN: >30% BSA. All involve mucosal erosions (oral, ocular, genital). Mortality: SJS 1-5%, TEN 25-35%. SCORTEN prognostic scoring system. Most commonly drug-induced: sulfonamides, anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine), allopurinol, NSAIDs (piroxicam), nevirapine. HLA associations: HLA-B*5801 (allopurinol), HLA-B*1502 (carbamazepine in Southeast Asians).

choices.text: SJS involves mucous membranes; TEN does not
choices.feedback: Both SJS and TEN involve mucous membranes. The distinguishing factor is the extent of skin detachment.

choices.text: SJS is always drug-related; TEN can be idiopathic
choices.feedback: Both are most commonly drug-induced. Infections (Mycoplasma, HSV) can also cause SJS.

choices.text: TEN requires antibiotics; SJS requires antivirals
choices.feedback: Treatment for both focuses on drug withdrawal, supportive care (burn unit), and possibly IVIG or cyclosporine. Neither requires antibiotics or antivirals unless secondary infection occurs.

questions.explanation: SJS and TEN are distinguished by the percentage of body surface area (BSA) of epidermal detachment: SJS involves <10% BSA, SJS-TEN overlap involves 10-30% BSA, and TEN involves >30% BSA. Both SJS and TEN involve mucous membranes (oral, ocular, genital). Both are predominantly drug-induced (though infections like Mycoplasma can trigger SJS). Treatment is supportive for both — neither requires specific antivirals or antibiotics as primary therapy.

questions.stem: A patient started on allopurinol 3 weeks ago develops fever, diffuse morbilliform rash, facial edema, eosinophilia, and elevated liver enzymes. What is the diagnosis?

choices.text: Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms (DRESS syndrome)
choices.feedback: Correct! DRESS: delayed drug hypersensitivity (2-8 weeks after drug initiation). Cardinal features: rash (morbilliform, often with facial edema), fever, eosinophilia and/or atypical lymphocytes, internal organ involvement (liver most common, also kidney, lung, heart). Common culprits: allopurinol (HLA-B*5801), anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin), sulfonamides, dapsone, minocycline. HHV-6 reactivation is part of the pathophysiology. Treatment: stop drug, systemic corticosteroids, monitor organ function. Mortality 5-10%.
Serum sickness causes fever, rash, arthralgias, and proteinuria but not typically marked eosinophilia and hepatitis with facial edema.
Simple drug rash does not involve systemic inflammation, organ damage, or eosinophilia. This presentation has concerning systemic features.
Viral exanthems can cause rash and fever but typically don't cause eosinophilia, facial edema, and hepatitis in the setting of a new medication.

questions.explanation: This presentation — fever, diffuse morbilliform rash, facial edema, eosinophilia, and elevated liver enzymes occurring 2-6 weeks after starting a new medication (allopurinol) — is classic for DRESS syndrome. DRESS is characterized by the RegiSCAR criteria: rash, eosinophilia (>700/μL or >10%), systemic organ involvement (liver most common), lymphadenopathy, and fever, typically developing 2-8 weeks after drug initiation. Serum sickness typically presents with fever, arthralgia, and urticaria 1-2 weeks after exposure. Simple drug rash lacks eosinophilia and organ involvement. Viral exanthem would not explain the temporal drug relationship and eosinophilia.

questions.stem: Before prescribing allopurinol to a patient of Southeast Asian or African American descent, which pharmacogenomic test should be performed?

choices.feedback: G6PD testing is needed before primaquine, dapsone, or rasburicase — not allopurinol.
TPMT testing is for azathioprine/6-mercaptopurine dosing, not allopurinol.

Correct! HLA-B*5801 confers a dramatically increased risk of allopurinol hypersensitivity (DRESS and SJS/TEN). Prevalence: 6-8% in Southeast Asians, 3-4% in African Americans, <2% in Caucasians. ACR guidelines: test HLA-B*5801 before starting allopurinol in high-risk populations. If positive → absolute contraindication. Use febuxostat as alternative. Similarly: HLA-B*1502 before carbamazepine in Southeast Asians (SJS risk), HLA-B*5701 before abacavir (hypersensitivity).
CYP2D6 is relevant for codeine, tamoxifen, and some psychiatric drugs, not allopurinol.

questions.explanation: HLA-B*5801 testing should be performed before prescribing allopurinol to patients of Southeast Asian (prevalence ~6-8%) and African American (prevalence ~3-4%) descent, as this allele is strongly associated with allopurinol-induced DRESS and SJS/TEN. The ACR 2020 guidelines conditionally recommend HLA-B*5801 testing before allopurinol initiation in all patients. If positive, allopurinol is contraindicated and febuxostat is an alternative. G6PD testing is for hemolytic risk with certain drugs. TPMT testing is for thiopurines. CYP2D6 is for codeine/tamoxifen metabolism.

questions.stem: What is the most critical initial management step for SJS/TEN?

choices.text: Apply topical corticosteroids to all affected areas
choices.feedback: Topical steroids are not the primary treatment. Systemic management and drug withdrawal are critical.
SJS/TEN is not a histamine-mediated reaction. Antihistamines are not effective treatment. Drug withdrawal and supportive care are essential.

choices.text: Immediately discontinue the causative drug and transfer to burn unit/ICU
choices.feedback: Correct! Management priorities: (1) STOP the offending drug immediately — each day of continued exposure after diagnosis worsens mortality. (2) Transfer to burn unit or ICU for wound care (similar to burn management). (3) Supportive: fluid resuscitation, pain management, nutritional support, wound care with non-adherent dressings. (4) Ophthalmology consult (corneal damage risk). Controversial treatments: IVIG, cyclosporine (best evidence), etanercept, systemic steroids (debated). SCORTEN score predicts mortality.
Antibiotics are only indicated if secondary infection develops. The immediate priority is different.

questions.explanation: The most critical initial step in SJS/TEN management is immediately discontinuing the causative drug — early withdrawal is the single most important prognostic factor, reducing mortality significantly. Patients should be transferred to a burn unit or ICU for specialized wound care, fluid resuscitation, pain management, temperature regulation, and infection surveillance. Topical corticosteroids are not adequate primary treatment. Antihistamines do not address the pathology. Empiric antibiotics may be needed for secondary infection but are not the critical first step — and some antibiotics (sulfonamides) may be the causative agent.

questions.stem: What is the Nikolsky sign and what does a positive result suggest?

choices.text: Pinpoint bleeding when a psoriatic scale is removed, confirming psoriasis
choices.feedback: That describes the Auspitz sign seen in psoriasis, not the Nikolsky sign.

choices.text: Blanching of a skin lesion with pressure, suggesting vascular origin
choices.feedback: That describes diascopy, not the Nikolsky sign.

choices.text: Ring of erythema around a central blister, suggesting bullous impetigo
choices.feedback: This describes a different clinical sign. Nikolsky involves epidermal sloughing with lateral pressure.

choices.text: Lateral pressure on apparently normal skin causes epidermal separation, suggesting SJS/TEN or pemphigus
choices.feedback: Correct! Nikolsky sign: gentle lateral mechanical pressure on normal-appearing skin → epidermis separates/slides off → positive. Indicates loss of epidermal cohesion. Seen in: SJS/TEN (full-thickness epidermal necrosis), pemphigus vulgaris (intraepidermal acantholysis), staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS — subcorneal cleavage). A positive Nikolsky sign in the setting of drug rash + mucosal involvement = high suspicion for SJS/TEN → urgent skin biopsy for confirmation, immediate drug withdrawal.

questions.explanation: Nikolsky sign is positive when lateral pressure applied to apparently normal-appearing skin causes the epidermis to separate and slide off the underlying dermis, forming a blister or denuded area. It suggests loss of epidermal cohesion, seen in SJS/TEN (full-thickness epidermal necrosis) and pemphigus vulgaris (intraepidermal acantholysis). Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding) is associated with psoriasis. Diascopy (blanching with pressure) assesses vascular lesions. The description of ring erythema around a blister does not correspond to a named clinical sign for bullous impetigo.

questions.stem: An infant presents with profuse vomiting 2 hours after ingesting rice cereal for the third time. No urticaria or respiratory symptoms. The infant becomes pale and lethargic. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: IgE-mediated reactions occur within minutes (<2 hours), typically with urticaria, angioedema, or respiratory symptoms. This presentation lacks these features.
Gastroenteritis is not reproducibly triggered by the same food on each exposure. The recurrence with rice cereal specifically is classic for FPIES.

Correct! FPIES: non-IgE-mediated food hypersensitivity. Acute: repetitive vomiting 1-4 hours after ingestion, pallor, lethargy, ± diarrhea 5-10 hours later. Can cause dehydration and hypotension mimicking sepsis. No skin/respiratory symptoms. Common triggers: milk, soy, rice, oat, fish. Diagnosis: clinical — IgE testing is NEGATIVE. OFC is confirmatory but can provoke severe reaction. Management: avoid trigger food, IV fluids for acute episodes, ondansetron (may abort vomiting). Most children outgrow FPIES by age 3-5.
Pyloric stenosis causes projectile vomiting but is non-bilious, occurs at 2-6 weeks of age, and is not food-specific. FPIES is triggered by specific food proteins.

questions.explanation: This presentation — profuse repetitive vomiting 1-4 hours after food ingestion (rice cereal), absence of cutaneous or respiratory symptoms, and reproducibility with repeated exposures — is classic acute FPIES. FPIES is a non-IgE-mediated food allergy involving T-cell-mediated intestinal inflammation. IgE-mediated food allergy typically presents within minutes with urticaria, angioedema, or anaphylaxis. Gastroenteritis would not recur specifically with one food. Pyloric stenosis presents with projectile vomiting in the first weeks of life, not with specific food triggers.
questions.citation: FPIES. StatPearls. [Open Access] | JACI FPIES Consensus Guidelines. [Open Access]

questions.stem: How does FPIES differ from IgE-mediated food allergy in terms of pathophysiology and testing?

choices.text: FPIES is IgG-mediated and diagnosed by IgG4 food panels
choices.feedback: IgG4 food panels have no validated clinical utility. FPIES pathophysiology involves T-cell mediated intestinal inflammation.

choices.text: FPIES and IgE-mediated allergy are identical in mechanism
choices.feedback: They are mechanistically distinct: FPIES is T-cell/innate immune mediated; IgE-mediated allergy involves mast cell degranulation via IgE cross-linking.

choices.text: FPIES is diagnosed by elevated tryptase during episodes
choices.feedback: Tryptase is elevated in IgE-mediated anaphylaxis and mast cell disorders. FPIES does not involve mast cell degranulation.

choices.text: FPIES is T-cell/non-IgE mediated — skin prick tests and specific IgE are typically negative
choices.feedback: Correct! FPIES is a non-IgE, cell-mediated food hypersensitivity: T cells and innate immune cells in the intestinal mucosa → local inflammation → increased intestinal permeability → fluid shifts. SPT and specific IgE are NEGATIVE (unless co-existing IgE sensitization, which occurs in ~25% and may predict prolonged course). No validated biomarker — diagnosis is clinical. FPIES can coexist with IgE-mediated allergy to OTHER foods. Note: atypical FPIES can develop IgE sensitization over time.

questions.explanation: FPIES is a T-cell-mediated (non-IgE) gastrointestinal food allergy. Unlike IgE-mediated allergy, skin prick tests and serum-specific IgE to the trigger food are typically negative (though ~5-20% may have concurrent IgE sensitization, termed 'atypical FPIES'). There are no validated laboratory biomarkers for FPIES. Diagnosis relies on clinical history and oral food challenge. IgG4 food panels are unvalidated and should not be used for any food allergy diagnosis. FPIES and IgE-mediated allergy have fundamentally different mechanisms. Tryptase is not characteristically elevated in FPIES.
questions.citation: FPIES. StatPearls. [Open Access] | JACI FPIES Consensus Guidelines. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What medication can be administered during an acute FPIES episode to stop vomiting?

choices.feedback: Correct! Ondansetron (5-HT3 antagonist): can abort FPIES vomiting episodes. Dose: weight-based dosing IV/IM (max 16 mg). Mechanism likely: blocks serotonin-mediated vagal afferent stimulation of the vomiting center. Used during supervised OFCs as rescue medication. For acute episodes at home: ondansetron ODT can be given if tolerating oral. Plus IV fluids for dehydration. Severe episodes with shock: normal saline bolus + ondansetron.
Steroids have not been shown to be effective in acute FPIES episodes. Ondansetron and IV fluids are the mainstay.

Epinephrine is for IgE-mediated anaphylaxis. FPIES vomiting is not mast cell-mediated and does not respond to epinephrine (unless there is concurrent IgE-mediated reaction with hemodynamic instability).
Antihistamines do not treat FPIES, which is not histamine-mediated.

questions.explanation: Ondansetron (Zofran) IV or IM is the recommended rescue medication during acute FPIES episodes, as it can rapidly control the profuse vomiting that characterizes acute reactions. The 2017 FPIES Consensus Guidelines recommend ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg IV/IM (max 16 mg). IV fluid resuscitation is also critical for dehydration and potential hypotension. Methylprednisolone has no established role in acute FPIES management. Epinephrine is not first-line for FPIES (which is non-IgE-mediated) unless there is cardiovascular compromise. Diphenhydramine targets histamine-mediated symptoms not present in FPIES.

questions.stem: What is the most common trigger food for FPIES in the United States?

choices.feedback: Correct! Most common US FPIES triggers: cow's milk/formula, soy (50% cross-react with milk FPIES), grains (rice, oat — rice is the most common SOLID food trigger), and fish/egg. In Asia, fish and shellfish are more common triggers. Key: rice and oat FPIES is unique to FPIES — these are rarely IgE-mediated allergens. Many infants react to multiple triggers. Breastfed infants rarely develop FPIES (maternal food proteins in breast milk may promote tolerance).
Peanut is a common IgE-mediated food allergy trigger. FPIES triggers tend to be different foods.
Wheat can trigger FPIES but less commonly than milk, soy, or rice in the US.
Shellfish FPIES occurs but is not the most common trigger in the US. Cow's milk is the most common overall trigger.

questions.explanation: Cow's milk (and cow's milk-based formula) is the most common FPIES trigger in the United States, followed by soy. Together, milk and soy account for the majority of infant FPIES cases. Among solid foods, rice and oats are the most common triggers. Peanut is a common IgE-mediated allergen but an uncommon FPIES trigger. Wheat can cause FPIES but is less common than milk. Shellfish FPIES is rare and typically occurs in older children/adults.
questions.citation: FPIES. StatPearls. [Open Access] | JACI FPIES Consensus Guidelines. [Open Access]

questions.stem: At what age do most children outgrow FPIES?

choices.text: Never — FPIES is a lifelong condition
choices.feedback: Unlike many IgE-mediated allergies that can be lifelong, most FPIES resolves in childhood.
Starting solids can actually trigger FPIES in previously unaffected infants. Resolution takes years, not weeks.

choices.text: By age 3-5 years for milk/soy; potentially longer for grains and fish
choices.feedback: Correct! Most children outgrow FPIES: milk/soy: majority resolve by age 3-5. Solid food FPIES (rice, oat): may resolve slightly later. Fish/shellfish FPIES: can persist into later childhood or adolescence. Supervised oral food challenge (OFC) is used to assess tolerance — performed in clinic with IV access. Children with concomitant IgE sensitization to their FPIES trigger tend to have a more prolonged course.
6 months is too early — most children are still reactive at this age.

questions.explanation: Most children outgrow FPIES to milk and soy by age 3-5 years. Resolution timing varies by trigger food: milk/soy FPIES typically resolves by age 3-5, while grain FPIES (rice, oats) and fish/shellfish FPIES may persist longer. Resolution is confirmed by a supervised oral food challenge (OFC) in a medical setting. FPIES is not a lifelong condition in most cases. It does not resolve immediately upon starting solids — it develops with solid food introduction. Six months is far too early for expected resolution.

questions.stem: A patient presents with recurrent episodes of flushing, hypotension, abdominal cramping, and urticaria pigmentosa on exam. Serum tryptase is persistently elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Carcinoid causes flushing and diarrhea but not urticaria pigmentosa or elevated tryptase. Check 5-HIAA for carcinoid.
Pheochromocytoma causes paroxysmal hypertension (not hypotension), headache, and diaphoresis. Check metanephrines, not tryptase.

Correct! Systemic mastocytosis: clonal proliferation of mast cells. Cardinal features: skin (urticaria pigmentosa — brown-red macules/papules that urticate with stroking = Darier sign), constitutional (flushing, pruritus), GI (cramping, diarrhea), cardiovascular (hypotension, syncope, anaphylaxis), bone (pain, osteoporosis). Labs: persistently elevated baseline serum tryptase (>20 ng/mL), bone marrow biopsy shows mast cell aggregates, KIT D816V mutation (>90%). Treatment: H1 + H2 antihistamines, mast cell stabilizers (cromolyn), epinephrine for anaphylaxis.
Chronic urticaria causes hives but not urticaria pigmentosa, persistently elevated tryptase, or the multi-system involvement described.

questions.explanation: Recurrent flushing, hypotension, abdominal cramping, and diarrhea with urticaria pigmentosa (characteristic brown maculopapular skin lesions) suggests systemic mastocytosis — a clonal proliferation of mast cells infiltrating extracutaneous organs (bone marrow, liver, spleen, GI tract). Systemic symptoms result from mast cell mediator release (histamine, prostaglandins, leukotrienes). Diagnosis requires bone marrow biopsy showing mast cell aggregates, KIT D816V mutation (>90% of adults), and elevated baseline serum tryptase (>20 ng/mL).

questions.stem: What is the Darier sign and what condition is it associated with?

choices.text: Dermatographic wheal formed by stroking normal skin
choices.feedback: Dermatographism involves urticaria on normal skin. Darier sign specifically involves urtication of lesional skin in mast cell disease.

choices.text: Pitting of the nail plate seen in psoriasis
choices.feedback: Nail pitting is associated with psoriasis. Darier sign refers to urtication of mastocytosis lesions.

choices.text: Urtication (wheal-and-flare) when a lesion of urticaria pigmentosa is stroked, pathognomonic for cutaneous mastocytosis
choices.feedback: Correct! Darier sign: stroking or rubbing a mastocytosis skin lesion → local mast cell degranulation → wheal (urtication) and surrounding flare at the lesion site. Pathognomonic for cutaneous/systemic mastocytosis. The lesions of urticaria pigmentosa contain dense mast cell infiltrates. Skin biopsy confirms: >15 mast cells/HPF with aggregates, CD25/CD2 expression on mast cells (aberrant markers). Can be elicited in clinic by gentle rubbing with a tongue depressor.

choices.text: Blanching ring around a melanocytic nevus
choices.feedback: A halo around a nevus describes Sutton nevus (halo nevus). Darier sign involves urtication of mast cell skin lesions.

questions.explanation: The Darier sign is elicited by firmly stroking a skin lesion of urticaria pigmentosa (UP), producing a localized wheal-and-flare response (urtication) with possible erythema and pruritus. This occurs because UP lesions contain dense mast cell infiltrates that degranulate with mechanical stimulation. A positive Darier sign is pathognomonic for cutaneous mastocytosis. It helps distinguish UP from other pigmented skin conditions. The sign may be more prominent in children and should be elicited gently to avoid systemic mediator release.

questions.stem: What is the role of serum tryptase in evaluating mast cell disorders?

choices.text: Baseline tryptase >20 ng/mL is a WHO diagnostic criterion for systemic mastocytosis; acute elevation confirms mast cell degranulation
choices.feedback: Correct! Two uses of tryptase: (1) Baseline (>20 ng/mL): WHO minor criterion for systemic mastocytosis. (2) Acute: drawn within 1-2 hours of anaphylaxis/reaction → peaks at 1 hour, normalizes by 6-8 hours. Acute tryptase >baseline × 1.2 + 2 = confirms mast cell degranulation. Important: baseline tryptase should be measured when patient is well (not during a reaction). Hereditary alpha-tryptasemia (HαT): extra TPSAB1 gene copies → elevated baseline tryptase without mastocytosis, but increased anaphylaxis severity.

choices.text: It is only useful during acute allergic reactions
choices.feedback: Tryptase is useful both during acute reactions AND as a baseline screening tool for mastocytosis.

choices.text: Normal tryptase rules out all mast cell disorders
choices.feedback: Tryptase can be normal in cutaneous mastocytosis and some cases of mast cell activation syndrome. It is one criterion among several.

choices.text: Tryptase is only elevated in carcinoid syndrome
choices.feedback: Carcinoid syndrome is diagnosed by elevated 5-HIAA, not tryptase. Tryptase is a mast cell-specific marker.

questions.explanation: Serum tryptase serves dual diagnostic roles in mast cell disorders: (1) Baseline tryptase >20 ng/mL is a minor WHO diagnostic criterion for systemic mastocytosis, reflecting increased total mast cell burden. (2) Acute tryptase elevation (>2 + 1.2 × baseline, measured 15 min to 3 hours after symptom onset) confirms mast cell degranulation during an anaphylactic/anaphylactoid event. A persistently elevated baseline tryptase warrants bone marrow evaluation for systemic mastocytosis, even without skin lesions.

questions.stem: What genetic mutation is present in >90% of adult systemic mastocytosis cases?

choices.feedback: Correct! KIT D816V: gain-of-function point mutation in the tyrosine kinase domain of c-KIT (the receptor for stem cell factor, SCF). Present in >90% of adult systemic mastocytosis. Drives constitutive mast cell proliferation independent of SCF. Therapeutic target: midostaurin (used for advanced SM) and avapritinib (selective KIT D816V inhibitor — used for advanced SM, better specificity). This mutation also confers resistance to imatinib (unlike wild-type KIT mutations in GIST).
FIP1L1-PDGFRA is associated with hypereosinophilic syndrome and responds to imatinib, not specifically mastocytosis.
BRAF V600E is associated with melanoma, hairy cell leukemia, and Langerhans cell histiocytosis, not mastocytosis.
JAK2 V617F is the hallmark mutation of polycythemia vera and other myeloproliferative neoplasms, not mastocytosis.

questions.explanation: The KIT D816V gain-of-function mutation is present in >90% of adult systemic mastocytosis cases. This somatic mutation in the tyrosine kinase receptor KIT (CD117) on mast cells leads to constitutive ligand-independent activation, driving clonal mast cell proliferation and survival. Detection of KIT D816V in bone marrow is a WHO minor diagnostic criterion for systemic mastocytosis. The mutation also predicts resistance to imatinib (which targets wild-type KIT) and guides selection of targeted therapies such as midostaurin or avapritinib.

questions.stem: A patient with systemic mastocytosis is scheduled for surgery. What perioperative precaution is essential?

choices.feedback: While some mastocytosis patients take aspirin for prostaglandin D2-mediated flushing, it is not a universal perioperative protocol and can trigger reactions in sensitive individuals.

choices.text: Pre-medicate with H1/H2 blockers and corticosteroids; avoid mast cell degranulating triggers; have epinephrine available
choices.feedback: Correct! Mastocytosis perioperative protocol: Pre-medication: H1 blocker (cetirizine/diphenhydramine), H2 blocker (famotidine), ± prednisone 24-48 hours before. Avoid triggers: morphine/codeine (direct degranulators), NSAIDs (in sensitive patients), rapid temperature changes, mechanical trauma to skin. Ensure: epinephrine at bedside, warm OR, gentle tissue handling. Anesthesia considerations: avoid histamine-releasing agents (atracurium, succinylcholine — use rocuronium, cisatracurium). Alert all team members.

choices.text: Withhold epinephrine auto-injector for 7 days preoperatively
choices.feedback: Epinephrine should be readily available, not withheld. Mastocytosis patients are at high risk for perioperative anaphylaxis.
Mastocytosis patients are at high risk for perioperative mast cell degranulation and anaphylaxis.

questions.explanation: Perioperative management of systemic mastocytosis requires: (1) H1 and H2 antihistamine premedication (cetirizine + famotidine), (2) corticosteroid premedication (methylprednisolone), (3) avoidance of known mast cell degranulating agents (morphine, atracurium, NSAIDs, vancomycin rapid infusion, dextrans), (4) epinephrine immediately available, (5) temperature extremes avoided, (6) minimal mechanical tissue trauma. Anesthesia consultation is essential. Regional anesthesia may be preferred when feasible.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old develops urticaria, vomiting, and wheezing within 10 minutes of eating scrambled eggs. What is the underlying immunologic mechanism?

choices.feedback: Type III reactions (serum sickness, Arthus reaction) involve antigen-antibody complexes and complement. They do not cause acute urticaria/anaphylaxis to food.
T-cell mediated (Type IV) reactions are delayed (hours-days) and present differently (contact dermatitis, FPIES). This immediate reaction is IgE-mediated.
Complement-mediated cytotoxicity (Type II) targets cell-surface antigens (hemolytic anemia, transfusion reactions). Not the mechanism of food allergy.

Correct! Immediate food allergy: prior sensitization produces food-specific IgE → bound to mast cell/basophil FcεRI receptors → re-exposure → cross-linking of IgE → mast cell degranulation → histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes, prostaglandins → urticaria, angioedema, vomiting, bronchospasm, anaphylaxis. Onset within minutes to 2 hours. Top 9 allergens: milk, egg, peanut, tree nut, wheat, soy, fish, shellfish, sesame. Diagnosis: skin prick testing or serum specific IgE + clinical history.

questions.explanation: Urticaria, vomiting, and wheezing within minutes of egg ingestion in a 2-year-old represents IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation. Egg-specific IgE cross-links surface-bound IgE on mast cells, causing immediate degranulation with release of histamine (urticaria, vasodilation), leukotrienes (bronchoconstriction/wheezing), and other mediators (GI smooth muscle contraction/vomiting). The rapid onset and multisystem involvement confirm Type I hypersensitivity.

questions.stem: What landmark trial demonstrated that early peanut introduction reduces peanut allergy in high-risk infants?

choices.feedback: The PETIT trial (Japan) studied early egg introduction, not peanut. LEAP is the specific peanut trial.
HealthNuts is an epidemiologic cohort study on food allergy prevalence, not an intervention trial for early introduction.

Correct! The LEAP (Learning Early About Peanut allergy) trial: high-risk infants (severe eczema/egg allergy) randomized to early peanut introduction (age 4-11 months) vs avoidance → 81% reduction in peanut allergy at age 5. Changed guidelines globally: early introduction (around 4-6 months) is now recommended for high-risk infants, with prior evaluation if severe eczema or existing food allergy. The LEAP-On follow-up showed protection persisted even after a year of avoidance.

The EAT (Enquiring About Tolerance) trial studied early introduction of 6 allergenic foods in a general population. Per-protocol analysis showed benefit, but intention-to-treat was not significant. LEAP is the landmark peanut trial.

questions.explanation: The LEAP (Learning Early About Peanut Allergy) trial, published in NEJM 2015, demonstrated that early introduction of peanut-containing foods to high-risk infants (age 4-11 months with severe eczema and/or egg allergy) reduced peanut allergy development by approximately 80% compared to peanut avoidance until age 5. This landmark randomized controlled trial fundamentally changed infant feeding guidelines worldwide, replacing previous advice to delay allergenic food introduction. The LEAP-On follow-up study confirmed sustained benefit even after a period of avoidance.
questions.citation: LEAP Trial. Du Toit et al. NEJM 2015. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What is the gold standard diagnostic test for food allergy?

choices.text: Serum specific IgE level above the 95% predictive decision point
choices.feedback: Specific IgE levels above certain thresholds have high PPV for clinical reactivity but are not 100% accurate and are not the gold standard.
BAT measures basophil degranulation markers in vitro and shows promise for food allergy diagnosis but is not yet standard clinical practice or the gold standard.

Correct! DBPCFC is the gold standard: patient consumes increasing amounts of the food (or placebo) in a blinded fashion → observed for allergic reaction. Used when history + testing are inconclusive. Open challenges are often used clinically (less burdensome, adequate when objective symptoms expected). Must be performed in a supervised medical setting with epinephrine available. SPT and sIgE have good NPV but variable PPV — they show sensitization, not necessarily clinical allergy.
CRD (e.g., Ara h 2 for peanut, omega-5-gliadin for wheat) improves specificity over whole-extract IgE but is not the gold standard. It helps predict clinical reactivity and severity.

questions.explanation: The double-blind, placebo-controlled oral food challenge (DBPCFC) is the gold standard for food allergy diagnosis. Neither patient nor provider knows which challenge contains the food allergen vs placebo, eliminating subjective bias. Increasing doses are administered at 15-30 minute intervals with monitoring for objective allergic signs. Clinical alternatives include open food challenges (adequate for most clinical decisions) and component-resolved diagnostics (which improve specificity of serologic testing).

questions.stem: A patient with known peanut allergy accidentally ingests peanut at a restaurant and develops throat tightness, diffuse urticaria, and hypotension. What is the first-line treatment?

choices.feedback: Antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts but NOT first-line for anaphylaxis. Epinephrine is the ONLY first-line treatment.
Albuterol helps bronchospasm and steroids may prevent biphasic reactions, but neither addresses the life-threatening cardiovascular/airway component. Epinephrine first.

Correct! Anaphylaxis first-line: IM epinephrine(1:1000 concentration) in the anterolateral thigh. Can repeat every 5-15 minutes. NO contraindications to epinephrine in anaphylaxis. Delay in epinephrine is the #1 factor in fatal anaphylaxis. Adjuncts (given AFTER epi): H1 antihistamine (diphenhydramine), H2 blocker (famotidine), albuterol for bronchospasm, IV fluids for hypotension, corticosteroids for biphasic prevention. Observe 4-6 hours minimum.
There is no sublingual epinephrine tablet for anaphylaxis. IM injection into the anterolateral thigh is the correct route and site.

questions.explanation: Intramuscular epinephrine is the immediate first-line treatment for anaphylaxis from accidental peanut ingestion. With throat tightness and difficulty breathing (indicating upper airway edema and/or bronchospasm), this is anaphylaxis requiring immediate epinephrine — delay increases mortality risk. Epinephrine's alpha-1, beta-1, and beta-2 effects simultaneously address vasoconstriction, cardiac output, and bronchodilation. After epinephrine: call emergency services, position supine (legs elevated), prepare for repeat dosing, and monitor in ED for 4-6+ hours.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A patient started on allopurinol 3 weeks ago develops high fever, facial edema, diffuse morbilliform rash, lymphadenopathy, and eosinophilia with elevated liver enzymes. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.text: Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms (DRESS syndrome)
choices.feedback: Correct! DRESS syndrome: onset 2-8 weeks after drug initiation. Classic features: high fever, facial edema (characteristic), diffuse rash, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia/atypical lymphocytes, and internal organ involvement (hepatitis most common, also nephritis, pneumonitis, carditis). Common culprits: allopurinol, anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine), sulfonamides, dapsone, minocycline. HHV-6 reactivation often occurs. Treatment: withdraw drug, systemic corticosteroids for severe organ involvement. Mortality ~10%.

Serum sickness presents with fever, rash, arthralgias, and lymphadenopathy but does NOT typically cause eosinophilia or hepatitis. The 2-8 week latency and internal organ involvement pattern indicates DRESS.
TEN involves widespread epidermal detachment (>30% BSA) with mucosal erosions. The eosinophilia, lymphadenopathy, and hepatitis pattern is DRESS.
SJS presents with mucosal involvement and epidermal detachment (<10% BSA), not the systemic organ involvement pattern described. This constellation is DRESS.

questions.explanation: This presentation is DRESS syndrome: fever, facial edema, diffuse morbilliform rash, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia, and hepatitis 2-3 weeks after starting allopurinol. Key distinguishing features from serum sickness (lacks eosinophilia and organ damage), TEN (epidermal detachment, not morbilliform rash), and SJS (mucosal erosions with skin detachment <10% BSA). DRESS has a longer latency period (2-8 weeks) compared to SJS/TEN (1-3 weeks) and features prominent eosinophilia and internal organ involvement as hallmarks.

questions.stem: What clinical feature most reliably distinguishes SJS/TEN from other severe drug reactions?

choices.feedback: Arthritis and serositis suggest serum sickness or drug-induced lupus, not SJS/TEN.

choices.text: Mucosal erosions with epidermal detachment (positive Nikolsky sign)
choices.feedback: Correct! SJS/TEN hallmarks: painful mucosal erosions (oral, ocular, genital — at least 2 mucosal sites) + epidermal detachment with positive Nikolsky sign (lateral pressure causes epidermis to slide off). Classification: SJS <10% BSA detachment, SJS-TEN overlap 10-30%, TEN >30%. Skin biopsy: full-thickness epidermal necrosis. Key culprits: sulfonamides, allopurinol, anticonvulsants, NSAIDs (piroxicam), antibiotics. SCORTEN predicts mortality. Treatment: withdraw drug, supportive care (burn unit), consider IVIG or cyclosporine.
Eosinophilia is more characteristic of DRESS syndrome. SJS/TEN may have normal or only mildly elevated eosinophils.

choices.text: Facial edema as the initial sign
choices.feedback: Facial edema is characteristic of DRESS syndrome, not SJS/TEN.

questions.explanation: The clinical feature that most reliably distinguishes SJS/TEN from other severe cutaneous drug reactions is mucosal erosions (oral, ocular, genital) combined with epidermal detachment (positive Nikolsky sign). This full-thickness epidermal necrosis with mucosal involvement is unique to the SJS/TEN spectrum. Arthritis and serositis are features of serum sickness or drug-induced lupus. Eosinophilia is characteristic of DRESS syndrome. Facial edema as an initial sign is more suggestive of DRESS or angioedema.

questions.stem: Which pharmacogenomic test should be performed before prescribing allopurinol in patients of Southeast Asian or African American descent?

choices.feedback: G6PD testing is required before primaquine and dapsone, not allopurinol.
CYP2C19 testing is relevant for clopidogrel and some PPIs, not allopurinol.

Correct! HLA-B*5801 is strongly associated with allopurinol-induced DRESS and SJS/TEN. Prevalence: ~6-8% in Southeast Asian descent (Korean, Han Chinese, Thai), ~3-4% in African Americans, <2% in Caucasians. ACR guidelines recommend testing before starting allopurinol in at-risk populations. If positive → absolute contraindication to allopurinol → use febuxostat instead. Similar principle: HLA-B*1502 testing before carbamazepine in Southeast Asian populations (SJS/TEN risk).
HLA-B*5701 is tested before abacavir (HIV drug), not allopurinol. HLA-B*5801 is the allopurinol-associated allele.

questions.explanation: HLA-B*5801 testing is recommended before allopurinol in Southeast Asian and African American patients due to high allele prevalence and strong association with allopurinol hypersensitivity (DRESS, SJS/TEN). G6PD testing is for hemolytic anemia risk. CYP2C19 genotyping guides clopidogrel/PPI dosing. HLA-B*5701 testing is for abacavir (HIV medication) hypersensitivity — a different HLA allele for a different drug.

questions.stem: What is the SCORTEN score used for?

choices.feedback: DRESS is diagnosed using the RegiSCAR scoring system, not SCORTEN. SCORTEN is specific to SJS/TEN.

choices.text: Determining which drug caused the reaction
choices.feedback: SCORTEN does not identify causative drugs. The ALDEN algorithm helps assess drug causality in SJS/TEN.

Correct! SCORTEN predicts mortality in SJS/TEN based on 7 parameters (1 point each): age >40, malignancy, heart rate >120, BSA detachment >10%, serum urea >28 mg/dL, glucose >252 mg/dL, bicarbonate <20. Score 0-1: 3% mortality; 2: 12%; 3: 35%; 4: 58%; ≥5: 90%. Should be calculated within 24 hours of admission and repeated at day 3. Scores ≥3 warrant burn unit/ICU management.

choices.text: Classifying drug allergy severity on a universal scale
choices.feedback: SCORTEN is specific to SJS/TEN prognostication, not a universal drug allergy severity scale.

questions.explanation: SCORTEN (Score of Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis) predicts mortality in SJS/TEN based on 7 parameters assessed within the first 24 hours: age >40, malignancy, heart rate >120, initial BSA of epidermal detachment >10%, serum urea >28 mg/dL, glucose >252 mg/dL, and bicarbonate <20 mEq/L. Each parameter scores 1 point. Mortality ranges from ~3% (score 0-1) to >90% (score ≥5). SCORTEN is not used to diagnose DRESS (that uses RegiSCAR criteria), determine causative drug, or classify general drug allergy severity.

questions.stem: What is the most common cause of long-term morbidity in SJS/TEN survivors?

choices.feedback: Respiratory involvement (bronchial epithelium sloughing) can occur acutely but chronic pulmonary fibrosis is uncommon compared to ocular sequelae.
Acute renal injury can occur during SJS/TEN but chronic renal failure is not the most common long-term complication.
Hepatic involvement can occur acutely but chronic hepatitis is not the most common long-term sequela.

Correct! Ocular involvement occurs in ~80% of SJS/TEN cases and is the leading cause of long-term morbidity. Acute: conjunctival erosions, pseudomembrane formation. Chronic: cicatricial conjunctivitis, symblepharon (adhesions), corneal neovascularization/scarring, chronic dry eye (meibomian gland destruction), limbal stem cell deficiency → progressive vision loss. Early ophthalmology consultation essential. Amniotic membrane transplant may help prevent scarring. Other sequelae: mucosal stenosis, skin pigment changes, PTSD.

questions.explanation: Ocular complications are the most common cause of long-term morbidity in SJS/TEN survivors, affecting 50-90% of patients. These include chronic cicatricial conjunctivitis, symblepharon, corneal scarring, dry eye syndrome, entropion/trichiasis, and visual impairment progressing to blindness. Early ophthalmologic consultation and aggressive eye care during the acute phase are critical to minimizing permanent damage. Pulmonary fibrosis, renal failure, and chronic hepatitis can occur but are much less common long-term sequelae than ocular complications.

questions.stem: A 4-month-old presents with profuse vomiting 2 hours after first cow's milk formula exposure, progressing to pallor and lethargy. IgE testing to milk is negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Intussusception presents with colicky pain, currant jelly stools, and a sausage-shaped mass — not the temporal food-exposure pattern of FPIES.
IgE-mediated reactions occur within minutes and present with urticaria/angioedema/wheezing. This delayed vomiting with negative IgE is FPIES.
Pyloric stenosis causes projectile non-bilious vomiting but is not temporally related to specific food exposure and presents at 2-6 weeks of age.

Correct! FPIES is a non-IgE, cell-mediated food allergy. Acute FPIES: profuse repetitive vomiting 1-4 hours after ingestion, progressing to lethargy, pallor, hypothermia, and hypovolemic shock. Common triggers: cow's milk, soy (infants), rice, oats (solid food FPIES). Diagnosis: clinical (IgE-negative, no urticaria). Labs during acute episode: elevated neutrophil count, thrombocytosis, metabolic acidosis. Treatment: IV fluid resuscitation, ondansetron (can abort acute episode). Confirmed by supervised oral food challenge.

questions.explanation: This 4-month-old with profuse vomiting 2 hours after cow's milk formula, progressing to pallor and lethargy, presents with acute FPIES. The delayed onset (1-4 hours), profuse vomiting, absence of skin/respiratory symptoms, and progression to pallor/lethargy (from dehydration and potential methemoglobinemia/acidosis) are hallmarks.
