Intussusception presents with intermittent colicky pain, currant-jelly stools, and a sausage-shaped mass. IgE-mediated milk allergy presents within minutes with urticaria/angioedema. Pyloric stenosis causes projectile vomiting in the first 2-8 weeks of life, not triggered by specific proteins.

questions.stem: What medication can be used to treat an acute FPIES episode?

choices.feedback: Cromolyn stabilizes mast cells but FPIES is not mast cell-mediated. Ondansetron targets the serotonin pathway involved in FPIES vomiting.

Correct! Ondansetron (weight-based dosing IV/IM, max 16 mg) can rapidly resolve vomiting in acute FPIES. Mechanism: FPIES involves serotonin release from intestinal enterochromaffin cells → stimulates vagal afferents → vomiting center activation. Ondansetron blocks 5-HT3 receptors. Given during supervised food challenges as rescue. Also: aggressive IV fluid resuscitation for dehydration/shock. Steroids may be used for severe/refractory episodes.
Antihistamines are ineffective in FPIES because it is not histamine-mediated (non-IgE, no mast cell degranulation).
Epinephrine is for IgE-mediated anaphylaxis. FPIES is non-IgE-mediated, and epinephrine does not address the underlying pathophysiology (though it can be used for severe hypotension as rescue).

questions.explanation: Ondansetron (IV or IM, 0.15 mg/kg, max 16 mg) is the recommended treatment for acute FPIES episodes per consensus guidelines. It rapidly controls the profuse vomiting and may help prevent progression to dehydration and lethargy. IV fluid bolus (normal saline 20 mL/kg) addresses hypovolemia. Oral cromolyn sodium has no role in acute FPIES management. Oral diphenhydramine targets IgE-mediated histamine release, which is not the mechanism of FPIES. IM epinephrine is not first-line for FPIES unless there is hemodynamic instability suggesting anaphylaxis overlap.

questions.stem: What is the natural history of FPIES?

choices.text: It persists lifelong and requires permanent food avoidance
choices.feedback: Unlike some IgE-mediated food allergies, FPIES has a favorable natural history in most children.

choices.text: It converts to IgE-mediated allergy in 50% of cases
choices.feedback: Conversion to IgE-mediated allergy occurs in only ~5% of FPIES cases (atypical FPIES), not 50%.

choices.text: Most children outgrow FPIES by age 3
choices.feedback: Correct! FPIES typically resolves by age 3-5 years. Resolution rates: milk/soy FPIES ~60-90% by age 3; grain FPIES may resolve later. Confirmed by supervised oral food challenge (OFC) — the only way to determine tolerance. OFC protocol: graded doses over 2-4 hours with 4-6 hour observation. IV access recommended. Have ondansetron and fluids available. Some patients (~5%) develop IgE sensitization over time (atypical FPIES) with potentially worse prognosis.

choices.text: FPIES only resolves with oral immunotherapy
choices.feedback: OIT is used for IgE-mediated food allergy. FPIES resolves spontaneously with age; management is strict avoidance until outgrown, confirmed by supervised challenge.

questions.explanation: Most children outgrow FPIES by age 3-5 years, particularly for milk and soy triggers. FPIES does not persist lifelong in the majority of cases. It does not convert to IgE-mediated allergy in 50% of cases — while a small percentage (~5-20%) may develop concurrent IgE sensitization (atypical FPIES), this is not the expected natural history. Oral immunotherapy is not an established treatment for FPIES — resolution occurs through natural immune maturation, confirmed by supervised oral food challenges.

questions.stem: Which foods are the most common triggers for solid-food FPIES in infants?

choices.feedback: Wheat can cause FPIES but is less common than rice/oats. Shellfish FPIES is uncommon in infants.

Correct! Solid-food FPIES triggers: rice (#1 in the US), oats, sweet potato, banana, avocado, egg, fish. Notably, grains are MORE common triggers in solid-food FPIES than the classic IgE-mediated allergens (peanut, tree nut). Milk and soy are the most common triggers for liquid-food FPIES in young infants. ~30-40% of FPIES patients react to multiple foods. Breastfed infants can develop FPIES to maternal dietary proteins (rare).
Sesame and mustard are emerging IgE-mediated allergens but are not typical FPIES triggers.
Peanut and tree nuts are common IgE-mediated allergens but rarely cause FPIES.

questions.explanation: Rice and oats (grains) are the most common solid-food FPIES triggers in infants in the United States. This is clinically important because rice cereal is often recommended as a first complementary food, and FPIES reactions may be the first allergic manifestation. Other solid-food triggers include barley, sweet potato, squash, and fish. Wheat and shellfish are less common FPIES triggers. Sesame and mustard are more associated with IgE-mediated allergy. Peanut and tree nuts are overwhelmingly IgE-mediated allergens, not typical FPIES triggers.
questions.citation: FPIES. StatPearls. [Open Access] | JACI FPIES Consensus Guidelines. [Open Access]

questions.stem: How is FPIES diagnosed?

choices.text: Serum specific IgE and skin prick testing
choices.feedback: IgE-based tests are NEGATIVE in FPIES (it is non-IgE-mediated). They cannot diagnose FPIES.
Endoscopy is not routinely needed for FPIES diagnosis. Biopsy may show non-specific findings (villous atrophy, eosinophilic infiltration) but is not diagnostic.

choices.text: Elimination diet followed by serum tryptase measurement
choices.feedback: Tryptase is elevated in IgE-mediated anaphylaxis (mast cell activation). It is typically NORMAL in FPIES because mast cells are not the primary effector cells.

choices.text: Clinical history of reproducible episodes + confirmed by supervised oral food challenge
choices.feedback: Correct! FPIES diagnosis is primarily clinical: reproducible symptom pattern (repetitive vomiting 1-4 hours post-ingestion, ± diarrhea at 5-10 hours, ± lethargy/pallor) + improvement with food avoidance. No specific biomarker. Confirmed by supervised oral food challenge (gold standard). During acute episode: neutrophilia with left shift, thrombocytosis, metabolic acidosis may support diagnosis. Atopy patch testing and fecal biomarkers are investigational but not standard.

questions.explanation: FPIES is diagnosed primarily by clinical history: reproducible episodes of profuse vomiting (±diarrhea) occurring 1-4 hours after ingestion of a specific food, resolving with food avoidance, and confirmed by supervised oral food challenge (OFC) in a medical setting equipped for IV access and resuscitation. There are no validated biomarkers — SPT and specific IgE are typically negative. Endoscopy with biopsy is not required for diagnosis (though it may show eosinophilic infiltration if performed). Elimination diet is part of management, but serum tryptase is not a diagnostic tool for FPIES.

questions.stem: A patient presents with recurrent flushing, abdominal cramping, diarrhea, and anaphylaxis. Skin exam shows urticaria pigmentosa (brown macules that urticate on stroking). What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Pheochromocytoma causes episodic hypertension, headache, palpitations, and diaphoresis — but not urticaria pigmentosa or GI symptoms.

Correct! Systemic mastocytosis: clonal mast cell proliferation affecting skin and ≥1 extracutaneous organ. Urticaria pigmentosa (Darier sign: stroking → local urticaria) is the characteristic skin finding. Symptoms from mast cell mediator release: flushing, pruritus, abdominal pain, diarrhea, anaphylaxis, bone pain. Major criterion: multifocal dense mast cell infiltrates on bone marrow biopsy. Minor criteria: serum tryptase >20 ng/mL, aberrant CD25+ mast cells, KIT D816V mutation, >25% spindle-shaped mast cells. Diagnosis: 1 major + 1 minor, or 3 minor criteria.
Carcinoid causes flushing and diarrhea but does NOT cause urticaria pigmentosa. The skin findings are pathognomonic for mastocytosis.
HAE causes recurrent angioedema without urticaria. The urticaria pigmentosa and systemic mediator symptoms point to mastocytosis.

questions.explanation: Recurrent flushing, abdominal cramping, diarrhea, and anaphylaxis in an adult with elevated baseline serum tryptase strongly suggests systemic mastocytosis. Diagnosis requires evaluation per WHO criteria: bone marrow biopsy showing multifocal mast cell aggregates, KIT D816V mutation, aberrant CD2/CD25 expression on mast cells, and baseline tryptase >20 ng/mL. Management includes H1/H2 antihistamines, leukotriene antagonists, trigger avoidance, and epinephrine auto-injectors for anaphylaxis risk.

questions.stem: What is the most useful serum biomarker for systemic mastocytosis?

choices.feedback: Urine 5-HIAA is the screening test for carcinoid syndrome (serotonin-producing tumors), not mastocytosis.

Correct! Baseline serum tryptase >20 ng/mL is a minor WHO criterion for systemic mastocytosis. Tryptase is constitutively secreted by mast cells — baseline level reflects total mast cell burden. In anaphylaxis: acute tryptase peaks at 60-90 minutes (measure within 1-4 hours). Baseline tryptase >11.4 ng/mL suggests increased mast cell burden. Serial tryptase tracks disease progression. Note: elevated tryptase also seen in myeloid neoplasms with eosinophilia and hereditary alpha-tryptasemia.
Total IgE is elevated in atopic conditions but is not specific for mastocytosis.
Histamine is released during mast cell activation but has a very short half-life (minutes) making it impractical for screening. Tryptase is more stable and useful.

questions.explanation: Serum tryptase is the most useful serum biomarker for systemic mastocytosis. A baseline level >20 ng/mL is a WHO minor diagnostic criterion reflecting increased mast cell burden. Serial tryptase measurements track disease progression or treatment response. Acute elevations during symptomatic episodes confirm mast cell activation. Total tryptase (reflecting both pro-tryptase from mast cell proliferation and mature tryptase from degranulation) is the standard clinical assay.

questions.stem: What mutation is found in >90% of adult systemic mastocytosis cases?

choices.feedback: JAK2 V617F is associated with polycythemia vera and other myeloproliferative neoplasms, not mastocytosis.
BCR-ABL (Philadelphia chromosome) is the hallmark of CML. Mastocytosis is driven by KIT mutations.
BRAF V600E is associated with melanoma, hairy cell leukemia, and Erdheim-Chester disease, not mastocytosis.

Correct! KIT D816V gain-of-function mutation: present in >90% of adult systemic mastocytosis. KIT is a receptor tyrosine kinase (CD117) that regulates mast cell growth, survival, and differentiation. D816V → constitutive activation → clonal mast cell proliferation. Imatinib (KIT inhibitor) does NOT work for D816V (conformational resistance). Midostaurin and avapritinib are active against D816V. Testing: PCR on bone marrow or peripheral blood.

questions.explanation: KIT D816V is the hallmark somatic mutation in >90% of adult systemic mastocytosis. This gain-of-function point mutation at codon 816 (aspartate to valine) in the KIT tyrosine kinase receptor causes constitutive activation, driving uncontrolled mast cell proliferation. Detection by PCR or next-generation sequencing in bone marrow is a WHO minor diagnostic criterion. The mutation confers resistance to imatinib but sensitivity to midostaurin and avapritinib.

questions.stem: What triggers should mastocytosis patients avoid to prevent mast cell degranulation?

choices.text: Only known food allergens based on IgE testing
choices.feedback: Mastocytosis patients can have non-IgE-mediated degranulation from many triggers beyond just food allergens.

choices.text: Extreme temperatures, alcohol, opioids, NSAIDs, insect stings, physical/emotional stress, and certain medications
choices.feedback: Correct! Mast cell degranulation triggers in mastocytosis: physical (extreme heat/cold, friction, exercise), pharmacologic (opioids — morphine/codeine directly degranulate mast cells; NSAIDs; alcohol; radiocontrast media; some anesthetic agents — avoid succinylcholine, prefer fentanyl over morphine), biologic (Hymenoptera stings — life-threatening anaphylaxis, recommend VIT), emotional stress. All patients should carry 2 epinephrine auto-injectors. Premedication protocols before procedures: cetirizine + famotidine + montelukast ± prednisone.

choices.text: Only insect stings, since these are IgE-mediated
choices.feedback: While Hymenoptera stings are a major risk, mastocytosis patients must avoid MANY non-IgE triggers that directly activate mast cells.
Physical triggers (temperature changes, exercise, friction) and emotional stress can also provoke dangerous mast cell mediator release.

questions.explanation: Mastocytosis patients must avoid triggers that cause mast cell degranulation: extreme temperatures (hot baths, cold exposure), alcohol, opioid analgesics (morphine, codeine), NSAIDs, Hymenoptera stings (bee/wasp), vigorous exercise, emotional stress, and certain medications (vancomycin rapid infusion, radiocontrast, neuromuscular blocking agents). These triggers can precipitate severe mediator release reactions including anaphylaxis. Patients should carry epinephrine auto-injectors at all times and wear medical alert identification.

questions.stem: A patient with known systemic mastocytosis develops anaphylaxis from a yellow jacket sting. What long-term therapy is recommended?

choices.feedback: Omalizumab can be used as adjunct to VIT or if VIT is not tolerated, but it is not the standard replacement for VIT in mastocytosis with venom anaphylaxis.

choices.text: Oral cromolyn sodium for mast cell stabilization
choices.feedback: Oral cromolyn treats GI symptoms in mastocytosis but does NOT protect against venom-induced anaphylaxis. VIT is the specific intervention.

Correct! Venom immunotherapy (VIT) is strongly recommended for mastocytosis patients with venom anaphylaxis — and should be continued LIFELONG (unlike general population where 3-5 years may suffice). Mastocytosis patients have higher risk of fatal anaphylaxis from Hymenoptera stings and higher relapse rates if VIT is discontinued. Pretreatment with omalizumab may improve VIT safety and tolerability. All mastocytosis patients should also carry 2 epinephrine auto-injectors regardless of VIT status.
Epinephrine is essential but there is a specific immunotherapy intervention for venom-allergic mastocytosis patients.

questions.explanation: Patients with systemic mastocytosis who experience anaphylaxis from Hymenoptera stings require lifelong venom immunotherapy (VIT). Unlike the general population where VIT may be discontinued after 3-5 years, mastocytosis patients have a persistent increased mast cell burden that confers ongoing anaphylaxis risk. Studies show that discontinuing VIT in mastocytosis patients leads to recurrence of systemic sting reactions. VIT should be maintained indefinitely regardless of negative re-sting challenge results.
questions.citation: Mastocytosis. StatPearls. [Open Access] | Allergy Immunotherapy. StatPearls. NBK535367. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A patient presents with recurrent episodes of non-pruritic angioedema affecting the face and extremities, plus episodic severe abdominal pain, WITHOUT urticaria. Family history reveals similar episodes. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: ACE-inhibitor angioedema is also bradykinin-mediated and presents similarly, but lacks the family history and episodic pattern from childhood. Always ask about medications.
Anaphylaxis includes urticaria, bronchospasm, hypotension, and responds to epinephrine. HAE presents with isolated angioedema without urticaria and does NOT respond to epinephrine.
CSU with angioedema would include urticaria (hives) and typically pruritus. The ABSENCE of urticaria and family history points to HAE.

Correct! HAE: autosomal dominant, caused by C1-inhibitor (C1-INH) deficiency or dysfunction. Key features: recurrent angioedema WITHOUT urticaria or pruritus (bradykinin-mediated, NOT histamine-mediated), episodic abdominal pain (intestinal wall edema mimicking acute abdomen), potential fatal laryngeal edema. Antihistamines and epinephrine are INEFFECTIVE. Types: I (85%): low C1-INH level; II (15%): normal level, reduced function; III: normal C1-INH, gain-of-function Factor XII mutations. Diagnosis: low C4 (screening), then C1-INH level + function.

questions.explanation: Recurrent non-pruritic angioedema affecting the face, extremities, and abdomen (causing severe abdominal pain from intestinal wall edema) without urticaria is the classic presentation of hereditary angioedema (HAE). HAE is caused by C1-inhibitor deficiency (Type I, 85%) or dysfunction (Type II, 15%), leading to unregulated bradykinin production via the contact activation pathway. The absence of urticaria and pruritus distinguishes HAE from histamine-mediated angioedema. Family history is present in ~75% of cases.

questions.stem: Why are antihistamines and epinephrine ineffective for HAE attacks?

choices.feedback: HAE is not caused by complement-mediated cell destruction. The swelling mechanism is different.

choices.text: HAE is mediated by bradykinin, not histamine
choices.feedback: Correct! HAE pathophysiology: C1-INH normally inhibits kallikrein and Factor XIIa. C1-INH deficiency → uncontrolled kallikrein activity → excessive cleavage of high-molecular-weight kininogen → overproduction of bradykinin → increased vascular permeability → angioedema. Since histamine and mast cells are NOT involved: antihistamines, corticosteroids, and epinephrine do NOT work. Effective treatments target the bradykinin pathway: icatibant (bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist), ecallantide (kallikrein inhibitor), C1-INH replacement (Berinert, Cinryze), lanadelumab (anti-kallikrein mAb for prophylaxis).

choices.text: The swelling occurs too deeply in submucosal tissue for medications to reach
choices.feedback: Medication delivery is not the issue. The ineffectiveness is due to the pathway: bradykinin, not histamine, drives the swelling.

choices.text: HAE patients develop tachyphylaxis to these medications
choices.feedback: Tachyphylaxis is not the issue. These medications target the wrong pathway entirely.

questions.explanation: HAE attacks are mediated by bradykinin, not histamine. Bradykinin is generated through unregulated activation of the contact/kallikrein-kinin system due to C1-inhibitor deficiency. Antihistamines target histamine H1 receptors, corticosteroids suppress inflammation, and epinephrine causes vasoconstriction through adrenergic receptors — none of these pathways address bradykinin-mediated vascular permeability. Effective HAE treatments target the bradykinin pathway: C1-inhibitor replacement, icatibant (bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist), or ecallantide (kallikrein inhibitor).

questions.stem: What is the best screening test for HAE?

choices.feedback: IgE is not relevant to HAE (which is bradykinin-mediated, not IgE-mediated). IgE testing is for allergic conditions.

Correct! C4 is the best screening test — LOW in ~95% of HAE patients even between attacks (C1-INH normally inhibits C1s, which cleaves C4; without C1-INH → constitutive C4 consumption). Normal C4 essentially rules out HAE Types I and II. If C4 low → confirm with: C1-INH quantitative level (low in Type I), C1-INH functional assay (low in Type II, level may be normal). C3 is typically NORMAL in HAE (unlike acquired angioedema where C1q is low). C1q normal in hereditary HAE, low in acquired C1-INH deficiency.
Tryptase is a mast cell marker for mastocytosis/anaphylaxis, not HAE.
CH50 tests the classical complement pathway overall and may be low in HAE, but C4 is more sensitive and specific as a screening test.

questions.explanation: Complement C4 level is the best screening test for HAE. C4 is consumed during complement activation and is almost always low (<50% of normal) during and between HAE attacks due to unregulated C1-inhibitor activity. A normal C4 essentially excludes HAE Types I and II. If C4 is low, confirmatory testing includes C1-inhibitor quantitative level (low in Type I) and C1-inhibitor functional activity (low in both Type I and Type II). C3 is typically normal in HAE. C1q is normal in hereditary forms but low in acquired C1-inhibitor deficiency.

questions.stem: What is first-line acute treatment for an HAE attack?

choices.feedback: Correct! Acute HAE treatment: C1-INH concentrate (Berinert IV, Ruconest IV) — replaces the deficient protein directly. Icatibant (SC) — bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist, blocks bradykinin at its receptor. Ecallantide (SC) — kallikrein inhibitor (risk of anaphylaxis, administer in healthcare setting). All should be available for on-demand self-administration. Treat ALL attacks early — laryngeal attacks are potentially fatal and unpredictable. For prophylaxis: lanadelumab (anti-kallikrein mAb SC q2-4 weeks) or berotralstat (oral kallikrein inhibitor).
IVIG is used for autoimmune conditions and primary immunodeficiencies, not HAE. It does not address the bradykinin pathway.

choices.text: Fresh frozen plasma as the only option
choices.feedback: FFP contains C1-INH and can help in emergencies when specific therapies are unavailable, but it also contains substrates (FXII, prekallikrein) that could theoretically worsen attacks. Specific C1-INH replacement or bradykinin pathway inhibitors are preferred.
Corticosteroids and antihistamines are ineffective in HAE (bradykinin-mediated, not histamine-mediated).

questions.explanation: C1-inhibitor concentrate (plasma-derived: Berinert, Cinryze; recombinant: Ruconest) is first-line acute treatment for HAE attacks. It directly replaces the deficient or dysfunctional protein, restoring regulation of the contact activation and complement pathways and reducing bradykinin production. Other acute treatments include icatibant (subcutaneous bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist) and ecallantide (subcutaneous kallikrein inhibitor). Fresh frozen plasma can be used if specific therapies are unavailable. Traditional allergic angioedema treatments (epinephrine, antihistamines, corticosteroids) are ineffective.

questions.stem: Which medication class can trigger HAE attacks and should be avoided?

choices.feedback: Statins are not known to trigger HAE attacks. ACE inhibitors and estrogen-containing medications are the key triggers to avoid.
Beta-blockers are not known to trigger HAE attacks (though they may complicate anaphylaxis treatment in other settings).
PPIs do not affect the bradykinin/kallikrein pathway and are safe in HAE.

Correct! ACE inhibitors are absolutely CONTRAINDICATED in HAE. ACE (angiotensin-converting enzyme) = kininase II, which degrades bradykinin. ACE inhibitors → decreased bradykinin degradation → even more bradykinin accumulation in patients already overproducing it → severe/fatal angioedema. Also avoid: estrogen-containing medications (OCP, HRT) — estrogen increases Factor XII and kallikrein expression → more bradykinin production → attack trigger. Use ARBs instead of ACEi; use progesterone-only contraceptives instead of combined OCP.

questions.explanation: ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in HAE because ACE (angiotensin-converting enzyme) is a key enzyme that degrades bradykinin. ACE inhibitor use blocks bradykinin degradation, causing further accumulation of the mediator that is already overproduced in HAE due to C1-inhibitor deficiency. This can precipitate severe, potentially life-threatening angioedema attacks. ACE inhibitors can also cause bradykinin-mediated angioedema in non-HAE patients (0.1-0.7% incidence). Angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs) do not inhibit ACE and are generally considered safe alternatives.

questions.stem: A patient presents with recurrent episodes of non-pruritic angioedema of the face, extremities, and abdomen without urticaria. ACE inhibitors have been excluded. C4 is consistently low. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: CSU-associated angioedema is typically accompanied by urticaria (hives) and is pruritic. This presentation lacks both features.

Correct! HAE: autosomal dominant deficiency of C1 esterase inhibitor (C1-INH). Type I (85%): low C1-INH level and function. Type II (15%): normal level, low function (dysfunctional protein). Type III: normal C1-INH (Factor XII mutations, often estrogen-dependent). Key features: NON-pruritic, NON-urticarial angioedema; abdominal attacks (GI edema mimicking acute abdomen); laryngeal edema (life-threatening). Screening: C4 is LOW between AND during attacks. Confirm with C1-INH quantitative + functional assays.
Idiopathic angioedema is a diagnosis of exclusion. Consistently low C4 points to HAE — a treatable inherited condition.

choices.text: Allergic angioedema from occult food allergy
choices.feedback: Allergic angioedema is IgE-mediated and usually accompanied by urticaria and pruritus, and responds to antihistamines/epinephrine.

questions.explanation: Non-pruritic angioedema without urticaria, affecting face, extremities, and abdomen with a positive family history, is classic HAE due to C1-inhibitor deficiency. HAE should be suspected when angioedema is recurrent, occurs without urticaria/pruritus, involves the GI tract (causing severe abdominal pain that may mimic surgical abdomen), and does not respond to antihistamines/corticosteroids/epinephrine. Screening with C4 level followed by C1-inhibitor quantitative and functional assays confirms the diagnosis.

questions.stem: Why are antihistamines, corticosteroids, and epinephrine INEFFECTIVE for HAE attacks?

choices.text: The dosing is too low for HAE
choices.feedback: No dose of antihistamine or steroid will treat HAE because the mechanism is bradykinin, not histamine.

choices.text: HAE is an autoimmune condition requiring immunosuppression
choices.feedback: HAE is a genetic complement deficiency, not autoimmune. Treatment targets the bradykinin pathway.
The issue isn't resistance — the mediator pathway is completely different.

choices.text: HAE is bradykinin-mediated, not histamine-mediated — these drugs target the wrong pathway
choices.feedback: Correct! HAE angioedema is caused by excess bradykinin (not histamine): C1-INH normally inhibits kallikrein and Factor XIIa → without C1-INH, uncontrolled kallikrein activity → excessive bradykinin generation → increased vascular permeability → angioedema. Histamine-targeting drugs (antihistamines, epinephrine, steroids) do NOT affect the bradykinin pathway. HAE-specific treatments target bradykinin directly: icatibant (bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist), ecallantide (kallikrein inhibitor), C1-INH replacement (Berinert, Cinryze, Haegarda), lanadelumab (anti-kallikrein monoclonal antibody for prophylaxis).

questions.explanation: HAE is bradykinin-mediated, not histamine-mediated. C1-inhibitor deficiency leads to unregulated activation of factor XII and kallikrein in the contact activation system, generating excess bradykinin. Bradykinin increases vascular permeability through B2 receptors on endothelial cells, independent of mast cells and histamine. Antihistamines block H1 receptors (irrelevant pathway), corticosteroids suppress inflammatory gene transcription (too slow and wrong mechanism), and epinephrine's vasoconstriction is temporary and does not address the underlying bradykinin excess.

questions.stem: What is the first-line on-demand treatment for an acute HAE attack?

choices.text: C1 esterase inhibitor (C1-INH) concentrate — plasma-derived or recombinant
choices.feedback: Correct! Acute HAE attack treatment: (1) IV C1-INH concentrate (Berinert — plasma-derived, or Ruconest — recombinant): replaces the deficient protein. (2) Icatibant (SC): bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist — blocks bradykinin action directly. (3) Ecallantide (SC): kallikrein inhibitor — blocks bradykinin generation. All are effective within 30-60 minutes. Laryngeal attacks are life-threatening emergencies requiring immediate treatment + airway monitoring. Patients should carry on-demand medication at all times.
Corticosteroids are ineffective for HAE because the mechanism is bradykinin-mediated, not inflammatory.
FFP contains C1-INH and CAN be used if specific treatments are unavailable, but it also contains kallikrein substrates that could theoretically worsen attacks. It is not first-line.
Epinephrine is for IgE-mediated anaphylaxis. It does not treat bradykinin-mediated HAE angioedema.

questions.explanation: C1-INH concentrate is first-line on-demand treatment for acute HAE attacks. Plasma-derived options (Berinert for acute attacks, Cinryze for prophylaxis) and recombinant C1-INH (Ruconest/conestat alfa) directly replace the deficient protein. Administration should occur as early as possible during an attack — earlier treatment leads to faster resolution. Alternative acute treatments include icatibant (bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist, self-injectable) and ecallantide (kallikrein inhibitor). All HAE patients should have on-demand therapy available at all times.

questions.stem: What long-term prophylaxis options are available for patients with frequent HAE attacks?

choices.feedback: ACE inhibitors are CONTRAINDICATED in HAE — they inhibit bradykinin degradation and can trigger or worsen attacks.

choices.text: Lanadelumab (anti-kallikrein monoclonal antibody), subcutaneous C1-INH, or oral berotralstat
choices.feedback: Correct! Modern HAE prophylaxis: Lanadelumab (Takhzyro): anti-kallikrein monoclonal antibody, SC q2-4 weeks, reduces attacks by ~87%. SC C1-INH (Haegarda): twice-weekly SC injection. Berotralstat (Orladeyo): first oral kallikrein inhibitor, daily pill. Older options: attenuated androgens (danazol — limited by virilization, hepatotoxicity), tranexamic acid (antifibrinolytic — less effective). Goal: reduce attack frequency and severity, improve quality of life.

choices.text: Daily antihistamines and avoidance of triggers
choices.feedback: Antihistamines do not prevent HAE attacks because the mechanism is bradykinin, not histamine.
Corticosteroids are NOT effective for HAE prophylaxis. Long-term steroids would add side effects without benefit.

questions.explanation: Long-term prophylaxis options for frequent HAE attacks include: (1) Lanadelumab (Takhzyro) — a monoclonal antibody targeting plasma kallikrein, given subcutaneously every 2-4 weeks, reduces attack frequency by >70%. (2) Subcutaneous C1-INH (Haegarda) — regular replacement therapy given twice weekly. (3) Berotralstat (Orladeyo) — an oral plasma kallikrein inhibitor taken daily. Older options include attenuated androgens (danazol) which upregulate C1-INH hepatic synthesis but carry significant androgenic side effects. IV C1-INH (Cinryze) given every 3-4 days is another option.

questions.stem: Which medication class is CONTRAINDICATED in patients with HAE?

choices.feedback: PPIs are safe in HAE and may be used for GI symptoms. ACE inhibitors are the critical contraindication.
Statins are not contraindicated in HAE. ACE inhibitors are the key drug class to avoid.
Beta-blockers are not specifically contraindicated in HAE, though they should be used cautiously with epinephrine for anaphylaxis management.

Correct! ACE inhibitors inhibit angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE/kininase II), which is responsible for bradykinin degradation. In HAE patients (already bradykinin excess): ACE inhibitors → further impaired bradykinin clearance → can trigger severe, prolonged angioedema attacks. Also: estrogen-containing contraceptives can worsen HAE (estrogen increases Factor XII and kallikrein activity). Use progestin-only contraception instead.

questions.explanation: ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in HAE because they block the degradation of bradykinin — the exact mediator that is already overproduced in HAE due to C1-inhibitor deficiency. ACE inhibitor use can precipitate severe angioedema attacks and worsen disease severity. This applies to all ACE inhibitors (enalapril, lisinopril, ramipril, etc.). ARBs (losartan, valsartan) are acceptable alternatives for hypertension management in HAE patients, as they do not affect bradykinin metabolism.

questions.stem: What is the primary benefit of using a spacer/valved holding chamber (VHC) with a metered-dose inhaler (MDI)?

choices.text: It increases the total dose delivered from the canister
choices.feedback: The canister delivers the same dose regardless. The spacer affects how much reaches the lungs vs deposits in the oropharynx.

choices.text: It converts the medication to a finer mist than a nebulizer
choices.feedback: Spacers do not change particle generation — the MDI creates the aerosol. Spacers allow large particles to settle out, improving the fine-particle fraction that reaches the lungs.

Correct! Spacer/VHC benefits: (1) Holds aerosolized medication in suspension → allows slow inhalation even without perfect coordination with actuation. (2) Reduces particle velocity → less impaction on oropharynx (reduces thrush risk with ICS, reduces local side effects). (3) Filters out large particles → delivers more respirable particles (<5 μm) to lower airways. (4) Lung deposition increases from ~10-15% (MDI alone) to ~20-40% with spacer. Critical for: children, elderly, patients with poor coordination, ANY patient on ICS.

choices.text: It sterilizes the medication before inhalation
choices.feedback: Spacers do not sterilize anything. Their role is aerosol particle management and coordination assistance.

questions.explanation: A spacer/valved holding chamber (VHC) reduces oropharyngeal deposition of inhaled medication by slowing aerosol velocity and allowing larger particles to settle in the spacer rather than impacting the back of the throat. This improves lung deposition from approximately 10-15% to 20-25% of the delivered dose. A spacer does not increase the total dose from the canister — it improves the fraction reaching the lungs. Spacers do not produce finer mist than nebulizers. Spacers do not sterilize medication. The clinical benefit is most important for inhaled corticosteroids, where reduced oropharyngeal deposition decreases the risk of oral candidiasis and dysphonia.

questions.stem: What is the correct technique for using an MDI with a spacer?

choices.text: Shake MDI → attach to spacer → exhale normally → actuate once → slow deep inhalation → hold breath 10 seconds
choices.feedback: Correct! MDI + spacer technique: (1) Shake MDI 5 seconds, (2) Remove cap, attach to spacer, (3) Exhale normally (away from spacer), (4) Place mouthpiece in mouth with seal, (5) Actuate ONE puff into spacer, (6) Slow deep inhalation over 3-5 seconds (or multiple tidal breaths for young children using mask), (7) Hold breath 10 seconds, (8) Wait 30-60 seconds between puffs. Common errors: multiple actuations before inhaling (particles aggregate and settle), breathing too fast, not holding breath.

choices.text: Hold the spacer upside down while inhaling for better delivery
choices.feedback: Spacers should be held horizontally or upright. Inverting them can cause medication to pool and valve malfunction.

choices.text: Actuate 4 puffs into spacer at once, then breathe normally for 1 minute
choices.feedback: Multiple actuations into the spacer before inhaling reduces delivery — particles aggregate and settle on spacer walls. One puff at a time.

choices.text: Exhale into the spacer, actuate MDI, and take rapid deep breath
choices.feedback: Never exhale into the spacer (blows medication out). And rapid deep breathing reduces lung deposition.

questions.explanation: Correct MDI + spacer technique: (1) Shake MDI well, (2) attach MDI to spacer, (3) exhale normally (away from spacer), (4) place mouthpiece in mouth and seal lips, (5) actuate ONE puff into spacer, (6) inhale slowly and deeply over 3-5 seconds, (7) hold breath for approximately 10 seconds, (8) exhale normally. Each puff should be actuated separately — multiple puffs into the spacer at once reduces delivery efficiency as particles settle and interact. Holding the spacer upside down would impair particle delivery. Rapid deep breaths increase oropharyngeal impaction, defeating the spacer's purpose.

questions.stem: What side effect of inhaled corticosteroids is specifically reduced by spacer use and mouth rinsing?

choices.feedback: Adrenal suppression is a systemic effect from absorbed ICS. Spacer use primarily reduces local effects.
Growth suppression is a systemic effect. While spacer use may reduce total systemic absorption slightly, its primary benefit is reducing local oropharyngeal deposition.

Correct! ICS deposition on oral/pharyngeal mucosa → local immunosuppression → Candida overgrowth → thrush. Prevention: (1) Spacer/VHC reduces oropharyngeal deposition by 50-90%. (2) Rinse mouth (gargle and spit) after EVERY ICS dose. (3) Use lowest effective ICS dose. Other local ICS effects reduced by spacer: dysphonia (hoarseness from laryngeal myopathy), cough, throat irritation. If thrush develops: nystatin swish-and-swallow or clotrimazole troches.
Osteoporosis is a systemic effect of high-dose ICS over prolonged periods. Spacer use primarily addresses local oropharyngeal effects.

questions.explanation: Oropharyngeal candidiasis (oral thrush) is the ICS side effect specifically reduced by spacer use and mouth rinsing. ICS particles deposited in the oropharynx create a local immunosuppressive environment favorable for Candida overgrowth. Spacers reduce oropharyngeal deposition, and rinsing/spitting removes residual drug. Adrenal suppression and osteoporosis are systemic effects related to overall absorbed dose, not local oropharyngeal deposition. Growth suppression in children is a systemic concern related to total ICS dose and duration, not amenable to spacer intervention.

questions.stem: At what age can most children transition from MDI + spacer with mask to MDI + spacer with mouthpiece?

choices.feedback: Most 2-year-olds cannot reliably form a seal around a mouthpiece or coordinate inhalation. Mask remains appropriate.
Most children can use a mouthpiece by age 4-5. Waiting until 12 is unnecessary.
6-month-olds cannot form a seal around a mouthpiece or coordinate inhalation. They require a mask.

choices.text: Around age 4-5, when they can form a seal and coordinate slow inhalation
choices.feedback: Correct! Age-based device selection: 0-3 years: MDI + spacer with tight-fitting MASK (tidal breathing — multiple breaths through spacer). 4-5+ years: MDI + spacer with MOUTHPIECE (can coordinate slow deep breath + breath hold). 6+ years: may use DPI (dry powder inhaler) if adequate inspiratory flow. Mask fit is critical: air leaks reduce delivery dramatically. Crying during administration reduces lung deposition (mouth breathing preferred over nasal). Nebulizers are alternatives for any age but less portable and more time-consuming.

questions.explanation: Most children can transition from MDI + spacer with face mask to MDI + spacer with mouthpiece around age 4-5, when they can reliably form a lip seal around the mouthpiece and coordinate slow, steady inhalation. Before this age, face masks are necessary because young children breathe through both nose and mouth and cannot maintain a mouthpiece seal. Age 2 is too young for mouthpiece coordination. Age 12 is unnecessarily late — most children master mouthpiece technique well before this. Six months is far too young for any mouthpiece use.

questions.stem: A patient reports their rescue inhaler is not working as well. What is the most common reason for MDI treatment failure?

choices.feedback: Correct! Studies show 70-90% of patients use MDIs incorrectly. Most common errors: failure to shake canister (suspension medications settle), poor hand-breath coordination (actuating too early/late), breathing too fast (increases oropharyngeal deposition, reduces lung delivery), not holding breath (reduces small-particle deposition), not waiting between puffs, expired medication. Assessment: have patient demonstrate technique at EVERY visit. Even experienced patients develop technique drift. Adding a spacer corrects most coordination errors.
While cold weather can reduce MDI output slightly, technique is overwhelmingly the most common cause of perceived treatment failure.
Bronchodilator resistance is extremely rare. The most common issue is technique-related.

choices.text: Drug interaction with other inhaled medications
choices.feedback: Drug interactions between inhaled medications are rare. Poor technique is the dominant cause of MDI treatment failure.

questions.explanation: Poor inhaler technique is the most common reason for MDI treatment failure, affecting up to 70-80% of patients. Common errors include poor actuation-inhalation coordination, inhaling too fast, not shaking the MDI, actuating multiple puffs simultaneously, and failing to hold breath after inhalation. Before escalating therapy, clinicians should always assess and correct inhaler technique. Canister malfunction from cold weather is rare. Medication resistance does not develop with inhaled bronchodilators or corticosteroids. Drug interactions between inhaled medications are not a recognized cause of treatment failure.

questions.stem: Which asthma phenotype is most likely to respond to anti-IL-5 biologic therapy?

choices.feedback: Isolated EIB is typically managed with pre-exercise SABA or daily ICS and does not require biologic therapy.

choices.text: Severe eosinophilic asthma with persistently elevated blood/sputum eosinophils
choices.feedback: Correct! Eosinophilic asthma: elevated blood eosinophils (≥150-300 cells/μL) and/or sputum eosinophils (≥2%). IL-5 is the key cytokine driving eosinophil maturation, survival, and activation. Anti-IL-5 biologics: mepolizumab, reslizumab (anti-IL-5), benralizumab (anti-IL-5Rα — also depletes eosinophils via ADCC). Reduce exacerbations by 50%, allow oral steroid reduction, improve lung function in eosinophilic phenotype.
Obesity-related asthma is often non-eosinophilic (neutrophilic or pauci-granulocytic) and responds poorly to biologics targeting type 2 inflammation.
Allergic/IgE-driven asthma responds best to anti-IgE therapy (omalizumab). Anti-IL-5 targets a different pathway.

questions.explanation: Anti-IL-5 biologics (mepolizumab, reslizumab) and the anti-IL-5 receptor antibody benralizumab specifically target the eosinophilic pathway. They are indicated for severe eosinophilic asthma with persistently elevated blood eosinophils (typically ≥150-300 cells/μL depending on the agent). Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction is usually managed with short-acting beta-agonists or ICS. Obesity-related asthma is typically T2-low and does not respond to eosinophil-targeting biologics. Allergic asthma with high IgE is better targeted by omalizumab (anti-IgE).

questions.stem: What biomarker helps identify patients with Type 2 (T2-high) asthma who are candidates for biologic therapy?

choices.text: Blood eosinophils, FeNO (fractional exhaled nitric oxide), and serum IgE
choices.feedback: Correct! T2-high asthma biomarkers: Blood eosinophils ≥150-300/μL → eosinophilic phenotype → anti-IL-5 (mepolizumab, benralizumab), anti-IL-4Rα (dupilumab). FeNO ≥25 ppb → airway eosinophilic inflammation, IL-13 driven → responds to ICS, dupilumab, tezepelumab. Total IgE elevated + specific allergen sensitization → allergic phenotype → omalizumab (anti-IgE). T2-low asthma (low eosinophils, low FeNO, low IgE): fewer biologic options, may respond to tezepelumab (anti-TSLP), azithromycin, or bronchial thermoplasty.
Spirometry measures lung function severity but does not identify the inflammatory phenotype needed for biologic selection.
Procalcitonin is a marker for bacterial infection, not asthma phenotyping.
CRP and ESR are nonspecific inflammatory markers not useful for asthma phenotyping.

questions.explanation: T2-high asthma is characterized by eosinophilic airway inflammation driven by IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13. The three key biomarkers that identify T2-high patients are blood eosinophils (≥150-300 cells/μL), FeNO (≥20-25 ppb indicating eosinophilic airway inflammation and iNOS upregulation), and serum total IgE (relevant for omalizumab eligibility, 30-700 IU/mL). Spirometry alone measures airflow obstruction but does not characterize the inflammatory phenotype. Procalcitonin is a bacterial infection marker, and CRP/ESR are nonspecific inflammatory markers not used for asthma phenotyping.

questions.stem: What biologic therapy targets the broadest range of asthma phenotypes, including both T2-high and some T2-low patients?

choices.feedback: Correct! Tezepelumab targets thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), an epithelial-derived alarmin that sits UPSTREAM of the entire type 2 inflammatory cascade. By blocking TSLP: reduces eosinophils, IgE, FeNO, and IL-5/IL-13 simultaneously. NAVIGATOR trial: reduced exacerbations regardless of baseline eosinophil count — the ONLY biologic effective across T2-high AND some T2-low patients. Indicated for severe asthma ages ≥12 regardless of phenotype. Represents a paradigm shift in asthma biologics.
Mepolizumab is effective only in eosinophilic asthma (T2-high, eosinophil-dominant). It does not benefit T2-low patients.
Omalizumab only targets allergic (IgE-mediated) asthma — a subset of T2-high.
Dupilumab blocks IL-4 and IL-13 signaling — effective in eosinophilic and allergic asthma (T2-high) but not proven in T2-low asthma.

questions.explanation: Tezepelumab (anti-TSLP) targets thymic stromal lymphopoietin, an epithelial alarmin released upstream of the entire T2 inflammatory cascade. By blocking TSLP, it reduces eosinophils, FeNO, and IgE levels. It is the only biologic indicated for severe asthma without phenotype or biomarker limitations, meaning it can benefit both T2-high and some T2-low patients. Mepolizumab and benralizumab target only the IL-5/eosinophil pathway. Omalizumab targets only IgE-mediated allergic inflammation. Dupilumab blocks IL-4/IL-13 but is still T2-pathway specific.
questions.citation: Asthma. StatPearls. NBK430901. [Open Access] | Tezepelumab: anti-TSLP antibody, indicated for severe asthma without phenotype limitations. PMC9316404. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A patient with allergic asthma has a total IgE of 450 IU/mL, positive skin tests to multiple aeroallergens, and persistent symptoms despite high-dose ICS/LABA. Which biologic is most appropriate?

choices.feedback: Tezepelumab is approved for severe asthma broadly, but omalizumab specifically targets the IgE pathway most relevant to this patient's allergic phenotype.
Mepolizumab targets eosinophilic inflammation. While this patient may have eosinophilia, the allergic phenotype (high IgE, positive SPT) makes omalizumab the most targeted choice.
Benralizumab targets the IL-5 receptor and is best for eosinophilic phenotype regardless of allergic status.

Correct! Omalizumab: monoclonal antibody that binds free IgE → prevents IgE binding to FcεRI on mast cells/basophils → reduces allergic cascade. Indications: moderate-severe allergic asthma (positive SPT/sIgE + total IgE 30-1500 IU/mL) ages ≥6. Dosing based on weight and IgE level (q2 or q4 weeks SC). Also reduces anaphylaxis risk (increases threshold) — now also approved for food allergy (reduces accidental reaction severity). Monitor for anaphylaxis risk (carry epinephrine after injections).

questions.explanation: Omalizumab (anti-IgE) is the first-line biologic for severe allergic asthma with documented perennial allergen sensitization and IgE levels between 30-700 IU/mL (this patient has IgE 450 IU/mL with positive skin tests). Dosing is weight- and IgE-based, given subcutaneously every 2-4 weeks. Tezepelumab targets TSLP and is phenotype-agnostic but would not be the most targeted choice when the allergic phenotype is clearly established. Mepolizumab and benralizumab target IL-5/eosinophilic pathways and are preferred when eosinophilia is the dominant feature rather than IgE-mediated allergy.

questions.stem: How does FeNO (fractional exhaled nitric oxide) help guide asthma management?

choices.text: FeNO diagnoses asthma versus COPD definitively
choices.feedback: While high FeNO suggests eosinophilic inflammation (more common in asthma), it is not diagnostic. Some COPD patients have high FeNO (asthma-COPD overlap).

choices.text: FeNO ≥25 ppb indicates eosinophilic/T2 airway inflammation
choices.feedback: Correct! FeNO reflects IL-13-driven inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) expression in airway epithelium → surrogate for eosinophilic/T2 inflammation. FeNO ≥25 ppb (adults): suggests T2-high → likely to respond to ICS and anti-T2 biologics. FeNO <25 ppb: less likely to respond to ICS escalation → consider alternative diagnoses or T2-low phenotype. Serial FeNO monitoring: helps assess ICS adherence (non-adherence → rising FeNO), guides therapy adjustment, and predicts exacerbation risk. Caveats: elevated by viral URI, reduced by smoking and bronchoconstriction.
Airway obstruction is measured by spirometry (FEV1). FeNO measures airway inflammation type.

choices.text: FeNO is only useful at initial diagnosis, not for monitoring
choices.feedback: FeNO is valuable for both initial phenotyping AND longitudinal monitoring (adherence, treatment response, exacerbation risk).

questions.explanation: FeNO ≥25 ppb indicates eosinophilic/T2 airway inflammation because eosinophilic inflammation upregulates inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) in respiratory epithelium, increasing exhaled NO. Elevated FeNO predicts corticosteroid responsiveness, helps identify biologic candidates, and can monitor adherence to ICS therapy. FeNO does not definitively distinguish asthma from COPD (overlap exists). It measures airway inflammation, not obstruction severity (that is spirometry's role). FeNO is useful for ongoing monitoring, not just initial diagnosis — serial measurements guide ICS dose adjustments and assess treatment response.

questions.stem: What does a positive skin prick test (SPT) indicate?

choices.text: The patient is clinically allergic and must avoid the allergen
choices.feedback: A positive SPT indicates sensitization (presence of specific IgE) but NOT necessarily clinical allergy. Many sensitized individuals are tolerant.

choices.text: The patient will develop anaphylaxis on next exposure
choices.feedback: SPT wheal size does NOT reliably predict reaction severity. A small wheal does not mean mild reaction, and a large wheal does not guarantee anaphylaxis.
SPT detects IgE, not IgG. IgG to foods is a normal physiologic response to food exposure and is not clinically meaningful.

choices.text: The patient is SENSITIZED (has allergen-specific IgE) but clinical relevance must be confirmed by history
choices.feedback: Correct! SPT detects cutaneous mast cell-bound IgE. A wheal ≥3 mm greater than negative control = positive = sensitization. Sensitivity ~90%, specificity ~50%. Positive predictive value varies widely (50-100% depending on allergen and clinical context). MUST be correlated with clinical history. Sensitized ≠ allergic (many people have positive SPT to foods they tolerate). The clinical decision to avoid an allergen depends on concordance between testing and symptoms, not testing alone.

questions.explanation: A positive SPT indicates the presence of allergen-specific IgE on cutaneous mast cells (sensitization), not necessarily clinical allergy. Sensitization rates exceed clinical allergy rates — up to 50% of individuals with positive SPTs to aeroallergens may be clinically tolerant. Clinical relevance requires correlation with symptoms upon natural allergen exposure. A positive SPT does not predict anaphylaxis on next exposure. SPT detects IgE-mediated (Type I) sensitization, not IgG-mediated responses.
questions.citation: Allergy Immunotherapy. StatPearls. NBK535367. [Open Access] | Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: When should serum specific IgE (ImmunoCAP) be preferred over skin prick testing?

choices.text: When the patient has dermatographism, extensive eczema, cannot stop antihistamines, or has high anaphylaxis risk
choices.feedback: Correct! Specific IgE blood testing preferred when SPT cannot be reliably performed: Dermatographism (false-positive SPT), extensive skin disease (no uninvolved skin for testing), cannot discontinue antihistamines (suppress SPT — cetirizine needs 3-10 day washout), high anaphylaxis risk (SPT can rarely trigger systemic reaction), recent anaphylaxis (may be false-negative due to mast cell depletion). Also useful for: component-resolved diagnostics (CRD), monitoring allergen immunotherapy response, and standardized quantitative results.

choices.text: As a first-line screening test for all patients
choices.feedback: SPT is generally preferred as first-line when available — it's faster, cheaper, and provides immediate results with equivalent or better sensitivity.

choices.text: When skin testing shows equivocal results and you need a definitive answer
choices.feedback: Specific IgE is no more definitive than SPT — both detect sensitization. A positive sIgE does not confirm clinical allergy any more than a positive SPT does.

choices.text: Only for food allergy, never for aeroallergens
choices.feedback: Specific IgE testing is valid for both food and aeroallergens. The choice between SPT and sIgE depends on patient factors, not allergen type.

questions.explanation: Serum specific IgE testing (ImmunoCAP) is preferred over SPT when: (1) dermatographism causes false-positive SPTs, (2) extensive eczema/dermatitis limits available skin testing sites, (3) antihistamines cannot be discontinued (e.g., chronic urticaria), or (4) the patient has a history of severe anaphylaxis making SPT riskier. SPT remains the first-line test when feasible due to higher sensitivity, lower cost, and immediate results. Serum IgE is not more definitive than SPT for equivocal results — both measure sensitization. Serum IgE testing works for both food and aeroallergens.

questions.stem: What is component-resolved diagnostics (CRD) and when is it useful?

choices.text: DNA testing for genetic predisposition to allergies
choices.feedback: CRD is an immunologic test measuring specific IgE antibodies, not a genetic test.

choices.text: Measuring IgG4 levels to different food components
choices.feedback: IgG4 food panel testing has no validated clinical utility. CRD specifically measures IgE to allergenic protein components.

choices.text: Measuring IgE to individual allergenic proteins within a source to improve diagnostic specificity
choices.feedback: Correct! CRD: measures IgE to individual allergenic components rather than whole extracts. Examples: Peanut: Ara h 2 IgE (storage protein) = high risk for systemic reactions; Ara h 8 IgE (PR-10 homolog) = likely oral allergy syndrome only (cross-reactive with birch pollen, mild). Milk: casein IgE = persistent allergy; whey proteins = may outgrow. Tree nuts: seed storage proteins = genuine allergy; PR-10 = cross-reactivity (often tolerated). CRD helps avoid unnecessary dietary restrictions and predicts likelihood of outgrowing allergy.

choices.text: Measuring total IgE levels by component fractions
choices.feedback: CRD does not fractionate total IgE. It measures IgE to SPECIFIC allergenic proteins within a source.

questions.explanation: Component-resolved diagnostics (CRD) measures IgE to individual allergenic proteins (molecular components) within an allergen source, improving diagnostic specificity. For example, peanut CRD distinguishes sensitization to stable storage proteins (Ara h 2 — high anaphylaxis risk) from labile PR-10 proteins (Ara h 8 — oral allergy syndrome only). This helps predict reaction severity and guide management. CRD is not DNA/genetic testing. IgG4 measurement to food components is unvalidated and not recommended. Total IgE fractionation is not CRD.
questions.citation: Component Resolved Diagnostics. JACI Practice Parameter. [Open Access]

questions.stem: Which allergy testing methods have NO scientific validity and should NOT be used?

choices.text: Skin prick testing and serum specific IgE
choices.feedback: Both SPT and sIgE are validated, evidence-based diagnostic tools. They are the cornerstone of allergy diagnosis.

choices.text: IgG food panels, applied kinesiology, cytotoxic testing, and electrodermal testing
choices.feedback: Correct! Unvalidated allergy tests: IgG/IgG4 food panels (IgG to food = normal immune response to food exposure, not pathologic), applied kinesiology (muscle testing while holding allergen), cytotoxic/leukocyte activation tests, electrodermal testing (Vega test), hair analysis, provocation-neutralization. These lead to unnecessary dietary restrictions, malnutrition, anxiety, and delayed appropriate care. Major allergy societies (AAAAI, EAACI, ACAAI) recommend AGAINST these tests.
Oral food challenges (especially DBPCFC) are the GOLD STANDARD for food allergy diagnosis.

choices.text: Patch testing and basophil activation testing
choices.feedback: Patch testing is validated for contact dermatitis (Type IV hypersensitivity). Basophil activation testing is an emerging validated research/clinical tool.

questions.explanation: IgG food panels, applied kinesiology, cytotoxic testing (ALCAT), and electrodermal testing (Vega testing) have no scientific validity for diagnosing allergies and are explicitly not recommended by AAAAI, ACAAI, and EAACI. IgG antibodies to foods represent normal immune exposure, not allergy. These unvalidated tests lead to unnecessary dietary restrictions and delayed appropriate diagnosis. SPT and serum specific IgE are validated first-line allergy tests. Oral food challenges are the gold standard for food allergy diagnosis. Patch testing (for contact dermatitis) and basophil activation testing are scientifically validated methods.
questions.citation: AAAAI Position Statement on Unproven Diagnostic Tests. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A patient's SPT to cat is 8 mm wheal but they report living with cats without symptoms. What should you advise?

choices.text: They are sensitized but clinically tolerant
choices.feedback: Correct! This illustrates the key principle: sensitization ≠ clinical allergy. A positive SPT means IgE antibodies are present, but without concordant symptoms, the patient is clinically tolerant. No intervention needed for asymptomatic sensitization. Important: if the patient develops nasal/ocular/pulmonary symptoms upon increased exposure, the sensitization becomes clinically relevant. Allergen testing should always be interpreted in the context of clinical history — NEVER used as a sole basis for allergen avoidance or dietary restriction.

choices.text: Immediate cat removal — the SPT is positive so they're allergic
choices.feedback: A positive SPT alone does not mandate avoidance. Clinical correlation is essential.

choices.text: Repeat testing in 6 months — the result might have been false positive
choices.feedback: The result is a true positive for sensitization. Repeating the test will likely give the same result. The issue is interpretation, not test validity.
Immunotherapy is indicated for clinically relevant allergy causing symptoms, not for asymptomatic sensitization.

questions.explanation: A positive SPT with no clinical symptoms represents sensitization without clinical allergy (clinically tolerant). Up to 50% of individuals sensitized to cat allergen (Fel d 1) on SPT tolerate cat exposure without symptoms. Management should be guided by clinical history, not SPT results alone. Immediate cat removal based solely on SPT positivity is inappropriate in an asymptomatic patient. The result is likely a true positive (sensitization) rather than false positive, so repeating adds no value. Prophylactic immunotherapy for an asymptomatic sensitized patient is not indicated — AIT requires both sensitization and correlating symptoms.

questions.stem: An emergency medicine resident reviews anaphylaxis fatality data. Which factor is most consistently associated with fatal anaphylaxis outcomes?

choices.text: Failure to administer IV corticosteroids within 30 minutes

questions.explanation: Delayed or absent epinephrine administration is the factor most consistently associated with fatal anaphylaxis outcomes across multiple studies and fatality registries. Epinephrine's alpha-1 (vasoconstriction), beta-1 (positive inotropy/chronotropy), and beta-2 (bronchodilation, mast cell stabilization) effects make it the only drug that addresses all pathophysiologic mechanisms of anaphylaxis simultaneously. Wrong concentration errors are important but less common than omission/delay. IV corticosteroids have delayed onset (4-6 hours) and are adjunctive, not first-line. Fluid resuscitation is supportive but does not replace epinephrine.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A 22-year-old with known peanut allergy develops throat tightness and diffuse urticaria after accidental exposure. She has her EpiPen but wants to wait and see if symptoms improve. What is the correct counsel?

choices.text: Administer the EpiPen immediately — throat tightness with urticaria after a known allergen meets anaphylaxis criteria
Give oral diphenhydramine first and use epinephrine only if she develops respiratory distress
Drive to the emergency department before using the EpiPen so medical supervision is available
Administer the EpiPen only if she develops hypotension or loses consciousness

questions.explanation: Throat tightness with diffuse urticaria after known allergen exposure meets diagnostic criteria for anaphylaxis (involvement of skin/mucosal tissue PLUS respiratory compromise). Immediate epinephrine administration is critical — delay is the primary risk factor for fatal outcomes. Waiting to 'see if symptoms improve' allows progression to airway obstruction, cardiovascular collapse, or both. Oral diphenhydramine alone is insufficient for anaphylaxis. Driving to an ED before administering epinephrine wastes critical time. Waiting for hypotension or loss of consciousness means waiting for cardiovascular collapse — epinephrine should be given at the first signs of anaphylaxis.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A medical student asks why epinephrine is first-line for anaphylaxis rather than antihistamines or corticosteroids. Which explanation best describes epinephrine's multi-receptor mechanism?

choices.text: Alpha-1 vasoconstriction reverses hypotension and edema; beta-1 increases cardiac output; beta-2 causes bronchodilation and stabilizes mast cells
Direct antagonism of histamine H1 and H2 receptors combined with leukotriene inhibition
Stimulation of adrenal cortisol release producing rapid anti-inflammatory effects
Primarily beta-2 bronchodilation with minimal cardiovascular effects at IM doses

questions.explanation: Epinephrine's effectiveness in anaphylaxis derives from its action on multiple adrenergic receptors simultaneously: (1) Alpha-1: vasoconstriction reverses hypotension, reduces mucosal edema, and decreases angioedema; (2) Beta-1: positive inotropic and chronotropic effects increase cardiac output; (3) Beta-2: bronchodilation relieves bronchospasm, and increased intracellular cAMP in mast cells and basophils inhibits further mediator release. No other single agent addresses all these pathways. Epinephrine does not directly antagonize histamine receptors. It does not stimulate adrenal cortisol release (that is ACTH's role). Its cardiovascular effects are clinically significant and essential, not minimal.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: When prescribing epinephrine auto-injectors at discharge after an anaphylaxis episode, how many devices should be prescribed and why?

choices.text: Two devices — approximately 10-20% of episodes require a second dose, and biphasic reactions may occur
One device — a single IM dose resolves virtually all anaphylaxis episodes
Two devices — the second is intended for a family member or bystander to use simultaneously
Three devices — one for home, one for work, and one for travel

questions.explanation: Two epinephrine auto-injectors should be prescribed because approximately 10-20% of anaphylaxis episodes require a second dose due to incomplete initial response, and biphasic reactions (recurrence of symptoms hours after initial resolution) may occur in up to 20% of cases. Having two devices ensures a second dose is available without delay. A single device is insufficient given the documented need for repeat dosing. The second device is for the same patient (repeat dosing), not for a bystander. Three devices with location-specific allocation, while practical advice for daily carry, is not the standard prescribing rationale.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What is the mechanism by which allergen immunotherapy (AIT) achieves clinical tolerance?

choices.feedback: IgE may decrease over time but is not eliminated. The mechanism involves immune modulation, not IgE elimination.

choices.text: Induction of allergen-specific IgA in the nasal mucosa
choices.feedback: While mucosal immunity changes occur, the primary mechanism is Treg induction and IgG4 blocking antibodies, not IgA.

choices.text: Desensitization of mast cell histamine receptors
choices.feedback: AIT does not desensitize histamine receptors. It modulates the upstream immune response to the allergen.

choices.text: Immune deviation from Th2 to Th1/Treg responses with increased blocking IgG4 antibodies
choices.feedback: Correct! AIT mechanisms: (1) Induction of allergen-specific T regulatory cells (Tregs) → produce IL-10, TGF-β → suppresses Th2 inflammation. (2) Immune deviation: shift from Th2 (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13) toward Th1 (IFN-γ) profile. (3) Increased allergen-specific IgG4 (blocking antibody) → competes with IgE for allergen binding → prevents mast cell cross-linking. (4) Decreased mast cell/basophil reactivity over time. (5) Long-term: decreased allergen-specific IgE. Unique among allergy treatments: AIT is disease-modifying (not just symptom control).

questions.explanation: Allergen immunotherapy (AIT) works by shifting the immune response from a Th2-dominant pattern to a Th1/T-regulatory cell (Treg) pattern. Tregs produce IL-10 and TGF-β, which suppress eosinophils, mast cells, and basophils while promoting antibody class switching from IgE to IgG4 blocking antibodies. IgG4 competes with IgE for allergen binding, preventing mast cell/basophil cross-linking and degranulation. AIT does not eliminate allergen-specific IgE — IgE initially rises then gradually declines over years. IgA plays a role in SLIT but is not the primary mechanism. Mast cell histamine receptors are not directly desensitized by AIT.

questions.stem: What are the two routes of allergen immunotherapy administration?

choices.feedback: Oral immunotherapy exists for food allergy, but conventional aeroallergen immunotherapy uses different routes.
IV and IM routes are not used for allergen immunotherapy due to high systemic reaction risk.

Correct! SCIT: weekly injections during build-up (3-6 months) → monthly maintenance × 3-5 years. Must be administered in medical office with 30-minute observation (anaphylaxis risk ~0.1% per injection). SLIT: daily sublingual tablet/drops (self-administered at home after first dose). Available tablets: Grastek (grass), Ragwitek (ragweed), Odactra (dust mite). SLIT: safer (local reactions common, systemic rare), more convenient. SCIT: more allergens can be combined, stronger evidence for polysensitized patients.

choices.text: Topical skin patches and nasal sprays
choices.feedback: Epicutaneous immunotherapy (EPIT) for food allergy is investigational. Nasal spray immunotherapy is not standard practice.

questions.explanation: The two established routes of allergen immunotherapy are subcutaneous (SCIT) and sublingual (SLIT). SCIT involves escalating-dose injections given in a medical office with 30-minute observation periods, reaching a maintenance dose given every 2-4 weeks. SLIT uses allergen tablets or drops placed under the tongue daily; the first dose is given under medical supervision, then subsequent doses are self-administered at home. Oral immunotherapy (OIT) exists for food allergies but is distinct from traditional AIT. IV and IM routes are not used. Epicutaneous immunotherapy (EPIT) via skin patches is investigational.

questions.stem: What is the most serious adverse effect of subcutaneous immunotherapy (SCIT)?

choices.text: Anaphylaxis — risk ~0.1% per injection, most reactions within 30 minutes
choices.feedback: Correct! SCIT carries a small but real risk of systemic anaphylaxis. Risk factors: uncontrolled asthma (strongest predictor), dosing errors, injections during peak allergen season, high sensitivity. Safety protocols: 30-minute in-office observation after every injection, epinephrine immediately available, assess for asthma control before each injection, dose reduction if large local reactions. Fatalities: ~1 per 2.5 million injections. This is why SCIT requires medical supervision (unlike SLIT which can be home-administered after first dose).
AIT does not cause immunosuppression. It modulates the immune response specifically to the treated allergens.
Local reactions are common but not serious. The most concerning adverse effect is systemic.

choices.text: Development of new allergies from the injected extracts
choices.feedback: AIT does not cause new allergic sensitizations. Evidence suggests it may PREVENT development of new sensitizations and reduce asthma risk.

questions.explanation: Anaphylaxis is the most serious adverse effect of SCIT, occurring at a rate of approximately 0.2% per injection. Most systemic reactions occur within 30 minutes of injection, which is why a 30-minute in-office observation period is mandatory. Fatal anaphylaxis is extremely rare but has been reported. Risk factors include uncontrolled asthma, dosing errors, and concurrent beta-blocker use (which impairs epinephrine response). SCIT does not cause chronic immunosuppression — it modulates specific immune pathways. Injection site reactions (local swelling, redness) are common but not the most serious. AIT does not create new allergies from injected extracts.

questions.stem: How long must allergen immunotherapy be continued to achieve long-lasting benefit after discontinuation?

choices.text: Lifelong — benefits stop immediately upon discontinuation
choices.feedback: Unlike pharmacotherapy, AIT's disease-modifying effects persist after discontinuation — this is its key advantage.

choices.text: 3-5 years of maintenance therapy for sustained disease-modifying effect
choices.feedback: Correct! AIT: minimum 3 years, typically 3-5 years of maintenance for durable disease modification. Benefits persist for years AFTER discontinuation (unlike all other allergy medications which work only while being taken). SCIT: build-up (weekly × 3-6 months) → maintenance (monthly × 3-5 years). SLIT: daily × 3-5 years. Evidence: effects persist ≥3 years after stopping 3-year course. Disease modification: reduces symptom severity, medication use, prevents new sensitizations, and may prevent allergic rhinitis → asthma progression in children. This makes AIT unique among allergy treatments.
6 weeks is far too short. Immune modulation requires months to years of consistent allergen exposure to achieve durable tolerance.
3 months is only the build-up phase for SCIT. This is insufficient for lasting disease modification.

questions.explanation: Clinical guidelines recommend 3-5 years of maintenance allergen immunotherapy to achieve sustained disease-modifying benefits that persist after discontinuation. Studies show that 3 years of SCIT or SLIT provides continued clinical benefit for at least 2-3 years after stopping therapy. AIT also has preventive effects — it can reduce development of new allergen sensitizations and prevent progression from allergic rhinitis to asthma (demonstrated primarily in children). Benefits do not require lifelong therapy. Six weeks or 3 months is insufficient to achieve disease modification — the build-up phase alone typically takes 3-6 months.

questions.stem: Which patients are the BEST candidates for allergen immunotherapy?

choices.text: Patients with non-allergic rhinitis and negative allergy tests
choices.feedback: AIT requires IgE-mediated sensitization. Non-allergic rhinitis does not respond to AIT because there is no IgE-mediated component to modulate.

choices.text: All patients with any positive allergy test
choices.feedback: AIT requires clinical correlation — a positive test alone without concordant symptoms is not an indication.
AIT is approved for ages 5+ for SCIT (some SLIT products for different ages). It is effective in both children and adults.

choices.text: Patients with IgE-mediated allergic rhinitis/asthma with confirmed sensitization to clinically relevant allergens, whose symptoms are inadequately controlled by avoidance and medications
choices.feedback: Correct! Ideal AIT candidates: (1) IgE-mediated disease (allergic rhinitis, allergic asthma, venom allergy) confirmed by SPT/sIgE, (2) Clinical symptoms correlating with sensitized allergens, (3) Inadequate control with avoidance + pharmacotherapy OR desire to reduce medication burden, (4) Limited number of clinically relevant allergens (polysensitization less predictable). Contraindications: uncontrolled/severe asthma (risk of fatal anaphylaxis), beta-blocker use (makes anaphylaxis harder to treat), immunosuppressed (less effective). Venom immunotherapy is especially effective: reduces sting anaphylaxis from 60% to <5%.

questions.explanation: Ideal AIT candidates have confirmed IgE-mediated disease (positive skin prick test or serum specific IgE) with clinical symptoms correlating to the identified allergens, AND inadequate control despite allergen avoidance measures and pharmacotherapy. AIT is indicated for allergic rhinitis, allergic asthma, and Hymenoptera venom anaphylaxis. Non-allergic rhinitis patients (negative allergy tests) have no IgE-mediated component to target. Not all patients with positive allergy tests need AIT — only those with clinically relevant sensitization and inadequate symptom control. AIT can be used in children but is not limited to those under 5.

questions.stem: What is the primary indication for immunoglobulin replacement therapy (IVIG/SCIG)?

choices.feedback: While high-dose IVIG is used for some autoimmune conditions, the PRIMARY indication is immunodeficiency.
IV iron is used for iron deficiency. IVIG provides IgG antibodies for immune deficiency.
IVIG/SCIG provides immunoglobulin G antibodies, not vitamins.

choices.text: Primary antibody deficiency disorders to prevent recurrent infections
choices.feedback: Correct! Immunoglobulin replacement: provides passive IgG antibodies to patients who cannot produce sufficient antibodies. Primary indications: Primary humoral immunodeficiencies (CVID, X-linked agammaglobulinemia, specific antibody deficiency), secondary hypogammaglobulinemia (CLL, multiple myeloma, post-rituximab). Goal: maintain IgG trough levels ≥500-800 mg/dL to reduce infection frequency. Routes: IVIG (intravenous, q3-4 weeks, hospital/infusion center) or SCIG (subcutaneous, weekly or biweekly, self-administered at home). SCIG: more stable IgG levels, fewer systemic reactions, improved quality of life.

questions.explanation: The primary indication for immunoglobulin replacement therapy is primary antibody deficiency disorders (e.g., common variable immunodeficiency, X-linked agammaglobulinemia, specific antibody deficiency) to prevent recurrent sinopulmonary and systemic bacterial infections. These patients cannot produce adequate protective antibodies. IVIG/SCIG provides passive IgG antibodies. Secondary indications include IVIG's immunomodulatory effects in autoimmune conditions (ITP, Kawasaki disease, Guillain-Barré) at higher doses, but replacement for primary immunodeficiency remains the core indication.

questions.stem: What is the key advantage of subcutaneous immunoglobulin (SCIG) over intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG)?

choices.text: SCIG can be given intramuscularly as well
choices.feedback: SCIG is specifically subcutaneous. Intramuscular immunoglobulin (IMIG) exists but is rarely used for replacement therapy due to volume limitations and pain.

choices.text: Self-administration at home with fewer systemic adverse reactions
choices.feedback: Correct! SCIG advantages: (1) Home self-administration → improved quality of life, fewer hospital visits. (2) More stable IgG levels (no peak-trough variation of monthly IVIG). (3) Fewer systemic reactions (headache, chills, anaphylaxis are rare vs IVIG). (4) No IV access needed (important for children, patients with poor veins). Disadvantages: local site reactions (swelling, redness — common but usually mild), more frequent administration (weekly vs monthly), limited per-site volume (may need multiple sites). Training essential for proper technique.
SCIG may have modest cost advantages (no infusion center fees) but the primary advantages are clinical and quality-of-life related.

choices.text: SCIG achieves higher peak IgG levels
choices.feedback: SCIG actually produces LOWER peak levels but more stable steady-state levels (less peak-trough variation). This is an advantage, not a limitation.

questions.explanation: SCIG's key advantage over IVIG is the ability to self-administer at home, with fewer systemic adverse reactions. SCIG provides steadier-state IgG levels (avoiding the peak-trough fluctuations of monthly IVIG), which may improve clinical outcomes and quality of life. SCIG has a much lower rate of systemic reactions (headache, fever, anaphylaxis) because absorption is gradual from subcutaneous tissue. Local injection site reactions (swelling, erythema) are common but mild. SCIG eliminates the need for IV access and infusion center visits.

questions.stem: What serious adverse reaction can occur with IVIG infusion, especially in patients with IgA deficiency?

choices.feedback: IVIG-associated hemolysis can occur (from anti-A/anti-B isohemagglutinins in the product) but it is not specific to IgA deficiency and is not the most serious concern.
While aseptic meningitis can occur with IVIG, the IgA-specific concern is different.
IVIG does not cause immunosuppression. It provides supplementary IgG. High-dose IVIG for autoimmune conditions has immunomodulatory effects but does not cause permanent immunosuppression.

choices.text: Anaphylaxis from anti-IgA antibodies in IgA-deficient patients
choices.feedback: Correct! Patients with complete IgA deficiency can develop IgG anti-IgA antibodies → anaphylaxis when exposed to IgA in standard IVIG products (which contain trace IgA). Prevention: screen for IgA deficiency and anti-IgA antibodies before IVIG. If at risk: use IgA-depleted products (Gammagard S/D) or switch to SCIG (lower systemic exposure, fewer reactions). Other IVIG adverse effects: infusion reactions (headache, chills, fever — rate-dependent), aseptic meningitis, thrombotic events (especially in elderly, hypercoagulable states), renal failure (sucrose-containing products).

questions.explanation: IgA-deficient patients (~1:500 prevalence) may develop IgG or IgE anti-IgA antibodies. When these patients receive IVIG (which contains trace IgA), anaphylaxis can occur from anti-IgA-mediated immune complex formation or IgE-mediated mast cell activation. This is the most feared serious adverse reaction of IVIG.
