Prevention: screen for IgA deficiency before starting IVIG, use IgA-depleted products (e.g., Gammagard S/D) for IgA-deficient patients, and consider SCIG as an alternative (lower IgA content, slower absorption reduces systemic reaction risk).

questions.stem: How do you monitor the effectiveness of immunoglobulin replacement therapy?

choices.feedback: CBC monitoring is not the primary measure of Ig replacement efficacy. IgG trough levels and infection rates are the key parameters.

choices.text: Skin prick testing to common allergens
choices.feedback: Skin testing is for allergy diagnosis, not for monitoring immunoglobulin replacement effectiveness.

Correct! Monitoring: (1) IgG trough level: measured just before next IVIG dose (or any time for SCIG — steady-state). Target varies by patient but generally ≥500-800 mg/dL; some patients require higher levels (individualize based on clinical response). (2) Clinical endpoints: reduced frequency of sinopulmonary infections, fewer antibiotic courses, fewer hospitalizations, improved quality of life. (3) Monitor for end-organ damage: pulmonary function tests (bronchiectasis prevention), CT chest if needed. Dose adjustments based on both trough levels AND clinical response.
IgE is not a relevant monitoring parameter for Ig replacement. IgG trough levels and infection frequency are used.

questions.explanation: Immunoglobulin replacement therapy effectiveness is monitored by IgG trough levels measured just before the next infusion (for IVIG) or at steady state (for SCIG). The goal is typically ≥500-800 mg/dL, with many experts targeting trough levels that minimize breakthrough infections in the individual patient. Clinical response (reduction in infection frequency and severity) is equally important. Some patients require higher trough levels for adequate protection. Trough levels should be measured every 3-6 months and after dose adjustments.

questions.stem: In which clinical scenario is serum specific IgE (sIgE) testing preferred over skin prick testing?

choices.text: When the patient has severe eczema, dermatographism, or cannot stop antihistamines
choices.feedback: Correct! sIgE (ImmunoCAP) preferred when: extensive dermatitis/eczema (insufficient normal skin for SPT), dermatographism (false-positive SPT), unable to discontinue antihistamines (interfere with SPT), history of severe anaphylaxis (very small risk of systemic reaction from SPT), uncooperative patient (young children). Advantages: unaffected by medications or skin conditions, quantitative results, no anaphylaxis risk. Limitations: lower sensitivity than SPT, more expensive, delayed results.

choices.text: When testing for contact dermatitis to metals or fragrances
choices.feedback: Contact dermatitis is Type IV (cell-mediated) and diagnosed by PATCH testing, not SPT or sIgE.

choices.text: When the patient has normal skin and is not taking any medications
choices.feedback: In this ideal scenario, SPT is preferred (faster, cheaper, more sensitive). sIgE is preferred when SPT is impractical.

choices.text: When the clinician suspects a non-IgE-mediated allergy like FPIES
choices.feedback: sIgE tests are NEGATIVE in non-IgE-mediated conditions like FPIES. Neither SPT nor sIgE can diagnose non-IgE food allergies.

questions.explanation: Serum specific IgE (sIgE) testing is preferred when SPT cannot be performed reliably: severe eczema (insufficient normal skin), dermatographism (false-positive wheals from mechanical pressure), or inability to stop antihistamines (which suppress SPT). Contact dermatitis is Type IV (cell-mediated) hypersensitivity diagnosed by patch testing, not sIgE. Normal skin with no interfering medications favors SPT as first-line. FPIES is a non-IgE-mediated food allergy — sIgE would be negative and unhelpful.

questions.stem: What is the intradermal skin test primarily used for in allergy practice?

choices.feedback: Latex allergy can be tested by SPT (where available) or sIgE. Intradermal testing is used more broadly for drugs and venoms when SPT is inconclusive.
Intradermal testing is NOT recommended for food allergy — too high false-positive rate and increased systemic reaction risk.

choices.text: Screening for all environmental allergies before starting immunotherapy
choices.feedback: SPT is the standard for environmental allergy testing before immunotherapy. Intradermal testing is used selectively, not as first-line screening.

choices.text: Drug allergy testing and venom allergy evaluation when SPT is negative but clinical suspicion remains
choices.feedback: Correct! Intradermal testing (injection of dilute allergen into dermis): higher sensitivity than SPT (detects lower levels of IgE) but lower specificity (more false positives). Primary uses: penicillin allergy evaluation (standardized protocol: SPT first → if negative, intradermal), Hymenoptera venom allergy (SPT negative with convincing sting reaction history), and some drug allergies. NOT recommended for food testing (unacceptable false-positive rate, increased anaphylaxis risk).

questions.explanation: Intradermal skin testing (IDT) is primarily used for drug allergy evaluation (especially penicillin) and venom allergy testing when SPT is negative but clinical suspicion remains high. IDT is more sensitive but less specific than SPT — it can detect lower levels of IgE sensitization. For Hymenoptera venom allergy, IDT at concentrations up to 1 μg/mL is performed when SPT is negative in patients with convincing sting anaphylaxis history. IDT is not used exclusively for latex. It is not first-line for food allergy (high false-positive rate and risk of systemic reactions). It is not a routine screening tool for environmental allergies.

questions.stem: A patient with seasonal rhinoconjunctivitis has a positive SPT to birch pollen. They also report oral tingling when eating raw apples, cherries, and celery. What explains this?

choices.text: They have developed new IgE-mediated food allergies to multiple fruits
choices.feedback: This pattern is not independent food allergy but pollen-food cross-reactivity.

choices.text: Oral allergy syndrome (pollen-food allergy syndrome) due to cross-reactive PR-10 proteins
choices.feedback: Correct! OAS/PFAS: IgE directed against birch Bet v 1 (PR-10 protein family) cross-reacts with homologous proteins in raw fruits/vegetables/nuts: apple (Mal d 1), cherry (Pru av 1), celery (Api g 1), carrot, hazelnut, soybean. Symptoms: isolated oropharyngeal pruritus/tingling with RAW food (proteins denatured by cooking → cooked forms tolerated). Rarely progresses to systemic anaphylaxis (<2%). Diagnosis: clinical history + CRD showing Bet v 1 and cross-reactive component IgE. Management: avoid raw trigger foods, or cook/microwave them. Birch immunotherapy may reduce OAS symptoms.

choices.text: Histamine intolerance from a diamine oxidase deficiency
choices.feedback: Histamine intolerance causes systemic symptoms (headache, flushing, GI) from histamine-rich foods, not the isolated oral symptoms triggered by specific raw plant foods described here.

choices.text: Non-allergic food intolerance from enzyme deficiency
choices.feedback: The reproducible pattern with specific raw fruits that cross-react with birch pollen is classic OAS, an IgE-mediated cross-reactivity phenomenon.

questions.explanation: Oral allergy syndrome (OAS/pollen-food allergy syndrome) occurs due to cross-reactive PR-10 proteins shared between birch pollen (Bet v 1) and fruits/vegetables (apple Mal d 1, cherry Pru av 1, peach Pru p 1, carrot Dau c 1). Patients experience oral/pharyngeal tingling, itching, and mild angioedema when eating raw cross-reactive fruits. These are heat-labile proteins, so cooked forms are typically tolerated. This is not new primary food allergy — the sensitization is driven by birch pollen IgE cross-reacting with homologous food proteins. Histamine intolerance presents with broader systemic symptoms unrelated to specific pollen seasons.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What distinguishes Type 2 (T2-high) asthma from non-Type 2 asthma?

choices.text: T2-high asthma only occurs in children
choices.feedback: T2-high asthma occurs in both children and adults. It is actually the predominant phenotype across all ages.

choices.text: Non-T2 asthma is always caused by occupational exposures
choices.feedback: Non-T2 asthma has many causes (obesity-related, neutrophilic, smoking-related) and is not limited to occupational triggers.

choices.text: T2-high asthma is always mild and well-controlled
choices.feedback: T2-high asthma can range from mild to severe. Many severe asthma patients have T2-high phenotypes.

choices.text: T2-high features eosinophilic inflammation, elevated FeNO, and response to biologics targeting IL-4/IL-5/IL-13
choices.feedback: Correct! T2-high asthma: driven by Th2 cytokines (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13) → eosinophilic airway inflammation, elevated FeNO (>25 ppb), elevated serum IgE, blood eosinophils >150-300. Responds to: ICS (backbone), anti-IgE (omalizumab), anti-IL-5/IL-5R (mepolizumab, benralizumab), anti-IL-4Rα (dupilumab), anti-TSLP (tezepelumab). Non-T2 (T2-low): neutrophilic or paucigranulocytic, lower FeNO, poorer ICS response. Biomarker-guided phenotyping determines biologic eligibility.

questions.explanation: T2-high asthma is driven by Th2 lymphocytes and ILC2 cells producing IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13, resulting in eosinophilic inflammation, elevated FeNO, elevated IgE, and responsiveness to biologics targeting these pathways. Non-T2 (T2-low) asthma features neutrophilic or paucigranulocytic inflammation with normal eosinophils and FeNO, and responds poorly to corticosteroids. T2-high asthma occurs in both children and adults. Non-T2 asthma has multiple causes beyond occupational exposures (obesity, smoking, infections). T2-high asthma ranges from mild to severe and is not always well-controlled — severe T2-high asthma is the primary indication for biologic therapy.
questions.citation: Asthma. StatPearls. NBK430901. [Open Access] | PMC5765385. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What biomarker is most useful for guiding ICS dose adjustments in asthma?

choices.feedback: PEF measures airflow obstruction (functional status) but does not reflect underlying inflammation type. FeNO more directly indicates eosinophilic inflammation and ICS responsiveness.

Correct! FeNO reflects eosinophilic airway inflammation driven by IL-13 → upregulates iNOS in airway epithelium → increased NO production. FeNO-guided treatment: high FeNO (>50 ppb adults) suggests ICS-responsive inflammation → increase ICS; low FeNO (<25 ppb) suggests non-eosinophilic inflammation or good control → consider ICS reduction. Studies show FeNO-guided management reduces exacerbations. Confounders: smoking ↓, atopy ↑, viral infection ↑, allergen exposure ↑, age, height.
Sputum neutrophils identify neutrophilic asthma (typically ICS-unresponsive). FeNO is the practical biomarker for ICS-responsive eosinophilic inflammation.
Total IgE helps determine omalizumab eligibility/dosing but is not used for ICS dose titration.

questions.explanation: FeNO is the most useful biomarker for guiding ICS dose adjustments because it directly reflects eosinophilic airway inflammation responsive to corticosteroids. Elevated FeNO in a patient on ICS suggests poor adherence, inadequate dosing, or persistent T2 inflammation requiring step-up. Decreasing FeNO after ICS initiation confirms therapeutic response. PEF measures airflow obstruction but not the inflammatory phenotype driving it. Sputum neutrophil counts reflect neutrophilic inflammation (not ICS-responsive). Serum total IgE changes slowly and is primarily used for omalizumab dosing, not routine ICS adjustment.

questions.stem: Which biologic targets IL-5 and is approved for severe eosinophilic asthma?

choices.feedback: Omalizumab targets IgE (anti-IgE), not IL-5. It is approved for moderate-severe allergic asthma.
Secukinumab targets IL-17A and is used for psoriasis and spondyloarthropathies, not asthma.

Correct! Mepolizumab (Nucala): anti-IL-5 monoclonal antibody → reduces eosinophil production and survival → decreases blood and sputum eosinophils. Approved for severe eosinophilic asthma (blood eos ≥150 or ≥300 depending on criteria). Also: benralizumab (anti-IL-5Rα — depletes eosinophils via ADCC, near-complete depletion), reslizumab (anti-IL-5, IV). Dupilumab targets IL-4Rα (blocks both IL-4 and IL-13). Tezepelumab (anti-TSLP) works upstream and is effective in both T2-high and some T2-low asthma.
Dupilumab targets IL-4 receptor alpha (blocks IL-4 and IL-13), not IL-5. It works on a different T2 pathway.

questions.explanation: Mepolizumab is a monoclonal antibody that directly binds and neutralizes IL-5, the key cytokine driving eosinophil production, maturation, and survival. It is indicated for severe eosinophilic asthma (blood eosinophils ≥150 cells/μL) as add-on maintenance therapy. Omalizumab targets IgE, not IL-5. Secukinumab targets IL-17A (used for psoriasis/ankylosing spondylitis, not asthma). Dupilumab targets the IL-4 receptor α subunit, blocking both IL-4 and IL-13 signaling — it is approved for moderate-to-severe eosinophilic asthma but works via the IL-4/IL-13 pathway, not IL-5.

questions.stem: A patient with severe asthma has normal FeNO, normal eosinophils, high BMI, and poor response to high-dose ICS. Which asthma phenotype is this?

choices.feedback: EIB is triggered by exercise and responds well to pre-exercise SABA. This patient's profile suggests a distinct non-T2 obesity phenotype.

Correct! Obesity-related asthma: often non-T2 phenotype with normal eosinophils and FeNO, predominantly adult-onset, female predominance. Mechanisms: mechanical (reduced lung volumes, airway closure), metabolic (systemic inflammation, insulin resistance, adipokines), and oxidative stress. Poor ICS response. Management priorities: weight loss (even 5-10% improves control), exercise, address GERD/OSA (common comorbidities). Biologics targeting T2 pathways generally less effective. Emerging: thermoplasty, anti-TSLP (tezepelumab shows some T2-low benefit).
Allergic eosinophilic asthma would have elevated FeNO and eosinophils with good ICS response. This is T2-low.
AERD features nasal polyps, aspirin sensitivity, and is typically eosinophilic (T2-high). This patient has T2-low features.

questions.explanation: This presentation — severe asthma with normal FeNO, normal eosinophils, high BMI, and poor ICS response — is classic for obesity-related non-T2 (T2-low) asthma. Obesity contributes to asthma through mechanical restriction, systemic inflammation (adipokines, IL-6, TNF-α), and metabolic dysregulation rather than eosinophilic pathways. These patients respond poorly to ICS because their inflammation is not T2-driven. Weight loss is a key therapeutic intervention. Exercise-induced bronchospasm presents with symptoms only during/after exercise. Allergic eosinophilic asthma would show elevated eosinophils and FeNO. AERD (Samter triad) features asthma, nasal polyps, and NSAID sensitivity.
questions.citation: Asthma. StatPearls. NBK430901. [Open Access] | PMC5765385. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What defines aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD)?

choices.text: Drug-induced lupus from chronic aspirin use
choices.feedback: Drug-induced lupus is caused by hydralazine, procainamide, and isoniazid, not aspirin. AERD is a respiratory syndrome, not an autoimmune condition.

choices.text: Allergic asthma that worsens with any NSAID due to IgE-mediated drug allergy
choices.feedback: AERD is NOT IgE-mediated. It is caused by dysregulated arachidonic acid metabolism.

choices.text: Asthma with nasal polyps that only occurs in aspirin factory workers
choices.feedback: AERD is not an occupational disease — it can occur in anyone. It is an intrinsic dysregulation of prostaglandin/leukotriene metabolism.

choices.text: The triad of asthma, nasal polyps, and respiratory reactions to COX-1 inhibitors (aspirin/NSAIDs)
choices.feedback: Correct! AERD (Samter's triad): chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps + asthma + respiratory reactions to aspirin/NSAIDs (COX-1 inhibitors). Pathophysiology: COX-1 inhibition → arachidonic acid shunted to 5-LOX pathway → overproduction of cysteinyl leukotrienes (LTC4, LTD4, LTE4) → bronchoconstriction, nasal congestion, eosinophilic inflammation. Diagnosis: aspirin challenge (oral or nasal). Management: aspirin desensitization — reduces polyp recurrence and improves asthma; leukotriene modifiers; biologics (dupilumab effective for polyps + asthma). COX-2 selective agents (celecoxib) usually tolerated.

questions.explanation: AERD (aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease), also known as Samter triad, is defined by three co-occurring conditions: (1) asthma, (2) chronic rhinosinusitis with recurrent nasal polyps, and (3) respiratory hypersensitivity reactions to COX-1 inhibitors (aspirin and NSAIDs). The mechanism is non-IgE-mediated — COX-1 inhibition shunts arachidonic acid metabolism toward the lipoxygenase pathway, increasing cysteinyl leukotrienes (LTC4, LTD4, LTE4) while reducing protective PGE2. Reactions typically occur within 30 minutes to 3 hours of NSAID ingestion. AERD affects approximately 7% of adult asthmatics. Leukotriene receptor antagonists (montelukast) and aspirin desensitization are key management strategies.
questions.citation: Aspirin-Exacerbated Respiratory Disease. PMC3005316. [Open Access] | PMC5951071. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What is the primary benefit of using a spacer (valved holding chamber) with a metered-dose inhaler (MDI)?

choices.feedback: Correct! Spacers provide multiple benefits: slow aerosol velocity → smaller particle size reaches lower airways → improved lung deposition (increases from ~10-15% without spacer to ~20-25% with spacer). Reduces oropharyngeal deposition → fewer local side effects (thrush, dysphonia from ICS). Eliminates need for precise hand-breath coordination → critical for children, elderly, and patients with poor technique. Reduces the fast-moving "cold Freon" effect that causes patients to stop inhaling.

choices.text: It converts the medication to a dry powder formulation
choices.feedback: A spacer works with MDIs (pressurized aerosol), not dry powder inhalers. DPIs have their own inhalation technique and do not use spacers.

choices.text: It increases the total dose delivered by the MDI
choices.feedback: A spacer does not increase the total dose. It improves delivery efficiency of the existing dose.

choices.text: It eliminates the need for rinsing the mouth after ICS use
choices.feedback: Spacers reduce but do not eliminate oropharyngeal deposition. Mouth rinsing after ICS is still recommended to prevent candidiasis and dysphonia.

questions.explanation: A spacer reduces oropharyngeal deposition by slowing aerosol velocity and allowing large particles to settle before inhalation, improving the respirable fraction reaching the lower airways. It does not convert medication to dry powder formulation — that is a different device class (DPI). It does not increase the total dose delivered by the MDI — it improves where the dose is deposited. While spacers reduce oropharyngeal ICS deposition, mouth rinsing is still recommended as an additional protective measure and is not eliminated by spacer use alone.

questions.stem: A patient using an ICS-MDI develops oral thrush. What is the most effective intervention?

choices.feedback: Oral corticosteroids would cause MORE immunosuppression and more candidiasis risk. The goal is to improve inhaler technique.
Stopping ICS risks uncontrolled asthma. The solution is improving drug delivery technique, not stopping treatment.
Systemic antifungals are unnecessary for ICS-related thrush. Fixing technique (spacer + rinsing) addresses the root cause.

choices.text: Add a spacer and ensure mouth rinsing after each ICS use
choices.feedback: Correct! Oral candidiasis (thrush) from ICS is caused by oropharyngeal steroid deposition → local immunosuppression → Candida overgrowth. Prevention: spacer use (reduces oropharyngeal deposition by 80%+), rinse mouth and spit after EVERY ICS dose, use lowest effective ICS dose. Treatment of active thrush: topical antifungal (nystatin swish-and-swallow or clotrimazole troches). If persistent, consider: switching to DPI (different deposition pattern) or prodrug ICS (ciclesonide — activated in lungs, minimal oropharyngeal activity).

questions.explanation: For ICS-associated oral thrush, the most effective intervention combines adding a spacer (to reduce oropharyngeal drug deposition) and ensuring consistent mouth rinsing with water and spitting after each ICS use. These measures together address the root cause — local immunosuppression from oropharyngeal ICS deposition. Switching to oral corticosteroids would worsen immunosuppression systemically. Discontinuing all inhaled medications leaves asthma uncontrolled. Prophylactic oral fluconazole treats the symptom without addressing the cause and adds unnecessary drug burden.

questions.stem: What is the correct technique for using a dry powder inhaler (DPI)?

choices.text: Slow, gentle inhalation with a 10-second breath hold
choices.feedback: Slow inhalation is for MDIs. DPIs require the opposite — fast and forceful inhalation.

choices.text: Quick, deep, forceful inhalation to generate sufficient inspiratory flow
choices.feedback: Correct! DPI technique: exhale away from device (moisture deactivates powder) → seal lips around mouthpiece → inhale quickly and deeply (peak inspiratory flow rate >target rate depending on device) → hold breath 5-10 seconds. DPIs are breath-actuated (no propellant) → the patient's inspiratory force disperses the powder into respirable particles. Key difference from MDI: MDI = slow steady inhalation; DPI = fast forceful inhalation. Patients with severe obstruction or children <5 may lack sufficient inspiratory force for DPIs → use MDI + spacer instead.

choices.text: Multiple shallow breaths in rapid succession
choices.feedback: Multiple shallow breaths do not generate the sustained inspiratory flow needed to disaggregate and disperse the dry powder formulation.

choices.text: Shake vigorously before each use, then breathe normally
choices.feedback: Shaking is for MDIs (to suspend propellant). DPIs should NOT be shaken. Breathing normally is insufficient — forceful inhalation is required.

questions.explanation: Dry powder inhalers (DPIs) require a quick, deep, forceful inhalation to generate sufficient inspiratory flow (typically ≥30-60 L/min depending on device resistance) to deaggregate and aerosolize the powder formulation. This is the key difference from MDI technique, which requires slow, steady inhalation. Slow, gentle inhalation describes MDI technique and is incorrect for DPIs. Multiple shallow breaths do not generate adequate flow. DPIs should NOT be shaken before use — shaking can cause powder loss. DPIs are breath-actuated and do not require actuation-inhalation coordination.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old child needs inhaled albuterol for acute wheezing. What is the preferred delivery device?

choices.feedback: Breath-actuated MDIs require coordinated inspiratory effort that a 3-year-old cannot reliably perform. Spacer + mask is needed.

choices.text: MDI with spacer and face mask
choices.feedback: Correct! For children <4-5 years: MDI + spacer with attached face mask (tight seal over nose and mouth). The child breathes tidally (normal breaths) through the mask for 5-6 breaths after actuation. Advantages over nebulizer: faster delivery, more portable, equivalent drug delivery, less crying/distress. For children >4-5 years: MDI + spacer with mouthpiece (transition from mask). Nebulizer is alternative when: child is in severe distress, unable to cooperate with MDI + spacer, or in emergency settings.
Children <5 typically cannot generate sufficient inspiratory flow for DPIs.
Without a spacer, MDI coordination is impossible for a 3-year-old, and oropharyngeal deposition would be excessive with minimal lung delivery.

questions.explanation: For a 3-year-old with acute wheezing, the preferred delivery device is an MDI with spacer and face mask. This combination is at least as effective as nebulization for bronchodilator delivery in young children and offers advantages of portability, faster administration, and lower cost. Breath-actuated MDIs require coordinated inspiratory effort beyond a 3-year-old's ability. DPIs require forceful inhalation (≥30 L/min) that young children cannot generate reliably. Direct MDI without spacer results in excessive oropharyngeal deposition and minimal lung delivery in any patient, especially young children.

questions.stem: What is the most common reason inhaled medications fail to control asthma?

choices.feedback: Airway remodeling does contribute to severe asthma over time, but it is far less common as a cause of poor control than technique and adherence issues.

Correct! Studies show 70-80% of patients use inhalers incorrectly, and ICS adherence rates are only ~50%. Incorrect technique → drug deposited in mouth/throat instead of lungs → subtherapeutic lung doses → poor control despite adequate prescriptions. At every visit: assess technique (observe patient using device), verify adherence (prescription refill records, electronic monitoring), and address barriers. Common errors: not shaking MDI, poor hand-breath coordination (MDI without spacer), insufficient inspiratory flow (DPI), not holding breath, exhaling into DPI.
True pharmacologic resistance to ICS is rare. The most common issue is patient-related.
Beta-blockers can worsen asthma but this is uncommon as a primary cause of medication failure. Poor technique is overwhelmingly the most common reason.

questions.explanation: Poor inhaler technique and non-adherence are the most common reasons inhaled medications fail to control asthma, accounting for a majority of apparent treatment failures. Studies show up to 70-80% of patients demonstrate at least one critical inhaler technique error, and adherence to daily controller medications averages only 50%. Before diagnosing refractory asthma, clinicians must verify technique, adherence, and trigger avoidance. While airway remodeling occurs in chronic severe asthma, it is not the most common cause of treatment failure. Medication resistance does not develop to ICS or bronchodilators. Beta-blocker interaction, while important, is uncommon.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of allergen immunotherapy (AIT)?

choices.text: Desensitization of mast cells through repeated histamine exposure
choices.feedback: AIT does not directly desensitize mast cells through histamine. It works upstream through T-cell immune modulation and blocking antibody production.
AIT does not eliminate sIgE. Initially sIgE may even increase. The mechanism involves immune deviation and tolerance.

choices.text: Suppression of all immune responses including protective immunity
choices.feedback: AIT is allergen-specific — it modulates the response to the specific allergens administered without broadly suppressing immunity.

choices.text: Immune deviation from Th2 to Th1/Treg response with production of blocking IgG4 antibodies
choices.feedback: Correct! AIT mechanism: repeated allergen exposure → induces allergen-specific Tregs (producing IL-10, TGF-β) → immune deviation from Th2 to Th1 → decreased IL-4, IL-5, IL-13 → reduced eosinophilic inflammation. Also: increased allergen-specific IgG4 (blocking antibodies) that compete with IgE for allergen binding → prevent mast cell cross-linking. Long-term: decreased mast cell and basophil reactivity, mucosal IgA production. Disease-modifying: prevents new sensitizations and progression from rhinitis to asthma.

questions.explanation: AIT induces immune deviation from a Th2-dominant response (driving IgE, eosinophilia, and allergic inflammation) to a Th1/Treg response. Tregs secrete IL-10, which promotes IgG4 blocking antibody production, suppresses allergen-specific IgE over time, and reduces mast cell, basophil, and eosinophil recruitment. IgG4 antibodies compete with IgE for allergen binding, preventing cross-linking of surface IgE on mast cells. AIT does not work by desensitizing mast cells through repeated histamine exposure. IgE is not completely eliminated. AIT is allergen-specific and does not suppress protective immunity broadly.

questions.stem: What is the minimum recommended duration for allergen immunotherapy to achieve disease-modifying benefits?

choices.feedback: Standard recommendation is 3-5 years. Beyond 5 years provides diminishing additional benefit for most patients, though some may benefit from longer courses.

Correct! Standard AIT duration: 3-5 years for sustained disease modification. Benefits that persist after discontinuation: reduced symptoms, decreased medication use, prevention of new allergen sensitizations, prevention of asthma development in children with allergic rhinitis. Subcutaneous immunotherapy (SCIT): weekly buildup → monthly maintenance. Sublingual immunotherapy (SLIT): daily tablet or drops. Discontinuing before 3 years → higher relapse rates. Some patients (e.g., mastocytosis with venom allergy) require lifelong therapy.
One year of AIT provides symptomatic improvement but is insufficient for the sustained, disease-modifying immunologic changes.
Six months is too short for disease modification. Symptom improvement begins around 3-6 months, but sustained benefit requires years of treatment.

questions.explanation: A minimum of 3-5 years of maintenance immunotherapy is recommended for lasting disease-modifying benefits. This duration allows sufficient immune remodeling — Treg induction, IgG4 production, and gradual IgE decline — to persist after treatment discontinuation. Shorter durations (6 months, 1 year) may provide temporary symptom improvement but are insufficient for sustained tolerance. Ten years is longer than necessary for most patients, though some may choose to continue if they had relapse after initial discontinuation.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a contraindication to initiating subcutaneous allergen immunotherapy?

choices.text: Concurrent allergic rhinitis and mild persistent asthma
choices.feedback: Mild-moderate allergic asthma with rhinitis is actually an ideal indication for AIT — it can prevent asthma progression.

Correct! Uncontrolled/severe asthma with FEV1 <70% is a contraindication because: higher risk of severe systemic reactions during injections, risk of fatal bronchospasm. Asthma must be stabilized (FEV1 >70%, well-controlled symptoms) before starting SCIT. Other contraindications: concurrent beta-blocker use (relative — complicates epinephrine treatment if reaction occurs), unstable cardiac disease, immunodeficiency (may not mount therapeutic response). NOT a contraindication: pregnancy (don't start, but can continue if already on maintenance).
While SCIT is used more cautiously in very young children, age <5 alone is not an absolute contraindication. SLIT tablets are approved for ages 5+.
Polysensitization is common and does not preclude AIT. Immunotherapy mixes can include multiple relevant allergens.

questions.explanation: Severe uncontrolled asthma is a contraindication to SCIT because it significantly increases the risk of life-threatening anaphylaxis and severe bronchospasm during immunotherapy. Patients with active asthma exacerbations should not receive injections until asthma is stabilized. Concurrent allergic rhinitis and mild persistent asthma is actually a common and appropriate indication for AIT. Age under 5 is a relative consideration (not absolute contraindication) — AIT is used in young children when benefits outweigh risks. Multiple allergen sensitizations do not contraindicate AIT; multi-allergen immunotherapy is routinely prescribed.

questions.stem: What advantage does sublingual immunotherapy (SLIT) have over subcutaneous immunotherapy (SCIT)?

choices.text: SLIT is more effective than SCIT for all allergens
choices.feedback: SCIT generally shows larger effect sizes than SLIT. The advantage of SLIT is safety and convenience, not superior efficacy.

choices.text: SLIT works in 2 weeks compared to 6 months for SCIT
choices.feedback: Both SLIT and SCIT require months to achieve clinical benefit. Neither works in 2 weeks.

choices.text: SLIT can be administered at home with significantly lower risk of systemic reactions
choices.feedback: Correct! SLIT advantages: home administration after first dose in clinic (no monthly office visits), very low anaphylaxis risk (~1 in 100 million doses vs ~1 in 1 million for SCIT), no needle injections, better adherence in some populations (children, needle-phobic). Available SLIT tablets: grass (Grastek), ragweed (Ragwitek), dust mite (Odactra), mixed grass (Oralair). SLIT limitations: limited allergen options compared to SCIT (can customize SCIT mixes), local oral side effects (sublingual pruritus/swelling), daily dosing required.

choices.text: SLIT completely replaces the need for pharmacotherapy
choices.feedback: Both SLIT and SCIT reduce but typically do not completely eliminate the need for symptom-relief medications.

questions.explanation: SLIT's primary advantage is home administration after the first supervised dose, with a significantly lower risk of systemic reactions compared to SCIT. SLIT anaphylaxis is extremely rare (estimated 11 nonfatal cases per billion doses). The most common SLIT adverse effects are local oromucosal reactions (itching, swelling under the tongue). SLIT is not more effective than SCIT for all allergens — comparative data suggest similar or slightly lower efficacy for some allergens. SLIT does not work faster than SCIT. SLIT does not eliminate the need for pharmacotherapy — it is used as adjunctive disease-modifying therapy.

questions.stem: What is the most important safety measure when administering subcutaneous immunotherapy injections?

choices.text: Administering injections only in hospital emergency departments
choices.feedback: SCIT is routinely administered in allergy outpatient offices with proper safety protocols. Emergency department setting is not required.

choices.text: Pre-treat with oral antihistamines 30 minutes before every injection
choices.feedback: Antihistamine pre-treatment is optional (may reduce local reactions) but is not the most critical safety measure.

choices.text: 30-minute in-office observation period after each injection with epinephrine immediately available
choices.feedback: Correct! Mandatory 30-minute observation: most systemic reactions (including anaphylaxis) occur within 30 minutes of SCIT injection. Requirements: trained staff to recognize anaphylaxis, epinephrine auto-injectors/vials immediately available, resuscitation equipment, written emergency protocol. Assess patient before each injection: check for current asthma symptoms, illness, recent reaction. Measure FEV1 or PEF if asthmatic. Reduce dose if: previous systemic reaction, new vial (potency variation), gap in schedule, uncontrolled asthma.

choices.text: Aspiration before injection to ensure the needle is not in a blood vessel
choices.feedback: While aspiration is commonly taught, the 30-minute observation period is the most critical safety measure, as systemic reactions require immediate recognition and treatment.

questions.explanation: The 30-minute in-office observation period after each SCIT injection is the most critical safety measure because most anaphylactic reactions to SCIT occur within this window. Epinephrine and emergency resuscitation equipment must be immediately available, and personnel must be trained in anaphylaxis management. Injections are given in medical offices (not necessarily emergency departments) where a physician is present. Pretreatment with oral antihistamines is sometimes used but is not the most important safety measure and may mask early signs of systemic reactions. Aspiration before injection is a technique consideration but not the primary safety protocol.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man on metoprolol for heart failure develops anaphylaxis to a bee sting. His IM epinephrine does not adequately resolve hypotension. What is the pathophysiologic basis and recommended next agent?

choices.text: Beta-blockade may blunt epinephrine's beta-adrenergic effects; administer IV glucagon which increases cAMP independent of adrenergic receptors
Beta-blockers completely neutralize all epinephrine activity; switch to norepinephrine infusion
Beta-blockers cause epinephrine to produce unopposed alpha vasoconstriction; administer IV phentolamine
Beta-blockers prevent mast cell stabilization; administer IV cromolyn sodium

questions.explanation: Beta-blockers (metoprolol) competitively antagonize beta-1 and beta-2 adrenergic receptors, blunting epinephrine's positive inotropic/chronotropic effects (beta-1) and bronchodilatory/mast cell stabilizing effects (beta-2). IV glucagon is the recommended next agent because it increases intracellular cAMP through glucagon receptors independent of the beta-adrenergic pathway, providing positive inotropic and chronotropic effects despite beta-blockade. Beta-blockers do not completely neutralize all epinephrine activity — alpha-1 vasoconstriction is preserved. Phentolamine (alpha-blocker) would worsen hypotension. IV cromolyn has no role in acute anaphylaxis management.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: When prescribing epinephrine auto-injectors at discharge after an anaphylaxis episode, how many devices should be prescribed and why?

choices.text: Two devices — approximately 10-20% of episodes require a second dose, and biphasic reactions may occur
One device — a single IM dose resolves virtually all anaphylaxis episodes
Two devices — the second is intended for a family member or bystander to use simultaneously
Three devices — one for home, one for work, and one for travel

questions.explanation: Two epinephrine auto-injectors are prescribed at discharge because 10-20% of anaphylaxis episodes require a repeat dose, and biphasic reactions may recur hours after initial resolution. Patients and caregivers should be trained on proper auto-injector use, understand that the second device is for repeat dosing of the same patient if symptoms recur or persist, and carry both devices at all times. A single device is insufficient. The rationale is repeat dosing need, not simultaneous use by a bystander. Location-specific distribution is practical but not the primary prescribing justification.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A nurse asks whether any conditions contraindicate administering epinephrine to a patient with suspected anaphylaxis. Which statement is most accurate?

choices.text: There are no absolute contraindications to IM epinephrine for life-threatening anaphylaxis
Pregnancy is an absolute contraindication due to uterine artery vasoconstriction
Sulfite allergy contraindicates EpiPen use since it contains sodium metabisulfite
Age over 65 with coronary artery disease is an absolute contraindication

questions.explanation: There are no absolute contraindications to IM epinephrine for life-threatening anaphylaxis. The benefit of treating a potentially fatal condition always outweighs the risks. Pregnancy is not a contraindication — maternal death from untreated anaphylaxis is a far greater risk to mother and fetus than epinephrine's effects. While EpiPen contains sodium metabisulfite, sulfite sensitivity is a relative concern that does not contraindicate use in anaphylaxis. Elderly patients with coronary artery disease may have increased cardiovascular risk from epinephrine, but untreated anaphylaxis carries a greater mortality risk.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What advantage does subcutaneous immunoglobulin (SCIG) have over IVIG?

choices.text: SCIG achieves higher peak IgG levels
choices.feedback: SCIG actually produces LOWER peaks and HIGHER troughs (more stable levels) compared to IVIG's high peak/low trough pattern.

choices.text: SCIG is significantly cheaper than IVIG
choices.feedback: The immunoglobulin product cost is similar. SCIG saves on infusion center/nursing costs but the drug expense is comparable.

choices.text: Home self-administration, stable serum IgG levels, and fewer systemic side effects
choices.feedback: Correct! SCIG advantages: self-infusion at home (independence, no IV access, no hospital visits), more stable/physiologic IgG levels (avoids IVIG peak-trough cycling → patients feel better consistently), fewer systemic side effects (headache, fatigue less common than IVIG), no IV access-related risks. SCIG disadvantages: local infusion site reactions (swelling, redness — usually improve over months), more frequent dosing (weekly vs monthly), lower volume per site (multiple sites may be needed). Facilitated SCIG (Hylenex + Ig): allows larger volumes per site, monthly dosing.

choices.text: SCIG can treat autoimmune conditions while IVIG cannot
choices.feedback: Actually, IVIG is used for autoimmune/inflammatory conditions at high immunomodulatory doses. SCIG is primarily used for replacement therapy at lower doses.

questions.explanation: SCIG advantages over IVIG include: home self-administration (eliminating infusion center visits), stable serum IgG levels (weekly/biweekly dosing avoids peak-trough fluctuations), and fewer systemic side effects (gradual subcutaneous absorption vs rapid IV infusion). SCIG allows greater patient autonomy and schedule flexibility. Local injection site reactions are the main side effect. SCIG does not require IV access, reducing infection and thrombotic risks associated with venous access.

questions.stem: What is the most common serious adverse effect of IVIG infusion?

choices.text: Severe infusion reactions (headache, fever, chills) and, rarely, aseptic meningitis, thrombotic events, or hemolytic anemia
choices.feedback: Correct! IVIG adverse effects: Common: headache (most common), fever, chills, myalgia, fatigue, nausea — usually rate-related (slow infusion rate). Serious: aseptic meningitis (severe headache, nuchal rigidity — resolves in days), thromboembolic events (stroke, PE, DVT — risk increased with high dose, hypercoagulable states), hemolytic anemia (anti-A/anti-B isoagglutinins in product → DAT-positive hemolysis, especially in non-O blood type patients at high doses). Special: IgA-deficient patients with anti-IgA IgE → anaphylaxis risk (use IgA-depleted product).
True IgG-mediated anaphylaxis is rare. The most dangerous IVIG reactions occur in specific patient populations.
IVIG can cause osmotic nephropathy (especially sucrose-containing products) but this is not the most common effect. It's seen primarily in patients with pre-existing renal disease.
IVIG provides passive antibody protection; it does not cause immunosuppression. High-dose immunomodulatory IVIG may temporarily modulate immune responses in autoimmune conditions.

questions.explanation: The most common serious adverse effects of IVIG include severe rate-related infusion reactions (headache, fever, chills, myalgias, nausea), and rare but serious complications: aseptic meningitis (severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia within 24-48 hours), thromboembolic events (stroke, DVT, PE, MI — especially in patients with cardiovascular risk factors), acute kidney injury (particularly with sucrose-containing formulations), and hemolytic anemia (from anti-A/anti-B antibodies in IVIG). Anaphylaxis in IgA-deficient patients is the most feared reaction.

questions.stem: An IgA-deficient patient needs immunoglobulin replacement. What precaution is necessary?

choices.text: Give oral immunoglobulin instead of IV or SC
choices.feedback: Oral immunoglobulin is not absorbed systemically and is not a viable replacement therapy route.

choices.text: No special precautions needed — all IVIG products are safe
choices.feedback: IgA-deficient patients with anti-IgA antibodies can have severe anaphylactic reactions to standard IVIG containing trace IgA.

choices.text: Use IgA-depleted immunoglobulin product and pre-medicate; consider SCIG route
choices.feedback: Correct! Selective IgA deficiency (~1:500 prevalence) + anti-IgA IgE antibodies → risk of anaphylaxis from IgA in standard Ig products. Precautions: use IgA-depleted IVIG product (e.g., Gammagard S/D — very low IgA content), pre-medicate with antihistamines/corticosteroids, slow initial infusion rate with close monitoring, have epinephrine available. SCIG may be safer (slower absorption, less likely to trigger systemic reactions). Not all IgA-deficient patients have anti-IgA antibodies — test first. Most IgA-deficient patients don't need Ig replacement unless they also have IgG subclass deficiency.

choices.text: Administer standard IVIG but at half the normal dose
choices.feedback: Reducing the dose does not address the anaphylaxis risk from IgA content. An IgA-depleted product is needed.

questions.explanation: For IgA-deficient patients requiring immunoglobulin replacement, precautions include: (1) use IgA-depleted IVIG products (e.g., Gammagard S/D, which has the lowest IgA content), (2) premedicate with antihistamines and corticosteroids, (3) consider SCIG as an alternative route (lower IgA content and slower absorption reduce anaphylaxis risk), (4) have epinephrine immediately available during infusions, and (5) start with slow infusion rates. Some IgA-deficient patients tolerate standard IVIG products if they lack anti-IgA antibodies.

questions.stem: A patient with suspected penicillin allergy reports a childhood rash. What is the most appropriate initial evaluation?

choices.text: Penicillin skin testing followed by oral challenge if negative
choices.feedback: Correct! Penicillin skin testing (PST): puncture + intradermal with major determinant (penicilloyl-polylysine/Pre-Pen) and minor determinants. Negative predictive value >97%. If skin test negative → graded oral amoxicillin challenge to confirm tolerance. This approach safely de-labels >90% of patients and allows access to first-line beta-lactams. AUA/AAAAI guidelines strongly recommend allergy evaluation before labeling patients.
Serum IgE (ImmunoCAP) can be used but has lower sensitivity (~50%) than skin testing. Skin testing remains the gold standard for penicillin allergy evaluation.

choices.text: Direct oral amoxicillin challenge without any preliminary testing
choices.feedback: For patients with a history of severe reactions (anaphylaxis), skin testing should precede oral challenge. Direct challenges are acceptable only for very low-risk histories (GI side effects, remote mild rash).
Over 90% of patients labeled penicillin-allergic are NOT truly allergic when formally tested. Avoidance without evaluation leads to suboptimal antibiotic choices.

questions.explanation: For suspected penicillin allergy with a childhood rash history, the standard evaluation is penicillin skin testing (major determinant penicilloyl-polylysine and minor determinant mixture, plus penicillin G) followed by graded oral amoxicillin challenge if skin testing is negative. Over 90% of patients labeled penicillin-allergic are found to be tolerant on formal evaluation. Serum-specific IgE alone has insufficient sensitivity. Direct oral challenge without preliminary skin testing is inappropriate in patients with a history consistent with possible IgE-mediated reaction. Permanent beta-lactam avoidance is unnecessarily restrictive — de-labeling allows access to first-line antibiotics.
questions.citation: Drug Allergy. AAAAI/ACAAI Practice Parameter. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What does a positive skin prick test (SPT) to an aeroallergen indicate?

choices.feedback: A positive SPT indicates sensitization (presence of allergen-specific IgE), NOT necessarily clinical allergy. Must correlate with symptoms.
SPT detects Type I (IgE-mediated) immediate hypersensitivity, NOT Type IV (T-cell mediated delayed). Patch testing is used for Type IV reactions.

choices.text: IgE-mediated sensitization that must be correlated with clinical history
choices.feedback: Correct! Positive SPT = wheal ≥3 mm larger than negative control = presence of allergen-specific IgE on mast cells. Sensitization ≠ clinical allergy. Many sensitized individuals are asymptomatic. Clinical relevance requires correlation with: timing of symptoms relative to exposure, symptom pattern, exposure history. Serum-specific IgE (sIgE) provides similar information quantitatively. Component-resolved diagnostics (CRD) can help distinguish true allergy from cross-reactivity.
SPT does not measure complement activation. It measures IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation (Type I hypersensitivity).

questions.explanation: A positive SPT to an aeroallergen indicates IgE-mediated sensitization — allergen-specific IgE bound to cutaneous mast cells causes wheal-and-flare upon allergen cross-linking. This must always be correlated with clinical history to determine clinical relevance. A positive SPT alone does not confirm clinical allergy requiring avoidance. SPT detects Type I (IgE-mediated) hypersensitivity, not Type IV (cell-mediated/delayed) or complement-mediated responses.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: Which medications must be withheld before skin prick testing and for how long?

choices.feedback: Inhaled corticosteroids do NOT suppress skin test reactivity and do NOT need to be withheld.

choices.text: Antihistamines for 3-7 days depending on the agent
choices.feedback: Correct! Antihistamines suppress the wheal-and-flare response → false negatives. Withdrawal times: first-gen (diphenhydramine): 3-5 days; second-gen (cetirizine, loratadine): 3-7 days; hydroxyzine: 7+ days; tricyclic antidepressants (doxepin): 7+ days. Medications that do NOT need to be stopped: systemic corticosteroids (short courses), inhaled corticosteroids, leukotriene modifiers, decongestants, beta-agonists.
Leukotriene receptor antagonists (montelukast) do NOT suppress skin prick test results and do not need to be withheld.
Only medications that block histamine H1 receptors need to be withheld. Most medications have no effect on skin testing.

questions.explanation: First-generation antihistamines (diphenhydramine, chlorpheniramine) must be withheld for 3 days, second-generation antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine) for 3-7 days, and hydroxyzine for 7 days before SPT. Antihistamines suppress the histamine-mediated wheal response, causing false-negative results. Inhaled corticosteroids do not need to be withheld — they do not affect cutaneous mast cell reactivity. Leukotriene inhibitors (montelukast) do not significantly suppress SPT. Oral corticosteroids in short courses have minimal effect on SPT (may suppress if used >1 week at high doses). There is no need to stop all oral medications.

questions.stem: Component-resolved diagnostics (CRD) for peanut allergy reveals high Ara h 2 IgE. What does this indicate?

choices.text: Tolerance to roasted but not raw peanut
choices.feedback: Ara h 2 is heat-stable, so patients sensitized to it typically react to both raw and roasted peanut.

choices.text: High risk of systemic/anaphylactic reaction to peanut
choices.feedback: Correct! Ara h 2 is a 2S albumin storage protein — the most predictive component for clinical peanut allergy and anaphylaxis risk. Stable to heat/digestion → triggers systemic reactions. Compare: Ara h 8 (PR-10, birch-peanut cross-reactivity → usually mild oral symptoms only), Ara h 9 (lipid transfer protein → variable severity). CRD helps distinguish true peanut allergy from cross-reactivity and guides oral food challenge decisions.
Cross-reactivity with birch pollen is associated with Ara h 8 (PR-10 protein), not Ara h 2.

choices.text: Outgrowth of peanut allergy is likely
choices.feedback: High Ara h 2 levels are associated with persistent allergy and higher reaction severity, making outgrowth less likely.

questions.explanation: Ara h 2 is a 2S albumin storage protein in peanut that is the strongest predictor of clinical peanut allergy and systemic/anaphylactic reactions. It is a stable protein resistant to heat and digestion, meaning sensitization to Ara h 2 correlates with reactions to all forms of peanut (raw, roasted, processed). In contrast, Ara h 8 (PR-10, birch cross-reactive) predicts oral allergy syndrome only, and Ara h 9 (lipid transfer protein) has geographic variability. High Ara h 2 IgE does not predict tolerance to roasted peanut (storage proteins are heat-stable). It indicates genuine peanut allergy, not just birch cross-reactivity, and does not predict outgrowth.

questions.stem: When is serum tryptase most useful in allergy diagnosis?

choices.text: To determine which foods to eliminate
choices.feedback: Tryptase does not identify specific allergens. It measures overall mast cell degranulation and mast cell burden.
Serum tryptase is not useful for allergic rhinitis diagnosis. Skin testing or sIgE is used.
Immunotherapy response is monitored clinically (symptom improvement) and sometimes with blocking antibodies (IgG4). Tryptase is not a monitoring tool for immunotherapy.

choices.text: To confirm anaphylaxis retrospectively and screen for mast cell disorders
choices.feedback: Correct! Serum tryptase: Acute draw (15 min–3 hours post-event) → elevated peak tryptase confirms mast cell degranulation/anaphylaxis. Baseline tryptase >20 ng/mL (or >11.5 using updated criteria) suggests systemic mastocytosis. Also elevated in: mast cell activation syndrome, some hematologic malignancies. Calculate: peak tryptase > 1.2× baseline + 2 = clinically significant. Always obtain baseline tryptase if anaphylaxis workup.

questions.explanation: Serum tryptase is most useful for: (1) retrospective confirmation of anaphylaxis — peak tryptase drawn 1-2 hours after onset (elevated >11.4 ng/mL supports mast cell degranulation), and (2) screening for mast cell disorders (mastocytosis) when baseline tryptase is persistently elevated. Baseline tryptase >20 ng/mL warrants bone marrow biopsy for systemic mastocytosis. Tryptase does not identify specific food allergens for elimination diets. It plays no role in diagnosing seasonal rhinitis (which does not involve significant systemic mast cell degranulation). It is not used to monitor immunotherapy response.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: Which biomarker best identifies Type 2 (T2)-high asthma amenable to biologic therapy?

choices.feedback: Procalcitonin is a marker of bacterial infection, not asthma phenotyping.
Sputum neutrophils indicate non-T2 (neutrophilic) asthma, which currently has fewer targeted biologic options.
Total IgG is not relevant to asthma phenotyping. Total IgE and allergen-specific IgE are the relevant immunoglobulin markers.

choices.text: Blood eosinophils ≥150 cells/μL and/or FeNO ≥20 ppb
choices.feedback: Correct! T2-high asthma biomarkers: blood eosinophils (≥150-300 cells/μL, threshold varies by biologic), FeNO (fractional exhaled nitric oxide, ≥20 ppb suggests eosinophilic airway inflammation), and total/specific IgE. T2-high includes: allergic asthma (IgE-driven → omalizumab), eosinophilic asthma (IL-5 → mepolizumab, benralizumab; IL-4/13 → dupilumab). T2-low asthma: neutrophilic or paucigranulocytic, fewer biologic options.

questions.explanation: T2-high asthma amenable to biologic therapy is identified by blood eosinophils ≥150 cells/μL and/or FeNO ≥20 ppb (GINA thresholds). These biomarkers reflect IL-5-driven eosinophil production and IL-13-driven iNOS upregulation in airway epithelium, respectively. Together they predict response to biologics targeting IL-4, IL-5, IL-13, IgE, or TSLP. Procalcitonin is a bacterial sepsis marker. Sputum neutrophil counts identify neutrophilic (non-T2) inflammation. Serum IgG has no role in asthma phenotyping (IgE is relevant for allergic asthma, not IgG).

questions.stem: A patient with severe eosinophilic asthma (blood eosinophils 500 cells/μL) fails high-dose ICS/LABA. Which biologic targets IL-5?

choices.feedback: Dupilumab targets IL-4Rα (blocking both IL-4 and IL-13). While effective in eosinophilic asthma, it does not target IL-5 specifically.
Omalizumab binds IgE and is indicated for allergic asthma with elevated IgE, not specifically for eosinophilic asthma.

Correct! Anti-IL-5 biologics for eosinophilic asthma: Mepolizumab (anti-IL-5 mAb → prevents IL-5 from binding receptor on eosinophils), Reslizumab (also anti-IL-5, IV administration), Benralizumab (anti-IL-5Rα → directly depletes eosinophils via ADCC — most rapid eosinophil reduction). All reduce exacerbations by ~50% and enable OCS sparing. Selection based on: eosinophil count, IgE level, comorbidities (nasal polyps → dupilumab preferred).
Tezepelumab targets TSLP (thymic stromal lymphopoietin), an upstream epithelial cytokine. It is effective across phenotypes but does not specifically target IL-5.

questions.explanation: Mepolizumab (anti-IL-5) is specifically indicated for severe eosinophilic asthma uncontrolled on high-dose ICS/LABA. With blood eosinophils of 500 cells/μL, this patient has clear eosinophilic phenotype. Mepolizumab reduces exacerbations by approximately 50% and allows oral corticosteroid dose reduction. Dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα) is also effective for eosinophilic asthma but works via IL-4/IL-13, not IL-5. Omalizumab (anti-IgE) requires documented allergen sensitization and elevated IgE. Tezepelumab (anti-TSLP) is phenotype-agnostic but the question specifically asks which targets IL-5.

questions.stem: What distinguishes T2-low asthma from T2-high asthma?

choices.text: Normal eosinophils/FeNO, neutrophilic or paucigranulocytic inflammation, and poor response to corticosteroids
choices.feedback: Correct! T2-low asthma: normal blood eosinophils (<150) and FeNO (<20 ppb), often neutrophilic sputum pattern. Associated with: obesity, smoking, occupational exposures, older onset. Poor response to ICS. Limited biologic options — tezepelumab (anti-TSLP) shows some benefit. Management focuses on: LAMA add-on, macrolides (azithromycin for neutrophilic), bronchial thermoplasty, weight loss if obese.

choices.text: T2-low asthma responds better to inhaled corticosteroids
choices.feedback: T2-LOW asthma actually responds POORLY to inhaled corticosteroids — this is one of its defining features.
Fixed obstruction (asthma-COPD overlap) can occur in both T2-high and T2-low phenotypes and is not a distinguishing feature.
Viral triggers are common in all asthma phenotypes. This does not specifically distinguish T2-low from T2-high.

questions.explanation: T2-low asthma is characterized by normal blood eosinophils and FeNO with neutrophilic (sputum neutrophils >61%) or paucigranulocytic (few inflammatory cells of any type) inflammation. Critically, T2-low asthma responds poorly to inhaled and systemic corticosteroids, which primarily target eosinophilic inflammation. Potential drivers include obesity, smoking, infections, and neutrophilic pathways (IL-17, IL-8). T2-high asthma actually responds better to ICS. Fixed airflow obstruction can occur in both phenotypes with long-standing disease. Viral-triggered exacerbations are common across all asthma phenotypes.
questions.citation: Asthma. StatPearls. NBK430901. [Open Access] | PMC5765385. [Open Access]

questions.stem: FeNO is measured at 45 ppb in a patient with uncontrolled asthma on medium-dose ICS. What is the most likely interpretation?

choices.text: Ongoing eosinophilic inflammation suggesting poor ICS adherence or need for ICS step-up
choices.feedback: Correct! FeNO > 25 ppb in adults (>20 in children) indicates ongoing eosinophilic airway inflammation. In a patient on ICS with high FeNO: (1) Check adherence/inhaler technique (most common reason), (2) Check for allergen exposure driving ongoing inflammation, (3) Consider stepping up ICS or adding biologic. FeNO is suppressed by ICS when taken correctly, so persistent elevation despite ICS suggests non-adherence or refractory T2 inflammation.
FeNO ≥ 25 ppb suggests eosinophilic/T2-high inflammation, NOT non-eosinophilic.

choices.text: The patient needs antibiotics for a respiratory infection
choices.feedback: Elevated FeNO indicates eosinophilic inflammation, not bacterial infection. Procalcitonin or CRP would be more relevant for infection.
FeNO of 45 ppb is elevated and indicates ongoing T2 inflammation. Normal FeNO is < 25 ppb in adults.

questions.explanation: FeNO of 45 ppb is elevated (normal <25 ppb) and indicates ongoing eosinophilic airway inflammation despite medium-dose ICS. The most common explanations are poor ICS adherence (most frequent cause), incorrect inhaler technique, or genuine need for ICS dose escalation/add-on therapy. This finding confirms T2/eosinophilic inflammation, not non-eosinophilic asthma. Elevated FeNO does not indicate bacterial infection requiring antibiotics. A FeNO of 45 ppb is distinctly abnormal in asthma and warrants clinical action — adherence assessment should come first, followed by step-up therapy if adherence is confirmed.

questions.stem: Which biologic is appropriate for BOTH severe eosinophilic asthma AND comorbid chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps?

choices.feedback: Correct! Dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα) blocks both IL-4 and IL-13 → broad T2 inflammation suppression. Indicated for: moderate-severe eosinophilic asthma, chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps (CRSwNP), atopic dermatitis, eosinophilic esophagitis, and prurigo nodularis. The shared T2 pathway makes it ideal for asthma + polyps comorbidity. Reduces exacerbations, improves FEV1, shrinks polyps, and reduces need for sinus surgery.
Omalizumab treats allergic asthma and is approved for chronic urticaria, but dupilumab has stronger evidence for the nasal polyps indication.
Benralizumab (anti-IL-5Rα) effectively treats eosinophilic asthma but is not indicated for nasal polyps. Dupilumab covers both.
Tezepelumab is approved for asthma but is not currently indicated for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps.

questions.explanation: Dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα) blocks both IL-4 and IL-13 signaling, targeting the shared receptor subunit. It is indicated for both severe eosinophilic asthma AND chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps (CRSwNP), making it uniquely appropriate for patients with both comorbidities. Clinical trials show significant reduction in both asthma exacerbations and nasal polyp size. Omalizumab (anti-IgE) is approved for allergic asthma but not specifically for CRSwNP. Benralizumab (anti-IL-5Rα) depletes eosinophils but does not have the same dual indication for nasal polyps as dupilumab. Tezepelumab is approved for severe asthma but not yet for CRSwNP.
questions.citation: Asthma. StatPearls. NBK430901. [Open Access] | PMC9316404. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman develops diffuse urticaria, throat tightness, and hypotension within minutes of a bee sting. What is the correct first-line treatment dose and route?

choices.text: IM epinephrine in the anterolateral thigh
choices.feedback: Correct! IM injection in the anterolateral thigh (vastus lateralis) provides the fastest and most reliable absorption. The thigh has better blood flow than the deltoid, especially during shock.
IV epinephrine is reserved for cardiac arrest or refractory anaphylaxis with hemodynamic monitoring. In the acute setting without IV access or monitoring, IM is safer and first-line.
Nebulized epinephrine treats upper airway edema (stridor) but does not provide systemic effects needed for anaphylaxis (hypotension, bronchospasm).
Subcutaneous absorption is slower and less reliable than IM, especially in shock when peripheral vasoconstriction limits subcutaneous blood flow. Deltoid is also inferior to thigh.

questions.explanation: For anaphylaxis (urticaria + throat tightness + hypotension), the correct first-line treatment is IM epinephrine (0.3-0.5 mg of 1:1000/1 mg/mL in adults) injected in the anterolateral thigh. The thigh provides rapid absorption due to high vascularity. IM is preferred over IV push (which risks cardiac arrhythmias and is reserved for refractory cases with continuous monitoring). Nebulized epinephrine treats upper airway edema but does not address systemic hypotension. Subcutaneous injection in the deltoid has slower, less reliable absorption than IM thigh injection.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: An emergency medicine resident reviews anaphylaxis fatality data. Which factor is most consistently associated with fatal anaphylaxis outcomes?

choices.text: Failure to administer IV corticosteroids within 30 minutes

questions.explanation: Delayed or absent epinephrine administration is the most consistent risk factor for fatal anaphylaxis across fatality registries worldwide. Studies show that most anaphylaxis deaths occur in patients who either did not receive epinephrine or received it too late after symptom onset. Prompt IM epinephrine is the single most important intervention to prevent progression to cardiovascular collapse and death. Wrong concentration and IV corticosteroid timing are secondary concerns. Fluid resuscitation supports hemodynamics but cannot substitute for epinephrine's receptor-mediated effects.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A medical student asks why epinephrine is first-line for anaphylaxis rather than antihistamines or corticosteroids. Which explanation best describes epinephrine's multi-receptor mechanism?

choices.text: Alpha-1 vasoconstriction reverses hypotension and edema; beta-1 increases cardiac output; beta-2 causes bronchodilation and stabilizes mast cells
Direct antagonism of histamine H1 and H2 receptors combined with leukotriene inhibition
Stimulation of adrenal cortisol release producing rapid anti-inflammatory effects
Primarily beta-2 bronchodilation with minimal cardiovascular effects at IM doses

questions.explanation: Epinephrine is first-line for anaphylaxis because of its unique multi-receptor mechanism: alpha-1 vasoconstriction (reverses hypotension, reduces angioedema), beta-1 positive inotropy/chronotropy (increases cardiac output), and beta-2 bronchodilation plus mast cell/basophil stabilization (reduces further mediator release via cAMP elevation). No other single drug addresses all three pathophysiologic components of anaphylaxis simultaneously. Antihistamines only block one mediator. Corticosteroids have delayed onset (hours). Beta-2 effects alone would not address hypotension.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman on propranolol develops anaphylaxis to IV contrast. After two appropriate IM epinephrine doses and 2L crystalloid, she remains hypotensive with heart rate 52. What is the next pharmacologic intervention?

choices.text: IV glucagon, which bypasses beta-adrenergic blockade via direct cAMP activation
choices.feedback: Correct! Glucagon activates adenylyl cyclase independently of the beta receptor, increasing intracellular cAMP. This provides inotropic and chronotropic support even when beta receptors are blocked.

choices.text: IV atropine to treat the bradycardia before repeating epinephrine
choices.feedback: Atropine addresses vagal-mediated bradycardia but does not overcome beta-blocker-induced resistance to epinephrine in anaphylaxis.

choices.text: IV methylprednisolone for refractory anaphylaxis from immune complex deposition
choices.feedback: Steroids have no role in acute anaphylaxis management — onset is hours, too slow for an emergency. They may help prevent biphasic reactions but do not treat acute refractory symptoms.

choices.text: Higher-dose IM epinephrine to overcome the beta-blockade
choices.feedback: Simply increasing epinephrine dose does not overcome pharmacologic beta-blockade effectively and increases risk of alpha-mediated hypertension and arrhythmias.

questions.explanation: In beta-blocker refractory anaphylaxis (propranolol blocks both beta-1 and beta-2 receptors), IV glucagon is the next agent because it activates adenylyl cyclase and increases cAMP independent of the beta-adrenergic receptor pathway. Glucagon provides positive inotropic and chronotropic effects, and bronchodilation, bypassing the beta-blockade. IV atropine may treat bradycardia but does not address hypotension from vasodilation or bronchospasm. IV methylprednisolone has delayed onset and does not treat acute refractory anaphylaxis. Higher-dose IM epinephrine faces the same beta-receptor blockade.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: After successful treatment of anaphylaxis with one dose of IM epinephrine, how long should the patient be monitored before discharge?

choices.text: Minimum 4-6 hours; longer observation for severe reactions, beta-blocker use, or history of biphasic reactions
1 hour is sufficient if all symptoms have resolved completely
24-hour mandatory inpatient admission for all anaphylaxis episodes regardless of severity
Observation until antihistamine effects fully resolve all cutaneous symptoms

questions.explanation: After successful anaphylaxis treatment, patients should be monitored for a minimum of 4-6 hours, with longer observation for severe initial presentations, beta-blocker use, or history of biphasic reactions (which occur in up to 20% of cases, typically within 4-12 hours). One hour is insufficient — biphasic reactions can occur well beyond this window. Mandatory 24-hour admission for all episodes is not evidence-based and not practical for mild, rapidly resolving cases. Observation is not determined by antihistamine effect resolution — it is based on biphasic reaction risk.
questions.citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of allergen immunotherapy (allergy shots)?

choices.text: Immune deviation from Th2 to Th1/Treg responses with production of blocking IgG4 antibodies
choices.feedback: Correct! Allergen immunotherapy induces: (1) Immune deviation: shifts from Th2 (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13) toward Th1 and Treg (IL-10, TGF-β). (2) Blocking antibodies: increases allergen-specific IgG4 → competes with IgE for allergen binding → prevents mast cell activation. (3) Reduced mast cell/basophil reactivity. (4) Decreased tissue eosinophilia. Clinical: reduces symptoms, medication needs, and prevents new sensitizations. Disease-modifying — effects persist years after completion.
Immunotherapy does not desensitize histamine receptors. Antihistamines block histamine receptors; immunotherapy modifies the upstream immune response.
Immunotherapy actually causes a transient INCREASE in specific IgE early on, followed by gradual decline. The key mechanism is IgG4 blocking antibodies and Treg induction.

choices.text: Suppression of all immune responses via immunosuppressive drugs
choices.feedback: Immunotherapy does not use immunosuppressive drugs. It modifies the specific allergic immune response.

questions.explanation: Allergen immunotherapy induces immune deviation from Th2 to Th1/Treg responses. Key mechanisms include Treg-mediated IL-10 production, suppression of eosinophils and mast cells, and class switching from IgE to IgG4 blocking antibodies that compete with IgE for allergen binding. Histamine receptors are not directly desensitized by AIT. IgE is not depleted — it gradually declines over years while IgG4 rises 10-100 fold. AIT is allergen-specific immunomodulation, not broad immunosuppression via drugs.

questions.stem: A patient receiving subcutaneous immunotherapy (SCIT) develops generalized urticaria and throat tightness 15 minutes after injection. What is the immediate treatment?

choices.feedback: Albuterol addresses bronchospasm but not the systemic anaphylaxis (hypotension, angioedema). Epinephrine is needed for the systemic reaction.
Steroids take hours to work and do not treat acute anaphylaxis. Epinephrine is the first-line treatment that must be given immediately.

choices.text: IM epinephrine, then monitor and activate emergency protocol
choices.feedback: Correct! Systemic reaction during SCIT = anaphylaxis protocol: (1) IM epinephrine immediately, (2) supine position with legs elevated, (3) supplemental oxygen, (4) IV access and fluids, (5) H1 + H2 antihistamines, (6) observe minimum 30-60 minutes after stabilization. All SCIT offices must have: epinephrine, resuscitation equipment, trained staff, 30-minute post-injection observation requirement. Dose adjustment: future injections reduced (typically back 2-3 dose levels).
Oral antihistamines are adjunctive only. This presentation requires immediate epinephrine — throat tightness suggests anaphylaxis.

questions.explanation: Generalized urticaria with throat tightness after SCIT injection is anaphylaxis requiring immediate IM epinephrine (0.3-0.5 mg of 1:1000 concentration in the anterolateral thigh). This is the first-line treatment — delay increases mortality risk. After epinephrine, activate emergency protocol, monitor vital signs, and prepare for potential second dose if symptoms persist. Nebulized albuterol alone is insufficient — it addresses bronchospasm but not systemic anaphylaxis. IV methylprednisolone may help prevent biphasic reactions but has delayed onset and should not replace epinephrine. Oral diphenhydramine alone is inadequate for anaphylaxis — antihistamines are adjunctive, not primary treatment.
questions.citation: Allergy Immunotherapy. StatPearls. NBK535367. [Open Access] | Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

questions.stem: How does sublingual immunotherapy (SLIT) differ from subcutaneous immunotherapy (SCIT)?

choices.text: SLIT can be administered at home after the first dose, with lower systemic reaction risk
choices.feedback: Correct! SLIT (sublingual tablets/drops): administered at home daily after supervised first dose in clinic. Lower anaphylaxis risk than SCIT (no deaths reported in US clinical trials). Available SLIT: grass (Grastek), ragweed (Ragwitek), dust mite (Odactra). SCIT advantages: broader allergen combinations, possibly higher efficacy, more evidence for multiple allergens. SLIT advantages: home administration, better adherence for some, lower systemic reaction risk. Both are disease-modifying.

choices.text: SLIT is more effective than SCIT for all allergens
choices.feedback: Head-to-head comparisons generally show SCIT has somewhat greater efficacy, though SLIT has sufficient evidence for key allergens.

choices.text: SLIT requires weekly clinic visits like SCIT
choices.feedback: A major advantage of SLIT is home administration — no weekly clinic visits needed (unlike SCIT which requires in-office injections).

choices.text: SLIT works only for food allergies
choices.feedback: SLIT is available for environmental allergens (grass, ragweed, dust mite). Oral immunotherapy (OIT) is used for food allergies — a related but distinct approach.

questions.explanation: SLIT can be self-administered at home after the initial supervised dose in the clinician's office, offering greater convenience than SCIT (which requires every injection in a medical setting with 30-minute observation). SLIT also has a significantly lower risk of systemic reactions and anaphylaxis. SLIT is not universally more effective than SCIT. SLIT does not require weekly clinic visits — daily home administration is the standard. SLIT is available for aeroallergens (grass pollen, ragweed, dust mite), not exclusively food allergies.

questions.stem: What is the typical duration of allergen immunotherapy to achieve lasting disease modification?

choices.feedback: Unlike many chronic disease treatments, immunotherapy achieves lasting modification — most patients maintain benefit after 3-5 years of treatment.
12 months is insufficient for lasting disease modification. At least 3 years is needed based on clinical trial evidence.
6 months is typically still in the build-up phase. Insufficient for disease modification.

Correct! Recommended duration: 3-5 years of maintenance therapy. Evidence shows: sustained clinical benefit persists 7-12+ years after discontinuation (disease-modifying). Additional benefits: prevents new allergen sensitizations and reduces risk of allergic rhinitis progressing to asthma in children (preventive effect). Shorter courses (<3 years) may provide temporary relief but do not achieve lasting immune modification. SCIT: build-up phase (weekly) + maintenance (monthly); SLIT: daily throughout.

questions.explanation: Three to five years of maintenance allergen immunotherapy is the recommended duration for lasting disease modification. This timeframe allows sufficient immune remodeling (Treg expansion, IgG4 production, gradual IgE decline) to produce sustained clinical benefit persisting years after discontinuation. Lifelong therapy is not required. Twelve months or 6 months is insufficient — the build-up phase alone takes 3-6 months, and sustained tolerance requires years of maintenance.

questions.stem: Which condition is the ONLY current established indication for venom immunotherapy?

choices.feedback: Large local reactions to fire ants do not require VIT. Systemic reactions do.
There is no established immunotherapy for mosquito allergy. Mosquito allergy is managed with avoidance, antihistamines, and topical treatments.
Chronic urticaria is treated with antihistamines and omalizumab, not venom immunotherapy.

choices.text: Hymenoptera venom anaphylaxis confirmed by positive skin test or sIgE
choices.feedback: Correct! Venom immunotherapy (VIT) is indicated for patients with a history of systemic (anaphylactic) reaction to Hymenoptera sting AND positive venom skin test or serum-specific IgE. Covers: honeybee, yellow jacket, hornet, wasp, fire ant. VIT is 97-98% effective at preventing future systemic reactions (most effective form of immunotherapy). Duration: typically 5 years, possibly lifelong for highest-risk patients (prior severe anaphylaxis, elevated baseline tryptase, mast cell disorders).

questions.explanation: Venom immunotherapy (VIT) is specifically indicated for patients with systemic (anaphylactic) reactions to Hymenoptera stings (bees, wasps, hornets, yellow jackets, fire ants) confirmed by positive venom skin testing or elevated venom-specific IgE. VIT reduces the risk of future anaphylaxis from approximately 60% to less than 5%. Large local reactions alone (even if extensive) are not an indication for VIT — these carry very low risk of future systemic reactions. Mosquito bite hypersensitivity does not have an established VIT protocol. Chronic idiopathic urticaria is not venom-related and is not treated with VIT.

questions.stem: A patient prescribed a dry powder inhaler (DPI) demonstrates poor technique. Which error is UNIQUE to DPI devices?

choices.feedback: Breath-holding after inhalation (5-10 seconds) is important for both MDIs and DPIs. This is not unique to DPI technique.

choices.text: Exhaling into the device before inhalation
choices.feedback: While exhaling into some DPIs can clump the powder with moisture, the most critical and unique DPI error is insufficient inspiratory flow rate.
Shaking is required for MDIs (to suspend medication in propellant). DPIs do not require shaking — they are breath-activated powders.
