Myelomeningocele is an open defect with exposed neural tissue. This presentation is a closed/occult defect.
Anencephaly is absence of the brain and calvarium — a cranial defect, not lumbosacral.

questions.stem: The midgut undergoes physiologic herniation into the umbilical cord during which weeks of development?

choices.feedback: By week 14, the midgut should already have returned to the abdomen with proper rotation completed.
Weeks 3-4 is when gastrulation and neurulation occur. Midgut herniation is later.

Correct! During weeks 6-10, the midgut herniates into the umbilical cord (physiologic herniation) because the abdominal cavity is too small. During herniation: the midgut rotates 270° counterclockwise around the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). Returns to abdomen by week 10-12. Failure to return → omphalocele. Abnormal rotation → malrotation (risk of midgut volvulus). Abnormal closure of the abdominal wall → gastroschisis (lateral to umbilicus, no sac).
This is far too late for midgut herniation. The GI tract is well-established by this time.

questions.stem: What is the total rotation of the midgut around the SMA axis during normal development?

choices.feedback: 180° would be incomplete rotation (malrotation). Normal is 270° counterclockwise.

Correct! Normal midgut rotation: 270° counterclockwise around the SMA. Stage 1 (during herniation): 90° CCW. Stage 2 (return to abdomen): 180° CCW. Total: 270°. This places the duodenojejunal junction (ligament of Treitz) to the left of midline at L2 and the cecum in the right lower quadrant. Malrotation: incomplete rotation → Ladd bands (duodenal obstruction) + narrow mesenteric pedicle → volvulus risk. Nonrotation: 0° → small bowel right, colon left.
360° would return to the starting position. Normal rotation is 270° counterclockwise.
The rotation is counterclockwise, not clockwise, and much more than 90°.

questions.stem: The foregut gives rise to which of the following structures?

choices.feedback: Jejunum through proximal 2/3 of transverse colon is MIDGUT (SMA territory).
These are HINDGUT derivatives (IMA territory), not foregut.

choices.text: Esophagus, stomach, proximal duodenum (to ampulla of Vater), liver, pancreas, and biliary system
choices.feedback: Correct! Foregut = pharynx to proximal duodenum (at ampulla of Vater/major papilla). Blood supply: celiac trunk. Derivatives include: esophagus, stomach, proximal duodenum, liver (hepatic diverticulum), gallbladder/biliary tree, ventral and dorsal pancreas, spleen (from dorsal mesogastrium mesenchyme). The midgut (SMA) extends from ampulla to proximal 2/3 transverse colon. Hindgut (IMA) = distal 1/3 transverse colon to upper rectum.
Trachea and lungs derive from the foregut (respiratory diverticulum) but foregut also includes all GI structures listed above.

questions.stem: Failure of the tracheoesophageal septum to properly divide the foregut results in what congenital anomaly?

choices.feedback: Diaphragmatic hernia results from failed closure of the pleuroperitoneal membrane, not tracheoesophageal septation.

Correct! During week 4, the tracheoesophageal septum divides the foregut into ventral (trachea) and dorsal (esophagus) tubes. Abnormal septation → esophageal atresia (EA) ± tracheoesophageal fistula (TEF). Most common type (85%): proximal esophageal atresia with distal TEF. Presentation: polyhydramnios (can't swallow amniotic fluid), newborn with excessive drooling, choking with first feed, unable to pass NG tube. VACTERL association: Vertebral, Anal atresia, Cardiac, TEF, Esophageal atresia, Renal, Limb.
Pyloric stenosis is hypertrophy of the pyloric muscle, not a septation defect.
Meckel diverticulum results from persistent vitelline (omphalomesenteric) duct — a midgut anomaly, not foregut septation.

questions.stem: Meckel diverticulum is a remnant of which embryologic structure?

choices.feedback: Mesonephric (Wolffian) duct gives rise to male reproductive structures (vas deferens, epididymis, seminal vesicles), not Meckel diverticulum.
Notochord remnant is the nucleus pulposus of intervertebral discs. Not related to Meckel diverticulum.

Correct! Meckel diverticulum: persistence of the proximal vitelline (omphalomesenteric) duct — the embryonic connection between midgut and yolk sac. Rule of 2s: 2% of population, 2 feet from ileocecal valve, 2 inches long, 2 types of ectopic tissue (gastric and pancreatic), presents by age 2, males 2× more symptomatic. Complications: painless GI bleeding (ectopic gastric mucosa → acid secretion → ulceration), obstruction, intussusception, diverticulitis (mimics appendicitis). Diagnosis: Meckel scan (technetium-99m pertechnetate — uptake by ectopic gastric mucosa).
Allantois remnant is the urachus (median umbilical ligament) — a urinary structure, not GI.

questions.stem: The respiratory system develops as a ventral outgrowth of which embryologic structure?

choices.feedback: The respiratory diverticulum arises from the foregut, not the midgut.
Pharyngeal pouches give rise to middle ear, tonsils, parathyroids, thymus — not the respiratory tract.
Neural crest cells contribute to craniofacial structures and cardiac outflow tract, not the respiratory system.

Correct! During week 4, the respiratory diverticulum (lung bud) forms as a ventral outpouching of the foregut endoderm. The tracheoesophageal septum separates it from the dorsal esophagus. The endoderm → epithelial lining of airways and alveoli (type I and type II pneumocytes). The surrounding splanchnic mesoderm → cartilage, smooth muscle, pulmonary vasculature. Branching morphogenesis: 23 generations of airway branching. Lung development stages: embryonic → pseudoglandular → canalicular → saccular → alveolar.

questions.explanation: Correct: Foregut endoderm (laryngotracheal diverticulum).

questions.stem: At what gestational age do type II pneumocytes begin producing surfactant, making extrauterine survival possible?

choices.feedback: By 34-36 weeks, surfactant is usually mature. Production begins earlier (24-28 weeks) and matures progressively.
This is the pseudoglandular stage — airways are forming but gas exchange is not yet possible.

Correct! Surfactant production begins around 24-28 weeks (late canalicular/early saccular stage). Type II pneumocytes produce surfactant (primarily dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine/DPPC) which reduces alveolar surface tension → prevents atelectasis. Surfactant maturity assessed by lecithin/sphingomyelin (L/S) ratio ≥ 2.0 or presence of phosphatidylglycerol (PG). Preterm infants < 34 weeks: high risk for respiratory distress syndrome (RDS). Prevention: antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone) accelerate surfactant production.
Surfactant is fully mature well before term. The critical threshold is 24-28 weeks when production begins.

questions.explanation: Correct: 24-28 weeks (canalicular to saccular transition).

questions.stem: Which stage of lung development is characterized by formation of terminal sacs lined by type I and type II pneumocytes?

choices.feedback: Correct! Saccular stage: terminal sacs (primitive alveoli) form, lined by thin type I pneumocytes (gas exchange) and type II pneumocytes (surfactant production). Capillaries grow close to epithelium → blood-air barrier forms → gas exchange becomes possible. Stages: Embryonic (3-7 wk): lung bud, trachea, main bronchi. Pseudoglandular (5-17 wk): conducting airways. Canalicular (16-26 wk): respiratory bronchioles, capillary network. Saccular (26-36 wk): terminal sacs. Alveolar (36 wk-8 yr): mature alveoli.
Pseudoglandular stage: conducting airways form (bronchi, bronchioles) but no gas exchange structures.
Alveolar stage involves maturation and multiplication of alveoli. Terminal sacs form in the preceding saccular stage.
Embryonic stage only forms the basic lung buds, trachea, and main bronchi — no gas exchange structures.

questions.stem: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH) most commonly occurs on which side, and what is the main cause of mortality?

choices.feedback: Correct! CDH: 85% left-sided posterolateral (Bochdalek) hernia — failure of pleuroperitoneal membrane closure. Abdominal viscera herniate into thorax → compress the developing lung → pulmonary hypoplasia (ipsilateral > contralateral) + pulmonary hypertension. Mortality from pulmonary hypoplasia/persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN), not from the hernia itself. Prenatal diagnosis: polyhydramnios, absent stomach bubble in abdomen, mediastinal shift. Management: stabilize (avoid bag-mask ventilation), intubate, decompress stomach → ECMO if needed → delayed surgical repair.

choices.text: Bilateral; respiratory failure from absent diaphragm
choices.feedback: Bilateral CDH is very rare and usually lethal. Unilateral left-sided Bochdalek is by far the most common.
Morgagni hernias are anterior/retrosternal and are rare (<5% of CDH). They are often asymptomatic.
Right-sided CDH is less common (~15%). The liver partially protects against right-sided herniation.

questions.stem: Antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone) given to a mother at risk for preterm delivery accelerate fetal lung maturity by what mechanism?

choices.text: Stimulating type II pneumocyte maturation and surfactant production
choices.feedback: Correct! Betamethasone crosses the placenta → accelerates type II pneumocyte differentiation → increases surfactant synthesis and secretion + thins the alveolar-capillary membrane → improved gas exchange. Given at 24-34 weeks gestation if preterm delivery anticipated within 7 days. Reduces RDS by ~50%, IVH, NEC, and neonatal mortality. Standard: betamethasone IM × 2 doses, 24 hours apart. Rescue course may be given if >14 days since initial course.

choices.text: Opening the fetal ductus arteriosus to increase pulmonary blood flow
choices.feedback: The ductus arteriosus is already open in the fetus. Corticosteroids affect lung maturation, not ductus patency.

choices.text: Inhibiting prostaglandin synthesis to prevent premature labor
choices.feedback: Prostaglandin inhibition (indomethacin) is a tocolytic strategy. Corticosteroids work by accelerating lung maturity, not preventing labor.
Corticosteroids don't contain surfactant. They stimulate the fetal lung to produce its own surfactant.

questions.stem: The permanent kidney (metanephros) develops from the interaction of which two structures?

choices.feedback: Correct! Reciprocal induction between: (1) Ureteric bud (from mesonephric duct) → collecting system (collecting ducts, calyces, renal pelvis, ureter) and (2) Metanephric mesenchyme (metanephric blastema) → nephron (glomerulus, PCT, loop of Henle, DCT). GDNF from mesenchyme → attracts ureteric bud; ureteric bud signals → mesenchyme differentiates. Failure of induction → renal agenesis. Three sequential kidneys: pronephros (degenerates) → mesonephros (temporary) → metanephros (permanent, week 5+).
Allantois contributes to the urachus and part of the bladder dome. Cloaca divides into urogenital sinus and rectum. Neither forms the kidney.
Pronephros degenerates by week 4. Mesonephros functions temporarily. Neither becomes the permanent kidney.
Neural crest cells don't contribute to kidney formation. The intermediate mesoderm is the correct germ layer origin, specifically the metanephric mesenchyme and ureteric bud.

questions.stem: Bilateral renal agenesis results in which lethal neonatal condition?

choices.feedback: Correct! Bilateral renal agenesis → no fetal urine → severe oligohydramnios → Potter sequence: pulmonary hypoplasia (lethal — lungs need amniotic fluid for development), limb deformities (compression), flattened facies (Potter facies), growth restriction. Incompatible with life due to pulmonary hypoplasia. Unilateral renal agenesis: compatible with life, contralateral kidney hypertrophies. Potter sequence can occur from any cause of severe oligohydramnios (obstructive uropathy, bilateral MCDK, PROM).
Nephrotic syndrome requires kidneys to be present but dysfunctional. Bilateral agenesis means no kidneys at all.
Prune belly syndrome involves urinary tract dilation, absent abdominal wall musculature, and cryptorchidism — not renal agenesis specifically.
Hydrocephalus is a CNS condition not directly related to renal agenesis.

questions.explanation: Correct: Potter sequence (oligohydramnios → pulmonary hypoplasia, limb/facial deformities).

questions.stem: During fetal life, the kidneys ascend from the pelvis. Abnormal ascent can result in which anomaly?

choices.feedback: Medullary sponge kidney involves cystic dilation of collecting ducts, not abnormal ascent.
Duplication results from premature bifurcation of the ureteric bud, not abnormal ascent.

Correct! Metanephros initially forms in the pelvis (weeks 5-6) → ascends to the lumbar retroperitoneum by week 9. Failed/incomplete ascent → pelvic kidney (remains in pelvis — usually asymptomatic). If kidneys fuse at the lower poles during ascent → horseshoe kidney (fused at lower poles, trapped beneath the IMA which prevents further ascent). Horseshoe kidney: most common renal fusion anomaly (~1:400), associated with Turner syndrome, usually asymptomatic but increased risk of UPJ obstruction, stones, and infections.
Renal artery stenosis is typically acquired (fibromuscular dysplasia, atherosclerosis), not an ascent anomaly.

questions.stem: The bladder is primarily derived from which embryologic structure?

choices.feedback: Mesonephric duct contributes to the trigone region (ureteral orifices incorporate via common excretory duct absorption) but the bladder body derives from the urogenital sinus.
Metanephric mesenchyme forms the nephron, not the bladder.
Paramesonephric (Müllerian) duct forms uterus/tubes/upper vagina in females, not the bladder.

Correct! During weeks 4-7, the urorectal septum divides the cloaca into: anterior urogenital sinus → upper portion becomes the bladder (and prostatic/membranous urethra in males), lower portion becomes penile/vaginal structures. The allantois connects to the bladder apex → normally obliterates to form the urachus (median umbilical ligament). Patent urachus → urine draining from umbilicus. Urachal cyst → midline infraumbilical cystic mass.

questions.explanation: Correct: Urogenital sinus (upper portion).

questions.stem: A patent urachus presents with which clinical finding?

choices.feedback: Meconium from the umbilicus would suggest a patent omphalomesenteric (vitelline) duct, not a urachal anomaly.

Correct! Patent urachus: persistent communication between the bladder dome and umbilicus. Presents with clear/urine-like drainage from the umbilicus after cord separation. Embryology: the allantois normally obliterates → urachus → median umbilical ligament. Failure of obliteration → patent urachus (complete), urachal cyst (partial — midline infraumbilical mass, risk of infection), urachal sinus (drains from umbilicus but doesn't connect to bladder), or urachal diverticulum (bladder outpouching). Treatment: surgical excision.
Bloody discharge from the urethra suggests urethral pathology, not a urachal anomaly.
Inguinal hernias result from patent processus vaginalis, not urachal anomalies.

questions.stem: Somites differentiate into which three regions?

choices.feedback: Correct! Somites (42-44 pairs) form from paraxial mesoderm → each differentiates into: Sclerotome (ventromedial) → vertebrae and ribs (cartilage/bone). Myotome (central) → skeletal muscle of the trunk and limbs. Dermatome (dorsolateral) → dermis of the back. Each somite level corresponds to a segmental nerve → dermatome/myotome pattern persists in adults. Limb muscles derive from somitic myotome cells that migrate into the limb buds under Pax3/Myf5/MyoD control.

choices.text: Notochord, neural plate, and lateral plate
choices.feedback: These are separate embryologic structures, not divisions of somites.
These are germ layers, not somite divisions. Somites are mesodermal structures that subdivide differently.

choices.text: Endochondral bone, intramembranous bone, and cartilage
choices.feedback: These are bone formation pathways, not somite divisions.

questions.stem: Limb bud development is initiated during which embryonic period, and what structure organizes proximal-distal growth?

choices.feedback: Correct! Limb buds appear at week 4 (upper) and week 5 (lower). The AER (thickened ectoderm at the distal edge): maintains proximal-distal growth by keeping underlying mesenchyme (progress zone) in a proliferative undifferentiated state via FGF signaling. Other limb patterning: Zone of polarizing activity (ZPA): posterior mesenchyme → anterior-posterior axis (Sonic hedgehog/SHH → digit identity). Dorsal-ventral: Wnt7a (dorsal) → dorsal structures. Loss of AER → limb truncation (amelia/meromelia). Thalidomide disrupted limb bud development → phocomelia.
Growth plates form later during ossification. Initial limb patterning occurs in weeks 4-8 under AER direction.
The notochord induces neural plate formation. Limb development involves different signaling centers.

choices.text: Week 8; the zone of polarizing activity
choices.feedback: ZPA organizes anterior-posterior patterning (digit identity), not proximal-distal growth. Limb buds appear earlier than week 8.

questions.explanation: Correct: Week 4-5; the apical ectodermal ridge (AER).

questions.stem: Craniosynostosis results from premature closure of which structures?

choices.feedback: Fontanelles are membrane-covered spaces at suture intersections. Craniosynostosis involves premature SUTURE closure, not just fontanelle issues.

choices.text: Cartilaginous synchondroses of the skull base
choices.feedback: Skull base synchondroses (spheno-occipital) are involved in skull base growth but craniosynostosis specifically refers to calvarial suture closure.

Correct! Cranial sutures normally remain open during childhood to allow brain growth. Premature fusion → craniosynostosis → restricted growth perpendicular to the fused suture → compensatory growth parallel to it → characteristic skull deformities. Sagittal (most common, ~50%): scaphocephaly (long, narrow). Coronal (bilateral): brachycephaly (short, wide). Metopic: trigonocephaly (triangular forehead). Lambdoid: plagiocephaly (occipital flattening). Syndromic: Crouzon, Apert (with syndactyly), Pfeiffer — FGFR mutations.
Epiphyseal plates are in long bones. The skull vault involves different ossification centers.

questions.explanation: Correct: Cranial sutures (fibrous joints between skull bones).

questions.stem: The vertebral column forms through which type of ossification?

choices.feedback: Intramembranous ossification forms flat bones of the skull. Vertebrae form by a different process.

Correct! Sclerotome cells (from somites) migrate around the notochord and neural tube → form cartilaginous vertebral models → replaced by bone through endochondral ossification. Each vertebra forms from portions of TWO adjacent somites (resegmentation) → allows spinal nerves to exit between vertebrae. The notochord regresses in the vertebral body regions but persists as the nucleus pulposus within intervertebral discs. Defective sclerotome development → vertebral anomalies (hemivertebra, butterfly vertebra, spina bifida).
Periosteal apposition contributes to bone widening but the initial vertebral formation is endochondral from cartilage models.
Neural crest-derived membranous bone forms the craniofacial skeleton, not the vertebral column.

questions.explanation: Correct: Endochondral ossification (from sclerotome-derived cartilage models).

questions.stem: Thalidomide exposure during the first trimester caused which limb deformity?

choices.feedback: Syndactyly results from failure of programmed cell death between digital rays. Phocomelia is the classic thalidomide defect.
Polydactyly results from excess SHH signaling or ZPA activity, not thalidomide toxicity.

choices.text: Phocomelia (absent/shortened proximal limbs with hands/feet attached near trunk)
choices.feedback: Correct! Thalidomide inhibited angiogenesis and disrupted FGF/AER signaling during critical limb development window (days 24-36 post-fertilization). Result: phocomelia (seal limbs) — absent or severely shortened proximal limb segments with relatively preserved hands/feet. Thalidomide disaster (1950s-60s): demonstrated the concept of teratogenic critical periods. Now used for: multiple myeloma, ENL (erythema nodosum leprosum) — with strict REMS program to prevent fetal exposure.
Clubfoot has multifactorial etiology and is not the characteristic thalidomide deformity.

questions.stem: The thyroid gland develops from an evagination of the floor of which embryonic structure?

choices.feedback: Rathke's pouch gives rise to the anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis), not the thyroid.

Correct! Thyroid follicular cells arise from the foramen cecum (base of the tongue) at the junction of the anterior 2/3 and posterior 1/3 → thyroid diverticulum descends along the thyroglossal duct to its final pretracheal position by week 7. The thyroglossal duct normally degenerates. Persistent duct → thyroglossal duct cyst (midline neck mass that elevates with swallowing and tongue protrusion). Parafollicular C cells (calcitonin) arise from the ultimobranchial body (4th-5th pharyngeal pouch neural crest).
The 1st pouch gives rise to the middle ear cavity and Eustachian tube, not the thyroid.
The 4th pouch gives rise to the superior parathyroid glands and parafollicular C cells. The thyroid follicular tissue has a different origin.

questions.explanation: Correct: Foramen cecum of the tongue (floor of primitive pharynx).

questions.stem: The anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis) develops from which embryologic structure?

choices.feedback: Neural crest cells form the adrenal medulla, not the anterior pituitary.
The 3rd pouch gives rise to the inferior parathyroids and thymus, not the pituitary.

Correct! Dual origin of the pituitary: Anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis): from Rathke's pouch — an upward evagination of oral ectoderm → produces FSH, LH, ACTH, TSH, GH, PRL. Posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis): from infundibulum — downward extension of diencephalon neuroectoderm → stores/releases ADH and oxytocin (made in hypothalamus). Craniopharyngioma: benign tumor arising from Rathke's pouch remnants — suprasellar, calcified, cystic, compresses optic chiasm (bitemporal hemianopsia).
The diencephalon (neuroectoderm) gives rise to the POSTERIOR pituitary (neurohypophysis), not the anterior.

questions.explanation: Correct: Rathke's pouch (oral ectoderm evagination from the roof of the mouth).

questions.stem: The adrenal medulla is derived from which embryologic cell population?

choices.feedback: Intermediate mesoderm gives rise to the adrenal CORTEX, not the medulla.
Surface ectoderm gives rise to epidermis, lens, and anterior pituitary — not the adrenal medulla.

Correct! Dual origin of the adrenal gland: Cortex: from intermediate mesoderm (mesodermal) → produces cortisol, aldosterone, androgens (fetal adrenal cortex: DHEA for placental estrogen synthesis). Medulla: from neural crest cells → modified sympathetic ganglion cells (chromaffin cells) → produces epinephrine and norepinephrine. Tumors of adrenal medulla: pheochromocytoma (adults — from chromaffin cells), neuroblastoma (children — from sympathetic neural crest precursors, most common extracranial solid tumor of childhood).
Foregut endoderm gives rise to GI/respiratory epithelium, liver, pancreas — not the adrenal gland.

questions.explanation: Correct: Neural crest cells (neuroectoderm).

questions.stem: A thyroglossal duct cyst presents as which clinical finding?

choices.text: Posterior triangle mass with nerve involvement
choices.feedback: Posterior triangle masses suggest lymphadenopathy or vascular malformations, not thyroglossal duct cyst.

choices.text: Submandibular mass that enlarges with eating
choices.feedback: A mass enlarging with meals suggests a salivary gland stone (sialolithiasis) in the submandibular gland, not a thyroglossal cyst.

choices.text: Midline neck mass that moves with swallowing and tongue protrusion
choices.feedback: Correct! Thyroglossal duct cyst: midline (or just off-midline) mass, usually at or below the hyoid bone. Classic test: elevates with swallowing (attached to hyoid via duct remnant) AND with tongue protrusion (duct connects to foramen cecum base of tongue). Contains thyroid tissue in ~1-2%. Treatment: Sistrunk procedure (excision of cyst + central hyoid bone + tract to foramen cecum) to prevent recurrence. Must confirm normal thyroid tissue exists elsewhere (US) before excision — the cyst may contain the patient's only thyroid tissue.

choices.text: Lateral neck mass anterior to the sternocleidomastoid
choices.feedback: Lateral neck masses anterior to the SCM are typically branchial cleft cysts, not thyroglossal.

questions.stem: The parathyroid glands develop from which pharyngeal pouches?

choices.feedback: The 5th pouch is rudimentary (ultimobranchial body → C cells). The 6th pouch does not contribute to parathyroid development.
1st pouch → middle ear. 2nd pouch → palatine tonsils. Neither forms parathyroids.
Only the 3rd and 4th pouches form parathyroid tissue.

Correct! Counterintuitive: 3rd pouch → INFERIOR parathyroids (descend further because they migrate with the thymus). 4th pouch → SUPERIOR parathyroids (shorter migration, stay closer to origin). 3rd pouch also → thymus (migrates caudally to mediastinum, pulling inferior parathyroids down). Clinical pearl: ectopic inferior parathyroids can be found anywhere from the angle of mandible to the mediastinum (following thymic descent path). DiGeorge syndrome (22q11.2): failed 3rd/4th pouch development → absent parathyroids (hypocalcemia) + absent thymus (T-cell immunodeficiency).

questions.stem: A child presents with a cystic mass anterior to the sternocleidomastoid muscle with a fistula opening at the anterior border. Which branchial cleft anomaly is most likely?

choices.feedback: First cleft anomalies are located near the ear (preauricular/parotid region) and often connect to the external auditory canal.

Correct! Second branchial cleft anomalies are the most common (95%). Cyst: anterior border of SCM, at junction of upper and middle thirds. Fistula: external opening at anterior SCM border → tract passes between internal and external carotid arteries → opens internally at the tonsillar fossa. Present in childhood as painless lateral neck mass, may enlarge with URI. Treatment: complete surgical excision of cyst + entire tract.
Third cleft anomalies are rare and present lower in the neck, often on the left, with internal opening at the piriform sinus.
Fourth cleft anomalies are very rare, present on the left, and tract courses near the recurrent laryngeal nerve.

questions.stem: The pharyngeal (branchial) arches give rise to specific structures. Which arch forms the muscles of mastication and the mandible?

choices.feedback: The second arch (Reichert's cartilage) forms the stapes, styloid process, lesser horn of hyoid, and muscles of facial expression (CN VII).
The fourth arch forms most laryngeal cartilages, cricothyroid muscle, and pharyngeal constrictors (CN X — superior laryngeal nerve).
The third arch forms the greater horn of hyoid, stylopharyngeus muscle (CN IX).

Correct! First pharyngeal arch derivatives — Meckel's cartilage: mandible, malleus, incus, sphenomandibular ligament. Muscles: muscles of mastication (temporalis, masseter, pterygoids), mylohyoid, anterior belly of digastric, tensor tympani, tensor veli palatini. Nerve: CN V (trigeminal — V2, V3). Mnemonic: 'First arch = M's' — Meckel's, Mandible, Malleus, Muscles of Mastication, Maxillary artery.

questions.explanation: Correct: First arch (Meckel's cartilage).

questions.stem: A child has a pit anterior to the ear with recurrent drainage. CT shows a tract extending toward the external auditory canal. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! First branchial cleft anomalies: Type I (ectodermal only — preauricular cyst) and Type II (ectodermal + mesodermal — tract extends from periauricular region to external auditory canal or parotid). The relationship to the facial nerve (CN VII) is critical: tract typically passes lateral, medial, or through the facial nerve branches. Surgical excision requires superficial parotidectomy approach to preserve CN VII.
Preauricular sinuses are common and benign (small pits anterior to helix). A tract extending to the EAC on imaging indicates a more complex first branchial cleft anomaly.
Preauricular tags are remnant tissue, not fistulous tracts. This presentation with a tract toward the EAC indicates a branchial cleft anomaly.
Dermoid cysts are midline (nasal, submental) or periorbital. A periauricular tract to the EAC is a first cleft anomaly.

questions.explanation: Correct: First branchial cleft anomaly (Type II).

questions.stem: During cardiac development, the primary heart tube undergoes looping. What does dextrocardia with situs inversus indicate about cardiac looping?

choices.text: Premature fusion of the endocardial cushions
choices.feedback: Endocardial cushion defects cause AV septal defects (common in Down syndrome), not situs abnormalities.

Correct! Normal: D-loop (rightward) → bulbus cordis moves right → normal great vessel relationship. Situs inversus: L-loop (leftward) → mirror-image anatomy → dextrocardia with all organs mirrored. If complete situs inversus (including abdominal organs): usually structurally normal heart, low risk of CHD. Situs ambiguus (heterotaxy): abnormal left-right patterning → high risk of complex CHD (asplenia/polysplenia syndromes).
Neural crest migration affects outflow tract septation (conotruncal defects), not cardiac looping direction.
Failure of looping would result in a straight tube — not situs inversus.

questions.explanation: Correct: Normal but mirror-image looping (L-loop instead of D-loop).
questions.verifiedExplanation: When the primary heart tube loops to the left instead of the right (L-looping or levocardia with situs inversus), the result is dextrocardia with situs inversus — a mirror-image arrangement of cardiac and abdominal organs. Isolated dextrocardia (without situs inversus) is associated with complex congenital heart defects in up to 95% of cases. Complete situs inversus with dextrocardia (Kartagener syndrome when associated with ciliary dyskinesia) has a much lower incidence of structural heart defects.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: abnormal heart tube looping causes dextrocardia and situs abnormalities

questions.stem: What embryologic structure gives rise to the interatrial septum, and what defect results from its incomplete formation?

choices.text: Septum primum and septum secundum → ASD
choices.feedback: Correct! Atrial septation: septum primum grows down → ostium primum closes → ostium secundum forms in septum primum → septum secundum grows (right side) → foramen ovale (functional R→L shunt in fetal life). Patent foramen ovale (PFO): septum primum doesn't fuse with secundum (present in ~25% of adults). Ostium secundum ASD: excessive resorption of septum primum or inadequate septum secundum → most common ASD type. Ostium primum ASD: endocardial cushion defect (associated with Down syndrome).
Sinus venosus ASD is a specific, less common ASD type. The primary atrial septation involves septum primum and secundum.
Truncus arteriosus septation creates the aorta and pulmonary artery. PDA results from failure of ductus arteriosus closure, a separate structure.
Bulbus cordis contributes to the outflow tracts, not the atrial septum.

questions.verifiedExplanation: The interatrial septum forms from two structures: septum primum (grows down from the roof of the atrium) and septum secundum (grows to the right of septum primum, forming the fossa ovalis). The foramen ovale is the opening between these two septa. Failure of proper septation results in atrial septal defects (ASDs): ostium secundum ASD (most common, defect in septum primum at the fossa ovalis), ostium primum ASD (endocardial cushion defect), and sinus venosus ASD (near the SVC or IVC entry).

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: septum primum and septum secundum form the interatrial septum

questions.stem: Neural crest cells contribute to which cardiac structures, and what defects result from their abnormal migration?

choices.feedback: AV valves derive from endocardial cushions, not neural crest cells.
Coronary arteries derive from proepicardial organ, not neural crest. Neural crest contributes to outflow tract septation.
The SA node develops from sinus venosus mesenchyme, not neural crest cells.

Correct! Cardiac neural crest cells (from pharyngeal arches 3, 4, 6) migrate to form the aorticopulmonary septum, separating the truncus arteriosus into aorta and pulmonary artery. Abnormal migration → conotruncal defects: Tetralogy of Fallot (unequal septation), persistent truncus arteriosus (no septation), D-TGA (spiral septation failure), DORV. DiGeorge syndrome (22q11.2 deletion): neural crest defect → conotruncal cardiac defects + thymic/parathyroid hypoplasia + facial dysmorphism.

questions.explanation: Correct: Aorticopulmonary septum → conotruncal defects (Tetralogy of Fallot, truncus arteriosus, TGA).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Neural crest cells contribute to the aorticopulmonary septum, which divides the truncus arteriosus into the aorta and pulmonary artery. They also contribute to pharyngeal arch artery remodeling, the thymus, parathyroid glands, and craniofacial structures. Defects in neural crest migration result in conotruncal defects (truncus arteriosus, TGA, TOF, DORV), as well as DiGeorge syndrome features (22q11.2 deletion): thymic aplasia, hypoparathyroidism with hypocalcemia, and cardiac outflow tract abnormalities.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: neural crest cells form aorticopulmonary septum; defects cause conotruncal anomalies

questions.stem: What fetal vascular structure connects the pulmonary artery to the aorta, and what occurs if it fails to close?

choices.feedback: The umbilical vein becomes the ligamentum teres. Its persistence does not cause PDA.
The foramen ovale connects the right and left atria, not the pulmonary artery to the aorta.
Ductus venosus (bypasses hepatic sinusoids) closes to become the ligamentum venosum. This is unrelated to PDA.

Correct! Ductus arteriosus: connects pulmonary artery to descending aorta → shunts blood away from fetal lungs (low-resistance placenta provides oxygenation). Closes within 24-48 hours after birth (functional closure via smooth muscle contraction triggered by increased O2 and decreased prostaglandins). PDA: failure to close → left-to-right shunt → pulmonary overcirculation. Treatment: indomethacin/ibuprofen (prostaglandin inhibitors) → surgical ligation if refractory. Prostaglandin E1 keeps PDA OPEN when needed (duct-dependent lesions).

questions.explanation: Correct: Ductus arteriosus → patent ductus arteriosus (PDA).
questions.verifiedExplanation: The ductus arteriosus connects the pulmonary artery to the descending aorta in fetal circulation, shunting blood away from the high-resistance pulmonary vasculature. It remains patent due to low oxygen tension and prostaglandins (PGE2). At birth, rising oxygen levels and falling prostaglandins trigger ductal constriction, with functional closure within 24-72 hours and anatomic closure (forming the ligamentum arteriosum) by 2-3 weeks. Failure of closure results in patent ductus arteriosus (PDA), classically associated with a continuous 'machinery' murmur.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: ductus arteriosus connecting pulmonary artery to aorta, closes after birth

questions.stem: In what congenital heart lesion is the great artery relationship described as 'egg on a string' on chest X-ray?

choices.feedback: TOF shows a boot-shaped heart (coeur en sabot) due to RVH and absent pulmonary artery segment.
TAPVR may show a 'snowman' (figure-8) appearance on CXR, not 'egg on a string.'

Correct! D-TGA: aorta arises from RV, pulmonary artery from LV → parallel circulations (incompatible with life unless mixing occurs via PDA, ASD, or VSD). CXR: 'egg on a string' — narrow mediastinum (great arteries are parallel/anterior-posterior rather than crossing) + enlarged heart. Embryology: failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to spiral during division. Most common cyanotic CHD in neonates. Emergency: prostaglandin E1 (keep PDA open) → balloon atrial septostomy (Rashkind) → arterial switch operation (Jatene).
Truncus arteriosus shows increased pulmonary vascularity and a single outflow tract. The 'egg on a string' pattern is specific to TGA.

questions.explanation: Correct: D-Transposition of the great arteries (D-TGA).
questions.verifiedExplanation: D-Transposition of the great arteries (D-TGA) features a parallel circulation where the aorta arises from the right ventricle and the pulmonary artery from the left ventricle. The great artery spatial relationship is described as the aorta being anterior and to the right of the pulmonary artery. D-TGA is the most common cyanotic congenital heart disease presenting in the first 24 hours of life. Survival depends on mixing between the two parallel circuits (via PDA, ASD, or VSD). Prostaglandin E1 keeps the PDA open until balloon atrial septostomy (Rashkind) and definitive arterial switch operation.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: D-TGA with aorta anterior and rightward relative to pulmonary artery

questions.stem: The thyroid gland descends from which embryologic structure, and what anomaly results from failed descent?

choices.text: Foramen cecum at the base of the tongue → thyroglossal duct cyst or lingual thyroid
choices.feedback: Correct! Thyroid development: arises from the foramen cecum (floor of pharynx, base of tongue) → descends via the thyroglossal duct → final position anterior to trachea (by week 7). Thyroglossal duct normally obliterates. Failed descent: lingual thyroid (functional thyroid tissue at base of tongue — may be the ONLY thyroid tissue). Duct remnant: thyroglossal duct cyst (midline neck mass that moves with swallowing AND tongue protrusion). Treatment: Sistrunk procedure (excise cyst + central hyoid bone + tract to foramen cecum).
Parafollicular C cells (calcitonin-producing, neural crest origin) give rise to medullary thyroid carcinoma, but the thyroid gland itself descends from the foramen cecum.
The third pharyngeal pouch gives rise to the inferior parathyroids and thymus, not the thyroid gland.
The fourth pharyngeal pouch gives rise to the superior parathyroids and ultimobranchial body (C cells). The main thyroid arises from the foramen cecum.

questions.stem: Which pharyngeal pouch gives rise to the parathyroid glands, and what clinical significance does this have?

choices.text: First pharyngeal pouch → all four parathyroids
choices.feedback: The first pharyngeal pouch forms the middle ear cavity and Eustachian tube, not parathyroids.

choices.text: Second pharyngeal pouch → parathyroids and tonsils
choices.feedback: The second pharyngeal pouch forms the palatine tonsils, not the parathyroids.

choices.text: Third pouch (inferior parathyroids) and fourth pouch (superior parathyroids) — explains variable position of inferior glands
choices.feedback: Correct! Third pouch → inferior parathyroids + thymus (descend together — long migration path → inferior glands are MORE variable in position, can be found from mandible to mediastinum). Fourth pouch → superior parathyroids (short migration → more consistent position near posterior thyroid). Clinical significance: during thyroidectomy/parathyroidectomy, superior glands are predictably posterior to the recurrent laryngeal nerve; inferior glands require wider search. Ectopic parathyroid in thymus is common.
The fifth pharyngeal pouch (if present) is rudimentary and contributes to ultimobranchial body/C cells, not parathyroids.

questions.stem: The adrenal cortex and adrenal medulla have different embryologic origins. What are they?

choices.feedback: Correct! Adrenal cortex: derived from intermediate mesoderm (same as kidneys and gonads) → produces cortisol, aldosterone, androgens. Adrenal medulla: derived from neural crest cells (neuroectoderm) — essentially modified postganglionic sympathetic neurons → produces catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine). This dual origin explains: distinct tumors (cortex → adenoma/carcinoma; medulla → pheochromocytoma/neuroblastoma), separate blood supply, and different regulatory mechanisms.
Only the medulla is neural crest-derived. The cortex is from intermediate mesoderm.
Only the cortex is mesodermal. The medulla has a different origin.

choices.text: Cortex from endoderm; medulla from mesoderm
choices.feedback: Endoderm forms GI/respiratory epithelium, not adrenal tissue. The cortex is mesodermal; the medulla is neural crest.

questions.explanation: Correct: Cortex from mesoderm (intermediate mesoderm); medulla from neural crest (ectoderm).

questions.stem: The midgut undergoes physiologic herniation and rotation during development. What is the normal degree and axis of midgut rotation?

choices.text: 270° counterclockwise around the superior mesenteric artery
choices.feedback: Correct! Midgut development: herniates into the umbilical cord (week 6) → rotates 270° counterclockwise around the SMA → returns to abdomen (week 10) → fixation. Normal result: duodenojejunal junction (ligament of Treitz) in left upper quadrant, cecum in right lower quadrant. Malrotation: incomplete rotation → abnormal mesenteric fixation → narrow SMA pedicle → risk of midgut volvulus (surgical emergency — can cause total midgut ischemia).
90° rotation is incomplete and would predispose to malrotation.
Rotation is counterclockwise around the SMA, not clockwise around the celiac axis.
360° would be overrotation. Normal is 270° counterclockwise.

questions.stem: What causes tracheoesophageal fistula (TEF), and what is the most common type?

choices.text: Failure of the urorectal septum → rectoperineal fistula
choices.feedback: Urorectal septum defects cause anorectal malformations, not TEF.

choices.text: Lateral body fold failure → omphalocele with esophageal defect
choices.feedback: Lateral fold defects cause omphalocele (abdominal wall), not TEF.

choices.text: Neural crest migration failure → absent esophageal ganglia
choices.feedback: Absent ganglia describes Hirschsprung disease (aganglionic colon), not TEF.

choices.text: Incomplete separation of the trachea from the esophagus by the tracheoesophageal septum; most common: proximal esophageal atresia with distal TEF
choices.feedback: Correct! The tracheoesophageal septum divides the foregut into ventral trachea and dorsal esophagus. Incomplete separation → TEF (with or without esophageal atresia). Most common type (85%): proximal esophageal atresia with distal TEF (Type C/Gross classification). Presentation: polyhydramnios prenatally; postnatally — drooling, choking with first feed, inability to pass NG tube. Associated: VACTERL (Vertebral, Anorectal, Cardiac, TE fistula, Renal, Limb anomalies).

questions.stem: Meckel diverticulum follows the 'rule of 2s.' What does this mean?

choices.text: It occurs in 2% of females and 2% of males equally
choices.feedback: Meckel's is more symptomatic in males (2:1 ratio). The rule includes but extends beyond sex ratio.

choices.text: Found in 2 abdominal quadrants and has 2 blood supplies
choices.feedback: Meckel's diverticulum is located in the ileum (RLQ) and is supplied by a remnant of the vitelline artery. The rule of 2s refers to different parameters.

choices.text: Diagnosed with 2 imaging modalities and requires 2 surgeries
choices.feedback: Meckel's is typically diagnosed with one nuclear scan and treated with one surgery (diverticulectomy or segmental resection).

choices.text: 2% prevalence, 2 feet from ileocecal valve, 2 inches long, 2 types of ectopic tissue, presents by age 2
choices.feedback: Correct! Rule of 2s: 2% of the population, 2 feet proximal to ileocecal valve, 2 inches long, 2 types of ectopic tissue (gastric > pancreatic), symptomatic usually before age 2, 2:1 male predominance. Embryology: remnant of the vitelline (omphalomesenteric) duct. Most common presentation in children: painless rectal bleeding (ectopic gastric mucosa → acid secretion → ulceration of adjacent ileal mucosa). Diagnosis: Meckel (Technetium-99m pertechnetate) scan — detects ectopic gastric mucosa.

questions.stem: What are the two most common neural tube defects, and what causes them?

choices.text: Dandy-Walker malformation and Arnold-Chiari from cerebellar maldevelopment
choices.feedback: These involve posterior fossa/cerebellar abnormalities. While Chiari II is associated with myelomeningocele, NTDs are primarily about neural tube closure failure.

choices.text: Holoprosencephaly and lissencephaly from neuronal migration failure
choices.feedback: These are disorders of brain development/migration, not primary neural tube closure defects.

choices.text: Microcephaly and macrocephaly from abnormal brain growth
choices.feedback: Abnormal head size is not a neural tube defect. NTDs specifically involve failure of neural tube closure during weeks 3-4.

choices.text: Anencephaly (rostral) and spina bifida (caudal) from failure of neural tube closure; prevented by folic acid
choices.feedback: Correct! Neural tube defects (NTDs): Anencephaly: failure of rostral (cranial) neural tube closure → absent brain/calvarium → incompatible with life. Spina bifida: failure of caudal neural tube closure → spectrum from spina bifida occulta (bony defect only) → meningocele (meninges herniate) → myelomeningocele (spinal cord + meninges herniate — most severe viable form). Prevention: folic acid 400 μg/day preconception (reduces NTD risk by 50-70%). High-risk women (prior NTD): 4 mg/day. Elevated maternal serum AFP and acetylcholinesterase in amniotic fluid suggest open NTD.

questions.stem: A newborn has a midline lumbosacral mass with exposed neural tissue. What is this defect and what is the immediate management?

choices.text: Myelomeningocele — sterile moist dressing, prone positioning,
choices.feedback: Correct! Myelomeningocele: herniation of spinal cord + meninges through a vertebral defect. Immediate management: sterile saline-soaked gauze covering, prone positioning (protect the sac), IV antibiotics (prevent meningitis), surgical closure within 24-72 hours. Long-term: >90% develop neurogenic bladder (CIC), hydrocephalus requiring VP shunt (~80%), orthopedic issues (club feet, hip dysplasia), cognitive impacts. Prenatal repair (MOMS trial): improves motor outcomes if performed before 26 weeks gestation.
Meningocele (meninges only, no neural tissue) is less urgent. Myelomeningocele (exposed cord) requires urgent coverage and repair.
Occulta is a bony defect only without exposed tissue. Exposed neural tissue indicates myelomeningocele — a much more severe defect requiring urgent management.
Encephalocele is a cranial defect. Lumbosacral exposed neural tissue is myelomeningocele requiring urgent intervention.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Myelomeningocele — sterile moist dressing, prone positioning, and surgical closure within 24-72 hours.

questions.stem: During neural development, what is the role of the notochord?

choices.text: Induces overlying ectoderm to form the neural plate through Sonic Hedgehog signaling
choices.feedback: Correct! The notochord: (1) Secretes Sonic Hedgehog (Shh) → induces overlying ectoderm to thicken into the neural plate → neural tube formation (neurulation). (2) Patterns the ventral neural tube: Shh gradient establishes motor neurons ventrally. (3) Remnant becomes the nucleus pulposus of intervertebral discs. Notochord abnormalities: chordoma (rare malignant tumor from notochord remnants — typically sacrum or clivus).

choices.text: Forms the meninges surrounding the brain and spinal cord
choices.feedback: Meninges derive from mesoderm (dura) and neural crest (arachnoid/pia), not the notochord.
The notochord does not become the vertebral column (which comes from somitic sclerotome). The notochord becomes the nucleus pulposus.

choices.text: Gives rise to all peripheral nerves
choices.feedback: Peripheral nerves arise from neural crest cells, not the notochord. The notochord provides signaling, not neural tissue.

questions.stem: Limb development proceeds in three axes. What signaling center controls the proximal-distal axis?

choices.feedback: Wnt7a controls the dorsal-ventral axis (back of hand vs palm), not proximal-distal outgrowth.

choices.text: Apical ectodermal ridge (AER) via FGF signaling
choices.feedback: Correct! Three axes of limb development: Proximal-distal: AER (apical ectodermal ridge — thickened ectoderm at limb bud tip) → FGFs (fibroblast growth factors) → maintain underlying mesenchyme proliferation → progressive distal outgrowth (shoulder→arm→forearm→hand). Anterior-posterior: ZPA → Sonic Hedgehog (Shh) → digit patterning. Dorsal-ventral: Wnt7a (dorsal ectoderm) → determines dorsal (back of hand) vs ventral (palm). AER disruption → limb truncation (amelia/phocomelia — as in thalidomide embryopathy).
Hox genes pattern the axial skeleton and specify limb identity (arm vs leg) but the AER directly controls proximal-distal limb outgrowth.
ZPA (posterior mesenchyme) controls the anterior-posterior (thumb to pinky) axis via Sonic Hedgehog.

questions.stem: Thalidomide exposure during limb development causes phocomelia. What structure does thalidomide disrupt?

choices.feedback: Notochord disruption affects neural tube development, not limb formation directly.

choices.text: Apical ectodermal ridge (AER) via anti-angiogenic and FGF signaling disruption
choices.feedback: Correct! Thalidomide inhibits angiogenesis and disrupts FGF/AER signaling → loss of proximal limb segments → phocomelia (hands/feet attached directly to trunk — 'seal limbs'). Critical window: days 20-36 post-fertilization (limb bud emergence). Also causes: ear, eye, and cardiac defects. Now used therapeutically (multiple myeloma, ENL) with strict pregnancy prevention programs (REMS). Historical tragedy drove modern teratology and drug safety regulation.
While thalidomide may affect some neural crest-derived structures, the limb defects are primarily due to AER/angiogenesis disruption.
Somites give rise to vertebrae, ribs, and muscle. Phocomelia results from disrupted limb bud outgrowth signaling, not somite pathology.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Apical ectodermal ridge (AER) via anti-angiogenic and FGF signaling disruption.

questions.stem: DiGeorge syndrome results from abnormal development of which pharyngeal structures?

choices.feedback: The fifth arch is vestigial. The sixth arch contributes to laryngeal structures. DiGeorge is a third/fourth pouch defect.
First cleft → external auditory canal; first membrane → tympanic membrane. These are not involved in DiGeorge syndrome.

choices.text: Third and fourth pharyngeal pouches → thymic and parathyroid hypoplasia
choices.feedback: Correct! DiGeorge syndrome (22q11.2 microdeletion): abnormal third/fourth pharyngeal pouch + cardiac neural crest development. Classic features: Cardiac defects (conotruncal — interrupted aortic arch, truncus arteriosus, TOF), Abnormal facies, Thymic aplasia/hypoplasia (T-cell immunodeficiency), Cleft palate, Hypocalcemia (absent/hypoplastic parathyroids from 3rd/4th pouches). Mnemonic: CATCH-22. Variable expressivity — spectrum from severe to very mild.
First/second arch defects cause facial/jaw abnormalities (Pierre Robin, Treacher Collins). DiGeorge affects third/fourth pouches.

questions.stem: Which pharyngeal pouch gives rise to the thymus?

choices.feedback: The fourth pouch gives rise to the superior parathyroid glands and ultimobranchial body (C cells). The thymus comes from the third pouch.

Correct! Third pharyngeal pouch: ventral wing → thymus, dorsal wing → inferior parathyroid glands. The thymus and inferior parathyroids descend together (long migration from pharynx to thorax). This explains: (1) ectopic thymic tissue can be found anywhere along this migration path (neck to anterior mediastinum), (2) inferior parathyroids are more variable in position than superior (shorter 4th pouch migration), (3) DiGeorge (22q11.2 deletion) → 3rd/4th pouch defects → thymic aplasia + hypoparathyroidism.
The first pouch forms the middle ear cavity and Eustachian tube.
The second pouch forms the palatine tonsils, not the thymus.

questions.explanation: Correct: Third pharyngeal pouch (ventral wing).

questions.stem: The kidneys develop through three sequential stages. What are they, and which persists as the definitive adult kidney?

choices.text: Metanephros → mesonephros → pronephros in reverse order
choices.feedback: Development proceeds from cranial to caudal: pronephros → mesonephros → metanephros (not reverse).

choices.text: Pronephros → mesonephros → metanephros; the metanephros becomes the definitive kidney
choices.feedback: Correct! Pronephros (week 4): rudimentary, degenerates. Mesonephros (weeks 4-8): functions temporarily, duct persists as Wolffian duct (vas deferens, epididymis, seminal vesicles in males). Metanephros (week 5+): definitive kidney — forms from two sources: ureteric bud (from mesonephric duct → collecting system: ureter, renal pelvis, calyces, collecting ducts) and metanephric mesenchyme (→ nephrons: glomeruli through DCT). Reciprocal induction between these two tissues is essential.

choices.text: Pronephros → mesonephros → metanephros; the mesonephros persists
choices.feedback: The mesonephros degenerates (though its duct, the Wolffian/mesonephric duct, persists in males). The metanephros is the definitive kidney.

choices.text: Pronephros → mesonephros → metanephros; the pronephros persists
choices.feedback: The pronephros is the most primitive and completely degenerates by week 4. The metanephros is the definitive kidney.

questions.stem: Failure of the ureteric bud to branch properly results in which congenital anomaly?

choices.feedback: ADPKD and ARPKD are genetic ciliopathies (PKD1/PKD2 or PKHD1 mutations), not ureteric bud defects.
Horseshoe kidney results from fusion of the lower poles during ascent, not ureteric bud branching failure.
VUR results from abnormal ureteral insertion into the bladder trigone (short submucosal tunnel), not ureteric bud branching failure per se.

choices.text: Renal agenesis or multicystic dysplastic kidney (MCDK)
choices.feedback: Correct! The ureteric bud induces metanephric mesenchyme to differentiate into nephrons (reciprocal induction). Failed ureteric bud development → no induction signal → renal agenesis (unilateral: often asymptomatic; bilateral: Potter sequence — fatal). Abnormal ureteric bud branching/interaction → multicystic dysplastic kidney (MCDK — non-functioning, cystic, no renal pelvis). Duplicated ureteric bud → duplex collecting system ± ectopic ureter.

questions.stem: Pulmonary surfactant production begins at what gestational age, and what is its clinical significance?

choices.text: Type II pneumocytes begin producing surfactant around weeks 24
choices.feedback: Correct! Type II alveolar cells produce surfactant (primarily dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine/DPPC + surfactant proteins SP-A, B, C, D). Production: begins ~24-28 weeks, matures ~35 weeks. Insufficiency → respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) — alveolar collapse, hyaline membranes, V/Q mismatch. Treatment: exogenous surfactant (rescue therapy), antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone) to accelerate surfactant production if preterm delivery anticipated. L/S ratio ≥ 2:1 or positive PG (phosphatidylglycerol) in amniotic fluid indicates lung maturity.

choices.text: Week 20 — premature infants never have surfactant
choices.feedback: Surfactant production begins around weeks 24-28, with mature levels reached by 35 weeks.
Surfactant production begins well before term. Near-term infants (35+ weeks) typically have adequate surfactant. RDS is primarily a problem of prematurity before 34 weeks.

choices.text: Week 12 — surfactant is present from early development
choices.feedback: The respiratory system is still in the pseudoglandular stage at week 12 (branching airways, no gas exchange structures). Surfactant production requires Type II pneumocyte maturation much later.

questions.explanation: Key detail: 28; mature by 35 weeks — deficiency causes RDS of prematurity.

questions.stem: What congenital anomaly results from a defect in the pleuroperitoneal membrane?

choices.feedback: Correct! The diaphragm forms from four structures: septum transversum (central tendon), pleuroperitoneal membranes (posterolateral), esophageal mesentery (crura), and body wall mesoderm. Bochdalek hernia: posterolateral defect from failed pleuroperitoneal membrane closure → abdominal organs herniate into thorax → lung compression → pulmonary hypoplasia. Left-sided 80-85% (right side closes earlier + liver protection). Presentation: respiratory distress, scaphoid abdomen, bowel sounds in chest. Emergency: intubation (NO bag-mask — distends stomach), surgical repair after stabilization.
Pectus excavatum is a chest wall deformity from excessive costal cartilage growth, not a diaphragmatic defect.
TEF results from tracheoesophageal septum failure, not pleuroperitoneal membrane defects.
Pulmonary sequestration is non-functioning lung tissue with a systemic arterial supply, not related to pleuroperitoneal membrane.

questions.explanation: Correct: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH) — most commonly left-sided (Bochdalek).

questions.stem: What are the stages of lung development in order?

choices.feedback: Correct! Lung development stages: Embryonic (weeks 3-7): lung bud from foregut, primary bronchi. Pseudoglandular (weeks 5-17): all conducting airways branch (16 generations — 'gland-like' appearance, no gas exchange). Canalicular (weeks 16-25): respiratory bronchioles, Type I & II pneumocytes begin differentiating, capillaries approximate airways. Saccular (weeks 24-36): terminal saccules (primitive alveoli), surfactant production increases. Alveolar (week 36 to age 8): true alveoli mature, septation increases surface area. Viability threshold (~24 weeks) coincides with canalicular stage when gas exchange first possible.
This is the reverse order. Development proceeds from embryonic to alveolar.
Pseudoglandular comes before canalicular, and saccular before alveolar.
Embryonic stage comes first, and canalicular precedes saccular. The correct order begins with embryonic.

questions.stem: A neonate with DiGeorge syndrome has severe cyanosis. Echocardiogram shows a single arterial trunk overriding both ventricles with a VSD. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Persistent truncus arteriosus: failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to divide the truncus into aorta and pulmonary artery → single great vessel receiving blood from both ventricles (always with a VSD). Neural crest defect — strongly associated with DiGeorge/22q11.2 deletion. Classified by branch pulmonary artery origin (Collett-Edwards or Van Praagh). Treatment: surgical repair in first weeks of life (Rastelli procedure — VSD closure + RV-to-PA conduit).
AP window is a defect between two separately formed great arteries. In truncus, the septation never occurred.
TOF has a separate (though small) pulmonary artery. A single trunk overriding the septum with a VSD suggests a different conotruncal defect.
PDA is a connection between two separate great arteries. Truncus arteriosus means the separation never occurred — only one trunk exists.

questions.explanation: Correct: Truncus arteriosus (persistent).

questions.stem: Which embryologic cell population is responsible for aorticopulmonary septum formation?

choices.feedback: Secondary heart field contributes to the outflow tract myocardium and right ventricle, but the aorticopulmonary septum itself is neural crest-derived.
Endocardial cushions form AV septum and valves. The outflow tract septum requires a different cell population.

Correct! Cardiac neural crest cells migrate from the pharyngeal arch region to form the aorticopulmonary septum — the spiral partition that divides the truncus into aorta and pulmonary artery. Neural crest ablation → conotruncal defects: truncus arteriosus, TOF, D-TGA, interrupted aortic arch, DORV. DiGeorge (22q11.2): neural crest dysfunction → conotruncal defects + thymic aplasia + hypoparathyroidism.
Proepicardial cells contribute to coronary arteries and cardiac fibroblasts, not the aorticopulmonary septum.

questions.stem: A neonate presents with bilious vomiting on day 1 of life. Upper GI series shows a 'corkscrew' pattern of the duodenum. What is the diagnosis and urgent management?

choices.text: Pyloric stenosis — pyloromyotomy after correction of alkalosis
choices.feedback: Pyloric stenosis presents at 2-6 weeks with NON-bilious projectile vomiting. Bilious vomiting on day 1 suggests obstruction BELOW the ampulla of Vater.

choices.text: Duodenal atresia — nasogastric decompression and elective repair
choices.feedback: Duodenal atresia shows a 'double bubble' sign. The corkscrew pattern is specific for malrotation with volvulus — a true emergency.

choices.text: Necrotizing enterocolitis — antibiotics and bowel rest
choices.feedback: NEC typically presents in premature infants after feeding initiation, not day 1 in a term neonate with a corkscrew UGI pattern.

choices.text: Midgut volvulus from malrotation — emergent Ladd procedure
choices.feedback: Correct! Bilious vomiting in a neonate = midgut volvulus until proven otherwise (surgical emergency). Malrotation → narrow SMA mesenteric base → midgut twists → volvulus → intestinal ischemia → can lose entire midgut within hours. Diagnosis: upper GI series (corkscrew/bird's beak duodenum, abnormal DJ junction position). Treatment: emergent Ladd procedure — detorse volvulus, lyse Ladd bands, widen mesenteric base, appendectomy, place cecum in LLQ (intentional non-rotation).

questions.stem: A term neonate fails to pass meconium in the first 48 hours and develops abdominal distension. Rectal exam produces an explosive gush of stool and gas. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Imperforate anus is identified on physical exam — no anal opening visible. This patient had a rectal exam performed, so the anus is patent.
Small left colon (seen in infants of diabetic mothers) can mimic Hirschsprung but usually resolves spontaneously.
Meconium ileus presents with distal ileal obstruction and is associated with CF, but the explosive decompression on rectal exam is more characteristic of Hirschsprung.

Correct! Hirschsprung disease: absence of ganglion cells (Auerbach and Meissner plexuses) in the distal colon → functional obstruction. Classic presentation: failure to pass meconium within 48 hours (99% of healthy term neonates pass meconium by 48 hours) + abdominal distension + explosive decompression on rectal exam. Caused by failure of neural crest cell migration (cranial to caudal — rectosigmoid most common). Transition zone on contrast enema. Diagnosis confirmed: rectal suction biopsy showing absent ganglion cells + hypertrophied nerve trunks.

questions.stem: What is the diagnostic gold standard for Hirschsprung disease?

choices.feedback: Manometry shows absent RAIR (rectoanal inhibitory reflex) in Hirschsprung — useful screening tool but biopsy is needed for definitive diagnosis.
Abdominal X-ray may show distal bowel obstruction with dilated loops but cannot distinguish Hirschsprung from other causes of neonatal obstruction.
Contrast enema can suggest Hirschsprung (dilated proximal colon → transition zone → narrow aganglionic distal segment) but is not the gold standard for confirmation.

choices.text: Rectal suction biopsy showing absent ganglion cells
choices.feedback: Correct! Rectal suction biopsy: taken 2-3 cm above the dentate line (above the normal zone of physiologic aganglionosis). Findings: (1) absent ganglion cells in submucosal (Meissner) and myenteric (Auerbach) plexuses, (2) hypertrophied acetylcholinesterase-positive nerve fibers. Can be performed at bedside without anesthesia. Anorectal manometry (absent rectoanal inhibitory reflex) supports but does not confirm. Full-thickness biopsy for equivocal cases.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Rectal suction biopsy showing absent ganglion cells and nerve trunk hypertrophy.

questions.stem: A horseshoe kidney is found incidentally on imaging. What prevents normal ascent and what is the most common complication?

choices.text: The inferior mesenteric artery traps the fused isthmus, preventing full ascent → UPJ obstruction and stones
choices.feedback: Correct! Horseshoe kidney: lower poles fuse across midline (isthmus) during ascent → trapped by the inferior mesenteric artery at L3-L4 (does not ascend to normal L1-L2 position). Most common renal fusion anomaly (~1:400). Complications: UPJ obstruction (altered ureteral anatomy → hydronephrosis), nephrolithiasis (urinary stasis), UTIs, increased risk of Wilms tumor in children. Most are asymptomatic and discovered incidentally. Associated with Turner syndrome (45,X).

choices.text: The aortic bifurcation prevents descent → ectopic pelvic kidney
choices.feedback: Horseshoe kidneys are trapped during ascent (not descent), and the IMA (not aortic bifurcation) is the blocking structure.

choices.text: Fusion is blocked by the diaphragm → respiratory problems
choices.feedback: The diaphragm does not impede renal ascent. A different abdominal structure traps the fused kidney.
While ureteral anatomy may be altered in horseshoe kidney, bilateral VUR is not the primary complication. UPJ obstruction from the abnormal renal position is more common.

questions.stem: A newborn with coarctation of the aorta becomes critically ill when the ductus arteriosus closes. What is the emergent treatment?

choices.feedback: Indomethacin CLOSES the PDA — this would be catastrophic in ductal-dependent coarctation. PGE1 to OPEN the ductus is needed.
Septostomy is used for D-TGA (to allow mixing). Coarctation requires PDA maintenance, not atrial communication.
Surgical repair is definitive but the patient needs stabilization first. The ductus must be reopened.

choices.text: Prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) infusion to reopen the ductus arteriosus
choices.feedback: Correct! Coarctation of the aorta (juxtaductal): narrowing near the ductus arteriosus → lower body perfusion depends on the PDA. When PDA closes → severe hypoperfusion of lower body → metabolic acidosis, renal failure, NEC. PGE1 (alprostadil): reopens and maintains PDA → restores lower body perfusion. Side effects: apnea (have ventilator ready), fever, hypotension. Definitive: surgical repair (resection + end-to-end anastomosis) or catheter-based intervention once stabilized.

questions.stem: A male fetus at 20 weeks has bilateral hydronephrosis, thick-walled bladder, and oligohydramnios. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Prune belly (Eagle-Barrett) syndrome includes absent abdominal musculature, cryptorchidism, and urinary tract anomalies — but oligohydramnios with keyhole sign on ultrasound is most specific for PUV.
Bilateral UPJ obstruction can cause hydronephrosis but would not explain the thick-walled bladder or oligohydramnios pattern as well as lower tract obstruction.
Bilateral MCDK would show cystic kidneys without a dilated bladder. The thick-walled bladder and urethral dilation point to lower tract obstruction (PUV).

Correct! Male + bilateral hydronephrosis + thick-walled bladder + dilated posterior urethra (keyhole sign) + oligohydramnios = PUV until proven otherwise. Oligohydramnios: decreased fetal urine output → reduced amniotic fluid → risks Potter sequence (pulmonary hypoplasia, limb deformities, facial compression). Prenatal: consider vesicoamniotic shunting if severe oligohydramnios before viability. Postnatal: catheter drainage → cystoscopic valve ablation.

questions.stem: A child with Down syndrome is diagnosed with an atrioventricular septal defect (AVSD). What embryologic structure failed to develop properly?

choices.feedback: Septum secundum defects cause ostium secundum ASD (most common ASD type), not the complete AVSD pattern.
Sinus venosus defects cause sinus venosus ASD (associated with anomalous pulmonary venous drainage), not AVSD.

Correct! Endocardial cushions: mesenchymal tissue in the center of the heart → form the AV septum, mitral and tricuspid valves, and contribute to atrial and ventricular septa. Failed development → AVSD (atrioventricular septal defect, previously called endocardial cushion defect): ostium primum ASD + inlet VSD + common AV valve. Strongly associated with trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) — ~40% of cardiac defects in Down syndrome are AVSD. Treatment: complete surgical repair at 3-6 months.
Neural crest defects cause conotruncal anomalies (truncus arteriosus, TOF), not AVSD.

questions.stem: What is the most common congenital heart defect overall?

choices.feedback: Bicuspid AV is very common (~1-2% of population) but is often classified separately from structural CHD. Among traditional CHD, VSD is most common.
ASD is common but not the most common overall. It is the most common CHD diagnosed in adults.
PDA is common in premature infants but VSD is more prevalent overall.

Correct! VSD is the most common congenital heart defect (~30% of all CHD). Types by location: perimembranous/membranous (most common — 80%), muscular, inlet (endocardial cushion-related), outlet/supracristal. Small VSDs: often close spontaneously (especially muscular). Large VSDs: left-to-right shunt → pulmonary overcirculation → heart failure → if uncorrected, Eisenmenger syndrome (irreversible pulmonary hypertension with shunt reversal → cyanosis). Surgical closure when symptomatic or significant shunt (Qp:Qs > 2:1).

questions.explanation: Correct: Ventricular septal defect (VSD).

questions.stem: A child presents with a midline neck mass that elevates with tongue protrusion. What is the diagnosis and definitive treatment?

choices.feedback: Midline dermoid cysts can occur but do NOT move with tongue protrusion. Movement with swallowing AND tongue protrusion is pathognomonic for thyroglossal.

choices.text: Branchial cleft cyst — excision without hyoid resection
choices.feedback: Branchial cleft cysts are LATERAL (anterior to SCM). This midline mass with tongue protrusion movement is a thyroglossal duct cyst requiring Sistrunk procedure.

choices.text: Reactive lymph node — antibiotics and observation
choices.feedback: Lymph nodes are typically lateral. A midline mass moving with tongue protrusion is essentially diagnostic of thyroglossal duct cyst.

Correct! Thyroglossal duct cyst: remnant of the thyroglossal duct (thyroid descent pathway from foramen cecum to pretracheal position). Key sign: moves with swallowing (attached to hyoid) AND tongue protrusion (attached to base of tongue via tract). Sistrunk procedure: excise cyst + central body of hyoid bone + tract up to foramen cecum (reduces recurrence from 50% with simple excision to <5%). Always confirm normal thyroid tissue elsewhere (ultrasound) — the cyst may contain the patient's ONLY thyroid tissue (lingual thyroid scenario).

questions.explanation: Correct: Thyroglossal duct cyst — Sistrunk procedure (cyst + central hyoid + tract to foramen cecum).

questions.stem: Before performing a Sistrunk procedure for a thyroglossal duct cyst, what preoperative study is essential?

choices.feedback: FNA is rarely needed for classic thyroglossal cysts. It may be considered if malignancy is suspected (rare — 1% papillary thyroid carcinoma in thyroglossal cysts, typically in adults).

choices.text: Thyroid ultrasound to confirm normal thyroid tissue exists in the pretracheal position
choices.feedback: Correct! Critical: the thyroglossal duct cyst may contain ectopic thyroid tissue — and in rare cases (1-2%) this may be the patient's ONLY functioning thyroid tissue (lingual thyroid that failed to descend). Thyroid ultrasound before surgery confirms a normal thyroid gland exists in its usual pretracheal location. If no normal thyroid seen → thyroid scan to locate functioning tissue → avoid excising the only thyroid → patient would become permanently hypothyroid. Also check thyroid function (TSH/T4).

choices.text: CT with contrast of the neck
choices.feedback: CT may be useful for complex cases but is not the essential preoperative study.
Barium swallow evaluates the esophagus. It has no role in thyroglossal duct cyst workup.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with nasal congestion, clear rhinorrhea, and sneezing that worsen in spring and fall. She has bluish discoloration under her eyes and a transverse nasal crease. Nasal examination shows pale, boggy turbinates. Which immunoglobulin mediates her condition?

choices.feedback: IgG mediates type II and III hypersensitivity reactions. Allergic rhinitis is type I (IgE-mediated).
IgA is the predominant mucosal immunoglobulin but does not mediate allergic rhinitis.
IgM is the first antibody produced in a primary immune response and does not mediate allergic rhinitis.

Allergic rhinitis is an IgE-mediated type I hypersensitivity reaction. Allergen cross-links IgE on mast cells → degranulation → histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins. Pale turbinates, allergic shiners, and the nasal salute crease are classic exam findings.

questions.explanation: References: Allergic Rhinitis. StatPearls. NBK538186. [Open Access]

verifiedReferences.title: Allergic Rhinitis
verifiedReferences.authors: Akhouri S, House SA

questions.stem: A patient with chronic allergic rhinitis is started on intranasal corticosteroids. Which mechanism best explains their therapeutic effect?

choices.feedback: This describes montelukast, a second-line option for allergic rhinitis.
This describes cromolyn sodium, which prevents mast cell degranulation but is less effective than intranasal corticosteroids.

Intranasal corticosteroids are first-line for moderate-persistent allergic rhinitis. They suppress IL-4, IL-5, IL-13, reduce eosinophil recruitment, and decrease mucosal edema. They address both early and late-phase allergic responses.
This describes antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine), not corticosteroids.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Suppression of inflammatory cytokines and reduction of mucosal eosinophilic infiltration.

verifiedReferences.title: Allergic Rhinitis
verifiedReferences.authors: Akhouri S, House SA

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with perennial allergic rhinitis fails to improve despite combination therapy with intranasal corticosteroids and oral antihistamines. Skin prick testing reveals sensitivity to dust mites and cat dander. What is the most appropriate next step?

choices.feedback: Systemic corticosteroids should be avoided long-term for allergic rhinitis due to side effects. Immunotherapy addresses the underlying immune mechanism.
Surgery may help with structural obstruction but does not address the immunologic basis of allergic rhinitis.
Ipratropium addresses rhinorrhea only and does not treat the underlying allergic mechanism.

Allergen immunotherapy (SCIT or SLIT) is indicated for patients with moderate-severe allergic rhinitis refractory to pharmacotherapy. It works by shifting immune response from Th2 to Th1, increasing blocking IgG4 antibodies, and inducing regulatory T cells. Duration is typically 3-5 years.

questions.explanation: References: Allergic Rhinitis. StatPearls. NBK538186. [Open Access]

verifiedReferences.title: Allergic Rhinitis
verifiedReferences.authors: Akhouri S, House SA

questions.stem: Which of the following is the most common comorbidity associated with persistent allergic rhinitis?

choices.feedback: Nasal polyps are more associated with non-allergic rhinitis, aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease, and chronic rhinosinusitis.
While GERD can cause chronic cough and laryngeal symptoms, it is not the most common comorbidity of allergic rhinitis.

Up to 40% of allergic rhinitis patients have comorbid asthma (the 'united airway' concept). The nasal and bronchial mucosa share similar inflammatory pathways. Treating allergic rhinitis improves asthma control. Other comorbidities include sinusitis, otitis media, and sleep disturbance.
Nasal obstruction can worsen OSA, but asthma is the most commonly associated comorbidity with allergic rhinitis.

questions.explanation: References: Allergic Rhinitis. StatPearls. NBK538186. [Open Access]

verifiedReferences.title: Allergic Rhinitis
verifiedReferences.authors: Akhouri S, House SA

questions.stem: A patient asks about the difference between seasonal and perennial allergic rhinitis. Which allergen is most commonly responsible for perennial symptoms?

choices.feedback: Perennial allergic rhinitis is most commonly caused by dust mites, pet dander, cockroach allergens, and mold. Seasonal rhinitis is driven by tree, grass, and weed pollens with predictable temporal patterns. Dust mite allergens (Der p 1, Der f 1) are the most common perennial trigger worldwide.
Birch pollen causes seasonal (spring) allergic rhinitis.
Grass pollen causes seasonal (spring/summer) allergic rhinitis.
Ragweed is a fall seasonal allergen, not a perennial trigger.

questions.explanation: References: Allergic Rhinitis. StatPearls. NBK538186. [Open Access]

verifiedReferences.title: Allergic Rhinitis
verifiedReferences.authors: Akhouri S, House SA

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman develops generalized urticaria, wheezing, and hypotension within minutes of receiving IV penicillin. Her blood pressure is 70/40 mmHg. What is the first-line treatment?

choices.feedback: IM epinephrine is the FIRST-LINE treatment for anaphylaxis. It acts on alpha-1 receptors (vasoconstriction, reduces edema), beta-1 (increases cardiac output), and beta-2 (bronchodilation, decreases mast cell mediator release). Must be given IM in the anterolateral thigh for rapid absorption. Delay in epinephrine is the #1 factor in anaphylaxis fatalities.
Corticosteroids may help prevent biphasic reactions but have delayed onset (4-6 hours) and are NOT effective for acute anaphylaxis management.
Antihistamines are adjunctive only—they treat urticaria/pruritus but do NOT reverse hypotension or airway compromise. Never use as first-line for anaphylaxis.
Albuterol is adjunctive for bronchospasm but does not address hypotension or systemic anaphylaxis. Epinephrine must come first.

questions.explanation: Intramuscular epinephrine (0.3-0.5 mg of 1:1000 concentration) in the anterolateral thigh is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis. Epinephrine acts on alpha-1 receptors to increase peripheral vascular resistance (reversing hypotension), on beta-1 receptors to increase cardiac output, and on beta-2 receptors to cause bronchodilation and suppress further mediator release from mast cells. IM injection in the vastus lateralis provides more rapid and reliable absorption than subcutaneous administration. IV methylprednisolone, diphenhydramine, and albuterol are adjunctive therapies but should never delay or replace epinephrine. Half of anaphylaxis-related fatalities occur in the first hour, making rapid epinephrine administration critical.

references.supportingQuote: Rapid treatment should be initiated with intramuscular epinephrine if any of these symptoms are present

questions.stem: A patient treated for anaphylaxis in the ED improves with epinephrine, but 6 hours later develops recurrent hypotension and dyspnea. What is this phenomenon called?

choices.feedback: Biphasic anaphylaxis occurs in up to 20% of cases, typically 1-72 hours after initial resolution (most within 8-10 hours).
