Risk factors include severe initial reaction, delayed epinephrine, and need for multiple epinephrine doses. This is why patients should be observed for at least 4-6 hours (some guidelines say 24 hours for severe reactions).
Serum sickness is a type III hypersensitivity reaction occurring 1-2 weeks after drug exposure, not hours.
Anaphylactoid reactions are non-IgE mediated (direct mast cell activation) and present similarly to anaphylaxis but are not a recurrence pattern.
Rebound bronchospasm relates to short-acting bronchodilator use, not systemic anaphylaxis recurrence.

questions.explanation: Biphasic anaphylaxis occurs in up to 20% of patients, with recurrent symptoms peaking 8 to 11 hours after the initial reaction, even after successful initial treatment. While clinically significant in approximately 4-5% of diagnosed anaphylaxis cases, this second reaction can be fatal. Risk factors for biphasic reactions include severity of the initial reaction, persistence of symptoms, and need for more than one dose of epinephrine. This is why patients should be observed for at least 4-6 hours after an anaphylactic episode, and high-risk patients should be observed even longer. Serum sickness is a delayed Type III hypersensitivity reaction occurring days to weeks after exposure. Anaphylactoid reaction is an outdated term now consolidated under "anaphylaxis." Rebound bronchospasm describes isolated airway reactivity, not a systemic recurrence.

references.supportingQuote: Anaphylactic reactions can also occur biphasically in up to 20% of cases with recurrence peaking 8 to 11 hours after the initial reaction

questions.stem: Which laboratory test, when drawn within 1-2 hours of a suspected anaphylactic event, best supports the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Total IgE indicates atopic predisposition but does not confirm acute anaphylaxis.

Tryptase is released from mast cell degranulation and peaks 60-90 minutes after anaphylaxis onset. A level >11.5 ng/mL supports the diagnosis. It is particularly useful when the clinical picture is ambiguous. Serial levels (at onset, 1-2 hours, and 24-hour baseline) improve diagnostic sensitivity.
CRP is a nonspecific inflammatory marker that does not distinguish anaphylaxis from other conditions.
Histamine has a very short half-life (minutes) making it impractical for clinical use. Tryptase is more stable and clinically useful.

questions.explanation: Serum tryptase is the preferred confirmatory biomarker for anaphylaxis when drawn within 1-3 hours of symptom onset. Tryptase is released from mast cell degranulation and remains elevated for several hours, providing a practical diagnostic window. A level >11.4 ng/mL or >1.2x baseline + 2 ng/mL supports anaphylaxis. Serum histamine, while elevated during anaphylaxis, peaks within minutes and has a half-life of only about 1-2 minutes, making it impractical to measure in most clinical scenarios. Serum IgE reflects sensitization but does not confirm an acute event. CRP is a nonspecific inflammatory marker with no role in acute anaphylaxis diagnosis. Baseline tryptase should also be measured later to screen for underlying mast cell disorders.

references.supportingQuote: Serum tryptase can be considered for confirmation of an anaphylactic episode as it remains elevated for several hours

questions.stem: A patient on a beta-blocker presents with anaphylaxis that is refractory to multiple doses of epinephrine. Which medication should be considered next?

choices.feedback: Phenylephrine is a pure alpha-agonist and may increase blood pressure but lacks the beta-2 bronchodilatory effects needed in anaphylaxis.
Atropine treats bradycardia but does not address the bronchospasm or vasodilation of anaphylaxis.
Vasopressin may be used as a pressor in refractory shock but does not specifically address beta-blocker antagonism in anaphylaxis.

Glucagon bypasses beta-adrenergic blockade by activating adenylyl cyclase independently of beta receptors. It increases cardiac output and decreases bronchospasm. Dose: 1-IV over 5 minutes. Beta-blockers make anaphylaxis more severe and epinephrine-resistant because they block the therapeutic beta-agonist effects of epinephrine.

questions.explanation: Glucagon is recommended for patients on beta-blockers who have anaphylaxis refractory to epinephrine. Beta-blockers blunt the effects of epinephrine by occupying beta-adrenergic receptors. Glucagon bypasses beta-receptors entirely by directly activating adenylyl cyclase, increasing intracellular cyclic AMP (cAMP) in the myocardium. This produces positive inotropic and chronotropic effects independent of the adrenergic pathway. The recommended IV dose is 1-5 mg in adults, administered over 5 minutes. Common side effects include nausea and vomiting, so airway protection is important. Phenylephrine is a pure alpha-agonist and does not address bronchospasm. Atropine treats bradycardia but not the distributive shock mechanism. Vasopressin may be used as a second-line vasopressor but does not specifically address beta-blockade.

references.supportingQuote: Glucagon is the reversal agent for beta-blockers and can be used as such if needed in cases where anaphylaxis is resistant to treatment in patients with beta-blockade
Glucagon is effective in treating refractory anaphylaxis that does not respond to epinephrine

questions.stem: According to diagnostic criteria, which scenario meets the definition of anaphylaxis?

choices.text: Localized swelling at the site of a bee sting
choices.feedback: A large local reaction is not anaphylaxis. Anaphylaxis requires systemic involvement beyond the sting site.

choices.text: Isolated urticaria and angioedema without systemic symptoms
choices.feedback: Urticaria/angioedema alone without respiratory or cardiovascular involvement does not meet anaphylaxis criteria.

choices.text: Acute onset of urticaria with hypotension after known allergen exposure
choices.feedback: Anaphylaxis criteria: (1) acute onset of skin/mucosal involvement + respiratory compromise OR hypotension, OR (2) two or more of: skin, respiratory, GI, or cardiovascular involvement after likely allergen exposure, OR (3) hypotension alone after known allergen. Skin findings are present in ~80% of cases but are NOT required for diagnosis.
Vasovagal syncope causes bradycardia and pallor, not tachycardia, urticaria, or bronchospasm.

questions.explanation: Anaphylaxis is diagnosed clinically using consensus criteria. One key criterion is: known allergen exposure plus involvement of 2 or more body systems. In this case, urticaria (integumentary) plus hypotension (cardiovascular) after a known allergen satisfies this requirement. A third diagnostic scenario also qualifies: known allergen exposure plus hypotension alone (systolic <90 mmHg or >30% decrease from baseline). A localized sting reaction is not anaphylaxis because it involves only one site without systemic features. Isolated urticaria and angioedema without respiratory or cardiovascular compromise does not meet anaphylaxis criteria. Vasovagal syncope is neurally mediated and lacks urticaria, angioedema, or bronchospasm — key distinguishing features from anaphylaxis.

references.supportingQuote: Known exposure to an antigen and hypotension or likely exposure and symptoms involving 2 or more body systems

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant presents with erythematous, weeping, crusted plaques on the face and extensor surfaces of the extremities. The mother reports intense itching. Which cytokine pathway is most central to the pathogenesis of this condition?

choices.feedback: These innate cytokines contribute to inflammation broadly but are not the central pathway in atopic dermatitis.
Th17 pathway is central to psoriasis pathogenesis, not atopic dermatitis.
Th1 cytokines are involved in contact dermatitis and psoriasis, not acute atopic dermatitis (though chronic AD lesions may show Th1 involvement).

Atopic dermatitis is driven by Th2 cytokines: IL-4 and IL-13 promote IgE class switching and impair filaggrin expression (skin barrier dysfunction). IL-5 recruits eosinophils. IL-31 mediates pruritus. In infants, lesions classically affect the face and extensors; in older children/adults, flexural distribution predominates.

questions.explanation: References: Atopic Dermatitis. StatPearls. NBK448071. [Open Access]

questions.stem: Which genetic mutation is most strongly associated with atopic dermatitis and explains the impaired skin barrier?

choices.feedback: Filaggrin is essential for epidermal barrier integrity—it aggregates keratin filaments and its breakdown products form natural moisturizing factor. FLG mutations are the strongest genetic risk factor for AD, present in ~30% of patients. Impaired barrier → increased transepidermal water loss and allergen penetration, driving the Th2 inflammatory cascade.
Keratin 14 mutations cause epidermolysis bullosa simplex, not atopic dermatitis.
CARD15/NOD2 mutations are associated with Crohn's disease, not atopic dermatitis.
HLA-B27 is associated with ankylosing spondylitis and seronegative spondyloarthropathies.

questions.explanation: Key detail: of-function mutation.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old child with moderate atopic dermatitis has failed adequate emollient therapy and low-potency topical corticosteroids. What is the most appropriate next step?

choices.feedback: Treatment follows a stepwise approach: (1) emollients + trigger avoidance, (2) low-potency TCS (face/folds), (3) medium-potency TCS (body), (4) calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus/pimecrolimus) for steroid-sparing, (5) dupilumab for moderate-severe refractory disease. Medium-potency TCS is appropriate for body lesions when low-potency fails.
Dupilumab (anti-IL-4R alpha) is indicated for moderate-severe AD refractory to topical therapies, but would be premature before optimizing topical management.
Immunotherapy targets IgE-mediated conditions (rhinitis, venom allergy). Its role in AD is limited and not standard of care.
Cyclosporine is reserved for severe refractory AD after topical therapies have failed. It carries significant toxicity risks.

questions.explanation: Key detail: potency topical corticosteroid for body lesions.

questions.stem: A child with severe atopic dermatitis develops grouped vesicles on an erythematous base within areas of eczematous skin. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Contact dermatitis causes well-demarcated patches/plaques but not the characteristic grouped vesicles of HSV.

Eczema herpeticum is a medical emergency caused by HSV dissemination in atopic skin. It presents with monomorphic punched-out erosions or grouped vesicles in areas of eczema. The impaired skin barrier in AD allows HSV to spread widely. Treatment: IV acyclovir. Must distinguish from bacterial superinfection (impetigo), which shows honey-crusted lesions.
Molluscum presents as dome-shaped, umbilicated papules, not grouped vesicles. Though common in AD patients.
S. aureus superinfection (impetigo) shows honey-crusted lesions, not grouped vesicles on erythematous base.

questions.explanation: References: Atopic Dermatitis. StatPearls. NBK448071. [Open Access]

questions.stem: The 'atopic march' refers to the natural progression of atopic diseases. Which sequence correctly describes this progression?

choices.text: Allergic rhinitis → asthma → food allergy → atopic dermatitis
choices.feedback: Allergic rhinitis typically develops after AD, not before.

choices.text: Food allergy → atopic dermatitis → asthma → allergic rhinitis
choices.feedback: While food allergy can occur early, AD typically precedes food allergy development in the atopic march.

choices.text: Asthma → allergic rhinitis → atopic dermatitis → food allergy
choices.feedback: This is the reverse of the typical atopic march. AD is usually the first manifestation.

choices.text: Atopic dermatitis → food allergy → allergic rhinitis → asthma
choices.feedback: The atopic march describes the typical temporal progression: AD often appears first in infancy, followed by food allergy, then allergic rhinitis and asthma in childhood. The epicutaneous sensitization theory suggests that allergen exposure through impaired skin barrier (AD) drives systemic IgE sensitization, leading to subsequent allergic diseases.

questions.explanation: References: Atopic Dermatitis. StatPearls. NBK448071. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A 45-year-old healthcare worker develops well-demarcated, erythematous, vesicular plaques on her hands 48 hours after wearing new latex gloves. What type of hypersensitivity reaction is this?

choices.feedback: Type III reactions involve immune complex deposition (e.g., serum sickness, Arthus reaction).
Type II reactions involve IgG/IgM antibodies against cell surface antigens (e.g., hemolytic anemia).

Allergic contact dermatitis is a T-cell-mediated type IV hypersensitivity reaction. The delay (24-72 hours) reflects the time needed for antigen-presenting Langerhans cells to process hapten-protein complexes and activate sensitized T cells. Distinct from type I latex allergy (immediate IgE-mediated urticaria/anaphylaxis).
Type I latex allergy causes immediate urticaria, angioedema, or anaphylaxis—not delayed vesicular dermatitis.

questions.explanation: References: Contact Dermatitis. StatPearls. NBK532866. [Open Access]

questions.stem: Which diagnostic test is the gold standard for confirming allergic contact dermatitis?

choices.feedback: Serum IgE testing identifies type I allergen sensitization, not T-cell-mediated contact allergy.
Skin prick testing detects type I (IgE-mediated) allergy, not type IV contact sensitization.
Biopsy shows spongiotic dermatitis but cannot identify the specific causative allergen.

Patch testing applies suspected allergens to the skin under occlusion for 48 hours, then reads at 48 and 96 hours. Positive results show erythema, papules, and vesicles at the application site. The standard series includes nickel, fragrance mix, formaldehyde, rubber chemicals, and preservatives. It identifies type IV sensitizers.

questions.explanation: References: Contact Dermatitis. StatPearls. NBK532866. [Open Access]

questions.stem: What is the most common contact allergen in North America?

choices.feedback: Formaldehyde is a common allergen in cosmetics and textile finishes but is less prevalent than nickel.
Urushiol causes the most common plant-related contact dermatitis but nickel is the most common overall contact allergen identified on patch testing.

Nickel is the #1 contact allergen worldwide, found in jewelry, belt buckles, cell phones, and medical devices. Prevalence is ~15-20% in women. Other top allergens: fragrance mix, balsam of Peru, formaldehyde, and neomycin. Nickel allergy presents as eczematous dermatitis at sites of metal contact.
Latex allergy has decreased significantly due to reduced use of powdered latex gloves.

questions.explanation: References: Contact Dermatitis. StatPearls. NBK532866. [Open Access]

questions.stem: How does irritant contact dermatitis differ from allergic contact dermatitis?

choices.text: Irritant contact dermatitis is confirmed by positive patch testing
choices.feedback: Patch testing identifies allergic contact sensitization. Irritant contact dermatitis shows negative patch tests.

choices.text: Irritant contact dermatitis always presents with vesicles
choices.feedback: Irritant contact dermatitis often presents as dry, fissured, erythematous patches without vesiculation.

choices.text: Irritant contact dermatitis is mediated by IgE antibodies
choices.feedback: Irritant contact dermatitis is not immune-mediated. It results from direct tissue damage.

choices.text: Irritant contact dermatitis does not require prior sensitization and can occur on first exposure
choices.feedback: Irritant contact dermatitis is a non-immunologic reaction caused by direct chemical/physical damage to the skin. It can occur on first exposure (no prior sensitization needed), affects anyone with sufficient exposure, and is typically dose-dependent. Allergic contact dermatitis requires prior sensitization (first exposure → sensitization → re-exposure → reaction).

questions.explanation: References: Contact Dermatitis. StatPearls. NBK532866. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A patient develops linear, vesicular, intensely pruritic lesions on exposed skin areas 24 hours after hiking. Which allergen is responsible?

choices.feedback: Urushiol is the oleoresin in Toxicodendron species (poison ivy, oak, sumac). The linear distribution ('phytodermatitis') reflects plant contact pattern. Urushiol binds to skin proteins forming haptens → Langerhans cell processing → T-cell activation. Treatment: high-potency topical corticosteroids for localized disease; systemic prednisone for widespread involvement.
Insect bites cause papular urticaria, not linear vesicular lesions.
Phytophotodermatitis causes hyperpigmented, non-vesicular lesions in sun-exposed areas after plant + UV exposure.
Dermatophyte infections (tinea) cause annular, scaly plaques with central clearing, not linear vesicles.

questions.explanation: References: Contact Dermatitis. StatPearls. NBK532866. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A patient with a documented penicillin allergy (childhood rash, details unknown) requires antibiotic therapy for infective endocarditis. Penicillin skin testing is negative. What is the most appropriate next step?

choices.feedback: Desensitization is indicated when skin testing is POSITIVE but the patient needs penicillin. Negative testing means it is not needed.

choices.text: Administer penicillin, as negative skin test indicates <1% risk of immediate reaction
choices.feedback: Penicillin skin testing has a 97-99% negative predictive value for immediate (type I) reactions. Most patients (~90%) with reported penicillin allergy are NOT truly allergic when formally tested. Negative skin test + graded oral challenge safely de-labels the patient, enabling optimal antibiotic selection and reducing use of broader-spectrum alternatives.
While vancomycin is an alternative, de-labeling the allergy allows use of the most effective drug. Vancomycin has inferior efficacy for MSSA endocarditis.

choices.text: Obtain serum-specific IgE to penicillin for confirmation
choices.feedback: In vitro IgE testing for penicillin has lower sensitivity than skin testing and would not change management here.

questions.explanation: Approximately 80-90% of patients who report a penicillin allergy are ultimately able to tolerate penicillins. A negative penicillin skin test (using both major and minor determinants) has a high negative predictive value (>95%), indicating less than 1% risk of an IgE-mediated immediate reaction. In the setting of infective endocarditis, where penicillin or a penicillin-class antibiotic is the treatment of choice, a negative skin test allows safe administration. Desensitization is reserved for patients with positive skin tests who require penicillin. Vancomycin is inferior for endocarditis caused by susceptible organisms. Serum-specific IgE has lower sensitivity than skin testing and would not change management when skin testing is already negative.

references.supportingQuote: 80% to 90% of people once considered allergic are ultimately able to tolerate penicillins

questions.stem: A patient develops fever, facial edema, diffuse morbilliform rash, lymphadenopathy, and eosinophilia 3 weeks after starting phenytoin. Liver enzymes are significantly elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: SJS presents with mucosal erosions, targetoid lesions, and skin detachment, not eosinophilia and lymphadenopathy.
Serum sickness causes fever, arthralgias, and rash but typically lacks eosinophilia and significant hepatitis.
Drug-induced lupus presents with arthralgias, serositis, and anti-histone antibodies, not eosinophilia and acute hepatitis.

DRESS syndrome is characterized by the triad of rash, eosinophilia, and organ involvement (most commonly hepatitis) with delayed onset (2-8 weeks). Common triggers: anticonvulsants (phenytoin, carbamazepine, lamotrigine), allopurinol, sulfonamides. Associated with HHV-6 reactivation. Mortality is 5-10%, primarily from hepatic failure.

questions.explanation: DRESS syndrome (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms) is a severe delayed hypersensitivity reaction (Type IV) presenting 2-8 weeks after drug initiation. The classic triad is fever, rash, and internal organ involvement (most commonly hepatitis). Key distinguishing features include facial edema (especially periorbital), eosinophilia (>700/μL), atypical lymphocytosis, and lymphadenopathy. Aromatic anticonvulsants (phenytoin, carbamazepine, lamotrigine) are among the most common culprits. SJS/TEN involves mucosal erosions, targetoid lesions, and epidermal detachment — not eosinophilia or lymphadenopathy. Serum sickness-like reaction causes fever, rash, and arthralgias but not eosinophilia or hepatitis. Drug-induced lupus presents with joint pain, serositis, and positive anti-histone antibodies, typically from procainamide or hydralazine.

references.supportingQuote: Type 1 and type 4 hypersensitivity reactions mediate the most common allergies to penicillin

questions.stem: What is the cross-reactivity rate between penicillins and first-generation cephalosporins?

choices.feedback: The 10% figure is outdated and was based on flawed early studies. Modern data demonstrate ~1-2% cross-reactivity.

The historical 10% cross-reactivity rate was inflated by contaminated early cephalosporin preparations. Current data show ~1-2% cross-reactivity with first-generation cephalosporins (similar R1 side chain) and even lower rates with later generations. The key determinant is shared R1 side chain structure, not the beta-lactam ring itself. Cefazolin has a unique side chain and very low cross-reactivity.
While the risk is low, it is not zero. Some cross-reactivity exists, particularly when side chain structures overlap.
This dramatically overestimates the risk. Actual cross-reactivity is approximately 1-2%.

questions.explanation: The often-quoted 10% cross-reactivity rate between penicillins and cephalosporins is a historical overestimate, partly due to penicillin contamination during early cephalosporin manufacturing. Current evidence shows overall cross-reactivity with cephalosporins is approximately 1-3%, with first-generation cephalosporins having slightly higher rates (~1-2%) due to greater R1 side-chain structural similarity with penicillins. Cross-reactivity is driven by side-chain similarity, not the shared beta-lactam ring. Second-generation cephalosporins show negligible cross-reactivity, and third/fourth-generation cephalosporins have an even lower risk. Among patients with confirmed positive penicillin skin tests who receive cephalosporins, the reaction rate is approximately 2%. For patients with only a reported history (without skin testing), the rate drops to approximately 1%.

references.supportingQuote: Overall cross-reactivity with cephalosporins less than 3% and more commonly with first and second generation cephalosporins

questions.stem: A patient with aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD) needs aspirin for secondary cardiac prevention. What is the appropriate approach?

choices.text: Switch to a COX-2 selective inhibitor for cardiac prevention
choices.feedback: COX-2 inhibitors increase cardiovascular risk and are not used for cardiac prevention. COX-2 inhibitors are generally tolerated in AERD but serve a different purpose.

choices.text: Avoid aspirin permanently and use clopidogrel alone
choices.feedback: Clopidogrel alone is inferior to aspirin-containing regimens for cardiac prevention. Desensitization allows safe aspirin use.

choices.text: Aspirin desensitization followed by daily maintenance dosing
choices.feedback: Aspirin desensitization is indicated for AERD patients who require aspirin for cardiac or anti-inflammatory indications. It involves gradually increasing oral doses over 1-2 days under medical supervision. Once desensitized, the patient must take aspirin daily—if a dose is missed for >48 hours, re-desensitization is required. Desensitization works by depleting leukotriene-producing pathways.

choices.text: Premedicate with corticosteroids and antihistamines before each dose
choices.feedback: Premedication does not prevent aspirin reactions in AERD. Desensitization is the correct approach.

questions.explanation: Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD), also known as Samter triad (asthma, nasal polyps, aspirin/NSAID sensitivity), is caused by dysregulated arachidonic acid metabolism with overproduction of cysteinyl leukotrienes. When aspirin is medically necessary for cardiac prevention, aspirin desensitization followed by daily maintenance dosing is the recommended approach. Desensitization involves graded oral challenge over 1-2 days in a monitored setting, starting with small doses and gradually increasing. Once desensitized, daily dosing must continue without interruption (>48 hours off aspirin requires re-desensitization). COX-2 inhibitors do not provide equivalent antiplatelet effects for cardiac prevention. Clopidogrel alone is inferior to aspirin-containing regimens for secondary cardiac prevention. Simple premedication does not prevent the COX-1-mediated reaction in AERD.

references.supportingQuote: Current research suggests that similarities between the chemical structures of the R-side chains determine cross-reactivity

questions.stem: Which Gell and Coombs classification describes the mechanism of drug-induced hemolytic anemia caused by penicillin?

choices.feedback: Type I reactions cause urticaria, angioedema, or anaphylaxis—not hemolytic anemia.

Penicillin binds to RBC membranes, acting as a hapten. IgG antibodies form against the penicillin-RBC complex → complement activation → extravascular hemolysis. This is the classic example of type II hypersensitivity. Type I = immediate/IgE (anaphylaxis). Type III = immune complex (serum sickness). Type IV = delayed/T-cell (contact dermatitis).
Type III reactions cause serum sickness, vasculitis, and glomerulonephritis—not direct antibody-mediated RBC destruction.
Type IV reactions are T-cell-mediated (contact dermatitis, DRESS). They do not cause hemolytic anemia.

questions.explanation: Penicillin-induced hemolytic anemia is a classic example of Type II (antibody-mediated cytotoxicity) hypersensitivity. Penicillin acts as a hapten, binding covalently to proteins on the red blood cell surface. IgG antibodies are then produced against the penicillin-RBC complex, leading to Fc receptor-mediated phagocytosis by splenic macrophages (extravascular hemolysis) and/or complement activation with direct lysis (intravascular hemolysis). The direct Coombs test is positive. Type I (IgE-mediated) reactions cause immediate hypersensitivity (urticaria, anaphylaxis). Type III (immune complex) reactions cause serum sickness. Type IV (delayed-type) reactions are T-cell mediated and include contact dermatitis and DRESS syndrome. Other drugs causing Type II hemolytic anemia include cephalosporins, methyldopa, and quinidine.

references.supportingQuote: Type 1 and type 4 hypersensitivity reactions mediate the most common allergies to penicillin; serious allergic reactions are Type 1 mediated by IgE

questions.stem: A 32-year-old patient presents with persistent eosinophilia (2,500 cells/μL). Stool ova and parasites are negative, and the patient has no travel history. Which cytokine is most responsible for eosinophil production and survival?

choices.feedback: IFN-gamma is a Th1 cytokine that actually inhibits eosinophil recruitment.
IL-2 is primarily a T-cell growth factor.

IL-5 is the primary cytokine driving eosinophil differentiation, maturation, activation, and survival. It is produced by Th2 cells, ILC2s, and mast cells. Anti-IL-5 therapies (mepolizumab, reslizumab) and anti-IL-5R (benralizumab) are used to treat eosinophilic conditions. IL-3 and GM-CSF also contribute but are less specific.
IL-4 drives B-cell IgE class switching and Th2 differentiation but is not the primary eosinophil growth factor.

questions.explanation: References: Eosinophilia. StatPearls. NBK560929. [Open Access]

questions.stem: The differential diagnosis for eosinophilia can be remembered by the mnemonic NAACP. What does this stand for?

choices.text: Neutrophilia, Anemia, Arthritis, Cardiac disease, Pneumonia
choices.feedback: This mnemonic does not correspond to eosinophilia differential.

choices.text: Neoplasm, Allergy/Atopy, Adrenal insufficiency, Connective tissue disease, Parasites
choices.feedback: NAACP covers the major categories: Neoplasms (lymphoma, hypereosinophilic syndrome), Allergic/Atopic diseases (asthma, AD, drug reactions), Adrenal insufficiency (loss of cortisol-mediated eosinophil apoptosis), Connective tissue diseases (EGPA, eosinophilic fasciitis), and Parasitic infections (most common cause worldwide).

choices.text: Nasal polyps, Aspirin sensitivity, Asthma, Chronic sinusitis, Peripheral eosinophilia
choices.feedback: This describes Samter's triad/AERD features, not the NAACP eosinophilia differential.

choices.text: Nausea, Abdominal pain, Asthma, Cough, Pruritus
choices.feedback: These are symptoms of eosinophilic conditions, not the differential diagnosis framework.

questions.explanation: References: Eosinophilia. StatPearls. NBK560929. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A patient with persistent eosinophilia >1,500/μL for 6 months, no identifiable secondary cause, and evidence of end-organ damage (cardiomyopathy) is diagnosed with hypereosinophilic syndrome. What mutation should be tested for?

choices.feedback: JAK2 V617F is associated with polycythemia vera and other myeloproliferative neoplasms but not HES.

FIP1L1-PDGFRA is a gain-of-function tyrosine kinase fusion found in myeloproliferative variant HES. It responds dramatically to imatinib (even at low doses). Testing via FISH or RT-PCR is essential because it determines treatment: imatinib-responsive vs. corticosteroid/anti-IL-5 therapy for lymphocytic or idiopathic variants.
BCR-ABL defines chronic myelogenous leukemia. While CML can have eosinophilia, it is not the defining mutation of HES.
BRAF V600E is associated with Langerhans cell histiocytosis and melanoma, not HES.

questions.explanation: References: Eosinophilia. StatPearls. NBK560929. [Open Access]

questions.stem: Which organ is most commonly affected by end-organ damage in hypereosinophilic syndrome?

choices.feedback: CNS involvement (thromboembolic events, neuropathy) can occur but cardiac involvement is the primary concern.

Cardiac involvement is the leading cause of morbidity/mortality in HES. Eosinophil granule proteins (major basic protein, eosinophil cationic protein) are directly cardiotoxic. Progression: acute necrosis → thrombotic stage (mural thrombi) → fibrotic stage (restrictive cardiomyopathy). Echocardiography and cardiac MRI are essential for monitoring.
Pulmonary involvement occurs in HES but cardiac damage is more common and carries higher mortality.
Hepatic involvement can occur but is less frequent and less dangerous than cardiac involvement.

questions.explanation: Correct: Heart (endomyocardial fibrosis).

questions.stem: At what eosinophil count threshold is eosinophilia defined, and at what level does it become 'severe'?

choices.feedback: Classification: mild (500-1,500/μL), moderate (1,500-5,000/μL), severe (>5,000/μL). Risk of end-organ damage increases significantly above 1,500/μL and is almost certain above 5,000/μL. Normal eosinophil count is <500/μL (typically 1-3% of WBC). The HES diagnostic criterion requires sustained >1,500/μL for >6 months.
These thresholds are too low. Normal eosinophils range up to 500/μL.
These thresholds are too high. Eosinophilia begins at >500/μL.
The threshold for eosinophilia is 500/μL, not 1,000/μL.

questions.explanation: References: Eosinophilia. StatPearls. NBK560929. [Open Access]

questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy presents with recurrent sinopulmonary infections. Quantitative immunoglobulins show markedly low IgG, IgA, and IgM. Flow cytometry reveals absent B cells with normal T cell counts. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: IgA deficiency has isolated low IgA with normal IgG, IgM, and B cell counts.

XLA is caused by BTK (Bruton tyrosine kinase) mutation → B-cell maturation arrest at pre-B cell stage. Presents at 6-9 months (when maternal IgG wanes). Findings: absent/very low B cells, panhypogammaglobulinemia, absent tonsils/lymphoid tissue, normal T cells. Susceptible to encapsulated bacteria. Treatment: lifelong IVIG replacement.
DiGeorge syndrome is a T-cell deficiency (thymic hypoplasia) with normal B cell counts.
CVID typically presents later (teens-30s) and has low but present B cells with variable immunoglobulin levels.

questions.explanation: X-linked agammaglobulinemia (XLA, Bruton agammaglobulinemia) results from mutations in Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) on the X chromosome, causing arrest of B-cell development at the pre-B cell stage. Key features: onset after 6 months when maternal IgG wanes, recurrent sinopulmonary infections with encapsulated organisms, markedly reduced or absent B cells (<2%) on flow cytometry, profoundly low IgG, IgA, and IgM, and normal T-cell numbers and function. Selective IgA deficiency has normal B cells and only IgA is low. DiGeorge syndrome (22q11.2 deletion) causes T-cell deficiency due to thymic hypoplasia, not B-cell absence. Common variable immunodeficiency (CVID) has normal or low B cells present but dysfunctional, and typically presents later (ages 20-40) with hypogammaglobulinemia.

references.supportingQuote: X-linked agammaglobulinemia results from mutations in BTK causing absence of mature B cells and profoundly low immunoglobulin levels

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant presents with Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, oral thrush, and failure to thrive. Absolute lymphocyte count is markedly low. What test is most critical for diagnosis?

choices.feedback: This presentation is classic for severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID)—a pediatric emergency. T-cell enumeration confirms absent/severely reduced T cells. SCID can be T-B+NK-, T-B-NK+, or T-B-NK- depending on the genetic defect. Newborn screening via TREC assay detects SCID before infections occur. Treatment: hematopoietic stem cell transplant.
Immunoglobulins may be low in SCID but the critical defect is T-cell absence. Low IgG could also reflect maternal antibody decline.
CH50 tests complement pathway function, which is not the issue in SCID.
NBT tests for chronic granulomatous disease (neutrophil oxidative burst defect), not T-cell deficiency.

questions.explanation: Severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) presents in the first months of life with opportunistic infections (Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia is classic), persistent oral thrush, chronic diarrhea, and failure to thrive. The hallmark is profound lymphopenia with markedly reduced or absent T cells. T-cell lymphocyte subset panel (CD3, CD4, CD8 enumeration by flow cytometry) is the critical diagnostic test, identifying the specific SCID phenotype (T-B+NK+, T-B-NK-, T-B+NK-, etc.) which guides genetic testing. Quantitative immunoglobulins may be normal initially due to residual maternal IgG. CH50 evaluates the complement cascade, not lymphocyte function. The nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) test screens for chronic granulomatous disease (phagocyte oxidative burst defect), which presents later with catalase-positive organism infections.

references.supportingQuote: T-cell enumeration by flow cytometry is essential for diagnosis of SCID

questions.stem: A patient with recurrent Neisseria meningitidis infections should be evaluated for deficiency in which immune component?

choices.feedback: Terminal complement deficiency (C5-C9) impairs formation of the membrane attack complex (MAC), which is essential for killing Neisseria species. Patients have recurrent meningococcal and gonococcal infections. Screen with CH50 (total hemolytic complement)—a level of zero indicates complete pathway deficiency. Also test AH50 for alternative pathway.
T-cell defects predispose to opportunistic infections (PJP, CMV, fungi), not specifically Neisseria.
IgG subclass deficiency causes recurrent sinopulmonary infections, not specifically recurrent Neisseria.
Neutrophil defects (CGD) cause infections with catalase-positive organisms, not Neisseria.

questions.explanation: Deficiency of terminal complement components (C5-C9) impairs formation of the membrane attack complex (MAC), which is uniquely important for killing encapsulated Neisseria species. Patients present with recurrent or severe Neisseria meningitidis or N. gonorrhoeae infections. Screening is done with CH50 (total hemolytic complement), which is absent or markedly reduced in terminal complement deficiency because the assay requires all components (C1-C9) for complete hemolysis. T-cell deficiency predisposes to viral and opportunistic infections. IgG subclass deficiency causes recurrent sinopulmonary infections but not specifically Neisseria. Neutrophil oxidative burst testing (DHR flow cytometry or NBT) diagnoses chronic granulomatous disease, which predisposes to catalase-positive organisms (S. aureus, Aspergillus), not Neisseria.

references.supportingQuote: Terminal complement deficiency C5-C9 predisposes to recurrent Neisseria infections

questions.stem: Which primary immunodeficiency is the most common, and what is the clinical significance?

choices.feedback: XLA is much rarer (~1:200,000) than IgA deficiency.

IgA deficiency is the most common primary immunodeficiency. Most patients are asymptomatic. Symptomatic patients may have recurrent sinopulmonary or GI infections, autoimmune diseases, and celiac disease. Critical pearl: risk of anaphylaxis with blood transfusions containing IgA (anti-IgA antibodies). Use IgA-depleted or washed blood products.
CGD is a rare phagocyte defect (~1:250,000).
CVID is the most common symptomatic primary immunodeficiency (~1:25,000) but not the most common overall.

questions.explanation: Selective IgA deficiency is the most common primary immunodeficiency, with a prevalence of approximately 1:300-500 in Caucasian populations. Most patients are asymptomatic; symptomatic patients may have recurrent sinopulmonary and GI infections, and increased risk of autoimmune diseases (celiac disease, SLE, rheumatoid arthritis) and atopy. A critical clinical concern is the risk of anaphylactic transfusion reactions in patients with anti-IgA antibodies who receive IgA-containing blood products. Diagnosis requires serum IgA <7 mg/dL with normal IgG and IgM levels in a patient older than 4 years. XLA is much rarer (~1:200,000). CGD presents with recurrent catalase-positive infections. CVID is the most common symptomatic primary immunodeficiency (~1:25,000) but not the most common overall.

references.supportingQuote: Selective IgA deficiency is the most common primary immunodeficiency

questions.stem: A child with eczema, thrombocytopenia with small platelets, and recurrent infections is most likely to have which immunodeficiency?

choices.feedback: AT presents with cerebellar ataxia, oculocutaneous telangiectasias, and IgA deficiency—not thrombocytopenia.

WAS triad: Eczema, Thrombocytopenia (characteristically small platelets—this is unique!), and recurrent Infections (both humoral and cellular defects). X-linked recessive, caused by WASP gene mutation affecting cytoskeletal regulation in hematopoietic cells. Also predisposed to autoimmunity and lymphoma. Treatment: HSCT.
Hyper-IgE (Job syndrome) has eczema and infections but features coarse facies, retained primary teeth, and high IgE—NOT thrombocytopenia.
IPEX has immune dysregulation, polyendocrinopathy, and enteropathy due to FOXP3 mutation—not thrombocytopenia with small platelets.

questions.explanation: Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome (WAS) is an X-linked recessive disorder caused by mutations in the WAS gene encoding WASp protein, which is critical for actin cytoskeleton regulation in hematopoietic cells. The classic triad is: eczema, thrombocytopenia with characteristically small platelets (micro-thrombocytopenia), and recurrent infections (particularly with encapsulated organisms). The small platelet size distinguishes WAS from immune thrombocytopenia, where platelets are normal or large. Patients also have increased risk of autoimmune disease and lymphoid malignancies. Ataxia-telangiectasia presents with cerebellar ataxia, oculocutaneous telangiectasias, and elevated AFP. Hyper-IgE (Job) syndrome features coarse facies, retained primary teeth, cold abscesses, and markedly elevated IgE. IPEX syndrome (FOXP3 mutation) causes severe autoimmunity with enteropathy, dermatitis, and endocrinopathy.

references.supportingQuote: Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome presents with eczema, thrombocytopenia with small platelets, and recurrent infections

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with recurrent wheals lasting less than 24 hours each, occurring almost daily for the past 8 weeks. Individual lesions resolve completely without bruising. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Urticarial vasculitis lesions are painful (not pruritic), last >24 hours, and leave ecchymosis/residual hyperpigmentation.
Acute urticaria lasts <6 weeks and often has identifiable triggers (foods, drugs, infections).

Chronic urticaria is defined as wheals occurring most days for >6 weeks. In chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU), individual lesions resolve within 24 hours without residual marks. Up to 50% have autoimmune etiology (anti-FcεRI or anti-IgE autoantibodies). Important distinction: if lesions last >24 hours or leave bruising → consider urticarial vasculitis (biopsy needed).
EM presents with targetoid lesions (three-zone pattern), not migratory wheals.

questions.explanation: Chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU) is defined as recurrent wheals, angioedema, or both occurring for more than 6 weeks without a consistent external trigger. Key diagnostic features in this vignette: individual lesions last <24 hours, resolve completely without residual bruising or pigmentation, and symptoms are near-daily. Urticarial vasculitis is distinguished by lesions lasting >24 hours that leave bruising or hyperpigmentation (requires biopsy showing leukocytoclastic vasculitis). Acute urticaria by definition lasts <6 weeks. Erythema multiforme presents with targetoid lesions (concentric color zones), often associated with HSV infection or drugs, and is morphologically distinct from wheals. CSU is often autoimmune, with IgG autoantibodies against IgE or the high-affinity IgE receptor (FcεRI) on mast cells.

references.supportingQuote: Chronic urticaria is defined as urticaria occurring on most days of the week for more than 6 weeks

questions.stem: A patient with chronic spontaneous urticaria fails to respond to standard-dose second-generation antihistamines. According to guidelines, what is the recommended next step?

choices.feedback: Chronic oral corticosteroids are not recommended for CSU due to side effects. Short bursts may be used for acute exacerbations only.
First-generation antihistamines have more side effects (sedation, anticholinergic) without superior efficacy. Up-dosing second-gen is preferred.

choices.text: Increase antihistamine dose up to 4x the standard dose
choices.feedback: Step-up approach: (1) standard-dose second-gen antihistamine, (2) up-dose to 2-4x standard dose, (3) add omalizumab (anti-IgE). Up-dosing is safe with second-generation (non-sedating) antihistamines like cetirizine or fexofenadine. This controls ~60-70% of refractory patients before needing omalizumab.
Omalizumab is step 3, after up-dosing antihistamines to 4x has been tried and failed.

questions.explanation: Per international consensus guidelines (EAACI/GA²LEN/EuroGuiDerm), the stepwise treatment of chronic spontaneous urticaria is: Step 1: standard-dose second-generation H1-antihistamine → Step 2: increase to up to 4 times the standard dose → Step 3: add omalizumab (anti-IgE monoclonal antibody) → Step 4: add cyclosporine. Updosing is preferred before adding biologics because it is effective in an additional 40-60% of patients without significant safety concerns (second-generation antihistamines have a wide therapeutic index). Oral corticosteroids should be avoided for chronic use due to long-term adverse effects. First-generation antihistamines have inferior safety profiles (sedation, anticholinergic effects). Omalizumab is Step 3, not Step 2, in the algorithm.

references.supportingQuote: Increasing second-generation antihistamines up to four times the standard dose is recommended before adding additional agents

questions.stem: A patient on an ACE inhibitor develops recurrent angioedema of the lips and tongue without urticaria. What is the mechanism?

choices.feedback: IgE-mediated angioedema would present WITH urticaria and respond to antihistamines/epinephrine.
Complement-mediated angioedema occurs in hereditary angioedema (C1-INH deficiency), not ACE-I angioedema.

choices.text: Bradykinin accumulation due to decreased ACE-mediated degradation
choices.feedback: ACE inhibitors block the degradation of bradykinin by ACE (kininase II). Excess bradykinin causes vasodilation and vascular permeability → angioedema. Key features: angioedema WITHOUT urticaria, occurs in ~0.1-0.7% of ACE-I users, more common in Black patients, can occur years after starting the medication. Treatment: discontinue ACE-I, avoid ARBs initially. Epinephrine/antihistamines are often ineffective.
Histamine-mediated angioedema presents with urticaria and responds to antihistamines. ACE-I angioedema is bradykinin-mediated.

questions.explanation: ACE (angiotensin-converting enzyme) is responsible for degrading bradykinin, a potent vasodilator and mediator of vascular permeability. ACE inhibitors block this enzyme, leading to bradykinin accumulation in tissues. Excess bradykinin acts on B2 receptors in the submucosa, causing vasodilation and increased vascular permeability, resulting in angioedema — typically of the lips, tongue, and oropharynx. Importantly, this is not IgE-mediated (no urticaria is present), does not involve mast cell degranulation, and does not respond to antihistamines or epinephrine. Treatment involves stopping the ACE inhibitor and, in severe cases, administering icatibant (bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist) or C1-inhibitor concentrate. ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema occurs in 0.1-0.7% of patients and is more common in African Americans. Switching to an ARB is generally safe, with cross-reactivity reported at <10%.

references.supportingQuote: ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema is mediated by bradykinin accumulation

questions.stem: Which finding on skin biopsy would distinguish urticarial vasculitis from chronic spontaneous urticaria?

choices.feedback: This is the typical finding in chronic spontaneous urticaria—no vessel wall destruction.
Granulomas suggest sarcoidosis or granulomatous vasculitis, not urticarial vasculitis.

choices.text: Leukocytoclastic vasculitis with fibrinoid necrosis of vessel walls
choices.feedback: Urticarial vasculitis shows small vessel vasculitis: neutrophilic infiltration, leukocytoclasia (nuclear dust), fibrinoid necrosis, and extravasated RBCs. CSU biopsy shows perivascular edema with mild mixed infiltrate without vessel wall damage. Clinical clues for urticarial vasculitis: lesions lasting >24 hours, painful rather than pruritic, residual purpura/hyperpigmentation.
Eosinophils can be present in both conditions and are not distinguishing.

questions.explanation: Urticarial vasculitis is a small-vessel vasculitis that mimics urticaria clinically but is distinguished by biopsy findings of leukocytoclastic vasculitis with fibrinoid necrosis of vessel walls, neutrophilic infiltration, nuclear dust (leukocytoclasia), and red blood cell extravasation. Clinical clues that suggest vasculitis over simple urticaria include: lesions lasting >24 hours, pain or burning rather than pruritus, residual purpura or hyperpigmentation after resolution, and associated systemic symptoms (arthralgias, fever, glomerulonephritis). Hypocomplementemic urticarial vasculitis syndrome is associated with anti-C1q antibodies and can overlap with SLE. Simple CSU biopsy shows only dermal edema with sparse perivascular lymphocytes and scattered eosinophils — no vessel wall destruction.

references.supportingQuote: Biopsy distinguishes urticarial vasculitis from chronic urticaria by demonstrating leukocytoclastic vasculitis

questions.stem: Hereditary angioedema (HAE) is caused by deficiency of which protein?

choices.feedback: Factor H deficiency causes atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome.
MBL deficiency causes increased susceptibility to infections, not angioedema.

HAE types I (85%) and II (15%) involve C1-INH deficiency or dysfunction. C1-INH regulates complement, kallikrein, and coagulation pathways. Deficiency → excess bradykinin → angioedema. Distinguishing features: NO urticaria, family history (autosomal dominant), does NOT respond to epinephrine/antihistamines. Acute treatment: C1-INH concentrate, icatibant (bradykinin B2 antagonist), or ecallantide (kallikrein inhibitor).
C3 deficiency causes recurrent infections, not angioedema.

questions.explanation: Hereditary angioedema (HAE) is caused by deficiency or dysfunction of C1-esterase inhibitor (C1-INH), a serine protease inhibitor that regulates the complement, contact (kallikrein-kinin), coagulation, and fibrinolytic pathways. Type I HAE (~85% of cases) has low C1-INH levels and low function. Type II (~15%) has normal or elevated C1-INH levels but impaired function. Both types result in unregulated kallikrein activity, leading to excess bradykinin production, which causes episodic angioedema without urticaria. Laboratory findings include low C4 (useful screening test, low even between attacks), low C1-INH level or function, but normal C3. Factor H deficiency causes atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome. Mannose-binding lectin deficiency increases infection susceptibility. C3 complement deficiency causes recurrent pyogenic infections.

references.supportingQuote: Hereditary angioedema is caused by deficiency of C1-esterase inhibitor

questions.stem: A 45-year-old beekeeper develops generalized urticaria and wheezing 5 minutes after a bee sting. He has no previous history of sting reactions. After treatment in the ED, what is the recommended outpatient evaluation?

choices.text: Immediate skin prick testing in the ED before discharge
choices.feedback: Testing immediately after a reaction may yield false negatives due to mediator depletion and IgE consumption. Wait 4-6 weeks.
CRD (e.g., Api m 1 for honeybee) can help differentiate true sensitization from cross-reactivity but is supplementary to whole venom testing, not a replacement.

choices.text: Venom skin testing or serum-specific IgE 4-6 weeks after the reaction
choices.feedback: Testing should be performed 4-6 weeks post-reaction to avoid the refractory period (IgE may be transiently depleted). Skin testing has higher sensitivity than serum IgE. If positive, venom immunotherapy (VIT) is indicated for patients with systemic reactions + positive testing. VIT reduces risk of future anaphylaxis from ~60% to ~2%.

choices.text: No further testing needed if symptoms resolved with treatment
choices.feedback: All patients with systemic venom reactions should be evaluated for venom-specific IgE and considered for immunotherapy.

questions.explanation: After a systemic reaction to a Hymenoptera sting, venom skin testing or serum-specific IgE should be performed 4-6 weeks after the event. This waiting period is important because IgE levels may be transiently depleted during and immediately after anaphylaxis (refractory period), leading to false-negative results. Testing in the ED before discharge would be unreliable for the same reason. Skin testing is more sensitive than serum-specific IgE and is the preferred diagnostic method. Component-resolved diagnostics (CRD) can help distinguish genuine sensitization from cross-reactivity (e.g., Api m 1 for honeybee, Ves v 5 for yellow jacket) but is used as an adjunct, not alone. Patients with systemic reactions require evaluation because venom immunotherapy (VIT) reduces the risk of future anaphylaxis from 60% to <5%.

references.supportingQuote: Serum tryptase can be considered for confirmation; evaluation by an allergist is recommended after recovery

questions.stem: What is the recommended duration of venom immunotherapy for a patient with a history of anaphylaxis to yellow jacket venom?

choices.feedback: One year is insufficient to achieve lasting protection. Minimum recommended duration is 5 years.
Most patients can safely discontinue after 5 years, though some high-risk groups warrant indefinite therapy.
Three years provides incomplete protection. Five years is the standard minimum.

choices.text: At least 5 years, with consideration for indefinite therapy in high-risk patients
choices.feedback: Standard VIT duration is 5 years, which provides lasting protection in ~80-90% of patients. Consider indefinite therapy for: severe life-threatening reactions, honeybee allergy (higher relapse rate), mast cell disorders, or elevated baseline tryptase. The maintenance dose is typically 100 mcg of each relevant venom monthly.

questions.explanation: Venom immunotherapy (VIT) is recommended for at least 5 years in patients with a history of systemic anaphylaxis to Hymenoptera stings. After 5 years, the risk of a systemic reaction to a sting drops to approximately 5-10% (from 60% without treatment). Indefinite therapy should be considered in high-risk patients, including those with: systemic mastocytosis or elevated baseline tryptase, history of very severe (near-fatal) reactions, honeybee allergy (which has higher relapse rates after discontinuation), and those whose occupation or lifestyle results in frequent sting exposure. One year is insufficient to achieve durable protection. Lifelong therapy for all patients is not recommended by current guidelines, as most achieve lasting tolerance after 5 years. Three years is insufficient based on available evidence.

references.supportingQuote: Follow-up with allergist is recommended for long-term management including immunotherapy evaluation

questions.stem: A patient with known mastocytosis (elevated baseline tryptase) is stung by a wasp and develops anaphylaxis. Why are patients with mast cell disorders at particularly high risk?

choices.text: Increased mast cell burden leads to greater mediator release upon IgE-triggered degranulation
choices.feedback: Patients with mastocytosis have an expanded mast cell population, so IgE-mediated activation releases a disproportionately large amount of histamine, tryptase, and other mediators. They have higher rates of severe/fatal anaphylaxis from venom stings. Management: indefinite VIT, always carry two epinephrine auto-injectors, avoid beta-blockers. Baseline tryptase >11.5 ng/mL in venom allergy patients should prompt evaluation for clonal mast cell disease.

choices.text: Mast cell disorders cause IgE deficiency, preventing immune regulation
choices.feedback: Mastocytosis does not cause IgE deficiency. The risk comes from increased mast cell numbers and mediator burden.
Epinephrine receptors function normally in mastocytosis. The severity is due to massive mediator release.

choices.text: Clonal mast cells produce allergen-specific IgE at higher rates
choices.feedback: The issue is not increased IgE production but increased mast cell numbers amplifying the downstream response.

questions.explanation: Patients with mastocytosis (clonal mast cell proliferation) have an increased total body mast cell burden, meaning that when IgE-mediated degranulation occurs, a disproportionately large amount of histamine, tryptase, and other mediators are released. This leads to more severe and potentially fatal anaphylaxis. Elevated baseline serum tryptase (>11.4 ng/mL) is a marker for underlying mast cell disease and is an independent risk factor for severe sting anaphylaxis. These patients should receive venom immunotherapy indefinitely (not just 5 years) and always carry two epinephrine autoinjectors. The other answer choices are incorrect: mastocytosis does not cause IgE deficiency, does not impair epinephrine receptor function, and clonal mast cells do not produce allergen-specific IgE (IgE is produced by plasma cells derived from B lymphocytes).

references.supportingQuote: Release of numerous chemical mediators from the degranulation of basophils and mast cells after re-exposure to a specific antigen

questions.stem: A child develops a large local reaction (10 cm swelling) at the site of a bee sting that peaks at 48 hours. What is the risk of systemic anaphylaxis with future stings?

choices.text: Approximately 5-10%, and venom immunotherapy is generally not indicated
choices.feedback: Large local reactions (swelling >10 cm lasting >24 hours) have only a 5-10% risk of progression to systemic reaction with future stings. VIT is generally NOT recommended for large local reactions alone because the risk-benefit ratio doesn't favor it. Management: local wound care, antihistamines, short corticosteroid course if needed. Prescribe epinephrine auto-injector for patient reassurance.

choices.text: Greater than 60%, and venom immunotherapy is mandatory
choices.feedback: The 60% risk applies to patients with prior systemic anaphylaxis, not large local reactions.

choices.text: Zero—large local reactions never progress to systemic reactions
choices.feedback: While the risk is low (5-10%), progression to systemic reaction can occur.

choices.text: Approximately 50%, and skin testing should be performed immediately
choices.feedback: This significantly overestimates the risk for large local reactions.

questions.explanation: Large local reactions (LLRs) to insect stings are defined as swelling >10 cm in diameter, contiguous with the sting site, peaking at 24-48 hours and resolving over 5-10 days. These are IgE-mediated late-phase reactions. The risk of a future systemic anaphylactic reaction in patients with LLRs is approximately 5-10%, compared to 60% in patients with prior systemic anaphylaxis. Because the risk is relatively low and LLRs themselves are not life-threatening, venom immunotherapy is generally not indicated. Patients should be counseled on avoidance measures and may benefit from carrying an epinephrine autoinjector as a precaution, though this is not universally recommended. The >60% risk figure applies only to those who have already had a systemic reaction. The risk is never zero, but it is far less than 50%.

references.supportingQuote: Clinical presentation often begins as a mild allergic reaction with cutaneous flushing, pruritus and urticaria

questions.stem: Which of the following Hymenoptera is most commonly responsible for fatal sting anaphylaxis in the United States?

choices.feedback: Yellow jackets are the most common cause of venom anaphylaxis in the US due to their aggressive behavior, ground-nesting habits (easy to disturb), and attraction to food/beverages. Other significant species: honeybees (retained stinger), paper wasps, hornets, and fire ants (unique venom with piloidal alkaloids). Cross-reactivity exists between wasp species but not between wasps and honeybees.
Honeybees cause significant stings but are less aggressive than yellow jackets. Their stinger remains embedded, limiting venom delivery.
Fire ants are significant in the southeastern US but yellow jackets cause more fatal anaphylaxis nationally.
Paper wasps cause venom allergy but are less commonly implicated in fatal reactions than yellow jackets.

questions.explanation: Yellow jackets (Vespula species) are the most common cause of fatal Hymenoptera sting anaphylaxis in the United States. Several factors contribute: they are aggressive, can sting repeatedly (unlike honeybees which lose their barbed stinger), nest in the ground where they are easily disturbed, and are attracted to food and drinks at outdoor gatherings, increasing accidental exposure. Honeybees (Apis mellifera) are responsible for more stings in beekeepers but are less aggressive and usually sting once. Fire ants (Solenopsis invicta) are the most common cause of sting anaphylaxis in the southeastern US specifically but not nationwide. Paper wasps (Polistes) are less aggressive than yellow jackets and tend to cause fewer fatalities. Of note, sensitization patterns vary geographically — in areas where fire ants are endemic, they may be the predominant cause.

references.supportingQuote: Common inciting sources include exposure to insect stings; the most common causes include bee stings and fire ant bites

questions.stem: A 4-month-old infant presents in winter with nasal congestion, wheezing, tachypnea, and subcostal retractions. Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation and peribronchial thickening. What is the most common causative organism?

choices.feedback: RSV causes ~70% of bronchiolitis cases in infants <12 months. Peak incidence is 2-6 months during winter. RSV infects bronchiolar epithelium → necrosis, edema, mucus plugging → air trapping and wheezing. Treatment is primarily supportive (suctioning, hydration, supplemental O2). Palivizumab prophylaxis for high-risk infants.
Mycoplasma typically affects school-age children and adolescents, not infants.
S. pneumoniae causes bacterial pneumonia with lobar consolidation, not bronchiolitis.
Influenza can cause respiratory illness in infants but accounts for only ~5% of bronchiolitis cases.

questions.explanation: Correct: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV).

questions.stem: Which of the following interventions has the strongest evidence of benefit in the management of acute bronchiolitis?

choices.feedback: AAP guidelines recommend AGAINST routine bronchodilator use in bronchiolitis. A trial may be considered but should be discontinued if no improvement.
Corticosteroids have no proven benefit in bronchiolitis and are not recommended.

AAP guidelines emphasize supportive care only: nasal suctioning, hydration (IV if oral intake is poor), and supplemental O2 for sustained SpO2 <90%. Evidence does NOT support routine use of bronchodilators, corticosteroids, antibiotics, or chest physiotherapy in bronchiolitis.

choices.text: Nebulized hypertonic saline in the ED
choices.feedback: Hypertonic saline may have modest benefit in hospitalized patients but is not recommended for ED use.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Nasal suctioning and supportive care with hydration and oxygen monitoring.

questions.stem: Which premature infant population qualifies for palivizumab (Synagis) prophylaxis?

choices.text: Infants born <29 weeks gestational age during their first RSV season
choices.feedback: Palivizumab is a monoclonal anti-RSV antibody given monthly during RSV season. Current AAP criteria: infants born <29 weeks GA in first year, children with hemodynamically significant CHD or chronic lung disease of prematurity in first 2 years. Given IM monthly (max 5 doses per season). Nirsevimab (long-acting) is now an alternative single-dose option.

choices.text: Infants born at 37 weeks with no comorbidities
choices.feedback: Late preterm infants without CHD or CLD do not meet criteria for palivizumab.

choices.text: Only infants with documented RSV infection
choices.feedback: Palivizumab is prophylactic—given BEFORE infection, not as treatment.

choices.text: All premature infants regardless of gestational age
choices.feedback: Palivizumab is not indicated for all premature infants. Specific GA and comorbidity criteria must be met.

questions.stem: A 3-month-old with bronchiolitis has apneic episodes. Which feature of this infant's presentation increases the risk of apnea?

choices.text: Young age (<2 months) or prematurity history
choices.feedback: Risk factors for RSV-associated apnea: age <2 months (especially <1 month), prematurity (especially <32 weeks), and low birth weight. Apnea may be the presenting sign before respiratory symptoms appear. Mechanism: central apnea from immature brainstem respiratory center. These infants require hospitalization and cardiorespiratory monitoring.
Male sex is a risk factor for bronchiolitis severity but not specifically for apnea.
High fever does not specifically predict apneic episodes in bronchiolitis.
Smoke exposure increases bronchiolitis risk but is not a specific predictor of apnea.

questions.stem: Why are infants more susceptible to bronchiolitis than older children?

choices.text: Smaller airway caliber with proportionally greater resistance from mucosal edema
choices.feedback: Poiseuille's law: resistance ∝ 1/r⁴. Small infant airways have dramatically increased resistance with even minor mucosal edema. A 1mm circumferential edema reduces infant airway cross-section by ~75% vs ~20% in adults. Additionally, infants have fewer collateral ventilation channels, less cartilaginous airway support, and immature immune responses.
Maternal IgG transfers across placenta and is present in early infancy. The issue is airway caliber, not antibody absence.
Surfactant deficiency is relevant to RDS in premature neonates, not bronchiolitis pathophysiology.

choices.text: Higher viral load exposure from daycare
choices.feedback: While daycare increases exposure, the primary reason for severity is the anatomical airway difference.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child presents at night with a barky cough, inspiratory stridor, and hoarseness following 2 days of URI symptoms. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Epiglottitis presents with drooling, tripod position, high fever, and muffled voice—NOT barky cough. It has dramatically decreased with H. influenzae type b vaccination.

Croup is the most common cause of stridor in children 6 months-3 years. Classic presentation: barky/seal-like cough, inspiratory stridor, hoarseness, worse at night. Caused by parainfluenza virus (types 1 and 3 most common). Subglottic edema narrows the airway → steeple sign on AP neck X-ray.
Foreign body aspiration presents with acute onset of choking/coughing without preceding URI symptoms.
Bacterial tracheitis presents with high fever, toxic appearance, and thick purulent secretions—typically more severe than viral croup.

questions.explanation: Correct: Viral croup (laryngotracheobronchitis).

questions.stem: A child with moderate croup has stridor at rest but no significant distress. What is the most effective initial treatment?

choices.feedback: Albuterol acts on lower airway smooth muscle and is ineffective for subglottic edema in croup.
Dexamethasone is the cornerstone of croup treatment. A single oral dose reduces airway edema within 2-6 hours. Its long half-life means no repeat dosing is needed.
Croup is viral—antibiotics have no role unless bacterial superinfection (bacterial tracheitis) is suspected.
Despite traditional use, cool mist/humidified air has NOT been shown to improve croup outcomes in clinical trials.

questions.stem: A child with severe croup has stridor at rest, significant chest wall retractions, and appears anxious. What additional treatment should be given alongside dexamethasone?

choices.feedback: Heliox may provide temporary benefit by reducing turbulent airflow but is not first-line treatment.
Oral dexamethasone is preferred even in severe croup due to its efficacy and longer half-life. IV steroids are reserved for patients unable to tolerate oral dosing.

Nebulized epinephrine provides rapid (within minutes) temporary relief via mucosal vasoconstriction and reduced subglottic edema. Effect lasts ~2 hours, then may rebound. Patients must be observed for at least 2-4 hours after administration. Given for moderate-severe croup (stridor at rest, significant work of breathing). Dose: 0.5 mL of 2.25% racemic epinephrine or 5 mL of 1:1000 L-epinephrine.
Intubation is reserved for impending respiratory failure unresponsive to epinephrine and steroids. Less than 1% of croup cases require intubation.

questions.stem: What imaging finding on anteroposterior neck radiograph is characteristic of croup?

choices.feedback: Widened prevertebral space suggests retropharyngeal abscess.
The thumbprint sign indicates epiglottic swelling on lateral neck X-ray, seen in epiglottitis.

choices.text: Air-fluid level in the maxillary sinus
choices.feedback: This finding suggests sinusitis, not croup.

The steeple sign represents symmetric subglottic narrowing on AP view, giving the trachea a tapered, church-steeple appearance. Normal subglottic region has a shouldered appearance. Important: croup is a clinical diagnosis—imaging is NOT routinely needed. X-ray is useful when diagnosis is uncertain or to exclude foreign body or epiglottitis (thumbprint sign).

questions.stem: Which virus is the most common cause of croup?

choices.feedback: Adenovirus can cause croup but is a much less common cause than parainfluenza.

Parainfluenza accounts for ~75% of croup cases. Type 1 is most common overall; type 3 causes more severe disease. Other causes: RSV, adenovirus, influenza, human metapneumovirus. Parainfluenza has a predilection for the subglottic larynx. No vaccine is currently available for parainfluenza.
RSV more commonly causes bronchiolitis in infants than croup.
Rhinovirus causes common cold symptoms but rarely causes significant croup.

questions.explanation: Correct: Parainfluenza virus (types 1 and 3).

questions.stem: A 9-month-old infant can sit unsupported, transfer objects between hands, and says 'mama' nonspecifically. The infant does not yet have a pincer grasp. Is this developmentally appropriate?

choices.text: No—sitting unsupported should occur by 4 months
choices.feedback: Independent sitting typically develops at 6-7 months. At 4 months, infants have head control and may sit with support.

choices.text: No—pincer grasp should be present by 6 months
choices.feedback: At 6 months, infants have a raking/palmar grasp. Pincer grasp develops at 9-12 months.

choices.text: No—saying 'mama' nonspecifically is delayed for 9 months
choices.feedback: Nonspecific 'mama/dada' babbling is appropriate at 9 months. Specific use of 'mama/dada' is expected at 12 months.

choices.text: Yes—pincer grasp typically develops at 9-12 months
choices.feedback: At 9 months: gross motor—sits well, crawls, pulls to stand; fine motor—raking grasp, transfers objects (pincer grasp emerges 9-12 months); language—babbles with consonants, nonspecific 'mama/dada'; social—stranger anxiety, object permanence developing. This child is meeting milestones. Pincer grasp absence at 9 months is not a red flag.

questions.stem: At what age should a child be referred for evaluation if they are not walking independently?

choices.feedback: While many children walk at 12 months, it is normal to not walk until 15-18 months.
Waiting until 24 months delays identification of significant motor delays. Referral should occur by 18 months.

Most children walk independently by 12-15 months. The upper limit of normal is 18 months. Not walking by 18 months warrants developmental evaluation for neuromuscular disorders, CP, or global developmental delay. Red flags at any age: loss of previously acquired skills (regression), persistent toe-walking, asymmetric movement.
Independent walking is not expected at 9 months. Most infants are cruising furniture at this age.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a red flag for autism spectrum disorder at 18 months?

choices.text: Absence of pointing to show interest
choices.feedback: Key ASD red flags at 18 months: no pointing to show interest, no shared attention (joint attention), no response to name, limited eye contact, and no pretend play. Protodeclarative pointing (pointing to share, not just to request) is one of the most sensitive early markers. M-CHAT-R screening is recommended at 18 and 24 months.

choices.text: Not yet combining two words into phrases
choices.feedback: Two-word phrases typically emerge at 18-24 months. Absence at 18 months alone is not an ASD-specific red flag.

choices.text: Stranger anxiety when approaching new adults
choices.feedback: Stranger anxiety is a normal developmental phenomenon (peaks 9-12 months) and is not an ASD indicator.

choices.text: Preferring to play alongside rather than with peers
choices.feedback: Parallel play is developmentally normal until age 3-4. Cooperative play emerges later.

questions.explanation: Correct: Absence of pointing to show interest (protodeclarative pointing).

questions.stem: A 4-year-old child can hop on one foot, draw a square, count to 10, and engage in cooperative play. Which milestone would you NOT expect at this age?

choices.text: Engaging in cooperative play with peers
choices.feedback: Cooperative play emerges at 4-5 years, preceded by parallel play (2-3 years).
Hopping on one foot is a 4-year milestone.
Copying a square is a 4-year fine motor milestone (circle at 3, cross at 4, triangle at 5).

Reading simple words typically emerges at 5-6 years. At age 4: gross motor—hops on one foot, throws overhand; fine motor—draws square, copies cross, uses scissors; language—4-5 word sentences, tells stories, counts to 10; social—cooperative play, imaginary friends, understands turn-taking. The described child is age-appropriate.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child has a vocabulary of 10 words and does not combine words into two-word phrases. The child follows simple one-step commands and points to desired objects. What is the most appropriate next step?

choices.feedback: At 24 months, expected vocabulary is ~50 words with 2-word combinations. Fewer than 50 words or no word combinations at 24 months is a language delay. Following commands and pointing indicate receptive language and social communication are relatively intact, but expressive delay warrants early intervention. Earlier referral leads to better outcomes through neuroplasticity.

choices.text: Order an MRI of the brain
choices.feedback: Neuroimaging is not first-line for isolated expressive language delay. Speech-language evaluation should precede imaging.
Waiting another year delays intervention during a critical period for language development.

choices.text: Reassure parents that boys often speak later
choices.feedback: While boys may have slightly later language emergence, the degree of delay described (10 words, no combinations at 24 months) warrants evaluation, not reassurance.

questions.stem: A previously healthy 18-month-old presents after a 2-minute generalized tonic-clonic seizure during a febrile illness (temp 39.5°C). The child is now alert and neurologically normal. What is the most appropriate management?

choices.feedback: Anticonvulsant prophylaxis is NOT recommended for simple febrile seizures. The recurrence risk (~30%) does not justify medication side effects.

choices.text: Obtain emergent CT scan of the head
choices.feedback: Neuroimaging is not indicated for simple febrile seizures with normal neurologic exam.

choices.text: Perform lumbar puncture to rule out meningitis
choices.feedback: LP is considered if meningitis signs are present or in children with incomplete immunization history. It is not routine for simple febrile seizures in well-appearing, fully immunized children.

choices.text: Identify and treat the source of fever; no neuroimaging, EEG, or labs required for a simple febrile seizure
choices.feedback: Simple febrile seizure criteria: age 6mo-5yr, generalized, duration <15 min, does not recur within 24 hours, normal neurologic exam. AAP guidelines: no routine labs, LP, neuroimaging, or EEG needed. Focus on identifying fever source. Parental reassurance is essential—simple febrile seizures do NOT increase epilepsy risk significantly (2% vs 1% general population).

questions.explanation: A simple febrile seizure in an otherwise healthy child aged 6 months to 5 years requires identification and treatment of the fever source but does NOT require neuroimaging, EEG, or antiepileptic medications. Simple febrile seizures are benign, generalized, last <15 minutes, and do not recur within 24 hours. The priority is finding and treating the underlying infection (otitis media, viral URI, UTI, roseola). LP should be considered if there are meningeal signs or if the child is <12 months (meningeal signs may be absent). Antipyretics do not prevent febrile seizure recurrence.
questions.verifiedExplanation: A simple febrile seizure in an otherwise healthy child aged 6 months to 5 years requires identification and treatment of the fever source but does NOT require neuroimaging, EEG, or antiepileptic medications. Simple febrile seizures are benign, generalized, last <15 minutes, and do not recur within 24 hours. The priority is finding and treating the underlying infection (otitis media, viral URI, UTI, roseola). LP should be considered if there are meningeal signs or if the child is <12 months (meningeal signs may be absent). Antipyretics do not prevent febrile seizure recurrence.

questions.stem: Which feature distinguishes a complex febrile seizure from a simple febrile seizure?

choices.text: Duration greater than 15 minutes, focal features, or recurrence within 24 hours
choices.feedback: Complex febrile seizure = any of: (1) duration >15 minutes, (2) focal onset or features, (3) recurrence within 24 hours, (4) postictal Todd's paralysis. Complex febrile seizures warrant closer evaluation (consider LP, EEG, imaging) as they carry slightly higher risk of future epilepsy (~4-12% vs ~2%).
The height of fever does not differentiate simple from complex febrile seizures.
Family history increases risk of recurrence but does not classify the seizure as complex.
Timing relative to sleep does not distinguish simple from complex febrile seizures.

questions.explanation: Complex febrile seizures are distinguished from simple febrile seizures by three features: duration >15 minutes, focal features (unilateral movements, Todd's paralysis), or recurrence within 24 hours. Any ONE of these makes the seizure 'complex.' Complex febrile seizures carry a slightly higher risk of subsequent epilepsy compared to simple febrile seizures (4-6% vs 1-2%, compared to 1% baseline population risk). Children with complex febrile seizures may warrant more extensive workup including neuroimaging and EEG, particularly if focal features are present. However, the overall prognosis remains good.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Complex febrile seizures are distinguished from simple febrile seizures by three features: duration >15 minutes, focal features (unilateral movements, Todd's paralysis), or recurrence within 24 hours. Any ONE of these makes the seizure 'complex.' Complex febrile seizures carry a slightly higher risk of subsequent epilepsy compared to simple febrile seizures (4-6% vs 1-2%, compared to 1% baseline population risk). Children with complex febrile seizures may warrant more extensive workup including neuroimaging and EEG, particularly if focal features are present. However, the overall prognosis remains good.

questions.stem: The parents of a child who had a simple febrile seizure are very anxious and ask about recurrence. What is the approximate recurrence risk?

choices.feedback: This underestimates the recurrence risk. Approximately one-third of children will have a recurrence.

choices.text: 50% but only if there is a family history of epilepsy
choices.feedback: Recurrence risk is ~30-35% regardless of family history of epilepsy (though family history of febrile seizures is a risk factor).
This significantly overestimates the recurrence risk.
