~30-35% of children will have at least one recurrence. Risk factors for recurrence: young age at first seizure (<18 months), lower temperature at seizure onset, shorter duration of fever before seizure, family history of febrile seizures, and frequent febrile illnesses. Despite recurrence risk, prophylactic anticonvulsants are NOT recommended.

questions.explanation: The recurrence risk after a first simple febrile seizure is approximately 30-35% overall. Risk factors for recurrence include: younger age at first seizure (<18 months), lower temperature at time of seizure, shorter duration of fever before seizure, family history of febrile seizures, and seizure occurring in the first hour of illness. Children with all risk factors present may have recurrence rates exceeding 70%, while those with none may have rates below 20%. Despite the recurrence risk, daily prophylactic antiepileptic drugs are not recommended due to their side effect profile and the benign nature of simple febrile seizures.
questions.verifiedExplanation: The recurrence risk after a first simple febrile seizure is approximately 30-35% overall. Risk factors for recurrence include: younger age at first seizure (<18 months), lower temperature at time of seizure, shorter duration of fever before seizure, family history of febrile seizures, and seizure occurring in the first hour of illness. Children with all risk factors present may have recurrence rates exceeding 70%, while those with none may have rates below 20%. Despite the recurrence risk, daily prophylactic antiepileptic drugs are not recommended due to their side effect profile and the benign nature of simple febrile seizures.

questions.stem: What is the most important risk factor for developing epilepsy after febrile seizures?

choices.feedback: Male sex is a minor risk factor for febrile seizures overall but not a strong predictor of epilepsy.

choices.text: Febrile seizure occurring during a viral illness vs. bacterial
choices.feedback: The type of febrile illness does not determine epilepsy risk.
Temperature height does not predict epilepsy development.

choices.text: Complex febrile seizures with multiple recurrences
choices.feedback: Risk of epilepsy: simple febrile seizure ~2%, complex febrile seizure ~4-12%, status febriliticus ~30-40%. Other risk factors: neurodevelopmental abnormalities, family history of epilepsy, and recurrent complex febrile seizures. Most children with simple febrile seizures have excellent long-term outcomes with no effect on IQ or behavior.

questions.explanation: Complex febrile seizures with multiple recurrences are the most important risk factor for developing epilepsy after febrile seizures. Other risk factors include family history of epilepsy (not just febrile seizures), neurodevelopmental abnormalities prior to the febrile seizure, and short duration of fever before seizure onset. The baseline risk of epilepsy after a simple febrile seizure is only about 1-2% (similar to the general population risk of ~1%). With multiple risk factors, the risk rises to 4-6%. Simple febrile seizures alone do not significantly increase epilepsy risk, which is why they are considered a benign condition.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Complex febrile seizures with multiple recurrences are the most important risk factor for developing epilepsy after febrile seizures. Other risk factors include family history of epilepsy (not just febrile seizures), neurodevelopmental abnormalities prior to the febrile seizure, and short duration of fever before seizure onset. The baseline risk of epilepsy after a simple febrile seizure is only about 1-2% (similar to the general population risk of ~1%). With multiple risk factors, the risk rises to 4-6%. Simple febrile seizures alone do not significantly increase epilepsy risk, which is why they are considered a benign condition.

questions.stem: At what age range are febrile seizures most common?

choices.text: 6 months to 5 years, peaking at 12-18 months
choices.feedback: Febrile seizures affect 2-5% of children ages 6 months to 5 years. Peak incidence is 12-18 months. They occur in the setting of rapid temperature rise rather than absolute temperature height. HHV-6 (roseola) and influenza are common triggers. By definition, febrile seizures do not occur before 6 months or after 5 years—seizures with fever outside this range require different evaluation.
While febrile seizures can occur up to age 5, the peak is at 12-18 months, not 3-7 years.
Seizures with fever in neonates are NOT febrile seizures and require urgent evaluation for meningitis/sepsis.
Febrile seizures by definition occur before age 5. Seizures with fever after age 5 warrant epilepsy evaluation.

questions.explanation: Febrile seizures are most common between 6 months and 5 years of age, with peak incidence at 12-18 months. They occur in approximately 2-5% of children in this age range. Febrile seizures outside this window should prompt consideration of alternative diagnoses. Before 6 months, serious infections (meningitis, encephalitis) must be strongly considered. After 5 years, afebrile seizure disorders become more likely. The developing brain's increased susceptibility to seizures during this age window reflects the immaturity of inhibitory (GABAergic) neuronal networks relative to excitatory networks.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Febrile seizures are most common between 6 months and 5 years of age, with peak incidence at 12-18 months. They occur in approximately 2-5% of children in this age range. Febrile seizures outside this window should prompt consideration of alternative diagnoses. Before 6 months, serious infections (meningitis, encephalitis) must be strongly considered. After 5 years, afebrile seizure disorders become more likely. The developing brain's increased susceptibility to seizures during this age window reflects the immaturity of inhibitory (GABAergic) neuronal networks relative to excitatory networks.

questions.stem: A 9-month-old infant presents with episodic crying, drawing knees to chest, and currant-jelly stool. Between episodes, the child appears lethargic. Abdominal examination reveals a sausage-shaped mass in the right upper quadrant. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Volvulus presents with bilious vomiting and acute abdomen, typically in neonates.
Pyloric stenosis presents with nonbilious projectile vomiting at 3-6 weeks of age, not bloody stools.
Meckel's causes painless rectal bleeding, not episodic colic with mass.

Classic triad: colicky abdominal pain (episodic crying with leg flexion), currant-jelly stools (blood + mucus from mucosal ischemia), and palpable sausage-shaped mass (typically RUQ or epigastric). Peak age: 5-12 months. Most common type: ileocolic. Lead point usually idiopathic (Peyer's patch hypertrophy from viral illness) in children <2 years.

questions.stem: What is the first-line treatment for ileocolic intussusception in a hemodynamically stable infant without peritonitis?

choices.feedback: Bowel prep has no role in intussusception management.

choices.text: Air or hydrostatic enema reduction under fluoroscopic or ultrasound guidance
choices.feedback: Pneumatic (air) or hydrostatic (saline) enema reduction is first-line, with ~80-90% success rate. Air enema is preferred at many centers (higher success rate, lower perforation risk vs barium). Contraindications: peritonitis, perforation, hemodynamic instability. If enema fails or there are contraindications, surgical reduction is required.
Surgery is reserved for enema failure, perforation, peritonitis, or hemodynamic instability.
Observation alone risks progression to bowel ischemia and perforation. Reduction must be attempted.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old child presents with intussusception. Given the atypical age, what should be evaluated?

choices.text: Pathologic lead point such as Meckel diverticulum, lymphoma, or polyp
choices.feedback: In children >2 years, intussusception is more likely to have a pathologic lead point (PLP): Meckel diverticulum, lymphoma (especially Burkitt's), polyps, Henoch-Schönlein purpura (IgA vasculitis), or duplication cysts. PLP prevalence increases with age. After reduction, CT or surgical evaluation may be needed to identify the lead point.
While early rotavirus vaccines (RotaShield) had intussusception risk, this is relevant for infants, not 4-year-olds.
Celiac disease does not cause intussusception through lead points.
Appendicitis causes RLQ pain with peritoneal signs, not intussusception.

questions.stem: What is the most useful initial imaging study when intussusception is suspected?

choices.feedback: Plain X-ray may show a soft tissue mass or paucity of bowel gas in the RLQ but has low sensitivity (~50%). It is less accurate than ultrasound.
Barium enema is both diagnostic and therapeutic but ultrasound is preferred for initial diagnosis to avoid unnecessary intervention.
CT can diagnose intussusception but involves radiation and is typically not needed when ultrasound is available.

Ultrasound is the preferred initial study: the 'target sign' (or 'doughnut sign') on transverse view shows concentric rings of bowel wall, with near 100% sensitivity and specificity. It avoids radiation exposure. The 'pseudokidney sign' on longitudinal view shows the intussusceptum within the intussuscipiens.

questions.stem: After successful enema reduction of intussusception, what is the recurrence rate, and when does it typically occur?

choices.feedback: This significantly overestimates the recurrence rate.
This underestimates the recurrence rate. ~5-10% of children will have recurrence.
Recurrence is ~10% and typically occurs within 72 hours, not weeks later.

Recurrence rate after enema reduction is ~5-10%, with most occurring within 24-72 hours. Recurrent intussusception can usually be managed with repeat enema reduction. After 3+ recurrences, surgical evaluation is warranted to rule out a pathologic lead point. Parents should be educated on warning signs for recurrence.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old child presents with 6 days of high fever, bilateral nonexudative conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue, cervical lymphadenopathy (>1.5 cm), edema of hands and feet, and polymorphous rash. What is the diagnosis and most critical complication to prevent?

choices.feedback: SJS presents with targetoid lesions and mucosal erosions, not the constellation of Kawasaki criteria.

Kawasaki disease is a systemic vasculitis affecting medium-sized arteries, especially coronary arteries. Diagnosis requires fever ≥5 days + 4/5 criteria: (1) bilateral conjunctival injection (nonexudative), (2) oral changes (strawberry tongue, lip fissuring), (3) cervical lymphadenopathy >1.5cm (usually unilateral), (4) extremity changes (edema, erythema, desquamation), (5) polymorphous rash. Coronary aneurysms develop in 25% of untreated cases.
Measles has cough, coryza, Koplik spots, and starts with maculopapular rash that spreads cephalocaudally.
Scarlet fever has exudative pharyngitis and sandpaper-like rash with Pastia lines. Kawasaki does not have pharyngeal exudate.

questions.stem: What is the first-line treatment for Kawasaki disease and when should it ideally be administered?

choices.feedback: Infliximab may be used for IVIG-resistant Kawasaki but is not first-line treatment.
Corticosteroids may be added for IVIG-resistant cases but are not first-line monotherapy.

choices.text: IVIG single infusion plus high-dose aspirin
choices.feedback: IVIG + aspirin is the standard treatment. IVIG reduces coronary aneurysm risk significantly. Aspirin is given at anti-inflammatory doses during the acute phase, then at antiplatelet doses for 6-8 weeks.
Kawasaki is not an infectious process requiring antibiotics.

questions.explanation: Key detail: dose aspirin, ideally within 10 days of fever onset.

questions.stem: A child with suspected Kawasaki disease has had 6 days of fever but only meets 2 of the 5 clinical criteria. CRP is elevated and echocardiogram shows coronary artery dilation. What is the appropriate classification and action?

choices.text: Incomplete Kawasaki disease; treat with IVIG and aspirin
choices.feedback: Incomplete/atypical Kawasaki disease: fever ≥5 days + 2-3 criteria + laboratory findings (elevated CRP/ESR, anemia, elevated ALT, pyuria, thrombocytosis after day 7). Echocardiographic findings of coronary involvement confirm the diagnosis. Incomplete KD is more common in infants <6 months and older children—the populations at highest risk for coronary complications. Treatment is the same as classic KD.
sJIA can mimic Kawasaki but does not cause coronary artery dilation.

choices.text: Defer treatment until all 5 criteria are met
choices.feedback: Waiting for full criteria risks missing the treatment window. Incomplete KD with coronary findings warrants treatment.

choices.text: Not Kawasaki disease; viral exanthem requiring supportive care only
choices.feedback: Coronary artery abnormalities with fever and some criteria strongly suggest incomplete Kawasaki. Failing to treat risks coronary aneurysms.

questions.stem: After treatment for Kawasaki disease, when should follow-up echocardiography be performed?

choices.text: No follow-up echo if initial IVIG was given within 10 days
choices.feedback: Even with timely treatment, 3-5% develop coronary abnormalities. Follow-up imaging is essential.
Daily inpatient echos are unnecessary. The standard follow-up schedule is at 2 weeks and 6-8 weeks.
A single echo at 1 year would miss early coronary changes that develop within weeks.

choices.text: At 2 weeks, 6-8 weeks, and potentially longer if abnormalities are detected
choices.feedback: Echocardiographic follow-up: baseline, 2 weeks, and 6-8 weeks after treatment. Most coronary changes appear within 6 weeks. If normal throughout, long-term cardiac risk is low. If aneurysms are present, follow-up extends with stress testing, CT angiography, and potentially lifelong anticoagulation for giant aneurysms (>8 mm).

questions.stem: Why is Kawasaki disease particularly dangerous in infants younger than 6 months?

choices.text: Higher rate of incomplete presentation leading to delayed diagnosis and increased coronary complications
choices.feedback: Infants <6 months often present atypically/incompletely, leading to diagnostic delays. They have the highest rate of coronary artery aneurysms, partly because they are less likely to meet full diagnostic criteria and treatment is delayed. Clinicians must have a high index of suspicion for KD in any infant with prolonged fever even without classic findings.

choices.text: Higher likelihood of aspirin causing Reye syndrome in this age group
choices.feedback: While Reye syndrome is a concern with aspirin in children, the benefit of aspirin in KD outweighs this risk. Reye syndrome is more associated with varicella/influenza.

choices.text: Increased risk of IVIG anaphylaxis in young infants
choices.feedback: IVIG anaphylaxis risk is not age-specific to infants.

choices.text: Greater viral load causing more severe vasculitis
choices.feedback: Kawasaki is not caused by a virus (etiology unknown), so viral load is not a factor.

questions.stem: A 4-week-old firstborn male presents with progressive, nonbilious, projectile vomiting after feeds. Physical examination reveals a palpable olive-shaped mass in the epigastrium. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: GERD causes effortless regurgitation, not forceful projectile vomiting with a palpable mass.
Volvulus presents with BILIOUS vomiting and acute abdomen. Pyloric stenosis produces nonbilious emesis because the obstruction is proximal to the ampulla of Vater.

Classic presentation: 3-6 week old, firstborn male, nonbilious projectile vomiting, hungry immediately after vomiting ('hungry vomiter'), palpable pyloric olive in the epigastrium/RUQ, visible gastric peristalsis. Risk factors: male sex (4:1), firstborn, macrolide exposure (erythromycin). Pathology: hypertrophy and hyperplasia of pyloric circular muscle.
Duodenal atresia presents in the first days of life with bilious vomiting and 'double bubble' sign on X-ray.

questions.stem: What electrolyte abnormality is classically associated with hypertrophic pyloric stenosis?

choices.feedback: Hyperkalemia with acidosis occurs in renal failure or adrenal insufficiency, not pyloric stenosis.
This occurs with diarrhea (loss of bicarbonate), not vomiting.

Persistent vomiting of gastric acid (HCl) → loss of H+ and Cl- → metabolic alkalosis. Kidneys compensate by excreting HCO3- but retain Na+ at the expense of K+ and H+. Result: hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis with paradoxical aciduria (kidneys excrete H+ to retain Na+/K+). Electrolyte correction is essential BEFORE surgery.
Respiratory alkalosis is caused by hyperventilation, not GI losses.

questions.stem: What is the imaging study of choice to confirm hypertrophic pyloric stenosis?

choices.feedback: Endoscopy is not used to diagnose pyloric stenosis in infants.

choices.text: Abdominal ultrasound showing pyloric muscle thickness >3 mm and channel length >15 mm
choices.feedback: Ultrasound criteria: pyloric muscle thickness >3 mm (single wall) and pyloric channel length >15-17 mm. Ultrasound is 95-100% sensitive and specific. The 'target sign' on transverse view shows the thickened hypoechoic muscle surrounding the echogenic mucosa. Upper GI series (string sign) is an alternative if US is equivocal.
Plain X-ray may show gastric distension but cannot directly visualize pyloric muscle thickness.
CT is unnecessary radiation exposure for a diagnosis easily confirmed by ultrasound.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Abdominal ultrasound showing pyloric muscle thickness >3 mm and channel length >15 mm.

questions.stem: What is the definitive treatment for hypertrophic pyloric stenosis?

choices.feedback: Atropine has been studied as a nonsurgical alternative in some settings but pyloromyotomy remains the standard of care.

Ramstedt pyloromyotomy involves incising the hypertrophied pyloric muscle longitudinally down to (but not through) the submucosa. It is curative. Critical: surgery is NOT an emergency—first correct dehydration and electrolyte abnormalities (especially K+ and Cl-). Feeds can typically resume within 4-6 hours postoperatively.

choices.text: Emergent surgery regardless of electrolyte status
choices.feedback: Pyloric stenosis is NOT a surgical emergency. Electrolyte correction prevents perioperative complications (arrhythmias from hypokalemia, post-intubation apnea from alkalosis).
Endoscopic balloon dilation is not the standard treatment for infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis.

questions.stem: A mother asks whether the macrolide antibiotic she was given postpartum could have caused her infant's pyloric stenosis. What is the evidence?

choices.text: Only azithromycin is associated, not erythromycin
choices.feedback: Erythromycin has the strongest association. Azithromycin may also carry risk but the data is less robust.

choices.text: The association only applies to maternal prenatal exposure, not infant exposure
choices.feedback: Direct infant exposure in the first 2 weeks carries the highest risk, though maternal exposure may also contribute.

choices.text: Erythromycin exposure in the first 2 weeks of life is associated with increased risk of pyloric stenosis
choices.feedback: Epidemiologic studies show a 2-3 fold increased risk of pyloric stenosis with erythromycin given in the first 2 weeks of life (strongest association). Azithromycin has a smaller but possible association. Maternal macrolide use during late pregnancy or breastfeeding may also carry some risk. The mechanism is thought to involve erythromycin's prokinetic (motilin agonist) effect stimulating pyloric muscle hypertrophy.

choices.text: No association exists between macrolides and pyloric stenosis
choices.feedback: Multiple studies have demonstrated an association, particularly with early neonatal erythromycin exposure.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a 40 pack-year smoking history presents with painless gross hematuria. Cystoscopy reveals a papillary lesion on the lateral bladder wall. Biopsy shows high-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma confined to the lamina propria. What is the stage and initial treatment?

choices.feedback: T2 involves muscularis propria invasion. This lesion is T1 (lamina propria only), which is managed initially with TURBT + BCG.

choices.text: Stage T3; neoadjuvant chemotherapy then cystectomy
choices.feedback: T3 extends through the bladder wall into perivesical tissue. This lesion is confined to lamina propria (T1).

choices.text: Stage T1 (lamina propria invasion); transurethral resection of bladder tumor (TURBT) followed by intravesical BCG
choices.feedback: T1 high-grade urothelial carcinoma invades lamina propria but NOT muscularis propria. Treatment: TURBT for resection + restaging TURBT at 2-6 weeks (high risk of residual disease) + intravesical BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guérin) immunotherapy. BCG is superior to intravesical chemotherapy for high-grade non-muscle-invasive disease. BCG activates immune response against residual tumor cells.

choices.text: Stage Ta; TURBT alone with surveillance
choices.feedback: Ta is confined to the mucosa (no lamina propria invasion). This biopsy shows lamina propria involvement = T1.

questions.stem: What is the most significant modifiable risk factor for urothelial carcinoma of the bladder?

choices.feedback: Smoking is the #1 risk factor, responsible for ~50% of bladder cancers. Aromatic amines and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons are excreted renally and damage urothelium. Risk increases with intensity and duration. Other risk factors: occupational chemical exposure (aromatic amines in dye/rubber/leather industries), cyclophosphamide, chronic Schistosoma haematobium infection (squamous cell carcinoma).
Diet is not a major risk factor for bladder cancer.
Alcohol is not a significant risk factor for bladder cancer.
Chronic UTIs may increase squamous cell carcinoma risk but smoking is the dominant modifiable risk factor for urothelial carcinoma.

questions.stem: A patient with BCG-unresponsive non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer (T1 high-grade after adequate BCG) refuses radical cystectomy. What is an established treatment alternative?

choices.feedback: Pembrolizumab is used for BCG-unresponsive high-risk NMIBC in patients who are ineligible for or decline cystectomy. It represents a bladder-sparing option. Response rate ~40%. Other options include intravesical therapies (gemcitabine/docetaxel, nadofaragene firadenovec gene therapy). Radical cystectomy remains the gold standard for BCG failures.
If the patient is BCG-unresponsive (failed adequate BCG with maintenance), further BCG is unlikely to help.
Radiation alone is not standard for NMIBC. Trimodal therapy (TURBT + chemoradiation) is a bladder-sparing option for muscle-invasive disease.
Oral methotrexate is not an approved treatment for BCG-unresponsive bladder cancer.

questions.explanation: Correct: Pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1 immunotherapy).

questions.stem: What percentage of newly diagnosed bladder cancers are non-muscle-invasive (stages Ta, T1, CIS)?

choices.feedback: The proportion is closer to 75% non-muscle-invasive.
Most bladder cancers are caught at a non-muscle-invasive stage. Only ~25% are muscle-invasive at diagnosis.

~75% of bladder cancers present as NMIBC (Ta ~50%, T1 ~20%, CIS ~5%). The remaining ~25% are muscle-invasive (≥T2) at diagnosis. NMIBC has high recurrence rates (50-70%) and progression risk to muscle-invasive disease (10-30% depending on grade). This is why surveillance cystoscopy is essential.
While most are NMIBC, ~25% present as muscle-invasive, so >90% is an overestimate.

questions.stem: Which histologic type of bladder cancer is most strongly associated with chronic Schistosoma haematobium infection?

choices.feedback: Bladder adenocarcinoma is associated with urachal remnants and bladder exstrophy.

Chronic S. haematobium infection causes granulomatous inflammation → squamous metaplasia → squamous cell carcinoma. This is the predominant bladder cancer type in endemic regions (Egypt, sub-Saharan Africa). In Western countries, >90% of bladder cancers are urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma. Other SCC risk factors: chronic indwelling catheters, bladder stones.
Urothelial carcinoma is the most common type in Western countries but is not the type associated with schistosomiasis.
Small cell carcinoma of the bladder is rare and is not associated with schistosomiasis.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man presents with urinary frequency, nocturia, weak stream, and hesitancy. Digital rectal examination reveals a uniformly enlarged, smooth, nontender prostate. PSA is 2.1 ng/mL. What is the first-line pharmacologic treatment?

choices.feedback: BPH is not an infectious process. Antibiotics have no role unless concurrent UTI.

Alpha-1 blockers (tamsulosin, alfuzosin, silodosin) relax prostatic smooth muscle and bladder neck, providing rapid symptomatic relief (within days). They are first-line for moderate-severe LUTS/BPH. Side effects: orthostatic hypotension, retrograde ejaculation, intraoperative floppy iris syndrome. For large prostates (>30-40g), adding a 5-alpha reductase inhibitor (finasteride/dutasteride) reduces prostate volume over months.
Anticholinergics treat overactive bladder (urgency/frequency) but can worsen urinary retention in BPH.
5-alpha reductase inhibitors work slowly (3-6 months for full effect) and are most beneficial for larger prostates (>30-40g). Alpha-blockers provide faster symptom relief.

questions.stem: A patient on tamsulosin for BPH develops acute urinary retention. After catheter decompression, what is the most appropriate definitive surgical treatment?

choices.feedback: Radical prostatectomy removes the entire prostate and is indicated for prostate CANCER, not BPH.

choices.text: Suprapubic catheter placement as permanent management
choices.feedback: Long-term catheterization is associated with infection and complications. Definitive surgical correction is preferred.
Orchiectomy eliminates testosterone and is used for metastatic prostate cancer, not BPH.

TURP is the gold standard surgical treatment for BPH when medical therapy fails or complications develop (retention, recurrent UTIs, bladder stones, hematuria, renal insufficiency). It resects obstructing prostatic tissue transurethrally. Complications: retrograde ejaculation (~65%), bleeding, TUR syndrome (dilutional hyponatremia from glycine irrigation—use saline bipolar to avoid). Alternatives: HoLEP, UroLift, Rezūm.

questions.explanation: Correct: Transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP).

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of finasteride in treating BPH?

choices.text: Blocks androgen receptors on prostatic cells
choices.feedback: Androgen receptor antagonists (e.g., flutamide, bicalutamide) are used for prostate cancer, not BPH.

choices.text: Relaxes prostatic smooth muscle via alpha receptor blockade
choices.feedback: This describes alpha-blockers (tamsulosin), not finasteride.

choices.text: Inhibits 5-alpha reductase, preventing conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone (DHT)
choices.feedback: 5-alpha reductase converts testosterone to DHT, which drives prostate growth. Finasteride (type II inhibitor) and dutasteride (types I and II) reduce prostate volume by 20-30% over 6-12 months. Side effects: decreased libido, erectile dysfunction, decreased ejaculate volume. Important: reduces PSA by ~50%, so double the measured PSA when screening for cancer.

choices.text: Inhibits GnRH release from the hypothalamus
choices.feedback: GnRH agonists/antagonists are used for prostate cancer (chemical castration), not BPH.

questions.stem: Which validated questionnaire is used to assess the severity of lower urinary tract symptoms in BPH?

choices.feedback: ECOG measures overall functional status in oncology patients, not urinary symptoms.

The IPSS (also called AUA Symptom Score) is a 7-question validated tool scoring 0-35: mild (0-7), moderate (8-19), severe (20-35). Questions address incomplete emptying, frequency, intermittency, urgency, weak stream, straining, and nocturia. It guides treatment decisions and monitors response. Includes a quality-of-life question.
NIH-CPSI assesses chronic prostatitis/pelvic pain symptoms, not BPH.
Gleason score grades prostate cancer histopathology, not BPH symptoms.

questions.explanation: Correct: International Prostate Symptom Score (IPSS).

questions.stem: Which zone of the prostate is most commonly involved in BPH?

choices.feedback: BPH arises from the transition zone surrounding the proximal urethra. As it enlarges, it compresses the urethra causing obstructive symptoms. In contrast, prostate cancer most commonly arises from the peripheral zone (detected by DRE). Central zone surrounds the ejaculatory ducts. This anatomic distinction explains why BPH causes early urinary symptoms while peripheral zone cancers may be asymptomatic until advanced.
The peripheral zone is where ~70% of prostate cancers arise, not BPH.
This is a non-glandular area and not the site of BPH development.
The central zone surrounds the ejaculatory ducts and is uncommonly the site of BPH or cancer.

questions.explanation: Correct: Transition zone (periurethral).

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man has a PSA of 8.5 ng/mL and an abnormal DRE with a firm nodule. TRUS-guided biopsy shows Gleason 3+4=7 adenocarcinoma in 3 of 12 cores. MRI shows no extracapsular extension. What is the clinical stage?

choices.feedback: MRI shows no extracapsular extension, ruling out T3.
T1c means the cancer was not detected on DRE. This patient has a palpable nodule.

Clinical staging: T1 = not palpable (found on biopsy for elevated PSA); T2a = palpable in half of one lobe; T2b = more than half of one lobe; T2c = both lobes; T3 = extracapsular extension; T4 = invades adjacent structures. A firm nodule on DRE = at least T2. MRI showing no extracapsular extension supports organ-confined disease. Gleason 3+4=7 is Grade Group 2 (intermediate risk).
T4 involves invasion of bladder, rectum, or pelvic wall, which is not present here.

questions.stem: For localized intermediate-risk prostate cancer (Gleason 3+4=7), what are the standard treatment options with similar long-term oncologic outcomes?

choices.feedback: ADT alone is not curative for localized disease and is used as adjuvant therapy with radiation or for advanced/metastatic disease.

choices.text: Radical prostatectomy or definitive radiation therapy (external beam ± brachytherapy)
choices.feedback: For localized intermediate-risk prostate cancer, both radical prostatectomy and radiation therapy provide excellent long-term disease control with similar 10-15 year outcomes. Choice depends on patient preference, age, comorbidities, and side effect profile. Surgery: risk of urinary incontinence and ED. Radiation: risk of bowel symptoms, urinary irritation, and late ED. Active surveillance is primarily for low-risk (Gleason 3+3=6) disease.
Active surveillance is standard for very-low and low-risk disease (Gleason 3+3=6, Grade Group 1). Intermediate-risk disease generally warrants definitive treatment.
Chemotherapy is used for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, not localized disease.

questions.stem: What is the most common site of metastatic spread in prostate cancer?

choices.feedback: Pulmonary metastases can occur but bone is the predominant site.

Prostate cancer characteristically produces osteoBLASTIC (bone-forming) metastases, in contrast to most other cancers which cause osteolytic lesions. Most common sites: axial skeleton (spine, pelvis, ribs). Detected by bone scan (technetium-99m). Elevated alkaline phosphatase suggests bony involvement. Other metastatic sites: lymph nodes, liver, lungs.
While liver metastases occur, bone is by far the most common site.
Brain metastases from prostate cancer are uncommon.

questions.stem: What does a rising PSA after radical prostatectomy (biochemical recurrence) indicate?

choices.feedback: BPH cannot recur after complete prostate removal.
The prostate does not regenerate after complete removal. Any PSA rise indicates cancer recurrence.
While one value should be confirmed, persistent PSA rise after prostatectomy is not a lab error—it indicates recurrence.

choices.text: Residual or recurrent prostate cancer, since all PSA
choices.feedback: After radical prostatectomy, PSA should be undetectable (<0.2 ng/mL). Rising PSA = biochemical recurrence, indicating residual local disease or micrometastatic disease. Defined as PSA ≥0.2 ng/mL on two consecutive measurements. Rapid PSA doubling time (<3 months) suggests systemic disease; slower rise suggests local recurrence amenable to salvage radiation. PSMA-PET scan localizes recurrence.

questions.explanation: Key detail: producing tissue should have been removed.

questions.stem: Which Gleason Grade Group is associated with the best prognosis?

choices.feedback: GG3 is intermediate-unfavorable risk with worse prognosis than GG1 or GG2.
GG5 represents the most aggressive prostate cancer histology.

ISUP Grade Groups simplify Gleason scoring: GG1 (3+3=6), GG2 (3+4=7), GG3 (4+3=7), GG4 (4+4=8, 3+5=8, 5+3=8), GG5 (4+5=9, 5+4=9, 5+5=10). GG1 has excellent prognosis with near-zero cancer-specific mortality at 10 years. Most GG1 cancers are managed with active surveillance. The primary/secondary Gleason patterns matter: 3+4 has better prognosis than 4+3 (more aggressive primary pattern).

choices.text: All Gleason scores have similar prognosis
choices.feedback: Gleason/Grade Group is one of the strongest prognostic factors in prostate cancer.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents with a painless, firm testicular mass that does not transilluminate. Ultrasound confirms a solid intratesticular lesion. What is the initial management?

choices.text: MRI of the testes before any intervention
choices.feedback: Ultrasound is the primary imaging. MRI may help in equivocal cases but should not delay orchiectomy for a suspicious solid mass.
Transscrotal biopsy/orchiectomy is CONTRAINDICATED—it can seed tumor into scrotal lymphatics, changing the metastatic pattern.
A solid intratesticular mass in a young man is cancer until proven otherwise. Antibiotics are appropriate for suspected epididymitis/orchitis, not solid masses.

Radical inguinal orchiectomy (NOT transscrotal biopsy) is the diagnostic and initial therapeutic procedure for suspected testicular cancer. The inguinal approach maintains the spermatic cord for pathologic evaluation and avoids violating scrotal planes (prevents local recurrence). Pre-operative serum tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH) and chest/abdomen/pelvis CT are essential for staging.

questions.stem: Which tumor marker pattern (elevated AFP, normal beta-hCG) is most consistent with which testicular germ cell tumor type?

choices.feedback: Pure seminomas may mildly elevate beta-hCG (~10% of cases) but NEVER elevate AFP.

Tumor markers by type: AFP elevated → yolk sac tumor (also mixed GCTs); beta-hCG elevated → choriocarcinoma (also some seminomas); LDH → tumor burden marker. Pure SEMINOMAS NEVER elevate AFP—if AFP is elevated, a nonseminomatous component is present regardless of histology. This distinction is critical because treatment protocols differ for seminoma vs. NSGCT.
Choriocarcinoma characteristically elevates beta-hCG dramatically, not AFP.
Mature teratomas typically do not significantly elevate AFP or beta-hCG.

questions.explanation: Correct: Yolk sac tumor (endodermal sinus tumor).

questions.stem: What is the most significant risk factor for testicular cancer?

choices.feedback: Trauma does not cause testicular cancer, though it may prompt examination that discovers an existing mass.
STIs cause epididymitis/orchitis but are not risk factors for testicular cancer.

Cryptorchidism increases testicular cancer risk 4-8x, even after orchiopexy. Risk applies to BOTH testes (not just the undescended one), suggesting a shared underlying abnormality. Orchiopexy before puberty may reduce risk and allows easier surveillance. Other risk factors: prior testicular cancer, family history, testicular dysgenesis syndrome, Klinefelter syndrome.
Vasectomy has not been shown to increase testicular cancer risk.

questions.explanation: Correct: Cryptorchidism (undescended testis).

questions.stem: Stage I seminoma after radical orchiectomy has an excellent prognosis. What are the management options?

choices.feedback: BEP (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin) is reserved for advanced (stage II-III) or relapsed disease. It is overtreatment for stage I seminoma.

choices.text: Surveillance, single-agent carboplatin, or adjuvant radiation—all with >95% cure rate
choices.feedback: Stage I seminoma cure rate is >99%. Options: (1) active surveillance (preferred for compliant patients—relapse rate ~15-20%, salvageable), (2) single-dose carboplatin (reduces relapse to ~5%), (3) adjuvant radiation to para-aortic nodes (largely replaced by carboplatin). Surveillance is increasingly favored to avoid treatment toxicity given the excellent salvage rates.
RPLND is primarily used for nonseminomatous tumors, not stage I seminoma.

choices.text: No further treatment or follow-up needed
choices.feedback: Even with excellent prognosis, surveillance with CT/markers is essential to detect the ~15-20% who relapse.

questions.stem: What is the peak age incidence for testicular germ cell tumors?

choices.feedback: This age range is more typical for BPH and prostate cancer, not testicular cancer.
Testicular cancer is uncommon in elderly men. Testicular lymphoma is the most common testicular malignancy in men >60.

Testicular cancer is the most common solid malignancy in men aged 15-35. Seminomas peak slightly later (30-40 years) than NSGCTs (20-30 years). It is one of the most curable cancers when detected early. Monthly testicular self-examination is recommended for high-risk individuals. Incidence has been increasing over recent decades in Western countries.
While yolk sac tumors occur in young children, the overall peak incidence of testicular GCTs is in young adults.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old boy presents to the ED with acute onset severe left scrotal pain, nausea, and vomiting. The left testis is high-riding with a horizontal lie and an absent cremasteric reflex. What is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Ice, elevation, and analgesics with follow-up in 24 hours
choices.feedback: Delay risks permanent testicular loss. Torsion requires emergent surgical intervention.
Epididymitis is more common in sexually active adults. In adolescents with acute scrotum, torsion must be excluded first.

choices.text: Emergent surgical exploration—do NOT delay for imaging
choices.feedback: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency. The classic findings (acute scrotal pain, high-riding testis, horizontal lie, absent cremasteric reflex) have high clinical specificity. Testicular salvage rate: >90% if detorsion within 6 hours, ~50% at 12 hours, <10% after 24 hours. Do NOT delay surgery for ultrasound when clinical suspicion is high. Manual detorsion can be attempted ('open the book' = lateral rotation) while awaiting OR.
Ultrasound is useful when the diagnosis is uncertain, but should NOT delay surgical exploration when clinical findings strongly suggest torsion.

questions.stem: During surgical exploration for testicular torsion, the affected testis appears viable after detorsion. What additional procedure should be performed?

choices.feedback: Orchiectomy is performed only if the testis is non-viable (necrotic) after detorsion. A viable testis is preserved.

After detorsion, the affected testis is fixed to the dartos fascia (orchiopexy) to prevent recurrence. The CONTRALATERAL testis must also be fixed because the bell-clapper deformity (inadequate fixation of tunica vaginalis) that predisposes to torsion is bilateral in most cases. Failure to fix the contralateral testis risks future torsion.

choices.text: No fixation needed if the testis reperfuses
choices.feedback: Without orchiopexy, torsion will likely recur. Fixation is always performed.

choices.text: Fixation of only the affected testis
choices.feedback: Both testes must be fixed. The bell-clapper deformity is typically bilateral.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy presents with acute scrotal pain and a blue dot visible through the scrotal skin on the upper pole of the testis. Cremasteric reflex is intact and the testis is in normal position. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: The 'blue dot sign' on the upper pole of the testis is pathognomonic for torsion of the appendix testis (a Müllerian duct remnant). Key distinguishing features from testicular torsion: intact cremasteric reflex, testis in normal position, tenderness localized to the upper pole. Treatment is conservative: NSAIDs, scrotal support, activity restriction. Resolves in 5-7 days. No surgery needed.
Epididymitis in prepubertal boys is uncommon and presents with posterior tenderness, not a blue dot.
Testicular torsion presents with a high-riding testis, horizontal lie, and absent cremasteric reflex—none present here.
Tumors present as painless masses, not acute pain with a blue dot sign.

questions.stem: What anatomical abnormality predisposes to testicular torsion?

choices.feedback: Retractile testes move between scrotum and inguinal canal due to a brisk cremasteric reflex but are not at increased risk for torsion.
Patent processus vaginalis predisposes to inguinal hernia and hydrocele, not testicular torsion.
Hypospadias is a urethral meatus malposition that does not predispose to torsion.

The bell-clapper deformity occurs when the tunica vaginalis attaches high on the spermatic cord rather than the posterior testis, allowing the testis to swing freely within the tunica like a clapper in a bell. This permits the testis to rotate on the spermatic cord → torsion → vascular compromise. Present in ~12% of males and is bilateral in most cases.

questions.explanation: Correct: Bell-clapper deformity (high attachment of tunica vaginalis).

questions.stem: Which clinical finding is most useful to distinguish testicular torsion from epididymitis?

choices.feedback: While torsion is more common in adolescents and epididymitis in adults, age alone is insufficient to differentiate.

choices.text: Absent cremasteric reflex in torsion vs. present in epididymitis
choices.feedback: The cremasteric reflex (stroking the inner thigh → ipsilateral testicular elevation) is absent in ~99% of testicular torsion cases and present in epididymitis. Other distinguishing features: torsion has sudden onset without urinary symptoms, high-riding testis; epididymitis has gradual onset, posterior tenderness, possible urinary symptoms, positive Prehn sign (relief with testicular elevation—though unreliable).
Pyuria suggests urinary tract infection/epididymitis but its absence does not exclude epididymitis or confirm torsion.
Both conditions are typically unilateral. Bilaterality does not distinguish them.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset severe right lower quadrant pain, nausea, and vomiting. She is afebrile. Pelvic ultrasound with Doppler shows an enlarged right ovary (8 cm) with absent arterial flow. What is the most appropriate management?

choices.feedback: Current practice favors detorsion with conservation. Even dusky-appearing ovaries often recover after detorsion.

choices.text: CT scan with IV contrast before any surgical decision
choices.feedback: CT delays definitive treatment. Ultrasound with Doppler is adequate for diagnosis. Absent flow + clinical picture = surgical emergency.

choices.text: IV antibiotics and observation for 24 hours
choices.feedback: Delay risks permanent ovarian loss. Torsion requires emergent surgical intervention.

choices.text: Emergent laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian conservation
choices.feedback: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency requiring laparoscopic detorsion. Unlike historic teaching, the ovary should be detorsed and CONSERVED even if it appears dusky/edematous—most ovaries recover function. Oophorectomy only if clearly necrotic after detorsion and observation. Risk factors: ovarian cysts/masses >5 cm (most common), OHSS, pregnancy. The right ovary torsions more commonly than left (sigmoid colon stabilizes the left).

questions.explanation: OB/GYN management requires systematic assessment of gestational age, maternal hemodynamic status, and fetal well-being. Evidence-based guidelines from ACOG guide management decisions for obstetric emergencies and gynecologic conditions.

questions.stem: A pregnant woman at 10 weeks gestation presents with sudden pelvic pain. Ultrasound shows a 6 cm corpus luteum cyst with the ovary twisted. Why is torsion more common in early pregnancy?

choices.text: Corpus luteum cyst enlargement provides a lead point and increases ovarian mobility
choices.feedback: In early pregnancy, the corpus luteum enlarges to produce progesterone, creating a mass effect that predisposes to torsion. The growing uterus also shifts pelvic anatomy. Torsion is most common in the first trimester. Management: laparoscopic detorsion (safe in pregnancy). If the corpus luteum must be removed before 10-12 weeks, exogenous progesterone supplementation is needed to prevent miscarriage.
Dehydration is not a mechanism for ovarian torsion.

choices.text: Increased estrogen causes ligamentous laxity allowing the ovary to twist
choices.feedback: While ligamentous changes occur in pregnancy, the primary risk factor is the enlarged corpus luteum acting as a lead point.

choices.text: Increased pelvic blood flow prevents venous return, causing swelling and torsion
choices.feedback: Increased blood flow does not directly cause torsion. The mass effect of the corpus luteum is the primary mechanism.

questions.explanation: OB/GYN management requires systematic assessment of gestational age, maternal hemodynamic status, and fetal well-being. Evidence-based guidelines from ACOG guide management decisions for obstetric emergencies and gynecologic conditions.

questions.stem: Which ultrasound finding is considered most specific for ovarian torsion?

choices.text: Simple anechoic cyst within the ovary
choices.feedback: Simple cysts are common and not specific for torsion.
This can occur with hemorrhagic infarction but is not the most specific finding.
Free fluid can be seen with torsion but is nonspecific (also seen with ruptured cyst, ectopic pregnancy).

choices.text: Whirlpool sign (twisted vascular pedicle on Doppler)
choices.feedback: The whirlpool sign represents the twisted ovarian pedicle seen on color Doppler and is highly specific for torsion. Other findings: enlarged ovary (>5 cm), peripheral follicles displaced to cortex ('string of pearls'), free pelvic fluid, absent or decreased arterial/venous flow. Note: presence of Doppler flow does NOT exclude torsion (intermittent torsion, dual blood supply, or early torsion may preserve some flow).

questions.explanation: Cervical cancer screening: Pap smear starting age 21, co-testing (Pap + HPV) ages 30-65. HPV 16 and 18 cause ~70% of cervical cancers. Ovarian cancer: often presents late with vague symptoms (bloating, early satiety, pelvic pain). CA-125 is elevated but not specific. Endometrial cancer: most common gynecologic malignancy, presents with postmenopausal bleeding. Risk factors: unopposed estrogen, obesity, PCOS, tamoxifen. Diagnosis: endometrial biopsy.

questions.stem: In pediatric patients, what is the most common cause of ovarian torsion?

choices.feedback: Ovarian carcinoma is extremely rare in pediatric patients.
Dermoid cysts are the most common ovarian tumor in young women and can cause torsion in adolescents/adults, but in prepubertal children, normal ovaries are the most common.

In children, ~50% of ovarian torsion occurs with a normal ovary (vs. adults where a cyst/mass is usually present). This is because the long utero-ovarian ligament in prepubertal girls allows greater ovarian mobility. Symptoms may mimic appendicitis. High index of suspicion is needed for any girl with acute lower abdominal/pelvic pain. Oophoropexy may be performed to prevent recurrence.
Endometriomas are uncommon in prepubertal patients.

questions.explanation: Correct: Normal ovary (no pathologic cyst or mass).

questions.stem: After laparoscopic detorsion of a torsed ovary, what follow-up is recommended?

choices.text: No follow-up needed if the ovary was detorsed successfully
choices.feedback: Follow-up imaging is important to confirm ovarian recovery and detect any underlying pathology.

choices.text: Repeat ultrasound in 6 weeks to assess ovarian viability and perfusion
choices.feedback: Follow-up imaging confirms ovarian recovery (normal size, follicular development, restored blood flow). Most conserved ovaries recover function even if they appeared compromised during surgery. If a cyst was the lead point, consider cystectomy at the same or a subsequent procedure. Patients should be counseled about recurrence risk (~10%) and to seek care promptly for future sudden pelvic pain.
Routine bilateral oophoropexy is not standard. It may be considered for recurrent torsion or in pediatric patients.

choices.text: Start oral contraceptives to suppress ovulation permanently
choices.feedback: OCPs may reduce functional cyst formation but are not routinely prescribed after torsion.

questions.explanation: OB/GYN management requires systematic assessment of gestational age, maternal hemodynamic status, and fetal well-being. Evidence-based guidelines from ACOG guide management decisions for obstetric emergencies and gynecologic conditions.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman presents with bilateral lower abdominal pain, purulent cervical discharge, and cervical motion tenderness. Temperature is 38.5°C. Which organisms are most commonly implicated?

choices.feedback: HSV causes genital ulcers, not ascending pelvic infection.
T. pallidum causes syphilis, not PID.

PID is typically polymicrobial: N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis initiate infection, then vaginal anaerobes (Bacteroides, Prevotella) and facultative organisms (E. coli, streptococci) ascend. Diagnosis is clinical: minimum criteria = lower abdominal pain + cervical motion tenderness or adnexal/uterine tenderness. Additional criteria: fever, mucopurulent discharge, elevated ESR/CRP, positive GC/CT test.
GBS is important in neonatal sepsis and pregnancy but is not a primary PID pathogen.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis (along with vaginal flora).

questions.stem: A patient with PID develops a 7 cm complex adnexal mass with internal debris. She has high fever and peritoneal signs. What is the most likely complication?

choices.feedback: TOA develops in ~15% of PID cases when infection walled off by omentum/bowel creates an abscess. Imaging: complex adnexal mass with internal echoes, septations, fluid-debris levels. Management: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin/sulbactam + doxycycline OR clindamycin + gentamicin). If not improving in 48-72 hours → percutaneous drainage or surgery. Ruptured TOA is a surgical emergency with high mortality.
Ectopic pregnancy causes adnexal mass but with positive pregnancy test and different clinical picture.
Torsion presents with sudden pain and absent Doppler flow, not complex mass with fever and peritoneal signs in the context of PID.
Endometriomas are 'chocolate cysts' associated with endometriosis, not acute PID.

questions.explanation: Correct: Tubo-ovarian abscess (TOA).

questions.stem: What is the most feared long-term complication of PID?

choices.feedback: Cervical stenosis is not a common PID complication.
Prolapse is related to pelvic floor weakness, not PID.

choices.text: Infertility due to tubal scarring and adhesions
choices.feedback: PID-related tubal factor infertility occurs in ~8% after 1 episode, ~20% after 2 episodes, and ~40% after 3+ episodes. Inflammation causes tubal mucosal damage, fimbrial agglutination, and pelvic adhesions. Other long-term complications: ectopic pregnancy (6-10x increased risk), chronic pelvic pain (18%), Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome (perihepatitis). Early treatment is critical to prevent sequelae.
PID does not significantly increase ovarian cancer risk.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Infertility due to tubal scarring and adhesions.

questions.stem: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome is a complication of PID. What does it involve?

choices.feedback: Splenic abscess is not associated with PID or Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome.

choices.text: Perihepatitis with inflammation of the liver capsule
choices.feedback: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome involves inflammation of the hepatic capsule (Glisson's capsule) and adjacent peritoneum, creating fibrous adhesions between the liver and anterior abdominal wall ('violin string' adhesions). Presents with RUQ pain that may mimic cholecystitis. Caused by transperitoneal or lymphatic spread of N. gonorrhoeae or C. trachomatis. CT may show hepatic capsule enhancement.
Asherman syndrome (intrauterine adhesions) results from endometrial trauma, not perihepatitis.

choices.text: Tubo-ovarian abscess rupture into the peritoneum
choices.feedback: TOA rupture is a separate complication of PID, not Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome.

questions.explanation: Best answer: Perihepatitis with inflammation of the liver capsule and adhesions ('violin string' adhesions).

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and distended neck veins 3 days after a stab wound to the left chest. His blood pressure drops further on inspiration. Which hemodynamic finding is most characteristic of this condition?

choices.text: Low right atrial pressure with elevated left ventricular pressure
choices.feedback: Tamponade raises right-sided pressures due to external compression. Low RA pressure would argue against tamponade and suggest hypovolemia instead.

choices.text: Equalization of diastolic pressures across all four cardiac chambers
choices.feedback: Cardiac tamponade causes compression of all chambers, leading to equalization of diastolic pressures. Right atrial, right ventricular diastolic, pulmonary artery diastolic, and PCWP converge within 5 mmHg of each other — a hallmark finding on right heart catheterization.

choices.text: Elevated left ventricular end-diastolic pressure with normal right-sided pressures
choices.feedback: This pattern suggests isolated left heart failure, not tamponade. Tamponade affects all chambers symmetrically due to external compression by pericardial fluid.

choices.text: Markedly elevated pulmonary artery pressures with right ventricular dilation
choices.feedback: This describes pulmonary hypertension with right heart failure. In tamponade, the chambers are compressed — not dilated — and PA pressures are not selectively elevated.

questions.explanation: The correct answer (Equalization of diastolic pressures across all four cardiac chambers) reflects current evidence-based cardiology guidelines. Key cardiovascular principles include systematic assessment of hemodynamic status, risk stratification using validated scores, and guideline-directed pharmacotherapy based on condition severity and patient characteristics.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer develops progressive dyspnea. Echocardiography reveals a large pericardial effusion with right atrial and right ventricular diastolic collapse. Which finding on physical exam would you most expect?

choices.text: Kussmaul sign with inspiratory rise in JVP
choices.feedback: Kussmaul sign (paradoxical rise in JVP with inspiration) is classically associated with constrictive pericarditis and restrictive cardiomyopathy, not tamponade. In tamponade, JVP typically falls with inspiration.

choices.text: Wide splitting of S2 with inspiration
choices.feedback: Wide S2 splitting relates to delayed pulmonic valve closure (RBBB, pulmonic stenosis). It is not a feature of tamponade, where heart sounds are classically distant/muffled.

choices.text: Loud pericardial friction rub on auscultation
choices.feedback: A friction rub suggests pericarditis with minimal effusion where pericardial surfaces can still rub together. A large effusion separates the layers, making the rub disappear.

Pulsus paradoxus — an exaggerated (>10 mmHg) drop in systolic BP during inspiration — occurs because the septum shifts leftward during inspiration in tamponade, reducing LV filling. This is the most sensitive bedside finding for hemodynamically significant tamponade.

questions.explanation: Cardiac tamponade presents with Beck's triad: hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and jugular venous distension. The pathophysiology involves pericardial fluid accumulation impairing diastolic filling. Pulsus paradoxus (>10 mmHg drop in systolic BP with inspiration) is a key clinical sign. Echocardiography shows diastolic collapse of the right atrium and ventricle. Treatment is emergent pericardiocentesis. Electrical alternans on ECG (alternating QRS amplitude) is specific but not sensitive.

questions.stem: A patient with Beck's triad undergoes emergency pericardiocentesis. During the procedure, 50 mL of fluid is aspirated and the systolic blood pressure rises from 70 to 110 mmHg. What explains the dramatic hemodynamic improvement with such a small volume removal?

choices.text: The aspirated fluid was compressing the coronary arteries causing ischemia
choices.feedback: While tamponade can reduce coronary perfusion, the acute hemodynamic improvement is due to restored chamber filling, not coronary decompression. Coronary flow improves as a consequence of improved hemodynamics.

choices.text: The pericardial pressure-volume curve is steep once the elastic limit is exceeded
choices.feedback: The pericardium has a J-shaped compliance curve. Once its elastic limit is reached, even small additional fluid causes massive pressure increases. Conversely, removing even 50 mL can dramatically reduce intrapericardial pressure and restore cardiac filling.

choices.text: Removal of inflammatory mediators in the pericardial fluid reduces systemic vasodilation
choices.feedback: The hemodynamic improvement is immediate and mechanical, not related to inflammatory mediators. The steep pericardial compliance curve explains why small volume changes cause large pressure changes.

choices.text: Fluid removal triggers a vagal reflex that improves cardiac contractility
choices.feedback: There is no vagal reflex mechanism that improves contractility from pericardiocentesis. The improvement is purely mechanical — reducing external compression on the heart allows chambers to fill properly.

questions.explanation: The correct answer (The pericardial pressure-volume curve is steep once the elastic limit is exceeded) reflects current evidence-based cardiology guidelines. Key cardiovascular principles include systematic assessment of hemodynamic status, risk stratification using validated scores, and guideline-directed pharmacotherapy based on condition severity and patient characteristics.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with lupus develops a pericardial effusion that accumulates slowly over 3 weeks. He is comfortable at rest with normal vital signs despite a 600 mL effusion on echo. What explains his hemodynamic stability?

choices.text: Young patients have more compliant ventricles that compensate for external compression
choices.feedback: Ventricular compliance does not compensate for external compression in tamponade. The key factor is pericardial compliance — the rate of accumulation determines how much the pericardium stretches.

choices.text: Lupus pericardial fluid is low in protein and exerts less oncotic pressure
choices.feedback: The protein content of the fluid does not determine hemodynamic impact. The critical factor is the rate of accumulation relative to pericardial compliance, not the fluid composition.

choices.text: Anti-inflammatory treatment has reduced pericardial inflammation and stiffness
choices.feedback: While treatment may help, the primary reason for stability is the slow rate of accumulation allowing pericardial adaptation. Even untreated slow effusions can be well-tolerated hemodynamically.

choices.text: Slow accumulation allows the pericardium to stretch and accommodate larger volumes
choices.feedback: The pericardium can gradually stretch with slow fluid accumulation, accommodating up to 1-2 liters before causing hemodynamic compromise. In contrast, acute accumulations of even 150-200 mL can cause tamponade because the pericardium cannot stretch quickly.

questions.explanation: Cardiac tamponade presents with Beck's triad: hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and jugular venous distension. The pathophysiology involves pericardial fluid accumulation impairing diastolic filling. Pulsus paradoxus (>10 mmHg drop in systolic BP with inspiration) is a key clinical sign. Echocardiography shows diastolic collapse of the right atrium and ventricle. Treatment is emergent pericardiocentesis. Electrical alternans on ECG (alternating QRS amplitude) is specific but not sensitive.

questions.stem: An emergency physician performs a bedside echocardiogram on a trauma patient and sees a pericardial effusion. Which echocardiographic finding best indicates hemodynamic significance and need for emergent drainage?

choices.text: Pericardial effusion measuring greater than 2 cm in depth
choices.feedback: Effusion size alone does not determine hemodynamic significance. A slowly accumulating 3 cm effusion may be well tolerated, while a rapidly developing 1 cm effusion can cause tamponade. Chamber collapse is the key finding.

choices.text: Right ventricular free wall diastolic collapse
choices.feedback: RV diastolic collapse is the most specific echocardiographic sign of hemodynamically significant tamponade. It indicates that intrapericardial pressure exceeds RV diastolic pressure, causing the thin-walled RV to indent during diastole. This finding, combined with clinical signs, warrants emergent pericardiocentesis.

choices.text: Increased left ventricular ejection fraction on visual estimate
choices.feedback: A hyperdynamic-appearing LV may occur in tamponade due to reduced preload (small, vigorously contracting ventricle), but this is not a specific sign of tamponade and should not drive management decisions.

choices.text: Presence of any pericardial fluid posterior to the left ventricle
choices.feedback: Small pericardial effusions are common and often physiologic (up to 50 mL is normal). Mere presence of fluid is not sufficient to diagnose tamponade — hemodynamic compromise must be demonstrated.

questions.explanation: Cardiac tamponade presents with Beck's triad: hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and jugular venous distension. The pathophysiology involves pericardial fluid accumulation impairing diastolic filling. Pulsus paradoxus (>10 mmHg drop in systolic BP with inspiration) is a key clinical sign. Echocardiography shows diastolic collapse of the right atrium and ventricle. Treatment is emergent pericardiocentesis. Electrical alternans on ECG (alternating QRS amplitude) is specific but not sensitive.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old IV drug user presents with fever, a new holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration, and multiple round opacities on chest X-ray. Blood cultures grow methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus. Which valve is most likely affected?

choices.feedback: IV drug use-associated endocarditis most commonly affects the tricuspid valve (>50% of cases). The murmur increases with inspiration (Carvallo sign), indicating right-sided origin. Septic pulmonary emboli from tricuspid vegetations explain the multiple round lung opacities. S. aureus is the most common organism.
Pulmonic valve endocarditis is exceedingly rare, even in IVDU. The tricuspid valve is the overwhelmingly most common right-sided valve affected.
The mitral valve is the most commonly affected in non-IVDU endocarditis, but the combination of IVDU, inspiratory murmur enhancement, and septic pulmonary emboli all point to the tricuspid valve.
Aortic valve endocarditis would cause an early diastolic murmur and systemic (not pulmonary) emboli. The inspiratory augmentation and pulmonary findings point to the right heart.

questions.explanation: Valvular heart disease assessment combines auscultation with echocardiography. Aortic stenosis: crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at right upper sternal border radiating to carotids; severe when valve area <1.0 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg. Mitral regurgitation: holosystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla. Intervention thresholds are based on symptom onset and echocardiographic severity per 2020 ACC/AHA guidelines.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a prosthetic aortic valve develops fever, chills, and a new regurgitant murmur 8 months after surgery. Blood cultures grow coagulase-negative staphylococci. TEE shows a paravalvular abscess. Which feature of this presentation classifies it as early vs. late prosthetic valve endocarditis?

choices.text: Presence of paravalvular abscess automatically classifies this as early PVE
choices.feedback: While abscesses are more common in early PVE, they can occur at any time. The classification is based on time from surgery, not complications.

choices.text: The new regurgitant murmur indicates valve dehiscence typical of late PVE
choices.feedback: Valve dehiscence and new murmurs occur in both early and late PVE. The defining criterion is timing — within 12 months is early, after 12 months is late.

choices.text: Coagulase-negative staphylococci indicates late PVE regardless of timing
choices.feedback: CoNS are actually the hallmark organisms of early PVE, introduced during surgery. While they can occasionally cause late PVE, the organism does not override the time-based classification.

choices.text: Onset within 12 months of surgery makes this early prosthetic valve endocarditis
choices.feedback: Early prosthetic valve endocarditis (PVE) occurs within 12 months of implantation and is typically caused by nosocomial organisms acquired perioperatively — especially coagulase-negative staphylococci. Early PVE has higher mortality and more frequent perivalvular extension than late PVE.

questions.explanation: Infective endocarditis diagnosis uses Modified Duke Criteria: 2 major, 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor criteria = definite IE. Major criteria: (1) positive blood cultures with typical organisms (viridans streptococci, Staph aureus, HACEK) and (2) echocardiographic evidence of vegetation. Empiric treatment for native valve: nafcillin/oxacillin + gentamicin (or vancomycin if MRSA suspected). Prosthetic valve: vancomycin + gentamicin + rifampin.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman presents with 3 weeks of fever, fatigue, and weight loss. Exam reveals splinter hemorrhages, Janeway lesions, and new-onset aortic regurgitation. Three sets of blood cultures are drawn. Which modified Duke criterion combination would establish a definite diagnosis of endocarditis?

choices.text: Positive echocardiogram showing vegetation plus persistently positive blood cultures with a typical organism
choices.feedback: Two major Duke criteria establish definite endocarditis. Major criteria are: (1) typical microorganism from 2 separate cultures or persistently positive cultures, and (2) evidence of endocardial involvement (vegetation, abscess, or new valvular regurgitation on echo). This is the most common combination leading to definite diagnosis.

choices.text: Positive echocardiogram plus new aortic regurgitation count as two major criteria
choices.feedback: Vegetation on echo and new valvular regurgitation both fall under the same major criterion (endocardial involvement). They count as one major criterion, not two. You need a second major criterion (microbiology) for definite diagnosis.

choices.text: A single positive blood culture plus Janeway lesions
choices.feedback: A single positive culture does not meet a major criterion (requires 2 separate cultures or persistently positive). Janeway lesions count as one minor criterion (vascular phenomena). This combination is insufficient.

choices.text: Three of five minor criteria: fever, vascular phenomena, immunologic phenomena
choices.feedback: Three minor criteria alone make a 'possible' diagnosis, not definite. Definite diagnosis requires either 2 major criteria, 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor criteria under the modified Duke criteria.

questions.explanation: Infective endocarditis diagnosis uses Modified Duke Criteria: 2 major, 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor criteria = definite IE. Major criteria: (1) positive blood cultures with typical organisms (viridans streptococci, Staph aureus, HACEK) and (2) echocardiographic evidence of vegetation. Empiric treatment for native valve: nafcillin/oxacillin + gentamicin (or vancomycin if MRSA suspected). Prosthetic valve: vancomycin + gentamicin + rifampin.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with Streptococcus gallolyticus (formerly S. bovis) bacteremia is diagnosed with aortic valve endocarditis. What additional evaluation is most important to recommend?

choices.text: HIV testing given the association with immunosuppression
choices.feedback: S. gallolyticus endocarditis is not an AIDS-defining illness nor associated with HIV. Its unique clinical association is with colorectal malignancy.

choices.text: Colonoscopy to evaluate for colorectal malignancy
choices.feedback: S. gallolyticus (bovis biotype I) bacteremia has a strong association with colorectal neoplasia — found in 25-80% of cases. The bacterium translocates through colonic mucosal defects caused by polyps or tumors. Every patient with S. gallolyticus bacteremia requires colonoscopy regardless of age or symptoms.

choices.text: Liver biopsy to evaluate for hepatic abscess
choices.feedback: While S. gallolyticus can rarely cause liver infections, the critical association is with colorectal neoplasia. Colonoscopy, not liver biopsy, is the essential additional workup.

choices.text: CT of the chest to rule out lung cancer
choices.feedback: S. gallolyticus is specifically associated with colorectal neoplasia, not lung cancer. The GI tract is the portal of entry, and colonoscopy is the appropriate screening study.

questions.explanation: Infective endocarditis diagnosis uses Modified Duke Criteria: 2 major, 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor criteria = definite IE. Major criteria: (1) positive blood cultures with typical organisms (viridans streptococci, Staph aureus, HACEK) and (2) echocardiographic evidence of vegetation. Empiric treatment for native valve: nafcillin/oxacillin + gentamicin (or vancomycin if MRSA suspected). Prosthetic valve: vancomycin + gentamicin + rifampin.

questions.stem: A patient with native mitral valve endocarditis caused by viridans group streptococci develops worsening heart failure despite appropriate antibiotics for 2 weeks. Echo shows severe mitral regurgitation with a flail leaflet. Which is the strongest indication for surgical intervention?

choices.text: Persistent fever after 5 days of appropriate antibiotics
choices.feedback: Persistent fever at 5 days is common and not yet an indication for surgery. Uncontrolled infection is defined as persistent fever/bacteremia after 7-10 days of appropriate therapy. At 2 weeks with worsening HF, the HF itself is the primary surgical indication.

choices.text: Isolation of viridans streptococci which are inherently difficult to eradicate
choices.feedback: Viridans streptococci are actually among the most antibiotic-susceptible endocarditis organisms with high cure rates. The organism itself is not a surgical indication — the hemodynamic consequences are.

choices.text: Vegetation size greater than 10 mm on the mitral valve
choices.feedback: While vegetation size >10 mm on the mitral valve is a relative indication for surgery (embolic risk), it is not as strong an indication as refractory heart failure. Size alone, without embolism or heart failure, is a class IIa recommendation.

choices.text: Heart failure due to severe valvular dysfunction not responding to medical therapy
choices.feedback: Refractory heart failure from severe valve dysfunction is the most common and strongest indication for surgery in endocarditis. A flail leaflet causing severe MR with progressive heart failure despite antibiotics requires urgent surgical intervention — delay increases mortality.

questions.explanation: Heart failure classification uses both EF-based categories (HFrEF ≤40%, HFmrEF 41-49%, HFpEF ≥50%) and NYHA functional class (I-IV). BNP/NT-proBNP are useful biomarkers for diagnosis and prognosis but should be interpreted in clinical context. Elevated BNP (>100 pg/mL) or NT-proBNP (>300 pg/mL) supports the diagnosis but can be elevated in other conditions (renal failure, PE, sepsis).

questions.stem: A 4-year-old child presents with acute stridor, drooling, high fever, and a toxic appearance. The child is sitting upright in a tripod position and refuses to swallow. A lateral neck radiograph shows the 'thumbprint sign.' Which is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Direct laryngoscopy at bedside to visualize the epiglottis
choices.feedback: Bedside laryngoscopy in a child with suspected epiglottitis is dangerous — it can trigger laryngospasm and complete airway obstruction. Visualization should only occur in the OR where emergent surgical airway is immediately available.

choices.text: Immediate preparation for airway management in the operating room
choices.feedback: This presentation (stridor, drooling, fever, tripod positioning, thumbprint sign) is classic epiglottitis. The priority is securing the airway in a controlled environment (OR) with anesthesia and ENT/surgery backup. Agitation from examination or IV placement can precipitate complete obstruction.

choices.text: Nebulized racemic epinephrine and systemic dexamethasone
choices.feedback: Nebulized epinephrine and steroids are the treatment for viral croup (subglottic disease), not epiglottitis (supraglottic disease). The thumbprint sign, drooling, and toxic appearance distinguish epiglottitis from croup.

choices.text: CT of the neck with IV contrast to evaluate for abscess
choices.feedback: Imaging delays airway management in a child with impending obstruction. CT requires the child to lie flat, which can worsen obstruction. Airway stabilization takes absolute priority over additional imaging.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents with severe sore throat, trismus, muffled 'hot potato' voice, and uvular deviation to the left. CT shows a rim-enhancing collection in the right peritonsillar space. After IV antibiotics, what is the definitive treatment?

choices.text: Needle aspiration or incision and drainage of the peritonsillar abscess
choices.feedback: Peritonsillar abscess requires drainage — either needle aspiration or I&D — in addition to antibiotics. Antibiotics alone have high failure rates. Needle aspiration is often first-line as it is less invasive; I&D is performed if aspiration fails or the abscess is large. Uvula deviates AWAY from the abscess.
Quinsy tonsillectomy is reserved for recurrent peritonsillar abscesses or failed drainage. First-line treatment is needle aspiration or I&D, which is simpler, lower risk, and effective in most cases.

Antibiotics alone are insufficient for an established peritonsillar abscess. While appropriate antibiotics are important adjuncts, the collection must be drained to achieve resolution and prevent complications like parapharyngeal space extension.

choices.text: Oral steroids and warm salt water gargles with outpatient follow-up
choices.feedback: A formed abscess requires procedural drainage. While steroids may reduce inflammation, conservative management without drainage risks complications including airway compromise, parapharyngeal extension, and Lemierre syndrome.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man on warfarin presents with unilateral profuse epistaxis from the posterior nasal cavity that does not respond to anterior nasal packing and direct pressure for 20 minutes. His INR is 4.2. Which is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Emergent surgical ligation of the external carotid artery
choices.feedback: External carotid ligation is a last resort for life-threatening epistaxis refractory to all other measures. The stepwise approach is anterior packing → posterior packing → angiographic embolization → surgical ligation.

choices.text: Posterior nasal packing and INR reversal with vitamin K and fresh frozen plasma
choices.feedback: Refractory posterior epistaxis requires posterior packing (balloon catheter or gauze) plus reversal of the coagulopathy. The supratherapeutic INR (4.2) is a major contributing factor. Posterior packing controls the bleeding mechanically while coagulation is corrected. All patients with posterior packs require ICU monitoring.

choices.text: Continuation of anterior packing with topical oxymetazoline spray
choices.feedback: Anterior packing has already failed for 20 minutes. Posterior bleeding sources (typically from the sphenopalatine artery) require posterior packing. Continued anterior measures will not address a posterior source.

choices.text: Observation with head of bed elevation and ice packs
choices.feedback: Active posterior epistaxis on anticoagulation is not appropriate for observation. This is a potentially life-threatening situation requiring posterior packing and coagulopathy reversal.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset vertigo, nausea, nystagmus, and difficulty walking. The head impulse test is normal, nystagmus is direction-changing, and the test of skew reveals vertical misalignment. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: BPPV causes brief episodes triggered by position changes with characteristic torsional nystagmus on Dix-Hallpike testing. It does not cause persistent vertigo, skew deviation, or direction-changing nystagmus.
Ménière presents with episodic vertigo plus hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness. It would not cause a positive skew deviation or direction-changing nystagmus — those are central signs.
Vestibular neuritis causes a positive (abnormal) head impulse test, unidirectional nystagmus, and no skew deviation. All three HINTS findings here point away from a peripheral cause.

choices.text: Central vertigo, most likely a posterior circulation stroke
choices.feedback: The HINTS exam (Head Impulse, Nystagmus, Test of Skew) differentiates central from peripheral vertigo. A positive skew deviation (vertical misalignment), direction-changing nystagmus, and normal head impulse test all point to a CENTRAL cause. This triad has higher sensitivity for posterior fossa stroke than early MRI.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old child presents after inserting a small disc battery into her right nostril 2 hours ago. On exam, the battery is visible in the anterior nasal cavity. What makes this a true ENT emergency compared to other nasal foreign bodies?

choices.text: The metallic composition causes immediate anaphylaxis from nickel allergy
choices.feedback: While nickel allergy exists, anaphylaxis from a nasal battery is not the concern. The emergency is tissue necrosis from the electrical current and alkali production at the battery surfaces.

choices.text: Battery current causes liquefaction necrosis of the nasal mucosa and septum within hours
