choices.feedback: Disc batteries generate an electrical current that causes alkaline liquefaction necrosis through the mucosa, cartilage, and bone. Septal perforation can occur within 2-4 hours. This is the only nasal foreign body that requires truly emergent removal — other objects can be managed more electively.

choices.text: Disc batteries expand with moisture and can obstruct the nasopharynx
choices.feedback: Batteries do not significantly expand. The danger is chemical and electrical tissue destruction, not mechanical obstruction. The urgency relates to the progressive necrosis that begins within hours of mucosal contact.

choices.text: Battery acid directly dissolves the nasal cartilage through acidic corrosion
choices.feedback: The mechanism is alkaline liquefaction necrosis (not acidic). The battery generates hydroxide ions at the negative pole, creating a highly alkaline environment that causes progressive tissue destruction.

questions.stem: A patient who has been taking chronic daily prednisone for 6 months for rheumatoid arthritis abruptly discontinues the medication. She presents with severe fatigue, hypotension, nausea, and abdominal pain. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: While chronic steroid use increases infection risk, the presentation with hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and abrupt steroid withdrawal makes adrenal crisis the primary diagnosis. Sepsis can precipitate adrenal crisis in patients with suppressed HPA axes.

SIADH causes hyponatremia but not hyperkalemia or hypotension. In SIADH, aldosterone function is preserved so potassium remains normal. The triad of hypotension, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia after steroid withdrawal is adrenal crisis.

Chronic exogenous glucocorticoids suppress CRH and ACTH, causing adrenal atrophy. Abrupt discontinuation removes cortisol replacement while the HPA axis remains suppressed, triggering adrenal crisis. Hyponatremia (impaired free water excretion) and hyperkalemia (loss of cortisol's mineralocorticoid effect) are characteristic.

choices.text: Addisonian crisis from autoimmune adrenal destruction
choices.feedback: Primary autoimmune adrenalitis (Addison disease) develops over months to years, not acutely after steroid withdrawal. The history of exogenous steroid use and abrupt discontinuation makes secondary adrenal insufficiency the mechanism.

questions.explanation: Key detail: pituitary-adrenal axis suppression.

questions.stem: A patient presenting with suspected adrenal crisis has blood drawn for a cortisol level. While awaiting results, what is the most appropriate immediate treatment?

choices.text: IV dexamethasone because it won't interfere with the cortisol assay
choices.feedback: While dexamethasone doesn't cross-react with cortisol assays, it lacks mineralocorticoid activity needed in acute crisis. Hydrocortisone is preferred.

choices.text: IV hydrocortisone bolus plus aggressive IV normal saline
choices.feedback: Adrenal crisis is a medical emergency — never wait for lab confirmation. IV hydrocortisone is the drug of choice because it provides both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid effects. Aggressive fluid resuscitation corrects hypovolemia and hypotension.

choices.text: Oral fludrocortisone to correct the mineralocorticoid deficiency
choices.feedback: Fludrocortisone is used for chronic mineralocorticoid replacement in primary adrenal insufficiency. In acute crisis, IV hydrocortisone at stress doses provides sufficient mineralocorticoid activity.

choices.text: Wait for cortisol results before treating — premature steroids may mask the diagnosis
choices.feedback: Never delay treatment for lab confirmation in suspected adrenal crisis. Mortality is high without immediate glucocorticoid replacement. Draw labs then treat immediately.

questions.explanation: Adrenal insufficiency: primary (Addison's — autoimmune destruction, elevated ACTH, hyperpigmentation, hyperkalemia) vs secondary (pituitary — low ACTH, no hyperpigmentation, no hyperkalemia). Diagnosis: cosyntropin (ACTH) stimulation test — cortisol should rise >18 mcg/dL. Adrenal crisis: hypotension, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia — treat with IV hydrocortisonebolus + NS. Chronic: hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone (primary only).

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with known Addison disease presents to the emergency department with vomiting and diarrhea from viral gastroenteritis. His home medications include daily hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone replacement. His blood pressure is 100/60 mmHg. What is the appropriate glucocorticoid management?

choices.feedback: Maintenance doses are inadequate during physiologic stress. Normal adrenals dramatically increase cortisol output during illness; a suppressed/destroyed adrenal gland cannot.

choices.text: IV stress-dose glucocorticoid, then taper to stress-level maintenance
choices.feedback: Any physiologic stress (illness, surgery, trauma) in a patient with adrenal insufficiency requires stress-dose steroids. IV hydrocortisone is preferred because it provides both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid activity.

choices.text: Double the mineralocorticoid dose since the patient is losing fluids
choices.feedback: Mineralocorticoid dose does not need to increase during stress — it is the glucocorticoid that must be increased. At stress doses, hydrocortisone provides sufficient mineralocorticoid activity.

choices.text: IV methylprednisolone for its superior anti-inflammatory properties
choices.feedback: Methylprednisolone has minimal mineralocorticoid activity. In adrenal crisis, hydrocortisone is preferred because it covers both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid needs.

questions.explanation: Adrenal insufficiency: primary (Addison's — autoimmune destruction, elevated ACTH, hyperpigmentation, hyperkalemia) vs secondary (pituitary — low ACTH, no hyperpigmentation, no hyperkalemia). Diagnosis: cosyntropin (ACTH) stimulation test — cortisol should rise >18 mcg/dL. Adrenal crisis: hypotension, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia — treat with IV hydrocortisonebolus + NS. Chronic: hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone (primary only).

questions.stem: An ACTH stimulation (cosyntropin) test is performed on a patient with suspected adrenal insufficiency. Baseline cortisol is 3 μg/dL and the 60-minute stimulated cortisol is 8 μg/dL. Which interpretation is correct?

choices.text: Consistent with ACTH resistance rather than true adrenal insufficiency
choices.feedback: ACTH resistance (familial glucocorticoid deficiency) is extremely rare. The far more common explanations are primary adrenal destruction or secondary HPA suppression. The clinical context guides further differentiation.

choices.text: Normal response because cortisol more than doubled from baseline
choices.feedback: The cosyntropin test is interpreted by absolute peak value, not fold-change from baseline. The cortisol must reach ≥18 μg/dL regardless of the starting point. Doubling from 3 to 8 is still a failed response.

choices.text: Equivocal result requiring repeat testing in 4 weeks
choices.feedback: A stimulated cortisol of 8 μg/dL is clearly abnormal (well below the 18 μg/dL threshold). This is diagnostic, not equivocal. The patient needs treatment and further workup to determine primary vs. secondary etiology.

A normal cosyntropin stimulation test requires the 30- or 60-minute cortisol to reach ≥18 μg/dL (500 nmol/L). A stimulated value of only 8 μg/dL confirms inadequate adrenal reserve. The next step is to measure ACTH to distinguish primary (elevated ACTH) from secondary (low/normal ACTH) adrenal insufficiency.

questions.explanation: Key detail: stimulated cortisol should exceed 18 μg/dL.

questions.stem: Which clinical feature best helps distinguish primary adrenal insufficiency from secondary adrenal insufficiency?

choices.feedback: Both primary and secondary adrenal insufficiency cause fatigue and hypotension from cortisol deficiency. These symptoms cannot distinguish between the two forms.

choices.text: Presence of hyperkalemia and metabolic acidosis
choices.feedback: While hyperkalemia is more common in primary AI (from aldosterone deficiency), it can occur in both forms. It is not as specific a distinguishing feature as hyperpigmentation, which is pathognomonic for primary AI.

choices.text: Hyperpigmentation of skin creases and buccal mucosa
choices.feedback: Hyperpigmentation occurs only in PRIMARY adrenal insufficiency because elevated ACTH (from loss of cortisol negative feedback) co-stimulates melanocyte-stimulating hormone (MSH) — they share the POMC precursor. In secondary AI, ACTH is low, so hyperpigmentation is absent. This is the single most distinguishing clinical feature.
Hypoglycemia can occur in both primary and secondary AI due to cortisol's role in gluconeogenesis. It does not distinguish between the two forms.

questions.explanation: Adrenal insufficiency: primary (Addison's — autoimmune destruction, elevated ACTH, hyperpigmentation, hyperkalemia) vs secondary (pituitary — low ACTH, no hyperpigmentation, no hyperkalemia). Diagnosis: cosyntropin (ACTH) stimulation test — cortisol should rise >18 mcg/dL. Adrenal crisis: hypotension, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia — treat with IV hydrocortisonebolus + NS. Chronic: hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone (primary only).

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man who recently returned from a hotel convention presents with high fever, nonproductive cough, diarrhea, confusion, and hyponatremia. Chest X-ray shows a rapidly progressive lobar consolidation. Gram stain of sputum shows many neutrophils but no organisms. Which diagnostic test is most appropriate for the suspected pathogen?

choices.feedback: While influenza can cause severe pneumonia, the combination of GI symptoms, hyponatremia, confusion, and hotel exposure with poor Gram stain is far more suggestive of Legionella than influenza.

This presentation (hotel exposure, high fever, GI symptoms, hyponatremia, CNS changes, poor Gram stain) is classic for Legionella pneumophila. The urine antigen test detects L. pneumophila serogroup 1 (responsible for ~80% of cases) with high specificity and rapid turnaround. Legionella stains poorly with standard Gram stain but is visible with Dieterle silver stain.

Legionella does not grow on standard blood culture media. It requires buffered charcoal yeast extract (BCYE) agar supplemented with L-cysteine and iron. Blood cultures will miss this organism entirely.

choices.text: Sputum Gram stain with acid-fast bacilli staining
choices.feedback: Legionella is not acid-fast. It is a gram-negative rod that stains poorly with conventional Gram stain (it takes up faint pink color, often missed). The urine antigen or culture on BCYE agar are the appropriate diagnostic tools.

questions.stem: Which unique growth requirement distinguishes Legionella pneumophila from other gram-negative bacteria?

choices.text: Obligate anaerobic conditions with supplemental hemin and vitamin K
choices.feedback: Anaerobic growth with hemin and vitamin K describes Bacteroides fragilis. Legionella is an aerobic organism that grows on BCYE agar under standard atmospheric conditions.

choices.text: Requirement for 5-10% CO2 atmosphere and chocolate agar
choices.feedback: CO2-enriched chocolate agar supports fastidious organisms like Neisseria and Haemophilus, not Legionella. Legionella specifically requires cysteine and iron, which chocolate agar does not provide.

choices.text: Obligate requirement for L-cysteine and iron, grown on BCYE agar
choices.feedback: Legionella has an absolute requirement for L-cysteine (amino acid) and iron for growth. Buffered charcoal yeast extract (BCYE) agar provides these nutrients. This is why Legionella does not grow on standard blood agar, chocolate agar, or MacConkey agar — none supply cysteine.

choices.text: Growth only on Thayer-Martin medium with selective antibiotics
choices.feedback: Thayer-Martin medium is selective for Neisseria species (gonorrhoeae and meningitidis). Legionella requires BCYE agar — it cannot grow on Thayer-Martin.

questions.stem: A Legionella outbreak is traced to the cooling tower of a hospital. Which mechanism explains how this intracellular pathogen survives in water systems and infects humans?

choices.text: Legionella forms endospores in water that are inhaled and germinate in lung tissue
choices.feedback: Legionella does not form endospores — that ability is limited to genera like Bacillus and Clostridium. Legionella persists in water by living inside amoebae, which provide protection from environmental stresses.

choices.text: Biofilm formation on water pipes allows direct fecal-oral transmission to humans
choices.feedback: While Legionella does form biofilms in water systems, transmission is through inhalation of contaminated aerosols, not fecal-oral. There is also no person-to-person spread.

choices.text: Legionella produces a heat-stable toxin in water that causes pneumonia when ingested
choices.feedback: Legionella does not produce a preformed toxin. Disease results from actual bacterial infection of alveolar macrophages after inhalation of contaminated aerosols. Ingestion of contaminated water does not typically cause disease.

choices.text: Legionella replicates within amoebae in water, then infects alveolar macrophages via inhaled aerosols
choices.feedback: Legionella is a facultative intracellular parasite that replicates within free-living amoebae (Acanthamoeba) in water systems. This intracellular survival strategy in amoebae pre-adapts it to survive within human alveolar macrophages using similar mechanisms. Infection occurs by inhaling contaminated aerosols — NOT person-to-person transmission.

questions.stem: Which antibiotic class is first-line for treatment of Legionella pneumonia?

choices.text: Fluoroquinolones or macrolides because they achieve high intracellular concentrations
choices.feedback: As an obligate intracellular pathogen within macrophages, Legionella requires antibiotics that penetrate cells effectively. Fluoroquinolones (levofloxacin) and macrolides (azithromycin) achieve excellent intracellular levels. Beta-lactams are ineffective because they cannot reach Legionella inside cells.

choices.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for its dual mechanism of action
choices.feedback: TMP-SMX is not effective against Legionella. The drug of choice must achieve high intracellular concentrations — fluoroquinolones and macrolides are the standard of care.

choices.text: Beta-lactams such as amoxicillin-clavulanate for broad gram-negative coverage
choices.feedback: Beta-lactams cannot penetrate macrophages to reach intracellular Legionella and are therefore completely ineffective. This is a critical board-tested concept — community-acquired pneumonia treatment must include atypical coverage when Legionella is suspected.

choices.text: Aminoglycosides for their bactericidal activity against gram-negative rods
choices.feedback: Aminoglycosides have poor intracellular penetration and limited activity against Legionella. Additionally, their mechanism (30S ribosomal binding) is less effective against intracellular pathogens.

questions.stem: A previously healthy 45-year-old presents with community-acquired pneumonia. Which finding would most increase your suspicion for Legionella over typical bacterial pathogens?

choices.text: Bulging fissure sign on chest X-ray with mucoid sputum
choices.feedback: The bulging fissure sign with mucoid sputum is classic for Klebsiella pneumoniae, typically in alcoholic patients. This finding is not associated with Legionella.

choices.text: Rust-colored sputum with rigors and pleuritic chest pain
choices.feedback: Rust-colored sputum with rigors and pleurisy is the classic presentation of Streptococcus pneumoniae lobar pneumonia. Legionella typically causes a dry or minimally productive cough.

choices.text: Hyponatremia, elevated liver enzymes, and diarrhea accompanying the pneumonia
choices.feedback: Legionella characteristically causes multisystem disease: hyponatremia (SIADH), transaminitis, diarrhea, confusion, and relative bradycardia. This extrapulmonary involvement distinguishes it from typical bacterial pneumonia. The combination of pneumonia plus GI/CNS/lab abnormalities should always trigger Legionella testing.

choices.text: Bilateral interstitial infiltrates with lymphopenia on CBC
choices.feedback: Bilateral interstitial infiltrates with lymphopenia are more suggestive of viral pneumonia (influenza, COVID-19) or Pneumocystis. Legionella typically produces focal consolidation, often with rapid progression.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents to the ED after a motorcycle accident with BP 78/50, HR 130, cool extremities, and collapsed neck veins. Which type of shock is most likely?

choices.feedback: Cardiogenic shock would show distended neck veins, pulmonary edema, and cold extremities. The collapsed neck veins here point away from pump failure.
Neurogenic shock presents with hypotension and BRADYCARDIA (not tachycardia) due to loss of sympathetic tone. Skin is warm and dry, not cool.

Collapsed neck veins + hypotension + tachycardia + trauma = hypovolemic shock from hemorrhage. Flat JVD distinguishes this from obstructive (elevated JVD) and cardiogenic (elevated JVD) causes. Initial management: 2 large-bore IVs, crystalloid, then blood products.
Early septic shock typically shows warm, flushed skin with bounding pulses (distributive pattern). The cool extremities and trauma history favor hypovolemia.

questions.explanation: Motorcycle accident with tachycardia, hypotension, and signs of hemorrhage (visible bleeding, mechanism of injury) is classic hypovolemic shock — specifically hemorrhagic shock in a trauma setting. Hemorrhagic shock is classified by ATLS into 4 classes based on estimated blood loss: Class I (<15%, minimal symptoms), Class II (15-30%, tachycardia, narrowed pulse pressure), Class III (30-40%, tachycardia, hypotension, altered mentation), Class IV (>40%, life-threatening, obtunded). Management follows ABC priorities: airway, breathing, then circulation with hemorrhage control and volume resuscitation (blood products for significant hemorrhage, crystalloid as bridge).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Motorcycle accident with tachycardia, hypotension, and signs of hemorrhage (visible bleeding, mechanism of injury) is classic hypovolemic shock — specifically hemorrhagic shock in a trauma setting. Hemorrhagic shock is classified by ATLS into 4 classes based on estimated blood loss: Class I (<15%, minimal symptoms), Class II (15-30%, tachycardia, narrowed pulse pressure), Class III (30-40%, tachycardia, hypotension, altered mentation), Class IV (>40%, life-threatening, obtunded). Management follows ABC priorities: airway, breathing, then circulation with hemorrhage control and volume resuscitation (blood products for significant hemorrhage, crystalloid as bridge).

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with recent MI presents with JVD, hypotension, clear lungs, and muffled heart sounds. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: LV failure would present with pulmonary edema (crackles on lung exam), not clear lungs. The muffled heart sounds point to tamponade.

Beck's triad: hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds = cardiac tamponade. Post-MI free wall rupture can cause acute tamponade. Pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg confirms. Treatment: emergent pericardiocentesis.
Tension pneumothorax causes JVD and hypotension but with absent breath sounds on one side and tracheal deviation, not muffled heart sounds.
PE can cause JVD and hypotension but heart sounds are typically normal, and you'd expect tachypnea and pleuritic chest pain.

questions.explanation: JVD, hypotension, and clear lungs after MI is the classic presentation of cardiac tamponade — likely from ventricular free wall rupture, a catastrophic mechanical complication of MI occurring typically 3-5 days post-infarction. The clear lungs distinguish tamponade from cardiogenic shock (which typically has pulmonary edema). JVD occurs because the compressed heart cannot accept venous return. This is a surgical emergency requiring pericardiocentesis as a temporizing measure followed by emergent surgical repair. Other post-MI mechanical complications include papillary muscle rupture (acute mitral regurgitation) and ventricular septal rupture.
questions.verifiedExplanation: JVD, hypotension, and clear lungs after MI is the classic presentation of cardiac tamponade — likely from ventricular free wall rupture, a catastrophic mechanical complication of MI occurring typically 3-5 days post-infarction. The clear lungs distinguish tamponade from cardiogenic shock (which typically has pulmonary edema). JVD occurs because the compressed heart cannot accept venous return. This is a surgical emergency requiring pericardiocentesis as a temporizing measure followed by emergent surgical repair. Other post-MI mechanical complications include papillary muscle rupture (acute mitral regurgitation) and ventricular septal rupture.

questions.stem: Which hemodynamic profile is characteristic of early septic shock?

choices.text: High SVR, low cardiac output, low wedge pressure
choices.feedback: This describes hypovolemic shock — low volume (low wedge), poor output (low CO), compensatory vasoconstriction (high SVR).

choices.text: Low SVR, low cardiac output, high wedge pressure
choices.feedback: This mixed pattern might be seen in late septic shock with myocardial depression, but early septic shock characteristically shows HIGH cardiac output with LOW SVR.

choices.text: High SVR, low cardiac output, high wedge pressure
choices.feedback: This profile describes cardiogenic shock — pump failure leads to backup (high wedge), poor forward flow (low CO), and compensatory vasoconstriction (high SVR).

choices.text: Low SVR, high cardiac output, low wedge pressure
choices.feedback: Early (warm) septic shock = distributive physiology: massive vasodilation drops SVR, compensatory increase in cardiac output, low filling pressures. This distinguishes it from cardiogenic (high SVR, low CO, high wedge) and hypovolemic (high SVR, low CO, low wedge).

questions.explanation: Early (warm) septic shock has a characteristic hemodynamic profile: low systemic vascular resistance (SVR) from inflammatory vasodilation, high cardiac output (compensatory increase to maintain perfusion despite low SVR), and low pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP, reflecting relative hypovolemia from vasodilation and capillary leak). This profile distinguishes septic shock from cardiogenic shock (high SVR, low CO, high PCWP) and hypovolemic shock (high SVR, low CO, low PCWP). In late/decompensated septic shock, myocardial depression occurs and cardiac output falls, but SVR remains low.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Early (warm) septic shock has a characteristic hemodynamic profile: low systemic vascular resistance (SVR) from inflammatory vasodilation, high cardiac output (compensatory increase to maintain perfusion despite low SVR), and low pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP, reflecting relative hypovolemia from vasodilation and capillary leak). This profile distinguishes septic shock from cardiogenic shock (high SVR, low CO, high PCWP) and hypovolemic shock (high SVR, low CO, low PCWP). In late/decompensated septic shock, myocardial depression occurs and cardiac output falls, but SVR remains low.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with a stab wound to the chest develops hypotension, distended neck veins, and absent breath sounds on the left. What is the immediate next step?

choices.text: Needle decompression of the left chest
choices.feedback: Tension pneumothorax (JVD + hypotension + absent breath sounds + tracheal deviation) is a clinical diagnosis requiring immediate needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line. Do NOT delay for imaging.
Positive pressure ventilation before decompression would worsen tension pneumothorax by pushing more air into the pleural space.
Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis. Waiting for imaging in a hemodynamically unstable patient wastes critical time. Decompress first, image later.
Tamponade could cause JVD and hypotension, but the absent breath sounds on one side point to tension pneumothorax, not tamponade.

questions.explanation: Stab wound to the chest with hypotension, absent breath sounds on the affected side, and tracheal deviation away from the injury describes tension pneumothorax — a clinical diagnosis requiring immediate needle decompression without waiting for imaging. Needle decompression is performed at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line (or 4th-5th intercostal space, anterior axillary line) on the affected side, followed by chest tube placement. Tension pneumothorax causes obstructive shock by compressing the mediastinum, kinking the great vessels, and impairing venous return. This is one of the immediately reversible causes of traumatic cardiac arrest.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Stab wound to the chest with hypotension, absent breath sounds on the affected side, and tracheal deviation away from the injury describes tension pneumothorax — a clinical diagnosis requiring immediate needle decompression without waiting for imaging. Needle decompression is performed at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line (or 4th-5th intercostal space, anterior axillary line) on the affected side, followed by chest tube placement. Tension pneumothorax causes obstructive shock by compressing the mediastinum, kinking the great vessels, and impairing venous return. This is one of the immediately reversible causes of traumatic cardiac arrest.

questions.stem: Which vasopressor is first-line for septic shock refractory to fluid resuscitation?

choices.feedback: Dopamine is no longer first-line due to higher rates of arrhythmia compared to norepinephrine. It may be considered in patients with bradycardia but is otherwise inferior.
Epinephrine is second-line for septic shock, typically added when norepinephrine alone is insufficient. First-line for anaphylactic shock, not septic.

Norepinephrine is first-line per Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines. It provides potent alpha-1 vasoconstriction to raise SVR plus mild beta-1 inotropy. Target MAP ≥65 mmHg. Vasopressin is added as second-line if refractory.
Phenylephrine is a pure alpha agonist that can decrease cardiac output. It's reserved for situations where tachyarrhythmias limit norepinephrine use.

questions.explanation: Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor for septic shock that is refractory to fluid resuscitation. Per Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines, norepinephrine should be titrated to target MAP ≥65 mmHg. Its alpha-1 predominant action addresses the core pathophysiology of sepsis — inappropriate vasodilation. Vasopressin (up to 0.03 units/min) is added as a second agent when norepinephrine alone is insufficient, and may allow norepinephrine dose reduction. If further support is needed, epinephrine is the third-line agent. Stress-dose hydrocortisone is considered for patients requiring escalating vasopressor doses.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor for septic shock that is refractory to fluid resuscitation. Per Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines, norepinephrine should be titrated to target MAP ≥65 mmHg. Its alpha-1 predominant action addresses the core pathophysiology of sepsis — inappropriate vasodilation. Vasopressin (up to 0.03 units/min) is added as a second agent when norepinephrine alone is insufficient, and may allow norepinephrine dose reduction. If further support is needed, epinephrine is the third-line agent. Stress-dose hydrocortisone is considered for patients requiring escalating vasopressor doses.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old nursing home resident presents with fever, confusion, WBC 22,000, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, and BP 82/48 despite 30 mL/kg crystalloid. Which criteria define this as septic shock rather than sepsis alone?

choices.feedback: Sepsis-3 defines septic shock as sepsis with persistent hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥65 AND serum lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate volume resuscitation. This carries mortality >40%. The elevated lactate indicates tissue hypoperfusion.

choices.text: Two or more SIRS criteria plus suspected infection
choices.feedback: SIRS criteria + infection defined sepsis under older (Sepsis-2) criteria. Sepsis-3 uses SOFA score ≥2 for sepsis definition, and adds vasopressor dependence + lactate >2 for septic shock.
Transient hypotension responsive to fluids doesn't meet septic shock criteria. The key is PERSISTENT hypotension requiring vasopressors plus elevated lactate.
Blood culture positivity isn't required for sepsis or septic shock diagnosis. Many septic patients are culture-negative, and bacteremia alone doesn't define shock.

questions.explanation: Septic shock is defined as sepsis with persistent hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg AND serum lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. This definition (Sepsis-3, 2016) reflects tissue hypoperfusion that is not corrected by volume alone. Sepsis itself is defined as life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection, operationalized as an acute increase in SOFA score ≥2. The previous SIRS-based criteria were abandoned because SIRS is neither sensitive nor specific for infection-related organ dysfunction. Lactate elevation reflects anaerobic metabolism from tissue hypoperfusion.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Septic shock is defined as sepsis with persistent hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg AND serum lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. This definition (Sepsis-3, 2016) reflects tissue hypoperfusion that is not corrected by volume alone. Sepsis itself is defined as life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection, operationalized as an acute increase in SOFA score ≥2. The previous SIRS-based criteria were abandoned because SIRS is neither sensitive nor specific for infection-related organ dysfunction. Lactate elevation reflects anaerobic metabolism from tissue hypoperfusion.

questions.stem: In the initial management of sepsis, which intervention has the strongest evidence for reducing mortality when performed within the first hour?

choices.feedback: Hydrocortisone is reserved for refractory septic shock not responding to fluids + vasopressors. It's not part of the initial hour-1 management bundle.

Vasopressors are indicated only after adequate fluid resuscitation. Starting vasopressors before volume optimization can worsen organ perfusion. However, early norepinephrine may be considered alongside fluids in severe shock.

choices.text: Central venous catheter placement for CVP monitoring
choices.feedback: The ProCESS, ARISE, and ProMISe trials showed CVP-guided resuscitation (early goal-directed therapy) was NOT superior to usual care. CVP monitoring is no longer recommended in the Hour-1 bundle.

Hour-1 bundle: early antibiotics are the single most impactful intervention. Each hour of delay in antibiotics increases mortality ~4-7%. Obtain cultures BEFORE antibiotics if possible, but don't delay antibiotics for cultures. Combined with target rate crystalloid and lactate measurement.

questions.explanation: Early administration of broad-spectrum antibiotics has the strongest evidence for improving survival in sepsis. The Surviving Sepsis Campaign recommends antibiotics within 1 hour of sepsis recognition — each hour of delay is associated with increased mortality. Appropriate antibiotics should be given after blood cultures are drawn (but cultures should not delay antibiotics). Initial empiric coverage should be broad, targeting the most likely source, and then narrowed based on culture results (antibiotic stewardship). While IV fluids, vasopressors, and source control are all critical, the single intervention with the strongest mortality benefit is timely appropriate antimicrobial therapy.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Early administration of broad-spectrum antibiotics has the strongest evidence for improving survival in sepsis. The Surviving Sepsis Campaign recommends antibiotics within 1 hour of sepsis recognition — each hour of delay is associated with increased mortality. Appropriate antibiotics should be given after blood cultures are drawn (but cultures should not delay antibiotics). Initial empiric coverage should be broad, targeting the most likely source, and then narrowed based on culture results (antibiotic stewardship). While IV fluids, vasopressors, and source control are all critical, the single intervention with the strongest mortality benefit is timely appropriate antimicrobial therapy.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man undergoing appendectomy with sevoflurane anesthesia develops rapidly rising temperature (40.5°C), muscle rigidity, hypercarbia (ETCO2 80), and tachycardia. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?

choices.feedback: Tachycardia in MH is a secondary response to hypermetabolism. Treating the root cause with dantrolene resolves the tachycardia. Beta-blockers alone would be inadequate and potentially harmful.
Succinylcholine is a TRIGGER for MH, not a treatment! It's a depolarizing neuromuscular blocker that can initiate or worsen the hypermetabolic crisis.
Antipyretics don't work in MH because the fever is from uncontrolled skeletal muscle metabolism, not hypothalamic dysregulation. Cooling is adjunctive but dantrolene is essential.

Malignant hyperthermia (MH) triggered by volatile anesthetics (sevoflurane) or succinylcholine. Dantrolene is the specific antidote — blocks ryanodine receptor (RYR1) calcium release from sarcoplasmic reticulum. Dose: weight-based dosing IV, repeat q5min until symptoms resolve. Also: stop triggering agents, hyperventilate with 100% O2, active cooling.

questions.explanation: Surgical decision-making requires systematic assessment using evidence-based algorithms. Key principles include appropriate preoperative risk stratification, understanding surgical anatomy and pathophysiology, and recognizing conditions requiring emergent vs elective intervention.

questions.stem: Which genetic mutation underlies the majority of malignant hyperthermia susceptibility?

choices.text: SCN4A gene mutation affecting sodium channels
choices.feedback: SCN4A mutations cause myotonia congenita and hyperkalemic periodic paralysis, not MH. Different ion channel pathology.

choices.text: RYR1 gene mutation affecting the ryanodine receptor
choices.feedback: ~70% of MH cases involve mutations in the RYR1 gene encoding the skeletal muscle ryanodine receptor (calcium release channel on sarcoplasmic reticulum). Autosomal dominant inheritance. Mutations cause uncontrolled calcium release into myoplasm, triggering sustained muscle contraction and hypermetabolism.

choices.text: CLCN1 gene mutation affecting chloride channels
choices.feedback: CLCN1 mutations cause myotonia congenita (Thomsen/Becker disease), characterized by muscle stiffness but not the hypermetabolic crisis of MH.

choices.text: CACNA1S gene mutation affecting calcium channels
choices.feedback: CACNA1S (dihydropyridine receptor) mutations account for ~1% of MH susceptibility — a minor contributor. RYR1 is the major gene (~70%).

questions.explanation: Surgical decision-making requires systematic assessment using evidence-based algorithms. Key principles include appropriate preoperative risk stratification, understanding surgical anatomy and pathophysiology, and recognizing conditions requiring emergent vs elective intervention.

questions.stem: A patient with a family history of malignant hyperthermia requires general anesthesia. Which anesthetic plan is safest?

choices.text: Pretreatment with dantrolene before standard volatile anesthesia
choices.feedback: Prophylactic dantrolene is NOT recommended. It causes muscle weakness and doesn't guarantee prevention. The standard is to avoid triggering agents entirely and have dantrolene available for rescue.
ANY dose of volatile anesthetics can trigger MH in susceptible patients. There is no safe dose — complete avoidance is required.

choices.text: Total IV anesthesia with propofol and non-depolarizing neuromuscular blockers
choices.feedback: Safe agents for MH-susceptible patients: propofol, opioids, benzodiazepines, nitrous oxide, non-depolarizing NMBs (rocuronium, vecuronium), and all local anesthetics. AVOID all volatile agents (sevoflurane, desflurane, isoflurane) and succinylcholine. Have dantrolene available.

choices.text: Spinal anesthesia only, as regional blocks are contraindicated
choices.feedback: Regional and neuraxial anesthesia are actually SAFE in MH-susceptible patients. Local anesthetics (lidocaine, bupivacaine) do not trigger MH. This would be a reasonable alternative.

questions.explanation: Surgical decision-making requires systematic assessment using evidence-based algorithms. Key principles include appropriate preoperative risk stratification, understanding surgical anatomy and pathophysiology, and recognizing conditions requiring emergent vs elective intervention.

questions.stem: During a malignant hyperthermia crisis, which laboratory finding is most characteristic?

choices.feedback: MH causes HYPERkalemia from muscle cell lysis and metabolic ACIDOSIS from anaerobic metabolism. The opposite of this pattern.
This describes DIC. While DIC can occur as a late complication of MH, the characteristic lab findings are elevated CK, hyperkalemia, and acidosis.

choices.text: Elevated troponin with ST elevation on ECG
choices.feedback: While cardiac arrhythmias occur in MH (from hyperkalemia and acidosis), the primary pathology is skeletal muscle, not cardiac. CK elevation is from skeletal muscle (CK-MM), not cardiac (CK-MB/troponin).

choices.text: Markedly elevated creatine kinase with hyperkalemia and metabolic acidosis
choices.feedback: MH causes massive rhabdomyolysis → CK can exceed 100,000 U/L. Muscle breakdown releases K+ (hyperkalemia — can cause fatal arrhythmias), myoglobin (risk of renal failure), and lactate/CO2 (mixed metabolic/respiratory acidosis). Rising ETCO2 is often the earliest sign.

questions.explanation: Surgical decision-making requires systematic assessment using evidence-based algorithms. Key principles include appropriate preoperative risk stratification, understanding surgical anatomy and pathophysiology, and recognizing conditions requiring emergent vs elective intervention.

questions.stem: What is the gold standard diagnostic test for malignant hyperthermia susceptibility?

choices.feedback: EMG findings are not characteristic of MH susceptibility. Myotonic discharges suggest myotonia congenita or myotonic dystrophy, which are different conditions.
Genetic testing is useful if positive (confirms susceptibility), but a negative result doesn't rule out MH — only ~70% of families have identifiable RYR1 mutations. CHCT remains the gold standard.

choices.text: Caffeine-halothane contracture test on muscle biopsy
choices.feedback: The CHCT exposes biopsied muscle to caffeine and halothane in vitro and measures contracture force. Positive = enhanced contracture. Sensitivity ~97%, specificity ~78%. Requires specialized centers. Genetic testing for RYR1 is increasingly used but a negative genetic test doesn't exclude susceptibility (not all mutations identified).
Resting CK may be mildly elevated in some MH-susceptible individuals, but it's not sensitive or specific enough for diagnosis. Many susceptible patients have normal baseline CK.

questions.explanation: Surgical decision-making requires systematic assessment using evidence-based algorithms. Key principles include appropriate preoperative risk stratification, understanding surgical anatomy and pathophysiology, and recognizing conditions requiring emergent vs elective intervention.

questions.stem: A patient in the ICU develops pulseless electrical activity (PEA). Which of the following is NOT a reversible cause in the 'H's and T's' mnemonic?

choices.feedback: VFib is a shockable rhythm, not a cause of PEA. The H's: Hypovolemia, Hypoxia, Hydrogen ions (acidosis), Hypo/Hyperkalemia, Hypothermia. The T's: Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade, Toxins, Thrombosis (PE/MI). PEA treatment: epinephrine + identify/treat underlying cause.
Tamponade is one of the T's — a classic reversible cause of PEA. Fluid around the heart prevents filling, so there's electrical activity but no effective cardiac output.
Hyperkalemia is one of the H's. It causes widened QRS, peaked T waves, and can progress to PEA or asystole. Treatment: calcium gluconate, insulin/glucose, bicarb.
Tension pneumothorax is one of the T's. Increased intrathoracic pressure prevents venous return, causing PEA. Treatment: needle decompression.

questions.explanation: The correct answer (Ventricular fibrillation) reflects current evidence-based cardiology guidelines. Key cardiovascular principles include systematic assessment of hemodynamic status, risk stratification using validated scores, and guideline-directed pharmacotherapy based on condition severity and patient characteristics.

questions.stem: During cardiac arrest with ventricular fibrillation, what is the correct sequence after the first defibrillation attempt?

choices.text: Immediately check for pulse and rhythm
choices.feedback: Pausing CPR immediately after defibrillation is harmful. Even if defibrillation restores an organized rhythm, the heart needs perfusion support. Complete 2 minutes of CPR first.

choices.text: Resume CPR for 2 minutes, then reassess rhythm
choices.feedback: Per ACLS: after ANY defibrillation, immediately resume CPR for 2 minutes (5 cycles of 30:2) before rechecking rhythm. This allows time for the heart to regain perfusion. Epinephrineq3-5 min. After second shock, consider amiodarone IV. Minimize interruptions in chest compressions.

choices.text: Intubate the patient before further interventions
choices.feedback: Advanced airway management should not interrupt chest compressions. Bag-valve-mask ventilation is adequate initially. Intubation can happen during ongoing CPR without pausing compressions.
Amiodarone is given after the SECOND failed defibrillation (refractory VF/pVT), not after the first shock. And CPR should never be delayed for drug administration.

questions.explanation: ACLS for shockable rhythms (VF/pVT): high-quality CPR + defibrillation. After each shock, immediately resume CPR for 2 minutes (5 cycles of 30:2) before rechecking rhythm. Epinephrine is given after the second shock. Amiodarone is given after the third shock for refractory VF/pVT. The key principle is minimizing interruptions to chest compressions — every pause reduces coronary perfusion pressure.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with fatigue and pallor. Labs show hemoglobin 8.2 g/dL, MCV 68 fL, ferritin 8 ng/mL, and TIBC 450 μg/dL. Which finding is most consistent with this diagnosis?

choices.text: Low serum iron, low ferritin, elevated TIBC
choices.feedback: Iron deficiency anemia shows the classic triad of low iron, low ferritin (most sensitive marker), and elevated TIBC reflecting increased transferrin production. The microcytic picture (MCV 68) fits iron deficiency. Always investigate the cause — menstrual losses in premenopausal women, GI bleeding in men and postmenopausal women.

choices.text: Low serum iron, elevated ferritin, low TIBC
choices.feedback: This pattern suggests anemia of chronic disease, where iron is sequestered in macrophages. Ferritin is an acute phase reactant and rises with inflammation, distinguishing it from iron deficiency.

choices.text: Normal serum iron, elevated ferritin, elevated TIBC
choices.feedback: This combination is physiologically inconsistent. Ferritin and TIBC generally move in opposite directions — ferritin reflects iron stores while TIBC reflects transferrin availability.

choices.text: Elevated serum iron, low ferritin, normal TIBC
choices.feedback: Elevated serum iron with low ferritin is an inconsistent pattern. In sideroblastic anemia, iron is elevated with ring sideroblasts on smear.

questions.explanation: This question tests hematologic clinical reasoning. Key hematology principles: interpret CBC and peripheral smear findings systematically, classify anemias by MCV, recognize coagulopathies by PT/PTT patterns, and identify oncologic emergencies requiring urgent intervention.
questions.verifiedExplanation: This presentation with low hemoglobin, low MCV, and the combination of low serum iron, low ferritin, and elevated TIBC is the classic laboratory profile of iron deficiency anemia. Ferritin is the most sensitive and specific single test — <15 ng/mL is virtually diagnostic. TIBC (total iron-binding capacity) is elevated because the body upregulates transferrin production to capture more iron. Peripheral smear shows microcytic, hypochromic red cells with target cells and pencil cells. In reproductive-age women, menstrual blood loss is the most common cause.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: low ferritin, low serum iron, elevated TIBC in iron deficiency

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with chronic kidney disease stage 4 has hemoglobin 9.0 g/dL, MCV 88 fL, normal iron studies, and reticulocyte count 0.5%. What is the most likely primary mechanism of his anemia?

choices.feedback: Folate deficiency causes macrocytic anemia (elevated MCV), not the normocytic picture seen here. This patient has stage 4 CKD and may not yet be on dialysis.
Hemolytic anemia typically shows elevated reticulocyte count (appropriate marrow response), elevated LDH, low haptoglobin, and indirect hyperbilirubinemia — none of which are present here.

CKD causes normocytic anemia primarily through decreased renal erythropoietin (EPO) production. The low reticulocyte count confirms inadequate bone marrow response. Treatment includes erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs) once iron stores are replete. Target Hb is generally 10-11.5 g/dL — higher targets increase cardiovascular risk.
While CKD patients can have elevated hepcidin contributing to functional iron deficiency, the normal iron studies here point to EPO deficiency as the primary mechanism.

questions.explanation: Anemia classification by MCV: microcytic (iron deficiency, thalassemia, chronic disease, sideroblastic), normocytic (acute blood loss, chronic disease, renal failure, hemolysis), macrocytic (B12/folate deficiency, MDS, liver disease, hypothyroidism). Iron deficiency shows low ferritin, low iron, high TIBC, low transferrin saturation. B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils and can cause subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (posterior columns + lateral corticospinal tracts).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Decreased erythropoietin (EPO) production by the failing kidneys is the primary mechanism of anemia in chronic kidney disease (CKD). EPO is produced by peritubular interstitial cells in response to hypoxia; as these cells are destroyed by progressive nephron loss, EPO production falls. The anemia is normocytic and normochromic with a low reticulocyte count (hypoproliferative). Treatment includes erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs) and iron supplementation. Target hemoglobin is 10-11.5 g/dL — overtreating increases cardiovascular risk.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: decreased EPO production as primary mechanism of CKD anemia

questions.stem: A peripheral blood smear from a patient with severe anemia shows hypersegmented neutrophils (≥6 lobes). Which additional laboratory finding would you most expect?

choices.text: Normal MCV and positive direct Coombs test
choices.feedback: A positive direct Coombs test indicates autoimmune hemolytic anemia, which typically shows spherocytes on smear, not hypersegmented neutrophils.

Hypersegmented neutrophils are pathognomonic for megaloblastic anemia (B12 or folate deficiency). MCV is elevated (often >110 fL). Both deficiencies raise homocysteine, but only B12 deficiency elevates methylmalonic acid — this distinction helps identify the specific deficiency.
Low MCV with elevated RDW suggests iron deficiency anemia, which shows microcytic hypochromic cells and target cells on smear — not hypersegmented neutrophils.

Schistocytes (fragmented RBCs) indicate microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (TTP, HUS, DIC), not megaloblastic anemia. Though MCV can be elevated in both, hypersegmented neutrophils specifically point to megaloblastic disease.

questions.explanation: Anemia classification by MCV: microcytic (iron deficiency, thalassemia, chronic disease, sideroblastic), normocytic (acute blood loss, chronic disease, renal failure, hemolysis), macrocytic (B12/folate deficiency, MDS, liver disease, hypothyroidism). Iron deficiency shows low ferritin, low iron, high TIBC, low transferrin saturation. B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils and can cause subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (posterior columns + lateral corticospinal tracts).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils (≥5 lobes) on peripheral smear in a patient with macrocytic anemia (elevated MCV >100) strongly suggests megaloblastic anemia from B12 or folate deficiency. The mechanism is impaired DNA synthesis with normal RNA/protein production, causing nuclear-cytoplasmic dyssynchrony. Elevated homocysteine occurs in both B12 and folate deficiency. Elevated methylmalonic acid is specific to B12 deficiency. This distinguishes megaloblastic causes from non-megaloblastic macrocytosis (alcohol, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications).

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: hypersegmented neutrophils and elevated MCV in megaloblastic anemia

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate presents with macrocytic anemia. Her B12 level is normal. What is the most appropriate next step?

choices.feedback: B12 is already normal. The macrocytic anemia in the setting of methotrexate use strongly suggests folate deficiency, not B12 deficiency.
Abrupt discontinuation is unnecessary. Folate supplementation can treat and prevent methotrexate-related macrocytic anemia while allowing the patient to continue this effective DMARD therapy.
Bone marrow biopsy would be premature before checking the most likely cause. Drug-induced folate deficiency from methotrexate is common and should be investigated first with simple lab testing.

choices.text: Check folate level and consider supplementation
choices.feedback: Methotrexate inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, causing functional folate deficiency and megaloblastic anemia. Check folate levels — most patients on methotrexate should receive folic acid supplementation to prevent this. Leucovorin (folinic acid) is the rescue agent for methotrexate toxicity.

questions.explanation: Anemia classification by MCV: microcytic (iron deficiency, thalassemia, chronic disease, sideroblastic), normocytic (acute blood loss, chronic disease, renal failure, hemolysis), macrocytic (B12/folate deficiency, MDS, liver disease, hypothyroidism). Iron deficiency shows low ferritin, low iron, high TIBC, low transferrin saturation. B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils and can cause subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (posterior columns + lateral corticospinal tracts).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Methotrexate is a folate antagonist (inhibits dihydrofolate reductase), and macrocytic anemia in a patient on methotrexate should prompt assessment of folate status. Leucovorin (folinic acid) is the rescue agent for methotrexate toxicity, but routine supplementation with folic acid (1 mg/day) is standard practice for patients on weekly methotrexate for RA. If macrocytic anemia develops, check folate level and increase supplementation. B12 should also be checked to avoid masking B12 deficiency with folate replacement.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: folate supplementation for patients on methotrexate

questions.stem: A patient presents with hemoglobin 6.5 g/dL, reticulocyte count 12%, LDH 850 U/L, haptoglobin <10 mg/dL, and indirect bilirubin 3.2 mg/dL. The direct Coombs test is negative. A peripheral smear shows schistocytes. Which condition best explains these findings?

choices.feedback: G6PD deficiency shows bite cells and Heinz bodies on smear during acute hemolytic crises, not schistocytes. Crises are typically triggered by oxidative stress (infections, fava beans, certain drugs).

The combination of Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia (elevated LDH, low haptoglobin, elevated indirect bilirubin, high reticulocytes) with schistocytes on smear indicates microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA). TTP is the classic MAHA with the pentad of MAHA, thrombocytopenia, renal failure, neurologic changes, and fever. Urgent plasma exchange is the treatment.
Hereditary spherocytosis shows spherocytes (not schistocytes) with positive osmotic fragility test. It's a membrane defect causing extravascular hemolysis.
AIHA would show a positive direct Coombs test and spherocytes on smear — not schistocytes. The negative Coombs test rules out antibody-mediated hemolysis.

questions.explanation: This question tests hematologic clinical reasoning. Key hematology principles: interpret CBC and peripheral smear findings systematically, classify anemias by MCV, recognize coagulopathies by PT/PTT patterns, and identify oncologic emergencies requiring urgent intervention.
questions.verifiedExplanation: This constellation — severe anemia with markedly elevated reticulocyte count (indicating brisk hemolysis), very high LDH, and thrombocytopenia — combined with schistocytes on smear points to thrombotic microangiopathy. Given the pentad (microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, neurologic changes, renal impairment, fever), TTP should be strongly suspected. ADAMTS13 activity testing is diagnostic. Treatment is urgent plasma exchange — do NOT delay for ADAMTS13 results if clinical suspicion is high.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: TTP with microangiopathic hemolytic anemia and thrombocytopenia

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with metastatic pancreatic cancer develops diffuse oozing from IV sites, petechiae, and hematuria. Labs show platelets 35,000/μL, PT/INR elevated, fibrinogen 85 mg/dL, and D-dimer >20 μg/mL. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Liver failure can cause thrombocytopenia and prolonged PT, but fibrinogen is typically preserved until end-stage disease, and D-dimer elevation would not be this extreme. The clinical context of malignancy with bleeding strongly favors DIC.
ITP causes isolated thrombocytopenia with normal coagulation studies and normal fibrinogen. The coagulation factor consumption (prolonged PT, low fibrinogen) and elevated D-dimer point to DIC.

DIC is characterized by simultaneous activation of coagulation and fibrinolysis, leading to consumption of platelets and clotting factors. The classic lab pattern is thrombocytopenia, prolonged PT/aPTT, low fibrinogen (<150 mg/dL), and markedly elevated D-dimer. Malignancy is a common trigger. Treatment focuses on the underlying cause plus supportive care with blood products.
Vitamin K deficiency prolongs PT but does not cause thrombocytopenia or significantly elevate D-dimer. Fibrinogen would be normal.

questions.explanation: This question tests hematologic clinical reasoning. Key hematology principles: interpret CBC and peripheral smear findings systematically, classify anemias by MCV, recognize coagulopathies by PT/PTT patterns, and identify oncologic emergencies requiring urgent intervention.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Diffuse oozing from multiple sites (IV lines, surgical wounds, mucosal surfaces) in a patient with cancer is the hallmark presentation of acute DIC. Underlying malignancy (particularly pancreatic adenocarcinoma, acute promyelocytic leukemia) triggers the coagulation cascade through tissue factor release, causing widespread microvascular thrombosis that consumes platelets and clotting factors, paradoxically leading to bleeding. Lab hallmarks: thrombocytopenia, prolonged PT/aPTT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer, and schistocytes.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: DIC with consumption of platelets and clotting factors causing paradoxical bleeding

questions.stem: In a patient with acute DIC secondary to sepsis, which is the most appropriate initial management priority?

choices.text: Administer tranexamic acid to stop fibrinolysis
choices.feedback: Antifibrinolytics like tranexamic acid are generally contraindicated in DIC because inhibiting fibrinolysis while coagulation is active can worsen microvascular thrombosis and organ damage.
Plasma exchange is the treatment for TTP, not DIC. While FFP replacement may be needed in DIC to replace consumed clotting factors, the volume exchange of plasmapheresis is not indicated.

Therapeutic heparin is controversial and generally contraindicated in acute DIC with active bleeding. Low-dose heparin may be considered in chronic/compensated DIC with thrombotic predominance (e.g., Trousseau syndrome), but not in acute hemorrhagic DIC.

choices.text: Treat the underlying sepsis with antibiotics and source control
choices.feedback: The cornerstone of DIC management is treating the underlying cause. In sepsis-triggered DIC, this means aggressive antibiotics and source control. Supportive measures (platelets, FFP, cryoprecipitate) address bleeding symptoms, but DIC will not resolve without treating the trigger. Transfuse platelets if <10,000 or <50,000 with active bleeding; give cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <100 mg/dL.

questions.explanation: DIC is a consumptive coagulopathy with simultaneous thrombosis and bleeding. Lab findings: elevated PT/PTT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer, low platelets, schistocytes on smear. Most common triggers: sepsis, trauma, malignancy (especially acute promyelocytic leukemia), obstetric complications (amniotic fluid embolism, placental abruption). Treatment: address the underlying cause, supportive transfusion (platelets if <10K or bleeding, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <100, FFP for active bleeding with prolonged PT/PTT).
questions.verifiedExplanation: The most critical step in managing acute DIC is treating the underlying cause — in sepsis, this means antibiotics and source control. DIC is always secondary to another process, and no amount of blood product replacement will resolve DIC if the trigger continues. Supportive measures include platelet transfusion (if <10,000 or <50,000 with active bleeding), cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL, and FFP for prolonged PT/aPTT with bleeding. Heparin is generally avoided in acute hemorrhagic DIC.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: treating the underlying cause is the cornerstone of DIC management

questions.stem: Which peripheral blood smear finding is most characteristic of DIC?

choices.feedback: Howell-Jolly bodies are nuclear remnants seen in asplenic patients. They indicate absent splenic filtering, not DIC.

Schistocytes result from mechanical shearing of RBCs as they pass through fibrin strands deposited in the microvasculature. This microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA) is a hallmark of DIC. Other MAHA causes include TTP/HUS — distinguish by clinical context and ADAMTS13 levels.
Spherocytes indicate hereditary spherocytosis or autoimmune hemolytic anemia (partial phagocytosis of antibody-coated RBCs), not the mechanical fragmentation of DIC.
Rouleaux (stacked coin appearance) suggests elevated globulins as in multiple myeloma or chronic inflammation, not DIC.

questions.explanation: Correct: Schistocytes (fragmented red blood cells).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Schistocytes (fragmented RBCs) are the characteristic peripheral smear finding in DIC, resulting from mechanical shearing of red cells through fibrin strands deposited in small vessels. Their presence confirms microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA), which is a hallmark of DIC. The complete DIC lab profile includes: schistocytes on smear, thrombocytopenia, prolonged PT/aPTT, low fibrinogen, and markedly elevated D-dimer/FDPs.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: schistocytes from microangiopathic hemolysis in DIC

questions.stem: A postpartum patient develops acute DIC with fibrinogen of 65 mg/dL and active hemorrhage. Which blood product is most specifically indicated to raise her fibrinogen?

choices.feedback: PRBCs replace oxygen-carrying capacity but contain no clotting factors or fibrinogen. They may be needed concurrently but don't address the coagulopathy.

Cryoprecipitate is the concentrated source of fibrinogen (each unit raises fibrinogen ~5-10 mg/dL), along with factor VIII, vWF, and factor XIII. It is specifically indicated when fibrinogen is <100 mg/dL with bleeding. A typical dose is 10 units. Obstetric causes of DIC include placental abruption, amniotic fluid embolism, HELLP, and retained products.
Platelets address thrombocytopenia but do not contain fibrinogen. This patient's primary need is fibrinogen replacement for her critically low level.

FFP replaces all clotting factors but has relatively low fibrinogen concentration per volume. It is useful for prolonged PT/PTT but cryoprecipitate is more efficient for specifically raising fibrinogen.

questions.explanation: DIC is a consumptive coagulopathy with simultaneous thrombosis and bleeding. Lab findings: elevated PT/PTT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer, low platelets, schistocytes on smear. Most common triggers: sepsis, trauma, malignancy (especially acute promyelocytic leukemia), obstetric complications (amniotic fluid embolism, placental abruption). Treatment: address the underlying cause, supportive transfusion (platelets if <10K or bleeding, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <100, FFP for active bleeding with prolonged PT/PTT).
questions.verifiedExplanation: In acute DIC with fibrinogen <100 mg/dL and active bleeding, cryoprecipitate is the blood product of choice because it is the most concentrated source of fibrinogen (also contains factor VIII, vWF, and factor XIII). Each unit of cryoprecipitate raises fibrinogen by approximately 5-10 mg/dL. A typical dose is 10 units. FFP provides all clotting factors but is a less concentrated fibrinogen source. Target fibrinogen >100-150 mg/dL in actively bleeding patients.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen replacement in DIC

questions.stem: Which laboratory finding best distinguishes DIC from severe liver disease as the cause of coagulopathy?

choices.text: D-dimer markedly elevated with low fibrinogen
choices.feedback: DIC shows markedly elevated D-dimer (from active fibrin formation and lysis) combined with low fibrinogen (consumed). In liver disease, D-dimer may be mildly elevated (decreased hepatic clearance) but fibrinogen is often preserved or even elevated (acute phase reactant) until end-stage. Additionally, factor VIII is elevated in liver disease (made by endothelium) but consumed in DIC.
Both DIC and liver disease decrease antithrombin III — consumed in DIC and underproduced in liver disease. This doesn't distinguish them.

choices.text: Prolonged PT with normal platelet count
choices.feedback: Both conditions can prolong PT. Liver disease may spare platelets initially, but this alone doesn't distinguish them. DIC characteristically consumes platelets.
Elevated indirect bilirubin can occur in both — from hemolysis in DIC and from hepatocyte dysfunction in liver disease. It's not a reliable distinguishing feature.

questions.explanation: DIC is a consumptive coagulopathy with simultaneous thrombosis and bleeding. Lab findings: elevated PT/PTT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer, low platelets, schistocytes on smear. Most common triggers: sepsis, trauma, malignancy (especially acute promyelocytic leukemia), obstetric complications (amniotic fluid embolism, placental abruption). Treatment: address the underlying cause, supportive transfusion (platelets if <10K or bleeding, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <100, FFP for active bleeding with prolonged PT/PTT).
questions.verifiedExplanation: D-dimer markedly elevated with low fibrinogen best distinguishes DIC from severe liver disease. Both conditions can show prolonged PT/aPTT and thrombocytopenia, but in liver disease, D-dimer is only mildly elevated (liver produces fibrinogen and clears D-dimer; in liver failure, fibrinogen production drops but D-dimer clearance also drops, keeping levels relatively low). In DIC, the massive fibrin deposition and secondary fibrinolysis generate markedly elevated D-dimer while fibrinogen is actively consumed.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: markedly elevated D-dimer with low fibrinogen distinguishes DIC from liver disease

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man receiving chemotherapy for AML presents with temperature 38.5°C and absolute neutrophil count (ANC) of 300/μL. After blood cultures are drawn, what is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic?

choices.feedback: Neutropenic fever (ANC <500 + temp ≥38.3°C single or ≥38.0°C sustained) requires urgent empiric antipseudomonal coverage. IDSA guidelines recommend monotherapy with cefepime, meropenem, or piperacillin-tazobactam. Antibiotics must be started within 60 minutes — delays increase mortality. Add vancomycin only for specific indications (mucositis, catheter infection, skin/soft tissue, hemodynamic instability, prior MRSA).

Vancomycin is not routinely added to empiric coverage. It should be reserved for specific indications. Aztreonam alone provides gram-negative but not reliable gram-positive coverage and is typically combined with vancomycin only in severe beta-lactam allergy.

Oral fluoroquinolone plus amoxicillin-clavulanate is an option for LOW-RISK outpatient neutropenic fever (MASCC score ≥21). This AML patient receiving intensive chemo is high-risk and requires IV antipseudomonal therapy.

Fluoroquinolone monotherapy is inadequate for high-risk neutropenic fever. Levofloxacin is used for prophylaxis during expected prolonged neutropenia, but empiric treatment of febrile neutropenia requires antipseudomonal beta-lactam therapy.

questions.explanation: This question tests hematologic clinical reasoning. Key hematology principles: interpret CBC and peripheral smear findings systematically, classify anemias by MCV, recognize coagulopathies by PT/PTT patterns, and identify oncologic emergencies requiring urgent intervention.
questions.verifiedExplanation: This patient has febrile neutropenia (temperature ≥38.3°C or ≥38.0°C sustained for 1 hour, with ANC <500/μL or expected to decline to <500). The standard empiric treatment is anti-pseudomonal monotherapy with cefepime, meropenem, or piperacillin-tazobactam. Cefepime is first-line at most institutions. Blood cultures must be drawn (both peripheral and from any central line) before starting antibiotics, but antibiotics should NOT be delayed awaiting results. Vancomycin is added only for specific indications (suspected line infection, skin/soft tissue infection, hemodynamic instability).

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam monotherapy as empiric treatment for febrile neutropenia

questions.stem: A neutropenic patient on empiric cefepime for 4 days remains febrile with negative cultures and no identified source. ANC remains <100/μL. What is the most appropriate next step?

choices.feedback: Escalating to a broader antibacterial without evidence of resistant bacterial infection is generally not recommended. The concern at 4+ days of persistent fever is fungal, not bacterial resistance.

Granulocyte transfusions are not standard practice and carry significant risks. G-CSF may be considered to hasten neutrophil recovery, but the immediate concern of persistent fever needs antifungal coverage.
Never discontinue empiric antibiotics in persistently febrile neutropenic patients with ANC <100. The risk of overwhelming bacterial sepsis is too high. Continue antibacterials until ANC recovery.

Persistent febrile neutropenia despite 4-7 days of appropriate antibacterials raises concern for invasive fungal infection (Aspergillus, Candida). Guidelines recommend adding empiric antifungal therapy or a preemptive approach guided by galactomannan/beta-D-glucan and CT chest. Caspofungin/micafungin or voriconazole are appropriate choices. Continue the antibacterial regimen.

questions.explanation: Correct: Add empiric antifungal therapy (e.g., micafungin or voriconazole).
questions.verifiedExplanation: Persistent fever after 4-7 days of appropriate empiric antibacterial therapy in a neutropenic patient warrants addition of empiric antifungal therapy (typically IV micafungin, caspofungin, or amphotericin B), as invasive fungal infection (Aspergillus, Candida) becomes a significant concern with prolonged neutropenia. CT of the chest and sinuses should be obtained. Galactomannan and beta-D-glucan assays can support the diagnosis. Voriconazole is preferred if Aspergillus is suspected based on imaging.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: empiric antifungal therapy after 4-7 days of persistent fever in neutropenia

questions.stem: Which risk stratification tool is used to identify low-risk neutropenic fever patients eligible for outpatient oral antibiotic therapy?

choices.feedback: CURB-65 stratifies community-acquired pneumonia severity, not febrile neutropenia risk.
SOFA (Sequential Organ Failure Assessment) assesses organ dysfunction in sepsis but is not specifically designed for neutropenic fever risk stratification.

MASCC score ≥21 identifies low-risk patients suitable for oral outpatient therapy (ciprofloxacin + amoxicillin-clavulanate). Variables include burden of illness, hypotension, COPD, solid tumor (vs hematologic malignancy), dehydration, and age. High-risk features include expected prolonged neutropenia (>7 days), ANC <100, significant comorbidities, and hemodynamic instability.
Wells score is used for DVT/PE probability assessment, not neutropenic fever risk stratification.

questions.explanation: Correct: MASCC score (Multinational Association for Supportive Care in Cancer).
questions.verifiedExplanation: The MASCC (Multinational Association for Supportive Care in Cancer) score is the validated risk stratification tool for febrile neutropenia. A score ≥21 identifies low-risk patients who may be eligible for oral antibiotics (e.g., ciprofloxacin + amoxicillin-clavulanate) and potentially outpatient management. Factors scored include: burden of illness, hypotension, COPD, solid tumor (vs hematologic), no dehydration, outpatient status, and age <60. High-risk patients (MASCC <21) require IV antibiotics and hospitalization.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: MASCC score ≥21 identifies low-risk febrile neutropenia patients

questions.stem: In a patient with febrile neutropenia, which of the following is a specific indication to ADD vancomycin to the empiric regimen?

choices.text: Positive urine culture for E. coli
choices.feedback: E. coli urinary tract infection is a gram-negative infection adequately covered by the empiric antipseudomonal regimen. Vancomycin adds gram-positive coverage, which is not needed here.
Profound neutropenia alone is not an indication for vancomycin. The empiric antipseudomonal beta-lactam provides adequate initial gram-positive coverage for most patients.

IDSA guidelines specify adding vancomycin for: suspected catheter-related infection, skin/soft tissue infection, hemodynamic instability/septic shock, pneumonia with MRSA risk, blood cultures positive for gram-positive cocci pending identification, or severe mucositis with fluoroquinolone prophylaxis. Vancomycin should be discontinued at 48-72 hours if cultures are negative for resistant gram-positives.
High fever alone does not warrant vancomycin addition. The height of fever does not predict gram-positive vs gram-negative infection.

questions.explanation: Key detail: related bloodstream infection with erythema at the exit site.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Vancomycin is added to empiric anti-pseudomonal therapy in febrile neutropenia specifically when catheter-related infection is suspected (tunnel infection, port-site erythema, positive line cultures), hemodynamic instability/septic shock, skin or soft tissue infection, known MRSA colonization, or severe mucositis with concern for viridans group streptococcal bacteremia. Vancomycin is NOT part of routine empiric coverage and should be discontinued at 48-72 hours if cultures are negative and no indication persists.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: vancomycin added for specific indications including suspected catheter infection

questions.stem: A neutropenic patient's ANC recovers to 800/μL. They have been afebrile for 48 hours on cefepime with negative cultures. What is the next step regarding antibiotics?

choices.feedback: With ANC recovering and fever resolved, prophylactic antibiotics are not needed. Fluoroquinolone prophylaxis is considered during expected prolonged neutropenia, not after recovery.

choices.text: Continue cefepime for a total of 14 days
choices.feedback: A fixed 14-day course is not appropriate for culture-negative febrile neutropenia. Duration is guided by ANC recovery and clinical response, not a predetermined timeframe.
There is no indication to add metronidazole. With ANC recovery, clinical improvement, and negative cultures, the appropriate action is to stop antibiotics.

When ANC recovers to ≥500/μL and the patient has been afebrile for ≥48 hours with negative cultures and no identified infection, empiric antibiotics can be safely discontinued. If an infection was identified, complete a full treatment course regardless of ANC recovery. Premature discontinuation in still-neutropenic patients risks relapse.

questions.explanation: Febrile neutropenia (ANC <500/mcL or expected to fall below 500 within 48h + temperature ≥38.3°C or ≥38.0°C sustained for 1h) is an oncologic emergency. Blood cultures before antibiotics, then empiric anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam (cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam, or meropenem) within 1 hour. Add vancomycin only for specific indications (hemodynamic instability, skin/soft tissue infection, catheter-related infection). MASCC score ≥21 identifies low-risk patients eligible for outpatient management.
questions.verifiedExplanation: Antibiotics can be safely discontinued in a febrile neutropenia patient when the ANC recovers to >500/μL and the patient has been afebrile for at least 48 hours. If cultures are negative and the patient is clinically well, some guidelines allow discontinuation when the ANC is recovering (>100/μL with upward trend) if the patient has been afebrile ≥48 hours. Continuing antibiotics beyond necessary duration increases the risk of C. difficile and resistant organism selection.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: antibiotics discontinued when ANC recovers above 500 and patient afebrile for 48 hours

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with diabetes presents with a non-healing plantar ulcer for 6 weeks. On exam, a metal probe inserted into the wound contacts bone. What does this finding most strongly suggest?

choices.feedback: Charcot foot involves neuropathic joint destruction and can mimic osteomyelitis on imaging. However, the probe-to-bone test through an active ulcer points to infectious osteomyelitis. MRI with gadolinium and bone biopsy help distinguish the two.
While a deep abscess could underlie the ulcer, the probe reaching bone indicates direct bony contact and strongly suggests osteomyelitis.
Cellulitis involves only soft tissue. The ability to probe to bone through a deep ulcer strongly suggests the infection has extended to the periosteum or bone itself.

The probe-to-bone test has high specificity (89%) for osteomyelitis in diabetic foot infections. When a sterile probe can palpate bone through an ulcer, the pretest probability of osteomyelitis is very high. Confirm with MRI (most sensitive/specific imaging) and obtain bone biopsy/culture when possible to guide targeted antibiotic therapy (typically 6 weeks IV).

questions.explanation: Infectious disease management follows the principles of identifying the pathogen, selecting appropriate empiric therapy, then narrowing based on culture and sensitivity. Antibiotic stewardship optimizes outcomes while minimizing resistance.
questions.verifiedExplanation: A non-healing plantar ulcer probing to bone in a diabetic patient is highly suggestive of osteomyelitis. The 'probe-to-bone' test has a positive predictive value of approximately 89% in high-risk patients. Diabetic foot osteomyelitis most commonly involves the metatarsal heads and phalanges. S. aureus is the most common organism overall, but diabetic foot infections are often polymicrobial. MRI is the imaging gold standard, and bone biopsy with culture is the diagnostic gold standard for guiding antibiotic therapy.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: probe-to-bone test in diabetic foot ulcer

questions.stem: Which imaging modality is considered the gold standard for diagnosing osteomyelitis?

choices.feedback: Plain films miss early osteomyelitis — periosteal reaction and lytic changes require 30-50% bone loss to become visible, typically 10-14 days after onset. X-rays are useful as initial screening but have poor sensitivity for early disease.

Three-phase bone scan has high sensitivity (~95%) but low specificity (~33%) — it detects increased bone turnover from any cause. It's useful when MRI is unavailable or contraindicated but produces many false positives.

MRI is the imaging gold standard for osteomyelitis with sensitivity 90-100% and specificity 80-90%. It detects bone marrow edema, periosteal reaction, soft tissue involvement, and abscess formation. Gadolinium enhancement helps delineate viable vs necrotic tissue. X-rays are often normal for 10-14 days after onset; bone changes lag behind infection.
CT shows cortical bone detail and sequestra better than MRI but is inferior for detecting bone marrow edema and early infection. It's useful when MRI is contraindicated.

questions.explanation: Osteomyelitis: S. aureus is the most common cause in all age groups. Hematogenous spread most common in children (metaphysis of long bones). Diagnosis: MRI (most sensitive imaging), blood cultures, bone biopsy for definitive culture. Treatment: 4-6 weeks of IV antibiotics targeted to culture (empiric: nafcillin/oxacillin or vancomycin if MRSA risk). Surgical debridement for chronic or complicated cases.
questions.verifiedExplanation: MRI with gadolinium contrast is the gold standard imaging modality for osteomyelitis, with sensitivity and specificity both exceeding 90%. MRI demonstrates bone marrow edema (T1 low signal, T2/STIR high signal), periosteal reaction, and soft tissue involvement. CT is useful for evaluating cortical destruction and sequestrum. Three-phase bone scan is an alternative when MRI is contraindicated, with high sensitivity (~95%) but lower specificity. Plain radiographs are insensitive early — lytic changes require 30-50% bone loss to be visible, typically appearing after 10-14 days.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: MRI gold standard imaging with sensitivity and specificity exceeding 90%

questions.stem: A patient with hematogenous vertebral osteomyelitis has blood cultures growing methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus. What is the recommended treatment duration?

choices.feedback: Oral antibiotics alone are insufficient for vertebral osteomyelitis. While some protocols transition to oral agents after initial IV therapy, 10 days is far too short for osteomyelitis treatment.

choices.text: 6 weeks of IV nafcillin or cefazolin
